title: Abbreviations and Glossing-Symbol Conventions
summary: The tables of interlinear-gloss abbreviations, morpheme-boundary symbols, and notation conventions used in the grammar, with a concordance reconciling divergent conventions across the source literature.
part: Part XXIII — Reference Apparatus (Back Matter)
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Abbreviations and Glossing-Symbol Conventions
Part: Part XXIII — Reference Apparatus (Back Matter)
Summary: The tables of interlinear-gloss abbreviations, morpheme-boundary symbols, and notation conventions used in the grammar, with a concordance reconciling divergent conventions across the source literature.

Morpheme boundary symbols and the interlinear format

The grammar's interlinear examples follow the Leipzig Glossing Rules
comrie2015 , adapted for the morphological structure of Ainu. A
minimal example has three aligned lines: a morphemic segmentation line ( m ),
a gloss line ( g ), and a free translation ( tr ). An optional
surface-form line ( ain ) appears where the orthographic surface differs from
the segmentation; an original-script line ( orig ) preserves katakana source
text where the segmentation is editorial. The methodology governing source
attribution, example selection, and the layout of example headers is set out in
glossing-abbreviations-and-citation .

Three symbols mark morpheme structure within a word:

symbol
function
morphemic / gloss pair

-
affix boundary — derivational morphology or plural stem suffixes
ray-ke / die- caus
'kill'

=
person-marker boundary (orthographic convention — not a clitic claim)
ku=kor / 1sg.a =have 'I have it'

.
portmanteau dot — joins fused atoms within one gloss position
1sg.a , 4.s , 2pl.o ,
track. poss

The personal indexes ( ku= , e= ,
eci= , a= , =an ,
=as , and the object prefixes) are uniformly marked with
= throughout the grammar. This follows the orthographic convention begun by
Nakagawa (from his 1980s data-management practice, promulgated in the 1986
語りの中の生活誌 and now dominant in Hokkaidō teaching and
descriptive materials): the = keeps inflectional person marking visually
distinct from derivation and makes the dictionary headword recoverable. It is expressly
not the Leipzig-glossing clitic boundary — Nakagawa himself notes his usage is
unrelated to that convention nakagawa2024 58–60 . Other sources notate the
same elements differently: Satō and Ijäs write them with hyphens, Hattori and Tamura solid,
Kirikae with a space; examples quoted from those sources are renotated to =
here. The markers' actual morphological status — clitic-like for
=an , affix-like for the prefixal set — is mixed and contested, and
is treated in person-marking-architecture and
personal-affix-template-and-ordering boundary-status .
Derivational material — applicative prefixes, the causative suffix
-ke , the reflexive and reciprocal prefixes, and the plural
stem suffix -pa — is separated with - .

The portmanteau dot . joins fused atoms within a single gloss position
without inserting a morpheme boundary on the morphemic line. Two uses arise: (1)
person-and-role portmanteaux ( 1sg.a for a single morpheme
encoding both first-person singular and agent), and (2) a lexical-root gloss combined
with a grammatical atom ( track. poss for
ru-we 'the trace of —'). Every atom in a dot-joined sequence
must exist in the inventory below.

ku=ray-ke | 1SG.A=die-CAUS | I kill (something). | Illustrating the three boundary symbols: = separates the person marker ku= from the stem (an orthographic convention, not a clitic analysis); - separates the verb root ray from the CAUS suffix. The portmanteau gloss 1SG.A joins two atoms with the dot, expressing person (1SG) and role (A) from a single morpheme.

Gloss abbreviation inventory

All grammatical-category labels in gloss lines are drawn from the table below. The
grammar's <Ex> component validates every uppercase token at build
time and throws if an atom is absent. Lexical glosses — the ordinary English words
that translate roots and stems — are lowercase and do not require registration.
The inventory follows the Leipzig Glossing Rules base set comrie2015
extended with categories specific to the description of Hokkaido Ainu.

Notes on role labels A, S, and O

A , S , and O are the core
argument-role labels of the S/A/O framework used throughout the grammar: A for the
transitive subject (agent), S for the sole argument of an intransitive verb, and O
for the transitive object (patient). The choice of these labels over
erg and abs reflects the
mixed alignment of the Hokkaido paradigm — where 1SG is nominative–accusative (S=A≠O),
the second and third persons are neutral (S=A=O), and the first-person plural exclusive
and the fourth person are tripartite (S≠A≠O) — so neither an ergative nor a nominative
characterization covers the whole system. The paradigm and the alignment question are
set out in person-marking-architecture .

Notes on the fourth person

4 labels the formal fourth person by form class, following
Nakagawa's framework of four numbered persons nakagawa2024 163–179 .
INDEF is used when the gloss targets the referential value —
indefinite or impersonal — rather than the form class. The two atoms may appear in
portmanteau sequences: 4.A, 4.S, and 4.O index the three role slots of the
fourth-person form set. The full referential range of the fourth person — inclusive
'we', impersonal agent-defocusing, honorific second person, narrative first person,
and logophoric reference — is treated in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Notes on POSS and ANTIP

POSS glosses two distinct but related phenomena: the person-possessive
prefix slot ( ku=cise , 1sg.poss =house
'my house'), and the affiliative/possessed suffix that marks the "belonging form" of
a noun or that nominalizes a clause-internal constituent ( ru-we ,
track- poss , 'the trace of —'). The concept form versus the
affiliative (possessed) form is defined in concept-form-affiliative-form ;
the English label POSS here corresponds to what the Japanese tradition calls 所属形
shozokuei , sometimes rendered AFF (affiliative) in English-medium work
bugaeva2022 .

ANTIP glosses the detransitivizing prefix
i- throughout the grammar, following Bugaeva's explicit
antipassive analysis bugaeva2021antip . Its analysis and its boundary with
the homophonous fourth-person object prefix i= are treated in
antipassive-detransitive-i .

Notes on evidential labels

Four labels cover the Hokkaido Ainu evidential system: INFR
(inferential), REP (reportative), PERS (personal
— evidential of direct personal knowledge), and EVID (generic
evidential, used where the specific type is not at issue or is analytically
undifferentiated in the source). These map onto the nominalization-plus-copula
evidential construction described in evidential-schema-overview . The
relationship to the more fine-grained labeling of Dal Corso (2018) is set out in
the concordance section below.

Dialect and locality labels

Every interlinear example carries a dialect tag indicating which variety of Ainu it
represents. The closed set of recognized tags is listed below; the tag appears in the
example header as the dial attribute. Finer locality — the specific
community, speaker, or recording site — is recorded in the place field rather
than as a new dialect tag. The dialect survey and the principles governing example
selection are set out in dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

tag
variety

HK Hokkaido (dialect not further specified)

SAR Saru

CHI Chitose

ISH Ishikari

TOK Tokachi

HOR Horobetsu

SHI Shizunai

ASA Asahikawa

YAK Yakumo

SA Sakhalin (cited for contrast)

KU Kuril (cited for contrast)

The tag HK is used where a source covers more than one Hokkaido locality or where
the specific site cannot be determined. Sakhalin (SA) and Kuril (KU) examples appear
only to illuminate contrasts with the Hokkaido system and are always tagged as such.
No ad-hoc locality tags are introduced; the place field carries any
further detail.

Notation conventions

Several conventional marks appear outside the three-line gloss proper:

mark
use

*
Placed before a word or morpheme: (a) reconstructed or diachronically
postulated form (Proto-Ainu); or (b) ungrammatical form in a cited or
constructed example. Context distinguishes the two uses.

?
Marginal, degraded, or speaker-variable acceptability.

Ø
Zero exponent (null morph); used in paradigm tables and in glosses where
a grammatical category is present but has no phonological realization (third
person in Hokkaido Ainu is the main case).

‹grade›
Evidence-grade label scoping over the immediately preceding claim. The
closed set is: ‹consensus›, ‹contested›, ‹corpus-confirmed›,
‹corpus-suggested›, ‹speculative›. Unmarked prose is treated as consensus
and is not labelled; analyses original to this grammar are marked
‹speculative› and identified as such in the prose.

The Latin-script phonemic transcription — the grammar's primary writing system for
Ainu forms — is described in latin-phonemic-transcription . Orthographic
variation, word-division conventions, and the relationship between different
romanization standards in the literature are covered in
orthographic-standardization-and-word-division . Technical grammatical
terms used throughout the grammar are defined in
glossary-grammatical-terms .

Cross-source glossing concordance

The descriptive literature on Hokkaido Ainu uses divergent glossing conventions.
English-medium typological treatments and Japanese-medium reference grammars do not
share a unified standard, and examples drawn from these sources and re-glossed
here may carry different labels than the originals. The table below maps the most
consequential divergences.

The evidential labels warrant the most attention for readers moving between this
grammar and Dal Corso (2018). Dal Corso employs a fine-grained eight-term system
organized by two parameters — source reliability and event accessibility — yielding
dir.rsn (direct/reasoning), dir.knw
(direct/knowledge), ind.vis (indirect/visual),
ind.rep (indirect/reportative), ind.sens
(indirect/sensory), and their extensions dalcorso2018 304–309 . This
grammar uses four simplified labels (INFR, REP, PERS, EVID) that correspond to
the grammatical construction rather than to Dal Corso's semantic parameters; the
mapping is discussed alongside the construction in evidential-schema-overview .

Refsing (1986) predates the Leipzig Glossing Rules refsing1986 and uses
a non-standardized labeling system with lowercase labels; readers consulting her
Shizunai paradigm tables should use this grammar's abbreviation inventory as the
mapping reference. The Japanese-medium grammars of Satō (2008) and Nakagawa (2024)
work predominantly with Japanese metalanguage rather than Latin-script gloss
abbreviations sato2008 ; nakagawa2024 ; where they do supply
English-style labels in comparative sections, these are generally compatible with
the inventory here.

The AFF (affiliative) versus POSS (possessive) difference reflects a genuine
terminological divergence between traditions. Bugaeva aligns with the Japanese
所属形 ( shozokuei ) label — translating it as "affiliative" — while this
grammar uses POSS to emphasize the morphological category (the "belonging form"
that the noun takes when possessed or when it heads a nominalized constituent).
The concept form versus affiliative-form opposition, and the morphological evidence
bearing on the choice, are defined in concept-form-affiliative-form .

title: Abilitative easkay / eaykap (Ability and Possibility)
summary: easkay 'be able to' and its lexicalized negative counterpart eaykap 'cannot' encode root ability and possibility.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Abilitative easkay / eaykap (Ability and Possibility)
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: easkay 'be able to' and its lexicalized negative counterpart eaykap 'cannot' encode root ability and possibility.

The abilitative set and its position in the TAM system

Hokkaido Ainu encodes ability and inability through a set of transitive verbs that
grammaticalize as auxiliaries. Nakagawa's 表75 inventories eight
such forms under the label 他動詞の助動詞的用法 'transitive verbs used
auxiliarially' nakagawa2024 314–319 ; Bugaeva places the same set in
her third TAM frame beside the true auxiliaries ( a ,
nankor ) and the pair verbs
( kor an , wa an ), distinguished by the
property that person affixes fall on the main verb rather than on the ability predicate
bugaeva2012 494 . The full architecture of these three frames is set
out in tam-overview .

form
standalone gloss
auxiliary value
nuance
source

easkay
be able
can, is able to
general ability or possibility
nakagawa2024 315

eaykap
be unable
cannot, is unable to
broad inability (lack of skill or circumstance)
nakagawa2024 315

koyaykus
—
cannot (situational)
inability due to a circumstantial obstacle specifically
nakagawa2024 316

niwkes
cannot finish
cannot do to completion
capacity limit; the task exceeds the agent's resources
nakagawa2024 316

etoranne
it is tedious, troublesome
finds it troublesome to; reluctant
unwillingness; the agent is able but will not
nakagawa2024 316

nukuri
—
too draining to do
the action is too costly or exhausting for the agent
nakagawa2024 316

amkir
know by acquaintance
have done, have experience of
experiential positive
nakagawa2024 314

eramiskari
not know
have never done
