nakagawa2024 314

The two highest-frequency members, easkay be able to and
eaykap be unable to , together rank among the most frequent modal
forms in the corpus ( easkay 1,324 tokens, rank 139;
eaykap 1,082 tokens, rank 169) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The
experiential pair amkir and eramiskari
share the same constructional frame and the same lexical-negation pattern; their
interaction with the negation system is developed in
negation-ability-cognition-predicates .

The V(ka)Vt frame: morphology and the person locus

All eight members of the set share the
tam-overview transitive-verb-auxiliary frame : the main predicate
V carries the person affixes, and the ability auxiliary
Vt follows it person-unmarked. This distinguishes the
construction from the pair-verb frame ( kor an ,
wa an ), where both the main verb and the existential carry
person affixes, and aligns it with the basic auxiliaries
( a , nankor ,
easirki ), which are also person-unmarked
nakagawa2024 314 ; bugaeva2012 494 ‹consensus›.

The particle ka even, also may intervene between the main verb and the
ability auxiliary. This insertion is a feature of the entire transitive-verb-auxiliary
class and is blocked for the basic auxiliaries nakagawa2024 314 . Under
inability, ka functions as a scalar amplifier — adding a sense
of 'not even' — and is especially frequent before eaykap in
subordinate and causal clauses.

The morphological shape of easkay and
eaykap reflects the
applicative-e e- applicative prefix : the roots
askay be able and aykap be unable also occur without
this prefix as intransitive predicates with the same meanings
nakagawa2024 315 . In the auxiliary function the
e- is part of the lexical form rather than a live derivational
operation, and person affixes go exclusively on the main verb.

One additional feature separates this class from other auxiliaries: the preceding verb
root can be incorporated directly into easkay , producing a
compound to which the person suffix then attaches as a unit:

sinotca-easkay=an | song.compose-be.able=4.S | I could compose songs. | The verb root sinotca 'compose/perform songs' is incorporated into easkay; the fourth-person suffix =an attaches to the whole compound as a unit. The pattern parallels incorporated desideratives such as arpa=rusuy (Nakagawa 2024: 314–315). | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8(2) [aa-irc/007#380]

The compound sinotca-easkay behaves as a single verb stem for
person-affix attachment; the periphrastic sequence would place the affix on the main
verb sinotca alone
nakagawa2024 314–315 .

easkay: ability and possibility

easkay be able to, can covers general ability — whether rooted in
acquired skill, physical capacity, or favourable circumstance — and possibility. It
combines with intransitive and transitive main verbs without distinction
nakagawa2024 315 . In the example below the main verb is intransitive
and the scalar ka 'even' intervenes:

aoka ka sikunu=an easkay pe ne na | we even survive=4.S be.able NMLZ COP FIN | We too can survive. | The fourth-person inclusive suffix =an indexes the subject of sikunu 'survive'; easkay is person-unmarked. pe ne na nominalizes the ability clause and predicates it with the copula. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8(2) [aa-irc/007#127]

With a transitive main verb the person affixes reflect the standard agent–object
alignment of that verb, and easkay remains unmarked:

yaykata kina poka a=kar easkay hi neno | by.oneself greens just 4.A=gather be.able NMLZ since | from when she could gather wild greens by herself | The fourth-person agent prefix a= marks the transitive verb kar 'gather'; easkay follows without any affix. The nominalized hi neno frames the ability event as a temporal onset point. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 9 [aa-irc/009#305]

Both examples confirm that the ability construction is a full finite clause: it takes
the same nominalization apparatus as any other predicate, and the ability auxiliary
occupies the slot above the main predicate, not a bound morphological position within it.

eaykap and koyaykus: inability distinguished by source

eaykap be unable to, cannot is the dedicated negative counterpart of
easkay . The relationship is lexical: inability is encoded in
eaykap as a separate form, and negating
easkay with somo does not produce the
same value nakagawa2024 315 ; ijas2023 ‹consensus›.
eaykap is semantically neutral as to whether the source of
inability lies in the agent (lack of skill, physical incapacity) or in external
circumstance. The scalar ka 'even' frequently precedes it in
subordinate clauses:

soyne=an ka eaykap kusu | go.out=4.S even be.unable because | because I cannot go outside, | soyne 'go outside' is intransitive; =an is the fourth-person S affix; ka eaykap is the scalar-amplified inability expression in a causal clause. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#48]

aynu mosir ka ta ney ta pakno an=an ka eaykap kusu | human land even LOC wherever LOC until exist=4.S even be.unable because | because I cannot stay in the human realm indefinitely, | The main verb is an 'exist/stay' with fourth-person =an; ka eaykap expresses the inability to continue. The concessive ka ta 'even LOC' before the locative phrase and the second ka before eaykap are both scalar. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#214]

Because ka occupies the slot between the main verb and
eaykap , the standard clausal negator
somo can negate the main verb without scoping over the ability
predicate. The following example denies the main verb action, leaving
eaykap outside the scope of
somo and producing a double-negative reading:

somo a=ahunke eaykap pe ne na | NEG 4.A=let.in be.unable NMLZ COP FIN | There is no way one cannot let (you) in. | somo negates the main verb ahunke 'let in'; eaykap 'be unable' lies outside the scope of negation, yielding the reading 'cannot not let in'. The clause-final pe ne na nominalizes and predicates the proposition. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8(2) [aa-irc/007#211]

The scope pattern is the same as for clause-internal negation before
ruwe ne (see ruwe-ne-inferential ): the negator
targets the inner predicate, and the outer modal or evidential element keeps its
position above it. The
negation-somo-standard-clausal standard clausal negation system
provides the full treatment of somo scope.

koyaykus narrows the source of inability to the situational dimension: it
applies when the agent is in principle capable but a circumstance prevents the action,
rather than when the agent simply lacks skill or capacity
nakagawa2024 316 . The contrast with
eaykap is one of reason rather than degree. Ijäs illustrates
the distinction in a constructed example ijas2023 :

e=ma eaykap yakun atuy or ta e=sus ka koyaykus | 2SG.S=walk be.unable COND sea place LOC 2SG.S=swim even circ.unable | If you cannot walk, you cannot even swim in the sea. | Ijäs Lesson 9 constructed example. eaykap covers intrinsic inability (cannot walk); koyaykus marks a circumstantial obstacle that prevents the swimming. The scalar ka reinforces koyaykus. | ijas2023

The morphological structure of koyaykus — comprising the
applicative-ko ko- applicative prefix , the
reflexive yay- , and a root kus — is
internally visible, but the sources consulted do not derive the circumstantial-obstacle
semantics compositionally from this decomposition
nakagawa2024 316 ‹speculative›.

Lexical negation and the negation-ability interface

The ability predicates in this set do not combine with the standard clausal negator
somo not . Ability and inability are lexicalized in separate words
rather than derived by negating a single base, and the same holds for the experiential
pair amkir / eramiskari
nakagawa2024 314–316 ; ijas2023 ‹consensus›. The pattern
of suppletive ability lexemes — a positive predicate paired with a dedicated negative
rather than a negated positive — is typologically widespread and consistent with the
tendency for high-frequency ability expressions to lexicalize negation directly
bybee1994 .

A community observation (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-25) adds that the
ability periphrases, including easkay and its set, serve in
the contexts where languages with infinitives would use a bare infinitival complement;
nominalization by pe , hi , or
kuni substitutes when a nominal form is required, fitting the
general head-marking profile of Ainu in which the verb always carries person
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›.

The relationship between the transitive-auxiliary pair
easkay / eaykap and their intransitive
roots askay / aykap parallels the
applicative-e e- applicative derivational pattern, but the auxiliary
function is grammaticalized and the e- is no longer a live
derivation in this context nakagawa2024 315 ‹speculative›.

The full treatment of lexical negation in ability and cognition predicates — including
the experiential pair and the role of ka strengthening
( ka eramiskari ) — belongs to
negation-ability-cognition-predicates . The deontic-necessity forms
( easirki , kuni ,
yak pirka ) are treated in
deontic-necessity , and the epistemic and intentive auxiliaries
( nankor , kusu ne ,
rusuy ) in epistemic-irrealis-modality .

title: Accent in Compounds and under Affixation and Cliticization
summary: How prosodic-word formation reorganizes accent in compounds and when affixes and clitics attach.
part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Accent in Compounds and under Affixation and Cliticization
Part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
Summary: How prosodic-word formation reorganizes accent in compounds and when affixes and clitics attach.

The prosodic word and accent reapplication

In all standard-accent dialects of Hokkaido Ainu, high pitch is assigned to σ1 when σ1 is
closed (has a coda), and to σ2 otherwise — with a memorized set of lexical exceptions (see
pitch-accent-placement-rule ). These rules evaluate the prosodic word
as their domain: a prefix or compounding element that fuses with a base creates a new
prosodic word, and the placement rules are reapplied from the new left edge
nakagawa2024 52–54 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1–5.2 ;
sato2008 §2.6 . Prefixation may therefore shift accent inward, and
compounding suppresses all accent peaks after the first. Affixes divide accordingly: those
that enter the prosodic word of their base shift its accent; those that remain outside it
leave the base accent intact. The accent data thereby provide one of the clearest
phonological diagnostics for the prefix/clitic boundary in the person-affix paradigm. The
Yakumo irregular system, where 原則2 shifts one position further right, produces different
surface outputs for the same derivations but applies the same prosodic-word logic; its
characterization is given in pitch-accent-placement-rule .

Compound nouns: first-element priority

When two or more free forms combine as a compound noun, the result carries a single accent
peak. nakagawa2024 54 states the general outcome as a first-element
priority rule: only the front element's lexical accent survives; all syllables belonging
to rear elements are low. For a polysyllabic front element, the accent it bears in
isolation — as assigned by the placement rules to that element's own σ1 — is retained
unchanged.

Compound accent: first-element priority (standard-accent dialects)

front element
accent
compound
compound accent
gloss
source

kamuy
kamúy
kamuy+nomi
kamúynomi
'pray to a deity'
nakagawa2024 54

horka
hórka
horka+reye+p
hórkareyep
'crayfish'
nakagawa2024 54

cise
cisé
cise+kor+kur
cisékorkur
'householder'
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-12-09)

kotan
kotán
kotan+kor+kur
kotánkorkur
'village chief'
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-12-09)

ni (monosyllabic)
ní
ni+tek
nítek
'branch'
nakagawa2024 54

The accent positions follow from the placement rules applied to the front element in
isolation. kamúy has open σ1 ( ka ) → 原則2
→ accent on σ2 mú ; that accent is the sole high syllable of
kamúynomi . hórka has closed σ1
( hor ) → 原則1 → accent on hór ; that accent
survives in hórkareyep . The monosyllabic
ni carries the single available accent, giving
nítek .

For cisékorkur and kotánkorkur , the community
reports that both are single words with the front-element accent as their sole peak
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-12-09) ‹corpus-suggested›. The assignments
follow from the principle: cise 'house' has open σ1
( ci ) → 原則2 → accent on sé , which becomes
the sole peak of cisékorkur 'householder';
kotan 'village' has open σ1 ( ko ) → 原則2 →
accent on tán , retained in kotánkorkur
'village chief'.

The first-element priority is a strong default. shiraishi2022 §5.2 notes a
set of lexicalized compound nouns that carry the iambic pattern even when the front
element's isolated accent would predict otherwise: hasínaw ,
cepétu 'fish snout', nayétok 'stream
source' all show accent on σ2 of the compound, as if the form were an underived disyllabic
root. These represent lexicalized items requiring per-form verification ‹contested›. The
prosodic-unit question (mora vs syllable) bearing on the weight analysis is addressed in
mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis ; attested minimal-pair contrasts in
compound accent are listed in lexical-contrastive-accent-minimal-pairs .

somo and somósomo as a prosodic diagnostic

The negative adverb somo not illustrates the prosodic-word boundary
transparently. As an independent word, somo has open σ1
( so ) and thus accent on σ2 by 原則2:
somó . This is its accent in phrases such as
ka somó ki 'does not do even', where
somo is a separate prosodic word. When doubled to form the
intensified negation somosomo 'not at all, definitely not', the
pair becomes a single prosodic word: only the front element's accent survives, giving
somósomo with a single high syllable on mó
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-12-09) ‹corpus-suggested›. The contrast
ka somó ki (two prosodic words) versus
somósomo (one prosodic word) instantiates the same mechanism as
the compound table above. The negation system and somo are treated in
negation-somo-standard-clausal .

Derivational prefixes

The reflexive prefix yay-

The reflexive prefix yay- self, reflexive always opens the prosodic word
as σ1. yay is a closed syllable (CVC), so 原則1 places the
accent on σ1 — on yáy — regardless of the shape of the following
stem nakagawa2024 53 . The published example is
yáykoytak 'talk to oneself' (<
yay-ko-itak ). The community additionally reports
yáyesiru 'concentrate (on oneself)' as a further illustration
that yáy holds the accent whether the following syllable is
consonant-initial or vowel-initial
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-11-10, attributed to
nakagawa2024 ). Lexicalized yay- derivatives may
deviate and require per-form verification. The distribution of
yay- and the co-reflexive si- in Chitose
is examined in sato2007 ; the morphosyntax of both reflexive prefixes is set
out in applicative-system-overview .

Applicative prefixes as prosodic-word heads

The applicative prefixes e- APPL (body-part/location) ,
ko- APPL (goal/recipient) , and o- APPL (path) open the
prosodic word as a light (CV) σ1 when they attach to a base
nakagawa2024 53 ; bugaeva2006 . Because σ1 is open,
原則2 shifts the accent to σ2 of the new prosodic word, which is the σ1 of the original
base. The result is that prefixation moves the high syllable one position earlier in the
string compared with the free base.

Accent shift under the applicative e- (standard-accent dialects)

free / phrasal form
accent
e- form
accent
gloss (e- form)
source

puni
púni
e=puni
epúni
'lift up (something)'
nakagawa2024 53

kotan ne (phrase)
kotán ne
e=kotan-ne
ekótanne
'belong to a village, be a villager'
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-11-10)

In ekótanne , the prefix e- creates a new
light σ1 → 原則2 → accent on σ2 ( kó ). The free word
kotán accents σ2 ( tán ) by the same rule;
in the applicative form the high syllable has moved one position to the left. The
ekótanne example is a community extrapolation from the general
one-word analysis of applicative-prefixed forms
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-11-10), consistent with the published
epúni nakagawa2024 53 ‹corpus-suggested›. The
morphology and syntax of the applicative prefixes are treated in
applicative-system-overview .

Causative suffixes and the lexicalized koré

Derivational suffixes generally do not displace accent in Hokkaido Ainu
nakagawa2024 53 . The causative suffixes -re ,
-te , -e attached to monosyllabic bases
retain the base accent: e → ére ;
ku → kúre ;
kar → káre .

The verb koré 'give' is a well-attested departure. A regular
causative derivation from kor 'have' +
-e would yield *kóre (σ1
kor = CVC → 原則1 → σ1 accented); the attested form is
koré , with accent on σ2
nakagawa2024 53 ; shiraishi2022 §5.2 . Nakagawa's account
is that the morpheme boundary between kor and
-e has eroded sufficiently for the form to be treated as a
monomorphemic disyllabic root, whereupon σ1 ko is open → 原則2
→ accent on ré . The community proposes alternatively that
koré is a phonological contraction of
korpare 'give to someone', which independently yields σ2 accent
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-12-26) ‹contested›. Both accounts predict
the same surface accentuation; the accent koré is attested in the
Saru corpus:

topenpe atay poka ka somo a=kore | sweet price even also NEG 4.A=give | tópenpe atay póka ka somo a=koré | She couldn't even be given the price of one sweet. | The σ2 accent of koré reflects the iambic pattern of a lexicalized open-initial disyllable (σ1 ko = CV, open → 原則2). The co-existent ditransitive korpare 'give to someone' is attested alongside it in the Saru corpus. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子, isoytak 独話2, AA研アイヌ語資料 [aa-irc/004#105]

Person-affix accent

The shifting class: ku=, e=, ci=, en=

The person-marking prefixes ku= (1SG agent/subject),
e= (2SG), and ci= (inclusive 1PL) form
a single prosodic word with their base
nakagawa2024 52 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 ;
sato2008 §2.6 . Each prefix contributes a light (CV) σ1; 原則2 applies
and the accent falls on σ2 of the new word — one position earlier than the base accent
when the base's own σ1 was open:

Accent shift under person prefixes of the shifting class

base
accent
prefixed form
accent
gloss
source

hopuni
hopúni
ku=hopuni
ku=hópuni
'I rise'
nakagawa2024 52

nanu
nanú
e=nanu
e=nánu
'your face'
nakagawa2024 52

yupihi
yupíhi
ku=yupihi
ku=yúpihi
'my elder brother'
shiraishi2022 §5.1

In each case the base has open σ1 and accents σ2; after the CV prefix is added, σ1 of
the new prosodic word is the prefix itself (open) and σ2 — which is the original σ1 of
the base — receives the high pitch.

In Saru and Chitose, ku= and ci=
additionally lose their vowel before a base-initial vowel other than
i . The conditions for this vowel deletion are described in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction and
glides-w-y . The accent shift precedes deletion, so the shifted accent
survives in the contracted surface form: ku= +
omáre → accent shift → ku=ómáre → vowel
deletion → k=ómare ; ci= +
etú → c=étu
nakagawa2024 52 .

Before a base-initial i , vowel deletion is blocked and glide
formation ( i → y ) substitutes; the accent
shift still applies: ku=ipe → ku=ýpe /
kúype . When the base-initial i is in a
closed syllable, glide formation is itself blocked by the phonotactic constraint against
CCC clusters: ku= + inkar →
ku=ínkar with no glide and accent on σ2
nakagawa2024 52–53 .

The 1SG object prefix en= behaves analogously: it opens the
prosodic word as a closed σ1 ( en , CVC), so 原則1 places the
accent on σ1. In Nakagawa's textbook the phrase
wa én=kore shows the accent on én ,
consistent with this rule
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli 2024-11-10, attributed to
nakagawa2024 ) ‹corpus-suggested›.

The non-shifting class: a=, eci=

The indefinite-person prefix a= and the 2PL prefix
eci= leave the accent of the base unchanged
nakagawa2024 53 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 ;
sato2008 §2.6 . They attach outside the prosodic word of the stem:

Accent retention under person prefixes of the non-shifting class

base
accent
a= / eci= form
accent
gloss

omare
omáre
a=omare
a=omáre
'was put (somewhere)' (4th/indef.)

etu
etú
eci=etu
eci=etú
'your (pl.) nose'

onaha
onáha
a=onaha
a=onáha
'his/her mother' (4th/indef.)

a= and eci= also do not trigger the vowel
contraction that the shifting prefixes undergo in Saru and Chitose after the accent
shift. The combined phonological evidence — no shift, no contraction — aligns with the
morphosyntactic assessment in person-marking-architecture that
a= and eci= are prosodic clitics, while
ku= , e= , ci= , and
en= are prefixes proper. The position-class arrangement of person
affixes in the verb word is set out in verb-word-template .

Secondary accent on personal-number suffixes

When a personal-number suffix ( =an ,
=as ) attaches to a vowel-final verb stem, the suffix carries a
secondary (lower-level) accent alongside the primary stem accent
shiraishi2022 §5.1 . The verb iruska 'be angry'
with the plural suffix gives irúska=àn (primary accent á on
rú , secondary accent à on àn );
ipe 'eat' with =an gives
ipé=àn . When the stem ends in a consonant, the secondary accent
on the suffix is lost: cis=an 'we cry' and
sap=an 'we go out' carry only the primary accent on σ1 (both
stems have closed σ1 → 原則1) with no secondary peak on
=an shiraishi2022 §5.1 . The number-suffix
paradigm is described in verb-word-template and
person-marking-architecture .

Accent-neutral suffixes: -pa and the affiliative -hV

The plural suffix -pa and the affiliative (possessed) suffix
-hV — realizing as -hi ,
-he , -ha etc. according to the vowel-copy
rule — are both accent-neutral: the stem's placement-rule accent survives into the
suffixed form without adjustment nakagawa2024 53 ;
tamura1996 . They attach outside the prosodic word of the stem and do not
produce the reapplication that the prefixal and applicative morphology triggers. The
morphophonology of the affiliative suffix and the echo-vowel alternation are treated in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Prosodic-word status as a morphological diagnostic

The accent patterns reviewed in this chapter offer a phonological diagnostic for
grammatical category that is independent of morphological segmentation. A morpheme that
enters the prosodic word of its host and shifts the accent is a prefix in the prosodic
sense; a morpheme that leaves the host accent untouched is a clitic or a non-incorporating
suffix. Applied to the Hokkaido Ainu person-affix paradigm, the test produces: prefixes —
ku= , e= , ci= ,
en= ; clitics — a= ,
eci=
nakagawa2024 52–53 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 . The same
prosodic-word criterion distinguishes compound-internal structure (a single prosodic word,
one accent peak) from phrasal juxtaposition (two prosodic words with independent accent
peaks).

The compound formation discussed here and the sandhi processes of Part V share the same
domain boundary. Coda assimilation ( r+t→tt ,
n+s→ys , etc.) applies most consistently within the prosodic word;
the accent test provides an independent means of determining which phonological domain a
given morpheme occupies before examining the assimilation data. These interactions are
taken up in coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi and
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction . The underlying prosodic unit
and the weight analysis are addressed in
mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .

title: Adnominal Possession: Double Marking and the Appositive Construction
summary: NP-internal possession marked by a personal possessor prefix plus the affiliative suffix (ku= sapa-ha 'my head'), and the appositive/juxtaposed possessor-NP construction (sisam kotanuhu).
part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Adnominal Possession: Double Marking and the Appositive Construction
Part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
Summary: NP-internal possession marked by a personal possessor prefix plus the affiliative suffix (ku= sapa-ha 'my head'), and the appositive/juxtaposed possessor-NP construction (sisam kotanuhu).

The two possessive constructions

Hokkaido Ainu encodes adnominal possession without a genitive case particle or possessive
adposition. Two constructions divide the nominal lexicon by alienability. Inalienable nouns —
body parts, kinship terms, and a defined range of relational objects — carry a personal
possessive prefix and an
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology affiliative suffix
directly on the possessed noun: ku=tek-ehe 'my hand'. Alienable nouns
— tools, animals, places — are possessed via the transitive verb
kor have, hold , which takes the possessor as its A-argument:
ku=kor seta 'my dog'. The choice of construction is fixed by the noun
class, not by the speaker's construal of ownership in a given context
nakagawa2024 218 ; bugaeva2021poss 47 ‹consensus›.

type
template
example
gloss

inalienable (affiliative)
[POSSESSOR] POSS.PREFIX=N-AFF
ku=tek-ehe
'my hand'

alienable ( kor )
[POSSESSOR] POSS.PREFIX=kor N
ku=kor seta
'my dog'

The morphophonology of the affiliative suffix is treated in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology ; the concept/affiliative functional distinction
in
concept-form-affiliative-form ; the bound-noun status of obligatorily-possessed
nouns in nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns ; kinship terms and their irregular
affiliative forms in kinship-honorific-possession ; predicative possession in
predicative-possession-clauses . The present chapter addresses the NP-internal
structure: the personal-prefix paradigm, the configuration in which both an overt possessor NP
and a head-marked possessum appear together, nested possession, and the appositive
kor construction.

Personal possessive prefixes on inalienable nouns

The possessive prefix on an inalienable noun is the same form set as the transitive-subject
(A) prefix documented in person-marking-architecture . On a noun, the prefix
indexes the possessor as the "subject" of the possessive relation — the same morphological slot
that marks the agent of a transitive verb
sato2008 150 ; nakagawa2024 215–216 . Satō notes the
"backwards" feel: e=paro 'your mouth' is literally 'you-SUBJ mouth',
the possessor marked by what looks like a nominative subject affix
sato2008 150 .

person
prefix
tek 'hand'
otop 'hair'
sapa 'head'

1SG

ku= ( k= before V)

ku=tek-ehe
k=otop-i
ku=sápa(ha)

1PL.EXCL

ci= ( c= before V)

ci=tek-ehe
c=otop-i
ci=sápa(ha)

2SG
e=
e=tek-ehe
e=otop-i
e=páro(ho)

2PL
eci=
eci=tek-ehe
eci=otop-i
eci=sapá(ha)

4 / INDEF

a= (dial. an= )

a=tek-ehe
a=otop-i
a=sapá(ha)

3SG/PL
Ø
tek-ehe
otop-i
sapá(ha)

Accent shift and allomorphy follow the same rules as on verbs. The prefixes
ku= and e= move the pitch accent to the
syllable immediately after the prefix: ku=sápa ,
e=páro . The prefixes eci= and
a= do not shift the accent: a=sapá ,
eci=sapá . Before a vowel-initial stem, ku=
reduces to k= and ci= to
c= , with the accent on the first stem vowel:
k=ótop-i
sato2008 154 .

Each inalienable noun has two affiliative shapes: a short form (the base with the
minimal vowel-copy suffix) and a long form (short + -hV₁ ).
The two carry no semantic difference; the choice is prosodically conditioned and for many
nouns is simply lexically inherited nakagawa2024 219 ; sato2008 152–153 . In the paradigm above, parenthesised endings mark the optional long-form extension:
teke(he) , sapa(ha) , otopi(hi) .

The prefix-plus-affiliative complex is what Satō calls the personal form
(人称形 in his account): a noun form that presupposes and indexes a possessor
sato2008 155 . A third-person personal form such as
sik-ihi is referentially complete on its own —
'his/her/their eye(s)' — because the affiliative suffix alone signals the existence of a
possessor in discourse context; the possessor NP can be omitted without ambiguity
bugaeva2021poss 52, 80–81 . The same paradigm is presented in the Ijäs
(2023) learner grammar under the label "possessive prefixes", with the A-set identity made
explicit for learners ijas2023 .

A minimal illustration of the 2SG prefix in the possessed-noun slot: the noun
haw voice has the affiliative hawe voice-POSS
( haw-e , type ②). In
iteki [[e=haw-e] aste] — proposed as 'don't raise your voice' —
e=haw-e is parsed as the 2SG-possessed noun 'your voice' serving
as the object of aste raise ‹contested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-12-03, proposed
parse; the affiliative haw-e is independently attested as a
plain possessed noun at nakagawa2024 258 ).

ku=kema-ha ka arka ku=tek-ehe ka arka | 1SG.POSS=leg-POSS even be.sore 1SG.POSS=hand-POSS even be.sore | My leg hurts, and my hand hurts too. | Two 1SG-possessed body-part nouns, each as the intransitive subject of arka 'be.sore'. kema 'leg/foot' is type-① (vowel-final stem) with the long affiliative suffix -ha; tek 'hand' is type-③-2 with affiliative -ehe. | utari1994 | HK | Akor Itak ch. 5, sentence 8

Overt possessor NPs and the double-marking configuration

When the possessor is expressed as an overt noun phrase it is juxtaposed immediately before
the possessed noun. Ainu has no genitive case suffix or possessive adposition to mark the
possessor constituent nakagawa2024 217–218 . The result is a configuration
in which the possessum bears both the person prefix and the affiliative suffix — the
morphological load is carried entirely by the head noun — while the possessor NP is present
but structurally unmatched. This is head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 : the head of the NP, not the dependent, bears the relational morphology.

sisam kotan-u | Wajin village-POSS | the Wajin's village | The third-person (zero) prefix on the possessed noun is phonologically null; the affiliative suffix -u (short form of kotan 'village', type-③-3) is the sole overt possessive morpheme on the head. The possessor noun sisam 'non-Ainu, Wajin' is juxtaposed with no case marker.

Ikuresuye nispa santek-ehe situri | Ikuresuye lord descendant-POSS prosper | The descendants of Lord Ikuresuye will prosper. | The possessor phrase Ikuresuye nispa 'Lord Ikuresuye' is juxtaposed before the affiliative head santekehe 'descendants' (santek + affiliative -ehe). Third-person zero prefix leaves the possessive agreement slot phonologically silent; the affiliative suffix is the sole marker of the possessive relation on the head noun. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker tale 7 (aa-irc/005#114)

The following table summarises the semantic classes of nouns whose grammatical class requires
the affiliative construction, following nakagawa2024 219–221 :

class
description
examples (concept : affiliative)

body parts and worn items
anatomical parts; clothing normally worn; certain carried tools

sik : sikihi 'eye'; tek : tekehe 'hand';
tusa : tusaha 'sleeve'; tasiro : tasiroho
'mountain-knife'

part–whole
a part that belongs to a larger whole

nit : nicihi 'spear shaft'; at : atuhu
'cord'; rur : rurihi 'broth'

kinship
kin terms whose grammatical class demands the affiliative (see
kinship-honorific-possession )

yup : yupihi 'elder brother'; mat : macihi
'wife'; po : poho 'son'

material or origin
a product identified by its material constituent or place

ohaw : ohawe 'nettle stew'; cise : cisehe
'house (in verse and some dialects)'

inherently relational
nouns whose sense is relational: prices, smells, villages

atay : ataye 'price'; hura : huraha
'smell'; kotan : kotanuhu 'village'

Class membership is grammatical rather than purely semantic. The same object can fall into
either class depending on cultural construal: a regular bow ( ku ) is
typically alienable and takes kor , but a trap-bow is affiliative
( kuwehe ) because Ainu cosmology treats the prey's choice of whose bow
to fall to as inalienable — yet the same trap-bow can also be expressed with
kor when framed as an ordinary transferable object
nakagawa2024 229 ‹contested›. Whether the triggering condition for
affiliative marking is the specificity of the possessor or the grammatical demands of the
construction (a person prefix or relative-clause structure) is itself a live debate:
the older analysis requires a specific referential possessor
tamura2000 , while Nakagawa 2024: 227–233 argues the constraint is
structural — the affiliative appears wherever a person prefix or relative-clause configuration
demands it, regardless of possessor specificity ‹contested›.

The possessor NP and the possessed personal form are not tightly bracketed as a constituent.
Satō documents cases in which the possessor NP and the possessed noun are separated by topic
particles or other intervening material, with the personal form functioning as a signal that a
possessor NP exists somewhere in the discourse context rather than as part of a closely-bound
syntactic constituent sato2008 155–156 . Bugaeva's parallel formulation is
that the possessed suffix "registers a possessor but does not index its properties", so the
overt possessor NP can be omitted without loss of grammaticality
bugaeva2021poss 52, 80–81 .

Community discussion in the aynu-corpora Discord (thegodofneet, 2025-09-17;
aynucorporadiscord ) proposes that the companion/group noun
utar family, kin, companions has an affiliative form
utari(hi) — 'someone's companions/kin' — on the grounds that a group
of companions is hard to conceive without a relational anchor, making it pragmatically
obligatorily possessed like body-part and kin terms. This is consistent with Nakagawa 2024:
251–252 on the behaviour of utar in narrative ‹contested›.

Nested possession

When the possessor is itself a personal form — an inalienable noun bearing its own person
prefix and affiliative suffix — the result is a chain of possessed nouns, each indexed for its
own possessor. The innermost noun carries an overt person prefix; the next noun takes the
innermost noun's referent as its possessor, which is then marked by a zero third-person
prefix. Nakagawa 2024: 224 (ex. 259) illustrates this in a Saru narrative passage:

a=yup-ihi tures-ihi | 4.POSS=elder.brother-POSS younger.sister-POSS | my elder brother's younger sister | The 4th-person prefix a= on yupihi indexes the narrator's elder brother; the following turesihi carries a zero (3rd-person) prefix whose referent is that elder brother. Each noun enters the affiliative construction independently: yup 'elder brother' with affiliative -ihi (type-③-1); tures 'younger sister' with affiliative -ihi (type-③-1). | nakagawa2024:224 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 259; embedded in the clause nea a=tura a=yupihi turesihi, ne Iskar emko wa a=tura okkaypo a=eutannere

Each noun in the chain takes its own personal prefix, and the chain as a whole is not
syntactically tight: topic particles can interrupt it, as with the simpler two-noun case
above. Possessor-raising through noun incorporation — where the possessor of an incorporated
body-part noun is promoted to the clause subject position — is a related but distinct
phenomenon treated in noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding .

The kor appositive construction

Alienable nouns cannot receive the person prefix and affiliative suffix directly. The
transitive verb kor have, hold, carry intervenes between possessor and
possessum: the possessor fills the A-slot of kor as a person prefix;
the possessed noun follows as the implicit relativised object, remaining in the concept
(uninflected) form
nakagawa2024 218 ; sato2008 151 .

ku=kor seta hure toan kur kor cape retar | 1SG.A=have dog be.red that person have cat be.white | My dog is red; that person's cat is white. | A minimal alienable pair: 1SG possessor ku=kor vs the zero-prefix kor (3rd-person possessor). Both seta 'dog' and cape 'cat' are non-possessible alienable nouns and cannot carry a person prefix directly. | utari1994 | HK | Akor Itak ch. 5, sentence 9

Bugaeva's analysis, based on the Pacific-wide typology of appositive possession, treats the
kor construction as a finite relative clause headed by the transitive
verb kor , with the possessum as the gapped object:
[__ ku=kor] seta = 'the dog that I have'
bugaeva2021poss 55 . Evidence that kor retains full
verbal syntax includes the availability of perfective marking
( a=kor a kotan 'my former village'), plural suffixes, mirativity, and
free stacking of relative clauses over the same head
bugaeva2021poss 57–58 . The construction is therefore classified as an
appositive-verbal classifier — a Circum-Pacific possessive type in which a
finite verb serves as the possessive head rather than a case affix
bugaeva2021poss 52, 80–82 .

With a third-person possessor, kor loses its overt A-prefix and can
cliticise phonologically to the following head noun, yielding units such as
kor-ninkari 'X's earrings'. Bugaeva (2021) reads this as an in-progress
shift from the dependent-marking pattern of the full verbal construction toward a head-marking
configuration in which kor has become a clitic on the possessed noun
bugaeva2021poss 59 ‹corpus-suggested›.
This trajectory — verbal appositive classifier to head-marking clitic — parallels the path
already completed in the inalienable system, where the affiliative suffix now sits squarely on
the head.

The set of nouns attested in the kor construction is limited.
Tamura's Saru lexicon lists approximately fifty-seven such nouns, distributed across people
(24), places and environment (11), food and drink (7), tools and ritual objects (7), domestic
animals (1), and human attributes (7); wild animals and plants, owned by no one in the Ainu
world, are largely absent bugaeva2021poss 55–56 ; tamura1996 .
The affective extension of kor — as in
a=kor pon menoko 'my (dear) young girl' — adds a further function in
oral literature beyond straightforward ownership
bugaeva2021poss 56 .

Typologically, the inalienable affiliative construction is head-marking in the sense of
nichols1986 : the relational morphology (person prefix plus affiliative
suffix) is borne entirely by the head noun, with no case or adposition marking the possessor
dependent. The kor construction differs in placing the possessor index
on the intervening verb rather than on the possessed noun head, a configuration that Huber
(2025) places in a separate appositive-verbal column within her four-way attributive typology
(head-marking / dependent-marking / zero-marking / double-marking)
huber2025 248 . The broader Circum-Pacific context and its diachronic
implications are laid out in bugaeva2021poss 80–82 . Noun incorporation of
possessed body-part nouns and the interaction with the kor set
(alienable nouns cannot be incorporated in their possessed forms) are treated in
noun-incorporation-object .

title: The Adverbializer -no and Adverb Formation from Verbs
summary: The suffix -no deriving manner/degree adverbs from stative (property) verbs, set against related adverb-forming strategies.
part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Adverbializer -no and Adverb Formation from Verbs
Part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
Summary: The suffix -no deriving manner/degree adverbs from stative (property) verbs, set against related adverb-forming strategies.

Form and domain

The form no converts a predicate into an adverbial constituent. At the
word level it appears as a suffix on a descriptive intransitive stem —
moyre-no 'slowly', tunas-no 'quickly' — and the
output is a manner or degree adverb. At the predicate level it follows a saturated finite
predicate phrase, especially a stative predicate or a negated action predicate, and links that
predicate as an adverbial circumstance to the following one. The two uses share a form; the
boundary between suffix and post-predicate particle is difficult to draw exactly. Nakagawa
finds a particle analysis cleaner for the finite-predicate cases but observes that it cannot
account for the frozen quantity adverb poronno 'many, much', whose
status as a single, semantically distinct word is well established nakagawa2024 340–341 ‹contested›.

Property meanings belong to the intransitive verb in Ainu. Forms such as
poro be big, become big and pirka be good, become good are
predicates, and a stative reading ordinarily carries an inchoative one alongside it nakagawa2024 124 ; shibatani1990 33–34 ; sato2008 2 . The adverbializer takes
such a predicate as its input. The position of adverbial material relative to valency and
person morphology is set out in verb-word-template ; the broader status of property
predicates is treated in no-adjective-class-property-verbs .

Manner and degree adverbs

Descriptive intransitive stems form manner and degree adverbs by suffixation with
-no . Ijäs gives the learner formulation: moyre be slow
yields moyre-no 'slowly' ijas2023 . Shibatani's example
illustrates the pattern in a full utterance: tunas-no , from
tunas be fast , modifies the property predicate pirka be good; become good , which in the imperative carries its inchoative reading shibatani1990 33 .

tunas-no pirka | be.fast-ADV be.good | Get well quickly! | Shibatani cites the form in his discussion of property predicates as verbs; tunas-no derives the manner modifier from the descriptive intransitive stem tunas, and pirka carries its inchoative 'become well' reading. | shibatani1990:33 | HK

moyre-no | be.slow-ADV | slowly | Ijäs's Lesson 3 teaching example, illustrating the regular descriptive-stem-to-adverb derivation. | ijas2023

input (descriptive vi)
adverbial form
value
evidence status

tunas 'be fast'
tunas-no
manner adverb 'quickly'
shibatani1990 33

moyre 'be slow'
moyre-no
manner adverb 'slowly'
ijas2023 (constructed)

poro 'be big'
poronno
quantity adverb 'many, much'
ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 162

pon 'be small'
ponno
quantity adverb 'a little'
ijas2023

The lexicalized quantity forms poronno and
ponno are morphophonologically distinct from the transparent derivation.
Ijäs explicitly records poronno as the correct output and excludes the
regularized *porono ijas2023 ‹consensus›. The quantity
meaning of poronno brings it into contact with verbal number: subjects
modified by poronno may co-occur with either plural or singular
intransitive stems without a mechanical agreement rule nakagawa2024 162 ; sato2008 101 ‹contested›. That interaction is treated in suppletive-verbal-number .

Derived adverbs occupy no argument slot and leave the valence of the modified predicate
unchanged. Nakagawa's arity table assigns arity 0 to adverbial prefixes such as
ar- 'completely' and ru- 'slightly', which
occupy the same valence-neutral zone in the verb word nakagawa2024 137, 140 .
The suffix -no belongs with that side of the template. The
valence-changing morphology that contrasts with this neutral zone is surveyed in
transitivity-and-valence-classes .

Stative and negative-manner linkage

When no follows a saturated predicate phrase, it links that predicate as
an adverbial state or manner to the following predicate. Nakagawa places it in the
temporal-succession connective group alongside wa and
kor , but with a narrower aspectual profile: wa
links predicates whose first element delivers a result-state, kor marks
concurrent action, and no is conditioned on a stative first predicate or
a negated action predicate nakagawa2024 340–341 . Refsing's Shizunai data show
the same adverbializing value in that dialect, where the particle follows a prohibitive
predicate to supply the attendant negative state for the polite imperative refsing1986 241 .

The negative construction is the dominant use. The frame somo … ki no —
negating a predicate with the standard negative support verb ki 'do' and
then adverbializing the result with no — yields the 'without doing'
reading and is near-obligatory in that function; substituting wa or
kor is unexpected in negated contexts sato2008 45–46
‹consensus›. The support construction somo … ki itself is treated in
negation-somo-standard-clausal .

a=ona-ha somo mokor no kamuyoritak kor an | 4.POSS=father-POSS NEG sleep ADV pray CONJ exist | My father was praying to the gods without sleeping. | No adverbializes the negated intransitive predicate somo mokor, making it the attendant circumstance under which the progressive predicate kamuyoritak kor an holds. | nakagawa2024:340 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 614

ku=caranke ka somo ki no ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=complain even NEG do ADV 1SG.S=return | I went home without complaining. | The negative support frame ka somo ki, including the scalar particle ka, is adverbialised as a unit by no. The following predicate ku=hosipi retains its own first-person subject index. | sato2008:44 | SAR

iteke ukoyki no tup ne ukosaraye yan | PROH quarrel ADV two COP share POL | Divide it in two and share it without quarrelling! | Shizunai. No adverbialises the prohibitive predicate iteke ukoyki; the polite imperative yan closes the main predicate. | refsing1986:241 | SHI | Refsing ex. 418

Ijäs identifies the same two uses of no and presents a further
conditioning factor: she describes it as the choice when the linked clauses share a subject,
with wa favoured when they do not ijas2023 . The primary
grammars derive the no / wa alternation from
aspectual and polarity properties of the first predicate — stative or negated predicates take
no — and Refsing's Shizunai conjunctionalizer treatment is consistent
with that account nakagawa2024 340–341 ; sato2008 45–46 ; refsing1986 241 ‹contested›. Subject continuity is frequent in negated contexts because a manner condition is
naturally predicated of the same participant as the following event; the grammars do not extend
this tendency to a grammatical rule.

The connective inventory and the position of no in the wider
temporal-succession system are treated in clause-linkage-overview ; the syntax of
manner and degree adverbial subordination is in manner-and-degree-adverbial-clauses .

title: Adverbs, Degree Words, and Comparison Strategies
summary: The adverb word-class, the -no adverbializer, intensifiers, and the periphrastic encoding of comparison (Ainu has no dedicated comparative).
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Adverbs, Degree Words, and Comparison Strategies
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: The adverb word-class, the -no adverbializer, intensifiers, and the periphrastic encoding of comparison (Ainu has no dedicated comparative).

The adverb class

Hokkaido Ainu adverbs modify verb phrases, carry arity zero — they neither serve as
predicate heads nor fill argument slots — and take no nominative person prefix
nakagawa2024 273 ; nakagawa2022pos 473 . The class
boundary with prenominals (連体詞) turns on target: adverbs attach to the VP,
prenominals to the NP, and prenominals additionally bear no person prefix of any
kind nakagawa2024 116 . Postpositional adverbs form a subclass that
co-indexes a nominal antecedent; when that antecedent is pronominal the
postpositional adverb receives an objective person prefix — a morphological
signature shared with relational-and-spatial-nouns locative nouns and
distinct from verbal person inflection nakagawa2024 273, 280 .

Because this grammar assigns no adjective class to property-concept words — treating
them as stative intransitive verbs (see property-concept-predication ) —
the adverb–verb boundary matters directly for the description of manner and degree.
A property verb such as pirka be good, be well predicates with full
person marking; the deadverbal form pirka-no 'well, nicely'
derived from it carries arity zero and no person affix. Kindaichi, who did posit an
adjective class, treated these same forms as adjective-derived adverbials
kindaichi1936 123–124 ; in the modern analysis the derivation is
uniform verb → adverb nakagawa2022pos 474 .

Nakagawa identifies five formation routes for adverbs proper, plus a distinct
postpositional subclass treated below nakagawa2024 273–282 . The
five are: (i) basic/lexical adverbs (本来型); (ii) intransitive verb + the suffix
-no adverbializer ; (iii) bare intransitive conversion; (iv)
transitive verb conversion; and (v) directional formations built on
relational-and-spatial-nouns locative roots with pronominal prefixes
and directional suffixes. The full account of the -no adverbializer
suffix belongs to adverbializer-no ; the present chapter surveys its
output alongside the other types, then the degree and comparison systems.

Satō additionally distinguishes a demonstrative adverb subtype (指示副詞) and an
interrogative subtype (疑問副詞) alongside the basic and postpositional divisions
sato2008 2 ; Nakagawa subsumes both into the basic and prenominal
classes respectively nakagawa2024 273 . Refsing, working on
Shizunai, groups adverbs with conjunctions and interjections in a single particle
category refsing1986 74–76 ‹contested›. Nakagawa and Satō instead
distribute conjunctive-meaning items between conjunctive particles (接続助詞) and
adverbs, leaving no independent conjunction class
nakagawa2024 116–117 ; sato2008 3 .

Lexical adverbs

Basic lexical adverbs form a closed set spanning temporal, degree, and modal
functions. Nakagawa's Saru–Chitose inventory includes the members below
nakagawa2024 273 ; the degree/superlative member
iyotta most of all, above all is attested in Tamura's Saru
dictionary and recurs throughout the oral corpus
tamura1996 s.v. iyotta :

subclass
form
gloss

temporal
nani
soon, shortly

temporal
tane
now

temporal
ramma
always, every time

degree
sonno / sino
truly, really

degree
earkinne
very, extremely

degree / superlative
iyotta
the most, most of all

modal / negator
somo
not

modal / prohibitive
iteki
do not

The degree adverbs sonno truly and sino truly are
cross-class items: both also function as general prenominals meaning 'real, true'
when preceding a noun phrase ( sonno menoko 'a real woman')
nakagawa2024 283 . Used adverbially, sonno truly
scopes over the entire clause:

sonno ku=pewre or ta ku=nu yukar ne ru-we ne | truly 1SG.S=be.young place LOC 1SG.A=hear epic COP track-POSS COP | Truly, it is the epic I heard when I was young. | Nakagawa ex. 1. The sentential adverb sonno scopes over the whole predication. ku=pewre or ta is a temporal locative nominalization ('at the time of my being young'); ku=nu takes the 1SG.A prefix because nu 'hear' is transitive; the evidential ru-we ne closes the clause. | nakagawa2024:118 | SAR

The negators somo not and iteki do not differ
syntactically. somo not is the standard clausal negator (treated in
negation-somo-standard-clausal ); iteki do not operates
in the prohibitive domain covered by negation-prohibitive-iteki . A
community-observed distinction further separates the adverb
ikiya be careful not to, lest , which licenses normal person marking
on its complement clause ( ikiya seta e=na 'be careful not
to bite the dog'), from iteki do not , which interacts differently
with the following verbal complex and its person-prefix slot (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2025; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›.

The adverb patek only, just provides an instructive contrast
with Japanese だけ : patek only patterns as
a pure adverb and cannot head a nominal phrase, whereas Japanese
だけ is a grammaticalized noun. Community corpus analysis
records the traditional order ani patek 'with only this'
in classical texts (one token against zero of the reversed
patek ani ), with near-equal counts of both orders in
modern learner material; the reversal is attributed to nominal influence from
Japanese (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›.

Deverbal adverbs: the -no adverbializer and bare conversion

The suffix -no adverbializer attaches to intransitive verbs —
most productively to property verbs — to derive manner and degree adverbs:
pirka be good → pirka-no 'well, nicely';
moyre be gentle, be slow → moyre-no
'gently, slowly'; tunas be quick →
tunas-no 'quickly'. The resulting forms are adverbs with
arity zero and no person affix. Ijäs introduces the most frequent outputs in her
pedagogical grammar ijas2023 ; the phonological details of the suffix,
the relationship to the variant -nu adverbializer variant , and the
depictive and resultative readings of -no adverbials belong to
adverbializer-no . The direct consequence for degree is that any
property verb can furnish a degree adverb without the property-word becoming a
distinct category:

tunas-no pirka | be.quick-ADV be.good | tunasno pirka | Get well quickly! | Shibatani ex. 13 area. The -no adverbial tunas-no 'quickly' modifies the inchoative imperative pirka 'become well/healthy'. Property verbs serve directly as imperatives, exploiting the stative-to-inchoative polysemy common to Ainu intransitive verbs. | shibatani1990:19 | HK

A second group of intransitive verbs converts to adverbs without any suffix. The
most frequently attested members of this bare-conversion pattern are
tunas be quick; quickly ,
moyre be gentle; gently ,
hanke be near; nearby , and
tuyma be far; far away
nakagawa2024 273 . Bare and -no forms often
co-occur in the same dialect without semantic contrast; the choice may reflect
prosodic context or register ‹corpus-suggested›.

Transitive verb conversion produces a third group, whose members surface mainly as
postpositional adverbs (e.g., ani with, by means of ,
tura(no) together with ) and are documented in the following section
nakagawa2024 280–281 . Directional adverbs derived from locative
roots are treated immediately below.

Directional adverbs from locative roots

A productive series of directional adverbs is built by prefixing
e- (allative) or o- (ablative)
to a Type-2 locative noun root and adding the directional suffix
-ne or -un ; a second pattern
uses he- / ho- before the root
plus -asi
nakagawa2024 277–279 . In Ishikari, Tokachi, and eastern
Hokkaido dialects the initial h- drops, so
herepasi 'seaward' appears as
erepasi
nakagawa2024 279 . The Type-2 locative noun bases are
documented in relational-and-spatial-nouns ; selected directional
adverbs are given below (Nakagawa 2024: 277–279, 表68–69):

prefix
root
suffix
form
meaning

e- (allative)
kim 'mtn-side'
-ne
ekimne
toward the mountains

o- (ablative)
kim
-ne
okimne
from the mountains

e-
soy 'outside'
-ne
esoyne
to the outside

e-
pis 'beach-side'
-un
episun
toward the beach

he-
rep 'offshore'
-asi
herepasi
seaward (→ erepasi in E. dialects)

he-
mak 'inland/back'
-asi
hemakasi
inward, into the interior

Postpositional adverbs

Postpositional adverbs (後置副詞) take a nominal antecedent — typically the
immediately preceding NP — and carry the objective person prefix when the
antecedent is pronominal nakagawa2024 280 . Two diagnostics
distinguish them from case postpositions: they can stand independently at the head
of a clause, and they do not require a 〈place〉 -marked antecedent,
unlike the locative case particles
ta LOC (at, in) , wa ABL (from) , and
peka PERL (through)
nakagawa2024 281–282 . Compare:
ruyka kari 'across the bridge' (postpositional adverb
kari through, across with a bare noun antecedent; no
〈place〉 required) vs
riyopa or peka (the perlative case particle
peka PERL (through) requires the 〈place〉 -marked
locative noun or inside, place of )
nakagawa2024 281–282 .

Nakagawa divides postpositional adverbs into two formation types: a basic
(本来型, 表70) and a transitive-verb-conversion type (他動詞転換型, 表71)
nakagawa2024 280–281 . The member
pak(no) until, to the extent that of the basic type builds the
degree clauses treated in manner-and-degree-adverbial-clauses ;
akkari more than, exceeding of the conversion type serves as the
standard-of-comparison marker (see comparison section below):

type
form
gloss

basic (表70)
epitta
throughout, all over

koraci / neno
like, in the manner of (equative)

nepkor / nepkon
like, as if

os(i)
after, following

pak(no)
until, to the extent that (degree marker)

sekor
saying, as (quotative)

transitive-conversion (表71)
ani
with, by means of (instrumental)

tura(no)
together with (comitative)

akkari
more than, exceeding (comparative standard)

kari
through, across

pes
downstream along

turasi
upstream along

With a pronominal antecedent, the objective prefix attaches to the postpositional
adverb: en=akkari 'more than me',
en=sam 'beside me',
un=koraci 'like us'. The same
en= / un= series that marks
objects of transitive verbs appears here, confirming the objective rather than
nominative slot nakagawa2024 280–281 . With a noun antecedent the
noun simply precedes without marking:
seta koraci 'like a dog'.

Degree and intensification

Degree in Hokkaido Ainu is expressed through lexical degree adverbs, through
property verbs themselves (which predicate directly as intransitives), and in some
contexts through reduplication. The lexical degree adverbs
sonno truly, really and earkinne very, extremely
precede the modified predicate exactly as manner adverbs do:
sonno poro 'truly big / really large',
earkinne pirka 'very beautiful'
nakagawa2024 273 .

At the lower end of degree, pon be small — a stative property verb
meaning 'be small' — is used prenominally ( pon hekaci
'small child', nakagawa2022pos 475 ) and, in its adverbial
bare-conversion use, as a diminutive degree modifier meaning 'a little, slightly'
‹corpus-suggested›. Its logical counterpart in the degree domain is
poro be big, be great , which in composition and derivation conveys
augmentative force; neither is a bound degree morpheme.

Reduplication of property verbs and ideophonic forms yields intensified or
expressive degree readings — the patterns are analysed together with the broader
reduplication system in reduplication-phonology-rule-interaction .

The superlative degree adverb iyotta most of all, above all
immediately precedes the modified property predicate:
iyotta tanne 'the longest',
iyotta pirka 'the most beautiful'
tamura1996 s.v. iyotta . No comparative morpheme competes for this
slot, so iyotta bears the superlative function without formal ambiguity
with a comparative form.

Comparison: the akkari strategy and the absence of a morphological comparative

Hokkaido Ainu has no dedicated comparative morpheme — no suffix or particle whose
sole function is to inflect a property predicate for comparison. The analysis
follows directly from the no-adjective-class thesis: because property-concept words
are intransitive verbs, they carry no adjectival inflection and therefore no
comparative inflection
nakagawa2024 118 ; nakagawa2022pos 474 . In
Stassen's cross-linguistic survey of comparison strategies
stassen1997 , this places Ainu in the large type that expresses
comparison via a separate particle or postpositional element rather than by
morphological marking on the predicate.

The primary comparison construction uses the postpositional adverb
akkari more than, exceeding (表71 above) as the standard-of-comparison
marker. The standard NP immediately precedes
akkari more than ; when the standard is pronominal, the objective
prefix attaches to akkari more than in the usual postpositional
pattern:

tan cep en=akkari poro | this fish 1SG.O=more.than be.big | This fish is bigger than me. | Illustrates the akkari comparison construction: [subject-NP] [standard-O=akkari] [property-verb]. The standard 'me' is encoded by the 1SG.O prefix en= on akkari rather than by a postposition or case particle on a separate NP. With a noun standard: tan cep seta akkari poro 'This fish is bigger than a dog', with seta 'dog' as bare antecedent without any marking.

akkari more than carries the full comparative meaning; the property
verb itself ( poro be big , tanne be long , etc.) is
unchanged from its predicative form. Comparison is thus encoded entirely in the
postpositional layer rather than in predicate morphology.

For equative comparison ('as … as'), the postpositional adverbs
koraci like, in the manner of and neno like (basic
type, 表70) serve as the standard marker:
seta koraci poro 'as big as a dog'
nakagawa2024 280 . The overlap between the equative 'as … as' and
the manner 'like' reading of koraci like is common in languages
that use manner adverbs for equative comparison, and has not been distinguished
as a formal ambiguity in the sources read for this chapter ‹speculative›. Degree
and extent comparison using
pak(no) until, to the extent that — yielding proportional and
scalar clauses — is treated in manner-and-degree-adverbial-clauses .

Juxtaposition without an explicit standard marker also operates as a comparison
strategy in discourse contexts where the contrasted entities have been introduced:
two property predicates in adjacent clauses can be read as comparative without any
occurrence of akkari more than ‹corpus-suggested›. Its pragmatic
frequency relative to the akkari more than construction has not
been quantified from the sources read for this chapter.

title: Morphophonology of the Affiliative Suffix and Echo/Copy Vowels
summary: The vowel-copy mechanism producing echo/release vowels and the affiliative paradigm (-hV, -ihi, -uhu, -V): copy directionality, class membership, the phonological-vs-lexical conditioning dispute, and the internal-reconstruction source of -hV.
part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Morphophonology of the Affiliative Suffix and Echo/Copy Vowels
Part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
Summary: The vowel-copy mechanism producing echo/release vowels and the affiliative paradigm (-hV, -ihi, -uhu, -V): copy directionality, class membership, the phonological-vs-lexical conditioning dispute, and the internal-reconstruction source of -hV.

The short and long affiliative: the vowel-copy principle

The concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed) form of a common
noun differs from its concept form by a suffix whose vowel echoes the final vowel of the
stem. The suffix appears in two lengths: a short form consisting of the copy vowel
alone (for vowel-final stems this adds nothing visible to the concept form), and a
long form consisting of copy vowel + /h/ + copy vowel again, notated
-hV₁ where V₁ is the stem's last vowel
nakagawa2024 226–228 ; sato2008 152–153 . The two lengths
carry no semantic distinction; the choice between them is governed by prosody and speaker
rhythm, and Satō describes the usage split as "unclear"
sato2008 152 ‹contested›.

The possessive person-prefix that co-occurs with the affiliative form belongs to the
transitive-subject (A) prefix set — identical to the verbal A paradigm — as set out in
person-marking-architecture ; the full double-marking construction is treated in
adnominal-possession-double-marking . This chapter addresses only the
morphophonology of the suffix: its shape, the conditioning environments of its three
consonant-final classes, the irregulars, and the diachronic source of the /h/ element.

The conditioning sorts into three groups by the stem's final segment. Vowel-final stems
(type ①) produce the most transparent echo: the long form adds -hV₁ , the short
form adds nothing. Glide-final stems (type ②) take the uniform suffix -e(he) .
Consonant-final stems other than glides (type ③) belong to one of three lexically listed
sub-classes: the default -i(hi) , a minority echo-vowel class -V(hV) ,
and a further minority -u(hu) class restricted to stems whose last vowel is /a/
nakagawa2024 226–228 .

Type ①: vowel-final stems

When the concept form ends in a vowel, the short affiliative is identical to the concept
form; a bare third-person (zero-prefix) vowel-final noun is therefore ambiguous between
concept and affiliative readings and requires context for interpretation
nakagawa2024 227 ; sato2008 152 . The long form adds
-hV₁ , repeating the stem-final vowel:

concept form
gloss
short affiliative
long affiliative

sapa
head
sapa
sapa-ha

etu
nose
etu
etu-hu

ure
leg (literary)
ure
ure-he

totto
breast; mother (some dialects)
totto
totto-ho

po
child, son
po
po-ho

kema
leg, foot
kema
kema-ha

In each row the suffix vowel is identical to the stem-final vowel: /a/, /u/, /e/, /o/
respectively. Because the short affiliative coincides with the concept form, corpus
string-searches cannot separate them; frequency figures for long forms —
sapaha 203 tokens nakagawa2024 227 — are the only
clean affiliative counts for this class.

ku=kema-ha ka arka ku=tek-ehe ka arka | 1SG.A=leg-POSS also hurt 1SG.A=hand-POSS also hurt | ku=kemaha ka arka, ku=tekehe ka arka. | My leg hurts too; my hand hurts too. | kema-ha: type ①, V₁=/a/, long affiliative. tek-ehe: type ③-2, V₁=/e/, long affiliative. Both with 1SG.A possessor. The same sentence contrasts two different suffix classes. | utari1994 | HK | アコㇿイタㇰ ch. 5 [akor-itak/005#8]

Type ②: glide-final stems and the monosyllabic subtype

When the concept form ends in the glides /y/ or /w/, the affiliative suffix is uniformly
-e(he) , regardless of the preceding vowel quality
nakagawa2024 227 ; sato2008 152 :

concept form
gloss
short affiliative
long affiliative

patoy
lip
patoye
patoye-he

ikkew
waist
ikkewe
ikkewe-he

haw
voice
hawe
hawe-he

The affiliative hawe voice (possessed form) of
haw voice is attested as both an ordinary possessed noun ('X's voice') and,
fully grammaticalized, as one of the four formal-noun evidentials treated in
concept-form-affiliative-form . The two uses are morphologically identical;
only context separates them nakagawa2024 258–259 .

a=yup-ihi an=sa-ha haw-ehe ka isam | 4.A=elder.brother-POSS 4.A=elder.sister-POSS voice-POSS also absent | a=yupihi an=saha hawehe ka isam | There is no voice from his elder brother or elder sister. | haw-ehe: type ②, glide-final /w/, long affiliative -ehe. a=yupihi and an=saha are ③-1 kinship affiliatives; an= is the dialectal variant of 4th-person A prefix a=. | ilcaa1976 | SHI | 織田ステノ, uwepeker 民話 Moyuk（前半）, AA研アイヌ語資料 (静内) [aa-irc/017#52]

A formally parallel subtype arises with monosyllabic open-syllable (CV) nouns whose concept
form ends in /i/ or /u/. These surface as vowel-final but take the type ② suffix
-e(he) rather than the type ① echo -hV₁ , as if an underlying glide
— absent from the concept form — conditions the suffix
nakagawa2024 229 :

concept form
gloss
short affiliative
long affiliative
posited underlying stem

ci
penis
ciye
ciye-he
*ciy

pi
seed
piye
piye-he
*piy

ku
bow (weapon)
kuwe
kuwe-he
*kuw

ru
track, path
ruwe
ruwe-he
*ruw

Sakhalin Ainu, which preserves phonemic vowel length, has the corresponding nouns as
cii , kuu — long-vowel forms plausibly
reflecting an earlier *Ciy/*Cuw sequence and supporting the underlying-glide analysis
nakagawa2024 229 ; itabashi2001 ‹speculative›. The affiliative
ruwe is by far the most frequent of the four, since its
grammaticalization as the recognized-fact evidential places it in clause-final position
across a large share of narrative sentences; a clean affiliative count is not available
by string-search because ruwe is homophonous with the lexical noun
and with a reduced final particle nakagawa2024 258–259 .

Type ③: consonant-final stems

Nouns whose concept form ends in a consonant other than /y/ or /w/ belong to type ③.
Three suffix shapes compete, and class membership within type ③ is in large part lexically
determined sato2008 153 ; nakagawa2024 227–228 .

The default: ③-1 (-i/-ihi)

The most frequent sub-pattern inserts /i/ before the optional long-form
-hi , giving -i (short) or -ihi (long) regardless of the
stem's vowel quality. Kindaichi and Chiri's early description used
sik / shikihi as the paradigm case of
the distinction they called 具体形 'concrete form' versus 抽象形 'abstract form'
kindaichi1936 16 ; the present grammar follows
concept-form-affiliative-form Nakagawa's terminology (concept/affiliative).

concept form
gloss
V₁
short affiliative
long affiliative

am
fingernail, toenail
/a/

sik

/i/

/o/

otopi-hi

/u/
yupi
yupi-hi

sir
visible appearance, land, weather
/i/
siri
—

hum

/u/
humi
—

The kinship term yup elder brother takes the same ③-1 suffix as body
parts, confirming that sub-type membership cuts across semantic domains. The corpus
attests yupihi at 759 tokens and
sikihi at 132 tokens nakagawa2024 228 .
The affiliatives siri and humi are
lexically frozen in their short forms in the evidential construction; the long forms
sirihi and humihi appear in the plain
possessed-noun use only nakagawa2024 258–259 .

a=yup-i an a=sa-ha an | 4.A=elder.brother-POSS exist 4.A=elder.sister-POSS exist | I have an elder brother; I have an elder sister. | Classic story-opening family introduction (Huber 2025: 251, ex. 16). yup-i: ③-1 short affiliative (yup + -i). sa-ha: ① long affiliative (sa + -ha), showing type ① for comparison. | huber2025:251 | HK

The minority echo-vowel sub-patterns: ③-2 and ③-3

A lexically defined set of consonant-final stems takes a suffix whose vowel echoes
the stem's last vowel (③-2) rather than defaulting to /i/; a smaller set inserts /u/
regardless of stem vowel quality (③-3, restricted to V₁ = /a/ stems). Both sub-types
accept the same optional long-form extension. The conditioning matrix after Nakagawa
2024: 228, 表51:

sub-type
V₁ = /a/
V₁ = /i/
V₁ = /u/
V₁ = /e/
V₁ = /o/

③-1 (default)
-i(hi)
-i(hi)
-i(hi)

-i(hi)

—

V₁ = the stem's last vowel. The /i/ column of ③-2 is empty because all consonant-final
stems with V₁ = /i/ belong to ③-1. For V₁ = /a/ stems three outcomes are possible
(③-1, ③-2, ③-3), with no phonological cue distinguishing them; this is the hardest
sub-case for morpheme recognition
nakagawa2024 227–228 ; sato2008 153 .
Nakagawa notes that ③-3 — the insertion of /u/ for V₁ = /a/ stems —
"cannot be explained historically"
nakagawa2024 228 ‹speculative›.
