ku=tek-ehe piro hi ta ku=kor totto noya ham uk wa | 1SG.A=hand-POSS get.injured NMLZ LOC 1SG.A=have mother mugwort leaf take CONJ | ku=tekehe píro hi ta, ku=kor totto noya ham uk wa … | When my hand was injured, my mother gathered mugwort leaves … | tek-ehe: type ③-2, V₁=/e/, long affiliative. ku=kor totto 'my mother' takes the alienable kor construction, illustrating the affiliative vs kor split on the same line. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 横山裕之, noya, アイヌタイムズ [ainu-times/024/1#20]

The ③-1 default surfaces in compounds

Sub-type ③-1 is the unmarked pattern. A ③-2 or ③-3 stem that appears as the second
element of a compound reverts to the ③-1 suffix, regardless of its own lexical class
nakagawa2024 228 . The noun at cord, rope alone takes the
③-2 long affiliative atu-hu (V₁ = /u/); as the second element of
tar-at 'carrying-rope cord' the compound takes
tar-ac-i(hi) , the ③-1 form
nakagawa2024 228 . This alternation is the clearest synchronic evidence
that ③-1 is phonologically basic and ③-2/③-3 are lexically marked
sato2008 153 ‹corpus-suggested›.

The same diagnostic applies to the evidential formal nouns. The noun
sir visible appearance, land alone gives the short ③-1 affiliative
siri , which grammaticalizes as the situational evidential.
The formal noun siri blocks the long extension in its evidential
function — it is always the bare short form, never *sirihi —
suggesting that grammaticalization has lexically frozen the short form in this use
nakagawa2024 258–259 ‹corpus-confirmed›.

True irregulars

Two nouns have affiliative forms that none of the productive rules derives.
par mouth yields paro(ho) , not the expected
③-1 *parihi . Nakagawa traces this to the word's etymology:
par is a compound of pa 'upper part' and
or 'inside, place', so the ending -o(ho) is the locative
long-form of or rather than an affiliative suffix proper
nakagawa2024 230 . The second irregular, mor temple-hair
→ moru(hu) , is attributed to a morphological re-analysis of an
earlier stem moru nakagawa2024 230 ‹speculative›.

Kinship terms add further formal idiosyncrasies: address-form vs reference-form pairs
where the two stems take different affiliative classes (e.g., reference
yup ③-1 vs the address form yupo ①),
and frozen dialectal variants. These are treated in
kinship-honorific-possession .

Lexical conditioning: what is predictable and what must be memorized

Types ① and ② are phonologically predictable from the concept form's final segment:
any vowel-final stem belongs to ①; any /y/- or /w/-final stem (surface or underlying)
belongs to ②. Within type ③ the situation is different. Satō states that "there are many
exceptions … each must be memorised individually"
sato2008 153 , and Nakagawa 2024 concurs that ③-2 and ③-3 are lexically
listed. For V₁ = /a/ stems the task is hardest: a given noun may belong to ③-1, ③-2,
or ③-3 with no phonological predictor. For V₁ = /i/ the situation is simpler — all
/i/-stem consonant-final nouns take ③-1
nakagawa2024 227–228 .

The pedagogical grammar of ijas2023 presents the consonant-final class under
the label 'belonging form' and treats -ihi as the default suffix, consistent
with the ③-1 analysis. Ijäs does not distinguish ③-2 from ③-3 as labeled sub-classes;
body-part forms with non-default suffixes are presented as items to be memorised. For
the nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns obligatorily-possessed nouns that
constitute the core affiliative class, the lexical class assignment is part of each
noun's dictionary entry.

Diachrony: the source of -hV and the vowel-copy analysis

Chiri 1952 described the vowel quality of the affiliative suffix as an instance of
vowel harmony spreading a feature from the stem chiri1952 . The
present description reframes this as vowel copy : the suffix vowel is a
morphologically triggered copy of the stem's last vowel, not an autosegmental spread.
The distinction is empirically relevant: in type ③-2 the copy operates across an
intervening consonant ( tek → -e ), where a strictly
local harmony rule would require extra stipulation
nakagawa2024 226–228 ‹contested›.

The origin of the ③-2 echo-vowel class is addressed separately by Nakagawa 2024:
228, 247–248, who proposes that it derives from the locative long-form suffix productive
on relational nouns. The relational noun sut behind; grandmother takes
the locative long form sutu ; the same pattern recurs in the
compound tap-sut 'shoulder' → tap-sut-u(hu) ,
suggesting that the ③-2 suffix in such nouns was originally a locative ending that later
spread into the common-noun affiliative paradigm. Nakagawa describes the connection as
"intimately related" nakagawa2024 228, 247–248 , and notes that the
③-3 pattern (V₁ = /a/ → /u/) "cannot be explained historically"
nakagawa2024 228 ‹speculative›. Neither proposal has been independently
replicated. See relational-and-spatial-nouns for the relational-noun
long-form paradigm.

The /h/ in the long-form suffix ( -hV₁ ) has a proposed diachronic correlate.
Sakhalin Ainu, which preserves phonemic vowel length, has long vowels in nouns
corresponding to Hokkaido affiliatives with -hV ; Itabashi 2001 argues that
proto-Ainu coda consonants were lost in Hokkaido and partially compensated by pitch
accent, while their trace survives in Sakhalin long vowels itabashi2001 .
Under this account the /h/ in sapa-ha ,
siki-hi , etc. reflects a historical boundary consonant or coda
that became /h/ rather than zero in the possessive environment
nakagawa2024 229 ; vovin1993 ‹speculative›.

Sakhalin contrast and the -ike/-hike variant

Sakhalin Ainu uses the same vowel-copy mechanism but with the extended long form as the
more consistent default: where Hokkaido freely alternates short
hawe and long hawehe , Sakhalin typically
retains the extended shape. A community analysis of Sakhalin data observes that the stem
san yields sanihi via
san + insertion /i/ + -hi , and that
makan similarly yields makanihi ; the
analysis further notes a Sakhalin variant -hike in a subset of forms
( sani ike alternating with sanihi ),
where -ke may represent an additional nominal element appended to the affiliative
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-16) ‹speculative›. The distributional and
historical relationship between Sakhalin -hike and Hokkaido -hi has
not been formally investigated in the accessible sources ‹speculative›.

A distinct element -ike appears in a small set of Hokkaido compound nouns where
Chiri glosses it as 'person (who is X)' kindaichi1936 . The eastern-dialect
form onneke(he) 'parent/mother (Tokachi, Nemuro)' has been
analysed as onne 'old' + -ike , with the compound then
additionally taking the affiliative suffix ( onneke-he ), showing
that -ike here is a stem-forming element and the affiliative suffix is separate
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024-11-15) ‹speculative›. The affiliation
of this -ike to the affiliative paradigm, if any, is etymological rather than
synchronic and is not a productive pattern in Hokkaido Ainu.

title: Aims, Scope, and Design Philosophy of This Grammar
summary: What this reference grammar covers, whom it is for, and the integrated corpus-quantified, typologically-scored, diachronically-grounded method that distinguishes it from prior grammars.
part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Aims, Scope, and Design Philosophy of This Grammar
Part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
Summary: What this reference grammar covers, whom it is for, and the integrated corpus-quantified, typologically-scored, diachronically-grounded method that distinguishes it from prior grammars.

Aims and intended readership

This grammar provides a comprehensive English-language reference description of Hokkaido Ainu,
the primary indigenous language of Hokkaido, Japan. The object of description is the language's
phonological, morphological, morphosyntactic, and discourse structure as documented across the
Hokkaido dialect cluster — principally Saru, Chitose, Shizunai, Tokachi, Horobetsu, Mukawa, and
Ishikari — drawing on the full range of prior scholarship from Kindaichi and Chiri
kindaichi1936 through the modern reference grammars of Tamura
tamura1996 ; tamura2000 , Refsing refsing1986 , Shibatani
shibatani1990 , Satō sato2008 , Bugaeva bugaeva2022 , and
Nakagawa nakagawa2024 .

The intended readership is broad but assumes linguistic training. The grammar is addressed to
professional linguists working on polysynthesis, evidentiality, applicatives, alignment, or the
typology of head-marking languages; graduate students and advanced undergraduates in descriptive
and typological linguistics; Ainu studies researchers whose primary language of scholarship is
English or Japanese; documentation workers engaged with Ainu language materials; and community
members who approach the language through formal description. Readers without prior knowledge of
Ainu will find a structural overview in typological-profile and the historical and
cultural setting in ainu-people-homeland-history . Those already familiar with the
Japanese-language descriptive tradition may proceed directly to whichever domain interests them.

What a 'comprehensive reference grammar' commits to

A reference grammar is comprehensive in both depth and breadth: it covers every major
grammatical domain, provides attested examples for every claim it makes, states where the
literature disagrees and how seriously, and offers a quantitative profile of the phenomena it
describes wherever the data permit. The benchmark for breadth here is Nakagawa (2024)
nakagawa2024 , which organizes its description across 28 top-level chapters —
the most extensive single-authored description of Hokkaido Ainu yet published. The present
grammar targets roughly double that topic coverage, distributing 176 leaf chapters across 23
structural parts. The organizing sequence is the standard structural-descriptive one:
phonetics and phonology → morphophonology → word classes and nominal morphology → possession →
pronouns and demonstratives → personal affixes → verbal morphology → valency and voice → TAM →
evidentiality and modality → negation → basic clause structure → complex sentences and clause
linkage → discourse and information structure, bracketed by front matter, sources and
conventions, and back matter (diachrony, dialectology, contact, and texts).

Comprehensiveness means integrating all prior descriptions rather than supplanting them. Every
chapter cites the relevant treatments from the existing scholarship and states where they agree,
where they diverge, and what the grammar takes from each. The grammar claims comprehensiveness
of coverage and integration; it does not claim priority over any individual analysis.

Scope

Hokkaido Ainu as the object of description

Hokkaido Ainu is the primary object of description. It commands the great majority of the
accumulated corpus and the bulk of the modern descriptive literature, and its several dialects
differ enough to make dialect-internal comparison informative without requiring a separate
comparative grammar for each. The principal dialects drawn on are Saru (the most copiously
documented), Chitose, Shizunai, Tokachi, Horobetsu, Mukawa, and Ishikari. Finer locality
distinctions — Nibutani within Saru, Biratori, Honbetsu within Tokachi — are recorded in the
place field of each example rather than as separate dialect labels.

Sakhalin Ainu (also referred to as Enciw) and Kuril Ainu appear as external contrast material
throughout the grammar, presented in dedicated contrast boxes with their dialect tags
( SA , KU ) whenever they illuminate a specifically Hokkaido structural
choice. Several features of Hokkaido Ainu — the pitch-accent system, the four-term
evidential paradigm, the system of applicative preverbs, noun incorporation as a productive
syntactic operation, and the elaborate oral-literature register — have no direct counterpart
or show divergent organization in the Sakhalin variety; these receive expanded first-class
treatment, with Sakhalin drawn into comparison where the contrast is instructive. The
sociolinguistic situation, including the present state of the revitalization movement, is
treated in sociolinguistic-situation-revitalization .

The modern revitalization register — the standardized orthography and coined vocabulary
current among new-speaker communities — is described as a synchronic variety alongside the
documented oral tradition, with coinages and standardized forms explicitly flagged when they
differ from documented historical usage. The grammar's corpus frequency counts draw on
historical texts and conversation recordings rather than revival-norm materials, and the
distinction is maintained throughout.

Grammar, not dictionary

This is a grammar. Lexical material appears only as grammatical evidence: in examples, in
paradigm cells, and in class diagnostics. The grammar does not define senses of individual
lexemes beyond what is needed to gloss an example, and it links out to the available
dictionary resources rather than reproducing or paraphrasing their content. The dictionaries
and lexicographic records that underpin the grammar are surveyed in
written-sources-grammars-dictionaries ; the ethnographic and cultural context is
treated in ainu-people-homeland-history .

The four standing commitments

Four commitments run through every chapter. They are not segregated layers but part of how each
phenomenon is described from the outset.

Corpus quantification

Every frequency claim in the grammar is backed by counts from a large aligned corpus of
Hokkaido Ainu oral-literature and conversation data that includes material from the NINJAL
Corpus of Ainu oral literature ninjal2003 and the Glossed Audio Corpus of
Ainu Folklore glossedcorpus2016 . The corpus is treated as a register archive:
genre labels (uwepeker 'prose tale', yukar 'human epic', kamuy yukar 'divine epic', ritual
prayer, conversational recordings) permit per-genre frequency breakdowns. This genre-level
detail bears directly on several claims about TAM, evidentiality, and information-structural
phenomena whose distribution is register-conditioned. No predecessor grammar reports
systematic corpus frequencies for Hokkaido Ainu. The sampling methodology, dialect weighting,
and the conventions for citing figures are set out in full in
dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

Typological framing

Each grammatical domain is placed against a named cross-linguistic framework. At the level of
the language as a whole, the grammar produces a feature profile comparable to what WALS or
Grambank would supply for a set of typological parameters: overall structural type
(polysynthetic, head-marking, head-final, postpositional, modifier-before-head),
morphological alignment, valency-changing morphology, evidential paradigm type, number
marking, and numeral base. Within individual domains, standard typological reference works
supply the comparative scaffolding — the frameworks cited range from general morphosyntactic
typology to the specific literatures on alignment, evidentiality, applicatives,
nominalization, and noun incorporation. The purpose is to supply the comparative context that
makes descriptive choices legible to readers approaching from general linguistics.
typological-profile provides a condensed structural thumbnail of the whole
language that serves as an orientation map into the granular chapters.

Diachronic integration

Proto-Ainu reconstruction and grammaticalization pathways are embedded in the chapters
treating the relevant synchronic phenomena rather than quarantined in a separate historical
chapter. Where a diachronic source is established — the derivation of the evidential formal
nouns from possessed sensory nouns, the grammaticalization of postpositions from relational
nouns, the Sakhalin vowel-length correspondence to Hokkaido pitch accent — it is stated in
the synchronic chapter, with forward reference to the diachrony and contact parts of the
grammar (Parts XX–XXI). The genetic position of Ainu as a language isolate and the history
of its internal classification are treated in genetic-position-macro-comparison ;
the periodized history of its scholarly description is in
history-of-description-research .

The optional formal-analysis layer

Five domains of Hokkaido Ainu grammar have attracted sustained formal theoretical attention:
alignment and the personal-affix system (see alignment-split-intransitive and
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate ), the applicative preverb system
( applicative-system-overview ), noun incorporation
( noun-incorporation-object ), the nominalization-plus-copula evidential paradigm
( evidential-schema-overview ), and clause linkage and switch-reference-like
phenomena ( clause-linkage-overview ). For these five domains the grammar provides
an optional formal-analysis layer — explicit theoretical framing beyond the descriptive
synthesis — clearly sectioned so that readers interested only in the description can skip it
without losing continuity. Original re-analysis is confined to these five domains; in every
other chapter the grammar reports, synthesizes, and triangulates the existing literature.

The evidence-grading system

Every nontrivial or novel claim in the grammar carries an explicit evidence-grade label. Six
grades are in use. Consensus marks claims on which all major sources agree and which
are not in dispute. Contested marks genuine disagreements among sources: the competing
positions are stated, and none is declared settled. Corpus-confirmed marks claims
checked against the corpus and borne out by the data. Corpus-suggested marks claims
the corpus data point toward but do not conclusively establish. Speculative marks
plausible but unverified hypotheses, typically diachronic ones. Original-needs-review
marks new analyses proposed by the present grammar; this grade is restricted to the five formal
domains listed in the previous section and is flagged for editorial review.

Claims left ungraded are treated as consensus. A grade is placed at the end of the clause or
sentence it scopes, written as a small-caps label in guillemets:
‹contested›, ‹corpus-confirmed›, and so on. This is a working apparatus, not a
rhetorical device: a contested grade commits the grammar to stating the actual positions,
and a corpus-confirmed grade commits it to showing the relevant counts.

Prior descriptions and the position of this grammar

A full survey of the descriptive tradition — from the Edo-period wordlists and Batchelor
through Kindaichi, Chiri, and the modern era — appears in history-of-description-research ,
and a source-criticism audit of the major grammars, dictionaries, and text corpora is in
written-sources-grammars-dictionaries . The table below summarizes the book-length
treatments that define the field this grammar enters.

Work
Language
Dialect or scope
Format

kindaichi1936

Monograph grammar

chiri1942

Monograph grammar

refsing1986

Monograph grammar

tamura1996

Dictionary with integrated grammar

tamura2000

Chapter in survey volume

bugaeva2022
English

Handbook

nakagawa2024
Japanese
Hokkaido (Saru, Chitose, and others); 28 top-level chapters; 652 pp.
Comprehensive reference grammar

Each work contributes something indispensable. Kindaichi and Chiri establish the basic
morphological architecture that all subsequent grammars build on kindaichi1936 .
Refsing provides the most systematic morphosyntactic account in English of any single Hokkaido
dialect refsing1986 . Shibatani places Ainu within a typological account of the
languages of Japan shibatani1990 . Tamura's Saru dictionary and grammar monograph
remain the central data source for that dialect tamura1996 ; tamura2000 .
Satō's grammar gives the most thorough treatment of the Chitose verb system and Hokkaido
morphosyntax in the modern Japanese-language literature sato2008 . The Bugaeva
Handbook supplies extensive English coverage across topics from phonetics to oral literature and
from Hokkaido to Sakhalin bugaeva2022 . Nakagawa's grammar brings the description
of Hokkaido Ainu to its greatest single-volume comprehensiveness nakagawa2024 .

The grammar works from all of these, citing each directly. What the present description adds is
a corpus-quantified layer — systematic frequency counts for morphological cells, evidential
paradigm members, alignment environments, and sandhi rates — alongside cross-dialect integration
extending beyond the Saru–Chitose pair that dominates the modern literature, and an explicit
typological scoring at both the language level and within each domain. Where prior analyses
disagree — on the number of formal persons, on the morphological alignment of the paradigm, on
the word-class status of property concepts, on the syntax of the evidential constructions — the
disagreements are stated explicitly and graded.

Community leads and learner-grammar material

Two bodies of material outside the formal research literature bear on specific domains. The
aynu-corpora Discord community has accumulated analytical discussion of Ainu grammar across
several thousand archived messages, including morpheme identifications, pattern observations,
and occasional structural hypotheses aynucorporadiscord . These contributions are
treated as leads: points of departure for corpus investigation and source-checking. Where a
community observation has been verified against the scholarly literature or the corpus it is
integrated under the appropriate evidence grade and cited with the contributor's handle. Where
it has not been verified it is not included.

Ijäs (2023) ijas2023 provides an accessible English-language learner grammar of
Ainu, available as an online guide. Its structural analyses are cited where they present a
reading of a construction not covered in the research literature, and graded accordingly. It is
also cited for its treatment of revival-register conventions. Being addressed primarily to
learners rather than to the documentary record, its analyses of the teaching norm and of the
historical oral corpus are distinguished when citing it.

How to use this grammar

The grammar can be read linearly — beginning with typological-profile for a
structural orientation, then moving through the sources and conventions chapters of Part II
before entering the substantive description — or consulted chapter by chapter for specific
phenomena. Chapter numbering is sequential and book-wide, but cross-references are always given
by chapter title and slug, so individual chapters can be read independently without knowing
their sequence number.

The interlinear glossing conventions — the four-line format, morpheme-boundary symbols, and the
Ainu-specific gloss inventory — are documented in glossing-abbreviations-and-citation ;
the full abbreviation list is in abbreviations-glossing-symbols . The Latin
phonemic transcription used throughout the grammar is explained in
latin-phonemic-transcription . The oral-literature and spoken-language data sources
that supply most of the attested examples are surveyed in oral-literature-and-spoken-corpora .

The optional formal-analysis sections in the five theory-magnet domains are clearly delimited
within each chapter. Readers who want the descriptive account without the formal apparatus can
skip those sections; cross-references from the formal sections into the descriptive content are
one-directional, so the descriptive text stands without them. The bibliography at the close of
each chapter is generated automatically from the citations that appear in it and lists only
works actually cited in that chapter.

title: Ainu Loanwords and Toponymy in Japanese
summary: Ainu's imprint on Japanese — animal/fish/plant loanwords in standard and northern-dialect Japanese, and the Ainu (substrate) toponymy of Hokkaido and northern Honshu.
part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Ainu Loanwords and Toponymy in Japanese
Part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
Summary: Ainu's imprint on Japanese — animal/fish/plant loanwords in standard and northern-dialect Japanese, and the Ainu (substrate) toponymy of Hokkaido and northern Honshu.

Overview

Ainu's classification as a language isolate, surveyed in
genetic-position-macro-comparison , means that no genetic relationship accounts
for resemblances between Ainu and Japanese vocabulary. Demonstrable shared items are contact
loans, and the directionality of that contact is asymmetric by period and domain. The
companion chapter japanese-loanwords-in-ainu documents the large stratum of
Japanese vocabulary that entered Ainu after sustained post-medieval contact with the Wajin:
domesticated animals, material culture, administrative titles, and, from the Meiji era
onward, unmarked quotations of modern Japanese terms. The reverse current — Ainu material
entering Japanese — is narrower in scope and less systematically studied. Its primary
evidence is toponymic, and lexical loans proper are few. Vovin
vovin2022 §1 observes that "it is silently assumed that there are no Ainu
loanwords in Japanese" and acknowledges the absence of any comprehensive study; the same
observation is echoed in Ijäs's typological overview of the language
ijas2023 .

The geographical distribution of Ainu-origin place names testifies to a former Ainu
presence across a wider area than the historically documented homeland.
shibatani1990 §1 records that the toponymic distribution across Hokkaidō
is extensive and that "many place names in the northern part of Honshū indicate that they
might have once lived in that region as well"; Ainu place names also occur in southern
Kamchatka. Chiri Mashiho devoted major scholarly effort to this toponymic substrate
chiri1956 , and that dictionary remains the standard reference for individual
etymologies. Individual toponyms cited in this chapter as examples have been verified only
at the level of their Ainu component elements through the Tamura and Nakagawa dictionaries,
not against the primary place-name sources.

The toponymic substrate

A small set of Ainu geographic roots recur as diagnostic final elements in Japanese place
names, constituting the backbone of the toponymic substrate. Because these elements are
independently attested in the Ainu lexicon with stable meanings
tamura1996 s.v. nay, pet, us, oma, to, sir, nupuri, kotan ;
nakagawa1995 s.v. nay, oma , the toponymic analysis rests on solid
morphological footing at the elemental level even where the etymology of a specific
place name requires verification against chiri1956 . Japanese reflexes typically
show rendaku (sequential voicing: pet → -betsu ),
phonological reduction, or orthographic regularization through kanji choice — adaptations
governed by Japanese phonotactics, not by Ainu phonology
shibatani1990 §1 .

Ainu element
meaning in Ainu
Japanese reflex
illustrative toponyms
source

nay stream, tributary
'stream, tributary (of a locality's main river)'
-nai ナイ
稚内 Wakka nai , 木古内 Kiko nai , 歌志内 Utashi nai

tamura1996 s.v. nay ; vovin2022 §2

pet river
'river (the representative waterway of a locality)'

-betsu ベツ (rendaku of pet )

登別 Nobori betsu , 紋別 Mon betsu , 芦別 Ashi betsu , 幌別 Horo betsu

tamura1996 s.v. pet ; shibatani1990 §1

us be habitually at, grow at / -us(i)
'be habitually attached to/at'; nominalized: 'place where X habitually is'
-ushi / -us ウシ
有珠 U su ; 新冠 Nii kappu (< ni-kap-us-i , illustrative)
tamura1996 s.v. us, -us(i)

oma be located in/at / -oma(-i)
'be located in/at'; nominalized: 'place where X is'
-omai / -oma
苫小牧 Tomakomai (< to-mak-oma-i ?, illustrative)

tamura1996 s.v. oma ; nakagawa1995 s.v. oma

to lake, pond
'lake, pond'
-to / To- ト
洞爺湖 Tōyako (< to-ya 'lake-shore', illustrative)
tamura1996 s.v. to

sir land, visible landscape
'land, earth, visible landscape'
Sire- / Shir-
知床 Shiretoko (< sir-etok 'headland', illustrative)
shibatani1990 §1

nupuri mountain

Nuburi- / Nopporo- (reductions)
野幌 Nopporo (< nupuri ?, illustrative)

Kotan-

All etymologies in the table are illustrative; individual place names require verification
against chiri1956 and related primary sources. A specific Japanese name
may involve phonological reduction, analogical replacement, or overlay by later Japanese
morphology that obscures the Ainu source compound. The component Ainu forms are well
attested in the living lexicon; it is the mapping from a given Japanese string to those
components that calls for caution.

The nay ~ pet distinction

The contrast between nay stream, tributary and
pet river reflects a live semantic distinction in the Ainu lexicon, not
merely the presence of two suffix shapes. Tamura (1996, s.v. pet )
characterizes it from the Saru area: pet designates the
representative waterway of a locality — the Saru, Mukawa, and Monbetsu rivers all carry
this label — while a branch stream or tributary is nay . The
criterion is typicality, not size: even a small stream that defines its valley is
pet , while a large tributary of a major river remains
nay . Corpus frequencies reflect this asymmetry:
pet occurs 1,344 times (rank 140) against
nay 's 559 (rank 312) in the Hokkaido text corpus, consistent with
the broader referential range of the former.

nay san | stream go.down | The stream flows downstream. | Constructed illustration of nay as a geographic common noun. The motion verb san 'go down toward the sea/shore' takes nay as its subject; the construction shows nay as a free noun in an active predication. The san direction also underlies the panke/penke 'downstream/upstream' spatial terms that feed Hokkaido toponym patterns.

The semantic distinction matters for toponymic analysis: a -nai place name
designates a tributary or smaller mountain stream, while a -betsu name
marks the primary waterway of its valley. A common folk-etymological error equates
nay with 'pond'; nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-11,
corroborating Tamura 1996) registers this as a mistaken conflation: 'pond' or 'lake' is
to lake, pond , a semantically distinct root with its own toponymic reflex
aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The locative formatives -us(i) and -oma(i)

Two further formatives encode habitual or permanent location and are productive in
place-name formation.

The verbal element us be habitually at, grow at (Tamura 1996, s.v.
us ) means 'be habitually attached to, grow at'; the suffix
-us(i) nominalizes this to 'place where X habitually grows or is
found'. The compound ni-kap-us-i — ni
'tree' + kap 'bark' + -us-i 'habitually-at'
— yields 'place where bark trees stand', commonly cited as the source of Niikappu 新冠
(illustrative only; tamura1996 s.v. -us(i) ). The suffix also surfaces in
Usu 有珠 and several other Hokkaido names.

The element oma be located in/at 'be located in, be contained in' (Tamura
1996, s.v. oma ; Nakagawa 1995, s.v. oma )
forms the nominalized oma-i 'place where X is located', the source
of the Japanese -omai / -oma endings. In Ainu syntax,
oma also appears in postpositional constructions expressing
containment; this overlap between its lexical geographic use and its grammatical locative
function is discussed in relational-and-spatial-nouns .

A further geographic compound illustrates the compounding pattern that underlies Hokkaido
toponymy:

sir etok | land head | tip of the land; headland, cape | Constructed illustration of the compound underlying Shiretoko 知床 (Shibatani 1990 §1). sir 'land, visible landscape' combines with etok 'tip, head' to form a compound place name; the Japanese reflex preserves the Ainu compound structure.

This compounding pattern — a landscape attribute or associated entity plus a geographic
generic ( nay , pet ,
to , etc.) — is the typical structure of an Ainu place name; the
phonological adaptation that Japanese applies when borrowing such compounds is treated in
syllable-template .

The Tōhoku substrate and Vovin's Old Japanese evidence

The toponymic data extend beyond Hokkaidō into northern Honshū.
shibatani1990 §1 observes that many place names in northern Honshū suggest
historical Ainu presence there as well. Vovin vovin2022 §1–2 extends the
argument into the philological record: he proposes that a number of items in Eastern Old
Japanese — texts preserved in Man'yōshū books 14 and 20, compiled in the
8th century — are Ainu loans, reflecting a prehistoric Ainu substrate in the Kantō and
Tōhoku regions ‹contested›.

Selected Ainu loan candidates in Eastern Old Japanese, after Vovin (2022, Ch.6 §2). All
etymologies are Vovin's proposals and are contested; see text.

Old Japanese form
proposed Ainu source
proposed gloss
Man'yōshū attestation

'river, stream'

'headland, cape'

Vovin also argues that modern Japanese iruka 'dolphin' is an Ainu loan,
"isolated in Japonic; cf. Saru rika / Kuril
rika 'whale meat'" vovin2022 §2 ‹contested›.

This programme forms a minority and contested position. Vovin (2022 Ch.6 §1) himself
acknowledges the prevailing working assumption against Ainu loans in Japanese, and
individual etymologies in the table are disputable on grounds of sound correspondence and
comparative methodology; the full debate on Ainu's historical position and the validity of
deep-contact proposals belongs to macro-comparison-and-deep-contact-controversies
‹contested›.

At the community level, nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-01-22) proposes that the Iwate
place name Otomonai (乙茂内) — both a surname and a stream name in an area dense with
-nai endings — derives from Ainu
o-tum-nay 'head-middle-stream'. The analysis is morphologically
plausible within Ainu and consistent with the Tōhoku -nai substrate, but
has not been verified against chiri1956 or published place-name catalogues
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.

A case study: sikerpe and the Japanese siko-type

Ochiai (2026) provides a model of rigorous Ainu → Japanese lexical identification.
The Ainu form sikerpe Amur cork-tree fruit — fruit of the Amur cork tree
( キハダ Phellodendron amurense ) — is analysed as a reflex of
Proto-Ainu *siw kor pe 'thing that holds bitterness':
siw 'bitter' + kor 'hold, have' +
pe 'thing'. The proto-form shifted to
sikerpe through w -deletion and regressive
vowel assimilation ( o → e ) ochiai2026 196–199 .

siw-kor-pe | bitter-have-thing | (Proto-Ainu) thing that holds bitterness; ancestor of sikerpe 'Amur cork-tree fruit' | Constructed illustration of the reconstructed proto-form *siw kor pe per Ochiai (2026: 196). The attested Saru form sikerpe reflects w-deletion and regressive vowel assimilation (o → e). This is an analytical construction, not a form found in any attested Ainu text.

A cluster of Japanese dialectal names for the same tree — most conservatively
sikorope in Akita, with reduced forms sikoro ,
sikonohe , siko no ki elsewhere — are loans from Ainu
*si kor pe , borrowed before the Ainu vowel change to
*si ker pe had completed ochiai2026 199 . Two
complementary arguments support the claim. First, the Japanese siko- type
is morphologically opaque in Japanese and has no Japanese-internal etymology, while the
Ainu compound is transparent — the asymmetry that is diagnostic of borrowing direction.
Second, the Japanese form preserves the earlier vowel /o/ in the second syllable, showing
that the borrowing antedates the Ainu sound change; this gives a terminus ante quem for
the Ainu historical phonology, linking contact evidence to reconstruction
ochiai2026 197–199 . The reconstruction side of this analysis connects to
proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction .

Lexical loans beyond toponymy

Regional (matagi) vocabulary

Northern Honshū hunter vocabulary (matagi speech) furnishes candidates for Ainu lexical
retention in an area where the toponymic substrate already indicates historical Ainu
presence. Two items are community-proposed: antitwilight (aynu-corpora Discord,
2026-06-09) proposes matagi ɸina 'elder brother (matagi usage)' as a
reflex of Ainu pinne male, masculine , and matagi
hatagi / hattagi 'locust' as a reflex of Ainu
pattaki grasshopper aynucorporadiscord . Both proposals are
phonologically plausible — the initial p- → ɸ- change and the
h- variant reflect regular Japanese alternations — and they are consistent
with Vovin's OJ substrate thesis, but neither has been verified against published
philological sources for the matagi lexicon ‹speculative›.

Standard Japanese items attributed to Ainu origin

Several items in standard Japanese are widely attributed to Ainu origin in the popular
literature and encyclopaedic reference works. None could be verified in the sources consulted
for this chapter; they are listed with that status recorded.

Japanese word
meaning
proposed Ainu source
status

rakko
sea otter ( Enhydra lutris )
Ainu (source form not established here)
[UNVERIFIED in sources read]

tonakai
reindeer ( Rangifer tarandus )
Ainu (source form not established here)
[UNVERIFIED in sources read]

shishamo
willow smelt ( Spirinchus lanceolatus )
susam
[UNVERIFIED in sources read]

konbu
kelp ( Saccharina japonica )
Ainu (etymology disputed in popular sources)
[UNVERIFIED in sources read]

Asserting these as Ainu loans without a primary source risks repeating popular etymology.
Vovin's argument that iruka 'dolphin' is an Ainu loan — "isolated in
Japonic; cf. Saru rika / Kuril rika
'whale meat'" ( vovin2022 §2 ) — belongs to his OJ substrate programme and
carries the same contested status ‹contested›.

On Sakhalin, the contact partners were Nivkh and Uilta rather than Japanese, and the
Ainu loans that did cross language boundaries there went into those languages, not into
Japanese, as surveyed in northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu . The
Hokkaidō → Japanese transfer treated in this chapter is therefore a southern phenomenon
without a direct Sakhalin parallel.

Methodological cautions

Place-name etymology faces several systematic pitfalls that bear on the analysis above.

Japanese phonological adaptation erases information present in the Ainu source.
Coda consonants, the voiced/voiceless distinction absent from Ainu, and syllable-weight
differences are all reorganized by Japanese phonotactics, as described in
syllable-template . Rendaku produces pet →
-betsu ; recovering the Ainu source requires knowledge of both the Ainu
phonological inventory and the Japanese adaptation rules. Reading Ainu-derived place names
through kanji orthography produces folk etymologies — 稚内 as 'young
interior'? — that are wholly Japanese in structure and irrelevant to the Ainu source.

A second difficulty is the limited documentation of Ainu as spoken across many areas. As
discussed in sociolinguistic-situation-revitalization , recording of Ainu
varieties was geographically uneven, and for areas with no documented variety —
Hakodate, Erimo, Rumoi, Otaru — place-name reconstruction by extrapolation from
better-recorded dialects is at best speculative. The constraint that a proposed source
compound must be morphologically analysable within Ainu and that phonological
correspondences must be regular remains the methodological floor for any specific toponym
claim.

A third caution is convergence. Phonologically similar strings may arise from unrelated
sources, and it is possible that some Ainu geographic terms entered Ainu at an earlier
period from a continental substrate. Antitwilight (aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-15) has
proposed Korean-origin candidates for several Ainu roots, including
nay , casi , and others
aynucorporadiscord . This is unverified and has not been tested against the
comparative literature; it is noted here as an acknowledged edge case, not a claim
‹speculative›.

title: The Ainu People, Their Homeland, and History
summary: The ethnonym, geography, society, and contact history that frame the language and its present endangerment.
part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Ainu People, Their Homeland, and History
Part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
Summary: The ethnonym, geography, society, and contact history that frame the language and its present endangerment.

The ethnonym Aynu and related designations

The word aynu is a common noun meaning 'person, human being'; it is
also the ethnic self-designation of the people whose language this grammar describes
janhunen2022 §2.4 ; shibatani1990 §1.1 . In its ethnic use
the term identifies a person as a member of the Ainu community, as distinct from the gods
( kamuy ), and marks the boundary against neighbouring peoples. Its
territorial correlate is Ainu Moshir
( aynu mo-sir ) 'Ainu land', the indigenous name for the homeland
janhunen2022 §2.4 .

A second self-referential term, utar 'people', appears in affiliative
form as utari 'relatives, our people'; it was used as the basis for
the name of the Hokkaidō Utari Kyōkai ( 北海道ウタリ協会 ), the main
Ainu civic organization for much of the twentieth century
janhunen2022 §2.4 . The Sakhalin Ainu have a distinct self-designation,
enciw , which their modern descendants in Japan have adopted as a
distinguishing label
janhunen2022 §2.4 .

The exonym "Ainu" (in older literature also "Aino") is now standard in both Japanese and
international usage. Through much of the twentieth century the Japanese government applied
the term 旧土人 ( kyū-dojin , 'former aborigines') in official
documents, most notably as part of the 1899 legislation that governed the Ainu's legal status
ono2022 §2 . This usage was formally abandoned with the abolition of that
legislation in 1997. Within the Hokkaido Ainu community, the relative weight of
"Ainu" versus "Utari" as preferred public designations was contested during the 1980s and
1990s, and the discussion reflects continuing negotiations over political identity
‹contested›.

The Ainu homeland and its historical range

Hokkaido, the northernmost of Japan's main islands, was the core of the historical Ainu
territory janhunen2022 §2.1 ; shibatani1990 §1.1 . The same
ethnic population extended across the full chain of the Kuril Islands and across the southern
half of Sakhalin (Japanese: Karafuto; Ainu: Karapto ); a marginal
Ainu presence on the southernmost tip of Kamchatka is also attested in ethnonymic and
archaeological sources
janhunen2022 §2.1 .

Evidence for an earlier, more southerly range on Honshu comes principally from toponymy. The
endings -nay river, brook and -pet river , both characteristic
of Hokkaido river names, appear in dense clusters across the Tōhoku and Kantō regions,
pointing to Ainu (or an immediately ancestral variety) having been spoken well south of
Hokkaido at some period
shibatani1990 §1.1 ; chiri1956 . Linguistic borrowing evidence —
potential Ainu-derived words in early Japonic texts — has been advanced as additional support
for a Honshu range vovin2022 , though the direction and scale of those loans
remain a matter of specialist debate ‹contested›. The southern limit of former Ainu territory
on Honshu cannot be fixed with precision on present evidence ‹contested›. The genetic position
of the language within this spatial history is treated in
genetic-position-macro-comparison .

Within Hokkaido the Ainu dialect cluster is internally diverse: the dialects of the Saru,
Chitose, Ishikari, Tokachi, Horobetsu, Shizunai, Asahikawa, and Yakumo areas differ
phonologically and morphologically in ways that the corpus chapters track in detail. Sakhalin
Ainu (abbreviated SA in this grammar) and the now-extinct Kuril variety
( KU ) are cited comparatively; the dialect sample and method are set out in
dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

Subsistence, society, and the documentary record

Ainu subsistence in the historical period centred on fishing, hunting, and gathering, with
strong seasonal and ecological patterning shibatani1990 §1.1 ;
janhunen2022 §2.5 . Settlement followed rivers and coastlines, and the
vocabulary reflects this orientation: the various stages of the salmon's life cycle are
distinguished by roughly twenty distinct lexical items, and some fifty words for harbour seals
and more than twenty for types of whale point to the centrality of sea-mammal hunting in the
coastal zones shibatani1990 §1.1 . Among land animals, bears held a special
ceremonial role: the word kamuy god, bear serves both as the generic term for
the divine and as a specific designation for the bear, which was understood as a mountain god
that brought its meat to the village shibatani1990 §1.1 . Dogs figure
prominently in subsistence and ceremony alike.

The precolonial Ainu maintained a diversified economy that also included small-scale
horticulture and, via the Santan trade, the exchange of furs, dried fish, and lacquerware
for metal goods and silk manufactured further inland on the Asian continent
janhunen2022 §2.5 . Population size is uncertain for the precolonial period;
a figure close to 25,000, possibly more, has been suggested for Hokkaido alone
janhunen2022 §2.5 .

A rich tradition of oral literature — the heroic epics called yukar heroic epic ,
the divine epics called kamuy yukar divine epic , and the prose folktales
called uwepeker prosaic folktale — was the principal medium for transmitting
cultural knowledge and was performed in a register distinct from everyday speech. The oral
tradition and its register differences are directly relevant to grammar because the
kamuy yukar corpus contains the densest concentration of older morphological forms and
archaic vocabulary. The oral-literature genres and their linguistic properties are treated
in the corpus chapters; the grammatical differences between the oral-literary and
colloquial registers appear throughout the descriptive chapters.

The archaeological background: from Jōmon to the historical Ainu

The ancestors of the historical Ainu arrived on Hokkaido in connection with the Satsumon
culture (c. 700–1200 CE), an archaeological complex that spread northward from Honshu and
replaced the earlier local Epi-Jōmon traditions in southern Hokkaido
janhunen2022 §3 . The linguistic lineage that became Ainu has roots in the
Jōmon period on Honshu; Ainu can therefore be described as a "Jōmonic" language in the
sense that it likely preserves features that were broadly characteristic of pre-Yayoi Japan
janhunen2022 §3a . As a consequence, Hokkaido itself is a secondary
territory for the Ainu; their earlier homeland was on Honshu, from which the Satsumon
complex — and the ancestral Ainu language — was displaced northward by the Yayoi and
subsequent Yamato expansion
janhunen2022 §3 .

The Satsumon expansion on Hokkaido encountered the Okhotsk culture (c. 600–1600 CE), a
littoral and maritime complex that had spread to northern Hokkaido from Sakhalin
janhunen2022 §3 . The interaction and gradual absorption of Okhotsk
populations by the Satsumon bearers contributed both genetic and cultural material — and
potentially some linguistic features — to the complex that became the historically attested
Ainu ethnicity. The chronology of the Ainu territorial expansion can be traced approximately
from this archaeological evidence: northern Hokkaido was under Ainu ethnic domination by
roughly 1000 CE; southern Sakhalin was reached by roughly 1300 CE; and the Kurile Islands,
including the Northern Kuriles, were occupied by Ainu speakers as late as c. 1500–1600 CE
janhunen2022 §3 . All these dates are approximations, and the specific
identification of the Satsumon bearers as Ainu-speakers rests on an inference from
archaeological continuity rather than direct linguistic evidence ‹contested›.

Approximate chronology of the Ainu territorial and cultural record

Period / event
Approximate date
Source

c. 14,000–900 BCE (Jōmon period)
janhunen2022 §3a

janhunen2022 §3

c. 700–1200 CE
janhunen2022 §3

c. 600–1600 CE
janhunen2022 §3

c. 1000 CE
janhunen2022 §3

c. 1300 CE
janhunen2022 §3

c. 1500–1600 CE
janhunen2022 §3

Contact history: the Matsumae domain and Russian expansion

Japanese contact with the Ainu of southern Hokkaido predates formal political control by
centuries. The earliest surviving written records of the language, including the Matsumae no
koto — Ezo kotoba no koto of c. 1600 with 117 words compiled for use by merchants, come
from this trade context
refsing1986 17 . The Matsumae domain ( 松前藩 ),
established at the southern tip of Hokkaido under Tokugawa authority, held a monopoly on
trade with the Ainu through the Edo period; Ainu communities in the interior of the island
and in the Kurile Islands remained outside this domain's political control
janhunen2022 §2.1 . Commerce in furs, dried fish, and eagle feathers
constituted the principal economic link between the Ainu and the Japanese domains. Episodic
tensions over trade terms escalated into armed conflicts, most notably the Shakushain War of
1669–1672 and the Menashi-Kunashir uprising of 1789, both of which ended with Ainu defeat
and tightened Matsumae control ‹contested› (the interpretation of Ainu agency and
objectives is debated).

The broader trade network extending from Sakhalin into the Amur basin and ultimately
connecting with Qing China — the Santan trade ( 山丹交易 ) — supplied
the Ainu with metal goods and silk in exchange for northern products
janhunen2022 §2.1 . This trade also fostered sustained contact between the
Sakhalin Ainu and their Nivkh and Uilta neighbours, the linguistic traces of which are
reviewed in the Handbook's chapter on language contact
shiraishitangiku2022 . Russian exploration and colonization of Kamchatka from
the early eighteenth century brought a second colonial power into contact with the Kurile
Ainu; Sakhalin was transferred from Manchu to Russian control by the Treaty of Peking (1860),
and the island's status was subsequently settled between Japan and Russia by the Treaty of
St. Petersburg (1875), which assigned Sakhalin to Russia and the Kurile Islands to Japan
janhunen2022 §2.1 .

Meiji-era annexation and assimilation policies

The formal annexation of Hokkaido by the Meiji state and its opening to Japanese
colonization in 1869–1870 ended the Ainu's position as the dominant population of the
island within a few decades
janhunen2022 §2.1 . Japanese settler immigration proceeded rapidly: from
a figure close to the precolonial Ainu population of roughly 25,000, settlers came to
outnumber the Ainu by an ever-growing margin
janhunen2022 §2.5 . The Hokkaido Former Aborigines Protection Act of 1899
( 北海道旧土人保護法 ) placed the Ainu in a defined legal category
as wards of the state, assigned them small agricultural land plots, and legislated
their assimilation into Japanese society
ono2022 §2 . No formal legal prohibition of the Ainu language was enacted,
but the use of the language was restricted or effectively banned in schools, and children
were pressured to learn Japanese in place of Ainu; administrative discrimination reinforced
the suppression of public language use
ono2022 §2 .

The human cost of this period appears in the census figures available, though the figures
must be read with awareness that assimilation policy itself distorted their collection.
The 1940 census recorded 16,170 Ainu in Hokkaido; the 1935 census put the Sakhalin Ainu
population at 1,512; the Kurile Ainu were relocated to Shikotan in 1884, and by 1933 only
forty-one descendants survived — that variety of Ainu is now extinct
shibatani1990 §1.1 . After 1945, Sakhalin Ainu communities were evacuated
to Hokkaido; the last speaker of Sakhalin Ainu died in 1994
janhunen2022 §2.3 . Intercommunal marriage and the discontinuation of Ainu
census categories make reliable demographic estimates for the late twentieth century
difficult; figures reported in earlier surveys suggest a population of roughly 16,000 to
24,000 persons with Ainu ancestry in Hokkaido, with wide uncertainty depending on
self-identification criteria ‹contested›. By the second half of the twentieth century
the language had ceased to be transmitted to children in most Hokkaido communities
ono2022 §2 . The present sociolinguistic situation and the documentation
record are taken up in sociolinguistic-situation-revitalization .

From the 1997 law to the 2019 recognition

The 1899 act was abolished in 1997, when the Japanese Diet enacted the Ainu Culture
Promotion Act ( アイヌ文化の振興並びにアイヌの伝統等に関する知識の普及
及び啓発に関する法律 ). This legislation provided institutional funding for
cultural activities and language classes for the first time, but did not recognize
the Ainu as an indigenous people with specific rights
ono2022 §6 . A unanimous resolution by the Japanese parliament in 2008
recognized the Ainu as the indigenous people of Japan, though without statutory force
janhunen2022 §1 . The Act on Promoting Measures to Realize a Society
in Which the Pride of the Ainu People Is Respected, enacted in April 2019, superseded
the 1997 act: it provides the statutory definition of the Ainu as an indigenous people,
establishes support mechanisms for cultural and language activities, and authorized the
establishment of the National Ainu Museum ( Upopoy ), which opened
in Shiraoi in 2020
ono2022 §7 . The linguistic implications of revitalization policy —
including the standardization pressures that accompany institutionalized language classes
and the rise of new-speaker varieties — are discussed in
sociolinguistic-situation-revitalization .

The documentary record that this grammar draws on is a product of this entire history: the
bulk of the attested Hokkaido Ainu material was collected in the early-to-mid twentieth
century from elderly speakers in communities where the language was already in decline.
The unevenness of that record — richer for some dialects (Saru, Chitose) and thinner for
others — directly shapes what can and cannot be described with confidence, and the corpus
chapters note coverage gaps as they arise. The history of Ainu language description and
the main grammars and dictionaries consulted in this work are surveyed in
history-of-description-research ; the corpus method and dialect sample
are set out in dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

title: The Alienable/Inalienable Split and Possessive Classification
summary: Hokkaido Ainu's binary possessive split — obligatorily possessed inalienable nouns vs. periphrastically possessed alienable nouns — its semantic classes, gradience, and place on the alienability-typology map.
part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Alienable/Inalienable Split and Possessive Classification
Part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
Summary: Hokkaido Ainu's binary possessive split — obligatorily possessed inalienable nouns vs. periphrastically possessed alienable nouns — its semantic classes, gradience, and place on the alienability-typology map.

Two dimensions and three constructions

Hokkaido Ainu has no genitive case-particle, no possessive preposition or postposition, and
no adjectival inflection for possession. Possession is instead expressed either by
head-marking on the possessed noun itself or by a verb-based attributive clause using
kor have, possess . The language is "predominantly head-marking, exhibiting …
polysynthesis, and having a robust alienability opposition"
bugaeva2021poss 52 .

Bugaeva, Nichols & Bickel identify two independent grammatical dimensions that should not
be conflated bugaeva2021poss 46–47, 58–60, 81–82 . The first is
possessibility — whether a noun's argument structure includes a possessor slot at
all (optionally, obligatorily, or not at all). The second is the coding strategy —
whether the relationship, when licensed, is expressed by head-marking on the possessed noun
or by a verbal attributive clause with kor . These dimensions
correlate with the traditional alienability distinction but the correlation is fixed
grammatically rather than speaker-chosen: "a speaker does not decide to convey whether an
item can be owned … the choice of a noun determines the possessive construction"
bugaeva2021poss 47 .

The two grammatical dimensions of Hokkaido Ainu possession

dimension
values
Hokkaido Ainu realisation

possessibility
optionally possessible / obligatorily possessed / non-possessible

body parts and kin = obligatorily possessed; artefacts, animals, places =
optionally possessible; wild fauna and flora = typically non-possessible

coding strategy
inalienable (head-marked) / alienable (verbal attributive)
affiliative construction vs. kor construction

Three surface patterns cover "N₁'s N₂" in Hokkaido Ainu. The first two are possession
constructions; the third is a modifier–head relation (quality, material, or kind) and is not
possession nakagawa2024 217–218 ; sato2008 150–151 :

#
construction
template
relation
example

1
alienable ( kor )

N₁ kor N₂ concept

transferable possession

a=kor cip 'my canoe'

2
inalienable (affiliative)
A-prefix=N₂ affiliative
non-transferable possession

ku=tek-ehe 'my hand'

3
modifier (non-possessive)

N₁ concept N₂ concept

quality / material / kind

kane cise 'metal house'

The diagnostic that pattern (3) is not possession is person-substitutability: in patterns
(1) and (2) the possessor N₁ can be replaced by a person-marking affix
( ku=kor cip , ku=tek-ehe ); in (3) it cannot —
*ku=ru 'my footprint' is impossible, even though
seta ru 'a dog's footprint' is well-formed
nakagawa2024 218 . The two possession constructions are in complementary
distribution per noun: the construction is determined by noun-class membership, not by the
speaker's choice at utterance time nakagawa2024 218 ;
bugaeva2021poss 47 . Where that membership is gradient or
construal-sensitive, the picture is more complex (§4 below). The morphophonology of the
affiliative suffix is detailed in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology , and the
concept/affiliative distinction as a noun-class parameter in
concept-form-affiliative-form .

The kor construction

In the alienable construction the possessed noun (N₂) remains in the concept form and is
preceded by the transitive verb kor have, possess ; the possessor (N₁ or a
person-marking prefix) is the transitive subject of that verb:
a=kor cip 'my canoe',
a=kor makiri 'my knife',
a=kor hayokpe 'my armour'
nakagawa2024 218 . The semantics are those of alienable (transferable)
possession: the possessed entity's existence is independent of the possessor's, and transfer
to another person makes it theirs nakagawa2024 218 . The negative counterpart
replaces kor with sak lack ; predicative HAVE and BE
schemas are treated in predicative-possession-clauses , and the particle
kor 'while' that is homophonous with the verb in
simultaneous-kor-clauses .

ku=kor seta hure toan kur kor cape retar | 1SG.A=have dog be.red DIST.DEM person have cat be.white | My dog is red; that person's cat is white. | Minimal alienable pair: 1SG subject prefix ku= (first kor) vs. overt third-person possessor NP toan kur 'that person' (second kor). Both seta 'dog' and cape 'cat' remain in the concept form. | utari1994 | HK

Bugaeva et al. analyse the kor construction as an
appositive possessive with a verbal modifier : the attributive is a finite relative
clause with kor whose object is gapped and coreferential with the
head noun bugaeva2021poss 55 . The construction retains relative-clause
properties: kor can carry perfective and plural morphology and
supports relative-clause stacking
bugaeva2021poss 55–58 . With a third-person possessor,
kor loses overt person-marking and cliticises to the head noun —
Anna katkemat kor-ninkari — a step toward head-marking that
Bugaeva et al. characterise as ongoing grammaticalisation
bugaeva2021poss 59 ‹speculative›.

The kor construction also extends to belonging and
social dominion , not only transferable ownership. When lexicalised in the compound
N- kor - kur 'the one who holds N', it yields
titles of authority such as kotan kor kur 'village chief' (lit.
'village-holding person') and the divine epithet
nupuri kor kamuy 'the god who owns the mountain'
nakagawa2024 218–219 ; bugaeva2021poss 56 :

wa kotan kor kur a=ne wa an=an | CONJ village have person 4.A=COP CONJ exist=4.S | I was the village chief. | Narrator uses narrative fourth person (a=, =an) for first-person reference throughout the text. kotan kor kur marks social dominion rather than transferable ownership of the village. | ilcaa1976 | SAR

The set of nouns attested in the kor construction is lexically
bounded. Drawing on Tamura's dictionary, Bugaeva et al. identify six classes of alienable
nouns: people and kin (the largest class, 24 items including
aynu , hapo ,
nispa ), place and environment (11:
kotan , mosir ,
pet ), food and drink (7), tools and ritual objects (7:
emus 'sword', ikor 'treasures'), domestic
animals (including seta 'dog' and
pewrep 'bear cub'), and human attributes (7:
isoytak 'story', itak 'language',
rametok 'bravery'). Wild animals and uncultivated plants are absent
— nothing in the world is their owner
bugaeva2021poss 55–56 ; tamura2000 88 . A further affective
use of kor with people nouns adds a nuance of endearment:
a-kor pon menoko 'my dear young girl'
bugaeva2021poss 56 .

The affiliative construction

In the inalienable construction the possessed noun takes the
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed) form , derived from the
concept form by a suffix that copies or echoes a vowel of the stem, and simultaneously
carries a person-marking prefix drawn from the transitive-subject (A) set:
ku= (1SG), e= (2SG),
a= or an= (4th/indefinite), zero (3rd)
nakagawa2024 215–216 ; sato2008 151, 154 . Relational and
spatial nouns deviate from this pattern by taking the objective (O) prefix set
instead; they are treated in relational-and-spatial-nouns . The A-prefix
paradigm as a whole is set out in person-marking-architecture . The possessor
is thus encoded as a grammatical subject: Satō notes that
e=paro 'your mouth' is literally
'you- subject mouth-in-relation', which reads oddly when translated word-for-word
but reflects exactly the head-marking logic of the language
sato2008 150 .

ku=kema-ha ka arka ku=tek-ehe ka arka | 1SG.POSS=leg-POSS even hurt 1SG.POSS=hand-POSS even hurt | My leg hurts too, and my hand hurts too. | kema 'leg' is vowel-final (type I) and takes the affiliative suffix -ha; tek 'hand' is type III-2 and takes -ehe. Both carry the 1SG possessive prefix ku=. The possessed body-part NP is the single argument of the intransitive verb arka 'hurt'. | utari1994 | HK

The semantic classes of nouns that take the affiliative construction span five groups
nakagawa2024 219–221 :

Body parts and normally worn or carried items.
sik eye → sik-ihi ;
par mouth → par-o (irregular);
tek hand → tek-ehe ;
kema leg, foot → kema-ha . Clothing normally
worn is treated analogously ( a=tesma-ha 'my snowshoes') and
carried tools in some contexts ( a=tasiro-ho 'my mountain-knife')
nakagawa2024 219 .

Part–whole relations.
at sinta atu-hu 'cradle cord';
rur sayo ruri-hi 'gruel broth'
nakagawa2024 219–220 .

Kinship. A subset of kin terms takes the affiliative construction and
obligatory person-marking; the full paradigm, the address/reference contrast, and
dialect variation in kin-term assignment are set out in
kinship-honorific-possession
nakagawa2024 221–225 ; sato2008 151 .

Material-to-product.
ohaw ihawe-he 'the taste/essence of the soup'
nakagawa2024 220 .

Miscellaneous relational.
atay-e-he 'price';
hura-ha 'smell';
tokuy-e-he 'best friend';
asuru-hu 'rumour'
nakagawa2024 220 .

The sensory nouns haw voice , sir appearance, situation ,
hum sound, feeling , and ru track, path participate in the
affiliative construction as ordinary possessed nouns before their possessed forms
grammaticalised as evidential formal nouns (treated in
evidential-schema-overview
nakagawa2024 258–259 ). The possessive use of
hawe 'voice' is attested in ILCAA oral-literature texts
ilcaa1976 . Community analysis of the form
e=hawe aste 'raise your voice' similarly parses
e=hawe as the possessed noun '2SG.POSS=voice' under the verb
aste , confirming that hawe remains a
productive possessed form alongside its evidential use
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-12-03; aynucorporadiscord )
‹corpus-confirmed›.

The collective noun utar companions, kin, community members provides a
further illustrative case. A community-proposed analysis holds that
utari(hi) is the affiliative form of
utar , narrowing the sense toward '(someone's) companions / kin' in
the same way the affiliative form generally specifies a relational reading against the more
general concept form (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-09-17;
aynucorporadiscord ). This is consistent with Nakagawa's treatment of
k=utari as a possessed kin-group NP
nakagawa2024 251–252 ‹contested›.

A related set of affiliative-type prefixes — e-
'its tip/head of' and o- 'its end/base of' — prefix to verbs and
behave analogously to affiliative nouns in requiring a possessive antecedent
kobayashi2025 171 . These are homophonous with the applicative prefixes
e-/o- that increase valency by one; Kobayashi distinguishes them by
valency neutrality and by restriction to unaccusative intransitive hosts
kobayashi2025 171 ‹contested›. The applicative prefixes and the overlap
with relational nouns are taken up in relational-and-spatial-nouns .

Class membership: gradient and construal-sensitive

The core of the noun-class split is unambiguous. Body parts, genealogical kin terms, and
inalienable relational concepts are obligatorily possessed and take the affiliative
construction; artefacts, domestic animals, and transferable resources take
kor and cannot take a person-marking prefix directly
bugaeva2021poss 52, 55 . Satō's diagnostic is exact: 'money'
( kane ) takes only ku=kor kane ; the form
*ku=kane is impossible. 'Mouth' ( par ) takes
only e=paro ; *e=kor par is impossible
sato2008 150–151 . The two-construction distinction is documented in all
major descriptive and pedagogical accounts
nakagawa2024 217–221 ; sato2008 149–156 ;
ijas2023 .

Obligatorily-possessed nouns are bound : they almost never appear without possessive
morphology. When cited in isolation, speakers give the 3rd-person (zero-prefix) affiliative
form — sik-ihi 'eye(s), his/her eye', never bare
sik . In riddles, where no specific possessor is identified, the
4th-person (indefinite) A-prefix takes over:
a=sik-ihi 'people's eyes'
bugaeva2021poss 53 . These bound-noun properties, and the difference between
nominal subclasses with and without obligatory possession, are developed in
nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns .

When an obligatorily-possessed noun is incorporated with a non-coreferential possessor, the
antipassive-detransitive-i antipassive prefix i-
encodes the unspecified possessor and allows the bare noun root to incorporate:
i-sapa-kar 'cut someone's head',
i-rekut-tuye 'cut someone's throat'
bugaeva2021poss 53–54 . Hokkaido Ainu thus has two distinct
de-specifying strategies: the indefinite A-prefix a= , which retains
possessive morphology and keeps the noun as a free NP, and the antipassive
i- , which suppresses the possessor and licences incorporation.
These are treated in
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding
bugaeva2021poss 54, 81 .

Several nouns fall outside the clean bipartition. The noun
kotan village takes both constructions approximately equally:
a=kor kotan ~ a=kotanu . The noun
mosir world, land, island takes only the kor
construction in prose ( *a=mosirihi is unattested, though
mosiri appears in verse). The noun
cise house is dialect-split: Saru, Chitose, and Horobetsu prose uses only
kor for 'so-and-so's house', but verse and other dialects allow the
affiliative cisehe ; the compound
uncise 'home, dwelling-place' takes the affiliative exclusively
across dialects nakagawa2024 220–221 .

The clearest evidence for construal-sensitivity in class assignment comes from Nakagawa's
discussion of the trap-bow ( ku ). In the Ainu worldview, the prey
animal chooses whose trap-bow to fall to, so the bow always pre-belongs to a specific
person: it is construed as inalienable and takes the affiliative form
nakagawa2024 229 :

tan pe a=po-ho ku-wehe ne | this thing 4.A=son-POSS bow-POSS COP | This is my son's (bow). | Inalienable (affiliative) construction for the trap-bow. ku-wehe is the affiliative of ku 'bow'; poho is the affiliative of po 'son'. The possessor a=poho 'my son' is itself in the affiliative form. | nakagawa2024 | HK

The same bow, framed as an ordinary transferable object, takes the
kor construction nakagawa2024 229 :

tan ku a=po-ho kor ku ne | this bow 4.A=son-POSS have bow COP | This is a bow that my son has. | Alienable (kor) construction for the same referent. The concept form ku appears twice: as the topic NP and as the gapped object of kor. Nakagawa uses the pair to show that the construction choice is construal-sensitive, not fixed by the referent. | nakagawa2024 | HK

The inalienable frame foregrounds that the bow has a pre-assigned owner in a cosmological
sense; the alienable frame treats it as a tool in ordinary human hands. This pair is the
strongest available evidence that the noun-class split, while grammatical in its effects,
is not wholly referent-determined ‹contested›. A parallel diachronic observation runs in the
same direction: Bugaeva et al. note that loanwords such as
kosonde 'kimono' enter as non-possessible and take
kor , suggesting that unfamiliarity is the default entry point for
new nouns and that possessibility can be acquired over time
bugaeva2021poss 57, 76–77 ‹speculative›.

Analytical perspectives

The affiliative trigger has been debated in terms of both what the formal condition is and
what typological category it instantiates.

The oldest question in the descriptive literature is whether the affiliative form is licensed
by the specificity of the possessor or by a strictly grammatical
condition. Tamura held that N₂ takes the affiliative only when N₁ is specific: the contrast
between kamuy rus-ihi 'the specific bear's pelt' and
kamuy rus 'bear-pelt in general' was the key datum, the latter
approaching the status of a compound
nakagawa2024 231 . Satō objected that the affiliative appears with
non-specific possessors:
aynu opitta hon-ihi kor wa okay pe ne
'all humans have a belly' — where aynu opitta 'all people' is
maximally non-specific — carries the affiliative form hon-ihi
sato2008 155 ; nakagawa2024 231 ‹contested›.
Nakagawa's analysis shifts the frame entirely: the affiliative form appears wherever the
noun carries a person-marking A-prefix or occupies the modifier slot of a
relative-clause structure, regardless of possessor specificity
nakagawa2024 230–233 . Three constructions support this: the
indefinite-person affiliative ( a=teke munin pe ne na
'one's hand will rot', a general warning); affiliatives inside relative clauses (the
modifier 'with a long tail' requires sara-ha tanne , not
*sar tanne , because the modifier slot is a relative-clause
position); and affiliatives triggered by quantifiers
usa 'various' and nep 'whatever'
nakagawa2024 231–233 ‹contested›.

Satō's personal-form (人称形) analysis, developed in
concept-form-affiliative-form , addresses a different aspect. Once a
person-marking prefix combines with the affiliative form to create a personal form, the
result is a highly autonomous NP: Ø=sik-ihi alone — without any
overt possessor NP — is a complete noun phrase 'his/her eye'. At the same time, the
possessor NP and its personal form need not be adjacent: the noun phrase
menoko … anakne … kaskamuye 'the woman's guardian spirit' has the
topic particle anakne intervening. The personal form signals the
presence of a possessor somewhere in context rather than forming a tight N-N genitive
sato2008 155–156 . Bugaeva et al. converge on the same point: the
possessed suffix "marks a noun as requiring a possessor (which is then indexed with a
possessive prefix)" and "the possessor N₁ can easily be omitted without ambiguity as
the possessed suffix signals its presence"
bugaeva2021poss 52 .

Bugaeva et al.'s typological framing, drawing on nichols1986 , places
the Hokkaido system within the Circum-Pacific appositive possessive type but distinguishes
it by the fact that the modifier is a finite verbal clause ( kor )
rather than a nominal appositive
bugaeva2021poss 58–60, 80–82 . The two constructions together give the
language a mixed locus-of-marking profile: inalienable possession is head-marked on the
possessed noun (A-prefix + affiliative suffix), while alienable possession is encoded as a
verbal modifier. The adnominal double-marking and appositive constructions that arise at
the interface of these two strategies are examined in
adnominal-possession-double-marking ; the external-possession and
possessor-raising phenomena are treated in
external-possession-possessor-raising .

Huber's subjectivity analysis applies Ikegami's HAVE/BE-language typology and finds that
Ainu patterns with English (a HAVE-type language where the possessor is foregrounded as
subject) rather than Japanese (a BE-type language where the possessed entity is
topicalised) huber2025 254–257 . Even in the inalienable construction the
possessor is overtly indexed by the A-prefix —
ku=tek-ehe ku=kikkik 'I clapped my hands' — unlike the dropped
possessor of Japanese 「手を叩いた」. Huber proposes treating inalienability as a
subjectivity index and the possession-schema inventory as a gradient scale rather than a
binary; this proposal is explicitly framed as preliminary in her paper
huber2025 257 ‹speculative›.

title: Tripartite, Nominative-Accusative, and the Central Alignment Debate
summary: How the 1sg and indefinite cells show distinct A/S/O exponents (tripartite) against the accusative readings of other cells, and the system-wide synthesis — hierarchical/inverse vs active-stative vs tripartite — including ditransitive and clause-type alignment.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Tripartite, Nominative-Accusative, and the Central Alignment Debate
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: How the 1sg and indefinite cells show distinct A/S/O exponents (tripartite) against the accusative readings of other cells, and the system-wide synthesis — hierarchical/inverse vs active-stative vs tripartite — including ditransitive and clause-type alignment.

The alignment question

Hokkaido Ainu encodes the S/A/O distinction through person indexes that appear on the
finite verb — head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 . The
architecture of the four formal persons and the combination rules are set out in
person-marking-architecture ; the present chapter evaluates the typological
labels that have been applied to the paradigm and assesses how far hierarchical and
inverse analyses bear on the Hokkaido data. The split-intransitive question — whether
the S position subdivides by agentivity — belongs to
alignment-split-intransitive . Free-NP realization and the relationship
between indexing and nominal argument structure are taken up in
argument-realization-indexing .

Row-wise alignment

Reading the person-indexing paradigm cell by cell, the morphological alignment is
mixed. The standard Saru–Chitose forms, cited directly in Bugaeva, Nakagawa, Tamura,
and Satō, yield four distinct row patterns
bugaeva2012 471–472 ; nakagawa2024 165–169 ;
tamura1972 17–39 ; sato2008 31–32 .

person cell
A: transitive subject
S: intransitive subject
O: object
row alignment

1SG
ku=
ku=
en=
S=A≠O; nominative–accusative

1PL.EXCL
ci=
=as
un=
S≠A≠O; tripartite

2SG
e=
e=
e=
S=A=O; neutral

2PL
eci=
eci=
eci=
S=A=O; neutral

3
Ø
Ø
Ø
S=A=O; zero or absent

4 / INDEF
a= (dialectally an= )
=an
i=
S≠A≠O; tripartite

Bugaeva's characterisation follows from the table: 1SG is nominative–accusative
(S=A, distinct from O); second and third person are neutral (all three roles carry
the same form); the first plural exclusive and the indefinite/fourth-person rows
are tripartite, because each uses a distinct form for S, A, and O. She summarises
the language as "mixed (but basically tripartite)"
bugaeva2012 472, 498 ; bugaeva2008 32 .

The tripartite character of those two rows rests on a positional split: the 1PL.EXCL
A form ci= and the fourth-person A form
a= are prefixes on the transitive stem, while the S
counterparts =as and =an are suffixes
on the intransitive stem; the O forms un= and
i= are prefixal but formally distinct from both
nakagawa2024 166–167 ; bugaeva2012 472 . The Tokachi
example places the 1PL.EXCL A and S exponents within a single clause chain ‹consensus›:

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | The exclusive group is marked as A by the prefix ci= on the transitive o 'board' and as S by the suffix =as on the intransitive plural arki 'come'; the object counterpart un= is not triggered in this chain. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

The fourth-person O prefix i= indexes the undergoer in oral
narrative — including the narrating protagonist — and together with a=
for A and =an for S makes the tripartite character of that row
verifiable in a single utterance nakagawa2024 169 :

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=lodge CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The fourth-person O prefix i= indexes the narrative protagonist three times; with a= for A and =an for S, all three roles are kept formally distinct within the same paradigm row. | nakagawa2024:170 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

Bugaeva's three-way gradation of the indexes by prosodic and separability tests —
word-like =an and =as , clitic-like
a= and eci= , full prefixes such as
ku= and en= — and Nakagawa's independent
identification of =an as separable from the verb by particles
reflect a cline of morphological tightness that is orthogonal to the alignment classification
bugaeva2012 472–473 ; nakagawa2024 49, 183 ;
sakaguchi2024 ix, 62–64, 77–85 ‹contested›. The ordering diagnostics
belong to personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Catalogue of analyses

The literature assigns different labels because it addresses different questions:
synchronic Hokkaido exponence, historical reconstruction, Sakhalin comparison, and
the referential structure of the fourth-person set. The table below collects the
principal positions; attributions via Dal Corso are second-hand for this grammar.

analysis
principal locus
domain
status for Hokkaido

mixed nominative–neutral–tripartite
Bugaeva (read directly); Okuda 2015 (reported via Dal Corso)
Hokkaido person-indexing morphology
best-supported synchronic label for the paradigm ‹consensus›

mixed nominative–neutral–inverse
Dal Corso 2025
‹SA› Sakhalin person indexing
contrastive; Dal Corso warns the diachrony and hierarchical analysis may not apply to Hokkaido ‹contested›

ergative stage → neutral → nominative
Okuda 2015, as reported by Dal Corso
reconstructed Proto-Ainu
diachronic hypothesis; not a synchronic label for the attested paradigm ‹speculative›

hierarchical / participant-referentiality-mismatch
Dal Corso 2023/2025
chiefly ‹SA›, the fourth-person dimension
typologically suggestive for interaction cells; not demonstrated as replacing row-wise tripartite for Hokkaido ‹contested›

inverse as ancient pan-Ainu feature (remnants in eci=)
Satō 2014, reported by Dal Corso
Proto-Ainu diachrony
reported diachronic proposal ‹speculative›

second-person neutrality as original (empathy / deferred control)
Satō 2024, reported by Dal Corso
history of the 2SG/2PL rows
diachronic account of one row; synchronic neutrality is uncontested ‹contested›

zero third-person affix required
Satō 2023/2025; Bugaeva's Ø notation is compatible
Hokkaido valency and the two-affix cap
live disagreement against Nakagawa's no-affix analysis ‹contested›

Dal Corso's mixed nominative–neutral–inverse account is formulated for ‹SA› Sakhalin.
He dismisses the tripartite component for Sakhalin by arguing that the S and A forms
