(2020) on the promotion logic — is a proposal original to this grammar.

The chapter also treats applicative stacking — the co-occurrence of two applicative prefixes
on one verb — together with the two feeding relations, since stacking creates the complex
valency frames in which both paths may apply simultaneously. The individual prefixes
e- about / by means of / at , ko- toward / together with , and
o- into / toward a place are treated in
applicative-e , applicative-ko , and the system as a whole in
applicative-system-overview .

Applicative stacking

Up to two applicative prefixes appear on one verb simultaneously, each raising arity by one
bugaeva2014 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . The most productive
double-applicative patterns pair e- (Content, Instrument, Location,
Purpose) with ko- (Addressee, Goal, Comitative), in either order
— e-ko- or ko-e- . Bugaeva and Kobayashi
report no visible semantic difference between the two orders; in both,
e- contributes a Content or Instrument object and
ko- an Addressee or Goal
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 ‹contested›. Nakagawa places applicative
prefixes in the single slot immediately inside the person index and before the root, so
whether order encodes any semantic scope remains open
nakagawa2024 211 ‹speculative›.

With isoytak speak, tell , the double applicative yields a three-place frame
— an Addressee ( ko- ) and a Content argument
( e- ) alongside the base Agent:

e-ko-isoytak | APPL-APPL-speak | tell stories to someone about something | e- contributes the Content participant; ko- contributes the Addressee. Both applied objects are unmarked bare NPs cross-referenced by a single O-index. Nakagawa 2024: 211 gives e-yay-e-isoytak, with reflexive yay- filling the ko-slot. | bugaeva2006:188 | HK

The showcase documented by Shibatani — attributed to Chiri (1936: 169) — combines two
applicatives with two reflexives, an adverbial element, and an incorporated object on a
single root:

usa-oruspe a=e-yay-ko-tuyma-si-ram-suy-pa | various-rumor 4.A=APPL-REFL-APPL-far-REFL-heart-sway-PL | We keep wondering about various rumours. | we sway our hearts afar and toward ourselves over various rumours | Root suy-pa 'sway (PL)' carries e- and ko- (both applicative), yay- and si- (both reflexive), the adverbial tuyma 'far', and the incorporated object ram 'heart'. The valency and template bookkeeping in Xr:valency-voice-overview holds at every step. | shibatani1990:73; bugaeva2014 | HK | after Chiri 1936: 169; Shibatani ex. 131 / Bugaeva ex. 1

Bugaeva and Kobayashi's second showcase demonstrates re-intransitivization through stacking:
a frame extended by two applicatives is collapsed back to bivalent by incorporating one
applied object and reflexivizing the other
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 :

yay-ipe-e-ko-sunke | REFL-food-APPL-APPL-lie | eat meagre meals | ipe 'food' is incorporated as the e-object (Content); yay- fills the ko-object slot (Addressee = reflexive self). The resulting verb is transitive with a single indexed object. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.1.1 | HK

A text-attested case of comitative incorporation via ko- is visible
in the following Chitose passage, where the comitative objects are incorporated in sequence
with their respective verbs:

a=mun-ko-tata a=toy-ko-tata ayne | 4.A=grass-APPL-chop 4.A=soil-APPL-chop then | chopping along with the grass, chopping along with the soil, and then … | mun 'grass/weeds' and toy 'soil' are comitative objects first promoted by ko-, then incorporated. The implied patient (the demon's flesh) is cross-referenced by a=. Each verb-word is the output of the applicative-then-incorporation feeding sequence. | nakagawa2024:197 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 190

The feeding of oblique incorporation

The wordhood diagnostic in noun-incorporation-object establishes that only core
objects — nominals that stand in the argument position targeted by the outermost person
index — can incorporate into the verb word. A nominal held in a postpositional phrase
( ani with (instrumental) , ta at, in (locative) ,
kusu for, in order to (purpose) ) has no such position and cannot incorporate.
The applicative removes the postposition and assigns the nominal core-object status;
incorporation then proceeds. Nakagawa gives three parallel derivations
nakagawa2024 484 :

oblique phrase
applicative stage
incorporated form
role of the noun

at ani uk
at e-uk
at-e-uk
Instrument ('net', catching)

pira or un hokuste
pira ko-hokuste
pira-ko-hokus-te
Goal/Locative ('cliff', falling toward)

turepta kusu arpa
turepta e-arpa
turepta-e-arpa
Purpose ('lily-bulb digging')

The same three-step derivation is spelled out in Shibatani's analysis of a
comitative-instrument example shibatani1990 67 :

nea cep pone tura a=kuykuy | that fish bone with 4.A=bite | I bit the fish together with its bones. | Stage 1: pone 'bone' is an oblique held by the comitative postposition tura 'with'. It cannot incorporate. | shibatani1990:67 | HK

nea cep pone a=ko-kuykuy | that fish bone 4.A=APPL-bite | I bit the fish together with its bones. | Stage 2: ko- promotes pone to core object, dropping tura; pone is now an unmarked argument of the three-place verb. | shibatani1990:67 | HK

nea cep a=pone-ko-kuykuy | that fish 4.A=bone-APPL-bite | I bone-bit the fish. | Stage 3: pone incorporates into the verb word, landing outside ko- but inside the person index a=. The Mirror Principle is satisfied: ko- (morphologically inner, syntactically prior) sits between pone (morphologically outer, syntactically later) and the root kuykuy. | shibatani1990:67 | HK

The affix order in Stage 3 instantiates Baker's Mirror Principle
baker1988 : the morphological order mirrors the syntactic derivation because
applicative formation precedes incorporation. Shibatani extends this logic to a wordhood
diagnostic: the presence of an applicative prefix on a noun-verb sequence that is otherwise
ambiguous between NI and a phrasal reading resolves it unambiguously as incorporation
shibatani1990 63 . The form tumi osma 'war began'
is ambiguous, but o-tumi-osma with the locative applicative
o- is unambiguously an incorporated word.

Several forms in Chiri (1936: 93) place the incorporated noun before rather than after the
applicative — o-ni-pici alternating with
ni o-pici 'fall from a tree' — in apparent violation of mirror
ordering. Shibatani calls this a "minor irregularity" without resolving it
shibatani1990 71 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Baker's Case-theoretic prediction that applicative formation (P-incorporation) should be
impossible on unaccusative bases is falsified by Ainu
baker1988 ; shibatani1988 210–211 ;
shibatani1990 65–66 . Verbs such as mokor 'sleep'
freely combine with e- ( mokor a=e-wen
'I missed sleeping'; sake a=e-niste 'I am strong at wine'), contrary
to Case-theory's expectation ‹contested›. The implication for the feeding chain is that an
oblique associated with an intransitive base can be promoted and subsequently incorporated
or relativized by the same mechanism as one associated with a transitive base.

The feeding of relativization

Hokkaido Ainu relative clauses are prenominal and gap-based: the modifying clause precedes
the head noun and leaves a null gap in the position the head noun would otherwise occupy
within the clause. Subject and direct-object gaps are both accessible to this strategy
‹corpus-confirmed›; see prenominal-gap-relative-clauses for the full
construction. A nominal in an oblique role — held by a postposition — lies outside the reach
of gap-formation: Ainu has no mechanism for stranding a postposition in situ while extracting
its complement as a relative-clause head
keenancomrie1977 ; dalcorso2020 .

Applicative promotion removes the postposition and places the nominal at the direct-object
tier, where gap-formation is licensed. The applied object then stands in a
relativization-accessible position, and a prenominal relative clause can be formed with
that applied object as its head kaiser1998 ; dalcorso2020 . The
following constructed example illustrates the pattern, based on the instrument applicative
e-tuye attested in Nakagawa 2024: 194 (ex. 173):

sirkorkamuy a=e-tuye emus | standing.tree 4.A=APPL-cut sword | the sword with which [someone] cut the standing tree | The head noun emus 'sword' is interpreted as the e-object (instrument) of the relative clause. A null gap occupies the applied-object position within the clause; emus appears outside as the head. The construction is possible because e- has promoted emus from the Instrument oblique tier to the Direct Object tier.

The same promotion operates with ko- . A Goal or Addressee that would
appear with un 'to' or tura 'with' can head a
relative clause built on a ko- -derived predicate. Dal Corso documents
the construction for Southern Hokkaido texts and finds that applied objects — regardless of
which prefix produced them — pattern identically to base transitive objects in gap-formation
dalcorso2020 ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The Keenan–Comrie hierarchy frames the typological significance: Subject > Direct Object >
Indirect Object > Oblique > Genitive > Comparee keenancomrie1977 . Hokkaido
Ainu relativization is robustly accessible at Subject and Direct Object; bare Oblique is
inaccessible; the applicative closes the gap by importing the oblique into the Direct Object
tier. Kaiser (1998) demonstrates that the resulting applied object is treated as a
morphosyntactic direct object in the relative clause — there is no distinct 'applied-object
relativization' mechanism separate from ordinary object relativization
kaiser1998 . The synthesis that a single structural promotion underlies both the
NI-feeding and the relativization-feeding paths is a proposal original to this
grammar ‹speculative›.

Competing analyses of the applied-object position

What grammatical relation the promoted nominal bears — and therefore where it sits on the
accessibility hierarchy — is contested between two broad analytical traditions
keenancomrie1977 .

A relational account assigns the promoted nominal the relation of direct object from the
moment of applicative formation. The applied object inherits full direct-object properties,
including gap-licensability and incorporability, and stands at the Object tier of the
hierarchy. The primary supporting evidence is morphosyntactic: Ainu marks no case distinction
between the base Patient-object and the applied object of a ditransitive — both appear as
bare NPs, both are cross-referenced by the single O-index on the verb
nakagawa2024 192 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . Without a
formal difference between the two objects, assigning them to distinct tiers of the hierarchy
lacks morphosyntactic grounding.

Baker's (1988) head-movement account treats the applicative as P-incorporation: the
postposition moves into the verb, leaving its complement in a derived specifier position.
The applied object's accessibility to gap-formation and incorporation would then be a
secondary consequence of that specifier position — not a primitive direct-object property
baker1988 . Shibatani identifies the applicativization of intransitive bases
in Ainu as a direct refutation: Baker's Case-theory predicts this should be impossible, yet
it is productive in Hokkaido Ainu shibatani1988 210–211 ;
shibatani1990 65–66 ‹contested›.

A community observation reported in the aynu-corpora Discord is consistent with the
relational account. The verb sir-ko-peker 'spend the night' — where
ko- interrupts the derivationally closed stem
sir-peker — has been analysed (attributed to Satō, reported by
nukopoli, 2024-12-05 aynucorporadiscord ) as a construction in which the
ko- -object is promoted to the grammatical subject of the whole
predicate: ku=sir-ko-peker 'I spend the night' has the experiencer
— originally the applied object — in the subject index position. Parallel forms
maw-ko-wen and kur-ko-tunas are noted in
the same discussion. If the ko- -object can reach the subject
position in closed-stem environments, promotion to direct object in open-stem environments
is the weaker operation — collateral support for the relational account ‹contested›.

For double-applicative verbs, both applied objects are morphosyntactically equivalent (bare
NPs, single O-index), and both are in principle accessible to gap-formation or incorporation
individually. Whether both can independently head distinct relative clauses within a complex
sentence has not been investigated in the sources consulted ‹speculative›.

The two-slot ceiling, re-applicativization, and chained feeding

The verb word accommodates at most two person indexes — one A-type and one O-type — so the
ceiling on simultaneously indexed participants is two, regardless of how many applicatives
stack sato2023b 39 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §2 ;
nakagawa2024 210–212 . Extra applied objects must be reabsorbed by
reflexivization ( yay- self (direct) / si- self (indirect) ),
incorporation, or antipassivization before the verb is finalized.
The yay-ipe-e-ko-sunke example above instantiates two reabsorption
strategies on one verb. Bugaeva's (2014 abstract) characterization of Ainu as polysynthetic
in Baker's sense — with the caveat that it departs from the Mohawk prototype in word order,
in-situ wh , and unrestricted morphological causatives — places this
stacking capacity in typological perspective bugaeva2014 .

The feeding chain can extend across multiple derivational steps. Antipassive
i- reduces a transitive to intransitive by absorbing the object slot
with a generic argument; re-adding an applicative restores transitive valency with a new
object, which is then accessible to further incorporation or relativization by the same logic
as any other applied object
bugaeva2021antip ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 :

tokaci wa ek pewre kur monrayke e-i-kasuy | Tokachi from come young man work APPL-ANTIP-help | A young man from Tokachi helped with the work. | i-kasuy 'do helping' (antipassive, intransitive) is re-applicativized by e- (purpose 'with work'); monrayke 'work' becomes the applied e-object, restoring transitivity. This e-object is accessible to incorporation or relativization via the standard feeding logic. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.3 | SAR | Sunazawa 1983: 160; Handbook ex. 25c

The chained sequence — applicative, then antipassive, then re-applicativization, then
relativization or incorporation — is bounded only by the template order (person index |
nominal/applicative | root | forming suffix | causative | person index;
nakagawa2024 211 ) and the two-slot ceiling. Each step in the chain
either adds a slot (applicative, causative) or removes one (antipassive, reflexive,
incorporation), and the feeding paths are open whenever the net result is a nominal
standing at the direct-object tier ‹contested›.

Ijäs's Basic Lessons 3 and 9 do not cover applicative stacking or the feeding relations; the
lessons treat i- and -re as independent
operations without addressing their combination with applicatives or with relativization
ijas2023 . The learner grammar's entry point — applicatives as independent
valency-raisers — is the correct starting place, with the stacking behavior and its
downstream interactions belonging to advanced grammar.

title: The Applicative System: Overview and the Preverb-vs-Applicative Debate
summary: Bugaeva's reanalysis of e-/ko-/o- as applicatives promoting obliques to object, with the diagnostics and the terminological controversy.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Applicative System: Overview and the Preverb-vs-Applicative Debate
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: Bugaeva's reanalysis of e-/ko-/o- as applicatives promoting obliques to object, with the diagnostics and the terminological controversy.

Definition and valency effect

The three prefixes e- applicative: about, by means of, at ,
ko- applicative: toward, together with , and
o- applicative: into, toward a place are valency-increasing derivational
morphemes that promote a semantically peripheral participant to the status of a direct
object, in the sense established for applicative constructions cross-linguistically
peterson2007 ; bugaeva2006 186 ;
nakagawa2024 192 . In Ainu, object status means appearing as a bare noun
phrase in the argument frame of a transitive or ditransitive verb; there is no case
marking by which objecthood can be read off the noun itself, so the test is transitivity
and the agreement prefix the verb licenses
bugaevakobayashi2022 §2 ; sato2008 96 . Each of the
three applicatives raises arity by one: an intransitive base (vi, one argument) becomes
a transitive (vt, two arguments), and a transitive base becomes a ditransitive (vd,
three arguments) nakagawa2024 194 ; bugaeva2006 188 .
The person-marking diagnostics of
valency-voice-overview the valency system expose this arity shift: a
derived intransitive takes the suffixal S indexes =as and
=an , while a derived transitive or ditransitive takes the
prefixal A indexes ci= and a=
sato2008 96 ; nakagawa2024 137, 140 .

sirkorkamuy emus e-tuye | standing.tree sword APPL-cut | He cut a standing tree with a sword. | The instrument emus 'sword' is the applied object promoted by e-; tuye 'cut' moves from a two-place transitive to a three-place ditransitive. | nakagawa2024:193 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 173, after Monbetsu-chō 1969: 237

The applied object's semantic range is broader than the canonical benefactive or
instrumental applicative found in many languages. Bugaeva's corpus study of Chitose and
Saru applicatives maps the three prefixes onto instruments, locations, goals,
comitatives, benefactives, malefactives, recipients, sources, themes, content, causes,
paths, and purposes bugaeva2006 187–196 . Nakagawa's heading
充当接頭辞 gives the core paraphrases as 〜について・〜で以て・〜〈場所〉で
(about / by means of / at a place) for e- , and
〜に向かって・〜とともに (toward / together with) for ko- , with
o- restricted to 〜〈場所〉へ (toward a place)
nakagawa2024 194–199 ; sato2008 239–245 .

The sources do not slice this space identically. Satō's summary groups 手段・着点・場所
(means, goal, location) under e- and o-
together and gives ko- as 方向・手段 (direction, means) — extending
the instrumental sense to ko- as well
sato2008 239–245 . Bugaeva's corpus tables place Means-Instrument with
e- (13% of its uses) and record no instrument class for
ko- bugaeva2006 190 ; the divergence is one of
classification granularity rather than of data.

The three prefixes at a glance

The table below gives the core semantic range, arity effect, and most frequent
applied-object roles in Bugaeva's corpus sample for each prefix; full role distributions
and corpus examples are in applicative-e , applicative-ko , and
applicative-o .

prefix
core semantic range
arity
most frequent applied-object roles (Bugaeva 2006: 190–191)
frequency in lexicon

e-
about; by means of; at (location); for (purpose)
+1
Content 39%, Theme-Patient 17%, Instrument 13%
second most frequent

ko-
toward (person/non-place goal); together with
+1
Addressee 20%, Goal 19%, Comitative 18%, Benefactive 18%
most frequent

o-
into / toward (place goal only)
+1
Goal-into-place 57%, Location 29%
least frequent; largely verse

The lexical frequency order is
ko- > e- > o-
bugaeva2006 188 . o- is rare in prose and
confined largely to verse; Satō similarly marks it uncommon in his pedagogical treatment
nakagawa2024 199 ; sato2008 244 .

kanto kotor a=ko-hopuni | sky surface 4.A=APPL-fly.up | I flew up toward the sky. | ko- promotes the goal kanto kotor 'sky surface' to object position; hopuni 'fly up (vi)' becomes ko-hopuni 'fly up toward (vt)'. | nakagawa2024:193 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 174

Semantic roles of the applied object

Bugaeva's (2006) role classification of the Chitose/Saru corpus sample yields two
findings that bear on typological interpretation
bugaeva2006 188–196 . First, the most frequent role added by
e- is Content (39%), followed by Theme-Patient (17%); only at
third place do instrument and locative roles appear. Second, 75–96% of
e- applied objects across roles derive from intransitive bases,
meaning the applicative is introducing a new core object rather than re-encoding a
participant that already had an object-like coding
bugaeva2006 190–191 .

These figures reflect a typological property: most Ainu applicatives are
obligatory rather than promotional. In the canonical promotional type, an
oblique participant has a postpositional paraphrase that the applicative replaces
optionally; in Ainu, the majority of applied objects — especially the Content class of
e- — have no independent postpositional frame, so the
applicative construction is the only coding available. Bugaeva calls the Content-adding
use of e- the most productive class and 'very typical in its
atypicality' precisely because it sits outside the promotional prototype
bugaeva2006 186–187, 191–196 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . The cross-linguistic placement of Ainu in
applicative typology, drawing on Bugaeva's 2010 comparative study, is taken up in the
per-prefix chapters.

For ko- , Addressee (20%), Goal (19%), Comitative (18%), and
Benefactive (18%) together account for three-quarters of applied-object tokens; Source
and Recipient each contribute 5%, with the remainder across Cause, Range, and Location
bugaeva2006 190 . The o- distribution is
narrowest: locative roles (Goal-into-place 57%, Location 29%, Source/Theme 14%) cover
all attested instances, all paraphrasable by ta /
un 'at/to' bugaeva2006 191 . The role
percentages are from a single corpus sample and serve as approximations; no
pan-Hokkaido frequency study has been conducted ‹corpus-suggested›.

The prefix e-: four subtypes

Nakagawa distinguishes four subtypes of e- by the relationship
between the applied object and its postpositional paraphrase, where one exists
nakagawa2024 195 . The classification is reproduced here because it
identifies which uses are promotional (subtypes 1–2) and which are obligatory
(subtypes 3–4); full analysis and corpus examples are in applicative-e .

subtype
applied-object role
postpositional paraphrase
example

(1)
Instrument
replaceable by ani 'with, by means of'

emus e-tuye
(≈ emus ani tuye )

(2)
Purpose
replaceable by kusu 'for, in order to'

turepta e-arpa
(≈ turepta kusu arpa )

(3)
Content / Theme (non-replaceable)
no independent postpositional frame

nep e-simpay 'worry about something'

(4)
Location (place required)

replaceable by ta / un /
peka

to teksama e-an
(≈ to teksama ta an )

Subtype (3) is where e- functions as a non-causative
transitivizer adding a core Theme or Content object with no other syntactic route.
The derivation ikka steal (vi) → e-ikka 'steal
something (vt)' → ko-e-ikka 'steal something from someone (vd)'
illustrates the Content-adding step followed by a second applicative layer
bugaeva2006 191 .

A community-proposed etymology for
eykaun surpass, excel illustrates the Content / Theme-adding function in
a compound form: the verb may derive from e- applicativizing the
locative predicate ka un 'be above' →
eykaun 'surpass something', with a parallel
-u transitivizer yielding the doublet
kaunu (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024)
aynucorporadiscord . The proposal is consistent with the range of
e- 's Content / Theme uses, but the lexical history has not been
established in the peer-reviewed literature ‹speculative›.

The prefix ko- and the contrast with o-

ko- covers the roles associated with an animate or non-spatial
goal: Addressee, Goal, Comitative, Benefactive, Malefactive, Recipient, and Source are
all represented in Bugaeva's sample bugaeva2006 190 ;
nakagawa2024 197–198 . The diagnostic contrast with
o- is the type of goal argument:
ko- preferentially takes person or animal goals, while
o- is restricted to place nouns
nakagawa2024 198–199 ; bugaeva2006 191 . The following
Chitose minimal pair, with the same base verb ek come (singular) ,
demonstrates the distinction:

tumi o-ek | war APPL-come | War came to the village. | o- applied object = a place (village, contextually supplied); o- is paraphrasable by ta/un 'to (a place)'. | nakagawa2024:198 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 192

tumi ko-ek | war APPL-come | War came to [my] sister. | ko- applied object = a person (the sister); ko- is required for animate goals. | nakagawa2024:198 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 193

The comitative reading of ko- ('together with') is transparent
when the applied object is incorporated into the verb word:

a=mun-ko-tata a=toy-ko-tata ayne | 4.A=weed-APPL-chop 4.A=earth-APPL-chop then | I chopped it together with the weeds, together with the soil, and then … | mun 'weeds' and toy 'earth' are incorporated applied objects with comitative reading; the actual patient (the monster's flesh) is contextually implicit. | nakagawa2024:197 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 190

A community-proposed analysis of sirkokunne pass the night illustrates a
less common behavior: the form ku=sir-ko-peker may represent
ko- inserted inside the compound intransitive
sir-peker 'the sky brightens', with the applied object (first
person 'I') raised to subject of the whole derived verb, yielding 'I pass the night'
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024) aynucorporadiscord . This
object-to-subject raising analysis is not directly addressed in the accessible
peer-reviewed sources and is offered as a community-proposed reading of an opaque form
‹speculative›.

Stacking, productivity, and the feeding relation

Two applicatives may combine on a single verb. The most attested pairing is
ko-e- or e-ko- , where each prefix
encodes an independent applied object: e-ko-isoytak 'tell
stories about something ( e- ) to someone
( ko- )'. Bugaeva and Kobayashi report no visible semantic
difference between the two prefix orders in this combination, and Nakagawa gives
e-yay-e-isoytak as a further expansion that inserts the
reflexive yay- between the two applicatives, treating the whole
as one word bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 ;
nakagawa2024 211 . The arity ceiling on double applicatives follows from
the two-person-affix cap: a verb may carry at most one subject index and one object
index, so a third applied object is unindexable without compensatory
intransitivization bugaevakobayashi2022 §2 ;
sato2023b 39 . The Handbook illustrates the compensatory strategy in
yay-ipe-e-ko-sunke 'eat meagre meals', where three
applied-object positions are successively reduced — one object incorporated
( ipe 'food'), one reflexivized ( yay- )
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . The full range of applicative–reflexive
and applicative–causative stacking is in
applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding .

Across the lexicon, the applicatives are pervasive: Bugaeva estimates roughly one-third
of all verbs are applicative-derived, amounting to up to 7% of the entire Ainu lexicon,
and characterizes them as 'productive, ubiquitous, and versatile'
bugaeva2006 188 . The prefixes attach to almost every verb class,
with the exception of meteorological, bodily-process, and certain change-of-state verbs
at one extreme of the transitivity hierarchy and effective-action verbs at the other
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . Ijäs's learner grammar surveys transitive
and derived forms but does not extend to the applicative prefixes in the banked lessons;
the pedagogical description is in Satō's Lesson 31
ijas2023 ; sato2008 239–245 .

A further structural consequence of the applicative system is its role in enabling noun
incorporation. Oblique nouns — instruments and locations — cannot incorporate directly:
an applicative must first promote them to object status, and only the derived object
then incorporates into the verb word
shibatani1990 62, 66 ; nakagawa2024 484 . A verb-noun
sequence bearing an applicative prefix is therefore a reliable sign that incorporation
has occurred: o-tumi-osma (with o- ) is
unambiguously incorporated, while bare tumi osma is ambiguous
shibatani1990 63 . When incorporation takes place, the applicative
carries the postpositional meaning that would otherwise be expressed by a separate
postposition: at-e-uk 'catch with a net'
(= at ani uk ), pira-ko-hokus-te
'fall toward a cliff' (= pira or un hokuste )
nakagawa2024 484 . Shibatani (1990) demonstrates that Baker's
Case-theoretic prediction — that unaccusative bases are blocked from applicative
formation — is falsified in Ainu, which does applicativize unaccusatives
shibatani1990 66 ; baker1988 . Kaiser (1998) examines
the morphological properties of this interaction in detail
kaiser1998 ; the treatment is continued in
applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding and
noun-incorporation-object .

Homophonous prefixes and the arity computation

Three surface forms shared with the applicative prefixes carry different arity values and
require disambiguation by context and argument counting. Applicative
e- (+1) has two homophonous companions: the affiliative
e- 'the head/end of' (arity 0), which fills a part slot on the
object while requiring a separate possessor, leaving net arity unchanged; and the
second-person subject index e= , a person affix rather than a
derivational prefix nakagawa2024 192–193 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.3 . The lexical body-region prefix
he- 'head' (arity −1) provides a further contrast, filling the
object slot with the subject's own head region. The Chitose pair
he-puni 'lift one's own head (vi, −1)' against
e-puni 'lift someone's head (vt, +1)' separates applicative
from lexical prefix by the person-suffix test nakagawa2024 193 .
Applicative o- (+1) has a parallel affiliative twin
o- 'the bottom/base of' (arity 0). None of these can be glossed
without first computing the arity. The full homophony set is in
valency-voice-overview ; the affiliative morphology is in
concept-form-affiliative-form .

Terminological background

The applicative category was recognized early in Ainu grammaticology. Kindaichi and
Chiri (1936) named it 充当相 (applicative voice); Nakagawa notes
alongside it Chiri's earlier label 格の関係を表わす接頭辞
(prefixes expressing case relations) kindaichi1936 ;
nakagawa2024 192–193 . The typological rarity of productive applicatives
as a grammatical system in Eurasian languages — closest parallels in certain Caucasian
languages and Burushaski — was already remarked upon in this early literature and
confirmed by Bugaeva's later cross-linguistic survey bugaeva2006 185 ;
bugaeva2010 . The modern English term 'applicative' and the object-promotion
analysis of e- , ko- , and
o- are established in Bugaeva (2006, 2010) and consolidated in
the Handbook bugaeva2006 ; bugaeva2010 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . Nakagawa's own term
充当接頭辞 follows the same analysis
nakagawa2024 192 .

Baker's (1988) P-incorporation theory treats applicative formation as
preposition/postposition incorporation into the verb, promoting its complement to object;
Shibatani (1990) applies this framing to the Ainu data, and its most direct analytical
consequence is the feeding-relation account above
shibatani1990 65–66 ; baker1988 . The older
'case-relation prefix' terminology and the 'preverb' framing encountered for cognate
categories in some other languages do not figure as active analytical positions in the
recent Ainu literature: Bugaeva (2006), the Handbook, Nakagawa (2024), and Satō (2008)
adopt the applicative analysis consistently, and the terminological variation reflects a
historical shift in descriptive vocabulary rather than a substantive analytical
disagreement among the current sources
bugaeva2006 185–186 ; nakagawa2024 192–193 ‹consensus›.

title: Argument Realization: Affix Indexing and Free NP Order
summary: Grammatical relations encoded by bound person affixes rather than case or fixed position, with free, pragmatically governed NP order.
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Argument Realization: Affix Indexing and Free NP Order
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: Grammatical relations encoded by bound person affixes rather than case or fixed position, with free, pragmatically governed NP order.

Overview

The predicate in Hokkaido Ainu carries obligatory person indexes that identify its core
arguments: the sole argument S of an intransitive verb, and the agent A and patient O of a
transitive verb. Overt noun phrases for those same roles are optional and, when present, stand
in apposition to the index already on the verb rather than filling a case-marked argument slot.
The result is a head-marking language in the sense of nichols1986 : the
predicate head encodes grammatical relations, and the dependent nominal does not.
sato2025a 359–361 develops the contrast with dependent-marking languages such
as Japanese, where the argument NP bears obligatory case particles. bugaeva2012 461, 472 states the profile concisely: Ainu is agglutinating, polysynthetic, and head-marking, with
verb-final constituent order and a mixed (basically tripartite) morphological alignment.

This chapter asks how S, A, and O are realised at the clause level: through bound
indexes, through optional overt NPs, or through null. The person-affix paradigm itself — its
cells, allomorphy, and the S/A/O distinctions — belongs to
person-marking-architecture and the chapters that follow it. Morphological
alignment is pursued in alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate . Pragmatic word
order and topic fronting are treated in constituent-order-head-final and
pragmatic-word-order-dislocation . Reference tracking across clauses — zero
anaphora, the fourth person in narrative, the absence of switch-reference morphology — is taken
up in reference-tracking .

Obligatory indexing; the verb alone is a complete sentence

Person indexes are not optional agreement controllers: they are the grammatical realisation of
the argument itself. A finite verb without any overt NP is a complete, well-formed sentence.
The intransitive inkar look around with its first-singular prefix is
self-sufficient as a one-word utterance:

ku=inkar | 1SG.A=look.around | I look around. | After Tamura 1988: 72, cited in Satō 2025: 355. No overt pronoun or NP appears; the prefix ku= alone encodes the first-singular agent. | sato2025a:355 | HK

sato2025a 359–360 states the principle directly: "grammatical relations
(subject and object) … are not obligatory elements in Ainu; rather it is the personal affixes
that are obligatory for the predicates." bugaeva2012 472 formulates the same
point as pro-drop with obligatory indexing: the language drops its overt NPs freely because the
index already encodes the referent. A fuller transitive clause shows the same structure: without
the NP cep 'fish', a=koyki 'catch' is a
grammatically complete predication indexing a fourth-person agent and a third-person patient.

cep a=koyki | fish 4.A=catch | We catch fish. | Satō 2025: 363. The NP cep 'fish' is an optional appositive specifying the zero-marked third-person patient. The predicate a=koyki is grammatically complete without it. | sato2025a:363 | HK

Independent personal pronouns — kani 'I',
eani 'you' — are available for emphasis or contrast but are used more
sparingly than their Japanese counterparts, since the verbal index already does the referential
work nakagawa2024 163–165 ; bugaeva2012 472 . They are a
distinct word class from the bound indexes and are treated in
independent-personal-pronouns .

The structural status of overt NPs

When an overt NP does appear for a core role, its structural position relative to the verb is
not that of a case-flagged argument filling a valency slot. sato2025a 363–365 ,
working within Role and Reference Grammar, places the person index as the PRO inside the clausal
CORE — the actual argument — and positions the co-referential NP outside the CORE as
an optional, non-obligatory element. The Lakhota-parallel structure he gives for
cep a=koyki has the same logical structure (LS) whether the NP is
present or absent; the NP adds lexical specification without changing the underlying argument
count.

An immediate formal consequence is that no case particle marks the S/A/O distinction on the NP
itself. Ainu carries no markers corresponding to Japanese が or
を for core roles nakagawa2024 363 ; bugaeva2012 472 ; shibatani1990 22 . Two NPs in the same clause — say, an agent and a patient
— bear no formal distinction between them on the nominal. Role is readable from the verbal index
and from contextual plausibility; a consequence explored in
constituent-order-head-final is that the preverbal NPs may appear in any sequence.

The same appositive status holds for possessor NPs: the possessee is obligatorily head-marked
by a person prefix, so "pronominal possessor NPs are normally not used and even nominal
possessor NPs may easily be omitted if contextually recoverable" bugaeva2012 486 . ijas2023 captures the same pattern pedagogically, describing the verb as
having obligatory "pockets" for its arguments and the overt NP as redundant: "you would need
to convey the same information twice but that would be very uneconomical."

Null and zero arguments

"It is not necessary in Ainu for a noun phrase that is clear from the context to appear in a
sentence, and such a noun phrase is not even represented by a pronoun; even if a verb takes two
objects, it is normal for only one of them to be present" — bugaeva2022 (ch.14,
before ex.75). nakagawa2024 361 formalises this in the sentence template: a
NP "need not be present in the sentence as long as it can be supplied meaningfully from
context." The missing argument is read as a zero anaphor — not a phonologically null pronoun
alongside the index, but the index itself satisfying the valency slot with no additional NP
exponency.

A particularly clear case arises with three-place verbs. A verb hosts at most one subject index
and one object index ( person-marking-architecture ; object-indexing-monotransitive ), so the third argument of a ditransitive — typically the theme — is never indexed and
appears only as an optional bare NP or is null. Its reference remains unambiguous: "verbal
valency guarantees the interpretation of anaphoric reference without any personal affix"
sato2025a 368–369 . The following clause is the second in a two-sentence
passage; the preceding clause establishes the theme (a sweet). The second clause drops it
entirely, leaving only the human recipient and the first-singular agent overt or indexed:

ekasi ku=kore na | old.man 1SG.A=give FIN | I'll give it to the old man. | Satō 2025 ex. 21 (second clause). The theme — a sweet, established in the preceding clause tanpe topenpe ne na 'this is a sweet' — is null. The verb kore 'give' is three-place; valency alone guarantees the theme's interpretation. Only the human recipient ekasi 'old man' and the 1SG agent ku= are expressed. | sato2025a:368 | HK

The third person is zero-marked throughout the paradigm: the bare verb stem without any affix
expresses a third-person argument. sato2025a 369 argues that recognising a
possessive prefix a= (fourth person) already commits the analyst to a
zero third-person affix in the slot that a= contrasts with.
nakagawa2024 169 describes the same paradigm without explicitly positing a
zero affix ‹contested›; the status of the zero is examined in
person-marking-architecture . A community observation corroborates the
zero-3rd-person analysis: "the bare verb form = 3SG = citation/dictionary form" and "using a
possessive already concedes a zero 3rd person" (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-25,
asserted) aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Modes of argument realisation

sato2025a 371, 373–374 provides the master inventory of how a core argument
can be realised in a head-marking Ainu clause. The five modes are not mutually exclusive —
noun incorporation combines bound morphology with what would otherwise be an NP slot — but
they represent the primary structural options:

#
mode
example (Satō 2025)
gloss
note

1
person affix alone
ku=kore
'I give it'
default; the PRO in the clausal CORE is the argument

2
person affix + optional NP
cep a=koyki
'we catch fish'
NP appositional, outside CORE; redundant with the index

3
nominal prefix / noun incorporation
cep-koyki-an
'we fish'
argument fused into the verb stem; treated in noun-incorporation-object

4
valency-guaranteed NP, no index
wakka eci=ta-re
'I make you dip water'
unindexed theme; reference guaranteed by three-place valency alone

5
pseudo-incorporation (auxiliary)
a=e rusuy
'we want to eat it'

auxiliary rusuy want takes no person marker; the lexical verb carries
the index

sato2025a 371 draws a theoretical conclusion from this inventory: because
argument fulfilment is "sometimes by syntactic means and sometimes by morphological means," the
syntax/morphology boundary "is blurred in Ainu," a consequence of its head-marking character.
Mode 4 in particular — the theme as bare NP without an index — "partly shows a
dependent-marking characteristic" while remaining under head-marking control.
sato2025a 366–367 further uses the attested A-incorporation pattern
( ku=koy-yanke 'the waves wash me ashore') to argue against Baker's
( baker1988 229–232 ) syntactic head-movement theory of incorporation:
since the person index already anchors the argument inside the verb, incorporation need not be
derived by NP movement.

Are overt NPs arguments? The appositional-NP debate

Whether overt NPs that co-occur with verbal indexes should be analysed as true arguments of
the verb or as appositives to pronominal arguments already residing on the verb is an open
analytical question that the literature on Ainu explicitly engages ‹contested›.

The pronominal-argument position (associated with Jelinek 1984 and Baker 1988/1996,
as framed in bugaeva2022 ch.1 §4.3) holds that in head-marking languages the
bound indexes are the clause's actual arguments and co-referential NPs are mere appositives
that lexically qualify them — adjuncts, not arguments. Applied to Ainu, this analysis predicts
that an overt NP should be freely omissible (attested), freely ordered (attested), uncased
(attested), and potentially discontinuous (attested for floating quantifiers;
constituent-order-head-final ). As a theoretical classification it would place Ainu
in the nonconfigurational company of Mohawk or Lakhota. The full battery of diagnostics is
examined in nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument .

bugaeva2022 (ch.1 §4.3) names and entertains the pronominal-argument position but
does not adopt it for Ainu. Her preferred account, aligned with Mithun (2003, cited in Bugaeva
2022 ch.1 §4.3), is shared argumenthood : bound pronouns and coreferential NPs jointly
share argumenthood, neither alone being the sole argument. The Ainu-specific evidence she
marshals is the location-class noun (see §6 below): when both A and O are third-person and
therefore zero-indexed, what disambiguates the two roles is the semantic class of one of the
NPs — specifically whether it is a locative-type noun. If NPs were pure appositives with no
grammatical traction, their semantic class would have no leverage on role assignment; the fact
that it does points toward retained argumenthood.

sato2025a 363–365 , working in Role and Reference Grammar, gives a third
structural description: the person index is the PRO inside the clausal CORE, and the
co-referential NP sits outside the CORE — architecturally close to the pronominal-argument
analysis but without endorsing the PAH label or a nonconfigurational classification. Satō does
not invoke Jelinek's Pronominal Argument Hypothesis or describe Ainu as nonconfigurational; the
account is head-marking with an optional external appositive.

The current consensus is partial. That Ainu is head-marking, pro-drop, without core case, and
with freely ordered optional overt NPs is uncontested . Whether those NPs are
appositive adjuncts to pronominal arguments (Jelinek/Baker line) or share argumenthood with
the indexes (Mithun/Bugaeva line) is contested , and no Ainu specialist has
published a dedicated pronominal-argument study; the framing reaches Ainu only through
Bugaeva's and Satō's typological treatments, and Bugaeva's own preferred position argues
against the pure appositive reading. The chapter-length treatment is in
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument .

The location-class noun as role disambiguation

When both the agent and the patient of a transitive clause are third person — zero-indexed, no
case marking, no fixed order — the verbal indexes supply nothing to distinguish agent from
patient. This is the empirical gap at the centre of Bugaeva's counter-argument to the pure
appositive analysis. nakagawa2022pos (ch.14) documents the resolution: what
disambiguates the roles is the location-class status of a noun. Locative relational
nouns such as or place of , ka surface of , and
corpok below — attached after an NP — mark that NP as a Location argument,
leaving the other NP unambiguously as the theme or patient.

ci a=e-p saranip or oma-re | cooked.food 4.A=eat-NMLZ basket LOC be.in-CAUS | She put the cooked food into the basket. | Nakagawa 2022 ch.14 ex. 74 (Saru). The locative relational noun or 'place of' on saranip marks the basket as the goal (Location argument); ci a=e-p 'the cooked food' is therefore unambiguously the theme. Without or, the two NPs would be structurally indistinguishable from a third-person agent and patient. | nakagawa2022pos | SAR

The disambiguation is lexical and semantic, not morphological: it depends on the inherent or
augmented location-class status of one NP, not on a separate case affix. As
bugaeva2022 (ch.1 §4.4) notes, the mechanism is "particularly relevant when one
of the arguments is omitted" — when the non-location NP is null, the locative NP's class alone
tells the listener what role the missing argument fills. The contrast with Nivkh is instructive:
Nivkh also lacks case on S/O but fixes the object immediately preverbal and signals it by a
consonant mutation; Ainu uses neither device, relying on indexing and the location class
instead nakagawa2022pos . The locative noun system and its postpositional
extensions are treated in relational-and-spatial-nouns and
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events .

Reference tracking: zero anaphora and the fourth person

Because arguments are indexed on the verb and overt NPs are optional, participant continuity
across a narrative rests primarily on zero anaphora plus the verbal indexes themselves; pronouns
are not routinely redeployed to maintain reference. Overt NP re-mention is reserved for
(re)introduction, contrast, or scene change bugaeva2022 (ch.14);
nakagawa2024 361 .

The indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm fourth person does additional tracking
work in narrative. In oral texts it marks the protagonist's or quoted speaker's first person as
distinct from both the plain third-person cast and the narrating speaker's first singular,
functioning as an obviative-like "other first person" nakagawa2024 177–179 ;
bugaeva2008 40–43 . An observed instance: in an uwepeker telling, the narrator
alternates a= and ku= to keep the in-story
protagonist's speech separate from the narrator's own voice (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-07-04, asserted) aynucorporadiscord . This alternation is consistent
with bugaeva2008 40–43 's logophoric analysis of the fourth-person quotation
use and with nakagawa2024 174–179 's narrative first-person account. The fourth
person's full referential range and its interaction with topic-marking
anakne as for are treated in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm ,
fourth-person-honorific , and logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse ;
the wider reference-tracking apparatus — zero anaphora, clause chaining, and the absence of
morphological switch-reference — is taken up in reference-tracking and
clause-linkage-overview .

title: Causal and Purpose Clauses (kusu)
summary: The polyfunctional kusu/gusu marking reason 'because' and purpose 'in order to', its formal-noun origin, and its division of labour with the kuni purpose complementizer.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Causal and Purpose Clauses (kusu)
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The polyfunctional kusu/gusu marking reason 'because' and purpose 'in order to', its formal-noun origin, and its division of labour with the kuni purpose complementizer.

Overview: kusu in the causal–purposive group

Among Hokkaido Ainu's conjunctive particles, kusu stands out as the
only member whose primary meaning spans two semantic relations: it marks the preceding clause
as either the reason or cause of the following event, or the purpose or aim toward which the
following clause's action is directed. Nakagawa assigns it, together with
wa kusu and hi kusu , to group ⑥ (原因・目的,
'cause/purpose') of his seven-way semantic taxonomy of conjunctive particles
nakagawa2024 352–353 . Refsing places the same particle under her
"Consequential" grouping alongside kunine and
na for Shizunai refsing1986 238, 251–253 . Satō
treats it in the section adjacent to korka 'but', contrasting
purposive and causal readings sato2008 53–54 . The three sources agree on
the central fact: the single particle kusu covers both cause and
purpose, and the reading is determined by context.

Two reason-only compound particles restrict the semantic range.
Wa kusu foregrounds a completed event as the cause of the main
clause; the sequential wa signals that the preceding event actually
took place. Hi kusu is the formal-noun construction
hi ('the fact/time of') + kusu and similarly
carries reason only nakagawa2024 353 ; ijas2023 . The three
particles and their properties are given in the table below.

particle
function
event status of preceding clause
dialect range

kusu (variant
kus with final-vowel devoicing)
reason or purpose
unrestricted
pan-Hokkaido

wa kusu
reason only
completed/actualized event required
pan-Hokkaido

hi kusu
reason only
unrestricted
Saru/Chitose; rare and dialectally variable elsewhere

The final vowel of kusu is frequently devoiced in running speech,
yielding the variant kus ; this is a phonological process, not a
distinct lexeme. The segmentally identical material also enters the intentive auxiliary
kusu ne 'be going to; intend to' and the indirect-speech
complementizer use treated in kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements . The
inventory context for kusu is established in
clause-linkage-overview inventory the clause-linkage overview .

Purpose: 'in order to'

When kusu marks purpose, the preceding clause describes the goal or
aim of the action in the following clause, and the following clause is typically a motion
verb. This distributional tendency is strong and is explicitly noted by Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 353 . The purpose-marked clause occupies the adverbial slot of
a prospective complement: the event it describes has not yet occurred at the time of the
motion.

nina=an kusu soyne=an | gather.firewood=4.S PURP go.out=4.S | (Someone) went out to gather firewood. | Kusu marks the preceding activity as the purpose of the following motion verb soyne 'go out'. The fourth-person suffix =an indexes the narrator-external agent on both verbs. | nakagawa2024:353 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 670

The purposive reading is available whenever the following clause describes a transition,
displacement, or initiation rather than a completed past event. For purpose complements
governed by a transitive verb of thinking, saying, or wanting, the complementizer
kuni (purpose/irrealis complementizer) takes over the embedding role; the
boundary between adverbial kusu and complementizing
kuni is treated in kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements .
A rarer purpose-adverbial, kunine ( kuni +
copula ne ), is attested in Satō and Refsing but is less productive
than plain kusu in this function
sato2008 53 ; refsing1986 252 .

Reason and cause: 'because'

The reason/cause reading of kusu treats the preceding clause as the
motivation, antecedent condition, or cause of the following one. The preceding clause
typically describes a state of affairs that holds or held at the time the speaker asserts
the consequent. The following example shows kusu after a
desiderative clause: the speaker's desire to pursue is the stated reason for preparing the
boat.

kese a=anpa wa arpa=an rusuy kusu cip etoko a=oyki hine | behind 4.A=pursue CONJ go=4.S DES CONJ boat prow 4.A=prepare CONJ | Wanting to pursue and go, (she) made the boat ready. | Kusu marks the desiderative clause arpa=an rusuy ('wanting to go in pursuit') as the reason for the preparation. The sequential hine links that preparation to the next event in the narrative chain. | nakagawa2024:353 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 671, after Kawamura 1985: 30

When the cause is a realized state of affairs, the same kusu
applies. In the following example a formal-noun predicate ( p ne )
closes the cause clause before kusu introduces the consequence.

sisam itak ani ku=respa p ne kusu aynu itak sinep ka erampewtek pa | Japanese language INS 1SG.A=raise NMLZ COP CONJ Ainu language one EMPH not.understand PL | Because I raised them in Japanese, they do not understand a single word of Ainu. | The formal-noun predicate p ne nominalizes the cause clause; kusu marks it as causal. The plural marker pa on the matrix verb reflects multiple objects. The instrumental ani marks the means of upbringing. | nakagawa2024:353 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 672

Refsing documents the same structure in Shizunai: kusu introducing a
consequence after a stative cause clause ('yesterday since the weather was fine, the three of
us strolled over the mountain') refsing1986 251 . The cause-clause predicate
type (desiderative, formal-noun, stative) does not by itself determine the choice of
kusu ; the cause reading is the more accessible one wherever the
preceding clause describes a completed or persistent state.

The evidential–kusu scope order

A conjunctive particle attaches outside the clause-final evidential frame. When a cause
clause closes on an evidential nominalizer such as ruwe ne or
hawe ne , kusu follows the evidential rather
than intervening between the verb and it. Nakagawa states the ordering for all linkers:
predicate – evidential – connective nakagawa2024 393 . The following
attested example from Kawakami Matsuko's Saru oral narrative shows the reportative
evidential haw-e ne followed directly by
kusu .

e=hosipi haw-e ne kusu tane anakne a=eyayramkar pe ne na | 2SG.S=return voice-POSS COP CONJ now TOP 4.A=give.up NMLZ COP FIN | Because you came back (as they say), now I give up. | The reportative evidential haw-e ne (voice-POSS COP: 'it is said that') precedes kusu, marking the cause proposition as known by report before kusu signals it as the reason for giving up. The matrix clause closes on a formal-noun predicate pe ne with the final particle na. | ilcaa1976:aa-irc/021#271 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話 14 [aa-irc/021#271]

The ordering constraint has a corollary for the intentive auxiliary. When causal
kusu follows a ruwe ne evidential, the
string ruwe ne kusu is well formed and frequently attested;
however, the intentive kusu ne requires an opposite arrangement —
kusu ne ruwe ne — and is not licensed after
ruwe ne , making *ruwe ne kusu ne
ungrammatical nakagawa2024 393 . Nukopoli characterized this as a scope
hierarchy in which the intentive modal must scope outside the evidential, and the
characterization is corroborated by the published account
( aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024).
The evidential system and its interaction with clause-linking material is treated in
ruwe-ne-inferential the ruwe ne chapter .

wa kusu and hi kusu: reason-only compounds

The compound wa kusu restricts the meaning to cause and reason,
excluding the purpose reading. The sequential particle wa signals
that the preceding event is completed and its result-state is in force;
kusu then marks that actualized event as the cause of the main
clause nakagawa2024 353 ; ijas2023 . Because the preceding
event must be actualized for wa to apply, a future or hypothetical
purpose clause — which by definition has not yet occurred — is incompatible with
wa kusu . The following constructed example illustrates the
compound.

e=ek wa kusu ku=hosipi | 2SG.S=come CONJ CONJ 1SG.S=return | Because you came, I returned. | Wa kusu: wa closes the first clause as a completed event; kusu marks it as the cause of the return. The same sentence with plain kusu (e=ek kusu ku=hosipi) is grammatical but leaves the purpose reading marginally open; wa kusu disambiguates in favour of cause. Constructed after Nakagawa (2024: 353) and Ijäs (2023).

Satō cites an adversative frame in Chitose where wa kusu occurs
inside a sentence headed by korka 'but': the speaker wanted to go
to town but the addressee's arrival ( e=ek wa kusu ) caused
the cancellation sato2008 53 . The construction is well represented in
colloquial speech across Hokkaido.

Hi kusu is a distinct compound: the formal noun
hi the fact of; place, time nominalizes the preceding clause ('the fact
that …'), and kusu marks it as cause. The result closely
parallels plain kusu in meaning — reason only — but the
formal-noun nominalizer adds a more explicit factual presupposition. Nakagawa confirms
the reason-only reading and notes that hi kusu is common in Saru
and Chitose but rare and dialectally variable outside those varieties
nakagawa2024 353 .

Refsing identifies a set of lexicalized compounds built on
kusu / kus in Shizunai:
nekusu 'therefore', wakusu 'so that', and
the interrogative nepkus 'why' refsing1986 252 .
These frozen compounds represent the end-point of lexicalization within the same
grammaticalization pathway that gives rise to the free conjunctive use described in the
next section.

The free conjunctive use: 'therefore, that is why'

An ordinary conjunctive particle is bound to the predicate it closes and does not head a
sentence. Kusu is exceptional: it can appear sentence-initially as
a free connective adverb meaning 'therefore; that is why' (Japanese
そのために ), straddling the boundary between conjunctive particle and
conjunction nakagawa2024 353 . This free use is noted in the clause-linkage
overview; see clause-linkage-overview finite-linkage the particle/adverb
distinction . In this position, kusu links a preceding
discourse unit to the following clause as its consequence or result, functioning as a
clause-level connective adverb rather than a post-predicate particle.

Refsing's lexicalized Shizunai compound nekusu 'therefore' carries
the same discourse function in more grammaticalized form refsing1986 252 .
In contemporary teaching Ainu and learner speech the partially translated form
sorekusu — Japanese それ 'that' plus Ainu
kusu — occupies the same slot as a sentence opener meaning 'that is
why; therefore' ( aynucorporadiscord , thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord 2025)
‹contested›. The classical Hokkaido equivalent is the sentence-initial free
kusu documented by Nakagawa, or its Shizunai counterpart
nekusu ; sorekusu is a revitalization-context
form that integrates the Japanese demonstrative as a topic antecedent.

kusu ne: the intentive and prospective construction

When kusu is followed by the copula
ne be with no person-marking affix, the combination grammaticalizes toward
an auxiliary meaning 'be going to; intend to' — the intentive or prospective
nakagawa2024 435 ; refsing1986 252 . The absence of
person marking on ne is the diagnostic: ordinary copular predicates
carry person indexes, but in kusu ne the copula is a
grammaticalized element of the complex, and person is already encoded on the preceding
verb. Ijäs teaches the phonologically reduced form kusne alongside
the fuller kusu ne , reflecting reduction in everyday speech
ijas2023 .

paye=an kusu ne na | go=4.S PURP COP FIN | (She) intends to go. | Extracted from the quoted-speech context '…paye=an kusu ne na.' sekor hawean 'she said: I intend to go.' The copula ne bears no person index; paye=an already carries the fourth-person S suffix =an. The final particle na closes the quoted clause. | ilcaa1976:aa-irc/004#51 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2 [aa-irc/004#51]

The construction's semantics spans intentionality, prospective aspect (an event about to
occur), and future-oriented modality. Nakagawa places kusu ne in
the 意志・必然 (volition/necessity) section of his auxiliary inventory
nakagawa2024 435 . Nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) characterizes
kusu ne as covering both intentionality and tense, analogous to
English will ; this is a community-proposed extension of the description that
available published sources do not fully adjudicate
( aynucorporadiscord ) ‹speculative›. The modal and deontic extensions — in
particular the 'must, should' readings — are treated in
deontic-necessity , and the construction's place in the broader TAM system
in tam-overview .

Refsing additionally reports a Shizunai intentional construction
kusu ki , where the generic verb ki do replaces the
copula refsing1986 252 . This is a dialect-specific instantiation of the
same purposive-to-intentive grammaticalization path ‹corpus-suggested›.

The causal–purpose polysemy: how the readings are resolved

Several factors favour the purpose reading of bare kusu : a motion
or initiation verb in the main clause, a prospective or unrealized event in the marked
clause, and a goal-oriented relationship between the actor of the marked clause and the
actor of the main clause. Factors favouring the cause reading include completed or stative
predicates in the marked clause, an evidential tail before
kusu , and a disjoint event-time relationship between the two
clauses.

Satō makes the clearest separation: he treats kusu after a
formal-noun predicate as cause, and in proximity to a motion verb as purpose
sato2008 53–54 . Nakagawa gives both readings under a single entry and
notes the motion-verb clustering for purpose nakagawa2024 352–353 . Ijäs,
following Nakagawa, presents the gloss 'because / so / in order to' as a single continuum
and identifies motion-verb clustering as the most reliable purpose-cue in text
ijas2023 . The polysemy itself is uncontroversial across sources; what the
accessible sources do not provide is a formal-syntactic account that would predict the
reading from structure alone ‹speculative›.

Dialect range and Sakhalin contrast

Within Hokkaido, the distribution of the three particles is uneven.
Hi kusu is confirmed for Saru and Chitose; Nakagawa flags it as
rare and dialectally variable outside those varieties nakagawa2024 353 .
For Shizunai, Refsing's grammar documents kusu in all the
functions described here and adds the lexicalized compounds
refsing1986 251–253 .

The Sakhalin varieties present a typologically notable divergence. In Sakhalin Ainu,
kusu does not mark cause or purpose but instead covers the
action-continuation and result-state range that Hokkaido splits between
kor (simultaneous action) and wa
(result-state after completion) nakagawa2024 344 . The Sakhalin particle
is therefore a false cognate for Hokkaido purposes: segmentally identical but functionally
divergent. This contrast is summarized in clause-linkage-overview dialect-edges
and is relevant to simultaneous-kor-clauses .

title: The Morphological Causative -re / -e / -te
summary: The productive transitivizing causative suffix and its stem-conditioned allomorphy -re/-e/-te, encoding direct/manipulative causation.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Morphological Causative -re / -e / -te
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The productive transitivizing causative suffix and its stem-conditioned allomorphy -re/-e/-te, encoding direct/manipulative causation.

Place in the causative inventory

Hokkaido Ainu marks causation with a small set of verb-final suffixes. The productive
majority is one underlying form realized as three phonologically conditioned allomorphs:
-re after a vowel or the glides y ,
w ; -te after any consonant other than
r ; and -e after r
nakagawa2024 200–201 ; bugaeva2014 134 . In Nakagawa's
arity calculus it carries the value +1 : the original subject of the
base verb is reassigned to the object position of the derived verb, and a new causer
participant fills the subject position nakagawa2024 200–201, 431 .

Three further suffix types occupy the same post-stem slot: the forming suffixes
-V and -ke that build transitivity pairs,
the direct-causative / transitivizer -ka , and the valency-neutral
indefinite causative -yar / -ar . The
indefinite causative, which leaves the causee unexpressed and unindexed, is treated in
permissive-sociative-causative-yar ; the forming suffixes and
-ka are covered in transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs .
Causee marking, the double-object frame, and double causatives are developed in
causee-marking-ditransitive-double-causative . The present chapter concerns
-re / -te / -e
alone: their allomorphy, their arity effect, the productive versus non-productive split,
and the direct/indirect and coercive/permissive distinctions.

Allomorphy

The three surface forms are phonologically predictable from the stem-final segment
nakagawa2024 201 ; bugaeva2014 134 ; tamura2000 .
Tamura's Saru dictionary and Bugaeva's 387-verb Saru/HSW corpus sample converge on the
same conditioning environments. Satō's Chitose textbook gives the same three allomorphs
with the qualification "大体 by stem-final sound, but with exceptions", acknowledging a
residue of forms that resist the phonological prediction
sato2008 234–238 .

environment
allomorph
base
derived
meaning

after vowel; after y , w
-re
nu 'hear'
nu-re
'make/let hear, tell'

after vowel; after y , w
-re
siknu 'live, be alive'
siknu-re
'keep alive'

after vowel; after y , w
-re
tuy 'cut (vi)'
tuy-re
'make (sb) cut'

after consonant other than r
-te
sinot 'play'
sinot-te
'make/let play'

after consonant other than r
-te
ek 'come (SG)'
ek-te
'bring (SG), fetch'

after r
-e
nukar 'see'
nukar-e
'show'

One phonological property separates the productive suffix from the non-productive cluster:
it does not shift the pitch-accent kernel of the stem —
kú-re and sinót-te , not
*ku-ré or *sinot-té
bugaeva2014 130–131 .

The analysis of siknu-re 'keep alive' — intransitive
siknu live, be alive plus -re — is corroborated
in community linguistic discussion aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2026): the vowel-final stem takes the predicted allomorph without irregularity
‹corpus-confirmed›. A related community observation notes that lexicalized forms such
as iyairaykere ({' i=yairayke-re ,
lit. 'makes people grateful') and irankarapte
({' i=rankarap-te , lit. 'makes people greet') may carry an
"obligatory" or "deserving-of" reading, analogous to participial active/passive contrasts
in other languages aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024)
‹speculative›. The allomorphic conditioning in these forms follows the regular pattern;
the proposed extended reading of the construction is not confirmed in the primary literature.

Arity and the argument-frame shift

On an intransitive base the productive causative yields a transitive verb: the base's
sole argument (S) is reassigned to the object position (O), and the new causer argument
fills the subject position (A). The person-marking diagnostics of
person-marking-architecture confirm the output class: a derived transitive
takes the prefixal A indexes, while the base intransitive takes suffixal S indexes.
Nakagawa positions the causative suffix after the forming and plural material and before
the outer person index, placing it in the penultimate layer of the verb-word template
nakagawa2024 210–212 .

The base-and-derived pair for sinot play makes the argument-frame shift
visible. In the base clause matkaci 'girl' is the intransitive
subject; in the causative clause she is the object (causee), while the causer
hapo 'mother' takes the subject position:

matkaci sinot | girl play | The girl played. | nakagawa2024:431 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 954, after Tamura 1984

hapo matkaci sinot-te | mother girl play-CAUS | Mother made the girl play. | The base intransitive subject matkaci becomes the causee object; hapo is the new causer subject. The -te allomorph follows the consonant-final stem sinot. | nakagawa2024:431 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 954′

When the causee is cross-referenced on the verb by a person index, the argument
reassignment is morphologically explicit. Where the first person is the intransitive
subject ( ku=sinot 'I play'), causativization realigns the same
participant as the object en= :

hapo en=sinot-te | mother 1SG.O=play-CAUS | Mother made me play. | The first-person argument shifts from the subjective index ku= to the objective en=, diagnosing the arity increase from vi to vt. | nakagawa2024:431 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 955

On a transitive base, the productive causative yields a double-object verb (vd): the
original A becomes the O causee, the original O is retained as an inner object, and the
new causer is the derived A. The attested form
a=e=tuy-e-re 'I made you cut (the grapevines)' —
tuy-e 'cut' [vt] with -re added —
carries two simultaneous person indexes and is cited by Bugaeva and Kobayashi as evidence
that causativization of transitives is both licensed and productive
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.2 . Double causatives — the productive suffix
added to an already causativized base — are also attested, yielding a four-place verb;
the attested kor-e-re 'cause to give' is one such form
bugaeva2014 142–143 . Both the double-object frame and double causatives
are treated in causee-marking-ditransitive-double-causative .

Productive vs. non-productive causatives

Bugaeva's 387-verb Saru/HSW corpus sample provides the most comprehensive quantitative
treatment of the suffix inventory bugaeva2014 130, 143–144 . The four
suffix types divide by count, base-class, and causation type:

suffix
sample (n)
vi / vt base
causation type

-V
111
109 vi / 2 vt
direct (lexical)

-ke
9
9 vi / 0 vt
direct

-ka
38
33 vi / 5 vt
direct

-re / -te / -e
190
113 vi / 16 vt (+ 61 indirect)
direct (129) + indirect (61)

At 190 tokens, the productive suffix outnumbers the three non-productive types combined
and is the only one that covers both direct and indirect causation from both vi and vt
bases bugaeva2014 143 . It also attaches freely to derived stems — those
prefixed by applicatives or reciprocal u- — while the
non-productive suffixes do not kobayashi2015 33 . This combinatorial
range marks it as the productive default of Hokkaido Ainu causation ‹consensus›.

Nakagawa separates the forming suffixes -V and
-ke as 形成接尾辞 ('forming suffixes') building lexical
transitivity pairs, distinct in status from the grammatical causative
nakagawa2024 198–203 . Bugaeva's analysis subsumes even these under
'direct causative', treating the entire cluster as a valency-increasing operator set
with differing scopes bugaeva2014 129–130, 143 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.2 . The classification of the forming suffixes
as lexical versus grammatical causatives is ‹contested›; the relevant forms are
developed in transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs .

Direct and indirect causation; coercive and permissive use

When the base intransitive has an agentive subject, the productive causative yields an
indirect causative: the causee acts on its own in response to the causer's enabling,
permission, or direction. When the base is stative or non-agentive, the direct reading
predominates, with the causee as a highly affected non-volitional patient
bugaeva2014 130, 139 . The non-productive suffixes are limited to the
direct reading; the productive suffix spans both.

An earlier analysis by Fukuda (Tamura) restricted the productive suffix to coercive
causation; Bugaeva corrects this, citing Satō 2008, and documents permissive readings
from corpus data bugaeva2014 139, 141–142 . A verb of ingestion such as
e eat → e-re carries both coercive ('feed a
child') and permissive ('let an adult eat') readings contextually, following the
crosslinguistic pattern for ingestive verbs observed by Shibatani and Pardeshi (cited
in bugaeva2014 141–142 ). Satō 2008 lesson 30 presents the same
coercive/permissive ambiguity and derives it from the same form
sato2008 237 . The following attested example shows the causative of
ipe eat a meal — ipe-re 'feed' — in a Saru
narrative with an indefinite-agent fourth-person prefix:

un=ipe-re wa i=kore kur un=osura noyne sioran | 1PL.EXCL.O=eat-CAUS CONJ 4.O=give person 1PL.EXCL.O=abandon like seem | The person who had fed us then abandoned us, it seemed. | un= is 1PL.EXCL object (us); ipe-re shows -re after the vowel-final stem ipe 'eat a meal'. The verb heads a relative clause (kur un=ipe-re 'the person who fed us'). sioran = siri + an, visual-evidential existential. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | Hamada Takashi, 'pon cape asiknep'; Ainu Times no. 24, p. 3 (ainu-times/024/3#3), extract

Ijäs's Lesson 9 formulation that -re "simply turns intransitive
verbs into transitive verbs" is a workable approximation for the basic indirect
causative; it omits the allomorphic detail, the permissive reading, the causativization
of transitives, and the direct-causative uses ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

Motion verbs and restrictions

A subset of singular motion verbs takes -ke as their primary
causative suffix, and Nakagawa documents a semantic split within this class: when a
non-volitional object (cargo, luggage) is caused to move, the suffix
-ke is standard; when a volitional person is caused to move,
the productive -te is used instead
nakagawa2024 202–203 . The minimal contrast for
ahun enter (SG) at Nakagawa's examples 196–197:

i=teke uk hine i=ahun-te ruwe ne akus | 4.O=hand take CONJ 4.O=enter-CAUS track-POSS COP then | He took my hand and brought me inside. | ahun-te with a volitional human causee, cross-referenced by the logophoric 4th-person prefix i=. Contrast ahun-ke (Nakagawa ex. 196), used when the causee is non-volitional cargo. The -te allomorph follows the consonant-final stem ahun. | nakagawa2024:202 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 197

The -ke / productive-suffix alternation applies to
ran descend (SG) ( ran-ke for objects,
ran-te for persons) and
yan come ashore (SG) ( yan-ke for objects)
nakagawa2024 202–203 . The verb rikin go up (SG) takes
-ka ( rikin-ka ) in most dialects; its
reduplicated plural allomorph rikip does not feed a causative
*rikip-ka , the causative being formed on the singular stem
only aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2026)
‹corpus-confirmed›, consistent with the motion-verb causative paradigm documented by
Nakagawa nakagawa2024 202–203 .

One restriction spans all causative suffix types: zero-place meteorological verbs resist
any causativization, because there is no subject argument to reassign to the object
position. sir-pirka 'the weather is good' has no causative
*sirpirka-re
bugaeva2014 139 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.2 .
Among the non-productive suffixes, -ke additionally requires a
one-argument base; the productive suffix and -ka accept both
one- and two-argument bases bugaeva2014 139 .

Dialect variation, the -ka → -re/-te shift, and the Horobetsu switch-reference function

The productive suffix is encroaching on -ka territory across
Hokkaido dialects, with the shift most advanced in Saru and the southwest. Many verbs
that take -ka in northeastern Hokkaido appear with
-re/-te/-e in Saru; intra-dialectal doublets such as
nam-te ~ nam-ka in Saru document the
change in progress bugaeva2014 135–136, 143–144 ;
kobayashi2015 33 . Sakhalin varieties (‹SA›) preserve
-ka more broadly
bugaeva2014 135–136 .

The -ka suffix straddles the transitivizer and direct-causative
functions in a way the productive suffix does not. Satō 2008 flags some
-ke and -e forms as bleached
transitivizations — san → san-ke
'put out a fine', where no causer argument is introduced — distinct from the grammatical
causative sato2008 237–238 . The productive suffix is unambiguously
causative in function ‹consensus›.

In the Horobetsu dialect, Takahashi documents an extension of the productive causative
suffix into a switch-reference function: in coordinated clauses it marks that the
subject of the following predicate differs from that of the preceding one (different
subject), while in subordinated clauses it marks same-subject continuity
takahashi2017 102–106 . The attested Horobetsu forms —
é-re 'make eat', kasuy-re 'make help',
cis-te 'make cry', wen-te 'make bad' —
show the same allomorphic distribution as Saru and Chitose
takahashi2017 99–104 . Whether the switch-reference function is an
independent grammaticalization or a secondary extension of the causative meaning is not
settled in the accessible sources; no equivalent use is documented for Saru or Chitose
‹contested›.

title: Causee Marking, Ditransitive and Double Causatives
summary: How causativization of transitives builds three-place frames, the marking/indexing of the causee, and double causatives.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Causee Marking, Ditransitive and Double Causatives
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: How causativization of transitives builds three-place frames, the marking/indexing of the causee, and double causatives.

Overview

The productive causative suffix -re / -te / -e raises arity by one. Applied to an intransitive base it
creates a two-place transitive; applied to a transitive base it creates a three-place
ditransitive; applied a second time to a derived causative stem it produces a
four-place verb. The chapter follows the causee — the participant who is made or
permitted to act — through each of these steps: its expression as the O of a derived
transitive, the priority it takes over the theme when both objects compete for the
single O-index slot, and the behaviour of double causatives under the two-slot
ceiling. The allomorphy and the productive versus non-productive distribution of
causative morphology are developed in causative-morphological-re-e-te .
The indefinite causative -yar / -ar ,
which adds a causer while leaving the causee both unexpressed and unindexed, is
treated in permissive-sociative-causative-yar .

The causee as object

When -re/-te/-e derives a transitive verb from an intransitive
base, the former intransitive subject becomes the O of the causative verb. Person
marking shows this shift directly. The intransitive sinot play (intr)
takes the first-person A prefix ku= ; once the causative
-te is added, that same participant reappears as the
first-person object en= , and the new causer —
hapo 'mother' — occupies the A position, unmarked because
third-person A is zero nakagawa2024 431 .

ku=sinot | 1SG.A=play | I play. | nakagawa2024:431 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 954, after Tamura 1984

hapo en=sinot-te | mother 1SG.O=play-CAUS | Mother made me play. | The base subject ku= (1SG.A) reappears as the causee en= (1SG.O) of the causative; hapo 'mother' is the new causer, indexed by zero (third-person A is not marked). | nakagawa2024:431 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 955

The causee bears no case particle. Ainu objects are invariably unmarked, so the
causee surfaces as a bare NP alongside any O-set affix that cross-references it,
in exactly the same way the patient of any transitive does
bugaeva2014 142 ; nakagawa2024 201 . The same
principle carries into the three-place frame that arises when a transitive base is
causativized: the marking type does not change, only the number of competitors for
the single O-index slot increases.

Causative of a transitive: the three-place frame

When -re/-te/-e applies to a transitive verb (arity +2), the
result has arity +3. The new causer fills the A position, the original transitive
