subject (now the causee) fills one O position, and the original patient or theme
fills a second O position. Bugaeva (2014: 142) characterizes the causee in this frame
as an unmarked direct object in a double-object construction, parallel in its coding
to a recipient in an applicative-derived ditransitive
bugaeva2014 142 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.2 .

A corpus example from the Saru texts in the Nakagawa archive illustrates all three
participants at once. The base verb is tuy-e cut (transitive) , the
transitivized member of the tuy / tuy-e
pair; adding the causative -re raises arity to three. The
causer is indexed by a= (fourth person, functioning as
narrative first person), the causee 'you' by e= (2SG.O), and
the theme appears as a bare NP without any person affix on the verb:

a=e=tuy-e-re | 4.A=2SG.O=cut-TRVZ-CAUS | I made you cut the grapevines. | Attested form in context: ki kusu hat punkar a=e=tuy-e-re wa. The theme 'grapevines' (hat punkar) is a bare NP with no index on the verb; only the causer and causee are cross-referenced. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.1.2 | SAR | Nakagawa corpus K7803233UP.144; Handbook ex. 6c

Ijäs (Basic Lesson 3) uses the label 'ditransitive' for three-place verbs such as
epakasnu teach and kore give , and correctly identifies
a three-argument structure. Her accompanying claim that the indirect object falls
between subject and direct object in a fixed position is a pedagogical simplification:
Nakagawa's data show the two objects of a ditransitive in variable order
ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 363–365 ‹consensus›.

The table below tracks arity through the causative chain. The third row covers double
causatives, taken up in § 5 .

base verb
type
arity
causative output
output arity
participant frame

sinot 'play'
vi
+1
sinot-te
+2
causer (A-affix), causee (O-affix)

tuy-e 'cut sth'
vt
+2
tuy-e-re
+3
causer (A-affix), causee (O-affix), theme (bare NP)

omare 'put X in Y'
vd
+3
omare-re
+4
causer (A-affix), causee (O-affix), theme (NP), place (NP)

Person indexing: causee priority and secundative alignment

An Ainu verb carries at most two person affixes — one in the A-set and one in the
O-set — the two-slot ceiling described in valency-voice-overview
nakagawa2024 210–212 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §2 ;
sato2023b . In the three-place causative frame two objects compete for
the single O-slot. The attested indexing pattern, exemplified by
a=e=tuy-e-re , gives the O-slot to the causee and leaves the
theme as a bare NP.

This outcome places Ainu causative ditransitives in the secundative column of
ditransitive indexing typology (see object-indexing-monotransitive ): the
causee, as derived O, patterns with the O of a monotransitive verb for person-marking
purposes, rather than the original patient claiming that slot
sato2023b ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.2 . The pattern
mirrors what applies in applicative-derived ditransitives, where the applied object
— not the original patient — is indexed in the O-position ‹corpus-suggested›.

Whether the reverse indexing (theme indexed, causee as bare NP) ever occurs is not
settled in the sources consulted ‹speculative›.

A lexicalized sub-pattern involves the fourth-person object affix
i= serving as an indefinite causee. Forms such as
iyairaykere and irankarapte have been
proposed by nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, November 2024) as
i= + stem + -re/-te , glossable as
'cause people/someone to be grateful' and 'cause people/someone to greet' respectively.
On this reading i= occupies the O-slot as an indefinite
causee, and some lexicalized tokens drift toward an adjectival-modal reading
analogous to English excit-ing alongside excit-ed . The analysis is
not yet verified against the corpus and Nakagawa's (2024) treatment of these forms
has been judged insufficiently detailed by the same discussant
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›.

If the i= -causee reading is correct, causee expression in the
productive causative system presents a three-way gradient: a specific causee (named
NP plus O-set affix on the verb) → an indefinite causee
( i= -affix, cross-referenced but unspecified) → an unindexed
causee ( -yar , not cross-referenced at all). The antipassive
prefix i- can similarly fill the causee slot with a generic
object when applied to a causative-derived stem — Bugaeva's examples
i-newsar-ka 'amuse people' and
i-oripak-ka 'make people feel humble', reported via the
Handbook — and forms a related but distinct indefinite-causee strategy
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Double causatives

The productive suffix applies iteratively. A derived causative stem may itself serve
as the input to a second causativization, and the allomorphy rules apply at each
step. After the vowel-final first causative -e (the allomorph
after r ), the second causative surfaces as
-re (the allomorph after a vowel), producing the sequence
-e-re nakagawa2024 200–201 ;
bugaeva2014 134 .

kor have, hold (transitive) is a two-place verb. The first causative
suffix -e (after r ) yields
kor-e give (causative of kor: cause to have) (+3): the giver is the
new A, and the original possessor becomes a causee-beneficiary in the O position. A
second -re yields kor-e-re
(have- caus-caus , +4): an outer causer now makes the giver
give. Bugaeva (2014: 142–143) records kor-e-re as attested
once in her 387-verb Saru sample bugaeva2014 142–143 .

a=e=kor-e-re | 4.A=2SG.O=have-CAUS-CAUS | I caused you to give (something to someone). | The form kor-e-re 'cause to give' (have-CAUS-CAUS) is attested in Bugaeva (2014: 142–143); this constructed illustration shows the person-indexing pattern: the outer causer takes a= (fourth person, narrative first person) and the immediate causee-giver takes e= (2SG.O), while the recipient and the theme given have no index on the verb.

omarere cause someone to put X in Y provides a second documented
instance. The base oma be inside (a place) is a locative verb;
omare put X in Y (causative of oma) is its three-place causative;
omare-re is the double causative with four participants
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.2 .

The two-slot ceiling limits what can be cross-referenced in any four-place frame.
Of four participants, only the outer causer (A-slot) and the immediate causee
(O-slot) can be indexed by person affixes on the verb; the remaining objects must
be expressed as overt bare NPs. The asymmetry between a morphologically productive
stacking operation and its restricted expressibility in person marking explains the
marginal corpus status of double causatives. Bugaeva and Kobayashi observe that the
licensing of double-object frames and double causatives in Ainu is cross-linguistically
unusual, most languages blocking iterative morphological causativization
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.2 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Combinatorial limits and related morphology

The causative sits within a broader combinability system in which two applicatives,
two causatives, two incorporated objects, reflexive, and agreement indexes can
co-occur on a single verb — surveyed in valency-voice-overview
bugaeva2014 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . The two-slot
ceiling is the shared operational bound across all combinations
sato2023b ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §2 .

Kobayashi's argument-structure analysis is relevant background here. She separates
causative derivation — which always adds an agent-like participant, displacing the
original subject to O — from applicative derivation, which adds a non-agent participant
to the object set. The distinction predicts which slot a new participant targets and
clarifies when applicative and causative are alternatives for expanding the argument
frame kobayashi2015 33 ; kobayashi2020 . The non-causative
side is taken up in applicative-system-overview .

Takahashi (2017) reports that in Horobetsu the causative suffix acquires an additional
switch-reference function in clause linkage: in coordinate chains it marks a different
subject in the following clause; in subordinate chains it marks the same subject.
This function is not attested in descriptions of Saru or Chitose and does not bear
directly on causee marking, but it shows the causative suffix to be morphologically
multifunctional in at least one dialect
takahashi2017 102–106 ‹corpus-suggested›.

For the non-productive lexical causatives ( -V ,
-ke , -ka ) and their relationship to
transitivity pairs, see transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs ; for the arity
classes themselves, transitivity-and-valence-classes .

title: Citation vs Combining Stem Shapes and Support Vowels
summary: The systematic mismatch between a morpheme's citation (concept) shape and its bound combining shape — final-consonant support vowels, stem-final truncation, and consonant alternations exposed only under affixation or compounding.
part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Citation vs Combining Stem Shapes and Support Vowels
Part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
Summary: The systematic mismatch between a morpheme's citation (concept) shape and its bound combining shape — final-consonant support vowels, stem-final truncation, and consonant alternations exposed only under affixation or compounding.

Overview

In Hokkaido Ainu the shape a morpheme takes when listed in a dictionary — its
citation form — and the shape it takes when combined with an adjacent morpheme — its
combining form — frequently differ. The alternations are phonologically conditioned:
the coda of one morpheme interacts with the onset of the next to produce assimilation,
deletion, or consonant mutation. Four processes govern the principal alternations treated
here: (1) the behavior of coda /r/ at morpheme junctions and its vocalic release in
citation position; (2) the categorical palatalization of coda /t/ before a following onset
/i/; (3) vowel elision in the person prefixes ku= and
ci= before vowel-initial stems; and (4) the historical compound
assimilation visible in the elm-bark robe name
attus (underlying at-rus ).

The sandhi rules that propagate across word boundaries are described in
coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi ; the echo-vowel diagnostics for
distinguishing /r/-final from /rV/-final words are analyzed in rhotic-r ; glide
epenthesis at vowel hiatus is covered in glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution ; the
accent consequences of combining prefixes are treated in
accent-in-compounds-and-affixation . The affiliative suffix and the support vowels
it introduces — the /i/, /e/, /we/ alternants in possessed forms such as
hum-i , tek-e ,
ru-we — constitute a distinct citation/combining opposition routed to
concept-form-affiliative-form and
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Coda /r/: citation release and combining forms

The rhotic /r/ is phonologically exceptional because its citation (pre-pausal) realization
and its realization before a following morpheme diverge in two distinct ways. In citation
position, coda /r/ releases into a vocalic echo that copies the preceding nucleus:
kor [koɾ(o)], kur [kuɾ(u)],
kar [kaɾ(a)]. The echo-vowel carries no accent, does not feed the
nominalizer allomorphy, and disappears when /r/ resyllabifies before a following
vowel-initial morpheme
nakagawa2024 30 ; shiraishi2022 §3.2 . In combining position
before a consonant-initial morpheme, /r/ instead triggers regressive assimilation (r-sandhi):

Coda /r/ in combining position: principal assimilation outcomes

underlying
surface
rule
example

…r + t…
…tt…
/r/ → /t/ / _ /t/
a=kor tar → [aˈkottar] 'his possessions'

…r + n…
…nn…
/r/ → /n/ / _ /n/
a=kor nispa → [akonˈnispa] 'a wealthy man'

…r + c…
…tc…
/r/ → /t/ / _ /c/
retar cikap → [retatˈcikap] 'swan'

…r + s…
…ss…
/r/ → /s/ / _ /s/
ar suy → [asˈsuy] 'once'

…r + r…
…nr…
/r/ → /n/ / _ /r/ (dissimilation)
ku=kor rusuy → [kukonˈrusuy] 'I want it'

Sources: nakagawa2024 35–37 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1 ;
kindaichi1936 . The full assimilation paradigm and its domain conditions — including
the asymmetry between n-sandhi (bounded by the phrase) and r-sandhi (crossing phrasal and
sentence boundaries) — are treated in coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi .

Casual reduction of word-final /r/ and the koro/kor pair

In casual connected speech, word-final /r/ is susceptible to deletion, a reduction
distinct from the productive sandhi above. Attested reductions include
kur 'person' → [ku] and the combining stem
kor → [ko]
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-11) ‹corpus-suggested›. Standard
orthography notates underlying forms and does not write these reductions
nakagawa2024 37–38 .

The independent verb koro have, possess behaves differently from the
combining stem kor- . The /o/ of
koro is a genuine underlying segment, not an echo-vowel release,
as confirmed by nominalizer allomorphy: the /r/-final combining stem takes the C-allomorph
-pe ( kor-pe → 'what one has'), whereas a
genuine /rV/-final base would take the V-allomorph -p . The
diagnostics for separating /r/-final from /rV/-final words are fully described in
rhotic-r .

The t + i → ci rule at morpheme junctions

The palatalization rule /t/ → /c/ [ʨ] before a following onset /i/ is the one sandhi
alternation that applies categorically in all Hokkaido dialects without exception
nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1 ;
kindaichi1936 . Its formal statement:

t → c / _ i (at a morpheme junction, where /t/ is the coda of morpheme 1 and
/i/ is the onset of morpheme 2)

Resyllabification is prerequisite: the morphemic sequence …V.ti… resyllabifies to give …V.C,
placing the erstwhile coda /t/ in onset position before the /i/ syllable; the resulting /ti/
then palatalizes to /ci/ [ʨi]
kindaichi1936 ; shiraishi2022 §5.2 . The rule applies at compound
boundaries, derivational boundaries, and inflectional (possessive) boundaries alike.

t + i → ci: selected attested alternations

morpheme 1
morpheme 2
underlying
surface
gloss

mat 'woman'
ikor 'treasure'
mat-ikor
macikor
'women's treasure'

nit 'tooth'
-ihi (POSS suffix)
nit-ihi
nicihi
'his/her tooth'

rekut 'throat'
-i (POSS suffix)
rekut-i
rekuci
'his/her throat'

Sources: nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1 ;
kindaichi1936 .

The derivation of macikor from
mat woman + ikor treasure, goods illustrates the two-step
process:

Underlying: /mat-ikor/ — coda /t/ of
mat at the left edge of the /i/-initial morpheme
ikor

Resyllabification: /maˈtikor/ — coda /t/ moves to onset of the /i/ syllable,
giving the sequence /ti/

Palatalization: /ti/ → /ci/ [ʨi] → macikor

The compound is well attested in Saru narrative speech:

usa mat-ikor usa tamasay | various woman-treasure various bead.necklace | usa macikor usa tamasay | women's treasures and bead-necklaces | macikor is the surface form of the compound mat 'woman' + ikor 'treasure'; the coda /t/ of mat resyllabifies before the /i/-initial ikor and palatalizes to /c/. | biratori1969 | SAR | 川上まつ子, アイヌ語アーカイブ「ワシ神の化身と人間の娘 (1986)」[nam-archive/kawakami-minwa/09#465]

The rule is subject to one phonotactic constraint: if the /i/-initial morpheme begins a
closed syllable whose onset would create an impermissible three-consonant sequence, the
resyllabification is blocked and palatalization does not apply. See
syllable-template for the (C)V(C) canon and
glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution for the parallel blocking of glide weakening
before closed /i/-initial syllables. The possessive-boundary instances of the same rule are
documented in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Vowel elision in the combining forms of ku= and ci=

The first-person singular agent prefix ku= and the first-person plural
agent prefix ci= each have a reduced combining form before
vowel-initial stems. In the Saru, Chitose, and Mukawa dialects, the rule is:

/u/ → ∅ / ku= _ {a, e, u, o}
(the prefix vowel elides before a stem-initial /a/, /e/, /u/, or /o/;
parallel rule for ci=)

Before a stem-initial /i/, elision does not apply. Instead, the stem /i/ weakens to the
glide [y], giving the combining form ku=ype (from underlying ku=ipe) rather than *k=ipe
nakagawa2024 52 ; sato2008 notation p. xii ;
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Combining forms of ku= before vowel-initial stems (Saru/Chitose/Mukawa)

stem-initial vowel
rule
underlying
surface
gloss

/a/
ku= → k= (elision)
ku=oman
k=oman
'I go'

/e/
ku= → k= (elision)
ku=e
k=e
'I eat'

/o/
ku= → k= (elision)
ku=omare
k=omare
'I am sent'

/u/
ku= → k= (elision)
ku=uske
k=uske
'my place'

/i/
no elision; stem /i/ → [y]
ku=ipe
ku=ype [kúype]
'I eat a meal'

Sources: nakagawa2024 52–53 ; sato2008 notation ;
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28).

The block on elision before /i/ is phonotactic. If the prefix /u/ were deleted before a
closed syllable beginning in /i/ (e.g. ikra ), the result
*k=ikra would require a three-consonant onset, violating the (C)V(C) syllable canon.
Nakagawa (2024: 52–53) confirms the ungrammatical forms
* k=ipe and * ku=ykra and
* k=ikra alike, with only
ku=ikra grammatical before the closed /i/-initial stem.

k=oman | 1SG.S=go | I go / I am going. | Saru/Chitose combining form: ku= elides its /u/ before the /o/-initial stem oman. Underlying /ku=oman/ → surface k=oman.

ku=iunin | 1SG.S=ache | kuyunin | I ache. | Before the /i/-initial stem iunin, ku= retains its vowel; the stem-initial /i/ weakens to the glide [y], giving ku=yunin (Chitose; Nakagawa 2024: 40). *kuyyunin is ungrammatical because the glide is not an underlying phoneme.

The same elision process accounts historically for the pronoun kani I (pronoun) .
Community analysis traces kani to an underlying
ku=an-hi (first-person + existential root + nominal suffix), with the
prefix vowel /u/ elided before the /a/-initial stem — parallel to the reduction
ci=ep → cep 'fish'
aynucorporadiscord (gengojiro, 2024-02-05) ‹speculative›. On this account the
historical combining form and the present-day independent pronoun are related by the same
morphophonological rule, now lexicalized in kani .

The elision rule is restricted to southern Hokkaido (Saru, Chitose, Mukawa); most other
dialects retain ku= and ci= uncontracted
before all vowels nakagawa2024 52 . For the personal-affix accent
interactions triggered by prefix integration into the prosodic word, see
accent-in-compounds-and-affixation and
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction .

at-rus → attus: a compound assimilation

The elm-bark robe name attus (アットゥシ) derives from a compound
at-rus , with at designating the elm-bark cloth.
Two dialect reflexes are reported:

General Hokkaido: attus — the onset /r/ of
rus assimilates progressively to the preceding coda /t/ of
at , producing a geminate /tt/ and collapsing the /t.r/ cluster:
/at + rus/ → /at.tus/ → attus . The form
attus is corpus-confirmed (24 tokens) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Saru dialect: ahrus — a different outcome where
the coda /t/ of at weakens to /h/ rather than geminating, while
the /r/ is preserved. This Saru form is reported by the community and has not been
independently verified in a read primary source ‹speculative›.

aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-26). The progressive assimilation
/t + r/ → /tt/ producing attus is consistent with the cluster-
simplification isoglosses documented in eastern dialects by
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.3 (e.g. tupte →
tutte in Bihoro; hotke →
hokke 'lie down'). nakagawa2024 36 treats such
changes as historical word-form changes rather than synchronic alternations — a
classification that applies equally to the lexicalized attus . Broader
dialect consonant-assimilation correspondences are treated in
hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

hangan attus mi wa | magistrate attus.robe wear CONJ | The magistrate put on an attus robe and … | attus is the standard Hokkaido form of the compound at-rus; the /t + r/ cluster has assimilated to /tt/. Corpus count: 24 tokens. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 小松哲郎, Ainu Times vol. 12 (沙流・千歳) [ainu-times/012/2#5]

koré: two analyses of a lexicalized junction

The verb koré give (to someone) presents a case where the synchronic
combining-form analysis and a competing historical account remain unsettled.

Nakagawa's morphological analysis
nakagawa2024 53 : koré is the combining stem
kor 'have' plus the causative suffix -e ,
parallel to kay-e 'break (tr.)', tuy-e
'sell'. The surface accent koré (σ2 high) rather than
* kóre (σ1 high) follows from the iambic placement rule
reapplying to the whole as if monomorphemic once lexicalization has obscured the morpheme
boundary: /CV + CVC/ → σ2 accent. The same accent pattern holds for the parallel lexicalized
causatives treated in causative-morphological-re-e-te
shiraishi2022 §5.2 .

The community contraction account : koré is a
contraction of korpare , the ditransitive root 'give to sb' (corpus
frequency 153)
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-26). Both
koré and korpare are productive in attested
narrative speech; the accent koré (not
* kóre ) is agreed under both accounts. The corpus attests
koré (accent-marked) 30 times and kore
(unaccented, orthographic variant) 3120 times; the disparity reflects editorial marking
practice rather than speaker frequency ‹contested›.

The two analyses make different predictions about the combining system:
Nakagawa's account treats kor-e as a still-productive, if now
lexicalized, causative combination; the contraction account treats
koré as an opaque form derived from
korpare by phonological reduction, making the
kor + suffix boundary morphologically inert in this lexical item.
Neither is settled by the accessible sources.

The affiliative suffix as a support-vowel paradigm

A further class of citation/combining opposition involves the suffix vowel that consonant-final
nouns receive when they take the possessed (affiliative) form:
hum → hum-i 'his/her sound',
haw → haw-e 'his/her voice',
ru → ru-we 'the trace of —',
tek → tek-e 'his/her hand'. The vowel that
appears after the root consonant is conditioned by the root's final vowel, following
co-occurrence restrictions that are not simple spreading
shiraishi2022 §4.5 ; nakagawa2024 226–230 . This constitutes
a systematic citation/combining opposition — the citation form is the bare noun, the
combining (possessed) form adds the suffix vowel — and is treated in
concept-form-affiliative-form and affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

An additional morphologically restricted /h/-insertion between a vowel-final root and a
following possessive vowel — ona-a → ona-h-a
'his father', siki-i → siki-h-i 'his eyes'
(Kindaichi & Chiri 1936: 14, reported via shiraishi2022 n. 8 ) — is also
treated in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Summary of combining-form alternations

The table below collects the principal citation/combining alternations documented in this
chapter. For each rule, the underlying and surface forms are given; chapters treating the
full paradigm are indicated.

Combining-form alternations in Hokkaido Ainu: summary

rule
environment

mat-ikor
macikor

ku= + stem-initial {a, e, u, o}

§ citation-vs-combining-stem-shapes prefix-elision above

title: Clause Linkage in Ainu: Typology and Inventory
summary: Framing chapter mapping Ainu clause combining onto the coordination–cosubordination–subordination continuum and laying out the full inventory of clause-linking morphemes.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Clause Linkage in Ainu: Typology and Inventory
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: Framing chapter mapping Ainu clause combining onto the coordination–cosubordination–subordination continuum and laying out the full inventory of clause-linking morphemes.

Finite clauses joined by post-predicate particles

Hokkaido Ainu builds complex sentences by chaining fully finite clauses. Each linked predicate
keeps the ordinary verbal morphology of its own clause — person indexing, polarity, plural stem
choice, aspectual auxiliaries, and any clause-final evidential or nominalizer — and the link
between clauses is carried by a separate post-predicate particle such as
wa , hine , akusu ,
kor , kusu , or yakun . There
is no finite/non-finite split in the verb and no converb series: the Ainu verb does not oppose a
sentence-final form to a special linking form, so a Japanese rendering in
〜て gives no warrant for a converb analysis nakagawa2024 514 . The typological profile of the language states the same fact directly — Ainu has no
participles, converbs, or other standard non-finite forms bugaeva2022 . The
connectives are therefore particles that join finite clauses, glossed here as
conj and not as verbal suffixes.

a=yupi-hi i=resu hine oka=an pe ne hike | 4.POSS=elder.brother-POSS 4.O=raise CONJ exist.PL=4.S NMLZ COP CONJ | My elder brother raised me, and we lived on, and … | Each clause around hine is finite: i=resu carries its own fourth-person object index, oka=an its fourth-person S; pe ne closes the chain on a formal-noun predicate before the further connective hike. | nakagawa2024:346 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 634

The particle is looser than a verbal suffix in prosody as well as in structure. Nakagawa,
reporting Tamura, notes that a speaker may close the first clause with full sentence-final
intonation, pause, and only then produce the connective and the clause that follows it; on this
account the connective belongs to neither of the clauses it joins nakagawa2024 514 . Refsing records the same delayed continuation for Shizunai, where a pause may fall between
the verb expression and the conjunctionalizer refsing1986 238 . This autonomy
feeds a genuine disagreement over constituency: Nakagawa declines to posit a clause
( 節 ) unit or a main-versus-subordinate division for Ainu, holding that the
apparatus cannot be carried over from Japanese or English unchanged, while Refsing and the
Handbook describe the same surface domain with terms such as conjunctionalizer, adverbial-clause
marker, and subordinate clause nakagawa2024 510, 514 ; refsing1986 238 ; bugaeva2022 ‹contested›.

Two word-classes do the linking work, and keeping them apart resolves most of the descriptive
clutter. The post-predicate conjunctive particle ( 接続助詞 ) is bound to the
predicate it closes; a clause-initial connective adverb such as orowa(no)
‘and then’ or rapok(ke) ‘meanwhile’ is free and can stack after a
conjunctive particle. Refsing makes this the organizing cut of her chapter, between bound
conjunctionalizers and free conjunctions; Nakagawa keeps only the particles as a
助詞 class and folds the free connectives into the adverb phrase refsing1986 238 ; nakagawa2024 516 .

conjunctive particle ( 接続助詞 )
connective adverb ( 副詞句 )

position
bound, follows the predicate it closes
free, clause-initial

can begin a sentence
normally no
yes

examples
wa, hine, kor, no, akusu, kusu, yakun, korka, yakka, sekor
orowa(no), ora, rapok(ke)

relation to a host
requires the verb phrase it modifies
needs no host; may stack after a particle

Several of the particles are segmentally identical to material from other classes, and the
grammar resolves the clash by word-class and position rather than by form.
Wa is the sequential linker, the ablative case particle, and a
sentence-final emphatic; kor is a linker and the transitive verb ‘have’;
kusu is a causal/purpose linker, a free ‘therefore’, and part of the
intention auxiliary kusu ne ; no is a linker and
the adverb-forming suffix -no nakagawa2024 340, 353 ; ijas2023 .

Inventory and routing

Nakagawa organizes the Saru-based conjunctive particles into seven semantic groups — temporal
succession, unfolding event, condition, adversative, concession, cause/purpose, and
conjecture/manner — together with a parallel-listing use nakagawa2024 337–356 .
Refsing's Shizunai grammar reaches a near-identical seven-way taxonomy, with local realignments:
Shizunai uses kane for the simultaneity that Saru marks with
kor , and konno for an inevitable-consequence
conditional where Saru again uses kor refsing1986 238–250 .
The detailed treatment of each domain belongs to the chapters routed below; this chapter sets out
the system and the boundaries between them.

domain
main Hokkaido linkers
core function
detail chapter

sequential and integrated chaining
wa , ayne
sequence, manner, result-state, cause; protracted action reaching a result
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining

neutral chaining and discovery
hine , akusu , awa
ordered events; ‘did X and then found Y’
hine-akusu-switch-reference

simultaneity
kor , dialectal kane
overlapping events, the progressive, the habitual conditional
simultaneous-kor-clauses

condition, concession, adversative
yakun , yak , ciki ,
yakka , korka
hypothetical and factual premises, permission, counter-expectation
conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses

cause and purpose
kusu , wa kusu ,
hi kusu
reason, cause, aim; future and intention in kusu ne
causal-and-purpose-clauses

manner, state, degree, inference
no , pekor , noyne ,
kotom
state, negated manner, simile, evidential inference, degree
manner-and-degree-adverbial-clauses

reported speech and naming
sekor , dialectal ari / ani
direct quotation, sound and name frames; indirect complement markers
quotative-complementation-sekor

narrative cohesion
orowa , connective p / hike , tail-head repetition
recapitulation, new episode, discourse continuity
tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion

Whole-corpus word-form counts confirm that these items carry the descriptive load, with the
homonym caveat kept in view. In the aligned Hokkaido corpus wa (≈50,600),
kor (≈29,300), and kusu (≈16,000) are among the
commonest words, but those totals fold in the ablative and final-particle
wa , the verb kor ‘have’, and the future
kusu ne , and so overstate the connective. The cleaner items, where few
homonyms intrude, still show the shape of the system ‹corpus-suggested›:

particle
corpus count
primary function

hine 14,368 neutral clause-chaining

yakka 7,536 concession ‘even if’

sekor 6,991 quotative

yakun 4,516 hypothetical conditional

akusu 3,861 sequential discovery

korka 3,700 adversative

hike 3,159 loose link / adversative

awa 2,071 sequential discovery (verse)

The coordination–subordination continuum

The inventory spans a continuum rather than a clean coordinate/subordinate dichotomy. At one
edge, hine enumerates events in temporal order and takes no stance on how
they are related; at the other, the complementizing uses of sekor ,
kuni , kunak , yak , and
kusu place an entire clause under a verb of speech, thought, or command
nakagawa2024 338, 470–479, 507–513 . Most linkage falls between the poles: the
medial clause stays finite and keeps its own argument indexes while the chain is read as one
larger episode, the configuration that the coordination–subordination typology of foleyvanvalin1984 treats as cosubordination.

micipa ku=kar hine ohaw or k=omare | honewort 1SG.A=gather CONJ soup place 1SG.A=put.in | I gathered honewort and put it into the soup. | A plain hine sequence: two finite predicates, each with first-person A indexing, listed in order with no claim of dependence. | sato2008:44 | SAR

Wa binds more tightly. In Tamura's characterization, as transmitted by
Nakagawa, wa gathers two situations into one coherent event; Nakagawa
divides its readings into result-state, manner or method, and cause-and-effect nakagawa2024 339–342 . Satō gives the same three-way description and supplies the diagnostic that separates Ainu
wa from a same-subject converb: the two clauses it joins may have
different subjects, which a Japanese 〜て clause cannot sato2008 45–46 .

hekaci suma osura wa ontaro hokus | child stone throw CONJ barrel fall.over | The child threw a stone, and the barrel fell over. | A different-subject wa chain: the thrower is the subject of the first clause, the barrel of the second. | sato2008:45 | SAR

Akusu and awa sit at a third point: a realized
first event is followed by a discovered, witnessed, or newly unfolding second one, with the
causal connection left open. Nakagawa treats the two as semantically equivalent and conditioned
by register and dialect — Saru prose favours akusu , Saru verse and
several other varieties favour awa nakagawa2024 345 . Satō
describes the domain as factual condition and ties awa to oral literature
sato2008 47–48 .

kanpi ku=sanke ru-we ne akusu itasa kanpi ek ru-we ne | letter 1SG.A=send.out track-POSS COP CONJ in.return letter come track-POSS COP | I sent a letter, and a reply came. | Akusu attaches after a ruwe ne evidential tail: the first clause is already closed as an established fact before the discovery linker is added. | sato2008:47; sato2009:52 | CHI

Reference tracking without switch-reference morphology

Hokkaido Ainu tracks participants across a chain through the obligatory person indexes on each
finite verb and through discourse expectation; it has no same-subject/different-subject converb
morphology bugaeva2022 . The connectives carry only statistical tendencies. Refsing
finds that the predicates joined by wa share a subject in about ninety
percent of her Shizunai material, yet Satō shows the preference is not a constraint, since
wa tolerates different subjects refsing1986 240 ; sato2008 46 . The result is a same-subject-preferring chainer with no dedicated morphology.

The no / wa choice is derived from the first
predicate rather than from coreference. Nakagawa and Satō tie no to the
aspect and polarity of that predicate: stative predicates and negated action predicates take
no , with the high-frequency reading ‘without doing X’ under negation nakagawa2024 340–341 ; sato2008 45–47 . Ijäs offers the learner's heuristic that
no marks a same-subject negated clause and wa a
different-subject one; that framing is hers, and the primary grammars condition the choice on
polarity and aspect, not on subject identity ‹contested› ijas2023 .

ku=caranke ka somo ki no ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=complain even NEG do CONJ 1SG.S=return | I went home without complaining. | No follows a negated action predicate; both clauses share the first-person subject, so the choice of no over wa here tracks polarity, not a change of subject. | sato2008:44 | SAR

The nearest approach to a switch-reference device is discourse-structural. The Handbook's
descriptive grammar reports that the sequence of a perfective a auxiliary
and a connective such as korka , wa , or connective
p tends to fall where the scene, topic, or actor of a narrative changes,
the perfective marking a situation as completed and cut off from the present bugaeva2022 ‹speculative›. The exact author and page of that passage have not been confirmed against the
print edition, and the device is a clause-chaining boundary marker, not grammaticalized
switch-reference. Whether the sequential connectives hine and
akusu encode subject continuity in a switch-reference-like opposition or
instead encode discourse (un)expectedness is the central open question of the domain ‹contested›;
it is adjudicated against a corpus count of subject continuity in hine-akusu-switch-reference , and the reference-tracking toolkit as a whole is drawn together in reference-tracking .

Condition, concession, cause, and the mood of the matrix clause

The conditional system is organized by realis status and, distinctively, by the sentence type of
the matrix clause. Satō's dedicated study finds that Ainu conditionals are mood-constrained but
do not privilege the epistemic mood the way Japanese conditionals do: the language has no
conditional restricted to truth-judgement, yet it has conditionals that require an imperative or
interrogative matrix sato2009 53–54 . Yakun is the broadest
member, attested with declarative, interrogative, imperative, and volitional or conjectural
matrices, and so it bears most of the translator's load across Japanese
レバ , タラ , and ナラ sato2009 51–53 ; nakagawa2024 347 . Yak attaches to law-like or warning
premises and clusters with an evaluative pirka ‘good’ or
wen ‘bad’; ciki and conditional
kor are the mood-bound members, ciki introducing a
premise for a command or question and kor the general-causation
conditional that elsewhere requires a wh-interrogative matrix sato2009 50–51 ;
nakagawa2024 349–351 .

e=ype rusuy haw-e ne yakun tan kaboca e | 2SG.S=eat.a.meal DES voice-POSS COP CONJ this pumpkin eat | If you are saying that you are hungry, eat this pumpkin. | The protasis closes on the reportative tail hawe ne before yakun; the matrix is an imperative, one of the several moods yakun freely admits. | sato2009:52 | CHI | Satō ex. 28

Concession and counter-expectation sit alongside the conditionals. Yakka
‘even if, even though’ states that the matrix holds whatever the event of the marked clause, and
in the frames yakka pirka / yakka wen it grants or
withholds permission; korka ‘but’, transparently kor when
plus ka even , marks counter-expectation nakagawa2024 351–352 ;
sato2008 53 . Ijäs banks the same set for the learner, and her listing
construction illustrates the concessive cleanly:

apto as yakka upas as yakka wakka poro yakka sonno k=ek kusne wa | rain fall CONJ snow fall CONJ water be.big CONJ truly 1SG.S=come intend FIN | Even if it rains, even if it snows, even if there is a flood, I will truly come. | A teaching example built on a triple yakka listing; constructed, not corpus-attested. The future kusu ne appears here in its reduced one-word form kusne. | ijas2023

Cause and purpose are centred on kusu . Plain
kusu gives the purpose of the following event, especially with motion
verbs, or its reason; wa kusu and hi kusu are
reason-only forms that present the first event as actual nakagawa2024 352–353 ;
sato2008 53–54 ; refsing1986 251–252 . The same segmental material
enters the intention auxiliary kusu ne , the copula taking no person index,
so the detail chapter separates clause-linking kusu from future and
intention syntax causal-and-purpose-clauses .

sisam itak ani ku=respa p ne kusu aynu itak sinep ka erampewtek pa | Japanese language INS 1SG.A=raise NMLZ COP CONJ Ainu language one even not.understand PL | Because I raised them in Japanese, they do not understand even one word of Ainu. | Kusu marks the preceding formal-noun predicate p ne as the cause of the following clause; the matrix verb erampewtek takes the plural pa. | nakagawa2024:353 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 672

Interfaces with evidentiality, complementation, and reported speech

Conjunctive particles attach outside the clause-final evidential frame. A clause may close on a
nominalizer-plus-copula evidential such as ruwe ne or
hawe ne and then take a linker, giving attested orders
ruwe ne akusu , hawe ne yakun , and
hawe ne kusu ; the evidential sits above clause-internal aspect but below
the connective, so kusu ne ruwe ne is well formed while
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not nakagawa2024 393 . The general
order for a linked clause is thus predicate – evidential – connective. The inferential value is
treated in ruwe-ne-inferential and the system as a whole in evidential-schema-overview . Satō notes one cross-linguistically marked interaction at this seam:
akusu can follow a ruwe ne tail, where the Japanese
factual と / たら rejects a preceding
のだ sato2009 52–53 .

Several connectives are formal nouns pressed into linking service, and the same set runs a
grammaticalization cline. A formal noun can be a relative-clause head that fills an argument gap,
a complement marker that nominalizes a saturated clause, or a connective that is neither subject
nor object of the following clause; Nakagawa distinguishes the three by the argument structure
around the marker, and places connective p , pe ,
and hike ‘but, and’ at the connective end of the cline nakagawa2024 507–516 . This links clause combining to nominalization, treated in lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization and nominalization-p-pe .

Sekor is the bridge into reported speech. In Saru and Chitose it frames a
direct quote with a verb of speaking or thinking, names entities in the pattern
X sekor a=ye ‘call it X’, and frames cries and mimetic sound, its history
transparent in se make a sound, say ‘say’ plus kor while ‘while’
nakagawa2024 470 ; ijas2023 . It is also the one connective that
triggers person reassignment: a third party's quoted first person is expressed by fourth-person
forms while the narrator's own quoted speech keeps the first person, the mechanism behind the
fourth-person voice of oral narrative, which Bugaeva reanalyses as logophoricity nakagawa2024 470–471 ; bugaeva2008 40–44 . The interaction belongs with person-marking-architecture and is developed in quotative-complementation-sekor .

tap a=ye katu Piratur sekor a=ye haw-e ne | now 4.A=say way Piratur QUOT 4.A=say voice-POSS COP | The way it is said now is to call it Piratur. | Sekor frames the quoted name; the fourth-person a=ye and the reportative tail hawe ne close the naming frame. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 778, after Kawamura 1985: 30

Dialect and comparative edges

The inventory is dialectally uneven, with Saru and Chitose the best-described baseline in
Nakagawa and Satō and Shizunai a full reference grammar in Refsing. Shizunai realigns two cells:
kane marks the simultaneity that Saru assigns to
kor , and konno the conditional/consequential
function refsing1986 248–250 ; nakagawa2024 342, 347–348 .

a=kor katkemat utar an=oskur kane kesto an konno oka=an | 4.A=have lady PL 4.A=cherish CONJ every.day exist CONJ exist.PL=4.S | Cherishing our ladies, we lived on day after day. | Shizunai kane marks the simultaneity Saru would mark with kor, and konno the recurrent-time conditional; both differ from the Saru forms. | nakagawa2024:342 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 617; Shizunai, after Okuda

Tek is the clearest micro-debate. It is not a connective in Saru or
Chitose, where it is an auxiliary, yet in most other dialects it links clauses with an
‘immediately upon X’ sense. Nakagawa's synthesis is geographical: western dialects near Saru
carry the short-duration restriction, while eastern descriptions dispute whether short duration
belongs to the meaning at all, and one analysis makes its core function parallel listing nakagawa2024 343–344 ‹contested›. A related open question is whether the bare verb-plus-verb sequences of Tokachi,
Ishikari, and Shiranuka — where Saru requires an intervening connective — constitute a serial
verb construction nakagawa2024 518–521 ‹contested›. Sakhalin supplies a contrast
at the edge of the system, where sa kusu covers
the continuation and result-state range that Hokkaido splits between
kor and wa , rather than marking cause or purpose
nakagawa2024 344 .

Open points

The foundational descriptive source for the whole domain, Tamura's Saru study of the connective
particles, could be reached only through Nakagawa and Refsing; the classic characterization of
wa as fusing two events into one, and the formulation that the connective
belongs to neither clause, both rest on that intermediated witness ‹speculative›. The Handbook's
claims on negation asymmetry and on the perfective a scene-switch device
await verification against the print edition bugaeva2022 ‹speculative›. The
quantitative base is also thin: the available corpus figures are homonym-mixed word-form counts,
and the per-function rates that would settle how often connective wa
differs from the ablative, or how far hine and
akusu correlate with subject continuity, belong to hine-akusu-switch-reference and tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion ‹corpus-suggested›.

title: Cleft, Pseudocleft, and Nominalization-Based Focus
summary: Identificational and predicate-focus constructions built on nominalization plus the copula (…p ne, …hi ne) and their relation to the evidential schema.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Cleft, Pseudocleft, and Nominalization-Based Focus
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: Identificational and predicate-focus constructions built on nominalization plus the copula (…p ne, …hi ne) and their relation to the evidential schema.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu has no morphological focus affix and no dedicated cleft particle. The work of
marking focus, asserting propositions, and identifying referents falls to the same
nominalization-plus-copula architecture that underlies the evidential system. A finite clause
is nominalized by a light formal noun — p / pe ,
hi , or one of the four evidential heads ruwe ,
hawe , siri , humi —
and the result is predicated with the copula ne
nakagawa2024 390–392, 503–509 ; sato2008 134–135, 174–180 .
The copula predicates the nominalized proposition as an asserted or identified fact, and
the choice of formal noun and of the post-nominal predicate ( ne ,
an , or bare) encodes illocutionary force — declarative, content
question, or polar question (§5 below).

This chapter treats the information-structural function of three overlapping constructions:
(a) the p / pe and
hi nominalization cleft, which throws assertive or identificational
focus onto a participant or proposition; (b) the naming cleft
sekor a=ye p/hawe/katu ne , which identifies or labels a referent;
and (c) the evidential frame as an assertion-focus device, with cross-references to the
evidential chapters. Focus particles ( patek only ,
ka also, even ) and the kakari-musubi emphatic concord are surveyed in §7
with a pointer to topic-marking-anakne , where their full treatment appears.
The structural mechanics of p / hi as
nominalizers are in nominalization-p-pe and
formal-defective-nouns ; the copula is treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

The p/pe and hi nominalization cleft

The minimal frame is a person-marked VP followed by the nominalizer
p thing, one, fact (nominalizer) (after a vowel) or
pe thing, one, fact (nominalizer) (after a consonant), and then the
copula ne be, copula . The nominalizer has the arity of a formal noun
(−1 on Nakagawa's arity scale), so it saturates a gap left by one participant in the
embedded clause — producing a relative-clause reading ('the one who …', 'the thing that
…') — or nominalizes a whole saturated clause — producing a complement reading ('the
fact that …') nakagawa2024 261, 503–509 ; sato2008 174–180 .
When the result is predicated with ne , the copular sentence frames
the nominalized content as an assertable fact and throws focus onto either the predicate
nominal or the proposition as a whole sato2008 174–177 .

Ijäs's lesson on adnominal clauses presents the same pre-head gap strategy
without treating the copular focus function explicitly
ijas2023 . The arity principle that distinguishes relative-clause from
complement uses is stated precisely only in nakagawa2024 503–507
and sato2008 174–175 , and is the lens through which the focus function
is understood here.

Argument focus and predicate focus

Two focus readings arise depending on whether a participant gap remains in the embedded VP.
In the argument-focus reading the nominalizer heads a relative clause: the embedded
VP lacks one of its required participants, and the focused element — supplied as the
predicate nominal of the copula — identifies that participant. A minimal constructed
illustration:

kani ku=e pe ne | 1SG 1SG.A=eat NMLZ COP | I am the one who ate it. | The agent of ku=e 'I eat' provides the focused argument: the question implied is 'who ate it?' and kani 'I' is the copular predicate supplying the answer. The nominalizer pe leaves the VP with an unfilled object slot (arity +1 after the agent is indexed by ku=), creating the relative-clause gap that the focused predicate nominal fills. | SAR

In the predicate-focus reading the embedded VP is fully saturated — the fourth
person a= indexes the generic agent — and the nominalizer
nominalizes the whole proposition; the predicate nominal then supplies a property or
category asserted of the nominalized event:

cep a=e pe ne | fish 4.A=eat NMLZ COP | What one eats is fish. | Here the embedded clause a=e 'one eats (it)' is transitively saturated by the fourth-person agent a= and an implicit third-person object. The nominalizer pe then heads the NP 'the thing one eats', which is predicated as identical to cep 'fish': predicate-nominal focus. The same structure allows a proposition to be asserted as established fact when no explicit contrastive focus is intended. | SAR

The abstract nominalizer hi fact, place, time (nominalizer) works in the
same slot but is more abstract: Satō glosses it as 'こと / の / 時' and notes that it
"has almost no concrete meaning and nominalizes the whole clause"
sato2008 174, 169 . The distinction between
p / pe and
hi is one of concreteness: p / pe identifies a person, thing, or event; hi identifies a fact,
time, or circumstance. Both combine with ne for declarative force
and with an for content-question force (§5).

When the topical item is itself a nominalized clause, the topic particle
anak as for, topic can mark it, producing a construction that combines
topic and focus in a single sentence. Satō's example of contrastive
anak illustrates this interaction:

ku=nu hi anak k=eramuan pe ne korka tasa itak anak k=eranpewtek | 1SG.A=hear NMLZ TOP 1SG.A=understand NMLZ COP CONJ reply speech TOP 1SG.A=not.know | The question I heard I understand, but how to reply I do not know. | Both topic-marked NPs are nominalized clauses: hi nominalizes the VP ku=nu 'I heard (it)' and pe ne predicates the resulting proposition as understood fact. The second clause, tasa itak anak k=eranpewtek, contrasts it with the topic 'the reply', which lacks an explicit pe ne because the bare VP k=eranpewtek closes the sentence. The two anak forms are contrastive, not merely thematic; see topic-marking-anakne for the anak system. | sato2008:62 | SAR

The naming cleft: sekor a=ye … ne

The naming cleft identifies a referent by the name or label applied to it. Its standard
formula is [label] sekor a=ye [formal-noun] ne , 'the [word/fact]
of calling [something] [label] is'. The quotative complementizer
sekor quotative introduces the quoted name (see
quotative-complementation-sekor ), and a formal noun — most commonly
p / pe , or the voice-noun
hawe 'word of', or the manner-noun
katu 'way of calling' — nominalizes the whole
sekor a=ye clause. The copula
ne then predicates it:

tap a=ye katu Piratur sekor a=ye hawe ne | now 4.A=say manner-POSS Piratur QUOT 4.A=say voice-POSS COP | The current way of calling it is called Piratur. | The formal noun katu 'manner of' (affiliative of kat) nominalizes the VP tap a=ye 'one currently says/calls it', forming the topic 'the current manner of calling it'. The naming cleft Piratur sekor a=ye hawe ne then predicates that the word (hawe, affiliative of haw 'voice') of saying it is 'Piratur'. The choice of hawe over p highlights the auditory/linguistic character of the name. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 778, after Kawamura 1985: 30

The variant with p produces the most common naming formula —
[name] sekor a=ye p ne — which the community has discussed for its
valency structure: the nominalizer p here is
analysed as zero-valent (not filling an object slot in
a=ye ), because the transitive verb
ye (+2) is already saturated by the fourth-person agent
a= (−1) and the implicit zero third-person object (−1), leaving
p to nominalize the whole proposition rather than fill a
participant gap aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09) ‹contested›.
This analysis aligns with Nakagawa's arity account: the embedded clause is saturated
(arity 0), so p functions as a complement nominalizer rather than
a relative-clause head nakagawa2024 507–509 .

The logophoric narration that pervades yukar oral literature produces naming-cleft structures
in contexts where the fourth-person a= references the narrator
voicing a protagonist (see indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse ). In such passages
sekor a=ye p ne and its variant with
hawe are a stock device for supplying proper names and category
labels within the narrative nakagawa2024 392 .

True cleft, pseudocleft, or nominal predication?

Whether [VP] p/hi ne should be called a cleft, a pseudocleft, or
a nominal predication depends on two analytical choices that remain unresolved in the
literature: the structural status of the copula ne , and the
source-language content of "cleft" as a cross-linguistic category.

The nominal-predication position, implicit in Nakagawa's arity account, treats
[VP] p ne as a copular sentence with the nominalized NP
[VP] p as one argument and the predicate nominal as the other.
Nothing biclausal is required; the embedded VP is a finite clause that happens to be
nominalized, and ne is the ordinary copula
nakagawa2024 503–509 . On this reading the construction is formally
parallel to any other copular sentence — 'the thing one eats is fish' is no different in
type from 'this thing is a fish' — and calling it a cleft imports a typological category
from languages with a structurally distinct cleft construction.

The biclausal cleft position holds that the embedded clause bears the full
person and TAM marking of a main clause, and the copula is a full predicate verb; the
resulting structure is therefore biclausal — a main copular clause whose subject is
a nominalized subordinate clause. This is the reading Shibatani adopts when he calls
-p(e) and -i "nominationalizers" and
treats the construction as "appositive nominalization"
shibatani1990 83–84 . The embedded clause retains full finiteness (person
marking, number-sensitive verb choice, aspect, negation), which Bugaeva treats as the
defining typological feature of Ainu nominalization: because there is no finite/non-finite
morphological contrast, every embedded VP looks like a main clause, and nominalization is
always formally overt, never zero bugaeva2016 100, 222–225 .

Neither analysis settles whether these are "true" clefts in the narrow sense of a
bi-clausal structure with an empty-headed relative clause as one component. In many
typological frameworks a cleft requires a relativized pivot; in
[VP] p ne the nominalizer p may head
a relative clause (when the embedded VP is unsaturated) or a complement clause (when
it is saturated). On Nakagawa's arity principle, the choice is grammatically determined
case by case, so some tokens of the surface pattern are structurally clefts and others
are nominal predications ‹contested›. Satō's diagnostic — a nominalizer nominalizes a
complete clause; a formal noun heads a relative clause with a recoverable antecedent —
attempts to sort the cases but concedes the boundary is blurry
sato2008 174–175 .

Whether or not the label "cleft" applies, the IS function is consistent: the copular frame
[nominalized-clause] ne focuses the predicate nominal and
foregrounds the nominalized content as a recoverable, asserted fact ‹consensus›. The
debate over the structural label does not affect the description of that function. The
copula ne and the question of its verbal status are treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals ; the analysis of the embedded clause's
finiteness and constituency bears on the unresolved questions there.

Illocutionary force: the ne/an/∅ alternation

The tightest structural fact about the formal-noun system is that the copula slot
following the formal noun determines the illocutionary type of the whole clause. Satō
and Nakagawa document a four-way paradigm shared by all formal nouns
sato2008 136–137 ; nakagawa2024 392–396 :

sentence type
formal-noun tail
example

declarative (asserted fact)
NMLZ + ne

sirpirka ruwe ne 'it is that the weather is fine'

content (wh) question
NMLZ + an

ruwe an hi an 'what is the reason that …?'

polar (yes/no) question
NMLZ bare (copula omitted)

e=eraman hawe? 'did you understand?'

affirmative answer
NMLZ + un

ku=nu ruwe un 'yes, I heard it'

Satō's motivation for the three-way split is precise: the declarative
ne predicates the subject's nature or identity; the
content-question an asserts that the described entity
exists while its nature is the open question; and when even existence is at
issue, the copula drops entirely for the polar question
sato2008 136–137 . The content-question frame obligatorily uses
the existential an : the frame
*ruwe ne with a wh-word in the same clause is ill-formed; the
wh-word forces the switch to an . This is a rare case in Ainu
where the choice of formal-noun copula is not freely pragmatic but grammatically
determined by the presence of an interrogative pro-form (see
interrogative-strategies-question-particles ).

hemanta ne kusu ene siran hi an | why COP because thus appear NMLZ exist | Why has the situation come to be like this? | The wh-word hemanta ('why/for what reason') forces the existential an in place of the copula ne after the formal noun hi. The structure hemanta ne kusu is 'why/for what reason', and ene siran 'appearing thus' is the embedded VP. Content questions with a formal-noun tail systematically use NMLZ an (not NMLZ ne). | nakagawa2024:411 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 856

The same paradigm extends to p / pe :
a content question uses p an (with a wh-word) and the polar
question uses bare p or pe , while
pe ne ya? with the question final particle
ya is also attested for content questions
nakagawa2024 409–410 . The full range of interrogative strategies,
including the polar-question particle
he question particle (focus-scoping) and its interaction with the bare
formal-noun frame, is in interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

The evidential frame as focus and assertion

The four evidential formal nouns ruwe ,
hawe , siri , and
humi build exactly the same nominalization-plus-copula frame,
and they carry a corresponding IS contribution: they frame the embedded proposition as
established, speaker-endorsed fact. Nakagawa explicitly treats these formal nouns as
complementizers of the same type as p / hi
nakagawa2024 508 .

The IS function of each evidential frame differs by the nature of the speaker's epistemic
access. ruwe ne , the most common and most grammaticalized of the
four (comprising roughly 86% of evidential tokens in the Hokkaido corpus;
ruwe-ne-inferential ), presents the content as a known or inferred fact —
'it is the trace/fact that …' — and serves as the workhorse of narrative assertion and
scene-boundary marking. Community discussion confirms that
ruwe ne does scene-shift and summary (総括) work, is particularly
frequent in folktales and legends, and is used notably more by male speakers in oral
literature than by female aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09)
‹corpus-suggested›. Hirosawa connects this to its information-structural role as a
marker of discourse-new content within the speaker's territory of information
hirosawa2026 75–81 .

The full semantics, the male-register skew (a register fact of the evidential system,
distinct from the gendered sentence-final particles treated in
gendered-register-speech ; compare sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force ),
and the interaction with mirativity are described in the evidential chapters:
evidential-schema-overview , ruwe-ne-inferential ,
hawe-ne-reportative , siri-ne-situational ,
humi-ne-sensory .

One cross-cutting point that bears on this chapter: the ordering constraint that places
the evidential frame below connectives and final particles
( kusu ne ruwe ne ✓, *ruwe ne kusu ne ✗)
nakagawa2024 393 is a syntactic reflex of the same nominalization
architecture — the evidential NP is itself an argument position in the clause-linkage
hierarchy, not a free adjunct. Community discussion confirms this ordering
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Focus particles and kakari-musubi concord

Hokkaido Ainu marks delimiting and scalar focus with a class of adverbial particles
(副助詞) that add interpretive nuance to a following verb phrase, noun, or adverb
without changing its grammatical category
nakagawa2024 317–330 ; sato2008 63–70 ;
fukuda1961 . The core focus particles —
patek only, nothing but , ka also, even ,
hem also (additive) — are described with their full distributional and
discourse properties in topic-marking-anakne . The present section notes only
the points where they interact with the nominalization-based focus system.

patek 'only/nothing but' marks exhaustive focus. It attaches
after a VP with the support of dummy ki 'do' to re-verbalize
the construction for downstream connectives
nakagawa2024 317, 327–328 ; fukuda1961 .
The community notes that patek is an adverb and cannot itself
be nominalized as 'the only one of —' (unlike Japanese だけ, which derives from a noun);
the rising modern pattern patek ani instead of traditional
ani patek is attributed to Japanese-contact influence
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-16) ‹contested›.

Closer to a grammaticalized focus-concord is the kakari-musubi (係り結び)
system first identified by Chiri and described for Saru by Fukuda
fukuda1961 : a small set of emphatic particles
concords with a specific sentence-final particle at the clause end. Nakagawa and Satō
document the two productive Saru pairings
nakagawa2024 330 ; sato2008 73 :

emphatic particle
gloss
concordant SFP
reading

ta
'こそ' (emotional emphasis)
oka (optional)
counterfactual wish '〜だったらなあ'

tas(i)
'まさに〜こそ' (assured emphasis)
nek (optional)
emphatic assertion '〜のだよ'

The closing particle is optional in Saru — "not necessarily present"
fukuda1961 — which complicates calling this a true productive
focus-concord on the model of Japanese kakari-musubi. Whether the HA system constitutes
a grammaticalized construction or a set of frozen collocations is open
nakagawa2024 330 ; sato2008 73 ‹contested›.

cikap ta ku=ne | bird EMPH 1SG.S=COP | Would that I were a bird! | The emphatic particle ta 'こそ' scopes over the copular predication ku=ne 'I am/become', expressing a counterfactual wish. The concordant SFP oka may follow: cikap ta ku=ne oka! The pair is paired in the oral text with rera ta ku=ne 'would that I were the wind!', a traditional couplet form. | nakagawa2024:330 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 560, after Tamura 1988b: 33

newaanpe patek tasi a=eranak nek | that FOC EMPH 4.A=worry FIN | That alone is precisely what worries me. | Two focus particles stack: patek 'only/nothing but' restricts the domain, and tasi 'precisely' adds the emphatic-concord trigger. The concordant final particle nek closes the clause in the asserted-emphasis reading '〜のだよ'. The combination patek … tasi … nek expresses both exhaustive focus (nothing but that) and assured assertion (and that is precisely so). | nakagawa2024:337 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 594, after Chiba University 2015: 160

Satō also documents a more specialized kakari-musubi-like construction in which the
concept-form nouns ru 'trace', hum
'sound', sir 'appearance', or haw
'voice' precede the emphatic particle konna / kan , and the clause ends with a verb in the suffix -natara ,
-atki , or -rototke
sato2008 65–66 . This construction is
restricted to oral literature and its grammatical status is unclear; crucially, the
nouns before konna appear in their concept form
( ru , not ruwe ), unlike their use as
evidential formal nouns, a distribution that Satō flags as unexplained ‹contested›.

title: Assimilation and Cluster Simplification: Coda /r/, Nasals, and Clusters
summary: Regressive assimilation of coda /r/ (the most active sandhi trigger) and place assimilation of coda nasals, together with heterosyllabic cluster simplification, degemination, and juncture gemination across morpheme, compound, and word boundaries.
part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Assimilation and Cluster Simplification: Coda /r/, Nasals, and Clusters
Part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
Summary: Regressive assimilation of coda /r/ (the most active sandhi trigger) and place assimilation of coda nasals, together with heterosyllabic cluster simplification, degemination, and juncture gemination across morpheme, compound, and word boundaries.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu has two intersecting regressive assimilation systems operating across morpheme,
compound, and word junctures: coda /r/-sandhi and coda /n/-sandhi . In
/r/-sandhi, the coda /r/ of one morpheme is deleted and the following consonant geminates;
/r/ is thereby the most morphophonologically productive segment in the language
nakagawa2024 35–38 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1 ;
kindaichi1936 . Independently, the coda nasal /n/ undergoes two distinct
processes: it assimilates in place of articulation to a following labial or velar
(an allophonic rule), and before following continuants /s/, /y/, and /w/ it triggers a
morphophonological change — /n/-sandhi proper — which is more restricted in domain than
/r/-sandhi nakagawa2024 30, 36–37 ; shiraishi2022 §3, §6.1 .
A third, dialect-specific set of cluster simplifications is attested in eastern varieties,
most prominently Bihoro, but Nakagawa (2024: 36) treats these as historically fixed rather
than productive nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.3 .

All rules described here are regressive: the coda segment adapts to the following onset.
None are notated in the standard romanization orthography, which writes underlying forms
( ran wa , not ram ma ); both sandhi types are
nonetheless fully productive in speech nakagawa2024 37–38 . The phoneme
/r/ and its coda release are described in rhotic-r ; glide insertion at vowel
hiatus is treated in glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution ; the interaction of
these rules with personal-affix clitics is treated in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction .

Coda /r/-sandhi

When a /r/-final morpheme immediately precedes a consonant-initial morpheme, the coda /r/ is
deleted and the following consonant is lengthened, yielding a surface geminate. The rule
applies word-internally, at compound boundaries, and across word boundaries; it is not
blocked by pauses within an utterance, and it crosses sentence boundaries without
restriction (§4.1 below) nakagawa2024 35–37 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1 .

Shiraishi (2022: §6.1), drawing on Shiraishi (2001), characterizes the trigger consonants as
the coronal class — /n/, /c/, /t/, /r/ — with /s/ and /y/ also attested as triggers in
northern dialects (Chiri 1956: 343, cited in shiraishi2022 §6.1 ). The
assimilation can thus be stated as:

/r/ → C / _C[+coronal, ≠r]: the coda rhotic is deleted before a following coronal onset,
which then geminates.

nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1

/r/ + /n/

nakagawa2024 35 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1

/r/ + /s/

nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1

/r/ + /y/ (Chitose)

[jj]

/r/ + /r/ (dissimilation)

nakagawa2024 35

Dissimilation: /r/ before /r/

The sequence /r/+/r/ does not geminate to [rr] but dissimilates: the coda /r/ surfaces
as [n] before a following rhotic onset. Nakagawa (2024: 35) identifies
ku=kor rusuy 'I want it' → [kukonrusuy] as the only published
Hokkaido example of this dissimilation. The complementary statement alongside the
assimilation rule is:

/r/ → n / _r (dissimilation — avoidance of adjacent rhotics at syllable margins).

This dissimilatory outcome is the single exception within the /r/-sandhi system; all other
coronal triggers yield total assimilation.

Derivation walkthroughs

The derivations below move from underlying (orthographic) representations to surface
phonetic forms. Boundaries: = marks clitic attachment, space marks word boundary.

/r/-sandhi: underlying to surface

/r/ + /n/ → [nn]

ku=kor nispa | 1SG.A=have wealthy.person | [kukonnispa] | a wealthy man (of one's) | Productive /r/+/n/ → [nn]: the coda /r/ of kor assimilates to the following /n/ of nispa, yielding a geminate [nn] at the word boundary. Standard orthography writes ku=kor nispa.

ku=kor tar | 1SG.A=have bag | [kukottar] | a bag (that one has) | Productive /r/+/t/ → [tt]: the coda /r/ of kor assimilates to the following /t/ of tar, yielding [tt]. Standard orthography writes ku=kor tar.

Coda-nasal place assimilation

The coda nasal /n/ assimilates in place of articulation to a following labial or velar
consonant: it surfaces as [m] before /p/ and /m/, and as [ŋ] before /k/. These are
allophonic realizations fully predictable from the following context. The coda /m/ does not
undergo velar assimilation before /k/ and remains [m] in that environment
nakagawa2024 30 ; shiraishi2022 §3 .

rule
formal statement
example (underlying)
surface
gloss
source

an=pe
[ampe]
'true thing'
nakagawa2024 30

velar assimilation

hon=kor

'be pregnant'

aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-16), citing Tamura (1996)
‹corpus-confirmed›

/m/ — no velar assimilation
/m/ → [m] / _k
okamkir
[okamkir]
(verbal form)

aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹corpus-suggested›

The orthography consistently writes /n/ in all environments
( an=pe , hon=kor ,
hanku ), never the allophonic surface values [m] or [ŋ]. Nakagawa
(2024: 30) and Shiraishi (2022: §3) both treat [m], [n], [ŋ] as allophonic realizations of
the two phonemes /m/ and /n/, with /n/ conditioned by the following consonant and /m/
invariant.

Whether /n/ and /m/ are phonemically neutralized before labials is a point of orthographic
debate. Forms such as matanpus ~ matampus
are written with /n/ or /m/ before /p/ depending on the source; no consensus across
dictionaries and authors has been established ‹contested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-05). The loanword
kampi 'paper' (from Japanese kami ) raises the
same issue: an etymological kanpi versus a phonetically
assimilated kampi
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-16) ‹contested›.

Coda /n/-sandhi

Before the [+continuant] consonants /s/, /y/, and /w/, a coda /n/ does not assimilate in
place but is replaced by a glide or deleted: before /s/ and /y/ it surfaces as [j]
(romanized y ), yielding an apparent geminate with the following continuant; before
/w/ the nasal is deleted and only /w/ remains. Shiraishi (2022: §6.1), following Shiraishi
(2001), characterizes the trigger class as the continuants and describes n-sandhi as the
spread of the feature [+continuant] from the following onset onto the preceding coda. The
formal statement is:

/n/ → [j] / _{s, y}; /n/ → ∅ / _w

underlying sequence
surface
example (underlying)
surface form
gloss
source

/n/ + /s/
[js] → ys
pon seta
[poy seta]
'puppy' (small dog)
nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1

/n/ + /s/
[js] → ys
wen sisam
[wey sisam]
'bad Japanese person'
nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1

/n/ + /y/
[jj] → yy
pon yuk
[poy yuk]
'small deer'
nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1

/n/ + /y/
[jj] → yy
wen yupi
[wey yupi]
'bad elder brother'
nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1

/n/ + /w/
[w] (/n/ deleted)
yayan wakka
[yayaw wakka]
'pure water'
shiraishi2022 §6.1

The coda /m/ does not undergo this rule. The form
homsu (from hom-su ) surfaces as [homsu],
not *[hoysu], confirming that n-sandhi targets /n/ specifically and not the broader nasal
class; /m/ and /n/ feed different morphophonological processes before continuants
‹corpus-suggested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-03-06). The /n/+/s/ output [js] — written
as y in poy seta — behaves phonologically as a coda /y/
in the resulting syllable.

Domain asymmetry: /r/-sandhi and /n/-sandhi differ in boundary sensitivity

/r/-sandhi and /n/-sandhi share a regressive direction and a similar output structure
(gemination or deletion), but they differ fundamentally in the phonological domain over
which they apply. Shiraishi (2022: §6.2), reporting on Shiraishi (2001), places /n/-sandhi
within the prosodic phrase: it applies phrase-internally but never crosses a sentence
boundary. /r/-sandhi carries no such restriction — it is a pure phonological rule that
applies across all junctures including sentence boundaries.

The community independently observed this asymmetry: "r-連声は文境界を跨げるが n-連声は跨げない"
(r-liaison crosses sentence boundaries; n-liaison does not) ‹corpus-confirmed›
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024-11-23). The same contributor notes
an account of n-sandhi's locality: because n-sandhi is mediated by nasalization in the
coda, the sentence boundary disrupts the conditioning context, making the restriction
predictable from the mechanism. The formulation in Shiraishi (2022: §6.2) frames this
as a distinction between a P-structure (phrase-internal) rule and a pure phonological
(domain-free) rule.

The practical consequence is that inter-phrase /n/ sequences may or may not undergo
sandhi depending on whether a phrase boundary intervenes, while /r/ sequences at any
juncture assimilate in fluent speech regardless. Neither alternation appears in the
standard romanization nakagawa2024 37–38 .

Lexicalized sandhi outputs

Several Hokkaido Ainu lexical items preserve a sandhi output as their current lexical form,
the underlying source either obsolete or reconstructed.

The best-documented case is attus elm-bark robe . The proto-form is
reconstructed as at-rus ; the Saru dialect attests an intermediate
form ahrus (reflecting lenition rather than full assimilation); the
general Hokkaido form attus reflects the lexicalization of
/r/+/s/ → [ss] sandhi at the compound boundary
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-26) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The word
attus is attested 24 times in the corpus across multiple narrators
and collections (小松哲郎, 神崎雅好, 相原典明, アイヌタイムズ; 平賀さだも, 平取町アイヌ口承文芸),
confirming that the geminated form is the current lexical norm throughout Hokkaido.

A second piece of evidence for the productivity of /r/+/t/ → [tt] comes from a VITS-based
Ainu TTS system: the model independently surfaced the sequence
mosir ta 'land' + locative as [mositta] in generated speech,
apparently learning the assimilation from training data without explicit phonological rules
‹corpus-suggested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2026-05-20). This anecdotal ML observation does
not add to the primary-source evidence for the rule — established in
nakagawa2024 36 and shiraishi2022 §6.1 — but illustrates
how pervasive /r/-sandhi is in connected-speech corpora.

Among other compound-boundary cases, ar suy 'once more' surfaces as
[assuy] via /r/+/s/ → [ss]; retar cikap 'swan' surfaces as
[retat̚cikap] via /r/+/c/ → [t̚c]. The compound-internal
t+i → ci rule — illustrated by
mat-ikor → macikor — is the one sandhi rule
that applies without exception in all Hokkaido dialects
nakagawa2024 36 and is treated in rhotic-r .

Additional lexicalized forms noted by the community include
ketketcep (reconstructed from * ket~ket-se-pe )
and nisatcawot (from nisat-sawot ), both
showing /t/+/s/ → [tc] assimilation at compound junctures ‹speculative›
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2025-11-03).

Bihoro and eastern-dialect cluster simplification

Nakagawa & Fukazawa (2022: §3.3) document cluster simplifications in the Bihoro dialect
— and to varying degrees in Obihiro, Kushiro, Asahikawa, and Nayoro — that go beyond the
productive sandhi rules described above. In these varieties, specific heterorganic sequences
of two obstruents, or an obstruent plus fricative, are simplified to a geminate in specific
lexical items.

Nakagawa (2024: 36) treats these as historical word-form changes rather than synchronic
productive rules. The diagnostic is substitution-productivity: a Kushiro speaker who produces
hokke for 'lie down' does not produce
* pek kari for pet kari 'the river did' in
spontaneous speech, showing that the /tk/ → [kk] simplification is lexically specific and
not a live rule generating [kk] from all /tk/ sequences
nakagawa2024 36 . The community endorses this analysis: the changes are
word-specific in Bihoro and may reflect a proto-phoneme issue rather than a productive
synchronic rule ‹contested›
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024-11-17, citing Nakagawa & Fukazawa
2022: §3.3). The /rs/ → [ss] Bihoro cases are phonologically parallel to productive
/r/+/s/ → [ss] sandhi in §2, but speakers do not generalize the alternation to new /rs/
sequences. The broader dialect typology is treated in
hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

Rule inventory and domain summary

The productive rules of this chapter are collected below. Formal statements follow the
notation X → Y / Z (X undergoes change to Y in environment Z).

/r/-sandhi (assimilation)
