shiraishi2022 §6.1–6.2

The principal structural asymmetry in the system — /r/-sandhi is domain-free, /n/-sandhi is
phrase-bounded — is formulated by Shiraishi (2022: §6.2) as the distinction between a pure
phonological rule and a P-structure rule. Neither is written in the standard romanization;
both apply in connected speech nakagawa2024 37–38 .

These rules interact with the personal-affix system ( ku= before
vowel-initial stems; en= assimilation), treated in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction , and with the
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology possessed-form morphophonology of nouns.
The syllable structure that sets coda position is described in
syllable-template , and the consonant inventory from which all triggering
segments are drawn is described in consonant-inventory .

title: Concept Form vs. Affiliative (Possessed) Form
summary: The functional opposition between the bare concept form (概念形) and the affiliative/possessed form (所属形), and the rule that inalienable nouns are obligatorily affiliative when possessed.
part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Concept Form vs. Affiliative (Possessed) Form
Part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
Summary: The functional opposition between the bare concept form (概念形) and the affiliative/possessed form (所属形), and the rule that inalienable nouns are obligatorily affiliative when possessed.

Overview: two forms of the common noun

Every common noun in Hokkaido Ainu potentially has two citation shapes: a
concept form (概念形) expressing the noun as a free-standing concept, and an
affiliative form (所属形) expressing it as belonging to something or someone
nakagawa2024 217 . The concept form is the stem; the affiliative is a
derivative, produced by a suffix on that stem sato2008 152 . Earlier
scholarship described the contrast under the labels 抽象形 (abstract
form) and 具体形 (concrete form) kindaichi1936 ;
Nakagawa's own earlier work used 人称形 (personal form) for the
possessed shape nakagawa2024 217 .

The alternation underlies Hokkaido Ainu's bifurcated possession system. Inalienable nouns
— body parts, kinship terms, part–whole pairs, and related classes — take the affiliative
form when possessed and combine directly with a possessive person-prefix. Alienable nouns
remain in the concept form as the implicit object of the transitive verb
kor have nakagawa2024 218 ; sato2008 150–151 .
The alienable/inalienable split itself is set out in alienable-inalienable-split ;
the morphophonology of the affiliative suffix — the echo-vowel mechanism and suffix classes
— belongs to affiliative-suffix-morphophonology ; the full double-marking NP
(person-prefix plus affiliative suffix) is treated in
adnominal-possession-double-marking ; and kinship-term idiosyncrasies appear in
kinship-honorific-possession .

Formation of the affiliative form

The affiliative suffix attaches to the noun stem. Its shape is conditioned by the final
segment of the stem nakagawa2024 226–228 ; sato2008 152–153 .
Three types are recognised, each with a short and a long variant.

Type ① — vowel-final stems. The short affiliative is identical to the
concept form; the long affiliative adds -hV₁ where V₁ repeats the
stem's final vowel: sapa head → sapa /
sapa-ha .

Type ② — stems in -y or -w. The suffix is -e(-he) :
haw voice → haw-e(-he) ;
patoy lip → patoy-e(-he) .

Type ③ — other consonant-final stems. The basic (Type ③-1) suffix is
-i(-hi) : sik eye → sik-i(-hi) .
Lexically listed subsets take an echo vowel matching the stem's last vowel (Type ③-2):
tek hand → tek-e(-he) ;
kisar ear → kisar-a(-ha) . A small group with final
a takes -u(-hu) (Type ③-3):
nan face → nan-u(-hu) . Types ③-2 and ③-3 must be
memorised individually; Satō notes that the exceptions are numerous and must be learned
one by one sato2008 153 .

Type
Concept form
Short affiliative
Long affiliative
Gloss

sapa

head

① (vowel-final)

nose

② (-w coda)

voice

② (-y coda)
patoy

lip

③-1 (basic)
sik

sik-i-hi
eye

③-1
otop

otop-i-hi
hair

③-2 (lexical echo)
tek

tek-e-he
hand

③-2
kisar
kisar-a

ear

③-3 (V₁=a → -uhu)
nan
nan-u
nan-u-hu
face

Monosyllabic open stems ending in i or u behave as Type ② rather than ③,
taking -e(-he) :
ru track, path → ru-we(-he)
nakagawa2024 229–230 . The affiliative ruwe of
ru is the same form that serves as the evidential formal noun,
and the possessed-noun origin underlies all four evidential nominalizers treated in
ruwe-ne-inferential nakagawa2024 258–259 .

When a consonant-final noun appears as the second element of a compound, it reverts to the
③-1 pattern regardless of its isolated affiliative class:
at cord → at-u(-hu) (③-2) in isolation, but
tar-at → tar-ac-i(-hi) 'carrying-rope cord' inside a compound
nakagawa2024 228 . The distinction between an affiliative-suffixed noun in an
NP and a phonologically similar compound link is discussed in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Short and long affiliative forms

For all three types, the suffix has a short and a long variant. For consonant-final stems,
the short form ends in the echo vowel alone ( teke ) and the long form
adds a further syllable ( tekehe ). The two variants carry no
semantic difference; Satō writes that "the difference in meaning seems absent — how they
are distributed remains unclear" sato2008 152–153 ; Nakagawa confirms
that the split follows prosodic and rhythmic preference rather than grammar
nakagawa2024 219 . The long form is more frequent in oral-narrative verse
nakagawa2022pos 484 .

The possessive person-prefix system

When a common noun appears in the affiliative construction, it combines with a possessive
person-prefix drawn from the transitive-subject (A) affix set
nakagawa2024 215–216 ; sato2008 154 ;
bugaeva2021poss 52 . The possessor is thus grammatically encoded as the
agent-like argument of the noun. Satō captures the counterintuitive direction with a literal
gloss: e=paro 'your mouth' parses as 'you [A] mouth', the
possessor marked with the same form that marks a transitive subject on a verb
sato2008 150 .

Person
Prefix
With sapa 'head' (Type ①)
With sik 'eye' (Type ③-1)

ci=sapa(ha)

eci=
eci=sapa(ha)
eci=siki(hi)

4/INDEF
a=
a=sapa(ha)
a=siki(hi)

3 (sg/pl)
Ø
sapa(ha)
siki(hi)

Before vowel-initial stems, ku= reduces to
k= and ci= to c= ;
accent falls on the syllable following the prefix for ku= and
e= (e.g., ku-sápa ) but not for
a= or eci=
sato2008 154 . The complete person-prefix system across all argument roles
— intransitive-subject, transitive-subject, and object — is documented in
person-marking-architecture . Relational (locative) nouns such as
or place, interior and sam vicinity take the
objective (O) affix set rather than the A-set; their possessed forms are treated
in relational-and-spatial-nouns .

ku=kema-ha ka arka ku=teke-he ka arka | 1SG.POSS=leg-POSS also hurt 1SG.POSS=hand-POSS also hurt | My leg hurts too; my hand hurts too. | Both body-part nouns take the 1SG possessive prefix ku= and the affiliative suffix. The intransitive predicate arka takes each body-part personal form as its subject; the verb itself carries no person-marking (third-person zero on the body-part NP). | utari1994 | HK | Akor Itak textbook, ch. 5, no. 8

Semantic classes of affiliative nouns

Nakagawa identifies five groupings of nouns that enter the affiliative construction when
possessed nakagawa2024 219–221 :

Body parts and habitually worn items. The canonical inalienable class:
sik eye , tek hand , kema leg, foot , tears,
nasal mucus, and so on. Clothing and footwear normally worn on the body, and even
commonly carried tools, are treated grammatically like body parts:
ku=tesma-ha 'my snowshoes',
a=tasiro-ho 'my mountain-knife'.

Part–whole relations. The part belongs inherently to the whole:
sayo rur-i(-hi) 'the broth of the gruel';
nit op nic-i(-hi) 'the shaft of a spear'.

Material-product relations. The output belongs to its source:
ikaray ohaw-e(-he) 'nettle stew';
top cise(-he) 'bamboo house'.

Kinship terms — a large subset whose allocation between the affiliative
and the kor have construction varies by term and dialect; see
kinship-honorific-possession .

Miscellaneous inalienables whose belonging is inherent or relational:
atay price, value , hura smell, scent ,
tokuy close friend , asur rumour ,
kotan village (variable; see below).

The classification is grammatically fixed: the noun's class membership determines the
construction, not a speaker's inference about the nature of the possession
bugaeva2021poss 47 . A sword or dog is experientially "owned" but
grammatically non-possessible and must take kor have . The contrast is
sharpest in minimal pairs. Satō's diagnostic example — ku=kor
kani 'my money' (alienable; *ku=kanihi is ungrammatical)
versus ku=teke-he 'my hand' (inalienable) — shows that the split
cannot be inferred from semantics alone sato2008 150–151 .

Two nouns sit on the boundary. kotan village takes both constructions
roughly equivalently: a=kor kotan ~
a=kotanu(hu) . mosir world, land is restricted
to the concept construction in prose ( a=kor mosir ;
*a=mosirihi unattested in prose, though
mosiri appears in verse)
nakagawa2024 220–221 . cise house is dialect-split: in
Saru, Chitose, and Horobetsu prose the affiliative cisehe is not
used for 'X's house', but verse and some other dialects allow it, and the verse-compound
uncise 'home' is always affiliative
( a=uncise , never *kor uncise )
nakagawa2024 220–221 . Cultural construal can also shift the construction
for the same referent: a trap-bow ( ku bow (trap-bow) ) is usually alienable
( ku=kor ku ), but when the bow belongs existentially to whoever the
prey chooses, the same object takes the affiliative kuwehe
nakagawa2024 229 . The inalienable/alienable line is therefore
construal-sensitive for certain nouns, not fixed by the referent alone ‹contested›.

Community member thegodofneet (aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-09-17) proposed that
utari(-hi) is the affiliative of
utar family, companions, kin , with the suffixed form narrowing the sense
to 'X's companions/kin', on the grounds that companions belonging to no one are
conceptually difficult to realise and the form consistently presupposes a possessor
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. The corpus count for
utari is 1,501 tokens. The analysis extends — rather than
contradicts — Nakagawa's observation that k=utari 'my family'
is attested alongside the concept form nakagawa2024 251–252 ; the
semantic narrowing under affiliation is the community's addition.

ku=teke-he piro hi ta ku=kor totto noya ham uk wa | 1SG.POSS=hand-POSS be.wounded NMLZ LOC 1SG.A=have mother wormwood leaf take CONJ | When my hand was wounded, my mother would take wormwood leaves and … | The body-part noun tekehe takes the 1SG possessive prefix (affiliative construction); the kinship term totto 'mother/breast' takes ku=kor (alienable construction). The two constructions co-occur within one sentence. | ainukoraci1991 | CHI | Yokoyama Hiroyuki, Ainu Times no. 24

Obligatory possession and the non-possessed-noun form

Body parts and kinship terms are obligatorily possessed : they almost never
occur without a possessive prefix and affiliative suffix in natural speech
bugaeva2021poss 52–54 . When elicited in isolation — and in riddles,
where no specific possessor is named — speakers supply the fourth-person (indefinite)
prefix a= : a=sik-i-hi 'eyes (one's/
people's eyes)', not the bare stem sik
bugaeva2021poss 53 . These nouns function as bound stems in the sense
defined in nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns .

When an obligatorily-possessed noun is incorporated into a verb and the possessor is
non-coreferential with any core argument, the derivational prefix
i- antipassive / unspecified-possessor prefix encodes the unspecified
possessor: i-sapa-kar usi 'a barber shop' (lit. 'a place where
one does someone's head') bugaeva2021poss 53–54 . This is the
non-possessed-noun (NPN) marker, distinct from the antipassive
i- that demotes the transitive object; the two homophonous
prefixes are treated together in antipassive-detransitive-i . The
fourth-person prefix a= (which retains affiliative morphology
on the free noun) and the NPN i- (which enables possessor-less
incorporation) are two distinct de-specifiers
bugaeva2021poss 54, 81 .

The personal form and its syntax

The combination of person-prefix plus affiliative-marked noun constitutes what Satō calls
the personal form (人称形): the form the noun takes when it is related to a
particular possessor sato2008 155 . Because the possessor is indexed on
the noun itself, the personal form functions as an autonomous NP. The third-person form
siki(-hi) alone means 'his/her/their eye(s)' without any overt
possessor pronoun — unlike Japanese 目 , which requires
彼の to express the same content
sato2008 155 .

A consequence of this indexical autonomy is that the possessor NP and the possessed noun
form a looser constituent than a Japanese genitive phrase. Satō demonstrates that they
can be separated by particles and intervening words, with the personal form presupposing
only that some possessor NP is accessible in the discourse context
sato2008 155–156 . Bugaeva's formulation is parallel: the possessive
suffix "registers a possessor but does not index its properties", and "the possessor NP
can easily be omitted without ambiguity as the possessed suffix signals its presence"
bugaeva2021poss 52 . Stacking of personal forms — where the possessor of
one is itself a personal form — is structurally transparent, as the third-person (zero)
prefix on the inner noun is sufficient to license retrieval of the outer personal form as
its possessor.

makan uske ta a=teke-he ani makan uske ta | somehow place LOC 4.A=hand-POSS INS somehow place LOC | Somehow [the elder brother] took hold of my hand and … | In oral-narrative convention the fourth-person prefix a= identifies the narrator as the possessor of tekehe 'hand'. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 11, ILCAA Ainu materials (aa-irc/013), sent. 11

a=yup-ihi tures-ihi | 4.A=elder.brother-POSS younger.sister-POSS | that elder brother's younger sister | Nakagawa ex. 259, Saru. Two kinship personal forms stack: a=yupihi 'my/the elder brother' (4th-person A-prefix, Type ③-1 long affiliative -ihi) and turesihi 'his younger sister' (zero 3rd-person prefix, same affiliative class). The second personal form presupposes the first as its possessor via the zero prefix. | nakagawa2024:224 | SAR

The affiliative constraint: specificity, grammar, and non-possessive uses

Tamura identified the trigger for the affiliative as the specificity of the possessor N₁:
kamuy rusihi 'that specific bear's pelt' (affiliative) versus
kamuy rus 'bear-pelt in general' (concept form, approaching a
compound) nakagawa2024 231 . Satō challenged this with the counter-example
aynu opitta honihi kor wa okay pe ne 'all humans have a belly'
( honi-hi affiliative; possessor 'all humans' = non-specific)
sato2008 155–156 ‹contested›.

Nakagawa argues that the constraint is grammatical rather than semantic: the affiliative
appears wherever a person-prefix or a relative-clause structure demands it, regardless of
the possessor's specificity nakagawa2024 227, 230–233 . Three classes
of evidence bear on this.

General-warning contexts.
a=teke munin pe ne na 'your hand will rot' (a warning about
touching rainbows) uses the affiliative teke with the
fourth-person prefix even though no specific person is addressed; the prefix alone
forces the affiliative nakagawa2024 231 .

Relative-clause structure. 'A dog with a long tail' requires
sara-ha tanne seta (affiliative sara(ha) ),
not *sar tanne seta , even with an indefinite dog: the NP
structure in which the dog possesses its tail forces the affiliative on
sar nakagawa2024 231–232 .

Quantifier and pro-form triggers. The quantifier
usa 'various' and the pro-form nep
'what, whatever' independently induce the affiliative:
usa kamihi usa cepihi 'meat and fish of various kinds'
(Chitose); nep aynuhu e=ne 'whatever human you are'
( aynuhu = affiliative of aynu )
nakagawa2024 232–233 .

Nakagawa concludes that the received claim — that only some nouns possess an affiliative
form — is overstated: given the right relational structure, potentially any common noun
can take one nakagawa2024 227, 230 ‹contested›. The phonological
boundary between a suffixed affiliative and a bare compound second element is discussed
in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology . The possessed form also feeds
noun incorporation as discussed in noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding .

Community member nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-12-03) parsed
iteki e=hawe aste 'don't raise your voice' as
[iteki [[e=hawe] aste]] , with
e=hawe (2SG possessive prefix + Type ② affiliative of
haw 'voice') serving as a plain possessed-noun argument of
aste 'raise' aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›.
The analysis is consistent with the attested non-evidential, lexical use of
hawehe in the example below.

nekon ne wa a=yup-ihi an=sa-ha hawe-he ka isam ruwe ene an hi an | why COP CONJ 4.A=elder.brother-POSS 4.A=elder.sister-POSS voice-POSS even be.absent track-POSS thus exist NMLZ exist | How is it that neither my elder brother's nor elder sister's voice is to be heard? | hawehe is the long affiliative of haw 'voice' (Type ②: haw-e + -he); it serves here as the subject of the negative existential isam. Both kinship personal forms yupihi and saha carry affiliative suffixes stacked in the same clause. an= is a Shizunai dialectal variant of the 4th-person A-prefix a=. The clause-final ruwe ene an hi an is an indirect evidential frame; the trace-noun ruwe here is the same affiliative of ru discussed in the Formation section above. | ilcaa1976 | SHI | Oda Suteno, uwepeker 'Moyuk (part 1)', ILCAA Ainu materials (aa-irc/017), sent. 52

title: Conditional, Temporal, and Concessive Clauses
summary: The yak(un)/ciki conditionals, their temporal 'when' uses and realis/irrealis split, and the morphologically related concessives yakka 'even if' and korka 'although'.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Conditional, Temporal, and Concessive Clauses
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The yak(un)/ciki conditionals, their temporal 'when' uses and realis/irrealis split, and the morphologically related concessives yakka 'even if' and korka 'although'.

The conditional, concessive, and adversative subsystem

Hokkaido Ainu marks hypothetical, temporal, and concessive relationships between clauses
through post-predicate particles attached after the full finite predicate of the antecedent
clause. Each linked predicate is fully finite — it carries its own person indexing, polarity,
aspect, and any clause-final evidential — and the particle follows, belonging to neither of
the two clauses it joins nakagawa2024 514 ; refsing1986 238 .
The principal members of the conditional–concessive subsystem are:
yakun (hypothetical conditional),
yak if, when (habitual or evaluative) and
yakne (evaluative and oral-literary conditionals),
ciki / cik (mood-restricted conditional),
kor in its conditional use (habitual or temporal),
yakka (concessive and permissive), and
korka (adversative).

Two dimensions organize the system: the realis status of the antecedent clause, and the
sentence type the particle requires or permits in the matrix clause. Satō's dedicated study of
Chitose conditional expressions finds that Ainu conditionals impose no restriction tied to
epistemic-judgement mood, yet they do impose restrictions linked to directive and interrogative
illocution — a distributional pattern not predicted by hierarchies of conditionality derived
from Japanese sato2009 53–54 .

These particles are conjunctive particles ( 接続助詞 ) in Nakagawa's
classification: post-predicate, bound forms distinct from the free connective adverbs such as
orowa 'and then' and rapokke 'meanwhile',
which can stack after them nakagawa2024 337, 347–352, 516 . The architecture
of the whole clause-linking inventory is set out in clause-linkage-overview ; cause
and purpose particles are treated in causal-and-purpose-clauses , and the
complementizer uses of kuni , yak , and
kusu in indirect speech belong to
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements .

yakun — the hypothetical conditional

Yakun is the broadest and most frequent of the conditional particles,
attested in Saru and Chitose with declarative, imperative, interrogative, and conjectural
matrix clauses alike; Satō's study finds no mood restriction on it
sato2009 51–53 ; nakagawa2024 347 . Its semantic scope covers
the hypothetical one-off conditional: the protasis states a premise and the matrix presents
its consequence. Japanese distributes comparable functions across three conditional forms
(レバ, タラ, ナラ); Ainu lays them largely on yakun , so a Japanese
rendering must disambiguate by context sato2009 53 .

The etymology is transparent: conditional yak if, when plus the
allative/attributive postposition un to, toward (allative) , a segmentation
reported for Shizunai by Refsing and supported by morpheme decomposition
refsing1986 256 . The particle can also head a sentence independently, with
the anaphoric reading 'in that case, then', a free-standing use that marks its partial
grammaticalization beyond the strictly bound conditional
nakagawa2024 347 ; ijas2023 .

Refsing notes a 'when'-nuance in Shizunai realis contexts, where
yakun attaches after an event already in progress or completed,
yielding something closer to 'when X happened, Y followed'
refsing1986 256 ‹corpus-suggested›.

sirkunne yakun suy ruyanpe an nankor | get.dark CONJ again storm exist probably | If it gets dark, there will probably be another storm. | yakun with a conjectural main clause (nankor 'probably'); Satō uses this Chitose example to illustrate the absence of mood restrictions on yakun. The particle is compatible with nankor here, with an imperative elsewhere (Satō 2009: 52 ex. 28), and with a declarative in general statements. | sato2008:51; sato2009:51 | CHI

The protasis of a yakun conditional may carry its own evidential tail
before the particle, since the verified ordering is [VP] – evidential – connective. The
sequence hawe ne yakun 'if (it is said that) X' presents the protasis
as reported hearsay before conditionalizing it; ruwe ne yakun presents
it as established fact nakagawa2024 393 . The interface with the evidential
system is discussed in the final section of this chapter.

yak and yakne — habitual and evaluative conditionals

Where yakun handles one-off hypotheticals,
yak marks a habitual or law-like premise whose consequence the
speaker evaluates. Its hallmark is a strong tendency to be followed by the evaluative
predicates pirka be good or wen be bad , or by a directive
expression; it carries a warning nuance when the evaluation is negative
nakagawa2024 349 ; refsing1986 256–257 ;
sato2008 50–51 .

The idiomatic frame yak pirka 'it would be good if one did X'
functions as mild advice or prescription ('one should X'); its negative counterpart
yak wen 'it would be bad if one did X' conveys prohibition or warning
('one must not X') nakagawa2024 349 . Both frames can be reinforced by
anak / anakne ,
easir 'only then', or tasi
refsing1986 256–257 . These obligation frames differ from the permissive
frames yakka pirka / yakka wen built on
the concessive particle, treated below.

a=e=siknure yak isitoma=an | 4.A=2SG.O=let.live CONJ be.frightening=4.S | Keeping you alive is frightening. | Nakagawa ex. 651. yak with an evaluative-stative matrix (isitoma 'be frightening / dangerous'); the indefinite 4th-person agent a= covers an unspecified subject ('one'), and =an marks 4th-person S on the matrix predicate. The Japanese rendering 'お前を生かしておくと恐ろしい' confirms the warning-evaluation reading. | nakagawa2024:349 | SAR

In the Ishikari dialect the sequence yak easir (conditional
yak plus easir 'only then') extends the
evaluative conditional into an explicit obligation construction — 'it is only fitting if
one does X' — moving from evaluation toward necessity ‹contested›; the analysis is proposed
in community discussion and awaits corroboration from a primary grammar of Ishikari
(nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord 2026).

Yakne functions similarly to yak but is
associated with prayer and oral-literary register;
yakne pirka is extremely rare and the form appears mainly in the
fixed phrase wa ne yakne (sequential
wa plus copula ne plus conditional
yakne ), concentrated in kamuy yukar and prayer texts
nakagawa2024 349 .

ciki/cik — the mood-restricted conditional

Ciki (Shizunai, Samani, and Tokachi cik )
presents a premise for which the speaker issues a command or asks a question; the particle
consequently requires an imperative or interrogative matrix clause and is ill-formed with a
declarative sato2009 50–51 ; nakagawa2024 350 ;
refsing1986 255 . This places ciki at the opposite
end of the mood-freedom scale from yakun . The stock formula opening
instructions and demonstrations illustrates the imperative use:

itak=an ciki e=nu katu ene an hi | speak=4.S CONJ 2SG.A=hear manner thus exist NMLZ | As I speak, listen in the following manner. | Nakagawa ex. 655. ciki closes the premise clause (itak=an 'one speaks / as I speak'), and the matrix is a nominalized directive — the standard formula for opening a speech, recipe, or demonstration. The imperative force is embedded in the nominalized ene an hi 'the manner in which (you are to) listen'. | nakagawa2024:350 | SAR

Refsing characterizes Shizunai cik as carrying an assumption: the
speaker takes the protasis to be true or imminent and issues a directive or question on
that basis, distinguishing it from the purely hypothetical
yakun refsing1986 255 . In the Horobetsu and
Chitose dialects, ciki overlaps in use with the
sequential-discovery particle akusu — it can mark factual discovery
('did X, and then found Y') — a temporal reading absent from the mood-restricted Saru form
nakagawa2024 350–351 ‹contested›.

The etymology of ciki is not settled by the primary grammars. A
community-proposed segmentation reads the particle as ci (a
continuative or plural auxiliary attested in most dialects outside Saru and Chitose) plus
ki 'do', making the conditional a lexicalized construction; the
primary sources treat the form as an unanalyzed particle ‹contested›
(nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord 2024). A diachronic connection between the
conditional ciki and the homophonous adverb
ciki 'now' — pointing to a grammaticalization path from 'now (if
…)' toward the conditional–admonitive — is advanced in community discussion but likewise
awaits support from primary sources ‹speculative›
(antitwilight, aynucorporadiscord 2024).

Conditional kor — temporal and habitual

The particle kor while; when; have carries two distinct conjunctive uses —
simultaneity ('〜しながら') and a habitual or general-causation conditional ('〜するたびに /
〜すると') — that are sometimes indistinguishable in context
nakagawa2024 340, 347 ; ijas2023 . The simultaneity use is
treated in simultaneous-kor-clauses ; the present section concerns the
conditional reading.

In the conditional use, kor encodes general causation or a
recurrent event: 'whenever X, Y'. This differs from yakun (one-off
hypothetical) in that the protasis describes something that holds repeatedly or as a
general law. Satō finds that conditional kor takes a declarative
matrix in general-causation statements; for a one-off individual event the particle is
restricted to wh-interrogative matrices sato2009 51 . The Shizunai
equivalents are konno / koonno , and those of
Horobetsu, Shiranuka, and Kushiro are ko , all serving the
conditional function that Saru assigns to kor
nakagawa2024 348 ; refsing1986 248–249 .

sironuman kor cikap utar iwak | get.dark CONJ bird ASS go.home | When(ever) it gets dark, the birds go home. | Satō's illustration of the habitual-conditional kor: darkness is a recurrent condition and the birds' return a recurrent response. The associative plural utar after cikap collects the birds as a group. A formal-noun tail pe ne frequently closes the matrix of such clauses to foreground the generalization. | sato2008:49 | SAR

A formal-noun tail pe ne / p ne frequently
closes the matrix of a kor conditional, yielding the reading 'it
is the sort of thing that happens when X' and underlining the habitual or general-truth
character of the pattern nakagawa2024 347 . This is an instance of the
nominalizer p functioning as a connective after a saturated clause;
the formal-noun continuum is treated in clause-linkage-overview .

The realis and mood-constraint split: a comparative table

Satō's study, based on Chitose data with consultant Shirasawa Nabe, produces the following
typological finding: Ainu conditionals are mood-constrained but not in the way Japanese
conditionals are. Japanese conditionals tend to be organized around epistemic-judgement mood;
Ainu conditionals impose no such restriction on yakun , yet they
restrict ciki to imperative or interrogative matrices and restrict
conditional kor to declarative (for general truths) or
wh-interrogative (for individual events) sato2009 53–54 . The table below
summarizes the five particles Satō compares; the factual conditionals
akusu and awa are included for
completeness and are treated in detail in hine-akusu-switch-reference .

particle
type
matrix mood
Japanese ≈
key source

yakun
hypothetical (one-off)
unrestricted — declarative, imperative, interrogative, conjectural
レバ / タラ / ナラ
sato2009 51–53

yak
habitual / evaluative

evaluation ( pirka / wen ) or directive

タラ
sato2009 51 ; nakagawa2024 349

ciki / cik
individual conditional
imperative or interrogative only
レバ
sato2009 50–51 ; nakagawa2024 350

kor (conditional)
habitual / general causation
declarative (general truth) or wh-interrogative
ト
sato2009 51 ; nakagawa2024 347

akusu / awa
factual / realis
— (discovery; realized antecedent)
ト / たら（事実）
sato2009 52 ; nakagawa2024 345

Satō frames the finding against Japanese typology: Japanese has no conditional restricted
to imperative or interrogative matrices, whereas Ainu has two
( ciki and conditional kor ); Japanese has
conditionals restricted to epistemic-judgement mood, while Ainu has none. Whether this
constitutes evidence against a universal conceptual hierarchy of conditionality is an open
question ‹contested› sato2009 53–54 .

yakka — concessive and permissive

Yakka 'even if, even though' is the concessive particle. Corpus
counts place it at roughly 7,500 tokens in the aligned Hokkaido corpus, the highest among
the conditional–concessive particles and a signal of its frequency in narrative and daily
speech alike nakagawa2024 352 . The etymology is transparent: conditional
yak if, when plus scalar ka even, also , literally 'even if',
the same analysis given for Shizunai by Refsing refsing1986 254 . Dialect
variants yakkay (Tokachi, Shiranuka) and
yakkayki (Kushiro verse) carry an emphatic or rhythmic suffix found
also on korkay nakagawa2024 352 .

Nakagawa distinguishes two readings by the temporal orientation of the protasis. With a
future or hypothetical protasis, yakka yields a suppositional
concessive: 'whatever happens with X, Y holds'. With a past protasis the reading shifts
toward adversative: 'though X occurred, Y is/was so'
nakagawa2024 352 . The following example shows both
yakun and yakka in a single utterance:

kironnu=an yakun a=i=rayke yakun a=i=rayke yakka pirka | be.satiated=4.S CONJ 4.A=4.O=kill CONJ 4.A=4.O=kill CONJ be.good | If I am full, even if they kill me — let them; it is fine. | Nakagawa ex. 641. The first two clauses deploy yakun for successive hypothetical premises; the third uses yakka with the evaluative pirka in the concessive-permissive reading. The indefinite 4th-person agent a= covers an unspecified actor; i= marks the 4th-person object. | nakagawa2024:347 | SAR

The idiomatic frames yakka pirka and
yakka wen are the primary permissive and non-permissive
constructions of Hokkaido Ainu. Yakka pirka licenses an action for
the addressee ('you may X'); yakka wen withholds that license
('you may not X') ijas2023 ; refsing1986 254 . These differ
structurally and semantically from the obligation frames yak pirka
/ yak wen , which prescribe or prohibit an action as a habitual norm
rather than granting or denying a right. The permissive and deontic dimensions of the
conditional system connect to the broader modal apparatus in
epistemic-irrealis-modality and to the imperative constructions of
imperative-and-prohibitive .

e=e yakka pirka | 2SG.A=eat CONJ be.good | You may eat it. | Teaching illustration of the permissive yakka pirka frame, constructed after Ijäs L8 ex. 11-5. The Shizunai parallel e=kor yakka pirka 'you may keep it' (Refsing 1986: 254, ex. 448) confirms the construction across dialects. | ijas2023

A distinct use builds a parallel-listing construction from the pattern
[NP] ne yakka [NP] ne yakka … , surveying nominal alternatives under
the concessive and asserting that the main clause holds regardless of which alternative
obtains ('whether X or Y, both X and Y')
nakagawa2024 355–356 .

korka and hikeka — adversative

Korka 'but, however' marks the matrix clause as counter to the
expectation raised by the preceding clause. Corpus frequency of roughly 3,700 tokens
confirms its steady use across oral literature and modern prose
nakagawa2024 351 . The etymology is the same construction-type as
yakka : conditional kor when; while plus scalar
ka even, also — literally 'even when' — pointing to an original conditional
reading that has shifted entirely toward counter-expectation
nakagawa2024 351 ; ijas2023 . The dialect variant
korkay (Samani, Tokachi, Shiranuka, Kushiro) carries the same
emphatic suffix found in yakkay
nakagawa2024 351 .

nay poro p ne korka nay nutap ka hutne uske ne wa an | river big NMLZ COP CONJ river bend even narrow place COP CONJ exist | The river was big, but even the bends had become narrow. | Nakagawa ex. 662. The first clause closes on a formal-noun predicate p ne before korka; the particle spans the expectation set by the size of the river and the counter-result of narrow meanders. The resultative wa an at the end of the matrix presents a state that has come into being. | nakagawa2024:351 | SAR

Hikeka (Ishikari ikeka ; Shiranuka and
Kushiro ikekay ) occupies overlapping semantic space. Some analysts
classify it under concession (譲歩) rather than adversative (逆接), but its distribution in
attested texts is largely interchangeable with korka
nakagawa2024 351–352 ; sato2008 52 ‹contested›.
Etymologically it is the loose-link formal noun
hike thing, fact (linking noun) plus
ka even, also , a formation parallel to the
kor + ka source of
korka .

Interface with the evidential system

The conditional and concessive particles of this chapter attach outside the clause-final
evidential frame. The ordering [finite VP] – evidential nominalizer – connective is
verified: hawe ne yakun 'if (it is reported that) X',
ruwe ne akusu 'and (as established fact) Y was then discovered',
and ruwe ne korka 'though X is the case' are all attested
nakagawa2024 393 . The evidential sits above clause-internal aspect but
below the connective: kusu ne ruwe ne is well-formed while
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not, confirming that the connective scopes over
the evidential nakagawa2024 393 . The evidential paradigm is treated in
ruwe-ne-inferential .

A cross-linguistically notable asymmetry at this seam was identified by Satō: the factual
conditional akusu can follow a ruwe ne
tail — the functional equivalent of Japanese のだ — whereas the Japanese factual conditional
と / たら rejects a preceding
のだ . Ainu thus allows [clause — established fact —
discovery-link], while Japanese does not; this difference argues that the interaction of
evidentiality with clause-linking is organized differently in the two languages
sato2009 52–53 .

The deontic constructions built on the conditional system —
yak pirka / yak wen (obligation and
prohibition) and yakka pirka / yakka wen
(permission and non-permission) — intersect with the modal and epistemic system of
epistemic-irrealis-modality and with the imperative and prohibitive constructions
of imperative-and-prohibitive .

title: Conjunctions and Discourse Connectives
summary: NP-coordinating conjunctions (newa, tura) and clause-edge discourse connectives (orowano, nah …) as a minor class, distinct from the converbal clause-linkers of Part XVII.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Conjunctions and Discourse Connectives
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: NP-coordinating conjunctions (newa, tura) and clause-edge discourse connectives (orowano, nah …) as a minor class, distinct from the converbal clause-linkers of Part XVII.

Word-class status and the connective–clause-linker boundary

Hokkaido Ainu has no dedicated word class of conjunctions (接続詞). Nakagawa folds the
clause-initial discourse connectives treated here into the adverb class (副詞) and concludes
that no separate conjunction category is warranted
nakagawa2024 516, §21.2.3 . The structural ground for this classification is
the independence of these forms: they need no host predicate, impose no morphological
requirement on the following clause, and can occur sentence-initially after a pause or
paragraph break. This distinguishes them from the bound post-predicate 接続助詞 (clause-linking
particles) — wa , hine , kor , akusu , and their
relatives — which attach directly to a verbal stem and cannot stand alone. A further
diagnostic is stacking: the free connectives can follow a 接続助詞 without any intervening
predicate, yielding attested sequences such as … hine, orano …
'and then, after that …' nakagawa2024 516 ‹consensus›.

The bound clause-linkers and the switch-reference-like behaviour of
hine versus akusu are treated in
clause-linkage-overview and hine-akusu-switch-reference ;
the sequential particle wa as a clause-chaining device in its own right is in
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining . The present chapter covers only the
free, clause-initial layer, grouping its members by discourse function: the sequential–
temporal pair orano and rapokke ; the scene-shift adverb
tane ; the anaphoric-resumptive newaanpe ; the NP coordinators
newa and usa ; and the demonstrative-adjacent forms
nea and tap .

orano and orowano — the sequential connective

orano (variant forms orowano , ora , oraun ) is
the default narrative sequencer, glossed 'and then, after that'
nakagawa2024 516 . Its etymology is transparent: the relational-locative
noun or side, place, vicinity combined with the ablative source particle
wano from, away from yields orowano 'from that
place/point onwards', which has grammaticalized to a purely sequential reading. The
syncopated form orano is the most frequent in the aligned
corpus (rank 114; 1,927 tokens across Hokkaido and Sakhalin data); further reductions
to ora and oraun are dialectally attested
nakagawa2024 516 ‹corpus-confirmed›.

In both narrative and conversational discourse, orano can open a new clause
after a completed prior clause, or stack immediately after a 接続助詞 such as
akusu 'when/after'. The following example shows the latter stacking pattern:

sekor ku=hawean akusu orano | QUOT 1SG.S=say COND then | …after I said that, and then, … | orano follows the conditional–temporal particle akusu directly, at the boundary of a completed quotative frame and the next episode. The Saru narrator's spoken monologue shows this stacking as a routine narrative device. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子, isoytak 独話2 (AA研アイヌ語資料 音声資料); aa-irc/004#133

The combination of orano with the resumptive form newaanpe (§
below) recurs as a narrative construction: a completed event is nominalized and then
resumed by the anaphoric demonstrative, providing both temporal succession and referent
continuity within a single discourse move
nakagawa2024 516 . The function of such sequences in maintaining
episode cohesion is developed further in tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion .

rapokke and rapoketa — simultaneous temporal

rapokke and rapoketa mark temporal overlap or parallelism between
two narrative threads rather than strict sequentiality, and are glossed 'meanwhile, at
that time, at that juncture' nakagawa2024 516–517 . Both derive
transparently from the locative noun rapok that moment, that juncture
extended by a locative or temporal suffix, following the same etymological pattern as
orano from or . Their frequency in the aligned corpus is
considerably lower than that of orano , and they appear primarily in narrative
prose rather than conversation. Nakagawa gives no dialect restriction for these forms.

tane — temporal adverb and scene-shift marker

tane is the general temporal adverb 'now'; its discourse-connective use
emerges when it opens a new clause after a completed prior episode. In this position —
particularly after a clause nominalized by p fact, NMLZ or after an
episode-closing evidential frame — tane signals a shift in scene, time
reference, or central participant, functioning as a narrative reset
nakagawa2024 516 . There is no
separate discourse-particle lexeme: it is the ordinary temporal adverb 'now' that
acquires connective force from its position and the completion of the preceding unit.
The corpus rank (54; 4,272 tokens) therefore conflates the temporal-adverb and
scene-shift uses ‹speculative›; per-function counts would require disambiguation
across individual texts.

tane k=arpa | now 1SG.S=go | Now — after that — I set off. | Constructed minimal form. In attested narrative, tane typically follows a clause-final nominalizer p or a perfective auxiliary a closing an episode, and the shift in time reference is anchored to the new episode that follows.

The interaction of tane with the aspectual and TAM apparatus of the verb it
introduces belongs to tenselessness-and-time-reference ;
its role in signalling participant change across episodes is taken up in
reference-tracking and narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

newaanpe — the resumptive anaphoric connective

newaanpe is the form most distinctively positioned at the clause boundary
as a cohesive device, yet it is best analysed as an anaphoric demonstrative NP rather
than a pure conjunction. Its composition is transparent:
nea that (anaphoric demonstrative) + an exist, be present
+ pe thing, fact = 'that existing thing/fact'. The whole form occupies
nominal position, refers anaphorically to a proposition or entity just established in
the prior clause, and introduces the clause that predicates something of it — yielding
a resumptive-cohesive function glossed 'and as for that / and as for that (fact)'
nakagawa2024 516 . Its connective feel is structural (clause-boundary
position + invariably anaphoric reference) rather than intrinsic to its lexical class.

Corpus rank 1,349 (84 tokens) reflects the more specialized, narrative-internal role
of newaanpe compared with orano . The form has low homonymy —
unlike tane or tap — so the count is a relatively clean estimate
of its frequency ‹corpus-confirmed›.

honkor hi e-yay-oyamokte newaanpe | become.pregnant NMLZ APPL-REFL-be.surprised ANAPH | She was surprised herself that she had become pregnant, and as for that, … | newaanpe resumes the nominalized proposition (honkor hi 'the fact of becoming pregnant') as the topic NP of the next clause. The APPL prefix e- on oyamokte marks that the surprise is directed at the preceding fact; the REFL yay- marks the subject as co-referential with the surprisee. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子, uwepeker 民話20 (AA研アイヌ語資料 音声資料); aa-irc/030#82

The boundary between 'resumptive demonstrative NP' and 'discourse connective proper'
is not drawn in the available sources; the form participates in both the demonstrative
system ( demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness ) and in discourse-structural
patterns that overlap with topic marking ( topic-marking-anakne ). Whether
to treat it as a connective or as an instance of an anaphoric topic-NP in clause-initial
position is a matter of descriptive standpoint rather than an empirical question the
present data resolve ‹speculative›.

NP coordinators — newa and usa

Two additional forms operate at the level of nominal coordination rather than at the
clause boundary. newa 'and' joins two NPs into a single conjunctive NP and
marks the first member; it does not appear after the last member of the conjunction
nakagawa2024 322 . A sharp regional restriction applies: the Saru and
Chitose dialects use bare NP juxtaposition for nominal coordination and lack
newa in this function nakagawa2024 322 . Community discussion
corroborates the restriction independently — newa as an explicit 'and'-coordinator
is little used in modern Ainu and its distribution is dialectally conditioned
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-12) ‹corpus-suggested›.

A proposed morphological decomposition ne + wa — with
ne be (COP) and wa CONJ (sequential clause-linker) —
has been noted in community discussion as consistent with the connective meaning 'it
being so, and …' aynucorporadiscord (aomidori_cs_, 2023-12-12). This
decomposition is an informal proposal unsupported by a published etymological study
‹contested›.

seta newa cikap ci=e | dog and bird 1PL.EXCL.A=eat | We ate dog and bird. | newa marks the first NP (seta 'dog'); the second conjunct (cikap 'bird') follows directly. Saru and Chitose speakers would use bare juxtaposition (seta cikap ci=e) in the same context.

usa 'and, as well as, both … and …' serves as the Tokachi/Kushiro counterpart
for listing. Unlike newa , which marks only the first member, usa can
appear after each coordinated element, and it extends to verbal predicates as well as
nominal ones; in western Hokkaido the same form functions as an adverb rather than a
particle nakagawa2024 324 .

iku usa ipe usa ki | drink and eat and do | Both drink and eat. | Each listed verbal predicate receives usa; the construction is closed by the light verb ki 'do'. In western Hokkaido (including Saru/Chitose), usa does not function as a coordination particle. | nakagawa2024:324 | TOK

nea and tap — demonstrative-adjacent forms

The anaphoric determiner nea 'that (aforementioned)' is not a connective in
its own right but the demonstrative stem that underlies both newaanpe (§ above)
and topic-resumption constructions where an anaphoric NP headed by nea opens
the next clause. The demonstrative system and the proximal/distal/anaphoric paradigm are
in demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness .

tap and tapne present a dual picture. As temporal-emphatic adverbs
they carry the senses 'just now', 'thus', 'precisely this/that', positioning the clause
in the immediate narrative moment or resuming a prior referent with deictic force
nakagawa2024 516 . However, the phonological form
tap also functions as an emphatic particle (係助詞 in the kakari-musubi
framework) with the sense 'こそ/まさに', and corpus counts
(rank 143; 1,330 tokens) conflate the two uses. The boundary between the clause-initial
emphatic adverb and the emphatic particle is not settled in the sources consulted ‹speculative›.
The particle use is treated in cleft-nominalization-focus . Cross-reference
also demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness for the demonstrative continuum
within which both nea and tap sit.

Corpus counts are raw string-match totals from the aligned Hokkaido corpus (1,572,917 tokens;
45,225 types), which includes Sakhalin data and does not disambiguate homonyms. The counts
for tane (plain adverb vs. scene-shift marker) and tap (emphatic
adverb vs. emphatic particle) are therefore indicative upper bounds on each individual
function ‹speculative›. The count for newaanpe is relatively clean given the
form's low homonymy. Per-function and Hokkaido-only breakdowns require bespoke corpus
scripting.

title: Consolidated References and Bibliography
summary: The unified, type-classified bibliography of all grammars, articles, text editions, and dictionaries consulted, with a critical apparatus flagging key works not directly available.
part: Part XXIII — Reference Apparatus (Back Matter)
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Consolidated References and Bibliography
Part: Part XXIII — Reference Apparatus (Back Matter)
Summary: The unified, type-classified bibliography of all grammars, articles, text editions, and dictionaries consulted, with a critical apparatus flagging key works not directly available.

Consolidated references

This chapter gathers the 164 works cited across the grammar into a single
consolidated list, grouped by the variety or domain each work primarily concerns and
alphabetised by author within each group. Entries marked reported
are cited second-hand, through the source named in the citation, and were not consulted directly.
The same list is available as the running references page .

Hokkaidō Ainu

Primary sources and studies of the variety this grammar describes.

title: The Consonant Inventory and Its Phonetic Realization
summary: The twelve-consonant system /p t k c s m n r w y h/ (plus glottal stop), with instrumental detail on stop VOT, the affricate /c/, and intervocalic lenition.
part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Consonant Inventory and Its Phonetic Realization
Part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
Summary: The twelve-consonant system /p t k c s m n r w y h/ (plus glottal stop), with instrumental detail on stop VOT, the affricate /c/, and intervocalic lenition.

The phoneme inventory

Hokkaido Ainu has eleven consonant phonemes: /p t k c s h m n r y w/
nakagawa2024 27 ; sato2008 §2.2 ; shiraishi2022 §2 .
The inventory is organized by a straightforward place-and-manner grid. A twelfth segment,
the glottal stop [ʔ], is posited as a phoneme in some analyses and treated as a syllable-boundary
phonetic element in others; that debate and its implications for the syllable canon are addressed
in syllable-template .

labial
coronal
dorsal
glottal

stop
p
t
k
([ʔ])

affricate

c [tɕ~tʃ]

fricative

s

h

nasal
m
n

rhotic

r

glide
w
y [j]

Two consonants are excluded from syllable-final position: /c/ and /h/ never occur as
codas in Hokkaido Ainu nakagawa2024 27 ; sato2008 §2.3 ;
shiraishi2022 §3 . All other consonants appear in both onset and coda,
subject to the phonotactic constraints set out in syllable-template . The vowel
inventory that these consonants pair with is described in vowel-inventory .

Onset realizations

In syllable-initial position the consonants realize as follows
nakagawa2024 28 ; shiraishi2022 §3 .

phoneme
onset realization
notes

/p/
[p]
unaspirated; frequently perceived as [b] by Japanese listeners (see § Voicing )

/t/
[t] dental
dental articulation; perceived as [d] in voiced environments

/k/
[k]
velar; voicing less frequent than for /p/ and /t/ (see § Voicing )

/c/
[tɕ]~[tʃ]; [ts] before /u/
the [ts] allophone before /u/ caused older Japanese transcriptions to write cu as ツ

/s/
[s] (laminal [s̻]); [ɕ] before /i/
old records wrote si as シ and sa su as シャ・シュ

/h/
[h] before /a e o/; [ç] before /i/; [ɸ] before /u/
colouring is weaker than in Japanese, especially in older speakers

/m/
[m]

/n/
[n] dental
see § Nasals for coda assimilation

/r/
[ɾ] alveolar flap
also trill [r] and [d]-like articulations in some speakers; see § Rhotic

/y/
[j]

/w/
[w]

The affricate /c/ has a secondary realization [ts] before /u/. Native Ainu writers consistently
avoided the palatalized kana (チャ・チュ・チェ) for the syllables
ca cu ce ; Kirikae attributes this to the absence of a genuine
palatalization contrast at the /c/ position kirikae1997 102 . Recordings from
Chitose illustrate the conditioned [ts] allophone: the form
eci=kay 'you (pl.) do' surfaces as [et͡sɨ̥kaj] with concurrent vowel
devoicing aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-20;
corroborated by shiraishi2022 §3 ).

The fricative /s/ is laminal, with its precise place of articulation conditioned by the
following segment. Before /i/ — whether as onset or coda — /s/ palatalizes to [ɕ]
nakagawa2024 28 ; shiraishi2022 §3 . Community recordings
suggest the palato-alveolar realization before /i/ is phonetically distinct from Japanese [ɕ],
with less hissing and a blade rather than lamino-palatal contact
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹corpus-suggested›; this granularity
is not addressed in the academic literature consulted.

For /h/, colouring by the following vowel is well established
nakagawa2024 28 ; shiraishi2022 §3 , but the degree is weaker
in Ainu than in Japanese. Speakers born around 1900 showed particularly reduced colouring,
with /h/ before /u/ approaching a pure glottal articulation rather than the bilabial [ɸ]
(Tamura 1998, reported via shiraishi2022 §3 ). The suggestion that
[ɸ]~/u/ reflects Sprachbund contact with Japanese phonology has been raised in community
discussion but not established in published instrumental work
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹speculative›.

Coda realizations

The stops /p t k/ are unreleased in coda position — [p̚ t̚ k̚] — a property resembling
Korean final stops and distinct from English released codas
nakagawa2024 29 ; shiraishi2022 §3 . Community recordings
note that some older archival speakers produced fully released coda stops, with the implosive
realization more consistent for /p/ than for /k/
aynucorporadiscord (aomidori, 2023-12-05) ‹corpus-suggested›.

Coda /s/ shows allophony conditioned by the preceding vowel. After /i/, coda /s/ surfaces as
[ɕ]; after other vowels it is [sʲ]~[s]
nakagawa2024 29–30 ; shiraishi2022 §3 . The contrast appears
in cis 'cry' (→ [tɕiɕ]) vs as 'stand'
(→ [asʲ]~[as]). Before the particle wa , coda /s/ retains plain [s]
regardless of the preceding vowel: as wa 'standing, then' → [as wa],
with no palatalization aynucorporadiscord (aomidori, 2023-12-11; attributed to
Tamura 1996 Saru dictionary front matter, not directly verified here ‹speculative›).

Coda /m/ is invariant [m]. Coda /n/ undergoes place assimilation addressed below. Coda /r/
carries a vocalic release treated in § The rhotic /r/ . The glides /y/
and /w/ appear as coda off-glides [i̯] and [u̯], which contributes to the argument for their
consonant status (see § Glides ).

Nasals and nasal assimilation

Hokkaido Ainu has two nasal phonemes: /m/ (bilabial) and /n/ (dental). In onset position both
are phonetically invariant. As codas, /m/ remains [m], while /n/ undergoes regressive place
assimilation nakagawa2024 30 ; shiraishi2022 §3 :

/n/ → [ŋ] / __ /k/ (dorsal assimilation):
honkor 'be pregnant' → [hoŋkor];
kimunkamuy 'mountain deity' → [ki.muŋ.ka.muy]
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-16;
corroborated by nakagawa2024 30 ).

/n/ → [m] / __ /p m/ (labial assimilation):
anpe 'true thing' → [ampe]
nakagawa2024 30 .

These rules apply to /n/ only. The bilabial nasal /m/ does not shift to [ŋ] before /k/:
a form such as okamkir , containing underlying /m/ before /k/, retains
[m] aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹contested›. The orthographic
implication is that /m/ and /n/ remain distinctly spelled before dorsals — a practice
consistent across the standard Romanization — though the systematic phonetic documentation
of this non-assimilation across dialects and lexical items is not available in the sources
read for this grammar.

honkor | be.pregnant | be pregnant | Coda /n/ of the first syllable assimilates to the place of the following dorsal /k/, yielding surface [hoŋkor]. Contrast anpe [ampe], where coda /n/ assimilates to following labial /p/. | nakagawa2024:30 | SAR

The sandhi extension of these nasal assimilation rules across morpheme and word boundaries —
including /n/ + /s/ → [y] + /s/ (e.g. pon seta → [poy seta]
'puppy') — is treated in coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi .

Voicing

Hokkaido Ainu has no phonemic voicing contrast among obstruents. The pairs /p/~[b],
/t/~[d], /k/~[g], /s/~[z] are non-contrastive, and the phonemic transcription uses the
voiceless symbol throughout
nakagawa2024 25, 27 ; sato2008 §2.2 ;
shiraishi2022 §3.1 . The form hapo 'mother' and its
allophonic variant habo are the same phonemic form; similarly,
pet 'river' surfaces as [pet] or [bet] depending on context and speaker.

Voiced allophones arise in three phonetic environments
nakagawa2024 25, 27 ; shiraishi2022 §3.1 :
(i) intervocalic position ( hapo ~ [habo]);
(ii) post-nasal position ( tampe ~ [tambe]);
(iii) post-/r/ position ( unarpe ~ [unarbe]).
Of these, post-nasal voicing is the most frequent: Yamashita (2000) measured approximately 40%
application in that environment (reported via shiraishi2022 §3.1 ). Voicing
likelihood also varies by place of articulation: labial /p/ voices most readily, velar /k/
least. Asai (1969) observed that ape 'fire' frequently voices to [abe]
while cikap 'bird' seldom voices to [tɕigap] (reported via
nakagawa2024 28 ). Voicing additionally correlates with speaker sex; male
speakers voice more frequently than female speakers (Kindaichi 1928, reported via
nakagawa2024 27 ).

Voice onset time measurements from a Chitose recording session give approximate values of
+8 to +15 ms for /p/ and +20 ms for /k/ in onset position; an aspirated [kʰ] variant
reached +30 to +43 ms aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-20)
‹corpus-suggested›. These figures are consistent with an absence of contrastive aspiration
and substantial speaker latitude; a published acoustic VOT study of Hokkaido Ainu has not
been consulted for this grammar.

The rhotic /r/

The phoneme /r/ realizes primarily as the alveolar flap [ɾ]. In some speakers trill [r] and
[d]-like articulations occur as well; Sakhalin varieties have a retroflex [ʈ] that may be
hard to distinguish from [t] (reported via shiraishi2022 §3 ). Archival
recordings reportedly show /r/ with an approximant quality, and /s/ palatalizing more broadly
than before /i/ alone
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-05). Community discussion, citing the
front-matter phonology of Tamura's Saru dictionary, stresses that Ainu /r/ 'does not resemble
[l] at all' (aomidori, aynucorporadiscord , 2023-12-11); Shiraishi, by contrast,
lists [l] alongside [d] as possible realizations shiraishi2022 §3 . The
community characterization could not be page-verified in sources read for this grammar and is
reported here as a community assertion ‹speculative›.

In coda position /r/ carries a vocalic release — described as 'resounding' by Kindaichi
(1931, reported via shiraishi2022 §3.2 ), 'parasitic vowel' by Peng (1970,
reported via shiraishi2022 §3.2 ), and 'echo vowel' by Tamura (1988b,
reported via shiraishi2022 §3.2 ) — whose quality roughly copies the
preceding vowel: pirka 'good' → [pirĭka],
arki 'come (pl.)' → [arĭki]
nakagawa2024 30 ;
shiraishi2022 §3.2 . This release distinguishes /-r/ from /-rV/ sequences.
The minimal pair etor [etoro] 'snot' vs
etoro [etoːro] 'to snore' — where the /-rV/ word has a measurably
longer pre-rhotic vowel (Asai 1976, reported via shiraishi2022 §3.2 ) — is
now well established
nakagawa2024 30 ; shiraishi2022 §3.2 .

After voiceless stops /k p t/, coda /r/ devoices: nukar 'look at'
may surface as [nukaɾ̥ɨ̥h] aynucorporadiscord (aomidori, 2023-12-11)
‹corpus-suggested›. The sandhi diagnostics for /-r/ vs /-rV/ — resyllabification behavior,
nominalizer allomorphy, and the cross-word assimilation rules — are treated in
coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi .

Glides /y/ and /w/ as consonants

The glides /y/ [j] and /w/ [w] function as phonemic consonants in both onset and coda
nakagawa2024 29 ; shiraishi2022 §3 . An earlier analysis by
Kindaichi and Chiri kindaichi1936 treated these as the second elements of
diphthongs; the consonant analysis was established by Nakagawa and Shiraishi on the basis
of two independent diagnostics.

The first diagnostic is nominalizer allomorphy. The nominalizer suffix selects
-p after a vowel and -pe after a consonant;
morphemes ending in /y/ or /w/ take the consonant allomorph —
okay-pe , siw-pe — establishing that /-y/ and
/-w/ pattern with coda consonants shiraishi2022 §3 ;
nakagawa2024 29 . The second diagnostic is accent: syllables closed by /y/
or /w/ do not bear independent accent, unlike syllables closed by /i/ or /u/. Thus
kamuy 'deity' parses as ka.muy (two syllables,
accent on the second), and aynu 'person' as
ay.nu nakagawa2024 29 . Community members corroborate
this analysis through parallel allomorphy: the plural suffix
( ante → okayte ) and causative allomorphs
( -re/-te ) both treat /-y/ as a consonant coda
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2025-12-03) ‹consensus›.

In onset position, /y/ and /w/ appear before vowels in native morphemes, but the sequences
*yi , *wi , *wu are
absent from monomorphemic roots, arising only across morpheme boundaries
nakagawa2024 43–45 . These phonotactic gaps are part of the syllable
inventory treated in syllable-template .

The phonemic status of onset /y/ and /w/ is dialect-dependent and remains
unsettled ‹contested›. For Saru and Chitose, Nakagawa treats these as surface glides derived
at phoneme junctures, not underlying phonemes nakagawa2024 34–35 . In
Shizunai, the absence of contrastive pitch means that the distinction between [w] and [ʔ] at
a hiatus position — illustrated by the minimal pair
suwat 'pothook' vs suʔat 'pot bail' — must
be carried by the glide itself, making /w/ phonemically contrastive there
okuda2025 . Satō (2003) further posits underlying /y w/ in Chitose for
non-predictable hiatus positions while treating glide appearances after
ku- and e- as surface-derived (reported via
shiraishi2022 §4.3 ). Okuda concludes that no dialect-neutral statement
about glide phonemicity is possible okuda2025 .

The accent behavior of syllables closed by /y/ or /w/ is treated in
pitch-accent-placement-rule . Glide insertion, hiatus resolution, and
person-affix sandhi involving glides are in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction . For dialect-level consonant
variation — including Bihoro cluster assimilation
( tk → kk ,
rs → ss ) and
h -elision and insertion in eastern varieties — see
hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

title: Basic Constituent Order and Head-Final Syntax
summary: Rigid verb-final (S)OV clause order and pervasive head-finality, scored against Greenbergian word-order correlations.
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Basic Constituent Order and Head-Final Syntax
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: Rigid verb-final (S)OV clause order and pervasive head-finality, scored against Greenbergian word-order correlations.

The head-final profile

Hokkaido Ainu is verb-final. Every non-verbal element of the clause — nominals, adverbials,
negators, subordinate clauses — precedes the verb phrase, and the verb phrase occupies the
rightmost position in the sentence. This verb-finality holds without exception: no
grammatical process moves a finite verb to a non-final position
nakagawa2024 363 . The same head-final logic ramifies down into the noun
phrase — modifiers, relative clauses, and property verbs precede the head noun — and upward
into clause-level relations — subordinate clauses precede main clauses, and markers that
relate an NP to the predicate follow the NP as postpositions. Bugaeva gives the canonical
summary: "the basic constituent order in Ainu is SV/AOV. Modifiers are prepositive, and
subordinate clauses come before main clauses. All particles are postpositional. Topical NPs
tend to come first" bugaeva2012 472 .

The deep typological fact behind these orders is head-marking. Satō frames the profile
through Nichols's nichols1986 head-marking / dependent-marking
distinction: the verb, as clausal head, bears obligatory person-indexing suffixes and
prefixes that already satisfy all argument slots; overt NPs are accordingly optional, and
when present they are appositional to those indexes rather than positional carriers of
grammatical role sato2025a 359–361 . The surface resemblance to Japanese —
both verb-final, both postpositional — therefore conceals a fundamental typological
difference: Japanese is dependent-marking, with case particles on the NP encoding
grammatical relations; Ainu is head-marking, with person markers on the verb doing that
work sato2025a 359–360 ; bugaeva2012 466 .

For the person-indexing apparatus that underpins this system, see
person-marking-architecture . For the question of whether optional and freely
ordered overt NPs license a nonconfigurational / pronominal-argument analysis of Ainu,
and for the Ainu-internal counter-evidence from the
〈location〉 -noun class, see
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument and
argument-realization-indexing . For the overall typological placement of
Hokkaido Ainu, see typological-profile .

Verb-finality and the sentence template

Nakagawa (2024: 361, §10.1) schematizes the Ainu sentence as a left-to-right sequence:

[adverbials 0…x]   [NPs 0…n]   [verb phrase (n)]   [(evidential frame)]
(sentence-final particle)

Within the preverbal field, "there is no fixed rule for the order of NP–NP and
NP–adverbial; both need only be before the verb phrase"
nakagawa2024 361 . An evidential frame ( ruwe ne ,
hawe ne , and the rest: see evidential-schema-overview )
attaches to the right of the verb phrase; clause-linking particles
( wa , hine : see
clause-linkage-overview ) extend further right. Only a connective that
introduces the next sentence sits outside the sentence boundary.

Head-finality applies at every level of structure. The following table — adapted from the
Shibatani (1990: 23–24) inventory — shows the major construction types:

construction
order
example
gloss

noun + postposition
N – Postp
cise ta
'at home'

property verb + noun
PV – N
pirka kewtum
'good heart'

genitive / possessor + noun
Gen – N
sapo ninkarihi
'sister's earrings'

demonstrative + noun
Dem – N
toan seta
'that dog'

numeral + noun
Num – N
sine aynu
'one person'

relative clause + noun
Rel – N
[beko reska] sisam ‹ISH›
'a Japanese who raises cows'

adverb + verb
Adv – V
turasno paye
'go quickly'

main verb + auxiliary
V – Aux
a=e rusuy
'want to eat'

standard + equative + property verb
Std – Eq – PV
menoko kasuno okirasnu
'stronger than a woman'

clause + question particle
Cl – Q
pirka-p ne ya
'is (it) something good?'

shibatani1990 22 notes one structural feature that sits awkwardly
with the "ideal SOV" characterization: Ainu is simultaneously verb-final and heavily
prefixing, with person-marking affixes, applicative prefixes, and incorporated nouns
attaching to the left of the verb root. In the Greenbergian correlates of SOV order,
postpositions, Rel–N, and Gen–N are harmonic, but prefixing morphology is not
shibatani1990 22 . The prefixing pattern follows directly from
head-marking: the clausal head hosts its argument indexes and derivational material to the
left of the root, independently of the clause's surface word order ‹contested›.

Preverbal NP order: free or fixed?

Whether the ordering of agent (A) and object (O) NPs among the preverbal positions is
grammatically fixed is the central word-order debate in Ainu studies. Two readings of the
same data have been sustained in the literature.

Shibatani (1990: 22) argues that word order encodes grammatical role: "Since there are no
case inflections on nouns, word order plays as important a role as in English in determining
the grammatical functions of certain nouns." He presents the following pair as a minimal
role-contrast ‹contested›:

kamuy aynu rayke | bear person kill | The bear killed the man. | Shibatani ex. 23a. The verb rayke carries no overt person marker (both arguments are third person, zero-indexed). On Shibatani's reading, the first NP is agent and the second is object by linear position alone. | shibatani1990:22 | HK

aynu kamuy rayke | person bear kill | The man killed the bear. | Shibatani ex. 23b. With the NPs reversed, the person is now the agent and the bear the object. Shibatani allows OSV inversion when context or semantics prevents misinterpretation. | shibatani1990:22 | HK

Nakagawa counters with the observation that the same speaker, in the same session,
produces three distinct orderings of the three arguments of the four-place verb
epakasnu 'teach', all describing the same scene: 'my wife taught
our daughter women's work' nakagawa2024 363 ; reproduced in English in
nakagawa2022pos :

ex.
NP order
Ainu (Chitose)

700
IO – A – DO
menoko ne hike   a=macihi   menoko monrayke   epakasnu

701
IO – DO – A
menoko ne hike   menoko monrayke   a=macihi   epakasnu

702
A – IO – DO
a=macihi anakne   menoko-po   menoko monrayke   epakasnu

A further ordering (DO – A – IO) appears in a Saru instance by the same speaker
nakagawa2024 363–364 . Nakagawa concludes that "which one is the subject
cannot be grammatically determined" from word order or NP morphology; common sense — the
plausibility that a wife teaches a daughter, not the reverse — is what rules out the
inverted reading nakagawa2024 363 . Bugaeva independently states the same
finding: Ainu "does not show the syntactic relations subject and object either by case
marking or by word order" nakagawa2022pos ‹contested›.

Satō (2008: 2) holds a middle ground: roles are signalled by "語順 or 文脈 (word order
or context)", allowing that either mechanism may operate without assigning priority to
either.

Refsing's Shizunai data present a third position refsing1986 , §2.4,
reporting Chiri (1936: 167): a default SOV order when both subject and object are full NPs,
with systematic deviation to O–S–V when the subject is a prefixal person marker (the object
NP then precedes the prefix-bearing verb). Three-place clauses show "flexible" ordering,
with IO before DO as the most frequent pattern when the subject is a prefix (§13.4.3).
These regularities match Nakagawa's Chitose data as statistical tendencies, not as
categorical rules ‹contested›.

The zero-marking of third-person arguments — the bare verb stem simultaneously serves as
the third-person and citation form — means that when both A and O are third persons, no
index disambiguates the roles, and interpretation falls entirely to common sense or to the
〈location〉 -noun class of one of the NPs
sato2025a 369 . The third person bears no overt affix in the standard
Saru–Chitose paradigm; a possessive construction already concedes that third-person indexing
is zero-marked (nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord 2024-12-25, asserted, consistent
with sato2025a 369 ). See indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm for
the fourth-person form-set and its contrast with the zero-marked third person;
independent-personal-pronouns for the free pronouns.

Modifier order inside the NP

All prenominal modification is head-final: property verbs, relative clauses, demonstratives,
genitives, and numerals all precede the head noun. Nakagawa (2024: §10.5) builds the NP
compositionally, and the following table summarises the major modifier types:

modifier type
order
example
gloss
source

property verb (attributive)
PV – N
pirka kewtum
'good heart'
shibatani1990 23

N + N (concept + concept), compound
N₁ – N₂
cironnup sar
'fox tail (generic)'
nakagawa2024 379–381

N + N (possessor + possessed NP)
N₁ – N₂ POSS
cironnup saraha
'that specific fox's tail'
nakagawa2024 381–382

demonstrative + noun
Dem – N
toan seta
'that dog'
shibatani1990 23

numeral-adnoun + noun
Num – N
tu okkaypo
'two young men'
nakagawa2024 383–384

noun + numeral-noun
N – Num
ponmenoko sinep
'one young woman'
nakagawa2024 383–384

relative clause + noun (gap-based)
Rel – N
[a=kor kusu ne] menoko
'the woman I am to marry'
nakagawa2024 385

finite clause + formal noun
[Cl] – FN
[kikir rek] haw
'the sound of an insect crying'
nakagawa2024 386

The two N + N types differ in syntactic status. When both nouns are in the concept
(non-possessed) form, the construction is a compound and the two elements cannot be
separated: *sar tanne cironnup 'tail that-long fox' is
ungrammatical. When the first noun is in the concept form and the second is in the
possessed (affiliative) form, the construction is an appositive possessor–possessee pair
— two NPs — and the possessor can be displaced by relativization or topicalization:
saraha tanne cironnup 'the tail that is long (of the fox)'
is well formed nakagawa2024 380–382 . This appositive possession structure,
where the possessee is obligatorily head-marked and the possessor NP is optionally present
or absent, is discussed in cross-linguistic perspective by
bugaeva2021poss . See noun-noun-compounding for compound
nouns and noun-phrase-structure for the full NP typology.

Relativization is prenominal and gap-based: the head noun is placed at the right edge of
the modifying clause, and the verb's valency identifies which argument is missing. The
following example shows the Rel–N order in Chitose:

a=kor kusu ne menoko | 4.A=have PURP COP woman | The woman I am to marry. | the woman for the purpose of my having | Nakagawa ex. 753. The relative clause a=kor kusu ne 'that I am to have' precedes the head noun menoko 'woman'. The gap inside the relative clause corresponds to the object of the transitive verb kor. The construction yields a Rel–N order consistent with the general head-final profile. | nakagawa2024:385 | CHI

The full prenominal relative-clause system — including case-retention inside the relative,
headless relatives, and the continuum with nominalized complements — is treated in
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses .

Numerals occupy two alternative slots: a numeral-adnoun precedes the noun (Num–N, e.g.,
sine aynu 'one person') and a numeral-noun follows it (N–Num, e.g.,
ponmenoko sinep 'one young woman'); the two patterns are largely
interchangeable for common numerals nakagawa2024 383–384 . In addition, a
numeral-noun can float away from its head noun, separated from it by a focus particle,
while both together still fill a single argument slot:

toma ka sinep ka isam | mat FOC one FOC not.exist | There is not a single mat either. | Nakagawa ex. 750. The head noun toma 'mat' and the numeral-noun sinep 'one' are separated by the focus particle ka 'also/even'; yet isam 'not exist' is a one-place intransitive, so toma and sinep must together fill the single S slot. Nakagawa analyses [[toma] ka [sinep] ka] as a discontinuous NP — a classic indicator of pragmatically free constituent structure. Community discussion notes that the N + adverbial-numeral + V order (this N ka Num V pattern) appears frequently in Ainu oral texts, perhaps more so than comparable structures in Japanese (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-12, proposed ‹contested›). | nakagawa2024:383 | SAR

For the numeral and classifier system in full, see
numeral-classifiers-ordinals-and-quantifier-syntax .

Postpositions and the marking of obliques

Core arguments — the S/A subject and the O object — are never marked by a postposition or
case particle; their roles are encoded by person-marking on the verb
bugaeva2012 472, 476 ; shibatani1990 22 . Postpositions
mark only adjunct and oblique relations: locative, ablative, goal, instrumental, and
comitative. All follow their NP, consistently maintaining the N–Postp order.

Nakagawa (2024: 386–389, §10.6) and Bugaeva (2012: 476) distinguish two morphological
classes. Postpositional adverbs can stand alone without a preceding NP and some
can take person affixes; many derive from verbs still undergoing grammaticalization, such as
ani with, using (from 'hold') and tura together with .
Postpositional particles (格助詞) cannot stand alone, cannot be person-indexed,
and function as purely relational markers: the locative
ta at, in, on and un to, at (inanimate goal) , the ablative
wa from , and the perlative peka across, along belong to
this class. The translative particle ne as, becoming (homophonous with the
copula) marks the NP-as-role ('as', 'in the role of'). The classes overlap in translation;
the diagnostic is whether the element can form an adverbial phrase independently
nakagawa2024 388–389 . Full treatment is in
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events .

Negation, adjuncts, and topic fronting

The standard sentential negator somo not and the prohibitive
iteki don't appear before the verb phrase. This is preverbal placement but
not a special word-order fact: both are adverbs and take no person marker, so their
preverbal position follows the general Adv–V rule directly
nakagawa2024 365 ; shibatani1990 24 .

aynu patek somo ne | person only NEG COP | It is not only humans. | Nakagawa ex. 708. The negator somo occupies the adverb slot immediately before the copula ne; it takes no person marker and cannot predicate independently. Contrast Japanese ～ではない, where the negative element is a predicative auxiliary following the complement. | nakagawa2024:365 | SAR

Scope interactions of somo with TAM, evidentiality, and
evidential-outer-frame negation ( ruwe somo ne ) are treated in
negation-somo-standard-clausal .

The preverbal field as a whole is freely ordered with respect to adjuncts and oblique
phrases. Time adverbials, manner adverbs, and postpositional phrases can appear in any
sequence before the verb phrase. Refsing (§2.4) declines to formulate a rule for adjunct
ordering in Shizunai, finding it "very free"
refsing1986 .

Topical NPs gravitate to sentence-initial position regardless of grammatical role, and they
are often — though not obligatorily — overtly marked by the topic particle
anakne as for :

seta anakne pirka | dog TOP be.good | As for the dog, it is in good condition. | Constructed for illustration. The referent of seta could be agent, patient, or topical subject of predication depending on wider context; anakne marks pragmatic foreground regardless of role.

Community discussion confirms that anak/anakne is used more
sparingly than Japanese は : a referent already established in
discourse need not be re-marked, and the particle carries the weight of '～に関しては (as
regards ~)' rather than a routine topic reset (nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord
2024-11-09, asserted, consistent with shibatani1990 38 ). The full
distribution of anakne — including its contrast with bare
topic-initial placement, its scope, and the diachronic origin from
an-yak — is in topic-marking-anakne . Pragmatic
reordering beyond topic fronting, including right-dislocation and the afterthought
construction with light verb ki , is treated in
pragmatic-word-order-dislocation .

Certain adjunct-ordering details show sensitivity to language-contact pressure. The
traditional sequence ani patek (instrumental
ani before the focus adverb patek 'only')
is in retreat in modern use in favour of the reversed
patek ani . Community analysis argues that
patek is an adverb and cannot be nominalized as Japanese
だけ can, so placing it first — matching Japanese particle
ordering — represents Japanese-influenced reordering (nukopoli,
aynucorporadiscord 2024-12-16, proposed ‹contested›).

Questions do not disturb the basic constituent order. Content-question words —
hunna who , hemanta what , hunak where —
remain in situ at the position of the corresponding non-question constituent; there is no
wh-movement to a pre-clausal position
bugaeva2012 498 . Polar questions are signalled by the sentence-final
particle ya or by rising intonation. The full interrogative system
is in interrogative-strategies-question-particles . For focus-related
constructions including cleft-nominalization, see cleft-nominalization-focus .

title: The 'Appearance' Continuative: siran, siri…, and kane an
summary: siri/siran-based forms express durative, ongoing situations framed as observable appearance.
