part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The 'Appearance' Continuative: siran, siri…, and kane an
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: siri/siran-based forms express durative, ongoing situations framed as observable appearance.

The continuative field in overview

Four constructions complement the progressive kor an and the
resultative wa an described in
existential-aspect-an-oka : the appearance-based continuative built on
siran ( sir-an , 'appearance-exist'); the
merged durative kane an ; the resultative variant
hine an ; and the Tokachi–Ishikari pair
kan an / tek an . Each recruits the
existential verb an exist (singular) as its second predicate.
Yoshikawa groups all of them with kor an and
wa an under the cover term 「存在型アスペクト」, existential
aspectual forms — constructions that may express aspectual meanings without constituting
a grammatical aspect category in the strict sense yoshikawa2022 ;
nakagawa2024 446–449 . None of them is distributed uniformly across
dialects, and the forms centred on the sensory noun
sir appearance, visible state, land straddle the boundary between the
aspect system and the nominalization-plus-copula evidential paradigm treated in
evidential-schema-overview .

form
connector
aspectual value
dialect distribution

siran ( sir-an )
sir (appearance noun)
durative continuative; ongoing visible state; overlaps with the direct-perception evidential
cross-dialectal (via evidential system)

kane an
kane
merged progressive/resultative; noteworthy-situation nuance in Saru/Chitose; plain durative in Shizunai/Samani
Saru, Chitose, Shizunai, Samani

hine an
hine
resultative, like wa an
Saru, Chitose

kan an
kan
merged progressive/resultative; Tokachi leans progressive, Ishikari leans resultative
Tokachi, Ishikari

tek an
tek
resultative of change verbs (≈ Saru wa an )
Tokachi

siran — the appearance-based continuative

The noun sir appearance, visible state, land is the source both of the
evidential formal-defective-nouns formal noun
sir-i and of the existential-aspectual predicate
sir-an 'appearance-exist'. As the second predicate in a
pair-verb chain, sir-an replaces the plain existential
an and adds a perceptual dimension: the state or action named
by the first verb is not merely ongoing but visibly so, its appearance persisting for
the observer nakagawa2024 434–435 ;
dalcorso2018 304, 326–327 .

Nakagawa frames this function as the third of four temporal-reference strategies
available in the tenseless system. Where a time adverb places an event on the timeline
and a prospective form such as kusu ne points to the future,
clause-final siri anchors an action unfolding visibly in front
of the speaker — 「現在まさに目の前で行われている行為」 nakagawa2024 434–435 .
The form is not a tense marker; the present-ongoing temporal reading follows from the
evidential value, because if the speaker is currently perceiving the event, the event
is current.

Structurally, sir-an occupies the position of the existential
in the pair-verb frame: a lexical verb is linked to it by a simultaneous conjunction,
most commonly kor , the same conjunction that builds
kor an . The resulting contrast is between
V kor an (action in progress) and
V kor sir-an (action in progress, visibly apparent). Person
agreement follows the pair-verb pattern: both the lexical verb and the existential
component of sir-an carry the subject index
nakagawa2024 442 .

nea kur arpa kor sir-an | that person go CONJ appearance-exist | That person appears to be going. | Illustrates the appearance-based continuative: sir-an replaces plain an as the existential predicate, signalling that the ongoing action is perceptible to the observer. Compare nea kur arpa kor an 'that person is going', which carries no evidential colouring.

In attested usage, sir-an frequently appears embedded before
the inferential evidential ruwe ne , forming a double-evidential
stack: the visual state is packaged as an ongoing scene and then reframed as an
established fact. Dal Corso reads sir-an in this position as
the indirect visual evidential in his eight-form system, the form that applies when the
scene is accessible but the speaker treats the evidence as somewhat less immediately
processed than under the direct-perception
siri ne dalcorso2018 304, 326–327 .

supuya at kor sir-an ru-we ne anan | smoke rise CONJ appearance-exist track-POSS COP MIR | Smoke was rising, it turned out! | Dal Corso ex. 268. The visual-state predicate sir-an is embedded before the inferential ruwe ne, which repackages the proposition as a fact the speaker now recognizes with surprise. The stacking confirms that sir-an and ruwe ne are separate predicates with a fixed scope order: aspect/visual-state inside, inferential outside. | dalcorso2018:327 | HK | Nakagawa text, Dal Corso siglum NGF:176

The aspect–evidential boundary

The overlap between the continuative reading of siran and the
evidential functions of siri ne and
siri an is a recognized analytical difficulty. Nakagawa treats
the present-ongoing reading under temporal-reference strategy 3 without assigning it to
the aspect system proper nakagawa2024 434–435 . Yoshikawa, whose
existential aspectual forms group includes kor an ,
wa an , kane an , and
hine an , does not list siran among them;
the progressive reading is a pragmatic inference from the evidential value rather than
an independent category yoshikawa2022 . Dal Corso reads
siri an as one of eight evidential forms — the indirect visual
counterpart to the direct-perception siri ne — organized by the
parameters of source reliability and event accessibility
dalcorso2018 304, 313–315 .

These framings are not contradictory; they describe the same construction from different
angles. The present chapter treats siran /
siri ne as a form that licenses a continuative reading through
its evidential value, while locating it primarily in the evidential paradigm. The
direct-perception evidential siri ne and the indirect visual
siri an receive their full treatment in
siri-ne-situational . The stacking pattern
kor an siri ne — attested in Nakagawa's corpus of Saru Ainu —
supplies the most transparent diagnostics: the progressive marks the action as in
progress, the situational evidential marks the speaker as currently witnessing it, and
the scope order (aspect inside, evidential outside) is fixed
nakagawa2024 393 . When sir-an fills the
existential slot itself, the visual dimension is encoded in the existential predicate
rather than in a separate closing evidential, and the form sits at the intersection of
the two systems ‹contested›.

kane an — the merged durative

kane an is a pair verb of the same structural type as
kor an and wa an : a lexical verb is
linked to the existential an by the simultaneous conjunction
kane . Its aspectual behaviour differs in that it is unconstrained
by verb class: it accepts action verbs (progressive reading) and change-of-state verbs
(resultative reading) equally, merging the two values into a single durative continuative
nakagawa2024 446–447 ; yoshikawa2022 ;
sato2006 84–85 . Chiri's classic label for it is 持続態 durative ,
set against 進行態 progressive ( kor an ) and
完了態 perfective ( wa isam /
wa okere ) in his three-way aspect classification
chiri1942 503–504 ; takahashi2022 .

The dialectal picture divides into two patterns. In Saru and Chitose,
kane an is marked: alongside its durative value it carries a
nuance of remarkable or unexpected situation. This nuance connects it to the standalone
auxiliary kane (Nakagawa 2024: 309–310 table 74), which Tamura
characterizes as marking an unanticipated action or state and Satō as encoding degree
— a semantic base that grammaticalizes toward simultaneity in some contexts
nakagawa2024 309–310, 446–447 ; takahashi2022 . In Shizunai
and Samani, kane an carries no such nuance and functions as the
plain imperfective, replacing both kor an and
wa an without selectional constraint on verb class
nakagawa2024 447 ; sato2006 84–85 ‹contested›.

The aspect-neutral character of kane an in Saru is visible
within a single clause chain: the form can take a resultative reading on one conjunct
and a progressive reading on the next.

asir sapa tuye kane an hine mina kane an wa | be.new head cut CONJ exist CONJ laugh CONJ exist CONJ | having cut the new head and laughing | The first kane an follows the change verb tuye 'cut' with a resultative reading — the new head has been cut and the state persists; the second follows the action verb mina 'laugh' with a progressive reading — laughter is ongoing. The same form covers both, unlike kor an (progressive only) and wa an (resultative only). | nakagawa2024:446 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 1004

Person agreement follows the pair-verb pattern: both the lexical verb and
an carry the subject index, and the plural existential
oka replaces an with a plural subject
( kane oka ), parallel to kor oka and
wa oka nakagawa2024 441, 446 .

hine an, kan an, and tek an — further dialect variants

hine an uses the sequential-temporal conjunction
hine in the existential-aspectual construction and functions as
a resultative like wa an : the conjunction encodes that the
situation has come to obtain as a result of the lexical verb's action. Yoshikawa lists
it among the Saru–Chitose existential aspectual forms; Chiri's gloss
'… hine an = be …-ing' applies the durative label to it
yoshikawa2022 ; chiri1942 503–504 .

kan an is the Tokachi and Ishikari counterpart of Saru–Chitose
kane an : it merges progressive and resultative values into a
single durative continuative. The form's aspectual centre of gravity differs between
the two dialects: Tokachi usage leans progressive, Ishikari usage leans resultative
nakagawa2024 448 ; takahashi2022 .

a=tura wa cepkoyki=an kan an pe a=ki kan oka=an ainne | 4.A=accompany CONJ catch.fish=4.S CONJ exist NMLZ 4.A=do CONJ exist.PL=4.S CONJ | while we were fishing together | kan an in Tokachi progressive use: the ongoing collective action of fishing persists as a state. The nominalizer pe packages the first kan an clause; a=ki kan oka=an continues the chain with a plural subject marked by oka=an. | nakagawa2024:448 | TOK | Nakagawa ex. 1016, Tokachi

poro cise as kan an na | be.big house stand CONJ exist FIN | A large house was standing there. | kan an in Ishikari resultative use: the posture verb as 'stand' enters kan an where Saru would use wa an, presenting the house as being in the resultant state of having stood up. | nakagawa2024:448 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 1019, Ishikari; originally Otsuka 1990: 45

tek an (Tokachi) covers the resultative of change verbs,
corresponding to Saru wa an in selectional behaviour. Nakagawa
traces a connection to the Saru auxiliary tek 'do briefly /
suddenly', which shows how the same root grammaticalizes along different paths in
different dialect communities — as a momentary-action auxiliary in Saru, as a
resultative pair-verb connector in Tokachi
nakagawa2024 448–449 ‹speculative›.

The Tokachi first-person restriction on kor an

Tokachi presents a distributional constraint on kor an that
elevates kan an into a distinct functional role. In Tokachi,
kor an cannot take a first-person subject: where another
dialect would say ku=nukar kor ku=an 'I am watching', Tokachi
requires ku=nukar kan ku=an with
kan an . Takahashi's hypothesis is that
kor an encodes the speaker's observation of an event, a
meaning that clashes with a first-person subject who is themselves the agent; the
construction is therefore confined to second- and third-person subjects in this dialect.
No equivalent restriction has been documented for Saru, Chitose, or any other Hokkaido
variety takahashi2022 ‹contested›.

Takahashi invokes a cross-linguistic parallel with Shuri Ryūkyūan, where the
progressive equivalent likewise bars first-person agents; the Tokachi restriction is
thus positioned as an instance of a typologically attested pattern rather than an
isolated anomaly takahashi2022 . The further question — whether the
restriction reflects a residual connection between kor an and
the visual-evidential domain, such that first-person use is blocked because first
persons cannot be their own external observers — remains open in the sources ‹speculative›.

Grammaticalization and the degree of aspect

All the forms in this chapter share a grammaticalization path: a conjunction
( kane , hine ,
kan , tek ) or a sensory noun
( sir ) links a lexical verb to the existential
an , and the compound develops an ongoing-state reading from the
literal meaning 'the state of V-ing exists' bybee1994 . This is the
same path as kor an and wa an in
existential-aspect-an-oka ; the cross-dialect variation concerns which
conjunction is recruited and whether the result merges or maintains the progressive /
resultative distinction.

Yoshikawa's assessment is that none of these forms has reached obligatory grammatical
aspect: the existential an retains semantic content, person
marking is overt on both predicates, and the construction is optional. They are 'forms
that may express aspectual meanings but are not grammatical aspectual forms'
yoshikawa2022 . The cross-dialectal evidence supports this. The
Shizunai–Samani kane an , which has lost the
remarkable-situation nuance retained in Saru and Chitose, appears to be at a more
advanced stage: the form is more bleached, its selectional freedom more complete.
Different dialect communities have therefore travelled different distances along a single
grammaticalization trajectory ‹corpus-suggested›. The phasal, iterative, and habitual
extensions of the aspect system are treated in
phasal-iterative-habitual-aspect .

title: The Copula ne and Predicate-Nominal Clauses
summary: Equational and class-membership predication with the copula ne, its person indexing, and its disputed verb-vs-particle status.
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Copula ne and Predicate-Nominal Clauses
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: Equational and class-membership predication with the copula ne, its person indexing, and its disputed verb-vs-particle status.

Overview

ne be, become (copula) ranks as the second most frequent token in the Hokkaido
corpus of nearly 1.6 million running words, behind only the existential
an exist, be (located)
nakagawa2024 §4.2.2 . The count conflates several homophones — the predicate-nominal
copula, a mutative case postposition, and anaphoric/interrogative roots — but the copular use
accounts for a substantial share. Predicate-nominal clauses are accordingly among the most
pervasive structures in the language.

When the predicate of a finite clause is a noun phrase, the copula
ne must appear after it. Without ne , a sequence
of two noun phrases does not constitute a grammatical clause; the element occupying the
predicate position in Ainu is always a verbal head
nakagawa2022pos 474 . This sets the construction apart from the other two
major non-dynamic predicate types: the existential-locational-an-oka existential
an / oka(y) , which handle existential and
locational predication, and the
property-concept-predication property-concept verbs ( pirka
'be good', poro 'be big'), which predicate directly without any copula.
The distribution is determined by argument structure: ne is required
when and only when the predicate is a nominal
sato2008 20–21 ; refsing1986 145 . Ijäs's pedagogical grammar
covers the copula in its treatment of basic clause types ijas2023 .

Clause template

The predicate-nominal clause has the shape
[Subject] [Complement-NP] ne . The complement takes
no case marker and immediately precedes the copula. Satō isolates the three-constituent
structure as 主語 + 補語 + 指定詞 ne
sato2008 20 :

toan kur arikinne wen pe ne | that person very bad thing COP | That person is a very bad one. | sato2008:20 | SAR

The complement may be a bare noun, a noun modified by an adnominalised verb, or itself a
nominalised clause refsing1986 145, 272 . Refsing states that
ne "cannot form a sentence by itself" and "always needs a nominal
complement" refsing1986 272 . Nakagawa's constituent parse of a copular
sentence — [Adverbial][Complement-NP][ne VP][ruwe ne clause-final] —
confirms that the copula heads the verb phrase with everything to its left serving as
complement or adverbial nakagawa2024 361 .

Person marking and verbal status

ne takes the transitive-subject (A) person affixes and never the
object (O) affixes. The three primary sources state the same morphological fact in different
analytical terms. Nakagawa classes it as a 2-place verb (2項動詞), sub-typed コピュラ
nakagawa2024 132 . Satō treats it as a distinct subclass 指定詞 (designative),
separated from ordinary transitives because it inflects and negates differently
sato2008 21 . Shibatani characterises it as taking "the same personal affixes
as do transitive verbs" on the grounds that it requires a complement to be semantically
complete shibatani1990 19 . Refsing and Tamura file it as a fourth verbal
subclass, the copula refsing1986 145 . For the earlier tradition, Kindaichi
called it an "incomplete intransitive" glossed 'become', and neither Kindaichi nor Chiri
applied the concept of copula to it — Tamura first identified it as such, as Refsing
documents refsing1986 145 ; kindaichi1936 ‹contested›. All
analyses agree on the morphological reflex: the 1SG prefix on
ne is ku= (A set), not
en= (O set).

aynu ku=ne | Ainu 1SG.A=COP | I am (an) Ainu. | nakagawa2024:132 | SAR

Nakagawa's minimal pair establishes the constraint directly.
okkayo ku=ne 'I am a man' is grammatical; the restructured
*tanpe kar pe en=ne , which places the object prefix
en= on the copula, is ungrammatical. The intended reading ('it is
me who made this') requires a different clause: kani tanpe ku=kar
nakagawa2024 132 . The complement noun phrase receives no person prefix;
only the subject position does. The person paradigm is set out in
person-marking-architecture .

person (Saru/Chitose)
prefix on ne
attested form
source

1SG
ku=
aynu ku=ne 'I am an Ainu'
nakagawa2024 132

2SG
e=
repunkur e=ne 'you are a foreigner'
refsing1986 145

3 (zero)
Ø
Taanpe seta ne 'this is a dog'
refsing1986 145

4 / indefinite
a=
atuy kor kamuy a=ne 'he is the sea-god'
nakagawa2024 132

The copula does not supplete for number: a single form ne serves both
singular and plural subjects nakagawa2024 146 . This distinguishes it sharply
from the existential an (sg) / oka(y) (pl),
which shows obligatory number suppletion tracking the subject
nakagawa2024 146 ; refsing1986 145 . Number suppletion in the
existential is treated in existential-locational-an-oka ; the claim that the copula
suppletively has a plural form is addressed in § Competing analyses below.

Semantic readings

Refsing describes the copula as covering "the equative and attributive meanings of the copula"
without filling existential or locative roles
refsing1986 145 ; nakagawa2022pos 474 . The attested uses fall
into three functional types. The sources consulted do not apply the Higgins four-way
classificational/specificational/identificational grid; the following typology is editorial
synthesis from the attested data, not a claim drawn from any single source
‹speculative›.

Classificational (class-membership): the subject is assigned to a category;
the complement is non-referential, denoting a kind. This is the default and most frequent use
nakagawa2024 132 ; sato2008 20 :

Taanpe seta ne | this dog COP | This is a dog. | refsing1986:145 | SHI

Equative / identificational : both subject and complement are referential noun
phrases placed in an identity relation refsing1986 145 :

Eani anak repunkur ne | 2SG TOP foreigner COP | You are a foreigner. | refsing1986:145 | SHI

Specificational / naming : the subject is a nominalised clause and the
complement supplies the label. The formal-noun evidential constructions
( hawe ne , ruwe ne ) are structurally of this
type — the formal noun nominalises a clause and ne predicates the
result. A naming instance at Nakagawa 2024: 392 exemplifies the pattern:
tap a=ye katu Piratur sekor a=ye hawe ne 'the way it is now said is
"Piratur"' nakagawa2024 392 . Whether Hokkaido Ainu morphosyntactically
distinguishes specificational from identificational readings is not addressed by any read
source ‹speculative›.

The stative–inchoative duality

ne carries no morphological mark for change of state: it covers both
'be X' and 'become X', a duality shared by other Ainu verbs including the existential
an nakagawa2022pos 477 . The same sentence
Tan-kur poro nispa ne can mean 'this person is a great chief' or
'this person became a great chief'; context determines the reading
shibatani1990 19 :

Tan-kur poro nispa ne | this-person big chief COP | This person is / became a great chief. | Shibatani ex. 15c. The same form is felicitous under both the stative ('is a chief') and the inchoative ('became a chief') readings; temporal context disambiguates. | shibatani1990:19 | ISH

The stative/inchoative range does not extend to property-concept verbs:
*hure ne 'become red' and *poro ne 'become big'
are ungrammatical. Property words already predicate inchoatively without a copula; inserting
ne after them is the classic learner error
nakagawa2024 119 . See property-concept-predication and
no-adjective-class-property-verbs .

Attributive ne in noun-noun compounds

Between two nouns, ne establishes identity or class membership within
the noun phrase — what Refsing classes as an attributive postposition alongside
un and kor
refsing1986 160–161 :

okkay ne po | man COP child | boy (lit. 'a child that is a man') | refsing1986:160 | SHI

mat ne unma | woman COP horse | mare (lit. 'a horse that is a woman') | refsing1986:160 | SHI

A third Refsing example: sapa ne kur 'chief' (lit. 'person who is a
head') refsing1986 160–161 . The construction is the attributive counterpart
of the predicative copular clause; ne is the same item in an
NP-internal position. For the concept-form and possessed morphology of noun heads in such
compounds, see concept-form-affiliative-form and
adnominal-possession-double-marking .

The mutative ne

The inchoative 'become' reading of ne grammaticalises further into a
mutative/translative case postposition marking the result of a change, glossed
transl :

unma ne kar | horse TRANSL make | make (it) into a horse | refsing1986:145 | SHI

Refsing notes that the dynamic 'become' use is the same ne that
functions elsewhere as the copula, now in a result-marking role
refsing1986 145 . A community discussion (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord
2024-11-13) characterises this as 転成格 '〜に（なる）' (mutative case) and observes that
~ne an — mutative ne plus the existential
an — corresponds transparently to 'become X', while
~ne kor is awkward because Japanese 'に持つ' ('hold as') is not a
natural expression aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. The grammaticalization
pathway from 'be' to a translative/result marker is cross-linguistically well attested
hoppertraugott2003 . The conjunctive particle
hine (< hi + ne ) is
a further grammaticalised descendant; see clause-linkage-overview .

Negation: X ka somo ne

Copular predicates are negated by the idiom X ka somo ne : the scalar
particle ka 'even' and the negative adverb
somo are inserted between the complement and the copula. The
alternative *ne ka somo ki does not occur
sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 407 :

tan pe anak seta ka somo ne wa | this thing TOP dog even NEG COP CONJ | This thing is not a dog, and … | sato2008:22 | SAR

This pattern sets the copula apart from property-concept verbs, which use standard clausal
negation ( somo V / V ka somo ki ), and from the
existential, which is negated by the suppletive lexical negative
isam :

predicate type
negation pattern
example
source

copula ne
X ka somo ne
seta ka somo ne 'is not a dog'
sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 407

existential an / oka
suppletive isam , or somo an
nep ka isam 'there is nothing'
nakagawa2024 450

property verb
somo V / V ka somo ki
merayke=an ka somo ki 'I am not cold'
nakagawa2024 127

Full treatments are in negation-somo-standard-clausal and
negation-existential-possessive-isam .

One under-described feature is that ne in
ka somo ne frequently carries no subject-person prefix —
and this prefix-drop also occurs, less regularly, outside negation. No read grammar catalogues
the behaviour; Satō 2008: 22 attests the idiom without commenting on its person-marking
pattern. A community observation (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-25) flags it as a
genuine micro-gap in the description, noting only that the reason is unclear
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. The interaction of copula negation with
TAM and the formal-noun tail is treated in nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation .

The formal-noun evidential tail

At the right edge of a clause, ne closes the formal-noun evidential
construction: a clause is nominalised by one of four bound formal nouns
( ruwe , siri , hawe ,
humi ), and the result is predicated with the copula. Structurally this
is the predicate-nominal clause described above, with a nominalised-clause subject. The copula
here is systematically opposed to the existential an : formal-noun +
ne marks the declarative; formal-noun + an
marks the content question nakagawa2024 392–395 :

frame
illocution
note

NMLZ + ne
declarative
copula predicates the nominalised clause as recognized fact

NMLZ + an?
content question
existential replaces copula; a question word opens the clause

bare NMLZ
polar question / exclamation
copula absent; insubordinated nominalized clause

Aynu ku=ne ru-we ne | Ainu 1SG.A=COP track-POSS COP | I am an Ainu. | The copular clause ku=ne is nominalised by the formal noun ruwe (the possessed form of ru 'track') and the outer copula ne predicates the whole as recognized fact. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 横山裕之, アイヌタイムズ 51, p. 1

The construction further cannot modify a common noun
( *pirka ruwe ne kewtum is ungrammatical), and its interaction with
clause-linking material is ordered — kusu ne ruwe ne is well-formed
but *ruwe ne kusu ne is not nakagawa2024 393 .
A community discussion (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09) confirms both constraints
aynucorporadiscord . The formal nouns that make the construction possible are
defined in formal-defective-nouns ; the evidential system as a whole is in
evidential-schema-overview ; and ruwe ne specifically in
ruwe-ne-inferential .

Competing analyses of ne

Four analytical disputes run through the literature and bear directly on how
ne is characterised.

Copula vs. 2-place verb vs. special subclass. The positions are surveyed in
§ Person marking and verbal status above ‹contested›. Practically, all analyses
converge on the same morphological facts: A-set prefixes only, no O prefix, no sentence-final
use without a complement. The terminological disagreement — whether to call
ne a copula subtype of the verb, a distinct 指定詞, or simply a
transitive-type verb — has no consequence for the glossing conventions used in this grammar
(which follow nakagawa2024 132 in treating it as a 2-place verb sub-typed COP).

Copula vs. existential — separation or overlap. Refsing states the division
most clearly: ne covers equative and attributive meanings while
an / oka covers existential and locative ones,
with no overlap refsing1986 145 . Nakagawa and Satō maintain the same
two-way partition: ne is 2-place, an is
1-place nakagawa2024 132, 146 ; sato2008 3–4, 20–21 .
Shibatani, by contrast, files the locational clause
ku-kor kotan ta oray-as 'we were in my village' under "Copular
sentences" and labels oray a "plural suppletive copula form"
shibatani1990 19 — conflating the existential plural with the copula ‹contested›.
The form oray does not appear in the Hokkaido corpus as a copula and
is unattested in that function in any other read source ‹corpus-suggested›; the Shibatani
example is most plausibly an existential locational clause.

Number invariance of the copula. On the Nakagawa/Refsing account, the copula
shows no number suppletion — one form ne for both singular and plural
subjects — in contrast to the existential pair an /
oka(y) nakagawa2024 146 . The only apparent
counterevidence is Shibatani's oray , dismissed under the preceding
point ‹consensus›.

Classificational/specificational typology. The sources consulted use "equative"
and "attributive/class-membership" as the descriptive labels for the semantic range of
ne refsing1986 145 ; nakagawa2022pos 474 .
No source applies the Higgins-style four-way grid to Ainu, and no read source tests whether
Hokkaido Ainu morphosyntactically distinguishes a specificational from an identificational
reading. The three-way table in § Semantic readings is editorial synthesis
‹speculative›.

The pronominal-argument question. Whether the overt complement NP is a
syntactic argument or an adjunct on a pronominal-argument head is unresolved. Refsing's
observation that ne "always needs a nominal complement" and "cannot
form a sentence by itself" refsing1986 272 points toward argument status, but
because the complement is never person-indexed, it occupies the same formally indeterminate
position as other non-pronominalised objects in a head-marking language
nakagawa2024 132 . The broader question is pursued in
person-marking-architecture ‹contested›.

title: Cyrillic and Multi-Script Rendering
summary: The Cyrillic transcription of Ainu in the Russian and Sakhalin tradition and the interoperability of Latin, katakana, and Cyrillic renderings.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Cyrillic and Multi-Script Rendering
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: The Cyrillic transcription of Ainu in the Russian and Sakhalin tradition and the interoperability of Latin, katakana, and Cyrillic renderings.

Three transcriptional traditions

Scholarly and documentary work on Ainu has operated in three transcriptional traditions. The
Latin phonemic transcription developed by the twentieth-century descriptive tradition
is the primary script of the major reference grammars tamura1996 ;
refsing1986 ; nakagawa2024 and of modern corpus annotation. The
katakana syllabary , extended with small-kana coda characters, is the dominant script
of native-speaker writing and of modern Hokkaido institutional materials; Nakagawa's annotated
text editions and the NINJAL oral-literature corpus present Ainu in katakana. A third body of
material, the output of the Russian scholarly tradition on Sakhalin Ainu from the
early twentieth century onward, uses a Cyrillic-based transcription modelled on Russian
phonographic conventions.

All Ainu forms in this grammar appear in the Latin system, normalized from whatever source
script they originate in. The phoneme-to-grapheme mapping is documented in
latin-phonemic-transcription ; the katakana syllabary and its small-kana
extensions are the subject of katakana-and-small-kana-codas .

The Latin phonemic transcription

The Latin conventions followed here match the modern Hokkaido descriptive standard. The
affricate phoneme is written c , not ch ; the fricative phoneme
s , not sh — a normalisation reflecting the absence of a
palatal-vs-non-palatal phonemic contrast and now standard in scholarly and community usage
kirikae1997 ; nakagawa2006 ; community discussion (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord, 2023) aynucorporadiscord . Native-speaker writers
and the major descriptive grammars converge on this choice: Kirikae's survey of Ainu-authored
orthographic practice shows that speakers who wrote their language themselves consistently
avoided the yōon spellings kirikae1997 .

Prevocalic glottal onset is not marked in the modern literature; earlier transcriptions by
Tamura and others marked it with an apostrophe (e.g. 'a ), and an
apostrophe notation persists in some corpus metadata, but the omission has been standard since
the 1980s shiraishi2022 ; community discussion (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord,
2023) aynucorporadiscord . The boundary markers =
and - carry a specific contrast introduced by Nakagawa:
= marks personal-index boundaries and - marks
derivational boundaries. Nakagawa adopted this device in his 1986 text collection
nakagawa2024 49 , after which its use became general; community record
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) aynucorporadiscord . The
morphological implications of the distinction — whether individual indexes are clitics or
affixes — are examined in orthographic-standardization-and-word-division .

Katakana as a parallel notation

For sources in katakana, the katakana surface form is displayed alongside the Latin morphemic
analysis; the Latin form drives the interlinear glossing. Standard Japanese katakana covers
most Ainu CV syllables directly. The coda consonants of closed Ainu syllables — a structural
feature with no equivalent in standard Japanese orthography — are represented by small-kana
characters added in the Unicode Katakana Phonetic Extensions block (U+31F0–U+31FF). The table
below gives the vowel correspondences and the coda-consonant assignments; the full CV-syllable
mapping, including variant representations for the affricate c and the
handling of nasal assimilation, is documented in katakana-and-small-kana-codas .

Latin–katakana correspondences: vowels and coda consonants

Latin
Katakana
Notes

Vowels

a
ア

i
イ

u
ウ

e
エ

o
オ

Coda consonants (small kana appended to the syllable kana)

-k
ㇰ
U+31F0; sik 'eye' → シㇰ

-m
ㇺ
U+31FA; hum 'sound' → フㇺ

-n
ン
Full-size ン; a dedicated small ン ( 𛅧 , U+1B167) exists
for the non-moraic nasal coda but has limited font support; community discussion
(toracatman, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023) aynucorporadiscord

-p
ㇷ゚
Composed of ㇷ (U+31F7) + combining semi-voiced mark
(U+309A); nep 'what' → ネㇷ゚

-r
ㇻ ㇼ ㇽ ㇾ ㇿ
Vowel-indexed: ㇻ after a ,
ㇼ after i ,
ㇽ after u ,
ㇾ after e ,
ㇿ after o ; e.g.
sir 'appearance' → シㇼ ,
kor 'have' → コㇿ

-s
ㇱ
U+31F1

-t
ㇳ
U+31F3

The coda -r is the only one whose small-kana character encodes the
colour of the preceding vowel; the other codas each have a single character regardless of
vowel context. Font support for the full Unicode coda set, including the two-character
sequence ㇷ゚ for -p , varies across
platforms; the practical rendering situation is surveyed in
katakana-and-small-kana-codas .

The Cyrillic tradition in Sakhalin scholarship

Russian-language academic work on Sakhalin Ainu, beginning with fieldwork by linguists such
as Nikolai Nevsky in the early twentieth century, transcribed the language in Cyrillic modelled
on Russian phonographic conventions, with additional characters accommodating sounds absent from
Russian — a pattern familiar from Soviet minority-language orthographic policy; asserted by
nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) aynucorporadiscord . The dominant
accessible sources on Sakhalin Ainu consulted in this grammar arrive in Latin or katakana rather
than Cyrillic: Piłsudski's 1912 materials use a Latin-based Romanist transcription
pilsudski1912 , Murasaki's grammar and her later introductory volume present
Sakhalin Ainu in katakana murasaki1979 ; murasaki2025 , and Dal Corso's
re-edition of Murasaki's material and his study of the Piłsudski-corpus phonology apply the
Latin system dalcorso2021 ; dalcorso2024 .

Community members in the aynu-corpora orthographic discussions have proposed systematic Cyrillic
correspondence tables for a normalized Sakhalin Ainu Cyrillic orthography, modelling the system
on Soviet minority-language conventions (scheuewsuiski and nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord,
December 2024) aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. No such system has entered
formal institutional use. Forms from Cyrillic-script sources are transliterated to the Latin
system before they appear in any example or inline context in this grammar; the structural and
phonological contrasts that motivate citing Sakhalin material are treated in
sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast .

Script policy

The following conventions apply to all Ainu forms in this grammar:

Latin is canonical. Every Ainu form — whether cited from a Latin-script
source, from a katakana source, or transliterated from a Cyrillic source — appears in the
Latin phonemic transcription, in the morphemic line of an interlinear example or as an
italicised inline form.

Katakana is a parallel surface layer. For sources in katakana, the
katakana surface form is shown above the Latin morphemic analysis; the Latin line drives
the interlinear glossing.

Cyrillic is excluded from the text. No Cyrillic characters appear in this
grammar. Forms from Cyrillic-script sources are transliterated to the Latin system before
any citation; the source is then cited in the normal way.

Sakhalin and Kuril provenance is marked by the dialect tag. The
SA or KU tag on an example — drawn from
the fixed dialect-label set documented in
glossing-abbreviations-and-citation — is the sole in-text indicator that a
form is from Sakhalin or Kuril Ainu rather than Hokkaido. Japanese glosses and source
titles carry Japanese attribution and are not subject to the Ainu-script conventions above.

title: Demonstratives, Anaphora, and Definiteness
summary: The exophoric demonstrative distance system, the anaphoric/recognitional 'aforementioned' demonstrative, and definiteness in an article-less language.
part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Demonstratives, Anaphora, and Definiteness
Part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
Summary: The exophoric demonstrative distance system, the anaphoric/recognitional 'aforementioned' demonstrative, and definiteness in an article-less language.

Overview: a bipartite deixis system

Hokkaido Ainu demonstratives divide into two formally distinct sets. One set —
the deictic (on-site) forms — points to referents in the physical scene;
the other — the anaphoric forms — tracks referents established in
discourse. Japanese uses a single three-way この/その/あの
series for both functions; Hokkaido Ainu keeps the two apart with different lexical
roots nakagawa2024 284 . Kindaichi and Chiri's classical grammar
already identified the deictic prenominals as fusions of three locative roots
( te 'here', ta 'there',
to 'yonder') with the copula
an , while the anaphoric
ne -series points in the discourse stream, not in space
kindaichi1936 57–58 . Refsing's Shizunai description formalises
the whole system as a two-dimensional root paradigm
refsing1986 109 ; the typological framing of Diessel's
demonstrative taxonomy maps the anaphoric forms onto a recognitional–anaphoric
cluster distinct from both exophoric pointing and grammaticalized definite articles
diessel1999 .

Demonstrative prenominals of Saru Hokkaido Ainu nakagawa2024 283–284

type
singular
plural
value

deictic
tan
—
this (immediately at hand)

taan
taoka(y)
this / that (in speaker's sphere, not at hand)

toan
tooka(y)
that (away from both speaker and addressee)

toon
—
that (farther than toan )

anaphoric
ne
—
the (immediately just-mentioned)

nea
nerok
the aforementioned / that same (established referent)

newaan
newaoka(y)
the aforementioned (more emphatic)

Minor deictic prenominals in Saru include verse-register
tanan (pl. tanoka(y) ) and
emphatic tapan (pl. tapoka(y) ),
the latter most frequent in Chitose address contexts
nakagawa2024 284 . The independent personal pronouns are treated
in independent-personal-pronouns ; the interrogative and indefinite
pro-forms in interrogative-pro-forms .

Deictic prenominals

The etymology of the deictic prenominals is transparent. Kindaichi and Chiri
derive tan from ta + copula
an , and toan from
to + an ; the medial
taan is the long (information-richer) variant of
tan , and the plurals
tanokay / tonokay are formed by
fusing the root with the plural existential okay
kindaichi1936 58 ; nakagawa2024 288 . The
functional difference between tan and
taan parallels Japanese
この vs. その : tan is used when
the referent is immediately at hand, taan when it lies
within the speaker's perceptual sphere but is not immediately present, and
toan when it is away from both speaker and addressee
nakagawa2024 283–284 .
Community discussion has proposed an iconic account of the vowel gradient:
longer form encodes more spatial distance-information, front raised vowel signals
proximity, back lowered vowel signals remoteness ‹speculative›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2025-12-27). The functional
distribution is uncontroversial nakagawa2024 283–284 ; the
iconicity explanation is unverified.

taan cise pirka | DEM.PROX house be.good | That house (in sight, nearby) is good. | Illustrative. taan signals the referent is within the speaker's sphere but not immediately at hand, the closest HA equivalent to Japanese sono. Compare tan cise 'this house (right here)' and toan cise 'that house over there'. | SAR

The distal toan is also the principal vehicle for nominal
third-person reference: Hokkaido Ainu uses
toan kur 'that person' or
nea kur 'that person (aforementioned)' where languages
with grammaticalized third-person pronouns use them, since the third-person
pronouns of the paradigm treated in independent-personal-pronouns
are rare in actual discourse nakagawa2024 256 .

toan kur hunna an | DEM.DIST person who exist | Who is that person? | From アコㇿイタㇰ ch. 2. The question word hunna 'who' is the h-series form of Saru and Chitose; eastern and northern dialects have the n-series instead: toan kur nen ta an (see the chapter on interrogative pro-forms). The toan kur frame illustrates the demonstrative as the carrier of third-person deictic reference. | utari1994 | SAR

Anaphoric and recognitional demonstratives

The anaphoric prenominals — ne ,
nea , newaan — are formally
separate from the deictic set and cannot be used to point at a scene-present
referent. nea picks up a referent already established
in the discourse; in a narrative, nea menoko means
'the woman (who was just spoken of)' and does not admit a deictic
(scene-pointing) reading nakagawa2024 284 . The weaker
ne marks a referent as immediately prior in the
discourse; newaan is more emphatic.

arpa akusu nea menoko | go CONJ DEM.ANAPH woman | …when (he) went, that woman (the one already introduced)… | Fragment from a Saru narrative. The form nea picks up a female character introduced earlier; no scene-pointing interpretation is possible in the narrative context. This discourse-tracking use accounts for the form's high corpus frequency. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子 uwepeker 民話 11, AA研アイヌ語資料 [aa-irc/013#94]

Corpus counts confirm the predominance of the anaphoric function in narrative
text: the surface string nea occurs approximately 1,984
times in the ainu-corpora aligned set — substantially more than
toan (c. 735 tokens) and
tanpe (c. 391 tokens) combined — consistent with its
role as the primary anaphora-tracking device of narrative ‹corpus-confirmed›
nakagawa2024 287 .
(Surface counts conflate any homophonous forms and are upper bounds.)

Third-person reference. Where Japanese uses
彼 / 彼女 , Hokkaido Ainu characteristically uses a
demonstrative NP — nea menoko 'that woman (referred to)',
toan kur 'that person (over there)'. The third-person
and fourth-person pronouns of the independent paradigm occur, but they are
rare; the demonstrative NP is the default anaphora-tracking strategy in oral
literature and in narrative prose
nakagawa2024 256 . The interaction of this strategy with
pronominal-argument interpretations of the person affixes is considered in
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument .

Dialect variation.
nea is pan-Hokkaido; most dialects outside Saru and
Chitose use nean (pronoun
neanpe ) — attested in Horobetsu, Shizunai, and
Ishikari, with both forms occurring in some Horobetsu and Ishikari speakers
nakagawa2024 285–286 . Shizunai and the dialects to the east
additionally have ikia (pl.
ikirok ; pronoun ikiap ),
a second anaphoric prenominal functionally close to
nea but more restricted in scope; it is rare in Saru
nakagawa2024 285 .
The Ijäs pedagogical grammar covers the core
nea forms alongside the deictic series
ijas2023 .

Demonstrative pronouns

Each prenominal may fuse with the formal noun
p thing (nominalizer) ( ~pe ) to produce a
demonstrative pronoun referring to a thing rather than modifying a noun head:
tan-pe 'this (thing)',
toan-pe 'that (thing)',
newaan-pe / nea-p 'the
aforementioned thing', and the eastern
ikia-p (Shizunai-and-east)
nakagawa2024 287 . The suffix
alternation -pe / -p follows
the phonological environment of the nominalizer, treated in
nominalization-p-pe .

tan-pe anak su sekor a=ye p ne | DEM.PROX-NMLZ TOP pot QUOT 4.A=say NMLZ COP | tanpe anak su sekor a=ye p ne | This is what is called a pot. | Nakagawa ex. 389. The demonstrative pronoun tanpe heads the topic NP; the predicate is the quotative frame sekor a=ye p 'what (one) calls', nominalised by p, closed by the copula ne. | nakagawa2024:287 | SAR

The indefinite pronoun nep 'something / what' — formed
from the same root ne- plus the nominalizer — is an
interrogative/indefinite, not a demonstrative. For anaphoric reference to a
thing, Hokkaido Ainu uses newaanpe or
neap , not nep
nakagawa2024 287 . This gap in the anaphoric pronoun paradigm
is an incidental consequence of nep 's grammaticalization
as an indefinite/interrogative; the full indefinite and interrogative series are
treated in interrogative-pro-forms .

Locative roots and case-marked forms

The three roots te here , ta there , and
to yonder function as locative nouns taking case particles
directly, not as prenominal modifiers. Only ta and
to enter the prenominal by fusing with the copula
( ta-an → tan ,
to-an → toan ). The proximal
root te does not produce a prenominal and cannot
precede a head noun — *te menoko is ungrammatical;
te only takes case particles
( te ta 'here', te un 'this way',
te wano 'from now') nakagawa2024 289 .

te ta k=an | here LOC 1SG.S=exist | I am here. | Constructed. The proximal locative root te takes the case particle ta. te cannot head an NP (*te menoko 'this woman' is ungrammatical) and never yields a prenominal form. | SAR

Case-marked forms of the deictic prenominals arise by ellipsis of the formal
noun hi before the case particle:
taan ta 'here' <
taan hi ta 'the-place-of-this at';
toan un 'over there (toward)' <
toan hi un nakagawa2024 288 .
These locative uses intersect with the relational-noun system; see
relational-and-spatial-nouns .

The Shizunai demonstrative grid and dialect variation

Refsing's Shizunai description assembles the demonstrative, interrogative,
and indefinite forms into a two-dimensional paradigm of roots and semantic
dimensions refsing1986 109 . It is the most explicit systematic
statement of the opposition's internal logic in any published Hokkaido
description.

Demonstrative–interrogative–indefinite root grid, Shizunai dialect (after Refsing 1986: 109, Diagram 2)

dimension
anaphoric ne-
proximal ta-
distal to-
interrogative
indefinite ( …ka )

Manner (adverb)
ene / ne koraci 'thus'
taa koraci 'in this way'
too 'in that way'
nekon 'how'
nekonka 'somehow'

Individual (thing / person)
nean pe 'the aforementioned'
taan pe 'this'
toon pe 'that'; tooka pe 'those'
nep 'what'; nen 'who'
nepka 'anything'; nenka 'anyone'

Place
—
taanta 'here'
toonta 'there'
neyta 'where'
neytaka 'somewhere'

Adnominal
nean / neoka / ne 'the said'
taan / tan 'this'
toon / ton 'that'; tooka 'those'
neun 'which'
—

Two observations follow from the grid. The anaphoric root
ne- is formally identical to the interrogative root of
nep 'what', nen 'who', and
neyta 'where', and to the copula
ne . Refsing flags this convergence as possibly more than
coincidence refsing1986 108 ‹speculative›. The
place-row entry for the anaphoric column is empty — a systematic gap: there is
no form *neta or *neyta
functioning as 'the aforementioned place'.

Intra-Hokkaido variation affects chiefly the anaphoric
prenominal and the distal pair.

nea vs. nean .
Saru and Chitose use nea ; most other Hokkaido
dialects (Horobetsu, Shizunai, Ishikari) use
nean (pronoun neanpe ), with
both forms occurring in some Horobetsu and Ishikari speakers
nakagawa2024 285–286 .

ikia / ikiap .
Shizunai and the dialects east of it have a second anaphoric prenominal
ikia (pl. ikirok ; pronoun
ikiap ), functionally close to
nea but appearing to be more restricted in discourse
scope. It is rare in Saru nakagawa2024 285 .

toan vs. toon .
Saru favours toan ; both forms occur in Chitose;
Obihiro and Tokachi avoid toan ; Ishikari lacks
toon
nakagawa2024 285 . Takahashi's Tokachi description documents
the local demonstrative system and the range of deictic forms available there
takahashi2011 .

Definiteness in an article-less language

Hokkaido Ainu has no grammatical article. Neither the deictic nor the anaphoric
demonstratives are obligatory in contexts where a definite-article language would
require an article; bare nouns occur freely in argument position without
referential specification nakagawa2024 283 ‹consensus›. The
language encodes identifiability through a cluster of constructions, none of
which individually constitutes an article system:

Anaphoric demonstrative
nea / ne marks a referent
as established and salient in discourse — the functional counterpart of the
definite article in post-introduction contexts. Its high corpus frequency
relative to the deictic forms (§3 above) is consistent with this
article-like role ‹corpus-suggested›.

Possessive morphology. The personal possessor prefix
combined with the concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative form
of an inalienable noun singles out the referent as uniquely identifiable
relative to its possessor. The alienable/inalienable split and the
semantics of obligatory possession are treated in
alienable-inalienable-split .

Proper nouns and naming are inherently referentially unique
and carry no additional definiteness morphology.

Discourse position. A sentence-initial topic NP is
typically presupposed as identifiable; first-mention NPs in non-topic
position are indefinite by discourse default, without any morphological
signal.

Whether nea should be analysed as a grammaticalized
definite article is contested ‹contested›. Against the article analysis:
nea is absent from many clearly definite contexts
where English or Japanese would require the article; it alternates with a bare
noun in narrative without apparent semantic contrast; and its distribution is
better described as anaphoric-recognitional — marking a referent as
recoverable from discourse — than as an absolute definiteness category
nakagawa2024 284 . Refsing glosses the Shizunai cognate as
'the said', which aligns with recognitional use rather than a grammaticalized
article refsing1986 109 . In Diessel's typology, recognitional
demonstratives track referents that are identifiable through shared knowledge or
discourse memory but are not fully grammaticalized articles
diessel1999 . The related claim — that the
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative form of certain nouns
carries definiteness-like semantics in post-possessive contexts — is evaluated
in that chapter. For the clause-level question of how topics and foci interact
with the demonstrative system, see noun-phrase-structure .

The interaction of the demonstrative system with clausal interrogation — in
particular with sentence-final question particles and with the interrogative
existential frame — is treated in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

title: Denominal and Deverbal Verb Derivation
summary: Non-valency processes that build verbs from nouns and from other verbs, including verb-verb compounding and noun/verb conversion.
part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Denominal and Deverbal Verb Derivation
Part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
Summary: Non-valency processes that build verbs from nouns and from other verbs, including verb-verb compounding and noun/verb conversion.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu derives verbal stems from nominal and verbal sources through mechanisms that must
be distinguished from the valency and voice operations treated in the chapters routed from
valency-voice-overview . Valency operations — applicatives, causatives, antipassive,
reflexive, and reciprocal — alter the verb's external argument count in a principled and largely
productive way. The processes documented below either place a base into an argument class without
changing that count, or build a new stem whose argument class is determined by the source. They
include the combination of bound nominal roots with verbal heads, the copula
ne be, become and the transitive verb kar make, do as routes for
nominal predication, adverbial prefixes, and the deverbal stem modifications that alter lexical
aspect or number without altering argument structure.

The architecture is head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 : a derived stem
must enter one of the four argument-count classes — zero-place ambient predicates, one-place
intransitives, two-place transitives, or three-place verbs with two bare objects — and the
person-index set then diagnoses the class of the result. Nakagawa assigns every formative a
numerical arity value so that the derived form's external argument capacity is computable from
the visible morphology nakagawa2024 136–139 . Satō accepts the same
head-marking premise but separates three simultaneously active valency dimensions: an invariant
argument-slot count, macrorole transitivity, and the morphological class of the derived form;
the copula ne and three-place verbs motivate the split sato2023b 42–61 .
The two accounts are presented together where they produce the same classification and
distinguished where they diverge ‹contested›.

formative or pattern
arity value (Nakagawa)
verb-forming role
chapter route

bound nominal root, e.g. me- 'coldness', sir- 'surroundings'
−1
denominal base in ambient and compound verbs; often yields a zero-place predicate
§ below; noun-incorporation-object for productive object incorporation

concept-form nominal prefixes ( i- , u- , yay- , si- )
−1
slot-filling; most are voice or valency operations
concept-form-affiliative-form ; voice chapters via valency-voice-overview

affiliative nominal prefixes ( e- 'the head of', o- 'the bottom of')
0
add body-part or positional material without changing the slot count
concept-form-affiliative-form

copula ne with a nominal predicate
+2 (Nakagawa); Satō: macrorole = 1
nominal predication and denominal stative or inchoative predicate
§ below; copula-ne-predicate-nominals

light verb kar 'make, do' with a nominal complement
+2 (lexical transitive)
'make/do N'; the word–phrase boundary is often unsettled
§ below; light-and-pro-verbs

adverbial prefixes ar- 'completely', ru- 'slightly'
0
modify degree or completeness; argument frame unchanged
verb-word-template

applicative e- , ko- , o-
+1
add or promote a non-agent object; valency-increasing
applicative-system-overview

productive causative -re , -e , -te
+1
add a causer argument; valency-increasing
causative-morphological-re-e-te

non-productive transitivity-pair suffixes ( -V , -ke , -ka )
0
lexical pair formation between stative or inchoative and causative or transitive stems
transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs

verbal-number suffix -pa , suppletive plural stems, reduplication
0
stem alternation for number or aspect; no effect on the argument frame
suppletive-verbal-number

Denominal ambient predicates

The most clearly documented denominal verbs in the Hokkaido evidence are ambient zero-place
predicates built from bound nominal roots. Nakagawa classifies
sirpirka be fine weather (< sir surroundings, weather +
pirka be good ), mean be cold (weather) (< me-
'coldness root' + an exist ), hawas it is said (<
haw voice + as stand ), sirkunne get dark , and
sitcuk become autumn as zero-place verbs: they take neither a subject nor an
object, and therefore no person index appears on them nakagawa2024 128–130 .
Shibatani describes the same ambient and weather predicates as a form of subject incorporation,
with the nominal element supplying the domain that an ordinary intransitive would predicate of
an external subject shibatani1990 33 . The fuller treatment of the incorporative
analysis is in noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding .

The autonomy of the source noun determines whether the string constitutes a single word or a
two-word sequence. The roots sir- and me- cannot
stand as ordinary nouns in the relevant predicates — *sir anak pirka and
*me ka an are ungrammatical — so sirpirka and
mean are single zero-place verbs. The noun apto rain ,
by contrast, is free, and Nakagawa leaves apto as 'it rains' undecided
between a zero-place compound and a two-word noun-plus-verb clause
nakagawa2024 129–130 ‹contested›. The same bound-vs-free diagnostic applies
to other weather-like expressions.

A set of temperature terms shows the zero-place and one-place contrast side by side. The
zero-place member describes the ambient climate and carries no person index; the one-place member
describes a felt bodily state and carries the intransitive subject index.

semantic domain
zero-place climate predicate
one-place felt-state predicate

cold
me-an
me-rayke

cool
sir-me-man
me-man

warm
sir-popke
popke

hot
sir-sesek
sesek

me-an cuk ne pe me-rayke=an ka somo ki no | coldness-exist autumn COP NMLZ coldness-kill=4.S even NEG do CONJ | It was a cold autumn, but I did not feel cold. | The zero-place climate predicate me-an carries no person index. The one-place felt-state predicate me-rayke carries the fourth-person S suffix =an. Both contain the coldness root me-, but they belong to different argument classes. | nakagawa2024:130 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 15, after Kayano 1974a

Property predicates — forms such as poro be big; become big and
pirka be good; become good — are intransitive verbs with no separate adjective
class; a stative reading ordinarily carries an inchoative one as well nakagawa2024 124 ; shibatani1990 33–34 . Their clausal behaviour is treated as a subclass in
no-adjective-class-property-verbs and property-concept-predication .
The denominal ambient predicates above are their zero-place counterpart: the nominal root takes
the same finite-verb template but licenses no external argument.

The copula ne as a denominal predicate head

The copula ne be, become is the primary path from a nominal expression to a
finite predicate. A noun or noun phrase precedes ne as a predicate
nominal, and the result is a two-place construction with a subject and a predicate nominal
argument. Nakagawa classifies ne as a two-place verb, yet it admits
only the subject person index: the object-indexed form *en=ne is
excluded in his diagnostic example, while the subject takes the transitive-subject prefix set
nakagawa2024 132 ; shibatani1990 49–50 .

okkayo ku=ne | man 1SG.A=COP | I am a man. | The subject carries the prefixal transitive-subject form ku=; no object index appears. Nakagawa contrasts this with the ungrammatical *en=ne in the same passage. | nakagawa2024:132 | HK | Nakagawa ex. 23

Satō uses this pattern to motivate the separation of three valency dimensions. The
argument-slot count of ne is two, since the construction predicates a
class or identity; its macrorole transitivity is one, since the predicate nominal is not a
referential undergoer and therefore licenses no object affix; its morphological valency is
transitive, which explains the transitive-subject-prefix pattern. Lexicalized denominal
compounds such as nispa a=ne 'I am a master' and
inne 'be numerous' (< ir-ne ) follow the
same transitive-prefix pattern sato2023b 52 ‹contested›. Nakagawa's surface
observation that ne never takes an object index and Satō's principled
account of why it does not are two descriptions of the same morphological fact.

Because Ainu property verbs carry an inchoative reading alongside their stative one, a noun plus
ne can similarly express both 'be X' and, in the appropriate aspectual
context, 'become X'. A productive denominal suffix -ne detached from
the copular construction has no independent support in the available evidence; lexicalized forms
such as inne are best analysed as the copula incorporated into a compound
stem rather than as the output of an autonomous verbalizing suffix ‹corpus-suggested›. The
syntax and semantics of predicate-nominal constructions are treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

kar and the word–phrase boundary

The transitive verb kar make, do supplies a second denominal route. A noun or
nominalized expression may function as the object of kar , and frequent
collocations may lexicalize as single verb stems. The synchronic default is that
kar is an independent lexical transitive verb. Nakagawa's example
tanpe kar pe en=ne , cited to show the copula's blocked object index,
shows kar functioning as the verb of a relative clause 'the one who
made this'; it takes ordinary transitive morphology and is clearly separate from
ne nakagawa2024 132 . Horobetsu
kar-ici 'make, do' — with the eastern plural suffix
ci inside the person index — is a verbal-number fact about the stem
itself, not evidence for a productive denominal verbalizer nakagawa2024 154–155 .

A transparent N kar sequence is therefore a two-word clause with
kar as its verb. A single derived verb stem arises only where
morphological diagnostics confirm it: fixed wordhood, phonological fusion, or person and
number morphology showing that the nominal element has been incorporated into the verb word
rather than occupying an external object slot. Nakagawa applies the same bound-vs-free
diagnostic to the weather predicates: bound roots such as me- and
sir- support a single-word analysis, while free nouns leave the
boundary unsettled nakagawa2024 129–130 . Without those independent tests,
the default analysis of N kar is a two-word clause ‹corpus-suggested›.
The light-verb and pro-verb uses of kar , including its behaviour with
incorporated objects, are treated in light-and-pro-verbs and
noun-incorporation-object .

Deverbal derivation without valency change

Several deverbal processes change the shape or the lexical-aspect profile of a verb stem without
altering its argument frame. Nakagawa assigns arity 0 to adverbial prefixes such as
ar- 'completely' and ru- 'slightly': they modify
the degree or thoroughness of the denoted event and leave the external argument count unchanged
nakagawa2024 137 . Their position in the verb template and their interaction
with person indexing are set out in verb-word-template .

Reduplication adds iterativity, continuity, or intensity without altering the argument count.
Nakagawa distinguishes root reduplication, partial-stem reduplication, and full-stem
reduplication: turi stretch (sth) yields tutturi
(root-reduplicated, marking repeated action), turiri
(partial, marking result-state persistence), and turituri (full, marking
sustained repetition). He treats reduplication as encoding lexical aspect rather than verbal
number, though the two interact at the surface nakagawa2024 151–153 ;
nakagawa2022 §1 .

The Type-C vowel-replacement alternation creates plural stems from singular ones without
changing argument count: tuye cut (sg) / tuypa cut (pl) ,
yasa tear (sg) / yaspa tear (pl) . Nakagawa assigns arity 0 to
the suffix -pa in this pattern, so the plural stem enters the same
argument class as the singular source nakagawa2024 137, 149–150 . The full
verbal-number system — Types A (suppletive), B ( -n/-p ), and C
( -V/-pa ), the auxiliary pa , and the relationship
between subject vs object plurality and stem choice — is treated in
suppletive-verbal-number .

Derived intransitives formed by prefixes such as si- ,
he- , and ho- on transitive bases inherit the
verbal-number type of their source. Nakagawa lists si-turi /
si-turpa 'stretch (intr)', ho-puni /
ho-punpa 'get up, fly up', and ho-yupu /
ho-yuppa 'run' as derived intransitives that still undergo the
Type-C alternation nakagawa2024 150–151 . Number agreement in these forms
follows the subject — the single argument of the intransitive — because the prefix has
converted the transitive base to an intransitive. The argument class of the output, not
the source, determines the number-agreement target nakagawa2024 145–146 .
The prefixes themselves, insofar as they create anticausative or middle-like readings, are
treated in the voice chapters via valency-voice-overview .

Transitivity pairs

Hokkaido Ainu has almost no productive lability: a single stem serving as both intransitive and
transitive is extremely rare. Satō reports only roski stand (pl) / stand sth up (pl)
as the standard case of a form that fills both slots, and he presents it as an exception rather
than a productive pattern sato2008 97 . The near-absence of lability means that
most intransitive–transitive correspondences are lexical pairs, partial derivations, or
suppletive-looking forms that require separate learning sato2008 97 ;
nakagawa2024 131–132 .

Three formal correspondence types occur. First, identical surface forms: only
roski . Second, partial formal similarity, as in
nunpa strain, wring (vt) and inunpa strain liquor for ritual offering
(vi), where the intransitive member is analyzable as the antipassive prefix
i- attached to the transitive base, though the pair has lexicalized to
distinct meanings sato2008 97 . Third, no formal similarity at all — suppletive-looking
pairs of basic vocabulary where the relationship between the two stems is not synchronically
derivational nakagawa2024 131 . Selected pairs of the third type are listed below.

meaning area
intransitive (1-place)
transitive (2-place)

look / see sth
inkar
nukar

hear / hear sth
inu
nu

steal (as activity) / steal sth
ikka
eikka

laugh / laugh at sth
mina
emina

eat a meal / eat sth specific
ipe
e

speak / say sth
itak
ye

think (intr) / think of sth
yaynu
ramu

The class membership of a form is diagnosed formally, not semantically. A vi takes the
suffixal fourth-person subject marker ( =an ) and the suffixal
first-person-plural exclusive marker ( =as ); a vt takes the
corresponding prefixal forms ( a= , ci= ).
The contrast between inkar and nukar —
glossed by the same Japanese verb 見る — appears within a single sentence:

inkar=an akus e=an ru-we a=nukar | look=4.S then 2SG.S=exist track-POSS 4.A=see | When I looked, I saw that you were there. | The vi stem inkar carries the fourth-person S suffix =an. The vt stem nukar carries the fourth-person A prefix a=. Both index the same narrative first person, but their different morphological position reveals the grammatical transitivity distinction. | nakagawa2024:131 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 18

Bugaeva and Kobayashi identify a related pattern of uncoded role alternation. Certain one-place
verbs may take either an agent-like or a theme-like subject without any change in form:
as stand can express both 'stand there (agent subject)' and 'stand upright
(theme subject)'. The alternation becomes visible under causativization — an agent subject
yields the indirect 'make sb stand' formation, a theme subject the direct 'stand sth up'
formation bugaevakobayashi2022 §5 . That distinction intersects with the
transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs transitivity-pair suffix system , where the
inventory of -V , -ke , and
-ka formations and their productivity are treated in detail.

Valency-changing derivation routed outward

Productive valency-changing operations form a separate system whose paradigms are documented in
the voice and valency chapters. Applicatives e- ,
ko- , and o- add or promote an object and raise
the verb's arity by one; the productive causatives -re ,
-e , and -te add a causer argument; antipassive
i- absorbs the object and yields an intransitive; reflexives
yay- / si- and reciprocal
u- fill the object slot internally so that a subject and an object of
the same person are not both indexed by person affixes bugaevakobayashi2022 §4 ;
nakagawa2024 137–139, 183 . Their paradigms are in
applicative-system-overview , causative-morphological-re-e-te ,
antipassive-detransitive-i , reflexive-yay ,
reflexive-possessive-si , and reciprocal-u .

The homophony of the prefix e- shows why holding these categories apart
matters. In one derived verb it is the applicative with arity +1 (adding an object); in another
it is the affiliative nominal prefix with arity 0 (contributing a body-part relation); in another
it is a second-person object index with arity −1. Nakagawa's worked examples
a=ureepunpa 'I kicked it up with my foot' (applicative, arity +1) and
a=koinawepunpa 'we offered inau' (affiliative, arity 0) are
distinguished only by the arity computation, since the resulting argument count differs
nakagawa2024 138–139 . The affiliative nominal prefix is defined in
concept-form-affiliative-form .

Locative-object verbs form a small class whose distinguishing property is that their object is
a bare place expression. Nakagawa lists oma be in / enter a place ,
osma plunge into a place , and kus pass through a place as
two-place verbs of this type. The single base-form three-place verb in his inventory is
o put X into a place ; derived three-place locative-object verbs such as
omare put into , osmare plunge into , and
kuste pass through are formed by applicative or causative processes from
non-locative bases nakagawa2024 212–213 .

upas tum a=oma wa karkarse=an | snow midst 4.A=enter CONJ roll=4.S | I sank into the snow and rolled. | The locative-object verb oma takes the bare place expression upas tum as its object; no case marker intervenes. The conjunctive particle wa links the clause to the following intransitive karkarse, which carries the fourth-person S suffix =an. | nakagawa2024:212 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 221

The derivational chain that Satō documents on the base ruska be angry at
illustrates the regularity of the full valency system: applicative ko-
raises the base to a three-place verb ( ko-ruska ); reflexive
yay- reduces it ( yay-ko-ruska ); antipassive
i- yields a one-place intransitive ( i-ruska );
applicative ko- re-applies ( ko-i-ruska ); and
causative -re raises that in turn ( i-ruska-re ,
ko-i-ruska-re , and further) sato2023b 40 ‹consensus›.
Each step alters the verb's argument class by a calculable amount; the individual formatives
are treated in the chapters named above.

Ijäs's L2–L3 framework

Ijäs's Basic Lessons 2 and 3 supply a pedagogical framing of the same system. Her zero-place,
one-place, two-place, and three-place classes correspond to Nakagawa's N-place inventory, Satō's
argument-slot count, and the Handbook valency chapter ijas2023 ;
nakagawa2024 125–139 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §§2–3 . Her
"pockets" image — person markers fill positions on the verb itself before and more obligatorily
than free noun phrases — is a learner-facing rendering of head-marking, and is especially close
to Satō's argument-slot language sato2023b 42–51 . Her statements that bare
verb forms carry no tense and that nouns are number-neutral agree with all primary sources
nakagawa2024 124–125 ; shibatani1990 33 .

Her Lesson 2 zero-place weather verbs match Nakagawa's zero-place category and correctly include
the inchoative reading of descriptive predicates such as hure be red; become red .
One qualification on her treatment: she presents apto as alongside the
bound-root zero-place forms, but apto rain is a free noun and Nakagawa leaves
the word-boundary status of the expression unresolved nakagawa2024 129–130
‹contested›.

numan hekaci cep koyki | yesterday boy fish catch | The boy caught fish yesterday. | Ijäs Lesson 3. A constructed two-place transitive frame; subject and object are third-person and carry no segmental person marker. The example is pedagogical and is not corpus-attested. | ijas2023

Two Ijäs simplifications require a caveat from the primary sources. Third person is segmentally
zero in all attested forms, and her explicit Ø- notation is descriptively
harmless; the theoretical status of that zero is nonetheless disputed, with Nakagawa treating it
as the absence of an affix and Satō positing a zero third-person affix to preserve the two-index
cap nakagawa2024 137 ; sato2023b 39 ; sato2025b 23–24 ‹contested›. Her presentation of the indirect object of a three-place verb in a fixed
position between subject and direct object reflects a teaching default; Nakagawa's examples
with epakasnu teach show that the two objects and the subject are freely
ordered by discourse conditions nakagawa2024 363–365 ‹consensus›.

Her forward reference to verbs with distinct singular and plural stems belongs with
suppletive-verbal-number ; her adverbial -no examples with
adverbializer-no . Neither stem alternation nor the adverbializer alters the argument
structure of the base.

title: Deontic Necessity and Obligation (kuni ne, kus ne)
summary: Necessity and obligation are expressed periphrastically via nominalized kuni ne / kunine and kus ne.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Deontic Necessity and Obligation (kuni ne, kus ne)
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: Necessity and obligation are expressed periphrastically via nominalized kuni ne / kunine and kus ne.

The deontic field

Hokkaido Ainu expresses necessity and obligation periphrastically. There is no bound deontic
affix; instead, three distinct constructions carry the semantic load. The auxiliary
easirki must, have to expresses strong obligation. The auxiliary
kuni should, be supposed to — the same element that heads purpose-complement
clauses — expresses normative expectation or scheduled necessity; combined with the copula
ne it forms the predicate kuni ne , the
principal deontic proposition form. A set of conditional predicates — yak pirka ,
yak wen , yakka pirka , yakka wen — provides a gradient from
advice to permission to futility nakagawa2024 309–310, 423–424 ;
bugaeva2012 493–494 . The prospective kus(u) ne ,
treated fully in epistemic-irrealis-modality , acquires a near-command reading
with a second-person subject and the final particle na
nakagawa2024 423 .

form
shape
deontic value
Nakagawa 2024

easirki
V easirki (bare auxiliary)
strong obligation — must, have to
309, 表74

kuni (ne)
V kuni / V kuni ne
normative expectation — should, be supposed to, be scheduled to
310, 表74

yak pirka
V yak pirka (na)
advice — would be good to, should
423

yak wen
V yak wen (na)
negative advice — should not, it would be bad to
423–424

yakka pirka
V yakka pirka
permission — may, it is all right to
424

yakka wen
V yakka wen
futility — no use even if, pointless
424

kus(u) ne na
V kus(u) ne na
near-command (2nd person) — you will/should … won't you
423

All of these forms occupy the pre-evidential slot in the clause. The scope ordering that
tam-overview establishes — kusu ne ruwe ne (grammatical)
vs *ruwe ne kusu ne (ill-formed) — holds for deontic modals
equally: deontic meaning is expressed inside the evidential predicate
nakagawa2024 393 . The imperative and the prohibitive, which express
directive force through the bare-stem form and the preverbal adverb
iteki , are treated in imperative-and-prohibitive .

easirki — obligation

easirki must, be obliged to is listed in Nakagawa's table of core Saru
auxiliaries (本来的な助動詞) alongside nankor ,
kuni , and rusuy
nakagawa2024 306–309, 表74 . Like other core auxiliaries it carries no
person index of its own; only the preceding main verb is inflected. Bugaeva glosses it as
"deontic mood (must)" and places it in the modal paradigm alongside
nankor (probability) and rusuy
(desiderative) bugaeva2012 493 . The strength of the obligation it
expresses — "must, cannot avoid" — places it at the stronger end of the deontic scale,
above the advisory yak pirka .

paye=an easirki p mak e=iki hawe an | go.PL=4.S must NMLZ what 2SG.A=do voice-POSS exist | I have to go — what are you doing? | The nominalizer p closes the easirki clause. The final mak e=iki hawe an is a rhetorical question in the hawe-frame existential evidential; the fourth-person =an functions as the narrative first person. | nakagawa2024:308 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 457

The auxiliary is invariant; no plural or evidential inflection attaches to it. Its position
is immediately after the main verb, with no conjunctive particle between them — the frame
shared by nankor , rusuy , and
nisa . The frequency of easirki in the
Hokkaido corpus is substantially lower than that of nankor
(approximately 1977 tokens) ‹corpus-suggested›, consistent with the stronger deontic
commitment it expresses and its restriction to contexts of genuine obligation.

kuni ne — normative expectation

kuni appears in Nakagawa's 表74 as a core auxiliary with the semantic range
"〜はずだ / べきだ / ことになっている": it should be the case that / one ought to /
it is scheduled or expected that nakagawa2024 310 . The same element is the
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements irrealis complementizer that heads
purpose complements and the object complements of desiderative and manipulation predicates.
The deontic auxiliary reading arises when kuni is predicated with
the copula ne in a main clause — V kuni ne
— such that the nominalized proposition 'that V' is presented as a normative expectation.
The grammaticalization path runs from kuni as a formal noun
nominalizing an irrealis proposition, through its complementizer use, to the main-clause
modal reading; the complementizer and purpose functions are the primary locus of
kuni , and the deontic reading is the most grammaticalized end of
that scale ‹speculative›.

In the Saru corpus kuni appears most often in complement position
before cognition verbs such as ramu 'think'. The aynu-corpora
Discord records this distribution: kuni a=ramu is the standard
structure for reported intention, with kuni nominalizing the
embedded clause before the cognition verb, rather than the unattested
sekor a=ramu
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

wente kuni ramu kor an pe ne aan ru-we ne hine | devastate COMP think while exist NMLZ COP PRF.MIR track-POSS COP CONJ | — it turned out there was something thinking to cause devastation, and … | kuni here is the irrealis complementizer nominalizing wente before the cognition verb ramu kor an 'is thinking'. This is the complement use; the main-clause deontic reading of kuni ne arises when no matrix verb hosts it. | biratori1969 | SAR | Kaizawa Tsurushino, uwepeker [biratori/005/004#40]

When kuni ne closes a main clause with no embedding cognition or
purpose verb, the reading shifts to normative expectation: 'it is the normative case that
V', which in context yields obligation or convention. This is semantically adjacent to
easirki at the stronger end and to yak pirka
at the weaker end, but kuni ne specifically encodes that the
situation is normatively ordained — by role, rule, or convention — rather than urgently
commanded nakagawa2024 310 .

a=an kuni ne na | 4.A=be should COP FIN | One ought to be there. | Constructed to illustrate the main-clause deontic use of kuni ne; modeled on Nakagawa 2024: 310 exx. 461–463 for the Saru dialect.

Interaction with the evidential layer follows the standard scope ordering: the deontic
predicate sits inside the evidential, so kuni ne ruwe ne
(obligation as established fact) is well formed and *ruwe ne kuni ne
is not nakagawa2024 393 . Negation goes inside the deontic operator:
somo V kuni ne expresses the normative expectation that one does
not V. Ainu has no infinitive; the nominalization-plus-copula strategy that yields
kuni ne is the same architecture that covers what infinitives do
