in European languages — a point the aynu-corpora Discord makes explicitly for the
kuni nominalizer family
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-25) ‹consensus›.

The yak pirka idioms: advice, prohibition, permission, and futility

A set of clause-final predicates is built on the conditional particles
yak 'if' and yakka 'even if' followed by
the evaluative adjectives pirka be good or wen be bad .
These four expressions cover advice, negative advice, permission, and futility
nakagawa2024 423–424 ; ijas2023 . Nakagawa groups them among
indirect and mitigated command forms because they are typically directed at a second-person
addressee and carry the second-person subject prefix — unlike the bare imperative, which
has no subject prefix. The illocutionary force follows from the evaluation: 'if you V,
[the situation] is good' yields advice or permission; 'if you V, [the situation] is bad'
yields negative advice or futility depending on whether the conditional is simple
( yak ) or concessive ( yakka )
nakagawa2024 423 ‹consensus›.

form
conditional type
value
approximate English

V yak pirka (na)
simple conditional + good
advice, recommendation
you should V; it would be good if you V

V yak wen (na)
simple conditional + bad
negative advice
you should not V; it would be bad if you V

V yakka pirka
concessive conditional + good
permission
you may V; it is fine even if you V

V yakka wen
concessive conditional + bad
futility, dismissal
there is no use V-ing; even if you V it will do no good

Permission via yakka pirka is negated with
somo inside the conditional to express 'you need not V' rather
than 'you must not V'; the latter is expressed by yak wen or, for
a direct prohibition, by iteki V
nakagawa2024 424 . The yak and
yakka particles are the same ones that govern conditional and
concessive adverbial clauses; their grammar is set out in
conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses .

somo a=ahunke eaykap pe ne na e=ahunke yak pirka na | NEG 4.A=enter.SG be.unable NMLZ COP FIN 2SG.A=enter.SG if be.good FIN | There is nothing stopping you from going in — you should go in. | The first clause establishes the possibility via double negation (somo eaykap 'it is not the case that one cannot'). The yak pirka na frame delivers the advice as a directive. The final particle na reinforces directive force. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8(2) [aa-irc/007#211]

e=e yakka pirka | 2SG.A=eat even.if be.good | You may eat it. | Ijäs Lesson 10 permission frame, constructed illustration. | ijas2023

e=sinki yakun e=sini yak pirka | 2SG.S=be.tired if 2SG.S=rest if be.good | If you are tired, you should rest. | Ijäs Lesson 10; yak pirka delivers advice conditional on a stated premise. | ijas2023

kus(u) ne na as near-command

The prospective auxiliary kus(u) ne — analysed in
epistemic-irrealis-modality as the conjunction kusu
plus the copula ne expressing intention or scheduled future
nakagawa2024 435 ; sato2008 85–86 — acquires a directive
reading when the subject is second person and the final particle
na closes the clause. In that context
e=arpa kus ne na 'you will go, then' functions as a softened
instruction nakagawa2024 423 . The same indirect-command function is
available with nankor na and p ne na
nakagawa2024 423 . Ijäs presents the same range for
kus(u) ne — 'be going to, intend to, plan to' — and the
analysis adopted here keeps the two-morpheme segmentation (conjunction
kusu + copula ne ) against her
pedagogical one-word spelling kusune
ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

kotan wente kus arki kus ne aan ru-we ne wa | village devastate PURP come.PL PURP COP PRF.MIR track-POSS COP CONJ | — it turned out that they had been coming to devastate the village, and … | kus ne here is the intentional/prospective, not the deontic near-command. The example shows the form in its narrative use, combined with the mirative aan and the inferential ruwe ne. The construction wente kus arki kus ne is a purpose-clause chain: 'coming (in order) to devastate'. | biratori1969 | SAR | Kaizawa Tsurushino, uwepeker [biratori/004/002#40]

e=arpa kus ne na | 2SG.S=go.SG PURP COP FIN | You should go / you will go, then. | Constructed to illustrate the near-command reading of kus ne na with a second-person subject; modeled on Nakagawa 2024: 423.

The construction iteki … kus ne na extends the prohibitive adverb
iteki into the mitigated-prohibition frame, producing 'be careful
not to V'; Nakagawa provides an attested example and Ijäs's rendering
iteki e=eyruska kus ne na hani! matches it
nakagawa2024 425 ; ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

Scope and the epistemic–deontic boundary

All the deontic forms treated in this chapter occupy the pre-evidential layer. Proposition-level
negation goes inside the deontic operator: somo V kuni ne expresses
the normative expectation that one does not V, while kuni ne itself
remains outside the scope of negation. For the yak idioms, negation
either enters the antecedent clause ( somo V yakka pirka 'need not
V') or is supplied by switching to the opposed evaluative adjective
( yak wen for yak pirka )
nakagawa2024 423–424 .

The boundary between epistemic and deontic modality is not categorical. The conjecture
auxiliary nankor , whose core value is probability, takes on
expectational coloring equivalent to a softened command when addressed to a second person
with na nakagawa2024 311 . The prospective
kus ne straddles intention and near-command. The forms
easirki and kuni ne carry no epistemic
probability component: easirki is a judgment of obligation,
kuni ne is normative expectation. The full treatment of
nankor and kus(u) ne as epistemic and
intentive forms belongs to epistemic-irrealis-modality ; the relation of the
kuni complementizer to purpose clauses is in
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements and
causal-and-purpose-clauses .

title: The Desiderative rusuy
summary: Postverbal rusuy expresses 'want to' and desire, raising the verb-vs-auxiliary question central to the part.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Desiderative rusuy
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: Postverbal rusuy expresses 'want to' and desire, raising the verb-vs-auxiliary question central to the part.

The auxiliary and its form

The postverbal auxiliary rusuy want to follows the lexical verb directly, with no
intervening conjunction. This places it in the auxiliary ( A ) frame that
tam-overview the TAM overview distinguishes from the pair-verb frame on the basis
of person marking: in V rusuy , only the lexical verb V carries person agreement; rusuy is morphologically invariant regardless
of the number or person of the experiencer
nakagawa2024 306, 312–313 ; bugaeva2012 493 .

frame type
shape
person marking
conjunction

auxiliary (A)
V rusuy
on V only; rusuy is invariant
none (immediate adjacency)

Nakagawa groups rusuy in 表74 alongside
a , aan , nankor ,
easirki , and several further items as 本来的な助動詞 (proper auxiliaries of
the Saru dialect). The defining property of all members of that table is the absence of an
independent verbal use — with one exception: rusuy alone retains a use
as a full verb alongside its auxiliary function
nakagawa2024 306 . That exceptional status is discussed in the section below.
Bugaeva classifies rusuy as a desiderative modal without further
qualification bugaeva2012 493 . Tamura's 1960 study of Saru auxiliaries
describes the form in its original Japanese-language analytical context
tamura1960 .

inkar=an rusuy | look=4.S DES | One wants to look; one has a wish to see. | Fourth-person (indefinite) subject: the person-agreement suffix =an attaches to the main verb inkar, and rusuy is bare. The fourth person here expresses a generic or narrator's first-person referent in the narrative context. | nakagawa2024:312 | SAR | Nakagawa exx. 473–476 (cited form)

The construction appears in Saru narrative, conversational, and formal texts alike, and the
accessible sources document no restriction to a particular speech level.

Fusion and lexicalization

rusuy exhibits a strong tendency to fuse phonologically with the
preceding verb stem, producing single lexicalized units. Two forms are explicitly documented for
Saru: arparusuy 'want to go' (from arpa go (SG) +
rusuy ) and iperusuy 'want to eat a meal' (from
ipe eat a meal + rusuy )
nakagawa2024 312–313 . The morphological closeness parallels the lexical
incorporation of an action-verb root into the abilitative easkay , which
yields fused forms such as sinotca-easkay 'be able to compose songs'
nakagawa2024 315 . Both auxiliaries therefore sit at an intermediate point on
the continuum from syntactic auxiliary to derivational suffix, and the desiderative fusions
represent points closer to the lexicalized end of that cline ‹corpus-suggested›.

Argument structure and the experiencer

The person who wants must be the same individual who would perform the action: in
V rusuy , the experiencer of the desire and the would-be agent or subject
of V are necessarily coreferential. No separate morphological device
marks this relation; it follows from the single person-agreement slot on
V . For a transitive lexical verb, agent and object agreement appear in
the normal position on V :

ku=e rusuy | 1SG.A=eat DES | I want to eat (it). | Ijäs's constructed illustration. The first-person agent ku= attaches to the transitive verb e 'eat'; rusuy is bare. The unspecified patient is understood from context. This is the simplest transitive desiderative form. | ijas2023

Ainu has no infinitive: the language does not permit a non-finite verbal complement. Where many
languages use a to-infinitive ('I want to go '), Ainu requires a finite auxiliary
construction such as V rusuy or, in other complementation contexts, a
nominalization with the formal nouns pe , hi , or
kuni . This follows from the head-marking architecture of the verb, under
which every finite predicate carries a person index. The desiderative construction handles one of
the core functions that infinitival complements serve in other languages
ijas2023 . Community discussion has made the same observation: nukopoli
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-25) notes that
ku=e rusuy , ku=e easkay , and the
kuni nominalization divide the work that a single to-infinitive carries
in English — a lead consistent with the typological profile of head-marking,
nominalization-dependent languages (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹speculative›.

Rusuy as full verb: the dual status

Nakagawa's explicit exception note — that rusuy is the only member of
表74 to retain an independent verbal use — implies a structural distinction between two modes of
use nakagawa2024 306 :

mode
shape
person marking
meaning

auxiliary
V rusuy
on V ; rusuy invariant
want to perform action V

full verb
rusuy alone or with object
on rusuy itself
want (a thing or state of affairs)

The diagnostic between the two modes is position and the locus of person agreement. When
rusuy follows a person-marked lexical verb and is itself bare, the
auxiliary reading holds. When rusuy receives its own person prefix
without a preceding person-marked verb, it functions as the clause's main predicate. The exact
frequency and distribution of the full-verb use are not documented with corpus examples in the
accessible sources; the distinction rests on Nakagawa's descriptive note ‹corpus-suggested›. The
abilitative easkay offers a partial parallel in that it too appears both
as an auxiliary following a person-marked verb and as a predicate in its own right; unlike
rusuy , however, it belongs to 表75 (the transitive-verb auxiliary group)
and is classified as a transitive verb throughout, making its dual status structurally different
nakagawa2024 314–315 . The rusuy case is therefore the
only instance in 表74 of a proper auxiliary with documented verbal currency.

Possession desire: kor rusuy

When the object of desire is a thing rather than an action, the standard strategy uses the
transitive possessive verb kor have, possess, hold as the lexical
V . The possessed entity — the thing wanted — appears as the object of
kor , and rusuy attaches in its normal auxiliary
position:

ku=kor rusuy | 1SG.A=have DES | I want it. | A nominal object precedes kor in the full clause: e.g. cep ku=kor rusuy 'I want a/the fish'. rusuy is in its auxiliary function; kor takes the normal person prefix.

kor rusuy is thus a sub-case of V rusuy in which
V is the possessive verb kor 'have'. The semantic
contrast it encodes — thing-desire versus action-desire — is crosslinguistically common in
languages that use a single desiderative auxiliary:

lexical V
construction
object of desire
gloss

inkar 'look'
inkar rusuy
action
'want to look'

arpa 'go (SG)'
arparusuy (fused)
action
'want to go'

ipe 'eat a meal'
iperusuy (fused)
action
'want to eat a meal'

kor 'have, possess'
kor rusuy
thing (possessed entity)
'want (it)'

The applicative-desiderative

The applicative prefix e- applicative (treated fully in applicative-e )
introduces a new patient, goal, or stimulus argument to an otherwise intransitive verb. When that
transitivized verb precedes rusuy , the result is an
applicative-desiderative expressing desire directed toward an explicit referent:

ku=e-arpa rusuy | 1SG.A=APPL-go DES | I want to go to/for it. | The applicative prefix e- introduces a goal or beneficiary argument to the intransitive motion verb arpa 'go (SG)'. Person agreement (ku= 1SG.A) attaches at the outer edge of the complex stem; rusuy is bare. The person-marking pattern is identical to the plain V rusuy frame.

The construction is the regular intersection of the desiderative auxiliary frame with the
applicative system. rusuy attaches in its normal postverbal position,
person marking follows the same rule (on the lexical verb only), and the semantic contribution
of e- is governed by the applicative semantics of
applicative-e . The accessible sources do not provide corpus examples specifically
for the e-V rusuy combination; the account here rests on the
independently documented behaviour of both components ‹corpus-suggested›. Where the lexical verb
is already transitive — as in ku=e rusuy 'I want to eat it' — the
applicative prefix is not needed, and the object agreement appears on the transitive stem in the
normal way.

Negation and scope

Negation of the desiderative complex places somo before the lexical verb
and its person agreement, bringing the whole V rusuy predicate into the
scope of negation:

somo ku=nukar rusuy | NEG 1SG.A=see DES | I do not want to see (it). | somo negates the V rusuy predicate as a unit. This is the expected scope position for sentential negation in the auxiliary frame: somo sits inside any outer evidential frame and takes the modal complex as its target.

The placement parallels the general negation pattern for the auxiliary frame: negation in Ainu
is clause-internal and sits inside any outer evidential projection
nakagawa2024 393 . Within the desiderative predicate itself, the nesting order
follows the auxiliary stacking constraint: aspect auxiliaries attach to the lexical verb, and
rusuy scopes over the resulting aspectual complex. An evidential frame
such as ruwe ne then closes the whole expression — the ordering
V rusuy ruwe ne 'it is a recognized fact that one wants to V' is
expected and the reverse *ruwe ne V rusuy would be ill-formed, in line
with the general constraint that the evidential takes widest scope among the clause-internal
operators nakagawa2024 393 . The full modal field in which
rusuy sits — alongside the epistemic nankor and
the prospective kusu ne — is treated in
epistemic-irrealis-modality , and the deontic space adjacent to the desiderative in
deontic-necessity .

Semantic range: community observations

The core value of rusuy is desiderative: it predicates a desire on the
part of the grammatical subject to perform the action of the preceding verb. In practice, and
particularly with verbs of bodily state or emotional reaction, the meaning can shade into an
imminent-event reading. The community member nukopoli has proposed a comparison with Chinese
要 (yào), which covers both 'want to' and 'be about to', citing
mokor rusuy 'want to sleep / be getting sleepy' and
cis rusuy 'want to cry / be about to cry' as illustrative cases
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-29). The same observation
raises a possible morphological link between rusuy and
rawe , but no independent phonological or semantic evidence supports this
etymology in the accessible sources ‹speculative›. The near-future shading is best understood as
a pragmatic inference from the desiderative meaning rather than a separate grammaticalized
prospective value; the grammaticalized prospective function in Ainu is carried by
kusu ne and related forms, treated in
epistemic-irrealis-modality .

title: Deverbal and Denominal Noun Derivation (Lexical Nominalizer Morphology)
summary: The morphology of forming nouns with -p/-pe, -i/-hi, and -kur, treated here as lexical word-formation, with syntactic nominalization/relativization deferred to Part XVI.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Deverbal and Denominal Noun Derivation (Lexical Nominalizer Morphology)
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: The morphology of forming nouns with -p/-pe, -i/-hi, and -kur, treated here as lexical word-formation, with syntactic nominalization/relativization deferred to Part XVI.

Scope: lexical word-formation and the syntactic cut

Hokkaido Ainu has no separate class of deverbal suffixes standing apart from the rest of the
morphology. The same formal elements that nominalise whole verbal phrases at the syntactic
level also operate at the word-formation level to produce stable lexical nouns. Three
morphemes dominate the lexical deverbal layer: the nominalizer
-p/-pe , the possessive/affiliative suffix
-i/-hi (with its vowel-harmonic variants
-e/-we ), and the lexical class-noun heads
kur person 'person' and usi place, time 'place/time'. The
diminutive -po and the full range of noun-to-noun compounding belong
to nominal-derivation-diminutive-augmentative and
noun-noun-compounding ; the reverse direction — noun-to-verb conversion — belongs
to denominal-deverbal-verb-derivation .

The boundary between a lexical deverbal nominal and a syntactic nominalized phrase turns on
the arity of the base. When all argument slots of a transitive verb are filled by bound
person morphology, the resulting -p -final form has no open
relativizable gap and is reanalysable as a dictionary-listed noun. When a gap remains —
when one slot is left open for the matrix argument — the construction is a syntactic relative
clause or complement, treated in nominalization-p-pe . Nakagawa formalizes this
with an arity calculus: a clause whose arity sums to zero supplies a complement; one summing
to +1 heads a relative clause nakagawa2024 503–504 . The
formal-defective-nouns formal-noun subclass that these heads belong to, and
the full complementizer syntax, are developed there and in
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

The -p/-pe nominalizer at word level

Allomorphy

The nominalizer appears as p after a vowel-final form and as
pe after a consonant-final one
sato2008 177 ; ijas2023 . The conditioning is phonological and
holds across Hokkaido dialects ‹consensus›.

VP-final segment
allomorph
example

vowel
p

ipe yakne pirka p — 'if you ate it, good'

consonant
pe

rok=an pe ne na — 'one ought to sit quietly'

huci na ipe yakne pirka p | grandmother FIN eat COND good NMLZ | Grandmother, it would be nice if you ate more! | Satō §22.1. The clause ipe yakne pirka ends in a vowel-final form; the nominalizer surfaces as p. The construction expresses an unfulfilled wish. | sato2008:177 | SAR

ratcitara rok=an pe ne na | quietly sit.PL=4.S NMLZ COP FIN | One ought to sit quietly. | Satō §22.1. The verb rok=an ends in a nasal; the nominalizer surfaces as pe. The construction carries a normative or prescriptive nuance. | sato2008:177 | SAR

Lexicalized agent/patient nominals

The most common word-formation output of -p/-pe at the lexical
level is a patient or agent nominal produced when both argument slots of a transitive verb
are filled by bound person morphology. aep food 'food' is the canonical
illustration: synchronically a common noun listed as a dictionary headword
tamura1996 s.v. aep , its morphological shape is
a=e-p — the indefinite fourth-person agent
a= , the transitive verb e eat (transitively) , and
the nominalizer — 'that which one eats'.

a=e-p | 4.A=eat-NMLZ | aep | food | that which one eats | Patient-nominal type. The subject slot is filled by the indefinite fourth-person agent a= and the patient slot is closed by -p; the result is a stable common noun with no open argument gap. The morphemic analysis is the standard decomposition; the surface form is aep.

When a gap is not lexically filled and one slot remains open, the form heads a syntactic
relative clause rather than a stable noun. Nakagawa's example makes the contrast:

kani ku=respa p anakne | 1SG 1SG.A=raise NMLZ TOP | those whom I raised | Nakagawa ex. 230. Here p heads a relative clause with an accessible object gap (the raised persons); this is the syntactic use. The lexicalized aep differs by having no gap and a conventionalized meaning. Both shapes are morphologically identical; the distinction is arity and degree of reanalysis. | nakagawa2024:214 | SAR

The lexicalized forms are patient nominals when the subject slot is filled and the
nominalizer closes the object position, and agent nominals when the object is lexically
saturated and the subject supplied by a person prefix. The instrument or manner reading —
'the thing associated with this action' — arises when neither argument is left open but the
predication describes an activity rather than a state; it is determined by the semantics of
the verb, not by morphological marking ‹consensus›.

Arity-slot nominalizations: ci= and a= with -p

A set of lexicalized nominals was formed when both argument slots of a transitive base were
filled — the subject slot by the first-person exclusive prefix
ci= , the patient slot by -p — before the
form stabilized as a dictionary entry. The word cep fish is the
most-discussed example: nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-06;
aynucorporadiscord ) proposes the analysis ci=e-p
'the thing that we (exclusive) eat', with phonological reduction
cie-p → cep on a par with the attested
contraction kuani → kani ‹contested›. No
primary source states the etymology directly; Tamura's Saru dictionary lists
cep without internal analysis
tamura1996 s.v. cep . The same community discussion (nukopoli, 2023-12-08;
aynucorporadiscord ) identifies cikarkarpe
'manufactured thing, handcraft' as ci=kar-kar-pe — exclusive
subject, reduplicated stem kar-kar 'make', nominalizer
pe after the consonant-final stem — consistent with the allomorphy
and the compositional meaning ‹contested›.

A denominal analysis for canup teaching, moral precept 'teaching, lesson'
rests on the same skeleton: nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-10;
aynucorporadiscord ) reads ca-nu-p , with
ca 'mouth' as the base, an associative element
-nu , and -p as nominalizer — 'the thing
one gives with the mouth, moral precept'. The compound verb
ecanupkar 'to teach' supports the noun status of
canup ; the internal segmentation remains unconfirmed in the
accessible grammars ‹contested›.

Alongside the ci= -initial type, the fourth-person agent prefix
a= fills the subject slot to produce indefinite-subject nominals:
a=e-p → aep 'food'. The alternation between
ci= (exclusive: we-not-you) and a=
(indefinite: one in general) reflects the person system described in
word-class-inventory-and-diagnostics , not a difference in the nominalization
itself.

Instrument nominalization: ani X p

A productive word-formation pattern builds instrument nouns via the postpositional adverb
ani with, by means of followed by a verbal stem and the nominalizer:
ani [V] p/pe = 'the thing with which one
V-s'. Nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-05-29; aynucorporadiscord )
documents this as a regular instrument-nominalization frame: ani
e=kanpinuye p 'pencil' (lit. 'the thing to write characters with'),
ani humi a=aste p 'musical instrument' (lit. 'the thing to make
sound with') ‹corpus-confirmed›. In each form the verb's argument slots are filled and
p closes the zero-arity predication; the instrument reading is
contributed by ani . The construction shows that the word-level
nominalizer can co-occur with adverbial adjuncts — not only with person prefixes — to
saturate the argument frame before nominalization.

The lexical–syntactic boundary

Two criteria distinguish a lexical nominal from a syntactic one.
Argument opacity : a lexicalized form has all slots filled and admits no
modification by a relativizing matrix argument; a syntactic form projects an accessible
gap. Compositionality : the meaning of a syntactic nominalization follows
from the predicate and the gap's role; a lexicalized item may carry conventionalized
meaning that has drifted from any compositional reading — 'fish' is not synchronically
recoverable as 'what we eat'.

The community observation (nukopoli, 2024-11-09; aynucorporadiscord ) that
-p(e) nominalizes without dropping an argument, unlike relativized
clauses, aligns with Nakagawa's arity account
nakagawa2024 503–504 : a=i=ye p 'the thing that
one says' is well-formed because both the subject slot ( a= ) and the
object slot ( i= ) are filled, whereas substituting a bare head noun
is impossible ( *a=i=ye kamuy ) ‹consensus›. The full syntax of the
construction — the headless-relative and complement continuum, TAM marking inside the
nominalized clause, insubordination in Tokachi — belongs to
nominalization-p-pe .

hoka e=o p ne na | fire 2SG.A=put.in NMLZ COP FIN | It is the thing to put on the fire! | Nakagawa ex. 1165. Both argument slots of e=o are filled via person morphology; p closes the zero-arity predication with a patient reading. The directive force is contextual, not contributed by p itself. | nakagawa2024:505 | SAR

The affiliative suffix as nominalizer: -i/-hi and -e/-we

The suffix that forms the concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed)
form of common nouns — -i/-hi in general notation, with
-e/-we as the shape after certain base types — also operates as a
derivational morpheme when applied to sensory and perceptual noun bases, producing formal
nouns capable of nominalizing a preceding clause. The resulting form is morphologically a
possessed nominal: ru-we is 'the trace of —', its implicit possessor
being the event described by the nominalized clause. The same possessed-noun morphology
underlies all four members of the Hokkaido evidential set
nakagawa2024 258, 392 ;
tamura1996 s.v. ru, sir, hum, haw .

Conditioning of the suffix allomorphs

The possessed-form suffix follows the same five-type conditioning that governs the general
affiliative paradigm nakagawa2024 223–227 ;
nakagawa2022pos 484 . The short form and the long form carry no semantic
difference; the choice is prosodic nakagawa2024 219 . For the four
sense-noun bases that grammaticalize as evidential formal nouns, the suffix type follows
directly from the base-final segment:

base (concept form)
base shape
affix type (Nakagawa 表51)
formal noun
grammaticalized information source

ru 'track, path'
CV open
③-like → +we(he)
ru-we
inferred / recognized fact

sir 'appearance, scene'
CVC (C ≠ y, w)
③-1 → +i(hi)
sir-i
directly visible situation

hum 'sound, sensation'
CVC (C ≠ y, w)
③-1 → +i(hi)
hum-i
non-visual sensation

haw 'voice'
CVCw → type ②
② → +e(he)
haw-e
verbal report, hearsay

Each short form ( ruwe, siri, humi, hawe ) is the form that heads the
evidential construction; the corresponding long forms ( ruwehe, sirihi,
humihi, hawehe ) exist as ordinary lexical possessed nouns but are blocked from the
evidential slot — Tamura marks this explicitly for each item
tamura1996 s.v. ruwe . The blocking of the long form from the evidential
slot shows that the formal-noun use has been grammaticalized beyond the general affiliative
paradigm ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Refsing, from Shizunai, reanalyses the same set as nominalizers rather than
possessed nouns, treating them as one of four sentence-conversion types alongside
conjunctionalization, quotationalization, and adnominalization
refsing1986 259–264 . Nakagawa retains the formal-noun word-class label
and treats the evidential role as a grammatical function (complementizer) that certain
formal nouns fill when arity is zero nakagawa2024 503–505 . The two
framings are compatible at the descriptive level, differing in whether the category label
or the functional role is foregrounded ‹contested›. Full treatment of the evidential
semantics and syntax belongs to ruwe-ne-inferential and the sibling evidential
chapters; what matters here is that the deverbal process is the possessive-suffix
morphology, and the evidential construction is the grammaticalization endpoint of a path
that begins at lexical word-formation.

Lexical class-noun heads: kur and usi/uske

Two common nouns operate as productive denominative heads:
kur person 'person' and
usi place, time (also usike ) /
uske place . Neither is an agreement morpheme; Hokkaido Ainu has no numeral
classifier system nakagawa2024 461 . They are ordinary common nouns that
follow a modifier (NP or VP) and supply a semantic class specification for the resulting
compound.

kur forms person-denoting nominals from place names via the allative
particle un : Sar un kur 'a Saru person',
Iyoci un kur 'a Yoichi person' nakagawa2024 234 .
Corpus rank 98 (2,456 tokens in the Hokkaido texts) reflects how productively it combines.
The word-class diagnostics that distinguish kur from the formal-noun
subclass — it heads an independent NP, takes attributive modifiers, and takes possession
marking — are set out in formal-defective-nouns .

Sar un kur | Saru ALLAT person | a Saru person | Nakagawa §5.2.6. Place name + allative particle un + class-noun head kur; the pattern is productive across place names. | nakagawa2024:234 | SAR

usi(ke) and uske head locational nominals from
a verbal phrase. Nakagawa lists them under the formal-noun inventory (2024: 257–258;
nakagawa2024 257–258 ); their full syntax as heads of VP nominalization
constructions belongs to lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization . The example
below shows usi nominalizing a VP to produce a location noun that
serves as goal of a motion-verb sequence:

nanu memke usi ku=oman wa ek ruwe ne | face shave place 1SG.S=go CONJ come track-POSS COP | I went (and came back) to the face-shaving place. | Nakagawa ex. 330, citing Yaegashi 2001: 13. The VP nanu memke 'shave the face' is nominalized by usi to produce 'the face-shaving place (barber's)', which then heads the goal argument of the motion-verb sequence ku=oman wa ek. The ruwe ne closes the matrix clause as inferential evidential. | nakagawa2024:258 | HK | Kushiro

Zero-conversion and the verb–noun interface

Many Hokkaido Ainu nouns have corresponding intransitive verbs with no overt marking of the
class difference. Property words like poro be big 'be big' serve as noun
modifiers — poro cep 'a big fish' — without any derivational suffix;
word-class membership is assigned by the syntactic slot, not the form
nakagawa2024 115–116 ; nakagawa2022pos 473 . This
zero-conversion is the default mechanism in compound nouns where an intransitive verb appears
as the first element: Nakagawa's taxonomy of compound types (2024: 267–272) — 1-arg-verb+N,
N+1-arg-verb, and 1-arg-verb+1-arg-verb — all reduce to N+N because the verbal element is
freely reanalyzed as nominal in the modifier position
nakagawa2024 267–272 .

inonno-itak prayer discourse, liturgical speech 'prayer discourse' (compound of
inonno pray, offer prayers + itak word, speech ) illustrates
this path: the intransitive verb inonno 'pray' modifies
itak 'speech' as the first element of a V+N compound with no
nominalizing suffix. What signals the nominal reanalysis is the compound's function as a
common-noun argument and the person-affix position outside the whole compound, not between
its elements. The formal diagnostics for whether a sequence is a compound or two syntactic
words belong to noun-noun-compounding .

Antitwilight (aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-06-02; aynucorporadiscord ) observes
that Ainu has very few pure-noun coordinate compounds or exocentric N+N combinations, because
noun-formation is verb-centred — nearly every noun can also serve as an intransitive verb, so
N+N and V+N compounds are morphologically indistinguishable at the surface
‹contested›. This sharpens Nakagawa's (2024: 116) principle that word-class membership is
defined by syntactic role rather than form. The verbal-number suppletion patterns that some
deverbal nominals feed (e.g. singular ek vs. plural
arki 'come') belong to suppletive-verbal-number .

Grammaticalization cline and the Sakhalin contrast

The morphemes treated here as word-formation devices extend along a grammaticalization cline
into clause-level nominalization and then into evidential and sentence-final positions.
Takahashi's study of the Horobetsu dialect shows that the formal-noun nominalizers — including
p/pe and the possessive-suffix set — undergo a further stage in
Horobetsu, functioning as adverbial clause markers and conjunction-like linkers in contexts
that the better-documented southern dialects (Saru, Chitose) handle with other strategies
takahashi2018 . The lexicalized deverbal nominals treated here are the initial
pole of the cline; the Horobetsu data mark the far end within Hokkaido ‹corpus-suggested›.

Bugaeva's comparison of Hokkaido and Sakhalin nominalization
bugaeva2016 identifies Sakhalin Ainu as having innovated in the nominalization
system relative to the common ancestor. One index of this difference is the treatment of the
evidential formal nouns: in Sakhalin varieties the long possessed form
( ruwehe , sirihi ) is used in the evidential
slot, whereas Hokkaido restricts that slot to the short forms and blocks the long form from
evidential use (see the conditioning table above). Whether this blocking is a Hokkaido
retention or an innovation is an open question; the Sakhalin data frame it as a candidate
for Hokkaido-internal change ‹speculative›. Full comparative treatment is in
ruwe-ne-inferential and the sibling evidential chapters.

title: The Dialect Sample and the Corpus-Quantification Method
summary: Which Hokkaido dialects are sampled, how Sakhalin/Kuril contrast is framed, and exactly how the grammar computes and reports corpus frequencies.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Dialect Sample and the Corpus-Quantification Method
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: Which Hokkaido dialects are sampled, how Sakhalin/Kuril contrast is framed, and exactly how the grammar computes and reports corpus frequencies.

The Hokkaido dialect sample

Hokkaido Ainu was spoken in regionally differentiated forms whose systematic divergences bear
on morphology, phonology, and lexical semantics alike. The grammar takes the
Saru (SAR) and Chitose (CHI) varieties as its descriptive
core. Saru is the most extensively documented Hokkaido dialect: Tamura's 1996 dictionary
tamura1996 supplies a dense lexical record; Nakagawa's annotated oral-literature
text collections from Nibutani and Biratori narrators give a genre-diverse discourse corpus
nakagawatexts2000 ; and Nakagawa's 2024 grammar takes Saru as the default Hokkaido
reference throughout nakagawa2024 . Chitose is the second depth-documented variety:
Nakagawa's 1995 dictionary provides the foundational Chitose lexical record nakagawa1995 ; Satō's 2008 grammar integrates Chitose systematically alongside Saru comparisons sato2008 ; and the NINJAL Glossed Audio Corpus of Folklore contributes additional Chitose material
glossedcorpus2016 . Six further dialect areas enter the description where sources
allow: Shizunai (SHI), documented most fully by refsing1986 ; Horobetsu (HOR), the
subject of Takahashi's morphosyntactic studies takahashi2017 takahashi2018 ; Tokachi (TOK), whose dialect is documented in Takahashi's Tokachi example collection takahashi2014texts and evidentiality and deixis studies takahashi2013 takahashi2011 ;
Ishikari (ISH), described by asai1969 ; and the more sparsely attested Asahikawa
(ASA) and Yakumo (YAK) areas. The tag HK marks data not assignable to a
specific locality. Table 1 summarises the sample, its primary sources, and the relative depth
of corpus coverage.

Table 1. Hokkaido dialect varieties, primary sources, and corpus coverage

The Hattori dialect dictionary hattori1964 and Satō's 2008 grammar provide
comparative paradigm data for several of these areas even where no monographic description
exists; those sources supply the supplementary attestation cited in phonological and
morphological chapters. Tamura Masashi's grammatical description of the Shiranuka dialect
tamuramasashi2011 adds a further locality for isolated phenomena.

Dialect classification and the isogloss basis

The classification of Hokkaido Ainu dialects rests on a sequence of lexicostatistic and
dialectometric studies beginning with Hattori and Chiri's 1960 founding survey hattorichiri1960 , which applied the 200-item Swadesh list to 19 localities (13 Hokkaido, 6 Sakhalin) and
established the basic quantitative structure of inter-dialect similarity. Asai's 1974 cluster
analysis of the same matrix, extended with Chitose data, produced the widely cited major
bipartition of Hokkaido dialects into a southwestern group centred on Saru and an
eastern-northern group asai1974 . Tamura's 1988 encyclopedia article formalised
this as the NE/SW division tamura1988 , a terminology adopted in subsequent
descriptive literature. Ono's reanalysis of the Asai matrix with updated statistical methods
partially confirms but also complicates that bipartition ono2020 , and Kirikae's
place-name isogloss study documents the geographical boundary of the par/car alternation across
Hokkaido kirikae1994 . The current state-of-the-art synthesis is Nakagawa and
Fukazawa's chapter in the 2022 Handbook, which evaluates the evidence for proposed boundaries
and recommends a cautious multi-feature approach nakagawafukazawa2022 . Fukazawa
and Ono's 2024 study reconsiders those boundaries with additional data fukazawaono2024 , and Kasuga's 2026 dialectometric analysis extends coverage to 75 localities kasuga2026 ‹corpus-suggested›. The full treatment of Hokkaido dialect classification and its
dialectometric basis is given in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry ; the phonological microvariation documented in the corpus is discussed in hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

For the purposes of chapter-level analysis, dialectal variation is introduced where a
phenomenon behaves differently across the tagged varieties and where the difference is
attested in the primary sources. Sporadic attestation for a peripheral area is noted but
not treated as systematic evidence. Where no source distinguishes dialects, the description
applies to the Saru–Chitose southern baseline and should be understood as such.

Sakhalin and Kuril Ainu as external contrast

Sakhalin Ainu (sometimes called Enciw; tag SA ) and Kuril Ainu (tag
KU ) are not objects of description in this grammar; they appear as systematic
external contrast points. Murasaki's 1979 grammar of Sakhalin Ainu murasaki1979 ,
Dal Corso's 2021 re-edition with translation and grammatical notes dalcorso2021 ,
Dal Corso's 2024 study of Piłsudski's corpus phonetics and morphosyntax dalcorso2024 , and Tangiku's 2022 Handbook chapter on HA–SA differences tangiku2022 supply
the Sakhalin data used for comparison. Piłsudski's 1912 text collection pilsudski1912 remains the primary philological witness for early-twentieth-century Sakhalin speech and
contributes the SA examples cited in several chapters. Kuril material is attested only in
historical sources and enters the grammar for specifically historical comparisons. Cross-dialect
contrasts (SA or KU examples) are presented in tagged contrast display; their comparative
status is indicated by the dialect tag alone, without repeated prose announcement. The
dedicated comparative treatment is in sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast .

The attested text corpus

Examples in this grammar are drawn from a body of attested Hokkaido Ainu discourse totalling
approximately 167,890 sentences. The Saru sub-corpus is the largest (around 77,964 sentences),
followed by the Shizunai/southern materials (approximately 43,295 sentences), a Mukawa
component (approximately 13,424 sentences), and the Chitose sub-corpus (approximately 4,420
sentences), with smaller contributions from Tokachi, Horobetsu, and other localities; numbers
reflect the alignment state at the time of authoring and will grow as further editions are
added. The genre composition of the corpus is described in oral-literature-and-spoken-corpora .

The component sources are:

The NINJAL Corpus of Ainu Oral Literature ninjal2003 , an aligned Hokkaido
narrative corpus with audio, covering Saru and Chitose narrators (including 木村きみ and
小田イト).

Nakagawa's annotated oral-literature text collection, volumes 1–24 nakagawatexts2000 , covering uwepeker (prose tales) and kamuy yukar from Saru and Chitose narrators
principally at Nibutani.

The NINJAL Glossed Audio Corpus of Ainu Folklore glossedcorpus2016 , an online
morphologically annotated corpus of Chitose and Saru folktales.

The ILCAA aligned Saru/Shizunai audio-text materials from recordings of 1976–1984 ilcaa1976 .

The Biratori Town Ainu oral-literature archive, Saru-dialect oral literature held by the
National Ainu Museum biratori1969 .

Chiba University Ainu language materials, covering Horobetsu and adjacent areas, with
narrators including Kanenari Matsu chiba2015 .

Kayano's conversational Ainu recordings kayano1987 and the Hokkaidō Utari
Kyōkai Akor Itak textbook utari1994 , contributing conversational and
pedagogical register.

The Ainu Times (Ainu Koraci) learner newsletter ainukoraci1991 , providing
Saru and Chitose texts by speakers including 神崎雅好 and 萱野志朗.

Each sentence in the corpus is associated with a source identifier, a narrator/author when
known, and a dialect tag. Where a specific document is cited in a chapter,
the example carries the appropriate cite attribute pointing to the underlying
published edition, never to an aggregating platform (see glossing-abbreviations-and-citation ).

Corpus-frequency methodology

Frequency claims in this grammar rest on string searches over the aligned corpus described
above. The primary unit of count is the clause or sentence boundary as aligned in each
source edition; where alignment is unavailable, the typographic sentence of the published
text serves as proxy. Counts are reported as raw token frequencies (attestation counts)
alongside the total search space (number of sentences or clauses in the relevant sub-corpus),
to allow readers to assess the density and dialect distribution independently.

Several caveats govern interpretation. String searches return all surface occurrences of a
form, including homophonous lexical items; chapters note when a form is polysemous and
flag the proportion of counts that can be unambiguously assigned to the grammatical use
under discussion. The corpus is not uniformly dialect-balanced: Saru and Shizunai materials
predominate, so raw frequency figures reflect that weighting. Where a claim requires
genre-internal comparison — for instance, comparing evidential token frequencies in yukar
versus conversational registers — the counts are restricted to the appropriate genre
sub-corpus and the size of that sub-corpus is stated. Claims based on corpus counts are
graded ‹corpus-confirmed› when the evidence is unambiguous across a sufficient
token base, and ‹corpus-suggested› when the count supports the claim but the
evidence is thin or restricted to one dialect or genre. The evidence-grade system is
described in full in glossing-abbreviations-and-citation .

Where a chapter reports a specific count, the format is: form, raw count, sub-corpus size,
and source or genre label (for example, ruwe ne : 16,350 tokens in the 167,890-sentence
Hokkaido corpus). Normalisation to occurrences per thousand sentences is supplied when
comparing across sub-corpora of substantially different sizes.

Attribution: the original-source rule

Every attested example in this grammar cites its underlying source — the specific published
edition in which the sentence appears, named with its narrator or author where identified
— and never a secondary aggregation platform. A sentence extracted from the NINJAL corpus
cites the underlying text edition and narrator; a sentence from the Biratori archive cites
the specific document held by the National Ainu Museum; a dictionary gloss cites the
dictionary (Tamura 1996, Nakagawa 1995, Kayano 1996) in whose pages it appears. Citing
an aggregating database or web platform in place of the underlying published edition
obscures evidentiary weight and fails the attribution rule.

Constructed examples — sentences composed to illustrate a grammatical point and not drawn
from any attested source — are explicitly marked constructed . There is no
intermediate category: an example either carries a cite attribute pointing to
a resolved bibliography key, or it is marked constructed . A grammaticality
judgment about a constructed sentence is the author's, derived from established patterns in
the descriptive literature; where the judgment is uncertain it is marked and sourced to
the nearest attested parallel. Narrator and locality information, when available, is given
in the place field of the example and is not repeated in the prose surrounding
it. The full glossing and citation conventions are set out in glossing-abbreviations-and-citation .

Evidence grading

Every nontrivial descriptive or analytic claim in the grammar carries an explicit evidence
grade drawn from a closed set. Uncontroversial, well-attested claims with consensus across
the major sources carry no inline grade (the default is ‹consensus› ). Claims that
the major sources disagree on are marked ‹contested› , with the positions named
and sourced. Claims checked against the corpus data are marked ‹corpus-confirmed›
(evidence robust) or ‹corpus-suggested› (evidence thin or indirect). Plausible
but unverified ideas, typically concerning diachrony or cross-dialectal inference beyond the
attested record, are marked ‹speculative› . Novel re-analyses by this grammar's
authors, permitted only within the five theory-magnet domains identified in aims-scope-design-philosophy , are marked ‹speculative› with the supporting reasoning attached.
Comprehensive grading conventions and examples of each grade are given in glossing-abbreviations-and-citation .

Community observations from the aynu-corpora archive

The aynu-corpora Discord community has accumulated a substantial body of grammatical
observation — morpheme analyses, form-class proposals, productivity assessments,
distributional generalisations — since approximately 2023 aynucorporadiscord .
These community observations enter the grammar when three conditions hold: the item bears
on a grammatical point covered in a chapter, it can be responsibly stated within the
grammar's evidentiary framework, and no prior peer-reviewed treatment addresses the specific
point. Each incorporated observation is cited with the member handle and date, graded
according to the confidence level recorded in the archive ( asserted items corroborated
by the corpus or literature receive a standard grade; proposed items are marked
‹contested›; speculative items are marked ‹speculative›; disputed items
are noted as community-contested). Community observations are leads, not authorities;
they supplement rather than displace the peer-reviewed descriptive literature.

title: The eci= Portmanteau (1A→2O) and Second-Person Plural Syncretism
summary: The cumulative eci= exponent encoding a 1sg subject acting on a 2nd-person object, and its syncretism with the 2pl subject/object marker eci=.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The eci= Portmanteau (1A→2O) and Second-Person Plural Syncretism
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The cumulative eci= exponent encoding a 1sg subject acting on a 2nd-person object, and its syncretism with the 2pl subject/object marker eci=.

The two functions of eci=

The prefix eci= occupies two cells in the Hokkaido Ainu
personal-index system. As the second-person plural index it marks the agent, subject,
or object of a predicate whose addressee participant is plural, in parallel with the
second-person singular e= . As the portmanteau for first-to-second
combinations it simultaneously indexes the subject and the object when a first-person
agent — singular or exclusive plural — acts on a second-person object, singular or plural.
The two functions share a single surface form, producing a syncretism that surrounding
context consistently resolves nakagawa2024 166–173 ; tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2012 471–474 .

The full S/A/O paradigm and its alignment patterns are set out in person-marking-architecture . The present chapter develops the two functions of eci= , the
syncretism between them, and the interaction with Hokkaido plural marking. Adjacent paradigm
material belongs to first-second-person-singular-affixes for e= and en= , to object-indexing-monotransitive for the
two-index cap and ditransitive object choice, and to person-number-interactions
for plural agreement.

structural environment
function of eci=
e= counterpart

intransitive predicate
2PL.S
e= 2SG.S

transitive predicate, O = 3rd person
2PL.A
e= 2SG.A

transitive predicate, A = 3rd person, O = 2nd person plural
2PL.O
e= 2SG.O

transitive predicate, A = 1st person (any number), O = 2nd person (any number)
1→2 portmanteau: indexes A and O simultaneously
eci= (no separate 2SG-only form exists for this cell)

Second-person neutrality: e= and eci=

Both e= and eci= carry no subject–object
distinction: the same form appears whether the addressee is the agent of a transitive verb,
the sole argument of an intransitive verb, or the object of a transitive verb with a
third-person agent bugaeva2012 471–472 ; sato2008 31–32 ;
tamura1972 27 . Tamura's formulation is explicit: the second-person affix
marks the subject and the object without distinction tamura1972 27 . The
S=A=O patterning places second person in the neutral column of the mixed alignment system,
alongside the zero third person, and against the nominative–accusative 1SG and the
tripartite 1PL.EXCL and fourth person ‹consensus›.

role
2SG
2PL (Saru–Chitose baseline)
2PL (Ishikari, Tokachi)

S: intransitive subject
e=
eci=
es=

A: transitive subject, O = 3rd person
e=
eci=
es=

O: object, A = 3rd person
e=
eci=
es=

sinen ne e=oman wa e=ray yakun | one COP 2SG.S=go CONJ 2SG.S=die COND | If you go alone and die, ... | Both intransitive verbs oman and ray carry e= because the addressee is singular; the role is the same (2SG.S) in both clauses. | nakagawa2024:167 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 106

eci=paye tek nean cise oro eci=epa cik anakne | 2PL.S=go.PL CONJ that house LOC 2PL.A=reach COND TOP | When you (plural) go and reach that house, ... | eci= marks 2PL.S on the intransitive paye in the first clause and 2PL.A on the transitive epa in the second: one form across two distinct syntactic roles within one sentence. | nakagawa2024:168 | TOK | Nakagawa ex. 116, after Sawai 1996: 69

Nakagawa's combination tables record eci= as the 2PL index in
Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, and Shizunai, while Ishikari and Tokachi also show
es= in the second-plural row nakagawa2024 166–172 .
Where a dialect supplies es= , it fills the same neutral paradigm
cell with the same functional range.

The 1A→2O portmanteau

When a first-person agent acts on a second-person object, eci=
indexes the complete local-person relation. The four subject/object number combinations —
1SG acting on 2SG, 1SG acting on 2PL, 1PL.EXCL acting on 2SG, and 1PL.EXCL acting on
2PL — all receive the same prefix. The stacked sequences
*ku=e= , *ku=eci= ,
*ci=e= , and *ci=eci= are unattested and
ungrammatical. Bugaeva enumerates all four covered combinations explicitly; Nakagawa's Saru
combination table places eci= in every 1→2 cell; Tamura's earlier
Saru analysis already treats the form as the second-person affix used in the
first-to-second-person relation bugaeva2008 38 ; bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; tamura1972 27–28 ‹consensus›.

A: subject
O: object
index
excluded analytic sequence

1SG
2SG
eci=
*ku=e=

1SG
2PL
eci=
*ku=eci=

1PL.EXCL
2SG
eci=
*ci=e=

1PL.EXCL
2PL
eci=
*ci=eci=

nep ka eci=kasuy pe an ya
2SG.O=help thing exist Q"
tr="Is there anything I can help you with?"
cite="nakagawa2024:173"
dial="SAR"
place="Nakagawa ex. 128, after Honda 2001: 44"
note="Nakagawa identifies eci= as the 1SG.A→2SG.O portmanteau. No separate ku= or e= appears; the single prefix satisfies both the agent and object indexing requirements of the transitive predicate kasuy."
/>

The portmanteau character of the 1→2 form is confirmed by the contrast with the 1→4
combination. Nakagawa's Saru combination table records eci=i=
when the agent is first person and the object is fourth person: the agent index
eci= and the fourth-person object i= appear
as separate prefixes nakagawa2024 170 . The 1→2 cells merge the two slots
into one, in keeping with the two-index cap: a verb carries at most one agent index and
one object index, and the portmanteau satisfies both positions simultaneously. The cap and
its consequences for three-argument verbs are treated in object-indexing-monotransitive .

Resolving the syncretism

A verb beginning with eci= is formally ambiguous among 2PL.S,
2PL.A, 2PL.O, and the 1→2 portmanteau. Several cues resolve the ambiguity. On an
intransitive predicate, which has no object slot, the form can only be 2PL.S. On a
transitive predicate with an overt third-person subject noun phrase, the 2PL.O reading
is forced; with an overt third-person object, the 2PL.A reading holds. The portmanteau
reading arises on a transitive predicate whose agent is a locally tracked first-person
participant. Ainu discourse tracks participants across turns through the bound indexes and
does not regularly deploy independent pronouns for reference maintenance; the free forms
eani (2SG) and ecioka (2PL) add emphasis
or mark contrast rather than supplying a referent from scratch
nakagawa2024 163–164, 250–251 ; tamura1971 3–5 .

The 2→1 configuration presents an asymmetry in Saru and Chitose. Where other Hokkaido
dialects form 2SG→1SG by combining a second-person agent index with the first-person
object en= , Saru uses bare en= alone,
suppressing the expected *e=en= ; Chitose extends this to 2PL→1SG,
making *eci=en= ungrammatical in that variety as well
nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 . The full 2→1
paradigm and its dialect variation are developed in first-second-person-singular-affixes .

Plural marking and eci=

The 2PL value carried by eci= is participant-number indexing and is
independent of verbal number. Hokkaido Ainu expresses verbal plurality through three further
devices: suppletive stem alternations such as arpa /
paye 'go', a root-attaching suffix -pa , and
— in Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari — an outer plural auxiliary that follows the fully
person-inflected verb nakagawa2024 140–159 ; bugaeva2012 471, 481 ; sakaguchi2024 12 . Each device occupies a distinct structural position
and can co-occur with eci= .

device
domain of plurality
position
co-occurrence with eci=

suppletive plural stem ( paye , arki , …)
S plurality in intransitives; O or event plurality in transitives
lexical stem selection
yes; eci=paye = 2PL.S + go.PL

stem suffix -pa
verbal / event plurality
inside person suffixes and causatives, attached to root
independent of the personal index

outer plural pa (Saru, Chitose, Ishikari)
agent / participant plurality
outside the fully person-inflected form
follows eci= verb when additional plurality is marked

Nakagawa's minimal pair illustrates the ordering diagnostic for the outer auxiliary versus
the stem suffix: iki=an pa has the outer pa
following the fourth-person suffix, while iki-pa=an has the stem
suffix -pa inside the person marker nakagawa2024 158 .
Sakaguchi formalises the criterion — a marker that attaches to the fully person-inflected
form is a clitic rather than an affix — for Sakhalin Ainu; the most directly relevant
Hokkaido evidence is the Saru/Chitose/Ishikari outer pa
sakaguchi2024 77–85, 163 ‹contested›. Person–number agreement and the
selection of plural stems are treated in person-number-interactions .

Ijäs's learner grammar and the reference paradigm

The online learner grammar by Ijäs introduces e= in Lesson 2 as
the second-person singular index on intransitive verbs; the plural counterpart is deferred
ijas2023 . Lesson 5 then presents eci= as the marker
for a first-person singular agent acting on a second-person singular object, and
e= as the second-person singular object form with a third-person
agent ijas2023 . Her pedagogical scope covers one number combination of the
portmanteau; the reference sources extend eci= to all four
first-to-second-person subject/object number combinations and additionally treat it as the
full 2PL index across A, S, and O ‹consensus›.

e=arpa | 2SG.S=go | You go. | Constructed from Ijäs Lesson 2. Her pedagogical hyphen notation (e-arpa) is normalized here to the personal-index boundary =. | ijas2023

tanpe eci=kore na
2SG.O=give FIN"
tr="I give this to you."
cite="ijas2023"
constructed
note="Constructed from Ijäs Lesson 5, presenting a 1SG-agent-plus-2SG-object construction. The reference paradigm generalises eci= to all four 1→2 number combinations. As a ditransitive, kore indexes the recipient (2nd person) in the object slot; the thing given (tanpe) is a bare noun phrase."
/>

Ijäs notes that second-person neutrality creates an interpretive ambiguity: a predicate
carrying e= alongside a third-person participant can be read with
the addressee as either agent or object unless additional context fixes the role
ijas2023 . The observation matches the structural picture: predicate valence and
the state of participant tracking supply what the person index alone leaves open.

Notation

This grammar writes the personal indexes with = , including
e= and eci= . The symbol is a house
convention for the personal-index boundary and makes no claim about morphological status.
Nakagawa writes all person markers with = for convenience,
explicitly noting that the symbol is unrelated to phonological or syllabic structure
nakagawa2024 49 . Bugaeva assigns graduated status on prosodic and
separability grounds: eci= is clitic-like — inseparable from the
stem and not shifting the accent kernel — while ku= ,
ci= , e= , en= ,
un= , and i= are full prefixes that do shift
it bugaeva2012 472–473 ‹contested›. Ijäs writes e-
and eci- with a hyphen and notes that equal signs are also in
common use ijas2023 ; the forms in this chapter are normalized accordingly.
The morphological boundary status of the Hokkaido personal indexes is treated in
personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

title: Epistemic Modality, Dubitative, Intentive, and Irrealis
summary: Probability nankor, the dubitative ya, intentive kusu ne, and counterfactual/irrealis interactions, plus the modal–evidential boundary.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Epistemic Modality, Dubitative, Intentive, and Irrealis
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: Probability nankor, the dubitative ya, intentive kusu ne, and counterfactual/irrealis interactions, plus the modal–evidential boundary.

The epistemic and irrealis modal field

Hokkaido Ainu expresses epistemic modality — the speaker's calibration of the probability or
certainty of a proposition — and irrealis modality — marking that an event has not yet been
actualized — through periphrastic auxiliaries that follow the lexical verb in the auxiliary
frame. In this frame the lexical verb carries person agreement while the auxiliary remains
morphologically unmarked, the same pattern as the perfective a and
the admirative aan treated in perfective-anterior-a .
Eight forms occupy this domain, ranging from epistemic probability to volitional commitment
to reportative hedging nakagawa2024 306–319 ; bugaeva2012 493–494 .
Across the typological literature on grammaticalization, the path from lexical verb to modal
auxiliary is well documented, and in Ainu most of these forms retain visible lexical identities
in the auxiliary set bybee1994 .

form
label
core value
full treatment

na → softened expectation or indirect command

this chapter

kusu ne (often kus ne )

na → near-command

this chapter

surely will; with tane 'now' → immediate future

this chapter

desiderative-rusuy

The TAM architecture that houses these auxiliaries — the distinction between the auxiliary
frame, the pair-verb frame, and the transitive-verb-auxiliary frame — is mapped in
tam-overview . The four strategies Ainu employs to recover temporal reference
without grammatical tense, among which the prospective auxiliaries play a central role, are
treated in tenselessness-and-time-reference . Deontic obligation and permission
( easirki ,
yak pirka ,
yakka pirka ) belong to deontic-necessity .

nankor — epistemic probability and conjecture

The auxiliary nankor probably follows the person-marked lexical verb and
indicates that the speaker regards the proposition as probable, likely, or to-be-expected
without asserting it as established fact: 推量「〜だろう」
nakagawa2024 311 . Bugaeva characterizes it as
"conjective probabilistic mood" bugaeva2012 493 . With a future or
present-state predicate the reading is probability ('probably will', 'is probably'); with a
past-referenced predicate it yields epistemic necessity ('must have'). In the corpus the
form returns 1,977 tokens at rank 108 — the highest frequency among the modal auxiliaries
— and it appears in narrative, spoken monologue, and chained clauses alike.

oro ta ek nankor kusu | there LOC come probably because | because he will probably come there, | First clause of a longer utterance; the continuation is 'let us live together for two or three years.' kusu here is the reason conjunction 'because', not part of the prospective construction kusu ne. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#106]

With a second-person subject and the sentence-final particle
na , nankor shifts from probability to a
softened expectation or indirect command — 「〜だろうよ」 — functioning
as a milder alternative to the bare imperative
nakagawa2024 311, 423 ; ijas2023 .
The same reading surfaces in prohibitive contexts: iteki … somo eci=ki nankor na
'you had better not not do it'
nakagawa2024 425 .

kari e=arpa nankor na | that.way 2SG.S=go probably FIN | You should go that way then. | Nakagawa ex. 469. With a second-person subject and final na, nankor functions as a softened directive rather than a probability statement. | nakagawa2024:311 | SAR

Phonologically, the string nankor na frequently assimilates to
nankonna in running speech nakagawa2024 311 .
The auxiliary can additionally appear after ruwe ne in the
post-evidential slot, where it hedges an already-evidentialized proposition as merely
probable; this post-nominal position — shared with the perfective a
and final particles — is part of the frame inventory for ruwe ne
detailed in
ruwe-ne-inferential frames the clause-final frames section
nakagawa2024 393 .

Ijäs (Lesson 10) proposes that the modal nankor derives from a
lexical verb nankor 'be trustworthy', but marks this derivation as
probable rather than established ijas2023 ‹speculative›. No grammar consulted
for this chapter traces the auxiliary to that lexical source, and the connection remains
unverified.

kus(u) ne — prospective intentional

The prospective construction consists of the purposive conjunction
kusu in order to, so that — phonologically reduced to
kus in running speech — followed by the copula
ne . Person agreement falls on the lexical verb and the
kusu ne string is morphologically frozen: the copula is not
separately inflected nakagawa2024 435 ; sato2008 85–86 .
As one of the four strategies for recovering future time reference without grammatical tense
(→ tenselessness-and-time-reference ), it marks an event as unrealized at the
moment of speaking. Three situational readings emerge from person and discourse context:

First-person intention: 'I intend to / I plan to' — the speaker commits
to an unrealized future action.

Scheduled future: 'it is to happen / it will happen' — the event is
foreseen as certain without necessarily implying personal volition.

Second-person near-command: with na → 'you
should / you're going to' — a directive softer than the bare imperative
(→ imperative-and-prohibitive ).

Nakagawa documents all three at 2024: 435, §14.1.2 (exx.966–969); Satō at 2008: 85–86.

an epitta paye=an kusu ne na | one entire go.PL=4.S PURP COP FIN | I intend to go, spending a whole night. | paye is the plural stem of arpa 'go'; =an is the fourth-person single argument, used here as the narrative first person. kusu ne na expresses the speaker's commitment to an unrealized future action. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak 2 [aa-irc/004#51]

nisatta arpa=an kusu ne na | tomorrow go=4.S PURP COP FIN | I will go tomorrow. | Constructed illustration of the scheduled-future reading with an overt time adverb. The form is neutral between volitional intention and an event foreseen as certain.

Ijäs writes the sequence as a single orthographic word, kusune ,
and tags it AUX or PRT-FIN; the analysis followed here retains the two-morpheme parse —
conjunction kusu plus copula ne — treating
the one-word spelling as a pedagogical convention rather than a morphological claim
nakagawa2024 435 ; ijas2023 .
The element kusu functions independently as a reason or purpose
clause conjunction (→ conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses ); the prospective
auxiliary is the specific combination kusu +
ne , with the copula predication turning an unrealized purpose into a
future-directed commitment.

The scope of kusu ne is strictly clause-internal: it sits below the
evidential frame, so kusu ne ruwe ne is well-formed ('it is a fact
that one intends to') while *ruwe ne kusu ne is ungrammatical
nakagawa2024 393 . The interaction with the evidential system is developed
in the scope section below.

Near-future auxiliaries: oasi, etokus, etokoyki

Three additional auxiliaries extend the prospective periphery beyond
kusu ne
nakagawa2024 316–318, 435 :

oasi will certainly happen — 'surely will happen'
( 将来確実に〜が起こる ); a stronger certainty than the merely
probable nankor . With tane 'now' it
yields an imminent reading, 'is just about to' nakagawa2024 318 .

etokus be about to — 'be about to (do)': the event is
on the point of beginning nakagawa2024 316 .

etokoyki be getting ready to — 'be getting ready to':
the agent is actively in the preparatory stage for the action
nakagawa2024 435 .

tane paye=an oasi | now go.PL=4.S will.certainly | I am just about to go. | Constructed illustration of oasi with tane 'now', giving the imminent reading. Nakagawa (2024: 318, exx.500–501) documents this combination.

arki etokus na | come.PL be.about.to FIN | They are about to arrive. | Constructed illustration of etokus; arki is the plural stem of ek 'come'.

All three are catalogued in Nakagawa's 表74 (本来的な助動詞, the primary Saru-dialect auxiliary
inventory) and in Bugaeva's English inventory
nakagawa2024 306, 316–318, 435 ;
bugaeva2012 493–494 .
No banked corpus examples are currently available for these three forms beyond the grammar
descriptions; the claim that they carry the stated meanings rests on Nakagawa's documented
paradigm ‹corpus-suggested›.

Desiderative rusuy and expectation kuni

rusuy want to is the desiderative auxiliary: 'want to do'. The lexical verb
takes the person affix and rusuy follows unmarked; a strong tendency
toward phonological incorporation produces forms such as
arparusuy 'want to go' and
iperusuy 'want to eat'
nakagawa2024 312–313 ; bugaeva2012 493 .
Argument structure, distribution with negation, and the incorporated variants are treated
fully in desiderative-rusuy .

A typological comparison has been proposed in the community: nukopoli
(aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-29) observes that rusuy parallels
Mandarin Chinese 要 ( yào ), which similarly covers both
desiderative ('want to') and near-future readings, while
kusu ne resembles English will in combining intentionality
with temporal commitment aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. No grammar read for
this chapter develops this typological comparison.

kuni should, be supposed to straddles the epistemic and deontic domains.
As an auxiliary it marks expectation or obligation ('should, be supposed to, it is expected
that'); as a subordinating particle it introduces complement clauses; and as a formal noun it
heads nominalized purpose structures — a=an kuni uske 'a place where
one should be' (Nakagawa 2024: 310, ex.461). The deontic uses and the
nominalized-complement function are treated in
deontic-necessity and
nominalized-complements-control-raising respectively.

manu — reportative hedging

manu reportedly, so-called is listed among the primary auxiliaries
(本来的な助動詞) at Nakagawa 2024: 310–311 (exx.464–467). Its core value is attributive or
reportative hedging: 'the so-called, reportedly, it is said that' — the speaker presents
the proposition as attributed, named, or received rather than directly asserted.

onne ne manu p | be.old COP reportedly NMLZ | the thing called old age | Nakagawa ex. 464. manu predicates the nominalized clause as attributed or named: the speaker presents the concept 'old age' as a received designation rather than a directly asserted fact. | nakagawa2024:310 | SAR

Nakagawa observes that manu approximates the Sakhalin
sentence-final hearsay marker — the cognate form in Sakhalin Ainu — while remaining a
distinct Hokkaido auxiliary, not a particle nakagawa2024 311 .
Within the Hokkaido system it sits on the boundary between the epistemic modals treated here
and the reportative evidential centered on hawe ne
(→ hawe-ne-reportative ). Unlike hawe ne , which
nominalizes a full clause and predicates it as verbal report,
manu inserts inside the predication and signals attribution of a
name or inherited category rather than a cited information channel. Corpus token counts are
currently unreliable for manu given surface-form homophony; it is
treated as infrequent in the Saru record.

Scope and the evidential interface

The epistemic and irrealis auxiliaries treated in this chapter all occupy clause-internal
scope — below the evidential frame. The ordering constraint is strict:
kusu ne ruwe ne is well-formed (the prospective claim is nominalized
and predicated as established fact), while *ruwe ne kusu ne is
ungrammatical because the prospective auxiliary cannot appear in the post-evidential position
nakagawa2024 393 . Negation belongs inside the frame in the same way:
somo ki ruwe ne negates the verb before the formal noun, not after
it. The full account of this layering appears in
ruwe-ne-inferential scope the scope and finiteness section .

Members of the aynu-corpora community have independently noted the same asymmetry: nukopoli
(aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09) observes that while
ruwe ne kusu (reason clause: 'because it is a fact that…') remains
well-formed, *ruwe ne kusu ne as a prospective construction after an
evidential is ruled out, attributing this to a clause hierarchy in which modality must remain
inside the outer scope of the evidential aynucorporadiscord ;
nakagawa2024 393 .

Corpus data supply chains that make the scope layering concrete. The most common
multi-auxiliary configuration places the prospective inside the admirative
aan , which is in turn nominalized by
ruwe ne — a three-level stack in the order
prospective ≺ mirative ≺ evidential.

kotan wente kus arki kus ne aan ru-we ne wa | village ravage PURP come.PL PURP COP PRF.MIR track-POSS COP CONJ | and it had turned out that they were going to come to ravage the village, | The inner purpose clause kotan wente kus arki 'come to ravage the village' is followed by kus ne (prospective: they intended to), then aan (admirative: the speaker realizes this after the fact), then ruwe ne (evidential: presented as established fact). wa closes the clause and chains it to the following predication. | biratori1969 | SAR | Kaizawa Tsurushino, Biratori [biratori/004/002#40]

nankor behaves differently from kusu ne in
this respect. As an epistemic auxiliary it can appear either before
ruwe ne — inside the evidentialized proposition ('it is a fact that
X probably…') — or after it, in the post-evidential slot where it hedges the evidentialized
claim as merely probable rather than fully asserted nakagawa2024 393 .
This dual positional freedom is shared by the perfective
a and final particles, both of which may follow
ruwe ne without being embedded inside the proposition; the full
inventory of post-nominal elements is set out in
ruwe-ne-inferential frames the clause-final frames section .

The conjunction kusu in conditional, purpose, and reason clauses
— as distinct from the prospective kusu ne — is treated in
conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses . The full interface between the modal
auxiliaries and the evidential paradigm, including stacking patterns and the
evidential-to-mirative cline, belongs to mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

title: A Glossed Everyday-Conversation Text
summary: A passage of recorded everyday conversation glossed and annotated to illustrate spontaneous spoken syntax, sentence-final particles, and connected-speech reduction.
part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: A Glossed Everyday-Conversation Text
Part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
Summary: A passage of recorded everyday conversation glossed and annotated to illustrate spontaneous spoken syntax, sentence-final particles, and connected-speech reduction.

The passage and its source

The passage glossed in this chapter is drawn from the Asahikawa section of
utari1994 , a conversational textbook produced by the Hokkaidō Utari Kyōkai to
accompany a set of VHS recordings. Two speakers are documented: Sugimura Fusa (杉村フサ)
and Kawamura Tome (川村トメ), both of Asahikawa (旭川市). The exchange depicts a
neighbour's visit — calling at the door, being invited in, exchanging a well-being greeting,
and closing with a weather remark — and brings into a single connected stretch several
constructions that are analysed in separate chapters of this grammar: the polar-question
frame of the evidential, the 1SG and 2SG person prefixes, the causal connective
kusu , and the inferential auxiliary
nankor . The dialect is Asahikawa ( nakagawa2024 82–83 ), a northern Hokkaido variety; the transcription uses the romanization conventions of the
original publication, with small-kana codas rendered as their consonant values.

The register is everyday colloquial speech, sharply distinct from the elevated
vocabulary of oral-literature genres ( uwepeker-prose-tale ;
inonno-itak-ritual-prayer ) and from the gendered-register differences
documented in gendered-register-speech . The published source is
utari1994 2–3 ; Asahikawa section, speakers Sugimura Fusa and Kawamura Tome.

Arrival and entry

Sugimura calls through the closed door, addressing her friend as sapo
(older sister, a close-kin address term). The existential verb
an exist, be appears without a person prefix: in Hokkaido Ainu, the 3rd
person is zero-marked ( person-marking-architecture ), and the intended referent
— the woman inside — is a 3rd-person entity from the caller's perspective. The
formal noun ruwe in its bare clause-final position, without the following copula
ne , builds the polar-question frame rather than the declarative
evidential ( ruwe-ne-inferential frames the polar-question frame ).

sapo an ru-we | older.sister exist track-POSS | サポ、アン ルウェ？ | Older sister, are you there? | Polar question via bare ruwe (without ne). The argument of an is zero-marked (3rd person, the sister inside the house). The same formula with an explicit 2SG.S prefix appears later in the same exchange (see §3 below). | utari1994:2 | ASA | Sugimura Fusa, Asahikawa

Kawamura responds and invites her visitor in. The directive particle
ya please (softener) in Asahikawa corresponds to
yan in Saru and most other varieties (a difference noted in a footnote of the
original publication utari1994 2 ); it functions as a polite imperative
softener, not as the default bare imperative
( sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force ). The motion verb
ahun enter is the singular form; its suppletive plural is
ahup . A single visitor is addressed, hence the singular
( suppletive-verbal-number ).

o ahun ya | INTERJ enter SOFT | オ、アフン ヤ。 | Come on in, please. | o is a non-lexical interjection (Japanese sā 'well then' equivalent). ya in ASA functions as the imperative softener yan of other dialects; the footnote in the source makes this explicit. | utari1994:2 | ASA | Kawamura Tome, Asahikawa

The well-being exchange

Later in the visit Sugimura enquires after Kawamura's well-being, now with an explicit
2nd-person prefix on the same existential verb an . The contrast between the
zero-marked opening question ( an ruwe? , §2) and the prefix-marked
well-being question shows the full range of the person system operating on a single verb
stem: 3rd-person referents are zero-marked, while 1SG and 2SG carry distinct prefixes
throughout Hokkaido ( first-second-person-singular-affixes ).

sapo totekno e=an ru-we | older.sister in.good.health 2SG.S=exist track-POSS | サポ、トテクノ エアン ルウェ？ | Sister, have you been well? | e= is the 2SG.S prefix (intransitive subject), marking the addressee as the sole argument of an. totekno (in good health, well) is an adverb modifying the predicate. The polar-question ruwe frame is the same as in §2. | utari1994:2 | ASA | Sugimura Fusa, Asahikawa

totekno ku=an wa | in.good.health 1SG.S=exist CONJ | トテクノ クアン ワ。 | I am well. | ku= is the 1SG.S prefix, mirroring the 2SG.S e= of the question. The clause-linking particle wa (CONJ) closes the turn without a following clause; in conversational Ainu, a wa-final utterance signals a complete turn while leaving the chain formally open (see Nakagawa 2024: 345). | utari1994:2 | ASA | Kawamura Tome, Asahikawa

Person marking in the Ainu verb is obligatory in careful speech; its omission in the
opening formula above reflects conversational reduction where the referent is fully
recoverable from the situation ( nakagawa2024 163 ; sato2008 100–101 ). The conjunctive particle wa connecting or closing clauses is
treated in sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining .

A weather exchange

The visit closes with a brief weather exchange. Kawamura's remark is a biclausal
construction: a causal adverbial clause in kusu because followed by a
main-clause assertion. The causal connective belongs to the pattern treated in
causal-and-purpose-clauses . The property verb
merayke be cold is an intransitive predicate whose subject — the weather
— is zero-marked at 3rd person.
