numan poronno upas as kusu tanto sonno merayke na | last.night much snow fall because today truly be.cold FIN | ヌマン、ポロンノ ウバシ アシ クス タント ソンノ メライケ ナ。 | Because there was a lot of snow last night, today is truly cold. | The causal clause (numan … kusu) provides the reason; the main clause (tanto … na) asserts the result. as 'stand/fall' is the standard verb for snowfall (upas as = snow falls). The sentence-final particle na is a soft assertion marker. | utari1994:3 | ASA | Kawamura Tome, Asahikawa

Sugimura's agreeing response uses the same property verb
merayke be cold with the 4th-person intransitive suffix
=an . The 4th / indefinite person, whose S-role clitic is =an ,
shifts the perspective from a specific 3rd-person subject to a general experiencer
( indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm ). The existential-type aspect form
V=an is the same suffix in its aspectual function, discussed
in existential-locational-an-oka .

sonno merayke=an | truly be.cold=4.S | ソンノ メライケアン。 | It really is cold. | merayke=an: the 4th-person intransitive suffix =an attaches to the property verb, yielding a general-experiencer reading ('it is cold out'). Compare merayke na above, where the subject is 3rd-person zero-marked. | utari1994:3 | ASA | Sugimura Fusa, Asahikawa

Kawamura rounds off the exchange with a prediction, using the inferential auxiliary
nankor . Unlike ruwe ne , which presents content as
already-established knowledge ( ruwe-ne-inferential ), nankor
encodes a tentative, inference-based assertion: the speaker reasons from current conditions
rather than perceiving or reporting the future event directly. The auxiliary is discussed
as part of the epistemic-modal paradigm in epistemic-irrealis-modality and at
the evidential-mirative interface in mirativity-scope-grammaticalization . The
Asahikawa form undergoes sandhi: nankor followed by the final
particle na surfaces as nankornā , rendered
in the source's Katakana as ナンコンナー.

nisatta sirpirka nankor na | tomorrow be.fine.weather INFR FIN | ニサッタ シリピリカ ナンコンナー。 | Tomorrow will probably be fine. | nankor encodes inference-based assertion (INFR): the speaker has no direct evidence about tomorrow's weather and reasons from current conditions. The Katakana ナンコンナー represents the ASA sandhi form nankor + na → nankornā. | utari1994:3 | ASA | Kawamura Tome, Asahikawa

Synopsis of constructions

The seven utterances above cover a cluster of constructions whose full analyses belong
to separate chapters. The table maps each to its primary grammatical feature.

utterance
primary feature
treated in

sapo an ru-we?
polar-question evidential frame (bare ruwe ); 3rd-person zero-marking
ruwe-ne-inferential ; person-marking-architecture

o ahun ya
directive with softener ya (ASA ≈ yan elsewhere); suppletive singular ahun
sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force ; suppletive-verbal-number

sapo totekno e=an ru-we?
2SG.S prefix e= ; polar-question frame
first-second-person-singular-affixes ; ruwe-ne-inferential

totekno ku=an wa
1SG.S prefix ku= ; clause-linking wa
first-second-person-singular-affixes ; sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining

numan poronno upas as kusu tanto sonno merayke na
causal clause with kusu ; property verb predication
causal-and-purpose-clauses ; property-concept-predication

sonno merayke=an
4th-person suffix =an ; existential-type aspect
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm ; existential-locational-an-oka

nisatta sirpirka nankor na
inferential auxiliary nankor
epistemic-irrealis-modality ; mirativity-scope-grammaticalization

The passage also illustrates a characteristic feature of Ainu conversational structure:
each turn-final predicate controls the evidential or modal framing for the whole turn —
ruwe in the questions, wa in the
open-chain response, bare na in the assertions, and
nankor na in the inference. The parallel between this conversational
pattern and the evidential-turn structure of oral-literature frame-text is noted in
nakagawa2024 392–396 ; the contrast with oral-literature patterning is
illustrated in narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

title: The Nominalization-plus-Copula Evidential Schema
summary: The architecture of the Ainu evidential system: a clause-nominalizing formal noun (ruwe/siri/hawe/humi) plus the copula ne/an encodes the speaker's source of information.
part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Nominalization-plus-Copula Evidential Schema
Part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
Summary: The architecture of the Ainu evidential system: a clause-nominalizing formal noun (ruwe/siri/hawe/humi) plus the copula ne/an encodes the speaker's source of information.

The nominalization-plus-copula schema

Hokkaido Ainu encodes the speaker's source of information through a productive construction:
a finite verb phrase is nominalized by a bound formal noun and the result is
predicated with the copula ne or an existential or posture verb.
The language has no dedicated evidential verbal affix. The four formal nouns that build the
construction — ruwe , siri ,
hawe , and humi — are each the possessed form
of a common sensory noun, and they carry their sensory origin into their evidential meaning
nakagawa2024 258, 390–392 ; hirosawa2026 64 .

The structural template is [CLAUSE]-FORMAL.NOUN + ne : the nominalized
event is presented as the possessed trace, appearance, voice, or feeling of something, and the
copula closes the predication. The formal noun specifies the information channel; the copula
anchors the proposition to the speaker's direct or reflective perspective. Four
direct evidential forms use the copula ne ; a parallel set of
indirect counterparts replaces it with the existential an , the action
verb ki , or the posture verb as
dalcorso2018 304–314 . The construction belongs to the broader nominalization
system treated in nominalization-p-pe and
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization ; the formal-noun word class that makes
it possible is defined in formal-defective-nouns .

tap a=ye katu Piratur sekor a=ye haw-e ne | this 4.A=say manner Piraturu QUOT 4.A=say voice-POSS COP | This way of saying it is called Piraturu, so they say. | hawe (haw-e, 'the voice of —') nominalizes the embedded clause; ne closes it as a direct reportative evidential. The doubled a=ye sequence illustrates the quotation-plus-report structure typical of hawe ne. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 778; underlying source: Kawamura 1985: 30

The four formal nouns: morphological sources and evidential values

Tamura's Saru dictionary records the source nouns, their regular possessed forms, and the
evidential formal nouns derived from them tamura1996 s.v. ru, sir, haw, hum, ruwe, siri, hawe, humi .
Nakagawa tabulates the evidential values nakagawa2024 258 .

formal noun
source noun
possessed gloss
original meaning
evidential value
chapter

ruwe
ru track, path
'the trace of —'
跡・道 track, path
fact recognized through knowledge or reasoning
ruwe-ne-inferential

siri
sir appearance, land, weather
'the appearance of —'
様子・天候・大地 appearance, weather, land
directly visible situation
siri-ne-situational

hawe
haw voice
'the voice of —'
声 voice
verbal report or hearsay
hawe-ne-reportative

humi
hum sound, feeling
'the sound/feeling of —'
音・感じ sound, feeling
non-visual sensation (sound, smell, taste, touch, bodily sense)
humi-ne-sensory

The regular possessed forms of the source nouns may carry an additional ending —
ruwe(he) , humi(hi) — but the evidential
formal nouns block this extension: *ruwehe and
*humihi are ungrammatical in the evidential function
tamura1996 s.v. ruwe, humi . Whether the concept form
( sir , haw ) and the corresponding possessed
form carry a usage or nuance difference is explicitly left unresolved in Tamura's dictionary
tamura1996 s.v. sir ‹speculative›. The possessed morphology is significant
in its own right: a noun in its possessed form grammatically presupposes an antecedent, so
these nominalizers formally encode that the speaker already has the relevant situation in hand
sato2008 135 .

Typological placement: the C1 system

Mapped onto Aikhenvald's evidential typology aikhenvald2004 63–65 , the Ainu
system belongs to the C1 type: visual, non-visual sensory, inferred, and reported
sources of information are grammatically distinguished
hirosawa2026 64 ; takahashi2013 129 . The type is realized
analytically through a nominalization-plus-copula construction, not through dedicated verbal
affixes — this makes Ainu a textbook case of the nominalization-based evidential strategy
dalcorso2018 24–25 ; takahashi2022 ‹consensus›.

The cell assignments are approximate. ruwe ne covers the
inferred cell (information from reasoning or assimilated knowledge),
siri ne the visual cell,
humi ne the non-visual sensory cell, and
hawe ne the reported cell. The boundary between the visual
( siri ) and the knowledge-from-trace ( ruwe )
cells is contested in the recent literature and is discussed in
ruwe-ne-inferential visual-puzzle the ruwe ne chapter ‹contested›.

Direct and indirect evidentiality: the eight-form system

Dal Corso's morphosyntactic account resolves the four formal nouns into an
eight-form system organized by two parameters: source reliability (REL)
and event accessibility (ACCES) dalcorso2018 304–337 . The four
direct forms use the copula ne (internal, speaker-anchored
perspective); the four indirect forms replace it with the existential
an , the action verb ki , or the posture verb
as (external perspective; 2018: 313–314). Source reliability is
not epistemic modality: it specifies the psychophysical or cognitive channel of acquisition
and says nothing about the speaker's degree of certainty
dalcorso2018 339 .

ACCES+ (direct, copula ne )
ACCES− (indirect, an / ki / as )

REL+ (highest reliability)
ruwe ne
— (no indirect *ru -form in southern Hokkaido)

siri ne
siri an , siri ki

humi ne
humi as

REL− (lowest reliability)
hawe ne
hawe as

The underlying sense hierarchy runs Sight > Smell/Taste–Touch > Hearing, yielding a
coarser visual/non-visual split: ruwe ne and
siri ne are the higher-reliability direct members,
humi ne and hawe ne the lower
dalcorso2018 312 . A structural asymmetry distinguishes the
ru -series: in Saru, Chitose, and Biratori the trace-noun enters
only direct evidentiality, and there is no indirect *ru an counterpart
to siri an dalcorso2018 309, 326 . The
hawe as indirect form has, moreover, nearly fully specialized as a
reportative — for quotation when the source is overt and for hearsay when it is
absent — while humi as is never reportative
dalcorso2018 328–330 . The per-form indirect semantics are treated in
siri-ne-situational , hawe-ne-reportative , and
humi-ne-sensory .

Token frequencies

ruwe ne dominates the paradigm quantitatively. In Dal Corso's Hokkaido
reference texts it accounts for 1508 of 2232 evidential tokens — roughly two thirds of all
eight forms, and more than five times the next most frequent form
( hawe ne , 298 tokens) dalcorso2018 72 . The asymmetry
is independently noted by Tamura and by Yoshikawa: ruwe ne outstrips
the other forms in oral-narrative (口承文芸) registers, where it functions as the default
frame-text predicate hirosawa2026 73 . Community discussion characterizes
ruwe ne in the same terms — frequent especially in oral narratives and
legends, functioning like Japanese のだ to close narrative segments
and mark topic shifts (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09)
aynucorporadiscord .

Token frequencies in Dal Corso's Hokkaido reference corpora dalcorso2018 72

form
tokens
% of eight-form total

ruwe ne 1508 67.56%

hawe ne 298 13.35%

siri ne 149 6.68%

hawe as 94 4.21%

siri an 59 2.64%

humi ne 45 2.02%

siri ki 42 1.88%

humi as 37 1.66%

These counts derive from Dal Corso's specific reference texts (Kayano, Tamura, Nakagawa,
Bugaeva), not from the full Hokkaido corpus, and should be treated as indicative of the
skew rather than as absolute proportions ‹corpus-suggested›. The dominance of
ruwe ne is consistent with its status as the unmarked, most
grammaticalized member of the paradigm. The genre distribution of evidential usage across
narrative registers is treated in narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Analytical frameworks

Four analytical traditions describe the same construction from different angles, and they
partially conflict.

The oldest is the mood (法) tradition. Kindaichi identified seven distinctive moods
in Ainu; within that taxonomy siri is the
見説法 (seeing-speech mood), hawe the
聞説法 , humi the
感説法 , and ruwe the
確説法 / 事実法
kindaichi1936 ; hirosawa2026 65–68 . The mood label captures the
speaker-oriented, assertive character of these forms but does not isolate their source
semantics.

The modern evidentiality framing, now standard in the descriptive literature,
places the system in the typological framework of information-source encoding
aikhenvald2004 . It is adopted by Takahashi
takahashi2013 129 , Dal Corso dalcorso2018 , Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 392 , and Hirosawa hirosawa2026 64 . Dal
Corso's is the most detailed morphosyntactic account (§ Direct and indirect evidentiality above).

The predication-type framing (Takahashi 2013) adds a dimension orthogonal to source
channel: in Tokachi, the visual nominalizer sir is restricted to
stage-level (transient, scene-describing) predication, and individual-level (permanent)
properties take ru regardless of the information channel
takahashi2013 131–133 . The ontological type of the predicate thus
independently constrains the available evidential ‹consensus›.

The information-structure framing is Hirosawa's (2026). He argues that
ruwe ne additionally marks discourse-new information, parallel to
Japanese のだ , and unconditionally declares the content to lie inside
the speaker's territory of information hirosawa2026 75–81, 89 . Dal Corso
explicitly argues that the territory-of-information account is inapplicable to Hokkaido
direct evidentiality dalcorso2018 335–337 . These two recent treatments
disagree on the right framework for ruwe ne ‹contested›. Community
discussion, summarizing Yoshikawa (2020), characterizes ruwe ne as
semantically unmarked relative to the other forms — lacking their specific evidentiary
highlighting — and as functioning to close a narrative segment and transition to the next
(場面転換・統括), consistent with Hirosawa's analysis (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord,
2024-03-17) aynucorporadiscord ; this observation is treated here as
corroboration of the literature rather than an independent authority.

Ijäs's pedagogical grammar presents the system under the evidentiality framing without
adopting any of the specific theoretical positions above
ijas2023 . The pedagogical description aligns with the standard evidential
account and is useful for language learners but does not adjudicate the analytical disputes.

The shared clause-final frame paradigm

All four formal nouns participate in a shared set of clause-final frames that vary the
following element and the sentence type. Nakagawa arranges these into a paradigm of four
patterns nakagawa2024 392–396 :

frame
sentence type
note

NMLZ + ne
declarative (平叙文)
direct evidential; may be followed by a , nankor , final particles, clause-linkers

NMLZ + an
content question (内容疑問文, with question word)
existential replaces copula; the formal noun stays in position

NMLZ (bare)
polar question / exclamation
insubordinated nominalized clause; Saru reinforces as ruwe he an?

NMLZ + un
affirmative answer
un is a final particle, not itself evidential

The bare polar-question frame is an instance of insubordination — a
once-embedded nominalized clause promoted to main-clause use
takahashi2013 132 . Negation operates inside the evidential scope: the
predicate is negated with somo before the formal noun, while the
formal noun remains outside the scope of negation
( …somo ki ruwe ne 'it is a fact that [one does not …]')
nakagawa2024 393 . The interrogative frames are treated in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles ; the existential frame in
existential-locational-an-oka the existential an chapter .

usey ku=ku wa keraan hum-i | tea 1SG.A=drink CONJ be.delicious sound-POSS | I drank tea — how delicious! | The bare formal noun hum-i as an exclamatory main clause, without ne. The insubordinated nominalized clause carries the exclamatory value alone; the copula is absent. | nakagawa2024:396 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 793

arki=an pa sir-i ne na | come.PL=4.S now appearance-POSS COP FIN | (We) have now arrived! | siri ne declarative with the sentence-final particle na. The suppletive plural come-form arki (vs. singular ek) encodes number inside the nominalized clause; the evidential scopes over the whole clause including this number distinction. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsu, ILCAA Ainu materials 007: 44

Scope over TAM and the finiteness of the embedded clause

The constituent nominalized by the formal noun is a finite, clause-sized predication. It
carries its own person marking, verb suppletion (singular ek vs.
plural arki 'come'), aspectual auxiliaries, perfective
a , and negation; the evidential formal noun scopes over that whole
TAM-marked clause nakagawa2024 393 . The layering is transparent from
ordering: the formal noun may be followed by aspectual material
( a , nankor ), final particles
( na , wa ), and clause-linkers
( hine , kusu ), but the intentive
kusu ne ('intends to') cannot follow the evidential:
kusu ne ruwe ne is well-formed but
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not
nakagawa2024 393 . The evidential thus sits above clause-internal aspect
but below clause-linkers and intentive modality. Community discussion independently observes
that this ordering follows from the clause hierarchy placing the evidential inside modality
operators (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09)
aynucorporadiscord .

Dal Corso ties the copula vs. existential/posture-verb choice to perspective: the copula
ne expresses an internal, speaker-anchored viewpoint and hosts the
direct forms; an , as , and
ki express an external viewpoint and host the indirect forms
dalcorso2018 313–315 . A structural polysemy follows for
siri an : it is three-way ambiguous between the interrogative
existential frame, the indirect visual evidential, and an aspectual resultative-progressive —
a polysemy treated in siri-ne-situational and
continuative-siri-siran-kane-an . The interaction of the evidential with the
wider clause-final TAM and negation apparatus is treated in
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation and
copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

Predication type and the Tokachi stage-level constraint

In Tokachi the choice among the formal nouns is constrained not only by information source
but also by the ontological type of the predicate. Takahashi's Tokachi study shows that the
visual nominalizer sir is restricted to stage-level
(transient, scene-describing) predication; for individual-level (permanent
property) predicates — even those known by sight — Tokachi uses ru ,
which is unmarked for information type
takahashi2013 131–133 . Of the four, only sir carries
this restriction; hum , haw , and
ru function at both predication levels.

taan imo siwnin ru an | this potato be.green track exist | This potato is green. | An individual-level (permanent) property takes the bare concept form ru even though it is visually accessible: sir is barred from individual-level predicates in Tokachi. The existential an replaces the copula ne, as is standard in Tokachi declarative evidential clauses. | takahashi2013:131–133 | TOK | Honbetsu; consultant: Sawai Tomeno

Takahashi parallels the constraint with the ban on individual-level complements of English
perception verbs; in Ainu the restriction surfaces on the nominalizer rather than on a matrix
verb. This finding sets bounds on any pure source-channel account of the formal-noun choice:
the grammar of predication independently constrains the available evidential ‹consensus›.
Its implications for the ruwe ne / siri ne
boundary are discussed in ruwe-ne-inferential predication-type the
ruwe ne chapter .

Grammaticalization and the nominalization-to-evidential cline

The formal nouns are partially, not fully, grammaticalized from their source nouns. The
diachronic path is already stated in the early grammars: the source nouns function first as
lexical nouns, then as clause-nominalizing heads in embedded complements, then as clause-final
evidential sentence-enders takahashi2013 134 ; takahashi2018 111 .
At each stage the lexical semantics of the source noun remains visible, but the syntactic
freedoms of a full noun are progressively lost. The evidential formal nouns admit no
demonstrative or attributive modifier of their own, cannot head an ordinary common-noun
relative clause, and are fixed in linear position
dalcorso2018 24–25 ; nakagawa2024 393 .

Dal Corso stresses that the degree of grammaticalization varies across the system:
ruwe ne is the most grammaticalized of the four Hokkaido direct
forms, with the Sakhalin particle manu at the far end of the cline
as a fully fused suffix dalcorso2018 24–25 . The Sakhalin nominalization
innovations and their contrast with Hokkaido are set out in bugaeva2016 .
The path from noun to nominalizer to clause-final particle belongs to the wider
nominalization-grammaticalization cline treated in
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

Dialect microvariation

The semantic organization of the system — four channels, direct vs. indirect — is consistent
across Hokkaido; its morphosyntactic realization varies. Four dialect-level patterns stand out.

Saru, Chitose, Biratori (the core of Dal Corso's data) use the possessed
forms ruwe / siri / hawe / humi and maintain the eight-form
direct/indirect system dalcorso2018 304–337 .

Tokachi (and frequently Ishikari) close the clause with the bare concept
forms ru / sir / haw / hum rather than the possessed forms
nakagawa2024 110, 390 ; takahashi2013 129–131 .
Allomorphs sin (for sir ) and
aw (for haw ) are attested
takahashi2013 130–131 .

Horobetsu grammaticalizes the possessed-form nominalizers a step further
into adverbial-clause markers and conjunction-like particles — a use not reported for
well-described Saru takahashi2018 107–112 . Kindaichi and Chiri had already
noted the nominalizers in conjunction-like uses; Takahashi shows Horobetsu has
systematized this extension, bringing evidentiality into the set of categories expressed in
adverbial clauses alongside aspect and modality — an instance of 'versatile nominalization'
takahashi2018 112 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Asahikawa and Shiranuka show an inferential ru an ,
with the trace-noun entering the indirect frame in apparent complementary distribution with
the siri an of the southern dialects
dalcorso2018 326 . This is the one Hokkaido context where
ru forms indirect evidentiality; the distribution is flagged by Dal
Corso as requiring a wider-dialect study ‹corpus-suggested›.

Dialect coverage is solid for Saru/Chitose/Biratori and Tokachi; thin or second-hand for
Ishikari, Asahikawa, Shiranuka, and Shizunai. The dialect classification is set out in
the dialectology chapters; the TAM-evidential variation across registers is in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Double evidentiality

Two evidential expressions may stack in one clause; this is more frequent in Hokkaido than
in Sakhalin dalcorso2018 331–333 . Two constructions recur. In the first,
a character's indirect source is placed within the narrator's
ruwe ne , yielding both the character's subjective source and the
narrator's assertion of the event as traditional knowledge:

supuya at kor sir-an ru-we ne anan | smoke rise while appearance-exist track-POSS COP MIR | Smoke was rising — it turned out! | Dal Corso ex. 268. The indirect visual siri an is embedded under the direct ruwe ne; the admirative tail anan adds the surprised-retrospective value (glossed MIR). The supporting outer form is systematically ruwe ne, never siri ne — evidence that ruwe ne functions as a higher-scope operator that ratifies another evidential's output as established fact. | dalcorso2018:327 | HK | Nakagawa text, Dal Corso siglum NGF: 176

In the second pattern, ruwe ne reinforces a lower-reliability direct
form, boosting epistemic commitment: attested
hawe ne nankor ruwe ne 'maybe my god secretly let me know'
dalcorso2018 332 . Other attested combinations include
hawe as ruwe ne , siri ki siri ne ,
siri an ruwe ne , and
humi ne humi as
dalcorso2018 331–333 . The consistent outer position of
ruwe ne in stacked constructions supports its analysis as the
epistemic-incorporation operator of the paradigm, a role discussed in
ruwe-ne-inferential .

Mirativity and the admirative

The four formal nouns do not themselves encode mirativity (意外性). Dal Corso finds no
evidence that the Hokkaido evidential paradigm systematically generates mirative readings;
this contrasts with Sakhalin, where personal-knowledge evidentials can trigger mirative
interpretations through a deictic-gap mechanism
dalcorso2018 336 . In Hokkaido, mirative value attaches instead to the
perfective auxiliary aan and to anan
hirosawa2026 90 . Yoshikawa argues that aan
signals indirect evidentiality plus mirativity (reported via
hirosawa2026 90 ) yoshikawa2018 ‹speculative› (Yoshikawa
2018 cited second-hand).

The two mechanisms can co-occur. When aan places information outside
the speaker's expected knowledge and ruwe ne follows, the sequence
expresses surprise then reclamation: the speaker marks the event as unexpected
( aan ) and then declares it recognized fact ( ruwe ne ).
Hirosawa parallels this with Japanese みたいな + のだ
hirosawa2026 90 . The full treatment of the evidential-to-mirative interface,
evidential scope, and the cline from formal-noun evidentials to sentence-final particles is in
mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

title: The Existential-Aspect System: kor an Progressive and wa an Resultative-Perfect
summary: The existential verbs an (SG)/oka (PL) recruited as aspectual auxiliaries unifying kor an and wa an under one 'existential aspect': the progressive/continuative kor an and the resultative-perfect wa an, with number agreement, aktionsart conditioning, and grammaticalization.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Existential-Aspect System: kor an Progressive and wa an Resultative-Perfect
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: The existential verbs an (SG)/oka (PL) recruited as aspectual auxiliaries unifying kor an and wa an under one 'existential aspect': the progressive/continuative kor an and the resultative-perfect wa an, with number agreement, aktionsart conditioning, and grammaticalization.

The pair-verb frame and the existential auxiliary

Two periphrastic constructions recruit the existential verbs
an (singular) and oka /
okay (plural) as the second predicate in a clause chain. The
simultaneous frame V kor an marks an action or change in progress
and habitual situations; the sequential frame V wa an marks the
state that persists after a change has been completed
nakagawa2024 439–442 ; sato2008 195–199 ;
bugaeva2012 494–495 .

Both constructions belong to the tam-overview pair-verb frame : each predicate
retains its own person marking and its status as a finite verb. This finite independence is
the diagnostic that distinguishes them from fully grammaticalized aspect suffixes, and it is
the basis on which Nakagawa treats kor an and
wa an as forms that express aspect-like meaning rather than as
grammatical aspect proper nakagawa2024 442 . Yoshikawa develops the same
point under the cover term existential aspectual forms
( 存在型アスペクト形式 ): an here is less
grammaticalized than Japanese いる / ある ,
retaining its concrete existential sense, and the cline from lexical existential to
aspectual auxiliary has advanced without reaching obligatory inflectional morphology
yoshikawa2022 ; bybee1994 ‹contested›.

The locational and existential uses of an and
oka are covered in existential-locational-an-oka . The
suppletive SG/PL alternation underlying the pair belongs to
suppletive-verbal-number . The conjunctive particles
kor 'while' and wa 'and then' are analysed in
their wider clause-linking roles in simultaneous-kor-clauses and
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining . The merged and extended continuatives
kane an , kan an ,
tek an , and hine an belong to
continuative-siri-siran-kane-an , and the actional-perfect auxiliary
a / rok to perfective-anterior-a .

The suppletive existential as auxiliary

an and oka form one of the suppletive
singular/plural verb pairs that the Hokkaido verb system maintains: the singular stem is
an , the plural oka (realized as
okay in many phonological environments). The two are in strict
complementary distribution by the number of the controlling argument
nakagawa2024 142–145 ; bugaeva2012 471 ;
ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

In the aspectual periphrases this suppletion carries over directly: the auxiliary tracks
the same argument that would trigger the choice between the lexical forms. For
kor an and the standard intransitive pattern of
wa an , that argument is the shared subject; in the
transitive object-agreement pattern of wa an , it is the object.
The preserved SG/PL alternation is consistent with the partial-grammaticalization
assessment shared by Nakagawa, Yoshikawa, and Bugaeva: a fully fused aspect suffix
typically loses lexical alternations of this kind. Ijäs catalogues an / oka
as one of several suppletive pairs — alongside a / rok
'sit', ek / arki 'come', and
arpa / paye 'go' — whose number suppletion
extends into grammaticalized contexts ijas2023 ;
nakagawa2024 142–151 ‹consensus›.

kor an: progressive, habitual, and inceptive

The construction V kor an (plural: V kor oka )
joins the lexical verb to the simultaneous conjunctive particle kor
'while' and the existential verb. With action verbs — activities without an
inherent change of state ( apkas 'walk', mina
'laugh', sinot 'play') — the construction marks the action as
in progress at the reference time. With change verbs — predicates whose
completion involves a change of state or location ( as 'stand up',
hacir 'fall', ek 'come') — it marks the
process leading toward the change, not the resulting state, which is the domain of
wa an
nakagawa2024 440–441 ; sato2008 196 ;
bugaeva2012 494 .

yukkoterke kor an ayne | leap.at.deer while exist then | while (he) was springing upon the deer — | kor an with an action verb (yukkoterke, incorporating the noun yuk 'deer'); the progressive marks the action as in progress at a narrative reference point. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 1 [aa-irc/003#64]

The construction also covers habitual and repeated situations — the type
that Japanese translates with 〜している in iterative contexts — and
with change verbs approaching their culmination, an inceptive reading
('it is getting light'; Saru epuykehe tane sipuske kor oka
'the buds are now about to open')
sato2008 197 ; nakagawa2024 441 ;
bugaeva2012 494 .

sittokap kor pon seta turano sinot=an kor an=an ayne | noon when small dog with play=4.S while exist=4.S then | at noon I would always play with the puppy, and | Habitual value set by the temporal clause sittokap kor 'whenever it was noon'. Both sinot and the existential an carry the fourth-person suffix =an, the pair-verb signature (§agreement below). | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 9 [aa-irc/009#14]

When the subject is plural, an is replaced by
oka . The generic plural marker pa may
follow the main verb and precede the conjunctive:

uenewsar pa kor oka | chat PL while exist.PL | they are chatting | Plural subject: oka replaces an in the auxiliary slot. This is the predicate of Nakagawa's ex. 983; the full sentence is a=onaha turano uenewsar pa kor oka 'they are chatting with my father'. | nakagawa2024:441 | SAR

Person agreement in kor an

Because kor an is a chain of two finite clauses, the lexical verb
and the existential each take a person index independently, and both must agree with the
shared subject. The co-indexation is the clearest diagnostic of the pair-verb structure:
in the example above, the fourth-person suffix =an appears on
both sinot and the existential. The same holds with overt
second-person prefixes:

hemanta e=ciskar kor e=an | what 2SG.S=cry while 2SG.S=exist | What are you crying about? | The second-person prefix e= prefixes both ciskar 'cry' and the existential an; two independently indexed finite verbs joined by kor. | nakagawa2024:442 | SAR

The Tokachi first-person restriction

In Tokachi, kor an cannot co-occur with a first-person subject.
The progressive of a first-person event uses kan an instead:
ku=nukar kan ku=an 'I am watching it' is grammatical where
*ku=nukar kor ku=an is not. Takahashi proposes that Tokachi
kor an carries an observational component — presenting an event
as witnessed by the speaker — that is incompatible with the speaker being the agent
performing the action takahashi2022 ‹contested›. The restriction is
specific to Tokachi; no equivalent is reported for Saru, Chitose, or the remaining
Hokkaido varieties. The distribution of kan an is covered in
continuative-siri-siran-kane-an .

wa an: the resultative

The construction V wa an (plural: V wa oka )
joins the lexical verb to the sequential conjunctive particle wa and
the existential verb, denoting the state that exists after the completion of a change. The
canonical environment is a change verb : the nearest Japanese equivalents
are 〜ている in its result-state reading and
〜てある
nakagawa2024 439 ; sato2008 197–198 ;
bugaeva2012 494 ; sato2006 .

ek wa an pe hoppa kor | come CONJ exist NMLZ send.away CONJ | taking away what had arrived, and | ek wa an 'has come and is now here': the resultative of the motion-change verb ek. The nominalized clause ek wa an pe 'the thing that has arrived' functions as the object of hoppa. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8 (1) [aa-irc/006#146]

The motion verb ek provides the clearest minimal pair, because it
enters both frames with distinct readings:

ek kor an | come while exist | is coming / is on the way | kor an with ek: the change of location is in progress, the arrival not yet complete. Nakagawa ex. 986. | nakagawa2024:441

ek wa an | come CONJ exist | has arrived and is (now) here | wa an with ek: the motion is complete and the resultant state — being present at the destination — persists. Nakagawa ex. 987. | nakagawa2024:441

Stative verbs enter wa an under their inchoative
reading. The verb poro be big; become big 'be big / become big', read as
'become big', takes wa an to describe the reached state:
poro wa an 'has grown / is now big'
nakagawa2024 440 . Action verbs — activities without an
inherent endpoint — are excluded, since they leave no stable result for the existential
to predicate: *tapkar wa an (from tapkar dance ) and
*uenewsar wa an are ungrammatical
nakagawa2024 441 . Their completion falls to the auxiliary
a , treated in perfective-anterior-a .

isepo ray wa an | hare die CONJ exist | The hare is dead. | Satō's constructed diagnostic (Bugaeva ex. 72f). The punctual change verb ray 'die' yields a stable result state; *isepo ray kor an is bad — death is not an ongoing process. | sato2008:195; bugaeva2012:495

Satō extends the action-verb exclusion into a complementary-distribution claim: the actional
perfect — completion of an action, without implying a residual state — belongs exclusively to
the auxiliary a , while wa an covers only
stative perfects (a result state persists) sato2006 63 ;
sato2008 198 ‹corpus-suggested›. Bugaeva concurs that a separate
auxiliary-based construction handles the actional perfect
bugaeva2012 493, 495 .

mokor wa an: sleep as a result-state

Nakagawa draws attention to mokor wa an 'is asleep' as a case
where Ainu forces a choice that Japanese leaves ambiguous. The verb
mokor sleep, fall asleep is construed as a change of state — the act of
falling asleep — rather than as an ongoing activity, so the result state (being asleep)
is what wa an predicates. A progressive reading under
kor an is unavailable for this verb
nakagawa2024 444 . Japanese 眠っている is
ambiguous between the progressive and the resultative; West Japanese dialects resolve
the same ambiguity formally, with -shiyoru (progressive) and
-shitoru (resultative), paralleling the Ainu distinction.

mokor wa an | sleep CONJ exist | is asleep | Nakagawa ex. 998. Only the resultant-state reading is available: Ainu construes sleep as the result of falling asleep, not as an ongoing activity. | nakagawa2024:444 | SAR

Person agreement in wa an

As in kor an , both predicates in the chain carry independent
person marking. For wa an , however, the controller of the
existential depends on transitivity and which argument holds the resultant state
nakagawa2024 443 ; sato2008 198 :

pattern
condition
controller of an
illustrative form
gloss

(a) intransitive
V intransitive; subject persists in resultant state
S of V
hotke=an wa an=an
'I am / was lying down'

(b) transitive, agent
V transitive; agent = pivot of an
A of V
a=ehotke wa an=an
'I am lying on it'

(c) transitive, object
V transitive; object holds resultant state
O of V
a=o wa an
'(I) have put it in — it is in there'

In (a) and (b), both predicates bear the same person index, exactly as in
kor an . In (c) — the construction closest to Japanese
〜てある — the agent's action is complete and what persists is
the object in its new state; the existential an appears bare
when the object is third-person or inanimate, because no explicit person prefix is
required nakagawa2024 443 ; sato2008 198 ;
bugaeva2012 495 .

a=o wa an | 4.A=put.in CONJ exist | (I) have put it in — it is in there. | Object-agreement pattern (c): an indexes the object (the thing placed), not the fourth-person agent a=. The agent's action is presupposed; what the construction asserts is the object's resultant state (Nakagawa exx. 996–997). | nakagawa2024:443 | SAR

Perception and cognition verbs

Verbs of perception and cognition — nukar see, look at ,
eramuan understand , and nu hear — take either construction,
and the contrast between nukar kor an and
nukar wa an is the main analytical locus of debate within this domain.

Nakagawa's 1981 analysis attributed the distinction to the degree of active involvement of
the perceiver: nukar kor an is active, deliberate looking, while
nukar wa an is a passive coming-into-view of a scene
nakagawa2024 445 . Satō (2006) challenges this characterization. In his
account the primary contrast is between continuation of the perceiving action and
continuation of the post-acquisition knowledge state: nukar kor an
= 'is in the act of looking' and nukar wa an = 'has seen it and
now holds that knowledge'. Any impression of deliberateness is a contextual by-product of
the action/result distinction rather than its basis
sato2006 65 ; sato2008 199 . Nakagawa concedes in the 2024
edition that the question warrants further investigation
nakagawa2024 446 ‹contested›.

Yoshikawa adds a structural caution: for these verbs, the line between "continuation of an
aspectual relation" and "a coincidental co-occurrence of two situations" is genuinely unclear,
and the ambiguity is part of the broader argument that these constructions occupy a gradient
between clause chaining and grammaticalized aspect
yoshikawa2022 ‹contested›.

Grammaticalization status and typological framing

The cross-linguistic grammaticalization of an existential or locative verb into a progressive
or resultative auxiliary is well-attested typologically
bybee1994 ; comrie1976 . Hokkaido Ainu represents a
mid-stage on that cline. Three properties mark the constructions as only partially
grammaticalized ‹consensus›:

First, the source verb is morphologically and syntactically present — both predicates remain
independently finite and indexed. Second, the suppletive number alternation
an / oka is preserved, a property
typically lost when aspect auxiliaries fuse into bound morphemes. Third, aspect is not an
obligatory grammatical category in Ainu: the constructions can be omitted without
ungrammaticality, unlike the obligatory aspect marking of heavily aspect-marking languages
takahashi2022 ; yoshikawa2022 .

In Bugaeva's typology of Ainu periphrastic frames, the pair-verb construction is
distinguished from the auxiliary-verb frame (hosting a ,
nankor , rusuy , and their kin) precisely by
this double indexation: in the auxiliary frame only the main verb is person-marked, while
in the pair-verb frame both predicates are bugaeva2012 492–495 .

Dialect variation and the existential-aspect family

Several Hokkaido varieties deploy additional forms that overlap with or extend the
kor an / wa an field. Yoshikawa groups
kor an , wa an ,
kane an , and hine an under the cover term
existential aspectual forms, all tracing to the same grammaticalization source in the
lexical existential an , with individual varieties positioned at
different points along the cline
yoshikawa2022 ; yoshikawa2020 .

In Saru and Chitose, kane an covers both progressive and resultative
readings and adds a noteworthy-situation nuance that the simpler pair lacks; in Shizunai and
Samani it serves as a plain, unmarked equivalent of either construction. Tokachi and Ishikari
have kan an — leaning progressive in Tokachi, resultative in
Ishikari — and Tokachi additionally uses tek an for the resultative
of change verbs nakagawa2024 446–449 ; sato2006 84–85 .
The full dialect survey belongs to continuative-siri-siran-kane-an .

One community observation notes a correspondence between Hokkaido
wa an and a Sakhalin form hemaka in
cross-variety word lists (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, December 2024)
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. The sources consulted for this grammar do not
address this correspondence, and its functional equivalence requires independent
verification in the Sakhalin grammatical literature.

title: Existential and Locational Clauses (an / oka)
summary: The existential/locational predicates an (SG) and oka (PL), their number suppletion, presentational use, and the isam negative.
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Existential and Locational Clauses (an / oka)
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: The existential/locational predicates an (SG) and oka (PL), their number suppletion, presentational use, and the isam negative.

Semantics and clause structure

The intransitive verbs an exist, be, live (sg) (singular) and
oka exist, be, live (pl) / okay exist, be, live (pl)
(plural) are the existential and locational predicates of Hokkaido Ainu. Both animate
and inanimate subjects appear with them: a single verb covers the domain that Japanese
divides between いる (animate) and ある
(inanimate), so that 'there is a big house' and 'a person is there' are expressed by
the same predicate yoshikawa2022 629–630 ;
nakagawa2022pos 474 ; sato2008 3–4 .
Placed against the typology of intransitive predication
stassen1997 , the construction is an
existential-locational predicate: it asserts that something exists or is
found at a location, with the location expressed as an oblique.

The stative reading 'exist, be located, live' alternates with an inchoative one:
paykar an 'spring comes / it becomes spring' and
kunneywa an 'it becomes morning' show the onset-of-existence
value nakagawa2024 118–119 ; nakagawa2022pos 477 .
The same static–inchoative duality holds for the copula ne
and for property-concept verbs; neither is morphologically marked for the change
reading. The division of labour between the copula and the existential is treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

By raw corpus frequency, an ranks first in the approximately
1.57 million–token Hokkaido corpus (86,986 occurrences) and oka
twenty-third (9,637). Both figures fold in the fourth-person intransitive suffix
=an and the aspect-auxiliary uses treated elsewhere, so they
are upper bounds on the existential predicate proper; even so, the rank signals how
load-bearing this predicate type is nakagawa2024 146 ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Strict intransitivity and the locational frame

an is strictly intransitive: it takes one NP argument
(the entity that exists or is located) and no second bare NP. A location is
expressed as an oblique, canonically the locative postpositional phrase
N or ta (or bare N ta ), not as
a second NP argument. Satō's diagnostic makes this concrete: a sentence
modelled on a Japanese double-NP structure,
*Tokiyo anakne poro cise poronno an
('Tokyo big-houses many exist', treating Tokyo as a bare NP co-argument),
is disfavoured; the grammatical form marks the location overtly —
Tokiyo ot ta anakne poro cise poronno an
'in Tokyo there are many big houses' sato2008 3–4 .

The community formulation of the same rule is direct: an
is intransitive, so *A B an is ill-formed; 'A is at B'
is A B or ta an . The transitive locational verb
un be at (transitive) works differently — because it takes the
location as its object, A B un is well-formed
aynucorporadiscord ;
nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024 ‹consensus›.

The locative noun or place (possessed form of o) in
or ta is the concept-form-affiliative-form possessed /
affiliative form of the spatial root o 'place',
expressing 'the place of —'. The detailed distribution of
or vs bare ta in locational
constructions is documented for the Chitose dialect by
sato2021 .

poro cise an hine | big house exist CONJ | There was a big house, and — | Bare existential predication: a single NP subject; no second argument. The conjunctive hine continues the narrative clause chain. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawagami Matsuko, 'uwepeker 民話11', AA研アイヌ語資料 [aa-irc/013#85]

orwano ku=cise or ta ku=an kor | then 1SG.POSS=house place LOC 1SG.S=exist while | Then, while I was in my house, — | Locational predication: the oblique or ta 'at the place of' follows the location NP. The first-person intransitive prefix ku= indexes the subject on the existential stem. | nakagawa2024:166 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 104, after Sunazawa Kura 1983: 75

clause type
template
example

bare existential (sg)
[SUBJ-NP] an
poro cise an 'there is a big house'

bare existential (pl)
[SUBJ-NP] oka / okay
hekattar oka 'there are children'

locational (sg)
[SUBJ-NP] [LOC or ta] an
iwor or ta an 'is in the mountains'

locational (pl)
[SUBJ-NP] [LOC or ta] oka
kotan ta oka 'they are in the village'

negative existential
[SUBJ-NP] isam
nep ka isam 'there is nothing'

locational transitive (alt.)
[SUBJ-NP] [LOC-NP] un
kotan un 'be in the village (transitive un )'

Number suppletion: an (sg) and oka / okay (pl)

The existential verb is one of the canonical suppletive number pairs in Hokkaido Ainu
nakagawa2024 146 , a pattern documented cross-linguistically in
corbett2000 . The singular stem an is uniform
across all dialects. The plural stem varies: oka is the prevalent
form in Saru, Chitose, Shizunai, and Mukawa; okay prevails in
other dialects and, even within oka -dialects, appears before the
nominalizer pe / p
(thus okay pe , not *oka pe )
nakagawa2024 146 .

stem
number
dialectal distribution
notes

an
singular
all Hokkaido dialects

oka
plural
Saru, Chitose, Shizunai, Mukawa, and the corpus majority
rank 23 in the full corpus (9,637 tokens, homonym-mixed)

okay
plural
other dialects; before pe / p even
in oka -dialects
rank 96 in the full corpus (2,608 tokens, homonym-mixed)

isam
negative (suppletive)
all dialects
replaces an / oka(y) entirely
in negation; see § below

The plural stem is controlled by subject number in straightforward cases: a
singular subject nominal licenses an , a plural nominal
licenses oka . The full agreement mechanics are treated in
person-number-interactions .

mippoho tan kotan ta an | grandchild this village LOC exist | My grandchild lives in this village. | Singular subject controls singular an. The inalienable-possessed form mippoho encodes 'my grandchild'. Based on Nakagawa (2024: 146).

mippoutari tan kotan ta oka | grandchildren this village LOC exist.PL | My grandchildren live in this village. | Plural subject mippoutari 'my grandchildren' controls plural oka. Based on Nakagawa (2024: 146).

The fourth-person (honorific / narrative / inclusive) paradigm introduces a mismatch
between referential and grammatical number. When the intransitive fourth-person suffix
=an is attached, the existential stem is obligatorily the
plural oka — even when the argument is referentially
singular. The phrase sinen ne oka=an 'I am living alone'
(with sinen ne 'being only one, alone') illustrates this
directly: a singular first-person narrator uses the plural stem because the
fourth-person suffix requires it. Outside the Saru–Chitose core area, this
singular-referent / plural-stem pattern is, if anything, the pan-Hokkaido norm
nakagawa2024 160–161, 178 ‹corpus-confirmed›.
The fourth-person paradigm and its number behavior are treated in
person-marking-architecture and person-number-interactions .

a=kor kotan ta oar sinen ne oka=an wa | 4.A=have village LOC truly one COP exist.PL=4.S CONJ | I was living all alone in my village, and — | Singular-referent/plural-verb: the narrative first person triggers the fourth-person suffix =an, which requires plural stem oka, even though oar sinen ne 'truly alone' makes the single referent explicit. Constructed after Nakagawa (2024: 178, exx. 151–152; Shizunai and Kushiro narrators). | SHI

The negative existential isam

The negative existential isam not exist, be absent, die is the
suppletive lexical negative of an / oka(y) :
it occupies the same argument slot and takes person affixes normally
( e=isam 'you are not there / you are absent')
bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 450 .
Its historical depth is notable: isam 'ない' appears in the
1630 Matsumae no Kotoba wordlist, among the earliest documentary records of
the language. The full treatment of existential negation, including the
wa isam completive ('V and be gone'), is in
negation-existential-possessive-isam ; the standard clausal negation
somo is in negation-somo-standard-clausal .

The parallel negative for the possessive predicate kor 'have'
is the transitive sak lack, be without , which takes the possessor as
its subject and the lacked item as its object:
tamasay ka sak 'has no necklace even'.
This LACK/HAVE pairing parallels the EXIST/ISAM pairing and shows the same
suppletive negation strategy across the non-dynamic predicate system.

construction
value
notes

isam
negative existential: 'not exist, be absent, die'
suppletive; full person-affix paradigm; default negation of an / oka(y)

somo an
negated existential
standard-negation pattern; less common than suppletive isam

sak
negative possessive: 'lack, be without'
suppletive negative of kor ; transitive (possessor = A, lacked item = O)

V wa isam
completive / disappearance
'do V and be gone'; existential-negative as aspect auxiliary; see existential-aspect-an-oka

mak ne hine nea cawan isam isam | how COP CONJ that bowl not.exist not.exist | Where has that bowl gone? Gone? | isam as the whole predicate of a content question. The repeated isam in the source text marks rhetorical emphasis. | nakagawa2024:410 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 849

sinen ka isam ruwe ne | one even not.exist track-POSS COP | There was not even one. | sinen ka isam 'not even one exists': the negative-polarity particle ka 'even' combines with isam to express total absence (NPI construction). | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | Kanzaki Masayoshi, 'Nibutani kotan ta cip-sanke an', Ainu Times [ainu-times/003/0#21]

Grammaticalized uses of an

Beyond its core existential and locational function, an enters
several grammaticalized constructions that are cross-referenced here but treated in
full elsewhere.

Existential aspect auxiliaries

Combining an with a preceding conjunctive particle yields
the existential-type aspect periphrases:
V wa an (resultative/continuative, '〜ている'),
V kor an (progressive),
V kane an , and V hine an .
Yoshikawa documents these for the Saru and Chitose dialects and notes that
an in these constructions retains its concrete existential
value — it requires a personal affix and participates in subject-number agreement,
distinguishing it from a fully grammaticalized auxiliary
yoshikawa2022 629 ; sato2008 4 .
The negative counterpart wa isam (completive, '〜てしまう')
presses the negative existential isam into the same
periphrastic slot. The full paradigm of existential-type aspect forms is in
existential-aspect-an-oka ; the TAM overview is in
tam-overview .

Closely related is the stative-possessive kor wa an
('have-and-be', expressing resultant or persistent possession), where the
possessive verb kor is followed by resultative
wa an ; this sits at the possession–aspect interface and
is treated in predicative-possession-clauses .

Ambient predicates: mean = me + an

The zero-valency ambient predicate mean it is cold (weather)
'the weather is cold' is transparently analysable as
me 'cold' + an 'exist' — literally
'coldness exists' shibatani1990 19 ; nakagawa2024 126 .
It belongs to a class of ambient meteorological predicates formed by incorporating
an environmental noun into an or a similar root
( sirpirka 'the weather is good', from
sir - 'scene/land' + pirka ). These
are full verbs: the appearance of subjectlessness follows from noun incorporation,
not from the absence of a predicate. The one-place vs zero-place contrast —
zero-place mean 'the weather is cold' vs one-place
merayke=an 'I feel cold' — is developed in
property-concept-predication .

The ne / an mood alternation in clause-final evidential frames

In the sentence-final evidential construction, the copula ne
and the existential an alternate by illocutionary force.
A formal noun (possessed-form nominalizer) followed by the copula yields a
declarative — ruwe ne , hawe ne ,
siri ne , humi ne — while the same
formal noun followed by the existential forms a content question:
ruwe an? , hawe an? , and so on
nakagawa2024 392–395 .
The copula↔existential opposition is thus grammaticalized into a mood distinction
at the right periphery of the clause. The full evidential-frame paradigm is in
evidential-schema-overview ; the inferential
ruwe ne is treated in detail in
ruwe-ne-inferential , and the TAM and negation behavior of these frames
is in nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation .

Predicative possession: BE-type an and isam

The existential an (sg) / okay (pl)
and the negative isam also appear in a BE-type predicative
possession construction, restricted to
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative-form inalienable nouns
(body-part and kinship terms in their possessed morphology):
a=yupi an 'I have an elder brother' (literally
'my-elder-brother exists'), a=po-ho ka an 'I even have a son'
huber2025 251–253 .
This is structurally the same existential an serving a
possessive reading by virtue of the affiliative-form subject. The HAVE-type
kor construction handles the majority of predicative
possession; a community-proposed restriction — that inanimate possessors cannot
take kor and are paraphrased with the existential instead —
is a sharpening of the BE/HAVE split that awaits systematic corpus documentation
aynucorporadiscord ;
nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024 ‹contested›.
The full analysis of BE-type vs HAVE-type possession, the
kor wa an stative-persistence construction, and the role of
the affiliative form are in predicative-possession-clauses and
adnominal-possession-double-marking .

title: External Possession and Possessor Raising
summary: Constructions in which the possessor of an (inalienable) noun is realized as a core clausal argument (en=…sik 'my eye'), and their relation to possessor-stranding noun incorporation.
part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: External Possession and Possessor Raising
Part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
Summary: Constructions in which the possessor of an (inalienable) noun is realized as a core clausal argument (en=…sik 'my eye'), and their relation to possessor-stranding noun incorporation.

Overview

External possession (EP) refers to a construction in which the possessor of a noun
appears as a core argument of the main predicate rather than as a modifier inside
the possessed NP; the possessor is thereby assigned a grammatical function —
typically subject or object — by the verb rather than by the nominal head
nichols1986 56–70 . Hokkaido Ainu lacks a dative-shift EP
construction of the type common in European languages ('the wave hit him on
the shoulder'). The constructions available instead belong to three morphosyntactic
domains: (1) body-part noun incorporation (NI) that promotes the possessor to
intransitive-subject position; (2) natural-force NI that promotes the former object
to intransitive subject; and (3) the applicative prefixes, which raise a possessor
or beneficiary into direct-object position. No read source applies the term
"external possession" to Hokkaido Ainu directly; the label "possessor raising"
(所有者の主語への昇格) enters the literature with sato2016 and is
restated in kobayashi2025 166–167, 173–175 ‹contested›.

mechanism
result
primary source

body-part NI
possessor → intransitive subject

kobayashi2025 173–175 ; Satō (2012: 8, cited in
kobayashi2025 173 )

natural-force NI
patient/object → intransitive subject
kobayashi2025 166–167

applicative ( ko- / e- /
o- )
possessor/beneficiary → object

nakagawa2024 189, 424 ; bugaeva2010 749–801 ;
kaiser1998 157–178

antipassive i- (inverse: possessor suppression)
unspecified possessor absorbed; bare NI enabled
bugaeva2021poss 53–54

The constructional background lies in the possession system. Body-part and kinship
nouns are nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns obligatorily possessed
and take the concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative form with the
A-set person prefix whenever they occur with a specified possessor (see
alienable-inalienable-split ). When such a noun is incorporated into a
predicate, the possessor — normally indexed on the noun — may migrate to a
subject or object slot of the derived complex verb.

Body-part noun incorporation and possessor-to-subject raising

The most documented EP-equivalent in Hokkaido Ainu arises through body-part NI.
When a body-part noun in its affiliative (possessed) form is incorporated into an
intransitive verb, the resulting stem loses the person prefix on the noun; that
person — the possessor — resurfaces as the intransitive subject of the derived verb
kobayashi2025 173 . The derivational contrast is between the base
clause, where the body-part affiliative carries an overt person prefix, and the
incorporated clause, where the possessor appears only as the verb's subject suffix.

ku=kema-ha ka arka | 1SG.POSS=foot-POSS even hurt | My foot hurts, too. | Body-part affiliative kemaha (①-type: kema + -ha) as free clause argument of the intransitive predicate arka 'hurt'. The person prefix ku= marks the possessor on the noun directly — the base construction that feeds body-part NI. | utari1994 | HK | アコㇿイタㇰ Ch. 5, no. 8

After NI, the possessor prefix detaches from the body-part noun and surfaces instead
as the subject suffix of the complex predicate. Satō (2012: 8, cited in
kobayashi2025 173 ) and kobayashi2025 173 give the
following contrast:

tek-e-pase=an | hand-POSS-be.heavy=4.S | We became infirm in our hands. | hand-heavy we-became | The body-part tek 'hand' takes its short affiliative suffix -e (type ③-2: tek→teke) and is incorporated into pase 'be heavy'. The 4th-person intransitive subject =an surfaces as the subject of the derived verb, not as a prefix on the body-part noun. The reading is 'our/the-relevant-persons' hands became heavy/infirm': possessor raised to subject. | kobayashi2025:173 | CHI

kobayashi2025 173 characterises the promoting force as the higher
discourse salience of the possessor relative to the body part, citing Satō's
observation that "body-part nouns are prone to backgrounding because the possessor
is felt to be more salient" (Satō 2012: 20–21, as quoted in
kobayashi2025 173 ). This promoting force is NI-internal rather
than a separate syntactic operation. The construction overlaps with the
possessor-stranding cases treated in
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding , where the possessor NP
remains in the clause while the body-part noun is incorporated.

Natural-force noun incorporation and object-to-subject promotion

A second incorporation pathway produces an unaccusative from a transitive by
incorporating the natural-force subject (wave, wind, current) of the base verb.
Once incorporated, the natural force no longer occupies the subject position; the
base object is promoted to intransitive subject of the derived verb
kobayashi2025 166–167 .

ku=koy-yanke | 1SG.S=wave-lift | The wave lifted me. | I wave-got-lifted | Base transitive: koy (wave, A) yanke (lift) en= (me, O). After koy is incorporated, en= is promoted to intransitive subject; the 1SG.S form ku= surfaces on the derived unaccusative koy-yanke. Satō (2012: 8, cited in Kobayashi 2025: 167). | kobayashi2025:167 | CHI

konam rera-paru | leaf wind-blow | The leaf is blown by the wind. | Kobayashi 2025: ex. 4. rera 'wind' is incorporated into paru 'blow/fan'; the former object konam 'leaf' surfaces as the 3rd-person (zero) intransitive subject of the derived unaccusative rera-paru. | kobayashi2025:174 | CHI

kobayashi2025 175–176 shows that the derived unaccusatives from
natural-force NI feed the affiliative-type prefixes e- and
o- (treated in the following section), because those
prefixes select exclusively for unaccusative hosts. The two NI sub-types are
therefore connected: body-part NI makes the erstwhile possessor the subject of a
new intransitive; natural-force NI makes the erstwhile patient the subject of a
new unaccusative that can undergo further derivation ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Affiliative-type prefixes e- and o-

Alongside the full applicative system, kobayashi2025 171 identifies
a set of affiliative-type prefixes (所属形的接頭辞):
e- its tip/head of — and o- its base/tail-end of — .
These prefixes attach to a verb and function like the affiliative form of a possessed
noun: they presuppose a contextual possessor without altering the verb's valence
(valence impact: [0]). The following forms are documented in
kobayashi2025 171 :

prefixed form
base verb
reading

e-kay
kay 'break'
'its tip is broken'

e-en
en 'be pointed'
'its tip is pointed'

o-asin
asin 'go out'
'it slips out (from the end)'

o-uhuy
uhuy 'burn'
'its end is scorched'

Crucially, e- and o- in this
function attach only to unaccusative intransitives — verbs whose sole argument
is a theme or patient — not to unergatives (agent-subject intransitives)
kobayashi2025 171 ‹corpus-confirmed›. This selectional
restriction links the affiliative-type prefix to natural-force NI: a transitive
that undergoes natural-force NI becomes unaccusative and can then receive
e-/o- .

The strings e- and o- are
homophonous with the applicative-e e- applicative and the
applicative-o o- applicative , which increase arity by one. The two
uses are synchronically distinct in valence behaviour — the applicative adds an
argument; the affiliative-type prefix does not — but this distinction requires
verb-by-verb analysis and is not always surface-transparent
kobayashi2025 167 ‹contested›. The diagnostics for disambiguation
are set out in applicative-e and applicative-o .

The applicative route to external possession

The applicative prefixes ko- , e- , and o- each
increase a verb's arity by one, promoting a peripheral participant — possessor,
beneficiary, goal, or source — into the direct-object position
nakagawa2024 189, 424 ; bugaeva2006 813 ;
bugaeva2010 749–751 . When the promoted participant is the possessor
of an NP already present in the clause, the result is a construction in which the
possessor occupies object position rather than sitting inside the possessed NP — the
closest Ainu structural equivalent of dative EP.

kaiser1998 157–158 analyses a subset of these configurations as
the interaction of NI with applicative formation: a body-part noun is incorporated
while the applicative slot is filled by the body-part's possessor, yielding a
complex predicate in which the possessor is the new object. Hokkaido Ainu tolerates
such NI-plus-applicative stacks; their ordering and feeding relations are treated in
applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding . In
bugaeva2010 756–760 's typological survey (within the framework of
peterson2007 ), the ko- applicative is the
primary beneficiary/possessor-promotion prefix; e- adds a
locative or goal; o- adds a source or endpoint. The
per-applicative EP readings are documented in applicative-ko ,
applicative-e , and applicative-o , with the system-level
typological placement in applicative-system-overview .

The antipassive i- as a possessor-suppressor

When the possessor of an obligatorily possessed body-part or kinship noun is absent
or non-coreferential with any clause argument, Hokkaido Ainu offers two morphological
strategies bugaeva2021poss 53–54 . The first is the fourth-person
(indefinite) A-prefix a= 4.A / unspecified possessor , which keeps
the noun in its full affiliative form and indexes an unspecified possessor
( a=tekehe 'someone's hand'). The second is the derivational
antipassive prefix i- antipassive / NPN-marker , which enables the
body-part noun to be incorporated without possessive morphology — functioning as a
non-possessed-noun (NPN) marker, comparable in function to a nominal absolutive
bugaeva2021poss 53–54, 81 ; nam2021 62–74 .

i-sapa-kar usi | ANTIP-head-do place | a barber shop | a place where one does someone's head | Tamura (1996: 247), cited in Bugaeva et al. (2021: 53, ex. 16). The antipassive i- absorbs the possessor of sapa 'head', allowing bare incorporation without a person prefix on the noun. The possessor is unspecified and non-coreferential with any surface argument. | tamura1996:247 | SAR

i-rekut-tuye | ANTIP-throat-cut | (someone) cuts a/someone's throat | Bugaeva et al. (2021: 53, ex. 17), citing Okuda (1999). The possessor of rekut 'throat' is unspecified; i- licenses possessor-free incorporation of the body-part noun into the transitive tuye 'cut'. | bugaeva2021poss:53 | HK

The two strategies differ in what they encode about the possessor. The
a= strategy retains affiliative morphology and implies a
vague but retrievable possessor; i- suppresses the possessor
argument entirely, producing what bugaeva2021poss 81 terms a
non-possessible reading of the incorporated noun. In EP terms,
i- is the inverse operation to possessor-raising: it removes
the possessor from the noun's argument frame rather than promoting it to clause
level, enabling fully possessor-free NI. For the broader function of
i- as antipassive and detransitiviser, see
antipassive-detransitive-i . A related use of
i= as the fourth-person object clitic — homophonous with
the antipassive prefix but distinct in status — is documented in
person-marking-architecture .

Competing analyses

The three mechanisms surveyed above have been described in distinct analytical
vocabularies, and the descriptive literature does not converge on a unified
formal account of "external possession" in Ainu.

kobayashi2025 173–175 , following sato2016 , treats
body-part NI possessor-raising as an NI-internal process: the incorporated noun's
possessor is promoted to clause subject by virtue of the body-part's relative
discourse-salience deficit, and the result is an unaccusative predicate. This
account is accurate at the descriptive level but does not determine whether the
promotion is syntactic movement or base-generation of the subject.
bugaeva2021poss 53–54 approaches the same facts through the
lens of obligatory possession and argument structure: the inalienable body-part
noun requires a possessor as its argument, and when that possessor is suppressed
(by i- ) or left unspecified (by a= ),
the possessor slot is de-activated rather than raised. She does not frame the
resulting construction as possessor-raising. The applicative account in
kaiser1998 treats the EP reading as the product of the applicative
operation alone, with NI as an independent feeding process ‹contested›.

Whether these three routes — body-part NI promotion, natural-force NI promotion,
and applicative object-promotion — constitute instances of a unified Ainu EP
construction, or three typologically distinct processes that happen to yield
similar interpretations, has not been resolved in the accessible literature
‹contested›. Placing the Ainu facts within mithun1984 863–866 's
typology of NI types, body-part incorporation with possessor promotion would fall
at the boundary between Type II (lexical-level compounding) and Type III
(manipulation of discourse referents), but assigning the Ainu data precisely
requires corpus evidence on whether an independent possessor NP can co-occur with
the incorporated body-part noun — a question the sources consulted leave open
‹speculative›. The natural-force and applicative mechanisms sit closer to Type I
(simple argument reduction) and to applicative constructions respectively; their
co-existence in a polysynthetic head-marking language
nichols1986 is typologically expected
bugaeva2021poss 80–82 .

title: The Final-h Problem and Its Sakhalin Vowel-Length Reflex
summary: The diachrony of word-final and coda -h, the Hokkaido final-h controversy, and its systematic correspondence to phonemic vowel length in Sakhalin Ainu.
part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Final-h Problem and Its Sakhalin Vowel-Length Reflex
Part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
Summary: The diachrony of word-final and coda -h, the Hokkaido final-h controversy, and its systematic correspondence to phonemic vowel length in Sakhalin Ainu.

The h phoneme and the coda question in Hokkaido

Hokkaido Ainu h is an onset segment. It occurs word-initially —
hapo 'mother', huci 'grandmother' — and as the
opening consonant of the affiliative-suffix-morphophonology affiliative suffix
-hi/-he/-hu/-ha . It does not occur as a syllable coda
tamura1996 . The descriptive gap between the two scripts — Hokkaido retaining
original coda consonants, Sakhalin replacing them with
h — makes the phoneme appear to play an outsized role in cross-dialect
description, yet for Hokkaido the question is straightforward: the
h visible inside forms such as tek-ehe 'hand
(of someone)' or sik-ihi 'eye (of someone)' is suffix-initial, not
base-final. The base consonants — k in tek ,
k in sik — surface unchanged across all
Hokkaido morphological environments ‹consensus›.

The analysis that makes this transparent is Janhunen's (2020, via
alonso2022 §2.1 ) account of the affiliative
-VhV . The proto-Ainu noun base was disyllabic —
*tek.e 'hand', *sik.i 'eye' — with a final
vowel subsequently lost in the citation form. When the noun
hi 'thing, place, time' fused with the base, the lost base-vowel was
restored before the suffix-initial h : *tek.e
→ tek-ehe , *sik.i
→ sik-ihi . The h in the affiliative is
therefore the onset of hi , and the suffix vowel harmonises with the
base's final vowel: -ehe (base e ),
-ihi (base i ),
-uhu (base u ),
-aha (base a ). The full morphology belongs in
concept-form-affiliative-form and
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology ; the point here is that HA morphology
provides no surface evidence for an underlying word-final /h/ on noun stems ‹consensus›.

A separate h -related phenomenon in Hokkaido — the prothetic
h - of the Bihoro dialect ( haspa 'deaf' :
aspa ; ham 'nail' :
am ) — has been analysed as an independent Bihoro innovation driven
by disambiguation of initial-stressed forms, not as an archaism or an echo of any coda laryngeal
alonso2022 §2.2.5 . It is discussed in the broader consonant inventory
in consonant-inventory .

sik-ihi | eye-POSS | (someone's) eye; the affiliative/possessed form of sik | Constructed to illustrate the possessed form of sik 'eye'. The base retains its coda consonant k unchanged; the h is the onset of the grammaticalized suffix hi 'thing, place'. The connecting vowel matches the base's final vowel (here i), yielding sik-i-hi. The Sakhalin Raiciska form is likewise sik-ihi, showing the same k — compare the surface citation form sis (§2 below), where the coda has been debuccalized.

Debuccalization in Sakhalin and the affiliative evidence

In Sakhalin Ainu a systematic coda-weakening rule applies: stop and fricative codas
debuccalize to h ( p, t, k → h ), nasal
m merges with n , and coda
r is followed by a vowel that opens the syllable
tangiku2022 §2.3 ; alonso2022 §1 . A secondary rule then
shifts the resulting coda h to s after a front
vowel: HA sik → *sih →
sis (‹SA›RA). Community members nukopoli and aldehyde8908
independently documented these alternations in the aynu-corpora Discord —
kor → koh (nukopoli, 2024) and the
sik / sis split (aldehyde8908, 2025) aynucorporadiscord
— both fully corroborated by tangiku2022 §2.3 ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Hokkaido–Sakhalin coda correspondences (Raiciska ‹SA›; data from
alonso2022 §2.3 and tangiku2022 §2.3, §5 )

HA (Saru) citation form
‹SA›RA surface form
HA affiliative
‹SA›RA affiliative
gloss

tek
teh
tek-ehe
tek-ihi
'hand; stick'

sik

sis
(< * sih )

sik-ihi
sik-ihi
'eye'

cup
cuh
cup-uhu
—
'sun; moon'

mat
mah
mat-aha
mac-ihi
'woman; wife'

cep
(< * ci-ʔe-p )

ceh
cep-ehe
—
'fish'

kor
koh
—
—
'have; possess'

The mat / mah / mac-ihi triplet is the strongest case for internal
reconstruction via the affiliative. The ‹SA›RA surface form mah would
predict an affiliative * mah-ihi , but the attested form is
mac-ihi , with an affricate c absent from the
citation form of both dialects alonso2022 §1 ;
tangiku2022 §2.3, §5 . The affricate arises from the regular
t → c alternation before front i that applies
at the morpheme boundary when the ‹SA› uniform affiliative suffix -ihi
is appended: the underlying *t surfaces as
c before the connecting -i- . The affiliative
therefore functions as a transparent window onto the base consonant that debuccalization
hides on the citation form. In HA, where the affiliative connecting vowel matches the base's
own final vowel ( a for mat ), the same
alternation does not arise: HA mat-aha keeps
t .

The practical consequence — noted by community member nukopoli (2023,
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-confirmed› — is that ‹SA› concept-form
etymology is often unrecoverable from the citation form alone: a surface
-h coda could descend from any of
p, t, k , or (via the secondary rule) any front-vowel-final stem whose
coda lenited to h then shifted to s . The HA
cognate or the ‹SA› affiliative — often both — supply the disambiguation.

The phonemic status of ‹SA› coda h is itself debated.
Alonso de la Fuente alonso2022 §1 transcribes it as a velar fricative /x/,
reflecting its realization as [ax], [iɕ], [uf] conditioned by the preceding vowel. Tangiku
tangiku2022 §2.3 and community member thegodofneet (2025,
aynucorporadiscord ) treat it as a glottal or pharyngeal breath [h], citing the
hattori1964 dialect dictionary's description that only breath, not modal voicing,
exits, and raising the further question of whether the coda segment and the word-initial
onset h are the same phoneme ‹contested›. The two transcriptions
have no consequence for the diachronic correspondences laid out here, since both agree on
the underlying source consonants; but they diverge in the phonological analysis of
contemporary ‹SA› Ainu and its segmental inventory. The broader consonant inventory
discussion is in consonant-inventory .

‹SA›-internal variation further limits how far back the debuccalization can be projected.
The Taraika (east coast) and Tarantomari dialects preserve
p / t / k in coda position where the main ‹SA› dialects have
h , and Esutoru distinguishes -is from
-ih tangiku2022 §2.3 . Debuccalization thus
developed within the Sakhalin branch rather than in the common ancestor, and the HA forms
represent the conservative retention of whatever coda consonants Proto-Ainu had.

The Sakhalin vowel-length reflex and proto-Ainu prosody

Alongside the consonant correspondences, Hokkaido and Sakhalin diverge in their prosodic
systems. Sakhalin Ainu carries phonemic vowel length in open stem syllables
( a aa e ee i ii o oo u uu ); Hokkaido Ainu has a pitch accent and no
contrastive length tangiku2022 §2.1 ; alonso2022 §2.3 .
Hattori (1967, via alonso2022 §2.3 ; tangiku2022 §2.1 )
first stated the systematic correspondence: a ‹SA› long vowel in an open stem syllable aligns
with a HA high-pitch syllable in the same position. A closed-syllable constraint qualifies
the generalization: where the stem syllable carries a coda consonant (retained in HA,
debuccalized to h in ‹SA›), the ‹SA› vowel is short while HA
retains the high pitch on that syllable alonso2022 §2.3 .

Hokkaido pitch accent – Sakhalin vowel length (data from
alonso2022 §2.3 and tangiku2022 §2.1 ; acute mark =
HA high-pitch syllable)

HA (Saru)
‹SA›RA
stem syllable type
gloss

sísam
siisam
open: CV–
'Japanese (person)'

túki
tuuki
open: CV–
'sake cup'

húre
huure
open: CV–
'red'

hése
heese
open: CV–
'breathe'

súpki
suhki
closed: CVC– (SA debuccalized)
'reed' (< * sup+ki )

The súpki / suhki pair illustrates the closed-syllable constraint
directly. The compound *sup+ki 'reed' keeps a closed first syllable
regardless of dialect: in HA, p closes the syllable and the pitch
falls on súp- ; in ‹SA›, p → h closes the
syllable differently but the syllable remains closed, so the vowel is short. The resulting
‹SA› form suhki shows a short vowel in the first syllable and a coda
h , while HA súpki shows high pitch and the
original coda p .

The diachronic interpretation of the length–pitch correspondence is the most contested point
in proto-Ainu suprasegmental reconstruction. Three positions are current in the literature,
surveyed comprehensively in alonso2022 §2.3 and addressed in detail in
itabashi2001 :

(a) Length-first (Hattori 1967; De Boer 2010, both via
alonso2022 §2.3 ) : Proto-Ainu had contrastive vowel length and
no pitch accent; Hokkaido acquired its pitch system through prolonged contact with
Japanese, while Sakhalin preserved the older length contrast.

(b) Both length and pitch (Vovin 1993, via alonso2022 §2.3 ;
itabashi2001 ) : Proto-Ainu carried both phonemic length and pitch
simultaneously; the Sakhalin branch lost pitch under Amur–Sakhalin contact pressure,
retaining length; Hokkaido retained pitch and neutralised length.

(c) Pitch-first (Satō 2015–2016, via alonso2022 §2.3 ) :
Proto-Ainu had pitch accent only; ‹SA› length is a phonetically conditioned phenomenon
restricted to open syllables and represents a Sakhalin-internal development — possibly
compensatory lengthening following coda weakening — rather than an inherited contrast.

Alonso de la Fuente's own treatment is internally divided: §2.3 endorses view (c) as "most
economical", while §2.4 states that "Hattori was right when he suggested Proto-Ainu had only
vowel length, and no pitch accent; pitch accent may be the result of language contact after
Japanese influence" — a restatement of view (a)
alonso2022 §2.3, §2.4 ‹contested›. The two sections advocate opposite
positions without reconciliation; neither can be taken as Alonso's settled conclusion.
Itabashi itabashi2001 provides the dedicated historical treatment of the
SA-length ↔ HA-pitch diachronic relation; its arguments should be weighed before any
definitive account of PA prosody is committed to, and the fuller treatment belongs in
mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .

A trochaic-to-iambic shift hypothesis (Satō 2015–2016; Shiraishi 2017, both via
alonso2022 §2.3 ) adds a further layer: if Proto-Ainu or Common Ainu was
