trochaic and the pitch accent spread rightward, the resulting iambic system would leave
certain archaisms — ‹SA› noociw : HA
nocíw , ‹SA› niisew : HA
niséw (length ↔ low pitch in HA) — as the recoverable fingerprints
of the older system. Community member nukopoli (2023, aynucorporadiscord ) noted
the Hattori/Satō dispute directly, identifying the competing claims from within the
community's reading of the literature ‹contested›. Antitwilight (2024,
aynucorporadiscord ) takes a position against Satō's posited `*H` segment and
prefers Janhunen's vowel-dropping account (see also §1 on the affiliative), arguing that
introducing an unattested consonant for the purpose of accounting for exceptional accent is
less economical than assuming base-vowel loss ‹contested›.

The pitch-accent system of Hokkaido, including the placement rules and the dialect-internal
variation among standard-accent, irregular, and unaccented dialects, is treated in full in
pitch-accent-placement-rule and mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .
The proto-Ainu segmental reconstruction on which the coda correspondences above rest is in
proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction , and the broader Hokkaido–Sakhalin dialect
picture in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry and
sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast .

Orthographic and typological implications

For Hokkaido, the orthographic consequences of the debuccalization asymmetry are minimal:
HA retains original coda consonants on the surface, and the citation forms write exactly
what is heard — tek , sik ,
cup , mat . The apparent
h that appears medially in affiliative forms is always the
onset of the suffix -hi and is morphologically transparent.

For Sakhalin orthography the picture is more consequential. Writing the citation form as
mah , cuh , teh
follows the phonetic surface but conceals the base consonant that the affiliative restores
and the Hokkaido cognate corroborates. Thegodofneet (2025, aynucorporadiscord )
proposed writing the underlying p/t/k values directly and reading
them as h by convention — a strategy with analogues in French and
Korean orthographic traditions, which preserve historically silent consonants as relics
of earlier forms. Aldehyde8908 (2025, aynucorporadiscord ) countered that some
‹SA› subdialects genuinely surface with p/t/k (Taraika,
Tarantomari), so writing the surface form is the accurate representation of those dialects,
while the uniform h convention would misrepresent them ‹contested›.
The dispute reflects the genuine ‹SA›-internal variation noted above and has no consensus
resolution in the sources consulted. Ijäs ijas2023 addresses the SA–HA affiliative
alternation in pedagogical terms, making the base-consonant restoration accessible to
learners encountering ‹SA› citation forms for the first time.

Typologically, the ‹SA› debuccalization belongs to a widespread class of coda-weakening
processes in which place distinctions are neutralised toward a laryngeal default. The
resulting system — a single coda h for historically distinct
p, t, k — would be irrecoverable from ‹SA› alone; the Hokkaido
cognates and the ‹SA› affiliative together supply the two independent verification routes
that Alonso de la Fuente alonso2022 §1 calls a showcase of internal
reconstruction for an isolate. The genetic position of Ainu — an isolate for which no
comparative method in the strict sense applies — makes this type of internal, morphologically
mediated evidence especially load-bearing; for the macro-comparative context see
genetic-position-macro-comparison .

title: First-Person Plural and the Clusivity Question (ci=/=as, un=)
summary: The exclusive 1pl set — subjective ci=/=as and objective un= — and the contested inclusive/exclusive opposition with the indefinite person.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: First-Person Plural and the Clusivity Question (ci=/=as, un=)
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The exclusive 1pl set — subjective ci=/=as and objective un= — and the contested inclusive/exclusive opposition with the indefinite person.

The exclusive first plural paradigm

The first-person plural of Hokkaido Ainu is exclusively exclusive: ci=
as the transitive subject (A), =as as the intransitive subject (S),
and un= as the object (O) always refer to a group from which the
addressee is absent. Nakagawa, Tamura, Satō, and Bugaeva converge on this characterisation
without qualification nakagawa2024 163–164 ; tamura1972 18–19 ;
sato2008 31–32 ; bugaeva2012 471–472 .

The free pronoun for this row is Saru coka , with dialectal counterparts
Tokachi ciokay and ciutari . Both the Saru and
Tokachi forms are built on the transitive-subject prefix ci= followed
by the plural existential oka(y) 'there are', nominalized — so
coka(y) is, in origin, 'those who exist as
ci= -ers' tamura1971 3–5 ; nakagawa2024 253 . The three-cell paradigm, set within the full person architecture, is introduced in
person-marking-architecture .

The table below places the exclusive row beside the fourth-person/indefinite row, whose first
function is the inclusive 'we'. The contrast between the two rows is the structural basis of
clusivity in Hokkaido Ainu.

row
A: transitive subject
S: intransitive subject
O: object
referential value

1PL.EXCL — pronoun Saru coka , Tokachi
ciokay / ciutari

ci= (prefix)
=as (suffix)
un= (prefix)
we, excluding the addressee

4 / indefinite — pronoun Saru aoka , Tokachi
anokay / anutari

a= / dial. an= (prefix)

=an (suffix)
i= (prefix)
inclusive we; honorific you; impersonal one; logophoric narrator

Before a vowel-initial stem, ci= reduces to c= ,
parallel to the reduction of first-singular ku= to
k= in the same phonological context nakagawa2024 49, 166 ; bugaeva2012 467–468 .

Tripartite patterning: S, A, and O are distinct

The exclusive first plural is one of two tripartite rows in the Hokkaido paradigm: all three
role exponents differ in form and position. ci= precedes a transitive
stem as A; =as follows an intransitive stem as S; and
un= again precedes a transitive stem as O. The S/A side-switch —
prefix for A, suffix for S — is the morphological basis on which Bugaeva assigns this row a
tripartite classification bugaeva2008 32 ; bugaeva2012 472 ;
nakagawa2024 166–167 .

The Tokachi example below shows both subject functions within a single clause chain: the
exclusive group is the A of the transitive verb o 'board' and, in
the same utterance, the S of the intransitive plural motion verb arki
'come'.

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | ci= indexes the exclusive group as A of the transitive o; =as indexes the same group as S of the intransitive plural arki. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

The object prefix un= occupies the normal O-prefix slot of the
transitive verb alongside a distinct subject index. In the Saru example below, the transitive
agent is an indefinite/impersonal fourth person indexed by a= , while
the exclusive group stands as object, indexed by un= .

sanbyakugojuen a=un=kore hike ka | 350.yen 4.A=1PL.EXCL.O=give although even | although we were given 350 yen, … | The prefix sequence a=un= stacks a fourth-person (indefinite/impersonal) A and a first-plural-exclusive O on a single transitive stem. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2, AA Institute Ainu materials [aa-irc/004#109]

un= is formally separate from the fourth-person object
i= : the two prefixes belong to different paradigm rows and index
different participants. Their historical relationship — including whether Hokkaido
un= and the Sakhalin counterpart in= are
cognates of the exclusive O or of the indefinite O — is part of the broader alignment debate
and is pursued in alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate ‹contested›.

Clusivity: the exclusive row and the fourth-person inclusive

The semantic domain 'we' is distributed across two formal rows. The exclusive forms
ci= / =as / un= index a
speaker-only group; addressee-inclusive 'we' falls to the fourth-person row
a= / =an / i= . Nakagawa
identifies inclusive 'we' as the first of five referential values of the fourth person; Tamura
treats the same morphology as an inclusive first plural in her eleven-person Saru analysis; and
Bugaeva places the inclusive value at the centre of the indefinite paradigm nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 ; tamura1972 18–19, 39 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 .

eani hem paye=an ro | 2SG also go.PL=4.S HORT | Let you and me go too! | The hortative construction includes the addressee; the fourth-person S suffix =an appears on the plural stem paye, not the exclusive =as. | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 138

Tamura notes that coka is the genuine plural of
kani 'I', while aoka would more accurately be
described as a speaker-plus-addressee category — though she retains the label 'inclusive first
plural' for it tamura1972 19 . The exclusive row carries no inclusive value in
any Hokkaido description consulted. The complete morphology and the non-inclusive uses of
a= / =an / i= belong to
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and fourth-person-honorific .

Terminological disagreement over the fourth-person row does not affect the clusivity fact.
Nakagawa's 'fourth person' is a formal class label; Tamura distinguishes several persons whose
morphology is neutralised; Bugaeva's 'indefinite' groups inclusive, honorific, logophoric, and
impersonal functions; and Takahashi, on Tokachi, adopts 'inclusive person' as a unifying
functional term, arguing that the participant structure of the inclusive gives a principled
account of all the disparate uses nakagawa2024 174–179 ; tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; takahashi2015 205–206, 210
‹contested›. Across these analyses, addressee-inclusive 'we' takes the fourth-person forms.

Tokachi evidence and dialect scope

Takahashi's Tokachi description provides the most explicit formulation of the
exclusive/inclusive opposition for a single Hokkaido dialect. The exclusive side has
ci- , -as , and un- , with
free pronouns ciokay and ciutari ; the inclusive
side has a- / an- , -an ,
and i- , with anokay and
anutari takahashi2015 206–207 . Takahashi's hyphens for
personal markers are a source-internal convention; they correspond to the
= -notation for personal indexes used here.

Takahashi also notes that the inclusive row selects plural verb morphology even when the
referent is a single additional person: a sole addressee triggers plural stem agreement on an
inclusive-marked verb takahashi2015 209 . Nakagawa documents the same pattern
for the fourth-person honorific and hortative uses — one esteemed individual addressed with a
plural stem — and for the narrator in most dialects outside Saru and Chitose nakagawa2024 160–161, 174–176 . The person-number agreement consequences and the conditioning of plural stems are developed
in person-number-interactions and suppletive-verbal-number .

In oral-narrative texts, the exclusive series serves a genre-specific narrator function. In
kamuy yukar, the divine protagonist uses ci= / =as / un= as self-reference, distinguishing the kamuy narrator's
speech from the human first-person voice of uwepeker, which uses the fourth person nakagawa2024 602–604 . Whether this pattern derives from an original first-person plural strategy or a
register-internal specialisation is debated sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ‹contested›. These narrative and logophoric dimensions are treated in fourth-person-honorific and logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Sakhalin Ainu presents a different configuration. Sakaguchi reports that the
ci= -series functions as a grammatically plural encoding of the first
singular, while the fourth-person an= -series covers both first singular
and first plural without an exclusive/inclusive split of the Hokkaido type sakaguchi2024 26–27, 162–163 ‹SA›.

Morphological status of the indexes and notation

The = boundary on personal indexes and the -
boundary on derivational and number morphology are descriptive conventions, not a uniform
claim about morphological status. Bugaeva distinguishes three grades for the Hokkaido indexes,
using prosodic autonomy and separability as diagnostics: =as and
=an are word-like (they may be separated from the stem by intervening
material and have autonomous prosodic weight); a= and
eci= are clitic-like; and ku= ,
ci= , en= , and un= are
fuller prefixes that shift the accent kernel bugaeva2012 472–473 . Sakaguchi's
explicit diagnostics, formulated for Sakhalin material, classify -as
as root-attaching (affixal) and fourth-person =an as autonomous
(clitic-like) — reversing Bugaeva's ranking for =as — while both
agree that =an is the most autonomous of the subject indexes sakaguchi2024 ix, 62–64, 77–85, 163 ‹contested›. Nakagawa writes all person indexes with = as a
convenience notation, explicitly disavowing any clitic claim nakagawa2024 49 .
The ordering diagnostics and the clitic/affix boundary question are pursued in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Ijäs's learner grammar writes the exclusive markers as ci- ,
-as , and un- , with hyphens used as a teaching
convention rather than a phonological boundary symbol ijas2023 . The constructed
example below shows the exclusive intransitive-subject marker in a simple locative clause.

cise or ta okay=as | house place LOC exist.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | We are at the house. | Constructed after the pattern of Ijäs's learner materials. The exclusive S suffix =as is written -as in that source; okay is the plural existential stem. | ijas2023 | HK

title: First- and Second-Person Singular Affixes (ku=/en=, e=/e=)
summary: The SAP-singular core: 1sg subjective ku= vs objective en=, and 2sg e= (syncretic across A/S/O), with their morphophonology and distribution.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: First- and Second-Person Singular Affixes (ku=/en=, e=/e=)
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The SAP-singular core: 1sg subjective ku= vs objective en=, and 2sg e= (syncretic across A/S/O), with their morphophonology and distribution.

Forms and alignment

In the Hokkaido Ainu person paradigm, the first- and second-person singular cells are
distinguished by whether the language maintains or collapses the S/A/O contrast within the row.
First singular keeps two distinct forms: ku= for the single argument of
an intransitive verb (S) and for the transitive subject (A), and en=
for the object (O). Second singular has one form, e= , in all three
roles. In alignment terms, the 1SG row is nominative–accusative (S=A≠O) and the 2SG row is
neutral (S=A=O)
nakagawa2024 165–169 ; bugaeva2012 471–472 ; sato2008 31–32 ; tamura1972 27 ; bugaeva2008 32 . The architecture of the
complete paradigm — including the side-encoding of the first plural exclusive and the fourth
person, the two-index cap on the verb, and the boundary-notation convention — is set out in
person-marking-architecture . The present chapter treats the 1SG and 2SG rows in
detail.

formal person
free pronoun (Saru)
A: transitive subject
S: intransitive subject
O: object
alignment

1SG
kani
ku= ; Saru/Chitose/Mukawa k= before
vowel-initial stems (a, u, e, o)
ku= ; same allomorphy
en=
S=A≠O; nominative–accusative

2SG
eani
e=
e=
e= ; 1→2 uses portmanteau eci= instead
S=A=O; neutral

The free pronouns kani and eani are optional
emphatic or contrastive expressions; ordinary finite clauses carry the person information on
the verb alone. Their etymology — kani from the 1SG prefix plus an
existential base; eani invariant across the surveyed Hokkaido dialects
— and their distribution relative to the verbal index are treated in
independent-personal-pronouns
tamura1971 3–5 ; nakagawa2024 250–253 .

First-person singular subject: ku= and k=

The prefix ku= indexes 1SG in both the intransitive-subject and the
transitive-subject roles; the two uses share a single form. This identity of S and A, set
against the distinct object en= , is the formal basis for describing the
1SG row as nominative–accusative
nakagawa2024 166–169 ; bugaeva2012 472 .

orwano ku=cise or ta ku=an kor | then 1SG.POSS=house place LOC 1SG.S=exist while | Then, while I was in my house, … | The intransitive existential ku=an indexes 1SG.S. The nominal ku=cise carries the homophonous first-person prefix in the possessive domain (glossed POSS here); nominal possession belongs to the chapter on the possessed form. | nakagawa2024:166 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 104, after Sunazawa Kura 1983: 75

In Saru, Chitose, and Mukawa, ku= reduces to k=
before stems beginning in a , u ,
e , or o :
ek 'come' yields k=ek , and
eramuan 'understand' and eunkeray 'receive'
appear as k=eramuan and k=eunkeray . Nakagawa
writes the Hidaka-western and Chitose convention as k(u)= , recording
the same alternation; the notation reflects phonological context, not two morphologically
separate forms
nakagawa2024 49, 166 ; bugaeva2012 467–468 .

aynu itak k=eramuan wa kusu icen k=eunkeray wa pirka pirka | Ainu language 1SG.A=understand CONJ because money 1SG.A=receive CONJ good good | Because I had learnt Ainu, I was able to receive money, which was very good. | Both transitive verbs show k=, the reduced allomorph of 1SG.A ku= before the vowel-initial stems eramuan and eunkeray. | nakagawa2024:168 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 111

Bugaeva classifies ku= as a full prefix: it is inseparable from the
stem and shifts the accent kernel, properties that set it apart from the more autonomous
intransitive-subject suffixes =an and =as , which
can be separated from the verb by intervening words and form their own prosodic units
bugaeva2012 472–473 . Nakagawa writes all personal indexes with
= as a practical orthographic convention, noting the symbol carries no
claim about clitic status nakagawa2024 49 . Satō and Tamura write these forms
with a hyphen; the learner grammar of Ijäs does likewise, stating the hyphen is a teaching
convention rather than a pronunciation symbol
ijas2023 . Sakaguchi's explicit clitic/affix criteria — developed primarily for
Sakhalin material, and applied to Hokkaido only by extension — place root-bound personal
indexes in the prefixal class and rank =an / =as
as the most autonomous subject markers; on these diagnostics, ku=
would belong to the fully prefixal end
sakaguchi2024 ix, 77–85, 163 ‹contested›. The ordering diagnostics are
developed in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Ijäs introduces ku= early as the obligatory subject marker on both
transitive and intransitive verbs, presenting it before the optional independent pronoun and
treating the pronoun as emphatic or clarifying rather than grammatically required
ijas2023 . This is consistent with the head-marking account of the Ainu verb given
by Satō sato2025a 361 and with the typological framework of
nichols1986 . An idiosyncratic note in Ijäs's phonetic guidance specifies
that initial i retains the fuller form and that careful Saru speech may
realise the u vowel before other vowels; these are L2-oriented
observations about pronunciation and do not affect the morphological alternation
ijas2023 .

ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=return | I go back. | Constructed following Ijäs Lesson 2, which uses this verb type to introduce 1SG.S on an intransitive. Ijäs writes ku-hosipi. | ijas2023 | SAR

First-person singular object: en=

The object counterpart of 1SG is en= . It indexes a first-person
singular participant in the O role: the patient of a monotransitive verb or, with three-place
verbs such as kore 'give' and epakasnu
'teach', the human recipient rather than the theme
nakagawa2024 169 ; sato2008 147 ; sato2023b 47 ;
sato2025a 368–371 . The secundative object-indexing pattern — recipient indexed,
theme left as a bare noun phrase — is developed in object-indexing-monotransitive .

en=epunkine wa en=kore yan | 1SG.O=protect CONJ 1SG.O=give IMP | Please protect me. | The imperative force supplies a second-person subject; both instances of en= mark the 1SG participant as the object/recipient. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, menokoyukar 'Kamuy yukar 4' [aa-irc/011#5]

nep kameaske ene ne yakkay en=kasuy kun pe an cik pirka hike | what monster thus COP even 1SG.O=help should NMLZ exist if good although | It would be good if any monster were to help me. | The prefix en= indexes the speaker as the object of kasuy 'help'; the agent is left to context. | nakagawa2024:170 | TOK | Nakagawa ex. 122, after Takahashi 2007: 63

A syncretism affects en= in the Saru and Chitose combination tables.
Across most Hokkaido dialects, a second-person subject acting on a 1SG object surfaces as a
second-person A exponent followed by en= — e=en= for 2SG→1SG or eci=en= for 2PL→1SG. In Saru and Chitose, the
expected second-person A exponent is absent, and bare en= covers the
2SG→1SG cell; Chitose extends the same bare form to 2PL→1SG. The bare
en= form is therefore shared between the 3→1SG and 2→1SG cells in
these dialects, and the subject is recovered from discourse or from an overt pronoun
nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 ‹contested›. The
full dialect-by-dialect combination table, and the parallel 1→2 portmanteau
eci= , are treated in eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

Ijäs presents the bare- en= situation to learners as a predictable rule:
when the 1SG participant is in O position and the subject is second or third person, the verb
shows only en= , with the subject inferred from context
ijas2023 . The constructed example below illustrates the Saru/Chitose 2SG→1SG
reading.

topenpe en=kore | sweets 1SG.O=give | You gave me sweets. | Constructed following Ijäs. The translation supplies a 2SG subject from context; in Saru/Chitose the verb shows only en=, which covers both 3→1SG and 2→1SG readings. The phenomenon is attested in Nakagawa 2024: 170, 173 and Satō 2008: 146–147. | ijas2023 | SAR

In the literary and oral-narrative register, the 2→1SG configuration maps to
i= , the fourth-person object index, with the subject again unexpressed
sato2008 261 . That use belongs to the functional range of the fourth person
and is treated in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Second-person singular: e=

The 2SG index e= is neutral across argument roles: one form marks
2SG.S, 2SG.A, and 2SG.O alike. This pattern is shared by the 2PL form
eci= , so neither the singular nor the plural second-person index
distinguishes subject from object
tamura1972 27 ; sato2008 32 ; bugaeva2012 472 .
On an intransitive verb, e= marks 2SG.S; on a transitive verb with a
third-person counterpart, e= may mark either 2SG.A or 2SG.O, and the
role is supplied by the co-occurring indexes and by discourse.

sinen ne e=oman wa e=ray yakun | alone COP 2SG.S=go CONJ 2SG.S=die if | If you go alone and die, … | Both intransitive verbs carry e= as 2SG.S; no object is present. | nakagawa2024:167 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 106

kunneywa wakka e=ku | morning water 2SG.A=drink | You drink water in the morning. | Constructed following Ijäs Lesson 3. The prefix e= indexes a 2SG transitive subject; the third-person object wakka receives no index. | ijas2023 | SAR

The role ambiguity of e= on a transitive verb is inherent to the
neutral row. In a clause such as kanpisos e=kore , the prefix may signal
either 2SG.A (the addressee gives the book to someone) or 2SG.O (someone gives the book to
the addressee), depending on which participant is third person; the gloss assigned in
interlinear analysis follows what the discourse establishes. Ijäs flags this ambiguity
explicitly in her fifth lesson as a diagnostic for learners
ijas2023 .

When a 1SG or 1PL.EXCL subject acts on a second-person object, the portmanteau
eci= replaces any sequence of separate first- and second-person
indexes. All four 1→2 combinations — 1SG→2SG, 1SG→2PL, 1PL.EXCL→2SG, 1PL.EXCL→2PL — surface
as eci= , so that forms such as *ku=e= and
*ci=e= do not occur
tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2008 38 ; bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 . The portmanteau's double role — 1→2 combination
exponent and 2PL index for S, A, and O — is treated in
eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

nep ka eci=kasuy pe an ya
2SG.O=help NMLZ exist Q"
tr="Is there anything I can help you with?"
cite="nakagawa2024:173"
dial="SAR"
place="Nakagawa ex. 128, after Honda 2001: 44"
note="The single exponent eci= encodes the 1SG.A→2SG.O combination; no separate ku= or e= appears."
/>

Second-person neutrality is the consensus descriptive position for Hokkaido Ainu
tamura1972 27 ; sato2008 32 ; bugaeva2012 472 .
Its diachronic status is more open: most treatments assume an earlier S/A≠O distinction was
lost, while Satō's reported 2024 position treats neutrality as original — a strategy of
empathy or deferred agency never preceded by a subject/object split
dalcorso2025align 194–195 ‹contested›. The alignment-level debate and the
competing system-wide accounts are taken up in
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

title: The Formal / Defective Noun Set (ruwe, hawe, siri, humi, hi, pe, kur)
summary: The closed set of bound 'formal' nouns that head nominalized and evidential constructions, defined here as a morphosyntactic subclass with shared diagnostics.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Formal / Defective Noun Set (ruwe, hawe, siri, humi, hi, pe, kur)
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: The closed set of bound 'formal' nouns that head nominalized and evidential constructions, defined here as a morphosyntactic subclass with shared diagnostics.

Overview and terminology

Among the four noun subclasses of Hokkaido Ainu, the formal noun (形式名詞
keishiki meishi ) occupies the lowest position in the independence hierarchy: none
of its members can stand alone as a predicate argument. Each must be preceded by a verb
phrase, a prenominal, or — in one exceptional case — a bare noun, from which it draws its
referential content nakagawa2024 257 ; nakagawa2022pos 480–482 .
The diagnostic that separates formal nouns from the three other subclasses (common, locative,
and pronominal nouns) is set out in word-class-inventory-and-diagnostics .
Nakagawa also calls them noun particles (名助詞 meijoshi ), reflecting
their particle-like attachment to the preceding element
nakagawa2024 257, 295 .

The terminology used for this class is unsettled ‹contested›.
sato2008 174–178 divides what Nakagawa treats as a single class into two:
nominalizers (名詞化辞 meishikaji ) head content clauses with full
argument structure, while formal nouns proper head relative clauses from which
one argument is missing. Both are distinct noun subclasses in his account. Nakagawa keeps
the single class label and instead distinguishes the role a formal noun plays
clause by clause: as relativizer it heads a clause with a missing argument; as
complementizer (補文標識 hōbun hyōshiki ) it heads a clause with full
argument structure, the diagnostic being arity — a clause summing to arity zero takes a
complementizer, one with a net argument gap heads a relative construction
nakagawa2024 503–504 . refsing1986 259–264 uses
nominalizer more broadly still, grouping items that other analyses class as
mood-morphemes, complementizers, and relative-clause heads within a four-type
nominalization typology ‹contested›. The full accounts of relativization and
complementation are in nominalization-p-pe and
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization ; the evidential constructions are in
evidential-schema-overview .

The inventory

nakagawa2024 257–258 lists two sets. The general set covers a range of
relational senses; the sensory-evidential set (his Table 66) consists of affiliative forms
of sensory nouns grammaticalized as evidential heads.

General formal nouns ( nakagawa2024 257 ; sato2008 175 )

form
core sense
antecedent types
primary function

p ~ pe
'thing, one'
VP; prenominal (not bare NP)
relative head; content complementizer; class prefix in pronoun fusion

hi
'thing, matter, time, place'
VP only
abstract-state complementizer; pronoun-formation base

hike
'the one (that …)'
VP; prenominal
anaphoric relative head (derived: hi + -ke )

katu
'state, manner'
VP
manner/state complementizer

uske
'place'
VP
place complementizer

usi(ke)
'time, place'
VP
time/place complementizer

kur
'person'
VP; NP
person-relativizer; class-term head in demonyms

utar
'people, companions'
VP; NP; prenominal
associative plural (see nominal-number-utar-transnumerality )

kuni / kunak
'(in order to …; that …; lest …)'
VP (finite)
purpose/intention complementizer

ihi
'thing' (Tokachi/Ishikari only)
VP
confined to the frame ene an ihi 'such a thing'

The four sensory-evidential formal nouns form a paradigmatic sub-set. Each is the
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative form of a sensory noun and heads
one evidential construction ( nakagawa2024 258 Table 66):

Sensory-evidential formal nouns ( nakagawa2024 258 ; tamura1996 s.v. ruwe, hawe )

formal noun
source noun
possessed morphology
evidential value

ruwe
ru track, path
ru-we 'the track of —'
fact known through reasoning or assimilated knowledge

siri
sir appearance, land, weather
sir-i 'the appearance of —'
directly visible situation

hawe
haw voice, sound
haw-e 'the voice of —'
verbal report or hearsay

humi
hum sound, sensation
hum-i 'the sound/feeling of —'
non-visual sensory information

The possessed morphology follows the standard affiliative pattern for monosyllabic
open-syllable nouns: ru → ruwe(he) ,
haw → hawe(he) , and so on
nakagawa2024 226 .

Shared diagnostics

Four properties distinguish formal nouns from the other noun subclasses as a group
nakagawa2022pos 480–482 .

Obligatory modification. A formal noun cannot constitute an NP on its own.
*pe , *hi , and
*kur are not grammatical arguments in isolation. This places formal
nouns at the foot of the independence hierarchy set out in Nakagawa's Table 48 — below
the locative noun's basic form, which at least requires only an immediately preceding NP.

No self-relativization. A formal noun cannot serve as the head of a
separate modifying clause in the ordinary sense:
*pirka ruwe 'a good track (as NP)' and
*pon kur 'a small person (as NP)' are ungrammatical as true
relativizations, where an external clause predicates something of the formal noun itself
nakagawa2022pos 480 .

No free attributive modifier. When a prenominal demonstrative precedes a
formal noun, the result is not a compositional NP but a fused pronoun-like form:
tan + pe →
tanpe 'this thing',
toan + pe →
toanpe 'that thing' nakagawa2024 287 .

Phonological fusion. p ~ pe and
hi fuse prosodically with the preceding element, behaving
synchronically like suffixes, even though each nominalizes the entire preceding VP rather
than only the adjacent word nakagawa2022pos 481 .

Distribution asymmetry within the class. The antecedent constraint is
not uniform: hi requires a VP antecedent;
p ~ pe takes a VP or a prenominal but not a bare NP;
utar exceptionally accepts a bare NP as well
( cikappo utar 'small birds') nakagawa2024 258 ;
nakagawa2022pos 482 .

The p ~ pe nominalizer

The nominalizer p thing, one ~ pe thing, one is the most
productive member of the set. It attaches to any VP and yields either a relative-clause
head (entity reading, with a missing argument) or a content complement (propositional
reading, full argument structure) sato2008 174–178 ;
nakagawa2024 503–506 . The allomorph is phonologically conditioned:
p follows a vowel-final VP; pe follows a
consonant-final VP sato2008 177 .

huci na ipe yakne pirka p | grandmother FIN eat COND be.good NMLZ | Grandmother, it would be good if you ate. | p after the vowel-final predicate pirka 'be good'. The clause ipe yakne pirka 'if one eats, (it) is good' is nominalised as a content complement. | sato2008:177 | SAR

ratcitara rok=an pe ne na | quietly sit=4.S NMLZ COP FIN | One should sit quietly. | pe after the consonant-final clitic =an (4th-person intransitive). The nominalised clause is predicated with the copula ne. | sato2008:177 | SAR

As a relative-clause head, p ~ pe nominalizes a VP with a missing
argument:

kani ku=respa p anakne | 1SG 1SG.A=raise NMLZ TOP | as for the ones I raised … | Nakagawa ex. 230. p heads a relative clause with a missing object (the raised entities); anakne marks the nominalized clause as a contrastive topic. | nakagawa2024:214 | SAR

A productive instrumental pattern uses p ~ pe to nominalize an
applicative VP: ani X p 'the thing with which one does X', as in
ani e=kanpinuye p 'pencil' (literally 'the thing with which you
write') and ani humi a=aste p 'musical instrument'
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-05-29) aynucorporadiscord . The
instrumental ani fills the applicative argument slot while
p nominalizes the whole VP ‹corpus-confirmed›.

When a prenominal demonstrative precedes pe , the result is a
demonstrative pronoun: tanpe 'this (thing)',
neap 'that previously mentioned thing', and the rest of the series
treated in demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness
nakagawa2024 287 . The full syntax of p ~ pe —
its arity-based relativization/complementation split and clause-linker interactions — is
in nominalization-p-pe .

hi and the other general formal nouns

The formal noun hi thing, matter, time, place accepts only a VP antecedent
and characteristically nominalizes the state of affairs described by the clause —
the content-complement reading — without a missing argument
nakagawa2022pos 482 .

sirkunne hi ka ku=eramuskari-no ku=mokor wa ku=an | become.dark NMLZ even 1SG.A=not.know-ADV 1SG.S=sleep CONJ 1SG.S=exist | I slept on without even knowing that it had grown dark. | Satō §22 ex. 1. hi nominalises the clause sirkunne 'it became dark'; the resulting NP is the complement of eramuskari 'not know'. The antecedent clause has no missing argument, confirming the content-complement function. | sato2008:175 | SAR

hi also supplies the morphological base of several personal
pronouns. The first-person singular kani derives from
ku=an-hi 'the thing/fact of my existing', reduced by regular
vowel contraction; c(i)oka(y) 'we (exclusive)' from
ci=oka(y) + reduction; and asinuma from
a= + sinuma
nakagawa2024 253 ; kindaichi1936 . The full pronoun paradigm
is in independent-personal-pronouns .

hike 'the one (that …)' is an anaphoric relative head derived from
hi by the concretizing suffix -ke — the same
element that yields arke 'half' and sake
'refrain-melody' from their bases nakagawa2024 247–248 . The suffix
-ike that appears in lexicalized items such as
onneike 'parent/elder' (< onne 'old' +
-ike 'person/entity', per Chiri's gloss; noted by antitwilight,
aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-15) aynucorporadiscord may represent the same
element in a lexicalized compound; the synchronic productivity of bare
-ike as a nominalizing suffix is a proposed analysis ‹contested›.

katu 'state, manner' nominalizes the manner or condition described
by a clause; it appears in the frame … katu ene an hi 'such is the
state of affairs' as a manner complementizer nakagawa2024 504 .
uske 'place' and usi(ke) 'time/place' head
NPs denoting the location or occasion of an activity:

nanu memke usi ku=oman wa ek ruwe ne | face shave place 1SG.S=go CONJ come.back track-POSS COP | I went to the place where one shaves — a barber's. | Nakagawa ex. 330 (Kushiro dialect, citing Yaegashi 2001: 13). usi nominalises the VP nanu memke 'shave one's face', producing an NP denoting a place; the evidential ruwe ne closes the matrix clause. | nakagawa2024:258 | HK

The Tokachi/Ishikari form ihi is a regionally restricted variant
confined in attested data to the fixed frame ene an ihi 'such a
thing' nakagawa2024 257 .

Nakagawa's diachronic hypothesis — that hi is the historical source
of the affiliative suffix -i (as in
sik-i(-hi) 'its eye') — is discussed under
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology ‹speculative›
nakagawa2022pos 493 .

kur and kuni: person and purpose nominals

The formal noun kur person heads NPs denoting a person or agent. As a
relativizer it takes a VP antecedent. As a class-term head it combines with the adnominal
un to form demonyms:
Sar un kur 'a Saru person',
Iyoci un kur 'a Yoichi person'
nakagawa2024 234 . In the Hokkaido corpus
kur ranks at position 98 (2,456 tokens), consistent with its dual
role as agent-denoting nominalizer in narrative and as a class-term head.

The formal noun kuni (in order to …; that …) is the purpose and intention
complementizer. It follows a finite clause and establishes its content as a goal or the
object of a mental-state verb: with the transitive verb ramu
'think/believe', the correct embedding is kuni a=ramu 'think that
…', contrasting with the quotative frame sekor a=ye 'say that …',
which selects a directly reported assertion rather than a propositional attitude
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09) aynucorporadiscord
‹corpus-confirmed›. The related kunak (lest …; whether …) carries a
negative-purpose or indirect-question reading.
sato2008 175 includes both in the nominalizer list alongside
hi and p ~ pe . Their full syntax —
interaction with purpose adverbials, desiderative and abilitative predicates, and control
— is in kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements .

The sensory-evidential quartet

ruwe , siri ,
hawe , and humi are the affiliative forms
of four sensory nouns and constitute the formal-noun stratum of the evidential system
nakagawa2024 258 ; tamura1996 s.v. ruwe, siri . Each
requires a finite VP antecedent, none stands alone as a predicate argument, and each is
followed by a predicate — typically the copula ne — that closes the
evidential construction.

A morphological point distinguishes evidential use from the full lexical affiliative: the
lexical form may carry an additional extension
( ruwehe , humihi ), whereas the evidential
formal noun blocks it — *ruwehe ne is ungrammatical as the
inferential evidential tamura1996 s.v. ruwe .

Dialect variation affects the morphological shape: southern Hokkaido (Saru, Chitose) uses
the possessed forms ruwe / siri / hawe / humi , while Tokachi and
Ishikari often use the bare concept forms ru / sir / haw / hum in
the same evidential construction nakagawa2024 390 . Both shapes head the
same frame.

The semantic distinctions among the four — the source-type hierarchy, the indirect evidential
frames ( siri an , hawe as ), and the scope of
the evidential over the embedded TAM system — are treated in the dedicated chapters:
ruwe-ne-inferential , siri-ne-situational ,
hawe-ne-reportative , and humi-ne-sensory . The architecture
common to all four is in evidential-schema-overview .

Grammaticalization: from lexical noun to formal noun

The sensory-evidential set makes the grammaticalization cline transparent. The lexical noun
ru track, path denotes a physical trace; its affiliative form
ruwe 'the trace of —' extends to any trace including an epistemic
one; clause-final [clause] ruwe ne then asserts the clause content
as established fact tamura1996 s.v. ruwe ;
nakagawa2024 258, 392–396 . The parallel paths for
sir , haw , and hum
are transparent from the inventory table. The result is only partial grammaticalization:
the lexical source remains recoverable and the choice of post-nominal predicate
( ne vs an ) still matters, placing these
forms at an intermediate point on the cline nakagawa2024 393 .

The non-evidential formal nouns stand at a later stage.
hi has no independent lexical noun use in modern Hokkaido Ainu; its
original sense survives only in the pronoun-formation structures described above and,
by Nakagawa's hypothesis, in the affiliative suffix -i .
p ~ pe is likewise fully bound; its affiliative-form origin in the
monosyllabic open-syllable class
(cf. ru → ruwe(he) ,
ci → ciye(he) ) is preserved in the
paradigm of monosyllabic nouns but no longer lexically active
nakagawa2024 226 .

The two-stage path — lexical noun → possessed formal noun → complementizer or evidential
head — is the mechanism through which Hokkaido Ainu builds clause-embedding without
infinitives or dedicated particle complementizers. The point has been noted in the
community discussion (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-25)
aynucorporadiscord : in a head-marking language where every embedded clause
carries its own person marking, the formal noun provides the NP wrapper that the embedding
construction requires, substituting for the infinitive strategies of other language types
‹contested›. The full treatment of relativization, complementation, and the arity
diagnostic is in nominalization-p-pe ,
nominalized-complements-control-raising , and
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

title: Honorific and Logophoric Uses of the Fourth Person
summary: The indefinite/fourth person used to refer respectfully to esteemed humans and kamuy, and as the self-reference device of the first-person narrator in oral literature (the hero's 'I'); the affix-paradigm facts of these uses, with full discourse deployment in Part XIX.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Honorific and Logophoric Uses of the Fourth Person
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The indefinite/fourth person used to refer respectfully to esteemed humans and kamuy, and as the self-reference device of the first-person narrator in oral literature (the hero's 'I'); the affix-paradigm facts of these uses, with full discourse deployment in Part XIX.

The honorific and logophoric functions in the fourth-person system

The honorific and logophoric values of the fourth person belong to the same formal row as its
inclusive and indefinite uses: the transitive-subject prefix
a= (dialectally an= ), the
intransitive-subject suffix =an , and the object prefix
i= . The S/A/O architecture of this tripartite row and its
agent-defocusing function are set out in person-marking-architecture and
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm . The present chapter treats two discourse-anchored
functions: respectful address to a socially elevated second person, and self-reference by a
narrated protagonist or by a speaker whose words are embedded inside a quotation
nakagawa2024 175–179 ; tamura1972 19–20, 28 ;
bugaeva2012 475–476 .

role
form
honorific value
narrative / logophoric value

A: transitive subject
a= , dialectally an=
honored addressee as actor on a transitive predicate
narrator or reported speaker as actor on a transitive predicate

S: intransitive subject
=an
honored addressee as intransitive subject
narrator or reported speaker as intransitive subject

O: object
i=
honored addressee as patient or recipient
narrator or reported speaker as patient or recipient

Nakagawa subsumes all these values under a single fourth-person label; Tamura's Saru analysis
separates the honorific second person and the quotative first person as distinct categories;
Bugaeva's cover term is indefinite, with the quotation use reanalysed as logophoric; Satō
discusses the literary and quotative functions under the literary-person problem without positing
a fourth-person label; Takahashi, working on Tokachi, uses "inclusive person" for the same
form range
nakagawa2024 174–179 ; tamura1972 19–20, 28, 39 ;
bugaeva2008 37–44 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ;
sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ;
takahashi2015 205–210 ‹contested›.

Honorific address to an exalted second person

The fourth-person row indexes a second person of elevated social standing. Nakagawa cites two
primary contexts: a woman addressing an adult man, and any speaker addressing a kamuy in
prayer; in both, the verb takes fourth-person morphology and a plural stem even when the
addressee is singular
nakagawa2024 175–176 ; bugaeva2012 475 . Tamura's Saru account
places the same frame alongside contexts of esteem toward an adult
tamura1972 28 .

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | The addressee is a single person; the fourth-person S suffix =an appears on the plural motion stem arki rather than the singular ek. The evidential frame ru-we an closes the question. | nakagawa2024:176 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140; a woman addressing an adult man

The plural-stem requirement in this single-referent frame is grammatically obligatory for the
honorific use. The same constraint — one referent triggering plural verbal morphology — operates
across the honorific, inclusive, and narrative fourth-person functions
nakagawa2024 160–161, 174–176 . Person–number interactions in the verbal system
are treated in person-number-interactions . The syntax of prayer address to a kamuy
and the attendant ritual formulas are developed in honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers .

Ijäs's online pedagogical grammar documents several present-day address strategies for L2
learners, including plural-stem imperatives with the final particle
yan final particle (request) as a routine polite request, and the use of
nispa gentleman; master and katkemat respected woman as address
terms for non-relatives ijas2023 . The following constructed example illustrates
the plural-stem polite imperative, a strategy structurally distinct from the fourth-person S
suffix =an used in the honorific paradigm:

eteun arki yan | here come.PL FIN | Please come here. | Ijäs pedagogical example. The plural stem arki and the final particle yan form a polite request without deploying fourth-person morphology; it is a parallel addressee-politeness resource, not an instance of the honorific paradigm described above. Ijäs further notes that attaching nispa and katkemat to personal names as titles is not uniformly attested in traditional usage; such address conventions belong to contact and revitalization contexts rather than to the inherited verbal honorification system. | ijas2023 | HK

The narrator's fourth person in oral literature

In Hokkaido oral narrative, the protagonist's self-reference is expressed by the fourth-person
row. The verb takes fourth-person morphology, and Japanese translations of the same passages
render the protagonist with first-person pronouns, because the referent is the narrated self
nakagawa2024 177–179, 562 . Satō discusses this under the literary-person
problem; Bugaeva places it alongside the logophoric use
sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ;
bugaeva2008 37–44 . All three grammatical roles are attested. The following Saru
example shows the intransitive-subject suffix and the possessive prefix together:

ratcitara hosipi=an yakka a=uni un tunasno hosipi=an pe ne kusu | slowly return=4.S though 4.POSS=home to quickly return=4.S NMLZ COP because | Although I returned slowly, because I was able to return home quickly, … | The intransitive stem hosipi takes the fourth-person S suffix =an; a= functions as the narrator's possessive prefix on uni 'home'. Saru and Chitose allow a singular stem with a singular fourth-person narrator. | nakagawa2024:167 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 109; singular Saru narrator

Saru and Chitose permit a singular narrator to appear with a singular verb stem, as in the
example above. Dialect variation is substantial: in Horobetsu, the verb may carry a singular
fourth-person index while the only available free pronoun is the plural
aokay ; in Shizunai, the free pronouns distinguish singular
anani from plural anoka , but the verb is
consistently plural nakagawa2024 174–176, 254–256 ‹contested›. These
opposite mismatches between pronoun number and verbal number across the dialect area indicate
that the number dimension of the narrator form diverged from a common source.

The object prefix i= indexes the narrator as patient or recipient
alongside a= and =an . A single predicate can
carry both the agent-defocusing a= and the narrator-object
i= :

a=rehe Ikuresuye sekor a=i=reko | 4.POSS=name Ikuresuye QUOT 4.A=4.O=name | I was named Ikuresuye. | a= on rehe is the narrator's possessive prefix; on reko it is an indefinite-agent prefix (someone named); i= indexes the narrator as the named object. The co-occurrence of fourth-person A as agent-defocuser and fourth-person O as narrator-object on one predicate yields a passive-like Japanese reading (私はイクレスイェと名付けられた). | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 7 [aa-irc/005#1]

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=shelter CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The object prefix i= recurs across three transitive predicates, indexing the narrator as the entity pitied, sheltered, and given to. The homophonous detransitivising prefix i- of the antipassive construction is morphologically and functionally distinct. | nakagawa2024:170 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

The distinction between the fourth-person object prefix i= and the
antipassive detransitiviser i- 'something' requires attention to
syntactic context. The antipassive is treated in antipassive-detransitive-i .

The debate over the literary person

Earlier analyses distinguished a "literary" person (雅語の人称) from an "everyday" person
(日常語の人称), treating the narrative fourth-person as a register marker. Nakagawa rejects
this bifurcation: prose tales (uwepeker) use fourth-person marking for the protagonist even
though their register is conversational, while prayers (inonno itak), though formally elevated,
use first-person ku= for the praying speaker. The controlling factor
is discourse role: a character's story-internal self-representation takes the fourth person;
a speaker's real-world first-person address takes ku=
nakagawa2024 600–602 .

Satō advances three objections to reducing the literary person to the indefinite
sato2004 179–184 . First, Ainu speakers and translators consistently interpret
the narrative form with first-person reference. Second, the indefinite in its agent-defocusing
function is referent-non-specific and incompatible with an overt free pronoun; yet narrators
use asinuma alongside fourth-person verbal morphology, indicating
referential specificity. Third, kamuy-yukar texts from Horobetsu use the first-plural
exclusive row ci=/=as/un= as the divine narrator's person — a fact
that resists derivation from an indefinite base nakagawa2024 602–604 .
Nakagawa counters that most texts mix the first-plural exclusive and the fourth person, and
that the uniformly first-plural presentation of Chiri Yukie's 1923 神謡集 is likely an
editorial regularisation, since Kanari Matsu's tellings of the same material show mixed usage.
The divine narrator pattern and its genre context are examined in
heroic-and-divine-verse .

Bugaeva's approach sidesteps the origin question by relocating the quotation-internal use to
logophoricity and documenting it in non-folklore Asahikawa reported-speech data
bugaeva2008 41–44 ; bugaeva2012 475–476 . Since the form
appears wherever a discourse source refers to itself — in oral literature and in everyday
reported speech alike — a register-based account cannot be primary. The three analyses agree
on the empirical core while diverging on grammatical category and diachronic origin ‹contested›.

Logophoric reference in reported speech

When a third party's speech is embedded after the quotative complementizer
sekor quotative particle , that speaker's self-reference appears as fourth-person
morphology in place of ordinary first-person ku= or
ci= . Tamura had already treated this as a "quotative first person" in
Saru; Bugaeva reanalyses it as logophoricity in the typological sense, where a dedicated form
tracks the self of the source of a reported utterance
tamura1972 19–20, 22–23 ; bugaeva2008 37–41 ;
bugaeva2012 475–476 .

a=ye wa anun katkemat a=nure sekor a=ye acapo hawean kor | 4.A=say CONJ other lady 4.A=tell QUOT 4.A=say uncle say while | "I will tell it and let another lady hear it," the uncle said. | Both predicates of the embedded quotation (a=ye, a=nure) carry fourth-person a= as the reported uncle's self-reference; Nakagawa notes that the actual first-person utterance would have used ku=ye and ku=nure. The narrator's own framing verb a=ye is fourth-person for the separate reason that it is the narrator's story-world first person. | nakagawa2024:179 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 153, after Tamura 1984: 22

The logophoric analysis receives support from non-folklore contexts. Bugaeva's Asahikawa data
show the same pattern in everyday reported speech, severing the connection to elevated register
bugaeva2008 41–44 . Nikitina and Bugaeva develop the syntactic argument
further, proposing that logophoric reported speech in Ainu is a structurally distinct category,
neither indirect speech nor ordinary direct speech nikitina2021 . The dialectal
scope remains uneven: Nakagawa confirms the phenomenon for Saru and Chitose, while its
extension to other dialects is less well documented nakagawa2024 179 ‹contested›.

The free pronoun asinuma (< a= +
sinuma 'oneself') is the fourth-person singular self-referential
pronoun. Bugaeva notes that the consultant Ito Oda consistently glossed
asinuma as Japanese 自分 'oneself', never as
私 'I', which is consistent with a logophoric interpretation: the
pronoun tracks the perspective of the contextually salient discourse source rather than fixing
the speaker of the utterance bugaeva2008 41–43 .

The morphology of the logophoric use is identical to that of the honorific and narrative uses:
a= , =an , and i= serve
all three. Disambiguation depends on discourse frame — quotative embedding, story narration,
or respectful address — together with the number of the verb stem and the presence of the
free pronoun asinuma or its plural counterpart
aoka . The clusivity contrast between the exclusive
ci=/=as/un= and the fourth person is developed in
first-person-plural-and-clusivity . The sekor construction and the syntax of
quotative complementation are treated in quotative-complementation-sekor . The
extended analysis of logophoric narration and reported discourse is in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

title: Gendered Speech and Pragmatic Registers
summary: Sex-of-speaker and register differences in final particles, interjections, and politeness, and the documentation gaps surrounding them.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Gendered Speech and Pragmatic Registers
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: Sex-of-speaker and register differences in final particles, interjections, and politeness, and the documentation gaps surrounding them.

Genre allocation and narrator sex

The most thoroughly documented gender dimension of Hokkaido Ainu is the performance norm that
distributes oral-literary genres by the sex of the traditional narrator. Satō's table for
Chitose specifies narrator sex as a genre-diagnostic parameter alongside melody,
sakehe refrain , and rhythm-tapping sato2008 259 ; Endō's
synthesis confirms the same allocation across the Hidaka–Iburi region and notes that the
norm applies to both the content and the formal-poetic apparatus of each genre
endo2022 .

genre
traditional narrator
refrain ( sakehe )
notes

yukar (heroic epic)
men
none (opening eyororpe only)

women may tell irupaye heroic-content retold as prose instead

menoko yukar (women's heroic verse)
women
yes (fixed per story)
marriage theme rather than battle; mid-Saru documented

kamuy yukar (divine epic)
women (and men)
yes (fixed per story)
god protagonist; short

uwepeker (prose folktale)
women (and men)
none
conversational to lightly elevated

kamuy nomi itak (prayer)
adult men only
—
improvised, personal melody; each prayer differs

inonno itak (incantation)
any sex, any age
—
fixed text; shared melody

upopo (seated song) and dance songs
mainly women
—
round-singing ( ukouk )

(After sato2008 259 and endo2022 .) The allocation is not
symmetrically two-way. Several genres are open to performers of either sex; the two genres
allocated exclusively to men are heroic verse ( yukar ) and prayer
( kamuy nomi ). Prayer is improvised with a personal melody and
structurally distinct from the fixed, shared incantation, which any speaker may perform endo2022 .

Genre norms operated as conventions, and their social character is clear from modern practice: Endō notes that modern revitalization
recordings document women reciting verse yukar ; the performers
themselves recognized this as a departure from tradition endo2022 . The grammar
and genre taxonomy of each form, including the narrator-person conventions and the
sakehe refrain, are treated in oral-literature-genre-taxonomy ,
sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure , and heroic-and-divine-verse .

The menoko yukar — women's heroic verse

The menokoyukar women's heroic verse retains the verse architecture of the
standard yukar — notably the sakehe refrain — while
replacing the battle theme with marriage. Its female protagonist holds the same superhuman
capacities as the male hero of the standard epic, including tusu shamanistic foresight and revival power ,
but the narrative turns on a marriage outcome endo2022 . The genre is documented
for mid-Saru (沙流中流); its presence in other Hokkaido regions is not established from the
sources read for this chapter ‹speculative›.

A Saru speaker's metalinguistic testimony illustrates the sakehe distinction. 川上まつ子 described the menoko yukar as a genre one
properly performs with the sakehe , and noted that she was narrating
one without it — treating it as an uwepeker — and that with the
sakehe the performance would be long, whereas without it, short (AA研アイヌ語資料
「menokoyukar 神謡6（節なし）」, 沙流, aa-irc/019, ll. 0, 66). This metalinguistic testimony
aligns with Satō's genre-feature table sato2008 259 : the
sakehe defines the genre boundary, not the thematic content. A
speaker switching off the refrain performs the same story in a different register.

menoko-yukar anak tup rep pakno ku=eraman | woman-epic TOP two three or.so 1SG.A=know | I know two or three women's epics. | After a statement by 川上まつ子 in the ニューエクスプレスプラス アイヌ語 recording series (沙流). Reconstructed morphemic segmentation; the original surface form is menokoyukar. tup and rep give an approximate count ('two or three'); pakno ≈ 'as many as, or so'. | SAR

Grammatically, the menoko yukar is not differentiated from the
male-narrator yukar by any morphological marker of the narrator's sex.
Both use the fourth-person narrator voice ( a= / =an )
and the elevated register ( atomte-itak ornamented/elegant language ) in the
same way endo2022 ; sato2008 259 . The narrator fourth person and
its logophoric function in oral epic are described in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse ; the elevated register and its
grammatical hallmarks in poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

Gendered ritual speech-acts

Two sets of thanks-expressions and accompanying gestures are sex-differentiated in
traditional practice. When receiving food or a gift, women say
hinna thanks/gratitude (lower-Saru hunna ) while
performing raymik index-finger nose-stroke gesture , stroking below the nose
with the index finger; men perform onkami reverential bow with both hands
without this interjection. A lesser degree of gratitude uses hioy'oy ;
a stronger reduplicated form hinna hinna marks stronger feeling
(scale: hioy'oy < hinna hinna ). Formal
thanks in ceremonial contexts uses iyayiraykere , applicable by either sex
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-26; asserted; consistent
with Endō's account of gendered oral-literature performance endo2022 ) ‹corpus-suggested›.

The greeting irankarapte was traditionally a man's formula, delivered with
onkami . In the revitalization period it has come into gender-neutral
use, recognized by speakers as a shift from traditional practice
bugaeva2022 . These gendered formulae belong to the broader system of deferential
address that includes the honorific fourth-person forms and prayer-specific epithets for the
deities, treated in honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers and
fourth-person-honorific .

Sentence-final particles and gendered interactional register

Whether specific sentence-final particles carry a sex-of-speaker feature in Hokkaido Ainu
— a structural parallel to the Japanese feminine わ versus masculine
ぞ / ぜ — cannot be answered from the sources
read for this chapter. Satō sato2008 , Endō endo2022 , and Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 supply no systematic gender-indexed inventory of sentence-final
particles for Hokkaido Ainu, and a search of the grammar literature yielded no attested
sex-conditioned particle pair ‹speculative›. For the pedagogical grammar, Ijäs likewise
addresses Hokkaido speech norms without specifying gendered SFP contrasts
ijas2023 .

Three primary sources that might bear on this question were not read for this chapter:
Fukuda's (Tamura's) 1961 description of the Saru SFP system
fukuda1961 , Kindaichi's writings on elegant-language and
genre-specific grammar kindaichi1935 , and Hattori's 1957 monograph on the
'special language of the older generations' ( nenchōshasō tokushugo ), the primary
source on age- and register-conditioned lexical repertoires. These constitute the main
empirical gap. Any future study would need to work from conversational recordings, which
are sparse in the available Hokkaido documentation relative to the predominantly
oral-literary aligned corpus.

The SFP inventory — na , wa , ne , ya ,
he and their illocutionary values — is treated without sex-of-speaker
stratification in sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force . The
evidential formal nouns that grammaticalize into sentence-final positions
( ruwe , hawe ) are covered in
evidential-schema-overview .

Honorific and age-conditioned registers

Age- and status-conditioned register is distinct from sex-of-speaker conditioning and is
better documented. The fourth-person affix set ( a= / =an )
functions as a second-person honorific in respectful address, drawing on the same forms that
serve as the narrator's voice in oral epic sato2008 261 . Satō lists the
elegant-language pronoun set — asinuma , aoka(y) ,
eani , ecioka(y) — as drawn from the
fourth-person paradigm and used in contexts of respectful address without sex-specificity
sato2008 261 . The full treatment of these honorific-fourth-person forms is
in fourth-person-honorific ; their intersection with kinship-term morphology in
kinship-honorific-possession .

Kindaichi's genre-density ranking — the density of elegant-language features increases from
greetings through prayer and incantation to negotiation ( caranke formal dispute-resolution oratory )
and peaks in heroic verse kindaichi1935 (via endo2022 ) — overlaps
with the narrator-sex allocation above: prayer and heroic verse, the male-allocated genres,
occupy the high-density end of the scale. The elegant register ( atomte
itak ) and its grammatical hallmarks — applicative case-marking by prefix rather than
particle, number-agreement override, and the distinct poetic lexicon — are described in
poetic-archaic-elevated-register . The register is not itself sex-indexed: a
narrator of either sex performing a genre that uses atomte itak
uses the same elevated morphology ‹consensus›.

Reliability of the record and open questions

The gendered-register evidence for Hokkaido Ainu stratifies by quality. Genre allocation by
narrator sex rests on genre tables, fieldwork descriptions, and native metalinguistic
statements extending from early twentieth-century documentation through Satō's Chitose-based
grammar sato2008 259 and Endō's Handbook synthesis endo2022 ; it
is the most robustly documented layer. The ritual speech-act differentiation
( hinna / onkami ) is attested in community
discussion consistent with the ethnographic picture but would benefit from independent
primary documentation. Sentence-final particle gender-indexing remains open and
unconfirmed ‹speculative›.

Two further documentary gaps limit what can be said. First, Hattori 1957 on the elders'
special register — the key inventory of age- and sex-conditioned non-everyday vocabulary —
is not held in the project repository and was not read; Fukuda/Tamura 1961
fukuda1961 on Saru SFPs and Kindaichi kindaichi1935 on elegant
language are held but were not read for this chapter. Second, the aligned Hokkaido corpus
is overwhelmingly oral-literary and genre-coded, so conversational distribution of any
potential gendered particle cannot be quantified from it directly. Together these gaps mean
that a strong claim about sex-indexed particles or a full inventory of gendered register
features in Hokkaido Ainu remains beyond what the present evidence supports.

Revitalization-era usage may be leveling some traditional allocations. The
irankarapte greeting is the clearest documented case; it is
possible but undocumented that other sex-specific formulae are similarly shifting
bugaeva2022 ‹speculative›. The sociolinguistic context of the revitalization
is treated in the opening chapter of the grammar.

title: The Genetic Position of Ainu: Isolate Status and Macro-Comparison Controversies
summary: Ainu as a language isolate, and a methodologically explicit survey of the contested macro-relationship and deep-contact proposals.
part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Genetic Position of Ainu: Isolate Status and Macro-Comparison Controversies
Part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
Summary: Ainu as a language isolate, and a methodologically explicit survey of the contested macro-relationship and deep-contact proposals.

Isolate status

Ainu has no demonstrated genetic relationship to any other language or language family. Every
attempt to connect it — whether to Japonic, Altaic, Austroasiatic, Austronesian, or Nivkh —
has failed to produce the regular sound correspondences across a systematic body of cognates
that the comparative method requires ‹consensus›. The assessment is stated plainly by
janhunen2022 §2 : Ainu "does not show unambiguous indications of being
associated with any other language family". alonso2022 §1 adopts the same
position and explicitly sets aside all prior affiliation proposals to concentrate on what can
be recovered by internal analysis alone.

Within its own lineage, Ainu is a small family. janhunen2022 §2 refers to it
as "Ainuic" (or "Kurilic", from the Russian exonym for the Kuril-speaking population), with
three member varieties: Hokkaido, Sakhalin, and the Northern Kuriles. The internal comparison
among these three is the only comparative operation available to Ainu linguistics; it is
treated in depth in proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction ,
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex , and
sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast , and the classification of Hokkaido dialects
within the Ainuic family is the subject of hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry .
The Sakhalin variety branched off first, approximately 800 years ago; the Kuril variety
diverged less than 600 years ago, following what janhunen2022 §3b calls the
"Great Migration" of the 13th century ‹consensus›. The deep genealogical history of the
family, and the ethnogenesis of the Ainu people, are treated in
ainu-people-homeland-history .

Typologically, Ainu is distinct from all its geographic neighbours, including Japanese.
janhunen2022 §3c notes it may be placed under the broad rubric of North
Pacific Rim typology, but stands apart even within that areal grouping.

The comparative method and what isolate status entails

For a language with no established relatives, three analytical tools are available, in
decreasing order of probatory force alonso2022 §1 : (i) internal
reconstruction — working backwards from dialectal and morphophonological alternations
to recover earlier forms — which alonso2022 §1 calls "by far the most
powerful" for Ainu; (ii) contact-induced change analysis , chiefly Ainu interactions
with Japanese and, on Sakhalin, with Nivkh; and (iii) typological reasoning , to be
"used with utmost caution" since structural parallels between languages provide heuristics
for the reconstruction scenarios but carry no genealogical weight.

The time depth reachable by internal reconstruction is shallow. Without a comparative method
drawing on related sister languages, reconstruction "with wild speculation excluded" takes
Ainu back roughly 1,000 years alonso2022 §1 . The one firm external date is
the 13th-century separation of Common Ainu into its descendant varieties (§ proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction ). The Pre-Proto-Ainu and Proto-Ainu stages reconstructed by
vovin1993 and revised by alonso2022 rest entirely on internal
reconstruction, without an external comparative check.

The standard for assessing claims of genetic relationship is the presence of regular
sound correspondences across a sizeable set of basic-vocabulary items — correspondences
that cannot be explained by borrowing, universal sound-change tendencies, or chance
resemblance. Typological similarity (shared SOV order, similar phoneme inventories) and
individual look-alike pairs fall short of this standard, as does the observation that two
languages share structural features that typologists group together aareally. The macro-comparison
proposals surveyed below are evaluated against this criterion.

The macro-comparison proposals

The following table summarises the major genetic or deep-contact proposals for Ainu, their
principal evidence, and their present standing. Detailed assessment follows.

Proposal
Key proponents / period
Grade
Decisive consideration

Altaic / Ural-Altaic
19th–early 20th c. tradition
‹speculative›
No systematic cognate set with regular correspondences; Altaic itself without consensus
status; alonso2022 §1 sets aside all such proposals

Austroasiatic
Vovin (1993) — background framework for RPA
‹speculative›; rejected by reviewers
All three published reviewers (Sidwell 1996; Abondolo 1997; Witczak 1999, via
alonso2022 §1 ) concluded the Austroasiatic background assumption
vitiates the consonant reconstruction

Japonic / Japanese–Korean
Chamberlain (1887); various; Vovin 2022 (reverse direction)
‹contested› as contact question; rejected as genetic claim
Shared features explained by Ainu substrate in Japanese ( janhunen2022 §3c ); early loanwords in both directions; no systematic sound correspondences
establishing common descent

Austronesian / Malayo-Polynesian
Invoked typologically by Alonso 2022
typological heuristic only
Typological parallels (e.g. Chamorro gw < *w ) used as
reconstruction motivation, not as evidence of descent; no genetic claim is made
( alonso2022 §2.2.3 )

Nivkh (Ghilyak) / 'Paleo-Asiatic'
Geographic grouping; Shiraishi & Tangiku 2022
contact, not genetic
Ainu and Nivkh are both isolates; extensive Sakhalin-area loanword exchange is
documented but both languages remain structurally distinct ( shiraishitangiku2022 §3 ; janhunen2022 §3c )

'Paleo-Asiatic' (Paleosiberian) as a unit
19th–20th c. geographical tradition
rejected as genetic unit
A geographic wastebasket grouping (Ainu, Nivkh, Yukaghir, Chukotkan, Itelmen),
not a demonstrable clade; each member is an independent isolate or family ( janhunen2022 §3c )

Altaic and related proposals

Proposals linking Ainu to Altaic languages (Turkic, Mongolic, Tungusic) and to the
"Ural-Altaic" macro-family circulated in comparative philology from the 19th century onward.
alonso2022 §1 explicitly sets aside all such proposals — "ignoring all
previous attempts at seeking relatives" — to work on internal reconstruction alone. The Altaic
hypothesis itself lacks consensus status, and no systematic cognate set with regular Ainu
correspondences has been advanced in the peer-reviewed literature ‹speculative›.

Community members have proposed individual Mongolic or Korean connections for specific Ainu
words (antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord 2024–2025 aynucorporadiscord ), but
isolated look-alike pairs — the kind of comparison that motivated the earliest hypotheses —
are precisely what the comparative method excludes as evidence. Without a systematic
correspondence set covering basic vocabulary, such proposals cannot be evaluated as genetic
claims ‹speculative›.

The Austroasiatic problem

The most consequential internal methodological problem in Proto-Ainu reconstruction arose from
vovin1993 's choice to use Austroasiatic as the typological template for his
consonant inventory, positing a bloated 12-consonant stop system to explain the distribution
of the affiliative suffix. All three published reviewers of vovin1993 independently
concluded that this background assumption vitiates the consonant reconstruction: Sidwell
(1996), Abondolo (1997), and Witczak (1999), as summarised by
alonso2022 §1 . alonso2022 's revised reconstruction abandons the
Austroasiatic template and proposes a leaner inventory that better matches the Ainu
phonological profile (see proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction ) ‹speculative›.

Nivkh and the northern contact zone

Ainu and Nivkh (Ghilyak) share an extensive zone of contact, centred on Sakhalin, and
lexical material has moved in both directions. shiraishitangiku2022 §3
document items including
kaya (from Nivkh, 'fish-skin garment, sail'),
san (from Nivkh cʰaŋ , 'offering shelf'), and
the shared term tunakay 'reindeer' (→ Japanese
トナカイ tonakai ), reconstructed from
Pre-Proto-Ghilyak *tola-ŋa.y . These are contact items, not
inherited cognates; Ainu and Nivkh are both independent isolates, and their structural
differences exclude the hypothesis of common descent ‹consensus›. The
contact layer is the subject of northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu .

The label "Paleo-Asiatic" (or "Paleosiberian") groups Ainu, Nivkh, Yukaghir, Chukotkan
languages, and Itelmen on geographic grounds. It is a useful shorthand for geographically
unaffiliated languages of Siberia and the North Pacific, but carries no implication of common
ancestry; each member is an independent isolate or small family
janhunen2022 §3c ‹consensus›.

The Japonic question

The relationship between Ainu and Japonic (the family including Japanese and Ryukyuan) is the
most actively debated contact question, even though the genetic claim — that the two
descend from a common ancestor — is rejected by the specialist consensus
janhunen2022 §3c ‹consensus›. Three distinct positions exist in the
literature, and they need to be held apart.

Japonic as a recent intrusion. janhunen2022 §3a places the
Japonic languages' arrival in the Japanese archipelago in the Yayoi/Kofun period (roughly
900 BCE–500 CE), as a migration from the Korean Peninsula that replaced or marginalized
the Jōmon languages. On this account, Ainu is not related to Japonic but to the substrate
population that Japonic replaced or absorbed; structural features shared between Ainu and
Japanese reflect Jōmon-language influence on the incoming Japonic varieties — what
janhunen2022 §3c calls "Jōmonization of Japanese" — rather than common
descent. This position, sometimes termed the substrate hypothesis , explains the
typological resemblances without positing genetic relatedness ‹consensus›.

Early Japanese loanwords in Ainu. A secure stratum of Old Japanese loans
in Hokkaido Ainu is attested by phonological fossils.
sippo 'salt' and
pasuy 'chopstick' preserve Old Japanese features — word-initial
*p and the diphthongoid *uy — that had already been lost by the time
Old Japanese sources were compiled, placing the borrowing "around the middle of the first
millennium CE" janhunen2022 §3c . The culturally significant term
kamuy 'deity' also belongs to this early contact stratum.
Later loans, such as Saru
cikenki (Standard Japanese つけぎ
tsukegi 'matches') and
antuki (Standard Japanese 小豆
azuki , with prenasalization from the Tōhoku substratum), document
the continuing contact through the medieval period alonso2022 §1 . These
loanwords are treated fully in japanese-loanwords-in-ainu ‹consensus›.

Ainu loans in Old Japanese — Vovin's reverse-direction thesis.
vovin2022 §§1–6 argues the traffic also ran in the other direction: that
Ainu was once spoken throughout the Japanese archipelago (from the Kantō and Tōhoku regions
to Kyūshū) and left identifiable loanwords in the earliest Japonic records, notably in
eastern and western Old Japanese poetry of the 万葉集
Man'yōshū . Proposed loans include
iruka 'dolphin' (< Ainu rika 'whale-fat'),
kuta 'house' (< kotan 'village, settlement',
with a household↔village semantic shift parallel to Latin vicus ),
porö 'large' (< poro ), and a set of place
names in the Kantō and Tōhoku regions. The loan phonology proposed by vovin2022
includes: Ainu *-kk- → OJ *-k- ; Ainu medial *-k- (half-voiced)
→ OJ *-ŋg- ; and Ainu final consonants lost before OJ vowels. This is a live claim
that has attracted attention but has not achieved consensus ‹contested›; it is evaluated
in full in ainu-loanwords-and-toponymy-in-japanese .

Ethnogenesis — the Jōmon inheritance and internal classification

Ainu's roots lie in the Jōmon period (12,000+ BP), and Ainu is the sole surviving
representative of the Jōmon language complex, its continuity into the present "an accident"
of demographic history janhunen2022 §3a . The Jōmon population spoke a range
of languages, of which only the Ainu lineage is traceable; the others were absorbed or
replaced.

The geographic picture is counterintuitive: Janhunen argues that Ainu's homeland is
Honshu, not Hokkaido . The ancestors of the modern Ainu people moved northward,
associated with the Satsumon material culture (c.700–1200 CE), colonizing Hokkaido (northern
areas c.1000 CE), southern Sakhalin (c.1300 CE), and the Kuril Islands (c.1500–1600 CE)
janhunen2022 §3a–3b . The Emishi ( 蝦夷 ) of
northern Honshu — a recurrent presence in early Japanese historical sources — are viewed as a
"generic proto-historical complex" ancestral in part to the Ainu
janhunen2022 §3a . The Sakhalin ethnonym enciw
(cognate with emi₁si , Ebisu, Ezo, *emisV~*emiciV ;
Kindaichi 1960, via janhunen2022 §4 ) preserves the Honshu connection at the
vocabulary level ‹corpus-suggested›.

A community member has proposed that the Sarunkur group of the Saru valley represents an
Aomori-origin migrant population with connections to the Jōmon cylindrical-pottery culture
(aomidori_cs_, aynu-corpora Discord 2023 aynucorporadiscord ). The proposal is
consonant with Janhunen's Honshu-origin thesis, but the specific pottery connection is not
supported by the archaeological record as presented in the accessible sources ‹speculative›.

In the north, the Ainu population on Sakhalin absorbed the Okhotsk culture
(c.600–1600 CE), whose language may have been an early form of Ghilyak/Amuric
janhunen2022 §3b–3c . This contact episode introduced the items documented
by shiraishitangiku2022 and helped shape the morphological distinctiveness of
the Sakhalin variety. The linguistic legacy of the Okhotsk-culture contact remains
incompletely disentangled from the Ainu substrate ‹speculative›.

The linguistic lineage is, in Janhunen's terms, "the one ethnic marker traceable
unambiguously back in time" for the Ainu people janhunen2022 §4 . The
ethnonyms aynu 'human being' (the endonym, of shallow diachronic
formation) and utari 'people' are internally analysable; the
exonym kur 'man', whose plural form (via Northern Tungusic -l )
is the origin of the Russian Kuríly 'Kurils', and the term for the
Japanese, sisam (reconstructed to Pre-Proto-Ainu
*sii+sam/o ), both point to a period of established ethnic
differentiation predating the Hokkaido colonization.

The recoverable contact layer and internal reconstruction

What the available methods do support, for Hokkaido Ainu, is a two-stratum contact
vocabulary — early Old Japanese loans dated to the first millennium CE (§ above) and a
