thinner Nivkh-mediated layer from the northern contact zone (§ northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu ) — and a body of internal reconstructions reaching back at most ~1,000 years. Within
that depth, internal reconstruction has produced robust results. The most celebrated is
the etymology of cep fish in Saru.

ci= e -p | 4.A= eat NMLZ | cep | fish (lit. 'the thing that everyone eats') | The surface Saru form cep continues Common Ainu *ciʔep; Horobetsu retains the medial glottal break (ciʔep) and Sakhalin Raiciska has ceh. The morpheme breakdown ci= (fourth-person agent prefix) + e (eat) + -p (nominalizer) is reconstructed by Alonso de la Fuente (2022: §4, Table 18), corroborated by the curated morpheme database (conf. 1.0). | alonso2022 | SAR

Two Eurasian Wanderwörter appear in the Hokkaido lexicon and locate Ainu in a network of
long-distance cultural contact.
kotan village, settlement is an Eurasian Wanderwort of continental origin
janhunen2022 §5 ; it is among the most frequent Hokkaido nouns
(rank 56 in the Hokkaido oral-literature corpus). The term
inaw ritual wood-shavings offering is reconstructed to a Tungusic source
(Ikegami 1980, via janhunen2022 §5 ), pointing to archaic northern contact
predating the Sakhalin-era Ainu–Nivkh–Uilta interaction. These items signal cultural
exchange, not genealogical relationship ‹consensus›.

The grammaticalization pathways recoverable through internal reconstruction — the possessive
suffix -hV from the noun hi 'thing', the
evidential nominalizers ruwe , siri ,
hawe , and humi from possessed sensory
nouns, and the applicative prefixes e- ,
ko- , and o- from locative/relational
nouns — are among the best-documented results of Ainu historical linguistics and are treated
in internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways . As Alonso notes,
"when it comes to functional words and TAM formatives, the etymology is still transparent"
alonso2022 §3.1 ; it is in the basic vocabulary where external comparison
would be decisive — and where it has consistently failed to produce the requisite
correspondence sets.

The macro-comparative and deep-contact debates are returned to in full detail in
macro-comparison-and-deep-contact-controversies , which assembles the
correspondence-set evidence — or its absence — for each proposal in one place.

title: Glide Epenthesis and Vowel-Hiatus Resolution
summary: Insertion of [w]/[y] transitional glides to break vowel hiatus at affixal, compound, and phrasal junctures, and the underlying-vs-epenthetic glide question at the morphophonology–phonetics interface.
part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Glide Epenthesis and Vowel-Hiatus Resolution
Part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
Summary: Insertion of [w]/[y] transitional glides to break vowel hiatus at affixal, compound, and phrasal junctures, and the underlying-vs-epenthetic glide question at the morphophonology–phonetics interface.

Overview

Morpheme composition in Hokkaido Ainu frequently places two vowels in adjacent position
across a prefix or morpheme boundary. Four main strategies resolve such hiatus sequences,
each conditioned by the type of boundary and the phonological shape of the adjacent
morphemes: (1) glide epenthesis , insertion of a transitional [y] or [w] between
the boundary vowels; (2) vowel weakening , in which a stem-initial high vowel /i/
or /u/ reduces to a glide after a preceding prefix; (3) glottal insertion , in
which [ʔ] marks the syllable onset; and (4) vowel deletion , in which one of the
adjacent vowels is elided. The present chapter covers strategies (1) and (2), together with
the phonotactic constraint that blocks both of them in certain environments. The
glottal-insertion strategy is treated in glottal-stop , and vowel deletion in
the context of personal-prefix sandhi is covered in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction .

Glide epenthesis applies only after specific prefixes — the
indefinite/antipassive prefix i- ANTIP , the reciprocal
u- RECP , and variably the reflexive si- REFL — and only
at a prefix boundary, not at a compound morpheme boundary. Vowel weakening is also
prefix-conditioned. Both are blocked when a glide before a closed syllable would create an
illicit onset cluster, because the (C)V(C) syllable canon of Hokkaido Ainu forbids
consonant clusters within a single syllable: see syllable-template
nakagawa2024 41–42 .

The glide transition (わたり) and its non-phonemic status

Nakagawa nakagawa2024 34 defines わたり 'glide, transition' as the sound
produced while the vocal tract reconfigures between two successive phonemic targets in
normal-tempo speech. In his analysis of Saru and Chitose, this transition is not an
independent phoneme. The word iyomante 'the bear-spirit-sending
ceremony' — phonemically /i-omante/ (< the indefinite-object prefix
i- ANTIP + omante send away ) — is pronounced [iyomante]
in connected speech, but the [y] between prefix and stem is a nonphonemic transition that
standard orthography does not treat as a separate segment nakagawa2024 34 .
Likewise uwepeker 'tell each other stories' is phonemically
/u-epeker/ (< u- RECP + epeker tell a story ), and the [w]
at the prefix–stem juncture is a transitional sound rather than a grammatically independent
consonant.

Two diagnostics establish non-phonemic status for Saru and Chitose. The nominalizer
allomorphy test: morphemes ending in a genuine /y/ or /w/ take the consonant-allomorph
-pe of the nominalizer (e.g. okay-pe ,
siw-pe ), while vowel-initial stems take -p
even when preceded by an epenthetic glide: iyomante-p , not
* iyomante-pe shiraishi2022 §3 ;
nakagawa2024 34 . The double-glide test (described in §5 below) provides a
second criterion for vowel-weakening contexts. The general analysis of /y/ and /w/ as
consonant phonemes in the inventory — and the diagnostics that establish root-phoneme /y/
/w/ — is developed in glides-w-y . Whether the transition [y]/[w] at morpheme
boundaries is phonemic in varieties other than Saru and Chitose is a genuinely contested
question, treated in §8.

Glide epenthesis at the i- and u- prefix boundary

Glide epenthesis operates at the juncture of a vowel-final prefix and a
vowel-initial stem. The relevant prefixes are the indefinite-object/antipassive
i- ANTIP and the reciprocal prefix u- RECP
kindaichi1936 13 ; shiraishi2022 §4.3 . After /i/ a
[y]-glide appears; after /u/ a [w]-glide. The rules:

R1  ∅ → y / i- ___ V  (at a prefix boundary)

R2  ∅ → w / u- ___ V  (at a prefix boundary)

Table 1. Glide epenthesis at the i- and u- prefix boundary

prefix
stem
phonemic form
surface form
gloss
source

i- ANTIP
omante
/i-omante/
[iy.o.man.te]
'sends it away'
nakagawa2024 34

i- ANTIP
oikir
/i-oikir/
[iy.oi.kir]
'piles things up'
shiraishi2022 §4.3

i- ANTIP
ekarkar
/i-ekarkar/
[iy.ek.kar.kar]
'makes things'
shiraishi2022 §4.3

i- ANTIP
uta
/i-uta/
[iy.u.ta]
'quarrels with someone'
shiraishi2022 §4.3

u- RECP
epeker
/u-epeker/
[uw.e.pe.ker]
'tell each other stories'
nakagawa2024 34

u- RECP
ekarkar
/u-ekarkar/
[uw.ek.kar.kar]
'make things together'
shiraishi2022 §4.3

u- RECP
atte
/u-atte/
[uw.at.te]
'help each other'
shiraishi2022 §4.3

u- RECP
osurpa
/u-osurpa/
[uw.os.sur.pa]
'cross together'
shiraishi2022 §4.3

The epenthetic glide is determined by the prefix vowel — /i/ triggers [y], /u/ triggers
[w] — independently of the quality of the following stem vowel. Table 1 shows y-insertion
before /o/, /oi/, /e/, and /u/, and w-insertion before /e/ and /o/. A derivation for the
canonical pair:

Derivation: /i-omante/ → [iyomante]

step
form
rule

underlying
/i-omante/
—

R1 applied
/i- y omante/
∅ → y / i- ___ V

surface
[iy.o.man.te]
syllabification

i-omante | ANTIP-send.away | iyomante | sends it away; (ceremonially) performs the bear-spirit-sending | The [y] glide is epenthetic at the prefix–stem boundary. Phonemic form /i-omante/. Nominalizer takes the V-allomorph: iyomante-p, not *iyomante-pe, confirming the stem is phonemically vowel-initial. | SAR

u-epeker | RECP-tell.story | uwepeker | tell each other stories; tell stories together | The [w] glide is epenthetic at the prefix–stem boundary. Phonemic form /u-epeker/. The variant spelling uepeker (without the written glide) occurs in some orthographic traditions. | SAR

The compound-boundary block

Satō (1996, 2003, cited in Shiraishi 2022 §4.3) establishes on Chitose data that R1
and R2 are morphosyntactically conditioned: they apply only at a productive prefix
boundary — after i- ANTIP , u- RECP , and variably
si- REFL — and not after the second-person-plural prefix
eci- 2PL.A , after applicative ru- , nor at the juncture
between an incorporated noun and its verbal host
shiraishi2022 §4.3 .

The contrast between a prefix boundary and a compound morpheme boundary is illustrated
by a community observation: the compound i-o-ci (roughly 'the
place where things are many') surfaces as ioci , not
* iyoci , because the morpheme boundary is a compound juncture
rather than a productive prefix–stem juncture
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The pair
iyocispare vs. niocire further illustrates
the conditioning: in iyocispare the productive
i- prefix triggers the glide; at the boundary in
niocire the conditions for R1 are not met and no glide appears.

The bear-ceremony word iyomante is informative here. Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 34 , Okuda okuda2025 , and Satō all assign a
glide-bearing form to this word because the i- +
omante boundary retains sufficient compositional productivity
for R1 to apply (cf. kamuy-omante 'god-sending'). Okuda (2025)
notes that in Shizunai, morphologically transparent instances may surface without the
glide, as iomante . The debate over this word is therefore
incidental to the blocking question; it is the morphosyntactic conditioning, not this
specific form, that divides the analyses.

Whether si- REFL consistently triggers R1 across dialects is uncertain.
Satō (1996/2003, via Shiraishi 2022 §4.3) includes it in the trigger class, while
community discussion notes dialectal variation in its behavior
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹corpus-suggested›. For the
reflexive prefix itself, see reflexive-possessive-si .

The *CVVC constraint as a blocker of glide formation

The (C)V(C) syllable canon prohibits consonant clusters in onset position and disallows
more than one vowel per syllable nakagawa2024 41–42 ;
shiraishi2022 §4.1 . When glide formation at a morpheme boundary would
produce a string that violates this canon, the process is blocked.

The first-person-singular agent prefix ku- 1SG.A before a stem whose
first syllable is closed (CVC) shows the blocking most directly. In /ku-ikra/ 'I send
it', weakening the stem-initial /i/ to [y] would place [y] immediately before the coda
/k/ of ikra 's first syllable, producing *[yk] — an illicit CC
onset at the following syllable boundary. The vowel is therefore not weakened:
ku-ikra is the surface form, with the vowel hiatus [ku.ik.ra]
maintained nakagawa2024 52–53 ;
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

ku-ikra | 1SG.A=send | I send it/them | Stem-initial /i/ is not weakened to [y]: *ku-ykra would create the illicit onset cluster [yk]. Surface form: [ku.ik.ra] with vowel hiatus maintained. Contrast ku-ipe → ku-ype (§5), where the stem begins with an open syllable. | SAR

The applicative prefix e= APPL before the same stem provides a parallel:
/e=ikra/ cannot surface as *[eykra] because [yk] is an illicit onset. The actual form
is eíkra [e.ík.ra], where the /i/ of
ikra bears accent as a syllable nucleus — confirming it has not
been reduced to a glide nakagawa2024 52–53 ;
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The open-σ1
stems itasa and iwanke contrast with
ikra : e= + itasa → eytasa ; e= + iwanke
→ eywanke . The open first syllable (CV) of those stems permits
the glide without creating a cluster.

Table 2. The *CC-onset block and the open-syllable licence

input
σ1 shape
would-be surface
constraint
actual surface

/ku-ikra/
closed (CVC: ik- )
*[kuykra]
*CC onset [yk]
[ku.ik.ra] ✓

/e=ikra/
closed (CVC: ik- )
*[eykra]
*CC onset [yk]
[e.ík.ra] ✓

/ku-iunin/
open (CV: iu- )
[kuyunin]
— (no cluster)
[ku.yu.nin] ✓

/e=itasa/
open (CV: i- )
[eytasa]
— (no cluster)
[ey.ta.sa] ✓

The constraint has direct practical consequences for corpus normalization. The io→iyo and
ue→uwe patterns cannot be applied uniformly across all morpheme boundaries: community
member thegodofneet (2025-04-24, aynucorporadiscord ) notes that no form
* eciyoka for eci-oka is attested in the
corpus ‹corpus-confirmed›. A rule-based normalization that applied io→iyo or ue→uwe at
every juncture would produce unattested forms. Glide-bearing spellings are appropriate
only where the morphosyntactic conditions of §3 and the phonotactic licence of §4 are
jointly satisfied and where corpus evidence confirms the variant.

Vowel weakening after personal and applicative prefixes

Vowel weakening (母音弱化) converts a stem-initial /i/ or /u/ to a glide after a
preceding prefix. No new segment is inserted; the high vowel already present at the stem
onset is converted to a consonantal element and loses the ability to bear accent
nakagawa2024 40 . The process has been verified for Saru and Chitose;
its distribution in other Hokkaido varieties requires further documentation
nakagawa2024 40 .

The rules:

R3  /i/ → [y] / V= ___ (stem-initial /i/, open first syllable)

R4  /u/ → [w] / V= ___ (stem-initial /u/, open first syllable)

Table 3. Vowel weakening after personal and applicative prefixes (Saru/Chitose)

prefix + stem
surface form
gloss
source

ko- + itak
koytak
APPL-speak 'talk to someone'
nakagawa2024 40

e- + ikoytupa
eykoytupa
APPL-talk.to.each.other
nakagawa2024 40

e- + ukoytak
ewkoytak
APPL-RECP.speak
nakagawa2024 40

yayko= + uepeker
yaykowepeker
REFL.APPL=tell.story
nakagawa2024 40

In koytak (< ko= + itak), the stem-initial /i/ of
itak 'speak' weakens to [y]; in
yaykowepeker , the stem-initial /u/ of
uepeker weakens to [w]. The open-first-syllable restriction of §4
applies here: weakening is possible only when the stem's σ1 is open (CV), so no onset
cluster results.

A double-glide test distinguishes R3/R4 from the presence of an underlying /y/ or /w/ in
the stem. If the stem-initial segment were an underlying /y/ or /w/, weakening it to [y]
or [w] would yield a geminate. The verb iunin 'ache' has a
phonemic stem-initial /i/ (not */yi/); with the prefix ku- 1SG.A it
surfaces in Chitose as [kuyunin], with a single [y], not *[kuyyunin]
nakagawa2024 40 . The same test in Saru:
somo eyunin ya? (not * eyyunin ). The single
[y] confirms that R3 weakens a stem vowel rather than inserting a new glide alongside an
underlying one.

The accent consequences of vowel weakening — the nucleus shift when a formerly accentable
σ1 vowel becomes a non-accentable glide — are treated in
accent-in-compounds-and-affixation . For the related deletion of the prefix
vowel in ku- and ci- before non-/i/ vowels
(ku-omare → k=omare), see personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction
and person-marking-architecture .

Glottal insertion when glide cannot appear

When the morphosyntactic conditions of §3 do not permit glide epenthesis and the adjacent
vowel is non-high so that R3/R4 weakening does not apply, [ʔ] fills the hiatus. Tamura
(1970: 586–587, 1996: xv, cited in Shiraishi 2022 §4.3) notes that the glottal onset is
most clearly audible when the second vowel is accented or belongs to a closed syllable:
aˈʔep 'food', aˈʔoypep 'tableware',
eˈʔikka , teˈʔeta 'a long time ago'. In
non-prominent environments the transition is gradual rather than sharply glottalized
shiraishi2022 §4.3 .

A morphologically restricted h-insertion applies within the
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed) form of nouns when
two vowels would be adjacent: onaa →
ona-h-a 'his/her father';
sikii → siki-h-i 'his/her eyes'
(Kindaichi & Chiri 1936: 14, cited in Shiraishi 2022 §4.3 n.8). This process is
restricted to possessive derivation and is treated in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Whether [ʔ] at these junctures constitutes an underlying phoneme /ʔ/ or is a predictable
boundary marker is contested; the debate is taken up in glottal-stop .

Vowel deletion as an alternative hiatus strategy

Vowel deletion provides a fourth resolution route, particularly for non-high vowels at
morpheme boundaries where glide formation is unavailable. Shiraishi
shiraishi2022 §4.3 documents:
keran (< kera=an 'is delicious'),
sisotta (< siso or=ta 'on the fireplace'),
and noya noya (< noye a noye a ). Deletion
is conditioned by speech rate and prosodic context and is not in free variation with glide
epenthesis at the same boundary. The full account, including the accent interactions that
block resyllabification, is in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction .

The phonemic status of the glide transition across dialects

Three analyses of the morpheme-boundary [y] and [w] bear on whether they are underlying
phonemes or surface transitions, and the three rest on genuine cross-dialectal differences.

Nakagawa's non-phonemic analysis (Saru/Chitose). The transition [y] and
[w] at prefix boundaries are non-phonemic in Saru and Chitose: their appearance is
entirely predictable from the shape of the adjacent morphemes, and neither the nominalizer
allomorphy diagnostic nor the double-glide test provides evidence for underlying /y/ /w/
at these junctures nakagawa2024 34–35 .

Okuda's phonemic analysis (Shizunai). In the Shizunai variety, which
lacks contrastive pitch accent, the glide vs. glottal distinction at a morpheme boundary
carries lexical weight that accent handles elsewhere. The minimal pair first observed by
志賀雪湖 (Okuda 2025 okuda2025 ): [suwat] 'pothook' — an opaque, fused compound
— vs. [suʔat] 'pot bail' — a transparent su- + at with the boundary maintained. In an
accentless dialect this /w/ vs. /ʔ/ contrast is phonemically distinctive, giving /suwat/
vs. /suʔat/ (= /suat/). Okuda further documents the pairs /u'a/ ~ /uwa/, /i'o/ ~ /iyo/,
/i'e/ ~ /iye/ as additional Shizunai minimal pairs, and concludes that "one cannot say,
without specifying dialect, that わたり /y/ /w/ are non-phonemic"
okuda2025 ‹contested›.

Satō's two-process analysis (Chitose). Satō (2003, cited in Shiraishi
2022 §4.3) proposes that in Chitose, [y] and [w] in sequences /ie/, /ia/, /io/, /iu/,
/ue/, /ua/, /uo/ at a morpheme boundary occur in the same phonetic environment as their
glottal-stop-bearing counterparts and contrast with them; he therefore posits underlying
/y/ /w/ phonemes in those contexts. The glide after ku- and
e= prefixes, by contrast, arises through surface glide formation
(i → y, u → w) — a distinct process from lexical /y/ /w/. The analysis thus involves two
interacting processes: underlying phonemes in some contexts, and surface-phonological
glide formation in others shiraishi2022 §4.3 ‹contested›.

In Saru and Chitose the nominalizer-allomorphy and double-glide diagnostics do not reveal
evidence for underlying /y/ /w/ at the prefix boundaries treated in §3; the non-phonemic
analysis is adopted for those varieties. In Shizunai the accentless system exposes a
contrast masked elsewhere, and the glides there are phonemically distinct from [ʔ]. The
question is structurally linked to the /ʔ/-phoneme debate — positing /ʔ/ at all
vowel-initial syllables reanalyses the Shizunai contrasts differently — and both are
considered together in glottal-stop ‹contested›.

title: Glides, Vowel Hiatus, and the Diphthong Question
summary: The phonemic-vs-epenthetic status of /w/ and /y/, glide epenthesis resolving vowel hiatus, and the analysis of ay/uy/oy/aw/iw/ew as coda-glide (VC) sequences rather than true diphthongs.
part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Glides, Vowel Hiatus, and the Diphthong Question
Part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
Summary: The phonemic-vs-epenthetic status of /w/ and /y/, glide epenthesis resolving vowel hiatus, and the analysis of ay/uy/oy/aw/iw/ew as coda-glide (VC) sequences rather than true diphthongs.

The analytical question

Hokkaido Ainu words frequently end in sequences pairing a vowel with a following
y [j] or w [w]:
áynu 'human', kamúy 'spirit',
ohaw 'soup', kéwtum 'heart',
ináw 'prayer-wand'. Two analyses have been proposed for these sequences.
The first treats the final element as a coda consonant — /y/ or /w/ — parallel to the coda
stops, nasals, and /r/ of the inventory. The second treats the sequences as falling diphthongs,
with the final element a non-syllabic vowel ([i̯] or [u̯]), giving bimoraic nuclei
[ai̯], [au̯], [ei̯], [eu̯], [oi̯], [ui̯]
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11). Phonetically the two analyses are
indistinguishable: both produce the same output, and neither predicts any difference in
production or perception
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11). The question is whether
post-vocalic y / w behave morphologically and
prosodically as consonants or as the second element of a vowel nucleus.

This chapter covers the phonemic status of coda y and
w and the related question of the marginal onset sequences
yi , wi , and wu .
The full phoneme inventory is in consonant-inventory and
vowel-inventory ; the (C)V(C) syllable canon in syllable-template .
The allophonic glide that appears at morpheme-boundary hiatus — the [j] in
i-y-ekarkar — is a separate process treated in
glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution .

The consonant analysis

The standard modern analysis treats post-vocalic y and
w as coda consonants, assigning syllables like
ay , muy , haw , and
kew to the CVC slot of the (C)V(C) canon alongside
cep , sin , and ker
nakagawa2024 29 ; shiraishi2022 §3 .
Three diagnostics converge on this position.

Nominalizer allomorphy

The participant/event nominalizer has two allomorphs conditioned by the final segment of the
base: -p attaches to vowel-final stems and
-pe to consonant-final stems
shiraishi2022 §3 ; nakagawa2024 29 . The selection is
phonological and applies without exception across the lexicon. Stems ending in
y and w consistently take
-pe , grouping with the consonant-final class. For the full treatment
of these nominalizers see nominalization-p-pe .

stem
final segment
nominalizer
derived form
approximate gloss

poro 'big'
/o/ — vowel
-p
poro-p
'the big one'

pon 'small'
/n/ — nasal
-pe
pon-pe
'the small one'

an 'exist'
/n/ — nasal
-pe
an-pe
'the existing thing'

okay 'be (pl.)'
/y/ — glide
-pe
okay-pe
'those who are (pl.)'

siw 'jostle'
/w/ — glide
-pe
siw-pe
'the jostling ones'

On the diphthong analysis the final element of okay and
siw is a non-syllabic vowel, which should trigger the vowel allomorph
-p ; the occurring forms okay-pe and
siw-pe would then be exceptions requiring an auxiliary stipulation.
On the consonant analysis they are regular: /y/ and /w/ are consonants, and
-pe is the expected allomorph shiraishi2022 §3 .

Plural-suffix allomorphy

The same consonant–vowel boundary governs the distributive plural suffix, which surfaces as
-te after a consonant-final stem and -re after
a vowel-final stem. The plural of ante is
okayte (< okay + -te ),
selecting the consonant allomorph on the evidence that stem-final /y/ is a consonant;
vowel-final stems take -re
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2025-12-03) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The pattern
mirrors the nominalizer allomorphy: /y/ at stem-end triggers the consonant-class allomorph
in both environments independently.

Syllabification and pitch-accent placement

In the pitch-accent dialects of Hokkaido Ainu (Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, and related
varieties), accent placement is determined by the weight of the first syllable: a closed
(CVC) first syllable bears the rising pitch nucleus (↗), while an open (CV) first syllable
passes the peak to the second syllable nakagawa2024 50 . The system is
described in full in pitch-accent-placement-rule . Words whose first syllable
ends in y or w pattern as CVC-closed
(Rule 1), not as open syllables followed by an additional vowel:

form
syllabification
σ1 type
accent rule
gloss

áynu
/áy.nu/
CVC — closed
Rule 1: σ1 high
'human'

kéwtum
/kéw.tum/
CVC — closed
Rule 1: σ1 high
'heart'

kamúy
/ka.múy/
CV — open
Rule 2: σ2 high
'spirit'

ináw
/i.náw/
CV — open
Rule 2: σ2 high
'prayer-wand'

ohaw
/o.háw/
CV — open
Rule 2: σ2 high
'soup'

áynu /áy.nu/ has two syllables; the first is CVC-closed and thus
high under Rule 1. Under the diphthong analysis, ay is a V+V nucleus
rather than a CVC syllable, and Rule 1 — which keys on the presence of a coda consonant —
has no basis to apply; an additional rule or stipulation would be required. The consonant
analysis lets the standard placement rules cover these forms without exception
nakagawa2024 29, 50 ;
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11). The same simplification holds in
compounds: kamúy in kamúynomi 'pray to a
spirit' retains its Rule 2 accent as the first element, the coda /y/ keeping the compound
syllable count transparent; see accent-in-compounds-and-affixation .

The diphthong analysis

Kindaichi & Chiri kindaichi1936 (cited via
shiraishi2022 §3 ) analysed post-vocalic y and
w as the second elements of falling diphthongs. Early romanization and
katakana transcription followed this view: kamuy was written カムイ
(four kana), treating the final element as a full vowel; aynu appeared
as アイノ, reflecting the approximate quality of the rounded unstressed second element
nakagawa2024 26, 29 . The orthographic reform that Chiri Yukie
(知里幸恵, c. 1923) introduced — kamuy , ohaw ,
kewtum for カムイ, オハウ, ケウトゥム — was motivated by exactly the
morphological and prosodic evidence set out in §2, not by any change in phonetic realization
nakagawa2024 29 .

Phonetically the diphthong analysis is accurate. The sequences are realized as falling
diphthongs — [ai̯], [au̯], [ei̯], [eu̯], [oi̯], [ui̯] — in which the second element is
non-syllabic aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11). Nothing in the acoustic
signal distinguishes a coda consonant from a non-syllabic vowel in this position: the two
analyses predict identical phonetic outputs. The diphthong analysis is a coherent phonetic
description; its weakness is at the morpho-phonological level, where it forces the statement
that a "non-syllabic vowel" triggers the consonant-class allomorphs of both the nominalizer
and the plural suffix.

Assessment

The consonant analysis is adopted by Nakagawa nakagawa2024 29 , Shiraishi
shiraishi2022 §3 , and Satō sato2008 §2.2 , and is the
working assumption of this grammar ‹consensus›. The three diagnostics in §2 — nominalizer
allomorphy, plural-suffix allomorphy, and pitch-accent placement — all turn on the same
categorical distinction (consonant vs. vowel-final stem), and all place /y/ and /w/ in the
consonant class without exception. The diphthong analysis requires an auxiliary rule
reclassifying the non-syllabic vowels as consonant-class triggers in three separate
morpho-phonological environments; no evidence favours this stipulation over the simpler
consonant phonemicization ‹contested›.

The community formulation captures the symmetry: reading /y/ and /w/ as consonants makes the
consonant-like morphological behaviour fall out; reading them as vowels makes the phonetic
falling-diphthong character explicit; neither reading misrepresents the phonetics
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11). Post-vocalic
y and w are phonological coda consonants /y/
and /w/; their surface realization as the non-syllabic elements [i̯] and [u̯] of falling
diphthongs is their expected phonetic output in that position.

Onset glides

Word-initial and post-consonantal y [j] and
w [w] are onset consonants without analytical controversy:
yuk deer , yam potato , wakka water ,
wen bad . Their onset and coda realizations are phonetically identical —
onset [j] = coda [i̯], onset [w] = coda [u̯] — which is consistent with a single
phoneme appearing in both positions, parallel to /m n r/ in Hokkaido Ainu
nakagawa2024 27–29 ; shiraishi2022 §3 .

Marginal sequences: yi, wi, wu

Three onset–vowel sequences are absent from Hokkaido Ainu monomorphemic roots:
yi , wi , and wu .
Nakagawa states that no root has any of these as its initial syllable, and that syllables of
the shape yi-C , wi-C ,
wu-C do not exist in the language nakagawa2024 43–45 ;
the same point is made in Shiraishi shiraishi2022 §4.2 , with attribution to
Kindaichi 1931:13 and Tamura 1988b:14. A palatal glide before a high front vowel ([ji]) and a
labio-velar glide before a high back rounded vowel ([wu], [wi]) are articulatorily
near-redundant transitions, and similar restrictions are typologically common. The phonotactic
constraint is parallel to the absence of the onset ti from Ainu roots —
its place in the table is empty, not filled by a rare allophone
nakagawa2024 43–44 .

Boundary-derived occurrences

The sequences [ji] and [wu] arise at morpheme boundaries through resyllabification. When
a /y/-final coda immediately precedes an /i/-initial syllable across a morpheme or compound
boundary, the coda resyllabifies as the onset of the following syllable, producing surface
[ji]; the analogous process generates [wu] at a /w/–/u/ juncture.
Shiraishi shiraishi2022 §4.2 cites
yayirwakikor (< yay irwaki kor ) as a compound
containing surface [ji] at an internal boundary, and
awun mimtar (< aw un mimtar ) as containing
[wu]. These are boundary-derived occurrences, not root-internal.

The Tamura–Nakagawa dispute on yi/wi/wu phonemic status

Whether yi , wi , and
wu should be recognized as phonemic onset sequences — rare but not
impossible — is genuinely disputed ‹contested›. Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 43–45 treats them as categorically restricted to morpheme
boundaries and derives their surface occurrences entirely from resyllabification. The
position attributed to Tamura in community discussion holds that they are independent onsets
comparable in phonemic status to word-initial [j] in English year or [w] in
would , and that the rarity of roots with these onsets is a phonotactic gap rather
than a categorical exclusion aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28).

The dispute partly tracks the glottal-stop question. On Nakagawa's no-glottal-stop analysis,
vowel-initial syllables begin with a bare syllable boundary; a /y/-final coda abutting
an /i/-initial syllable produces boundary-derived [ji] by resyllabification. On the
glottal-stop analysis (Hattori, Tamura), a contrast between /y/-onset and /ʔ/-onset is
available at precisely those positions, which in principle licenses phonemic onset status for
[ji], [wi], and [wu] aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28);
nakagawa2024 31–33, 43–45 . The glottal-stop debate is developed in
syllable-template .

The minimal pair yayráyke 'commit suicide' (three syllables,
/yay.ray.ke/) vs. yaírayke 'give thanks' (four syllables,
/ya.i.ray.ke/) illustrates the practical stakes of syllabification at this juncture
nakagawa2024 42 : the two words differ in syllable count and in whether the
reflexive morpheme yay- is present, but neither read source provides
acoustic data bearing on whether the i in
yaírayke ever surfaces as [ji] in connected speech.

Glide phonemicity in Shizunai: a dialect-specific contrast

In the accent dialects — Saru, Chitose, and related varieties — the accentual contrast between
fossilized and transparent compounds renders the question of /w/ phonemicity at hiatus
redundant: pitch, not glide presence, carries the contrast. In Shizunai (east Hidaka), an
accentless dialect, this alternative is unavailable. Okuda okuda2025 reports a
minimal pair from a Shizunai speaker (Oda Steno) that is unique in the Hokkaido documentation:

form
IPA
gloss
structural character

suwat
[suwat]
'pothook' (炉かぎ)
fossilized compound; /w/ phonemic, contrast with [ʔ] below

su'at
[suʔat]
'pot bail' (鍋のつる)
transparent su 'pot' + at ; boundary [ʔ]

suwat [suwat] and su'at [suʔat] are
phonetically distinct. In an accent dialect this contrast would be carried by pitch — the
fossilized compound would have a fixed accent pattern differing from the transparent form; in
Shizunai, where pitch is not distinctive, the glide vs. glottal-stop difference bears the
entire lexical distinction. Parallel contrasts for /y/ at front-vowel hiatus in Shizunai —
/u'a/ ~ /uwa/, /i'o/ ~ /iyo/, /i'e/ ~ /iye/ — extend the argument beyond this single pair
okuda2025 . Okuda's conclusion: "one cannot say, without specifying the dialect,
that the glides y / w are non-phonemic"
okuda2025 ‹contested›.

The Shizunai data do not overturn the Nakagawa–Shiraishi analysis of Saru and Chitose, where
accent carries the contrast the glide would otherwise mark. They demonstrate that /y/ and /w/
phonemicity at hiatus is a dialect-dependent property: phonemic in accentless Shizunai,
non-phonemic in the accent dialects
okuda2025 ; nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 . For the classification of
Shizunai as accentless and its implications, see pitch-accent-placement-rule .

Interaction with person-affix accent

The coda-consonant status of /y/ and /w/ has one further prosodic consequence. When
ku= or e= prefixes to a stem beginning with /i/
or /u/, vowel-weakening converts the vowel to a glide, producing a CVC-closed first syllable:
ku= + ipe →
ku=ype /kúype/ (σ1 = /kuy/, closed, Rule 1: accent on σ1)
nakagawa2024 52–53 . The derived coda /y/ is treated by the accent system in
exactly the same way as root-internal coda /y/ in áynu . The full
treatment of vowel-weakening and person-prefix accent shifts is in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction and
accent-in-compounds-and-affixation .

title: Glossary of Grammatical Terms
summary: An alphabetical, validated glossary of the grammatical terminology used throughout the grammar, with trilingual equivalents and cross-references to the chapters that define each term.
part: Part XXIII — Reference Apparatus (Back Matter)
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Glossary of Grammatical Terms
Part: Part XXIII — Reference Apparatus (Back Matter)
Summary: An alphabetical, validated glossary of the grammatical terminology used throughout the grammar, with trilingual equivalents and cross-references to the chapters that define each term.

Scope and conventions

This glossary defines the grammatical and linguistic terms used throughout the grammar.
Entries are alphabetized by the English head-term. Each entry gives a concise definition
oriented to Hokkaido Ainu, notes where the analysis is unsettled ‹contested›, and
cross-references the chapter or chapters where the term is most fully analyzed and
exemplified. Ainu forms follow the Latin transcription adopted in
latin-phonemic-transcription ; grammatical abbreviations used here follow
the inventory in glossing-abbreviations-and-citation .

Terms

affiliative form

The possessed or combining shape of a noun, as distinct from its citation shape (see
concept form ). The affiliative suffix — realized as
-he , -i , -e , or
other allomorphs depending on the stem — attaches to produce the possessed noun used
inside possessive phrases, nominalized constructions, and evidential frames:
ru-we 'the trace of —', hum-i
'the sound/feeling of —'. The same morphological category underlies the grammar's
glossing abbreviation poss
nakagawa2024 55–61 ; sato2008 .
See concept-form-affiliative-form and
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

agent (A)

In the S/A/O notation used throughout this grammar, A designates the subject
of a transitive verb — the argument that in a canonical action event controls the
action and triggers the A-position person-index morpheme. Following
foleyvanvalin1984 . See person-marking-architecture .

alignment (morphological)

The pattern by which person-marking morphology groups or distinguishes the three core
argument roles S, A, and O. Hokkaido Ainu displays mixed alignment: the first
singular is nominative–accusative (S=A≠O), the second and third persons are neutral
(S=A=O), and the first-plural-exclusive and fourth-person rows are tripartite (S≠A≠O)
‹contested›. bugaeva2012 472, 498 characterizes the overall
system as basically tripartite; dalcorso2025align argues for a
mixed nominative–neutral–inverse account of the Sakhalin paradigm. See
person-marking-architecture , alignment-split-intransitive ,
and alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

antipassive

A valency-reducing operation that detransitivizes a transitive verb, suppressing or
demoting the object; the verb then takes intransitive S-position morphology. In
Hokkaido Ainu the prefix i- something/someone (glossed
antip ) is the primary antipassive device. The prefix is
homophonous with but distinct from the fourth-person object
i= bugaeva2021antip ; nam2021 .
See antipassive-detransitive-i .

applicative

A valency-increasing morphological operation that promotes an oblique argument
(beneficiary, goal, instrument, comitative, or location) to direct object status,
making it indexable on the verb. Hokkaido Ainu has three productive applicative
prefixes: e- on/at/with (spatial/instrumental),
ko- to/for (goal/benefactive), and
o- in/among/from (spatial immersion/motion-through)
bugaeva2006 ; bugaeva2010 ; peterson2007 .
See applicative-system-overview , applicative-e ,
applicative-ko , and applicative-o .

aspect

Grammatical encoding of the internal temporal structure of an event — whether it is
presented as a bounded whole (perfective) or as an ongoing process (imperfective).
Hokkaido Ainu lacks an obligatory tense category (see tenselessness )
but marks aspect periphrastically through auxiliary constructions: the anterior/perfective
auxiliary a , existential-frame aspectuals
( kor an , wa an ), and the mirative
auxiliaries aan / anan
comrie1976 ; takahashi2022 .
See tam-overview and phasal-iterative-habitual-aspect .

bound noun / obligatorily possessed noun

A noun that cannot appear in its bare citation form without a possessor. Body-part,
kinship, part-whole, and relational nouns form this class; they require a
personal-prefix possessor or a nominal possessor in the affiliative form
nakagawa2024 ; sato2008 .
See nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns .

causative

A valency-increasing operation that adds a causer argument to the structure of a base
predicate. Hokkaido Ainu causative morphology centers on the suffix
-re (with dialectal allomorphs including
-e and -te ) on intransitive bases,
with additional forms in Horobetsu
nakagawa2024 ; sato2008 .
See causative-morphological-re-e-te .

clitic

A morpheme that is phonologically dependent on a host word but syntactically freer
than a canonical affix; written in this grammar with the boundary symbol
= . The personal-index morphemes of Hokkaido Ainu range from
fuller prefixes (e.g. ku= , en= ) to
more clitic-like items (e.g. =an ,
=as ); their precise morphological status is disputed
‹contested› bugaeva2012 472–473 ; nakagawa2024 49, 183 ;
sakaguchi2024 ix, 77–85 .
See personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

clusivity

The grammatical distinction within first-person plural between inclusive
reference (speaker plus addressee) and exclusive reference (speaker and
others, excluding the addressee). Hokkaido Ainu has a dedicated exclusive
first-plural series ( ci= / =as /
un= ), while inclusive 'we' falls within the fourth-person
forms nakagawa2024 163–164 ; bugaeva2012 472, 475 .
See first-person-plural-and-clusivity and
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

concept form

The citation or free shape of a noun, as distinct from its affiliative (possessed)
shape (see affiliative form ). Some stems show different shapes in
these two environments: the evidential formal nouns appear in their concept form
( ru , sir ,
haw , hum ) in certain Tokachi
constructions, and in their affiliative form
( ru-we , sir-i , etc.) in the standard
evidential frame nakagawa2024 55–62 ; takahashi2013 .
See concept-form-affiliative-form .

copula

A predicating element that links a subject to a non-verbal predicate (nominal,
stative, or spatial). The Hokkaido Ainu copula ne be, be equal to
predicates nominal and property complements; it also hosts the direct evidential
constructions ( ruwe ne , siri ne , etc.)
and takes intransitive S-position person marking
nakagawa2024 224–232 ; sato2008 .
See copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

desiderative

A modal category expressing the subject's desire or wish to perform or experience an
action. The Hokkaido Ainu desiderative is formed with the auxiliary
rusuy want to , which follows a nominalized or subordinated form of
the predicate nakagawa2024 .
See desiderative-rusuy .

ditransitive

A verb requiring three arguments: an agent-like subject, a recipient-like primary
object, and a theme-like secondary object. Hokkaido Ainu caps person indexes at two
per verb; with three-place predicates such as kore give , the object
index targets the human recipient while the theme surfaces as an unindexed noun phrase
sato2023b ; nakagawa2024 363–365 .
See valency-voice-overview and object-indexing-monotransitive .

evidential / evidentiality

A grammatical category encoding the speaker's source of information for the
proposition expressed. Hokkaido Ainu marks evidentiality analytically: a finite clause
is nominalized by a bound formal noun derived from a sensory noun, and the
result is predicated with the copula or an existential verb. Four formal nouns build
the core paradigm — ruwe (inferential/direct knowledge),
siri (situational/visual), hawe
(reportative), humi (sensory) — realizing the C1 type in the
typology of aikhenvald2004 63–65
dalcorso2018 ; nakagawa2024 258, 390–396 .
See evidential-schema-overview .

existential predicate

A predicate asserting the existence or presence of an entity or state of affairs.
Hokkaido Ainu uses an exist, be (singular) and
oka exist (plural) as the primary existential predicates; both also
serve as host predicates in indirect evidential frames
( siri an , hawe as ) and in continuative
aspectual periphrases nakagawa2024 ; sato2008 .
See existential-locational-an-oka .

formal noun / defective noun

A member of the closed set of bound nouns that function as clause-nominalizing heads
in evidential, complementation, and relative-clause constructions:
ruwe , siri ,
hawe , humi ,
hi , pe / p , and
kur . They require an adnominal modifier (the nominalized
clause) and cannot take independent demonstratives or adjectival modifiers of their
own, distinguishing them from ordinary lexical nouns
nakagawa2024 385–396 ; sato2008 131–137 .
See formal-defective-nouns .

fourth person (4)

The form-set — a= / an= (A),
=an (S), i= (O) — that covers the
referential ground outside persons 1–3. Its range includes inclusive first plural,
indefinite/agent-defocusing, honorific second-person address, narrative first person,
and logophoric reference inside quotations. Terminology is unsettled: Nakagawa labels
the set 'fourth person', Tamura divides it into eleven separate persons, Bugaeva
analyses the quotation use as logophoric, and Satō treats the forms as polysemous
‹contested› nakagawa2024 164, 174–179 ;
tamura1972 18–20 ; bugaeva2008 40–44 ;
sato2008 203–208 .
See indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and
fourth-person-honorific .

grammaticalization

The diachronic process by which a lexical or less-grammatical item develops into a
more grammatical one, typically with phonological reduction, increasing obligatoriness,
and narrowed distribution. The Hokkaido Ainu evidential system illustrates partial
grammaticalization: the sensory nouns ru ,
sir , haw ,
hum have moved toward clause-nominalizers and evidential heads
while retaining traces of their lexical origin, with the degree of grammaticalization
varying across Hokkaido dialects hoppertraugott2003 ;
dalcorso2018 24–25 ; takahashi2018 .
See evidential-schema-overview and
internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways .

head-marking

A typological property in which grammatical relations between a head and its
dependents are indexed on the head rather than (or in addition to) on the dependents.
Hokkaido Ainu is a canonical head-marking language: the verb bears person indexes for
its subject and object, while nominal arguments carry no case suffixes
nichols1986 ; sato2025a 361 .
See person-marking-architecture .

inferential (evidential)

An evidential value signaling that the speaker's information comes from reasoning,
trace evidence, or knowledge already processed and assimilated, rather than from
direct perception or hearsay. The Hokkaido Ainu formal noun
ruwe (from ru track, path ) heads the
inferential/direct-knowledge evidential ruwe ne , the most
frequent and most grammaticalized member of the four-term system
dalcorso2018 305, 327 ; nakagawa2024 392 .
See ruwe-ne-inferential and evidential-schema-overview .

logophoric reference

A form of pronominal reference that tracks the self-referent of a quoted or embedded
speaker within reported speech, distinct from both ordinary third person and the
matrix-clause first person. In Hokkaido Ainu the fourth-person prefix
a= / an= and object
i= serve logophoric reference inside quoted speech,
identifying the embedded speaker/object independently of the matrix participant set
bugaeva2008 ; nikitina2021 .
See logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse and
fourth-person-honorific .

mirativity

A grammatical or pragmatic category encoding the speaker's surprise or the
counter-expectational character of the event asserted. In Hokkaido Ainu, mirative
meaning is contributed chiefly by the perfectve auxiliaries
aan and anan ; the evidential
ruwe ne may co-occur with these forms but does not itself
lexicalize surprise ‹contested› dalcorso2018 336 ;
hirosawa2026 90 .
See mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

nonconfigurationality / pronominal-argument hypothesis

Two related analytical positions on languages with relatively free constituent order
and systematic argument omission. Nonconfigurationality holds that such
languages lack the structural positions that configure subjects and objects in
languages like English. The pronominal-argument hypothesis proposes that the
obligatory person indexes on the verb are the true syntactic arguments, with overt
nominal phrases functioning as discourse-level adjuncts rather than core arguments.
Whether either analysis applies to Hokkaido Ainu is debated ‹contested›.
See nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument and
constituent-order-head-final .

nominalization

The morphological or syntactic conversion of a verb or predication into a nominal
constituent. Hokkaido Ainu nominalizes extensively: the suffixes
-p / -pe (thing/patient nominalizer) and
-i / -hi (fact/manner nominalizer) form
derived nouns; formal-noun heads ( ruwe ,
hi , pe ) nominalize entire clauses in
evidential, complementation, and relative-clause constructions
nakagawa2024 383–396 ; sato2008 119–140 .
See nominalization-p-pe and
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

noun incorporation

A morphological process in which a noun — typically representing an object, oblique,
or subject participant — is compounded into the verb stem, forming a single
polymorphemic verbal word. Hokkaido Ainu is one of the typologically well-documented
cases, with object, oblique, and subject incorporation all attested; incorporated
nouns interact with applicative morphology and relativization
mithun1984 ; shibatani1988 ; satoni2022 .
See noun-incorporation-object ,
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding , and
noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate .

number (verbal and nominal)

The grammatical category encoding the count of individuals in a referent set. Hokkaido
Ainu verb number is marked through suppletive stem alternations
(e.g. ek / arki 'come') and through the
suffix -pa . Nominal number is transnumeral : see the
entry for transnumerality
corbett2000 ; nakagawa2024 141–159 .
See suppletive-verbal-number and
nominal-number-utar-transnumerality .

object (O)

In the S/A/O notation, O designates the patient-like argument of a transitive
verb — the argument indexed by the object-position morphemes
(e.g. en= , i= ,
un= ). Following foleyvanvalin1984 .
See person-marking-architecture and
object-indexing-monotransitive .

oblique

An argument or adjunct that is not indexed on the verb as a core subject or object —
typically a locative, source, goal, instrument, or comitative participant expressed
by a postpositional phrase. The applicative prefixes promote obliques to indexable
object status (see applicative ). See
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events and
applicative-system-overview .

perfective

An aspect value that presents an event as a completed, bounded whole, typically
locating it in the speaker's anterior domain. The Hokkaido Ainu anterior/perfective
auxiliary a attaches after the verb (and after evidential
frames) and contributes both boundedness and anteriority; it is the source of
mirative readings in certain constructions
nakagawa2024 289–295 ; takahashi2022 .
See perfective-anterior-a and tam-overview .

person (grammatical)

A deictic category encoding the role of the referent in the speech act: first
(speaker), second (addressee), third (other), and fourth (the residual set covering
inclusive, indefinite, honorific, and logophoric). Hokkaido Ainu formally distinguishes
four persons, each with singular and plural, through proclitic and enclitic indexes on
the verb nakagawa2024 163–179 ; tamura1972 .
See person-marking-architecture .

pitch accent

A prosodic system in which a single high-pitch position (or a H→L fall) within the
prosodic word is linguistically distinctive, as opposed to a full lexical-tone system
(where every syllable carries a tonal specification) or a simple stress system.
Hokkaido Ainu has a pitch-accent system with largely predictable high-pitch placement
governed by syllable weight and position; the language also divides into accented and
accentless dialect classes shibatani1990 52–58 ;
nakagawa2024 33–51 . See pitch-accent-placement-rule and
mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .

polysynthesis

A typological property of languages in which words — particularly verbs — routinely
incorporate multiple participant-tracking and relational morphemes, producing complex
words that translate as full clauses in analytic languages. The extent to which
Hokkaido Ainu counts as polysynthetic in the technical sense, versus merely
morphologically complex, is debated in the noun-incorporation literature ‹contested›
shibatani1988 ; mithun1984 .
See noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate .

portmanteau

A single morpheme that simultaneously realizes two or more grammatical values that
would otherwise require separate morphemes. In Hokkaido Ainu,
eci= is the portmanteau for first-person A acting on a
second-person O (covering 1SG→2SG, 1SG→2PL, 1PL.EXCL→2SG, and 1PL.EXCL→2PL),
replacing the separate first- and second-person indexes that other combinations use
tamura1972 27–28 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 .
See eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

possession / possessive

The grammatical encoding of a part-whole, kinship, or owner–property relation between
two nominals. Hokkaido Ainu marks possession on the possessed noun via the affiliative
suffix and/or a coreferential personal-prefix possessor; certain nouns are
obligatorily possessed (see bound noun )
nakagawa2024 55–77 ; sato2008 .
See affiliative-suffix-morphophonology and
adnominal-possession-double-marking .

reciprocal

An argument-structure operation marking mutual action: each member of a plural set
simultaneously acts on and is acted upon by the others. Hokkaido Ainu uses the prefix
u- reciprocal prefix for this function
nakagawa2024 204–209 ; sato2008 .
See reciprocal-u .

reflexive

An argument-structure operation in which the subject and object of a transitive verb
are co-referential — the agent acts on itself or on something within its own domain.
Hokkaido Ainu employs two reflexive prefixes: yay- reflexive prefix
(general action reflexive) and si- reflexive/possessive prefix
(possessive/inner-domain reflexive), with partly overlapping but distinct distributions
sato2007 ; kobayashi2010 .
See reflexive-yay and reflexive-possessive-si .

relative clause

A nominally modifying clause that restricts or characterizes the head noun. Hokkaido
Ainu uses a prenominal gap strategy: the relative clause precedes the head noun and
contains a positional gap rather than a resumptive pronoun; the verb retains its full
person marking inside the clause. The strategy covers subjects and objects with equal
ease, placing Ainu high on the Keenan–Comrie accessibility hierarchy
keenancomrie1977 ; nakagawa2024 370–383 .
See prenominal-gap-relative-clauses and
headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses .

reportative (evidential)

An evidential value signaling that the speaker's information derives from verbal
report or hearsay rather than personal observation or inference. The Hokkaido Ainu
reportative formal noun is hawe (from
haw voice ); the construction hawe ne predicates
the clause content as something the speaker has heard reported
dalcorso2018 ; nakagawa2024 393–394 .
See hawe-ne-reportative .

S, A, O (argument roles)

The three core argument roles tracked by morphological alignment systems in this
grammar. S is the sole argument of an intransitive verb; A is the
agent-like argument of a transitive verb; O is the patient-like argument of a
transitive verb. The notation follows foleyvanvalin1984 and the
Leipzig Glossing Rules. See person-marking-architecture .

sensory evidential

An evidential encoding information received through non-visual sensation: sound, touch,
smell, or an internal bodily feeling. The Hokkaido Ainu sensory formal noun is
humi (from hum sound, feeling ); the
construction humi ne marks non-visual direct evidentiality
dalcorso2018 ; nakagawa2024 394 .
See humi-ne-sensory .

situational evidential

An evidential encoding information derived from direct visual observation of an
accessible, ongoing situation. The Hokkaido Ainu situational formal noun is
siri (from sir appearance, land ); the
construction siri ne marks direct visual evidentiality.
In Tokachi the evidential is further restricted to stage-level (transient)
predications, with individual-level properties taking the inferential form instead
takahashi2013 ; dalcorso2018 .
See siri-ne-situational .

split intransitivity

A typological pattern in which intransitive subjects divide into two sub-classes —
agentive ( S A ) and patientive ( S O ) — each
aligned differently with the transitive argument roles. In Hokkaido Ainu the
person-agreement paradigm does not itself split the intransitive subject by agentivity;
the relevant split surfaces in verb classes, noun incorporation, and valency
alternations nakagawa2024 126–127 ; stassen1997 .
See alignment-split-intransitive .

switch-reference

A clause-linking mechanism that marks whether the subject of a subordinate clause is
the same participant (SS) or a different participant (DS) from the subject of an
adjacent or matrix clause. In Hokkaido Ainu the clause-linkers
hine conjunctive/sequential particle and
akusu consequential/switch particle encode a related opposition;
whether this constitutes switch-reference in the strict typological sense is debated
‹contested› nakagawa2024 425–440 ; sato2008 247–256 .
See hine-akusu-switch-reference .

TAM (tense–aspect–mood)

The cluster of grammatical categories encoding temporal location (tense), internal
event structure (aspect), and speaker stance toward the event (mood/modality).
Hokkaido Ainu lacks an obligatory tense but marks aspect and modality periphrastically
through auxiliary and particle constructions
comrie1976 ; nakagawa2024 284–320 .
See tam-overview .

telicity

A lexical-semantic property distinguishing predicates that include a natural endpoint
(telic: an activity with a bounded goal, e.g. 'write a letter') from those that do
not (atelic: unbounded activities, e.g. 'run'). Telicity interacts with the
distribution of aspect auxiliaries and with some valency alternations in Hokkaido Ainu
comrie1976 ; takahashi2022 .
See tam-overview .

tenselessness

The absence of an obligatory grammatical tense category — no morpheme or construction
that compulsorily locates an event as past, present, or future relative to speech
time. Hokkaido Ainu expresses temporal reference through adverbs, aspect auxiliaries,
and context, rather than through a dedicated tense paradigm
nakagawa2024 ; sato2008 .
See tenselessness-and-time-reference .

transnumerality

A property of nominal number systems in which the unmarked noun form is unspecified
for the singular/plural distinction, able to refer to one or more individuals without
grammatical marking of that contrast. Hokkaido Ainu nouns are transnumeral; the only
dedicated nominal plural element is the associative
utar group, people, companions , which marks a social collective rather
than a pure plurality corbett2000 ;
nakagawa2024 78–82 .
See nominal-number-utar-transnumerality .

tripartite alignment

A morphological alignment pattern in which all three core argument roles — S, A, and O
— are marked by formally distinct, non-overlapping morphological exponents. Two rows of
the Hokkaido Ainu person paradigm display this pattern: the first-plural-exclusive
( ci= / =as /
un= ) and the fourth person
( a= / =an /
i= ) bugaeva2012 472 ;
nakagawa2024 163–174 .
See person-marking-architecture and
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

valency

The number and kind of arguments a predicate requires or licenses. Intransitive
predicates have valency 1 (one S argument), transitives have valency 2 (A + O), and
ditransitives have valency 3. Hokkaido Ainu has a rich set of valency-changing
operations: causatives and applicatives increase valency; antipassives and
middle/anticausatives decrease it; noun incorporation can further reduce the
effective valency of the verbal complex bugaevakobayashi2022 ;
nakagawa2024 196–228 .
See valency-voice-overview .

verb template

The fixed hierarchical ordering of morphological positions within the Hokkaido Ainu
verb word. From outermost to innermost: personal-index prefixes (A/O) → applicative,
causative, antipassive, reflexive, and reciprocal prefixes → the stem → stem-forming
plural suffix ( -pa ) → personal-index suffixes
( =an , =as for intransitive S)
nakagawa2024 183, 196–199 ; tamura1972 35 .
See verb-word-template and
personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

title: Interlinear Glossing, Abbreviations, and Citation Conventions
summary: How to read the grammar's examples — the Leipzig-based interlinear format, morpheme segmentation, the gloss and abbreviation inventory, and the source-attribution sigla.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Interlinear Glossing, Abbreviations, and Citation Conventions
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: How to read the grammar's examples — the Leipzig-based interlinear format, morpheme segmentation, the gloss and abbreviation inventory, and the source-attribution sigla.

The interlinear format

Examples of Ainu data follow the three-line interlinear format of the Leipzig Glossing Rules
comrie2015 . The morphemic line segments each Ainu word at morpheme
and person-marker boundaries; the gloss line aligns one label with each morpheme; the
free translation gives an idiomatic English rendering. A fourth line, the literal
translation , is supplied where the morpheme-by-morpheme reading illuminates an opaque or
partially grammaticalized form.

Two further lines may precede the morphemic line. A surface line gives the unsegmented
written form when phonological alternation produces a surface string that differs from the
underlying segmentation — for example, when a stem-initial vowel triggers reduction of a
preceding person prefix. A source-script line gives the original katakana (or other)
orthography when an example is transcribed from a katakana source, and is accompanied by the
romanized segmentation immediately below it. The Latin phonemic transcription is described in
latin-phonemic-transcription and the katakana conventions in
katakana-and-small-kana-codas .

The morphemic and gloss lines must contain the same number of space-delimited tokens; each token
groups one Ainu word together with its prefixes and suffixes, and the corresponding gloss token
mirrors that internal structure using boundary symbols described below. A mismatch in token count
causes a build error.

ku=nu ru-we ne | 1SG.A=hear track-POSS COP | I heard it. | Illustrates the format: the morphemic line segments at person-marker (=) and affix (-) boundaries; the gloss line aligns one label per morpheme. The possessed evidential formal noun ruwe (ru-we, 'the trace of —') is discussed in the evidential chapters.

Morpheme boundary conventions

Three boundary symbols appear within tokens in the morphemic and gloss lines:

symbol
use
morphemic
gloss

-
affix boundary (derivational and nominal)
ru-we
track- poss

=
person-marker boundary (orthographic convention, all personal indexes)
ku=nu
1sg.a =hear

.
portmanteau: two grammatical values fused in one gloss atom
a=
4.a =

The = symbol is used for all personal-index morphemes as an orthographic
convention of the Nakagawa lineage, not as Leipzig clitic notation — Nakagawa states
explicitly that his = is unrelated to the general-linguistic affix/clitic
display convention, and adopted it to keep inflectional person marking apart from
derivation and the dictionary headword recoverable nakagawa2024 58–60 .
Satō and Ijäs notate the same morphemes with hyphens. Morphologically the set is mixed:
at least two members — the intransitive-subject markers =an and
=as — show degrees of independence that set them apart from typical
inflectional affixes nakagawa2024 49, 183 ;
bugaeva2012 472–473 , while the prefixal markers pattern with ordinary
affixes. The choice of = throughout is thus deliberately neutral on which
markers are true clitics and which are affixes; that classification is examined in
personal-affix-template-and-ordering boundary-status ‹contested›.

The dot records a portmanteau — two grammatical values encoded in a single exponence — without
claiming that they form a morphologically complex unit. In the fourth-person paradigm the prefix
a= encodes the fourth person acting as the agent of a transitive verb;
it is glossed 4.a , where 4 marks person and
a marks the person-marking-architecture S/A/O role .
Each component of every portmanteau is listed independently in the abbreviation set, so that
4.a , 1sg.a , and 2pl.o
are all resolvable atom by atom.

Grammatical glosses and the abbreviation set

Every morpheme in the gloss line receives either a lexical gloss in lower case —
an ordinary English word for a stem or root (e.g. hear , house ,
track ) — or a grammatical gloss in
small capitals . Each small-capitals item is an abbreviation atom
drawn from the fixed set tabulated in abbreviations-glossing-symbols ; no ad-hoc
or unlisted atom is permitted. When a grammatical category appearing in the data lacks a
registered atom, the atom is added to that table before the example enters any chapter. The
set is validated at build time: an unregistered capital-letter sequence in a gloss line causes
a build error.

The role labels s , a , and
o follow the usage of bugaeva2012 471 :
s is the single argument of an intransitive verb,
a is the agent-like argument of a transitive verb, and
o is the patient-like argument. Person is identified by the numerals
1 , 2 , 3 , and
4 in the sense of nakagawa2024 163 : the numerals
are form-class labels, not an inherent claim about the semantic content of each form.
The fourth-person set — whose referential range covers the indefinite agent-defocusing use,
the narrative first person, the addressee-inclusive 'we', the honorific second person, and
the logophoric subject of reported speech — is the subject of
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and fourth-person-honorific .

Evidential glosses follow the abbreviation set directly: evid marks
a generic evidential category, infr the inferential, and
rep the reportative. The formal nouns that build the
nominalization-plus-copula evidential constructions ( ruwe ,
siri , hawe , humi ) are
glossed by their compositional morpheme labels ( poss , cop )
rather than by a holistic evidential tag, since the grammaticalization of those forms is partial
and the morpheme-level analysis remains transparent dalcorso2018 24–25 ; this
choice is consistent with the convention of nakagawa2024 258 . The evidential
system is described in evidential-schema-overview .

Dialect tags and example attribution

Every attested example carries a dialect tag drawn from the fixed set below. Finer locality —
village, speaker, or archive signum — is recorded in a separate place field and does not
modify the tag itself. The tag hk is used when the source does not
specify a variety more precisely. Sakhalin ( sa ) and Kuril
( ku ) examples appear exclusively as labelled contrasts; Hokkaido
is the variety under description throughout. The corpus and the dialect sample are described
in oral-literature-and-spoken-corpora and
dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

tag
variety

HK Hokkaido (variety not further specified)

SAR Saru

CHI Chitose

ISH Ishikari

TOK Tokachi

HOR Horobetsu

SHI Shizunai

ASA Asahikawa

YAK Yakumo

SA Sakhalin (labelled contrast)

KU Kuril (labelled contrast)

Each attested example cites the underlying source — the narrator, edition, or author whose
data it contains — rather than any aggregating database. A corpus example is attributed to the
edition ( nakagawa2024 , dalcorso2018 , tamura1996 ) or to the
narrator and archive slot directly. Works consulted only at second hand are flagged as
reported in the bibliography and render with a distinguishing badge. An example
constructed to illustrate a contrast or paradigm that is not directly quotable is marked
constructed example ; every example is either attributed or constructed, with no third
category.

Source citation and the bibliography

In-text citations use the author–year format, with a colon and page number when a specific
location matters: nakagawa2024 163 or bugaeva2012 471 .
All cited keys are registered in a central bibliography; a key that does not appear in the
registry causes a build error, so the citation apparatus is closed under the set of registered
works.

The key scheme is lastnameYEAR : all-lowercase ASCII, no diacritics, no spaces.
Japanese authors are keyed by romanised surname with macrons stripped
( 中川裕 → nakagawa2024 ;
佐藤知己 → sato2008 ). Multi-author works key on the first
author's surname. Collisions within the same author and year receive a lower-case suffix letter
in order of first citation ( sato2023a , sato2023b ); a topical suffix
is used instead when it aids orientation ( bugaeva2021poss ,
bugaeva2021antip ). The complete bibliography is gathered in
consolidated-references-bibliography .

Page references on examples follow the same colon-delimited syntax used in in-text citations,
and may include section numbers, example numbers, or volume references where pagination is
absent (e.g. nakagawa2024 §13.2 ). Multiple sources may be joined with a
semicolon within a single citation field.

Evidence-grade labels

Claims that go beyond the well-established consensus, or that represent genuinely contested
positions in the literature, are tagged with a closed set of evidence-grade labels. The label
is placed at the end of the sentence or clause it scopes over, in guillemets:
‹corpus-confirmed› . Unmarked prose is treated as consensus-level; only claims that
depart from that default require a label.

grade
meaning

consensus
all major sources agree; well-attested and uncontroversial

contested
sources genuinely disagree; the positions are presented without settling the question

corpus-confirmed
a claim verified against the corpus; the data bear it out

corpus-suggested
the corpus points toward the claim but the evidence is thin or indirect

speculative
plausible but unverified, e.g. a diachronic hypothesis or a gap-filling inference

original-needs-review
new analysis advanced in this grammar; flagged for editorial review

The grade original-needs-review is restricted to the five domains in which new
analysis is permitted: alignment, applicatives, noun incorporation, evidentiality, and
clause linkage. The corpus-based grades presuppose the corpus described in
dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

title: The Laryngeals: Glottal Stop, /h/, and the Final-h Question
summary: The laryngeal consonants: the glottal stop as obligatory-onset filler versus phoneme, onset and coda /h/ with coda-h neutralization, and the word-final -h controversy and its Sakhalin vowel-length reflex.
part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Laryngeals: Glottal Stop, /h/, and the Final-h Question
Part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
Summary: The laryngeal consonants: the glottal stop as obligatory-onset filler versus phoneme, onset and coda /h/ with coda-h neutralization, and the word-final -h controversy and its Sakhalin vowel-length reflex.

Laryngeal consonants

Hokkaido Ainu has one laryngeal phoneme, /h/, and a phonetically audible glottal closure [ʔ]
whose phonological status divides analysts. The two laryngeal elements have distinct
distributional profiles: /h/ occupies the onset of its syllable with vowel-conditioned
allophony; [ʔ] marks the onset of vowel-initial syllables as a phonetically automatic boundary
cue in the majority analysis, though it functions as a lexically contrastive phoneme in the
accentless Shizunai dialect
nakagawa2024 31–33 ; shiraishi2022 §4.3 ;
okuda2025 .

segment
phonological status
syllable position
note

/h/
phoneme (consensus)
onset only

three allophones conditioned by the following vowel; absent as a coda in HA
nakagawa2024 27

[ʔ]

allophone / boundary marker in accent dialects (dominant analysis); phoneme /ʔ/ in
Shizunai okuda2025

vowel onsets at word-initial and juncture positions

phonemic contrast with [w] demonstrated for Shizunai; no monomorphemic minimal pair in
accent-dialect varieties nakagawa2024 31–33

The glottal stop [ʔ]

Phonetic distribution

A glottal onset [ʔ] is phonetically present before every vowel-initial syllable in Hokkaido
Ainu — word-initially and at internal morpheme and word junctures.
nakagawa2024 31 traces the earliest systematic observation to Kindaichi
(1928), a Tōhoku-Japanese speaker who characterized Ainu as inserting a glottal closure
before each vowel-initial syllable. The stop's auditory salience varies with phonetic
context. Tamura (1970: 586–587, cited via shiraishi2022 §4.3 ) distinguishes
a perceptible stop — arising when the following vowel carries lexical accent and/or occurs
in a closed syllable — from a barely perceptible transition that amounts to no more than the
syllable-division point. Attested forms in which the stop is clearly audible include
[aˈʔep] 'food', [aˈʔoypep] 'tableware', [oˈʔatteke], [eˈʔunpipka], and [teˈʔeta]
'long ago' (Tamura 1970: 586, cited via shiraishi2022 §4.3 ).

phonetic environment
realization
source

vowel-initial syllable; following vowel accented and/or in a closed syllable
[ʔ] clearly audible
Tamura 1970: 586, cited via shiraishi2022 §4.3

vowel-initial syllable; following vowel unaccented and/or in an open syllable
[ʔ] minimal or absent; gradual onset tension
Tamura 1970: 586–587, cited via shiraishi2022 §4.3

after a coda consonant that resyllabifies onto the following vowel
[∅]; coda consonant fills the onset slot

nakagawa2024 43 ; see syllable-template

The HA syllable canon (C)V(C) permits at most one onset consonant per syllable
(see syllable-template ). When a preceding morpheme ends in a consonant, that
consonant resyllabifies as the onset of the following vowel-initial syllable:
cip-o → [ci.po] 'board a boat',
mat-ak → [ma.tak] 'younger sister'
nakagawa2024 43 . No [ʔ] appears in those resyllabified forms, because the
onset slot is already filled. The glottal closure thus marks syllables whose onset position
remains empty — a phonetically automatic default rather than a lexically specified segment.

Phonemic status

Whether [ʔ] constitutes an underlying phoneme /ʔ/ in Hokkaido Ainu is contested
shiraishi2022 §4.3 . Two positions have been held since the mid-twentieth
century:

analysis
proponents
principal arguments

phonemic /ʔ/
Hattori (1961); Tamura (early work)

(i) Positing /ʔ/ reduces the syllable canon to only CV and CVC — an economy gain.
(ii) The distribution of [w] versus [ʔ] at some junctures resists full derivation from
higher-level rules alone nakagawa2024 31 .

allophonic [ʔ], non-phonemic

Chiri (1942: 461) chiri1942 ; Refsing (1986: 68)
refsing1986 ; Nakagawa (2024) nakagawa2024

(i) No minimal pair exists in monomorphemic words: word-initial [e] and [ʔe] never
contrast. (ii) Positing /ʔ/ forces widespread deletion rules wherever resyllabification
applies. (iii) In accent dialects, the accent on
parúnpe (accent on -un- , not σ1) is incompatible with
an underlying /ʔ/ filling the σ1 onset, which would make σ1 closed and predict accent
there by 原則1 (see pitch-accent-placement-rule )
nakagawa2024 32–33, 44 .

The accent argument applies only in pitch-accent varieties. In the accentless Shizunai
dialect it does not hold, and phonemic /ʔ/ has been demonstrated there (§ below). The
non-phonemic analysis stands for the accent-dialect majority and is adopted here for those
varieties ‹contested›.

Juncture gradation and prosodic integration

The phonetic realization of [ʔ] at morpheme and word boundaries varies with the prosodic
integration of the flanking elements. Three degrees of boundary tightness are audible in a
single compound across careful and casual speech. In
itakorapiru=an 'be telling the story in full', the most separated
pronunciation is [itak|orapiru…] with a full juncture stop; an intermediate version shows
[itak⁀orapiru…] with partial resyllabification alongside an audible [ʔ]; and the most
integrated realization is [ita.ko.rapiru…] with full resyllabification and no glottal.
Acoustic measurement confirms that the presence or absence of a voiceless interval between
the coda /k/ and the following /o/ distinguishes the [−C.ʔV] and [+C.V] realizations
‹corpus-suggested›
( aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli, 2024-12-21).

The suffix =an (fourth-person intransitive subject) provides a clear
test after a stop-final stem. After sap depart, leave the suffix
=an does not trigger resyllabification of the coda /p/ onto the
following vowel; a juncture [ʔ] appears instead, yielding [sapʔan]
‹corpus-suggested›
( aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli, 2023-12-27).

sap=an | depart=4.S | (we/they) depart | Illustrates the C + =an juncture. The suffix =an does not trigger resyllabification of the preceding stop; juncture [ʔ] appears before the vowel, yielding [sapʔan] rather than *[sa.pan]. Compare the tighter-bound ku=, e=, ci= prefixes where resyllabification applies.

The prefixes ku=, e=, ci= are prosodically integrated with their
stem, forming a single prosodic word and triggering vowel-deletion and accent-shift processes
(see personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction and
pitch-accent-placement-rule ). The prefix a= , which does
not shift lexical accent, is accordingly less tightly bound
nakagawa2024 53 .
Acoustic observation of a=kor itak 'there is speech' yields
[a.ko.rəʔi.tak]: the echo vowel [ə] follows coda /r/ in
kor , and [ʔ] appears at the compound boundary before
itak , while the a=–kor boundary shows no juncture stop
‹corpus-suggested›
( aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli, 2024-03-18).

a=kor itak | 4.A=have speech | (someone) speaks; there is speech | Pronounced [a.ko.rə.ʔi.tak]: the echo vowel [ə] follows coda /r/ in kor, and [ʔ] marks the compound boundary before itak. The person-prefix boundary a=–kor shows no juncture stop. The a= prefix does not trigger the accent shift that ku= and e= trigger, consistent with weaker prosodic integration.

These observations together point to a gradient of boundary tightness within the
person-marking system: the productive object/agent prefix class
( ku=, e=, ci= ) is the most integrated; the
a= boundary is tighter than a compound word boundary but looser than
the productive-prefix class; and the =an suffix boundary is the
least integrated of the person-marking elements examined here. Glide insertion at hiatus
and the broader juncture phonology are treated in
glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution .

The Shizunai minimal pair

The accentless Shizunai dialect (静内; classified as 無アクセント by
nakagawa2024 50 ; see hokkaido-phonological-microvariation )
presents an environment in which [ʔ] and [w] carry lexical contrast. Okuda (2025), drawing
on fieldwork by 志賀雪湖, reports a minimal pair for juncture types that in accent varieties
are kept distinct by pitch placement rather than by the laryngeal itself
okuda2025 :

suwat | pothook | pothook (hook above the hearth for suspending pots) | Pronounced [suwat]. An opaque compound; the /w/ belongs to the fossilized root and does not derive from the compositional glide-epenthesis rule. | okuda2025 | SHI

su at | pot cord | pot handle (the wire bail of a pot) | Pronounced [suʔat]. A transparent compound of su 'pot, kettle' and at 'rope, cord'. In Shizunai the juncture [ʔ] at the su–at boundary is in contrastive distribution with the /w/ of suwat above. | okuda2025 | SHI

Because Shizunai lacks distinctive pitch accent, the contrast [suwat] : [suʔat] cannot be
attributed to accent-placement differences as it would be in an accent dialect.
Okuda (2025) concludes that in Shizunai both the glide /w/ and the [ʔ] at transparent
compound boundaries are phonemic, and adds the explicit caution that this finding does not
generalize: "one cannot say, without specifying dialect, that the transitional glide y/w is
non-phonemic" okuda2025 .
Parallel contrasts are reported for /u ʔ a/ : /u w a/ and
/i ʔ o/ : /i y o/ in Shizunai okuda2025 ‹contested›.
The glide phonemicity question across Hokkaido dialects is examined in
glides-w-y .

Emphatic glottal insertion

A morphologically conditioned process inserts [ʔ] to break a repeated vowel in emphatic or
intensified word forms, shifting or heightening the form's meaning. The set of participating
roots is lexically restricted. Kindaichi (1931, cited via
shiraishi2022 §2.2 ) documents the pattern in Saru:

base form
emphatic form
gloss shift

wen [wen]
weʔen [weʔen]
'bad' → 'very bad'

to [to]
toʔo [toʔo]
'far away' → 'far away (emphatic)'

teta [teta]
teʔeta [teʔeta]
'a few days ago' → 'long ago'

The [ʔ] in emphatic forms carries semantic weight and is invariant under conditions that
suppress the boundary glottal elsewhere. This distinguishes it from the phonetically automatic
onset [ʔ] discussed above, which yields to resyllabification.

The phoneme /h/

Onset distribution and allophony

The phoneme /h/ occurs only in syllable-onset position in Hokkaido Ainu, categorically
excluded from the coda
nakagawa2024 27 ; sato2008 §2.3 .
Three allophones are conditioned by the following vowel
nakagawa2024 28 ; shiraishi2022 §3.1 :

environment
allophone
example

/h/ before /a/, /e/, /o/
[h]

hapo [hapo] 'mother';
humi [humi] 'sound, sensation'

/h/ before /i/
[ç]
hine [çine] 'and then' (conjunctive particle)

/h/ before /u/
[ɸ]
huci [ɸuci] 'grandmother, elderly woman'

The conditioning is audible but less categorical than in standard Japanese. Tamura (1998: 41,
cited via shiraishi2022 §3.1 ) reports that speakers born around 1900 showed
only weak vowel-coloring of /h/, with the [ɸ] before /u/ approaching a glottal quality in
