particular tokens. All three realizations are predictable allophones of a single phoneme /h/;
no minimal pair distinguishes them for the same following vowel
(see consonant-inventory ).

Coda /h/ and the final-h question

Coda /h/ is absent as a synchronic segment in Hokkaido Ainu: no native Hokkaido word
carries a laryngeal in coda position
nakagawa2024 27 ; sato2008 §2.3 .
In Sakhalin Ainu, by contrast, a coda breath-release /h/ (realized as a voiceless breath,
not [x]; Hattori 1964: 34, cited via nakagawa2024 27 ) is productive and
reflects historical HA coda /r/ among other sources
(see final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex ).

Within Hokkaido Ainu, the possessed form
siki-h-i 'his/her eyes' (from siki eye ) contains
an epenthetic /h/ inserted before the possessive suffix. This /h/ is a morphological
insertion restricted to the
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology affiliative formation and does not
represent an underlying coda in the root
(Kindaichi & Chiri 1936: 14, cited via shiraishi2022 n.8 ).
Whether certain citation forms of stems carry an underlying final /h/, and what diachronic
relationship that /h/ bears to the Sakhalin coda and to Sakhalin vowel length, is the
'final-h question' treated in
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex .

title: hawe ne — the Reportative / Hearsay Evidential
summary: hawe ne ('it is the voice/report that'), marking information acquired through speech or hearsay, distinct from but adjacent to the sekor quotative and reported-discourse system.
part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: hawe ne — the Reportative / Hearsay Evidential
Part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
Summary: hawe ne ('it is the voice/report that'), marking information acquired through speech or hearsay, distinct from but adjacent to the sekor quotative and reported-discourse system.

Place in the evidential paradigm

hawe ne is the reportative member of the Hokkaido Ainu evidential
paradigm. Like its three siblings it consists of a
formal-defective-nouns formal noun in the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form followed by the copula
ne be, copula : where ruwe ne signals a fact
recognized through knowledge or reasoning, siri ne a directly
visible situation, and humi ne a non-visual sensation,
hawe ne signals that the information was acquired verbally —
through hearing someone speak, receiving a report, or encountering hearsay. The
architecture common to all four is set out in evidential-schema-overview ; the
other three evidentials are treated in
ruwe-ne-inferential the inferential ruwe ne ,
siri-ne-situational the situational siri ne , and
humi-ne-sensory the sensory humi ne .

In Dal Corso's eight-form analysis, hawe ne is the direct
evidential of hearing (DIR.HRN), the lowest-reliability direct form in the paradigm; its
indirect counterpart is hawe as (IND.HRN), treated in
§ hawe as below dalcorso2018 304–306, 337 . In Dal
Corso's reference texts, hawe ne accounts for 298 of 2232
evidential tokens (13.35%), and hawe as for a further 94 (4.21%),
making the two hawe -forms together the second most frequent pair
after ruwe ne dalcorso2018 72 .

Morphology: hawe is haw-e, 'the voice/word of —'

The source noun is haw voice . Tamura's Saru dictionary defines
haw as '(人間・動物の)声、(バイオリンなどの)音' — the voice of a human or
animal and the sound of certain instruments; general inanimate sounds such as a drum are
expressed instead with the separate noun hum sound, feeling
tamura1996 s.v. haw, hawe . The ordinary possessed form is
hawehe 'the voice of —'. The evidential formal noun is
hawe = haw-e , the voice noun plus its
possessive suffix, but like the other formal nouns of the evidential paradigm it blocks
the additional possessive extension: the form with a further
-he is ungrammatical as the evidential
( *hawehe ) tamura1996 s.v. hawe ;
nakagawa2024 258 . Glossing it as voice-POSS tracks this
morphological origin.

The lexical noun haw voice remains productive alongside the formal noun.
The community notes the prohibitive construction iteki e=hawe aste
'do not raise your voice' — where e=hawe is the second-person
possessed form of haw and aste raise, cause to stand
is the lexical verb — as evidence that the person-marked lexical noun is still active,
distinct from the evidential formal noun which takes no person prefix
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-12-03) ‹contested›.
A related observation: sekor haw=an '(one) says thus' and
sekor isoytak '(one) narrates thus' are both attested as
quotative expressions, differing in whether a general act of speaking or more formal
narrating is meant aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord,
2025-06-03) ‹corpus-suggested›. In sekor haw=an the verb
haw=an is the lexical 'to speak / give voice', not the formal
noun; the two functions of the root coexist.

Chiri described the diachronic shift as running from 'there is a voice/rumor of X'
( 「云々の声がある」「云々の噂がある」 ) to the hearsay assertive
kindaichi1936 (cited via hirosawa2026 66 ), following the
same cline documented for all four evidentials: sensory noun > possessed formal noun
> clause-final evidential takahashi2018 111 .

Meaning: verbal source and reportative function

Nakagawa defines hawe ne as indicating that the speaker judges
from information received verbally ( 言葉で聞いた情報から判断していること )
nakagawa2024 392 . Three overlapping functions are documented in the
sources.

The core use is the direct report : the speaker heard a specific person say
something, and hawe ne encodes that hearing as the information
source. Tamura describes this as '誰かが言ったことに基づいて述べる; 他人の発言の紹介、自分の発言の
さらなる説明' — presenting what someone said, or elaborating on one's own earlier statement
tamura1996 s.v. hawe (cited via hirosawa2026 67 ).

The second function is hearsay : the information reaches the speaker through
general social knowledge or rumor rather than from a named interlocutor. The example in
Hirosawa (2026: 71, ex. 3), where a narrator attributes a plague to a divine figure's
anger on the basis of circulating report rather than personal reasoning, illustrates
this function; the example is drawn from Tamura 1988 audio materials (narrator Wateke,
Saru) hirosawa2026 71 .

Third, Nakagawa notes for Chitose that hawe ne may mark the
speaker's own statement, as in the framing expression
hawe ne yakun 'if that is the case / given those words'
nakagawa1995 (cited via hirosawa2026 68 ) ‹corpus-suggested›.
In this use, the form makes explicit the verbal nature of what was just said.

Dal Corso places hawe ne at the lowest-reliability position of
the four direct evidentials, reflecting the sense hierarchy
sight > touch/taste/smell > hearing. Source reliability in his
framework is a property of the acquisition channel, not a claim about epistemic certainty
— hawe ne specifies how the information was obtained without
committing the speaker to a degree of confidence in its truth
dalcorso2018 305–306, 327, 339 ‹consensus›.

Hirosawa (2026: 81) observes that near-identical sentences can alternate
hawe ne and ruwe ne — one version
presenting the proposition as heard speech, the other as assimilated knowledge — without
a sharp semantic break hirosawa2026 81 ; dalcorso2018 306, 339 .
The boundary between the two forms is gradient rather than categorical ‹contested›.

The sekor … hawe ne quotative frame

The most prominent environment for hawe ne in the Saru and
Chitose corpus is the quotative frame built with the complementizer
sekor (quotative) . A clause of quoted content is followed by
sekor and a speaking verb, with hawe ne
at the clause edge:

[quoted content] sekor [verb of saying] hawe ne

sekor marks the boundary of the quoted content; the speaking
verb specifies the act of utterance; hawe ne then asserts that
the speaker's information derives from hearing. The full analysis of
sekor and direct speech framing is in
quotative-complementation-sekor . The use of reported-speech frames in oral
literature is described further in logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

tap a=ye katu Piratur sekor a=ye haw-e ne | thus 4.A=say manner Biratori QUOT 4.A=say voice-POSS COP | The way one says it is 'Biratori' (so they call it). | hawe ne predicates the naming 'Biratori' as verbally transmitted knowledge — the speaker heard this name used, rather than independently assigning it. Compare the ruwe ne version at Hirosawa (2026: 81, ex. 24), where the same naming is presented as assimilated personal knowledge, illustrating the gradient boundary between the two forms. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 778; underlying source Kawamura 1985: 30, Saru

The community has proposed that Saru sekor is analytically
se + kor 'saying while holding', and that
ari / ani — the equivalent in other dialects — preserves an
older quotative form, with sekor a Saru-centred innovation
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09; 2024-12-06)
‹contested›. These etymological questions belong to
quotative-complementation-sekor ; the relevant point here is that the
sekor … hawe ne combination is the canonical reported-speech
construction in the Saru/Chitose corpus ‹corpus-confirmed›.

hawe as: the indirect reportative

The indirect counterpart of hawe ne is
hawe as (phonologically hawas ), formed by
replacing the copula ne with the posture verb
as stand , the same auxiliary used in humi as .
The table below summarizes the two hawe -forms along Dal Corso's
organizing parameters dalcorso2018 304–330 .

hawe ne
hawe as

Dal Corso type
DIR.HRN — direct hearing
IND.HRN — indirect hearing

post-formal-noun element
copula ne
posture verb as 'stand'

source access
close, personally heard
distant or unspecified

function
direct report (named or proximate speaker)
quotative (overt speaker) or hearsay (absent source)

hawe as has specialized so thoroughly as a reportative that it
is the only indirect form with a robust hearsay reading:
humi as is consistently inferential and never reportative, while
hawe as handles both the quotative use (overt speaker named) and
the hearsay use (source diffuse or absent)
dalcorso2018 328–330 ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Nispa-utar nisatta ekimne sekor haw-as pe ne kusu | rich.man-PL tomorrow go.to.mountains QUOT voice-stand NMLZ COP PURP | The men of standing would go to the mountains the next day (so it was rumored), and therefore … | hawe as in the hearsay function: no specific speaker is named. The embedded hawe as clause is nominalized with pe ne before kusu, relating the rumored event to what follows. hawe as scopes over the full reported content. | dalcorso2018:330 | CHI | Bugaeva collection (Ito Oda, Chitose 1998–2000); Dal Corso siglum BUG:377; Dal Corso ex. 272

As a lexical item, hawe as means 'a voice is heard; someone
speaks; a sound resounds' — the compositional reading of 'voice stands'
dalcorso2018 318 . The evidential is an extension of this lexical
meaning, and in some corpus occurrences the boundary between a literal sound event and
a reportative attribution is genuinely ambiguous ‹speculative›.

Clause-final frames and illocution

The four formal nouns share a paradigm of clause-final frames; Nakagawa arranges them
as: NMLZ + ne declarative, NMLZ + an
content question (with a question word), bare NMLZ polar question
and exclamation, and NMLZ + un affirmative answer
nakagawa2024 392–396 . The hawe column is as
follows.

frame
value
notes

hawe ne
declarative, verbal-source evidential
direct reportative; core function of this chapter

hawe an?
content question (with question word)
existential frame; analogous to ruwe an?

hawe? / hawe!
polar question / exclamation
insubordinate, reduced; hawe! marks strong emotion at reported content

hawe ta an?
emphatic content question
Shizunai, Horobetsu, Shiranuka; ta inserted before the existential

arpa haw-e ne | go.away voice-POSS COP | He went away (so I hear / so they say). | Minimal declarative hawe ne: the formal noun plus copula predicates the verb phrase as verbally sourced information. Any finite verb phrase may appear before hawe ne in this position.

The polar reduction hawe! follows the insubordination pattern
documented for the other bare-nominalizer forms: a once-dependent nominalized clause
used as a main clause to express surprise or strong emotion at the reported content
nakagawa2024 395 ; takahashi2013 132 . Interrogative
frames in the wider system are treated in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

The hawe ta an? variant with an inserted
ta is dialectally restricted, attested in Shizunai and
Horobetsu and apparently absent from Saru/Chitose
nakagawa2024 394 . Whether ta here is the
locative/temporal postposition or a separate interrogative element is not settled in
the accessible sources ‹speculative›.

Scope over TAM and the embedded clause

The clause dominated by hawe ne is a fully finite predication:
it carries its own person marking, aspect, and perfective auxiliaries.
hawe ne scopes over the entire TAM-marked clause, and negation
belongs inside the evidential ( … somo ki hawe ne 'it is said
that [one] does not …'), not on hawe ne itself
nakagawa2024 393 . Post-evidential material — the perfective
a , final particles, conjunctions, and nominalizers — may follow
hawe ne , exactly as for ruwe ne ; the
evidential sits above clause-internal aspect but below clause-linkers.

e=hosipi haw-e ne kusu tane anakne a=eyayramkar pe ne na | 2SG.S=return voice-POSS COP PURP now TOP 4.A=resign NMLZ COP FIN | Because you returned (so they/you said), I will now resign myself. | hawe ne in a causal subordinate clause (hawe ne kusu 'because it is said/heard that …'). The evidential scopes over the embedded proposition; kusu then relates the reported event to the main clause. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakamimatsuko, uwepeker minwa 14 (second half), ILCAA Ainu materials, aa-irc/021#271

The ordering restriction — hawe ne kusu is grammatical while
*kusu hawe ne is not in this meaning — confirms that the
evidential sits at the outer edge of the VP and inside the scope of clause-linkers, the
same layering found for ruwe ne nakagawa2024 393
‹consensus›. What accessible sources do not yet settle is the precise constituency
status of hawe ne within the nominalized projection ‹speculative›.

Double evidentiality and narrative register

hawe as and hawe ne both enter
double-evidential constructions with ruwe ne . The most frequent
pattern is … hawe as ruwe ne , where a character's indirect
verbal source is layered under the narrator's ruwe ne , which
presents the event as traditional shared knowledge
dalcorso2018 331–333 . A second pattern,
… hawe ne nankor ruwe ne , uses
ruwe ne as an epistemic reinforcer on a lower-reliability direct
form dalcorso2018 332 . In both, the outer
ruwe ne can ratify an already evidentially marked proposition
as established fact; it behaves as a knowledge operator that scopes over the other
evidential. The wider picture of evidential stacking is in
mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

ran haw-as ruw-e ne | descend voice-stand track-POSS COP | She came down (the voice said — and so the narrator presents as traditional knowledge). | Double evidential: hawe as encodes the character's indirect verbal source; ruwe ne layers the narrator's framing of the event as story-world fact. The supporting outer form is systematically ruwe ne, never siri ne — a distributional argument for ruwe ne's role as a knowledge-ratifying operator. | dalcorso2018:331 | SAR | Kayano (Kaizawa Tsurushino, Biratori); Dal Corso siglum KAY; Dal Corso ex. 273

In narrative texts hawe ne marks the narrator's explicit
attribution of a proposition to a verbal source, contrasting with the narrative default
ruwe ne , which presents events as traditionally known facts. The
genre-level distribution of the evidential forms is treated in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Diachrony and dialect microvariation

The shift from sensory noun to formal noun is transparent and is noted in the earliest
grammars: haw 'voice' > haw-e
(possessed) 'the voice of —' > hawe ne 'it is said that …',
parallel to the evolution of sir , hum ,
and ru tamura1996 s.v. haw, hawe ;
kindaichi1936 ; takahashi2018 111 . Dal Corso's general
assessment applies: the grammaticalization is partial — the lexical source noun is still
visible in the blocking of -he , and the lexical verb
haw as 'a voice sounds' remains active — so the shift is a
matter of degree rather than categorical completion dalcorso2018 24–25
‹consensus›.

Dialect realization of the reportative evidential varies in the same way as the other
three forms:

Saru, Chitose, Biratori — the possessed form
hawe in the full eight-form direct/indirect system
dalcorso2018 304–337 .

Tokachi (and frequently Ishikari) — the bare concept form
haw closes the clause rather than the possessed
hawe ; aw is an attested Tokachi
allomorph; iruska haw ne 'was angry (reportedly)' exemplifies
the pattern nakagawa2024 110, 390 ; takahashi2013 130–131 .

Horobetsu — the possessed-form nominalizer
hawe grammaticalizes a step further into adverbial-clause and
conjunctive-particle uses not reported for well-described Saru, extending the
'versatile nominalization' cline takahashi2018 107–112 .

The dialect geography of the quotative particle that co-occurs with
hawe ne mirrors this variation: Saru and Chitose use
sekor while other dialects use ari / ani ,
a distribution that community discussion has analyzed as a Saru-centred innovation
against a more widely preserved older form aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-06) ‹contested›. The full dialect picture of the quotative
is described in quotative-complementation-sekor .

title: Headless, Internally-Headed, and Noun-Modifying Clause Analyses
summary: The analytic question of whether Ainu adnominal clauses are externally-headed RCs, headless/internally-headed relatives, or a unified 'general noun-modifying clause' type.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Headless, Internally-Headed, and Noun-Modifying Clause Analyses
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The analytic question of whether Ainu adnominal clauses are externally-headed RCs, headless/internally-headed relatives, or a unified 'general noun-modifying clause' type.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu builds noun-modifying clauses prenominally: a finite verb phrase precedes a
head noun with no relative pronoun and no change to the verb's morphology; the relativized
argument's role is recovered from a gap in argument structure and from any postpositions
retained inside the clause nakagawa2024 503 ; bugaeva2015 47 ;
shibatani1990 39 . When no lexical noun is available or intended as the head,
a member of the closed class of formal-defective-nouns formal nouns fills
that role, supplying a generic semantic type — 'person', 'thing', 'people', 'place', 'time'
— while the clause itself names the referent. In continuous Hokkaido Ainu text these
formal-noun heads appear more often than common-noun heads in relativization contexts
bugaeva2015 47 .

Two further analytical questions organize the later sections. The first is whether any
Hokkaido Ainu constructions approach the internally-headed pattern of noun modification —
in which the notional head is expressed inside the modifying clause rather than outside it
— and whether that pattern involves a form of
noun-incorporation-object noun incorporation . Dal Corso raises this for
southern Hokkaido Ainu indirect evidential constructions and proposes a
classificatory-incorporation analysis dalcorso2020 47, 51–52 . The second
is whether the perception-complement constructions built with possessed sensory formal nouns
are biclausal — an external formal noun heading a relative clause — or whether they have
grammaticalized toward monoclausal complex predicates. Both questions remain contested in
the literature ‹contested›.

The p thing (nominalizer) ~ pe thing (nominalizer) formal
noun in its RC-head and complementizer uses is treated in nominalization-p-pe ;
the gap strategy, the accessibility hierarchy, and postposition stranding in
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses ; lexicalized forms that crystallized from
headless relative clauses in lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization ; and the
evidential clause-final uses of the sensory formal nouns in
evidential-schema-overview and in ruwe-ne-inferential .

The formal-noun inventory for headless nominalizations

The formal nouns that serve as generic heads form a compact, closed set. Nakagawa lists
them as a distinct word class defined by weak lexical autonomy — they follow another element
to build an NP — and shows that they carry arity −1, on a par with common nouns in their
citation form nakagawa2024 257–258, 261–262 . This arity value allows a
formal noun to fill the open argument slot of a clause with total arity ≥ +1 and produce a
headless relative-clause nominal nakagawa2024 261–262, 504 .

formal noun
semantic type of head
example

p ~ pe
thing; person (generic)
a=e p 'what one eats'

kur (< kuru person )
person (polite)
ku=kor-kur 'the one I have; husband'

utar
people (plural)
arki utar 'the people who came'

hi ~ i
thing, time, place (abstract)
oro ta cise kar hi 'the place where (X) built a house'

usi ~ uske
place, time
a=nu usi 'a place to ask about'

hike
the one, side (≈ 'one that')
ki hike 'the one that does so'

Among person-type heads, p ~ pe is maximally generic and extends to
human referents with a pejorative or collective edge in those contexts;
kur (< kuru person ) selects specifically for a
personal referent and carries a polite register. Shibatani describes
-kur as a phrasal nominalizing suffix in Classical/yukar Ainu,
productive alongside -p(e)
shibatani1990 33–34 :

ku=kor-kur | 1SG.A=have-NMLZ | the one I have; my husband | Classical Ainu (Itadori/Ishikari data in Shibatani 1990). The person-type suffix -kur ( utar head provides person semantics with plurality. Bugaeva's
accessibility-hierarchy study records it as a headless head for clauses with a plural human
referent:

cise soy pakno arki utar | house doorway up.to come.PL people | the men who came up to the doorway of the house | arki is the suppletive plural of ek 'come'; 3rd-person subjects have zero person-marking, so the verb appears without a prefix. utar 'people' is the formal-noun head of the headless relative clause. | bugaeva2015:47 | SAR

The p ~ pe formal noun extends to human agents when context provides
that reading. Bugaeva attests a pair of parallel headless nominals whose open argument is
the subject of a motion verb:

kem sawot pe tumi sawot pe | famine flee NMLZ war flee NMLZ | those who flee famine, those who flee war | pe heads two parallel clauses whose argument gap is the S/agent of sawot 'flee'. The thing-type nominalizer pe reads as 'person' by contextual inference; no person prefix appears because the referent is generic/3rd-person. | bugaeva2015:48 | SAR

Headless nominalizations across the accessibility hierarchy

The argument positions available to headless nominalizations are the same as those available
in externally-headed relative clauses. Under the noun-phrase accessibility hierarchy of
keenancomrie1977 , Hokkaido Ainu relativizes subjects, agents, objects,
and a range of obliques through the gap strategy; the alienable possessor and the standard
of comparison resist this because of island constraints and the absence of an accessible
comparee position respectively bugaeva2015 47–49 . These exclusions carry
through to the headless type: there is no headless relativization of an alienable possessor
or a comparison standard in Bugaeva's data.

For oblique participants, the governing postposition stays inside the relative clause when
the head is a formal noun, as it would with a common-noun head. The place-type formal noun
usi ~ uske heads clauses with a locative, dative, or directional
gap:

eun a=nu usi ka isam | to 4.A=hear place even NEG.exist | there was no place to ask about | The dative/directional postposition eun 'to, about' remains inside the relative clause; usi fills the gap as the headless place-head. The fourth-person prefix a= marks a generic participant. The construction follows the same postposition-stranding pattern as an externally-headed oblique relative. | bugaeva2015:49 | SAR

Nakagawa's arity accounting governs the boundary between a headless relative-clause nominal
and the complementizer use of the same formal noun. When the clause preceding
p ~ pe or hi is already saturated (arity 0),
the formal noun nominalizes the event or fact as a whole rather than filling a participant
gap nakagawa2024 261–262, 507 . The complementizer use is treated in
nominalized-complements-control-raising and for
p ~ pe specifically in nominalization-p-pe .

The appositive construction and the GNMCC question

Shibatani identifies a distinct pattern in Classical/yukar Ainu that he calls the
appositive construction : a clause followed by a bare sensory noun in its
concept form — without the possessive suffix — where the noun names the perceptual
character of the event described by the clause
shibatani1990 41 :

aynu ek hum | person come sound | the sound of a man's coming | Classical Ainu (Itadori/Ishikari data). The bare concept form hum 'sound' — not the possessed hum-i — takes the preceding clause as its content. Shibatani reads this as a construction intermediate between a relative clause and an evidential nominalization. | shibatani1990:41 | HK

okkayo cis sir | man cry appearance | a sight of a man's crying | Again the bare concept form sir 'appearance', not the possessed siri. Compare the evidential siri ne, where the possessed form sir-i heads the clause-final nominalization and is closed by the copula ne. | shibatani1990:41 | HK

In the standard description of the Saru/Chitose perception-complement construction, the
sensory noun carries the possessive suffix — haw-e 'the voice
of—', sir-i 'the appearance of—', hum-i
'the sensation of—' — and the suffix cross-references the preceding finite clause as the
implicit possessor sato2008 175–178 ; nakagawa2024 508 .
On that analysis the construction is an externally-headed formal-noun relative clause: the
head carries arity −1, the possessed suffix makes the semantic link between head and clause
overt, and the possessed formal noun is then an argument of the matrix perception verb or
the copula.

Bugaeva's relativization study and Dal Corso's subsequent work both observe a distributional
difference when the sensory noun appears in its bare (non-possessed) form alongside an
existential or posture verb: hum as 'there is a sensation of',
siri an 'there is an appearance of'
bugaeva2015 100 ; dalcorso2020 52 . In this variant the
possessed link that sustains the external-RC analysis is absent. Bugaeva reads the
distribution as showing characteristics of a general noun-modifying clause construction
(GNMCC) — a type in which a nominal element classifies the clause referent from inside
rather than standing as an external head:

pirka aynu a=ne hum as | good person 4.A=COP sound exist | (someone) felt like a good person | The bare hum 'sound/sensation' (not the possessed hum-i) combined with the existential as 'stand/exist'. Bugaeva reads this as an emerging GNMCC pattern in which hum classifies the type of sensation rather than heading a gapped external relative clause. Dal Corso 2020: 52 cites the same distributional contrast in his structural analysis. | bugaeva2015:100 | SAR

The possessed and non-possessed variants have different distributional profiles: the
possessed head functions as an argument of a matrix perception verb
(e.g. nukar see , nu hear ), while the bare-noun variant
appears as a predication in its own right, typically clause-final. Whether the distributional
difference warrants a distinct structural analysis — GNMCC versus external RC — or reflects
a stage of bleaching along the same grammaticalization cline is not settled by the sources
accessible here ‹corpus-suggested›.

Dal Corso's internally-headed RC and classificatory-incorporation proposal

Dal Corso develops the GNMCC observation into a formal structural claim specific to the
indirect evidential constructions of southern Hokkaido Ainu. He argues that
siri ki , siri an ,
hawe as , and humi as are instances of
internally-headed relative clauses (IHRCs) in which the sensory noun is incorporated
into the event clause as a classifier rather than serving as an external head
dalcorso2020 47, 51–52 . The proposal comprises two components: (i) an
IHRC claim — the notional head is expressed inside the modifying clause — and (ii) a
classificatory noun incorporation (CNI) claim — the mechanism is a type of noun
incorporation in which the incorporated noun classifies the event described by the clause
dalcorso2020 52 .

The argument turns on the contrast between direct and indirect evidential pairs. The
direct forms ( siri ne , hawe ne ,
humi ne ) carry the possessive suffix and close with the copula
ne ; the possessive suffix cross-references the preceding clause as
the possessor, sustaining an external-RC analysis. The indirect forms replace
ne with existential or posture verbs ( an ,
as , ki ), and the sensory noun appears
without possessive morphology; Dal Corso reads the absence of possessive marking as a
diagnostic that the sensory noun is incorporated into the clause as a classifier rather
than standing as an external head outside it dalcorso2020 51–52 .

The analysis has not been adopted by Bugaeva, Satō, or Nakagawa in their accounts of
Hokkaido Ainu ‹contested›. Bugaeva's relativization study treats the same perception
complements as noun-complement structures where the possessed nominal head cross-references
the clause as a whole, and characterizes the non-possessive variant as an emerging GNMCC
pattern rather than a settled IHRC structure
bugaeva2015 79, 92, 100 . Nakagawa and Satō describe the sensory formal
nouns as complementizers of the perception domain without positing an IHRC subcase
nakagawa2024 508 ; sato2008 175–178 . The CNI claim further
intersects with the broader question of what constitutes noun incorporation in a language
where the nominalization-to-lexicalization cline already accounts for many apparent
incorporation effects; the incorporation dimension is taken up in
noun-incorporation-object .

The wider biclausal-versus-monoclausal question — whether [clause]
ruwe ne , [clause] hawe ne , and their kin are biclausal (an
external formal noun heading the preceding clause, the copula predicating the result:
tamura2000 227 ; sato2008 175 ) or are grammaticalizing
toward monoclausal complex predicates ( bugaeva2016 306–315 ;
dalcorso2018 24–25 ) — is addressed with its evidence in
nominalization-p-pe and ruwe-ne-inferential . No constituency
proof in the sources available here settles the question ‹contested›.

The nominalization-to-lexicalization cline

Headless relative clauses sit at one end of a cline whose other end is a lexical noun.
Bugaeva documents a set of Hokkaido Ainu nouns that crystallized historically from
[VP]–p or [VP]–i nominalizations
bugaeva2016 240–251 :

lexicalized noun
historical source (morphemes)
meaning

aep food (< a=e-p )
4.A=eat-NMLZ
'food' (lit. 'thing one eats')

ci=ronnu-p
1PL.A=kill-NMLZ
'fox' (lit. 'things we kill')

ape-o-i
fire-enter-NMLZ
'hearth' (lit. 'place where fire enters')

These forms illustrate Bugaeva's Stage II of Hokkaido Ainu nominalization development: a
nominalizing word follows a full finite clause and the result gradually freezes into a noun,
the clause eventually compressing into a stem bugaeva2016 243, 385–397 .
The productive synchronic counterpart is the headless RC with
p thing (nominalizer) ~ pe thing (nominalizer) ; the line
between productive construction and lexicalized noun is determined by morphological
productivity shibatani1990 33–34 .

Nakagawa's diagnostic distinguishes an active relative-clause nominalization from a compound
noun: the nominalization retains the postposition or applicative that marks the head's
semantic role inside the clause, while a compound leaves the relation between its members
lexically implicit. The contrast with instruments is sharpest:
a=e-suke-p 'kitchen utensil' retains the applicative
e- that licenses the instrument, whereas the plain compound
sukemakiri 'kitchen knife' does not
nakagawa2024 506–507 . The lexicalized inventory is treated in
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

Ijäs observes that place nouns such as ipeusi restaurant derive historically
from headless relative clauses with usi place as the head, and that
lexicalized usi -type nouns need no accompanying relational noun when
used synchronically, unlike the productive headless-RC construction
ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 258 . The observation fits Bugaeva's
Stage-II account and the productive/fossilized distinction Nakagawa draws
bugaeva2016 245–251 .

Dal Corso's CNI proposal connects the cline back to the IHRC discussion. If the bare
sensory noun in the indirect evidential constructions is an incorporated classifier, it
occupies an inside-clause position analogous to the nominalizing element in the
lexicalization cline, rather than the external-head position of the possessed formal noun.
The proposal would extend the incorporation-nominalization interface already visible in the
lexicalized nouns above to a productive grammatical construction, making classificatory
incorporation a third strategy alongside externally-headed and headless relative clauses.
Whether this extension is warranted by the southern Hokkaido data, or whether the indirect
evidential constructions are better handled as partial grammaticalization of the
external-RC pattern, is among the questions Dal Corso raises without fully settling
dalcorso2020 52 ‹contested›.

title: Glossed Verse: A Yukar Heroic Epic and a Kamuy Yukar God-Song
summary: Two glossed verse texts — a yukar heroic-epic passage and a kamuy yukar divine self-narration with sakehe refrain — set side by side with comparative commentary on meter, refrain, and the first-person/logophoric narrator.
part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Glossed Verse: A Yukar Heroic Epic and a Kamuy Yukar God-Song
Part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
Summary: Two glossed verse texts — a yukar heroic-epic passage and a kamuy yukar divine self-narration with sakehe refrain — set side by side with comparative commentary on meter, refrain, and the first-person/logophoric narrator.

The sung verse genres at a glance

Among Hokkaido Ainu narrative genres, two are sung verse: the heroic epic
( yukar / sakorpe ) and the
divine epic ( kamuy yukar / oyna ). Both
are performed in the poetic-archaic-elevated-register elevated register
( atomte-itak ), both narrate their story in the first person
from the protagonist's viewpoint, and both draw on a dense stock of formulaic couplets and
parallelism. They diverge in several formal parameters that are diagnostically useful
sato2008 259 ; endo2022 §2.4.2–2.4.3 :

feature
heroic epic
divine epic

Ainu terms (SW Hokkaido)
yukar
kamuy yukar

Ainu terms (E Hokkaido)
sakorpe (Tokachi)
oyna (Tokachi); tuytak (Samani)

melody
yes; no fixed melody per story
yes; fixed melody per story

sakehe refrain
no (opening eyororpe only)
yes; fixed per story

rhythm-tapping ( rep / repni )
yes
no

typical length
long (tens of minutes to several nights)
short (minutes to roughly an hour)

traditional narrator
men
women (and men)

protagonist
superhuman human youth
a deity ( kamuy )

hero-person marking (Saru/Chitose)
a= / =an
a= / =an (Saru); ci= / =as (Iburi)

Both genres are narrated in the first person using the
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm fourth-person (indefinite) prefixes
a= / =an (see the section on the fourth-person narrator, below)
rather than the everyday first-person ku= .
The register, verse form, and grammatical hallmarks they share are treated jointly here;
the full genre taxonomy — including prose tales, prayer, and song — is set out in
oral-literature-genre-taxonomy .

The heroic epic: yukar and sakorpe

The yukar is the longest and most prestigious genre in
Hokkaido Ainu oral literature. The name itself encodes performance: Kayano glosses it as
'to imitate / mimic' — the narrator heard the story (perhaps in a dream) and now
re-tells it kayano1996 s.v. yukar . In south-west Hokkaido the Saru and Chitose
term yukar is standard; eastern varieties use
sakorpe (lit. 'the thing with sakor ') in Tokachi
endo2022 §2.4.3 . The Samani term yayerap and the
prose-content alternative irupaye (yukar material recited without melody)
mark additional regional and performative variation.

A yukar performance is sung to a melody that belongs to the
narrator rather than to the story: each performer re-melodizes a borrowed narrative, and the
tune may shift mid-performance endo2022 §2.4.3 . The narrator and listeners
keep time by beating the hearth-rim with a stick,
repni ('rhythm-stick'), the act called
rep keep time sato2008 259 . Listeners may add a chorus ( hetce ).
There is no fixed sakehe refrain, which is the single clearest
formal contrast with the divine epic. Satō notes an opening formula
eyororpe that may repeat at the very start of a performance and
reads it as a possible vestige of a refrain that the heroic epic once shared with the divine
epic ‹speculative› sato2008 259 .

The protagonist is a parentless superhuman youth — in Saru texts typically
Poy-ya-un-pe or Pon Sinutapka-un-kur ;
in eastern Hokkaido Pon Otasam/Otasut-un-kur . The hero flies,
dives, and travels underground; battles are not governed by a good-vs-evil worldview
but by heroic competition endo2022 §2.4.3 .
Female protagonists appear in the menoko yukar subgenre, which
keeps the heroic format but centres on marriage rather than battle.
The term rametok bravery; brave person; hero ( ram-etok
'spirit-tip') stands alone for the hero-concept; a second term
cipapa appears in Chiri Mashiho's Shinyōshū annotations
but is not documented in other dictionaries
aynucorporadiscord (gengojiro, 2024-02-13, asserted ) ‹speculative›.

The yukar is traditionally narrated by men; women tell the prose equivalent
irupaye instead
sato2008 259 . Modern revitalization practices have
women reciting verse yukar , knowingly departing from the traditional
allocation endo2022 §2.4.3 . The gendered narrator convention connects to the
broader pattern of genre allocation by sex treated in
gendered-register-speech .

The divine epic: kamuy yukar, oyna, and Chiri Yukie

In the divine epic the narrator speaks in the voice of a deity — a
kamuy : an animal, plant, fire, water, weather phenomenon, or
object — telling its own story. The Saru/Chitose term is
kamuy yukar ; Tokachi uses oyna
for the same genre endo2022 §2.4.2 . The term
oyna is, however, ambiguous across the dialect area:
in coastal Hidaka and Iburi it labels a distinct genre whose protagonist is the
culture-hero Oynakamuy (Aynurakkur / Okikurmi) and which uses no
sakehe , while in Tokachi and Sakhalin it covers the divine
epic proper endo2022 §2.4.4 ;
nakagawa1995 s.v. oyna . At Saru, the term is absent from Hiraga
Sadamo's (平賀サダモ) usage — she subsumed what Kindaichi and Kubodera classified as
sacred biographies under her yukar label, a dialectally
conditioned genre-boundary that resists a single universal taxonomy
tamura1996 s.v. oyna ‹contested›.

In contrast to the heroic epic, each kamuy yukar is sung to a
fixed melody that belongs to the story itself, not to the narrator
sato2008 259 . Performances are shorter (minutes to roughly an hour)
and are associated traditionally with women narrators, though men may also
perform them endo2022 §2.4.2 .

The canonical published collection is Chiri Yukie's
アイヌ神謡集 ( Ainu Shin'yōshū , 1923)
chiri1923 , thirteen Horobetsu/Iburi
kamuy yukar with parallel Japanese translation. Compiled by
Yukie Chiri (知里幸恵, 1903–1922), it remains the most widely read primary text of
Ainu oral literature and provides the main evidence for the Iburi convention
of using the exclusive first-plural ci= / =as
as a singular deity-narrator marker (see the section on fourth-person narration, below).
The chapter title formulae in this collection — for example
AEKIRUSHI — have been analysed as
a=e-kir-usi 'the place one has memory of' (after 片山龍峯 2003)
rather than as a reflex of ekiru 'turn back'
aynucorporadiscord (0penke, 2024-03-19, proposed ) ‹contested›.

The sakehe refrain

The sakehe (variant saha , Chiri 1955)
is the defining feature of the kamuy yukar : a fixed, often
semantically opaque refrain inserted into the narration. It is fixed per story —
the same kamuy yukar story-type may carry a different
sakehe in different localities
endo2022 §2.4.2 .
See sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure for verse-structural detail; the
present section concerns the grammatical and philological facts.

The word sakehe is the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form of
sake wine, sake : sake-he ,
'the sake of (something)'. Tamura's Saru dictionary distinguishes this
accented form ( sákehe 'melody; refrain') from the
unaccented noun for 'sake-rice-wine' (also sake )
tamura1996 s.v. sakehe . The formal identity of the two is reflected
in line-internal puns where both readings are live simultaneously.

The sakehe is inserted every line or every few lines,
depending on the story; the insertion pattern is fixed for a given text but not
uniform across the genre endo2022 §2.4.2 . The content ranges from
the transparently onomatopoeic — the cuckoo-deity's sakehe
han kakkok kakkok imitates its own call
endo2022 §2.4.2 — to the semantically opaque.
The function of the sakehe is debated: Nakagawa (2001b, via
endo2022 §2.4.2 ) holds that it originally indexed the protagonist,
but many sakehe have no recoverable referential link; and the same story-type
does not carry the same sakehe across localities ‹contested›.

hancikiki sake-he ci=kar | refrain sake-POSS 1PL.EXCL.A=make | (hancikiki!) — [we] brewed sake. | The sakehe hancikiki is the fixed refrain for this sparrow-sake-party kamuy yukar. It precedes the narrative phrase sake-he ci=kar 'we brewed sake'; here sake-he uses the possessed form of sake as an ordinary noun ('the sake'), homophonous with the genre term sakehe 'refrain'. The first-person ci= (1PL.EXCL) marks the deity/group of characters. | biratori1969 | SAR | 鍋澤ねぷき (Nabezawa Nepuki), 平取町アイヌ口承文芸, 'カムイユカㇻ ハンチキキ（スズメの酒盛り）'

The Biratori corpus preserves the narrator 貝澤とぅるしの (Kaizawa Turushino) explicitly
labelling the phenomenon: after reciting the thunder-deity's refrain
hum pakpak , she glosses it
humihiruy だわ。sakehe. 'it's a loud sound — [that's] the sakehe'
biratori1969 . This is one of the clearest native metalinguistic testimonies
on the genre term itself.

Similarly, the mid-Saru narrator 川上まつ子 (Kawakami Matsuko, ILCAA corpus)
describes how the menoko yukar ('female heroic epic') differs
from the prose alternative she uses: one is told with the
sakehe ( sakekoye ), the other
as an uwepeker (prose tale), and the version with
sakehe runs long while the prose version is short
ilcaa1976 .

Verse line and metre

Okuda's analysis of Hokkaido Ainu verse identifies two distinct metrical systems,
deployed by genre and musical rhythm
okuda2022 §3 :

system
rule
typical genre
4-syllable lines

syllable-count (音節指向)
each line has a fixed syllable count; the basic count is 5
lyric ( yaysama )
disfavoured (< 3% in Hatozawa's lyric texts)

accent-orientation (アクセント指向)
the first pitch accent falls within the first disyllabic foot, regardless of line length
divine epic ( kamuy yukar )
admitted when accent falls on syllable 1 (~85–100% of 4σ lines)

The syllable-count metre — with its 5-syllable basic line — was the established description
from Kindaichi (1931) and Tamura (1973) onward
okuda2022 §2.1 . Okuda's addition is the accent-orientation metre
identified in divine-epic texts. In his scansion of
Amamecikappo narrated by Hatozawa Wateke (Monbetsu; Tamura 2001),
14 of 90 non-refrain lines are 4 syllables, and 12 of those 14 carry their first
accent on syllable 1 — consistent with accent-orientation rather than syllable-count
okuda2022 §3.3 . The scansion pattern is illustrated below (● = first
accented syllable):

Selected verse lines from Amamecikappo (Hatozawa Wateke, Monbetsu;
Tamura 2001, via okuda2022 §3.3 )

syllabified line
σ
accent pattern
gloss

si-né-a-mam-pus
5
○●○○○
'one ear of grain'

ci-tá-ta-ta-ta
5
○●○○○
'I crushed and crushed'

kí-wa-tap-ne
4
●○○○
'and then'

c-é-a-hun-ke
4
●○○○
'I invited (it) into the house'

Okuda further reports that the same reciters' lyric poems are overwhelmingly 5-syllable,
confirming that the two systems are not speaker-dependent but genre- and
rhythm-dependent okuda2022 §3.2 .
He claims no other language is documented to use two such metre types by one
reciter okuda2022 §5.1 . A further hypothesis holds that the
syllable-count system entered Ainu via contact with the Japanese 7-5 mora metre,
with accent-orientation as the native baseline — but Okuda notes there is no direct
evidence for this at present okuda2022 §5.2 ‹speculative›.

On the question of rhyme and alliteration: Philippi (1979, via
okuda2022 §2.2 ) held that rhyme in Ainu verse is 'quite accidental'
and alliteration 'sporadic'. Tangiku (2020), as reported from community discussion,
argues that an 18th-century yukar text (ルウェサニウンクㇾ) shows deliberate head-rhyme,
end-rhyme, and imperfect rhyme
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-13, asserted , citing Tangiku 2020)
‹contested›. The question of whether Ainu verse rhyme is a real compositional device
or an artefact of a small phoneme inventory remains open.

To hit the target syllable count, poets employ a recognized set of techniques:
formulaic verse phrases, syllable-appropriate word choice, presence/absence of
final particles, insertion of the dummy verb ki do between verb
and following element, uncontracted ku- person marker,
a line-initial prefixed u , and the suppression of vowel
elision that occurs freely in prose speech
okuda2022 §2.1 ; sato2008 264 .

Fourth-person narration: the hero or deity as speaker

All sung verse genres, and prose narrative as well, are told in the first person from the
protagonist's point of view throughout, with a closing
identification formula revealing who 'I' was
endo2022 §2.4 ;
sato2008 259–261 . The 'first person' is, however, expressed not
by everyday ku= (1SG) but by the
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm fourth-person set:
transitive-subject a= , intransitive-subject
=an , object i=
(Saru / Chitose baseline) sato2008 260–261 ;
bugaeva2008 40–44 . The typological status of this convention — whether
it represents genuine first-person narration throughout or a logophoric construction
framing the whole text as a reported speech act — is unsettled
bugaeva2008 40–44 ; sato2004 ‹contested›.
The logophoric analysis is developed in logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Satō (2008: 259–261) offers a pragmatic motivation he calls the
indirection principle (間接性の原理): oral literature originated as
a deity's narration inside a dream, and the taboo on a deity addressing a living
human directly motivates a non-everyday first-person marker that increases
indirectness sato2008 259–261 . He presents this explicitly as a
hypothesis of his own, dissenting from the standard 'shamaness-oracle origin' theory
(巫女の託宣起源説) ‹contested›. The discourse–narration connection is taken up in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Saru and Chitose: a= / =an

In Saru and Chitose yukar and kamuy yukar the hero or deity is indexed throughout
by the fourth-person prefixes a= (transitive subject)
and =an (intransitive subject), identical in form to the
everyday indefinite and inclusive uses of the fourth person
sato2008 260 . Corpus attestation of the narrative
a= comes predominantly from prose tale (uwepeker) texts
ilcaa1976 , where the same convention applies. The Abuta
(虻田, Iburi) yukar narrated by Tōshima Tanke shows the same set:
rek kor okay 'is crying' (durative; l.46),
paye=an anki 'I am about to go' (prospective; l.113)
sato2009yukar §2.2.1 . The verb sakehawki
'to sing/recite (a yukar)' appears in the same text, an Iburi-specific term
sato2009yukar §2.2.1 .

Iburi: ci= / =as as singular deity marker

In Iburi kamuy yukar — above all in Chiri Yukie's
アイヌ神謡集 (Horobetsu, 1923) — the deity-narrator uses the
exclusive first-plural ci= / =as
consistently as a singular first person
sato2008 261 ; chiri1923 .
In Chitose, ci= / =as may appear
at the opening and then revert to a= / =an
sato2008 261 . The hero-person marker is thus both
genre-conditioned and regionally conditioned, forming a two-way system
across Hokkaido:

region
hero/deity A prefix
hero/deity S suffix
sources

Saru, Chitose (baseline)
a=
=an
sato2008 260

Iburi (Horobetsu; Chiri Yukie)
ci=
=as
sato2008 261 ; chiri1923

Chitose (opening only)
ci= → a=
=as → =an
sato2008 261

The architecture of these person cells is developed in
person-marking-architecture ;
the honorific and logophoric readings of the fourth-person set in
fourth-person-honorific .

Number-agreement override in the verse register

Everyday Saru/Chitose allows the intransitive suffix =an
only with plural verb stems: arki=an 'we come (PL)', never
* ek=an (the singular stem ek
'come.SG' does not take =an in prose). In the
elevated verse register, =an does combine with singular
stems to mark the hero-narrator as fourth person
sato2008 263 :

k=ek | 1SG.S=come.SG | I come. | Everyday Saru/Chitose: 1SG subject on the singular suppletive stem ek.

ek=an | come.SG=4.S | I come. (verse; hero-narrator) | Verse register (atomte-itak): the fourth-person intransitive suffix =an appears with the singular stem ek. In prose =an is restricted to plural stems (arki=an 'we come.PL'); the singular + =an combination is a diagnostic of the verse register (Satō 2008: 263).

This override extends to non-Saru/Chitose dialects which in prose also attach
=an only to plurals; the singular-stem combination is
a verse-register diagnostic across southern Hokkaido
sato2008 263 .

Grammatical hallmarks of the verse register

Both genres use the poetic-archaic-elevated-register elevated register
( atomte-itak , lit. 'decorated language'), which differs from
everyday speech ( yayan-itak ) along four grammatical
parameters identified by Satō sato2008 263–264 :

Applicative case-marking

Everyday Ainu marks locatives and goals by free particles
( ta for locative, un for
goal, etc.). The verse register replaces these with applicative verbal
prefixes ( e- , o- ,
ko- ), producing longer, morphologically heavier
verb forms sato2008 263 . The contrast is attested in
the Chitose data Satō provides:

tanto Ukurmem ta ku=sirepa | today Ukurmem LOC 1SG.A=arrive | Today I arrived at Ukurmem. | Everyday (yayan-itak): the locative goal is marked by the free particle ta. | sato2008:263 | CHI

kamuy menoko uni-hi tane anakne a=ko-sirepa na | divine woman house-POSS today TOP 4.A=APPL-arrive FIN | Now I arrived at the goddess's house. | Verse register (atomte-itak): the goal is marked by the applicative prefix ko- on the verb, in place of a particle. The fourth-person a= indexes the hero-narrator. This is Satō's illustrative contrast ex. 20. | sato2008:263 | CHI

The applicative system itself is described in applicative-system-overview ,
and its interaction with the verse register in
poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

Longer, more polysynthetic words

Verse register words are structurally heavier than their everyday counterparts
through elaborated prefixation and the use of periphrastic expressions where
prose would use a single stem. Satō's examples include
soyna-sanma o-si-ray-e (lit. 'let oneself be pushed out')
for everyday soyne 'go out', and
kamuy ne manu p (lit. 'what is said to be a god') for
kamuy 'god/deity'
sato2008 264 . The periphrastic tendency also serves the syllable-count
metre (§ above).

Distinct lexicon

The verse register maintains a vocabulary stratum not used in everyday speech.
Genre-specific terms include the elegant kin terms
ona father (elegant) and unu mother (elegant) ,
contrasting with the colloquial and regionally variable
haca / hapo
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2026-06-15, asserted ;
consistent with the distinct elegant lexicon described in
endo2022 §3 ).
The modifier kamuy meaning 'splendid' (as opposed to
its deity meaning) is attested in heroic epic but not in prayer — an
instance of genre-internal lexical differentiation within the elevated
register itself endo2022 §3.4 .
This register lexicon is catalogued in
poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

Parallelism and formulaic phrasing

Couplets and parallel lines are the backbone rhetorical device of both sung genres
sato2008 264 ; endo2022 §4 . Lines do not need to
rhyme or metrically match to count as a couplet — the requirement is semantic
or structural correspondence only. The most productive template is
tu … / re … 'two … / three …', which does not mean an
exact count but a rhetorical plural ('many'):
otu maw-kur-puni kane / ore maw-kur-puni kane
'raising two winds / raising three winds' (= many winds)
sato2008 264 . Formulaic descriptive phrases are memorized
in bulk and redeployed across texts, serving syllable-count requirements while
conveying conventional praise or scene-setting; for example,
tuk-no pinni si-kopa-yar / aynu pito
'a fine person like a grown Manchurian-ash tree'
(containing si-kopa-yar 'like', not used in everyday
speech) endo2022 §4 .
The full couplet and word-pair inventory is treated in
parallelism-couplets-and-word-pairs .

Reference-tracking across the long stretches of yukar narration relies on the
same fourth-person set throughout, with quotative
sekor + speech verb ( ye ,
hawki , isoytak ) closing and
re-opening direct-speech frames. The density of sekor
in verse is addressed under
reference-tracking and
quotative-complementation-sekor ;
the reportative tail hawe ne / hawe as
as a narration-closure device is treated in
hawe-ne-reportative .

Genre-boundary debates

Several questions about the verse genres remain open in the literature:

Is Ainu oral literature genuine first-person literature?
The uniform use of the fourth-person set throughout all three narrative genres is
typologically unusual. Whether it constitutes true first-person (direct-style)
narration or a logophoric construction that frames the entire text as reported
speech is unsettled
endo2022 §2.4 ; bugaeva2008 40–44 ‹contested›.

Genre-boundary cases.
The aynu-corpora community has identified 織田ステノ's
kamuy yukar '怠け者の子供' as resistant to genre
classification: it has a long sakehe (*manayta sanke tar panke tar kocupu*) but
a short body and ends as a yayeisoytak (self-narration)
by a human boy — 'neither kamuy yukar nor yukar, and (having a melody) not prose'
aynucorporadiscord (gengojiro, 2025-12-12, proposed )
‹contested›. This is consistent with Endō's own caveat that regional genre
inventories do not map cleanly onto any single scheme
endo2022 §2.5 .

The residual refrain.
Satō's reading of the opening eyororpe as a vestige of a
lost yukar refrain is an inference without independent textual support
sato2008 259 ‹speculative›.

Two metres or one?
The long-standing syllable-count description (Kindaichi 1931; Tamura 1973, via
okuda2022 §2.1 ) and Okuda's accent-orientation metre are treated
as genre- and rhythm-differentiated by Okuda himself, but the relationship
between them — whether one is derivative of the other or they are independent —
is not settled ‹contested›
okuda2022 §5 .

title: The hine/akusu Contrast and Switch-Reference-Like Linkage
summary: The sequential connectives hine and akusu and the long-running debate over whether they encode same-/different-subject (switch-reference) or discourse (un)expectedness.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The hine/akusu Contrast and Switch-Reference-Like Linkage
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The sequential connectives hine and akusu and the long-running debate over whether they encode same-/different-subject (switch-reference) or discourse (un)expectedness.

The two particles and the question they raise

Two conjunctive particles anchor this chapter. hine and then (neutral chainer)
closes a finite clause and places it before the next in temporal order, without committing to
any causal, manner, or integrative relation between the two events.
akusu and then (discovery) closes a realized first event and opens the clause
in which a consequence is encountered or witnessed. Both are bound, post-predicate particles —
not verbal suffixes, not converbs — and both join fully finite clauses that carry their own
person indexing, polarity, aspect, and any evidential tail, in the pattern established for the
whole inventory in clause-linkage-overview . The typological question they raise —
whether their distribution tracks same-subject versus different-subject reference in the manner
of switch-reference morphology foleyvanvalin1984 — runs through the
descriptive literature on Ainu clause-linking. The evidence supports a discourse-semantic
account rather than a grammaticalized subject-tracking one: neither particle carries a
same-subject or different-subject feature, and the observed correlations with subject continuity
follow from the particles' discourse-semantic roles rather than from an independent
reference-tracking function.

hine: neutral temporal enumeration

hine places two events in temporal sequence and takes no stance on how they are
related. Nakagawa's characterization is precise: hine "lists events in
time order" without any commitment to causal, manner, or purpose connection
nakagawa2024 338 . The bond is therefore looser than
wa and (integrative chainer) , which fuses two acts into one coherent event —
presenting the first as the method, result-state, or cause of the second — and looser than
akusu , which signals a discovered or witnessed consequence
nakagawa2024 338–339 ; sato2008 44 ; refsing1986 242 .

In Saru and Chitose prose, hine is the dominant chainer for the stock
opening that establishes the narrator's household before the story begins. The formula
… an hine oka=an — one or more existence clauses strung together and
closed by the plural existential 'we lived on' — recurs with minimal variation across the
genre. The pattern is so regular that Refsing identifies hine as more
frequent in folk tales than in colloquial speech refsing1986 242 :

a=onaha an a=unuhu an hine oka=an | 4.POSS=father exist 4.POSS=mother exist CONJ exist.PL=4.S | My father was there, my mother was there, and we lived on. | Nakagawa ex. 601; a stock prose-tale opening. The three items — father, mother, living — are listed in order with hine as a pure temporal enumerator, no causal claim made. | nakagawa2024:338 | SAR

Corpus texts show the same frame pivoting into the episode proper via the formula
oka=an pe ne hike 'and we lived on, and …', where the formal-noun
connective hike and (loose link) opens the narrative event:

rikun kanto ta a=saha i=resu hine oka=an pe ne hike | above sky LOC 4.POSS=elder.sister 4.O=raise CONJ exist.PL=4.S NMLZ COP CONJ | My elder sister in the sky raised me, and we lived on, and … | The hine chain closes on oka=an pe ne hike, the standard narrative-pivot formula. The formal-noun pe and the loose-link hike then open the episode proper. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子「uwepeker 民話15」, AA研アイヌ語資料, aa-irc/022#0

Etymology, phonology, and dialect distribution

The particle derives from the nominalizer hi the fact that; the thing that
plus the copula ne be, is : hi + ne >
hine (Tamura 1972b: 158, via refsing1986 243 ;
Kindaichi 1960: 193, via refsing1986 243 ). The source material is reached
here through intermediaries; the etymology is reported, not independently verified. It aligns
with a wider pattern in which nominalization-plus-copula material grammaticalizes into
clause-linkers, visible also in the evidential system (see ruwe-ne-inferential ).

Phonologically, word-initial h is regularly dropped in normal-speed
speech, and the resulting vowel i fuses with a preceding final
consonant: ek hine 'came-and' → [ekine];
a=ne hine → [aneine]. Ishikari, where initial
h is generally absent, writes the form
ine . In rapid speech the vowel itself may elide to bare [ne]
nakagawa2024 338 ; refsing1986 243 .

Dialect distribution is uneven. Tokachi uses hinne (e.g. Honbetsu)
and hine in alternation; Shiranuka and Kushiro write
ine ; Honbetsu lacks both forms entirely, the chaining function
falling to other resources. In Shiranuka, frequency is lower than
wa and tek combined
nakagawa2024 338 . The whole-corpus count of approximately 14,368 tokens —
the highest-frequency temporal-sequence particle with few competing homonyms — is a usable
signal of the particle's prominence, with the usual caveat that Hokkaido and Sakhalin
varieties are not separated in the aggregated count ‹corpus-suggested›.

akusu and awa: sequential discovery

akusu and awa belong to Nakagawa's group-② 'unfolding event' (事態の展開)
connectives, alongside hike and
ayne and finally (after a protracted action)
nakagawa2024 345–347 . Their core function is to present a completed first
event followed by a second event that comes to the participant's attention — "the anterior
event actually happened, and then coincidentally (偶発的に) the posterior event occurred"
sato2009 52 . Satō classes the construction as a factual condition
(事実的条件): the protasis is realized rather than hypothetical, and the apodosis reports the
encountered consequence sato2008 47 ; sato2009 52 .

The discovery flavor of akusu — English 'whereupon', 'and then (I
found/saw that)' — is not a causal claim. Nakagawa makes this explicit: the causal connection
between E1 and E2 is irrelevant to the particle; what it marks is that E2 comes to the
attention of a participant after E1 is complete, whatever the causal structure may be
nakagawa2024 345 :

nea pon yuk a=rayke akusu | that small deer 4.A=kill CONJ | After killing that small deer, … | The fourth-person agent a= completes the killing; akusu opens the clause in which the narrator discovers or experiences what follows. The continuation is not preserved in the banked fragment. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子「isoytak 独話1」, AA研アイヌ語資料, aa-irc/003#73

Akusu attaches after a clause-final evidential tail — confirming the
ordering [VP]–evidential–connective established for the system as a whole. When the first
clause closes on ruwe ne , the anterior event is presented as an
established fact before the discovery linker opens the next clause:

kanpi ku=sanke ru-we ne akusu itasa kanpi ek ru-we ne | letter 1SG.A=send.out track-POSS COP CONJ in.return letter come track-POSS COP | I had sent a letter, and in return a letter came. | Satō ex. 16 / Satō 2009 ex. 32. The discovery linker attaches outside the ruwe ne evidential tail. The cross-linguistic asymmetry with Japanese — factual と/たら cannot follow preceding のだ, but Ainu akusu can follow ruwe ne — is discussed in §5. | sato2008:47; sato2009:52 | CHI

The akusu/awa register and dialect split

Akusu and awa are semantically equivalent;
no descriptive source establishes any difference in meaning. Their distribution is
conditioned by register and dialect: in Saru, prose texts favour
akusu while verse texts heavily favour
awa ; Horobetsu and Ishikari use awa
regardless of register; Shizunai and Tokachi use akusu
nakagawa2024 345 . Satō's characterization of awa
as the oral-literature (口承文芸) variant aligns with the verse skew in Saru
sato2008 47–48 ‹consensus›:

particle
Saru prose
Saru verse
Horobetsu / Ishikari
Shizunai / Tokachi
etymology

akusu / akus
primary
rare
rare
primary
a (PFV) + kusu (consequential)

awa
rare
primary
primary
rare
a (PFV) + ?

The etymology of akusu as a (perfective
auxiliary) + kusu (consequential) is supported by Refsing and is
consistent with the particle's semantics — a completed event ( a :
'situation done and closed') from which a consequence follows
( kusu ) refsing1986 248 . The second morpheme of
awa has not been identified with confidence
refsing1986 248 . Shizunai uses the phonologically reduced form
akus (word-final vowel devoicing and deletion)
refsing1986 248 .

Whole-corpus word-form counts register approximately 3,861 tokens for
akusu and 2,071 for awa . Both are among the
cleaner particle counts with few homonyms, and the ratio is consistent with a mixed-register
corpus containing proportionally more prose than verse ‹corpus-suggested›. Per-function
counts that would separate Hokkaido from Sakhalin are not available from the current search
tools.

The switch-reference question

Switch-reference — dedicated morphological marking on clause-linkers that co-indexes subject
continuity between clauses foleyvanvalin1984 — is absent from Hokkaido
Ainu. The language has no converbs, no non-finite verb forms, and no SS/DS feature on any
conjunctive particle bugaeva2022 ; nakagawa2024 514 . What can be
asked is whether the choice among particles statistically or semantically correlates with
subject continuity, and whether any such correlation is grammaticalized or follows from the
particles' discourse-semantic content.

Statistical tendencies and their source

The one quantified figure in the literature is Refsing's Shizunai count: approximately
ninety per cent of the predicate pairs joined by wa share a subject
refsing1986 240 . No comparable count exists for
hine or akusu in any Hokkaido variety; the
quantitative base is a single informant's Shizunai material. The
ninety-per-cent figure is consistent with a preference but demonstrably not with a
constraint: Satō shows that wa tolerates different-subject junctions
without any change in form, as in the child-throws-stone / barrel-falls example where the
thrower and the barrel are different participants — a junction Japanese
〜て cannot make sato2008 45–46 . No primary grammar
states a same-subject requirement for hine , and its neutral-listing
semantics entails no such restriction nakagawa2024 338 ;
refsing1986 242 .

The simultaneity particle kor while (simultaneous) provides a direct test:
it takes different-subject complements without restriction, as in Saru
ku=horipi kor en=nukar 'she looked at me while I danced'
ijas2023 , corroborated by an oral-literature example in Satō
sato2008 49–50 . Shizunai kane "generally" takes
same-subject complements but exceptions are attested refsing1986 250 . Across
the particle inventory, any same-subject preference is a graded tendency, not a binary SS/DS
grammatical feature.

Ijäs offers the pedagogical heuristic that negated clauses take
no when the two predicates share a subject and
wa when they do not ijas2023 . Nakagawa and Satō derive
the no / wa choice from the aspect and polarity
of the first predicate — stative and negated action predicates take
no — rather than from subject identity
nakagawa2024 340–341 ; sato2008 45–47 . The Ijäs framing is
noted because it has entered pedagogical circulation ‹contested›; the primary descriptive
sources do not support it.

Expectedness and discourse stance

Nakagawa's and Satō's analyses locate the hine /
akusu contrast in event structure and narrative stance, not in
subject continuity. The neutral temporal enumerator hine naturally
chains a single protagonist's sequential acts — producing a predominance of same-subject
pairs without encoding subject identity. The discovery connective
akusu marks that the narrator encounters or witnesses the second
event after the first is complete, a framing that often foregrounds a new or unexpected
participant and thereby produces an apparent different-subject tendency. The observed SS/DS
distributions are statistical consequences of discourse function, not evidence for a
grammaticalized opposition nakagawa2024 338, 345 ;
sato2008 44, 47 ‹consensus› (for the basic semantic descriptions).

The narrative boundary device

The nearest structural analogue to a switch-reference signal is discourse-organizational
rather than morphological. The Handbook's descriptive grammar notes a tendency for the
sequence of the perfective auxiliary a perfective (situation complete)
followed by a connective such as korka but, although ,
wa , or the formal-noun connective p thing (formal noun as connective)
to fall at transitions of scene, topic, or acting participant in narrative
bugaeva2022 ‹speculative›. The perfective marks the anterior situation as
completed and cut off from the present; an adverb of time typically follows to open the new
episode. The printed location of this observation has not been confirmed against the
Handbook print edition, so the claim is treated as speculative.

The device is a clause-chaining boundary convention. No morpheme on the connective particle
encodes subject identity; the signal comes from the co-occurrence of the perfective auxiliary
with a particular linker at a discourse transition. Subject identity across a chain is
tracked by the obligatory person affixes on each finite predicate, the mechanism drawn
together in reference-tracking .

Graded positions

The competing positions, arranged by evidence grade:

position
source
grade

No morphological switch-reference; conjunctive particles carry no SS/DS feature;
person affixes track argument continuity
bugaeva2022 ; nakagawa2024 514
consensus

wa tends same-subject (≈90% in Shizunai) but imposes no
grammatical constraint
refsing1986 240 ; sato2008 45–46
corpus-suggested (one dialect, one informant; no Saru count for hine)

hine / akusu encode discourse stance
(neutral ordering vs discovery), not subject continuity
nakagawa2024 338, 345 ; sato2008 44, 47
consensus

no / wa conditioned by SS vs DS
ijas2023
contested — primary grammars condition on polarity/aspect, not subject identity

Perfective a + korka /
p tends to fall at scene and actor transitions
bugaeva2022 (Handbook Part II, page unverified)
speculative

The SS tendency of hine and the apparent DS lean of
akusu both follow from discourse semantics; no separate
reference-tracking feature is warranted; corpus annotation of subject continuity at
these junctions would provide the empirical test
synthesis from the above; no single source states this directly
original-needs-review

The ‹speculative› synthesis draws on what each source establishes independently.
Hine neutrally chains events along a single narrative strand — which
by default is the protagonist's sequential acts — hence the same-subject predominance
without a grammatical constraint. Akusu frames the second event as
encountered or discovered after the first is complete, a perspective that frequently involves
a new or surprising participant and thereby shows an apparent different-subject lean. Neither
particle requires a particular subject configuration; the SS/DS distributions reflect the
default narrative configuration of each connective's domain. A corpus annotation of subject
continuity across hine and akusu junctions
in Saru text would supply the empirical test ‹speculative›.

The evidential–connective interface

Conjunctive particles attach outside the clause-final evidential frame. The ordering
[VP]–evidential–connective, established for the system as a whole in
clause-linkage-overview , means that akusu can follow a
ruwe ne tail, yielding … ruwe ne akusu … . The
evidential sits above clause-internal aspect but below the clause-linker, so
kusu ne ruwe ne is grammatical and
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not nakagawa2024 393 .

Satō identifies a typologically notable property of this interface. Japanese factual conditional
と / たら cannot follow a preceding
のだ , yet Ainu places no such constraint on the
ruwe ne akusu sequence: the first event is presented as
knowledge-established fact and the discovery linker then opens the encountered consequence
sato2009 52–53 . The construction presents the two layers as separately
stackable because they occupy distinct positions in the clausal hierarchy.

A member of the aynu-corpora Discord community corroborates both the frequency and the
discourse function of this sequence. nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) observes that
ruwe ne is associated with scene transition and summary framing in
prose narrative, and that ruwe ne akusu functions as a compound of
that discourse anchoring with the discovery connective
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative› — corroborated by sato2009 52 . The
observation is consistent with the evidential+linker ordering described by Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 393 and with the ruwe ne system set out
in ruwe-ne-inferential .

Dialect edges

The particles described above rest primarily on Saru and Chitose attestation. Several
dialect-specific details bear on the comparison. Shizunai uses
akus (the word-final vowel devoiced and deleted) for the same discovery
function refsing1986 248 . Horobetsu and Ishikari favour
awa regardless of register, while prose-oriented Shizunai and Tokachi
use akusu as the default nakagawa2024 345 . The
category is stable across the dialect continuum; phonological shape and genre weighting shift,
not the underlying semantic function.

Tokachi uses hinne alongside hine for neutral
chaining; Honbetsu Tokachi shows neither form. These micro-variations caution against
projecting Saru frequency data onto the dialect cluster as a whole
nakagawa2024 338 . The full topology of the conjunctive-particle system across
Hokkaido dialects is surveyed in clause-linkage-overview ; the conditional and
concessive members of the system are treated in
conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses .

Open questions

The foundational Saru description of the conjunctive particles, Tamura's 1972 study of the
〜して constructions (Tamura 1972b), is the primary source for nearly
every claim about hine in the grammars consulted here, but it was not
read directly — all attributed page numbers reach it through
nakagawa2024 and refsing1986 ‹speculative› (the page attributions are second-hand).
The quantitative base is thin: Refsing's ninety-per-cent same-subject count for
wa in Shizunai is the only published figure; no per-function counts
for hine or akusu in any Hokkaido variety
exist in the accessible literature. The whole-corpus totals — approximately 14,368 for
hine , 3,861 for akusu , 2,071 for
awa — are homonym-mixed, Hokkaido-and-Sakhalin combined, and carry no
subject-continuity annotation. Whether akusu junctions show measurably
lower same-subject rates than hine junctions — the empirical test of
the ‹speculative› synthesis — remains open and requires bespoke corpus annotation
over a tagged Saru text. The reference-tracking apparatus as a whole is drawn together in
reference-tracking ; narrative cohesion and tail-head linkage are treated in
tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion .

title: Historical Orthographies and the Batchelor Tradition
summary: The pre-phonemic Latin orthographies of Hokkaido Ainu — Batchelor's Hepburn-derived system and its lineage through Kindaichi, Kannari Matsu, and Chiri Yukie — their systematic correspondences with the modern standard, and how texts written in them are read and converted today.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Historical Orthographies and the Batchelor Tradition
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: The pre-phonemic Latin orthographies of Hokkaido Ainu — Batchelor's Hepburn-derived system and its lineage through Kindaichi, Kannari Matsu, and Chiri Yukie — their systematic correspondences with the modern standard, and how texts written in them are read and converted today.

The Hepburn-derived tradition

Before the phonemic romanization documented in latin-phonemic-transcription took
hold in the 1950s and 1960s, Hokkaido Ainu was written in Latin script mainly in orthographies
derived from the Hepburn romanization of Japanese. The system that fixed this tradition in
print is John Batchelor's. His An Ainu–English–Japanese Dictionary appeared in 1889
and went through a second (1905), third (1926), and fourth edition (1938)
batchelor1905 ; batchelor1938 ; nakagawa2024 90 , and his
Bible translations — above all the 1897 New Testament チコロ・ウタラパ
( Chikoro utarapa ), transmitted together with the Psalms and Jonah —
constitute the largest body of connected Ainu text in this orthography
batchelor1897 ; refsing1986 18 . Batchelor wrote
ch for the affricate and sh for the palatalized
sibilant, on the model of the Hepburn values for Japanese, and until phonemic transcription
displaced it this style was the ordinary way of romanizing Ainu nakagawa2024 53 ;
nakagawa2006 3 ; kirikae1997 .

The tradition was transmitted to the Ainu writers whose texts anchor the modern corpus.
Kannari Matsu, whose notebooks preserve a vast corpus of heroic epics, was a
graduate of 愛隣学校 , the mission school Batchelor founded in Hakodate,
where she was taught English and the Latin alphabet; her correspondence with her sister was
conducted entirely in romaji nakagawa2006 24 . Chiri Yukie's
アイヌ神謡集 chiri1923 , the collection of divine epics that
became the model for later Ainu-authored writing, is likewise written in a Hepburn-style
orthography, and Kirikae judges the romanization of both women to be a direct continuation of
Batchelor's practice, nurtured by his publications and by the aunt–niece connection between
them kirikae1997 ‹consensus›. On the academic side the same conventions passed to
Kindaichi Kyōsuke and his students; Kubodera Itsuhiko retained the Hepburn style throughout
his career nakagawa2006 3 ; kirikae1997 .

Batchelor was a missionary who learned the language for evangelical work, and his
transcription reflects the ear and habits of an English speaker rather than an analysis of
the sound system refsing1986 18 . Contemporary and later assessments diverge
sharply. Chiri Mashiho's アイヌ語入門 devotes some fifteen pages to
mistranslations, phantom words, and wrong forms in the dictionary and concludes that "it would
be closer to the truth to say that it consists solely of flaws"
chiri1956nyumon (translation from refsing1986 18–19 ). Laufer had
already written that Batchelor "almost neglected" the sound system and that his transcription
was "no more than an attempt at adopting the English alphabet to the writing of Ainu"
laufer1917 198 , quoted in majewicz2022 §5 . Refsing's
assessment is more measured: the dictionary and the grammar bound with it are too inaccurate
to serve as a primary source, yet given the scarcity of early materials they retain value when
used critically, the English glosses often being closer to the mark than the Japanese ones
refsing1986 18–19 . Nakagawa notes that after Chiri's critique researchers
stopped citing the dictionary openly, while judging it still highly usable for its period and
coverage nakagawa2024 90 . In this grammar Batchelor's works are treated as
philological witnesses: their forms are cited for what they attest about nineteenth-century
usage, and every form is read through the correspondence rules of the next section. The place
of these works in the source base is set out in written-sources-grammars-dictionaries .

Systematic features of Batchelor's orthography

Batchelor's spellings deviate from the modern standard in recurrent, largely rule-governed
ways. The table gathers the correspondences; the subsections below document each group from
the published comparisons — Kirikae kirikae1997 , Nakagawa's orthographic surveys
nakagawa2006 3–5 ; nakagawa2024 53–54 , and the Sakaguchi–Watanabe
re-edition of Batchelor's 1924 Ainu Fireside Stories
sakaguchi2019 243–245 — and from the 1897 New Testament
batchelor1897 .
