—

Digraphs, voiced letters, and spurious vowels

The digraphs ch and sh continue the Hepburn
values for Japanese and carry no contrast in Ainu, which has a single affricate and a single
sibilant phoneme; Nakagawa's correspondence table for the Hepburn style pairs
shik with sik eye and achapo
with acapo uncle nakagawa2024 53–54 ; kirikae1997 .
Batchelor, Kindaichi, and Chiri Yukie all also wrote the voiced letters
b d g beside p t k , although voicing is not
contrastive in Ainu nakagawa2024 54 . In Batchelor's hands the voiced
spellings are unstable: the dictionary enters both arage and
arake as headwords for what is one word, modern
arke one of a pair kirikae1997 . The Fireside Stories texts write
invariant tambe gusu for tanpe kusu 'therefore'
and show the spurious ei spellings chisei for
cise house and heikachi for
hekaci boy sakaguchi2019 244 .

eani pirka cep somo ne | 2SG good fish NEG COP | Eani pirika chep shomo ne. | You are not a good fish. | sakaguchi2019:245–246

tanpe kusu tan mosir ka ta okay ruw-e ne | this.thing because this land surface LOC exist.PL track-POSS COP | Tambe gusu tam moshiri ka ta okai ruwe ne. | That is why they are in this land. | sakaguchi2019:245–246

The second example compresses most of the segmental rules into one clause: voiced
b and g for /p/ and /k/, the digraph
sh , an echo vowel on moshiri , and
i for the coda glide of okai ~
okay exist (plural) . The coda-glide rule — syllable-final
i and u where the modern standard writes
y and w — is likewise regular:
kamui ~ kamuy spirit , kaukau ~
kawkaw hail nakagawa2024 53–54 .

Echo vowels and syllable-final r

The most consequential deviation is the paragogic vowel written after closed syllables,
above all after /r/: Batchelor's dictionary has koro ,
kara , mokoro where the modern standard writes
kor have , kar make , mokor sleep
kirikae1997 ; nakagawa2024 54 . Phonetically the flap [ɾ] releases
into a short vocalic offglide echoing the preceding vowel, and Kirikae locates the error in
taking that offglide for an intended vowel kirikae1997 . Refsing adds a filter on
the transcriber's side: all speakers of the period were bilingual, Japanese phonotactics
supply no final consonants, and every earlier record had been written in kana, so recorded
final vowels — especially after r — reflect Japanese influence on the
transcription as much as on the speech refsing1986 55–56 .

The recognition that -r -final syllables contrast with
-rV sequences is credited by Nakagawa to Chiri Yukie: her
神謡集 spellings distinguish nukar and
pirka from Batchelor's nukara and
pirika , and Kindaichi changed his own practice under her influence
from the 1923 アイヌ聖典 onward nakagawa2024 54
‹consensus›. Kindaichi's testimony, quoted by Kirikae, is that Yukie owned the second
edition of Batchelor's dictionary and had gone through it erasing the final vowels of the
koro -type entries one by one; Kirikae traces today's
arke -type spellings to her practice kirikae1997 . Before
the change, Kindaichi analysed the verb of inkar’an awa 'when I
looked' as a lemma inkara whose final syllable lost its vowel before
the person marker, and wrote an apostrophe as the elision mark — a notation that survives
throughout Batchelor's own texts (next subsection) kirikae1997 . The phonemic
status of the coda is treated in consonant-inventory .

Person marking and word division

Batchelor's orthography segments no
personal-affix-template-and-ordering person markers . The fourth-person
prefix is written solid with the verb, so that a=e '4.A=eat' appears
as ae and a=ye '4.A=say' as
aye :

somo a=e ya | NEG 4.A=eat Q | Shomo ae ya! | Is it not eaten? | sakaguchi2019:245–246

The first- and second-person markers, by contrast, are written as separate words and take
sentence-initial capitals like English pronouns: the New Testament has
Ku goro Michi for ku=kor mici 'my Father'
batchelor1897 John 20:17 , and phrase-internal
echi otta ku ek for eci=or ta ku=ek 'I came
to you' batchelor1897 1 Cor. 2:1 . Where a person marker follows an
r -final stem, the apostrophe of the false-lemma analysis appears:
Batchelor's lemma for 'to look' was ingara , so the fourth-person form
is spelled Ingarʼan , with ʼ marking the "elided" vowel
of the lemma rather than any phonological event:

inkar=an | look=4.S | Ingarʼan | Behold! (lit. one looks) | batchelor1897:Rev. 1:7

The same mark recurs in gurʼanak ( kur anak ),
eramʼan-hi (a misparse of eraman know as
eram + an ), and
moshirʼun ( mosir un )
batchelor1897 Matt. 13:11; 1 Cor. 2:6 . Word division is idiosyncratic in
both directions. Postpositional groups are fused — otta for
or ta , and with a preceding noun
moshirʼotta for mosir or ta :

Ecipt mosir or ta e=kira | Egypt land place LOC 2SG.S=flee | Echipt moshirʼotta ekira | flee to the land of Egypt | batchelor1897:Matt. 2:13

Univerbated particles are conversely split: anak ne for
anakne topic particle and son no for
sonno truly sakaguchi2019 245–246 . Hyphens join the
nominalizing hi place/time/thing to its verb ( ene itak-hi for ene itak hi 'thus he spoke')
batchelor1897 Matt. 2:13 ; sakaguchi2019 245 , without being
applied consistently elsewhere. The principles that govern the modern segmentation are
given in orthographic-standardization-and-word-division word-spacing .

Dialect mixture

A Batchelor text is not a dialectally uniform record. In the Fireside Stories the quotative
appears as ari thus (quotative) in the first tale but as
sekor thus (quotative) — the Saru–Ishikari form — in others, both occur
within a single tale elsewhere in the collection, and six of the fifty tales are
transpositions of Sakhalin material from Piłsudski pilsudski1912 into a
nominally Hokkaido corpus; the lexical profile makes single authorship of the texts
implausible, and one tale is attributed to a Shizunai writer
sakaguchi2019 243–244 ‹consensus›. Batchelor's own grammatical framework
compounds the problem: he treated the person markers as pronouns, an analysis not corrected
until Kindaichi refsing1986 22–23 . Forms cited from this corpus in the
present grammar are therefore identified by source, never by dialect tag.

From Batchelor to the modern standard

Nakagawa tracks the passage from the Hepburn tradition to the modern standard through one
example set — aynu person , horkew wolf , cis cry ,
cis=an 'we cry' — respelled across the successive systems
nakagawa2006 3–5 :

System
'person'
'wolf'
'cry'
'we cry'

Hepburn style (Batchelor; Kindaichi; Kubodera)
ainu
horkeu
chish
chisan

Chiri Mashiho (1942)
ajnu
horkeŭ
čiš
čisan

Chiri Mashiho (1950s)
aynu
horkew
chis
chisan

Phonemic, dialect-dictionary style (Hattori–Tamura)
ʼaynu
horkew
cis
cisan

Phonemic, Akor Itak style (the modern standard)
aynu
horkew
cis
cis=an

Each row resolves one layer of the Hepburn inheritance. Chiri Mashiho, who had used the
Hepburn style in the 1936 grammar co-authored with Kindaichi kindaichi1936 , moved
in 1942 to one-letter-per-phoneme spellings for the glides and the affricate
( j ŭ č ), while still splitting /s/ between s and
š by position; in his 1950s work he abandoned the diacritic letters —
Nakagawa attributes the retreat to printing costs — leaving ch as the
one remaining digraph nakagawa2006 3–4 ; nakagawa2024 55–56 .
The phonemic system established in the 1950s by Hattori and Tamura, and disseminated through
the 1964 dialect dictionary hattori1964 , completed the one-phoneme-one-letter
principle with c , wrote the glottal onset ʼ as a phoneme,
and wrote transitional glides ( ʼuwepeker ); the style codified in
Akor Itak utari1994 drops both and segments the person markers with
= nakagawa2006 4–5 ; nakagawa2024 56–57 . The
resulting standard, its remaining points of variation, and the boundary-marking conventions
are documented in latin-phonemic-transcription other-romanizations and
orthographic-standardization-and-word-division history ; the parallel kana
tradition, which the same writers used alongside romanization, is treated in
katakana-and-small-kana-codas .

Reading Batchelor materials today

The working method for making a Batchelor-orthography text usable is the paired re-edition:
the original spelling is reproduced intact and a modern respelling ( 表記変更 ) is printed beside it. Sakaguchi and Watanabe's edition of the Fireside Stories states the
policy explicitly: capitalization of the original is retained; where the text's
rV spelling leaves it undecidable whether a vowel is present, the vowel
is bracketed rather than silently resolved ( or(o)wano ); and the
respelled text marks morphophonemically fused sequences with an underscore
( or_ ta , itak h_i ), keeping the underlying words
recoverable while recording the sandhi sakaguchi2019 245–247 . The same
principles transfer to the New Testament and the dictionary citations.

Most of the distance between the two orthographies is mechanical. The segmental
correspondences of the features table are exceptionless rewrite rules, and the echo vowels
resolve against a lexicon of coda-final stems. A rule-plus-lexicon conversion of the 1897 New
Testament, checked against respelling practice of the Sakaguchi–Watanabe type, resolves
roughly seven-eighths of tokens; the residue is concentrated where conversion stops being
orthographic — the segmentation and placement of person markers, which depend on
morphological analysis of the clause rather than on spelling ‹corpus-confirmed›. Reading
Batchelor therefore means re-doing the person-marking analysis that his orthography never
encoded; the analysis itself is given in
personal-affix-template-and-ordering , and the citation conventions for examples
drawn from pre-phonemic sources in glossing-abbreviations-and-citation .

title: The History of Ainu Language Description and Previous Research
summary: A periodized historiography of Ainu linguistics from Edo-period records to the modern typological and corpus-computational turn, situating this grammar in that lineage.
part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The History of Ainu Language Description and Previous Research
Part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
Summary: A periodized historiography of Ainu linguistics from Edo-period records to the modern typological and corpus-computational turn, situating this grammar in that lineage.

Edo-period records and the philological tradition

The earliest systematic documentation of the Ainu language dates from the Edo period (17th–19th
centuries), when Japanese interpreters and officials stationed at trading posts on Hokkaido
compiled word-lists, phrase-books, and glossaries for practical administrative use. The most
comprehensive of these, described by refsing1986 17 , is the
蝦夷方言藻汐草 ( Ezo hōgen moshiogusa , 'An Ezo dialect miscellany'),
compiled by the interpreter Uehara Kumajirō and the official Abe Chōzaburō and first issued in
1792. It contains roughly three thousand lexical entries organized by semantic field, a list of
common phrases, and short texts, and it served as a primary reference for subsequent Japanese
scholarship. The late Meiji period added further lexical resources, among them the Saru-dialect
topical conversation dictionary of kanazawa1898 .

The Edo-period manuscript tradition has received philological attention in recent scholarship.
fukazawa2017 examines the Kaga-ke ( 加賀家 ) documents — a
set of administrative and linguistic records from the Matsumae domain — in a dissertation that
subjects these materials to systematic critical scrutiny. The Sino-Ainu glosses embedded in the
蝦夷島奇觀 ( Ezoshima kikan ) have been studied by
yasuoka2023 , contributing to a digitally oriented philological programme that
bridges the manuscript tradition and modern corpus resources.

These sources function primarily as practical aids for Japanese-Ainu communication rather than
as linguistic analyses. They provide invaluable lexical witnesses, particularly for varieties
spoken in regions where communities ceased to transmit the language before systematic recording
was possible, but they contain no morphological analysis and their phonological transcriptions
require careful critical interpretation. Their evidentiary status is assessed in
written-sources-grammars-dictionaries .

Batchelor, Piłsudski, and early Western documentation

Basil Hall Chamberlain's 1887 monograph chamberlain1887 marshalled detailed
linguistic arguments against relating Ainu to Japanese or any Altaic family, concluding that it
must be treated as genealogically unclassifiable. His points were subsequently reviewed and
extended by Kindaichi Kyōsuke, and the isolate status has remained the scholarly consensus; the
question is examined in genetic-position-macro-comparison .

The most influential early description available in a European language came from John Batchelor,
a British Anglican missionary who spent several decades working among the Hokkaido Ainu. His
dictionary — held in this project's sources in the batchelor1905 edition and in
its final revised form of batchelor1938 — combined Ainu-English-Japanese glosses
with a grammatical sketch, and it remained outside Japan the most accessible major source
material on the language for several decades refsing1986 18 . Among Batchelor's
contributions was a Roman-alphabet transcription system for Ainu, the most direct antecedent of
the Latin-script orthographic tradition examined in glossing-abbreviations-and-citation .
The limitations of the dictionary, however, were noted by his Japanese successors: Refsing
refsing1986 18–19 documents Chiri Mashiho's extended criticism cataloguing
mistranslations, phantom entries, and unreliable forms, and concludes that the work requires a
critical and cautious approach. The grammatical sketch shares the presupposition of European
missionary-grammar categories and requires verification against the later native-speaker-based
record.

Bronisław Piłsudski's collection pilsudski1912 , compiled from fieldwork on
Sakhalin, supplies an early and substantial documentary record of the Sakhalin Ainu variety.
The significance of that collection for Sakhalin phonetics, phonology, and morphosyntax has
been reassessed in dalcorso2024 , and the texts have been re-edited with
translation and grammatical notes by dalcorso2021 . The Sakhalin materials feature
in this grammar as systematic external contrast, marked with the dialect tag
sa ; the historical and geographic setting shared between Hokkaido and
Sakhalin Ainu is outlined in ainu-people-homeland-history .

Kindaichi and Chiri: the classical Japanese descriptive tradition

Kindaichi Kyōsuke (1882–1971) established Ainu studies as a recognized academic discipline in
Japan, bringing sustained scholarly productivity and the institutional standing of the Tokyo
Imperial University to a field that had previously lacked both. His primary interest lay in the
oral-literature tradition — particularly the heroic epics known as yukar — and his grammatical
work was conceived as an instrument for that philological programme
kindaichi1933 . Among the textual materials he drew on were yukar notebooks
compiled by the Ainu narrator Kannari Matsu, who wrote down her repertoire in romanized Ainu
over several years in Kindaichi's home, eventually filling seventy notebooks
refsing1986 19 . The oral-literature tradition itself is surveyed in
oral-literature-and-spoken-corpora .

Kindaichi's analytical contributions to Hokkaido Ainu grammar were substantial. Refsing
identifies two advances in particular: he demonstrated that the language's person-indexing
elements are personal affixes on the verb rather than the free pronouns that Batchelor's
description had posited, and he distinguished the abstract nominal form from the personal-reference-bearing
concrete (possessed) form — an opposition later refined by Tamura and central to the nominal
morphology of this grammar refsing1986 21–22 .

Chiri Mashiho (1909–1961), an Ainu scholar, studied under Kindaichi at Tokyo Imperial University
— the first Ainu student admitted to that institution — and his graduation thesis became the
joint grammar kindaichi1936 , アイヌ語法概説 ('An outline of Ainu
grammar'), co-authored with Kindaichi. Published in 1936, it remained the standard Japanese
reference work for decades. Chiri subsequently undertook fieldwork on Sakhalin and produced a
grammar centred on the Sakhalin dialects chiri1942 . His later scholarly career
turned toward lexicography and etymological research; he began a projected ten-volume classified
dictionary of Ainu, completing three volumes on plants, animals, and human beings respectively
before his death in 1961 chiri1953 .

Chiri Yukie (1903–1922) compiled the first published collection of Ainu kamuy yukar (divine
epics) with Japanese translations chiri1923 , a text-publication project that
established the model of romanized Ainu with facing-page translation and drew the oral-literary
tradition into print for the first time.

The descriptive framework of Kindaichi and Chiri organized grammar around traditional Japanese
parts-of-speech categories — noun, pronoun, adjective, adverb, verb, particle — with
grammatical relations treated semantically: the analyst begins from a meaning-type (nominative,
locative, dative) and asks how the language expresses it. Refsing notes that this approach
presupposes categories from the analyst's prior framework rather than deriving them from the
distributional behavior of Ainu morphemes, and produces a coherent overview whose ordering
reflects the analyst's starting-point as much as the structure of the language
refsing1986 40 . The contrast between this tradition and the formal-distributional
analysis of Tamura is a recurring reference point in the history of Ainu grammaticography.

The modern descriptive era: Tamura, Refsing, Shibatani, and Satō

Post-war descriptive work began with two large comparative projects by Hattori Shirō. The
lexicostatistic study he undertook with Chiri Mashiho surveyed 19 Ainu localities — 13 on
Hokkaido, 6 on Sakhalin — using a 200-item Swadesh list and established the first quantitative
basis for internal dialect classification hattorichiri1960 . The Ainu dialect
dictionary he subsequently edited hattori1964 covers the same 19 localities and
remained the foundational pan-dialectal lexical resource for decades; the current state of the
dialect classification that builds on that data is described in
dialect-sample-and-corpus-method . The Sakhalin variety was described in the
grammar of murasaki1979 , later re-edited and annotated by
dalcorso2021 . Asai Tōru produced a grammar of the Ishikari dialect
asai1969 , extending the descriptive record to a variety north of the Saru basin.

Tamura Suzuko — publishing through the 1950s and 1960s under her maiden name Fukuda — built
the Saru dialect into the best-described variety of Hokkaido Ainu. Her early articles on Saru
verbal morphology fukuda1956 , auxiliary verbs tamura1960 , and particle
systems fukuda1961 applied distributional criteria to morpheme classification,
departing from the semantic ordering of the Kindaichi-Chiri tradition. Refsing's comparison
shows that Tamura was among the first to delimit morpheme classes by formal and positional
behavior — for instance, restricting the possessed (belonging) form to a defined semantic class
of nouns, where Kindaichi had treated the opposition as applicable to all nouns
refsing1986 22, 42–47 . Her Saru-dialect dictionary tamura1996
remains the most comprehensive single-dialect Ainu dictionary and is used throughout this
grammar as a primary lexical resource. The English-language grammar
tamura2000 made the Saru description accessible to non-Japanese readership,
though it was not available to this project for direct consultation.

Refsing's 1986 monograph refsing1986 on the Shizunai dialect applied the
distributional programme more explicitly, organizing morpheme classes by positional evidence
alone and providing substantial coverage of a Hokkaido variety otherwise under-described in the
literature. Its introduction — a critical survey of the history of research through the early
post-war period — is the most thorough historiography of Ainu linguistics available in English
prior to the Handbook, and large parts of the historical account in the present chapter draw on
that survey.

Shibatani's 1990 chapter on Ainu shibatani1990 , in his typological survey of the
languages of Japan, situated the language within the cross-linguistic typological literature at
a scale that earlier descriptions had not attempted. He characterized Hokkaido Ainu as
head-marking, polysynthetic, SOV, and organized for evidential distinctions through a
nominalization-plus-copula construction — a characterization confirmed and refined by subsequent
scholarship; see typological-profile . His 1988 article shibatani1988
had placed Ainu noun incorporation within the cross-linguistic typology of polysynthesis,
establishing a framework for the incorporation analysis taken up in later work.

Satō Tomomi's grammar sato2008 is the most comprehensive Japanese-language
description since Tamura's, covering Hokkaido Ainu morphology, syntax, argument structure,
valency operations, and information structure, with particular depth on the Chitose variety.
Together with Tamura's Saru dictionary and grammar, it forms one of the two principal
Japanese-language reference works against which this grammar triangulates its analyses.

Bugaeva, Nakagawa, and the corpus-computational turn

The scholarship of the early 21st century has advanced on two fronts simultaneously: deeper
domain-specific analysis of grammatical subsystems, and the development of digitally accessible
annotated corpora. Article-length studies have addressed domains that the reference grammars
treated only summarily, and the resulting findings have in several cases revised or substantially
refined the standard analyses.

Bugaeva Anna's research has reshaped understanding of several grammatical subsystems. Her work
on Ainu applicatives bugaeva2006 bugaeva2010 provided a
cross-linguistically situated analysis of the three applicative prefixes
e- , ko- , and o- ; her
analysis of the antipassive bugaeva2021antip placed Ainu within the comparative
typology of antipassive constructions; and her work on possession
bugaeva2021poss and causatives bugaeva2014 has refined the standard
valency analysis. Her treatment of the diachrony of anticausatives bugaeva2025
extends the historical dimension of the valency description. The Handbook of the Ainu language
bugaeva2022 , which she edited, assembles specialist treatments of every major
domain: phonology shiraishi2022 , verbal valency
bugaevakobayashi2022 , dialectology nakagawafukazawa2022 , oral
literature endo2022 okuda2022 , historical and comparative linguistics
alonso2022 , and the revitalization movement ono2022 . The Handbook
constitutes the most comprehensive reference work on Ainu available in English.

Nakagawa Hiroshi's nakagawa2024 — アイヌ語広文典 ('An extensive
grammar of the Ainu language') — is the most recent single-author reference grammar of
Hokkaido Ainu. It surveys the full grammatical range from phonology through complex
clause-linkage, drawing primarily on Saru and Chitose data, and provides detailed paradigm
tables with Japanese translation throughout. It is the primary reference grammar for this work,
cited throughout by section. A critical review of its analyses and relation to earlier grammars
is available in sato2025b .

Domain-specific monographs and dissertations have contributed substantially alongside the
reference grammars. Dal Corso's dissertation on Ainu evidentiality dalcorso2018
provided a semantico-pragmatic account of the formal-noun evidential system and a
morphosyntactic analysis of its construction and clause-final frames. Historical phonology has
been examined by itabashi2001 , who established the diachronic correspondence
between Sakhalin Ainu vowel length and Hokkaido Ainu pitch accent. The Handbook chapter by
alonso2022 critically reviewed earlier Proto-Ainu reconstruction proposals,
including those of vovin1993 , and advanced a revised segmental and suprasegmental
inventory. Dialectological classification has been reconsidered by
nakagawafukazawa2022 and in subsequent quantitative analyses.

The documentary and corpus infrastructure of Ainu linguistics has grown substantially since the
1970s. The annotated oral-literature text editions published by Nakagawa and others, the NINJAL
Corpus of Ainu oral literature ninjal2003 , and the Glossed Audio Corpus
glossedcorpus2016 have made the oral-narrative tradition available in digitally
accessible, aligned, and partially glossed form. Natural-language-processing-oriented work,
including the philological study of Sino-Ainu materials by yasuoka2023 , marks a
further extension of the field's methodological range. The sources underpinning this grammar's
corpus layer are described in oral-literature-and-spoken-corpora and
dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

The revitalization movement and the emergence of new-speaker varieties constitute a distinct
strand of contemporary research. Its history is surveyed in ono2022 and treated
in depth in sociolinguistic-situation-revitalization . The typological profile of
the language is mapped in typological-profile , and the genetic-relationship
question is treated in genetic-position-macro-comparison .

title: Hokkaido Dialect Classification and Dialectometry
summary: The internal dialect divisions of Hokkaido Ainu, the classification debate, and the new statistical/dialectometric reanalyses.
part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Hokkaido Dialect Classification and Dialectometry
Part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
Summary: The internal dialect divisions of Hokkaido Ainu, the classification debate, and the new statistical/dialectometric reanalyses.

The Hokkaido dialect landscape

The Ainu communities documented across Hokkaido in the late nineteenth and twentieth
centuries show both continuous gradient variation and a set of discrete isoglosses whose
distributions do not coincide. Three research traditions have engaged this situation on
different terms: pre-war geography-based groupings; post-war quantitative lexicostatistics
producing numerical similarity matrices and dendrograms; and more recent statistical
reanalyses of those same matrices with spectral clustering and correspondence analysis,
which contest the canonical groupings at several points.

This chapter traces the classification debate and the phonological and morphological
isoglosses that underlie it. Phonological microvariation below the isogloss level —
consonantal allophony, vowel reduction patterns, and prosodic sandhi — is treated in
hokkaido-phonological-microvariation ; the pitch-accent placement rules in
pitch-accent-placement-rule ; the consonant inventory in
consonant-inventory . Word-geographic distributions of individual lexical
items and the dialect dictionary materials belong to
hokkaido-lexical-dialectology-and-the-dialect-atlas . The proportional
weighting of each variety in the aligned corpus used throughout this grammar is specified
in dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

The founding lexicostatistic study

hattorichiri1960 applied a 200-item Swadesh-based word list to
nineteen localities : thirteen Hokkaido sites — Yakumo, Oshamambe,
Horobetsu, Biratori, Nukibetsu, Niikappu, Samani, Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro, Asahikawa,
Nayoro, and Sōya — together with six Sakhalin sites and Torii's 1903 Kuril materials.
Cognacy was scored ternarily: matched, unmatched, or uncertain (uncertain = 0.5), and
lexical similarity was then the percentage of shared items. A practical constraint limits
the analysis: only 91 of the 200 items actually entered the count, since the remainder
showed no cross-dialect stem difference and were uninformative for comparison
nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.1–2.3 .

Four conclusions emerged from the Hattori–Chiri matrix
nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.1 . The gap between Hokkaido and Sakhalin
varieties constituted the sharpest division in the data. Within Hokkaido, Sōya was the
variety closest to Sakhalin. Internal Hokkaido varieties clustered into groupings:
{Yakumo, Oshamambe}; {Nukibetsu, Biratori, Niikappu}; and {Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro}.
An east–west gap opened at the Shizunai–Niikappu line — the same
boundary that had demarcated the eastern
Menas un kur 'people of the east' from the western
Sum un kur 'people of the west' during Shakushain's War in 1669
nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.1 .

Asai 1974 and the three-way Major Division

asai1974 recalculated the Hattori–Chiri data by cluster analysis, adding
Chitose, and proposed the grouping known as the Major Division . The
first split placed {Hokkaido + North Kuril} against {Sakhalin}; Hokkaido itself divided
into four zones: North (Sōya), East (Obihiro / Kushiro / Bihoro / Asahikawa / Nayoro),
Central-South (Yakumo / Oshamambe / Horobetsu / Biratori / Nukibetsu / Niikappu), and
a marginal position for Samani nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.2 . The North
Kuril group appeared closer to the NE-Hokkaido cluster than to the Central-South cluster.

Several features of the dataset limit what the cluster analysis can show
nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.4 . The Sea-of-Japan and Sea-of-Okhotsk coasts
have almost no post-Meiji speech records, and several "dialect" rows represent a single
consultant's idiolect rather than a community variety. The site matrix is therefore
geographically skewed and thin at the margins ‹contested›.

Tamura's Northeast–Southwest bipartition

The most widely cited informal classification, tamura1988 , moves beyond
lexicostatistics to incorporate morphological and particle-level indicators. Tamura
divides Hokkaido into two broad zones, with Shizunai on the boundary line:

Northeast : North (Sōya), Central (Teshio, Ishikari), East (Tokachi,
Kitami, Kushiro, Eastern Hidaka);

Southwest : South (Western Hidaka, Eastern Iburi), West (Shiribeshi).

The NE/SW axis also tracks the distribution of culture-hero names in oral literature —
Okikurmi in the southwest,
Samayekur in the northeast — and, more broadly, the genre
designations that differ between the two halves
nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.3, §6 . Whether the literary distribution
reflects linguistic divergence, independent areal diffusion, or some combination is
not settled by the available sources ‹speculative›.

Dialectometric reanalyses

Ono 2020: spectral clustering

ono2020 reanalyses Asai's cognacy matrix (110 items over 21 lects) with
Spectral Clustering (MinmaxCut / Mcut algorithm). The methodological argument is that
Asai's matrix is a commutative monoid : only addition and comparison of
cognacy percentages are linguistically meaningful; converting them to "dissimilarity"
by subtraction destroys information. Ono further shows that both of Asai's clustering
methods (large-linkage, small-linkage) lack identifiability — they produce
identical dendrograms for very different matrices because they ignore mid-range values.
Spectral Clustering does not share this limitation.

The result is two groups, not three: Hokkaido (including North Kuril)
versus Sakhalin . The three-way Major Division is not statistically
supported ‹contested›. Within the Hokkaido cluster, North Kuril patterns most strongly
with the northeast Hokkaido group — Samani, Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro, Sōya — while
southwest Hokkaido forms a second tier. Ono notes this converges with earlier work
placing North Kuril close to northeast Hokkaido, while a separate observation by Satō
and Bugaeva (via ono2020 §4 ) clusters 19th-century Kuril items with
the Saru/Chitose zone — a tension the 2020 study does not resolve ‹contested›. Ono
also cautions that Asai's cluster analysis may never have been intended to
establish a canonical classification; its standard status in subsequent
literature may exceed its original scope.

Fukazawa and Ono 2024: boundary dialects

fukazawaono2024 takes a further methodological step: rather than
assigning dialects to groups — which forces a decision about where "boundary
dialects" belong — the framework selects a reference dialect , computes
similarity from all other lects to that centre, and visualises each variety's
"central-dialect-likeness" via correspondence analysis (対応分析)
and projection pursuit (射影追跡法). Applied to Ainu with Sōya (the
Sakhalin-facing margin) and Asahikawa (the NE–SW junction) as reference centres, the
approach reframes these "boundary dialects" as analytically primary objects rather
than residue from a grouping procedure. The published abstract reports that the
isogloss topology around Sōya and Asahikawa is more complex than any single
bipartition captures; the full empirical dataset has not been assessed here
‹speculative›.

Kasuga 2026: 75-locality quantitative classification

kasuga2026 extends the quantitative tradition to 75 localities ,
substantially broadening the geographic coverage of the Hattori–Chiri / Asai matrix. The
study — published in Japanese — has not been assessed in full for this chapter; its
specific sub-groupings and methodological choices remain to be synthesised ‹speculative›.

Classification traditions compared

Study
Method
Primary split
Internal Hokkaido grouping
Assessment

hattorichiri1960
Lexicostatistics (200-item list, 19 sites)
HA vs SA (large gap)
SW cluster; NE cluster; Shizunai boundary
Foundational data; only 91/200 items informative; site coverage skewed

asai1974
Cluster analysis (Hattori–Chiri + Chitose)
{HA + KA} vs SA
North (Sōya) / East / Central-South / Samani (marginal)
‹contested›: statistical method questioned by Ono 2020

tamura1988
Typological survey (morphology + particles)
NE vs SW within HA
NE {Sōya; Ishikari; Tokachi/Kitami/Kushiro/E.Hidaka} vs SW {W.Hidaka/E.Iburi; Shiribeshi}
Widely used descriptive baseline; incorporates morphological indicators absent from lexicostatistic studies

ono2020
Spectral clustering (Asai 1974 matrix)
HA (+KA) vs SA
NE-HA + N.Kuril cluster; SW-HA second tier
‹contested›: Major Division not supported; N.Kuril affiliation within HA remains unresolved

fukazawaono2024
Correspondence analysis + projection pursuit
Sōya and Asahikawa as reference centres
In progress; boundary-dialect topology primary
‹speculative›: boundary-dialect reframing; full empirical dataset not yet assessed

kasuga2026
Quantitative classification (75 localities)
Not yet assessed
75 sites; substantially expands geographic coverage of prior matrices
‹speculative›: sub-groupings and methodology not yet synthesised here

Phonological isoglosses

The isogloss data in this section draw on
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3, Tables 3–15 , whose underlying documentation
comes from hattori1964 and hattorichiri1960 (both reported
sources). The pitch-accent placement rules are developed in
pitch-accent-placement-rule .

Pitch-accent types

Three accent types are attested across Hokkaido
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 :

Standard (Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, Asahikawa, Nayoro, Obihiro,
Sōya): two placement rules — a closed first syllable takes high pitch on σ1; an
open first syllable takes high pitch on σ2.

Irregular (Yakumo, Oshamambe): when both σ1 and σ2 are open,
high pitch falls on σ3. Saru ipérekut corresponds to
Yakumo iperékut ; Saru
hopúni to Yakumo hopuní .

Unaccented (Shizunai, Urakawa, Samani, Bihoro, Kushiro,
Shiranuka): pitch is non-distinctive. Samani tends toward final-syllable high;
Shizunai shows no syllable-position relation to pitch at all. The origin of the
unaccented type in Hokkaido differs from the Sakhalin unaccented system, where
the driver is loss of vowel length rather than loss of pitch (see
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex ).

Word-initial h-elision

In the eastern standard-accent dialects, initial
h is elided when the pitch accent falls on the second syllable
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.2, Table 4 : Saru/Horobetsu
huráye 'wash' →
uráye in Asahikawa/Nayoro; Saru
hokú 'husband' → okú ;
hekáci 'child' → ekáci . The
conditioning is accentual and areal: Horobetsu, Biratori, Nukibetsu, Niikappu,
Samani, and Sōya retain initial h , while Obihiro, Kushiro,
Bihoro, Asahikawa, and Nayoro do not.

Regressive consonant assimilation

The eastern dialects, most extensively Bihoro, show regressive consonant assimilation
at morpheme and compound boundaries
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.3, Table 5 :
hotke 'lie' →
hokke (Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro);
sirsesek 'hot' →
sissesek (Bihoro, Asahikawa, Nayoro);
tupte → tutte (Bihoro);
upsor → ussor 'bosom'. The active
patterns include tk → kk ,
pt → tt ,
rs → ss ,
ps → ss ,
kp → pp , and
kt → tt .
nakagawafukazawa2022 treats these as historical word-form changes rather
than productive synchronic sandhi rules. A community reading, noting that Bihoro's
patterns are non-conditional and lexeme-specific, proposes them as evidence for a
distinct underlying Proto-Ainu consonant series
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, November 2024); the proposal goes
beyond the sources consulted and awaits verification ‹speculative›.

The par/car pseudo-correspondence

A small set of words in the mouth-region semantic field shows a geographic alternation:
par (southwest) : car (northeast and
Kuril) for 'mouth'; papus / patoy :
capus / catoy for 'lip'. Nayoro and Asahikawa attest both
forms. The alternation affects the labial–alveolar onset position within this
semantic field only; there is no comparable p-/c- alternation
for e-, i-, o-, u- initial words, which is why
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4, §5.1 call it a
pseudo-correspondence rather than a sound correspondence in the comparative
linguistic sense.

Place-name evidence from kirikae1994 supplies a geographic profile: in
the Ishikari watershed, par -type toponyms predominate (11 : 0
against car -type), while Nemuro–Kushiro coast place names are
exclusively car -type (13 occurrences). Community observation
independently noted that place names favour car broadly
across the northeast and the Kuril data
aynucorporadiscord (nishiyasu, May 2026), consistent with
kirikae1994 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Four analyses of the alternation appear in the literature, and they are genuinely
incompatible ‹contested›:

Diachronic merger (Kindaichi, Chiri, Naert 1958, via
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ): either
pa- > ca- historically, or both trace to a single
proto-form *Xa- .

Two lexical items from the start (Kirikae 1994, via
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ): Proto-Ainu already had distinct
p- and c- 'mouth' words, later
sorted geographically.

ca- as older (Nakagawa 1996, via
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ):
ca- is attested inside
par -region place names (e.g.
sanca or ta ), suggesting it was the earlier form in that
area too.

Separate morphemes ab initio (Fukazawa 2014/2017, via
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ):
ca- and pa- are independent
morphemes throughout their full distribution —
pa- also means 'head'; irreducible lexical pairs exist
( can : pan 'thin',
cas : pas 'run').

alonso2022 §2.2.6 adds a reconstruction perspective: the split
reflects a palatalized series in Proto-Ainu, with
*pʸaɾ as the reconstructed form and
*pʸ > ca- in one regional stream,
*p > pa- in another. This account integrates the
two-morpheme view with the palatalized-series hypothesis (see
proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction ), but Alonso himself flags the full
palatalized series as typologically motivated rather than philologically forced
‹speculative›.

Further phonological alternations

Additional phonological isoglosses documented by
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.5–3.8, Tables 7–17 include:

-iw : -uy metathesis — Bihoro and
Kushiro: siwnin → suynin 'green';
ciw → cuy 'stab';
nociw → nocuy 'star' (Kushiro);
riwka → ruyka 'bridge'.

Vowel alternations — u:o (Sōya
opas : standard upas 'snow');
o:e (Yakumo etop :
otop 'hair');
V(e,u):i (Samani / eastern
sitá : setá 'dog').

Consonant alternations — y:w (Yakumo
puwar, nuwe vs standard
puyar, nuye ); n:m
( nimak : mimak 'tooth' — Yakumo, Nukibetsu, and Niikappu
use mimak ; Sōya
ónke : ómke 'cough').

-y elision and extension — Saru/Chitose:
plural existential okay →
oka , extended to the pronoun
ciokay → coka . Eastern dialects
(Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro) show the inverse: particles
ka and korka acquire
-kay extensions
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.7, Table 17 . Community observation
confirms ka → kay as a Kushiro-diagnostic marker
aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, February 2026) ‹corpus-suggested›.

No phonological isogloss replicates the NE/SW bipartition exactly; each cuts the map
at a slightly different line, which is the fundamental reason that
"bundle of isoglosses" descriptions are more accurate than a single dividing line.

Morphological isoglosses

Person-marking type

The sharpest morphological isogloss separates Saru and Chitose from
every other Hokkaido variety. In Saru and Chitose, transitive-verb person marking is
carried entirely by prefixes; all other Hokkaido dialects use
circumfixes — an object prefix before the stem and a subject suffix
after it nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.1 . The 'I give to you' cell
illustrates the contrast: Saru/Chitose
eci=kore (prefix only) versus Horobetsu
e=kore=as and Ishikari
e=kore=an (circumfix in both).

eci=kore
2SG.O=give"
tr="I give it to you."
constructed
dial="SAR"
note="Saru-Chitose prefix-only transitive person marking: the portmanteau eci= is the sole marker for all first-person-A–second-person-O combinations. Horobetsu uses the circumfix e=kore=as; Ishikari e=kore=an. After Nakagawa & Fukazawa (2022: §4.1)."
/>

The implication is that Horobetsu patterns morphologically with the geographically
distant Ishikari and Tokachi varieties, not with the adjacent Saru region
nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.1 ‹contested›. The basic-vocabulary
lexicostatistic matrix does not capture this: Hattori–Chiri's 200-item list excluded
grammatical words and inflectional paradigms, so the morphological and lexical
isogloss pictures diverge at this point. Horobetsu is lexically Central-South but
morphologically northeast. The full person-marking paradigm and the architectural
implications are treated in person-marking-architecture .

Plural marking

The verbal plural morpheme shows a three-way geographic split
nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.2 :

Sakhalin :
(a)hci / hcin (the ‹SA› form; see
sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast );

Shizunai, Horobetsu, Shiranuka :
ci ;

Saru, Chitose, Ishikari :
pa .

The historical direction is recoverable:
ci is the older Hokkaido form, cognate with Sakhalin
(a)hci , and pa is an innovation that
spread within the Saru–Ishikari corridor, displacing
ci there
nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.2 . The verbal number system that houses
these morphemes and their interaction with the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form paradigm are treated in
person-marking-architecture .

Particles and grammatical words

Several grammatical morphemes distribute along isoglosses that match neither the
NE/SW bipartition nor the lexicostatistic clusters, illustrating how the different
layers of dialectal organisation fail to converge
nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.3, Tables 21–24 .

The allative particle ('toward') takes four attested forms:

un — Yakumo, Horobetsu, Chitose, Biratori, Shizunai;

en — Urakawa, Tokachi, Eastern Hidaka;

(e)ne — Shiranuka, Kushiro, Nemuro, Bihoro, Asahikawa,
Sōya, and also Sakhalin and Kuril;

ekota — an Asahikawa alternative form.

The examples below illustrate two of the attested allative forms in running discourse:

mac-ihi sapa-ha or un inkar | wife-POSS head-POSS LOC ALLAT look | (He) looked toward his wife's head. | Horobetsu allative un. Nakagawa & Fukazawa (2022: ex. 4), from a Chiri-lineage Horobetsu text. | nakagawafukazawa2022 | HOR

nean nupuri or en oman kor | that mountain LOC ALLAT go CONJ | when (we) went toward that mountain | Tokachi allative en. Nakagawa & Fukazawa (2022: ex. 5), from Sawai 1995: 61. | nakagawafukazawa2022 | TOK

The juxtapositional particle
newa (and/with) is absent in Saru and Chitose, where bare
juxtaposition does the same work. The successive particle
tek functions as a conjunction ('and then') in Shizunai,
Samani, Tokachi, Kushiro, Shiranuka, and Ishikari, but as an auxiliary meaning 'do
briefly' in Saru, Chitose, and Horobetsu; the Sakhalin cognate
teh is the older form (see
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex ). The
conditional particle shows a three-way split:
ko (Abuta, Horobetsu, Samani, Kushiro, Shiranuka) vs
kor (Tokachi, Ishikari, Saru, Chitose) vs Shizunai
konno (< kor-no );
ko is reconstructed as the older form
nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.3 . The
cohortative particle is ro in Saru, Chitose,
Horobetsu, Yakumo, Asahikawa, Nayoro, and Sōya;
rok in Asahikawa, Nayoro, and Obihiro; and
no in Urakawa and Samani.

The imperative particle
ya (singular) is specific to Ishikari — another morphological
feature that cross-cuts the lexical clusters
nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.3 .

Distribution types in linguistic geography

nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 , drawing on Nakagawa 1996, classifies the
geographic distribution patterns of Hokkaido isoglosses into six types:

Eastern–Western (approximating the NE/SW bipartition):
par / car 'mouth';
hempak / nempak 'how many';
hempara / nempara 'when'. The western form
nempara itself derives by analogy with the
ne- indefinite root ( h- → n- ) and is
therefore a secondary innovation even within the western zone.

ABA (peripheral conservatism, central innovation):
aw (peripheral) vs parunpe (central)
'tongue'. The compound parunpe (lit. 'mouth-inside-thing')
spread from the par -region and displaced
aw in the centre while the older form survived at the edges.

Saru–Chitose island : interrogatives
hemanta 'what', hunna 'who'; plural
existential oka ; indefinites
nep / nen ; prefix-only person marking. These features cluster
in Saru–Chitose but are absent or replaced in surrounding varieties.

Saru–Chitose + Sakhalin : the interrogative
hemata 'what' and the prefix
eci- '1→2' appear in Saru–Chitose and distant Sakhalin but
not in the geographically intervening Hokkaido varieties — a bridge distribution that
nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 describe as "hard to believe independent",
though the historical mechanism is unresolved ‹speculative›.

Pacific Coast : mici 'father' in a strip
running from the Kuriles through Horobetsu, crossing the east–west divide; the
place-name cluster koyka / koypok (Yamada 1982) follows a
similar path.

Ishikari Watershed : isolated innovations within an inland corridor,
e.g. iyapo 'father' at the Saru river mouth.

The interrogative morphology receives special attention in
nakagawafukazawa2022 §5.2 : most Hokkaido interrogatives are
historically indefinite pronouns repurposed via focus particles, while Saru and Chitose
retain a two-stem system with a distinct interrogative root
hVm / hVn and an indefinite root
ne- . Community discussion independently segmented
hunna as hun + na ,
hunak as hun + yak , and
hempak as hem + pak
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, December 2023), corroborated by the sources.

A community-proposed case for a 'bone' ABA distribution is worth noting:
pone in Hokkaido versus
poni in Sakhalin and Kuril, with La Pérouse 1787
Pauni as a candidate early Sakhalin attestation
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, June 2026 ‹speculative›). The ABA
reasoning is methodologically sound, but the forms and site attestations require
independent verification before the proposal can be taken further.

The Saru–Chitose group and its standing

Saru and Chitose emerge from all classification approaches as a coherent subgroup,
though the extent to which this distinguishes them from all other Hokkaido varieties or
only from their immediate neighbours remains contested. The lexicostatistic matrix places
Saru and Chitose together with Biratori, Nukibetsu, and Niikappu in the Central-South
cluster. Morphological features — prefix-only transitive marking, plural
pa , the contracted pronouns
kani (< kuani ) and
coka (< ciokay ), distinct interrogative
stems, and the 4th-person singular pronoun
asinuma — constitute a cluster that maps poorly onto the lexical
groupings nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.1, §5 ‹contested›.

The Saru–Chitose + Sakhalin bridge (shared
hemata , eci- ) is the outstanding puzzle
in the classification. Parallel independent retention of an archaic form, coastal-route
areal contact, or early divergence before the northeast varieties had innovated are all
consistent with the distribution. The genetic relationship between Hokkaido and Sakhalin
and the historical branching order are discussed in
sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast and
internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways .

The Sakhalin and Kuril branches as comparative benchmarks

Sakhalin and Kuril data appear in the classification literature as calibration points
for Hokkaido-internal distances. The one result robust across all methods is that the
Hokkaido cluster is more internally cohesive than its gap from Sakhalin
ono2020 §3 ; janhunen2022 §3b .
janhunen2022 §3b places Sakhalin's divergence from Common Ainu at
roughly 800 years and Kuril's at under 600, consistent with the Satsumon-culture
migration chronology — Kuril is historically as well as structurally closer to Hokkaido.

The Sakhalin-specific features that calibrate the gap — final-consonant debuccalization
( sik 'eye' → ‹SA› sis ;
cup 'sun/moon' → ‹SA› cuh ), the
vowel-length to pitch-accent correspondence, and the Nairo
r- → t- archaism — are documented in full in
sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast and
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex . The reconstruction of the
stage from which Hokkaido, Sakhalin, and Kuril all diverged is the subject of
proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction . Northern contact (Nivkh, Tungusic,
Manchu) is primarily a Sakhalin phenomenon and is treated in
northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu .

Coverage and the Hokkaido documentary record

All quantitative classifications rest on the matrix of
hattorichiri1960 , collected at thirteen Hokkaido sites. The matrix has
documented limitations nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.4 . The Sea-of-Japan
and Sea-of-Okhotsk coasts lack post-Meiji speech records; only one northern site, Sōya,
is included, and it was documented from a single consultant. Several "dialect" rows
represent a single speaker's idiolect rather than a community variety. Place-name
evidence from kirikae1994 supplements documentary records for areas without
spoken materials, but place-name attestations and 20th-century speech data are
chronologically incommensurable.

The corpus used throughout this grammar (209,878 sentences) is weighted heavily toward
Saru (77,964 sentences) and Shizunai (43,346), with all other varieties together making
up the remainder. Eastern and northern forms — Tokachi allative
en , the Kushiro particle extension
kay — appear too infrequently for distributional analysis. The
corpus weighting and its implications for frequency counts are specified in
dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

The philological programme initiated by fukazawa2017 and the Chiri-family
manuscript archive offer one corrective: historical documents (Kaga-ke, Matsuura,
Mamiya) and the Hattori 1964 comparative dialect dictionary preserve regional forms not
captured in post-WWII speech data. Lexical dialectology drawing on these sources is
treated in hokkaido-lexical-dialectology-and-the-dialect-atlas .

title: Lexical Dialectology and the Dialect Atlas
summary: Lexical microvariation across Hokkaido, the comparative dictionaries and Swadesh-style datasets, and the dialect atlas underpinning classification.
part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Lexical Dialectology and the Dialect Atlas
Part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
Summary: Lexical microvariation across Hokkaido, the comparative dictionaries and Swadesh-style datasets, and the dialect atlas underpinning classification.

The survey apparatus and its primary data

The systematic comparison of lexical variation across Hokkaido Ainu rests on a survey carried
out by Hattori Shirō and Chiri Mashiho in 1960. Working from a 200-item basic-vocabulary list
elicited at 19 localities — 13 across Hokkaido and 6 in Sakhalin ‹SA› — together with Torii's
1903 Kuril ‹KU› materials, they scored each item for cross-dialectal cognacy as positive,
negative, or partial ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.1 ). The 13 Hokkaido localities
were Yakumo, Oshamambe, Horobetsu, Biratori, Nukibetsu, Niikappu, Samani, Obihiro, Kushiro,
Bihoro, Asahikawa, Nayoro, and Sōya. The resulting cognacy percentages supplied the first
quantified measure of lexical distance between Hokkaido varieties and remain the empirical layer
beneath every subsequent dialectometric classification.

The survey's reach is narrower than the item count suggests. Of the 200 elicited items, only
91 actually contributed to the cognacy matrix — the remaining 109 showed no cross-dialectal
stem difference and therefore carried no discriminating information ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.3 ). Those 91 variable items are the effective data for Hokkaido lexical dialectology: a
diagnostically rich but typologically restricted window, concentrated on body parts, basic
environmental vocabulary, and core verbal concepts.

Two further resources expand and document the survey results. Hattori's 1964 dialect dictionary
( hattori1964 ) organized the comparative material across the 19 localities into a
single reference work, giving paradigmatic forms and attestations for each item. Fukazawa
(2025) has since brought to light a previously unpublished document: Chiri Mashiho's own
handwritten and annotated copy of the basic-vocabulary survey tables, preserved at the National
Ainu Museum ( fukazawa2025 ). These tables, covering at minimum the Yakumo,
Oshamambe, and Horobetsu localities, carry Chiri's marginal variant readings and hesitation
marks, providing a check on the published cognacy scores and revealing forms that may have been
suppressed in the final scoring.

Coverage and documentation gaps

The geographic distribution of the 13 Hokkaido localities is uneven. The Pacific coast and the
Ishikari–Hidaka corridor are represented by multiple sites; the Sea-of-Japan coast contributes
only Yakumo, and the Sea-of-Okhotsk coast only Bihoro. The northernmost point, Sōya, was
effectively a single speaker — Ben Kashiwagi — so its data reflect an idiolect rather than a
community sample ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.4 ). Many areas that certainly held
Ainu-speaking populations into the nineteenth century, particularly along the Sea-of-Japan
coast, left no post-Meiji lexical record at all ‹corpus-suggested›.

Kasuga's 2026 expansion to 75 localities, incorporating place-name evidence and historical
documents, begins to fill some of these gaps ( kasuga2026 ), but systematic
multi-speaker lexical data from the poorly attested regions remain largely absent. The place-name
record is geographically broader than the spoken-language survey, though it introduces its own
complications: toponyms reflect an earlier language state, their orthographic conventions vary
across sources, and phonological changes may have operated differently on frozen names than on
the living lexicon ‹speculative›.

Isogloss types and geographic distribution

Mapping the 91 variable items across the 13 survey localities produces several recurrent
distribution patterns. Nakagawa and Fukazawa, building on Nakagawa's earlier geographic
analysis, distinguish five types ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 ):

distribution type
example items
geographic reading

Eastern / Western

par /
car 'mouth';
hempak /
nempak 'how many';
hempara /
nempara 'when'

the primary Hokkaido split, broadly matching Tamura's NE/SW division

ABA (peripheral preservation)

aw (margins) vs
parunpe (center) 'tongue'

a central innovation has displaced the older form except at the geographic periphery;
read as retention at the edges

Saru–Chitose specific

hemanta 'what',
hunna 'who',
oka 'exist (pl)',
nep 'something'

Saru–Chitose innovations not shared across the NE/SW divide

Pacific Coast continuity
mici 'father' (Kuriles to Horobetsu)
coastal diffusion cutting across the NE/SW divide

Ishikari Watershed
iyapo 'father' (near Saru river mouth)
geographically isolated inland innovation

These five types do not bundle neatly into coinciding isoglosses. Each lexical item can produce
a different boundary, with particular localities appearing in opposite groups depending on
which item is considered. This non-congruence is consistent with a dialect continuum undergoing
diffusion from multiple centers rather than a single ancestral branching event ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 ; ono2020 §3 ) ‹contested›.

The eastern–western lexical divide

The most robust lexical signal dividing northeastern from southwestern Hokkaido is a set of
interrogative and temporal stems. The southwestern dialects (including Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu,
and Yakumo) use interrogative stems beginning in
hem- / hen- : 'when' is
hempara and 'how many' is hempak . The
northeastern dialects (Asahikawa, Nayoro, Sōya, Obihiro, Kushiro, and Bihoro) use
ne- -initial forms: nempara and
nempak ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §5.1 ). The Saru–Chitose
group adds its own specific interrogatives: hemanta 'what',
hunna 'who'.

Nakagawa and Fukazawa read the interrogative distribution as a diachronic contrast. The
Saru–Chitose group preserves two etymologically distinct stems — an interrogative
hVm- / hVn- base and a separate indefinite
ne- base — while most northeastern dialects generalized the indefinite
stem to interrogative function, yielding ne-mpara by analogy with other
ne- indefinites ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §5.2 ). On that
analysis the southwestern and Saru–Chitose patterns are retentions and the northeastern forms
are innovations ‹contested›. Community discussion at the aynu-corpora Discord independently
arrived at the same two-stem analysis — interrogative hem /
hun versus indefinite ne — and further
segmented hunna as hun + na and hempak as hem +
pak ( aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli 2023-12-08, proposed).
The segmentation is corroborated by the comparative data in Nakagawa and Fukazawa's analysis
‹corpus-confirmed›.

The allative particle as a morphological isogloss

A morphological item that closely parallels the lexical NE/SW divide is the allative postposition.
Southwestern dialects use un ; eastern Hidaka and Tokachi use
en ; and the northern and far-eastern dialects (Sōya, Asahikawa, Bihoro,
Kushiro, Nemuro) use ne or its extended form, with Asahikawa
additionally using ekota ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.3 ).
Because this particle distribution matches no other lexical or morphological isogloss exactly,
it functions as one of the single clearest indicators for locating an undescribed text within the
Hokkaido dialect area.

allative form
localities

un
Yakumo, Horobetsu, Chitose, Biratori, Shizunai

en
Urakawa, Tokachi, eastern Hidaka

(e)ne
Shiranuka, Kushiro, Nemuro, Bihoro, Asahikawa, Nayoro, Sōya

ekota
Asahikawa (as alternative)

nean nupuri or en oman kor | that mountain place ALLAT go while | when (they) went toward that mountain | Tokachi allative en. The locative noun or 'place' takes the allative en as a postposition; the verb oman 'go' is not suppletively plural here. | nakagawafukazawa2022 | TOK | Sawai 1995: 61, cited as example 5 in Nakagawa & Fukazawa 2022

mac-ihi sapa-ha or un inkar | wife-POSS head-POSS place ALLAT look | looking toward (at) my wife's head | Horobetsu allative un, contrasting with Tokachi en. The affiliative suffixes -ihi and -ha mark inalienable possession on wife and head respectively. | nakagawafukazawa2022 | HOR | Y. Chiri 2004: 40, cited as example 4 in Nakagawa & Fukazawa 2022

The allative alternation is treated as a morphological isogloss here rather than a purely
lexical one because the variation involves bound grammatical morphemes, not independent lexical
stems. Its geographic distribution nevertheless contributes to the same east–west picture
that the basic-vocabulary survey drew, and the two types of variation align without being
identical — a further demonstration that no single isogloss bundle defines the Hokkaido dialect
area as a whole.

The par / car 'mouth' isogloss

Among the lexical differences documented by the survey, the word for 'mouth' has attracted the
most analytical attention. Southwestern Hokkaido dialects use
par mouth and its affiliative form paro ;
northeastern dialects, Sakhalin ‹SA›, and Kuril ‹KU› varieties use
car mouth and caro . The related body-part terms follow
the same division: 'lip' is papus / capus or
patoy / catoy depending on region ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ).

The contrast superficially resembles a regular sound correspondence between a southwestern
/p/-initial and a northeastern /c/-initial dialect group, but it does not behave like one.
No other set of unrelated Hokkaido words shows the same /p/ ∼ /c/ alternation in any
systematic pattern; the variation is restricted to the 'mouth' semantic cluster and a small
number of body-part and motion terms ( can / pan
'thin'; cas / pas 'run'). Nakagawa and Fukazawa
therefore call it a "pseudo-correspondence": it is geographically distributed like a sound
change but is not phonologically general ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ).

Four analytical positions have been advanced for the origin of the split, all reported here via
Nakagawa and Fukazawa ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ):

Single-change account (Kindaichi, Chiri, Naert 1958): a historical change
*pa > ca propagated through the northeastern region, or both derive
from a single ancestral form *Xa .

Two-'mouth'-words account (Kirikae 1994): Proto-Ainu already had two distinct
words for 'mouth'; their regional distribution is a fossilized retention of this original
synonymy.

Age-area / ABA account (Nakagawa 1996):
car is the older form, preserved at the northeastern periphery and in
‹SA›/‹KU›, and retained in Saru-region place names (e.g.
sancaorta ); par is a Saru-Hidaka innovation
that spread through the southwest, eventually producing the compound
par-unpe 'tongue' (lit. 'that-which-lies-in-the-par-region').

Two-morpheme account (Fukazawa 2014/2017):
ca- and pa- were separate morphemes from the
outset, each appearing across a range of body-part and spatial vocabulary
( pa- also meaning 'head'), so the alternation reflects a productive
morphemic distinction rather than a lexical split or sound change.

All four accounts rest on the same survey dataset and on place-name evidence with its own
chronological uncertainties; none can be ruled out on the available materials ‹contested›.
Place-name counts — 11:0 in favor of par- in the Ishikari basin, 13
instances of car- in Nemuro and Kushiro combined (Kirikae 1994, reported
via nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ) — are consistent with a sharp geographic
boundary but do not settle the mechanism. A community discussion at the aynu-corpora Discord
(nishiyasu 2026-05-07, proposed) independently observed that place-name distributions favor
car in the northeast and east, strengthening the place-name evidence
without resolving the analytical question ( aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›.

A further phonological account, not among the four original proposals, reconstructs a
Proto-Ainu palatalized initial *pʸaɾ , whose northeastern reflex yielded
/c/ while the southwestern region generalized plain /p/ (reported via
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ) ‹speculative›. A community member (nukopoli
2023-12-08) independently proposed a related palatalization pathway via a reconstructed
intermediate *ti ( aynucorporadiscord , proposed) ‹speculative›;
the two mechanisms converge in positing a palatal intermediate but differ in the structural
details.

ABA distributions and retention at the periphery

Several variable items show an ABA distribution: the same form occurs at the geographic
margins of Hokkaido — both east and west — while a different form occupies the center. Under
the wave-model reading standard in Japanese dialectology and adopted here by Nakagawa (reported
via nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 ), the peripheral form (A) is typically the older
retention and the central form (B) is an innovation that has not yet completed its spread to
the edges.

The word for 'tongue' is the primary example. The peripheral dialects — Yakumo in the
southwest, Kushiro and Bihoro in the east — retain aw tongue . The central
dialects, including Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, and Asahikawa, use
parunpe , a transparent compound: literally 'that which lies inside the
par region / the mouth-interior'. The ABA reading takes
aw as the form reconstructable for pre-innovation Hokkaido and
parunpe as an innovation coined in the par -using
southwestern area and spreading outward without displacing aw at the
farthest edges ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 ) ‹speculative›. That interpretation
is also consistent with the Nakagawa 1996 age-area account of the
par / car alternation: if
par 'mouth' is itself an innovation displacing car ,
then parunpe 'tongue' ( par + locative/relational
element) is a secondary compound built on that innovation and will share the same geographic
profile — which is exactly what the survey data show ‹speculative›.

The 'father' terms offer a second, less clear-cut case. The form
mici appears along the Pacific Coast arc from the Kuril Islands through
the eastern coast to Horobetsu, a distribution that cuts across the NE/SW divide. The form
iyapo is recorded at an isolated inland point near the Saru river mouth.
Yamada's proposed "Pacific Tribe" — a hypothetical coastal population whose lexical traces are
visible in forms like mici and in place-name elements
( koyka / koypok ) — is a speculative
interpretation of this coastal continuity (reported via nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 )
‹speculative›.

Semantic field variation beyond the basic list

The 91-item variation set is limited to high-frequency basic vocabulary. Systematic
cross-dialectal comparison in other semantic fields — animal and plant names, technological
vocabulary, ceremony-related terms — is absent from the survey, and the dialect dictionary
( hattori1964 ) covers the same 19 localities but does not extend systematically
beyond the survey items. The domain of place names has been used as a partial supplement: because
toponyms encode older vocabulary and are geographically pinnable, they allow isoglosses to be
reconstructed for areas with no living-speaker data.

Fukazawa's 2025 edition of Chiri Mashiho's annotated survey tables ( fukazawa2025 )
adds a qualitative dimension to the Yakumo, Oshamambe, and Horobetsu data. Chiri's marginal
notes reveal the range of variant forms that a skilled lexicographer heard during elicitation
but collapsed in the final binary cognacy score — information that the published 1960 dataset
conceals. This document is a significant resource for reassessing the resolution of the survey
data, though it covers only three southwestern localities and cannot compensate for the absent
data from underattested regions.

One kinship term deserves note as a clear inter-dialectal lexical isogloss outside the
basic-vocabulary list: the terms for 'father' (see above, § aba-patterns ) and the
broader kinship vocabulary in general have not been systematically mapped across the survey
localities. Regional variation in kinship terminology is attested in scattered sources but
lacks a comprehensive comparative treatment comparable to the basic-vocabulary survey ‹corpus-suggested›.

Saru–Chitose as a lexically distinct group

Within the southwestern zone, the Saru–Chitose group shows a cluster of lexical and
morphosyntactic innovations that set it apart from neighboring Horobetsu and Biratori, even
though basic-vocabulary cognacy alone would place Saru, Chitose, and Horobetsu in a common
southwestern group. The distinctively Saru–Chitose items include the interrogative forms
hemanta 'what' and hunna 'who', the existential
plural oka , the indefinites nep 'something'
and nen 'someone', and the prefix eci- 'I to
you (pl)' ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 ).

Strikingly, several of these Saru–Chitose forms recur in ‹SA› Sakhalin: the form
hemata (the Sakhalin cognate of hemanta ) 'what'
and the prefix eci- appear in both Saru–Chitose and ‹SA›, yet are
absent from much of northeast Hokkaido. Nakagawa and Fukazawa find this distribution "hard to
believe [is] independent" and leave it as an unresolved puzzle about the internal phylogeny of
Ainu ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 ) ‹contested›. One reading is that these forms
are innovations shared by an ancestral coastal or southern group before the Sakhalin branch
diverged; another is that they are archaisms preserved separately in Saru–Chitose and ‹SA›
while the northeast innovated. Neither account has been demonstrated from the available data.

Lexical data and dialectometric classification

The cognacy matrix computed from the 91 variable items has been the primary input for the main
proposals about how Hokkaido dialects group. Asai's 1974 cluster analysis of this matrix
proposed a partition into northern (Sōya), eastern (Obihiro / Kushiro / Bihoro / Asahikawa /
Nayoro), central-southern (Yakumo / Oshamambe / Horobetsu / Biratori / Nukibetsu / Niikappu),
and eastern-Hidaka (Samani) zones for Hokkaido, together with a major split between the
Hokkaido+Kuril group and Sakhalin (reported via nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.2 ; ono2020 §2 ).

Ono's 2020 statistical reanalysis demonstrates that the claimed three-way "Major Division" of
Hokkaido / North Kuril / Sakhalin is not supported when methodologically appropriate algorithms
are applied to Asai's original data. Asai's linkage methods (single-linkage and complete-linkage
agglomerative clustering) ignore mid-range cognacy values that are precisely where the evidence
for sub-groupings lies; spectral clustering over the same matrix yields two groups — Hokkaido
plus North Kuril vs Sakhalin — without any robust internal three-way split ( ono2020 §3–4 ) ‹contested›. Within the Hokkaido+Kuril group, North Kuril clusters with the northeastern
Hokkaido localities (Samani, Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro, Sōya) rather than with the southwestern
group, while the southwestern and Saru–Chitose varieties form the second sub-cluster. Ono
also notes that Asai (1974) may never have intended to canonize a tripartite dialect
classification, yet his result became the standard reference beyond its original purpose.

Fukazawa and Ono's 2024 paper takes a complementary methodological direction ( fukazawaono2024 ). Rather than assigning each locality to a group, it maps the similarity of every Hokkaido
point to two "boundary" reference dialects — Sōya (the Hokkaido/Sakhalin boundary variety) and
Asahikawa (the NE/SW boundary variety) — through correspondence analysis combined with
projection pursuit. This dissolves the unresolvable question of which group a boundary dialect
belongs to and instead treats geographic gradience as the object of description. The resulting
picture is one of overlapping similarity fields converging on the boundary localities rather than
a set of discrete blocs.

A persistent tension runs through the classification literature: lexical and morphosyntactic
isoglosses do not always converge on the same groupings. The clearest case is Horobetsu.
Basic-vocabulary cognacy places Saru, Chitose, and Horobetsu together in a southwestern cluster;
person-marking morphology (Horobetsu uses prefix–suffix circumfixes while Saru–Chitose use
prefix-only marking) places Horobetsu with the geographically distant Ishikari and Tokachi
varieties ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.1 ) ‹contested›. Lexical distance is the
broadest signal; finer grammatical features track different, partly independent histories. The
morphosyntactic dimension is developed in hokkaido-phonological-microvariation , and
the full statistical classification debate in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry .

The Sakhalin and Kuril data as comparative evidence

The 1960 survey included six Sakhalin localities and the Torii 1903 Kuril materials alongside
the 13 Hokkaido points, giving the lexical comparisons a triangulable external dimension. For
the present chapter, two findings are directly relevant.

First, the form car mouth — northeastern in Hokkaido — is the standard word
for 'mouth' in ‹KU› and ‹SA›, attested in early Kuril sources including Krasheninnikov (1738)
and Golovnin (1811) and throughout the Sakhalin records (reported via nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ). If the age-area account of the par /
car split is correct (§ mouth-isogloss ), the agreement between
northeastern Hokkaido, ‹SA›, and ‹KU› in using car supports reading that
form as older, and par as an innovation limited to southwestern Hokkaido
‹speculative›.

Second, Ono's reanalysis of the full dataset, including the Sakhalin and Kuril points, places
North Kuril lexically closer to northeastern Hokkaido than to southwestern Hokkaido, with
Sakhalin forming a clearly separated external group ( ono2020 §3–4 ). This
topological finding — NE Hokkaido+Kuril as one cluster, SW Hokkaido as a second, Sakhalin as
the external comparator — is the most statistically defensible summary of the lexical evidence
as the dataset currently stands ‹contested›. The Sakhalin and Kuril varieties in their full
phonological and morphosyntactic dimensions are treated in sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast .

title: Hokkaido Dialect Microvariation: Phonology and Morphosyntax
summary: Inter-dialectal variation within Hokkaido in both phonology (s~š, coda treatment, accent-class membership) and morphosyntax (personal-affix forms, third-plural marking, plural strategies, evidential/causative inventories).
part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Hokkaido Dialect Microvariation: Phonology and Morphosyntax
Part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
Summary: Inter-dialectal variation within Hokkaido in both phonology (s~š, coda treatment, accent-class membership) and morphosyntax (personal-affix forms, third-plural marking, plural strategies, evidential/causative inventories).

Overview

The thirteen Hokkaido Ainu localities surveyed by hattorichiri1960 show
fine-grained phonological differences that cross-cut — and in some cases run orthogonal to —
the northeast/southwest bipartition established by tamura1988 .
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3 catalogues these systematically in a set of isogloss
tables whose data derive ultimately from hattorichiri1960 and
hattori1964 . Four domains carry the phonological distinctiveness: suprasegmental
type (pitch-accent vs unaccented), word-initial consonant elision, consonant-cluster
assimilation, and a pair of well-known segmental alternations
( pa- ~ ca- and
-iw ~ -uy ). Vowel quality and the
distribution of a few morphophonological alternations add further dimensions.
None of the isoglosses coincides precisely with the others, which is why
fukazawaono2024 prefer a framework of gradient boundary dialects over a
partition into discrete named groups.

The consonant inventory common to all Hokkaido dialects is set out in
consonant-inventory ; the vowel system in vowel-inventory ;
pitch-accent placement rules of the dominant standard type in
pitch-accent-placement-rule ; and the overarching dialectometric classification
in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry . Proto-Ainu
reconstructions invoked here are from proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction .

Pitch-accent microvariation

The most fundamental suprasegmental split within Hokkaido divides the field into three
accent types: a standard type shared by the majority of documented localities,
an irregular type in the far southwest, and a set of unaccented varieties
on the east coast and in part of the west
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 .

type
localities
accent-placement rule

Standard
Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, Asahikawa, Nayoro, Obihiro, Sōya
σ1 closed → σ1 high; σ1 open → σ2 high

Irregular
Yakumo, Oshamambe
σ1 and σ2 both open → σ3 high (rather than σ2)

Unaccented
Shizunai, Urakawa, Samani, Bihoro, Kushiro, Shiranuka
pitch non-contrastive lexically; Samani tends final-high; Shizunai shows no positional pattern

The standard accent type

In the standard type, a closed first syllable draws the high pitch to σ1; an open first
syllable places it on σ2. The rules operate on the bare lexical stem; cliticized person
prefixes shift the accent domain (see accent-in-compounds-and-affixation ).
The Saru–Chitose instantiation of this type is the baseline described by
ijas2023 and the system treated in pitch-accent-placement-rule .

The geographic spread of the standard type is non-contiguous. The Saru–Chitose group in
south-central Hokkaido, Horobetsu on the west coast, the inland Asahikawa–Nayoro corridor,
and the eastern Obihiro dialect share identical accent rules despite the intervening
unaccented belt. Sōya, at the northern tip of Hokkaido, also belongs here — a significant
fact given that Sōya is the Hokkaido locality most closely cognate to Sakhalin in the
lexicostatistic matrices of hattorichiri1960 and the dialectometric reanalysis
of ono2020 .

The Yakumo–Oshamambe irregular type

When both the first and second syllables of a word are open, Yakumo and Oshamambe place
the high pitch on the third syllable rather than the second. The contrast with
the standard type is audible on identical lexical stems
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 :

ipérekut | spatula | wooden spatula | Standard accent type: open σ1 forces accent to σ2. Accent mark (´) marks the onset of the high-pitch domain. Comparative form from Nakagawa & Fukazawa 2022 §3.1. | SAR

iperékut | spatula | wooden spatula | Irregular accent type: open σ1 and σ2 force accent to σ3. Same lexeme as the Saru form, differing only in accent position. Data from Nakagawa & Fukazawa 2022 §3.1. | YAK

The contrast recurs in verbs: Saru hopúni 'fly' vs Yakumo
hopuní . Whether the irregular pattern is a phonological
innovation at Yakumo/Oshamambe or an archaic retention from a stage before the standard
rule spread has not been settled; neither has any synchronic account of what structural
property of consecutive open syllables triggers the shift ‹contested›
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 .

Unaccented varieties

Six localities — Shizunai, Urakawa, Samani, Bihoro, Kushiro, and Shiranuka — have been
described as lacking phonologically contrastive pitch at the lexical level. The description
requires qualification: Samani tends toward a final-high pattern in practice; Shizunai
shows no stable positional pattern across the lexicon
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 .

These varieties did not lose their accent through the same mechanism as Sakhalin Ainu,
where the absence of pitch correlates with the retention of vowel length (and a
long-vowel/high-pitch correspondence with the standard Hokkaido type; see
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex ). In Hokkaido, the unaccented
dialects appear to represent a secondary pitch-neutralization within a variety that had
already developed the pitch-accent system — the opposite direction from Sakhalin's
retention of length as the suprasegmental distinction ‹contested›
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 ; itabashi2001 .
The broader prosodic comparison with Sakhalin is taken up in
mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .

Consonant microvariation

Word-initial h-elision

In eastern standard-accent dialects — Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro, Asahikawa, and
Nayoro — a word-initial h is absent when the accent falls
on σ2, that is, when σ1 is open. The elision environment is exactly the standard-type
accent context where the initial consonant carries no pitch-peak information; western and
southern dialects (Horobetsu, Biratori, Nukibetsu, Niikappu, Samani, Sōya) retain
h throughout
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.2, Table 4 :

word
gloss
h -retaining dialects
eastern (elision) dialects

huraye
'wash'
huráye
uráye

hoku
'husband'
