hokú
okú

hekaci
'child'
hekáci
ekáci

The synchronic status of the glottal onset that phonetically precedes the initial vowel
in some realizations of the eastern forms varies by dialect and speaker and connects to
the analysis of glottal-stop ; the relevant point here is that
h is absent as a segment, not merely reduced
‹corpus-suggested› nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.2 .

Regressive cluster assimilation

The most geographically concentrated consonant alternation in the Hokkaido field is a
set of regressive assimilation processes converting heterorganic clusters at morpheme
junctions into geminates or homorganic sequences. The alternations are confined to
the northeast and are most extensive in Bihoro
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.3, Table 5 :

cluster
result
base form
assimilated form
gloss
localities

tk
kk
hotke
hokke
'lie (down)'
Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro

rs
ss
sirsesek
sissesek
'hot (of weather)'
Bihoro, Asahikawa, Nayoro

pt
tt
tupte
tutte
'hang up'
Bihoro

ps
ss
upsor
ussor
'bosom'
Bihoro

kp
pp
sirpopke
sirpokke
'warm'
Bihoro, Sōya

kp
pp
hayokpe
hayoppe ~ ayoppe
'armor'
Bihoro

kt
tt
ioyamokte
ioyamotte
'be anxious'
Bihoro

nakagawa2024 treats these as historically altered word-forms specific
to those localities — lexical entries in the Bihoro dialect — rather than as the output
of a productive synchronic sandhi rule applying across the board.
A community discussant takes a different interpretive direction, suggesting that the
geographic concentration of the pattern in Bihoro points to an underlying "proto-phoneme"
(an ancestor consonant distinct from both of the modern alternants) whose collapse
produced the heterorganic clusters later simplified by assimilation ‹speculative›
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024-11-17). The relationship of the
assimilated forms to the broader coda-sandhi phenomena described in
coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi has not been worked out.

The pa-:ca- alternation

One of the most discussed phonological isoglosses in the Hokkaido field is the
distribution of pa- and ca- in a small
set of words related to 'mouth':
par (southwest) and car (northeast)
'mouth', papus ~ capus ~
patoy ~ catoy 'lip'. Nayoro and
Asahikawa attest both forms, marking the interior transition zone
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 .

The phenomenon is formally restricted: there is no comparable
pe- / ce- ,
pi- / ci- , or
po- / co- correspondence in the
lexicon, which is why nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 call it a
"pseudo-correspondence" rather than a sound correspondence in the strict sense.
Place-name evidence corroborates the geographic split:
kirikae1994 records eleven par -type place names
against zero car -type in Ishikari; in Nemuro and Kushiro the
ratio reverses (thirteen car -type). The Kuril attestations —
Krasheninnikov 1738 and Golovnin 1811 — record car for
'mouth', consistent with the northeastern and eastern distribution.
tamura1996 gives par as the Saru concept-form,
with the possessed form paro-ho .

Four analyses of the alternation have been proposed ‹contested›:

Kindaichi, Chiri, and Naert 1958 (via nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ):
a diachronic change pa → ca , or the
reconstruction of a single Proto-Ainu syllable *Xa
(with an indeterminate initial consonant).

kirikae1994 : Proto-Ainu already had two distinct 'mouth' lexemes; the
par and car forms are not cognates
but separate vocabulary items with overlapping reference.

Nakagawa 1996 (via nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 ): the
ca- form is the older one, evidenced by its appearance in
place-names and formulaic expressions ( sanca or ta ) inside
otherwise par -using regions — an ABA distribution where a
central innovation displaced the peripheral retention.

fukazawa2017 : ca- and pa-
were always distinct morphemes, with irreducible minimal pairs
can : pan 'thin' and
cas : pas 'run' coexisting in the
same dialects with distinct phonological histories.

alonso2022 §2.2.6 reconstructs PA *pʸaɾ for 'mouth',
treating the p / c split as a relic of a
Proto-Ainu palatalized consonant series in which *pʸ shifted
toward c in the northeast; this reconstruction is
typologically motivated rather than philologically forced ‹speculative›. A community
discussant proposes a related but distinct mechanism: that the alternation reflects a
Proto-Ainu syllable *ti , whose labialized variant underwent
palatalization via an intermediate glide (* pj →
* tj → c ), overlapping with Alonso's
palatalized-series account but positing a glide intermediate ‹speculative›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-08). None of the four analyses
has achieved consensus.

Metathesis: -iw and -uy

Bihoro and Kushiro show a systematic alternation in which the sequences
-iw and -uy are transposed relative to
the forms recorded elsewhere nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.5, Table 7 :

base form
Bihoro/Kushiro form
gloss

siwnin
suynin
'green'

ciw
cuy
'stab, prick'

nociw
nocuy
'star' (Kushiro)

riwka
ruyka
'bridge'

icaniw
icanuy
'Dolly Varden trout'

The base form has a glide following the vowel ( iw ); the
Bihoro/Kushiro form has the vowel following the glide
( uy = u + y ).
The alternation is limited to these two unaccented eastern dialects;
standard-accent dialects do not participate.

Additional, lexically specific metatheses occur in other dialects without forming a
regular alternation: piyar / puyar /
puray 'window' (three variants across the field),
nipek / nupek /
nikep 'light', and
askepet / aspeket 'finger' (Horobetsu)
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.5, Table 8 . Their relationship to the
-iw / -uy set is unclear.

Other consonant alternations: y/w and n/m

Two further consonant alternations are documented across the dialect field.
First, y and w alternate in a small
set of lexical items in the Yakumo area:
puyar and nuye where most dialects
have puwar and nuwe
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.6, Table 12 . This remains lexically specific.

Second, n and m vary in particular
words and localities: nimak ~
mimak 'tooth' (Yakumo, Nukibetsu, and Niikappu favour
mimak ); and
ómke ~ ónke 'cough' at Sōya versus
other dialects
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.6, Tables 13–14 .
A community discussant observes that the change
mi → ni (or its inverse) appears broadly
in Hokkaido and suggests the epicentre may lie in the Horobetsu area, where long-distance
assimilation could be the operative mechanism ‹corpus-suggested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-06). That hypothesis is consistent
with the nimak / mimak distribution but
requires systematic corpus verification before it can be stated as established.

An isolated consonant-copying phenomenon produces Sōya
purár against the more widespread
puwar 'fog/mist'; and the alternation
hútne ~ Bihoro húpne 'narrow'
represents a C:p correspondence that remains
lexically isolated nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.6, Table 15 .

Vowel alternations

Vowel quality shows inter-dialectal variation without organizing into simple geographic
zones. nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.5 (Tables 9–11) documents three vowel-pair
alternations:

vowel pair
base form
variant form
gloss
distribution

u ~ o
upas
Sōya opas
'snow'
Sōya o- vs most HA u-

u ~ o
okuyma
NE okoyma
—
NE o vs default u

o ~ e
otop
Yakumo etop
'hair'
Yakumo tendency toward e

o ~ e
menoko
Sōya menéko
'woman'
Sōya e ~ general o

e/u ~ i
seta
Samani sitá ~ setá
'dog'
eastern/Samani i vs southwest e

u ~ i
supuya
Yakumo sipuyá
'smoke'
Yakumo i- vs general u-

u ~ i
kumi
Bihoro kimi
'mold'
Bihoro i vs general u

These vowel variations do not produce a coherent geographic split comparable to the
accent types or the h-elision isogloss. Sōya (northwest) and Yakumo (southwest) both
show o / e alternations but in different
lexical items and different directions; Bihoro (northeast unaccented) and Samani (east
unaccented) share i -raising in different words.
The irregularity is consistent with the lexicostatistic picture: only 91 of the 200
Swadesh-list items used by hattorichiri1960 showed any cross-dialect stem
differentiation, meaning that the majority of the basic vocabulary is shared across all
localities without alternation nakagawafukazawa2022 §2.3 .

One fine-phonetic observation, currently unsystematically documented, concerns the
Mukawa recordings, where i is realized as [e] and
e as [ɛ] throughout — a general-lowering pattern not reported
from other localities and suggesting a dialect-level phonetic shift beyond the
sporadic lexical alternations above ‹corpus-suggested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-03-18).

-ay elision and eastern particle extension

In Saru and Chitose, the plural existential okay reduces to
oka by elision of the final -y ; the same
elision extends to the pronoun ciokay →
coka
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.7, Table 16 . This is not a general rule
affecting all -ay -final sequences; it is confined to this
functional item and its derivatives.

In the eastern dialects (Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro), the pattern runs in the opposite
direction. Particles that appear as ka ,
yakka , and korka in standard
dialects acquire a final -y :
kay , yakkay ,
korkay
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.7, Table 17 .
The geographic separation of the two changes — southwest elision, east extension —
argues against treating them as a single diachronic development; they are most
plausibly independent innovations at opposite ends of the dialect field.

Isogloss geography and the dialect divisions

The isoglosses surveyed above do not partition Hokkaido into a clean binary or ternary
arrangement. Each phenomenon has its own geographic distribution, and the bundles of
overlapping isoglosses reproduce only approximately the northeast/southwest bipartition
of tamura1988 . A summary of the main isoglosses and their geographic
alignment:

phenomenon
southwest / standard
northeast / eastern
source

pitch accent type
standard (σ1-open→σ2 high) — Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, Obihiro, Sōya
unaccented — Bihoro, Kushiro, Shizunai, Samani; standard — Asahikawa, Nayoro
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1

word-initial h
retained — Horobetsu, Biratori, Niikappu, Sōya
elided before accented σ2 — Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro, Asahikawa, Nayoro
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.2

cluster assimilation tk→kk etc.
absent
Bihoro most extensively; Obihiro, Kushiro, Asahikawa, Nayoro partially
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.3

par / car 'mouth'
par — Saru, Chitose, Ishikari
car — Nemuro, Kushiro; both — Nayoro, Asahikawa
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4

-iw / -uy metathesis
absent
Bihoro, Kushiro
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.5

okay → oka
Saru, Chitose
absent
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.7

particle ka → kay
absent
Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.7

Bihoro occupies a distinctive structural position. It is unaccented, shows the
-iw / -uy metathesis, and carries the most
extensive cluster assimilation — yet also shares the
car -type 'mouth' with the northeast and the eastern particle
extension. The independent prothetic h that Bihoro developed on
certain vowel-initial forms (Bihoro haspa 'deaf',
ham 'nail' vs general aspa ,
am ) is a separate localized innovation analysed by
alonso2022 §2.2.5 as a prothetic strategy to disambiguate homophones —
distinct from the original h elided in the surrounding
eastern dialects.

Two "boundary dialect" positions merit specific mention. Sōya (the ‹SA›–HA border) combines
standard-type accent and h -retention with some northeastern vowel
forms ( opas for 'snow') and one cluster-assimilation form
( sirpokke ), placing it between northeast and northwest on multiple
phonological parameters simultaneously. Asahikawa (the NE/SW interior border) has standard
accent, eastern h-elision, both par and car ,
and the rs→ss assimilation — again a profile that spans the main
isogloss bundles. fukazawaono2024 argue that such localities are better treated
as reference points for measuring gradient similarity than as dialect members to be assigned
to one group.

The car -type's correlation with Kuril forms — Krasheninnikov (1738)
and Golovnin (1811) ‹KU› both record car — and the Sōya–Sakhalin
closeness in the lexicostatistic matrices are taken up in
sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast .
The morphological isoglosses that partially parallel the phonological division —
in particular the Saru/Chitose prefix-only person marking — are described in
person-marking-architecture .

The overall picture is one of overlapping, non-coextensive isoglosses producing a
dialect continuum rather than discretely bounded varieties. This is consistent with
ono2020 's finding that the three-way "Major Division"
(Hokkaido/Sakhalin/Kuril) does not survive statistical testing and that even within
Hokkaido the internal structure is gradient — a conclusion that the multi-parameter
isogloss geography here supports at the segmental and prosodic levels
ono2020 ; nakagawafukazawa2022 §6 .

title: Honorific, Ritual, and Taboo Registers
summary: Referent/addressee honorification, the language of prayer, and hunting/taboo avoidance speech as register-specific grammar.
part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Honorific, Ritual, and Taboo Registers
Part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
Summary: Referent/addressee honorification, the language of prayer, and hunting/taboo avoidance speech as register-specific grammar.

Overview: a three-axis register system

Hokkaido Ainu speech is sociolinguistically stratified along three partially independent axes.
The poetic/everyday axis — the contrast between atomte-itak decorated/ornamented language (雅語, poetic–elegant register) and
yayan-itak ordinary language (日常語/口語) — is the broadest division, shared
across verse and prayer genres; its archaic morphology and formulaic vocabulary are treated in
poetic-archaic-elevated-register , and the genre system that controls which register
is appropriate is described in oral-literature-genre-taxonomy . The
honorific/deferential axis, realized through the fourth-person address forms and the
associated pronoun set, is analysed formally in fourth-person-honorific and
kinship-honorific-possession . The ritual-substitution and taboo axis,
in which specific vocabulary items are replaced or avoided in ceremonial, deity-address,
or kinship contexts, is the focus of the present chapter, set against the sociolinguistic
conditioning shared by all three axes.

The three axes do not constitute a clean binary of "high" versus "low" language.
nakagawa2001b (via endo2022 ) argues that elegant and everyday
Ainu occupy positions on a continuum keyed to verse-vs-prose and
fixedness-vs-improvisation, and proposes that a classification by genre-type —
yukar-specific phrases versus prayer-specific phrases — may be more analytically effective
than the elegant/everyday dichotomy alone ‹contested›. The substitution phenomena treated
here are social rather than metrical: the determining factor is the relationship between
speaker and addressee — between worshipper and deity, between kin-avoidance pairs, between
the hunter and the spirit of the quarry.

The honorific address system: fourth-person forms and the pronoun set

The deferential or honorific address register routes second-person reference through the
fourth-person prefix a= and suffix =an rather
than the everyday second-person affixes, producing a construction where
'you did X' or 'you are Y' is morphologically encoded as if the agent were fourth-person
sato2008 261 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 . The same prefix-set
indexes the indefinite agent-defocusing function in everyday speech and the first-person
hero-narrator in oral literature; its deferential reading arises specifically in contexts where
the referent is identifiable and the social relationship calls for deference — address to an
elder, to a chief ( nispa ), or to a deity (see
fourth-person-honorific and person-marking-architecture ).

A dedicated honorific pronoun set co-occurs with these verbal affixes. sato2008 261
lists the following elegant-register second-person pronouns for the Chitose variety:

function
everyday pronoun
honorific / elegant pronoun
source

2SG (subject / agent)
eani
asinuma
sato2008 261

2PL (subject / agent)
ecioka(y)
aoka(y)
sato2008 261

The everyday second-person pronouns eani and
ecioka(y) share morphological origin with the second-person verbal
affixes. The honorific alternatives asinuma and
aoka(y) are drawn from the fourth-person paradigm and function as
free pronouns in deferential speech — between a junior and a senior speaker, or between a
human worshipper and a divine interlocutor sato2008 261 . The structural
convergence of the honorific second-person, the indefinite agent-defocusing, and the
oral-literary hero-narrator on a single morphological paradigm is the subject of
fourth-person-honorific ; for the distribution by genre see
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

The permissive–sociative causative -yar functions additionally as a
register device in formal speech: the causee, when it is the deferential addressee, may be
left implicit rather than named directly, heightening the indirection appropriate to the
social relationship sato2008 261 . This strategy is treated in
permissive-sociative-causative-yar .

Elegant kinship terms as register diagnostics

A small set of kinship terms participates in the everyday/elegant distinction as straightforward
register alternants. The terms ona father (literary/elegant) and
unu mother (literary/elegant) are the literary or elegant (文語的雅語) kin
terms for 'father' and 'mother', attested in verse and formal contexts; the everyday
equivalents haca father (colloquial) and
hapo mother / father (colloquial, dialect-variable) vary more strongly by
dialect and belong primarily to everyday speech
endo2022 ; aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2026-06-15,
asserted). The possessed forms onaha someone's father and
unuhu someone's mother are the grammatically standard affiliative kin-noun
forms; the collective compounds onautari fathers / parents and
unuutari mothers / parents each carry the extended meaning 'parents (both)'
alongside the primary 'fathers' and 'mothers' readings aynucorporadiscord
(nukopoli, 2026-06-15, asserted) ‹corpus-suggested›.

The colloquial term hapo shows conspicuous dialect variation: it means 'mother' in
most Hokkaido varieties but 'father' in Chitose and Horobetsu, while in the Kuril varieties it
denotes 'elder sibling' aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, 2026-06-08, asserted,
citing Chiri Mashiho's comparative data). This lexical instability of the colloquial terms
against the relative stability of the elegant pair ona / unu
across verse traditions provides a further diagnostic value for the register distinction: the
literary terms are fixed precisely where the colloquial terms shift. The possessive morphology
and the full kinship-possession system are treated in kinship-honorific-possession .

Prayer language: genre-conditioned lexical substitution

The most extensively documented register-substitution phenomenon in Hokkaido Ainu is the
genre-conditioned alternation of fire-deity epithets across the performance contexts of divine
epic and prayer. In kamuy yukar (divine epic), the fire deity is addressed as
ape huci fire grandmother , the kin-term-based epithet shared with everyday
speech. In formal prayer ( kamuy nomi /
inonno itak ), the same deity is addressed by the relational epithet
i-resu huci grandmother who raised me or
i-resu kamuy deity who raised me , and the prayer text of 鍋沢モトゾウ
(Motoanrek, Monbetsu) preserves an additional prayer-only form,
mosir kor huci grandmother who rules the land , described in
endo2022 as "not found in any other genre". Conversely, the modifier
kamuy splendid / divine (intensifier in heroic epic) functioning as an
intensifier glossed 'splendid' occurs in the heroic epic but is absent from prayer
endo2022 . The full inventory of substitution pairs for this deity is:

register / genre
epithet
gloss
source

everyday speech / kamuy yukar
ape huci
'fire grandmother'
endo2022

prayer ( inonno itak )
i-resu huci
'grandmother who raised me'
endo2022

prayer ( inonno itak )
i-resu kamuy
'deity who raised me'
endo2022

prayer only (Monbetsu locality)
mosir kor huci
'grandmother who rules the land'
endo2022

The relational epithet i-resu huci encodes the deity-to-worshipper
bond explicitly: the prefix i- (antipassive / indefinite-object
marker) indexes 'me' as the implicit object, so the form is lexically 'the grandmother who
raised [me]', positioning the prayer as a speech-act by a person acknowledging filial
dependence on the deity. Using the everyday ape huci in formal prayer
would constitute a register mismatch; the substitution is not stylistic variation but a
categorical genre requirement endo2022 .

Prayer language is morphologically marked beyond the lexical substitutions. The
inonno itak register is applicative-heavy, encoding the case frame of
the verb by applicative prefixes ( e- , o- ,
ko- ) rather than by case particles — a hallmark of
atomte-itak generally, but especially concentrated in prayer
sato2008 263 . The prayer text cited in endo2022 (§2.3)
preserves doubled applicative prefixes on a single verb:

k=e-ko-onkami | 1SG.A=APPL-APPL-worship | I reverently worship. | Double applicative stacking (e- + ko-) on the worship verb, a morphological hallmark of the prayer register. The 1SG prefix ku= surfaces as k= through prevocalic reduction before the e- applicative. The full form in the prayer text also includes the pase- 'weighty/reverent' prefix; only the onkami clause is reproduced here. | endo2022 | HK | 鍋沢モトゾウ (Motoanrek), Monbetsu; prayer text ex. 13 in Endō (2022) §2.3

The formal sentence-final frame ruwe tapan (it is a recognized fact that) …
marks utterances as ceremonial assertions in ritual speech contexts, carrying the
assertive-present reading appropriate to public declaration rather than the ordinary
inferential use of ruwe ne tamura1996 s.v. ruwe .
The evidential system underpinning this frame is described in
evidential-schema-overview and ruwe-ne-inferential ; a fully
glossed prayer text is given in inonno-itak-ritual-prayer .

The indirection principle: taboo on direct deity–human speech

sato2008 259–261 proposes that the non-everyday person marking in Ainu oral
literature — the use of fourth-person a= / =an
where everyday first-person would use ku= — is rooted in a cultural
taboo: a deity or spirit ( kamuy ) addressing a living human directly
transgresses a boundary, and the non-everyday person marking softens that transgression by
increasing indirectness. Satō labels this the indirection principle
(間接性の原理), contrasting it with the standard "shamaness-oracle origin"
(巫女の託宣起源) theory and presenting it explicitly as a hypothesis rather than an
established result ‹speculative›. For the heroic and divine epic genres that most visibly
deploy this device, see heroic-and-divine-verse .

Whether or not Satō's diachronic hypothesis is accepted, the synchronic functional overlap
between the fourth person's honorific use (deferential address to human superiors) and its
oral-literary use (narration by divine or supernatural heroes) is structurally coherent:
both exploit the same fourth-person paradigm to mediate the speech-act through a form not
claimed by the ordinary grammatical first person of the interaction. The prayer epithet
alternation described above operates on the same logic at the lexical level: the worshipper
who addresses the fire deity as i-resu huci rather than
ape huci likewise routes the address through a relational frame
encoding the deity's prior act toward the worshipper, rather than naming the deity in a
neutral noun endo2022 .

Gendered ritual speech-acts

Ritual speech-acts are differentiated by the performer's sex in ways that partly overlap with,
and partly extend beyond, the narrator-gender allocation for oral-literary genres (treated
in gendered-register-speech ). Two complementary deferential-acknowledgment acts
are documented for the Saru basin: women utter
hinna thank you, how kind (women's word) (lower-Saru variant:
hunna ; degree form hioy'oy light thanks / how nice
< hinna hinna ) while performing raymik stroke below the nose with the index finger (gesture of receiving) ,
a gesture of acceptance offered when receiving food or a gift; men instead perform
onkami bow with both hands together (gesture of reverence) . The formal
general thanks-form iyayiraykere formal expression of gratitude is
unmarked for gender aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-26, asserted).

hinna | INTERJ | (Thank you — said by a woman while performing raymik.) | hinna (lower-Saru hunna) is the gendered women's expression of thanks for received food or gifts, accompanied by the raymik gesture (stroking below the nose with the index finger). The male counterpart is the gesture onkami (bowing with both hands joined), unmarked for a parallel verbal formula. The degree form hioy'oy ( onkami ; its
current use across genders in contemporary Ainu speech is described as a "transformed usage"
in the context of revitalization endo2022 . Prayer
( kamuy nomi ) is performed exclusively by adult men; seated songs
( upopo ) are primarily women's performative domain
endo2022 . The sentence-final particles associated with each genre and
gender are treated in sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force .

Taboo vocabulary: attested existence and the source gap

The existence of a special taboo vocabulary in Hokkaido Ainu — restricted word-forms for
hunting, bear-ceremony, and kinship-avoidance contexts — is attested independently in several
sources. tamura2000 catalogues "special terminology including taboo and prayer
words" as a vocabulary class of the language. The 1795 Edo-period record
Ezoki is reported in bugaeva2022 as describing "a kind of secret
language" observed among Ainu speakers — an early external testimony to a restricted
speech variety.

The primary monographic treatment of this vocabulary is Hattori (1957),
"Special language of the older generations among the Ainu" (nenchōshasō tokushugo,
年長者層特殊語), which was not held or consulted in the preparation of this grammar. No
systematic hunting-substitute or in-law name-avoidance word list was recoverable from the
read grammars or the aligned corpus ‹speculative›. What the sources consulted make available is
the mechanism — lexical substitution by a genre- or context-conditioned alternate
form — and several attested examples from the prayer and epic registers, as described above.
The hunting and bear-ceremony vocabulary, and the kinship-avoidance pairs, remain a gap
pending access to Hattori (1957) and to the taboo-marked entries in
tamura1996 .

Structurally, the attested substitution cases share a common property: the replacement is a
different lexeme or an elaborated periphrastic construction, not a phonological modification
of the everyday form. This is illustrated by the elegant-register periphrases documented
in sato2008 264 : everyday soyne go out is replaced in the
elegant register by an elaborate circumlocution; everyday kamuy god is
replaced by kamuy ne manu p 'what is said to be a god'. In each
case the substitute form encodes a relational or evaluative frame around the concept rather
than naming it directly — a structural parallel to the fire-deity epithet alternation and,
plausibly, to the bear-ceremony and hunting-taboo substitutions whose inventory remains
undocumented in the consulted sources. For the broader system of elegant periphrasis and
archaic morphology, see poetic-archaic-elevated-register ; for the genre
contexts in which these registers are activated, see
sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure .

title: Hortative, Optative, and Cohortative
summary: First-person hortative/cohortative built on =an plus clause-final particles, alongside optative and wish constructions.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Hortative, Optative, and Cohortative
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: First-person hortative/cohortative built on =an plus clause-final particles, alongside optative and wish constructions.

The hortative system

A set of sentence-final particles — ro , rok ,
and no , distributed by dialect — attaches to a finite clause and
converts it into an invitation to joint action. Nakagawa places these under the
sentence-final particles (終助詞) in the category of 勧誘, 'inducement or invitation',
seeking the addressee's agreement nakagawa2024 333–335 . In their most
frequent use the particles follow a clause whose subject is the inclusive fourth person,
giving the cohortative 'let us …'; with a first-person subject they shift into a
volitional or deliberative register. The related particle so
marks self-directed deliberation addressed to no one in particular. Together these uses
span the domain that descriptive grammars variously call hortative, cohortative, and
optative.

The system sits at the boundary of two adjacent areas. On one side is the directive
mood — imperative and prohibitive — treated in imperative-and-prohibitive ;
on the other is the desiderative and epistemic-modal field of
epistemic-irrealis-modality . The hortative differs from the imperative in
keeping person affixes intact on the finite verb and in proposing participation rather
than directing it; it differs from the desiderative rusuy want to in
that rusuy predicates an internal desire as an assertion while
ro addresses an invitation. The broader TAM and directive
architecture is mapped in tam-overview .

Dialect distribution of the hortative particles

The three phonological variants share the same function and occur in complementary
geographic zones across Hokkaido, with partial overlap in Ishikari
nakagawa2024 334–335 .

particle
dialect region

ro
Saru, Horobetsu, Chitose, Shizunai

rok
Tokachi, Shiranuka; Ishikari (alongside ro )

no
Urakawa, Samani

This chapter cites ro as the reference form. Attested examples
from other dialect regions carry their appropriate dialect tag; the grammatical
description applies across all three variants.

The cohortative: inclusive fourth-person subject

The canonical hortative clause is a finite predicate carrying the fourth-person subject
marker — the intransitive suffix =an or the transitive agent
prefix a= — followed by the hortative particle. The fourth
person in this environment receives an inclusive reading: it denotes the speaker and
addressee(s) acting together. This is the primary discourse context in which the
fourth-person subject functions as an explicit inclusive 'we'; the full paradigm and the
broader referential range are set out in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm
‹consensus›.

Number-sensitive suppletive verb pairs select the plural stem when the cohortative
implies a plural group. The motion verb arpa (singular 'go')
supplies its plural counterpart paye in a cohortative directed
at a group of participants nakagawa2024 142–145 .

paye=an ro | go.PL=4.S HORT | Let's go.

The particle attaches to clause chains in which each predicate retains its own person
marking and the sequential conjunctive wa links the component
clauses:

ney ta ka kuruma ani mosir epitta payoka=an wa inkar=an ro | when LOC even car INS land throughout go.around=4.S CONJ look=4.S HORT | Someday let us travel around the whole country by car and see it. | Both finite verbs carry the fourth-person suffix =an, the diagnostic of the pair-verb construction in which wa links two independent finite clauses. The hortative ro scopes over the full conjoined proposition. | nakagawa2024:333 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 575, after Honda 2001: 19

Both verbs in the chain carry =an , the same pattern found in
existential-aspect pair verbs such as kor an and
wa an (see tam-overview existential-aspect ):
each predicate is independently person-marked, and the final particle closes the chain
from outside. The inclusive 'we' reading that distinguishes the cohortative from a
generic or indefinite fourth-person clause is supplied by context and the hortative
particle together; the morphology of the fourth person itself is identical across these
uses.

Volitional and deliberative uses

Tamura observed that ro is not restricted to the fourth-person
inclusive base. With a first-person subject it expresses the speaker's own volition as
a proposal or offer rather than an invitation to joint participation, occupying what
the typological literature calls the volitional or optative range
nakagawa2024 335 .

k=arpa ro | 1SG.S=go HORT | Shall I go? / I'll go then. | With a first-person rather than fourth-person subject, ro shifts from a cohortative 'let us' to a volitional 'shall I / I will', presenting the speaker's intention as a proposal open to the addressee's response. | nakagawa2024:334 | HK | Nakagawa ex. 578, after Tamura

The Chitose dialect supplies a parallel transitive construction in which the speaker
proposes to act for the addressee's benefit. The second-person object prefix is overt;
the first-person agent is contextually supplied.

eci=kay ro | 2SG.O=carry HORT | Let me carry you! | Chitose form. The second-person object prefix eci= is overt while the first-person agent is contextually implied. Nakagawa analyses this as a 1SG-agent, 2SG-object hortative in which the speaker proposes to act for the addressee. | nakagawa2024:334 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 579

The related particle so marks deliberation directed at no one:
the speaker reasons through a choice aloud, translated roughly as '〜しようかな'
('I wonder whether I should …'). Whereas ro — even in its
1SG volitional use — addresses a proposal to the interlocutor,
so keeps the reasoning internal to the speaker
nakagawa2024 335 .

k=arpa so | 1SG.S=go DEL | (Hmm,) shall I go? | Deliberative so with a first-person subject; the speaker reasons aloud without directing the question to another participant. Contrast the cohortative paye=an ro, where the invitation is addressed to the interlocutor.

Ijäs's pedagogical guide carries a hortative clause in its own title:
a=eyaypakasnu ro 'let us study [Ainu]', in which the
fourth-person transitive agent prefix a= heads an inclusive
proposition and ro expresses the invitation
ijas2023 ‹corpus-confirmed›. The construction matches Nakagawa's
description of the standard fourth-person cohortative.

Relationship to the imperative, prohibitive, and desiderative

The formal contrast with the imperative is clear. The singular imperative is a bare verb
carrying no second-person subject prefix: arpa 'go!' The plural
or polite imperative adds the final particle yan to the plural
stem, again without a subject person prefix: hosippa yan 'go
back (all of you)!' The hortative, by contrast, preserves the full person-marking of
the finite clause — paye=an ro retains =an
on the verb — and the particle attaches outside that marked predicate
nakagawa2024 421–423, 334 ‹consensus›. The illocutionary distinction
follows from the morphological one: the imperative directs the addressee, while the
hortative proposes joint participation or the speaker's own contribution.

The prohibitive adverb iteki (Saru/Chitose form; dialect
variants treated in imperative-and-prohibitive ) scopes over the bare
imperative stem and does not combine with the person-marked hortative clause
nakagawa2024 424–425 . When a speaker wishes to counsel against a joint
course of action, a conditional or modal construction serves that function rather than
iteki fronted before a hortative clause.

The desiderative auxiliary rusuy want to occupies the pre-particle
auxiliary slot and predicates the speaker's internal desire as an assertion
nakagawa2024 312–313 . A sentence ending in rusuy
is a declaration of wanting, not an invitation; ro cannot
substitute for it in that position, and rusuy cannot occupy
the final-particle slot where ro sits. The desiderative
together with the prospective kusu ne and other irrealis and
epistemic auxiliaries are treated in epistemic-irrealis-modality .

title: humi ne — the Non-Visual Sensory Evidential
summary: humi ne ('it is the sound/feeling that'), marking evidence from non-visual senses — non-verbal sound and bodily/internal sensation — and inference from such cues.
part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: humi ne — the Non-Visual Sensory Evidential
Part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
Summary: humi ne ('it is the sound/feeling that'), marking evidence from non-visual senses — non-verbal sound and bodily/internal sensation — and inference from such cues.

Place in the evidential paradigm

Among the four direct evidential forms of Hokkaido Ainu,
humi ne marks that the speaker's information derives from a
non-visual sensory source: a physical sound, a smell or taste, a touch or bodily
sensation, or an internal impression that is neither visible nor verbally reported. The
construction — a nominalized clause headed by a
formal-defective-nouns formal noun and closed by the copula
ne — is common to all four terms and is described in
evidential-schema-overview . The other three direct forms are treated in
ruwe-ne-inferential the inferential ruwe ne ,
siri-ne-situational the situational siri ne , and
hawe-ne-reportative the reportative hawe ne .

Dal Corso's morphosyntactic account arranges the four direct forms along a source-reliability
scale: ruwe ne > siri ne >
humi ne > hawe ne . Source reliability in
this account designates the psychophysical channel of acquisition , not a degree of
epistemic certainty: a speaker using humi ne specifies non-visual
sensory access, not a lower confidence in the proposition
dalcorso2018 304, 312–313, 339 . The visual/non-visual macrodivision groups
ruwe ne and siri ne on the visual side and
humi ne and hawe ne on the non-visual
dalcorso2018 312 . Mapped onto the typology of
aikhenvald2004 63–65 , the system is C1-type (visual, non-visual
sensory, inferred, reported) hirosawa2026 64 .

humi ne is among the less frequent members of the paradigm. In Dal
Corso's Hokkaido reference texts it supplies 45 of 2232 evidential tokens (2.02%), with its
indirect counterpart humi as adding a further 37 (1.66%)
dalcorso2018 72 . Both counts place the sensory evidential as the third-most
frequent direct form and second-to-last in the full eight-form system ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Morphology: humi is hum-i, 'the sound/feeling of —'

The formal noun humi is the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed (affiliative) form of the noun
hum sound, feeling . Tamura's Saru dictionary records
hum (ordinary possessed form humi(hi) ) with
the primary senses 音・感じ 'sound; feeling/sensation'
tamura1996 s.v. hum, humi(hi) . The same source noun underlies the lexical
predicate humas ( hum-as 'to sound, there is
a noise'), where as stand is the posture-verb element that also appears in
hawe as and in other transient-state predicates such as
apto as 'it rains' and rera as 'the wind
blows' dalcorso2018 318, 329 .

As a formal noun in the evidential function, humi blocks the
additional possessive ending that the full lexical possessed form may carry: the regular
possessed noun is humi(hi) , but evidential
*humihi is ungrammatical — the same blocking that disallows
*ruwehe for the inferential
tamura1996 s.v. humi, ruwe ; nakagawa2024 258 . This
restriction, shared by all four formal nouns and discussed in
formal-defective-nouns , is a diagnostic of partial grammaticalization: the
form has shed ordinary head-noun syntax without yet becoming a bound morpheme
dalcorso2018 24–25 ‹consensus›. The standard gloss is
sound/feeling- poss : the morpheme is
hum-i (base plus possessive suffix), whose implicit possessor is
the nominalized clause.

The scope of hum sets it apart from the adjacent source noun
haw voice . Tamura is explicit: inanimate and non-vocal sounds — a drum,
ambient noise, a sound with no identified speaker — take hum , while
human and animal voice takes haw
tamura1996 s.v. haw . This lexical distinction is grammaticalized in the
evidential pair: humi ne for non-verbal sensory access,
hawe ne for verbal report and hearsay. The construction is a
copular predication over a nominalized clause — [clause] humi 'the
sound/feeling that [clause]', closed by the copula
ne (treated in copula-ne-predicate-nominals ).

Meaning: the non-visual sensory domain

The traditional label for this form is Kindaichi's
感説法 'feeling-mood', characterizing it as expressing 'it feels
like … / it seems like …' in the modality of uncertain sensory judgment
kindaichi1936 ; hirosawa2026 65–66 . The modern evidential
framing retains the source semantics. Tamura specifies humi ne as
appropriate for hearing, touch, and bodily or emotional feeling
hirosawa2026 67 . Nakagawa's Chitose dictionary extends the domain to
音・匂い・味・体感・直感 — sounds, smells, tastes, bodily sensations, and intuitions
obtained through any means except sight and verbal report
nakagawa1995 ; hirosawa2026 68, 71 . His 2024 grammar gives the
standard definition: humi ne marks that the speaker's judgment rests
on sensation other than sight and language
nakagawa2024 392 . Satō's question paradigm assigns 感覚・物音 'sensation,
physical sound' to the humi domain
sato2008 135 .

Dal Corso glosses the form dir.flt (direct evidential, feeling) and
lists the covered channels as hearing, smell, touch, taste, and an internal 'sixth sense'
dalcorso2018 306 . He documents a recurring sensor-stimulus mismatch: the
evidential derives historically from the noun meaning 'sound, feeling', yet it licenses
gustatory and other non-auditory stimuli
dalcorso2018 307, 311 . The following example shows a gustatory stimulus
under the sound-derived evidential — the embedded clause concerns being given food to eat,
not hearing a sound:

usa sisakpe a=i=y-e-re hum-i ne ya | various delicious.things 4.A=4.O=EP-eat-CAUS sound-POSS COP Q | Whether I was given all sorts of delicious things to eat… | Dal Corso ex. 242 (DIR.FLT). A gustatory stimulus (being given food) appears under the sound/feeling-derived humi ne, illustrating the sensor-stimulus mismatch discussed at pp. 307, 311. The verb complex a=i=y-e-re is a causative benefactive ('caused to eat for 4O'); ya is a question/dubitative particle. | dalcorso2018:306 | SAR | Wateke (Saru), Tamura audio materials; Dal Corso siglum TMA: 42

The extension from auditory to other senses is not unexpected: the source noun
hum sound, feeling already covers both auditory and somatic senses
lexically, and as it grammaticalized into a formal noun its scope broadened across all
perceptual channels not taken by siri (sight) or
hawe (verbal report). Chiri noted the expansion from sound to
taste, colour, and smell in the earliest grammatical account
hirosawa2026 66 . The following table shows the attested domains:

Sensory domains attested with humi ne

domain
typical situation
attested source

physical (non-verbal) sound
ambient noise, an unidentified shot or movement, a sound heard without visual source
hirosawa2026 71 ; ilcaa1976

taste / gustatory
evaluating food or drink; being given something to eat
dalcorso2018 306–307 ; nakagawa2024 395

touch / temperature
a surface felt to be frozen solid, cold, or altered in texture
kindaichi1936 ; hirosawa2026 66

internal emotional state
anger, fear, or emotion perceived as rising inside the speaker
ilcaa1976

internal bodily impression / intuition
a bodily transformation sensed from within; a vague somatic impression
dalcorso2018 306 ; ilcaa1976

Touch and temperature are attested from the early grammatical literature. Chiri's canonical
example, cited through Hirosawa, encodes the tactile state of a surface frozen solid:

rupus hum-i ne awan | freeze.solid sound-POSS COP MIR | It had completely frozen solid. | Chiri 1936: 192, cited via Hirosawa 2026: 66. A bodily-tactile state (freezing solid) is encoded with humi ne; the admirative tail awan carries the surprised-retrospective value. The source noun hum 'feeling' grounds the tactile domain in the same formal noun that covers sound. | hirosawa2026:66 | HK

Internal emotional states use the same construction. In the following example, the narrator
perceives anger welling up from within — an internal bodily impression with no external
auditory source:

a=ruska hum-i ne pekor yay-nu=an | 4.A=be.angry sound-POSS COP like REFL-hear=4.S | It felt as though anger was welling up. | The fourth-person prefix a= marks the agent in the narrative frame. The periphrasis humi ne pekor yay-nu=an 'it felt as though' is a common collocation combining the sensory evidential with the simulative pekor and the reflexive-perception verb yaynu; see the section on frames for related variants. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 'Folktale 13' (Saru); AA研 aa-irc/016, sent. 122

A common periphrasis in the corpus combines humi ne with a
simulative particle and a mental-state verb —
humi ne kunak ku=ramu 'I thought it felt like …' or
humi ne pekor yaynu=an 'it seemed as though …' — embedding the
sensory evidential as a complement to a perception or cognition verb. The following example
uses the kunak variant:

hum-as hum-i ne kunak ku=ramu kor ku=soyne a p | sound-stand sound-POSS COP COMP 1SG.A=think while 1SG.S=go.outside PFV NMLZ | húmas humi ne kunak ku=ramu kor ku=soyne a p | Thinking that it sounded like there was a noise, I went outside — but (nothing was there). | The contracted form húmas (= hum-as 'a sound arises') provides the sensory stimulus; humi ne encodes the speaker's non-visual sensory access to that sound; kunak introduces the complement of the cognition verb ramu 'think/feel'. The nominalized tail p sets the clause as backgrounded against a disappointed expectation. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 'Folktale 8 (Part 1)' (Saru); AA研 aa-irc/006, sent. 358

The boundary with siri ne is explicit in the definition:
siri encodes a directly visible situation or appearance, while
humi covers any percept not accessed through sight. The boundary
with hawe ne runs along the verbal/non-verbal divide:
hawe (from haw voice ) encodes information obtained
from words — spoken, reported, or overheard — whereas humi covers
physical sounds and bodily perceptions that are not themselves linguistic utterances
tamura1996 s.v. haw, hum .

Clause-final frames and illocution

humi participates in the shared clause-final frame paradigm
documented by Nakagawa nakagawa2024 392–396 and summarized in
evidential-schema-overview frames . The interrogative frames are treated in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

frame
illocutionary value
note

humi ne
declarative, non-visual sensory direct evidential
formal noun + copula; may take final particles and clause-linkers after it

humi an?
content question / dubitative (内容疑問文)
existential replaces copula; Tamura records hnta ninpa wa arpa humi
an? 'where might (she) have gone?'
tamura1996 s.v. humi

bare humi
polar question / exclamation
reduced, insubordinated frame; exclamatory force is common, especially for gustatory
and thermal sensation

humi ka isam
'not feel … at all'
total absence of the non-visual percept; uses the negative existential
isam ; recorded as a fixed expression
tamura1996 s.v. humi

The bare exclamatory humi! is the most distinctive member. It
packages an immediate sensory reaction — most frequently gustatory or thermal — as its own
utterance, without the copula:

usey ku=ku wa keraan hum-i | tea 1SG.A=drink CONJ be.delicious sound-POSS | usey ku=ku wa, keraan humi! | I drank tea — how delicious! | The bare formal noun humi functions as an exclamative main clause, without the copula ne. The gustatory evaluation triggers the non-visual sensory evidential; the insubordinated frame conveys immediate spontaneous reaction. | nakagawa2024:396 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 793, Chitose

Negation follows the pattern shared by all four evidentials: the proposition is denied within
the embedded clause (the pattern …somo ki humi ne 'there is no
feeling that [one does …]'), with humi ne remaining outside the
scope of negation. The fixed expression humi ka isam expresses the
complete absence of the relevant non-visual percept. The interaction of the evidential with
the wider TAM and negation apparatus is treated in
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation .

The indirect counterpart: humi as

The indirect sensory evidential humi as replaces the copula
ne with the posture verb as stand , shifting the
evidential reading from direct to more distanced sensory access: the speaker has sensory
grounds but presents them as slightly removed or inferential
dalcorso2018 306, 328 . Dal Corso glosses it
ind.flt (indirect evidential, feeling). The opposition copula
ne (internal, direct) vs posture/existential verb (external,
indirect) runs across all four formal nouns and is set out in
evidential-schema-overview eight-form .

A functional asymmetry distinguishes humi as from its verbal-source
counterpart. hawe as has largely specialized as a reportative —
encoding hearsay when the source is absent and quotation when it is overt
dalcorso2018 328–330 . humi as retains its sensory
basis throughout: it is consistently inferential from a non-verbal sensory impression and
is never reportative dalcorso2018 328 ‹corpus-confirmed›. As a lexical
predicate, humas ( hum-as ) means 'to make a
sound, to resound' dalcorso2018 318 . The physical-sound use is the most
frequent in the corpus:

aynu ek hum-i as | person come sound-POSS stand | The sound of someone arriving was heard. | The posture verb as replaces the copula ne: the narrator infers the person's arrival from an auditory impression rather than direct sight. The indirect humi as contrasts here with the direct humi ne that would encode the arrival as directly sensed. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 'Folktale 8 (Part 2)' (Saru); AA研 aa-irc/007, sent. 200

Double evidentiality with humi

Like the other formal nouns, humi enters double-evidential
constructions in which two evidentials stack within a single clause
dalcorso2018 331–333 . The combination humi ne humi as
stacks the direct and indirect sensory evidentials on the same non-visual channel:

poro cep a=ne hum-i ne hum-i as orano a=yupi-hi os ma=an wa | big fish 4.A=COP sound-POSS COP sound-POSS stand after.that 4.POSS=elder.brother-POSS follow swim=4.S CONJ | It felt as though I had become a great fish, and then I swam after my elder brother. | Dal Corso 2018: 333 (ex. 275 context). The direct humi ne presents the bodily transformation as directly felt; the immediately following humi as then hedges it as also inferential, conveying that the same sensation was simultaneously vivid and uncertain. Both evidentials draw on the non-verbal sensory channel. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 'Folktale 11' (Saru); AA研 aa-irc/013, sent. 32

This pattern differs from the double-evidential constructions in
ruwe-ne-inferential mirativity , where
ruwe ne ratifies an indirect form from a distinct channel as
established knowledge. In humi ne + humi as , the same sensory
channel provides both layers; the two evidentials together convey a closely felt percept
reported with residual uncertainty dalcorso2018 331–333 .

Scope, mirativity, and the admirative tail

The constituent nominalized by humi is a full finite clause
carrying its own person marking, aspect, and negation, over which the evidential then
scopes. The scope properties are shared across all four formal nouns and are set out in
evidential-schema-overview scope-tam ; nothing in the structure of
humi ne alters that general picture.

Mirativity is carried in Hokkaido Ainu by the auxiliaries aan /
awan and by anan , not by the formal-noun
evidentials themselves hirosawa2026 90 ;
dalcorso2018 336 . Chiri's freezing example above,
rupus humi ne awan , shows the admirative tail
awan occupying the post-evidential slot: the surprised-retrospective
reading comes from awan , not from
humi ne itself. The full treatment of mirativity and its interaction
with the evidential system is in mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

Diachrony and dialect variation

The grammaticalization path parallels that of the three sibling formal nouns. The noun
hum sound, feeling grammaticalized first into the possessed nominal
hum-i , and from there into a clause-final formal noun whose
implicit possessor is the nominalized event rather than a personal possessor
tamura1996 s.v. hum ; hirosawa2026 65–66 . The overall
cline — sensory noun > possessed formal noun > evidential — is shared by
sir , haw , and ru and
places the Ainu system within the nominalization-based evidential strategy discussed by
dalcorso2018 24–25 ; the path is set out for the full paradigm in
evidential-schema-overview grammaticalization . Dal Corso's account of the
degree of grammaticalization applies to humi as to the others:
partial, with residual nominal behaviour visible in the possessive blocking
dalcorso2018 24–25 .

Dialect variation in the realization of humi follows the
system-wide patterns:

Saru, Chitose, Biratori — the core of Dal Corso's data — use the
possessed form humi throughout, with the direct/indirect split
humi ne / humi as
dalcorso2018 304–337 .

Tokachi (and frequently Ishikari) close the clause with the bare concept
form hum rather than the possessed
humi ; the declarative frame attested by Takahashi appears as
… ran hum ne awan
takahashi2013 129–131 ; nakagawa2024 110 .

Horobetsu grammaticalizes the possessed-form nominalizers a step further
into adverbial-clause markers and conjunctive-particle uses not attested in well-described
Saru — an instance of what Takahashi calls 'versatile nominalization'
takahashi2018 107–112 .

Ijäs's pedagogical grammar treats humi ne under the evidential
framing without adjudicating the analytical disputes discussed in
evidential-schema-overview frameworks ijas2023 . The genre
patterning of humi ne within narrative texts and its distribution
relative to ruwe ne across frame-text, reported inner experience,
and direct speech are taken up in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

title: Imperative and Prohibitive (Directive Mood)
summary: Bare-stem imperatives, the -yan plural/polite imperative, and the iteki prohibitive form one directive-mood system.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Imperative and Prohibitive (Directive Mood)
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: Bare-stem imperatives, the -yan plural/polite imperative, and the iteki prohibitive form one directive-mood system.

Overview: the directive mood

The directive mood of Hokkaido Ainu distinguishes singular from plural or
polite addressees, positive from negative directives, and direct from softened
commands through a small set of formally separable constructions. The simplest
directive is the bare verb with no second-person subject prefix — the singular
imperative. The final particle yan marks the plural or polite
imperative; number-sensitive verbs require the plural stem alongside it. The
adverbial particle hani softens the command into a polite reminder,
and the preverbal adverb iteki (and its dialect equivalents) converts
any imperative into a prohibitive
nakagawa2024 421–426 ; sato2008 72–73 ;
ijas2023 .

construction
function
addressee / force

V
singular imperative
singular, direct

V.PL yan
plural / polite imperative
plural or polite, direct

V hani / V yan hani
softened imperative
any, mitigated

V wa en=/un= kore
benefactive request
any, request

iteki V
prohibitive
any, negative direct

V yak pirka / yakka pirka etc.
advice, permission (indirect)
2nd person, mitigated

The hortative particles ro , rok ,
and no , which build inclusive 'let us' directives on the
fourth-person verb, are treated in hortative-and-optative . The
detailed relationship of the prohibitive to sentential negation, its apprehensive
extension, and its position in prohibitive typology are developed in
negation-prohibitive-iteki . The overview of how directive mood
fits within the broader TAM and clause architecture is in
tam-overview .

The bare-stem singular imperative

A directive to a single addressee is the bare verb with no second-person subject
prefix. Object prefixes — en=
(1 sg . o ),
un= (1 pl . o ),
i= (4. o ) — remain on the verb;
only the subject prefix is suppressed
nakagawa2024 421–422 . The surface form is therefore
identical to a third-person or subjectless clause and receives its directive
reading from context and intonation.

hetak pusu wa inkar | INTERJ dig CONJ look | Come on, dig it up and look! | Two bare-stem verbs (pusu, inkar) linked by the sequential particle wa. The absence of any second-person subject prefix is the sole formal mark of the singular imperative. | nakagawa2024:422 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 911

Adding a second-person subject prefix to an imperative verb is ungrammatical
‹consensus› ijas2023 . A community exchange confirms the restriction:
the bare imperative of
un=epunkine 'guard [us]' carries only the beneficiary
object prefix un= and no subject marker; a form
e=un=epunkine or a=un=epunkine
is ungrammatical as an imperative (siljamimimii, aynu-corpora Discord,
2024-11-17) aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Ishikari alone attaches a sentence-final particle ya to signal the
singular imperative, a form that the southern and central dialects lack
nakagawa2024 422 .

The plural and polite imperative: yan

A directive addressed to multiple persons or framed politely takes the final
particle yan . For verbs that distinguish a singular from a plural
stem — intransitives such as hosipi return (SG) /
hosippa return (PL) and ek come (SG) /
arki come (PL) — the plural stem is obligatory and
yan cannot be dropped
nakagawa2024 422 .

ku=kor kuruma o wa hosippa yan | 1SG.A=have car board CONJ return.PL IMP | Get in my car and go home! | hosippa is the suppletive plural stem of hosipi 'return'; both the plural stem and the particle yan are obligatory for a plural or polite addressee. A bare *hosippa without yan is ungrammatical as a command. | nakagawa2024:422 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 914

The particle yan is homophonous with the intransitive
motion verb yan go ashore, come up from water . A community
discussion proposes that the imperative particle grammaticalized from this
motion verb — a path typologically paralleled by English come on and
Mandarin directional verbs in imperative idioms (antitwilight, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-11-18) aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.
The grammars list the motion verb and the final particle as separate entries
without deriving one from the other nakagawa2024 422 .

When hani follows yan , the two regularly fuse
to yanani
nakagawa2024 422–423 .

Softeners and benefactive requests: hani and wa kore

The particle hani (variant ani ) follows the
imperative verb, or the particle yan , and softens the
directive into a polite reminder
nakagawa2024 422–423 .

pirkano mokor hani | well sleep SOFT | Please sleep well! | hani follows the bare imperative mokor 'sleep' and converts a direct command into a polite wish. | tamura2000:242 | SAR

iteki omkekar yan hani | PROH shout IMP SOFT | Please don't shout. | hani follows yan in a prohibitive context; the sequence iteki … yan hani produces a softened negative directive to a plural or polite addressee. | sato2008:72 | SAR

A community discussion proposes that hani is
etymologically related to the motion verb hanke come near, approach ,
by a grammaticalization path parallel to the one proposed for
yan (antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-18)
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.

The pair verb wa kore do for (someone) adds a benefactive
reading. In this pattern the imperative verb is followed by the sequential
particle wa and the verb kore
'give / do-for', which takes an object prefix identifying the beneficiary:
en=kore 'do it for me',
un=kore 'do it for us'
nakagawa2024 423, 455 .

suke wa en=kore | cook CONJ 1SG.O=give | Please cook [it] for me. | Benefactive request pattern: the lexical verb takes the bare imperative shape, wa links it to kore, and the object prefix en= on kore identifies the beneficiary.

The prohibitive: iteki and its dialect variants

A prohibitive directive places a preverbal adverb before the bare-stem verb.
The most widespread form in the Saru corpus is iteki , found in Saru,
Chitose, Horobetsu, and Abuta; other Hokkaido dialects use parallel adverbs
nakagawa2024 424 .

form
dialect

iteki
Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, Abuta

itekke
Shizunai, Samani, Tokachi

etekke
Shiranuka, Bibai, Tokachi

ecik(k)i
Ishikari

iteki ape sam ta ek | PROH fire side LOC come | Do not come near the fire! | The prohibitive adverb iteki precedes the verb, which retains the bare imperative shape with no second-person subject prefix. | nakagawa2024:424 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 924

iteki is not restricted to bare imperatives: it also
precedes indirect command forms such as kusu ne na and
nankor na , and the combination
iteki … no V expresses 'don't do X; instead do V'
nakagawa2024 425 . The alternative adverbs
ikiya , ika , and inkian take a person-marked,
non-imperative verb rather than a bare stem ‹consensus›
nakagawa2024 425–426 .

A community analysis proposes a structural distinction between
iteki and ikiya :
ikiya is an ordinary adverb compatible with subject
marking on the following verb
( ikiya e=honi arka na
'be careful of [getting] a bellyache'), whereas iteki
specifically suppresses the subject prefix of the following verb, producing
the bare imperative shape (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-12-03)
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. The proposal awaits systematic
corpus testing.

On the Ishikari variant ecik(k)i , a community
observation notes a possible metathesis path
(* iteki > etiki
> eciki ; nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord,
2023-12-24) aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. Nakagawa treats
the dialect forms as synchronic equivalents without committing to a
derivational order nakagawa2024 424 .

The prohibitive's relationship to the standard sentential negation
somo , its asymmetric properties across clause types,
and its apprehensive extension are developed in
negation-prohibitive-iteki .

Indirect and mitigated directives

A family of constructions with person-marked verbs distributes directive force
along a scale from advice and permission to near-command. All carry the
second-person subject prefix, which distinguishes them formally from the
bare-stem imperative
nakagawa2024 423–424 ; ijas2023 .

construction
value

V yak pirka
'you should …' (advice)

V yak wen
'you should not …' (negative advice)

V yakka pirka
'you may …' (permission)

V yakka wen
'it is no use [even if] …' (futility)

V kusu ne na
'you will …' (prospective near-command)

V nankor na
'you probably should …' (softened expectation)

ikiya V na
'be careful not to …' (admonitive)

e=ahunke yak pirka na | 2SG.A=let.in COND be.good FIN | You should let them in. | The conditional-advice frame yak pirka na; the second-person subject prefix e= is present, in contrast to the bare-stem imperative which suppresses it. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8(2) [aa-irc/007#211]

e=kor hapo e=nukar nankor | 2SG.A=have mother 2SG.A=see probably | You should probably go and check on your mother. | With a second-person subject, nankor shades from probability into expectation or gentle suggestion; both verbs carry the second-person prefix e=. | tamura2000:243 | SAR

ikiya e=hacir na | ADM 2SG.S=fall FIN | Take care not to fall! | The admonitive adverb ikiya precedes a person-marked verb with the 2SG.S prefix e=; the presence of the subject prefix distinguishes this from the bare-stem prohibitive iteki. | tamura2000:177 | SAR

The deontic values encoded by these constructions — obligation, expectation,
and permission — are treated systematically in
deontic-necessity .

Person marking in the directive clause

The person-marking properties of the directive system converge on a single
fact: the bare imperative — both singular and plural/polite — suppresses the
second-person subject prefix. In declarative clauses the second-person singular
agent and single-argument subject each take the prefix e= ;
a verb without that prefix occupies the same slot as a subjectless or
third-person construction. The bare imperative occupies that slot, and the
addressee is identified pragmatically. Any object prefix that the argument
structure requires remains on the verb
nakagawa2024 421–423 . The
full paradigm of subject and object prefixes is set out in
person-marking-architecture .

The plural and polite particle yan does not restore the
subject prefix: it marks plurality or politeness through the particle alone,
leaving the verb stem prefix-free. The indirect-directive constructions —
yak pirka , nankor na , and their
kin — carry the second-person subject prefix as their own diagnostic property,
setting them formally apart from the direct imperative and connecting them to
the deontic modality system of deontic-necessity
nakagawa2024 423–424 ; ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

title: The Indefinite/Fourth Person: Forms, Reference, and Agent-Defocusing
summary: The morphology of the indefinite person (a=/an=/=an, i=) and its referential range — generic 'one', inclusive 'we', definite fourth-person reference — and its agent-defocusing 'passive'/impersonal use with the does-Ainu-have-a-passive debate.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Indefinite/Fourth Person: Forms, Reference, and Agent-Defocusing
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The morphology of the indefinite person (a=/an=/=an, i=) and its referential range — generic 'one', inclusive 'we', definite fourth-person reference — and its agent-defocusing 'passive'/impersonal use with the does-Ainu-have-a-passive debate.

The formal paradigm

Three verbal indexes make up the Hokkaido Ainu form-set called the fourth person by
Nakagawa, the indefinite by Bugaeva, and the inclusive person by Takahashi:
a= or dialectally an= as the subject of a
transitive verb (A role), =an as the subject of an intransitive verb
(S), and i= as the object (O). The full S/A/O architecture of all four
persons is laid out in person-marking-architecture ; this chapter covers the
internal paradigm and the referential values licensed by the row.

role
Hokkaido form (Saru/Chitose baseline)
position
principal values

A: subject of a transitive verb
a= , dialectally an=
prefix, before the transitive stem

inclusive or hortative agent; generic/impersonal human agent; honorific addressee;
narrative protagonist; possessor in fourth-person nominal forms

S: subject of an intransitive verb
=an
suffix, after the intransitive stem

inclusive or hortative subject; honorific addressee; narrative or logophoric subject

O: object of a transitive verb
i=
prefix, before the transitive stem

fourth-person patient, recipient, or undergoer; narrator's 'me/us'; honored or inclusive
object

The row is parallel in structure to the first plural exclusive
ci= / =as / un= : both
keep distinct forms for all three roles. The S/A distinction is expressed by the side of
attachment — a transitivity-conditioned position switch absent from the first singular and the
second person. That asymmetry is the morphological basis on which Bugaeva calls these two rows
tripartite bugaeva2012 471–472 ; bugaeva2008 32 ; nakagawa2024 165–169, 174 .

eani hem paye=an ro | you also go.PL=4.S HORT | Let you and me go too! | The addressee-inclusive hortative uses the plural stem paye with =an following as S. | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 138

Side-encoding and allomorphy

The subject index selects its position by transitivity. With an intransitive predicate, the
fourth-person subject appears as the suffix =an ; with a transitive
predicate, the same referent is indexed by the prefix a= . The
following sentence contains both on adjacent predicates of different transitivity nakagawa2024 166–167 ; bugaeva2012 471–472 .

rus patek a=mi wa hotke=an pa | fur only 4.A=wear CONJ sleep=4.S PL | We wore only fur and slept. | The transitive mi 'wear' takes the prefix a=; the intransitive hotke 'sleep' takes the suffix =an. The plural auxiliary pa follows outside the person index. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 7 [aa-irc/005#157]

In Saru and the dialects to the west, a= is the regular
transitive-subject form. Elsewhere the longer allomorph an= appears in
varying distributions: Tokachi uses an= before vowel-initial stems
without first-syllable accent and a= elsewhere; Ishikari and Samani
favour an= ; Shiranuka mostly has an= with
a= chiefly before ye say ; Shizunai shows no such
split. Even Saru, Chitose, and Horobetsu preserve an= in verse
formulae, and Nakagawa regards an= as historically prior nakagawa2024 167–168 ‹speculative›. The Hokkaido-wide quantitative distribution of the two allomorphs is
incompletely documented ‹corpus-suggested›.

The clitic-or-affix status of =an and a= is
contested: Bugaeva treats =an as word-like and
a= as clitic-like on the basis of prosodic independence and
separability; Nakagawa writes all person indexes with = as a
convenient notation and separately documents the high autonomy of =an ;
Sakaguchi's formal clitic criterion — developed on Sakhalin evidence — converges on the same
autonomy ranking for the fourth-person S form bugaeva2012 472–473 ; nakagawa2024 49, 183 ; sakaguchi2024 ix, 62–64, 77–85, 163 ‹contested›. Ordering and wordhood
diagnostics are treated in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

The plural auxiliary pa of Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari positions
itself outside the person index (stem =an pa ), while the stem-forming
plural suffix -pa sits inside (stem -pa=an ).
Nakagawa's minimal pair from Saru makes the contrast explicit nakagawa2024 158 ; sakaguchi2024 12 :

nenpoka iki=an pa wa siknu=an wa | somehow do=4.S PL CONJ survive=4.S CONJ | Somehow we managed and survived, and … | The plural auxiliary pa follows iki=an, external to the fourth-person S suffix =an. | nakagawa2024:159 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 87

irwak sinne sorekusu iki-pa=an | sibling like because do-PL=4.S | because we were like siblings … | The stem-forming suffix -pa precedes =an; the ordering is reversed relative to the auxiliary pa in the preceding example. | nakagawa2024:159 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 88

Referential range: five uses

Nakagawa lists five referential values for the same formal row — addressee-inclusive first
plural, honorific second person, indefinite or impersonal human agent, the narrator or
protagonist of oral narrative, and the quoted speaker inside reported speech — stressing that
no single meaning covers all five nakagawa2024 174–179 . Tamura distributes
the same Saru forms across several distinct persons in her eleven-person system; Bugaeva uses
the cover label "indefinite" and reanalyses the quotation use as logophoric; Satō treats the
forms as polysemous; Takahashi's Tokachi account adopts "inclusive person", arguing that the
inclusive value is primary and the other uses follow from it tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34, 39 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 ; sato2008 203–208, 259–262 ; takahashi2015 205–210 ‹contested›.

function
typical reference
number behaviour

① inclusive
speaker plus addressee, 'we including you'
plural verb stem; common in cohortative frames

② honorific addressee
esteemed second person; human addressing adult men or kamuy
plural stem even for a single addressee

③ indefinite / impersonal
generic human agent, 'people, one, someone'
plural stem; agent may appear in an or wa phrase

④ narrative self-reference
protagonist or narrator's "I" in oral narrative
singular stems possible in Saru/Chitose; plural elsewhere

⑤ logophoric reported speaker
the "I/we" of a quoted or reported speaker
dialectal scope; best established for Saru

Inclusive and impersonal reference

The inclusive value fills the gap left by the first plural exclusive. The set
ci= / =as / un=
excludes the addressee; addressee-inclusive 'we' therefore requires the fourth-person row
nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 ; tamura1972 18–19 ; bugaeva2012 472, 475 . Tokachi preserves the same clusivity split, and Takahashi observes that the
inclusive/fourth-person series triggers plural verb forms even where the referent would
otherwise be singular takahashi2015 206–209 .

In its impersonal or generic value, a= indexes an unspecified human
agent on a transitive predicate. The patient remains in the O role; the verb retains
transitive valence; an explicit agent-like participant may appear in a source phrase headed by
or wa 'from'. Japanese translations routinely render the construction
with a passive, but Ainu has no subject-promoting passive morphology: the agent does not
receive subject marking, and the patient is not promoted to a nominative argument tamura1972 34 ; sato2008 203–205 ; nakagawa2024 176–177 .

wen kamuy a=maketa wa a=rayke ru-we ne | bad god 4.A=defeat CONJ 4.A=kill track-POSS COP | The evil kamuy was defeated and killed. | The patient wen kamuy stays in the object role across two transitive predicates; a= supplies an unspecified human agent. | nakagawa2024:177 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 145

kamuy or wa a=epunkine wa kamuy or wa a=kasuy wa | god place from 4.A=protect CONJ god place from 4.A=help CONJ | being protected by the gods and helped by the gods, … | The source phrase kamuy or wa names the agent-like participant; the verbs carry fourth-person A and the patient is not promoted to grammatical subject. | nakagawa2024:177 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 146

Ijäs's learner grammar illustrates the same generic-human value, glossing
a=nu as 'people hear (it)' in a lesson on perceptible sounds ijas2023 . Her notation uses a hyphen where this chapter writes = ; that
reflects a pedagogical convention carrying no claim about clitic or affix status.

maw a=nu | breeze 4.A=hear | One hears the breeze. | Constructed from Ijäs Lesson 7, where a=nu illustrates the generic-human A value; maw 'breeze' fills the object slot. | ijas2023 | HK

The generic-agent prefix combines with the nominalizer
p ~ pe to yield participant nominalizations of
the type a=e-p 'food' (literally 'what people eat'). The following
example shows a=e p in a headless relative construction:

tapan kina a=e p ne toan kina somo a=e p ne | this plant 4.A=eat NMLZ COP that plant NEG 4.A=eat NMLZ COP | This plant is one that people eat; that plant is one that people do not eat. | The head p is the thing eaten; a= supplies the unspecified human eater. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 14 [aa-irc/020#27]

The morphology and arity diagnostics of p ~
pe are developed in nominalization-p-pe .

The fourth-person object i= and the detransitive prefix i-

The object index i= belongs to the same paradigmatic row as
a= and =an . In narrative and address it indexes
the fourth-person argument as patient, recipient, or undergoer — the narrator's 'me' or the
honored person as object nakagawa2024 169 .

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=lodge CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The repeated i= is fourth-person O, indexing the narrator/protagonist as object and recipient across three transitive predicates. | nakagawa2024:170 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

a=rehe Ikuresuye sekor a=i=reko | 4.POSS=name Ikuresuye QUOT 4.A=4.O=name | I was named Ikuresuye. | The naming formula combines fourth-person possessive a= on rehe 'name', fourth-person A on the predicate, and fourth-person O i= for the narrator/protagonist. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 7 [aa-irc/005#1]

The prefix i= is phonologically identical to the detransitivising
prefix i- 'something/someone', which incorporates a generic object and
reduces the verb's transitivity. The two are notated differently — =
for the person index, - for the derivational prefix — and they differ
syntactically: the person index occupies the O slot of an already-transitive stem, while the
antipassive derives an intransitive stem from a transitive base. Tamura already keeps the two
apart on functional grounds; their diachronic relationship is disputed. Older accounts derive
the detransitive prefix from the fourth-person object; Bugaeva argues in the opposite
direction, connecting the antipassive to O-incorporation of a generic noun; Nam proposes a
generic-noun source *hi/*ihi 'thing, place' for the detransitive
prefix directly tamura1972 35 ; bugaeva2021antip 235–239 ; nam2021 62–74 ‹contested›. The detransitive prefix and its morphology are treated in
antipassive-detransitive-i .

Honorific, narrative, and logophoric uses

Honorific use applies the fourth-person row to an esteemed addressee. Nakagawa describes
women addressing adult men and humans addressing kamuy in prayer; Tamura documents the same
politeness value for Saru; Satō groups it with the literary-person system of oral performance
nakagawa2024 175–176 ; tamura1972 28 ; sato2008 259–262 . The verb takes a plural stem even for a single referent, a number agreement conditioned
by register rather than by the count of persons.

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | A single addressee is indexed by =an on the plural motion stem arki; the plural stem reflects honorific register. | nakagawa2024:176 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140, woman addressing an adult man

In oral narrative the protagonist's first person is expressed by the same row. In Saru and
Chitose a singular narrator may appear with singular verb stems and the pronoun
asinuma ; Horobetsu and Shizunai display opposite mismatches between
verb number and the available pronouns nakagawa2024 174–179, 254–256 . Nakagawa
treats the narrative use as the indefinite person applied to an unanchored referent; Satō
argues the literary self-reference derives from a first-person strategy and cannot be
collapsed into the indefinite; Bugaeva, finding comparable uses in non-folklore Asahikawa
texts, removes the dependence on an elegant register entirely sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ; bugaeva2008 37–44 ‹contested›.

ratcitara hosipi=an yakka a=uni un tunasno hosipi=an pe ne kusu | slowly return=4.S although 4.POSS=home to quickly return=4.S NMLZ COP because | Although I returned slowly, because I was able to return quickly to my home, … | A singular narrator uses =an on both intransitive predicates and the possessive prefix a= on uni 'home'; the singular stem hosipi confirms singular-narrator morphology in Saru. | nakagawa2024:167 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 109

The fifth use — the quoted speaker's self-reference inside a reported-speech clause — is
analysed by Bugaeva as logophoric: the fourth-person row tracks the discourse-source of the
embedded proposition, functioning as a participant-tracking device independent of literary
genre bugaeva2008 37–44 ; tamura1972 19–20, 22–23 ; nakagawa2024 179 . The social and discourse dimensions of the honorific and logophoric values are developed
in fourth-person-honorific and logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Terminology and comparative note

The competing labels encode different analytical choices. Nakagawa's "fourth person" positions
the row as a formal residual class within a four-person paradigm, gathering everything that
persons one to three cannot express nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 . Tamura's Saru
analysis distributes the same forms across the inclusive, two quotative persons, the honorific
second, and the indefinite, treating the labels as functionally distinct values rather than a
single form-class tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34, 39 . Bugaeva's "indefinite (IND)"
foregrounds the generic and inclusive core; the quotation use is relabelled logophoric, a
move supported by its occurrence outside the narrative register bugaeva2012 474–476 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 . Takahashi's "inclusive person" gives the Tokachi
system a unified participant label, arguing that the inclusive function is primary takahashi2015 205–210 ‹contested›.
