Dal Corso applies the label "fourth person" to the cognate Sakhalin AN-series by word form,
while his diachronic reanalysis — which rejects the tripartite component for Sakhalin and
treats the cognate system as mixed nominative–neutral–inverse — is explicitly cautioned as
inapplicable to Hokkaido dalcorso2025align 187–188, 200, 213 ; sakaguchi2024 26–27, 162–163 ‹contested›. For Hokkaido, the tripartite characterization of
a= / =an / i= —
formalized in Bugaeva's mixed-alignment account and adopted in the architecture chapter —
remains the standard analysis bugaeva2012 472 .

title: Personal, Reflexive, and Emphatic Pronouns
summary: The free/independent personal pronouns and their emphatic/contrastive use alongside the obligatory bound affixes, plus the reflexive and emphatic 'self' nominals used as NP arguments, kept distinct from the verbal reflexive prefix (Part XI).
part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Personal, Reflexive, and Emphatic Pronouns
Part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
Summary: The free/independent personal pronouns and their emphatic/contrastive use alongside the obligatory bound affixes, plus the reflexive and emphatic 'self' nominals used as NP arguments, kept distinct from the verbal reflexive prefix (Part XI).

The independent pronouns and their place in the grammar

Hokkaido Ainu marks person obligatorily on the finite verb; the independent personal pronouns
are additive elements, not the primary vehicle of argument tracking. Nakagawa assigns them
arity 0 — they behave like adverbial adjuncts, are never obligatory, and cannot be
incorporated into a verb stem, unlike other noun phrases of arity −1
nakagawa2024 250, 262–263 . Kindaichi and Chiri captured the same point by
calling the pronoun a 贅語 'pleonasm', comparing
kuani ku=eraman 'I know' to Latin ego scio and French
moi je sais : the verbal index alone suffices, and the pronoun doubles it for emphasis
kindaichi1936 53 . Tamura's Saru description confirms the pattern —
pronouns function syntactically like adverbs, appearing only when a specific pragmatic
condition is met tamura1971 3–5 . The practical result is that pronouns appear
far less freely than in Japanese, and third-person forms are particularly rare: where Japanese
uses 彼/彼女, Hokkaido Ainu overwhelmingly prefers demonstrative phrases
( toan kur 'that person', nea menoko 'that
woman') nakagawa2024 250, 256 .

The sole pronoun that is dialect-invariant across all of Hokkaido is 2SG
eani . All other forms show at least some regional variation; the
central division separates Saru and Chitose (1SG kani ) from the rest
of Hokkaido (1SG kuani ), and the plural and fourth-person rows diverge
considerably across zones nakagawa2024 250–251 . The verbal affixes these
pronouns reduplicate are described in person-marking-architecture .

Reflexive marking in Hokkaido Ainu is realized verbally, not by dedicated reflexive
pronouns. The prefixes yay- and si- , which
redirect an action onto the subject or its possessor, are covered in
reflexive-yay and reflexive-possessive-si . The emphatic 'I myself /
you yourself' function that some languages express with a distinct intensive-pronoun series
is realized in HA by the independent pronoun plus a focus or topic particle — the same
pronouns described here, in their focus-hosting function.

Internal structure: the nominalised existential

The personal pronouns are not primitive forms. Tamura's foundational analysis derives each
one by attaching a person index to one of two existential roots — singular
an 'exist (SG)' or plural oka(y)
'exist (PL)' — and then nominalizing the result with the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed (affiliative) form suffix, whose base
is the formal noun hi thing, fact (formal noun)
tamura1971 3–5 ; nakagawa2024 253 . The formula is:
person-index + existential root + hi .

A diagnostic point secures this analysis. The plural pronouns use the
transitive-subject / possessive person prefixes ( ci= ,
a(n)= , eci= ) rather than the
intransitive-S suffixes ( =as , =an ) that a
finite plural-existential verb would carry. This shows that the base is a noun
oka(y)+hi 'the (plural) being', which is possessed, not a finite
verb oka(y) that is person-inflected
nakagawa2024 253 ; kindaichi1936 53 .

The formal noun hi surfaces in the singular pronouns but is lost in
the plurals. The singulars preserve it: ku=an-hi → kani (with
contraction), e=an-hi → eani ,
an-hi → anihi . In the plural series the sequence
okay-hi would produce an illicit syllable contact; hi is
suppressed, and the final -y of okay further
drops in Saru, yielding oka nakagawa2024 253 .

Composition of the personal pronouns (Saru/Chitose/Shizunai baseline; after Nakagawa
2024: 253, citing Tamura 1971: 3–5)

pronoun
person
segmentation

kani / kuani
1SG
k(u)= + an + hi (Saru/Chitose contracted; elsewhere
full)

eani
2SG
e= + an + hi

anihi / sinuma
3SG
an + hi (= ihi ); or the
sinuma -base type (see § independent-personal-pronouns subtypes )

anani / asinuma
4SG
an= + an + hi ; or a= + sinuma

c(i)oka(y)
1PL.EXCL
ci= + oka(y) (+ hi , suppressed)

ecioka(y)
2PL
eci= + oka(y) (+ hi ,
suppressed)

oka(y)
3PL
oka(y) (+ hi , suppressed)

a(n)oka(y)
4PL
a(n)= + oka(y) (+ hi ,
suppressed)

The 1SG contraction kuani → kani in Saru and Chitose follows the
same vowel-deletion process as ciep → cep 'fish', where the high
vowel of the prefix is absorbed by the adjacent syllable
aynucorporadiscord (gengojiro, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-02-05; asserted,
corroborated by nakagawa2024 250–251 ). Elsewhere in Hokkaido the uncontracted
form kuani is maintained.

The dialect paradigm

The full Hokkaido pronoun inventory spans six major dialect zones. The singular/plural
distinction is maintained throughout, though the plural and fourth-person rows show the
greatest variation nakagawa2024 250–252, tables 60–65 .

Independent personal pronouns across Hokkaido dialects (Nakagawa 2024: 250–251, tables
60–65)

Saru PL

Shizunai SG / PL

Corpus search returns kani at 502 tokens,
eani at 716, and asinuma at 668; by contrast,
the first-person exclusive plural coka reaches only 39 tokens
‹corpus-confirmed›. The disproportion between singular and plural frequencies matches
Nakagawa's description of the plural forms as genuinely marginal
nakagawa2024 250 . Clusivity in the first-person plural — exclusive
ci= / =as versus the fourth-person inclusive —
is treated in first-person-plural-and-clusivity .

Two further formation types

Beyond the existential-base type, two additional strategies contribute to the plural and
third/fourth-person rows nakagawa2024 254–255 .

Type II — person-prefix + utari : Several dialects
form plural pronouns with the
nominal-number-utar-transnumerality associative-plural noun
utar relatives, kin group (possessed form utari ):
Tokachi ciutari (1PL), eciutari (2PL),
anutari (4PL). These forms retain the lexical sense of
utari 'kin, associates' and stand "halfway between a pronoun and a
common noun" nakagawa2024 254 . The Saru/Chitose sequences
k=utari , a=utari similarly mean 'my / their
family' with a third-person (non-inclusive) verb, and only Tokachi
anutari — which triggers a fourth-person (inclusive) verb — functions
as a full plural pronoun 'we (including you)' nakagawa2024 254 .

Type III — sinuma base: The 3SG form
sinuma and 4SG asinuma are built on a
distinct base whose internal structure is unanalysed synchronically. Kindaichi posited a
literary 2SG form esinuma by analogy, but Nakagawa found no attested
example and treats it as a phantom form ‹speculative›
nakagawa2024 255 .

Functions: emphasis, contrast, and focus hosting

Nakagawa identifies three conditions that trigger an independent pronoun where the verbal
index alone would otherwise suffice nakagawa2024 255–256 . A neutral
predication ('I am here', 'he came') carries no pronoun; a self-introduction such as
*asinuma anak … a=ne 'I am —' would be almost never said without
special emphasis nakagawa2024 255 .

Emphasis

When a participant is singled out from alternatives ('I and not someone else will do it'),
the pronoun precedes the verb and co-indexes the verbal prefix. In the following Chitose
example, kani adds the emphatic reading on top of the
already-complete verbal index k= :

kani k=uomare kusu ne na | 1sg 1SG.A=clean.up PURP COP FIN | (I) will clean up, so… | Nakagawa ex. 321. The verbal index k= (reduced form of ku= before a vowel) already marks the 1SG agent; kani adds the emphatic 'I (not someone else)'. The clause continues; kusu ne na closes the coordinating frame. | nakagawa2024:255 | CHI

Kindaichi and Chiri's classical demonstration of the same construction places it
explicitly in the register of Latin and French emphasis:

kuani ku=eraman | 1sg 1SG.A=know | I know. | Kindaichi & Chiri §78. The full form kuani rather than contracted Saru kani reflects the older or literary register of the source. ku= is the complete 1SG.A index; kuani is strictly additive. | kindaichi1936:53 | SAR

Contrast

When two participants are explicitly opposed in consecutive clauses — 'you do X; I do Y'
— each is marked by the independent pronoun plus the topic marker
anak=ne . The following Saru dialogue from the Akor Itak teaching
material pairs eani anakne (addressee) against
kani anakne (speaker):

eani anak=ne onne kur neno e=yaynu hi | 2sg TOP old man like 2SG.A=think NMLZ | Do you think like an old man? | The following sentence in the source — kani anakne mokor akkari sus kusu pet or un k=arpa rusuy 'I'd rather go swimming in the river' — completes the contrast pair. Both pronouns carry anak=ne to mark the opposed topics. The pragmatics of topic fronting are developed in the chapter on topic-marking-anakne. | utari1994 | SAR | Akor Itak §4 [akor-itak/004#6], Saru

Focus-particle hosting

Focus and scalar particles such as ka 'even/also',
anakne (topic-focus), and hem must attach
to a noun phrase; they cannot host on a verbal index.
*ku= anakne and *ku= ka are
ungrammatical. An independent pronoun is therefore obligatory whenever such a particle
scopes over a participant nakagawa2024 256 . In the following Saru example,
asinuma hosts the scalar particle ka :

asinuma ka i=nukar | 4sg FOC 4.O=look.at | …(they) looked at me too. | Nakagawa ex. 324, Saru. The fourth-person object i= indexes the narrator as the entity looked at. The scalar particle ka 'even/also' attaches to the pronoun asinuma; without the pronoun there is no NP host for ka. The full range of fourth-person object i= uses is treated in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm. | nakagawa2024:256 | SAR

The same constraint governs topic fronting: kani anakne … is the
canonical pattern, impossible as *ku= anakne . The broader
implications for argument structure and pro-drop in HA are taken up in
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument , and the particle paradigm in
topic-marking-anakne .

The fourth-person pronoun and the singular/plural asymmetry

The fourth-person row is where the dialect paradigm shows its sharpest internal
contradictions, providing, in Nakagawa's words, "an important hint for the historical origin
of the fourth-person singular" that remains unresolved ‹contested›
nakagawa2024 251–252 .

In Saru and Chitose both the pronoun and the verb distinguish singular from plural: pronoun
asinuma (SG) vs aoka (PL), paralleled by
distinct singular and plural verbal forms. Shizunai makes the opposite move: the pronoun
preserves a singular/plural distinction ( anani SG vs
anoka PL), but the verb is invariably plural regardless
of which pronoun is used — the pronoun-level contrast survives without a verbal counterpart.
Horobetsu shows the mirror image: the verb retains a singular/plural opposition, but the
only available fourth-person pronoun is the plural-base
aokay , used even for a single narrator
nakagawa2024 252 . These "opposite twists" are left as an open diachronic
question by Nakagawa.

One community-proposed reconstruction treats the 4th-person prefix as
a- / =an < *an- , with Shizunai
anokay preserving the older form: the nasal-drop that produced
Saru/western a= vs Tokachi/Ishikari an=
would be blocked at pronoun level because the result would create hiatus
aynucorporadiscord (.k1270, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-03-22; ‹speculative›,
attributed to Nakagawa). The connection between this prefix form and the broader morphological
alignment of the fourth person is discussed in
person-marking-architecture and indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

In oral narrative, the fourth-person pronoun asinuma functions
routinely as the first-person narrative self, alternating with or supplementing the verbal
index ku= / k= . Corpus evidence places
asinuma at 668 tokens, predominantly in Saru narrative texts. The
fourth-person pronoun appears in this function with topic-focus particles — the same
focus-hosting role described above:

asinuma anak=ne iwor so ka a=e-hoyupu wa | 4sg TOP hunting.ground summit over 4.A=APPL-fly.about CONJ | asinuma anakne iwor so ka a=ehoyupu wa | As for myself, I flew about over the hunting ground… | The fourth-person pronoun asinuma serves as the first-person narrative self throughout this text. The topic marker anak=ne cannot attach to a verbal affix and requires the free pronoun as host. The ka here is the postposition 'over/on top of' (path marker with the applicative e-), not the scalar focus particle. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話8 (その1), ILCAA Ainu materials (沙流) [aa-irc/006#52]

The honorific and logophoric uses of the fourth person — including the use of a fourth-person
verb for a single esteemed addressee — are treated in fourth-person-honorific .
The full debate over terminology (fourth person, indefinite, inclusive) and the referential
range of the a= / =an / i=
affix set is carried in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Summary: A citation index of every quoted example by source edition and locus, organized further by genre, narrator/transcriber, and dialect, cross-referenced to the glossed-texts corpus.

Cited examples by source

This index lists every source from which an interlinear example is drawn, with the chapters
in which its examples appear and the number of examples taken from it. It is generated from the
909 attested and constructed examples across the grammar; constructed examples are
attributed to the source whose analysis they illustrate. Full bibliographic details are in
consolidated-references-bibliography .

Examples by dialect

The distribution of interlinear examples across the dialects and dialect labels used in the
grammar. Saru (SAR) and Chitose (CHI) form the descriptive core; other Hokkaido dialects and the
labelled Sakhalin (SA) contrast appear where documented.

title: Index of Grammatical Morphemes and Affixes

text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Index of Grammatical Morphemes and Affixes
Part: Part XXIII — Reference Apparatus (Back Matter)
Summary: An exhaustive finding list of every bound morpheme, clitic, and grammatical particle, organized by form with gloss, class, dialect variants, and section reference, doubling as a reverse-lookup grammar dictionary.

Index of grammatical morphemes

This index lists the person clitics (marked = ), bound affixes (marked
- ), and core grammatical particles and formatives that appear in the
grammar's interlinear examples, each with the chapters in which it is exemplified. It is generated
from the example corpus and is an index to attested usage, not an exhaustive morpheme dictionary;
the analysis of each form is given in the chapters cross-referenced. Lexical roots are omitted.

Chapter: Index of Subjects
Part: Part XXIII — Reference Apparatus (Back Matter)
Summary: An alphabetical topic and concept index keyed to chapter and section, with a parallel typological-feature sub-index for cross-linguistic look-up.

Index of subjects

This subject index is generated from the section headings of every chapter of the grammar. Each
entry links to the chapter or chapters in which the topic is treated; where a topic recurs, the
chapters are listed in reading order. For grammatical formatives see
index-of-grammatical-morphemes ; for cited examples by source see
index-of-examples-sources-dialects .

title: A Glossed Inonno-itak (Ritual Prayer)
summary: A ritual prayer (inonno-itak) text glossed and annotated, showcasing the elevated/honorific register, formulaic parallelism, and direct benedictive address to the kamuy.
part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: A Glossed Inonno-itak (Ritual Prayer)
Part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
Summary: A ritual prayer (inonno-itak) text glossed and annotated, showcasing the elevated/honorific register, formulaic parallelism, and direct benedictive address to the kamuy.

Prayer speech in the genre system

Prayer speech — inonno itak prayer words or kamuy nomi divine worship /
prayer ceremony — belongs to the chant (唱え言) macro-category of Ainu oral
performance, whose distinguishing feature is that the speaker's will is directed toward a
non-present audience: deities, spirits, the dead, or distant humans
endo2022 §2.3 . The chant category is set out alongside the narrative and song
categories in oral-literature-genre-taxonomy ; the present chapter treats the
grammatical and register properties of prayer speech specifically.

Within the chant sub-system, prayer ( kamuy nomi itak divine-worship words ) is
distinguished from incantation by improvisation: a prayer is composed fresh each time, to the
worshipper's own melody, whereas an incantation is memorised word-for-word and shared with a
fixed community tune endo2022 §2.3 . Prayer is further distinguished from the
speech-act of public negotiation ( caranke formal dispute / ceremonial debate ) by
its divine addressee. The genre boundaries are set out in the table below.

type
addressee
performer
form
fixed/improvised

prayer ( kamuy nomi itak ; inonno itak )
deities
adult men
melodic
improvised; personal melody

incantation
deities
any age or sex
melodic
fixed word-for-word; shared melody

negotiation ( caranke )
humans
adult men
melodic/spoken
improvised

greeting ( uwerankarap itak )
humans
mainly men
melodic
formulaic but flexible

funeral words
the dead
—
—
—

(After endo2022 §2.3 .) Both
kamuy nomi divine worship and inonno itak prayer words name the
prayer register, the former foregrounding the act of worship and the latter the word-substance of
the address. kayano1996 s.v. kamuynomi is silent on the etymology of
nomi worship, revere ; in attested prose, narrators embed both terms when
describing a ceremony. The corpus of 209,878 sentences returns kamuynomi 405 times
(rank 405) and inonno 203 times (rank 675), though these counts fold together
metalinguistic mentions in narrative texts with tokens in actual prayer performance
‹corpus-suggested›.

Performative context: kamuy-nomi and the ceremony

A kamuy nomi is an address to the divine world conducted at the altar
( nusa sacred stick altar ) or the hearth. The fire deity ( ape huci fire
grandmother ) is invoked first in every prayer, as the goddess who relays human words to the
gods; all subsequent divine recipients are addressed through her
endo2022 §2.3 . Prayer is structurally a two-stage act of communication:
worshipper → fire deity → target deity. The
right to lead prayer is restricted to adult men ‹consensus›; women's
formal expression of gratitude takes the verbal gesture of hinna thanks (women)
accompanied by raymik stroking below the nose with the index finger , while men
perform onkami bow with both hands (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-26,
asserted; aynucorporadiscord ).

The material offerings that accompany prayer — inaw wooden prayer-stick (shaved wood
offering) , fermented millet wine ( sake ), and dumplings ( sito ) — are
enumerated in narrative prose when speakers describe the ceremony from outside it. In one Saru
uwepeker, the narrator 川上まつ子 lists the offerings: kamuynomi atpa ne,
a=nomi wa inaw ne ciki sake ne ciki sito ne ciki pirka uske 'at the start of the
kamuy-nomi, [we] worship and (offer) fine things — inaw, sake, dumplings…'
ilcaa1976 (川上まつ子, AA研 uwepeker 12, Saru). The disjunctive
X ne ciki 'whether it be X' is an enumerating formula in elevated
narrative, not specific to prayer speech itself — see
oral-literature-genre-taxonomy the genre taxonomy chapter .

The ceremonies associated with prayer speech include the seasonal worship calendars
( Asirpanomi New Year's prayer , Cinomisirkamuynomi mid-year prayer ,
Asircepnomi first-salmon prayer ; nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-03-18,
asserted; aynucorporadiscord ) and the bear-sending ceremony. The verb forms for the
sending ceremonies are semantically distinct: omante send home (the bear cub) /
hopunire send to the mountain (wild-caught animals) /
iwakte send off (the dog) (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-17, asserted,
citing 欠ヶ端和也 2016; aynucorporadiscord ). The prayer speech that accompanies these
ceremonies follows the same grammatical register as all kamuy-nomi, regardless of the ceremony
type.

The register of prayer: atomte-itak

Prayer speech belongs to the elevated register atomte itak ornamented/poetic
language (雅語), which is distinguished from everyday speech ( yayan itak ordinary
language ) by a set of grammatical and lexical properties described in
poetic-archaic-elevated-register . Kindaichi's ranking of genres by elegant-language
density — greeting < prayer < incantation < caranke <
yukar — places prayer above everyday greeting speech but below the heaviest
verse genres endo2022 §3 . In Nakagawa's reformulation, elegant and everyday
language are not a binary but stages on a verse/improvisation continuum, and the prayer
sub-register is sufficiently distinct from the yukar sub-register that Nakagawa proposes
a prayer-word vs. yukar-word classification as more analytically useful than the
simple elegant/everyday dichotomy endo2022 §3.4 ‹contested›.

Four defining properties of atomte itak (as formulated in Nakagawa 2001a[1987], via
endo2022 §3 ) apply to prayer speech: (1) verse structure built on
five- or four-syllable line units; (2) formulaic phrases deployed to meet the syllable count;
(3) morphologically more complex and polysynthetic forms than their everyday equivalents; and
(4) a distinct, genre-conditioned vocabulary. Properties (3) and (4) are examined in
§§ applicative morphology and
prayer vocabulary below; property (2) in
parallelism-couplets-and-word-pairs .

Grammatical hallmarks of prayer speech

Person marking: first-singular ku- in prayer

The most striking person-marking contrast between prayer speech and narrative oral literature is
that prayer uses the first-person singular prefix ku= 1SG agent
(and suffix =an 1SG intransitive subject in singular intransitives) where
verse narrative uses the fourth person a= 4th-person agent .
In narrative genres — heroic epic, divine epic, and prose tale — the protagonist narrates in a
non-everyday first person realized by the fourth-person affixes
a= / =an , which indexes the narrative voice with a degree of
indirection compatible with the "dream-origin" hypothesis for oral literature
sato2008 259–261 ; the full account of this fourth-person narrative function
is given in logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse and
fourth-person-honorific .

Prayer speech addresses the deities directly in the worshipper's own voice, and uses
ku= accordingly. An attested prayer text from the Monbetsu region (鍋澤モトゾウ
/Motoanrek, narrator; cited via endo2022 §2.3 ) shows the forms
ku-e-ko-pase-… and k=e-ko-onkami
'I reverently worship [you]' — both built on the first-person singular
prefix ku- / k- (reduced allomorph before a vowel-initial stem),
not the fourth person. The contrast with narrative is thus: fourth person in narrative
oral literature, first singular in prayer ‹corpus-suggested›. See
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and
person-marking-architecture for the person paradigm.

ku=onkami ruwe tapan na | 1SG.A=worship track-POSS DEM.PROX FIN | I offer worship (this is a fact). | Constructed to illustrate the 1SG prefix in prayer speech; the ruwe tapan na frame marks formal/ceremonial assertion — see §ruwe-tapan below.

Applicative case-marking

Prayer belongs to the elevated register's most characteristic morphological pattern:
locative and goal relations are expressed by applicative prefixes
( e- , o- , ko- ) rather than by postpositional case particles
sato2008 263 ; endo2022 §3.4 . The applicative system is
described in poetic-archaic-elevated-register ; what is distinctive in prayer
is the density of stacked applicatives — two or more prefixes on the same
verb, encoding simultaneously the goal deity and the manner of approach. The Monbetsu prayer
text cited above shows k=e-ko-onkami : the goal applicative
ko- goal/recipient applicative marks the deity as the target of worship, and
the additional e- path/domain applicative encodes the dimension of address
endo2022 §2.3 .

The contrast between everyday and elevated locative marking is illustrated in
sato2008 263 : the everyday form uses the particle
ta locative case particle — tanto Ukurmem ta
ku-sirepa 'I arrived at Ukurmem today' — whereas the elevated form marks the same
relation with the goal applicative ko- :

kamuy menoko uni-hi tane anakne a=ko-sirepa na | god woman house-POSS now TOP 4.A=APPL-arrive FIN | Now I arrived at the divine woman's house. | Satō 2008 ex. 20 (Chitose): elegant-register example contrasting with the colloquial particle-marked form. The goal applicative ko- incorporates the locative relation that would be marked by ta in everyday speech. Here the fourth-person a= marks the (narrative) first person, illustrating the register's applicative morphology in a verse context; in prayer proper, ku- would replace a= (see §person-marking). | sato2008:263 | CHI

In prayer speech the same applicative morphology applies, but with the first-singular
prefix. The pattern k=e-ko- combines two applicative prefixes:
ko- for the divine recipient and e- for the domain of address.
Multiple applicative stacking is an established property of the elevated register and is
treated typologically in poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

Prayer-specific vocabulary and honorific epithets

The lexical register of prayer is genre-conditioned in a stronger sense than the
elevated/everyday dichotomy alone would predict: certain epithets and noun phrases
occur only in prayer and are not found in everyday speech or in other verse
genres. Endō identifies this as a property that motivates treating prayer vocabulary as a
distinct sub-class of the elevated register rather than simply a variety of the yukar
lexicon endo2022 §3.4 .

The fire deity provides the clearest documented case. In everyday speech and in the
divine epic ( kamuy yukar ), the fire deity is called
ape huci fire grandmother . In prayer, two distinct epithets replace this:

genre / register
form
gloss
source

everyday; kamuy yukar
ape huci
'fire grandmother'
endo2022 §1, §3.4

prayer only
mosir kor huci
'grandmother who rules the land'
endo2022 §2.3

prayer; incantation
i-resu huci / i-resu kamuy
'the grandmother/deity who raised me'
endo2022 §1, §2.3, §3.4

The epithet mosir kor huci 'grandmother who rules the land' was documented in
the prayer text of 鍋澤モトゾウ (Motoanrek, Monbetsu; cited via endo2022 §2.3 )
and is described by Endō as "not found in any other genre". The epithet
i-resu huci (where i-resu- raise me; my raiser = 1.O-raise =
'the one who raised me') or its kamuy-variant
i-resu kamuy references the fire deity's role as the deity who reared and
protects the community, a relationship that everyday ape huci does not encode
endo2022 §3.4 . Endō (2009, cited via endo2022 §3.4 )
further notes that the modifier kamuy god; splendid (modifier) with the
meaning 'splendid' or honorific intensifier occurs in heroic epics but not in prayer, a
further sub-register distinction within elevated speech ‹corpus-suggested›.

The elegant kin terms documented in the lexicon also belong to this prayer-adjacent register:
ona father (literary/formal) and unu mother (literary/formal)
are the elevated equivalents of the colloquial
haca father (colloquial) / hapo mother (colloquial) (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-06-15, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ). Their
attestation in prayer contexts has not been independently verified from prayer texts, but
the community observation is consistent with the elegant-register lexical substitution
described in endo2022 §3 ‹corpus-suggested›.

The ruwe tapan ceremonial closure

Prayer utterances, and formal speech-acts that invoke prayer register, frequently close with
the frame ruwe tapan na , a ceremonial assertion tail. In
tamura1996 the frame is marked as a register-specific feature of formal/
ceremonial speech; it appears in endo2022 §2.3 prayer contexts and is
also documented in other elevated speech (the greeting and negotiation genres). The frame
combines the evidential formal noun ruwe track, fact (evidential nominalization)
— the same element treated as an inferential evidential in
ruwe-ne-inferential — with the proximate demonstrative
tapan this (proximate) in place of the copula ne . The effect is
to assert the clause content as an already-established fact that the addressee can witness
directly; in prayer speech, the deity is the witness
endo2022 §2.3 ; tamura1996 s.v. ruwe tapan .

sonno yayrayke kewtum ku=kor wa ku=onkami ru-we tapan na | truly be.grateful heart 1SG.A=have CONJ 1SG.A=worship track-POSS DEM.PROX FIN | sonno yayrayke kewtum ku=kor wa ku=onkamuy ruwe tapan na | Truly with a grateful heart I offer worship — this is a fact. | The source text has ku=onkamuy, a form with final -y not found in Saru/Chitose documentation of onkami; it may reflect Samani dialect phonology or a different stem shape. Morphemic line normalised to onkami; the ain line gives the source form. ruwe tapan na is the ceremonial assertion frame. | ainukoraci1991 | HK | Ōno Tetsuhito, Ainu Times issue 41 (Samani)

The full evidential system built on formal nouns is set out in
evidential-schema-overview . What the prayer use adds is that
ruwe tapan (versus the more common ruwe ne ) is
register-marked: it is a ceremonial variant found in formal genres and not in everyday
assertion. Whether the selection of tapan 'this' over ne (copula)
encodes a special speech-act value — presenting the prayer content as observable to the
divine audience — or whether it is simply a lexically frozen formulaic closure is not
settled by the available sources ‹speculative›.

Number agreement in the elevated register

A diagnostic of elevated speech reported for both Saru and Chitose is the
number-agreement override : in everyday speech, the suffix
=an combines only with a plural verb form (e.g., arki=an come.PL=4.S
'we come'), never with a singular stem ( *ek=an would be
ungrammatical in conversation). In the elevated register, =an may appear on a
singular verb form — ek=an 'I come (sg.)' — to index the
first-person narrative voice of the verse persona
sato2008 263 ; endo2022 §3.4 . As prayer speech uses the
first-singular ku= rather than the fourth-person a= , the
number-agreement override in its narrative form does not apply within prayer itself; but
the wider pattern of agreement relaxation is part of the atomte-itak grammar that prayer
shares with yukar and kamuy yukar.

Prayer vs. incantation: fixed and improvised chant

The prayer/incantation contrast rests on a single axis: prayer is improvised ("not memorised
like scripture; spun out by improvisation, different each time", Nakagawa 2016: 16, cited via
endo2022 §2.3 ), while incantation is word-for-word fixed and communally shared.
Both use melody; both address deities; both belong to the chant category. Grammatically, the
shared elevated-register morphology (applicatives, archaic vocabulary, number override) applies
to both, since both are composed in atomte itak . The improvised character of prayer
means that no canonical prayer text has the status of a fixed literary work in the way that a
kamuy yukar or yukar text does: prayers are attested only as specimens preserved in recordings
or transcriptions, not as memorized texts reproduced with authority by named successors
endo2022 §2.3 .

At the same time, prayer is heavily formulaic at the level of the phrase and the opening
sequence (address to the fire deity; enumeration of offerings), even though the full utterance
is improvised. This is the pattern Nakagawa identifies for the elevated register generally:
formulae — fixed verse phrases that are learned and re-deployed — are the unit of memorisation,
not the text endo2022 §3 . The formulaic patterns in prayer overlap with those
in yukar and kamuy yukar (couplet structures, tu ~ / re ~
'two ~ / three ~' intensification doublets, applicative periphrases) and are described in
parallelism-couplets-and-word-pairs .

Attestation and source coverage

Actual prayer texts preserved in audio or transcription are sparser in the accessible
Hokkaido corpus than narrative texts, for reasons Endō notes: prayer is a performative act
tied to ceremony, not a memorised genre reproduced for entertainment or transmission
endo2022 §2.3 . The Hokkaido corpus (209,878 sentences) contains
metalinguistic mentions of prayer words embedded in narrative (uwepeker, kamuy yukar) rather
than extended stretches of prayer speech itself. The primary documented prayer text accessible
for grammatical analysis is the Monbetsu (Biratori) example attributed to 鍋澤モトゾウ
(Motoanrek), cited and analysed by endo2022 §2.3 ; further attested prayer
language appears in the Biratori oral-literature archives
biratori1969 and in the ILCAA Ainu language materials
ilcaa1976 through prosodic and formulaic phrases embedded in narrators' own
metalinguistic commentary.

The renewal of prayer speech in the revitalisation era has produced written specimens — the
ainukoraci1991 periodical includes self-composed prayer formulas by speakers
including 大野徹人 (Samani) — but these are modern compositions; they attest living competence
in the prayer register rather than traditional continuous transmission. The evidential
tail ruwe tapan na glossed above (§ ruwe tapan )
comes from such a modern specimen. The distinction between documented traditional prayer texts
and revitalisation-era compositions is maintained throughout; the register properties described
in this chapter are drawn from traditional materials unless stated otherwise.

The grammar of the prayer register intersects with several other chapters of Part XIX and
Part X: the elevated applicative apparatus is treated comparatively across genres in
poetic-archaic-elevated-register ; the honorific and logophoric uses of the fourth
person (relevant to how deity address and narrative voice interact) are analysed in
fourth-person-honorific ; the genre-conditioned distribution of the evidential
system (including ruwe tapan ) appears in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre ; and the full honorific and taboo
register (including hunting-taboo vocabulary and in-law speech, which shares some properties
with prayer-register avoidance strategies) is in
honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers .

title: Interjections, Response Words, Ideophones, and Sound Symbolism
summary: The expressive minor classes — interjections and conversational formulae, ideophones/mimetics, and size/intensity sound-symbolic gradation.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Interjections, Response Words, Ideophones, and Sound Symbolism
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: The expressive minor classes — interjections and conversational formulae, ideophones/mimetics, and size/intensity sound-symbolic gradation.

Word-class status

Interjections (間投詞) occupy the most peripheral position in Hokkaido Ainu's part-of-speech
system. The diagnostic is syntactic: an interjection enters no grammatical relation with any
other clause element and constitutes a complete utterance by itself
nakagawa2024 290 ; sato2008 2–3 . This separates them
from adverbs-degree-comparison adverbs , which share the arity-zero slot but
modify a VP, and from particles, which attach to an autonomous element. The six-class system
and its diagnostics are laid out in word-class-inventory-and-diagnostics .

Refsing, working on Shizunai, treated interjections as a member of a single nominal
macro-class together with conjunctions and adverbs, rather than as an independent class
refsing1986 74, 76 . Nakagawa and Satō reject the lumping: adverbs and
conjunctions enter syntactic relations; canonical interjections do not
nakagawa2024 290 . The categories do, however, grade into each other at the
margin — directive words like hetak and hokure
can immediately precede an imperative verb, functioning as manner adverbs, while retaining
their classification as interjections in Nakagawa's analysis ‹contested›
nakagawa2024 292 .

The class is large and heavily dialect-specific: the same expressive domain may be covered by
phonologically unrelated forms in different Hokkaido varieties, and many items have no
straightforward equivalents across dialects nakagawa2024 290–292 .

The interjection inventory

Nakagawa organises the inventory under three functional types: response words, calls, and
emotion expressions nakagawa2024 290–293 . The following table synthesizes
the forms attested in the best-documented Hokkaido varieties, drawing on Nakagawa and Tamura
for Saru nakagawa2024 290–293 ; tamura1996 , and the pedagogical
grammar of Ijäs for the broader revitalization inventory ijas2023 .

Form
Function
Distribution

e
yes (assent; response to a statement)
all Hokkaido

ho
yes (response to being called)
all Hokkaido

irankarapte
greeting ('hello')
all Hokkaido (formal/written standard)

ikatay
greeting
Shizunai

iyairaykere
thank you (formal)
Saru, Chitose, and general Hokkaido

hioy'oy
thanks (light acknowledgment)
Saru, Chitose

hinna / hunna
thanks (women's register; given with gifts or food)
upper-stream Saru hinna ; lower-stream hunna

hetak
hurry (directive)
Saru, Chitose; etak in some eastern varieties

hokure
come on; get going (directive call)
Saru, Chitose

coco
here (call to a dog)
attested across Hokkaido varieties

The two response words e and ho are
distinguished by their discourse trigger: e responds to a proposition
(assent), while ho responds to a direct call or address. Both are
monomorphemic and stand alone as complete utterances.

e | INTERJ | Yes. | Assent particle responding to a proposition. Homophonous with the transitive verb e 'eat (something)', which is distinguished by its syntactic position and person-marking behaviour. | HK

ho | INTERJ | Yes? / Coming. | Response to being called by name or address; distinct from the propositional assent e. | HK

Greeting formulae and their morphological structure

irankarapte greetings is the pan-Hokkaido greeting formula in the modern
documentary record and is the form standard in revitalization pedagogy
nakagawa2024 290–291 ; ijas2023 . The Shizunai variety uses
ikatay in the same function; community discussion reports that
Asahikawa speakers use a phonologically distinct greeting not shared by southern varieties
(palestine_2026, aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-09-22, asserted;
aynucorporadiscord ). This strong dialect-specificity in the expressive vocabulary
extends to the whole interjection class nakagawa2024 290 .

irankarapte | INTERJ | Greetings! | Pan-Hokkaido greeting formula. Morphologically opaque when treated as an interjection; a proposed decomposition is discussed below. | HK

The morphological structure of irankarapte is not decomposed in the
descriptive grammars consulted, which treat it as a frozen formula. A community analysis
proposes i=rankarap-te , applying an i=…-te
causative construction to the verb rankarap 'approach, greet formally',
yielding something like 'let me greet you / the act of greeting' (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-11-25; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›. The same analysis is
proposed for iyairaykere thank you (formal) as
i=yairayke-re from yairayke be grateful , analogous to
the pattern 'the thing one ought to be grateful for' — a parallel noted there with English
excited / exciting ‹contested›. Neither proposal has been tested against corpus
attestations of the component morphemes, and they are best treated as working hypotheses.

Among the thank-you forms, a pragmatic scale runs from informal to formal. Community
discussion documents the Saru hierarchy as: hioy'oy — a light
acknowledgment appropriate for ordinary exchanges — is less formal than
hinna (lower-stream Saru hunna ), which in
turn is reinforced by reduplicated hinna hinna for greater warmth,
with iyayiraykere as the most formal register expression (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-26, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›.
The form hinna is specifically associated with women's speech and
accompanies the gesture raymik (touching the index finger beneath the
nose) when receiving gifts or food; the corresponding male gesture is
onkami (both hands). The gender-register conditioning of these forms
is treated in gendered-register-speech .

hioy'oy | INTERJ | Thanks! | Light informal acknowledgment in Saru and Chitose. Less formal than hinna or iyayiraykere. | SAR

The affirmative response word in dialogue is typically the bare assent particle
e , but the reduced formal-noun form
ruwe appears in some spoken contexts as a compact affirmative response
— a final-particle use of the same element that heads the inferential evidential construction.
Its relation to the full evidential predicate is treated in formal-defective-nouns .

Directive and affective interjections

hetak hurry; quickly and hokure come on; get going are
directive interjections urging speed or movement
nakagawa2024 292 . Both appear most commonly as standalone utterances, but
hetak can also precede an imperative verb, where it functions as a
manner adverb rather than as a pure interjection. Old documentary sources include sequences
interpretable as hetak ek 'come quickly' and
hetak hosipi 'go home quickly', where
hetak modifies the following imperative (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-11-24, proposed; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›.

hetak | INTERJ | Hurry! | Standalone directive interjection. The initial h is absent in some eastern Hokkaido varieties (etak). | SAR

hetak ek | quickly come | Come quickly! | Adverbial use of hetak before the imperative ek 'come'; a boundary case between the interjection and adverb classes. The 2SG imperative of ek is the bare stem. | SAR

The animal call coco — used to summon a dog — is phonologically
stable across the documented Hokkaido varieties nakagawa2024 292 . Its CVCV
shape is shared with the reduplicative mimetics discussed in the following section,
though no etymological connection is documented ‹speculative›.

Ideophones and reduplicative mimetics

A small set of nouns encodes inherent plurality or collective multiplicity through
reduplication — what Kindaichi and Chiri's grammar calls 疊語法 (reduplicative word
formation). The following inventory is drawn from their survey of Hokkaido forms
kindaichi1936 31 .

Form
Meaning
Semantic domain

chaichai
twigs, small branches
dispersed plant material

kaukau
hail
multiple falling objects

kankan
intestines
coiled, multiple-stranded tissue

ramram
fish scales
repeated small surface elements

taktak
balls, rounded objects
multiple discrete round things

These are nouns, not adverbs or particles; they occur as arguments of the verb rather than
as depictive adjuncts. Their shared morphological character — a CVC(V) base doubled to form
a disyllabic or tetrasyllabic whole — encodes the dispersed or multiple nature of the
referent: twigs as opposed to a single branch, hailstones as a mass, intestines as a coiled
plurality. Kindaichi and Chiri note that some inherently-plural nouns of this kind trigger a
plural verb form in the governing predicate, though the reduplicative noun itself carries no
obligatory agreement marking kindaichi1936 31 . The cross-chapter treatment
of reduplication phonology — including the templates, base-copy processes, and their
interaction with morphophonological sandhi — is in reduplication-phonology-rule-interaction .

kankan a=e | intestines 4.A=eat | They eat intestines. | Reduplicative noun kankan as a direct object. The 4th-person transitive agent a= marks unspecified human participants. Constructed to illustrate the argument-noun status of reduplicative mimetics.

These lexically closed reduplicative nouns are distinct from two other reduplication phenomena
in the language: verbal reduplication, which encodes iterativity or distributive plurality
(see reduplication-phonology-rule-interaction ), and pragmatic intensification by
repetition of an interjection or adverb (as in hinna hinna above).
The reduplicative nouns do not extend to new bases by productive rule; they are individually
listed in dictionaries rather than derived by a synchronic morphological operation
tamura1996 ; nakagawa2024 264–265 .

Sound symbolism: patterns and limits of the evidence

Whether Hokkaido Ainu has a productive phonesthemic system — systematic associations between
phonological shape and semantic value, of the kind extensively described for Japanese and
Korean — cannot be established from the descriptive literature accessible for this grammar.
The reduplicative nouns show that the language uses phonological form to encode distributed
plurality and collectivity, but whether the individual consonant or vowel choices within those
forms carry meaning independently of the reduplication is not demonstrated by any primary
source consulted ‹speculative›.

The closest approximation to size or intensity gradation in the available data is pragmatic
intensification through repetition: the scale
hioy'oy < hinna <
hinna hinna represents gradient expressiveness through reduplication
of the base form rather than through vowel or consonant substitution
aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-suggested›. The adverb inventory includes words with
degree-sensitive semantics ( adverbs-degree-comparison ), but no primary source
analyses these as phonesthemic.

Ideophones and formulaic refrain syllables in oral poetry — the ritual repetition units of
kamuy yukar and the formulaic diction of
uwepeker — constitute a distinct expressive register that intersects
with this chapter's topic. Their analysis as a poetic and ritual phenomenon belongs to
poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

Morphosyntactic integration

Canonical interjections integrate into discourse by standing outside clause structure entirely;
they do not head a predicate, fill an argument position, or modify a noun or VP. Mimetic
words, when used depictively, can integrate at three positions.

The most common integration is the adverbial slot (arity zero): a mimetic
word appears immediately before the verb it qualifies, in the same position as a common
adverb. The adverb class and its arity-zero definition are set out in
adverbs-degree-comparison . Manner adverbs like
moyre slowly, softly , tunas immediately , and
tuyma far away show that the preverbal modifier slot is productive for
motion- and manner-qualifying expressions nakagawa2024 273–282 .

moyre arpa | slowly go | (Someone) goes slowly. | Manner adverb moyre in the preverbal arity-zero slot. Constructed to illustrate the adverbial integration position available to mimetic words. | SAR

A second strategy uses the quotative postposition
sekor thus; saying (quotative) followed by a light verb, typically
ki do . The construction [depictive] sekor ki predicts
a depicted action or produced sound as something 'thus performed': the depictive content is
quoted under sekor and then asserted through the light verb. This is
the Ainu quotative frame analysed in quotative-complementation-sekor ;
its applicability to sound-depicting content specifically rests on limited corpus evidence
nakagawa2024 280 ‹corpus-suggested›.

kaukau sekor ki=an | hail QUOT do=4.S | It makes a rattling/pattering sound. | Ideophonic use of kaukau in the sekor ki light-verb frame. Constructed to illustrate the depictive quotative integration strategy.

The third strategy embeds an ideophonic content in the standard
nominalization apparatus — under a formal noun like
pe thing, one or hi thing, fact, time — and then uses the
nominalized clause as a clause argument. This integration route is not specific to ideophones
and does not mark depictive status specially; it is shared with all clause types. The
formal-noun complement system is described in formal-defective-nouns .

title: Internal Reconstruction and Grammaticalization Pathways
summary: Internal reconstruction from synchronic morphophonemic alternations, together with the major grammaticalization clines that feed the modern grammar.
part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Internal Reconstruction and Grammaticalization Pathways
Part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
Summary: Internal reconstruction from synchronic morphophonemic alternations, together with the major grammaticalization clines that feed the modern grammar.

The scope and tools of internal reconstruction

Ainu is a language isolate ( genetic-position-macro-comparison ): the comparative
method, which requires a set of related languages with regular correspondences, is unavailable.
Three tools remain for recovering Ainu prehistory: internal reconstruction , which
exploits alternations, paradigm irregularities, and dialect differences within Ainu itself;
contact-induced-change analysis , drawing on the documented Japanese, Nivkh, and
Tungusic contact layers; and typological inference , useful as a heuristic but carrying
no independent probatory value alonso2022 §1 . Internal reconstruction is by
far the most powerful of the three, accounting for essentially all dialectal differences that
can be connected to recoverable earlier forms. The time depth it can reach is shallow — roughly
1,000 years — bounded by the 13th-century Great Migration that separated Common Ainu into its
Hokkaido and Sakhalin branches alonso2022 §1, §4 .

The reconstructible ancestor of the modern Hokkaido varieties is termed Proto-Ainu (PA) ;
the immediate pre-Migration common ancestor is Common Ainu (CA) ; what internal
reconstruction reaches beyond PA through recursive application is termed Pre-Proto-Ainu
(Pre-PA). The segmental inventory and the contested suprasegmental reconstruction (length versus
pitch) are treated in proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction ; the SA/KU contrast
arising from the 13th-century split is treated in sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast .
This chapter focuses on grammatical morphology: what the alternation patterns within Hokkaido
Ainu, together with the SA contrast, allow us to infer about the diachronic sources of bound
morphemes and the grammaticalization clines that produced them.

Grammaticalization pathways — an inventory

The etymology of Hokkaido Ainu functional morphology is, by comparative standards, unusually
transparent. As Alonso observes, "when it comes to functional words and TAM formatives, the
etymology is still transparent" alonso2022 §3.1 . The major pathways fall into
four structural types. The theoretical framework for grammaticalization clines, layering, and
renewal drawn on here is hoppertraugott2003 .

grammaticalized morpheme(s)
synchronic function
lexical source
pathway
status

-(V)hV (affiliative suffix)
possessed/affiliative form of nouns
hi thing, time, place
lost base-final vowel + noun hi → fused suffix
‹contested› (two analyses, § internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways affiliative-hV below )

ruwe, hawe, siri, humi
clause-final evidential formal nouns

ru track, path , haw voice ,
sir appearance, land , hum sound, sensation

sensory noun → possessed form → evidential marker
partial grammaticalization; ‹corpus-confirmed›

e-, o-, ko- (applicative prefixes)
oblique-argument-introducing applicatives
locative/relational nouns (specific sources unclear)
relational noun → applicative prefix
‹speculative›

-an, -as, -kar, -kor, -ne, -sak
verbalizing/valency-changing suffixes

free verbs an be, exist (SG) , as stand ,
kar make , kor have , ne be (COP) ,
sak lack

free verb → bound verbalizer/valency suffix
‹consensus›

an (existential and aspectual)
existential predicate; durative/progressive aspect
posture verb 'sit (SG)' ( a sit down ; plural rok sit down (PL) )
posture verb → existential → aspect auxiliary
‹contested›

-p/-pe (nominalizer)
participant/thing nominalizer
pe thing, object
free noun → clause-final nominalizer
‹consensus›

ne (copula)
copula in predicate-nominal clauses
anaphoric pronoun ne
anaphoric pronoun → copula
‹speculative›

The affiliative suffix -(V)hV: two accounts

The concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed) suffix surfaces in
modern Hokkaido dialects as -(V)hV , where both vowels vary with the
base in complex ways. Two analyses of this distribution have been proposed, and the choice
between them has direct consequences for the PA vowel inventory.

Vovin (1993, via alonso2022 §2.1 ) attributed the distributional complexity to
a 17-vowel PA system: the suffix vowel was selected through vowel harmony with the final vowel
of the base. The machinery was extensive — twelve short and six long vowels — but all three
major reviewers of the 1993 monograph found it unmotivated by the available evidence (Sidwell
1996; Abondolo 1997; Witczak 1999, all via alonso2022 §1 ).

Janhunen (2020, via alonso2022 §2.1 ) offers a more economical account via
internal reconstruction. Pre-PA nouns were disyllabic: stem *CVC.V# .
When the second syllable reduced and was eventually lost, the lost vowel was reanalysed as an
incipient affiliative marker. Subsequently, the common noun hi thing, time, place
fused with this stranded vowel: base vowel + hi → -(V)hV .
The word tek hand illustrates the pathway: PA *tek.e
(disyllabic) → tek with loss of second syllable; lost e
+ hi → affiliative -ehe . Alonso calls this "a
triumph of internal reconstruction" and adopts it as the preferred account, concluding that the
PA vowel system contained only the modern five vowels alonso2022 §2.1 .

tek-ehe | hand-POSS | (one's) hand (affiliative/possessed form) | Affiliative form of tek 'hand'. In the Janhunen–Alonso analysis, -ehe = lost base-final vowel e + noun hi 'thing'. The SA cognate shows tek-ihi (vowel i by analogy): Janhunen (2020, via Alonso 2022 §2.1). | tamura1996 | SAR

Convergent evidence from the ‹SA› varieties supports the underlying hi
analysis: in ‹SA›, the base consonant resurfaces in the affiliative form even when it is
otherwise debuccalized — sik-ihi 'eye- POSS ' rather than
the expected *sis-ihi , which would follow from ‹SA›'s regular
k → s/_i rule (see final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex ).
The affiliative suffix must therefore preserve access to the underlying base consonant that is
otherwise hidden by final debuccalization tangiku2022 §2.3, §5 . Community
discussion records the same pattern: ‹SA› san + hi = sanihi ,
makan + hi = makanihi (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-16)
aynucorporadiscord , consistent with the hi -fusion account
‹corpus-confirmed›. The morphophonological details of the modern affiliative paradigm are
treated in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

The debate between the two accounts is ultimately ‹contested› rather than resolved: the
five-vowel reconstruction has the stronger case given the reviewers' response to Vovin's system,
but the internal reconstruction of a lost disyllabic base rests on inferences that cannot be
confirmed without a comparative method.

The evidential nominals: sensory nouns to clause-final markers

The four formal nouns that build the Hokkaido evidential system — ruwe ,
hawe , siri , humi — are
the concept-form-affiliative-form possessed forms of four sensory nouns, each
of which has grammaticalized into a clause-final evidential predication marker. The
grammaticalization path in each case runs: sensory noun → possessed formal noun → clause-final
evidential marker alonso2022 §3.1 ; kindaichi1936 ;
hirosawa2026 65 .

formal noun
source noun
possessed form
evidential value
approx. Hokkaido corpus tokens

ruwe
ru track, path
ru-we(he) 'the trace of —'
direct / knowledge-based recognized fact
c. 16,350

siri
sir appearance, land, weather
sir-i 'the visible state of —'
direct perception / situational
c. 607

hawe
haw voice
haw-e(he) 'the voice of —'
reportative / hearsay
c. 796

humi
hum sound, sensation
hum-i(hi) 'the sound/feeling of —'
non-visual sensory
c. 200

The grammaticalization is not complete: the lexical source meaning remains recoverable, and the
formal nouns retain nominal properties (they take the full affiliative suffix:
ruwehe , humihi ) while losing others (they do
not accept demonstrative or attributive modification in their evidential function)
dalcorso2018 24–25 . This partial grammaticalization is reflected in synchronic
dialect layering: Tokachi and Ishikari use the bare concept forms
ru / sir / haw / hum where Saru and Chitose use the possessed forms,
and Horobetsu has extended the possessed forms into conjunctive and adverbial-clause roles
takahashi2018 107–112 ; takahashi2013 129–131 . Chiri (1936,
via hirosawa2026 65 ) already noted the cline from 'there is a trace of X' to
'there is a fact of X' for ruwe .

The dialect distribution encodes the grammaticalization timeline as a spatial stratigraphy: bare
concept forms (Tokachi) represent the least grammaticalized stage; possessed forms (Saru) the
next; conjunction/adverbial function (Horobetsu) the most advanced. All three stages coexist
synchronically across the Hokkaido dialect space — a textbook case of layering
hoppertraugott2003 ‹corpus-confirmed› (corpus token counts above from string
search on the Hokkaido 167,890-sentence corpus). The full semantics and synchronic distribution
of each form belong to the evidential chapters: ruwe-ne-inferential ,
siri-ne-situational , hawe-ne-reportative ,
humi-ne-sensory ; the overall schema is in evidential-schema-overview ;
the formal defective noun class is defined in formal-defective-nouns .

The copula ne be, copula that closes the evidential predication also has a
grammaticalization history: Alonso identifies it as an anaphoric pronoun that developed a
copular function alonso2022 §3.1 . This shift is treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals ‹speculative›.

Free verbs grammaticalized as bound suffixes

The most transparent set of grammaticalization events in Ainu involves free verbs reanalysed as
derivational suffixes on nouns and verbs. Alonso documents six such suffixes with unambiguous
lexical sources (Alonso 2022 §3.1, Table 16; drawing on Dettmer 1989):

suffix
function
attested example
lexical source

-an
inchoative verbalizer ('become N')
cuk-an 'become autumn'
an be, exist (SG)

-as
non-agentive verbalizer
ruyanpe-as 'rain (vi.)'
as stand, be present

-kar
action verbalizer ('make/do')
sake-kar 'brew wine'
kar make, do

-kor
possessive stative verbalizer
hon-kor 'be pregnant'
kor have, hold, possess

-ne
dynamic verbalizer ('become, go')
soy-ne 'go outside'
ne be (copula)

-sak~-nak
privative verbalizer ('be without')
tum-sak 'be weak'
sak lack, be absent

alonso2022 §3.1, Table 16

The same free verbs continue in full predication and in compound verb constructions,
maintaining a synchronic continuum between free and bound use rather than a categorical split.
The morpheme database (mdb.aynu.org) corroborates the overlap: honkor =
hon 'stomach' + kor 'have' (conf. 0.85), and
the compound verb kor an (progressive) shows the same root in its free
form alonso2022 §3.1 . Community discussion adds evidence for ongoing productivity
of the -kar suffix in action-verbalizer derivations
( nimawekar, osorokar, sikayekar, yaykara ; nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-11-12) aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-confirmed›.

hon-kor | stomach-have | be pregnant | Morphological illustration. The suffix -kor 'have' derives possessive stative verbs from body-part nouns. Alonso (2022 Table 16, §175); mdb decomposition conf. 0.85.

Related pathways in the functional vocabulary follow the same template: ani hold
> instrumental/comitative particle 'with, by'; kusu reason, cause > causal
conjunction 'because'; hon + tom 'belly + middle' > locative 'in the
middle of' alonso2022 §3.1 . These are treated in the postposition and
clause-linkage chapters; they confirm that the free-to-bound pathway is an ongoing mechanism,
not a closed Pre-PA residue.

Aspectual an and the posture-verb pathway

The existential verb an be, exist (SG) derives from a posture verb meaning 'sit
down (SG)'; the suppletive plural rok sit down (PL) corroborates the source,
since the SG/PL suppletion retains the posture semantics alonso2022 §3.1 .
From existential predication, an extends to durative and progressive
aspect in the constructions kor an and wa an
(see existential-aspect-an-oka ).

A contested question is whether the personal suffix =an (4th-person
intransitive subject; also the inchoative -an in the table above)
derives from the same a=~an root. Nakagawa and Itabashi treat
an= as the historically older form from which the reduced
a= prefix descends nakagawa2024 §person ;
itabashi2001 . This derivation is disputed: nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord,
2026-04-29) notes that the high productivity of a= as an independent
prefix weighs against deriving it from the longer an= form, and proposes
an independent origin from the existential/aspectual root aynucorporadiscord
‹contested›. The synchronic person paradigm, where the question bears on its distribution, is
treated in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

The nominalizers -p/-pe and the cep showcase

The nominalizer suffix -p/-pe derives participant nouns and thing-nouns
from predicates. Its lexical source is the free noun pe thing, object , which
shifted from independent nominal to clause-final derivational suffix by the same pathway as the
evidential formal nouns above alonso2022 §3.2 . The synchronic distribution of
-p/-pe and the co-occurring event/place nominalizer
-i/-hi (< hi 'thing, place') is treated in
nominalization-p-pe .

The word cep fish provides the most completely reconstructible example of this
pathway and of internal reconstruction in Ainu generally. Its morphological analysis is:

ci=e-p | RES=eat-NMLZ | cep | fish (lit. 'the thing eaten [by all]') | alonso2022 | SAR
PA */ti-ʔe-p/ > CA */ciʔep/ > Saru cep (glottal stop lost), Horobetsu ciʔep (glottal stop retained as an archaism), SA ceh (final stop debuccalized). Triple-corroborated: Alonso (2022 §4 / Table 18); mdb curated decomposition (ci=, e 'eat', -p NMLZ; conf. 1.0); community analysis (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-06). The prefix ci= is glossed RES (resultative/inclusive) following Alonso 2022; a community alternative (nukopoli, 2023-12-21) proposes middle-voice for ci= here, parallel to the ci= discussed in the anticausative chapter."
/>

The three-way comparison — Saru cep , Horobetsu ciʔep ,
SA ceh — is Alonso's flagship illustration of the CA→dialect split
alonso2022 §4, Table 18 . Horobetsu retains the glottal stop
*ʔ from CA */ciʔep/ , Saru has lost it, and the
SA debuccalization (final p > h ) is the regular ‹SA› reflex. That three
independent phonological processes left distinct residues corroborates the reconstruction in a
way that a single-dialect analysis could not.

A community refinement (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-21) proposes that the
ci= prefix in this reconstruction reflects a middle/anticausative rather
than resultative function — the same ci= that produces
intransitive/middle stems in modern Ainu (see middle-anticausative-passive ) — with
the semantic reading 'the thing that gets-eaten (by itself)' rather than 'the thing that all
eat' aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. Both readings yield 'fish'; the
middle-voice alternative is of interest because ci= as a middle prefix
is otherwise well-attested in the modern grammar, whereas the resultative/inclusive reading
presupposes a PA function of ci= that is less clearly distinct in the
modern system.

The applicative prefixes — a disputed diachrony

The applicative prefixes e- , o- , and
ko- introduce oblique arguments as core verbal arguments. They are
treated functionally in applicative-system-overview and individually in
applicative-e , applicative-ko , applicative-o ;
bugaeva2006 and bugaeva2010 remain the primary analytical references.
Their diachronic origin Alonso assigns to the grammaticalization of locative/relational nouns
into verb prefixes, a widespread pathway typologically alonso2022 §3.1 ;
peterson2007 . The correspondence is transparent in type — relational nouns
shifting into applicative prefixes — but the specific source nouns for
e- and o- have not been individually identified
with confidence, making the claim ‹speculative› for those two prefixes.

A more structurally ambitious proposal appears in the community discussion. Antitwilight
(aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-06-04) argues that the applicative prefixes were originally
pre-nominal , not pre-verbal: certain compound nouns and fixed adverbs of the form
*e-ka-nok , *ka-o-suy would then be nominal
collocations later reanalysed as verb-prefix constructions when the prefix migrated leftward
to the verbal host aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. The hypothesis draws on the
well-attested presence of applicative-prefix forms inside nominal compounds in the modern
language (see noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate ) but has no direct
support in the Handbook sources read for this chapter. It is noted here as a community-generated
lead that warrants investigation of the old compound-noun record before further evaluation.

Layering, renewal, and what the dialect record shows

Internal reconstruction in Ainu proceeds primarily through the synchronic dialect record, where
different dialects have advanced different distances along the same grammaticalization clines,
leaving stages of a single pathway distributed across the Hokkaido dialect space. Two
contrasting patterns emerge: layering (coexistence of older and newer forms with overlapping
function) and renewal (the newer form replaces, rather than supplements, the older).

The evidential nominals are the clearest layering case. The Tokachi bare concept forms
ru / sir / haw / hum , the Saru possessed forms
ruwe / siri / hawe / humi , and the Horobetsu conjunctive extensions
of the same possessed forms coexist synchronically as three stages of one cline. The forms at
each stage are not synonymous across all contexts, and the distributional differences between
stages reveal the grammaticalization constraints: the possessed form ( ruwe )
has wider contextual distribution than the bare concept form ( ru ), and
the bare form retains combinatorial restrictions that the possessed form has shed
takahashi2013 129–131 ; takahashi2018 107–112 .

The plural marker in the Saru/Ishikari region illustrates renewal rather than layering. The
older plural form ci (surviving in Horobetsu, Shizunai, Shiranuka)
was replaced in the Saru/Ishikari core by an innovated pa , which spread
and displaced the older form in those dialects rather than accumulating beside it
nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.2 . The corresponding ‹SA› form (a)hci
is likely the archaic stage, placing ci as intermediate
tangiku2022 §7.5 .

The free-verb-to-bound-suffix pathways (§ above) offer the purest layering: the root
kor have, hold functions simultaneously as a free transitive verb, as a
compound element in the progressive kor an , and as the bound
possessive-verbalizer suffix -kor . No Hokkaido dialect has lost any of
these uses; the layers accumulated without replacement. Reconstruction of a relative chronology
for these layers — which function is oldest, and when the bound use separated from the free —
remains out of reach given the approximately 1,000-year horizon of reliable internal
reconstruction and the absence of any direct chronological evidence internal to Hokkaido Ainu
alonso2022 §1, §4 .

Classifier relics — a cautionary assessment

Alonso (2022 §3.2), following Austerlitz, Kindaichi, and Chiri (1936), considers whether
certain final consonants of body-part and nature nouns are relics of an earlier classifier or
noun-class system in Pre-PA. The proposed class markers include: *k
(body/round: tek 'hand', sik 'eye',
nok 'egg'); *t (long/rolling:
pet 'river', not 'chin',
at 'cord'); *m (long/small:
kem 'needle', yam 'chestnut'); and
*r (elongated: ker 'footwear',
sir 'rock') alonso2022 §3.2, Table 17–18 .

Alonso himself rejects the hypothesis on diagnostic grounds: classifier systems typically
require that the class marker occur independently as a free morpheme or in some derivational
use beyond noun-final position. Ainu's proposed class-final consonants fail this test — they
are invariably noun-final and never function as independent words or derivational triggers. He
concludes that the pattern "illustrates the dangers of wishing to see too much in the data"
(Alonso 2022 §3.2). The word-final consonant inventory of Hokkaido Ainu is phonologically
constrained (see consonant-inventory and hokkaido-phonological-microvariation )
but does not cross-classify nouns in any grammatically active synchronic sense. The productive
numeral classifiers of modern Hokkaido Ainu — -p~-pe (things) and
-n~-iw (people, from aynu human being ) — derive from
the common nouns pe and aynu through the
noun-to-nominalizer pathway already attested in the -p/-pe nominalizer
above; they are treated in numeral-classifiers-ordinals-and-quantifier-syntax
‹speculative›.

title: Interrogative, Indefinite, and Negative-Polarity Pro-forms
summary: The interrogative word set and content-question syntax, and the indefinite/negative-polarity series built on interrogative + ka, including their licensing across affirmative and negative contexts (scope/licensing detailed in Part XVI).
part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Interrogative, Indefinite, and Negative-Polarity Pro-forms
Part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
Summary: The interrogative word set and content-question syntax, and the indefinite/negative-polarity series built on interrogative + ka, including their licensing across affirmative and negative contexts (scope/licensing detailed in Part XVI).

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu has a set of interrogative pro-forms covering the semantic slots that in other
languages are filled by wh-words: things, persons, places, manners, times, quantities, and
identity. They are not a uniform part of speech. Nouns ( hemanta
'what', hunna 'who', hunak 'where'), adverbs
( makanak 'how', hempara 'when'), and prenominals
( hempak 'how many', inan 'which') all figure in
the paradigm, each with distinct syntactic distribution
nakagawa2024 411 . The same forms — or a set of n -initial
cognates — serve as indefinite pro-forms ('something', 'anyone', 'somewhere') when embedded in
non-interrogative clauses or combined with focus particles. Across Hokkaido, two competing root
families divide the dialects: an hem-/hun- series in Saru and Chitose,
and an nep/nen/ney/nekon series in most other varieties
nakagawa2024 415 . The clausal mechanisms that convert these wh-words into
questions — the existential question frame, question particles, intonation — are treated in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles ; the present chapter covers the
forms themselves, their morphological structure, their dialect distribution, and their
indefinite function.

The Saru inventory

The Saru dialect uses an h -initial series for most interrogative
slots. The inventory and the part-of-speech classification of each form are given below
nakagawa2024 411 :

meaning
Saru form(s)
part of speech

what
hemanta , hnta , hinta
noun

who
hunna
noun

where
hunak , hinak
noun (locative/〈place〉 class)

how
makanak , mak
adverb

when
hempara
adverb

how many
hempak
prenominal

which
inan , hinaan
prenominal

why

hemanta kusu ,
hemanta ne ,
hemanta ne kusu

adverb phrase

hunak belongs to the locative noun (〈place〉) subclass and takes
case particles directly — hunak ta 'where (at)',
hunak un 'where (to)', hunak wa 'where
(from)' — without the intermediate locative noun or 'place-of'
nakagawa2024 413 . The locative-noun subclass is set out in
relational-and-spatial-nouns .

hemanta and hunna take the existential
question frame rather than a copular predicate: 'what is it?' is
hemanta an? and 'who is it?' is
hunna an? nakagawa2024 411–413 . The sequence
hemanta ne is occupied by the 'why' periphrasis, where
ne is the copula; a bare hemanta ne?
therefore means 'why?' not 'what is it?'.

nupuri epunkine kamuy hunna ne ya | mountain each kamuy who COP FIN | Who should be the kamuy to guard each mountain peak? | hunna 'who' as a copular predicate with ne COP and the question particle ya. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子 'uwepeker 民話16', AA研アイヌ語資料, aa-irc/023#178

hemanta ne kusu ene po easir | what COP PURP like.this somewhat grow.well | Why has (he) grown so well like this! | hemanta ne kusu 'why' (lit. 'what-COP-because'). Three periphrases coexist with no clear distributional difference: hemanta kusu, hemanta ne, hemanta ne kusu (Nakagawa 2024: 412). | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子 'uwepeker 民話7', AA研アイヌ語資料, aa-irc/005#62

'Why' has no monomorphemic form. The three periphrases share the core
hemanta 'what' and differ in what follows it: the bare purposive
kusu , the bare copula ne , or both together.
Nakagawa reports "no clear meaning difference" among them
nakagawa2024 412 . The purposive linker kusu in
its causal function is treated in causal-and-purpose-clauses .

'How many' and 'which' are prenominals: they precede and modify a head noun rather than
standing alone. hempak 'how many' further grounds
hempakpe 'how many things', hempakiw
'how many people', and the intensive inehempak 'ever so many'
nakagawa2024 414, 420 . The human-count suffix
-iw and the thing-count suffix -pe are the
same suffixes found in the numeral system; see vigesimal-numeral-system .

Dialect geography: the h-series and n-series

Across Hokkaido, the Saru/Chitose hem-/hun- forms are replaced in
most other varieties by an n -initial series. The boundary follows
the established dialect division between 日高西部 (Saru, Chitose, Mukawa) and the central
and northern varieties nakagawa2024 415 ;
nakagawafukazawa2022 . The full dialect comparison, after Nakagawa's table 78,
is as follows:

meaning
Saru
Chitose
Horobetsu
Shizunai
Tokachi
Bihoro
Ishikari

what
hemanta
hemanta
nep
nep
nep
nep
nep

who
hunna
hunna
nen
nen
nen
nen
nen

where (at)
hunak ta
hunak ta
ney ta
ney ta
ney ta
ney ta
ney ta

where (to)
hunak un
hunak un
ney un
enon
enon
nene
ney ne

how
makanak
makanak
nekona
nekon
nekon
nekon
nekon

when
hempara
hempara
hempara
hempara
nempara
nempara
hempara

how many
hempak
hempak
hempak
hempak
nempak
nempak
hempak

which
inan
inan
inan
inkian
inkian
inkian
nean

The most internally fragmented cell is 'where (to)': Shizunai and Tokachi use
enon / onon , whose
e-/o- elements may correspond to the 'head/tail' directional
prefixes but whose -non remains unexplained
nakagawa2024 417 . The 'whose' or 'of which place' construction uses
ney un even in h-series dialects, because the
un there is the two-place relational verb 'belong to', not the
allative case particle nakagawa2024 417 . The relational-verb
un is discussed alongside the possession system in
relational-and-spatial-nouns .

The following pair from the アコㇿイタㇰ textbook illustrates the h/n split for 'who':

toan kur hunna an | DEM.DIST person who exist | Who is that person? | Western (Saru) h-series form: hunna 'who'. | utari1994 | SAR | アコㇿイタㇰ 第2章, akor-itak/002#17

toan kur nen ta an | DEM.DIST person who LOC exist | Who is that person? | Eastern/northern n-series form: nen 'who'; ta locative marks the predicative slot. The Japanese gloss is identical to the Saru version above. | utari1994 | HK | アコㇿイタㇰ 第2章, akor-itak/002#18

The Shizunai system as described by Refsing refsing1986 109 gives the
most explicit formal statement of the demonstrative–interrogative–indefinite grid, aligning
an anaphoric ne- series, proximal ta- series,
distal to- series, interrogative ne- root,
and indefinite …ka forms across person, place, and manner dimensions.
The overlap of the anaphoric ne , the interrogative
ne- root, and the copula ne in the
n-dialects may be "more than coincidence" refsing1986 108 ‹speculative›.
The demonstrative and anaphoric ne- system is treated in
demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness .

Morphology of the wh-roots

The internal structure of the hem-/hun- forms is not fully
transparent in the accessible grammars. Nakagawa notes that interrogatives are built on
two roots ( hem- and hun- ) plus a third,
geographically restricted root in mak- ( makanak )
nakagawa2024 415 . The n-series form
ney 'where/when (n-dialect)' is transparently segmentable:
ne + hi (the formal noun 'place/time'),
giving 'which place/time' nakagawa2024 416 . The Shizunai place-form
neyta adds the locative particle directly to this stem
refsing1986 109 .

A bimorphemic analysis of the h-series has been proposed in the community:
hunna 'who' = hun + na
(a human-count suffix); hunak 'where' =
hun + yak (the conditional
yak ); hempak 'how many' =
hem + pak (the limitative postposition
pak to the extent of ); hempara 'when' =
hem + pa + ara
(nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord , 2023-12-08) ‹contested›. The decomposition
is internally consistent with the two-root observation and would explain the h/n alternation
as operating at root level; it remains unverified in the peer-reviewed literature, and
the grammaticalization paths it requires are not reconstructed in the available sources.

The contracted Saru variants hnta and hinta
(from hemanta ) are spoken-register forms. Both allow the existential
question frame ( hnta an? 'what is it?') and the copular why-frame
( hnta ne? 'why?')
nakagawa2024 411–412 .
hemanta also functions as an ordinary noun meaning roughly
'thing-of-unknown-sort', and in Chitose kor hemanta! serves as
a term of abuse (lit. 'having a whatnot')
nakagawa2024 412, 415 . The classical grammar of
kindaichi1936 54–57 attests ne- -root interrogative
compounds — ne-kotan-un-pe 'person of which village',
ne-moshir-un-pe 'of which land' — and the literary prenominal
inki 'which' ( inki nishpa 'which lord'),
confirming the antiquity of the interrogative ne- element that the
modern n-dialects preserve.

The 'when' form hempara is treated as original; the Tokachi and
Bihoro n-series form nempara arose by analogical
h- → n- replacement under pressure from
the rest of the n-paradigm nakagawa2024 418 ‹speculative›.

Indefinite pro-forms and the particle =ka

The interrogative and indefinite ('some-', 'any-') functions are distributed across two
form-sets in h-series dialects. In Saru, the interrogative uses
hem-/hun- forms while the indefinite uses the
n -series: 'who?' = hunna but 'someone/anyone'
= nen ; 'what?' = hemanta but
'something/anything' = nep ; 'where?' =
hunak but 'somewhere' = ney
nakagawa2024 419 . The n-dialects have no analogous split: 'what?' and
'something' are both nep . Two slots are identical for both
functions in all dialects: 'how many' and 'which'
nakagawa2024 420 .

meaning
Saru interrogative
Saru indefinite

something/anything
