hemanta
nep (+ =ka )

someone/anyone
hunna
nen (+ ne yakka )

somewhere/anywhere
hunak
ney (+ ta )

sometime/anytime
hempara
hempara / ney ta (+ =ka )

however many
hempak
hempak , inehempak

whichever
inan
inan , hinaan

The asymmetry is not absolute. Residual mixing occurs in both directions: h-series forms
can appear as indefinites in some Saru texts, and Horobetsu — which uses
nep as its interrogative 'what' — uses
hemanta as its indefinite, reversing the Saru pattern
nakagawa2024 419 ‹corpus-suggested›. These exceptions point to ongoing
reorganisation rather than a categorical split.

Indefinite pro-forms combine with the focus/scalar particle =ka
'even, also' and the scalar concessive ne yakka 'even if':
nep ka 'anything (at all)', nen ne yakka
'anyone (at all)', ney ta ka 'anywhere'
nakagawa2024 419 . The scalar semantics of =ka
('even X') combined with an indefinite root yields a free-choice reading; the particle
itself belongs to the focus system described in
cleft-nominalization-focus .

nep ka a=kar ka etoranne | something FOC 4.A=do FOC be.troublesome | Anything (I) do is a bother. | nep (Saru indefinite 'something') + =ka (focus 'even') gives the free-choice reading 'anything'. Nakagawa ex. 901. | nakagawa2024:420 | SAR

Typologically, the pattern maps onto the cross-linguistic tendency — documented by
haspelmath1997 — for interrogative and indefinite functions to be
formally related but distinguished by particles or suppletive roots. In Ainu the
distinction in h-dialects is lexical (two root sets), with
=ka overlaid on the indefinite root rather than on the interrogative ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Syntactic position and embedding

Wh-words in Hokkaido Ainu are used in situ: they occupy the argument or adjunct position
of the constituent they replace and do not move obligatorily to a clause-peripheral slot
nakagawa2024 411 . This follows from the nonconfigurational properties of
the language discussed in nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument , where
nominal arguments are essentially free adjuncts and the verb indexes their referents
independently. A locative wh-word such as hunak un therefore
remains in the goal position of a motion verb:

cikap utar hunak un paye wa isam ru-we an | bird companions where ALLAT go.PL CONJ be.absent track-POSS exist | Where could the birds have gone? | hunak un 'where to' in situ, in the goal position of paye 'go (pl.)'. The evidential question frame ru-we an? marks the sentence as a content question. The Horobetsu author uses h-series hunak un rather than the expected ney un, illustrating the permeability of the h/n boundary in modern usage ‹corpus-suggested›. | ainukoraci1991 | HOR | 成田英敏, アイヌタイムズ, ainu-times/055/0#20

ne hemanta hunak un arpa | DEM what where ALLAT go | Where did that thing go? | hunak un 'where to' in situ within an embedded question; ne hemanta functions as an anaphoric pro-noun 'that thing/what-is-it'. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 横山裕之 'cakcak kamuy oruspe', アイヌタイムズ, ainu-times/033/2#10

The clausal mechanisms that convert an in-situ wh-word into a matrix question — the
existential frame an? , the question particle
ya , and intonation — are treated in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles . The wh-word itself takes no
special morphology in the question context.

Wh-words embed freely in indirect questions. The interrogative pro-form stays in situ
within the embedded clause, which is then nominalized by the clause-final formal noun or
the nominalizer p / pe . The
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative/possessed form is not triggered
on the wh-word itself; it applies to the relational nouns that head the embedding. This
parallels the general nominalization syntax described in
nominalized-complements-control-raising . Independent personal pronouns —
including the pro-forms built on the person-affix paradigm — are discussed separately in
independent-personal-pronouns ; the present pro-forms are not personal pronouns
but belong to the nominal and adverbial parts of speech catalogued above.

title: Interrogative Strategies, Question Particles, and Evidential Questions
summary: Polar and content questions — the particles ya/he, in-situ wh-words, biased/confirmational questions — integrated with the evidential interrogative/confirmational paradigm (ruwe un?, hawe ya?, siri ya?) and its evidence-source 'flip' from speaker to addressee.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Interrogative Strategies, Question Particles, and Evidential Questions
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: Polar and content questions — the particles ya/he, in-situ wh-words, biased/confirmational questions — integrated with the evidential interrogative/confirmational paradigm (ruwe un?, hawe ya?, siri ya?) and its evidence-source 'flip' from speaker to addressee.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu marks clause-level interrogation through four intersecting mechanisms:
rising intonation alone, sentence-final particles, a constituent-scoping focus-question
particle, and the sentence-type alternation on the formal-noun evidential tail.
Wh-pro-forms remain in their canonical argument or adjunct position throughout —
there is no wh-movement and no obligatory cleft for question formation
nakagawa2024 409–412 ; sato2008 136–138 .
The wh-pro-form inventory — hemanta what , hunna who ,
hunak where , hempara when , and their dialectal
alternants — is documented in interrogative-pro-forms ; this chapter
describes the clause-level framing strategies, the interrogative particles, and the
conventions for answering questions.

Two particles carry most of the segmental interrogative work.
he scopes over the constituent at issue rather than the whole clause,
placing it closer to a 副助詞 than to a canonical sentence-final particle in its
distribution ‹contested› fukuda1961 §8, §31 ;
nakagawa2024 323–324 . ya (post-consonant allomorph
a ) is a true sentence-final particle that also nominalizes a clause as
'whether …' in embedded questions nakagawa2024 332–333 . The
deepest structural mechanism, however, is the alternation among the copula
ne , the existential an , and the ∅-tail on the formal nouns
ruwe track-POSS , hawe voice-POSS ,
siri appearance-POSS , and humi sound-POSS : the
same nominal that closes a declarative as ruwe ne becomes
a polar question as bare ruwe and a content question as
ruwe an . The inferential evidential and its frame
alternations are treated fully in ruwe-ne-inferential ; the full
sentence-final particle inventory is in
sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force . A pedagogical overview
of question formation is in ijas2023 .

Polar questions: five strategies

Nakagawa identifies five overlapping strategies for forming polar (yes/no) questions,
any of which may combine with another nakagawa2024 409 :

strategy
segmental marker
position
notes

(a) rising intonation
∅
clause-final prosody
unmarked, minimal device; no instrumental pitch-track documentation available

(b) bare formal noun
ruwe, hawe, siri, humi alone (∅-tail)
clause-final
copula ne omitted; see ruwe-ne-inferential frames the frame alternations

(c) Q-particle ya / a
ya , post-consonant a
sentence-final
bare use may sound too direct; often reinforced by a formal-noun frame

(d) focus-Q particle he
he
after the focused constituent (may be non-final)
scopes a single constituent; also used for disjunctive alternative questions

(e) combined frame
ruwe he an , hawe he an , …
clause-final
bare formal noun + he + existential; Saru-favoured reinforced form

Strategy (a) — rising intonation without any segmental reflex — is the unmarked
polar-question device nakagawa2024 409 ; sato2008 136 .
It leaves no trace that would survive in written documentation; Fukuda's treatment
of sentence melody carefully separates prosodic questioning from the particle
system fukuda1961 §31 .
Strategy (b) uses the bare formal noun, the ∅-tail alternant of the evidential
predicate. The example below shows it with hawe voice-POSS :

e=eraman haw-e | 2SG.A=understand voice-POSS | Did you understand? | Bare formal noun hawe without following ne or an. Adding ne yields a declarative ('you understood'); adding an would open a content question requiring a wh-word. | sato2008:136 | CHI

The same ∅-tail applies to ruwe , here inside a conditional permission
request:

cip c=o yakka pirka ru-we | boat 1PL.EXCL.A=board even.if good track-POSS | May we board the boat? | Nakagawa 2024: 409b. The bare ruwe without ne or an marks the polar question. yakka 'even if' frames the conditional premise of a permission request. | nakagawa2024:409 | SAR

Strategy (e) reinforces the polar question by inserting he between the
bare formal noun and the existential an , producing the frame
ruwe he an :

tane pirka ru-we he an | now good track-POSS Q exist | Is it good now? | Combined polar-Q frame ruwe he an. ILCAA Ainu materials; narrator 織田ステノ, document 'reyep kamuy' (静内 dialect). | ilcaa1976 | SHI | 織田ステノ 'reyep kamuy', AA研アイヌ語資料 (静内)

The sentence-type alternation on formal-noun tails

When a clause is nominalized by one of the four formal nouns, the predicate following
the nominal determines sentence type. Satō documents the three-way pattern for all
four formal nouns sato2008 136–137 , confirmed in detail by
Nakagawa nakagawa2024 394–396 :

sentence type
formal-noun tail
source

declarative
NMLZ + ne (copula)
sato2008 136 ; nakagawa2024 394

content (wh) question
NMLZ + an (existential); expanded NMLZ + an hi an
wh-word required; sato2008 136 ; nakagawa2024 410

polar (yes/no) question
bare NMLZ (∅-tail; copula omitted)
sato2008 136 ; nakagawa2024 409

exclamative
NMLZ + an ( an optional)
sato2008 137 ; nakagawa2024 395

Satō proposes a structural motivation: the copula ne predicates the nature
of the subject, so the declarative asserts what something is ; the existential
an asserts that a situation exists while its nature remains open to the
wh-word in a content question; and the bare formal noun without either predicate
treats even the existence of the described state as the open question — the polar
reading sato2008 137 ‹speculative›. The account is Satō's own
working hypothesis, not a derivation shared across the sources consulted.

Nakagawa gives the content question an additional expansion:
NMLZ an hi an — existential, then the formal noun
hi time, place, fact (formal noun) , then existential again —
emphasizes the facthood of the unknown element nakagawa2024 410 .
The alternation also disambiguates near-identical wh-clauses:
hemanta an? = 'what (is there)?' versus
hemanta ne? = 'what is it?' / 'why?' — the choice of
an vs ne after a wh-word marks open-existence question vs
identification nakagawa2024 412 . The evidential-tail system and
the semantics of ne vs an are set out in
ruwe-ne-inferential frames the frames section and in
evidential-schema-overview .

The he particle: scope and category

Fukuda classifies he as a 副助詞 on distributional grounds: it attaches
after any constituent — noun, postpositional phrase, verb, adjective, or conditional
clause — and marks that constituent as the locus of uncertainty, so it may stand
inside the clause rather than at the sentence boundary
fukuda1961 §8 ; nakagawa2024 323–324 .
When it happens to fall at the clause end Fukuda notes a category straddle toward
the sentence-final class fukuda1961 §31 ‹contested›;
Nakagawa and Satō describe it functionally rather than categorially, emphasizing
its constituent-scoping behaviour
nakagawa2024 323–324 ; sato2008 63 .

The following example places he after a conditional clause, making the
conditional — not the final adjective — the questioned element:

a=rayke ciki he pirka | 4.A=kill COND Q good | Is it OK if I (rather than you) kill it? | Nakagawa 2024: 409d. he follows the conditional clause a=rayke ciki 'if I kill it'; the focus of the question is the conditional premise, not the final predicate pirka 'good'. | nakagawa2024:409 | SAR

When placed after a postpositional phrase, he turns that phrase into
a focused confirmation request, even as a fragment without a full predicate:

keman hi ta he | famine NMLZ LOC Q | At the time of the famine? | Nakagawa 2024: 409d. he scopes the locative phrase keman hi ta 'at the time of the famine' as a fragment confirmation request. | nakagawa2024:409 | SAR

In content questions, he marks the wh-adjunct cluster as the focal
unknown and can combine with the wh-word in the adjunct slot:

hnta kusu somo e=ipe he | where because NEG 2SG.S=eat Q | Why aren't you eating? | Nakagawa ex. 855. The Saru wh-adverb hnta (= hunak 'where'; used here adverbially as 'why') plus the reason marker kusu stay in the adjunct slot; he marks the reason cluster as the focal unknown. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

Satō notes that the most characteristic use of he is the disjunctive
frame X he … Y he … , which poses an either/or choice between
two alternatives sato2008 63 ; this overlaps with the
alternative-question strategy described below.

Three dialect-restricted particles cover overlapping territory.
hetap ( henta in some sources) marks formal or dubitative polar
questions in the area west of Shizunai. enta (Shizunai-area) marks the
specific element in doubt, paralleling he in narrow scoping.
omoun (Shizunai-area) signals a conjectural question
nakagawa2024 324, 326 . All three coexist with the pan-dialectal
he , which carries no dialect restriction.

The ya/a question particle

ya is a sentence-final particle that marks the clause as a question —
polar or content — without scoping any particular constituent
nakagawa2024 332–333 ; fukuda1961 §29 . Its
post-consonant allomorph a is not merely phonological reduction:
Nakagawa treats it as a genuine second particle form nakagawa2024 333 .

Satō observes that ya alone on a bare verb can sound abrupt or overly
direct; it frequently accompanies a formal-noun frame or a softening particle
sato2008 74 . The particle marks polar questions:

e=pirka ya | 2SG.S=good Q | Are you well? | Minimal polar question with sentence-final Q-particle ya. In practice, bare-verb questions with ya alone are often felt as too direct; a formal-noun frame (strategy b or e) is common in polite speech.

ya also closes content questions where the wh-word and a nominalized
predicate already supply the interrogative content. The Tokachi allomorph
a appears in a recognizable dialectal content-question frame:

taan pe nep tap an a | this thing what just exist Q | What is this? | Nakagawa ex. 879 (citing Takahashi via Nakagawa 2024: 412). The Q-particle a (post-consonant allomorph of ya) closes the Tokachi content-question frame nep tap an; taan pe 'this thing' is the topic. | nakagawa2024:412 | TOK

Beyond its SFP use, ya nominalizes a clause as 'whether …' when embedded
under a verb of knowing or investigating; in that function it is a complementizer,
not a sentence-final particle, and the embedded polar question is the object
complement of the matrix verb nakagawa2024 333 ;
sato2008 74 . The complementizer function is addressed in the
section on embedded questions below.

A community analysis proposes that the disjunctive conjunction
neya or is historically ne + ya
('be' + Q-particle → 'or'), the Q-particle supplying the alternative reading
‹contested› aynucorporadiscord (aomidori_cs_, 2023-12-12).
This etymology is consistent with the pattern but is not independently documented
in the sources consulted.

Content questions: wh in situ

Content questions retain the wh-pro-form in its canonical argument or adjunct
position. Nothing moves. When a formal-noun tail is present, the copula is
replaced by the existential an , producing ruwe an
/ hawe an etc., or the expanded
NMLZ an hi an frame
nakagawa2024 410 ; sato2008 136–137 .
Without a formal-noun tail, any of the polar-question devices — rising intonation,
ya , or he on the focal wh-element — also marks a content
question.

hemanta ne kusu ene sir-an hi an | what COP because like.this appearance-exist NMLZ exist | Why is it like this? | Nakagawa ex. 856. The wh-expression hemanta ne kusu 'what because = why' stays in the clause-initial adjunct position; sir-an is the impersonal 'it is so' (sir 'appearance' + an 'exist'); hi an is the expanded content-question frame (formal noun + existential). | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

The wh-pro-form inventory has a two-way split between question-word shapes
( hunna who , hemanta what ,
hunak where , makanak how ) and indefinite shapes
( nen someone , nep something ,
ney somewhere , neun somehow ). The indefinite
shapes combine with the additive particle ka also, even to produce
'someone/something/somewhere' in positive contexts and 'no one/nothing/nowhere'
under negation sato2008 137 ;
nakagawa2024 322–323 . The full paradigm and its dialectal variants
are in interrogative-pro-forms and
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

The constraint on the formal-noun tail means that the ne / an
choice disambiguates otherwise similar clauses. The declarative
hemanta ne? = 'what is it?' / 'why?' (copula, identifying)
contrasts with the existential content question
hemanta an? = 'what is there?', and the obligatory
existential tail in the presence of a wh-word ( *hemanta ne kusu
ene siran hi ne is ungrammatical as a question) follows from the sentence-type
alternation described in the preceding section
sato2008 136–137 ; nakagawa2024 412 .

Alternative and disjunctive questions

Disjunctive alternatives use the particle
hene or; for instance repeated after each alternative member
nakagawa2024 323–324 ; fukuda1961 §6 .
Tokachi and some eastern varieties use ene in this function
nakagawa2024 323 . For an alternative question, the same
hene disjunction frame receives a polar-question marking — rising
intonation, ya , or he after each alternative:

nisatta hene oyasim hene e=arpa | tomorrow or day.after or 2SG.S=go | Are you going tomorrow or the day after? | The NP-disjunction nisatta hene oyasim hene 'tomorrow or the day after' follows Nakagawa 2024: 323–324 (ex. 534); the polar-Q framing by rising intonation is constructed here. In natural speech ya or a final he may close the sentence instead.

The X he … Y he … frame identified by Satō as
he 's most characteristic use sato2008 63 extends the
same logic to alternative verbal predicates: placing he after each
alternative focuses the question on the choice between them.
hene also heads listing alternatives in declarations ('for instance,
tomorrow or the day after'); intonation and context distinguish the question
reading from the declarative listing.

Embedded questions

Embedded polar questions use ya as a nominalizing complementizer: the
clause ending in ya is treated as a nominal 'whether [proposition]'
and functions as the complement of a verb of knowing or asking
nakagawa2024 333 ; sato2008 74 . This
complementizer function is formally parallel to the nominalizers
p thing, fact (nominalizer) and
hi time, place, fact (formal noun) treated in
nominalization-p-pe .

Embedded content questions use a different frame. The complementizers
kunak , yak , and kuni introduce indirect-speech and
indirect-question complements under transitive reporting verbs such as
ramu think or ye say
bugaeva2008 35–37 ; nakagawa2024 470–472 .
These contrast with the quotative particle sekor , which introduces
direct and semi-direct speech under intransitive verbs; the transitivity split
( sekor ⇒ intransitive reporter, kuni ⇒ transitive reporter)
is confirmed by community observation
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09) ‹speculative›.
The direct/semi-direct/indirect speech continuum, person-switching in logophoric
speech, and the sekor / kuni contrast are treated in
clause-linkage-overview .

Answer and response strategies

Hokkaido Ainu has no grammaticalized 'yes' and 'no' particles used crosslinguistically
to confirm or deny a proposition directly. The standard response to a polar or
content question repeats the relevant verb — in its positive form to confirm, or
with negation somo to deny — and may add a reason clause
nakagawa2024 396 . Community documentation from across several
dialects corroborates this: in most dialects outside Saru, bare affirmative or
negative interjections are not used to answer a question, and the verb-repetition
strategy is employed exclusively. Saru aligns more closely with Japanese-style
polarity responses. Forms such as e ,
ho , and m are responses to being
addressed or called, not affirmation/denial particles
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-04) ‹speculative›.

One attested affirmative-response frame retains the formal noun and adds the
sentence-final particle un :

ku=nu ru-we un | 1SG.A=hear track-POSS FIN | Yes, I heard it. | Nakagawa ex. 794. The formal noun ruwe is retained in the affirmative answer; the final particle un (a soft-affirmation sentence-final particle, not the existential verb) closes the clause in a confirmatory register. | nakagawa2024:396 | HK

The particle wa (soft assertive) also frequently closes replies,
softening the declarative force of a repeated or corrective statement
nakagawa2024 334 ; fukuda1961 §21 . Neither the
Q-particles nor the response particles are gender-restricted: HA has no
sentence-final particle specific to men or women sato2008 72 .
The male-register skew noted for ruwe ne in oral narrative
is a property of the evidential class, not of the SFP class, and the two systems
should not be conflated ‹corpus-suggested›; see
gendered-register-speech for oral-register distinctions and
ruwe-ne-inferential semantics the semantics of ruwe ne for
the gendered-register side of the evidential.

title: Japanese Loanwords and Their Phonological Adaptation
summary: Lexical borrowing from Japanese — chronological strata, semantic domains, and numeral borrowing over the vigesimal base — together with the phonological nativization of loans (coda/cluster repair, segment mapping, accent assignment) as live evidence for the synchronic phonotactic grammar.
part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Japanese Loanwords and Their Phonological Adaptation
Part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
Summary: Lexical borrowing from Japanese — chronological strata, semantic domains, and numeral borrowing over the vigesimal base — together with the phonological nativization of loans (coda/cluster repair, segment mapping, accent assignment) as live evidence for the synchronic phonotactic grammar.

The Japanese contact layer

Vovin characterizes the lexical impact of Japanese on Hokkaido Ainu as making modern Ainu
"inundated by loanwords from Japanese" vovin2022 §1 . The flow has been
almost entirely one-directional for the modern period: domestic animals, trade goods,
administrative vocabulary, and — from the Meiji era onward — the full terminology of modern
institutions all entered Ainu from Japanese. The reverse direction, Ainu elements entering
Japanese through place names and regional vocabulary, is treated in
ainu-loanwords-and-toponymy-in-japanese ; contact with Nivkh and Uilta, which
shaped the Sakhalin lexicon but has no Hokkaido counterpart, is discussed in
northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu .

Vovin himself acknowledges that "there is no comprehensive study" of Japanese loanwords in
Ainu vovin2022 §1 . The account below draws on the etymology tags in Chiri's
classified dictionary chiri1953 , the word-formation remarks in Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 §1.3 , and attested corpus examples. Ainu is a language isolate
with no genetic relationship to Japanese (see genetic-position-macro-comparison ),
so the loans are a contact phenomenon and bring Japanese phonological structures that require
systematic restructuring to fit the Ainu syllable canon.

Phonological adaptation

Nakagawa states the diagnostic plainly: free lexical words of three or more syllables are
often loanwords ( 3音節以上の自立語は外来語であることが多い ), citing
tampaku tobacco as a typical case nakagawa2024 §1.3 . The
underlying phonological mismatch is clear: Japanese contrasts voiced and voiceless
obstruents; Ainu has no phonemic voicing (see syllable-template ). Japanese also
permits nasal-mora and long-vowel mora types that fall outside the Ainu (C)V(C) canon.
Every borrowed form is therefore reshaped by one or more of the following repair operations,
documented in Chiri's etymology tags chiri1953 and in Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 §1.3 .

Japanese source
Ainu output
Repair
Source

neko 'cat'
meko cat
initial n → m (place assimilation before labial-vowel sequence)
chiri1953 §266 [<Jap. neko ] 胆振・日高

uma / muma 'horse'
umma horse
gemination m → mm ; creates heavy σ1 determining accent placement
chiri1953 §293 [<日本語ウマ]; kayano1996

tabako 'tobacco'
tampaku tobacco
medial b → mp (prenasalized stop); a → a , o → u
chiri1953 §061 [<日本語たばこ] 幌別; nakagawa2024 §1.3

kami 紙 'paper'
kampi paper, document
final m → mp ; nasal assimilation before labial stop
ijas2023b ; aynucorporadiscord

kosode 小袖 'short-sleeved robe'
kosonte kimono, short-sleeved robe
medial d → nt
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.1

kasa 傘 'umbrella'
kasa umbrella
no repair (already CV.CV)
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024)

The obstruent repairs reflect Ainu's lack of phonemic voicing: Japanese voiced stops surface
voiceless or prenasalized ( b → p /
mp , d → t /
nt , g → k ). Prenasalization is the
preferred repair for medial voiced stops in a nasal-compatible environment, as both
tampaku tobacco and kampi paper confirm
‹corpus-confirmed›. Consonant clusters outside the (C)V(C) template are broken by
epenthesis; the default epenthetic vowel is o or a copy vowel —
a pattern attested in community comparisons of loanword shapes from multiple source
languages ‹contested› aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord,
2024-12-11 [227]). A further substitution affects the Japanese rhotic: Japanese /r/ has
an [l]-allophone that maps to Ainu n rather than to Ainu
r (a flap with no lateral allophone) ‹contested›
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, 2025-01-02 [400]).

The nasal spelling of prenasalized loans is an active orthographic debate. Nakagawa's
convention writes the etymological labial nasal (hence
tampaku , kampi ,
umma ), because /n/ and /m/ neutralize to [m] before /p/ and /m/.
Kana orthography uses ン throughout since kana encodes a single nasal mora. The choice
between kampi and kanpi reflects an
etymological or neutralization-based orthographic stance, not a phonological difference
‹contested› aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-16
[216, 217]).

One community-proposed case suggests that voicing may occasionally survive in a borrowed
form: the garment term cinciri has been proposed as a loan of
Japanese 綴れ ( tsuzure ) with the voiced affricate
retained ‹speculative› aynucorporadiscord (scheuewsuiski, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-11-05 [215]). The etymology is unverified against a primary dictionary
source.

Chronological strata

Early contact and Edo-period loans

The earliest stratum consists of culture-contact vocabulary that is fully integrated
into Ainu phonology and morphology. These forms appear in the nineteenth- and
early-twentieth-century texts collected by Chiri and Batchelor and are attested across
multiple dialects. The core items are animals not native to pre-contact Hokkaido,
material-culture goods brought by trade, and administrative terminology tied to the
Wajin presence.

Ainu form
Japanese source
Semantic domain
Dialect attestation
Source

umma horse
uma / muma
animals
北海道・樺太
chiri1953 §293

meko cat
neko
animals
胆振・日高
chiri1953 §266

peko cattle, cow
beko (dial.) / ushi
animals
北海道
chiri1953

tampaku tobacco
tabako
trade goods
幌別
chiri1953 §061

kosonte short-sleeved robe
kosode 小袖
clothing
Saru
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.1

kampi paper, document
kami 紙
materials / administration
北海道
chiri1953 ; ijas2023b

sake rice wine
sake 酒
food and drink
北海道
chiri1953

tono lord, official
tono 殿
social/administrative titles
北海道
chiri1953

tunci interpreter
tsūji 通事・通詞
social/administrative titles
北海道
aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, 2024-06-14)

The animal loans — umma horse , meko cat ,
peko cattle — fill gaps in the Ainu fauna lexicon for mainland
domestic species absent from Hokkaido before sustained contact. The corpus shows
umma horse with 146 tokens (rank 881 in the full vocabulary), making
it the most frequent Japanese animal loan by a large margin. The administrative items
reflect the institutional structure of Wajin–Ainu trade: tono lord
titles the Japanese trade-post supervisor, and tunci interpreter names
the bilingual go-between required in formal dealings — plausibly < archaic Japanese
通事/通詞 tsūji ‹speculative›
aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-06-14).

tampaku ku=ku rusuy | tobacco 1SG.A=smoke want | I want to smoke (tobacco). | tampaku ( tampaku-op 'tobacco pouch' and
toy-ta-tampaku 'home-grown tobacco'
kayano1996 . The following example shows two animal loans and an
administrative loan co-occurring in a single clause:

kes to an kor sihainin orowa peko umma koraci a=nepkire | every day exist while manager from cattle horse like 4.A=work-CAUS | Every day I was made to work like cattle and horses by the manager. | peko (cattle) and umma (horse) are early Japanese animal loans; sihainin (支配人 'manager, overseer') is a later administrative loan. 4.A marks an indefinite/impersonal agent with the narrator as implicit object of the causative. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 小松哲郎, アイヌタイムズ「Tamamusi Sadayû」[ainu-times/017/2#14]

The noun kampi paper, document is attested in the older sense of
'written order, official document' in oral-literature contexts, where the Wajin
administrative apparatus is a recurring plot element:

kampi an=euytek pe moyuk tono | document 4.A=dispatch NMLZ tanuki lord | the raccoon-dog lord, dispatched with a written order | kampi in the administrative sense 'written order'; tono (

Modern borrowings and the untranslated stratum

Meiji-era colonization and the assimilation policy that followed (documented in
sociolinguistic-situation-revitalization ) ended the productive nativization
of new Japanese vocabulary. Concepts introduced during the decades when Ainu was no
longer used in daily life simply appear as untranslated Japanese forms:
terebi 'television', sinbun
'newspaper', zeikin 'tax', ginkō
'bank', denwa 'telephone'. Ōno's formulation is precise: "no
Ainu vocabulary has been found to refer to televisions or computers … during the period
when these things became widespread, the Ainu language was rarely used in Ainu daily
lives" ono2022 §8.9 . The untranslated character of modern loans
distinguishes them from the early stratum: early loans received full phonological
repair; modern loans often retain Japanese syllable structure intact
‹corpus-confirmed›.

The contrast is visible when modern and early loans appear in the same sentence. An Ainu
Times text places the fully nativized early loan umma horse alongside
three unrepaired modern loans:

terebi sinbun or ta zeikin uk wa umma hok kur oruspe a=nu ruwe ne | television newspaper place LOC tax take CONJ horse buy person news 4.A=hear track-POSS COP | On television and in the newspapers, one hears the story of a person who took tax money and quietly bought a horse. | Modern loans terebi (TV), sinbun (newspaper), zeikin (tax) retain Japanese phonological form without repair. The early loan umma (horse) is fully nativized. ruwe ne is the inferential evidential treated in the evidential chapters. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 浜田隆史, アイヌタイムズ「zeikin oruspe」[ainu-times/017/5#5] (excerpt)

Contrast the handful of native-era coinages for Meiji-period technology, which
show that Ainu was still spoken when those concepts spread:
tunas-kampi 'telegram' (literally 'fast paper/letter',
Asahikawa) and a-e-y-nu-p 'telephone' (literally 'the thing
with which we hear one another') are built from native and nativized material rather than
borrowed from Japanese ono2022 §8.9 . Their existence marks the
relative chronology of language shift across technology domains.

Semantic distribution

Loans cluster in the semantic fields where post-contact material and administrative culture
arrived: domestic animals, trade goods, clothing, tools, money and administration, and in the
modern stratum technology and institutions nakagawa2024 §1.3 . Core
vocabulary — body parts, kinship terms, the natural-world lexicon, basic verbs, the native
numerals one through ten, and pronouns — is native. The vigesimal numeral system treated in
vigesimal-numeral-system is entirely native in its derivational base; classifiers
and ordinals in numeral-classifiers-ordinals-and-quantifier-syntax likewise draw
on native morphology. Property-concept predicates in property-concept-predication
are native verbal predicates. The pattern is the classic "borrow the culture, keep the core"
profile of contact lexicon.

The noun kampi paper, document illustrates how a fully nativized loan can
become a productive morphological base. Borrowed early as 'paper' (< kami
紙), it has generated a set of neologisms for educational and museum contexts in the
revitalization period: kampi-nukat-cise 'school' (paper–look-at–house),
kanpi pu 'library' (paper–storehouse),
imeru-kampi 'email' (lightning–paper)
ijas2023b §5 . A loanword functioning as a stem in
noun-incorporation-object noun incorporation and in productive compounding
is indistinguishable in its morphological behavior from a native root.

Degree of integration

Early loans are fully integrated. They bear Ainu accentuation according to the prosodic
rules described in mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis : the geminate in
umma horse creates a heavy first syllable that receives the accent nucleus
by the default rule for CVC.CV words, so the form sounds native, not Japanese. Early loans
also participate freely in compounding and incorporation (see examples in §3.1 above).
Native obligatorily-possessed nouns carry affiliative-suffix-morphophonology affiliative
suffixes in possessed contexts; borrowed nouns such as
umma horse and kampi paper are not
obligatorily possessed and do not display this morphology — a diagnostic that separates
many loans from native inalienable nouns.

Modern loans are less integrated. They frequently retain Japanese syllable structure,
and in the new-speaker texts produced in language-revitalization contexts, the boundary
between a nativized loanword and a code-switch from Japanese is structurally difficult to
establish ‹speculative›. The compound umma menoko illustrates how
native and loan morphology interact productively: the bare juxtaposition is ambiguous
between 'horse and girl' (appositive) and 'horse-girl' (compound); inserting the copula
explicitly as umma-ne-menoko removes the ambiguity ‹speculative›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-12 [356]).

Diagnostics for identifying Japanese loans

Four properties together identify a form as a Japanese loan in Hokkaido Ainu:

Syllable count. A free lexical root of three or more syllables is a
strong prior for borrowing nakagawa2024 §1.3 . Native Ainu roots are
predominantly mono- or disyllabic; polysyllabic roots arise through borrowing or
through productive — and morphologically transparent — affixal derivation.

Recognizable phonological repair. Prenasalization of a medial voiced
stop ( -mp- , -nt- ), gemination of a
stem-initial nasal consonant, or an unexpected initial
m- where a Japanese source has n- all
point to a Japanese etymon.

Absence of affiliative morphology. Obligatorily-possessed native nouns
display a concept/affiliative alternation (所属形; see
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology ). Most Japanese loans are not
obligatorily possessed and lack this alternation.

Chiri dictionary etymology tags. The tags [<日本語…] and
[<Jap.…] in chiri1953 mark the identified Japanese-loan inventory
and remain the most systematic primary record. Vovin notes the lack of a comprehensive
successor survey vovin2022 §1 .

The name for the source language itself illustrates one of the finer integration
distinctions. The two-word phrase sisam itak 'the speech of the
Wajin' (a descriptive NP) and the single compound
sisamitak 'Japanese [language]' (a language name) are argued to
differ semantically and phonologically: the former has two independent accentual words,
while the latter functions as a single compound with one accent domain. Writing them
together obscures the accentuation and conflates the two meanings ‹speculative›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-05 [299];
2024-12-20 [549]).

Ochiai's study of sikerpe Amur cork tree fruit (Phellodendron amurense)
demonstrates a methodological corollary from the reverse direction: a Japanese dialect
tree-name preserving a reconstructible intermediate Ainu form — *si kor pe
before the change to sikerpe — can be used both to date the
borrowing and to anchor the proto-Ainu reconstruction
ochiai2026 199 . The evidence for Ainu loans in Old Japanese, including
Vovin's Man'yōshū material, is taken up in
ainu-loanwords-and-toponymy-in-japanese and
macro-comparison-and-deep-contact-controversies .

title: Katakana Orthography and the Extended Small-Kana Codas
summary: The katakana writing of Ainu — the syllabary mapping, the extended small-kana coda letters, the modern normative standard, and historical kana systems.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Katakana Orthography and the Extended Small-Kana Codas
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: The katakana writing of Ainu — the syllabary mapping, the extended small-kana coda letters, the modern normative standard, and historical kana systems.

Katakana in Ainu writing

Katakana functions alongside the Latin phonemic transcription as one of two
co-primary scripts for Hokkaido Ainu. Most community publications, pedagogical
materials, and digital media produced in Hokkaido use katakana as their default
form, while the Latin transcription defined in latin-phonemic-transcription
prevails in the academic literature and in this grammar. Both scripts represent the same
underlying phonological system; the correspondence between them is described in
latin-phonemic-transcription and summarized in the coda correspondence
table below.

The katakana syllabary handles the open (CV) core of the Ainu syllable with standard
Japanese kana. The structural challenge lies in the closed (CVC) syllable: Ainu
allows a wide coda-consonant inventory — /p t k s m n r w y h/ — most of which
Japanese kana provides no dedicated symbol for in coda position. The solution is a
set of reduced-size katakana characters placed after the preceding CV mora to
represent the coda consonant. The modern normative form of this system derives from
the 1994 Hokkaido Utari Kyōkai textbook アコㇿイタㇰ
utari1994 , whose title ( Akor Itak , 'our language')
already illustrates the convention: ㇿ for coda /r/ and ㇰ for coda /k/. Nakagawa's
extensive grammar follows the same assignments throughout nakagawa2024 .

The standard CV syllabary

For open syllables, Ainu uses the inherited katakana inventory without structural
modification. The five vowels are ア /a/, イ /i/, ウ /u/, エ /e/, オ /o/; the
consonant-plus-vowel mora grid follows the Japanese kana table, but the phonemic
values of several cells diverge from modern Japanese reading conventions. ツ
corresponds to Ainu /tu/ and チ to /ti/ — reflecting the phonemically straightforward
/t/ plus the respective vowel, without the Japanese phonetic shifting of /tu/ to
[tsɯ] or /ti/ to [tɕi] nakagawa2024 . Similarly, ス = /su/ without
the Japanese fronting of /s/ before /u/; the allophonic conditions on /s/ and /c/
in Ainu are described in consonant-inventory .

The affricate /c/ and its katakana representation carry an orthographic history.
Kindaichi's early grammars and text editions introduced contracted kana
(yōon: チャ, チュ, チェ) for /ca/, /cu/, /ce/, treating Ainu /c/ as parallel to
Japanese /tʃ/ kindaichi1936 . This practice conflicted with the writing
habits of Ainu speakers themselves, who transcribed /c/ without the contracted
syllable convention: Kirikae documents that writers such as Sunazawa, Yamamoto, and
Nabewasawa consistently avoided yōon for /c/ kirikae1997 , and
Nakagawa interprets this uniformity as reflecting a shared native-speaker phonological
intuition nakagawa2006 . The normative standard dropped yōon for /c/
utari1994 ; community discussion has confirmed the consensus
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-17) ‹consensus›.
The full convergence history belongs to orthographic-standardization-and-word-division .

The Ainu coda inventory and phonotactic constraints on coda position are defined in
syllable-template ; the phonemes themselves are described in
consonant-inventory .

The extended small-kana coda system

A coda consonant in Ainu katakana is written as a small (subscript-sized) katakana
character placed immediately after the kana of the preceding mora. These characters
occupy the Katakana Phonetic Extensions block, U+31F0–U+31FF, added to the Unicode
Standard in version 3.2 (2002) specifically to support Ainu orthography. The block
encodes sixteen positions, each derived from the syllable kana in the same consonant
row as the coda value it encodes. The table below gives the standard coda
assignments; variants and special cases are discussed in the subsections that follow.

Latin coda
Katakana
Unicode position(s)
Notes

k
ㇰ
U+31F0 (small ku)

s
ㇱ
U+31F1 (small si)
standard; ㇲ (U+31F2, small su) found in some older or variant texts

t
ㇳ
U+31F3 (small to)

n
ン
U+30F3 (full-size N)
full-size ン; the small ン (𛅧 U+1B167) has been proposed but has limited font support — discussed in the /n/ subsection below

p
ㇷ゚
U+31F7 + U+309A (two code points)
composite: small hu (ㇷ) plus combining semi-voiced mark (゚); no pre-composed single character exists — discussed in the /p/ subsection below

m
ㇺ
U+31FA (small mu)

r
ㇻ ㇼ ㇽ ㇾ ㇿ
U+31FB–U+31FF (small ra ri ru re ro)
standard system selects by preceding vowel: ㇻ after /a/, ㇼ after /i/, ㇽ after /u/, ㇾ after /e/, ㇿ after /o/ — discussed in the /r/ subsection below

w
ウ
U+30A6 (full-size U)
no dedicated small kana; full ウ follows the preceding mora in glide sequences

y
イ
U+30A4 (full-size I)
no dedicated small kana; full イ follows the preceding mora in glide sequences

h
ㇵ ㇶ ㇷ ㇸ ㇹ
U+31F5–U+31F9 (small ha hi hu he ho)
member selected by preceding vowel; phonemic status of coda /h/ is disputed — discussed in the /h/ subsection below

The bilabial coda ㇷ゚

Coda /p/ has no pre-composed Unicode character in the Katakana Phonetic Extensions
block. The standard encoding combines small hu (ㇷ, U+31F7) with the combining
katakana-hiragana semi-voiced sound mark (゚, U+309A), yielding the two-code-point
grapheme cluster ㇷ゚. This parallels the pre-composed semi-voiced syllable kana
of Japanese (パ = U+30D1; プ = U+30D7, etc.), but no pre-composed equivalent exists
for the small form. Text-processing operations that count code points rather than
grapheme clusters will report ㇷ゚ as two characters; software producing Ainu katakana
must normalize the sequence at input and search time. Fonts that support the full
Katakana Phonetic Extensions block generally render the combination correctly
aynucorporadiscord (toracatman, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-03-28).

The /r/ coda series

Coda /r/ is distributed across five small kana — ㇻ ㇼ ㇽ ㇾ ㇿ (small ra, ri,
ru, re, ro; U+31FB–U+31FF) — one for each of the five Ainu vowels. The standard
convention selects the member whose vowel matches the vowel of the preceding
syllable: the title アコㇿイタㇰ illustrates ㇿ (small ro)
for the coda /r/ of kor , following the /o/ of
コ utari1994 .

The five-way choice is not universally observed. The Upopoy National Ainu Museum
uses full-size ル (U+30EB) for all coda /r/ positions, without the small-kana form
aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-16).
Some pedagogical and digital materials use ㇽ (small ru) invariably regardless of
preceding vowel ‹contested›. The morphophonological behavior of coda /r/ — its
assimilation triggers and the echo-vowel phenomenon — is described in
coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi ; the echo vowel mechanism
associated with the affiliative suffix is treated in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Coda /n/ and the small-ン question

Coda /n/ is written with full-size ン (U+30F3), the same character as the Japanese
moraic nasal. The borrowing is typographically convenient but phonologically
imperfect: Japanese ン is an independent mora that contributes its own weight to
the prosodic system, whereas Ainu coda /n/ is a non-moraic appendix in the same
CVC syllable as its preceding vowel nakagawa2024 ; shiraishi2022 .
To mark this difference, Unicode 12.0 (2019) added a small ン, 𛅧 (U+1B167,
KATAKANA LETTER SMALL N), in the Small Kana Extensions block (U+1B130–U+1B16F);
it has also been advocated in community discussions as the typographically appropriate
form for the Ainu coda nasal
aynucorporadiscord (toracatman, aynu-corpora Discord,
2023-12-08). Font support for U+1B167 remains sparse as of the mid-2020s and the
character is not in common use; full-size ン remains the practical standard
‹corpus-suggested›.

Coda /h/ in katakana

The five small h-row kana (ㇵ ㇶ ㇷ ㇸ ㇹ; U+31F5–U+31F9; small ha, hi, hu, he,
ho) are encoded for coda /h/ notation and follow the same vowel-agreement principle
as the /r/ series: ㇵ after /a/, ㇶ after /i/, ㇷ after /u/, ㇸ after /e/, ㇹ
after /o/. In practice, coda /h/ is the most orthographically variable member of
the Hokkaido system: the phonemic status of word-final /h/ remains disputed
nakagawa2024 , and many katakana-based texts either omit final /h/
entirely or mark it only where affixation causes its resurfacing as a full onset
consonant. The phonemic-status argument is treated in glottal-stop .

Glide codas /w/ and /y/

The glides /w/ and /y/ in coda position are written with the corresponding vowel
kana — ウ and イ respectively — at full size and without a small-kana reduction.
These sequences arise in the surface forms that descriptive grammars analyze as
coda-glide syllables (ay, uy, oy, aw, iw, ew and their cognates), rather than as
true diphthongs with a phonemically distinct glide nucleus
nakagawa2024 . Whether the underlying segment is a vowel, a phonemic
glide, or an epenthetic transition is a phonological question treated in
glides-w-y . No dedicated small-kana form exists for either position.

Historical development

The earliest sustained katakana transcriptions of Ainu, from the Meiji period onward,
used standard Japanese kana with no provision for coda consonants. Kindaichi Kyōsuke,
whose grammars and yukar editions from the 1930s established the classical
transcriptional tradition in Ainu linguistics
kindaichi1936 , rendered Ainu in full-size kana and introduced yōon
contractions (チャ, ショ, etc.) for palatalized syllables. Coda consonants in this
framework were typically indicated by writing the following syllable's onset kana in
the same position or were left unmarked, producing representations that obscured
syllable structure kirikae1997 .

The development of a systematic small-kana coda notation emerged from the work of
Ainu speakers and linguists in the postwar decades. Kirikae documents writing
practices that predate the Unicode standardization, including variant assignments
for individual coda positions kirikae1997 . Nakagawa traces the parallel
development of Ainu-authored phonemic transcription traditions, situating the kana
system in the broader history of Ainu-language literacy nakagawa2006 .
The 1994 Hokkaido Utari Kyōkai textbook consolidated the current standard assignments
and provided their widest early distribution utari1994 ;
ono2022 . Unicode 3.2 (2002) then gave the system stable, interoperable
code points across platforms and operating systems.

Font coverage followed at varying rates. As of the mid-2020s the Katakana Phonetic
Extensions block is supported by BIZ UDゴシック/UDMinchō, MS Gothic/Minchō, Meiryo,
and Yu Gothic/Minchō; some educational fonts such as UDデジタル教科書体 do not cover
it aynucorporadiscord (toracatman, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-03-28).
The toc-chapter for multi-script rendering and font interoperability is
cyrillic-and-multiscript-rendering .

Latin–katakana correspondence and round-tripping

The two scripts represent the same phonological system and conversion between them is
in principle mechanical, but several coda positions admit variant katakana realizations
that require a convention choice. Coda /s/ may appear as ㇱ or ㇲ; coda /r/ may use
any of the five members of the ra-series or the full-size ル; coda /h/ may be absent
or written with any of the five h-series members; and the punctuation and spacing
conventions differ between the 「，．」 style of utari1994 and the
「、。」 style more common in Nakagawa's materials nakagawa2024 . These
ambiguities mean that Latin-to-katakana conversion requires explicit convention
declarations, while katakana-to-Latin conversion is more consistently deterministic.
The word-spacing and boundary conventions that further complicate round-tripping are
addressed in orthographic-standardization-and-word-division .

From a typological perspective, the katakana system for Ainu sits in an unusual
structural position: the combination of a full CV baseline kana plus a subordinate
coda kana within a single syllable unit resembles an abugida — a semi-syllabic system
in which a base character carries an inherent vowel and a secondary mark modifies the
coda — more than a pure syllabary or an alphabet. This characterization has been
proposed in community discussion
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-03-30) ‹contested›;
no formal typological classification has been assigned to Ainu katakana in the
published linguistic literature.

title: Kinship and Honorific Possession
summary: Idiosyncrasies of kinship-term possession (irregular/suppletive affiliatives, vocatives) and honorific possession that marks esteemed possessors via the indefinite/fourth person.
part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Kinship and Honorific Possession
Part: Part VII — Possession and the Affiliative System
Summary: Idiosyncrasies of kinship-term possession (irregular/suppletive affiliatives, vocatives) and honorific possession that marks esteemed possessors via the indefinite/fourth person.

Kinship terms and the two possession constructions

Kinship terms in Hokkaido Ainu are nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns bound
nouns : they do not occur without possessive morphology in ordinary prose. Like body
parts, they are obligatorily possessed. Unlike body parts, they split between the two
possession constructions that organise the inalienable–alienable divide: some kin terms
take the concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative form with a direct
person-prefix ( ku=onaha 'my father'), while others remain in the
concept form governed by the transitive verb kor have
( ku=kor hapo 'my mother') nakagawa2024 221–226 ;
sato2008 151 . The assignment is lexically fixed and does not vary freely
within a single dialect.

Bugaeva places kinship terms alongside body parts as the core of the
obligatorily-possessed, inalienably-marked class in Ainu, yet the construction split shows
that "inalienable" covers two morphosyntactically distinct sub-patterns rather than a single
uniform category bugaeva2021poss 52–54 . The general morphosyntax of both
constructions and the affiliative-suffix morphophonology are treated in
adnominal-possession-double-marking and
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology ; the present chapter covers what is specific
to kinship and to the honorific use of the 4th-person possessive.

The affiliative-construction paradigm

Nakagawa organises Hokkaido kinship vocabulary into two sets: a
concept-construction set, whose members take N kor and
resist a direct person-prefix, and an affiliative-construction set, whose concept
stem and affiliative form are both in use nakagawa2024 222 . The
concept-construction set contains principally senior-generation terms:
mici father (Saru, Shizunai, Tokachi, Kushiro) ,
hapo mother (most dialects); father (Chitose, Horobetsu) ,
ekasi grandfather, elder man , huci grandmother, elder woman ,
acapo uncle , unarpe aunt . The affiliative-construction set is
given below, with short and long affiliative forms and, where attested, address forms.

Affiliative-construction kinship terms — Saru–Chitose baseline

concept form
short affiliative
long affiliative

ona

(concept-set ekasi used for address)

( ku=mataki , Nakagawa ex. 265)

( ku=matkarku , Nakagawa ex. 265)
