The affiliative-suffix allomorphs follow the same phonological patterns as body-part nouns,
documented in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology . Vowel-final stems (type ①:
ona , unu , sa ,
po ) take the optional suffix -hV₁ for the
long form — ona→onaha , unu→unuhu . Consonant-final
stems mostly follow type ③-1 ( -i(hi) ):
yup→yupi(hi) , sut→suci(hi) ,
mat→maci(hi) , tures→turesi(hi) . For
vowel-final stems the short affiliative is formally identical to the concept form; in the
3rd person a person-prefix is the only overt marker of the possessed reading
nakagawa2024 227 ‹consensus›.

The two address affiliatives yupo and sapo
carry an -o ending not predictable from the standard suffix classes.
Nakagawa lists them as lexically specified address forms without deriving the
-o morpheme synchronically nakagawa2024 225–226 .

Address versus reference: the vocative split

Seven affiliative-construction terms — ona ,
unu , sut , ekas ,
aca , yup , sa — are
restricted to third-person reference and narrative use; they cannot be used to address the
relative directly nakagawa2024 224 . For elder siblings the address forms
are yupo and sapo with
ku= ; for parents and grandparents the concept-construction terms
( mici , hapo , ekasi ,
huci ) serve as vocatives nakagawa2024 225–226 .

ku=sapo he | 1SG.A=elder.sister FIN | Sis! | Nakagawa ex. 263. Chitose direct address to elder sister using the specialised address affiliative sapo. The reference form ku=saha is not used vocatively. | nakagawa2024:226 | CHI

ku=yupo | 1SG.A=elder.brother | Elder brother! | Nakagawa ex. 264. Direct address to elder brother; the reference affiliative ku=yupihi is not used as a vocative. | nakagawa2024:226 | HK

Satō confirms ku=sapo and ku=yupo as the
securely attested direct-address (対称詞) forms; Nakagawa additionally reports
ku=mataki and ku=matkarku as vocatives to
younger kin, but their distribution is less fully documented
sato2008 151 ; nakagawa2024 226, ex.265 ‹contested›.

The split is grammatical, not a register choice: the construction assigned to a kin noun
determines what address form is even possible nakagawa2024 228 . Narrative
formulae use the reference forms in story-openings, where the logophoric narrator lists
family members:

a=ona-ha an a=unu-hu an hine oka=an | 4.A=father-POSS exist 4.A=mother-POSS exist CONJ exist.PL=4.S | My father was there, my mother was there, and we lived. | Nakagawa ex. 261. Standard Saru story-opening formula; the 4th-person A-prefix a= functions as the logophoric narrator's first person. Both ona→onaha and unu→unuhu are affiliative-construction terms; the concept-form terms mici/hapo do not appear in this referential slot. | nakagawa2024:225 | SAR

Obligatory possession, the 4th-person default, and the kin-group noun utar

Whether a kin term belongs to the concept-construction or the affiliative-construction set,
it does not appear bare in prose; every token carries possessive morphology. When no
discourse-salient possessor is active — in riddles, generic statements, or elicitation —
the 4th-person A-prefix a= supplies a default unspecified-possessor
value. Bugaeva reports that kin and body-part forms elicited in isolation are supplied by
speakers with a 3rd- or 4th-person index and the possessive suffix, never as a bare stem
bugaeva2021poss 53 .

The general kin-group noun utar family, companions, kin-group members takes
the affiliative form utari(hi) —
k=utari 'my household',
a=utari 'one's family (narrator's)' — and is among the
higher-frequency kinship-domain tokens in the corpus
nakagawa2024 251–252 . The community member thegodofneet (aynu-corpora
Discord, 2025-09-17) observes that the affiliative naturally narrows to a possessed reading
because a kin-group not belonging to anyone is conceptually difficult to express; this
parallels the bound-noun tendency across kin vocabulary
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. Community member nukopoli (aynu-corpora
Discord, 2026-06-15) further notes that the compounds
onautari and unuutari , literally 'the
father-group' and 'the mother-group', can designate 'parents' collectively
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›.

Honorific possession via the 4th person

The 4th-person prefix a= carries three uses that converge on kin
affiliatives: (i) the unspecified-possessor default described above; (ii) the logophoric
narrator first person in oral narrative; and (iii) an honorific use, where
the speaker marks an esteemed third-party possessor — a deity ( kamuy ),
a divine ancestor, or a high-status figure — with a= rather than
the zero 3rd-person form, treating the referent as too elevated for the ordinary 3rd-person
default nakagawa2024 174–179 ; tamura2000 . The three
values are formally identical; context, genre, and the referent's status together determine
the reading. The honorific-possession use is most consistent in kamuy yukar and ritual-register
texts, where divine possessors regularly appear in kinship constructions with
a= . This is one reason the frequency of
a= with kin affiliatives in the oral-literature corpus is
conspicuously higher than pure third-person reference would produce alone. The full treatment
of the 4th-person paradigm and its logophoric and honorific functions is in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and fourth-person-honorific ;
the register dimension belongs to honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers .

The following example from the ILCAA Shizunai corpus shows the logophoric-narrator value
of a= / an= with stacked kinship affiliatives
and a possessed voice noun in a single clause:

nekon ne wa a=yupi-hi an=sa-ha haw-ehe ka isam ruwe ne | how COP CONJ 4.A=elder.brother-POSS 4.A=elder.sister-POSS voice-POSS also not.exist track-POSS COP | How is it that my elder brother's and my elder sister's voice is not heard? | Three affiliative nouns — a=yupihi (elder brother), an=saha (elder sister; an= is the Shizunai dialectal variant of the 4th-person A-prefix a=), and hawehe (voice, long affiliative of haw 'voice') — stacked in one clause. The 4th-person prefixes function as logophoric narrator first person. The embedded ruwe ne closes the indirect-question frame. | ilcaa1976 | SHI | Oda Steno, ILCAA Ainu materials, Shizunai [aa-irc/017#52]; source: 織田ステノ, uwepeker 'Moyuk (zenhan)'

Predicative possession with kinship affiliatives

Kinship affiliatives combine with the existential verbs an exist (sg) and
okay exist (pl) to form predicative-possession clauses of the BE-type,
restricted to body-part and kin nouns huber2025 247, 251 . The construction
is the standard schema for story-opening family inventories:

a=yup-i an a=sa-ha an | 4.A=elder.brother-POSS exist 4.A=elder.sister-POSS exist | I have an elder brother and I have an elder sister. | Huber (2025) ex. 16; BE-type predicative possession with kinship affiliatives. The 4th-person a= is the logophoric narrator first person. | huber2025:251 | HK

The contrast between the two possession constructions can appear within a single clause when
a body part (affiliative) and a concept-construction kin term ( kor )
co-occur:

ku=teke-he piro hi ta ku=kor totto noya ham uk wa | 1SG.A=hand-POSS be.injured NMLZ LOC 1SG.A=have mother mugwort leaf take CONJ | When my hand was injured, my mother took mugwort leaves and … | ku=tekehe is affiliative-construction body-part possession; ku=kor totto is concept-construction kin possession. In this Chitose-influenced idiolect hapo means 'father', so totto 'breast' has been recruited as 'mother' and takes the kor construction. | ainukoraci1991 | CHI | Yokoyama Hiroyuki, Ainu Times no. 024/1 #20; source: 横山裕之, アイヌタイムズ (沙流・千歳)

The BE-type is unavailable for alienable nouns; its restriction to the inalienable domain
is argued in huber2025 251–253 . The full typology of predicative-possession
clauses is in predicative-possession-clauses .

Incorporated kinship expressions

Several kin-related meanings are expressed not by the full possession construction but
through lexicalised incorporated-noun + kor compounds functioning as intransitive
predicates denoting change-of-state or relational events rather than stative possession
huber2025 251–252 :

verb
decomposition
meaning
note

pokor
po 'child' + kor
give birth; have a child (change-of-state)
intransitive; stative counterpart a=poho an

matkor
mat 'wife' + kor
marry, take a wife
cf. reciprocal ukor 'marry each other'

honkor
hon 'belly' + kor
become pregnant
change-of-state, not stative possession of a belly

These compounds belong to a productive incorporation pattern; the reciprocal derivatives
ukokor 'share (children)' and
kopokor 'have a child with X' extend the set
huber2025 252 . The incorporation pattern is treated systematically in
noun-incorporation-object .

Dialect variation in kinship terms

The kinship lexicon shows some of the widest dialect spread in Hokkaido Ainu, and the
construction assignments in each dialect interact with local lexical choices
nakagawa2024 223–224 .

'Father' is the most variable kin term. The oldest reconstructed form is
aca (affiliative-construction; current in Sōya and Samani);
mici (concept-construction) is the baseline for Horobetsu,
Shizunai, Tokachi, Kushiro, Bihoro, and main Saru; iyapo appears
in lower Saru; hampe in Ishikari; and hapo
in Chitose. Where hapo means 'father', 'mother' shifts to
totto to avoid homonymy with the widespread
hapo 'mother' nakagawa2024 223 . The community
member thegodofneet (aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-06-08) cites Chiri's dialect survey for
the same mapping and notes that hapo extends further to 'elder
sibling' in Kuril varieties aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Eastern Hokkaido fusion forms. In Tokachi the compounds
konnarpe 'aunt' (< kor unarpe ),
koracapo 'uncle', and koruci 'grandmother'
have grammaticalised to the point where internal structure is opaque; 'so-and-so's aunt'
then requires kor konnarpe , treating the formerly bimorphemic form
as a simplex concept-form noun nakagawa2024 223, ex.257 ‹speculative›.

The younger-sister asymmetry reflects the bilateral-descent kinship system.
'Younger brother' is ak across Hokkaido; 'younger sister of a
sister' is matak (all-Hokkaido, from
mat-ak 'female younger-sibling'). 'Younger sister of a brother'
varies: Saru uses matapa (from mat-apa ,
denoting a more distant relation), Chitose uses macirpe , and
oral-narrative tradition generally uses tures(i)
nakagawa2024 223–224 .

The form onneke(he) 'mother / parent', found in the Tokachi
(Honbetsu) and Nemuro varieties, and onneike 'parent' in Asahikawa,
follows the standard type-① affiliative pattern:
onneke→onnekehe . The community member antitwilight (aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-11-15) proposes the etymology onne 'old' +
-ike 'person', with semantic narrowing from 'the elder [side of the
household]' to 'parent' and further to 'mother' in some varieties
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.

Across these variants the construction assignment — concept-set or affiliative-set — is
stable within each dialect: variation affects which lexical item is chosen, not the
morphosyntactic class it belongs to ‹consensus›.

title: The kuni Complementizer and Purpose Complements
summary: The irrealis complementizer/nominalizer kuni and the purposive kus(u) / kuni ne, marking future-oriented complements of manipulation, desire, and purpose.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The kuni Complementizer and Purpose Complements
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The irrealis complementizer/nominalizer kuni and the purposive kus(u) / kuni ne, marking future-oriented complements of manipulation, desire, and purpose.

kuni in the complementizer system

The particle kuni nominalizes a finite VP into a nominal whose content
is irrealis, prospective, or deontic — what the reporting subject expects, wishes, or presents
as obligatory. It is a member of the small set of complementizers that take a saturated VP and
make it the argument of a transitive matrix predicate, forming what Nakagawa calls the
indirect-speech ( 間接話法 ) system
nakagawa2024 475–476 . The complementizer yak marks
the retrospective or ongoing counterpart; hi and
p ~ pe nominalize without a temporal restriction
nakagawa2024 475–478 ; sato2008 176–177 . The closed class of
formal-defective-nouns formal nouns that includes
p ~ pe is defined there; the arity arithmetic
that separates complementizer from relative-clause use is developed in
nominalization-p-pe .

Three construction types exploit kuni . Before
ye say or ramu think , it nominalizes a VP expressing what the
reporting subject says will happen, believes should happen, or directs someone to do. Before
the formal noun p thing; (pejorative) person , it yields a purposive or deontic
nominal ( kuni p ). And before the copula
ne be directly, Classical Ainu data show an expectative predication meaning
'it must be that' or 'in order that' ( kuni ne )
shibatani1990 83–84 . These three are treated in the sections below.

Corpus search returns 6,113 tokens of kuni (rank 36), a figure that
conflates the complementizer with any phonologically identical material and gives an upper
bound on the construction's frequency.

The irrealis complement of ye and ramu

The matrix verbs that select kuni are
ye say and ramu think . Each member of the indirect-speech
complementizer set marks the temporal or epistemic stance the reporting subject takes toward
the reported content. kuni occupies the prospective end of the
system: the embedded VP describes what will, should, or is wished to come about
nakagawa2024 476 .

complementizer
temporal / epistemic value
matrix verbs

kuni
future / inferred / wished / directive
ye , ramu

kunak
future / inferred
ye , ramu

yak
already-happened / ongoing
ye , ramu , takar

kusu
directive (combined with a causative context)
ye

Two syntactic properties hold across all four members. Final particles of the main-clause type
( na , ya , etc.) cannot appear inside the
embedded clause; they are anchored to the speaker's own utterance, not to the reported one.
And the referential perspective inside the embedded clause shifts from the speaker's to the
reporting subject's: a first-person speaker becomes third or fourth person as the locus of
predication nakagawa2024 475 . These properties confirm that each
complementizer heads a nominal object: even yak , originally a
conditional conjunctive, cannot be followed directly by an intransitive verb
(* yak hawean 'that someone spoke' is ungrammatical as a standalone),
because as a nominal it must be the transitive verb's object
nakagawa2024 475–476 .

hosipi=an kuni a=ye | return=4.S PURP 4.A=say | I said I would return. | Nakagawa's own example. The fourth-person intransitive suffix =an marks the reported subject (the speaker's self-reference shifted to 4th person inside the embedded clause); kuni nominalizes the prospective VP as the object of ye. | nakagawa2024:476 | HK

ahun kuni a=ye akusu toykooripak | enter PURP 4.A=say CONJ be.very.abashed | When I told them to enter, they were very abashed. | kuni nominalizes the directive ahun 'enter' as the content of ye 'say'; akusu links the result clause temporally. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8 (part 2) [aa-irc/007#213]

ramu 'think' selects the same kuni
complement when the thinker's belief is prospective or deontic. The following illustration
conforms to the pattern Nakagawa establishes nakagawa2024 476 :

pirka kuni ku=ramu | be.good PURP 1SG.A=think | I think it would be good. | Constructed to illustrate the kuni complement of ramu; the pattern is established by Nakagawa 2024: 476, which lists both ye and ramu as matrix verbs for kuni. | HK

The distinction between kuni and kunak within
the prospective subsystem is the most discussed. Nakagawa assigns the directive reading
exclusively to kuni : a command or instruction reported under
ye takes kuni ; the two forms are not freely
interchangeable even in purely inferred contexts
nakagawa2024 476–477 ‹corpus-suggested›. The
kuni / yak contrast is cleaner: the pair
divides prospective from retrospective content, mirroring a tense–modality divide in the
reported proposition.

kuni p: purposive and deontic nominals

When kuni is followed by the formal noun
p (allomorph pe after a consonant-final stem),
the compound nominalizes the preceding VP and yields two related readings depending on how
the head is construed: a purposive nominal 'thing to V / what is to be done', and a deontic
or propositional nominal 'what will / must happen'. Both are well attested in the Saru corpus.

The purposive reading is clearest when kuni p denotes objects or
activities defined by their intended function. Two parallel purposive nominals appear in a
single clause:

míre kuni p ére kuni p nepki ne ki wa | clothe PURP NMLZ feed PURP NMLZ task COP do CONJ | making things to clothe (the grandchild) and things to feed (the grandchild) her task, | Two parallel kuni p nominals ('thing to clothe', 'thing to feed') are predicated as the grandmother's task via nepki ne ki wa; the construction names purpose-defined objects. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#355]

The deontic or propositional reading appears when kuni p is itself
predicated. Combination with the copula and the inferential formal noun
ruwe yields the frame
[VP] kuni p ne ruwe ne , which presents the VP-content as inevitable
or obligatory and asserts it as established fact (see ruwe-ne-inferential ):

a=e=túskote kuni p ne ru-we ne na | 4.A=2SG.O=tie.up PURP NMLZ COP track-POSS COP FIN | You will surely be tied up. | The fourth-person agent a= with the second-person object e= yields a passive-like reading; kuni p forms the deontic nominal, ne is the copula predicated over it, and ruwe ne frames the whole as recognized fact. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2 [aa-irc/004#130]

nen poka iki wa yayetuytak kuni p ne ru-we ne na | who even do CONJ make.a.living PURP NMLZ COP track-POSS COP FIN | Somehow, (the old man) will surely manage to make a living. | The embedded clause nen poka iki wa yayetuytak expresses the desired future outcome; kuni p ne ruwe ne presents its coming-to-pass as deontic and epistemically certain. nen poka 'somehow / by whatever means' is an indefinite expression built on the interrogative nen 'who'. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 10 [aa-irc/010#139]

The four main patterns built on kuni , ranging from indirect speech
through purposive nominal to the full epistemic deontic frame, are summarized in the
following table nakagawa2024 476 ; bugaeva2016 294 :

pattern
structural description
reading

[VP] kuni [ye / ramu]
kuni nominalizes VP as argument of matrix predicate
indirect speech / thought with prospective content

[VP] kuni p
kuni + formal noun p
purposive nominal 'thing to V', 'what is to be done'

[VP] kuni p ne
deontic nominal + copula
deontic predication 'is a thing that must / will happen'

[VP] kuni p ne ruwe ne
deontic nominal + copula + inferential formal noun
epistemic deontic 'it is surely a thing that must / will happen'

The p of kuni p is the same formal noun
treated in nominalization-p-pe . Its arity (−1) closes a purposive clause
with an open participant slot (purposive nominal reading) or nominalizes a saturated
irrealis clause as a whole (deontic reading). The complement-taking predicates that select
these nominals are discussed in nominalized-complements-control-raising ; the
overlap with the deontic modal domain in deontic-necessity .

kuni ne: expectative predication

Shibatani groups kuni followed directly by the copula
ne alongside the other formal-noun-plus-copula modal expressions
( ruwe ne , siri ne ,
hawe ne , humi ne ) under the label "expectative
mood". The form yields two readings in his Classical Ainu data: an epistemic 'it must be
that [VP]' and a purposive 'in order that [VP]'
shibatani1990 83–84 .

On this account, kuni itself functions as a formal-noun-like head
nominalizing the preceding VP; the copula predicates that nominalization. The two readings
correspond directly to the deontic and purposive readings of kuni p ne ,
where the additional formal noun p is present between
kuni and the copula. Whether bare
kuni ne and kuni p ne are diachronically
related variants or a single construction with optionally present
p is not settled by the sources read here ‹speculative›.

Shibatani's data are from Classical epic Ainu (yukar traditions of the Itadori and Ishikari
areas). No read source provides modern Saru or Chitose corpus examples of bare
kuni ne without the intervening p ; the banked
Saru corpus examples above all show the kuni p ne form. Nakagawa's
grammar, based on modern Saru and Chitose, records
kuni p ne ruwe ne as the productive frame
nakagawa2024 476 .

The formal-noun-plus-copula paradigm underlying both constructions connects
kuni to the evidential system: Nakagawa observes that clause-final
evidential phrases ( ruwe ne , siri ne , etc.)
constitute one type of complementizer applied to a main clause, and
kuni participates in the same nominal architecture
nakagawa2024 508 . The full paradigm is set out in
evidential-schema-overview . Purpose clauses built on
kuni in adverbial position are treated in
causal-and-purpose-clauses .

Morphological status and open questions

Two analyses of kuni appear in the literature and make different
predictions about its internal structure. Nakagawa treats it as a conjunctive particle
( 接続助詞 ) with formal-noun-like behavior
( 形式名詞的な用法 ) — a free form that nominalizes the preceding VP and
feeds it to a transitive matrix predicate, on the same footing as
yak and kunak
nakagawa2024 476 . On this analysis, kuni p is a
sequence of two free forms: the particle kuni followed by the formal
noun p .

Bugaeva's morphological analysis decomposes kuni-p as
kun-i-p — an obligation-related root
kun- (semantics uncertain), a possessive suffix
-i , and the nominalizer -p — yielding a deontic
head meaning 'the thing that must be done', without necessarily treating
kuni and p as syntactically separate
bugaeva2016 294 ‹contested›. The lexical source of the root
kun- remains unverified.

The broader constituency question — whether kuni p ne ruwe ne is
biclausal (a nominalizing particle and formal noun heading the preceding clause, closed by
the copula) or is grammaticalizing toward a monoclausal complex predicate — runs parallel to
the same debate for the evidential frame ruwe ne , discussed in
nominalization-p-pe and ruwe-ne-inferential . No read source provides
a constituency test that decides between them for the kuni
construction specifically ‹contested›
nakagawa2024 476 ; bugaeva2016 294 .

Shibatani's Classical data add a historical dimension: his "expectative mood" label groups
kuni ne with the possessed sensory-noun heads, suggesting the
formal-noun-like behavior Nakagawa observes reflects a partly grammaticalized noun origin —
the same diachronic cline by which ruwe ,
siri , and hawe moved from sensory nouns to
nominalization heads shibatani1990 83–84 ‹speculative›.

title: The Latin Phonemic Transcription
summary: The canonical romanization adopted as the grammar's primary script — its phoneme-to-grapheme mapping and conventions for /c/, the glottal stop, accent, and capitalization.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Latin Phonemic Transcription
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: The canonical romanization adopted as the grammar's primary script — its phoneme-to-grapheme mapping and conventions for /c/, the glottal stop, accent, and capitalization.

The phonemic principle

The Latin romanization used throughout this grammar is a phonemic transcription: one
grapheme per phoneme, with no attempt to represent predictable allophonic detail. It does not
attempt a phonetic (IPA-narrow) record of every surface variant, nor does it reflect the
abstract underlying representations required for morphophonological rules. Where a predictable
alternation is at issue — such as the realization of /s/ as [ɕ] before /i/, or the voicing of
stops in intervocalic position — the transcription writes the phoneme and the allophonic detail
is described in the relevant phonology chapter. The consonant inventory and its phonetic
realization are treated in consonant-inventory ; the vowel system in
vowel-inventory .

This transcription is the standard now used in Hokkaido Ainu descriptive linguistics and in the
major reference works that underpin this grammar: nakagawa2024 ,
sato2008 , bugaeva2022 , and tamura1996 . The same system
is employed in the present grammar's interlinear glosses and in all Ainu-language inline forms.
The parallel katakana orthography is documented in katakana-and-small-kana-codas ;
Sakhalin-tradition Cyrillic transcription, used only for contrast, is described in
cyrillic-and-multiscript-rendering .

Phoneme-to-grapheme correspondences

The inventory comprises five vowels and twelve consonants, listed in the table below alongside
their IPA equivalents and the primary allophonic notes that bear on the transcription.

Grapheme
IPA
Category
Notes

a
/a/
vowel
low central; the most frequent vowel in the Hokkaido corpus

e
/e/
vowel
mid front; realized approximately [e̞]~[ɛ̝], comparable to Japanese /e/

i
/i/
vowel
high front; conditions the palatalization of /s/ and /c/

o
/o/
vowel
mid back rounded; realized approximately [o̞]~[ɔ̝]

u
/u/
vowel
high back; fully rounded (strong labiality reported by some speakers)

p
/p/
stop
voiceless bilabial; intervocalic lenition to [b] in some dialects/registers

t
/t/
stop
voiceless dental/alveolar; intervocalic lenition to [d] in some environments

k
/k/
stop
voiceless velar; intervocalic lenition to [ɡ] in some environments

c
/t͡ʃ/
affricate

voiceless palato-alveolar affricate; the single grapheme c replaces
earlier ch , č , and ts variants in the literature

s
/s/
fricative

voiceless alveolar fricative; realized as [ɕ] (palato-alveolar) before /i/ and in
word-final position in some dialects; replaces earlier sh and š
variants

m
/m/
nasal
bilabial nasal; neutralizes with /n/ before bilabial consonants in some dialects

n
/n/
nasal
alveolar nasal; realized as [ŋ] before /k/

r
/r/
rhotic
alveolar flap [ɾ]; devoiced after voiceless stops; not a lateral

w
/w/
glide

labio-velar approximant; phonemic in onset position; also appears as a predictable
transitional glide at certain vowel-hiatus sites (see § glide-notation )

y
/j/
glide

palatal approximant; phonemic in onset position; also a predictable transitional glide (see
§ glide-notation )

h
/h/
laryngeal

glottal fricative in onset; word-final -h (the coda laryngeal) is a
special case — its phonemic status and historical origin are discussed in
glottal-stop

The system has no length distinction in vowels: Hokkaido Ainu does not contrast short and long
vowels phonemically (a property that distinguishes it from the Sakhalin varieties, where vowel
length is phonemic and corresponds diachronically to the Hokkaido word-final
-h itabashi2001 ). Hokkaido vowel sequences across morpheme
boundaries are sequences of two distinct vowel phonemes and are treated in
glides-w-y .

The choice of c, and s before i

The grapheme c for the palato-alveolar affricate /t͡ʃ/ is the letter
that has become standard in Hokkaido Ainu linguistics. Older transcriptions — including
missionary-era works and earlier twentieth-century philological writing — employed
ch , č , or occasionally ts for the same segment; these variants
survive in reprints and in writers unfamiliar with the modern scholarly standard
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023). The unambiguity of
c rests on a phonological fact: Hokkaido Ainu has no contrast between a
palatal and a non-palatal affricate, so a single letter covers the full distribution of the
phoneme without risk of collision with other segments kirikae1997 .

Native-speaker writing, from Chiri Yukie chiri1923 to the Ainu kana orthographies
documented by Kirikae kirikae1997 and Nakagawa nakagawa2006 , does not
write ca cu ce with contracted kana (チャ チュ チェ) but with the
straight syllabary forms. Kirikae attributes this to the absence of any phonological contrast
between a palatal and non-palatal series in Ainu: the affricate is uniformly palato-alveolar
regardless of the following vowel, and the kana typography that distinguishes large from small
character would imply a distinction that Ainu phonology does not make
kirikae1997 ‹speculative›.

The fricative s is written uniformly throughout, even though its
phonetic realization is [ɕ] (palato-alveolar) before /i/ and in certain coda environments.
Earlier transcriptions sometimes employed sh or š for the coda or pre-/i/
variant; the modern standard writes s in all positions and treats [ɕ]
as a predictable allophone sato2008 ; nakagawa2024 . The phonetics of
the alternation and its morphophonological consequences are developed in
consonant-inventory .

Glottal stop, vowel-initial words, and onset representation

Every syllable in Hokkaido Ainu has a consonantal onset. Words written with an initial vowel
letter — ek 'come', arki 'come (plural)',
opitta 'all' — have an underlying glottal stop [ʔ] as their onset, but
the modern romanization does not write this stop. Tamura's earlier analytical papers
represented it with an apostrophe ( 'a , 'i , etc.),
and the Kotobai no kenkyū variant and some early descriptive materials similarly mark
vowel-initial onsets; from roughly the 1980s onward, however, the apostrophe has been
systematically omitted in published Hokkaido Ainu linguistics, the convention followed here
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023). The suppression of the
apostrophe reflects a practical consensus rather than a settled phonemic analysis: whether the
glottal stop is a separate phoneme or a default onset-filler is a genuine analytical question
addressed in glottal-stop .

Word-final -h — which appears on many verbal and nominal stems in their
citation form (e.g. nupurh 'mountain god' in some dialects) — is a
distinct phenomenon: it is not a surface fricative but a laryngeal element that triggers
morphophonological alternations and corresponds to Sakhalin vowel length. Its treatment is
fully separated from the onset-glottal-stop question and is given in glottal-stop .

Pitch-accent diacritics: when and how accent is marked

Hokkaido Ainu has a lexically contrastive pitch-accent system. In citation forms and in
paradigm cells where the accentual distinction is under discussion, an acute diacritic marks the
first vowel of the accented mora: síne 'one',
tú 'two'. When accent is not relevant to the point being made, forms
are written without diacritics throughout. Running Ainu text — narrative examples, interlinear
glosses, and ordinary in-text citations — is unaccented unless the prosodic pattern is the
object of description; the pitch-accent chapters are the exception, not the rule
nakagawa2024 .

This policy mirrors the convention of nakagawa2024 and departs slightly from some
pedagogical materials that mark accent more systematically. Most reference works in the
literature — including tamura1996 , sato2008 , and
bugaeva2022 — do not mark accent on forms cited in grammatical discussion, and the
present grammar follows that tradition in its non-prosodic chapters. The accentual system, its
placement rule, and the accentless-dialect split are developed in
pitch-accent-placement-rule .

Morpheme and person-marker boundary notation

Two punctuation marks segment the internal structure of Ainu words in this grammar and in the
interlinear glosses documented in glossing-abbreviations-and-citation :

The equals sign = marks the boundary between a person marker
and its stem: ku=kor '1 sg.a =have',
a=nu '4. a =hear'. This is an orthographic
device of the Nakagawa lineage — expressly not the general-linguistic use of
= for phrasal clitics nakagawa2024 58–60 : it keeps
inflectional person marking visually distinct from derivation and makes the dictionary
headword recoverable. The markers' actual affix-versus-clitic status is mixed and
contested (see personal-affix-template-and-ordering boundary-status ).

The hyphen - marks derivational morpheme boundaries and
stem-internal junctures: yay-nu 'self-hear (listen)', e-nu ' appl -hear'. Derivational prefixes and suffixes form part of the
derived stem, which the person markers attach outside of.

The distinction between = and - originates in Nakagawa's personal
data-management notation of the early 1980s and entered print in the 1986 publication
『語りの中の生活誌』, from where it spread through Hokkaido Ainu teaching and descriptive
materials nakagawa2024 58–60 ; aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2024). It has never been universal: Tamura's dictionary and papers
print inflected forms solid or hyphenated, Refsing refsing1986 and Satō
sato2008 use hyphens for all bound morphemes ( ku-kor wa
k-ek ), and Ijäs ijas2023 follows the hyphen practice; Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 and the Handbook tradition bugaeva2022 use
= , and it is adopted here. The morphological and syntactic status of the
person markers is developed throughout Part V.

Word-division decisions — how compounds, incorporating forms, and postpositional groups are
spaced — are a separate question from morpheme-boundary notation and are treated in
orthographic-standardization-and-word-division .

Capitalization, proper names, and punctuation

Ainu words are written in lower case throughout, including in the Ainu-language line of
interlinear glosses. Sentence-initial capitalization does not apply to the Ainu line; only the
free-translation line follows ordinary English sentence capitalization. Personal names and
place names are capitalized when they appear in English running prose but retain lower case in
glossed lines and in in-text Ainu citations: sisam mosir 'Japan
(Wajin-land)', pirika menoko 'a beautiful woman'.

Punctuation within Ainu text follows the source when a text is being cited; the grammar's own
prose interpolations and constructed examples carry no internal Ainu punctuation. The
discrepancy between comma-period ( ，． ) and Japanese-style pause-stop
( 、。 ) conventions in different Hokkaido Ainu publications is a
document-production variable, not a linguistic distinction, and is not discussed further here.

Glide transcription and vowel hiatus

Sequences of two vowels from different morphemes — a=e '4. a =eat', u-epeker 'mutual.storytelling',
i-omante 'send (with 4. o )' — are written as
bare vowel sequences in this grammar, following Nakagawa's modern convention. An older style
associated with Tamura's publications from the 1970s onward inserts a transitional glide at the
hiatus site: uwepeker , iyomante
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024) ‹contested›. The choice
has phonological consequences: whether the transitional [w] and [j] are phonemic or predictable
epenthetic segments determines whether they should be written. The analysis adopted here treats
them as predictable and omits them in the romanization; the underlying analysis is developed in
glides-w-y . Phonemic /w/ and /y/ in onset position are always written (e.g.
wakka 'water', yuk 'deer').

Relation to the IPA and to other romanizations in the literature

The table below maps this grammar's graphemes to the IPA and to the conventions used in the
four main reference works: Tamura tamura1996 , Satō sato2008 , Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 , and the Handbook of the Ainu Language
bugaeva2022 .

This grammar

Satō (2008)
Nakagawa (2024)
Bugaeva (2022)
Older variants

a e i o u
/a e i o u/

p t k
/p t k/

/m n r w j h/

[ʔ] onset

apostrophe ʼ before vowels
