Across the four main sources, the segmental inventory is identical and the letter choices for
the consonants and vowels are uniform ‹consensus›. The differences are notational conventions:
the treatment of the glottal stop onset (suppressed everywhere in modern practice), the use of
= versus - for the person markers, and the insertion or suppression
of transitional glides at vowel hiatus. Tamura's 1996 dictionary uses only hyphens and inserts
glides; Satō likewise hyphenates the person markers; Nakagawa and the Handbook use
= ; the sources are divided on glide insertion.
These differences are confined to morpheme-boundary notation and to the analysis of hiatus; they
do not affect the phonemic letter values.

The Shiraishi chapter in the Handbook provides a systematic phonetic description alongside
phonemic notation shiraishi2022 ; where that chapter uses IPA symbols for phonetic
detail this grammar cites the relevant IPA and maps it to its own grapheme. Older grammars and
dictionaries — Batchelor batchelor1905 , Chamberlain chamberlain1887 ,
and the Kindaichi–Chiri tradition kindaichi1936 — use substantially different
letter choices that are not systematically enumerated here; readers encountering those sources
should be aware that their ch and sh renderings are equivalent to this
grammar's c and s , and that their morpheme
boundary conventions differ throughout.

The relation between the Latin romanization and the katakana orthography — including the
systematic letter-to-kana mapping, the extended small-kana coda letters, and round-trip
conversion issues — is documented in katakana-and-small-kana-codas .

title: Lexical/Contrastive Accent and Minimal Pairs
summary: How much accent is rule-derived versus lexically specified, demonstrated through pitch-only minimal and near-minimal pairs.
part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Lexical/Contrastive Accent and Minimal Pairs
Part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
Summary: How much accent is rule-derived versus lexically specified, demonstrated through pitch-only minimal and near-minimal pairs.

Predictable and lexically specified accent

The placement rules of the accent dialects — if σ1 is closed (CVC), σ1 bears high pitch;
if σ1 is open (CV), σ2 bears high pitch — assign accent to the large majority of the
Hokkaido Ainu lexicon nakagawa2024 50 . Shiraishi terms this the iambic (LH)
pattern: words with a heavy (closed) first syllable are trochaic in accent, and words with a
light (open) first syllable are iambic shiraishi2022 §5.1 . The full statement
of these rules and their interaction with syllable weight is the subject of
pitch-accent-placement-rule .

The rules leave a residue of words with open σ1 that are accented on σ1 against the
prediction. These carry lexically stored accent and must be individually memorized.
nakagawa2024 51 lists roughly twenty such items:
cápe 'cat', húci 'grandmother',
húra 'smell', húre 'red',
káni 'I (emphatic)', kéra 'taste',
míci 'father', mína 'laugh',
nína 'gather firewood', nísap 'suddenly',
núman 'yesterday', páse 'heavy',
sápo 'elder sister', sések 'hot',
síno 'really', sísam 'Japanese person',
túki 'cup', túnas 'fast',
úsey 'hot water', yúpo 'elder brother',
and further items beyond the list given there. Every word in the accent dialects is either
rule-assigned or lexically specified; there is no third status.

The person-marking prefixes ku= , e= , and
ci= are incorporated into the prosodic word and can shift accent
relative to its base; a= and eci= do not
trigger a shift. Compounds suppress the accent of all but the initial element. These
morphological and compositional effects on accent placement are treated in
accent-in-compounds-and-affixation and, for the person-affix interaction,
in person-marking-architecture .

Minimal and near-minimal pairs

The exception-list words create lexical contrasts wherever a segmentally identical (or
near-identical) word follows the regular rule. Three such pairs are established in the
descriptive literature nakagawa2024 51 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 :

form A
IPA (approx.)
gloss
form B
IPA (approx.)
gloss

nísap
[ní.sap̚]
'suddenly'
nisáp
[ni.sáp̚]
'shin'

nína
[ní.na]
'gather firewood'
niná
[ni.ná]
'crush'

útur
[ú.tuɾ]
'west side of a house'
utúr
[u.túɾ]
'between'

In each pair, form A carries lexical exception accent on σ1 and form B is rule-derived
with σ2 accent. The critical phonetic property is the rising nucleus
(上り核アクセント): what is distinctive is where the pitch ↗ rises, not where it
subsequently falls. A word accented on σ1 begins with high pitch and may remain high
throughout with no change of meaning, because the system encodes the onset of the
high span. A word accented on σ2 requires a ↗ rise at σ2; pronouncing it with an
σ1-initial high instead misidentifies it as the σ1-accented form
nakagawa2024 48–49 . This stands in contrast with Standard Japanese, whose
falling nucleus (下り核アクセント) marks the syllable after which pitch drops
shiraishi2022 §5 . A community observation from the Saru dialect captures
the asymmetry: the pronouns ku↗ani and
ku↗a↘ni are both acceptable, but
ku↘ani (Japanese-style falling nucleus) is perceived as deviant —
the rise position, not the fall, is what matters ‹corpus-confirmed›
( aynucorporadiscord ; nukopoli, 2024-12-09).

The nísap : nisáp pair also has a
diachronic dimension. Sakhalin Ainu preserves vowel-length distinctions in exactly the
positions where Hokkaido Ainu carries an accent distinction:

Hokkaido Ainu
gloss
Sakhalin Ainu

nísap
'suddenly'
niisah

nisáp
'shin'
nisah

húre
'red'
huure

sísam
'Japanese person'
siisam

túki
'cup'
tuuki

The long Sakhalin vowel occupies the syllable that carries initial accent in the Hokkaido
exception word; the short Sakhalin vowel corresponds to the Hokkaido rule-derived form.
This correspondence underpins the reconstruction of a proto-system in which these syllables
were prosodically marked — by vowel quantity, retained in Sakhalin and restructured as
pitch in Hokkaido nakagawa2024 27, 51 ; itabashi2001 . The
direction of that change and whether the ancestral contrast was quantity-based or
pitch-based remain disputed ‹contested› ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 ). The
SA–HA length–pitch correspondence independently confirms that the three minimal pairs above
represent genuine lexical contrasts.

Functional load of the accent contrast

The contrastive yield of the accent system is limited. The placement rules account for the
large majority of the lexicon; the exception list is closed and memorizable rather than an
open accidental class. The three pairs documented above appear to constitute the
best-established minimal/near-minimal set in the literature; nakagawa2024 51
acknowledges the list to be incomplete, which implies further near-minimal contrasts in
principle — any exception word whose segmental shape matches a rule-derived word — but such
additional pairs are not systematically tabulated in the available sources. Lexical
disambiguation relies primarily on morphosyntactic context, and the segmental form alone
resolves ambiguity in most encounters.

The typological character that emerges is a pitch-accent system with a predominantly
predictable accent assignment and a limited lexically stored residue. The formal typology
of rising versus falling nuclei and the mora-syllable question are treated in
mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .

The contrastive relevance of accent varies across dialects. In Shizunai, Urakawa, Samani,
Bihoro, Kushiro, and Shiranuka, pitch placement is not phonemically distinctive
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 ; nakagawa2024 49 , and the three
contrasts above are neutralized. Whether Shizunai should be classified alongside the accent
dialects — the position of refsing1986 73 — or treated as accentless,
as nakagawa2024 49–50 argues on the basis of variable archival
transcriptions, has not been settled ‹contested›. The practical consequence is that the
minimal pairs in this chapter are attributes of the Saru-type accent system and cannot be
assumed for the accentless eastern and southeastern varieties. The dialect typology is
addressed in hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

The Yakumo and Oshamambe varieties retain pitch distinctiveness but apply a rightward-shifted
rule: when both σ1 and σ2 are open, accent falls on σ3 rather than σ2
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 ; nakagawa2024 50–51 .
Forms that contrast in Saru by the exception-versus-rule distinction may therefore
pattern differently in Yakumo, since their accent is calculated from a shifted baseline —
Saru notákam corresponds to Yakumo notakám ,
and Saru sinúye to Yakumo sinuyé
( nakagawa2024 50–51 ). Whether the Yakumo rule interacts with the exception
list in the same way as the Saru rule — and so whether the same minimal pairs surface in
Yakumo — is not addressed in the available sources. The Yakumo system is described in
pitch-accent-placement-rule .

Accent of loanwords and reduplicated forms

Loanwords are adapted to the Hokkaido Ainu (C)V(C) syllable canon — consonant-cluster
repair, epenthesis, and syllable-boundary insertion — before entering the phonological
system, as described in syllable-template . Once adapted, they are in
principle subject to the same placement rules as native words. The Japanese borrowing
tampaku 'tobacco' has its initial syllable closed by coda
-m and would receive σ1 accent by 原則1
( támpaku ); the Russian-origin form
potoloko 'ceiling' begins with open σ1 and would receive σ2
accent by 原則2 ( potóloko ). Neither
nakagawa2024 nor shiraishi2022 states a loan-specific accent
rule, and the application of the placement rules to adapted loanforms is an inference
from the general description of the system rather than a directly documented observation.
Whether any loanword has joined the exception list with lexically stored non-rule accent
is unattested in the available sources.

A community discussion observed a potential tension with the compound
kamuynomi 'prayer ceremony'
( kamúy + nomí →
kamúynomi by first-element priority,
nakagawa2024 54 ). The Ainu accent on the first element is expected; the
observation concerned the different prosodic placement that this term acquires when it is
borrowed into Japanese-language usage ‹speculative›
( aynucorporadiscord ; palestine_2026, 2024-08-12). That is a property of the
borrowing in its receiving language. Within Ainu, kamuynomi follows
the first-element-priority pattern of all compounds, detailed in
accent-in-compounds-and-affixation .

For reduplicated forms, direct evidence from phonetically transcribed accent-dialect texts
is sparse. A community observation reports that somósomo — the
reduplicated form of somó 'even, also' — functions as a single
prosodic word accented on σ2, matching the base, and that the two-word string
ka somó ki 'even … too' assigns accent on the base word
independently ‹speculative› ( aynucorporadiscord ; nukopoli, 2024-12-09). The
pattern is consistent with first-element priority — in full reduplication the first copy
retains its accent and the echo copy is de-accented — but primary-source confirmation from
the phonetically transcribed descriptive literature is lacking. The prosodic-word domain and
its role in accent assignment are treated in accent-in-compounds-and-affixation .

title: Lexical-Head and Formal-Noun Nominalization (kur, uske, ruwe/hawe/siri/humi)
summary: Nominalization headed by lexical nouns (kur 'person', uske/usi 'place', ike) and by the grammaticalized formal-noun set (ruwe, hawe, siri, humi, hi, pe) acting as clause-nominalizing heads.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Lexical-Head and Formal-Noun Nominalization (kur, uske, ruwe/hawe/siri/humi)
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: Nominalization headed by lexical nouns (kur 'person', uske/usi 'place', ike) and by the grammaticalized formal-noun set (ruwe, hawe, siri, humi, hi, pe) acting as clause-nominalizing heads.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu forms nominalized clauses by placing a
formal-defective-nouns formal noun after a finite predicate. The verb retains its
ordinary person marking; the formal noun supplies the nominal head and converts the preceding
material into an NP. The class is closed and its members differ in the semantic profile they
impose — 'thing', 'person', 'place', 'appearance', 'voice', and so on — and in whether they
function as relative-clause heads, complementizers, or both
nakagawa2024 257–262, 503–513 ; sato2008 174–180 ;
bugaeva2016 226–228 .

The allomorphs p thing, person (nominalizer) ~
pe thing, person (nominalizer) , their participant-nominalization and
complementizer functions, and the arity diagnostic that distinguishes the two readings are
treated in nominalization-p-pe . The gap strategy, accessibility hierarchy, and
postposition stranding that characterize all relative clauses are covered in
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses . The clause-final evidential uses of
ruwe the trace of , siri the appearance of ,
hawe the voice of , and humi the sound/feeling of are treated in
evidential-schema-overview . The present chapter addresses the morphological contrast
between the concept form and the affiliative (possessed) form of these nouns,
the semantic profiles that govern head-selection across the formal-noun inventory,
hi thing, place, time in its several uses, the sensory-noun formal nouns as
non-evidential perception-complement heads, and the cline from clausal nominalization to
lexicalized noun.

Concept form and affiliative (possessed) form

Each formal noun has two shapes described in concept-form-affiliative-form . The
bare concept form (概念形) is the citation form of the noun, autonomous in a sentence.
The affiliative form , or possessed form (所属形), marks the noun as belonging
to an antecedent; it is the shape in which the sensory-noun formal nouns appear in their
evidential and perception-complement uses. For the four sensory nouns the two forms are
distinguished by a possessive suffix nakagawa2024 258, 表66 :

concept form
possessed form
POSS suffix
source meaning

ru
ruwe
-(w)e
'track, path'

sir
siri
-i
'appearance, state'

haw
hawe
-e
'voice'

hum
humi
-i
'sound, sensation'

Satō observes that a noun in its possessed form presupposes an antecedent — something it
belongs to — so that placing a sensory noun in this form after a finite clause grammatically
encodes that the described situation is already held as given by the speaker
sato2008 135 . This presupposition distinguishes evidential
ruwe ('the trace of —', implicating a recognized antecedent event)
from lexical ru ('a track'). Dialect evidence reinforces the contrast:
southern Hokkaido (Saru, Chitose) uses the possessed forms in the clause-final evidential
frame, while Tokachi and Ishikari close the clause with the bare concept forms
ru , sir , haw ,
hum
nakagawa2024 110, 390 ; takahashi2013 129–131 .

Principal formal nouns as nominal heads

All formal nouns have arity −1, which allows them to terminate a relative clause (closing an
arity ≥+1 gap) or a saturated complement clause (arity 0) alike
nakagawa2024 261–262 . The table below covers those relevant to nominalization;
the full catalogue with arity derivations appears in formal-defective-nouns .

form
gloss
as RC head
as complementizer
notes

p ~ pe
'thing; (pej.) person'
✓
✓
allomorphy by stem-final segment; see nominalization-p-pe

hi (~ i after C)
'thing, time, place'
✓
✓
maximally abstract; see below

hike
'the one/side'
✓
borderline
grammaticalizes toward adversative conjunction hikeka

katu
'manner'
—
✓
< kat 'form/shape' + POSS; assertive use

usi(ke)
'place, time'
✓
✓
pan-Hokkaido; uske specific to Saru/Chitose/Shizunai

kur
'person'
✓
—
< kuru 'person'; honorific/polite register

utar
'people, companions'
✓
—
follows a noun as well as a VP; marks a collective

ruwe / siri / hawe / humi
trace, appearance, voice, sensation
✓
✓
possessed forms; → clause-final evidential in evidential-schema-overview

The pair usi / uske are dialectally distributed:
uske is attested in Saru, Chitose, and Shizunai; the forms
usi and usike are more widely spread across
Hokkaido nakagawa2024 258 . Both head relative clauses without requiring the
relational noun or that movement-case postpositions need before
locative complements bugaeva2015 47 .
Shibatani lists the nominalizers -p(e) , -i ,
-ike , and -kur as productive phrasal and clausal
nominalizating suffixes in classical/yukar Ainu
shibatani1990 33–34 , corresponding to the word-class of formal nouns in
Nakagawa's synchronic description nakagawa2024 257–258 .

Division of labour across the inventory

The formal-noun head selected for a nominalized clause is determined primarily by the semantic
category of the reference being expressed. p ~ pe
and hi cover the widest range but along different dimensions:
p ~ pe profiles a concrete entity — a thing
or person that can be encountered or handled — while hi is maximally
abstract, covering fact, time, and place readings alike
nakagawa2024 257 ; sato2008 174 . Where the referent is
specifically a person and an honorific register is required,
kur (from kuru person ) is used;
utar extends this person head to a group. The place nouns
usi(ke) and uske head nominalizations that
refer to a location or time without the concrete/abstract ambiguity of
hi .

Satō's diagnostic sharpens the contrast between the relative-clause use and the complementizer
use for any formal noun. A formal noun functioning as a relative-clause head heads a
clause from which a participant has been gapped; the original sentence can be reconstructed by
reinserting that participant. A formal noun functioning as a complementizer
nominalizes the whole clause as a proposition, and no such reconstruction is possible
sato2008 174–175 . Both p ~
pe and hi fill either role depending on
the arity of the clause they follow; the sensory-noun heads serve both functions as well,
though their complement use is semantically restricted to perception contexts.

Person formal noun kur produces nominalizations whose referent is a
specific individual. In classical and epic texts these regularly lexicalize:
kotan kor-kur 'chief' (lit. 'person who has a village'),
ray-kur 'the dead' (lit. 'person(s) who die')
shibatani1990 34 . Compound nominalizations of this type remain analytically
transparent even after lexicalization, illustrating the continuity between productive RC
nominalization and the lexicalized stratum treated in the final section.

hi — semantic range and the place/time split

hi thing, place, time is the most abstract formal noun in the inventory; its
gloss covers 'thing, matter, fact', 'time when', and 'place where'. After a consonant it
surfaces as -i — as in the lexicalized
ape-o-i 'hearth', treated in the next section
nakagawa2024 257 ; sato2008 169 .

In the 'thing/fact' and 'time' readings, hi nominalizes a saturated
clause (arity 0) and functions as a complementizer. The nominalized clause may serve as the
grammatical subject of a matrix predicate:

ku=kemeyki kor ku=itak hi iyotta pirka | 1SG.A=sew CONJ 1SG.A=talk NMLZ most good | it is best to talk while sewing | my talking-while-sewing is most good | After Tamura 2000: 126. hi takes two person-marked, fully specified clauses and nominalizes the whole as the grammatical subject of iyotta pirka 'is most good'. | bugaeva2016:261 | SAR

Or as the object of a complement-taking predicate. In the following example,
hi appears twice: as the object-head of
a=ye 'say' (arity +1, 'what to say') and of
a=kar 'do' (arity +1, 'what to do'). In each case the clause before
hi has an open slot, so hi fills the head
rather than the complementizer role:

a=ye hi ka a=kar hi ka a=eramiskari | 4.A=say NMLZ even 4.A=do NMLZ even 4.A=not.know | not knowing what to say or what to do | ninjal2003 | CHI | Oda Ito, NINJAL corpus of Ainu oral literature (Chitose) [ninjal-folklore/026#61]

The 'place' reading calls for a different structure: the governing postposition is retained
inside the relative clause, marking hi as the locative head (arity +1
because the location slot is gapped):

oro ta cise kar hi | place LOC house build NMLZ | the place where someone built a house | ta (LOC) retained inside the RC signals the locative role of hi; compare the time reading below, where no postposition is present. | nakagawa2024:511 | HK

With the time and abstract-fact readings, the clause is saturated and
hi nominalizes it whole. The postposition that follows
hi is not part of the RC but a postposition governing the entire
nominalized clause:

pon=an hi wano | be.small=4.S NMLZ since | since I was small | hi nominalizes the intransitive clause pon=an (arity 0); wano 'since/from' governs the nominalized clause as a temporal adverbial. | nakagawa2024:510 | HK

Nakagawa frames the place/non-place split as a tendency. The 'place' reading is more concrete
and closer to an ordinary noun, so the grammatical relation requires overt expression; the
'time/fact' reading is abstract and freely nominalizes the whole clause. Okuda (1989), quoted
and refined by nakagawa2024 510–511 , first systematized this contrast. An
attested exception — seta utar osoma hi 'the dogs' privy', from
知里幸恵's kamuy yukar collection (Horobetsu), which uses a 'place' reading without a retained
postposition — shows the constraint does not hold categorically
nakagawa2024 511 ‹corpus-suggested›.

The locative formal nouns usi and uske offer an
alternative to hi for the 'place' reading. Because they are inherently
locative, they require no retained movement-case postposition. The directional
postposition eun 'toward' is instead retained for the oblique head:

eun a=nu usi ka isam | toward 4.A=ask place even not.exist | there was no place to ask about it | Bugaeva ex. 16. nu 'hear, ask about'; eun 'toward' retained inside the RC signals the directional role; usi 'place' is the formal-noun head. The NP is the argument of isam 'not exist'. | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

Ijäs observes that usi -headed compound nouns — such as
ipeusi 'restaurant' (lit. 'eating-place') — are historical RC
nominalizations, and that usi -words require no relational noun
or before movement-case postpositions
ijas2023 ‹consensus›. A locative relative clause with a plain common noun head
does require the relational noun:

oro ta k=an kotan | place LOC 1SG.S=exist village | the village I live in | Ijäs Lesson 11. oro 'place/vicinity' (relational noun) fronts before ta (LOC). With a usi-type head no oro would be needed. | ijas2023 | HK

Sensory-noun formal nouns as perception-complement heads

Apart from their clause-final evidential function (treated in evidential-schema-overview
and ruwe-ne-inferential the inferential ruwe ne ), the
possessed-form sensory nouns ruwe , siri ,
hawe , and humi function as nominal heads of
complement clauses whose matrix predicate is a perception or cognition verb. In this
intra-sentence use, the matrix verb follows the formal noun rather than closing the clause
sato2008 175–178 .

Each formal noun is selected by a specific subset of perception predicates:
nukar 'see' takes siri (the visual scene) or
hi (an abstract perceived content);
nu 'hear, ask about' takes hawe (verbal
content), humi (a non-verbal sensation), or
hi ; cognition predicates eramiskari
'not know' and eraman 'understand' take hi
or p ~ pe
sato2008 175–178 . The complement-taking predicates and the structural
properties of these complements are treated in nominalized-complements-control-raising .

Within the visual domain, Satō distinguishes the two available heads:
siri applies to an action or situation in progress at reference time
— a scene being witnessed — while ruwe applies to an already-existing
state or non-progressive action asserted as recognized fact
sato2008 178 . In the following example the speaker uses
siri to frame the visible absence of large trees as the scene that
prompted anger:

poro cikuni oar isam sir-i ku=nukar wa sonno ku=iruska | big tree at.all not.exist appearance-POSS 1SG.A=see CONJ truly 1SG.A=be.angry | I saw that there were no big trees at all, and was truly angry. | sir-i = sir 'appearance/state' + possessed suffix -i. siri heads the complement of ku=nukar 'I saw'; ku=iruska follows wa (CONJ) as a second, main-clause predicate. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | Kanzaki Masayoshi, Ainu Times (ainu-times/015/3#7)

The shift from this intra-sentence perception complement (matrix verb follows
siri ) to the clause-final evidential frame (copula
ne follows) instantiates Bugaeva's Stage II grammaticalization path:
the nominal head that was an argument of a perception predicate becomes the terminal element
of an evidential construction bugaeva2016 229–304 . The two uses remain
synchronically productive and distinct; the evidential use is the more grammaticalized
endpoint ‹consensus›.

The constituency of the whole construction — whether it is biclausal (a possessed noun heading
the preceding finite clause, closed by the copula) or is grammaticalizing toward a monoclausal
complex predicate — is unsettled. The biclausal analysis is held by Tamura, Okuda, and Satō,
and is endorsed by Nakagawa under the label 補文標識 'complementizer'
nakagawa2024 508 ; sato2008 175 . Bugaeva argues that the
erstwhile nouns are considerably grammaticalized and the construction is moving toward a
monoclausal complex predicate, supported by the fact that the formal noun no longer heads a
full NP ( *pirka ruwe ne kewtum is ungrammatical — no determiner or
attributive modifier can attach)
bugaeva2016 306–315 ; dalcorso2018 24–25 ‹contested›.

Lexicalized formal-noun nominalizations and the RC–compound boundary

A relative-clause NP of the form [(NP…) V nmz] can crystallize into a
single lexical noun when the constituent parts are reproduced as a unit with a conventional,
fixed meaning. Bugaeva traces a grammaticalization cline from productive RC nominalization to
lexicalized noun in the Hokkaido data, placing Hokkaido at Stage II of a broader areal
typology (Stage I = zero-nominalization; Stage II = nominalizing word; Stage III–IV in
Sakhalin = possessed verbal forms)
bugaeva2016 240–251, 385–397 . The lexicalized products are morphologically
transparent but synchronically function as ordinary nouns.

Two items illustrate the pattern with allomorphs of different formal nouns:

ci=ronnu-p | 1PL.A=kill-NMLZ | fox | thing(s) that we kill | ci= (1PL.A) + ronnu 'kill' + -p (NMLZ = formal noun p/pe, 'thing'). The generic patient 'things we kill' has been conventionalized as the species name for fox. | bugaeva2016:245 | HK

ape-o-i | fire-enter-NMLZ | hearth | the place where fire enters | -i is the allomorph of formal noun hi after a consonant, giving the 'place' reading. ape 'fire' is incorporated; o 'enter'. Contrast aep (4.A=eat-NMLZ) 'food', where -p gives the concrete 'thing' reading of p/pe. | bugaeva2016:245 | HK

The allomorph split across these two items reflects the same head-selection principle visible
in productive clausal nominalizations: -p ~ -pe
yields a concrete entity ('thing killed', 'thing cooked'), while
-i (allomorph of hi ) yields a location
('place where fire enters'). The semantic specialization survives lexicalization.

A relative-clause NP and a compound noun are superficially similar — both consist of a
predicate-like element preceding a noun — but the arity test separates them
nakagawa2024 506–507 . The compound
tureptani 'uba-lily digging-stick' juxtaposes
turep 'uba-lily' and tani 'digging-stick'
without marking the semantic relation between them; the RC reading 'the stick with which that
woman dug uba-lilies' requires the instrumental postposition
ani 'with' to be retained inside the clause, making the instrument
role explicit:

nea menoko ani turepta ni | that woman INS dig.uba.lily stick | the stick with which that woman dug uba-lilies | ani (INS) is retained inside the RC because ni 'stick/wood' fills the instrument role. The compound tureptani suppresses the grammatical relation; the RC must express it. | nakagawa2024:506 | HK

The diagnostic generalizes: in an RC nominalization the semantic relation between the
formal-noun head and the predicate is grammatically specified — via a retained postposition,
the verb's argument structure, or an applicative prefix. In a compound noun, that relation
is implicit. Lexicalized RC nominalizations such as ci=ronnu-p and
ape-o-i were once transparent RC structures; the grammatical marking
has become inert but remains recoverable from the morphology.

Ijäs's observation that usi -words such as
ipeusi 'restaurant' ('eating-place') descend historically from RC
structures aligns with Bugaeva's Stage II account and with Shibatani's finding that
-p(e) , -i , -ike , and
-kur function as both phrasal and lexical nominalizers
ijas2023 ; shibatani1990 33–34 ; bugaeva2016 240–251
‹consensus›. The productive and lexical levels are not sharply bounded; what counts as a new
RC formation versus a lexicalized item depends on the frequency and semantic
conventionalization of the combination.

title: Synopsis of Lexical-Semantic Fields
summary: A field-by-field synopsis of the Ainu lexicon — its semantic organization, the classified-dictionary tradition, and culturally salient vocabulary domains.
part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Synopsis of Lexical-Semantic Fields
Part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
Summary: A field-by-field synopsis of the Ainu lexicon — its semantic organization, the classified-dictionary tradition, and culturally salient vocabulary domains.

Overview

The native core of the Hokkaido Ainu lexicon is organized around a small number of semantic
fields whose internal structure feeds directly into the grammar. Kinship terms and body-part
nouns constitute the nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns obligatorily-possessed (bound)
noun class. Motion verbs supply the primary paradigm of singular/plural stem suppletion.
Colour terms and property-denoting natural-world vocabulary are encoded as stative verbs, with
no separate adjectival category. Demonstratives, locative nouns, and postpositions combine into
a layered spatial-deictic system anchored to river orientation. The table below maps each field
to its grammatically active sub-structure and to the chapters that give the full account. For
vocabulary lookup, the Itak dictionary carries the attested lexicon; this chapter is a finding
aid, not a word list.

semantic field
grammatically relevant feature
treated in depth in

kinship terms

obligatorily possessed; irregular/suppletive affiliatives; honorific possession via
4th/indefinite person

kinship-honorific-possession ,
alienable-inalienable-split

body-part nouns

obligatorily possessed; possessed forms feed external possession and S-incorporation
(valency-retaining)

external-possession-possessor-raising ,
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding

natural-world vocabulary

sir land, appearance -incorporation with natural-phenomenon subjects
(valency-canceling); lexicalized kina edible plant / mun weed, inedible plant folk-taxonomy distinction

noun-incorporation-object ,
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding

motion and directional verbs
SG/PL stem suppletion; path/directional semantics; river-anchored orientation

suppletive-verbal-number ,
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events

colour terms
encoded as stative verbs predicated without a copula; no separate adjective class

no-adjective-class-property-verbs ,
property-concept-predication

spatial demonstratives and locative nouns

three-term deictic contrast; obligatorily-possessed locative nouns; postpositional
layering

demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness ,
relational-and-spatial-nouns

The contact stratum — Japanese loans clustering in the domains of domestic animals, trade goods,
administration, and modern institutions — is treated in the contact-lexicon chapter. Loanword
phonological adaptation (voicing neutralization, cluster break-up, epenthesis) is treated in
syllable-template . The vigesimal numeral system and its classifier constructions
belong to vigesimal-numeral-system and numeral-classifiers-ordinals-and-quantifier-syntax ;
the genetic position and typological profile implied by the native core lexicon are discussed in
genetic-position-macro-comparison .

Kinship terms

The kinship vocabulary encodes a four-axis grid: sex and relative age of the referent in
sibling terms; sex of the referent in lineal terms. All kinship nouns are obligatorily
possessed: they cannot appear without a possessor prefix, and their possessed
( concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative ) form is built by the same suffix
alternation found on body parts and relational nouns (see
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology ) bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §5.1 ;
nakagawa2024 §22.2 . The following table gives the principal Saru-dialect terms
with their standard meanings; the kinship system and its possessive morphology are treated fully
in kinship-honorific-possession .

term
meaning (Saru baseline)
register

hapo mother
mother
adult colloquial

totto mummy (infant speech)
mother (infant-directed)
infant speech

michi father / aca father (variant)
father
adult

yupo elder brother
elder brother
adult

aki younger brother
younger brother
adult

sapo elder sister
elder sister
adult

matak younger sister
younger sister
adult

huci grandmother; fire goddess
grandmother; by extension, the fire goddess
adult

ekasi grandfather; elder man
grandfather; elder man (respectful)
adult

mat wife, woman
wife; woman
adult

hoku husband
husband
adult

po child (one's own)
child (one's own)
adult

The following constructed example shows the obligatory possessor prefix and the affiliative
suffix on a kinship noun:

ku=yup-ihi | 1SG.POSS=elder.brother-POSS | my elder brother | The 1SG possessor prefix ku= and the affiliative suffix -ihi co-occur; the possessed form yup-ihi suppresses the final vowel o of the citation form yupo. Third-person possessor is zero-marked: yup-ihi alone = 'his/her elder brother'.

A register split divides adult and infant-directed forms: totto mummy (infant speech) and popo big brother (infant speech) are the infant-directed counterparts of
hapo and yupo , attested in Nakagawa's infant-speech table for the Horobetsu
dialect nakagawa2024 523 . Literary and formal registers additionally have
ona father (literary) and unu mother (literary) as honorific
equivalents of the colloquial forms, though their distribution and exact mapping vary by dialect
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2026-06-15; aynucorporadiscord ).

Dialect variation within the kinship field is considerable. hapo denotes the mother
in most Hokkaido dialects but the father in Chitose and Horobetsu (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora
Discord 2026-06-08, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ; corroborated by
chiri1953 ) ‹corpus-suggested›, and the Kuril reflex carries a sibling reading. Such
semantic drift is characteristic of kin terms cross-linguistically. The possessive morphology of
kinship nouns, including their suppletive affiliative forms and the honorific use of the
fourth/indefinite person in possession, is treated in kinship-honorific-possession ;
the alienability classification they belong to is in alienable-inalienable-split .

Body-part terms

Body-part nouns are the second major obligatorily-possessed class. Like kinship terms, they
require a possessor and appear in the concept-form-affiliative-form possessed
(affiliative) form ; the suffix alternates among -e ,
-i , and -ihi depending on the noun's final
consonant and vowel quality (see affiliative-suffix-morphophonology )
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §5.1 . Core Saru terms include sik eye ,
tek hand , kema leg , nanu face ,
sapa head , kewtum heart, chest, mind ,
ramu heart, spirit, mind , honi belly ,
haw voice , and etor snot
tamura1996 s.v. .

sik-ihi | eye-POSS | his/her eye (possessed form) | Third-person possessor is zero; the affiliative suffix -ihi appears after consonant-final stems. Contrast tek-e 'his/her hand', where the shorter -e allomorph follows the stem-final k.

ku=tek-e | 1SG.POSS=hand-POSS | my hand | The 1SG possessor prefix ku= combines with the affiliative suffix -e on the body-part noun tek 'hand'.

Body-part nouns are the primary context for S-type noun incorporation with valency-retaining
effect: when a body-part noun is incorporated as the S argument of an intransitive verb, the
possessor of that body part surfaces as an added participant rather than being absorbed, so the
construction gains a slot rather than losing one bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.6 ;
sato2016 . This contrasts with the valency-canceling O-incorporation typical of
common object nouns (see noun-incorporation-object ). External possession — where
the possessor of a body-part object is indexed on the verb rather than carried adnominally — is
treated in external-possession-possessor-raising . The full account of body-part
S-incorporation is in noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding .

The near-synonymous pair kewtum heart, chest, mind and
ramu heart, spirit, mind is pervasive in compounds and in oral literature. Their
distribution is partly dialectal and partly a distinction between the physical chest/heart and a
more diffuse psychological or spiritual sense; both participate in compounding productively (e.g.
kewtum-pirka 'be good-hearted', ramu-pok
'think little of'). A community-proposed internal morphological analysis of
esikop parent as e-sik-o-p 'thing from which eyes come'
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-11, proposed; aynucorporadiscord )
illustrates the general productivity of body-part roots in derivation, though the specific
etymology has not been verified in the published literature ‹speculative›.

The natural world: geography, flora, fauna, and weather

Geographic vocabulary

Geographic vocabulary is among the richest and highest-frequency sub-fields of the native
lexicon. The core set includes kotan village, settlement (4,210 corpus tokens,
rank 56), pet river (the area's representative river) (1,344 tokens),
nay stream, tributary (559 tokens), to lake, pond ,
nupuri mountain , atuy sea ,
sir land, surface, appearance , and
mosir world, island, land tamura1996 s.v. ;
nakagawa1995 s.v. .

The pet / nay distinction encodes a cultural-geographic rather than a
purely scalar opposition. pet designates the main, named river of an
area — even a small flow, if it is the representative waterway of that locality — while
nay designates a branch stream or tributary, which may physically exceed
a local pet in volume. The community corroborates this distinction:
nay = mountain stream/tributary, to = pond
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-11, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ;
consistent with tamura1996 s.v. pet, nay ) ‹corpus-confirmed›. Both roots are
morphologically unanalyzable and drive the major toponym substrate of Hokkaido (Japanese
-betsu < pet ,
-nai < nay ), discussed in
genetic-position-macro-comparison .

sir land, surface, appearance is notably polysemous: it covers the physical
land surface, the external appearance or state of affairs, and the condition of the weather (as
in compounds sir-pirka 'the weather is good',
sir-kunne 'it grew dark'). The same root forms the base of the
situational evidential siri (see
siri-ne-situational the situational evidential ). The community notes that the
derived form sirka surface, surroundings inherits the full polysemy of the base
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-15, asserted;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Flora and the kina/mun distinction

The folk-taxonomy of plants is organized around ni tree, wood ,
kina edible or useful plant , and
mun inedible plant, weed . The kina / mun opposition lexicalizes a cultural distinction — useful/edible versus useless/noxious — that
carries no grammatical reflex but is visible throughout plant names and in compounds with
kina
vovin2022 Ch.6 n.5 . The assignment of a plant to one category or the other can
differ across dialects, depending on local usage and ecology.

Fauna

Core fauna terms include cep fish ,
kamuy bear; deity, supernatural being , cikap bird , and
yuk deer . kamuy occupies a grammatically and culturally central
position: in its unmarked referential use it denotes the brown bear as the foremost hunting
deity, but it extends to any powerful supernatural being, to forces that exceed ordinary
understanding, and to a range of compounded divine titles
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §1 . Its very high corpus frequency reflects its pervasive
presence in oral literature and ritual language. Domestic animals introduced through contact
are loanwords: umma horse (< Japanese うま ),
meko cat (< Japanese ねこ ), and
peko cow
chiri1953 §293, §266 .

A community-proposed internal analysis reads cep fish as
ci=e-p 'the thing (p) that one (ci=) eats (e)' — an argument-structure
nominalization in which the middle-voice prefix fills both subject and object slots (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-06, proposed;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹speculative›. The analysis is internally consistent with the
productive nominalizer -p , but the synchronic morphological boundary is
opaque in the modern form and the etymology has not been verified in the published descriptive
literature.

Weather and natural-phenomenon vocabulary

Weather vocabulary includes apto rain , rera wind , and
upas snow . The compound
sir-pirka the weather is good (lit. the land/appearance is good) is the
standard illustrative example of S-type noun incorporation with a natural-phenomenon subject:
the incorporated element sir fills the S slot of the intransitive stative
pirka be good, be beautiful , producing a valency-canceling effect in which the
incorporated noun — not an independent NP — bears the subject role
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.6 .

sir-pirka | land/appearance-be.good | The weather is good. | S-incorporation of the natural-phenomenon noun sir 'land, appearance, weather' into the intransitive stative verb pirka 'be good'. The incorporated noun fills the S slot; no independent subject NP appears.

A community-proposed reconstruction treats the initial vowel of
upas snow as secondary, with *o- as the original form,
against the folk-etymology parsing it as u-pas
(antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-23, proposed;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹speculative›.

Motion and directional verbs

Motion verbs are the primary locus of SG/PL stem suppletion in Hokkaido Ainu. The principal
suppletive pairs are arpa go (SG) / paye go (PL) ,
ek come (SG) / arki come (PL) , and
an be, exist (SG) / oka be, exist (PL)
nakagawa2022 ; bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.2 . Number agreement targets the S
or A argument of the verb; the outer plural suffix
-pa and the full account of suppletive and suffixal plural strategies are
in suppletive-verbal-number .

The path-encoding set includes san go seaward or downstream (movement toward
the sea or downslope), yan go inland, come up onto land (movement away from the
sea or upslope), oman go , hosipi return ,
ahun enter , and soyne go out . The
san / yan pair encodes a coast-relative or
river-relative directionality: san moves toward the water or its outlet
and yan moves away from it. These verbs combine with applicative
prefixes and directional roots to build complex path constructions (see
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events and
applicative-system-overview ) nakagawa2024 §6 .

The directional vocabulary extends to adverbial spatial terms:
penke upstream side, upper portion and
panke downstream side, lower portion locate entities relative to water flow
rather than to the speaker's body axis tamura1996 s.v. penke, panke . This
river-anchored orientation appears as a component in Hokkaido place names and governs
orientation in oral-literature narrative.

A morphological alternation recurs in several motion verb bases: beside the
-n -final form, a parallel -p -final form exists
( san / sap ,
yan / yap ,
ahun / ahup ). The pair is widely noted in Ainu
linguistics; the origin of the alternation — whether the -p descends
from the pluractional suffix -pa , from a distinct derivational suffix,
or from another source — is debated (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2026-05-18, proposed;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›.

Colour terms

The basic colour vocabulary of Hokkaido Ainu comprises four stative verbs. Ainu has no separate
adjective word class (see no-adjective-class-property-verbs ): colour terms are
intransitive stative verbs predicated directly without a copula, and they modify nouns
attributively in their concept form (see concept-form-affiliative-form )
nakagawa2024 §3 . The four basic terms and their corpus frequencies are:

stative verb
primary colour range
extended use
corpus tokens

retar be white
white
dawn brightness; purity
261

kunne be black, be dark
black, dark
nightfall ( kunne sir 'it grew dark')
—

hure be red
red
blood, fire, sunset
184

siwnin be blue-green (grue) / sinnin

blue-green (grue)
reported also for purple and yellow (see below)
—

kunne serves as the predicate of nightfall as well as of the colour black;
retar describes the light of dawn as well as the colour white
tamura1996 s.v. kunne, retar . The terms thus participate in the
temporal-lighting vocabulary beyond their core chromatic meanings. A community-proposed internal
etymology reads kunne as kur-ne 'to be like a shadow/person'
( kur shadow, person + copula ne be (copula) ) (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2024-06-18, asserted;
aynucorporadiscord ). The segmentation is internally consistent — the copula
ne productively derives stative verbs in Ainu — but has not been
confirmed in the published philological literature ‹speculative›.

siwnin is typologically notable: it covers the blue-green continuum without a primary
split, fitting the classic grue pattern of shibatani1990 §1 . Dictionary
records indicate that the term's range may extend beyond the blue-green merger to include purple
and possibly yellow in some usage (toracatman, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-05-30, asserted;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›, though no systematic colour-category survey
of Ainu has been conducted. A Tokachi example using siwnin in an
evidential construction appears in ruwe-ne-inferential ( taan imo
siwnin ru an 'this potato is green', takahashi2013 131–133 ), where the
individual-level property takes the trace-nominalizer evidential rather than the visual.

A community proto-Ainu reconstruction for retar be white proposes an earlier
cluster-initial form *tretar / *dretar on the basis
of a Taraika Sakhalin reflex tetar (antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord
2024-11-24, proposed; aynucorporadiscord ), consistent with the comparative program
of vovin1993 and alonso2022 ‹speculative›. This is noted as
a lead for the historical phonology; verification requires the primary comparative data.

Spatial deixis and locative nouns

The spatial deictic system has three interacting layers: a demonstrative series, a set of
obligatorily-possessed locative nouns, and a small set of postpositions. Their full accounts are
distributed across demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness ,
relational-and-spatial-nouns , and
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events . The synoptic inventory is:

layer
form(s)
spatial or discourse value

demonstratives

tan this (proximal) / taan

proximal (near speaker)

toan that (distal) / toon

distal (away from speaker)

nean that (anaphoric) / ne

anaphoric (discourse-given referent)

locative nouns (obligatorily possessed)
or place, location
general locative; possessed form oro

ka top, surface
superessive; possessed form kasi

sam side, vicinity
proximate lateral

tum middle, interior
interior/medial

pokna below, under
subessive

rikun above
superessive-high

postpositions
ta
static locative (LOC)

un
goal/allative

wa
source/ablative

The demonstrative series is three-way: proximal
tan / taan (near speaker), distal
toan / toon (away from speaker), and anaphoric
nean / ne (discourse-established referent)
nakagawa2024 §10 ; takahashi2011 . Within the proximal slot, the
longer form taan has been proposed to carry greater information content
or emphasis relative to tan , and a sound-symbolic account has been
proposed in which narrow front vowels index proximity and broader back vowels index distance
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2025-12-27, proposed;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›. The full discourse-anaphoric and definiteness
functions of the series are treated in demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness .

Locative nouns ( or , ka , sam , tum , etc.) are
obligatorily possessed: a referential head NP must serve as their possessor. The postposition
ta (LOC) then follows the possessed locative noun to form a spatial
phrase: pet or ta 'at the river', cise ka ta
'on top of the house'. Among these, or shows a grammaticalization drift in the
corpus, encroaching on the functions of a general locative case marker
sato2021 ; nakagawa2024 §11 .

pet or ta ku=an | river place LOC 1SG.S=exist | I am by the river. | The locative noun or 'place' takes the river NP pet as its possessor; the postposition ta marks static location. The possessor relation is adnominal: pet is 'the river's place'. First-person S is marked by the intransitive suffix =an on the existential verb.

The directional system is river-anchored at the adverbial level:
penke upstream side and panke downstream side locate entities
relative to water flow rather than to the speaker's body axis
tamura1996 s.v. penke, panke . This axis governs orientation in narrative and
appears as a component in Hokkaido place names. Its integration with the postpositional and
motion-event system — including its interaction with san go seaward and
yan go inland — is treated in
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events .

title: Light Verbs, Pro-Verbs, and the Auxiliary-Verb Construction
summary: The 'do' pro-verbs ki/iki, the structural support uses of ne and an, and the general auxiliary-verb (complex-predicate) construction.
part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Light Verbs, Pro-Verbs, and the Auxiliary-Verb Construction
Part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
Summary: The 'do' pro-verbs ki/iki, the structural support uses of ne and an, and the general auxiliary-verb (complex-predicate) construction.

The support-verb inventory

Hokkaido Ainu has a small set of high-frequency verbs whose lexical meanings are bleached in
particular constructions while their ordinary verbal morphology remains visible. The central
member is ki do , a transitive verb that supplies a verbal host for a preceding
event expression; the same support pattern appears in clausal negation, clause linkage under
negation, and some pro-verbal uses where the lexical content is recovered from context. The
copula ne be, become and the existential an exist also serve as
supports, especially in predicate-nominal, evidential, existential, and aspectual frames nakagawa2024 124–129, 132, 374 ; bugaeva2012 492–496 . The shared condition is structural: the support verb
supplies the finite predicate position, so person marking, negation, and clause-linking material
attach to a verb word of the kind described in verb-word-template .

support verb
ordinary valence
support use
diagnostic behaviour
main detail chapter

ki / iki 'do'
transitive; takes transitive-type subject marking
formal verb with an event or predicate complement; support for somo ki
in V (ka) somo ki , the lexical predicate keeps its person marking while ki is unindexed
negation-somo-standard-clausal

ne 'be, become'
two-place copular verb; the predicate complement is not indexed as an object
predicate-nominal support; closes formal-noun evidentials and nominalized clauses
subject affix appears, object affix is excluded
copula-ne-predicate-nominals

an 'exist'
intransitive existential; plural counterpart oka
existential predicate, component of zero-place weather verbs, and aspectual/evidential support
indexes its subject as S when a subject is present; zero-place compounds built on an index none
existential-locational-an-oka

The inventory rests on the same valence diagnostics as ordinary verbs. Intransitives take S
markers, transitives take A/O architecture, and the side-switching first-plural and fourth-person
forms provide the clearest test nakagawa2024 125–128, 165–169 ; sato2008 94–98 . Nakagawa's arity calculus counts person indexes, derivational prefixes, noun incorporation,
and base verbs in a single computation; Satō separates argument slots, macrorole transitivity,
and morphological valence, using ne as a central case where two argument
slots coexist with only one indexable macrorole nakagawa2024 136–139 ; sato2023b 42–52 ‹contested›. The support verbs preserve ordinary head-marking morphology even where their
lexical semantics is weak.

Ki as the formal verb of somo negation

The clearest grammaticalized use of ki is the periphrastic negative frame
V (ka) somo ki . The notional predicate precedes the negative adverb
somo , optionally followed by the focus/additive particle
ka ; ki supplies the finite verbal head that
somo negates. In Bugaeva's formulation, the preceding notional verb
functions as an object-like complement of ki ; Nakagawa describes the same
configuration as a use of the formal verb ki that verb-phrasifies the
preceding material bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 374, 407 . The
person index belongs to the notional predicate; ki itself is unindexed in
the periphrasis. The direct frame somo V places the negator before the
whole person-marked verb and does not split a person prefix from the stem sato2008 124 ; ijas2023 . Nakagawa finds no clear semantic difference between the two
arrangements, and Satō notes that ordinary verbal negation very often uses the periphrastic
pattern nakagawa2024 374 ; sato2008 22, 124 .

nep a=nukar ka somo ki korka | something 4.A=see even NEG do although | Although I did not see anything, | Nakagawa ex. 833. The lexical verb nukar bears the fourth-person A prefix; ki carries no person index. The indefinite nep receives its negative reading inside the scope of the negator. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

eci=yayeymontasa somo ki yakka | 2PL.S=take.revenge NEG do even.if | Even if you do not take revenge, | Nakagawa ex. 834. The same frame without ka; the intransitive predicate keeps its 2PL.S prefix while ki remains unindexed. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

The internal constituency of somo ki is disputed. Bugaeva and Nakagawa
keep the compositional analysis in which ki is the formal verb and the
preceding predicate is its complement bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 374 . Satō, in the Chitose description reported by Takahashi, treats
somo ki as a single auxiliary; Takahashi leaves the historical path open
sato2008 86 ; takahashi2016 74 ‹contested›. The disagreement
bears on constituency while both analyses place the construction in the same post-predicate slot.
The distribution and scope of the negator are developed in
negation-somo-standard-clausal .

Ki as event support and pro-verb

Outside negation, ki verbalizes event-denoting material and can stand
where English translations reach for 'do'. The complement may be a predicate stem, an event
nominal, or a nominalized clause, and its transitive subject is marked by the A-set, as expected
for a transitive verb. This use provides the bridge between ordinary lexical
ki and the negative support frame: both build a finite predicate out of
material whose own syntax is already present.

kira=an ka apkas=an ka eaykap no cis patek a=ki kor | flee=4.S even walk=4.S even cannot ADV cry only 4.A=do while | Unable to flee or to walk, doing nothing but cry, | The light predicate cis patek a=ki 'do only crying' shows ki with an overt event complement; the fourth-person A prefix appears on ki. | nakagawa2024:315 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 492

e=itak haw-e ku=nu hi renkayne itasaytak ku=ki kusu ne na | 2SG.S=speak voice-POSS 1SG.A=hear NMLZ following reply 1SG.A=do PURP COP FIN | I will listen to what you say and answer accordingly. | The event nominal itasaytak 'reply' is made predicative by ku=ki and combines with the future-intentional kusu ne; haw-e is glossed as a possessed noun. | nakagawa2024:435 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 966

The positive frame V ka ki also belongs here. Nakagawa cites
ipe ka a=ki and rok=an ka ki in comparing
post-predicate and preverbal negative patterns, and treats the event before
ka ki as the complement verbalized by ki nakagawa2024 374 . These examples license a restricted pro-verb analysis: ki can carry
the finite morphology for an event already specified by an adjacent complement ‹consensus›. They
do not demonstrate an unrestricted substitute for a missing verb phrase; broader VP-substitution
uses would require a separate corpus study ‹corpus-suggested›.

The variant iki is clearest in template diagnostics. The plural suffix
-pa and the outer plural clitic pa form a minimal
ordering pair with this verb: iki-pa=an has root-bound
-pa inside the fourth-person subject suffix, while
iki=an pa has the clitic after the person-inflected word nakagawa2024 156–159 . That ordering pair shows why support verbs still count as verbs: they take the ordinary
person and number morphology when they function as the predicate of a clause, even where their
lexical content is bleached.

Ki, somo ki, and clause linkage

A negated action predicate normally links to a following clause with no ,
yielding the common 'without doing X' reading. Nakagawa and Satō state the rule that stative
predicates and negated action predicates take no , with
wa excluded from this environment nakagawa2024 340–341 ;
sato2008 44–47 . The result is a three-level stack:
ki supplies the predicate of the negated clause,
somo negates it, and no links the whole to the
following clause.

ku=caranke ka somo ki no ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=complain even NEG do ADV 1SG.S=return | I went home without complaining. | The negated action predicate is closed by ki before no links it to the following clause. The clause-linkage system is treated in detail in the clause-linkage overview. | sato2008:44 | SAR

The same architecture appears below formal-noun nominalization and evidential predicates. A
negated verb phrase can stand inside the proposition headed by the possessed formal noun
ruwe ; ne then predicates the nominalized
proposition. The negation is internal to the nominalized clause, while the copular evidential
frame scopes over it nakagawa2024 393 .

sinep ka a=isamka ka somo ki ru-we ne kusu | one even 4.A=lose even NEG do track-POSS COP because | Since I did not lose even a single one, | Nakagawa ex. 779. The negated support frame a=isamka ka somo ki is embedded below ru-we ne; negation falls inside the evidential while the copular frame scopes over the whole proposition. The particle kusu links the evidential frame as a reason clause. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR

This ordering is a useful boundary test. In … somo ki ruwe ne the
proposition is negated and the evidential remains outside the scope of negation. When
somo falls on the copula of the formal-noun predicate, as in
ruwe somo ne , the result is a high-scope rhetorical or convictional
frame, a reading outside ordinary propositional negation nakagawa2024 393 . The
details of that outer frame are routed to ruwe-ne-inferential and
negation-somo-standard-clausal . For the linking inventory see
clause-linkage-overview .

Ne as copular support

The copula ne is formally transitive in valence classification, yet only
its subject is indexed. Nakagawa gives okkayo ku=ne 'I am a man' with a
transitive-type subject prefix and rejects object indexing on the predicate complement nakagawa2024 132 ; Shibatani likewise describes the copula as taking transitive subject morphology shibatani1990 50 . Satō's three-valency analysis accounts for the pattern by separating the two-place predicate
relation from indexable macroroles: the predicate nominal is a complement of the copular
relation, while only the subject is available for person indexing sato2023b 52
‹contested›.

frame
shape
support function of ne
routing

predicate nominal
NP ne
class-membership or identity predicate
copula-ne-predicate-nominals

clausal nominalization
[clause] p/pe ne
predicates a nominalized event, fact, or participant
nominalization-p-pe

formal-noun evidential
[clause] ruwe/hawe/siri/humi ne
turns a formal-noun nominalization into a clause-final evidential predicate
ruwe-ne-inferential

copular negation
NP ( ka ) somo ne
gives the negated nonverbal predicate a verbal host
negation-somo-standard-clausal

okkayo ku=ne | man 1SG.A=COP | I am a man. | Nakagawa ex. 23. The first-person prefix is the transitive-subject form; the predicate nominal okkayo is not indexed as an object. | nakagawa2024:132 | HK

Because ne is the ordinary support for predicate nominals, the negative
frame of copular clauses is NP (ka) somo ne . Satō notes that
ne ka somo ki , expected if the copula simply fed the ordinary
somo ki frame, is almost unattested sato2008 22 . The
copular construction keeps its own negation architecture, detailed in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals and negation-somo-standard-clausal .

The evidential example below combines both support verbs in a single sentence.
Ne predicates a zero-place weather verb in the first clause, while
ki hosts the support negation in the second. The layering makes it a
useful diagnostic for the whole support system.

mean cuk ne pe merayke=an ka somo ki no | be.cold.weather autumn COP NMLZ feel.cold=4.S even NEG do ADV | It was a cold autumn, but I did not feel cold. | The first clause uses ne in a predicate-nominal frame over the zero-place weather predicate mean; the second uses the support-verb negation merayke=an ka somo ki closed by no. | nakagawa2024:130 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 15, after Kayano 1974a

An between existential verb and support

The existential an remains an intransitive verb when it predicates
existence or location, and it enters support constructions from that source. One pathway is
lexical compounding into zero-place ambient predicates. Nakagawa derives
mean 'be cold (weather)' from a non-free root
me- plus an ; such predicates take no person index
because no subject slot remains nakagawa2024 128–129 . Shibatani analyses these
as subject-incorporation, and Ijäs teaches them as avalent weather verbs shibatani1990 33 ; ijas2023 . Where the first element is a free noun, the boundary is unsettled:
Ijäs treats apto as 'it rains' as an avalent unit, while Nakagawa leaves
the analysis open because apto is an autonomous noun ‹contested› nakagawa2024 129–130 .

domain
zero-place predicate
subject-taking intransitive counterpart
diagnostic

cold
mean
merayke
climate predicate vs. felt temperature

cool
sirmeman
meman
ambient state vs. experiencer state

warm
sirpopke
popke
ambient state vs. experiencer state

hot
sirsesek
sesek
ambient state vs. experiencer state

The v0/vi pairing in this table is directly grounded in Nakagawa's analysis: the
sir- prefix and the me- root are non-autonomous
elements that fuse with an into a zero-place compound, whereas the
felt-temperature verbs take a subject marked by the ordinary S-set nakagawa2024 127 ‹consensus›.

A second pathway keeps an as an overt support verb. Existential and
locational clauses are described in existential-locational-an-oka ; aspectual frames
such as kor an and wa an are treated in
existential-aspect-an-oka . In evidential constructions, the alternation between
copular ne and existential an contributes to the
direct/indirect opposition of formal-noun predicates, with siri an and
related forms routed to the evidential chapters. These support uses preserve the intransitive
origin of an while extending it into TAM and information-source
constructions ‹consensus› nakagawa2024 390–393, 446–449 .

Ijäs and the learner-grammar claims

Ijäs's treatment foregrounds the learner's problem: Ainu negation and auxiliary-like meanings
are built with independent verbs, so the person marker stays with the predicate that supplies the
argument frame. Her rule that somo precedes the verb and cannot split a
person marker from its stem matches Satō's and Nakagawa's description ‹consensus› ijas2023 ; sato2008 124 ; nakagawa2024 407 . Her identification of
ki as a dummy 'do' verb that fills the required verb position because
somo cannot stand as the predicate of a clause also matches the
compositional account in Bugaeva and Nakagawa ‹consensus› ijas2023 ; bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 374 .

niskur an ka somo ki | cloud exist even NEG do | It is not cloudy. | Ijäs negates the existential niskur an 'there are clouds' with the ka somo ki periphrasis; ki supplies the verb position because somo cannot head a clause on its own. Constructed pedagogical example. | ijas2023

Two Ijäs claims carry caveats. Her suggestion that postverbal
ka somo ki reflects Japanese influence on word order is not supported by
the read Hokkaido grammars and should be attributed to Ijäs alone ‹speculative› ijas2023 ; dalcorso2025neg 41–42 . Her presentation of lexical negative verbs as
blocking somo plus an affirmative counterpart also states a tendency too
strongly; the primary sources describe lexicalized negative predicates and preferences, while
coexistence with somo is independently attested in some environments
‹contested› ijas2023 .

Analytical boundaries

The support-verb system is outside the verb word proper, whose person indexes, valency prefixes,
incorporated nouns, and stem-internal suffixes are ordered in verb-word-template .
Valency-changing derivation — applicative, causative, antipassive — changes the argument frame
of the lexical stem; ki , ne , and
an provide a higher predicate host above the already-built stem. The
lexical semantics of the copula and existential are treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals and existential-locational-an-oka .

The degree of grammaticalization differs across the three verbs.
Somo ki may be analysed as a compositional support-verb construction or
as a single auxiliary; kor an and wa an behave
aspectually while still showing clause-chain morphology; ne is a
verb-like copula whose object indexing is blocked. These disagreements affect analysis, not the
descriptive ordering: person-marked lexical predicate first, support predicate above or after it,
and clause-level operators outside that support layer ‹contested›.

title: First-Person Narration, the Logophoric Fourth Person, and Reported Discourse
summary: The grammaticalized narrator viewpoint in sung epic and the logophoric/reported-speech system as deployed across genres.
part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: First-Person Narration, the Logophoric Fourth Person, and Reported Discourse
Part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
Summary: The grammaticalized narrator viewpoint in sung epic and the logophoric/reported-speech system as deployed across genres.

The reported-discourse system in overview

Hokkaido Ainu marks reported discourse with a quotative index (QI): a quotative
marker that follows the quoted string and is in turn followed by a speech or cognition verb.
The central marker is sekor , an adverbial conjunction
bugaeva2008 35–36 that is obligatory in direct and semi-direct
constructions. A structurally separate class of constructions uses bound complementizers
( kunak , yak , kuni )
with transitive reporting verbs and is treated as a complementation device in
quotative-complementation-sekor and
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements . This chapter covers the
sekor -mediated system: the transitivity split among reporting verbs,
the direct/semi-direct/indirect distinction, the 1st-to-4th-person shift inside quotes, the
logophoricity debate, the first-person narration convention of yukar and uwepeker, and the
hawe family of forms that bridges the quotative and evidential systems.
The evidential architecture within which hawe ne and
hawe as sit is described in
evidential-schema-overview and hawe-ne-reportative .

The quotative and evidential systems intersect at the noun haw voice : the
intransitive report verb hawean ('a voice exists' → 'say'), the
reportative evidential hawe ne ('it is a voice/report that'), and the
hearsay form hawe as ('a voice arises') all derive from the possessed
form haw-e 'the voice of —' nakagawa2024 398, 428 .
Reference-tracking across narrative spans, which the quotative machinery helps manage, is
described in reference-tracking .

sekor and the reporting-verb split

The etymology of sekor is transparent: it derives from
se 'make a sound / say' + kor 'while / while
holding', yielding roughly 'while saying' nakagawa2024 470 (nukopoli,
aynucorporadiscord , 2024) ‹corpus-confirmed›. In the southern Hokkaido dialects
it is fully grammaticalized as the quotative particle and no longer carries its compositional
meaning. The form following sekor is the reporting verb, and its
transitivity determines the construction type.

The constraint was first stated by chiri1942 572–573 and systematized by
bugaeva2008 36–37 : sekor co-occurs with
intransitive reporting verbs because the quoted string is adverbial — it is not the
object of the reporting verb. The complementizer constructions
( kunak / yak / kuni ) take
transitive verbs ( ye 'say', ramu
'think') because the complement clause is the object. The community discussion confirms this:
*sekor a=ramu is ungrammatical — ramu requires
a nominal object; the correct form is kuni a=ramu or, with the
intransitive cognate, sekor yaynu=an (nukopoli,
aynucorporadiscord , 2024) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The apparent exception is sekor a=ye : the transitive verb
ye occurs after sekor in a lexicalized frame
that is more frequent in the corpus than the intransitive alternative
sekor itak=an (nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord , 2024;
thegodofneet, aynucorporadiscord , 2025) ‹corpus-confirmed›.
nakagawa2024 470 acknowledges sekor a=ye as a
lexicalized exception to the transitivity constraint. ijas2023 presents both
sekor hawean and sekor a=ye as the two
standard quotative frames in her pedagogical grammar.

reporting verb
transitivity
gloss
follows sekor
notes

hawean
intransitive
'say'
✓

itak
intransitive
'speak'
✓
general 'speak'; less frequent with sekor

isoytak
intransitive
'narrate'
✓

yaynu
intransitive
'think'
✓
inner thought-report

ye
transitive (+2)
'say'
✓ (lexicalized exception)
sekor a=ye ; commoner than sekor itak=an

ramu
transitive (+2)
'think'
✗
use kuni a=ramu for indirect thought-report

somo itak=an hi ne sekor haw-e-an=an kor | NEG speak=4.S NMLZ COP QUOT voice-POSS-exist=4.S while | Saying, 'One does not speak.' | The intransitive haw-e-an=an follows sekor; the quoted string ends with the formal noun hi plus the copula ne. The 4th-person suffix =an inside the quote and on the reporting verb haw-e-an=an signals that the quoted speaker is other than the current narrator — a semi-direct shift treated in §3. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakamii Matsuko, isoytak 独話1, ILCAA Ainu materials aa-irc/003#57

Direct, semi-direct, and indirect reported discourse

bugaeva2008 34–35 identifies three construction types for Hokkaido Ainu,
distinguished by the treatment of person inside the quote and by the syntactic integration of
the complement. The table organizes the system:

type
quotative frame
1st person inside the quote
reporting verb

Direct (DRD)
sekor
as originally uttered
intransitive

Semi-direct (SDRD)
sekor
shifted to 4th/INDEF ( asinuma , a= , =an )
intransitive or lexicalized ye

Indirect (IRD)
kunak / yak / kuni
fully shifted to the reporter's deixis
transitive

The DRD/SDRD distinction rests on person: when the current narrator quotes herself, 1st-person
forms inside the quote are retained, and the construction is direct. When she quotes a
different speaker, the original 1st-person of the quoted speaker is replaced by the
4th/indefinite person — the pronoun asinuma for the singular
logophoric-like reference, the prefix a= on transitive stems, and the
suffix =an on intransitive stems — and the result is semi-direct
tamura2000 75 ; bugaeva2008 37–40 . The free pronoun
asinuma and the 4th-person affix paradigm are described in
independent-personal-pronouns and
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

asinuma arpa=an kusu ne sekor haw-e-an | 4.SG go.SG=4.S PURP COP QUOT voice-POSS-exist | She said, 'I [self] will go.' | The quoted speaker is a third party. The pronoun asinuma 'self' and the suffix =an mark the quoted speaker's 1st person via the 4th-person form — the semi-direct shift. A self-quotation by the same narrator would retain the 1SG prefix k= inside the quote. | bugaeva2008:38 | SAR | Tamura 1972: 377 ex. 9a, as cited in Bugaeva 2008: 38

kani k=arpa sekor ku=haw-e-an | 1SG 1SG.A=go.SG QUOT 1SG.S=voice-POSS-exist | I said, 'I will go.' | Self-quotation retains the 1SG prefix k= (reduced form of ku= before a vowel-initial stem) inside the quote. The reporting verb ku=haw-e-an marks the narrator as the 1SG subject of the intransitive hawean. Contrast the preceding example, where the same quoted content attributed to a third party takes asinuma arpa=an. | SAR

tamura2000 75 is explicit that the 4th-person-in-quote is not indirect
narration. bugaeva2008 38–43 formalizes a semi-direct category: the syntax of
the quote is direct, but the person form signals that the source of the quoted content is a
participant other than the current narrator. nikitina2021 sharpen the analysis:
across languages, logophoric speech shares its syntax with direct speech and none with
indirect; the Ainu semi-direct construction is syntactically direct-like and its person shift
is a logophoric device, not a symptom of syntactic subordination ‹contested›. The
clause-integration mechanics of the three types are treated in
clause-linkage-overview .

The logophoricity debate

Two analyses of the 4th-person-in-quote compete. tamura1972 calls it the
'quotative first person' (引用の1人称), treating it as a sub-use of the 4th person alongside
the inclusive, impersonal, and honorific uses.
nakagawa2024 179 lists the quotative use as use ⑤ of the 4th person without
positing a dedicated logophoric category. bugaeva2008 40–43 argues for a
genuinely logophoric analysis: the pronoun asinuma (literally
a=sinuma 'the self of one') is the singular logophoric form, invariably
glossed by the speaker Mrs. Ito Oda as じぶん 'self' rather than
わたし 'I'; it tracks the original speaker of the quoted discourse, not
the current narrator ‹contested›.

sato2008 259–262 and sato2004 179–184 treat the same forms
as polysemous, deriving the narrative-first-person use from a first-person-plural (inclusive)
strategy in which the 4th-person affixes index both protagonist and audience. The whole-range
of the 4th person and the competing analyses of its referential extent are laid out in
fourth-person-honorific .

The available evidence leaves several points unsettled. The logophoric
analysis is based on Saru–Chitose data (Mrs. Ito Oda, Biratori); whether
asinuma carries the same dedicated 'original speaker' meaning in other
Hokkaido dialects is unverified ‹speculative›. nakagawa2024 471 notes the
same dialect gap. The best-supported claim is: in southern Hokkaido, when a narrator quotes a
speaker other than herself, the quoted speaker's 1st-person morphology is systematically
replaced by the 4th-person set, and this replacement is not optional ‹corpus-confirmed›.
Whether the replacing forms constitute a grammaticalized logophoric subsystem or are a
deployment of the general 4th person is the contested analytical choice.

Yukar and uwepeker: narration in the protagonist's voice

The narrative first-person convention of yukar (heroic and divine epic) and uwepeker (prose
tale) consists in the reciter voicing the protagonist through the 4th/indefinite person forms
( a= , =an , i= ) rather
than the narrator's own 1st-person forms nakagawa2024 174–179 ;
sato2004 179–184 . The mechanism is structurally parallel to the semi-direct
shift in §3: the protagonist's perspective is indexed by the 4th-person set throughout the
tale, while any stepping-out narrator commentary, where it appears, uses the 1st person.
The oral-literature genres and their registers are described in
oral-literature-genre-taxonomy , and the TAM and evidential patterns by genre in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

The analytical question is whether the convention is a genre-bound pragmatic device or a
grammaticalized logophoric system. Several observations favour a convention-first reading:
(a) the 4th-person forms are identical to those marking indefinite agents in everyday speech;
(b) the distribution skews heavily by genre — the pattern is densest in yukar, lighter in
uwepeker, lighter still in everyday quoted speech nakagawa2024 176–177
‹corpus-suggested›; (c) sato2004 183 derives the narrative use from the
inclusive reading, on which the protagonist is voiced as 'we' (protagonist and audience
together). Against this, bugaeva2008 42–43 notes occurrences of
asinuma as the logophoric form outside oral literature, which would
push toward a grammatical analysis that is not genre-defined ‹contested›. The chapter's
conclusion is that a genre-convention analysis accounts for the distribution but does not
preclude a logophoric analysis of the form: the two claims are compatible if the logophoric
device originated in the narrative genre and extended into everyday quotation ‹speculative›.

The hawe family: report verb, reportative evidential, and hearsay

All three members of the haw voice family derive from the possessed form
haw-e 'the voice of —'. They differ in the predicate that follows the
possessed noun and, correspondingly, in what they assert about the source of information:

form
structure
function
notes

hawean
haw-e-an (voice- poss -exist)
intransitive report verb 'say'
quotative system; follows sekor in DRD/SDRD

hawe ne
haw-e ne (voice- poss cop )
reportative evidential 'it is a word/report that'
clause-final; limited to verbal information nakagawa2024 398

hawe as
haw-e as (voice- poss arise)
hearsay 'there is talk that, one hears that'
ambient or overheard information; corpus count 395 tokens ‹corpus-confirmed›

The reportative evidential hawe ne differs from the quotative frame in
scope: sekor … hawean presents a specific attributed speech event,
while hawe ne predicates the whole proposition as something the
speaker knows through words, without identifying a specific utterer
nakagawa2024 398 . The hearsay form hawe as extends
further: it reports ambient or indirectly received information, the 'sound' of something being
said rather than a specific attributed utterance:

inne topaha i=kesanpa wa arki haw-e as | large herd 4.O=chase CONJ come.PL voice-POSS arise | There arose the sound of a large herd chasing me and coming. | hawe as marks hearsay: the narrator reports a perceived event as ambient sound/speech rather than a specific attributed utterance. The 4th-person object i= references the narrator in the oral-narrative first person (cf. §5). | nakagawa2024:428 | SAR

The naming cleft X sekor a=ye hawe ne 'it is what is called X — so it
is said' layers both systems. The inner sekor a=ye lexicalized frame
embeds the name; the outer hawe ne frames the whole as a
conventionally reported fact:

tap a=ye katu Piratur sekor a=ye haw-e ne | now 4.A=say manner Piratur QUOT 4.A=say voice-POSS COP | The current way of calling it is 'Piratur' — that is what is said. | The sekor a=ye frame attributes the name; the outer hawe ne predicates the whole naming as conventionally/verbally reported fact.
