The 4th-person agent a= is used throughout because the name-givers are indefinite. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 778, after Kawamura 1985: 30

The sekor a=ye p ne naming cleft

A related but structurally distinct naming frame is X sekor a=ye p ne
'it is the thing that is called X'. Here p is the nominalizer 'thing /
the one', and ne is the copula (see
nominalization-p-pe ). The community discussion raises a valency point: in this
frame the nominalizer p is zero-valent — it is not the object of
ye . The transitive verb ye (+2) has its two
valency slots filled by the agent a= (−1) and the implicit 3rd-person
zero-object (−1), and p then nominalizes the whole clause from outside
(nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord , 2024) ‹contested›. If correct, the frame has the
same underlying structure as the general predicate-nominalization cleft described in
nominalization-p-pe , with sekor embedded inside.

Dialect variation: sekor and ari/ani

Sekor is the quotative marker of the southern Hokkaido dialects. Other
Hokkaido varieties use ari or ani in the same
quotative slot. nakagawa2024 471–472 notes the dialect distribution. The
community discussion proposes that ari / ani is
the older pan-Ainu quotative and that sekor is a Saru-region
innovation: an analytical form built from se + kor
that replaced ari / ani as the Saru dialect grew
as the cultural centre (nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord , 2024) ‹contested›. The
directionality of change is not independently datable from the available sources, and the
claim rests on internal evidence (the composition of sekor ) and the
dialect distribution, not comparative-historical reconstruction. The equivalent in Sakhalin
varieties ( nah ), occupying the same functional slot, is cited for
typological contrast only; its morphology and distribution belong to the Sakhalin contrast
chapter.

Honest gaps

Three significant uncertainties bear on the analysis of this chapter. First, the logophoric
vs. 4th-person debate (§4) remains open: the key evidence for a dedicated logophoric
pronoun asinuma comes from Saru–Chitose (Mrs. Ito Oda), and no study
has verified that the same 'self / original speaker' reading is obligatory in other dialects
bugaeva2008 42–43 . Second, the distributional boundary between the
genre-bound narration convention (§5) and a grammaticalized logophoric device is not resolved:
quantified evidence of asinuma in everyday versus oral-literary speech
is not available in the sources consulted ‹speculative›. Third, the non-Saru dialect behaviour of
the quotative system — what role ari / ani plays
in the DRD/SDRD/IRD split, and whether the same transitivity constraint holds — has not been
described at the same level of detail as the Saru–Chitose baseline in
bugaeva2008 and tamura2000 .

title: Macro-Comparison and Deep-Contact Controversies
summary: A critical, method-driven survey of proposed external genetic relationships and deep-contact scenarios for Ainu, framed by borrowing-scale and areal typology.
part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Macro-Comparison and Deep-Contact Controversies
Part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
Summary: A critical, method-driven survey of proposed external genetic relationships and deep-contact scenarios for Ainu, framed by borrowing-scale and areal typology.

The isolate baseline

Ainu has no confirmed genetic relative. Every proposal to link it to another
language family has been examined and set aside; Janhunen's verdict — that Ainu
"does not show unambiguous indications of being associated with any other language
family" — expresses the current consensus ‹consensus›
janhunen2022 §2 ; alonso2022 §1 ; vovin1993 .
Within its own lineage Ainu constitutes a small family — sometimes called Ainuic
or Kurilic — of three closely related varieties: Hokkaido, Sakhalin, and the
Northern Kuriles ( sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast ). The question
addressed here is not the isolate status itself, which is settled, but two
connected and genuinely open questions: the nature and direction of contact between
Ainu and neighbouring languages, especially Japonic; and whether any proposed
deep-time comparison or areal alignment survives methodological scrutiny. The
isolate status and its evidential basis are set out in
genetic-position-macro-comparison ; the ethnolinguistic context
in ainu-people-homeland-history .

For an isolate the comparative method is unavailable: there is no sister language
against which to project correspondences back into a proto-system. Three analytic
tools remain — internal reconstruction, contact-induced-change analysis, and
typology — with the important qualification that typological reasoning carries
no probatory weight for genetic questions and must be used "with utmost caution"
alonso2022 §1 . The methodologically recoverable depth is also
shallow: internal reconstruction reaches approximately 1,000 years without
unsupported extrapolation, terminating at a Common Ainu stage no earlier than the
thirteenth-century CE branching that separated the Sakhalin varieties from
Hokkaido alonso2022 §1, §4 . The reconstruction of Proto-Ainu and
Common Ainu is treated in proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction and
internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways .

The macro-comparison catalogue

The table below surveys the external comparisons that have been proposed for Ainu
and records their current status. Judgements follow Janhunen
janhunen2022 §2–3c , Alonso de la Fuente
alonso2022 §1 , and the three published reviews of Vovin (1993):
Sidwell (1996), Abondolo (1997), and Witczak (1999), all cited second-hand
via Alonso alonso2022 §1 .

proposal
main advocates
status
key objection

Altaic and Ural-Altaic
various, 19th–20th c.
‹consensus› rejected
Altaic itself lacks the consensus status of a genetic unit; shared
features are contact-induced or typological

Austroasiatic
Vovin (1993), as background assumption in RPA
‹consensus› rejected; vitiated Vovin's own reconstruction
All three reviewers flag that the Austroasiatic scaffolding
distorts the consonant tables in RPA (Sidwell 1996: 179, 183;
Abondolo 1997: 190; Witczak 1999: 221–223, cited via Alonso 2022 §1)

Japonic (genetic relationship)
Chamberlain (1887) and others
‹consensus› rejected as genetic; shared features reanalysed
as contact
No regular sound-correspondences across core vocabulary;
resemblances attributed to Jōmon substrate effects on
Japonic — "Jōmonization of Japanese" — rather than common
descent (Janhunen 2022 §3c)

Austronesian (contact/typological parallel)
Vovin (1993), heuristically in RPA
‹speculative› typological heuristic only; no genetic claim
Cited as a typological parallel for specific sound changes
(Alonso 2022 §2.2.3); Alonso makes no kinship claim

Nivkh / Amuric ("Paleo-Asiatic")
various; geographic grouping
‹consensus› contact, not genetic; "Paleosiberian" is a
geographic wastebasket
Lexical interaction is documented (Shiraishi & Tangiku
2022), but the languages remain typologically distinct;
see northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu

Korean (lexical comparisons)
various; community proposals
‹contested›–‹speculative›; no systematic correspondences
Individual resemblances fall within the range of chance
similarity; see §6 below

A structural point underlies the table. Proposing a genetic relationship for an
isolate places the entire burden of proof on the proposer, because the null
hypothesis — independent origin — accounts for all of the data without additional
apparatus. Lexical resemblances between any two languages occur by chance at a
predictable rate; only systematic sound-to-sound correspondences across a
significant portion of core vocabulary, independent of areal diffusion, constitutes
evidence for common descent alonso2022 §1 . No proposal for Ainu
has satisfied this standard.

Old Japanese loanwords into Ainu

Contact between Ainu and Japonic is the best-documented diachronic layer in
Ainu's external history, and the directionality is not in dispute for the early
stratum. A set of Ainu items preserve phonological features of Old Japanese or
Pre-Old-Japanese that had disappeared from Japanese before the Nara period,
placing the borrowing events "around the middle of the first millennium CE"
janhunen2022 §3c . The diagnostics are the retention of a labial
stop where later Japanese shows a fricative (Pre-OJ *p- → later Japanese
/h-/ ), and the preservation of sequences that subsequently monophthongized.
The regularly cited items are:

sippo salt — preserves Pre-OJ *siopo before the labial
lenition that yields Modern Japanese 塩 shio
janhunen2022 §3c .

pasuy prayer stick, ritual chopstick — compared with OJ
hashi 'chopstick'; Ainu retains the original stop p- lost
in Japanese janhunen2022 §3c .

kamuy deity, spirit — an early cultural borrowing whose
deep distribution across all Ainu lects confirms a pre-branching
age janhunen2022 §3c .

These items are morphologically opaque in Ainu — no segment maps onto a
recoverable Ainu morpheme — whereas inherited vocabulary often remains
internally analyzable. Compare cep fish , reconstructed as
Pre-PA *ti=(ʔ)e pe 'the thing eaten by everyone'
(< fourth-person prefix ci= + e eat +
nominalizer -p ), whose internal structure remains
transparent after a millennium of phonological change
alonso2022 §4, Table 18 .
( internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways .)
Opacity versus transparency of this kind is a standard heuristic for separating
loans from inherited vocabulary.

sippo ku=kor | salt 1SG.A=have | I have salt. | The form sippo 'salt' has no recoverable Ainu morpheme segmentation — diagnostic of a loanword. Contrast the fully transparent cep 'fish' ( cikenki (Saru;
< Standard Japanese 付け木 tsukegi 'matches'),
antuki (Saru; < 小豆 azuki
'azuki bean', with prenasalization from Tōhoku Japanese)
alonso2022 §1 . The two strata — pre-Nara and post-Nara — are
therefore separable through phonological shape alone.

The Eurasian Wanderwort kotan settlement, village — attested
throughout Hokkaido and appearing at 4,210 corpus tokens — sits outside this
Japanese-specific stratum. Janhunen (2022 §5) classifies it as a continental
Wanderwort; whether it entered Ainu via Japanese, via Tungusic, or through an
older continental network is unresolved ‹speculative›
janhunen2022 §5 ; the community has raised the same question
without resolving it aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight,
aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-15, speculative). The word
sisam Japanese person is, by contrast, an Ainu coinage:
reconstructible as Pre-PA *sii+sam/o with native
Ainu morphology, surviving in the Yakumo dialect as
samó janhunen2022 §4 .

The reverse direction: Ainu elements in early Japonic

The claim that Ainu was once spoken more broadly across the Japanese archipelago
and left loanwords in Old Japanese is the most contested diachronic proposal
currently appearing in peer-reviewed Ainu scholarship. Vovin (2022) argues that
Ainu extended through Kantō and Tōhoku before the Yayoi expansion, and that the
Man'yōshū — particularly East Old Japanese (EOJ) in books 14 and 20 and West Old
Japanese (WOJ) in books 17 and 19 — contains loans from this substrate ‹contested›
vovin2022 §§1–6 .

Among the specific etymologies Vovin proposes:

EOJ kuta 'house' ← Ainu kotan settlement
(village→house shift parallel to Latin vicus/villa )
vovin2022 §3 .

WOJ porö 'big' ← Ainu poro big, large
vovin2022 §4 .

OJ iruka 'dolphin' ← Ainu rika
'whale-fat' vovin2022 §2 .

OJ kanimba 'cherry' ← Ainu
karinpa 'cherry'
vovin2022 §5 .

Place names in Man'yōshū: Kanipa ← Ainu
ka-ne-pa 'upper bank';
Ikuri ← Ainu ikure
vovin2022 §3 .

The loan-phonology proposed is systematic: Ainu geminate clusters simplify
across the borrowing boundary (Ainu -kk- → OJ
-k- ); Ainu word-final consonants are lost in EOJ surface forms; and the
Proto-Japonic vowel shifts *o > u and *e > i in EOJ
environments are said to reflect the Ainu source shapes
vovin2022 §2 .

The argument has not been independently corroborated in either Old Japanese
philology or Ainu linguistics. The structural problem is that short monosyllabic
resemblances — like WOJ porö ~ Ainu poro — fall within the
range expected by chance between unrelated languages, and the required semantic
shifts must be stipulated rather than derived from cross-linguistically attested
patterns. Janhunen (2022) characterises the shared features under the more general
rubric of "Jōmonization of Japanese" — Ainu-type languages influencing Japonic
typologically — without endorsing individual lexical etymologies of this kind
janhunen2022 §3c . The substrate hypothesis and the
lexical-etymology program address different phenomena and should be kept
analytically separate: the former has structural support; the latter remains
unconfirmed ‹contested›.

The Jōmon-substrate thesis and Ainu ethnogenesis

Independent of the lexical-etymology debate, Janhunen (2022) develops a substrate
account of Japonic that is better evidenced and more broadly accepted. The Japonic
family arrived on the archipelago with the Yayoi agricultural expansion from the
Korean Peninsula, roughly between 900 BCE and 500 CE, and displaced the earlier
"Jōmonic" linguistic communities whose long prior presence shaped Japonic phonology,
morphology, and vocabulary in ways that remain incompletely disentangled ‹contested›
janhunen2022 §3a–3c . On this account, Ainu is the sole surviving
Jōmon language — its survival "an accident" in the northeast while the Japonic
expansion replaced the other Jōmon languages of the archipelago
janhunen2022 §3a .

The ethnolinguistic implication is that the Ainu homeland was not Hokkaido but
Honshu. The present Hokkaido distribution results from northward movement: the
Satsumon culture (c. 700–1200 CE) carried the Ainu linguistic ancestors northward,
reaching northern Hokkaido around 1000 CE, southern Sakhalin around 1300 CE, and
the Kuriles around 1500–1600 CE janhunen2022 §3a–3b . The
Emishi ( 蝦夷 ) of northern Honshu are most
plausibly treated as a proto-historical complex partly ancestral to the Ainu
janhunen2022 §3a . The Okhotsk culture (c. 600–1600 CE), whose
language Janhunen associates with early Ghilyak/Amuric, was absorbed by the
Satsumon population in northern Hokkaido and Sakhalin, contributing a borrowing
residue treated in northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu .

Community discussion connects the OJ ethnonym emi₁si with the
Sakhalin Ainu ethnonym enciw . Antitwilight (aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-12-23) proposes that OJ emi₁si borrows from a
pre-Proto-Ainu form *æmi-ti , with the segmentation
*ti = first-person plural, regularly giving
> enciw aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.
This partially converges with Janhunen's (2022 §4) note that
enciw ~ Emishi/Ebisu/Ezo
*emisV~*emiciV (citing Kindaichi 1960), though the
specific morpheme segmentation proposed by the community goes beyond the
published source and cannot be verified against it.

The word tunakay reindeer — which entered Japanese as
tonakai — is a probable loan from a pre-Proto-Ghilyak source
into Ainu, illustrating contact at the Okhotsk cultural interface ‹contested›
janhunen2022 §3c .

Toponymic substrate on Honshu

A Honshu Ainu homeland should have left a toponymic residue; place-name
substrates are well attested where a speech community has been displaced but not
entirely erased. Two bodies of candidate evidence circulate in the literature:
analysis of Tōhoku place names with elements cognate with productive Ainu
geographical vocabulary, and Vovin's (2022 §3) identifications of Ainu-origin
proper names in the Man'yōshū (§4 above).

Ainu geographical vocabulary is morphologically productive and well-documented
in Hokkaido: nay stream, small river , pet river ,
nupuri mountain , wakka fresh water , and
or place, location appear in hundreds of Hokkaido place names.
The community has noted specific Tōhoku cases: nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord,
2026-01-22, proposed) analyses the Iwate surname and toponym
乙茂内 ( Otomonai ) as Ainu
o-tum-nay 'head-middle-stream', citing the concentration
of 内 ( -nai ) toponyms in the surrounding area as
convergent evidence aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.
The morphological segmentation is plausible against known Ainu geographical
morphology, but confirmation requires independent phonological correspondence
evidence that is not yet available.

A systematic survey of Tōhoku toponymy against the Ainu geographical lexicon
remains a research gap. The existing evidence — from the matagi hunter-gatherer
vocabulary of Tōhoku, from the Emishi cultural zone extending considerably south
of modern Hokkaido, and from the individual proposals above — is suggestive but
not yet probative ‹speculative›.

Separating contact, inheritance, and chance

The central methodological problem for all the proposals above is the triage
between three origins for any given resemblance: inheritance from a common
ancestor; borrowing through contact; and chance. For an isolate, the inheritance
route is ruled out by definition for resemblances with established families.
The issue is therefore always whether a similarity is contact-induced or
accidental.

The standard diagnostics applied in the Ainu contact literature
shiraishitangiku2022 §3 are:

Direction of spread: does the item appear only in lects
geographically adjacent to the supposed source, or is it pan-Ainu? A
pan-Ainu distribution implies a pre-branching (Common Ainu or Proto-Ainu)
age, excluding recent contact.

Morphological analyzability: inherited vocabulary tends to
be internally structured; loans tend to be opaque. sippo salt
carries no recoverable Ainu morpheme; cep fish
(< ci=e-p ) is fully transparent — see
internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways .

Loan phonology: for a directed borrowing, the donor-language
form and the recipient-language form must be connected by phonologically
plausible, preferably recurrent, processes. Ad hoc adjustments stipulated
for a single item weaken the case considerably.

Systematic correspondence: a genetic or deep-contact
relationship requires recurrent sound-to-sound correspondences across a
significant portion of core vocabulary, not sporadic resemblances.

By these criteria the early Japanese→Ainu stratum (§3) meets most diagnostics
and is well established ‹consensus›. Vovin's reverse-direction program (§4)
proposes systematic loan-phonology but produces thin correspondence sets and
semantically variable links, and has not been replicated ‹contested›.
The Jōmon-substrate thesis (§5) operates at a depth that forecloses direct
lexical testing; it is evaluated primarily through population history and
typological alignment and is best treated as a working hypothesis rather than
a demonstrated result ‹contested›.

Two community discussions illustrate the methodological divide. The proposal that
Ainu si= (reflexive prefix, treated in
person-marking-architecture ) is cognate with Korean and Old Japanese
reflexive pronouns — aomidori_cs_, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-08
aynucorporadiscord — invokes forms short enough to match by chance
and a semantic field (self-reference) common enough to arise independently;
without a systematic correspondence framework the proposal cannot advance beyond
conjecture ‹speculative›. The set of proposed Korean-origin vocabulary items
( casi , notak ,
mose , nay ,
toy — antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-15,
proposed aynucorporadiscord ) faces the same difficulty:
casi 'fortification' in particular has a plausible
Ainu-internal structure and is not compelled to be a contact loan ‹contested›.
Both sets are the kind of etymologies that Alonso (2022 §1) and Janhunen
(2022 §2) explicitly bracket as undemonstrable under the current methodological
standard for Ainu.

The Amur / North Pacific areal layer

At the typological level Ainu shares features with North Pacific Rim languages —
polysynthetic morphology, head-marking, verb-final word order, extensive
nominalization-based clause linkage — that place it loosely within a broad
Northeast Asian typological zone, though Janhunen (2022 §3c) notes that Ainu
"stands apart" even within that grouping. The typological characterisation is
descriptive, not a genetic or even a firm areal claim.

The contact-driven Amur areal layer has a real lexical residue, but its
Hokkaido signature is thin. The primary northern-contact zone was Sakhalin,
where Ainu, Nivkh, Uilta, and Manchu (via the Santan trade) met; the lexical
traffic is documented in northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu .
The items that reached Hokkaido Ainu directly — kaya sail
(< Nivkh, with the 'fish-skin garment' sense retained in Sakhalin but reduced to
'sail' in Hokkaido), sintoko cask (morphologically opaque; possible
ultimate Eurasian Wanderwort), san offering shelf
(< Nivkh cʰaŋ , Uilta saan ) — are documented borrowings,
not indicators of genetic relationship
shiraishitangiku2022 §3 .
Whether the Sakhalin contact situation constituted a true Sprachbund
is left open by the most recent survey shiraishitangiku2022 §1 .

Alonso de la Fuente (2022 §2.4) observes that the reconstructed Proto-Ainu
consonant inventory looks typologically like a mixed Southwest/Northeast Eurasian
language that may have acquired some Southeast Asian-type features through contact:
the merger of *l and *ɾ , the
single fricative /s/ , and the bare stop series
/p t k/ are consistent with this areal profile. The
observation is typological inference, not a contact-language argument, and it
post-dates the genetic-affiliation question addressed in this chapter. The
inventory is documented in consonant-inventory and reconstructed in
proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction ‹speculative›
alonso2022 §2.4 .

title: Manner and Degree Adverbial Clauses
summary: Manner clauses, the clause-linking -no adverbializer, and degree/extent clauses (pakno 'to the extent that'), framed against Ainu comparison strategies.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Manner and Degree Adverbial Clauses
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: Manner clauses, the clause-linking -no adverbializer, and degree/extent clauses (pakno 'to the extent that'), framed against Ainu comparison strategies.

Scope of this chapter

The conjunctive-particle inventory of Hokkaido Ainu (surveyed in
clause-linkage-overview ) contains three sets of items whose semantics cluster
around manner, state, and degree rather than temporal sequence, conditionality, or causation. This
chapter treats all three: the conjunctive particle no (state/manner linker) in
its stative and negated-manner uses; the group ⑦ simile and evidential-inference particles
pekor as if (counterfactual) , apekor as if (strong) ,
noyne apparently (inference) , and
kotom it seems (literary) ; and degree and extent clauses built around the
postposition pakno up to, to the extent of . The chapter is bounded on two
sides: the manner-adverb-deriving suffix -no that attaches to stative
verb roots to form adverbs such as
pirkano well, in a good way belongs to the word-formation domain and is treated
in adverbializer-no ; the conditional and concessive particles, though partially
manner-like in their semantics, are documented in
conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses .

The no particle: stative and negated-manner clauses

The conjunctive particle no occupies the same post-predicate slot as
wa and hine (see
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining ), but its distribution is conditioned by the
aspectual and polar character of the clause it closes rather than by temporal semantics. Nakagawa
states the conditioning directly: no appears when the preceding
predicate is either stative, expressing a persistent condition, or an action verb under negation
nakagawa2024 340 . Satō identifies the high-frequency pattern as the 'without
doing X' reading after negation: in somo … no … constructions the choice
of no over wa or
kor is near-obligatory sato2008 45–46 . Refsing reports
the same distributional pattern for Shizunai, where no on a stative
predicate yields an adverbial-adjunct reading 'in the state of X' refsing1986 241 .

a=onaha somo mokor no kamuyoritak kor an | 4.POSS=father NEG sleep CONJ pray CONJ exist | My father, without sleeping, was praying to the gods. | no follows the negated predicate somo mokor 'does not sleep'; the main-clause progressive kamuyoritak kor an 'was praying' is expressed with the simultaneity particle kor plus the existential an. | nakagawa2024:340 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 614

somo ipe no arpa=an | NEG eat CONJ go=4.S | Without eating, she went. | The canonical somo V no construction: no is the required linker after a negated action predicate. Compare the unpreferred *somo ipe wa, which is disfavoured precisely because the preceding predicate is negated.

The particle no is segmentally identical to the manner-adverb suffix
-no that derives adverbs from stative verb roots (see
adverbializer-no ), and the two overlap where the host is a stative property verb.
The string pirka no arpa 'went well' admits either the particle analysis
— a stative-predicate clause [pirka] followed by the connective — or the
suffix analysis, where pirkano 'well' is a derived adverb modifying the
motion verb. Nakagawa notes that the particle analysis is more transparent for productive
negation patterns such as k=eyam no 'without hesitating', but that
frozen adverbs such as poronno 'a lot' — from
poron be big, be much , which does not mean 'big-ly' in compositional terms —
resist it; he concludes that a sharp line cannot be drawn between the particle and the suffix
nakagawa2024 340–341 . Ijäs presents no for the learner
alongside the claim that it tends to mark a same-subject context while
wa marks a different-subject one; that framing is Ijäs's own and is not
supported by the primary grammars, which derive the distribution from polarity and aspect
‹contested› ijas2023 . That debate is adjudicated in
clause-linkage-overview the overview chapter .

Simile and evidential inference: pekor, apekor, noyne, and kotom

Nakagawa nakagawa2024 353–355 places a cluster of four particles in group ⑦
of his semantic taxonomy, labelled 推量・様態 'conjecture/manner'. All four follow a finite
predicate in the standard post-predicate position, but they encode the speaker's epistemic stance
toward the preceding clause — either as a comparison against something the speaker knows to be
false (simile) or as an inference drawn from available evidence. The distinction matters for
translation and for analysis: pekor likens the main-clause state of
affairs to a counterfactual standard, while noyne and
kotom present a genuine inference the speaker holds about what is
actually the case sato2008 55–56 . These particles overlap with the broader
evidential system; their relation to the nominalizing evidentials is taken up in
evidential-schema-overview and ruwe-ne-inferential .

form
dialect distribution
semantics
register

pekor / pokor /
pokon

pan-HA
counterfactual simile 'as if [impossibly]'
neutral

apekor / apokon

pan-HA (Tokachi/Yakumo: apokon )
strong impossibility simile 'as if utterly, as if absolutely'
narrative, often epic hyperbole

noyne
Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, Shizunai
evidential inference 'apparently, looks as if' (from real evidence)
everyday speech

kotom(no)
pan-HA
evidential inference 'it seems, apparently' (from real evidence)
literary / 雅語

Sources: nakagawa2024 353–355 ; sato2008 55–56 .

The pekor cluster. Pekor and its dialectal allomorphs
pokor and pokon liken the main-clause situation
to the preceding clause while presupposing that the preceding clause is false or
counter-to-fact from the speaker's viewpoint nakagawa2024 354 . The intensified
apekor (allomorphs apokon ) strengthens this to
utter impossibility: Nakagawa's illustration is a figure in narrative so large that two or three
men stacked on one another would match the comparison, a physically impossible standard nakagawa2024 354 . A segmentally related postpositional pair —
nepkor / nepkon 'like (a noun)' — takes a
nominal host and is distinct from the clause-final particle both in its host category and in
its position nakagawa2024 354 .

nep ka e=nukar pekor e=yki | something EMPH 2SG.A=see CONJ 2SG.S=act | You are acting as if you can see something. | pekor follows the finite clause nep ka e=nukar 'you see something' and connects to the main clause e=yki 'you behave/act'. The presupposition is that nothing is actually visible — the comparison is counterfactual. Satō (2008: 56) uses this example to contrast pekor with noyne: noyne would instead report a genuine inference from evidence. | sato2008:56 | SAR

Noyne and kotom. Where pekor introduces a counterfactual
standard, noyne and kotom present what the
speaker genuinely infers. Noyne is the everyday-speech variant, attested
in Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, and Shizunai; kotom(no) carries a literary
register and is commonly found with judgment verbs such as
a=esanniyo 'I judged/surmised' nakagawa2024 354–355 .
Recurring collocation frames for noyne include
noyne ramu / yaynu 'think that',
noyne humas 'feel as if', and
noyne siran 'look as if' nakagawa2024 355 .

tane tamanko kor noyne okokokse hawe as | now egg have INFR cluck voice-POSS exist.PL | Apparently having just laid an egg, it is clucking kok-kok. | The subject (a hen) is implicit. kor is the transitive verb 'have'; noyne is the inferential clause-linker that marks the entire first clause [tane tamanko kor] 'now having an egg' as inferred rather than directly witnessed. okokokse is onomatopoeic 'kok-kok' used as a sound verb; hawe as 'there is the voice/sound of' closes the main clause. | nakagawa2024:355 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 687

Both noyne and kotom sit at the boundary of the
evidential system in evidential-schema-overview . Their collocational frames —
noyne siran 'look as if', noyne humas 'feel as
if' — parallel the situational evidential siri ne (visible scenes) and
the non-visual humi ne (sensations), but the two systems differ
structurally: noyne and kotom are conjunctive
particles and do not take the copula ne ‹consensus›.

Refsing's Shizunai grammar classifies pokon 'pretence' and
kotom 'seeming' together as "nominalizers of appearance" in her §14.12
conjunctionalization taxonomy, and lists alongside them a "comparative" formal noun
koraci refsing1986 259 . These subclass labels are
specific to Refsing's Shizunai analysis and are not foregrounded in Saru or Chitose
descriptions; the extent to which koraci is productive in other Hokkaido
dialects is not established in the accessible sources ‹speculative›.

Degree and extent: pakno and comparison frames

The postposition pakno up to, as far as, to the extent of heads a range of
extent and degree constructions in Hokkaido Ainu. Its core use is spatial or temporal — 'up to
the house', 'until now' — but the same form extends to degree: 'to the extent/degree of X'.
Nakagawa lists pakno in the postposition inventory without assigning it
a special comparative status; the degree reading follows from the general extent semantics
applied to a property or state rather than a location or time point
nakagawa2024 504 .

In the clause-linkage domain the most grammatically productive role of
pakno is in comparison frames. Bugaeva documents a prenominal
relative-clause strategy in which pakno marks the standard against
which a property of the head noun is measured. Because direct relativization of a standard of
comparison — the head noun filling a gap in a comparative clause — is not attested, Ainu
instead embeds a degree-adjunct clause prenominally: the construction '[to whose extent no rich
man exists] rich man' places the comparison standard inside the relative clause as a degree
adjunct rather than gapping the standard as an argument bugaeva2015 45, 49 .

a=ona pakno nispa isam nispa ne | 4.POSS=father equal rich.man not.exist rich.man COP | My father was a rich man with no equal. | pakno marks the degree of the father's wealth as the comparison standard. The prenominal clause [a=ona pakno nispa isam] 'to whose extent/degree no rich man exists' modifies the head nispa 'rich man'. The strategy amounts to a comparative without a dedicated comparative particle: no other rich man exists to the extent/degree of my father. See the full relativization analysis in the prenominal chapter. | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

The full relativization mechanism behind this construction is treated in
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses . Bugaeva identifies the type as an "equivalent of
the standard-of-comparison relativization" — a degree-adjunct workaround rather than a
grammaticalized comparative morpheme — and leaves open whether it constitutes a productive
strategy or a pragmatic repair ‹corpus-suggested› bugaeva2015 45 .

A further degree/comparison use surfaces in Satō's description of
kane . Alongside its core function as a simultaneity particle in
non-Saru/Chitose dialects (see simultaneous-kor-clauses ), Satō records a reading
glossed '〜ほどに' 'to the degree/extent that' — a degree-comparison value of the same form sato2008 54–55 . The extent to which this degree use is distinct from the simultaneity value, and how it
distributes across dialects, is not settled in the available sources ‹speculative›.

title: Middle, Anticausative, and the (Non-)Passive Question
summary: Middle/spontaneous and anticausative uses of si-/yay- and detransitives, and whether the a=/an= indefinite-agent construction constitutes a passive.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Middle, Anticausative, and the (Non-)Passive Question
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: Middle/spontaneous and anticausative uses of si-/yay- and detransitives, and whether the a=/an= indefinite-agent construction constitutes a passive.

The ci- middle prefix

Among the arity-decreasing voice prefixes, ci- middle prefix (object-resultative)
presents an event from the affected side and yields an intransitive verb whose sole argument is the
entity undergoing the action nakagawa2024 190–191 . Nakagawa labels the output
中動態 (middle voice) and gives the underlying semantics as "expressing,
from the affected side, an action one thing performs on another" nakagawa2024 190 .
The Handbook glosses the same prefix object-resultative ( RES ), emphasising
the affected argument's endpoint state bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 . Both labels
address the same morphosyntactic fact: the base transitive loses its agent position, arity drops
by one, and the base object surfaces as the intransitive subject. The terminological difference
between "middle" and "object-resultative" is not analytical ‹contested›.

The prefix reduces arity by exactly one. cari scatter something (transitive, +2)
gives ci-cari 'be scattered' (intransitive, +1);
sina tie something (transitive) gives ci-sina 'be tied'.
The prefixed form takes suffixal S-argument indexes (fourth-person =an ,
first-plural exclusive =as ) in place of the A-argument prefixes of the
transitive base — the standard transitivity diagnostic of valency-voice-overview .
The following table gives a selection of attested pairs.

base (vt)
gloss
ci- form (vi)
gloss
source

cari
scatter something
ci-cari
be scattered, lie scattered
nakagawa2024 190

sina
tie something
ci-sina
be tied
nakagawa2024 190

koyki
hunt, catch something
ci-koyki
be caught (→ cikoykip 'game animal')
nakagawa2024 191 ; kayano1996

car
scatter something
ci-car-i
be scattered (possessive-form variant)
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4

aynu kam ka upas ka ta ci-cari kamuyasi kam ka ci-cari kor | human flesh even snow even LOC RES-scatter evil.spirit flesh even RES-scatter while | Human flesh and snow lay scattered on the ground; the evil spirit's flesh too lay scattered, and … | Third-person subjects are morphologically zero; the intransitive form ci-cari takes no object. Both clauses show the affected entity (flesh, snow) as sole argument — the defining property of the ci- middle. | nakagawa2024:189 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 167

kem-ihi ci-car-i uske-he | blood-POSS RES-scatter-POSS place-POSS | the place where blood lay scattered | The possessed-form variant ci-car-i in a nominalised relative clause; ci-cari and ci-car-i are morphological doublets, the latter appearing in nominalised or attributive positions. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.4 | SAR | Handbook ex. 28, after Tamura 1996: 48

Typologically, middle voice marks spontaneous, patient-initiated, or affectee-oriented events
across many of the world's languages — the semantic space where a passive would otherwise be
expected but no agent is expressed shibatani1990 41–48 . In Hokkaido Ainu,
ci- occupies that niche: the source event is agentive (someone scatters,
someone ties), but the prefix profiles its outcome from the affected participant's perspective,
suppressing the agent position entirely.

Person restriction and the ci= / ci- homophony

The middle prefix ci- is restricted to third-person subjects. The form
*ku=ci-montapire ('I was informed') is ungrammatical; a first-person
affected participant instead requires the indefinite-agent construction with objective
cross-referencing: a=en=montapire 'the indefinite agent informed me as
object' nakagawa2024 190 . This restriction distinguishes
ci- sharply from the reflexive
yay- reflexive (direct) and the antipassive
i- antipassive , which are available across persons.

The prefix is homophonous with the first-person plural exclusive A-index
ci= , and both reduce arity by one — the index by filling the subject slot
with a specific 1PL.EXCL argument, the middle by suppressing the agent entirely. Nakagawa treats
them as "probably related but synchronically distinct" and notes a shared phonological behaviour:
before a , e , o the
i drops, yielding surface ca- ,
ce- , co- ( ci-opirasa →
copirasa ) nakagawa2024 190–191 . Disambiguation in context
relies on person morphology and arity: a middle verb takes suffixal S-indexes, while a first-plural
A-prefix takes the O-indexes typical of transitives.

Several ci- forms have lexicalized as uninflected bases. The noun
cikoykip game animal, prey — 'thing that is hunted' — freezes the middle of
koyki hunt, catch something , no longer perceived as a productive derivational
composite by speakers nakagawa2024 191 . Lexicalized middles of this type document
the prefix's historical productivity; the set of active, synchronically transparent pairs is smaller.

Community analysis: ci- as a productive middle

Discussion in the aynu-corpora Discord has proposed that ci- functions as
a systematically productive middle/anticausative across a wider set of lexemes than the published
literature has catalogued (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023–2024)
aynucorporadiscord . The proposal analyses several opaque-looking forms as transparent
middle derivations: copirasa 'spread out, widen (intr)' as
ci=opirasa (from transitive
opirasa spread something out );
ceosmare 'dash forward, plunge in' as
ci=e-osma-re (from an applicative-causative 'cause to thrust into'); and
cikarkarpe as ci=kar-kar-pe 'things being made'
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›.

The same line of analysis was applied to ci-koykarkar . On this reading the
ci- in hattoho ci-koykarkar is the middle prefix,
yielding a passive-like meaning 'restrictions are imposed on [us]', rather than the first-plural
A-index ci= , which would give 'we impose restrictions together'
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. The
disambiguation requires pragmatic and discourse context; surface form alone does not decide it.

These community analyses are leads rather than established findings: they extend the attested middle
use productively to forms the peer-reviewed descriptive literature has not formally analysed under
this label. The core claim — that ci- is a productive middle prefix —
is supported by Nakagawa and the Handbook; the community contribution is the extension to a broader
set of lexemes and the identification of middle ci- in forms previously
treated as first-plural-exclusive or as unanalysed compounds. A further diachronic hypothesis that
ci- derives from an earlier passive-like *ti ,
distinct in origin from the person index, was proposed but disputed on phonological grounds —
the affricate [t͡s] realised as
c also appears in non-palatalizing environments, casting doubt on a
straightforward palatalization account (nukopoli and aomidori, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023)
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.

Anticausative semantics and the si- overlap

The reflexive prefix si- reflexive (indirect) covers a semantic domain that
overlaps partially with the middle. Under Satō's analysis, adopted by Nakagawa and the Handbook,
si- marks the subject's indirect involvement — through another agent,
through circumstance, or through a force outside the subject's direct control sato2007 31–39 ; nakagawa2024 188–189 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . The
minimal pair yay-pusu 'float up volitionally (by swimming)' against
si-pusu 'float up of itself' illustrates the anticausative semantics of
si- : the event is profiled as spontaneous, with an affected participant
rising without an expressed agent sato2007 31–32 . Similarly, the transitive
turi lay something down, stretch something yields si-turi
'stretch, lie down (of itself)', which Tamura analyses as a pure intransitivizing derivation
tamura1996 622 . Kobayashi groups such pairs under "anticausativization" — a
transitivity alternation in which the causer is suppressed and the event presented as spontaneous
kobayashi2015 33 .

The morphological and person-marking properties of ci- and
si- remain distinct despite the semantic overlap.
ci- suppresses the agent position entirely and is restricted to
third-person contexts; si- fills the object slot with a self-participant
coreferent with the clause subject and is available across all persons. Spontaneous or
agent-backgrounded events with a first- or second-person participant therefore surface with
si- , not ci- . The full treatment of the
yay- / si- opposition belongs to
reflexive-yay and reflexive-possessive-si ; the present section records
only the semantic intersection with the anticausative domain.

Bugaeva's diachronic study of anticausatives in Ainu traces the historical pathways by which
transitive and causative verbs acquire intransitive partners via ci- ,
si- , and related morphology, setting them within the cross-linguistic
typology of inchoative-causative alternations bugaeva2025 . The inchoative-causative
interface — where a stem such as rewke become soft /
rewe soften something pairs a spontaneous intransitive with a transitive — is
treated in transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs ; the voice interpretation of
ci- and si- in such pairs is among the questions
that diachronic account addresses ‹corpus-suggested›.

Body-region prefixes he- and ho-

Two lexical prefixes of noun origin, he- 'head, tip' and
ho- 'base, bottom', also reduce arity by one by filling the object slot
with the subject's own body-region nakagawa2024 186–187 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 . puni lift something (transitive)
yields he-puni 'raise one's head' (intransitive) and
ho-puni 'get up, fly up' (intransitive);
kiru turn something yields he-kiru 'turn around';
tuye cut something yields ho-tuye 'shout'
(semantically opaque) nakagawa2024 186–187 .

These prefixes are the arity-decreasing (−1) counterparts of the homophonous affiliative
e- 'head of' and o- 'base of', which are
arity-neutral (0) and fill a slot while requiring their own possessor. The minimal pair makes
the arity contrast concrete: he-puni is intransitive and takes S-indexes,
while affiliative e-puni 'raise the head/end of something (belonging to
someone)' remains transitive nakagawa2024 192–193 .

he-puni=an kor somo a=nu wa kusu | head-rise=4.S while NEG 4.A=hear CONJ because | When [I] raised my head, [the sound] could no longer be heard. | The intransitive suffix =an on he-puni confirms that the body-region prefix he- has reduced the arity of puni 'lift' by one; the head-referent fills the object slot. The co-occurring a=nu is the indefinite-agent construction treated below. | nakagawa2024:192 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 171, partial

a=unuhu a=e-puni ruwe ne akusu | 4.A=mother.POSS 4.A=head.of-rise track-POSS COP when | [I] lifted my mother's head, and then — | The affiliative e- 'head of' (arity 0, not the applicative e-) keeps puni transitive; the object argument a=unuhu 'mother' is the entity whose head is being raised. Contrast the intransitive he-puni above, where the subject's own head is raised. | nakagawa2024:192 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 172, partial

Bugaeva and Kobayashi group he- and ho- together
with the ci- middle under the object-resultative heading, noting that they
"function similarly to O-incorporation" — a body-region semantics fills the object slot and makes
the verb intransitive bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 . Many surface forms are
semantically opaque: ho-tuye 'shout' transparently contains
tuye 'cut' with ho- 'base', yet the compositional
meaning is not recoverable nakagawa2024 187 .

The indefinite-agent construction: a= as a functional passive

When a transitive event is presented with an unspecified or backgrounded agent, Hokkaido Ainu uses
the fourth/indefinite person prefix a= fourth-person / indefinite agent — or the
dialectal variant an= in Ishikari and some other varieties — as the agent
argument of the transitive verb. The patient stays in object position; no promotion to subject
occurs. The construction is valency-neutral: the verb retains its transitive argument frame, with
the indefinite agent filling the A slot and the patient cross-referenced as O
nakagawa2024 428–429 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §1 .

seta or wa aynu a=kupapa | dog place from person 4.A=bite | A person was bitten by a dog. | The dog appears in the or wa 'from' demoted-agent phrase; the indefinite transitive prefix a= occupies the A slot; the patient aynu 'person' remains the grammatical object. | nakagawa2024:428 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 945″

nikekke wenkur sekor aynu or wa a=reko kor an yakka | wood-break poor.person QUOT people place from 4.A=call while exist.SG even.though | Even though [he] was called 'the wood-breaking pauper' by people — | a=reko 'the indefinite agent calls'; aynu or wa provides the demoted agent. The object (the called person) is not cross-referenced here because it is zero-marked third person. | nakagawa2024:429 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 946, after Tamura 1985: 42

sonno nispa or wa an=omap | very respected.man place from 4.A=love | [He] was greatly loved by a respected man. | The Ishikari variant an= replaces a= as the indefinite transitive prefix; the construction is otherwise identical. The patient is zero-marked (third person, object). | nakagawa2024:429 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 948, after Sunazawa 1983: 73

A demoted agent is expressed with the postpositional phrase or(o) wa
'from', taking the NP that would normally appear as the transitive subject
nakagawa2024 428–429 . The construction is frequent with unintended or inanimate
causers and in polite speech where direct naming of the agent is avoided. The full morphology,
referential range, and dialect distribution of the fourth/indefinite person are treated in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Satō's subjecthood diagnostic makes the non-promotion of the patient explicit. If the patient were
a true intransitive subject, a 'want to be loved' predicate should take bare
rusuy want to, desire with subjective marking. Instead the construction requires
the nominalizer hi together with objective cross-referencing on the verb
sato2008 205–206 :

huci oro wa a=en=omap hi ku=ki rusuy | grandmother place from 4.A=1SG.O=love NMLZ 1SG.A=do DES | I want to be loved by grandmother. | The patient is cross-referenced as object (en= '1SG.O') on the verb, and the nominalized clause hi is required by the desiderative. A construction with a true passive subject would take subjective marking and bare rusuy without hi — that form is ungrammatical. Satō describes the overall structure as 'a very peculiar passive' (受け身文としては極めて特殊な構造, Satō 2008: 205). | sato2008:205 | CHI

The distinction between the ci- middle and the
a= construction also emerges clearly here: in a
ci- clause the affected participant surfaces as the intransitive subject
and no agent may be expressed at all; in the a= clause the patient is
syntactically the object, an agent may appear in or wa , and the verb is
transitive by all formal tests nakagawa2024 429–430 .

Does Ainu have a true passive?

Hokkaido Ainu has no subject-promoting passive nakagawa2024 428 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §1 . Nakagawa states it directly:
それにあたるものはアイヌ語には無い 'there is nothing in Ainu that corresponds
to it' nakagawa2024 428 . The two constructions that carry the functional load a
passive would otherwise bear are structurally distinct from each other and from any classic
subject-promoting passive.

construction
patient's grammatical role
agent expression
arity effect

ci- middle
intransitive subject (S)
impossible
−1 (vi output)

a= / an= indefinite-agent
object (O) — no promotion
optional, in or wa phrase
0 (vt output)

subject-promoting passive (cross-linguistic prototype)
promoted to subject (S)
optional, in oblique phrase
−1 (vi output)

The ci- middle moves the base object into subject position — in that
respect it is the nearest Ainu analogue to a valency-decreasing passive — but it categorically
blocks agent expression nakagawa2024 430 . A classic passive combines
patient-as-subject with an optional by-phrase; no Ainu construction combines these two properties.
Nakagawa adds that the middle has a verse-register periphrastic use with the causative —
ci-ra-na-ran-ke (from ran descend , stretched to five
syllables) — where meaning is unchanged; this verse device is not available for the
a= construction nakagawa2024 430 .

The Handbook labels the a= / an= construction a
"valency-neutral impersonal passive" to capture its agent-defocusing function, while recognizing
that it lacks subject promotion bugaevakobayashi2022 §1 ‹contested›. Satō's
preferred formulation — "a half-active, very peculiar passive structure" — likewise foregrounds
the mismatch with cross-linguistic passive prototypes sato2008 205 ‹contested›.
Neither label implies that the construction is a passive in the structural sense; both describe the
same functional fact from different angles.

The functional distribution that results follows from the structural properties. For third-person
affected participants where no agent is at issue, ci- presents the event
from the affected side, and the patient-as-subject reading is the natural one. For first- or
second-person patients, or wherever the agent may need to be expressed,
a= / an= is used instead, with the patient
retaining object status and the verb its transitive morphology nakagawa2024 429–430 .
A by-phrase that co-occurs with patient-as-subject is unattested in the Hokkaido corpus
‹corpus-confirmed›.

Ijäs's learner grammar does not treat the passive question or the ci-
middle ijas2023 . The nominalizer hi that anchors Satō's
subjecthood diagnostic connects this discussion to the nominalization system of
nominalization-p-pe . The antipassive i- , which defocuses the
object from the subject's side, is the mirror-image operation and is treated in
antipassive-detransitive-i . The productive causative
-re / -te / -e , which
introduces an agent argument rather than suppressing one, is in
causative-morphological-re-e-te .

title: Mirativity, Evidential Scope, and Grammaticalization to Sentence-Final Particles
summary: System-level interactions: the evidential-to-mirative extension, evidential stacking and scope over negation and modality, aspect/perfect-based evidential strategies, and the cline from formal noun to sentence-final particle.
part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Mirativity, Evidential Scope, and Grammaticalization to Sentence-Final Particles
Part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
Summary: System-level interactions: the evidential-to-mirative extension, evidential stacking and scope over negation and modality, aspect/perfect-based evidential strategies, and the cline from formal noun to sentence-final particle.

Overview

Three properties of the Hokkaido Ainu evidential system are treated here because they apply
across all four formal-noun evidentials rather than belonging to any single term: the
expression of mirativity and its structural separation from the evidential nominalizers; the
scope of the evidential predicate over the TAM-marked clause it closes; and the
grammaticalization cline by which ordinary sensory nouns become clause-final formal-noun
evidentials. Double-evidential stacking is treated alongside grammaticalization because it
reveals the hierarchical position that ruwe ne has reached on that
cline. The individual evidentials are described in evidential-schema-overview ,
ruwe-ne-inferential , siri-ne-situational ,
hawe-ne-reportative , and humi-ne-sensory .

Mirativity and the auxiliary aan

Surprise at the content of a proposition — mirativity — is not encoded by any of the four
formal-noun evidentials. The mirative value in the Hokkaido corpus attaches instead to the
perfective auxiliary aan and to the related form anan
hirosawa2026 90 . dalcorso2018 336 states this explicitly:
Hokkaido Ainu shows no systematic evidence that the evidential paradigm itself generates
mirative meanings, in contrast to some Sakhalin configurations. The mirative reading arises
from aan / anan , not from the choice of
evidential nominalizer.

yoshikawa2018 argues that aan encodes not just mirativity
but also indirect evidentiality — placing the information at a remove from the speaker's
direct epistemic access — and that the two meanings are inseparable in this auxiliary
hirosawa2026 90 ‹contested›. dalcorso2018 336 , working from
a different theoretical framing, finds no grounds for aligning
aan with the indirect evidential paradigm and treats its mirative
effect as pragmatic. The debate turns on sources consulted second-hand; Yoshikawa's two
studies are cited here via hirosawa2026 90 . What the accessible sources
agree on is the descriptive fact: aan / anan
is the element that contributes mirativity ‹corpus-suggested›.

Within a clause the two elements can divide the semantic labour.
hirosawa2026 90 (ex. 33) analyses a Saru narrative example in which
aan places content outside the speaker's prior epistemic territory —
the structural basis of the surprise — while a following
ruwe ne reclaims that same content as established fact, producing the
'it turned out that…' reading. Hirosawa reads the sequence as a territory-conflict pattern
parallel to Japanese みたい followed by のだ :
outward epistemic displacement, then incorporation ‹contested›.

pirka menoko a=ne aan ru-we ne | good woman 4.A=COP MIR track-POSS COP | She turned out to be a beautiful woman! | Constructed after the attested pattern in Hirosawa 2026: 90 (ex. 33), Tamura audio materials 02-06: 42 (Saru). The auxiliary aan contributes the mirative reading; ruwe ne closes the proposition as established knowledge. The evidential itself is not the source of the surprise.

The same anan follows double-evidential constructions when a mirative
reading accompanies an indirect evidential stacked under
ruwe ne :

supuya at kor sir-an ru-we ne anan | smoke rise while appearance-exist track-POSS COP MIR | Smoke was rising — it turned out! | Dal Corso ex. 268. The indirect visual siri an is layered under ruwe ne; anan follows the entire complex and contributes the surprise reading. The mirative is anan, not the evidential predicate it follows. On the double-evidential construction siri an ruwe ne, see §5 below. | dalcorso2018:327 | HK | Nakagawa text; Dal Corso siglum NGF: 176

An early attestation of the mirative tail with the non-visual sensory evidential is preserved
in kindaichi1936 192 :
rupus humi ne awan 'it had completely hardened and frozen', where the
past-mirative tail awan (a form historically related to or identical
with aan ) follows humi ne . This pattern — any
of the four direct evidentials followed by a mirative tail — recurs in the corpus, confirming
that mirativity is an independent layer above the evidential rather than a property of any
particular nominalizer.

The pedagogical grammar ijas2023 presents the evidential nominalizers alongside
perfective and mirative auxiliaries without foregrounding the structural distinction between
them. The primary analyses dalcorso2018 336 ;
hirosawa2026 90 agree that this distinction is grammatically real, even where
they diverge on the semantics of aan itself.

The scope of the evidential predicate

All four formal-noun evidentials share the same scope geometry: the evidential predicate
(formal noun + copula or existential verb) closes a fully finite, TAM-marked clause. The
constituent before the evidential carries its own person marking, suppletive number
alternations, aspect and perfective auxiliaries, and propositional negation; the evidential
then takes scope over the entire embedded clause
nakagawa2024 392–393 ; takahashi2022 .

Propositional negation — the denial of the embedded proposition — is expressed by negating
the verb before the formal noun; the pattern
…somo ki ruwe ne predicates the negative proposition as a recognized
fact, with ruwe ne outside the scope of
somo . The same holds for all four siblings:
…somo ki siri ne , …somo ki hawe ne ,
…somo ki humi ne . The outer rhetorical frame
ruwe somo ne is categorically different — it negates the evidential
predication as a whole — and is discussed in
ruwe-ne-inferential frames nakagawa2024 393 .

paye somo ki ru-we ne | go NEG do track-POSS COP | (It is established that he) did not go. | Negation somo ki scopes over the embedded verb paye; ruwe ne predicates the resulting negative proposition as an established fact, outside the scope of somo. The parallel pattern holds for siri ne, hawe ne, and humi ne (e.g. …somo ki siri ne).

The ordering of the evidential with respect to other clause-level material follows a fixed
hierarchy. For ruwe ne , the clause-linkers
wa , hine , akusu ,
kusu , and nominal heads hi ,
p all follow the evidential; the purposive modal
kusu ne takes the evidential as its complement
( kusu ne ruwe ne is grammatical) but cannot follow it
( *ruwe ne kusu ne is ungrammatical) nakagawa2024 393 .
The community discussion at aynu-corpora Discord aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli,
2024-11-09) restates this as a layer-hierarchy principle — modal heads sit above the
evidential, taking it as complement rather than the reverse — a formulation corroborated by
Nakagawa's paradigm data ‹corpus-confirmed›. The evidential therefore occupies a position
above clause-internal aspect but below the clause-linking and modal projections that take a
finite clause as their argument.

kamuy ek ru-we ne wa paye=an | god come track-POSS COP CONJ go=4.S | A god came, and (we) went. | The clause-linker wa follows ruwe ne in the post-evidential slot, showing that clause-linking material attaches outside the evidential predicate. The embedded verb ek and the person marker =an carry the clause-internal TAM and agreement; ruwe ne scopes over all of it.

Person and number agreement inside the evidential clause is independent of anything outside
it. Suppletive verbal number alternations are resolved inside the evidential: the contrast
íne nispa ek ruwe ne (singular ek 'come')
vs. íne nispa arki ruwe ne (plural arki )
turns on agreement within the embedded clause. The formal noun and copula carry no agreement
of their own nakagawa2024 393 .

The formal status of the nominalized clause embedded under the evidential is not settled in
the sources consulted directly for this grammar. Its finiteness is evident from its TAM and person
marking. dalcorso2018 314–315 observes that the copula
ne of the direct forms expresses an internal, speaker-anchored
viewpoint while the existential and posture verbs of the indirect forms express an external
perspective, but stops short of a formal constituency analysis. Whether the embedded
constituent constitutes a full finite nominalization, a predicate-phrase nominalization, or a
construction-specific projection remains ‹speculative›. The general nominalization strategy
underlying the construction is treated in nominalization-p-pe and
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization ; the perfective auxiliary that may follow
the evidential is treated in perfective-anterior-a .

The grammaticalization cline

The four formal-noun evidentials derive from the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed forms of ordinary sensory nouns: the
path-noun ru track, path yields the possessed form
ru-we 'the trace of —', the appearance-noun
sir appearance, land yields sir-i , the voice-noun
haw voice yields haw-e , and the sound/feeling-noun
hum sound, feeling yields hum-i . The shift from
sensory noun to clause-final marker was recognized in the earliest grammatical descriptions
kindaichi1936 155–165 and is confirmed morphologically by Tamura's dictionary
entries and Chiri's etymology: ruwe derives from 'there is a trace of
X', shifted to 'there is the fact of X'
tamura1996 s.v. ru, ruwe ; hirosawa2026 65 . On the
typological framework of hoppertraugott2003 , the Ainu evidentials represent
an intermediate point on the layered cline from lexical noun to grammatical marker.

dalcorso2018 24–25 stresses that the result remains only partially
grammaticalized: source-noun semantics are still active in the synchronic distribution (each
form retaining a distinct sensory channel), the post-nominal predicate choice still matters
for the direct/indirect distinction, and the formal nouns have lost full noun-phrase syntax —
they admit no demonstrative or attributive modifier, block the extra possessive ending
( *ruwehe , *humihi as evidentials), and are
fixed in linear position tamura1996 s.v. ruwe . The Ainu system as a whole is
a graded category; no Hokkaido form approaches the full grammaticalization of the Sakhalin
particle manu ‹consensus›.

Within Hokkaido, the grammaticalization has advanced differently across dialects. Three stages
are directly attested in the sources, yielding an intra-areal cline ‹speculative›:

stage
clause-final form
representative dialect(s)
primary sources

I — concept form as clause-final marker
ru / sir / haw / hum (bare, uninflected)
Tokachi; frequently Ishikari

nakagawa2024 110, 390 ; takahashi2013 129–131

II — possessed form as formal-noun evidential
ruwe / siri / hawe / humi + copula/existential verb
Saru, Chitose, Biratori
dalcorso2018 304–337

III — possessed form as adverbial/conjunctive marker

ruwe / siri / hawe / humi (bare, clause-internal, no
following copula)

Horobetsu
takahashi2018 107–112

In Stage I dialects the bare concept form closes the clause where Saru uses the possessed
form: Tokachi iruska haw ne 'she was angry',
tuk ru ne 'it emerged', alongside Saru
iruska hawe ne and tuk ruwe ne
takahashi2013 130–131 ; nakagawa2024 110 . The allomorphs
sin (for sir ) and
aw (for haw ) are Tokachi-specific
takahashi2013 130–131 . Stage I and Stage II represent a morphological
difference — concept form vs. possessed form — rather than a semantic one; the four-way
source distinction is present at both stages.

Stage III, the Horobetsu development, takes the possessed-form nominalizer a step further.
takahashi2018 108, 111 documents uses in which the nominalizers function not
as complement-clause heads but as adverbial-clause markers — subordinating-conjunction-like
elements — without a following copula. The adversative use is the clearest instance:

sinki siri suke ki wa | be.tired appearance cook do CONJ | Though tired, (she) cooked. | Constructed after the adversative siri use documented in Horobetsu and in early records (Chiri 1923: 86, cited by Takahashi 2018: 108). The bare formal noun siri without a following ne functions as an adversative/concessive clause marker, a use not attested in well-described Saru. Takahashi 2018: 111 treats this as a further step in the grammaticalization of the possessed-form nominalizers. | HOR

takahashi2018 111–112 notes that this versatile adverbial use is anticipated
in Kindaichi & Chiri's 1936 description of nominalizers as conjunctive particles
kindaichi1936 155–160 , suggesting the Stage III path is of some age within
Horobetsu rather than a recent innovation. Takahashi connects this to what Noonan termed
'versatile nominalization' — a nominalization strategy that extends across both
complement-clause and adverbial-clause functions.

One dialect fact complicates a simple Stage I → II reading of the cline. In Asahikawa and
Shiranuka the trace-form ru enters the existential frame as an
inferential ( ru an ), apparently in complementary distribution with
the siri an of the southern dialects
dalcorso2018 326 . This gives ru an
indirect-evidential function that the southern varieties lack for this stem; it may reflect
convergence toward the Sakhalin existential pattern
( ruwehe ʔan ), but the sources are thin and Dal Corso flags the
comparison as requiring a wider-dialect study ‹speculative›.

The grammaticalization trajectory also bears on the frequency asymmetry within the direct
paradigm. yoshikawa2020 characterizes ruwe ne as
semantically unmarked among the four direct forms, capable of summarizing and marking scene
transitions much as Japanese のだ does — a discourse function
consistent with its being the most grammaticalized direct member
hirosawa2026 73 . The community discussion at aynu-corpora Discord
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-03-17) summarizes the same Yoshikawa 2020
characterization and notes the high frequency of ruwe ne in narrative
and its summative/transitional function, a point corroborated by Dal Corso's corpus counts
dalcorso2018 72 ‹corpus-confirmed›. Genre-specific evidential patterning is
treated in narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Double-evidential stacking

Double evidentials — two formal-noun evidential constructions stacked within a single clause —
are more frequent in Hokkaido than in Sakhalin dalcorso2018 331 .
dalcorso2018 331–333 documents three functional types from the Kayano,
Tamura, Nakagawa, and Bugaeva corpora:

type
combination
function

A — complementary sources

inner indirect evidential + outer ruwe ne
(e.g. …hawe as ruwe ne ,
…siri an ruwe ne )

character's reported/indirect source, narrator's knowledge-assertion over the whole

B — epistemic reinforcer

inner direct evidential + modal + outer ruwe ne
(e.g. …hawe ne nankor ruwe ne )

direct evidential of lower reliability closed by ruwe ne
as an assertion of the speaker's overall confidence

C — same-stem iteration

direct sensory + indirect sensory of the same stem
(e.g. …humi ne humi as… )

subjective direct sensation and the arising/occurrence of that sensation
presented as separate evidential moments

In Type A the outer ruwe ne functions as a ratifier. An indirect form —
typically hawe as (reportative indirect) or
siri an (visual indirect) — presents the content from a character's
perspective, and ruwe ne closes the utterance from the narrator's
perspective as traditional, shared knowledge dalcorso2018 331 . Attested Type A
combinations include hawe as ruwe ne ,
siri an ruwe ne , and siri ki siri ne
dalcorso2018 331–333 .

ek haw-e as ru-we ne | come haw-POSS stand track-POSS COP | (She) reportedly came — and so it was. | Constructed after the Type A combinations in Dal Corso 2018: 331 (e.g. Kayano text, ran hawe as ruwe ne; Dal Corso ex. 273, siglum KAY, narrator Kaizawa Tsurushino, Biratori). The inner hawe as (indirect reportative) presents the event from a reported source; the outer ruwe ne frames it as the narrator's established fact.

The outer form in Types A and B is systematically
ruwe ne and not siri ne ,
hawe ne , or humi ne . This asymmetry supports
the analysis developed in ruwe-ne-inferential :
ruwe ne functions as an epistemic incorporation operator — a form
that can ratify any already-evidentially-marked proposition as established knowledge —
rather than as a fourth parallel sensory channel. Among the direct members it is the one
whose source-noun semantics ('the trace of —') has bleached sufficiently to generalize across
all sources of evidence once they have been assimilated by the speaker.

Type B uses ruwe ne as an epistemic upgrade. A direct evidential of
relatively lower reliability is reinforced by a final ruwe ne asserting
the speaker's overall confidence in the proposition.
dalcorso2018 332 (ex. 276) cites a narrator's utterance of the form
hawe ne nankor ruwe ne — the modal
nankor 'probably' attached to hawe ne is then
closed by ruwe ne , which does not eliminate the uncertainty
introduced by nankor but frames the compound as an incorporated fact
from the speaker's perspective. This use is consistent with
ruwe ne 's position at the top of the source-reliability hierarchy in
Dal Corso's analysis.

Type C, same-stem iteration, occurs in narrative contexts where the speaker presents both a
subjective sensation and its arising as separate evidential moments:

poro cep a=ne hum-i ne hum-i as orano | big fish 4.A=COP hum-POSS COP hum-POSS stand after | Feeling as though I were a great fish, and as that sensation arose — | The direct humi ne presents the speaker's immediate non-visual sensation (feeling as though one is a fish); humi as presents the arising of that same sensation from an external, indirect-sensory standpoint. The two forms cooperate: direct inner experience followed by indirect-sensory report of its occurrence. The sentence continues with a clause describing the speaker swimming after her elder brother. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker, ILCAA Ainu materials (AA研アイヌ語資料) 013, line 32

dalcorso2018 326–327 adds a distributional argument bearing on the
siri forms. When the indirect visual
siri an appears in a double-evidential construction, the ratifying
outer form is consistently ruwe ne and never
siri ne . Were the two simply co-equal visual channels,
this selection would be unmotivated; it follows naturally if
ruwe ne occupies a higher position in the evidential hierarchy, one
at which its source-noun meaning has bleached enough to allow ratification of any other
evidential type. The distributional gap that Dal Corso's sense-hierarchy account left open
dalcorso2018 311–312 is therefore partially resolved by the double-evidential
distribution itself ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The stacking found in mirative contexts (§2 above) should be distinguished from
double-evidential stacking. In siri an ruwe ne anan , the final
anan is a mirative auxiliary, not a third formal-noun evidential;
the stacking is between two evidential predicates and a mirative auxiliary in sequence, not
among three co-equal evidential forms. Conflating the two construction types would obscure
the structural difference between the evidential system (formal noun + copula or existential
verb) and the mirative system (a perfective auxiliary above the evidential layer)
hirosawa2026 90 ; dalcorso2018 331, 336 .

title: The Prosodic Unit and the Accent-versus-Tone Analysis
summary: The theoretical analysis of the system: what bears prominence and whether Ainu prosody is culminative accent or a tonal melody.
part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Prosodic Unit and the Accent-versus-Tone Analysis
Part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
Summary: The theoretical analysis of the system: what bears prominence and whether Ainu prosody is culminative accent or a tonal melody.

Typological placement: pitch accent, not tone

Hokkaido Ainu has lexical pitch accent: the position of high pitch within a phonological word
is specified in the lexicon and contrasts meaning. Pitch is not assigned to every mora or
syllable independently, as in a tone language; a single accent nucleus per word marks the site
of the rising boundary, and syllables before it are uniformly low shiraishi2022 §5 ; nakagawa2024 48–49 ; sato2008 §2.6 . The system involves no
lexical tone and no vowel reduction correlating with accent position shiraishi2022 §5 . All three primary sources for Hokkaido Ainu agree on this characterization ‹consensus›.

The surface output phonetically resembles Standard Japanese pitch accent — Tamura (1988b: 13)
remarked on the similarity (reported via shiraishi2022 §5 ) — but the two
systems are organized in structurally opposite ways, as the next section sets out. Within the
pitch-accent type, the further question of whether the accent nucleus is best formalized as a
feature of the syllable or of the mora is taken up in
§  Mora or syllable? ; the question of whether the system
could be described as a restricted tone or register system is addressed in
§  Accent versus tone .

The rising-nucleus system

The decisive structural difference from Japanese is directional. Standard Japanese is a
falling-nucleus system (下り核): the nucleus marks the position after which pitch
drops, and syllables up to and including the nucleus are high. Hokkaido Ainu is a
rising-nucleus system (上り核アクセント): the nucleus marks the onset of the accented
syllable, the pitch rises at that point, and syllables following the nucleus remain high without
further contrast nakagawa2024 48–49 . The same asymmetry was recognized under
the term 昇り核 'rising kernel' by Hattori (1961) and Tamura (1970), reported via shiraishi2022 §5 . The consequence is that sustaining or extending the post-accent high plateau does not
change a word's identity, whereas shifting the pitch rise to a different syllable does.

The community illustration of this point is apt: the first-person pronoun
kuáni may be produced with a flat high plateau after the rise
( ku↗áni ) or with a fall after the peak
( ku↗á↘ni ) without any change of meaning, but a fall before the accent
( ku↘áni ) is perceived as anomalous or contrastive aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-09; corroborated by nakagawa2024 48–49 ) ‹corpus-confirmed›.
In the minimal pair nísap 'suddenly' : nisáp
'shin', only the syllable at which the ↗ rise occurs differs; the high plateau that follows is
common to both nakagawa2024 48–49, 51 .

The accented vowel also tends to lengthen allophonically in polysyllables, particularly in
north-eastern accent dialects — Kindaichi (1931: 4) noted forms such as
ˈnuːman 'yesterday' and poˈroː 'big',
describing the lengthening as "not conducted consciously" (reported via shiraishi2022 §5 ). This allophonic lengthening is distinct from the phonemic vowel length of Sakhalin Ainu
(§  Diachrony below) and carries no accent-analytical
implications.

Syllable weight and the placement rule

Accent placement in the standard accent dialects (Saru, Chitose, and their neighbors) follows a
two-rule system sensitive exclusively to the syllable structure of σ1 nakagawa2024 50 :

Shape of σ1
Weight
Accent on
Examples
Rule

(C)VC — closed
heavy
σ1
áynu , pónno ,
kútci , yáykosiramsuypa
原則1

(C)V — open
light
σ2
kamúy , reyé ,
itánki , irámmakaka
原則2

Only σ1's shape matters; word length beyond two syllables is irrelevant to placement. Shiraishi
reformulates the same pattern as an iambic (LH) foot : accent on σ2 when σ1 is light,
on σ1 when σ1 is heavy — a weight-sensitive rhythmic pattern widespread across languages shiraishi2022 §5.1 . Kindaichi and Chiri (1936: 11) concluded from their materials that "the iambic rule is
dominant" (reported via shiraishi2022 §5.1 ).

A class of lexical exceptions breaks the default: when σ1 is open yet accent falls on σ1, the
word must be memorized. Nakagawa lists around twenty such items, including
cápe 'cat', húci 'grandmother',
húre 'red', kéra 'taste',
míci 'father', mína 'laugh',
nísap 'suddenly', páse 'heavy',
sísam 'Japanese person', síno 'really',
túki 'cup', túnas 'fast', and
yúpo 'elder brother' nakagawa2024 51 . These exceptions
produce minimal accent pairs:

Exception form
Gloss
Regular form
Gloss

nísap
'suddenly'
nisáp
'shin'

nína
'gather firewood'
niná
'crush'

útur
'western side of a house'
utúr
'between'

These pairs establish that accent position is phonemically contrastive and that the weight rule,
though dominant, does not determine accent in the exception class. The full inventory of such
pairs is the subject of lexical-contrastive-accent-minimal-pairs ; accent behavior
under compounding and affixation is treated in accent-in-compounds-and-affixation .

Mora or syllable as the accent domain?

In Japanese pitch accent the mora is the accent-bearing unit: a CVC syllable may, in principle,
bear accent on either of its two moras. For Hokkaido Ainu the evidence points to the
syllable as the relevant domain.

Three observations converge on this conclusion. First, the placement rule
(§  above ) is stated entirely in terms of whether σ1 is open or
closed — syllable weight rather than mora count. No sub-syllabic accent position within a CVC
syllable is ever distinctive; all coda consonants (/p t k m n r s y w/) are equivalent in
their weight-conferring effect. Second, the phonetic realization — the pitch rise — is timed to
the onset of the accented syllable, not to an internal mora boundary. Third, the analysis of
coda /-y/ and /-w/ as consonants rather than as the second element of a diphthong (argued
independently in glides-w-y on morphological and phonotactic grounds) is
consistent with syllable-based accent:
ka.muy 'deity' (two syllables, σ2 = CVC) receives accent on σ2 as an
undivided unit; there is no evidence that the internal /-y/ constitutes a separate
accent-bearing mora nakagawa2024 29 ; shiraishi2022 §3, §5.1 .

Person-prefix accent shift provides additional evidence. The prefixes
ku= , e= , and ci=
integrate with the stem as a single phonological word; the weight of σ1 in the new word is
recalculated with the prefix included. A stem whose open σ1 would otherwise take σ2 accent by
原則2 shifts its accent one syllable toward the word boundary
nakagawa2024 52 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 :

ku=hopuni | 1SG.A=rise | I get up. | Stem hopúni: σ1 = ho- (open, CV), accent on σ2 (pu-) by 原則2. Under ku=, σ1 of the phonological word becomes ku- (open, CV); σ2 is the stem's ho-; accent shifts to ho-: ku=hópuni. Paradigm from Nakagawa (2024: 52) and Shiraishi (2022: §5.1). | SAR

The contrasting prefixes a= and eci= do not
trigger the shift: omáre → a=omáre retains the
stem's accent nakagawa2024 53 . These two prefixes are prosodically less
integrated with the stem, a gradient that parallels their position in the person-affix paradigm
(see person-marking-architecture and accent-in-compounds-and-affixation ).

A moraic restatement of 原則1/2 is formally possible: a closed σ1 has two moras (CV + C), so
accent could be described as associating with the second mora of the word — belonging to σ1
when σ1 is heavy, to σ2 when σ1 is light. This restatement covers the same data but posits
mora-counting that is not independently evidenced elsewhere in Ainu phonology. The sources consulted
do not argue for it: Shiraishi characterizes the pattern as iambic and syllable-based shiraishi2022 §5.1 , and Nakagawa states the rule in syllable-structure terms nakagawa2024 50
‹contested›.

Accent versus tone: the analytical question

The pitch-accent label is used in two overlapping senses in the Japanological tradition: as a
cover term for any system in which pitch is phonemic, and in the narrower sense of a system
with one positionally specified nucleus per word. All three primary sources use it in the
narrow sense as applied to Hokkaido Ainu. Pre-nuclear syllables are uniformly low;
