positive predicate and places a negative adverb in its scope. Satō formalises the division by
predicate type: a verbal clause is negated as [subject] V (ka) somo ki or
with preverbal somo directly before the verb, while a copular clause is
negated as [subject] [complement] (ka) somo ne , with the negator before
the copula-ne-predicate-nominals copula ne
sato2008 22 . Ijäs presents the same architecture for learners: copular ‘A is
not B’ is A B somo ne , agreeing with Satō’s paradigm and Nakagawa’s
ordering description ijas2023 ; sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 365 .

Preverbal somo

With a verbal predicate, somo stands immediately before the person-marked
verb. Satō states the adjacency as a rule: the negator precedes the verb, and the personal index
is part of the verb form, so the negator cannot intervene between the index and its stem —
somo ku=arpa ‘I do not go’, never *ku=somo=arpa
sato2008 124 ; nakagawa2024 407 . Ijäs gives learners the same
warning against splitting the personal affix from its stem with the negator ijas2023 .

noka-ha poka somo a=nukar | figure-POSS even NEG 4.A=see | One did not even see its figure. | Nakagawa ex. 832. The simple preverbal pattern; the possessed noun noka-ha carries the additive poka ‘even’, a polarity item licensed under somo. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

somo k=arpa wa | NEG 1SG.S=go.SG FIN | I will not go. | bugaeva2012:496 | CHI | Bugaeva ex. 73, siglum NN 28 (Nakagawa Chitose data)

For an ordinary verb the bare frame somo V is grammatical but, by Satō’s
report, used less often than the ka somo ki periphrasis of the next
section sato2008 22 . A modal or other postverbal element follows the verb in
its normal place, with the negator still at the front of the predicate:

tanto anak somo ek nankor wa | today TOP NEG come.SG probably FIN | He probably will not come today. | The negator precedes the verb ek; the dubitative nankor and the final wa stay in their postverbal positions. | sato2008:22 | HK

Standard negation here is symmetric in the sense of the typology of negation: the affirmative
and negative declarative clauses differ only by the added negative morpheme, with no separate
negative verb form or special negative agreement miestamo2005 ; takahashi2016 75 . Takahashi’s Tokachi weather predicate gives the minimal pair, the affirmative
ruyanpe ruy ‘it rained’ differing from its negation by
somo alone:

ruyanpe somo ruy | rain NEG be.intense | It did not rain. | the rain was not intense | Takahashi ex. 11b, against the affirmative ruyanpe ruy ‘it rained’ (ex. 11a); the clauses differ only in somo. | takahashi2016:75 | TOK

Preverbal somo also negates the existential-locational predicate
an exist, be located , where the positive verb stays available alongside the
suppletive negative existential isam . Ijäs draws a semantic line between
the two — isam for outright absence or disappearance,
somo an where the ‘live, be located’ reading of an
is to be preserved — but the exact split is her own formulation and is not stated in those terms
by the read grammars ‹contested› ijas2023 . The privative paradigm and possessive
uses of isam are treated in
negation-existential-possessive-isam .

The somo ki periphrasis

The second verbal frame places the notional predicate before somo ki ,
with optional ka between them: V ( ka )
somo ki . Bugaeva gives the compositional analysis: it is the verb
ki do that somo formally negates, while the notional verb
functions as an object-like complement of ki ; the notional verb keeps its
person marking and ki carries none bugaeva2012 496 .
Nakagawa treats the same string as one use of the formal verb ki , which
supplies a verbal host so that conjunctions and clause-final material can attach nakagawa2024 374, 407 . Ijäs’s pedagogical account agrees, describing ki as a dummy ‘do’ verb
filling the slot that somo cannot occupy on its own ijas2023 .
Against this, Satō’s description of Chitose, as reported by Takahashi, analyses
somo ki as a single auxiliary rather than as ki
negated over a complement; the internal constituency of the periphrasis is therefore genuinely
disputed ‹contested› sato2008 86 ; takahashi2016 74 . Refsing
records the construction for Shizunai as well refsing1986 214–215 .

nep a=nukar ka somo ki korka | something 4.A=see even NEG do although | Although I did not see anything, | Nakagawa ex. 833. The periphrasis with ka; the indefinite nep ka ‘anything’ is licensed under the negation. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

eci=yayeymontasa somo ki yakka | 2PL.S=take.revenge NEG do even.if | Even if you do not take revenge, | Nakagawa ex. 834. The same periphrasis without ka; yayeymontasa is intransitive, so eci= indexes the 2PL subject. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

Nakagawa finds no clear semantic contrast between the preverbal frame and the
somo ki frame, and likewise no special meaning in the presence or absence
of ka nakagawa2024 374, 407 ; Ijäs treats the longer
construction as broadly exchangeable with plain somo ijas2023 .
When ka is present it brackets the notional predicate as the focused
complement of ki :

ku=iruska ka somo ki wa | 1SG.S=be.angry even NEG do FIN | I am not angry. | The bracketed [ku=iruska ka] is the object-like complement that ki formally negates. | bugaeva2012:496 | SAR | Bugaeva ex. 74, siglum T2 239 (Tamura 2000: 239)

Ijäs additionally suggests that the postverbal ka somo ki arose as a
calque on Japanese verb-final negation, with preverbal somo as the older
Ainu pattern. No read Hokkaido source supports a contact origin, and Dal Corso’s diachronic
account of Ainu negation derives the periphrastic forms internally, so the contact hypothesis is
best left as her own conjecture ‹speculative› ijas2023 ; dalcorso2025neg 41–42 .

Copular negation and the particle ka

A predicate-nominal clause is negated by placing somo before the copula
ne , optionally with ka : NP
( ka ) somo ne sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 365 . Satō notes that the alternative ne ka somo ki , expected if
ne is fed through the ordinary somo ki frame as a
verb, is scarcely attested; the productive negator of the copular clause is
somo ne or ka somo ne sato2008 22 .

tan pe anak seta ka somo ne wa | this thing TOP dog even NEG COP FIN | This one is not a dog. | Satō ex. 6. Copular ka somo ne; somo precedes the copula ne, with ka on the complement seta. | sato2008:22 | HK

The particle ka in ka somo ki and
ka somo ne is the ordinary additive and focus particle ‘also, even, at
all’, here attached to the negated constituent. Nakagawa observes that negative clauses favour
ka — adding focus to the element whose absence is asserted sharpens the
negation — while its presence carries no meaning of its own nakagawa2024 323, 407 .
Satō isolates a distinct ‘negative particle’ ka , left untranslated in his
paradigms, while granting that it is hard to separate strictly from the additive particle; for
Bugaeva, following Tamura, the same ka focuses the complement with a
nuance of unexpectedness sato2008 124–125 ; bugaeva2012 496 . The
divergence is one of analysis and terminology over a single form ‹contested›. Ijäs presents
ka somo ne as the more emphatic equative negation, glossing
ka ‘also, even, at all’ ijas2023 .

The same ka builds the negative-polarity indefinites
nep ka ‘anything, and under negation nothing’ and
nen ka ‘anyone, no one’, whose licensing depends on the scope of the
negator over the whole clause; that interaction is treated in
negation-polarity-items-scope .

Negation under the evidential predicate

A clause closed by a formal noun plus ne — the
evidential-schema-overview evidential frames
ruwe ne , hawe ne ,
siri ne , humi ne — is negated by negating the
verb phrase inside the nominalization, leaving the evidential frame itself in place. Nakagawa
states the rule directly: the negative counterpart of such a clause makes the verb phrase before
the formal noun negative, so the negation scopes below the evidential nakagawa2024 393 .

sinep ka a=isamka ka somo ki ru-we ne kusu | one even 4.A=lose even NEG do track-POSS COP because | Since I did not lose even a single one, | Nakagawa ex. 779. The negated predicate a=isamka ka somo ki sits inside the evidential ru-we ne; the evidential itself is outside the scope of somo. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR

A surface-similar sequence in which the negator falls on the copula of the evidential,
ruwe somo ne , is not ordinary clausal negation but a rhetorical or
convictional construction, close to ‘was it not the case that …?’ nakagawa2024 393 .
The contrast — propositional negation inside the evidential against the high-scope rhetorical
frame on the copula — belongs with the evidential paradigm in
evidential-schema-overview .

Symmetry and subordinate asymmetry

In main declarative clauses, verbal and copular alike, somo negation is
symmetric: the negative clause retains the predicate architecture of the affirmative and adds
the negative adverb before the predicate miestamo2005 ; takahashi2016 75 . Subordinate clauses behave differently. Hokkaido temporal adverbial clauses link with
several conjunctive particles — wa for a successive event,
no for a stative one — but a negated adverbial clause selects only
no , and the successive wa is blocked takahashi2016 75–77 .

somo ipe no mokor | NEG eat.a.meal ADV sleep | He went to sleep without eating. | The parallel with the successive linker, *somo ipe wa mokor, is ungrammatical: a negated event links only with the stative no. | bugaeva2022; takahashi2016:76 | HK

The restriction is uncontroversial as a fact ‹consensus›; its explanation is the live
contribution. Following Tamura’s analysis that the negation of an action is construed as a
static state rather than as an event, Takahashi and the Handbook treat the
no -only pattern as an asymmetry driven by aspect, with the choice of
linker depending on polarity takahashi2016 76 ; bugaeva2022 .
Nakagawa’s account of no gives the same conditioning from the affirmative
side: no links a preceding phrase that is stative, negative in meaning,
or an action verb accompanied by a negative element nakagawa2024 340 . Whether
the dependency is best modelled as aspect government, as reduced transitivity, or pragmatically
remains open in the literature ‹contested›.

Boundaries and open questions

Several negative meanings adjacent to somo have their own predicate
architecture. The negative existential isam and the privative
sak lack express absence and lack with ordinary verbal syntax; the ability and
cognition negatives eaykap , koyaykus ,
eramiskari , and erampewtek form a lexical-negative
class; and the prohibitive iteki don't belongs to directive mood nakagawa2024 314–315, 424–425, 450 ; bugaeva2012 496–497 . These are routed to
negation-existential-possessive-isam ,
negation-ability-cognition-predicates , and
negation-prohibitive-iteki . Ijäs’s claim that the existence of a lexical negative
normally bars somo plus the corresponding positive verb states the
tendency too strongly: Dal Corso records southern Hokkaido somo kor ‘not
have’ beside privative sak , and the read grammars describe lexicalized
meanings and preferences rather than categorical blocking ‹contested› ijas2023 ; dalcorso2025neg 41 .

Dal Corso’s Sakhalin-focused comparative work links somo and
isam to an older negative-existential root
*sam , deriving somo from a form like
*samno and isam from antipassive
i- on that root; Ijäs reaches the same speculation for
isam , marking it as conjectural dalcorso2025neg 41–42 ;
ijas2023 . The read Hokkaido grammars state no such etymology, so the shared-root
proposal stands as a specific historical hypothesis, not as settled synchronic analysis
‹speculative›.

The standard negator varies dialectally. somo covers most of Hokkaido;
the eastern Pacific coast and parts of the east use other free negators in the same structural
slot, and the eastern dialects also have a rare suffix -ko that derives a
rhetorical negative adverb from a stative intransitive — a genuinely morphological negative in
an otherwise periphrastic system nakagawa2024 407–408 ; takahashi2016 73 ; dalcorso2025neg 41 .

negator
approximate distribution

somo
most of Hokkaido (north, south, west, and central-east)

omo , homo
eastern Pacific coast (Shiranuka, Kushiro)

henne
eastern Hidaka and Shizunai

senne , sen
Horobetsu and Yakumo

sien
Tokachi

The microdistribution is assembled from scattered dialect reports rather than from a full
conditioning study, and no rule is given for which negator a transitional locality selects
‹contested› nakagawa2024 407–408 ; dalcorso2025neg 41 . The wider
dialect classification is treated in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry .

title: The 'No Adjective Class' Thesis: Property Concepts as Stative Verbs
summary: Why pirka, poro, and other property words are analysed as stative intransitive verbs rather than adjectives, and whether any residual adjectival/quality-noun subclass survives.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The 'No Adjective Class' Thesis: Property Concepts as Stative Verbs
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: Why pirka, poro, and other property words are analysed as stative intransitive verbs rather than adjectives, and whether any residual adjectival/quality-noun subclass survives.

Overview

Words expressing property concepts —
pirka be good, be beautiful ,
hure be red ,
poro be big, grow ,
pon be small ,
wen be bad, be poor ,
tanne be long ,
pewre be young ,
méan be cold — are intransitive verbs in Hokkaido Ainu.
They take the nominative person affixes of the intransitive-subject set,
predicate without a copula, derive manner adverbs with the suffix
-no , and enter the same causative and applicative
morphology as action verbs. No separate adjective class is warranted
nakagawa2024 118 ; nakagawa2022pos 474 ;
sato2008 2 ; shibatani1990 19 ;
refsing1986 76 ‹consensus›.

Representative property verbs with 4.S predication and prenominal collocates (Saru baseline)

form
gloss
4.S predication
prenominal collocate
inchoative

pirka
'be good, be beautiful'
pirka=an
pirka menoko 'beautiful woman'
same form (contextual)

hure
'be red'
hure=an
hure cep 'red fish'
same form 'become red'

poro
'be big, be large'
poro=an
poro cise 'big house'
same form 'grow, become big'

pon
'be small'
pon=an
pon hekaci 'small child'
same form 'become small'

wen
'be bad, be poor'
wen=an
wen kamuy 'bad deity'
same form

tanne
'be long'
tanne=an
tanne ni 'long stick'
same form 'lengthen'

pewre
'be young'
pewre=an
pewre menoko 'young woman'
same form

méan
'be cold'
méan=an
—
same form 'become cold'

The clause-level consequences — property verbs predicating without a copula,
the person-index frame, the interaction with negation — are treated in
property-concept-predication . The word-class system as a whole,
including the diagnostic criteria that separate verbs from nouns, adverbs, and
adnouns, is established in word-class-inventory-and-diagnostics .
The -no adverbializer is documented in
adverbializer-no ; causative counterparts of property verbs
(e.g. pirka-re make good, cure ) are treated in
transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs .

The standard analysis: three lines of evidence

Three independently sufficient lines of evidence converge on the verbal
classification of property words
nakagawa2022pos 474–476 ; nakagawa2024 118 .

Syntactic identity: the person-marking frame

Property verbs occupy the predicate slot and receive the intransitive-subject
(S) person prefix — the same prefix set as action intransitives, and
distinct from the transitive-subject (A) set that nouns cannot host in this
position at all.
pewre be young and mina laugh both take
ku= as their first-singular subject:

sonno ku=pewre or ta ku=nu yukar ne ru-we ne | truly 1SG.S=be.young inside LOC 1SG.A=hear yukar COP track-POSS COP | That is truly a yukar I heard back when I was young. | Nakagawa ex. 1. The property verb pewre 'be young' takes the first-singular intransitive prefix ku= in the same slot as the action verb nu 'hear'; no morphological difference marks one as stative and the other as active. | nakagawa2024:118 | SAR

Third-person predication is zero-marked — the same zero as on all
third-person intransitive predicates, with no copula:

toan nupuri ri | that mountain be.tall | That mountain is high. | Shibatani ex. 13b. The property verb ri 'be tall/high' predicates the third-person subject nupuri 'mountain' with zero person marking and no copula. | shibatani1990:19 | HK

rera ruy | wind be.strong | The wind is strong. | Shibatani ex. 13c. | shibatani1990:19 | HK

The same pattern holds prenominally: a property verb precedes the noun
without any morphological change, identical to an action verb in attributive
position. pon hekaci 'small child'
(property verb pon be small + noun
hekaci child ) deploys the same verb-before-noun construction
as apkas utar arki ruwe ne
'the walking people came' (action verb apkas walk prenominally)
nakagawa2022pos 475 . In neither case does the verb carry
an attributive morpheme or particle.

Morphological identity: causative and applicative morphology

The causative suffix -re (and its allomorphs
-e , -ka ) attaches to
property verb stems when the semantics licenses a causative reading,
producing transitive counterparts exactly as it does from action intransitives
nakagawa2022pos 476 .
pirka-re make good, make beautiful, cure is derived from
pirka be good and
wen-re make bad from wen be bad .
Even roots that are predominantly stative in corpus use feed the causative:
kiyanne be older, be senior yields
kiyanne-re treat as a senior, defer to as an elder ,
showing that a latent inchoative channel is available even where spontaneous
onset is infrequent nakagawa2024 119 . The full transitivizer
system is treated in transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs .

Semantic identity: the stative–inchoative duality

Ainu intransitive verbs encode both the state and the transition into the
state in a single form
nakagawa2024 118–119 ;
nakagawa2022pos 477 ;
shibatani1990 19 .
poro be big; become big, grow reads as stative in one
context and as inchoative in another; the same holds for
hure be red; become red and
méan be cold; become cold .
This is not a property peculiar to property words: the copula
ne be; become , the existential
an exist; come into being , and
siknu be alive; revive show the same duality
nakagawa2024 118–119 .
The inchoative imperative of a property verb therefore takes the bare
stem directly:

tunasno pirka | quickly be.good | Get well quickly! | Shibatani (1990: 19). The bare property verb pirka 'be good / be healthy' receives an inchoative reading under the imperative frame. No dedicated inchoative form exists; the reading 'become good/healthy' is forced by context. | shibatani1990:19 | HK

The periphrastic inchoative with the copula is blocked.
*hure ne for 'become red' and
*poro ne for 'become big' are ungrammatical:
the inchoative reading is already carried by the bare property verb, and
the copula ne in that position constructs a
nominal predicate ('be/become a —'), a structurally distinct construction
treated in copula-ne-predicate-nominals
nakagawa2024 119 ; nakagawa2022pos 477 .

Diagnostic evidence

The three arguments above translate into a battery of formal diagnostics that
separate property verbs from the other word classes in Nakagawa's inventory
nakagawa2024 115–116 ; nakagawa2022pos 473 :

diagnostic
property verb ( pirka )
noun ( menoko 'woman')
adnoun ( tan 'this')

Takes intransitive-S person prefix
✓ ku=pirka
✗ (predication requires ne )
✗

Predicates without copula
✓ pirka=an
✗ requires kamuy ne
✗

Negated by somo…ki
✓ pirka somo ki
✗ (nominal negation somo ne )
N/A

Derives adverb with -no
✓ pirka-no 'well'
✗
✗

Prenominal modification without morphological change
✓ pirka menoko
✗ (requires relative clause)
✓ tan menoko

Takes causative -re/-e/-ka
✓ pirka-re
✗
✗

Nominalised by -p/-pe
✓ pirka p 'a good one'
✓ (head noun directly)
✓ tan-pe 'this thing'

The first four diagnostics align property verbs with verbs and against
both nouns and adnouns. Diagnostic five — prenominal modification without
morphological change — is shared between property verbs and adnouns, which
accounts for the apparent adjective-like quality of items like
pirka and pon be small in noun-modifying position.
Diagnostic seven shows that property verbs also enter the
nominalization-p-pe -p/-pe nominalization
slot exactly as action verbs do.

ijas2023 presents property verbs as intransitive stative verbs
throughout the pedagogical guide, with no separate adjective paradigm and with
stative versus inchoative readings resolved by context.
sato2008 2 groups property words in the intransitive verb
class without an adjective subclass.
refsing1986 76 reaches the same conclusion for the Shizunai
dialect.

The candidate counter-class: adnouns, poro and pon, and the historical analysis

Three items have been taken to suggest a residual adjective class: (a) the
adnoun (連体詞) class proper, (b) the specific items
poro be big, grow and pon be small , and
(c) Kindaichi's pre-1942 grammar.

The adnoun class

Nakagawa establishes a genuine adnoun class (連体詞) defined by
morphosyntactic criteria: members modify a noun, cannot serve as a
predicate, and take no person affix
nakagawa2024 116, 283 ; nakagawa2022pos 473 .
Its inventory comprises demonstratives
( tan 'this', toan 'that',
ne 'the aforementioned'),
numeric prenominals
( sine 'one-', tu 'two-'),
interrogatives ( hemanta 'which kind'),
indefinites, and a residual set of general adnouns —
oar 'one-of-a-pair',
kes 'every',
pinne 'male',
matne 'female',
oya 'other'
nakagawa2024 283 .
None of these carries a property meaning in the sense relevant to
adjective typology; class membership rests entirely on the morphosyntactic
diagnostics above. The demonstrative prenominals and their discourse-anaphoric
subsets are treated in demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness .

poro and pon: verbs with prominent prenominal distribution

poro be big, grow and pon be small appear
as predicates — a=kor cise poro=an
'my house is big' (constructed) — and as prenominal modifiers —
poro nupuri 'a big mountain'.
In prenominal position they are surface-identical to adnouns, because
neither position displays person-affix morphology. The predicate data,
however, show that they take S-affixes like other property verbs; Nakagawa
classifies them as verbs
nakagawa2024 118 ; nakagawa2022pos 474 .

Place names attest their prenominal frequency particularly clearly:
Poro-pet 'big river' and
Pon-bets 'small river' are compounds of the type
intransitive-verb-root + noun → nominal
nakagawa2024 267–268 , the same formation available to
any intransitive verb root. The broader verb-centredness of Ainu nominal
derivation — in which nearly every noun has a simultaneous intransitive-verb
reading and noun-formation operates principally through verbal roots —
supports classifying poro and
pon as verbs whose prenominal distribution happens
to be prominent
(antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-06-02)
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›.

Kindaichi's adjective class and its rejection

kindaichi1936 §189–190 posits a 形容詞 (adjective) class with
predicative person-inflection ( 形容詞の人稱變化 ),
attributive use ( 連体形 ), and a participial form
( 分詞形容詞 ). The predicative paradigm uses the
same intransitive-subject prefixes as verbs — the observation that
ultimately enabled the merger. chiri1942 argued that since
adjective-meaning words and action verbs share their full inflectional
frame, their person-marking morphology, and their clause-final behaviour,
positing two classes serves no formal purpose. Chiri coined
用詞 ( yōshi ) for the merged class, and the
no-adjective analysis has been standard since then ‹consensus›.
The term 用詞 is no longer in use; the merged class is simply
動詞 (verb) in current work
nakagawa2022pos 474 ; nakagawa2024 118 .

A residual nuance concerns the clause-linking particles
wa and no .
wa links clauses whose predicate describes a state
( pirka wa 'since it is good'), while
no links clauses describing a dynamic event
( somo ipe no 'without eating'). Because property
verbs cluster at the static pole, the contrast was sometimes read as a
syntactic trace of an adjective/verb boundary.
nakagawa2022pos 479 demonstrates that the split tracks the
static/dynamic semantic axis of the whole VP — it does not mark a
part-of-speech boundary within the verbal system ‹consensus›. The linking
contrast is examined in property-concept-predication .

Comparison and degree

Degree and comparison are expressed through verbal and adverbial means,
not through dedicated adjectival morphology. The postpositional adverb
akkari more than (X), exceeding takes an object-marked
antecedent and frames the comparison with a bare property verb predicate
unchanged in form: en=pirka akkari pirka=an
'you are more beautiful than I am' (constructed). Degree adverbs such as
earkinne very, extremely and
poronno greatly, very much modify a property verb directly,
and the equative uses neno similar to, like .
In every pattern, the property verb itself remains morphologically unchanged —
which follows from its verbal status, since Ainu verbs in general carry
no degree inflections. These strategies are documented with corpus examples
in adverbs-degree-comparison .

Typological framing

In the typological survey of intransitive predication, languages range from
those encoding property concepts in a dedicated open-class adjective
(English, Russian) to those using a verbal strategy, where property words
are simply intransitive stative verbs, to those using a nominal strategy
stassen1997 ; nakagawa2022pos 474 .
Hokkaido Ainu falls squarely in the verbal-encoding type by every formal
diagnostic: property words pattern with verbs in their morphology and
predication, and no morphological or syntactic slot is reserved for
adjectives alone ‹consensus›.

This places Ainu with Japanese and Korean, whose 形容詞
class is itself a closed verbal category. Nakagawa draws the parallel
explicitly: Ainu "adjectives" are verb-type — modifying a noun directly
without an attributive morpheme — in contrast to the noun-type strategy
where a linking particle or agreement marker intervenes
nakagawa2022pos 475 .
shibatani1990 19 states the outcome concisely:
"adjective-meaning words become predicates without any copula, in exactly
the same way as verbs."

A community hypothesis in the aynu-corpora discussion archive proposes
that the existential an exist, be present in forms like
pirka an 'be good; become good' might represent
a distinct aspect marker for onset of state — parallel to
ne an 'become X' alongside copular
ne 'be X' (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-15)
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.
The bare corpus data show pirka an collocating with
inchoative-oriented translations in certain narrative frames, but no primary
source in the descriptive literature distinguishes a grammaticalized
onset-of-state use of an here from its ordinary
existential value. The hypothesis is noted as a lead for further corpus
investigation.

title: Derivational Morphology on Nouns: Diminutive, Augmentative, and Size Modification
summary: Noun-internal derivation, principally the diminutive -po and augmentative/evaluative strategies, plus the pon/poro size-modifier boundary between morphology and syntax.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Derivational Morphology on Nouns: Diminutive, Augmentative, and Size Modification
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: Noun-internal derivation, principally the diminutive -po and augmentative/evaluative strategies, plus the pon/poro size-modifier boundary between morphology and syntax.

The diminutive suffix -po and the absence of an augmentative

The principal derivational suffix operating on Hokkaido Ainu nouns for size and affect is
-po diminutive suffix , which attaches to a noun stem to yield a derivative
meaning 'small X' or 'dear X' nakagawa2024 264–265 ;
kindaichi1936 25 ; ijas2023 . No productive augmentative suffix —
a suffix encoding 'large X' — is described for Hokkaido Ainu in any of the grammatical
treatments consulted. Upward size modification relies on the prenominal property verb
poro be big , used attributively before a noun without morphological change
(see no-adjective-class-property-verbs ). Morphological encoding of size is
therefore asymmetric: the diminutive has a suffix, the augmentative does not ‹consensus›.

Three features of -po merit individual treatment: its etymological identity with
the free noun po child, son , its lexicalization into a subset of animal
names where the base is no longer a free morpheme, and a Horobetsu colloquial meaning
extension to 'deceased (intimate)' not reported for other dialects
kindaichi1936 25 .

Morphology

-po attaches to the concept-form-affiliative-form concept form of a
noun stem. The output is itself a common noun in the concept form and may take the affiliative
suffix in the normal way nakagawa2024 264 . Nakagawa explicitly excludes the
suffix from noun-noun-compounding compound-noun formation : a form such as
cikappo is a modifier-derived nominal, not a compounding element, and
the person-affix and affiliative-suffix diagnostics that distinguish compounds from
modifier–noun sequences classify -po derivatives as single-word outputs that
function as ordinary free nouns nakagawa2024 265 . The form is invariant
across the Hokkaido dialects documented in the sources read here, and the suffix integrates
phonologically with no reported junctural changes.

base noun
gloss
diminutive
gloss
source

cikap
'bird'
cikappo
'small bird'
nakagawa2024 264

cep
'fish'
ceppo
'small fish'
nakagawa2024 264

supuya
'smoke'
supuyapo
'faint smoke'
nakagawa2024 264

menoko
'woman'
menokopo
'girl'
kindaichi1936 25

okkay
'male youth (bound root, Saru/Chitose)'
okkaypo
'young man, boy'
nakagawa2024 265

The table illustrates the range of base nouns: inanimate ( supuya ),
animate non-human ( cikap , cep ), and human
( menoko , okkay ). For the special status of
okkaypo — which is ambiguous across dialects between the DIM reading
and a noun–noun reading — see the section on the po child, son homophony
below.

Semantic values: diminutive and affective

Kindaichi and Chiri describe -po as a 指小辞
meaning 'small thing / dear thing' and trace it etymologically to the free noun
po child, son kindaichi1936 25 . Two semantic values are
present in the attested data and are recognized by subsequent treatments
nakagawa2024 264–265 ; ijas2023 .

The diminutive value encodes that the referent is smaller than the prototype of its
class. For ceppo 'small fish' this is size in the physical sense;
for supuyapo 'faint smoke' it is reduced quantity or intensity
rather than spatial extent — a scalar application of the suffix to a non-count noun.
The affective / hypocoristic value encodes endearment or intimacy without implying
reduced size. Kindaichi's example ku=sampepo 'my dear one' (lit.
'my little heart') illustrates pure affect: the referent is a person, not a physically small
object kindaichi1936 25 . The two values are not in complementary
distribution — the same form carries both potential meanings — and context selects the
dominant reading.

Corpus examples

The following attested examples from Saru and Horobetsu narrative texts show
-po in productive use. In all three, the DIM derivative freely combines with the
associative-plural formal noun utar people, companions (treated in
nominal-number-utar-transnumerality ), and in the second and third examples the
prenominal property verb pon be small stacks with the DIM derivative,
confirming that the suffix and the prenominal are independent strategies.

hinak wa arki wen cikap-po utar | where from come.PL bad bird-DIM ASS | the bad little birds that came from somewhere | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko 川上まつ子, uwepeker 民話15, ILCAA Ainu materials [aa-irc/022#23]

pon cep-po utar pirasa wa | be.small fish-DIM ASS spread.out CONJ | spreading out the small fish | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko 川上まつ子, uwepeker 民話14, ILCAA Ainu materials [aa-irc/020#61]

pon cikap-po utar hene euesayne | be.small bird-DIM ASS even flock.together | even small birds flocking together | chiba2015 | HOR | Kanenari Matsu 金成マツ, 六人の山子, 千葉大学報告書 [chiba-325/25#19]

The Saru examples confirm that cikappo and
ceppo behave as ordinary common nouns that take
utar as a plurality marker ‹corpus-confirmed›. The Horobetsu
example shows the same stacking in a second dialect, with the focus particle
hene 'even' scoping over the entire
pon cikappo utar phrase. The high frequency of
okkaypo alone — 1,457 tokens, rank 136 in the Hokkaido sub-corpus
— confirms that DIM derivatives are a live and frequent nominal class ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Lexicalization in animal names: isepo and the class-term question

Several Hokkaido Ainu animal names contain a synchronically opaque
-po where the base noun is not independently attested as a free
form. The best-known case is isepo rabbit . Two analyses have been proposed
in community scholarship, both identifying -po as the diminutive
suffix aynucorporadiscord .

One analysis holds that ise is an onomatopoeia for the rabbit's
high-pitched call, so isepo originated as 'the small/dear thing
that goes ise ' — an expressive base plus the diminutive (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2023-12-11; palestine_2026, 2024-08-28) aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.
A related line invokes the productive pattern by which an onomatopoeic base followed by
-se yields a verb (compare pawse
'a fox calls', where paw is the cry, and
rimse 'dance'), and treats ise in
isepo as the base of such a nominalized verb with
-po added (palestine_2026, 2024-08-28)
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. Neither analysis has been confirmed by a read
primary grammatical source; the status of ise as an independent
morpheme in contemporary Hokkaido Ainu is unclear ‹speculative›.

The broader pattern of class-term residues in animal and plant names — where a frozen
element meaning 'bird', 'fish', or 'tree' is embedded in a lexicalized form — is treated
in noun-noun-compounding . The -po pattern is
structurally distinct: rather than a class-term head retained from a transparent compound,
it is a derivational suffix that appears to have frozen at an earlier productive stage, once
the base ise lost independent currency.

The okkaypo ambiguity: suffix versus noun po 'child'

The suffix -po is homophonous with the free noun po child, son ,
and Nakagawa attributes the homophony to a shared etymology
nakagawa2024 265 . In okkaypo the ambiguity is
productive and dialect-sensitive. In Saru and Chitose, okkay does
not occur as a free noun — it appears only inside derivatives — so
okkaypo parses unambiguously as [bound root
okkay + DIM] 'young man'. In dialects where
okkay is a free noun meaning 'man', the form is genuinely
ambiguous: [ okkay + DIM] 'young man' versus
[ okkayo 'male' + po 'child'] = 'son'. The
unambiguous form for the N+N reading in those dialects is okkayopo
with the full base nakagawa2024 265 .

okkay-po utar | man-DIM ASS | young men (Saru: unambiguously DIM reading) | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko 川上まつ子, isoytak 独話1, ILCAA Ainu materials [aa-irc/003#26]

The free noun po child, son has an affiliative form
poho and enters the kinship paradigm as an inalienably-possessed
term: po:poho 'child — one's child'
nakagawa2024 222 . See kinship-honorific-possession and
concept-form-affiliative-form . The grammaticalization from free noun to
suffix plausibly runs through a modifier-like compounding stage ('small/dear child of' →
'small/dear instance of'), consistent with the shared affective semantics, though no
read source provides a detailed trajectory ‹speculative›.

Horobetsu: -po on an intimate as 'deceased'

Kindaichi records a Horobetsu colloquial pattern in which -po attached to a term
denoting an intimate acquires a 'deceased' reading:
ku=akipo 'my late younger brother' and
ku=kor operpo 'my late daughter'
kindaichi1936 25 . The semantic path from 'dear' to 'dearly departed' is
pragmatically transparent — the affective value of the suffix bleeds into memorializing
reference — but the extension is attested only in Horobetsu colloquial in the sources
read here, and no modern grammar corroborates it for other Hokkaido dialects. It is
recorded as a single-source, dialect-specific datum ‹corpus-suggested›.

Size modification without suffixation: prenominal pon and poro

The grammatical resources for noun-level size reference extend beyond -po . The
prenominal property verbs pon be small and poro be big
modify a following noun in the same position that all attributive stative verbs occupy,
without morphological change nakagawa2024 264 (see
no-adjective-class-property-verbs and noun-phrase-structure ).

Three distinctions between the suffixal and prenominal strategies matter grammatically.
First, -po derives a new lexical item — a common noun in the concept form —
while the prenominal is a syntactic modifier that can be independently relativized, negated,
and causativized: porore 'make big' (causative of the property verb)
has no morphological parallel on a DIM derivative. Second, the corpus examples above show
pon stacking freely with DIM derivatives
( pon cikappo , pon ceppo ), confirming that
the two strategies are grammatically independent. Third, the suffix asymmetry —
-po for small, no suffix for large — is not mirrored at the prenominal level,
where poro covers large size just as freely as pon covers small.
The sources do not explain the origin of this one-sided suffix system ‹consensus›.

For the wider landscape of noun-deriving morphology, including deverbal noun formation and
the productive nominalizer suffixes, see deverbal-denominal-noun-derivation .
For verbal number suppletives whose selection interacts with nouns headed by DIM derivatives,
see suppletive-verbal-number .

title: Nominal Number: Transnumerality, -utar, and the Locus of Plurality
summary: The nominal number system — bare-noun general number/transnumerality, the human pluralizer -utar/utar(i), and the division of labour between nominal and verbal plurality.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Nominal Number: Transnumerality, -utar, and the Locus of Plurality
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: The nominal number system — bare-noun general number/transnumerality, the human pluralizer -utar/utar(i), and the division of labour between nominal and verbal plurality.

Transnumerality: nouns unspecified for number

Hokkaido Ainu nouns carry no grammatical number. A bare noun names a referent without
specifying whether one or several are intended: suma stone is the form for
'a stone', '(the) stone', and 'stones' alike; the same holds for
cep fish , cise house , and
kamuy spirit, deity nakagawa2024 259 ;
kindaichi1936 30 . In Corbett's typology this is the transnumeral
pattern: the noun is underspecified for number rather than syncretically encoding a
singular or plural value through a single form
corbett2000 9–11 . Obligatory singular/plural agreement of the kind
found in English or French is absent.

suma | stone | a stone / the stone / stones | The bare transnumeral form; no morphological distinction between singular and plural readings.

Plurality may be made explicit by the
formal-defective-nouns formal noun
utar plural; relatives, people , but this addition is strictly optional. In
the 209,878-sentence Hokkaido corpus utar records 8,050 tokens
(rank 30 among distinct types) — high frequency reflecting pragmatic salience, not
grammatical compulsion ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The formal noun utar — distributional status

utar belongs to the closed class of formal nouns — weakly
autonomous items that form an NP only when preceded by another element
nakagawa2024 257–258 ; nakagawa2022pos 481–482 . Its
syntactic distribution is uniquely wide within that class. Whereas
hi ('thing/matter') takes only a VP host, and
p/pe ('thing/one') takes a VP or prenominal,
utar follows a VP, a bare common noun, or a prenominal — the
only formal noun that can immediately follow a noun without an intervening predication
nakagawa2024 258 ; nakagawa2022pos 482 .

formal noun
meaning
follows VP
follows bare NP
follows prenominal

hi
thing / matter / time
✓
✗
✗

p/pe
thing / one
✓
✗
✓

kur
person
✓
✗
✓

utar
plural / people
✓
✓
✓

Sources: nakagawa2024 257–258 ; nakagawa2022pos 482 .

When following a bare noun, utar adds a collective or associative
plural reading without predicational content:

cikap-po utar | bird-DIM ASS | small birds | Bare-NP host: cikappo is a noun; utar follows directly without a finite predicate. Nakagawa ex. 333. | nakagawa2024:258 | SAR

The broader distribution of formal nouns is treated in
formal-defective-nouns . For the p/pe nominalizer
in clausal contexts see nominalization-p-pe .

Semantics: associative reading and the animacy debate

The meaning of utar in pluralizing use is associative rather than
strictly additive. Kindaichi and Chiri's example acha utar arki
translates as 'uncle and his group came', not 'several uncles came': the form implies a
cluster of associates gathered around the named referent, potentially including members of
different categories
kindaichi1936 30 . Ijäs's pedagogical grammar presents the same
associative reading ijas2023 .

Whether utar is restricted to animate referents is a genuine
disagreement ‹contested›. Kindaichi and Chiri state that utar
applies to persons and animals ( kamuy utar ,
ainu utar , seta utar 'dogs',
cikap utar 'birds'), and that
suma utar 'stones' and ni utar 'trees'
are disallowed unless the referent is personified
kindaichi1936 30–31 . Nakagawa, working from Saru material, takes the
opposite position: utar freely pluralizes inanimates
( suma utar , ni utar ,
rus utar 'furs', coypep utar
'lacquerware'), the extension to all entities motivated by the animist conception that
every being carries a spirit nakagawa2024 259 . The Saru corpus attests
inanimate utar directly:

a=kor rus utar | 4.A=have fur ASS | (one's) furs | utar on the inanimate host rus 'fur/pelt' — corpus evidence against the animacy restriction. Corresponds to Nakagawa 2024: 259 ex. 334. | biratori1969 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, 'Sisam ni nare ta oyaji' (1986); National Ainu Museum archive [nam-archive/kawakami-minwa/13]

The discrepancy most likely reflects dialect variation rather than a uniform pan-Hokkaido
norm: the animacy restriction appears stronger in eastern and classical-norm speech,
while Saru extends utar to inanimates within the animist
conceptual frame. That the textbook illustrations
suma utar and ni utar themselves are
constructed forms without corpus attestation (corpus search: 0 hits) underlines
that neither side of this debate rests on a large attested base ‹contested›.

hinak wa arki wen cikap-po utar | where from come.PL bad bird-DIM ASS | the bad little birds that had come from somewhere | utar follows the diminutive-marked noun cikappo 'small bird' as an animate host. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, 'uwepeker minwa 15'; ILCAA Ainu materials [aa-irc/022]

The lexicalized plural hekattar

One nominal has grammaticalized a dedicated plural form through phonological fusion with
utar : hekaci child + utar
contracts to hekattar children , which serves as the fixed plural of
'child' kindaichi1936 31 ; nakagawa2024 259 . The corpus
records 297 tokens of hekattar (rank 509), confirming
lexicalization ‹corpus-confirmed›. The fused form can itself be followed by another
utar — hekattar utar — for additional
collective emphasis kindaichi1936 31 .

inne hekattar arki | many child-ASS come.PL | many children came | hekattar is the lexicalized fusion of hekaci + utar. The suppletive plural verb arki cross-indexes a plural subject. Kindaichi & Chiri §37. | kindaichi1936:31 | HK

A separate nominal strategy for inherent plurality is reduplication:
chaichai 'twigs', kaukau 'hailstones',
kankan 'intestines', and ramram 'scales'
carry plural semantics through their reduplicated structure without requiring
utar
kindaichi1936 31 . These are lexicalized; reduplication is not a
productive synchronic plural strategy. The productive resource for ad hoc pluralization
remains utar or the verbal number morphology described in
suppletive-verbal-number .

utar and utari(hi): the form–meaning distinction

The string utar covers two related but functionally distinct
items. The first is the formal noun pluralizer described above. The second is a full
common noun meaning 'relatives, kin, companions', with its own
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative form
utari (long form utarihi ), which surfaces under
possession nakagawa2024 250–252 :

ku=kor utari | 1SG.A=have kin | my relatives / my family | utari is the affiliative form of utar 'relatives/group'; ku=kor is the alienable-possession construction. The first-person referent is not included in the group (Nakagawa 2024: 251).

The referential scope of k=utari is strictly third-person: in
k=utari ku=tura 'I took my family', the speaker does not belong
to the group named. Likewise, a=utari opitta totek wa oka 'is
your family all well?' takes a third-person verb because the referent is the addressee's
relatives, not an inclusive 'you all' nakagawa2024 251 . The
-utari that enters Tokachi anutari 'we
(inclusive)' combined with a fourth-person verb edges toward pronominal function —
Nakagawa characterises the form as 'halfway between pronoun and common noun'
nakagawa2024 252 — but the full treatment of pronominal
-utari belongs to
independent-personal-pronouns .

The corpus records 1,501 tokens of utari (rank 130), mainly as
a possessed group or kinship noun. Community analysis has proposed that the affiliative
form is nearly obligatory in this function because 'companions belonging to no one' is
semantically anomalous — the noun almost always surfaces possessed
aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, 2025) ‹contested›. This aligns with the
general behaviour of the affiliative form across the Ainu noun paradigm: the possessed
shape presupposes a contextually available referent to which the named entity belongs
nakagawa2024 251–252 .

okkaypo utari ne yakka | young.man group COP even.though | even though they are young men | utari in a collective predication: 'young men (as a group)'. Extract from a larger sentence; ne is the copula. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, 'uwepeker minwa 7'; ILCAA Ainu materials [aa-irc/005]

The semantics of utar as a common noun extends to kinship idioms.
utar ni nare 'become utar with someone'
carries the meaning 'to marry (into a family)', since marriage incorporates one into a
kin group aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, 2025) ‹corpus-suggested›.
Kinship compounds on -utari can also name a pair rather than a
plurality of the base: onautari and
unuutari (literally 'father-group' and 'mother-group') denote
'parents' collectively aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2026)
‹corpus-suggested›. For kinship-term morphology in detail see
kinship-honorific-possession .

Locus of plurality: utar vs verbal number

When plurality is information-structurally relevant, Hokkaido Ainu has two independent
resources: nominal utar and the verb's own number morphology —
suppletive stem alternations ( ek /
arki 'come', arpa /
paye 'go') and the plural suffix
-pa nakagawa2024 141–159 . The two are
independent: a noun phrase can carry utar without triggering a
plural verb, and a plural verb can appear without nominal
utar . Certain mass-like nouns — supuya
'smoke', hura 'smell' — consistently govern plural verb forms
( supuya at , hura at ) even without
utar , encoding the diffuse nature of the referent at the verb
rather than the noun kindaichi1936 31 .

The plural verb stem is also sensitive to quantitative context. Small, specified counts
(two, three) regularly co-occur with the singular stem
( tu hekaci ek 'two children came'), while large or unspecified
quantities take the plural stem ( inne hekattar arki 'many
children came'); uncountable referents remain with the singular stem even alongside
'many' aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024; attributed to Satō)
‹corpus-suggested›. On transitive verbs the plural stem tracks plurality of the
object , following the absolutive-aligned pattern:
rayke (singular object) / ronnu (plural
object) aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023) ‹corpus-confirmed›. These
agreement thresholds and the S/O alignment of verbal number are developed in
person-number-interactions and suppletive-verbal-number .

In verse, speakers sometimes apply utar to a semantically
singular referent for metrical reasons, or omit it from a clearly plural one —
evidence that the form adds meaning rather than enforcing obligatory number agreement
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024) ‹contested›.

Sakhalin contrast

The Sakhalin varieties develop the cognate of utar into a more
morphologically explicit system. West Sakhalin has a suffix
-(a)hcin (East -(a)hsin ) attaching to
the affiliative form of the noun — eci=setaha-hcin 'your dogs',
an=niyehe-hcin 'our firewood' — alongside
-utara as a separate suffix
murasaki1979 85 ; nakagawa2024 259–261 .
Sakaguchi distinguishes the two Sakhalin suffixes: -(a)hcin
marks a homogeneous plural (all members standing in the same possessive relation),
while -utara may mark homogeneous or approximative plurality
and extends to personal names sakaguchi2024 12 ; baek2021 73 . Despite the richer morphology, Sakhalin number remains optional and non-obligatory:
-(a)hcin can attach even to nominalized clauses, so Sakhalin
too lacks a grammatical number category in the strict typological sense
nakagawa2024 261 . An etymology linking Sakhalin
utah to Hokkaido utar through historical
final *r has been proposed but is disputed on
comparative-phonological grounds
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024) ‹contested›.

title: Nominal Subclasses and Obligatorily-Possessed (Bound) Nouns
summary: The internal taxonomy of nouns — concrete vs abstract, common vs proper, and the class of 'incomplete'/bound nouns that cannot stand without a possessor.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Nominal Subclasses and Obligatorily-Possessed (Bound) Nouns
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: The internal taxonomy of nouns — concrete vs abstract, common vs proper, and the class of 'incomplete'/bound nouns that cannot stand without a possessor.

The four noun subclasses

A noun is defined as a word that can form an NP and serve as a verb's subject or object
nakagawa2024 214 . Within the class, four subclasses are distinguished by
two crossing diagnostics: whether the form can stand alone as an NP argument, and whether
it can serve as the head of a relative-clause structure
nakagawa2024 215–216 ; nakagawa2022pos 480–482 .
Crossing these two properties against the type of person affix the form takes and its
status as a locative expression (⟨place⟩) yields a six-row grid.

subclass
form
stands alone as argument?
can head a relative clause?
person affix type
functions as ⟨place⟩?

common noun (普通名詞)
concept form (概念形)
○
○

The rows fall into an autonomy hierarchy. Common nouns in the concept form are the most
independent; common nouns in the affiliative form presuppose a possessor relation but can
still stand as NP arguments. Locative nouns are always ⟨place⟩ and take objective rather
than nominative person affixes; the Type-1 basic form is the least autonomous among the
locative forms, requiring an antecedent NP. Pronouns can stand alone but cannot be
relativised. Formal nouns are the least autonomous of all: they require a preceding
modifier or clause and cannot appear as bare NP arguments
nakagawa2024 215–216 .

Pronouns and formal nouns receive full treatments in separate chapters:
independent-personal-pronouns and formal-defective-nouns .
Locative nouns are introduced in this chapter and developed in detail in
relational-and-spatial-nouns .

Common nouns: concept form and affiliative form

Every common noun has two forms. The concept form (概念形) presents the referent without
anchoring it relationally to anything else. The
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative form (所属形) presents it in
relation to a possessor or reference point, and it is the form that carries a personal
prefix indexing that possessor. Kindaichi & Chiri used the terms 抽象形 (abstract
form) and 具体形 (concrete form) for the same distinction
kindaichi1936 16 ; the current terminology, following Nakagawa and the
Bugaeva Handbook, replaces those labels without changing the underlying analysis
nakagawa2024 217 ; nakagawa2022pos 484 .

Three possession constructions are built on this opposition
nakagawa2024 217–219 :

#
construction
N₂ form
relation expressed
example

(1)
N₁ kor N₂ / affix= kor N₂
concept form
alienable
a=kor cip 'my/his boat'

(2)
N₁ N₂ -affix / affix=N₂ -affix
affiliative form
inalienable
ku=tekehe 'my hand'

(3)
N₁ N₂ (juxtaposition, no affix)
concept form
attributive (not possession)
menoko tek 'a woman's hand'

a=kor cip | 4.A=have boat | my/his boat | Alienable possession: the possessed noun cip 'boat' stays in the concept form; the possessor prefix a= (4th person, here functioning as a generic 'my/his') attaches to the possession verb kor.

ku=teke-he | 1SG.A=hand-POSS | my hand | Inalienable possession: the affiliative suffix -he attaches to the root teke, and the 1SG possessive prefix ku= marks the possessor. The concept form *ku=tek with a direct prefix is ungrammatical in this possessive sense.

menoko tek | woman hand | a woman's hand (attributive, not possessed) | Attributive juxtaposition, construction (3): menoko 'woman' specifies the attribute of tek 'hand' but is not its possessor in the grammatical sense. No person prefix can substitute for menoko here: *ku=tek is ungrammatical as a possessive.

The decisive diagnostic distinguishing (2) from (3) is the person-prefix substitution
test: the first NP of an inalienable construction can be replaced by a person prefix
( ku=sikihi 'my eye' — concept form sik ,
affiliative sikihi ), whereas the first noun of an attributive
construction cannot ( *ku=rus for 'bear-pelt'; only
kamuy rus or kamuy rusihi 'that specific
bear's pelt' is possible) nakagawa2024 218 . The morphophonology of
affiliative-form formation — the vowel-copy and echo-vowel suffixes — is treated in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology . The full semantics of the
concept/affiliative opposition, including its non-possessive (anaphoric and definiteness)
uses, are developed in concept-form-affiliative-form .

Inalienable nouns and the bound class

The inalienable construction (construction 2) covers several semantic fields. Nakagawa
identifies five main classes that favour the affiliative form when possessed:
(a) body parts and items typically worn or carried
( ku=tekehe 'my hand',
a=tesmaha 'my snowshoes');
(b) part-of-whole nouns
( op nicihi 'spear-shaft',
sayo rurihi 'gruel broth');
(c) a subset of kinship terms (see below);
(d) material-to-product nouns
( ikaray ohawe 'nettle stew',
top cise 'bamboo house');
and (e) a miscellaneous set including
ataye 'price',
hura smell (affiliative huraha ), and
kotanu 'village'
nakagawa2024 219–220 .

Within the broader inalienable class, a distinction can be drawn by degree of boundness.
Common nouns in the body-part and part-whole classes retain an independent concept form
that can appear in generic or classifier-like contexts: tek
'hand' can appear as a bare NP argument. By contrast, the Type-1 basic forms of locative
nouns (§ relational-and-spatial-nouns ) cannot appear at all as NP arguments
without an immediately preceding antecedent, and formal nouns cannot appear without a
preceding clause or prenominal modifier. These two groups represent the strictly bound
end of a gradient from "tends to be possessed" to "cannot appear without an antecedent"
nakagawa2024 238–240, 257 .

Conditions on the affiliative form

A long-standing descriptive question concerns what triggers the affiliative form
specifically. Tamura (1988a: 27–28, as reported by Nakagawa 2024: 228) proposed a
specificity condition: N₂ appears in the affiliative form only when N₁ is specific
and individuated ( kamuy rusihi 'that specific bear's pelt' vs
kamuy rus 'bear-pelt in general', which is almost a compound)
‹contested›. Satō objects that the affiliative does appear with non-specific possessors
— aynu opitta honihi kor 'all humans have a belly' — which
would be excluded by a strict specificity condition
nakagawa2024 228–229 ‹contested›. Nakagawa's 2024 account resolves
the tension by placing the conditioning in morphosyntax rather than semantics: whenever
a person prefix is present, the affiliative form is required; specificity then emerges
as a tendency rather than a categorical trigger, because inalienable possession of an
individuated entity is simply the most common context for person-prefix use
nakagawa2024 228–230 .

Nakagawa further notes that the sharp "has/lacks affiliative form" dichotomy, sometimes
presented as a fixed lexical property, is overstated: given an appropriate relational
context, most common nouns can in principle take an affiliative form
nakagawa2024 227 . The noun
utar plural marker / people / family illustrates the gradient: as a
formal-noun plural marker utar is uninflected, but as a common
noun 'relatives, companions' it has an affiliative form
utari(hi) . Community discussion has noted that, because it is
hard to conceive of 'companions not belonging to anyone', this common noun surfaces
almost exclusively in its affiliative form as 'N's family/companions'
aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, 2025-09-17) ‹contested›, extending
Nakagawa's account of the same ambiguity at nakagawa2024 251–252 .

Kinship terms: two grammatical classes

Kinship terms split into two sets with different possession constructions, and the
assignment is lexically specified. Terms using the
kor -construction (concept form): ku=kor
hapo 'my mother', ku=kor mici 'my father'. Terms using
the affiliative construction: ku=turesi 'my younger sister
(sister's viewpoint)', ku=yupihi 'my elder brother'
nakagawa2024 221–222 . Within Hokkaido there is considerable dialect
variation in the assignment of individual kinship terms to one set or the other
nakagawa2024 219–222 .

A further division concerns address vs reference. Several kinship terms — among them
ona 'father', unu 'mother',
yup 'elder brother', and sa 'elder
sister' — are restricted to third-person reference and cannot be used in direct
address; each has an address counterpart. The restriction correlates with the
grammatical pattern: only the affiliative form combines with a person prefix, so a
term that cannot be addressed directly tends not to appear with first- or
second-person possessor marking
nakagawa2024 221–223 . Kinship possession in full, including
suppletive address forms and the dialect-by-dialect distributions, is treated in
kinship-honorific-possession .

Membership, gradience, and dialect variation

Inalienable-class membership is partly dialect-specific and partly lexeme-specific.
kotan village, settlement takes both the
kor -construction and the affiliative construction equally in
Saru; mosir world, land uses only the
kor -construction ( a=kor mosir ;
*a=mosirihi is ungrammatical)
nakagawa2024 220 . These lexeme-specific idiosyncrasies are part of the
lexical entry and are not predictable from semantics alone. Refsing notes comparable
fluctuation in Shizunai, where some body-part stems allow either construction
refsing1986 74–76 .

The full alienable/inalienable distinction, adnominal possession constructions,
and the interaction of possession with nominal number and person marking are
developed in alienable-inalienable-split ,
concept-form-affiliative-form , and
adnominal-possession-double-marking .

Locational and relational nouns

Locative nouns form a small, closed subclass expressing spatial and temporal relations
('above', 'below', 'in front of', 'beside', 'inside'). Four properties set them apart
from common nouns nakagawa2024 238–240 ; nakagawa2022pos 490–491 :

The basic form cannot stand alone as an NP. Type-1 locative nouns
require an immediately preceding antecedent NP (the spatial reference point). Without
it, *ka wa ran 'came down from above' with bare
ka is ungrammatical; only the long form can head a clause
independently (§ below).

Long-form derivation differs from the common-noun affiliative.
Common nouns use a vowel-copy suffix (the -hV mechanism)
predictable from the stem's final vowel; locative long forms use a distinct suffix
set — -ke , -V ,
-si — that is lexically specified per item and follows
different morphophonological patterns. The overlap between the two systems may be
historical: Nakagawa hypothesises that common-noun type-③-2 morphology was borrowed
from the locative system nakagawa2024 226, 247 ‹speculative›.

Both forms take the objective person affix. Common nouns in the
affiliative construction take the nominative (possessive) prefix; locative nouns take
the objective prefix:
en=sam un 'to beside me'
(1SG.O prefix en= on sam beside )
vs ku=tekehe 'my hand'
(1SG.A/possessive prefix ku= on tek hand )
nakagawa2024 241 .

Both forms always function as ⟨place⟩. Locative nouns can be
directly followed by the locative particle
ta : kasi ta 'on top of it'. A
common noun cannot do this: *nupuri ta is ungrammatical;
'at the mountain' requires an intervening locative noun,
nupuri or ta ('at [the inside of] the mountain')
nakagawa2024 216, 241 .

Type-1 and Type-2 locative nouns

Nakagawa further divides locative nouns into two types
nakagawa2024 241–246 . Type-1 nouns require an immediately preceding
antecedent NP in the basic form; in the long form they can head a clause alone.
Type-2 nouns can stand in the basic form without naming a reference point, making them
syntactically closer to common nouns, though they retain the locative-noun properties
of ⟨place⟩ status and objective-affix agreement.

The distinction between etok (front of a moving entity or
linear path; temporal 'before') and kotca (front of a
stationary entity) carries real semantic weight:
i=etok omare 'placed in front of me [as a moving being]'
means the referent was set ahead on the path, not merely in the visual field
nakagawa2024 243 . Similarly, oka (back of
a moving entity; temporal 'after') and osmak (back of a
stationary entity) form a paired contrast.

The locative noun or inside, at the location of has developed an
extended use in several Hokkaido dialects, functioning as a broad oblique marker;
its grammaticalization in Chitose is documented in sato2021 .

kasi ta as=an | above LOC stand=4.S | I stood on top of it. | The long form kasi (

The Type-2 classification controversy

Some researchers classify Type-2 locative nouns — kim ,
soy , rik , and the like — as common
nouns on the grounds that they appear independently in the basic form, a property
otherwise confined to common nouns. Nakagawa retains them as locative nouns on two
grounds: their long-form morphology follows the locative-noun pattern rather than the
common-noun affiliative system, and their obligatory ⟨place⟩ status distinguishes them
from any common noun, which would require a locative noun to mediate its use with
ta nakagawa2024 245–246 ‹contested›.

One community observation proposes that the long forms of locative nouns carry a
definiteness or high-specificity marking, which might explain why first- and
second-person pronominal reference is expressed on the long form rather than the basic
form aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024-11-17) ‹speculative›.
This hypothesis is not attested in the primary-source grammars; it awaits
cross-dialectal elicitation.

The directional adverbs derived from Type-2 forms
( ekimne 'toward the mountains',
okimne 'from the mountains';
esoyne 'outward') and the full locative-noun
possessive paradigm are treated in relational-and-spatial-nouns .

Common vs proper nouns

Proper nouns form no grammatically distinct class in Hokkaido Ainu: they are an open
subset of common nouns, distinguished by reference type rather than by any morphological
marker, case distinction, or definiteness operator
nakagawa2024 233 ; nakagawa2022pos 483–484 .
Many place names are transparently compositional:
Poropet 'big river' ( poro + pet river ),
Siretoko 'tip of the land'
( sir land, appearance + etoko )
nakagawa2024 233 .

One property that distinguishes proper nouns from common nouns in practice: Hokkaido
personal and place names do not appear in affiliative forms under normal conditions,
unlike many common nouns
nakagawa2024 233 . Sakhalin Ainu permits affiliative forms of personal
names — ku=Harumihi 'my Harumi' is attested
murasaki1979 — but this pattern is absent from the Hokkaido data.

Personal-name avoidance, the practice of late and deliberately unflattering naming to
deflect malevolent attention, and the classification of kamuy names as proper vs common
reflect cultural conventions rather than grammatical rules
nakagawa2024 234–236 .

Pronouns and formal nouns in the subclass system

Personal pronouns — kani 'I', eani 'you
(2SG)', coka 'we (exclusive)' — share the
common-noun property of standing alone as NP arguments but differ from common nouns in
that they cannot head relative clauses: there is no grammatical Ainu equivalent of
English 'the I who is sleeping' nakagawa2022pos 482 . Their paradigms,
dialect variants, and the fourth-person forms are treated in
independent-personal-pronouns .

Formal nouns — also glossed as noun particles (名助詞) — occupy the bottom of the
autonomy hierarchy. Unable to appear as bare NP arguments, they nominalise a preceding
clause or prenominal element into an NP head
nakagawa2024 257–258 . The class includes
p thing (after vowel) ~ pe thing (after consonant) ,
hi thing, matter, time, place ,
katu state, manner ,
kur person ,
utar plural marker / people , and the evidential nominalizers
ruwe trace, track (possessed form: the trace of —) ,
siri appearance (possessed form: the appearance of —) ,
hawe voice (possessed form: the voice of —) , and
humi sound, sensation (possessed form: the sensation of —) ,
whose possessed forms build the evidential paradigm
nakagawa2024 258 .
The class is defined and its membership criteria set out in
formal-defective-nouns ; the nominalization function of
p/pe and hi in relative-clause and
complement constructions is treated in nominalization-p-pe ; the four
evidential nominalizers are treated in evidential-schema-overview ,
ruwe-ne-inferential , siri-ne-situational ,
hawe-ne-reportative , and humi-ne-sensory .

title: The Nominalizers -p/-pe and -i/-hi (Participant, Event, Place, Fact)
summary: The participant nominalizer -p/-pe ('the one that') and the abstract/event nominalizer -i/-hi ('the V-ing / place / time / fact that'): argument-role readings, the headless-relative continuum, internal clause TAM/person, and the insubordination of nominalized clauses.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Nominalizers -p/-pe and -i/-hi (Participant, Event, Place, Fact)
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The participant nominalizer -p/-pe ('the one that') and the abstract/event nominalizer -i/-hi ('the V-ing / place / time / fact that'): argument-role readings, the headless-relative continuum, internal clause TAM/person, and the insubordination of nominalized clauses.

Form and place among the formal nouns

The nominalizer p ~ pe turns a finite clause into a
noun phrase. It is a member of the closed class of
formal-defective-nouns formal nouns — light, weakly autonomous nouns placed after
another element to build an NP — and it supplies a generic nominal head meaning, in its concrete
uses, ‘the one who’ or ‘the thing that’. Hokkaido Ainu has no participial or otherwise
non-finite verb form for this work: the verb inside the nominalized phrase keeps its ordinary
person marking, polarity, number, and clause-internal morphology, and subordination is signalled
by the following formal noun rather than by any change in the verb
bugaeva2016 100, 226–228 ; nakagawa2024 503, 512–513 ; sato2008 174–175 .

The allomorphy is phonologically conditioned: the short form -p follows a
stem-final vowel, and -pe follows a stem-final consonant. The same split
holds whether the host is a single stative stem ( poro-p ‘the big one’,
pon-pe ‘the small one’) or a full clause ( a=e p
‘what one eats’, okay pe ‘what one carries’)
sato2008 175 ; refsing1986 260 ; bugaeva2022 ch. 14 .
Satō records a sandhi variant pa at the junction of
p and a following conjunctive wa
sato2008 175 .

stem-final segment
allomorph
representative forms

vowel
-p / clausal p

ami-p ‘clothes’, poro-p ‘the big one’,
a=e p ‘what one eats’

consonant
-pe / clausal pe

an-pe ‘one that is’, mokor-pe ‘the sleeping
one’, okay pe ‘what one carries’

The form spans lexical derivation and syntax without a break. Shibatani describes
-p(e) as deriving a noun for a person or thing characterized by the verb,
as in pirka-p ‘good thing’ and ray-pe ‘the dead’,
and as a productive phrasal and clausal nominalizer besides
shibatani1990 33–34 . In Bugaeva's areal account of nominalization, Hokkaido
expresses clausal nominalization through a nominalizing word placed after the clause, the type
[(np…) V p/pe] , a stage from which lexical items such as
aep food have crystallized; the Sakhalin varieties extended the same system to
possessed verbal forms bugaeva2016 243, 385–397 . Among the formal nouns,
p ~ pe keeps a ‘thing/person’ profile beside the
maximally abstract hi and the sensory-evidential heads
ruwe , siri , hawe , and
humi , whose inventory and possessed morphology are set out in
formal-defective-nouns and whose clause-final evidential use is treated in
evidential-schema-overview .

Participant nominalization: the one who, the thing that

In its participant reading, p ~ pe is a generic
head whose referent is fixed by the argument structure of the clause before it. The clause
contains a gap, and the role of that gap — agent, patient, or an oblique participant — is
recovered from the verb's valence and from any postposition left in place
nakagawa2024 512 ; bugaeva2015 47 . This is the headless edge of
the relative-clause system: where an ordinary relative clause precedes a lexical head noun,
p ~ pe supplies the head itself. The gap strategy,
the accessibility hierarchy, and the postposition-retention facts are developed in
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses , and the place of the headless type within the wider
typology of noun-modifying clauses in
headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses .

tapan kina a=e p ne toan kina somo a=e p ne | this plant 4.A=eat NMLZ COP that plant NEG 4.A=eat NMLZ COP | This plant is one that people eat; that plant is one that people do not eat. | The fourth person a= gives a generic human eater; p is the object head of each clause — the thing eaten. The vowel-final e selects the short allomorph -p. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 14 (first half) [aa-irc/020#27]

a=se wa okay pe | 4.A=carry.on.back CONJ exist.PL NMLZ | the things one is carrying along | pe is the object of se and the subject of okay; the consonant-final okay selects the allomorph -pe. | nakagawa2024:512 | HK

Lexicalized items preserve the syntax at word scale. aep food is
a=e-p , ‘thing one eats’, with the fourth-person transitive prefix and the
nominalizer bugaeva2016 245–251 . Nakagawa's contrast between the synthesized
a=e-suke-p ‘kitchen utensil’ and the compound
sukemakiri ‘kitchen knife’ shows the diagnostic: the relative-clause
nominalization keeps the applicative e- that licenses the instrument it
names, whereas a compound leaves the relation between its two nouns grammatically unspecified
nakagawa2024 506–507 . The lexicalized inventory is treated in
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

a=e-suke-p | 4.A=APPL-cook-NMLZ | kitchen utensil | thing one cooks with | The applicative e- licenses the instrumental participant that p nominalizes; the plain compound sukemakiri has no such prefix. | nakagawa2024:507; kayano1996:281 | SAR

An oblique participant is nominalized the same way, with the governing postposition retained
inside the clause so that the relation between head and predicate stays overt. With an
instrument the postposition ani ‘with’ remains, and
p names the instrument nakagawa2024 504 ; bugaeva2015 47–49 ; keenancomrie1977 .

ani usey a=ku p | INS hot.water 4.A=drink NMLZ | a thing to drink hot water with | The instrumental ani stays inside the clause; p is the generic instrument head, the object of ku 'drink'. | nakagawa2024:504 | HK

Ijäs teaches the same rule for relative clauses generally: the modifying verb phrase precedes
the head, there is no relative pronoun, and an oblique head keeps the postposition fronted within
the clause ijas2023 . Her examples are constructed teaching material and align with
Nakagawa, Satō, and Bugaeva.

ani cep ku=tuypa makiri | INS fish 1SG.A=cut knife | the knife I cut fish with | Ijäs Lesson 11. The instrumental ani is fronted and retained; the head here is the common noun makiri, the same gap strategy that p and pe carry without a lexical head. | ijas2023 | HK

Event and fact nominalization

When the clause before it is already saturated — every argument the verbs require is present —
p ~ pe nominalizes the event or fact as a whole and
serves as a complementizer rather than a participant head. Nakagawa treats some formal nouns,
p ~ pe among them, as filling this complementizing
role; Satō draws the line explicitly between a nominalizer ( 名詞化辞 ) that
turns a whole content clause into a noun phrase and a formal noun that heads a relative clause,
the first allowing no reconstruction of an underlying sentence nakagawa2024 503–509, 512–513 ; sato2008 174–180 .

ne kusu haweoka wa cis pa p ka a=erampewtek | COP because speak.PL CONJ cry PL NMLZ even 4.A=not.understand | I did not even understand why they were crying. | p is not an argument of speak or cry; the clause haweoka wa cis pa is complete (arity 0). p nominalizes the whole reason clause, which is then the object of a=erampewtek. | nakagawa2024:512–513 | HK

ratcitara rok=an pe ne na | quietly sit=4.S NMLZ COP FIN | One should sit quietly. | pe nominalizes a saturated intransitive; the predication pe ne na carries the deontic 'should' reading of Satō's content-clause use. | sato2008:177 | CHI

In this use p ~ pe stands beside the other
complementizing formal nouns — abstract hi and the sensory heads — which
differ in the construal they impose. The complement-taking predicates and the control and
raising diagnostics are treated in nominalized-complements-control-raising ; the
future-oriented sequence kuni p in
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements ; the quotative complementizer
sekor and the indirect-speech markers in
quotative-complementation-sekor ; and the clause-final evidential reflexes of the
sensory heads in ruwe-ne-inferential the inferential ruwe ne .

a=e=túskote kuni p ne ru-we ne na | 4.A=2SG.O=tie.up PURP NMLZ COP track-POSS COP FIN | You will surely be tied up. | The fourth-person agent a= with the second-person object e= yields a passive-like reading; kuni p forms a future/deontic nominal, and the framing predicate ru-we ne presents it as established fact. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2 [aa-irc/004#130]

Arity and the boundary between the two uses

Nakagawa's valence arithmetic keeps the participant and complementizer uses from collapsing into
one reading. A formal noun has arity −1, on a par with hi and
uske ; a clause that still requires a participant has positive arity, and
the formal noun fills the open slot, giving a relative-clause NP; a clause of arity 0 is complete,
and the formal noun nominalizes the whole of it, giving an event or fact nominal nakagawa2024 261–262, 504, 507 . The surface string [clause] p/pe is identical in both; the
grammatical relation differs.

clause before p ~ pe
role of p ~ pe
resulting NP
treated in

arity ≥ +1 (a participant slot is open)
generic head, ‘the one who / the thing that’
headless relative-clause NP
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses

arity 0 (the clause is saturated)
complementizer over the whole clause
event or fact nominal
nominalized-complements-control-raising

single stem or lexicalized predicate
derivational nominalizer
lexical noun
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization

Analyses and open questions

The descriptions agree on the substance — p ~ pe
builds lexical, phrasal, and clausal nominals, heads a generic participant in a relative clause,
and nominalizes a saturated clause as an event or fact — and differ on the labels. Shibatani and
Refsing use ‘nominalizer’ broadly; Satō separates the whole-clause nominalizer from the
relative-clause-heading formal noun; Nakagawa makes the formal noun a word class and the
complementizer a syntactic role available to some of its members; Bugaeva treats these light
bound nouns as the generic heads of gapped relative clauses shibatani1990 33–34 ;
refsing1986 259–264 ; sato2008 174–175 ; nakagawa2024 508 ; bugaeva2015 47 ‹contested›.

The constituency of the nominalized clause plus a following copula — the frame
[clause] p/pe ne — is the deeper disagreement, and it is the same one that
runs through the evidential system. One line analyses the structure as biclausal, a formal or
possessed noun heading the preceding clause and closed by the copula; another holds that the
erstwhile noun is grammaticalizing toward a monoclausal complex predicate. No source read here
offers a constituency proof that decides between them tamura2000 227 ; sato2008 175 ; bugaeva2016 306–315 ; dalcorso2018 24–25 ‹contested›.

Ijäs's observations on relative clauses fit this system, with two cautions. Her report that the
subject of a ditransitive resists being the head matches Bugaeva's finding that no example of an
A-of-three-place-verb head is attested; the construction is an attested gap rather than a settled
ungrammaticality ‹corpus-confirmed›. Her difficulty in finding ablative
wa(no) ‘from which’ or mutative ne ‘as which’ as
relative-clause heads is shared by the sources read here, so the forms she suggests for them are
left aside rather than presented as established ‹corpus-suggested› ijas2023 ; bugaeva2015 48 .

The same nominalizer feeds clause linkage at the far end of the cline: a bleached
p ~ pe works as an adversative conjunction
‘though, and yet’, no longer an argument of the following clause, and the Horobetsu nominalizers
grammaticalize a step further into adverbial-clause markers nakagawa2024 515–516 ;
takahashi2018 107–112 .

Token frequency confirms that pe is common but cannot measure this
construction directly. The corpus word-frequency table returns 13,249 tokens of
pe (rank 15), a figure that folds together the formal noun, the numeral
counter -pe , and lexical pe ‘water’, while the
short allomorph p is obscured by segmentation. A per-function count would
require construction-level annotation ‹corpus-suggested›.

title: Nominalized Complements and Control/Raising
summary: Complement clauses formed by nominalization (hi, -i, ruwe) under perception, cognition, and evaluative predicates, and the control/raising diagnostics of same-subject complex predicates.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Nominalized Complements and Control/Raising
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
