Summary: Complement clauses formed by nominalization (hi, -i, ruwe) under perception, cognition, and evaluative predicates, and the control/raising diagnostics of same-subject complex predicates.

Overview

Clausal complements in Hokkaido Ainu are formed through the same nominalization strategy that
builds relative-clause NPs: a finite, person-marked clause is followed by a formal noun or
complementizer that turns the whole into a nominal argument of a higher predicate. The verb inside
the embedded clause keeps its ordinary person affixes, polarity, and aspect morphology; no
non-finite or participial form exists
bugaeva2016 100, 226–228 ; nakagawa2024 503 . What separates a
complement clause from a relative clause is a single structural condition, the
arity of the embedded clause, treated in the section that follows. The inventory of
complementizers includes the abstract formal noun hi thing, fact, time, place ,
the sensory possessive forms siri appearance , hawe voice , and
humi sound, feeling , the concrete nominalizer p (thing) ~ pe (thing/person) , and the irrealis complementizer kuni (purposive/deontic) , together with a
set of indirect-speech markers treated in quotative-complementation-sekor . Their
grammaticalization from lexical nouns and their role in evidential clause-final constructions are
set out in formal-defective-nouns and evidential-schema-overview ;
p ~ pe as a headless relative-clause nominalizer is treated in
nominalization-p-pe , which cross-references the present chapter for the
complementizer use.

The arity-0 diagnostic

Nakagawa's arity (アリティ) accounting provides the structural boundary between a
relative clause and a complement clause. Every NP in the clause contributes −1 to a running
count; every argument slot required by the verb contributes +1. A formal noun itself has arity
−1. When the clause before a formal noun has total arity ≥ +1, a participant slot remains open
and the formal noun fills it as the head of a relative-clause NP. When the clause already has
arity 0 — every argument slot is satisfied — the formal noun nominalizes the whole clause as an
event or fact; this is the complement reading
nakagawa2024 261–262, 504, 507 .

arity of the embedded clause
role of the following formal noun
construction type

≥ +1 (a participant slot is unfilled)
head of the relative clause, fills the open slot
relative-clause NP → prenominal-gap-relative-clauses

0 (the clause is fully saturated)
complementizer, nominalizes the whole clause
complement clause (this chapter)

Satō's formulation of the same distinction is categorical: a nominalizer (名詞化辞)
nominalizes a whole content clause and allows no reconstruction of an underlying sentence,
whereas a formal noun (形式名詞) heading a relative clause can be so reconstructed
sato2008 174–175 . Nakagawa prefers to treat the formal nouns as a word class and
the complementizer function as a syntactic role available to some members of that class
nakagawa2024 508 . The same surface string [clause] hi
or [clause] p is parsed differently depending on which reading the arity
yields; examples below illustrate both.

The following illustrates the complement reading of hi . The embedded
clause hekattar tura sinot=an has arity 0:
sinot 'play' is an intransitive verb (+1) whose S slot is satisfied by
the fourth-person suffix =an (−1), leaving no gap.
hi therefore nominalizes the whole clause as a fact:

hekattar tura sinot=an hi ka a=onaha kopan | children COMIT play=4.S NMLZ even 4.A=father refuse | My father refused even that I play with the children. | The embedded clause hekattar tura sinot=an has arity 0 and hi nominalizes it as a fact-complement. Nakagawa's arity count: sinot (+1) with =an (−1) = 0. hi cannot be a relative-clause head here because there is no open slot to fill. | nakagawa2024:507 | HK

Complement-taking predicates

The matrix predicates that select a nominalized complement fall into three semantic groups:
cognition, perception, and speech/directive. A verb's argument structure places an upper bound
on the number of person affixes it can carry — typically one for the agent (A) and one for the
object (O); the complement clause then occupies the single object slot
nakagawa2024 503–509 . This is directly related to the
person-marking-architecture two-affix cap on the verb template .

predicate
gloss
preferred complementizer(s)
semantic class

nukar
see
sir-i , hi
visual perception

nu
hear, ask about
haw-e , hum-i , hi
auditory/non-visual perception

ramu
think
kuni , kunak , yak , sir-i , hum-i
cognition

eramiskari / eramuskari
not know, not understand
hi , p ~ pe
cognition (negative)

eraman / eramuan
understand, know
hi , p ~ pe
cognition

kopan
refuse, not want
hi
volition

ye
say, tell
kuni , kunak , yak , kusu
speech

eyam
worry
yak
emotive

takar
dream
yak
emotive/cognitive

The list above is drawn from Nakagawa and Satō and is not exhaustive
nakagawa2024 475–480, 503–513 ; sato2008 175–178 . The
complementizer preferences are typical but not always categorical: ramu ,
for instance, co-occurs with kuni for future and wished content, with
yak for past or ongoing content, and with the sensory heads
sir-i and hum-i when thought involves a
quasi-perceptual quality ‹corpus-suggested›.

hi as complementizer

The formal noun hi thing, fact, time, place is the maximally abstract
complementizer. Satō describes it as carrying "almost no concrete meaning" and functioning to
nominalize the entire clause before it
sato2008 169, 174 ; Nakagawa calls it a 補文標識 (complementizer) when its
preceding clause has arity 0
nakagawa2024 507 . In this role it appears most frequently as the object of
cognition predicates such as eramiskari 'not understand' and
kopan 'refuse':

a=ye hi ka a=kar hi ka a=eramiskari | 4.A=say NMLZ even 4.A=do NMLZ even 4.A=not.understand | not knowing what to say or what to do | Two parallel hi complements coordinated with ka 'even/also': a=ye hi 'the saying-thing' and a=kar hi 'the doing-thing' both serve as objects of a=eramiskari. Each embedded clause has arity 0 (the 4th-person agent a= fills the A slot of a transitive verb). | ninjal2003 | CHI | Oda Ito, アイヌ語口承文芸コーパス (NINJAL) [ninjal-folklore/026#61]

A nominalized clause headed by hi can also serve as the grammatical
subject of a stative predication. Bugaeva cites (after Tamura) a clausal-subject construction
in which a coordinated VP is nominalized by hi and predicated as 'best':

ku=kemeyki kor ku=itak hi iyotta pirka | 1SG.A=sew while 1SG.A=speak NMLZ most be.good | Speaking while sewing is best. | After Tamura (2000: 126). hi nominalizes the saturated coordinated clause ku=kemeyki kor ku=itak (arity 0: each transitive verb has its A slot filled by ku=). The nominalized NP is the clausal subject of the predication iyotta pirka 'is best'. | bugaeva2016:261 | SAR

The Okuda–Nakagawa analysis of hi distinguishes a 'place where' reading
(arity ≥ +1, RC head, requires a case postposition such as oro ta ) from
a 'time/thing/fact' reading (arity 0, complementizer, no postposition required)
nakagawa2024 510–511 . This distinction is a tendency, not absolute: the
Horobetsu example seta utar osoma hi 'the dogs' privy' (literally 'the
place dogs defecate') lacks oro ta yet takes the 'place' reading
nakagawa2024 511 ‹contested›. The 'place' analysis rests on Okuda (1989), read
only second-hand via Nakagawa here.

The sensory nominalizers as perception complements

The four possessed-form nominalizers ru-we , sir-i ,
haw-e , and hum-i function primarily as
clause-final evidential heads (treated in evidential-schema-overview and
ruwe-ne-inferential ), but Satō shows that three of them — sir-i ,
haw-e , and hum-i — also nominalize embedded
clauses as perception-object NPs under nukar 'see' and
nu 'hear/ask', with a semantic specialization:
sir-i marks an event in progress at reference time (visual perception),
haw-e a speech event heard, and
hum-i a non-visual sensation
sato2008 175–178 ; nakagawa2024 508 .

In this use the possessed-form nominalizer takes a saturated clause as its implicit possessor:
the 'appearance/voice/feeling of [the embedded event]'. The whole
[clause] sir-i or
[clause] haw-e then fills the O slot of the perception verb:

hekaci apkas sir-i ku=nukar | child walk appearance-POSS 1SG.A=see | I saw the child walking. | sir-i ( ne or existential an ) is the same
construction extended to main-clause predication
nakagawa2024 508 . The Dal Corso debate about whether these structures are
biclausal or partially grammaticalized monoclausal predicates applies equally here and is taken
up in the constituency section below.

kuni as complement marker

The complementizer kuni (purposive/deontic) nominalizes the preceding clause and
expresses content that is future-oriented, inferred, desired, or directed: what the matrix
subject says must happen, thinks should happen, or intends to happen
nakagawa2024 476 . Nakagawa classifies kuni as a
conjunction with formal-noun-like use (接続助詞の形式名詞的な用法); Bugaeva analyses the complex
kuni-p as deontic 'what should be'
bugaeva2016 294 . The purpose and adverbial uses of kuni
are treated in kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements ; the focus here is on the
complement-of-speech and complement-of-cognition uses.

With ye 'say', kuni marks a directive or an
anticipated future event reported in speech:

hosipi=an kuni a=ye | return=4.S PURP 4.A=say | I said I would return. | kuni nominalizes the saturated clause hosipi=an (intransitive 'return', +1 with =an −1 = 0) as the object of a=ye. The kuni complement presents the speaker's stated intention — a future event in the speaker's assertion. | nakagawa2024:476 | HK

ahun kuni a=ye akusu toykooripak | enter PURP 4.A=say when be.very.abashed | When I told them to enter, they were very abashed. | The directive use: the matrix subject (4th-person narrator) tells the addressees to enter. The complement's agent (the addressees) differs from the matrix S. akusu 'thereupon/when' links to the consequence clause. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8 (part 2) [aa-irc/007#213]

With ramu 'think', kuni expresses a wished or
expected state of affairs:

pirka kuni ku=ramu | be.good PURP 1SG.A=think | I think it would be good. | Constructed to illustrate the kuni + ramu pattern. pirka 'be.good' is a stative intransitive (arity +1); without an overt S it has arity +1 — but in this context the subject is pragmatically recoverable. For the complementizer reading, the argument is that the speaker's kuni-complement expresses wished or inferred content, not a specific participant gap. Compare the analysis of arity in Nakagawa (2024: 476). | HK

The combination kuni p forms a deontic or purposive nominal
bugaeva2016 294 . The embedded clause is nominalized by kuni
and then a second formal noun p further nominalizes the resulting
complex as 'a thing one should / will do':

míre kuni p ére kuni p nepki ne ki wa | clothe PURP NMLZ feed PURP NMLZ be.work COP do CONJ | making things to clothe (the grandchild) and things to feed (her) her work, | Two parallel kuni p nominals ('thing to clothe', 'thing to feed') coordinate as the content of nepki ne ki wa 'making (it) one's work'. Each kuni p packages the action as a purposive-deontic event-nominal. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#355]

The corpus count of kuni is 6,113 tokens (rank 36), a figure that
conflates the complementizer, purposive, and adverbial uses; no per-function count is derivable
from word-frequency data alone ‹corpus-suggested›.

Other indirect-speech complementizers

Beyond kuni , four elements function as complementizers in indirect
speech and thought contexts, each encoding a different epistemic or temporal profile
nakagawa2024 475–480 ; sato2008 176–177 :

complementizer
content-type
typical matrix predicate

kuni
future, wished, directed
ye , ramu

kunak
future, inferred (in speech)
ye , ramu

yak
past, ongoing, general truth
ye , ramu , takar

kusu
causative-directive 'that X do'
ye

ya
polar or content question embedded
eramiskari , eraman

A shared diagnostic: all of these elements nominalize the preceding VP and make it the object of
the matrix transitive. The form yakne is not an indirect-speech marker —
it is a conditional conjunction, distinct from the complementizer yak
nakagawa2024 477 . Final particles of the type used in main clauses cannot
appear inside these embedded complements, and person marking shifts from the original
speaker's viewpoint to the narrator's
nakagawa2024 475 . The kunak and yak complementizers, and the quotative
sekor , are treated in detail in
quotative-complementation-sekor .

ku=matnepo tanto ek kunak ye kor an | 1SG.A=daughter today come COMP say while exist | My daughter is saying she will come today. | kunak nominalizes the prospective clause ek 'come' as the content of ye 'say'. The periphrastic ye kor an ('is saying') marks ongoing speech. Person marking in the embedded clause (3rd person zero-marked ek) reflects the narrator's viewpoint, not the daughter's. | sato2008:177 | CHI

The embedded question complementizer ya introduces content questions and
polar questions as the object of cognition predicates such as
eramiskari 'not know'. Nakagawa distinguishes the polar-question use
('whether') from the content-question use ('what/where/when'), both employing
ya
nakagawa2024 510 .

Bare complementation

A small residue of complement constructions in the corpus involves no overt nominalizer: the
embedded clause appears without a following formal noun, directly as the argument of the matrix
predicate. Bugaeva identifies this as Stage I of her diachronic typology of Ainu nominalization
— the oldest stratum, in which a bare finite clause functions nominally without a nominalizing
word
bugaeva2016 196–200, 229 . Refsing similarly notes cases in Shizunai Ainu that
she tentatively treats as "ellipsis of the nominalizer"
refsing1986 . The construction is residual in the Hokkaido attested record
and not systematically documented for modern Saru or Chitose ‹corpus-suggested›. Its
relationship to compounding and to the lexicalized nominalization cline (where
p ~ pe has contracted into lexical items such as
aep food ) is treated in nominalization-p-pe and
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

Participant identity across the clause boundary

Because complement clauses in Hokkaido Ainu are finite and carry their own person morphology,
the embedded subject is always expressible: the verb inside the complement bears its own person
affixes independently of the matrix clause. No PRO or empty-category mechanism is posited in the
sources consulted, and no formal control or raising analysis of HA complements appears in the
literature surveyed here.

Nevertheless, patterns of participant identity are observable. With cognition predicates
( ramu , eramiskari ), the person marking of the
embedded clause in the corpus tends to match the matrix subject: the one who thinks or fails to
understand is also the agent of the thought or the one about whom the proposition holds. The
examples above show this — ku=ramu 'I think' with an embedded clause
about a state of affairs the speaker cares about, a=eramiskari 'one
does not understand' with an embedded clause about what that same person ought to say or do.
The regularity, however, follows from the semantics of cognition verbs, not from a grammatical
control mechanism, and divergent persons are possible where the content demands it ‹corpus-suggested›.

With the directive use of ye + kuni , the pattern
runs in the opposite direction: the matrix subject (the one who speaks) addresses the matrix
object (the addressee), and the embedded clause's agent is typically that addressee, not the
speaker. The corpus example from Kawakami Matsuko above — 'when I told them to enter' — shows
the fourth-person speaker ( a=ye ) directing a different group (the
addressees) toward an action ( ahun 'enter') whose agent goes unmarked in
the embedded clause because it is recoverable from discourse
ilcaa1976 ‹corpus-suggested›. The indirect-speech analysis that best captures this
is given in Nakagawa's treatment of indirect speech perspective shifts
nakagawa2024 475–476 .

The term raising (movement of an embedded subject to a matrix position) has no
attestation in the analytical literature on Hokkaido Ainu clausal complementation, and the
available data supply no diagnostic that would motivate such an analysis. The embedded clause
remains a nominalized finite clause whose internal arguments are expressed by the embedded verb's
own person affixes ‹speculative›.

The constituency of the nominalized clause

Whether the structure [clause] NMLZ ne is biclausal or a grammaticalizing
monoclausal complex predicate is a live debate that cuts across complement clauses, evidential
constructions, and the formal noun system generally. The two positions are:

The biclausal external-RC analysis treats the nominalized clause as a full NP whose
head is a possessed formal noun. The copula ne then closes the outer
predication. Tamura, Okuda, and Satō take this position
tamura2000 227 ; sato2008 175 .

The grammaticalization-to-monoclausal analysis holds that the erstwhile noun is
considerably bleached and the construction is moving toward a complex predicate. The diagnostic
is that a fully lexical noun can take demonstratives and attributive modifiers
( pirka menoko 'a beautiful woman'), whereas the evidential formal noun
cannot: *pirka ruwe ne kewtum is ungrammatical
bugaeva2016 306–315 ; dalcorso2018 24–25 . No read source
provides a constituency proof — a bracket test or movement diagnostic — that decides between
these ‹contested›. The same unresolved question is noted in the dedicated treatments of
ruwe ne in ruwe-ne-inferential and of
p ~ pe in nominalization-p-pe .

For the complement constructions treated in this chapter, the practical consequence is that the
formal nouns function synchronically as clause-final markers that package a finite clause as a
nominal argument. The diachronic source in lexical nouns remains transparent (Bugaeva's
nominalization-cline stages, bugaeva2016 229–304 ), and partial
grammaticalization explains why the same forms build RC heads, complement clauses, evidential
assertions, and — at the furthest end of the cline — conjunctions and adverbial-clause markers
nakagawa2024 515–516 ; takahashi2018 107–112 .

Ijäs's pedagogical grammar introduces relative clauses and notes that formal nouns such as
uske 'place/time' are best understood as nouns that have historically
originated from relative-clause constructions, without committing to a formal analysis of the
biclausal-vs-monoclausal debate
ijas2023 . This aligns with the historical-cline view and is graded as
consistent with the consensus on diachrony ‹consensus›.

title: Pro-Drop, (Non)configurationality, and the Pronominal-Argument Debate
summary: Argument ellipsis and null anaphora, and whether the bound affixes are the true arguments (Jelinek/Baker) — Ainu on the configurationality cline.
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Pro-Drop, (Non)configurationality, and the Pronominal-Argument Debate
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: Argument ellipsis and null anaphora, and whether the bound affixes are the true arguments (Jelinek/Baker) — Ainu on the configurationality cline.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu combines two properties whose joint presence creates genuine analytical tension.
The finite verb is clause-final without exception, and every phrase type is head-final: modifier
precedes head, postposition follows noun, subordinate clause precedes main clause
nakagawa2024 363 ; shibatani1990 22–24 . At the same time,
within the preverbal field the relative order of noun phrases is controlled by information
structure rather than grammatical function: the same three-argument verb
epakasnu 'teach' is attested with its arguments in at least four
different preverbal sequences in natural speech from a single speaker, and no case particle
indicates which NP is subject and which is object nakagawa2024 363–364 ;
bugaeva2022 . What fixes grammatical roles is the verb's obligatory person-index
system: bound argument-marking affixes appear on every finite predicate, and an overt NP is
structurally optional — an elaboration on a participant already indexed, not a required
structural position sato2025a 359–361 ; bugaeva2012 472 .

These properties place Ainu in the cross-linguistic neighbourhood of head-marking languages such
as Mohawk and Lakhota, whose argument NPs have been analysed as adjuncts to pronominal argument
positions inside the verb — the Pronominal-Argument Hypothesis (PAH) as reviewed by
bugaeva2022 . Whether that analysis extends to Ainu is a question the accessible
literature addresses but does not settle; the present chapter sets out the evidence, the
competing positions, and what Ainu unambiguously does and does not exhibit. The verb-finality
rule and the head-final NP structure are treated in constituent-order-head-final ;
the person-affix paradigm, alignment typology, and ditransitive indexing cap belong to
person-marking-architecture and its sibling chapters.

Obligatory head-marking and the appositional noun phrase

A finite verb in Ainu marks its arguments by means of bound person prefixes and, for some
persons, suffixes; these affixes are obligatory on every predicate. Satō, following the
head-marking framework of nichols1986 , describes the verb as the clausal head that
bears the central information about which participants fill its valence frame
sato2025a 361 . A finite verb alone, with no overt NPs at all, is a complete
and grammatical clause:

ku=inkar | 1SG.S=look.around | I look around. | Satō ex. 1 (after Tamura 1988: 72). A finite verb carrying only the first-singular subject prefix ku=. No overt subject NP is required; the clause is grammatically complete. The prefix ku= covers both S and A for 1SG; see the person-marking chapters. | sato2025a:355 | HK

When an overt NP does appear, its relationship to the verb is appositional rather than
structurally necessary. In Role and Reference Grammar terms, Satō places the person prefix (PRO)
inside the CORE as the actual argument, with any co-occurring NP outside the CORE as an
optional, non-obligatory adjunct sato2025a 363–365 . This is the
Lakhota-type structure: "the same logical structure gives rise to morphologically different
structures" depending on how many overt NPs are supplied
sato2025a 365 . The overt object NP in the following example holds precisely
that adjunct status:

cep a=koyki | fish 4.A=catch | We catch fish. | The fourth-person agent prefix a= is the grammatical subject argument; the NP cep 'fish' lexically specifies the zero-indexed object. The clause is complete without cep; the overt NP narrows what is caught but does not fill a structural position. | sato2025a:363 | HK

Personal pronouns, being informationally redundant with the person index, are used only for
emphasis or contrast and are rarer in Ainu than their Japanese counterparts
bugaeva2012 472 ; sato2025a 360 . The independent pronoun
kani 'I' can co-occur with ku=mina 'I laughed',
but without kani the clause still indexes a specific first-person
singular subject; the pronoun is emphatic, not anaphoric bugaeva2012 472 .

The possessor NP stands in the same appositional relationship to the possessed noun: the
possessee is obligatorily head-marked by an agreement prefix, so the possessor NP is normally
omitted and expressed only when referentially new or contrastive
bugaeva2012 486 . The community observation that the bare verb form with no
person affix is the third-person form and the citation form of the verb — with the zero-marking
implicitly conceding a zero third-person affix — is consistent with Satō's formal argument
that such a zero is required by the two-affix cap on finite verbs
sato2025a 369 (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-25, asserted;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›. This debate is developed in
person-marking-architecture sao-paradigm .

The free ordering of preverbal noun phrases

With intransitive predicates the single preverbal NP is unambiguously the S argument. With
transitives, the evidence is divided, and the ordering question is the analytical crux of this
chapter.

Shibatani uses the following minimal pair to argue that linear position determines grammatical
function:

kamuy aynu rayke | bear human kill | The bear killed the man. | Shibatani ex. 23a. Initial NP interpreted as agent; the only role indicator is linear position, since neither NP carries a case suffix and the verb rayke is zero-marked for third-person arguments. | shibatani1990:22 | HK

aynu kamuy rayke | human bear kill | The man killed the bear. | Shibatani ex. 23b. With order reversed, agent-patient assignment reverses. Shibatani (1990: 22) concludes that word order plays as important a role as in English in determining grammatical function. | shibatani1990:22 | HK

Both NPs are third person, zero-marked; role assignment differs only by position. Shibatani
concludes that word order "plays as important a role as in English in determining the
grammatical functions of certain nouns" shibatani1990 22 , allowing object-first
order only when context or semantics rules out an incorrect reading. Satō's introductory
description is comparably cautious: the role of an NP is "shown by word order or context"
sato2008 2 . The pedagogical grammar of ijas2023 likewise describes
Ainu as SOV and explains apparent flexibility by appeal to the zero third-person marker, such
that "if we put the linguists' zero mark (Ø) in our example sentence … the word order indeed
is correct." This framing is pedagogically effective but does not engage the evidence for
genuine order freedom.

That evidence comes from Nakagawa's Chitose data. Three utterances by the same speaker
describing the same scene place the three arguments of epakasnu 'teach'
in three different preverbal sequences, all translating as 'my wife taught our daughter
women's work' nakagawa2024 363, exx. 700–702 ; reproduced in English at
nakagawa2022pos exx. 71–73 :

example
preverbal order
Ainu (Chitose)

700
IO – A – DO
menoko ne hike a=macihi menoko monrayke epakasnu

701
IO – DO – A
menoko ne hike menoko monrayke a=macihi epakasnu

702
A – IO – DO
a=macihi anakne menoko po menoko monrayke epakasnu

A fourth order (DO – A – IO) is attested in a Saru text
nakagawa2024 363–364, ex. 703 . Nakagawa's conclusion is explicit: "since the
noun phrase itself carries no indicator of which is subject and which is object, which one is
the subject cannot be grammatically determined"; the disambiguation rests on "common sense"
and knowledge of the scene, not on syntactic position
nakagawa2024 363 . Bugaeva, drawing on the same data, states that Ainu "does
not show its subject and object either by case marking or by word order"
nakagawa2022pos . This is the ‹contested› heart of the question: whether role is
fixed by first-noun default (Shibatani, Satō 2008) or is genuinely free and recoverable only
via person-indexing and real-world plausibility (Nakagawa, Bugaeva). No corpus-level count of
A-before-O versus O-before-A frequencies in transitive clauses exists in the literature consulted;
the Nakagawa triple is the strongest available evidence, but it derives from one speaker in one
session ‹contested›.

The topic particle anak(ne) in example 702 is a distinct confound:
when a NP is overtly marked as topic it tends to come first regardless of grammatical role
nakagawa2024 364 ; bugaeva2012 472 . The fuller treatment of
topicalization and left-dislocation belongs to topic-marking-anakne and
pragmatic-word-order-dislocation . The community observation that
anak/anakne is used with far less frequency than Japanese
は , carrying approximately the weight of 'as regards' rather than a
default topic marker, is consistent with the descriptive literature's picture of a device that
marks rather than requires left-periphery position (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord,
2024-11-09, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ; cf. shibatani1990 38 )
‹corpus-suggested›.

A separate class of word-order evidence comes from right-dislocation: some Ainu texts place
the subject or object NP after the verb, attached via the light verb ki
'do', providing participant specification after the predicate has already established the
referential relations bugaeva2022 . This discourse-level device is a
processing-driven supplement to verb-final order, not a grammaticalized alternative to it.

Null arguments and zero anaphora

Null arguments are the default state of Ainu syntax. A verb's person affixes index every
participant, so overt NPs appear only when they carry additional discourse work — introducing a
referent, contrasting it, or marking it as topic. Nakagawa formalizes this in his sentence
template: a NP "need not be present in the sentence as long as it can be supplied meaningfully
from context, and the sentence still stands" nakagawa2024 361 . Satō, framing
the same observation under head-marking, writes that "grammatical relations (subject and
object) … are not obligatory elements in Ainu; rather it is the personal affixes that are
obligatory for the predicates" sato2025a 359–360 .

When a participant is contextually available, the standard strategy is zero anaphora: the
missing argument triggers no overt substitute, not even an independent pronoun. Bugaeva
illustrates this for a demoted oblique: in itanki huraye hine (itanki) ani
Ø=i=ko-i-puni 'she washed a bowl and served me food with (that bowl)', the second mention
of the bowl "can easily be omitted and the sentence would trigger a zero-anaphora interpretation
'holding (that bowl)'" bugaeva2012 476, ex. 22 . Ditransitives extend the same
pattern: the language's two-affix cap means a third argument cannot be indexed, so it is
expressed as a bare NP whose reference is guaranteed by verbal valency rather than by its own
person marking sato2025a 368–369 . The cap is treated in
person-marking-architecture syncretism and the object-indexing chapters.

The following attested clause illustrates how overt possessed-NPs in narrative serve to
reintroduce participants rather than maintain ongoing reference. Both NPs name the narrator's
parents, which context would have made recoverable, but appear here to foreground the two
co-subjects of the embedding:

sekor a=onaha a=unuhu haweoka kor i=kimatekka hine | QUOT 4.POSS=father 4.POSS=mother say.thus CONJ 4.O=encourage CONJ | Saying so, my father and my mother encouraged me on and then … | ILCAA aa-irc/009#154. Both co-subjects (a=onaha, a=unuhu) are overt; the object of kimatekka is zero, recovered from the 4.O prefix i= (narrator as object in logophoric-narrative context). All other participants in the surrounding passage are null. The fourth-person possessive a= marks the narrator's own kin in oral narrative; see the logophoric chapter. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, ILCAA Ainu materials, Saru

Reference continuity in oral narrative depends primarily on the unbroken chain of person
affixes across linked clauses, not on overt NP repetition. Re-introduction by NP marks a
scene change or new referential ground, a function treated in the reference-tracking chapter
( reference-tracking ).

Discontinuous noun phrases

A standard diagnostic for nonconfigurationality is the permissibility of discontinuous NPs:
two parts of one noun phrase separated by material that is not part of that phrase. Nakagawa
provides an attested Saru instance in which the head noun toma 'mat'
and its numeral sinep 'one' are separated by the focus particle
ka 'even', yet the predicate isam 'be absent'
is an intransitive verb requiring exactly one S argument:

toma ka sinep ka isam | mat FOC one FOC not.exist | There is not a single mat either. | Nakagawa ex. 750 (second clause). Full pair: sintoko utar ka opitta sinep ka isam. toma ka sinep ka isam. 'there is not a single box either; there is not a single mat either.' Nakagawa analyses [toma ka sinep ka] as one discontinuous NP filling the S slot of the intransitive isam; the arity of the verb (one place) rules out reading toma and sinep as separate arguments. | nakagawa2024:383 | SAR

Nakagawa frames this via verbal arity rather than via the nonconfigurationality literature, but
structurally it instantiates what that literature treats as quantifier float: the numeral has
drifted from its host noun into a position that an independent argument would occupy. The
community observation that the pattern N + adverbial-numeral + V ( pasuy sinep uk
'takes one chopstick') is "perfectly normal, maybe more frequent than in Japanese" is
consistent with quantifier float being productive in Ainu, though frequency claims remain
unverified (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-12, proposed;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›. The NP-internal ordering of head and
numeral is treated in noun-phrase-structure .

Configurationality diagnostics assessed

The standard diagnostics for nonconfigurationality, as applied to Ainu in
bugaeva2022 and sato2025a , pattern as follows:

diagnostic
Ainu status
key sources

Obligatory bound argument-marking (head-marking)
Yes — person affixes on every finite predicate
sato2025a 359–361 ; bugaeva2012 472

Overt NPs structurally optional
Yes — NPs are appositional to the verbal index, not required positions
sato2025a 363–365 ; nakagawa2024 361

Pragmatically free order of overt NPs
Yes, ‹contested› — multi-order variation attested; recovery by context and index
nakagawa2024 363 ; bugaeva2022

Null anaphora without pronominalization
Yes — missing arguments are zero, not pronominalized
bugaeva2012 476 ; nakagawa2024 361

Discontinuous NPs / quantifier float
Yes — attested (Nakagawa 2024: 383)
nakagawa2024 383

No case flagging on core S/A/O NPs
Yes — postpositions mark obliques only; core NPs are bare
bugaeva2012 472 ; shibatani1990 22

Verb positionally free (verb can appear anywhere)

No — verb is rigidly clause-final; this is the one firm constituent-order
rule

nakagawa2024 363 ; shibatani1990 22

Six of the seven diagnostics are positive. The seventh — rigid verb-finality — is a
counter-signal. In the best-described nonconfigurational languages (Warlpiri, Jiwarli), the
verb can appear in any position among the free NPs. Ainu's verb-finality is absolute:
every adverbial, every NP, every negative somo precedes the verb
phrase, but the verb phrase itself does not move. Nichols notes that head-marking languages
achieve word-order freedom through either verb-initial or verb-final strategies with equal ease
nichols1986 104 ; Ainu takes the verb-final option. Its preverbal field is
pragmatically ordered but bounded by the clause edge.

The combination — verb-final, head-marking, caseless core arguments, free preverbal NP order —
is typologically rare. Nakagawa observes that languages with SOV order that mark neither
subject nor object by case nor by fixed position "are probably relatively rare"; he cites Nivkh
as the nearest geographic neighbour and notes that Nivkh compensates with obligatory
immediately-preverbal object position plus a consonant-mutation dependency, while Ainu does
neither nakagawa2022pos .

The Pronominal-Argument Hypothesis and the Ainu counter-evidence

The Pronominal-Argument Hypothesis holds that in head-marking languages the bound affixes are
the actual grammatical arguments and the free NPs are adjuncts — lexical specifications of what
the pronominal arguments refer to, with no direct argument status of their own. This position
is associated with the work of Jelinek and of Baker, as reviewed in
bugaeva2022 and in foleyvanvalin1984 . The logical consequence for
syntax is that there are no argument positions for NPs to fill, which is why they can appear
discontinuously and in any order: they are not in the phrase structure at all. Ainu's profile
— obligatory affixes, optional NPs, free preverbal order, null anaphora, discontinuous NPs, no
case — fits this description wherever the accessible data bear on it ‹contested›.

Bugaeva presents the hypothesis explicitly and then argues against the pure appositive reading
for Ainu:

"There remains an important theoretical issue of whether NPs in head-marking languages are
arguments or clause constituents of any kind at all. A number of researchers consider them
mere appositives that lexically specify or qualify the actual arguments, which are on the
verb. … However, a more widely accepted view is that argumenthood is shared between bound
pronouns and coreferential NPs. As to Ainu, the latter view seems to fit better
because there is an extra <location> elaboration on the noun …"
bugaeva2022

The Ainu-specific counter-argument turns on a subset of nouns with inherent locative semantics.
When two third-person arguments of a three-place verb are both zero-marked, the locative-noun
class — nouns such as or 'place (of)', sam
'near', ka 'above', corpok 'below', as well as
the category of place-role nouns ( kim 'mountain-area',
pis 'beach-area') — distinguishes the Location argument from the Theme.
Nakagawa's Chitose example [ci a=e-p] [saranip or] oma-re 'she put the
cooked food into the basket' shows the mechanism: the NP saranip or
(basket + locative or ) is unambiguously the Goal because it contains a
locative noun, leaving the other NP as Theme; without or , the two
basket-and-food NPs would be ambiguous nakagawa2022pos . Bugaeva reads this as
evidence that the noun carries grammatically relevant information about its own argument role,
which a purely appositive / PAH NP would not need to do
bugaeva2022 .

The counter-argument has two acknowledged limits. First, it applies only when one argument is a
Location: in the many transitive clauses where neither argument is locative — including the
kamuy aynu rayke pair — the disambiguation rests on plausibility and
world knowledge, not on lexical noun class. Nakagawa is explicit that in those cases it falls
back on "common sense" nakagawa2024 363 . Second, the argument rests on a small
number of illustrative examples and has not been tested against a quantified corpus of
three-argument clauses with two zero-marked third-person NPs ‹contested›.

Satō's analysis, using Role and Reference Grammar, reaches a structurally PAH-like result —
person affixes fill the argument positions inside the CORE, NPs sit outside it as optional
adjuncts — but within a framework that does not endorse the PAH label, and it explicitly
rejects the head-movement analysis of baker1988 as unable to handle the attested
subject incorporation in Ainu ( koy-yanke 'the waves wash [me] ashore',
where the A argument is incorporated into the verb)
sato2025a 366–367 ; sato2016 . Noun incorporation and its
implications for argument structure are the subject of noun-incorporation-object
and noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate .

The three positions — PAH-type (NPs as adjuncts, affixes as sole arguments), shared
argumenthood (Bugaeva), and RRG-structural (Satō) — produce near-identical empirical
predictions for the phenomena described in this chapter; they differ in theoretical
commitments about what counts as an argument. No Ainu specialist has published a study
dedicated to the PAH or the nonconfigurationality hypothesis as such; the framing reaches Ainu
through Bugaeva's and Satō's typological discussions. Chapter authors should present the
classification as a live framing question rather than an established result ‹contested›.

What Ainu does not exhibit

Several properties typical of the best-described nonconfigurational languages are absent from
or unattested in Hokkaido Ainu, and these absences limit the scope of any nonconfigurationality
claim.

No morphological switch-reference. Warlpiri and many Australian languages
deploy dedicated same-subject / different-subject morphology on non-finite verb forms (converbs).
Ainu has no converbs and no morphological switch-reference
( clause-linkage-overview ; hine-akusu-switch-reference ). Reference
continuity across linked clauses is maintained by the obligatory person affixes on each finite
predicate, by zero anaphora, and by the fourth-person logophoric device — functional
substitutes, not formal equivalents of switch-reference morphology
bugaeva2012 498 .

No wh-movement. Content-question words remain in situ:
hunna 'who', hemanta 'what',
hunak 'where' occupy the same position they would hold in a
corresponding declarative, with no movement to a left-peripheral question position
bugaeva2012 498 . This is consistent with a head-marking typology but is shared
with many language types that are not nonconfigurational.

Verb-finality is rigid. Unlike Warlpiri, where the finite verb participates in
the general NP-scrambling freedom, the Ainu verb does not leave clause-final position. This
is the one reliable constituent-order fact: every preverbal element — NPs, adverbials, negative
somo , topic-marked constituents — is ordered by discourse and pragmatics,
but the verb-phrase occupies clause-final position without exception
nakagawa2024 363 ; shibatani1990 22 . The verb-final constraint
is the one point on which all sources, across the dispute about whether order is otherwise free,
agree.

No dedicated person-tracking morphology on the NP. Case suffixes on NPs exist
only for obliques (postpositional adverbs and case particles); core S/A/O NPs are bare. There
is no obviation morphology on the NP, no "obviative" nominal suffix marking a secondary
third-person referent. The fourth-person prefix set on the verb does analogous work for first-
and fourth-person participants in discourse, but it is verbal morphology, not nominal, and it
targets person, not rank-in-discourse ‹contested› ( indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm ).

Reference tracking across clauses

Reference continuity in Ainu oral narrative is maintained primarily by the verbal person-index
system combined with zero anaphora. A participant introduced by an overt NP in one clause is
reidentified in later clauses by the recurrence of the same person index on the finite
predicate, with no overt NP required. Re-mention by NP signals reintroduction, scene change,
or contrastive foregrounding — not ordinary anaphoric continuity.

The fourth-person form-set ( indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm ) performs additional
tracking work in oral narrative. It marks the protagonist's viewpoint and first-person reference
inside the reported world, distinct from both the narrating speaker's first person
( ku= ) and the unmarked third (zero). In performance, narrators alternate
a= (fourth-person agent) and ku= (first-singular
agent) to distinguish the in-story protagonist's speech from the narrator speaking as herself;
this alternation has been observed in attested uwepeker performance (thegodofneet,
aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-07-04, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ; consistent with
bugaeva2008 40–43 ) ‹corpus-suggested›. The logophoric analysis of the
fourth-person narration device is in logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

For clauses with two zero-marked third-person arguments, the locative-noun class provides a
lexical disambiguation device. When one argument is a locative noun or a noun modified by a
locative element ( or , sam , etc.), it is
unambiguously the Location argument, leaving the other NP as Theme or Patient
nakagawa2022pos . Outside that subset, disambiguation rests on plausibility. Ainu
therefore substitutes a combination of person-index transparency, lexical semantics, and
discourse context for the case-marking and fixed-position strategies that other SOV languages
use to track grammatical role across clauses bugaeva2022 .

Verbal number contributes independently in some environments: the transitive plural stems mark
object plurality — rayke → ronnu 'kill' — an
ergative-like S≈O≠A pattern in the number system that disambiguates S and O for zero-marked
third persons when the count of participants differs
bugaeva2022 (consistent with the community observation by nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2023-12-12, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ). The fuller treatment of verbal
number as a grammatical resource is in suppletive-verbal-number and
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

Clause-linkage particles pattern with participant continuity statistically but not
morphologically: wa correlates with same-subject sequences in Refsing's
Shizunai count, but the correlation is not grammatically enforced and carries no formal
same-subject / different-subject encoding
refsing1986 . This is treated in full in
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining and
hine-akusu-switch-reference .

title: TAM, Evidentiality, and Negation on Nonverbal Predicates
summary: How copular and other nonverbal predicates host the full sentence-final aspect, evidential, and negation apparatus through ne.
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: TAM, Evidentiality, and Negation on Nonverbal Predicates
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: How copular and other nonverbal predicates host the full sentence-final aspect, evidential, and negation apparatus through ne.

Overview

All three non-dynamic predicate types that occupy the predicate slot in a Hokkaido Ainu simple
clause — the copula-ne-predicate-nominals copula ne , the
existential-locational-an-oka existential an (sg) / oka(y) (pl) , and
property-concept-predication property-concept verbs — are verbs, and TAM and
negation attach to them through the same clausal mechanisms that operate on action verbs nakagawa2024 132, 359–361 ; sato2008 20–22 . The full TAM and aspect system is described in
tam-overview ; the evidential formal-noun frame in
evidential-schema-overview ; the person-marking that each predicate type takes in
person-marking-architecture . This chapter maps the interface: where each predicate
type departs from the verbal default in its negation strategy, how the perfect auxiliary
a attaches, how the inchoative reading arises without any dedicated
morphology, and how the ne / an alternation at
the clause edge encodes illocutionary force on non-dynamic predicates.

The three predicate types diverge most sharply in their negation strategies. The copula
ne has a dedicated frame, X ka somo ne , that
inserts the focus particle ka and the negator
somo between the predicate complement and the copula. The existential
draws on the suppletive lexical negative isam . Property-concept verbs,
being ordinary intransitive stative verbs, take the standard clausal negation described in
negation-somo-standard-clausal . Possessive kor have similarly has
the suppletive negative sak lack ; that construction is treated in
predicative-possession-clauses .

Negation strategies by non-dynamic predicate type

predicate type
affirmative
negation strategy
source

copula ne
[Compl] ne
[Compl] ka somo ne
sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 407

existential an / oka(y)
an / oka(y)
suppletive isam ; alternatively somo an
nakagawa2024 450 ; kanazawa1898

property-concept verb
PROP-V

somo PROP-V / PROP-V ka somo ki

nakagawa2024 127

possessive kor
[Possessor] [Possessed] kor
suppletive sak
nakagawa2024 323

Typologically, the distribution maps onto the hierarchy of non-dynamic predication that
stassen1997 identifies: equative/classificatory (copula
ne ), locational/existential ( an / oka(y) ), and attributive (property verbs) each carry a distinct negation exponent. The
recourse to dedicated negative lexemes for the existential and possessive types, alongside a
frame strategy for the copula, is consistent with the cross-linguistic pattern whereby
existential and possessive predicates frequently recruit non-standard negation
miestamo2005 ‹consensus›.

Negation of copular predicates: the ka somo ne frame

The standard negation of a predicate-nominal clause is
X ka somo ne , where the focus particle
ka 'even/also' and the negator somo insert
between the complement nominal and the copula sato2008 22 ;
nakagawa2024 407 :

tan pe anak seta ka somo ne wa | this NMLZ TOP dog even NEG COP CONJ | This is not a dog. | sato2008:22 | SAR

The alternative frame *ne ka somo ki — negating
ne via the standard verbal pattern V ka somo ki
— is ungrammatical sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 407 . Both Satō
and Nakagawa treat this as the defining idiosyncrasy that sets the copula apart from ordinary
verbs: its negation is expressed by modifying the complement position, not by post-verbal
negation of the verb itself. In the affirmative, the copula takes the transitive-subject
person set — okkayo ku=ne 'I am a man' — because it is analysed as a
2-place verb with a nominal complement nakagawa2024 132 ;
refsing1986 145 . In the ka somo ne frame, the copula
frequently carries no overt subject affix; this person-marking loss in the negated copular
frame is noted but not explained by any read primary source ‹speculative› (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2023; aynucorporadiscord ). The observation is consistent with
the third-person zero realisation that ne shows in affirmative clauses
with 3rd-person subjects, but its extension to other persons in the negative frame is a
genuine micro-gap. Person marking on the copula is set out in
person-marking-architecture and
copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

Separate from inner-scope ka somo ne , the construction
ruwe somo ne is an outer rhetorical frame in which
somo ne scopes over the evidential formal noun
ruwe rather than over the complement of the copula; it is treated in
ruwe-ne-inferential .

TAM on copular predicates: the perfect and the ne/an clause-edge alternation

The perfect/perfective auxiliary a attaches to the copula in the same
position as to any verb — after the finite verb and before any following formal-noun evidential
tail — and yields a completed or previously-true copular state:

pon cápe ku=ne a kusu | small cat 1SG.A=COP PFV PURP | Because I was a small cat — | 浜田隆史, アイヌタイムズ [ainu-times/024/3#4]. The perfective auxiliary a follows ku=ne, locating the copular state in the past. The clause links to what follows via the purposive-causal kusu. | ainukoraci1991 | CHI

All three non-dynamic predicate types share a static ⇄ inchoative duality that is read off
context without dedicated morphology: ne covers both 'be X' and
'become X', an / oka(y) both 'exist' and
'appear/come into being', and property verbs both 'be big' and 'grow big'
nakagawa2024 118–119 ; shibatani1990 19 ;
nakagawa2022pos 477 . The sequence
[complement] ne + existential an / oka=an makes the inchoative reading salient — a community member notes
that ~ne an corresponds to Japanese 〜になる 'become', while
~ne kor is not idiomatic ‹contested› (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora
Discord 2024; aynucorporadiscord ). This is consistent with the general dynamic
reading that an lends to a preceding predicate in the aspect periphrasis
(cf. V wa an resultative); the aspect-auxiliary use of the existential
is treated in existential-aspect-an-oka .

The ne / an alternation at the right clause edge
is grammaticalized into an illocutionary distinction. With the formal-noun evidential frame,
the copula ne closes the declarative and the existential
an closes the content question, while the bare formal noun marks polar
questions and exclamations nakagawa2024 392–396, §11.2.1–11.2.4 . The pattern
holds identically for non-dynamic predicates and verbal predicates:

Formal-noun frame: illocutionary alternation at the clause edge

frame
illocution
example

NMLZ + ne
declarative
a=ye hawe ne 'it is said (that …)'

NMLZ + an?
content question (with question word)
hemanta ruwe an? 'why (is it that …)?'

bare NMLZ?
polar question / exclamation
ruwe?

NMLZ + un
affirmative reply
ruwe un

The ordering constraint confirms the clause's layered structure: the formal noun and its
predicate sit outside inner-clause TAM material. The form
kusu ne ruwe ne 'because (it is a fact that)' is well-formed, but
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not, because a further
ne -predication cannot embed over an already-closed evidential
nakagawa2024 393 — confirmed against the primary source by a community member
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024; aynucorporadiscord ). The
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form morphology of the formal nouns
( ru-we , haw-e , sir-i ,
hum-i ) and the full evidential paradigm are treated in
evidential-schema-overview and ruwe-ne-inferential ; the interrogative
system is treated in interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

The existential an/oka(y) and its suppletive negative isam

The existential an (sg) and oka(y) (pl) do not
take the somo frame as their primary negation. The lexical verb
isam not exist, be absent is the suppletive negative partner of the existential
pair nakagawa2024 450 ; bugaeva2012 496 . The three-way
suppletion:

Existential suppletion: singular, plural, negative

singular
plural
negative (both numbers)

form
an
oka / okay
isam

isam is an intransitive verb that takes person affixes normally:
e=isam 'you are gone/absent'. Historical depth confirms its
stability: the form isam 'ない' is attested in the 1630
Matsumae no koto wordlist, making the negative existential one of the oldest recorded
Ainu predicate forms. The etymological decomposition of
isam as i- (antipassive) + root
*sam is ‹speculative› and is proposed independently by
dalcorso2025neg 41–42 and by ijas2023 (lesson 9); see
negation-existential-possessive-isam for fuller discussion. The
somo an construction is available as an alternative where a
denial reading is required rather than the lexical 'absence' meaning:

nisatta somo k=an | tomorrow NEG 1SG.S=exist | I won't be here tomorrow. | kanazawa1898 | SAR

The existential negative also feeds the aspect system.
V wa isam expresses that a state of affairs has become absent as the
result of the preceding event — roughly equivalent to Japanese 〜てしまう 'V and it's gone'
nakagawa2024 452, §15.2.1 . This mirrors the resultative
V wa an (〜ている), in which the existential carries the positive
persistent state. Both the aspect periphrasis and the number suppletion of
an / oka(y) are treated in
existential-aspect-an-oka ; the full locational and existential semantics in
existential-locational-an-oka .

Property-concept predicates: standard clausal negation

Property-concept verbs ( pirka be good , poro be big ,
wen be bad , pon be small , hure be red and their
class) are intransitive stative verbs and take the standard negation that any intransitive verb
takes: pre-verbal somo V , or the post-verbal frame
V ka somo ki nakagawa2024 127 :

merayke=an ka somo ki no | feel.cold=4.S even NEG do and | I don't feel cold, and … | Nakagawa ex. 15. The post-verbal ka somo ki no frame negates the property verb merayke 'feel cold'. The 4th-person S suffix =an marks the narrative first person; contrast the 0-place meteorological verb mean 'it is cold (weather)', which has no subject index. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR

The divergence between property-verb negation ( somo V /
V ka somo ki ) and copula negation ( X ka somo ne )
is the direct morphosyntactic reflex of the difference in predicate class: the copula is a
special 2-place verb with an idiosyncratic negation frame, while property verbs are ordinary
intransitives nakagawa2024 118, 407 ; nakagawa2022pos 474–475 .
The error of inserting the copula after a property word — *pirka ne
'be-good COP' — is flagged by Nakagawa as the characteristic learner mistake, and its
ungrammaticality confirms that the ka somo ne frame is copula-specific
and does not extend to the property-verb predicate type
nakagawa2024 119 . The word-class argument for property verbs as intransitive
verbs is made in no-adjective-class-property-verbs .

Scope of negation within the evidential frame

When a non-dynamic predicate is closed by the formal-noun evidential frame, negation sits
inside the evidential: the predicate is negated before the formal noun, and the
ruwe ne (or other formal-noun + copula) remains outside the scope of
negation, predicating the negated content as recognised fact.

nep eci=e-sirkirap ka somo ki ru-we ne na | something 2PL.A=APPL-worry even NEG do track-POSS COP FIN | You will not be troubled by anything. | Dal Corso ex. 128. The negation ka somo ki targets the verb sirkirap and sits inside the evidential frame; ruwe ne predicates the whole negated proposition as an established fact. | dalcorso2018:145 | SAR | Kaizawa Tsurushino, Biratori; Dal Corso siglum KAY: 2-2,18

For copular predicates under ruwe ne , the expected sequence is
[complement] ka somo ne ruwe ne : the
ka somo ne frame negates the copular predication, and the evidential
closes over it. The layering is confirmed by the same ordering constraint noted above:
kusu ne ruwe ne is licit because
kusu ne is inner-clause intentional material, but
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not, because the evidential cannot be embedded
under a further copular predication nakagawa2024 393 . The interaction of
negation and polarity items within the evidential scope is treated in
negation-polarity-items-scope ; the structure of the evidential frame in
ruwe-ne-inferential .

title: Northern Contact: Nivkh, Tungusic, and Manchu
summary: The Sakhalin/Amur contact zone — Nivkh and Tungusic (Uilta/Orok, Nanai)/Manchu lexical and structural contact, with Sakhalin Ainu as the principal locus.
part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Northern Contact: Nivkh, Tungusic, and Manchu
Part: Part XXI — Language Contact and the Lexicon
Summary: The Sakhalin/Amur contact zone — Nivkh and Tungusic (Uilta/Orok, Nanai)/Manchu lexical and structural contact, with Sakhalin Ainu as the principal locus.

The northern contact zone

Three genealogically unrelated languages occupied Sakhalin simultaneously and traded across it:
Ainu (an isolate), Nivkh/Ghilyak (an isolate), and Uilta/Orok, a Southern Tungusic language.
Manchu-origin goods and vocabulary reached the island via the Santan trade network from the Qing
frontier; Chinese commodities followed the same route without producing direct Ainu–Chinese
bilingualism. Whether this configuration constitutes a linguistic area in the strict sense —
a Sprachbund defined by structural convergence — Shiraishi and Tangiku explicitly leave for
future research shiraishitangiku2022 §1 . What is documented is directional
lexical borrowing; the structural case for areal convergence remains under-evidenced.

The contact layer as it reaches Hokkaido is thin. Most Nivkh, Tungusic, and Manchu vocabulary
stayed on Sakhalin, and loanwords travelling in the Ainu-to-continent direction barely reached
the mainland shiraishitangiku2022 §1 . A small residue —
kaya sail , sintoko cask , san offering shelf ,
tonto tanned hide — passed from the Sakhalin contact zone into Hokkaido Ainu,
together with the Eurasian Wanderwort kotan settlement, village and the
proposed Tungusic borrowing inaw ritual wood-shavings (ināw) . Contact
borrowing is distinct from genetic relatedness: all proposals linking Ainu genetically to Nivkh
or any other northern family have failed, and the Ainu–Nivkh relationship is one of early
lexical interaction between two isolates janhunen2022 §3c ‹consensus›. The
genetic question is addressed in genetic-position-macro-comparison .

Ainu–Nivkh contact: the lexical layer

Borrowing between Ainu and Nivkh ran in both directions on Sakhalin. Standard directionality
diagnostics — geographic spread within each family, morphological analysability, sound-change
plausibility, and dialect correspondences — must be applied case by case
shiraishitangiku2022 §3 . Multilingualism on the island was not a general
social condition: before the late nineteenth century it was "extremely limited and localized",
mediated by individual interpreters and trading contacts rather than widespread bilingualism
shiraishitangiku2022 §2.5 . The west-coast Sakhalin group called
Sirunkur , described as "originally Ainu but Nivkh-ized", documents a sociolinguistic
boundary zone rather than a stable bilingual population
shiraishitangiku2022 §2.2 .

Items that passed from Nivkh into Ainu on Sakhalin include nuso/nuhsu
'dog sled' (‹SA›; < Nivkh nuci ),
hankata 'birch-bark plate' (< Nivkh
haŋr̥ ), and ka 'sable trap' (< Nivkh
ha ). In the reverse direction, Sakhalin Ainu
tonto , sinduχ ,
upun 'winter storehouse', nikar 'ladder',
and muks 'swaddling cloth' appear in Nivkh
shiraishitangiku2022 §3.2–3.6 . Of the Nivkh-into-Ainu items,
kaya sail is the one with a clear Hokkaido reflex: Batchelor's dictionary
documents kaya in Sakhalin with the meaning 'fish-skin garment', and
Hokkaido retained the word with the narrower meaning 'sail'
batchelor1938 241 ; shiraishitangiku2022 §3.2 .

The early Ainu–Ghilyak contact layer reaches deeper than the Sakhalin trade period. Alonso de
la Fuente, revising Vovin's Proto-Ainu reconstruction, argues that Ainu-into-Ghilyak loans
preserve the proto-lateral PA *l- before its merger with
*r in all Hokkaido reflexes: the Proto-Ghilyak reflex
*l- corresponding to Vovin's PA *d- makes
sense if Ainu lent these words to Ghilyak before the lateral–flap merger had run its course
alonso2022 §2.2.1 . This is a secondary use of contact evidence to
triangulate segmental reconstruction; the reconstruction itself is treated in
proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction .

Among the deeper shared items, tunakay 'reindeer' — source of Japanese
トナカイ tonakai (treated in
ainu-loanwords-and-toponymy-in-japanese ) — is linked by Janhunen, following
Austerlitz (1976), to Pre-Proto-Ghilyak *tola-ŋa.y
‹contested› janhunen2022 §3c . The Sakhalin-only parallel
kaco 'shaman's drum', shared with ‹SA› Ghilyak
*kaco , points to a similar early contact stratum
janhunen2022 §3c . The reindeer was not native to Hokkaido proper;
tunakay passed through the Sakhalin trade route into Ainu cultural
vocabulary and then into Japanese.

The community note that Sakhalin Ainu nikubun 'a Nivkh person' derives
from the Sakhalin Nivkh form ɲiɣvŋ — distinct from the Amur-dialect
form ɲivx — is consistent with the Sakhalin contact setting and is
corroborated by Shiraishi and Tangiku's discussion of Ainu ethnonyms ‹contested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-11);
shiraishitangiku2022 §2 .

Tungusic, Manchu, and the Santan trade

The Santan trade ( 山丹交易 ) was the channel through which Qing-dynasty
Manchu goods reached Sakhalin and, through Sakhalin, the northern coast of Hokkaido. Uilta
(Orok), a Southern Tungusic language closely related to Evenki, occupied the east and central
coast of Sakhalin alongside Ainu communities. Direct Ainu–Uilta exchange, Manchu-mediated
trade contact, and the downstream movement of Chinese goods via the Santan network are
distinguishable layers even though the documentary record conflates them.

The offering shelf san offering shelf has cognates in both Nivkh
( cʰaŋ ) and Uilta ( saan ), making the direction
of borrowing difficult to establish without further philological evidence
shiraishitangiku2022 §3 ‹contested›. The comparison of person and number
marking across Uilta, Sakhalin Ainu, and Nivkh has been carried out systematically
baek2021 and is addressed in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry .

Manchu proper was reached through trade rather than direct contact. The Manchu word
hasaha 'scissors' entered Nivkh as haza ; the
Hokkaido Ainu word for the same object, hasami , is a Japanese borrowing
from the same Manchu ultimate source — showing that the same trade good reached Ainu and Nivkh
through different intermediaries shiraishitangiku2022 §3 .

The ritual vocabulary item inaw ritual wood-shavings (ināw) has been proposed
as a Tungusic borrowing by Ikegami (1980), cited by Janhunen ‹speculative›
janhunen2022 §5 . If correct, ināw / inaw — central to Ainu ceremonial life and frequent throughout the corpus — is among the oldest
identifiable northern borrowings in the Hokkaido lexicon, pre-dating the Sakhalin trading
system.

The Sakhalin Ainu numeral system carries the clearest structural trace of northern contact.
Hokkaido Ainu counts vigesimally; Sakhalin uses a decimal system above ten, with the word for
'hundred', tanku , established as a Tungusic borrowing, and the word for
'ten', kunkutu , proposed on the basis of early records as a loan from
Itelmen/Kamchadal tangiku2022 §6.1 ‹contested›. The community analysis of
kunkutu as an Itelmen item independently converges with Tangiku's
hedged position
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-22).

The Sakhalin Ainu vocabulary of neighbouring peoples is richer than its Hokkaido counterpart,
reflecting the island's position in the Qing-era trade network. Attested ‹SA› forms include
manciw 'Manchu', canta 'Santan person',
kakan 'Khan', and nuca 'Russian'; the word
kawren 'Korean' has been speculatively connected to
高麗 Kōrai ‹speculative›
aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-10);
shiraishitangiku2022 §2 . Hokkaido Ainu has no equivalent vocabulary at this
density: Japanese-mediated contact, treated in japanese-loanwords-in-ainu , was
the channel through which the wider world reached HA.

What reached Hokkaido

The following table summarises the northern loanwords with clear or well-argued Hokkaido
reflexes together with the principal ‹SA› items. Sakhalin-only forms are marked ‹SA›;
confidence grades follow the evidence-grade conventions of this grammar.

Ainu form
HA gloss / meaning
Direction
Source language (proposed form)
Confidence

kaya
