The Wanderwort kotan settlement, village is the northern-contact item most
deeply embedded in the Hokkaido lexicon. It ranks 56th by corpus frequency (4,210 tokens), is
pervasive in oral literature as a geographic and social term, and its opaque internal structure
in Ainu points to external origin — though the ultimate source cannot be identified
janhunen2022 §5 ‹speculative›. The community observation that
kotan is a continental loan is corroborated by Janhunen's Eurasian
Wanderwort characterization aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-11-15). The word is fully integrated morphologically, taking possessive prefixes
and entering compounds freely; the compound kotankorkur headman, village chief
illustrates this integration:

kotan-kor-kur ne | settlement-have-person COP | He is a headman. | Constructed to illustrate the morphological integration of the Wanderwort kotan. The compound kotan-kor-kur (settlement-have-person) is attested throughout the Hokkaido oral-literature corpus; the third person takes zero marking, leaving the copula ne bare. | HK

kaya sail presents a different trajectory: retained with a more restricted
meaning than its Sakhalin source form, the semantic narrowing to 'sail' reflects the maritime
trade context of Hokkaido Ainu's contact with the northern network:

kaya a=kar | sail 4.A=make | (They) make a sail. | Constructed minimal context. kaya is the Hokkaido reflex of the Nivkh loanword; the Sakhalin cognate covered fish-skin garments as well (Batchelor 1938: 241). | HK

Structural contact and the Sprachbund question

Whether Sakhalin constituted a Sprachbund has two separable sub-questions: whether Sakhalin
Ainu shows contact-induced structural changes attributable to Nivkh or Tungusic, and whether
the three languages converged as an areal unit. Shiraishi and Tangiku leave the Sprachbund
label for future research shiraishitangiku2022 §1 ; on current evidence,
lexical exchange is real and directional, while the structural case is thin.

Person and number marking across Uilta, Sakhalin Ainu, and Nivkh has been compared directly
baek2021 . All three languages mark third person with zero or near-zero morphology,
and all three attach plural affixes at the outer edge of the person-marked verbal complex.
Whether these parallel patterns reflect contact, shared typological pressures on head-marking
systems, or independent development cannot be resolved from the existing data ‹speculative›.

The phonological innovations distinguishing Sakhalin from Hokkaido Ainu — final-consonant
debuccalization, the vowel-length system — are internal to Ainuic and independent of northern
contact; their diachrony is treated in proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction . The
claim in one view of the prosodic debate that Hokkaido pitch accent arose through contact with
Japanese concerns the HA end, not Sakhalin alonso2022 §2.3–2.4 ‹contested›;
that debate is reviewed there and in internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways .

Evidence base and its limits

The primary source for northern-contact data is Shiraishi and Tangiku's survey
shiraishitangiku2022 , which draws on Piłsudski's early-twentieth-century Sakhalin
corpus pilsudski1912 , post-war word-list records, and secondary comparative Nivkh
and Tungusic material. Three limits bound what can be established. First, the documentation of
Sakhalin contact vocabulary is uneven across the island's dialects and social groups. Second,
directionality of individual loans is difficult to verify without comprehensive comparative
Nivkh fieldwork, some of which remains unpublished. Third, individual interpreters working
between Ainu, Nivkh, and Uilta could have introduced phonologically irregular intermediary
forms that complicate the etymological record
shiraishitangiku2022 §2.5 .

For Hokkaido, the corpus gives little direct traction on the northern loanword layer. Items
such as kaya , sintoko , and
san are cultural vocabulary with low narrative frequency; only
kotan settlement is a corpus staple. Dictionary evidence —
batchelor1938 and tamura1996 — together with the secondary literature
remain the principal witnesses for most items. Japanese-mediated contact, which produced a
larger and better-attested Hokkaido loanword layer, is treated in
japanese-loanwords-in-ainu . The Ainu contribution to Japanese in the reverse
direction, including tonto → Japanese todo 'sea
lion' and tunakay → tonakai 'reindeer', is treated
in ainu-loanwords-and-toponymy-in-japanese . Macro-comparison proposals that would
extend the lexical Ainu–Nivkh relationship into a genetic one are catalogued and critiqued in
macro-comparison-and-deep-contact-controversies .

title: Object (Patient) Noun Incorporation
summary: Productive object incorporation (N+V → intransitive), its diagnostics, and its interaction with the antipassive and valency prefixes.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Object (Patient) Noun Incorporation
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: Productive object incorporation (N+V → intransitive), its diagnostics, and its interaction with the antipassive and valency prefixes.

Object incorporation in the valency system

Object noun incorporation takes a nominal stem and absorbs it into a transitive verb as its
Patient argument, producing a single verb word that expresses subject, incorporated object, and
predicate together nakagawa2024 480–483 ; satoni2022 §1–2 .
In Nakagawa's arity calculus the transitive host carries arity +2 — one slot for a subject
argument, one for an object argument. The incorporated noun fills the object slot at arity −1,
and the resulting compound has arity +1: an intransitive verb
nakagawa2024 137, 480 .
cep fish and koyki catch, strike yield
cep-koyki 'fish-catch': the nominal is inside the verb word and the
object argument position is saturated.

The criterion for wordhood is the outer position of the person index
nakagawa2024 482–483 ; satoni2022 §2 . With a phrasal object
the index attaches to the verb alone: cep ku=koyki has the 1SG prefix
on koyki , with cep as a separate noun phrase.
Under incorporation the index precedes the entire noun-verb complex:
ku=cep-koyki 'I fish-catch'. With 4th-person marking the diagnostic is
decisive: cep-koyki=an can only be one intransitive word, because the
transitive root koyki cannot host the intransitive S suffix
=an on its own
nakagawa2024 482–483 . The overview of the valency system in
valency-voice-overview introduces this diagnostic; the present chapter develops
object-type incorporation specifically: the detransitivizing morphology, the semantic vocabulary
of incorporated nouns, and the typological position of Hokkaido Ainu Object NI.

Detransitivization and person-marking consequences

Incorporating the object reduces the verb's argument count by one and shifts its person marking
from the transitive to the intransitive paradigm. Satō's minimal pair makes the shift visible
satoni2022 §2 : the transitive pukusa a=ta carries the
4th-person A prefix a= , which marks an indefinite transitive agent. Once
pukusa lily bulb is incorporated, the predicate becomes intransitive
pukusa-ta=an , bearing instead the 4th-person S suffix
=an .

pukusa a=ta | lily.bulb 4.A=dig | Someone digs lily bulbs. | Transitive: a= marks an indefinite 4th-person agent; pukusa 'lily bulb' is a free NP object external to the verb. | satoni2022:§2

pukusa-ta=an | lily.bulb-dig=4.S | Someone digs lily bulbs. | After object incorporation the intransitive S suffix =an replaces the transitive A prefix a=. The noun pukusa is now inside the verb word and can no longer appear as an external NP. | satoni2022:§2

The suffix =an is impossible on a transitive verb: a transitive verb
with an indefinite 4th-person agent must carry the A prefix a=
sato2008 96 ; nakagawa2024 137, 140 . It therefore serves as the
definitive morphological test for intransitivity after Object NI. The same alternation appears
with kinakar herb-harvest : the incorporated intransitive is
kinakar=an , while the phrasal construction gives the transitive
kina a=kar with the A prefix; this pair is consistent with the analysis
in satoni2022 §2 and has been independently noted in community discussion
(thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024 aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The same intransitivization is attested across the Saru oral corpus. Kawakami Matsuko uses
cep-koyki=an repeatedly in narrative; the 4th-person S suffix
=an appears on what is otherwise the transitive root
koyki ilcaa1976 :

pet or ta ran=an wa suy cep-koyki=an rok ayne | river place LOC descend.SG=4.S CONJ again fish-catch=4.S PL.PRF then | I went down to the river and caught fish again, and at last … | The 4.S suffix =an on cep-koyki is the diagnostic: after O-NI the compound is intransitive. The compound cep-koyki 'fish-catch' occurs 144 times in the Hokkaido corpus (rank 848). | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 13; ILCAA aa-irc/016#14

The person-marking shift is the sole morphological trace of Object NI. There is no overt
incorporation morpheme, no case suffix on the noun stem, and no change to TAM morphology
nakagawa2024 480–483 ; satoni2022 §2 . The incorporated noun
leaves no external argument slot: the Patient NP can no longer appear in the clause as a
co-referential noun phrase. The two-affix ceiling — at most one subject index and one object
index on any verb — bounds the system: Object NI uses up the object slot, leaving only the
subject slot available sato2023b 39 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §2 .

The four NI types and Chitose frequencies

Satō classifies Hokkaido Ainu noun incorporation into four types by the grammatical relation of
the incorporated noun to the base verb
sato2016 83–84 ; satoni2022 §3 ; nakagawa2024 483–491 . The distribution below comes from a count of 517 NI tokens in the Chitose-dialect speech of
the narrator 白沢ナベ sato2016 83–84 ; satoni2022 §3 . No
pan-Hokkaido NI-type frequency study has been published; the percentages are Chitose-specific
and derive from a single narrator's corpus ‹corpus-confirmed›.

type
% (CHI)
n
Mithun (1984) parallel
treated in

Object NI
85.9
444
Type I — lexical compounding
this chapter

Intransitive natural-force subject NI
6.8
35
Type I
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding

Intransitive possessor-requiring subject NI
5.6
29
Type II — manipulates external case

Transitive natural-force subject NI
1.7
9
(Type I)

Object NI accounts for nearly seven eighths of all incorporation tokens. Its alignment with
Mithun's Type I — lexical compounding in which the incorporated noun names a general activity
type rather than a specific referent — is consistent with the Chitose data, though the
theoretical status of Ainu NI more broadly is a live debate
mithun1984 ; satoni2022 §5 ; sato2016 83–84 .
The three subject-NI subtypes, including the possessor-stranding construction that is
typologically the most marked, are treated in
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding .

Ijäs's Lesson 3 introduces the two-argument transitive frame with the constructed example
numan hekaci cep koyki 'the boy caught some fish'
ijas2023 , the phrasal form of the root that appears incorporated as
cep-koyki above. Her lesson does not treat incorporation; the contrast
between the phrasal and the incorporated frame is developed here ‹consensus›.

Semantic fields of incorporated objects

Satō's Chitose count yields a breakdown of the semantic domains from which Object NI compounds
draw their incorporated nouns satoni2022 §3.1 :

semantic field
share of O-NI tokens (CHI)
representative examples

Space / location
26%
soy-osma 'plunge outside'

Body parts
18%
sik-erayke 'stare at'

Plants
11%

ni-tuye 'cut trees'; pukusa-ta 'dig lily
bulbs'

Tools / house
7%

cise-kar 'house-build';
wakka-ta 'water-scoop'

Fire
5%
ape-ari 'make a fire'

Other / miscellaneous
33%
kem-eyki 'sew' (lit. 'thread-pierce')

The space category reflects the frequency of spatial compounds in which a location noun
functions as the direct Patient of the incorporating verb: these are Object NI, with the
locative noun already in the object grammatical relation. Oblique incorporations — instruments
and locatives that were first promoted to object position by an applicative before
incorporating — are a distinct construction treated in
noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate
shibatani1990 62, 66–67 ; nakagawa2024 484 . Body-part compounds
cluster around perception and contact verbs; plant, tool, and fire nouns reflect the
subsistence activities that are most elaborated in Ainu oral narrative. The following Chitose
example illustrates the tool / house type, with cise house absorbed into
kar make, build :

toy ku=hok wa or-o ta ku=cise-kar wa k=an ruwe ne wa | land 1SG.A=buy CONJ place-POSS LOC 1SG.S=house-build CONJ 1SG.S=exist track-POSS COP CONJ | I bought land, built a house there, and lived on. | ku=cise-kar: the 1SG prefix is the sole argument index on the compound, showing that cise 'house' has been absorbed and the resulting verb is intransitive. k=an is contracted ku=an 'I exist / live'. | satoni2022:§3.1 | CHI

Satō's corpus attests several NI↔phrase near-pairs —
apa asi / apa-asi 'close the door',
cep koyki / cep-koyki ,
wakka ta / wakka-ta — but these alternations are
few and not systematically productive; most Object NI compounds have no attested phrasal
counterpart satoni2022 §3.1 ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The genericity restriction and the lexical character of Object NI

The incorporated noun must be semantically unbounded: a generic, non-referring stem
satoni2022 §2 ; sato2008 225–226 . A common noun such as
cep 'fish' incorporates freely into
cep-koyki 'fish-catch'. A specific proper noun cannot:
*Sikotpet-tomotuye 'cross the Shikotsu river' is ungrammatical because
a river name is an inherently referring expression
satoni2022 §2 ; sato2008 225–226 .

Ainu Object NI groups with Mithun's Type I — lexical compounding in which the incorporated
noun names the type of object typically affected rather than a referential argument
mithun1984 ; satoni2022 §3.1, §5 ; sato2016 83–84 . Nakagawa places the same character in a distributional frame: NI compounds are commoner in
verse than in ordinary prose or conversation, and they represent a lexicalized stratum of the
verb vocabulary rather than a productive derivational slot
nakagawa2024 480 . Whether Ainu NI is best analysed as purely lexical, as
Baker's syntactic head-movement, or as a hybrid that must reference both grammatical relations
and semantic roles as Shibatani proposes is a dispute the sources consulted do not resolve; it is
taken up in noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate
shibatani1988 ; shibatani1990 60–66 ; baker1988 ‹contested›.

A small set of verbs permits simultaneous incorporation of two nominal stems, producing rare
three-element compounds. Nakagawa cites
cep-ya-o-kuta 'dump fish onto the shore' — incorporands
cep 'fish' and ya 'shore', mediated by the
applicative o- — and
itak-mak-kus-te 'use hidden words' as attested cases
nakagawa2024 488 . The general limit is one incorporated noun per verb
shibatani1990 72 , and these forms are marginal in the corpus
‹corpus-suggested›.

NI compounds that have lost any productive phrasal alternant are synchronically lexicalized
denominal verbs; the derivational pathway that produces them connects to the broader denominal
processes treated in denominal-deverbal-verb-derivation . A diachronic line also
links Object NI to the antipassive prefix i- antipassive : Bugaeva proposes
that i- arose from grammaticalization of O-incorporation of a generic
noun, with the 4th-person pronoun i= derived secondarily from the
antipassive bugaeva2021antip 235–239 , while Nam reconstructs the source as a
distinct generic-noun stem nam2021 63–74 ; the dispute belongs to
antipassive-detransitive-i ‹contested›.

Where Object NI is blocked — most critically at the possessor-requiring type (impossible because
an inflectional possessor makes the incorporated noun specific, and no reflexive rescue is
available) — the function passes to quasi-incorporation (QI), a tight phrasal unit
that is idiomatic but not yet a single verb word
satoni2022 §4, §6 ; sato2016 88–92 . The QI construction and
the markedness calculus governing the NI–QI division are developed in
noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate .

title: Oblique Incorporation and the Syntactic-vs-Lexicalist Debate
summary: Adverbial/oblique incorporation, its feeding of relativization, and the central syntactic-NI vs lexical-compounding controversy.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Oblique Incorporation and the Syntactic-vs-Lexicalist Debate
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: Adverbial/oblique incorporation, its feeding of relativization, and the central syntactic-NI vs lexical-compounding controversy.

Oblique incorporation via the applicative

The basic architecture of noun-incorporation-object noun incorporation in
Hokkaido Ainu is the person-affix diagnostic: when a nominal stem merges with a verb, the
person index moves to precede the entire noun-verb complex, making the incorporation a
one-word structure nakagawa2024 482–483 ; satoni2022 §2 .
Object (patient) and patient-like subject incorporation — the overwhelming majority —
follow this path directly. The route for oblique participants is different: an instrument, a
location, a source, a purpose, or a comitative argument cannot incorporate until an
applicative-system-overview applicative prefix has first promoted it to
core-object status shibatani1990 62, 66 ; nakagawa2024 484 ;
satoni2022 §1 . The further details of how applicatives stack and feed
relativization belong to applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding ; the
present chapter develops the oblique-to-incorporated derivation itself and the theoretical
debate over what that derivation implies.

Shibatani (1990: 67) traces the derivation through three steps, illustrated with the verb
kuykuy bite . In the source phrase, the comitative participant
pone tura ('bone' + comitative postposition
tura 'with') is a peripheral argument:
nea cep pone tura a=kuykuy 'I bit the fish with its bones'. The
applicative ko- applicative (goal / comitative) promotes
pone to an additional core object, dropping the postposition and
yielding the intermediate nea cep pone a=ko-kuykuy . The newly
created object then incorporates: the person prefix a= moves
outside the whole complex.

nea cep a=pone-ko-kuykuy | that fish 4.A=bone-APPL-bite | I bit the fish with its bones. | Shibatani ex. 122, stage 3 of the three-step derivation. The person prefix a= (4th person transitive agent) sits outermost, proving that pone-ko-kuykuy is a single word. The original patient nea cep 'that fish' remains as the surface transitive object. | shibatani1990:67 | HK

Two properties of the result bear on the theoretical debate in § noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate debate . First, the affix ordering is not arbitrary: the incorporated noun sits outside the
applicative, which sits inside the verb root — the order applicative-inside /
incorporated-noun-outside mirrors the derivational history, consistent with what Baker
(1988) terms the Mirror Principle baker1988 ; shibatani1990 70–71 . Second, the entire sequence remains a single verb word, because no person index
intervenes between the incorporated noun and the root.
kaiser1998 examines the applicative–incorporation interaction in Ainu
systematically; the constraints she identifies align with the analysis in Shibatani
(1990) and Nakagawa (2024: 484).

Postposition–applicative correspondences

Each postposition or case relation that can be expressed obliquely has a corresponding
applicative encoding the same relation as a core object, and only through that applicative
can the noun incorporate. Nakagawa (2024: 484) summarises the pairings; the table below
assembles them alongside the relevant applicative prefix and an incorporated-form gloss.

oblique marker / case
applicative
semantic role
incorporated form
meaning

ani 'with (instrument)'
e-
instrument
at-e-uk
'catch with a net' (cf. at ani uk )

or ta / un 'at / to'
e- / ko-
locative / allative
pira-ko-hokuste
'fall toward a cliff' (cf. pira or un hokuste )

kusu 'for (purpose)'
e-
purpose
turepta-e-arpa
'go to dig lily-bulbs' (cf. turepta kusu arpa )

tura 'with (comitative)'
ko-
comitative
mun-ko-tata
'chop with grass/scraps'

or un 'to (place)'
o-
goal (place only)
o-tumi-osma
'war arose there' (cf. ambiguous tumi osma 'war began')

Sources: nakagawa2024 484 ; shibatani1990 62–66 ;
satoni2022 §1 . The o- applicative (allative, place)
applicative is restricted to place nouns and is rare in prose
nakagawa2024 198–199 ; Shibatani (1990: 63) notes that the sequence
o-tumi-osma , with the applicative, is unambiguously incorporated,
whereas bare tumi osma is ambiguous between a phrase and a compound
in third-person contexts (see § noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate word-boundary ).

A corpus example illustrates the comitative feed in a running text. Nakagawa (2024: 198)
cites a Chitose narration in which two coordinate clauses apply
ko- to the comitative participants
mun grass, weeds and toy earth, soil , incorporating both
into the verb tata chop, mince :

a=mun-ko-tata a=toy-ko-tata ayne | 4.A=grass-APPL-chop 4.A=earth-APPL-chop CONJ | (I) chopped it with grass and chopped it with earth — | The ko- applicative encodes the comitative participant ('with'); the comitative postposition tura is dropped. The person prefix a= (4th person transitive agent) sits outside the noun-verb complex in each clause, confirming one-word status. The object of the chopping — the demon's flesh — is recoverable from context and left unexpressed. | nakagawa2024:197 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 190

Once the oblique has been applicativized and then incorporated, the resulting verb word is
structurally identical to an ordinary object-incorporated compound; the derivational history
is preserved only in the affix order (applicative inside the noun) and in meaning.
kaiser1998 treats the applicative–incorporation interaction as a single system
on these grounds.

Adverb incorporation and stacking limits

Adverbs incorporate directly into the verb without any applicative prerequisite. The
mechanism drops the adverbial suffix -no from the basic adverb form
and places the adverb stem immediately before the verb root
shibatani1990 71–72 ; nakagawa2024 484, 492 . The minimal
pair for pirka-no well, finely illustrates the contrast:

pirka-no a=ipere | well 4.A=feed | (I) fed them well. | Phrasal form; pirka-no 'well' is an adverb outside the verb word, shown by the person prefix a= sitting on ipere alone.

a=pirka-ipere | 4.A=well-feed | (I) fed them well. | Incorporated form; -no is dropped and the person prefix a= precedes the entire adverb-verb complex, proving one-word status. | shibatani1990:72 | HK

A noun and an adverb may co-incorporate — Shibatani (1990: 72) cites
kiraw-riki-kur-roski 'raise the horns high', with the noun
kiraw 'horn' and the adverb riki
'high, up' both inside one verb word. The one-noun-at-a-time limit is the productive
norm: Shibatani (1990: 72) states that two independently incorporable nouns cannot
ordinarily stack. Nakagawa (2024: 488) records rare exceptions
( cep-ya-o-kuta 'dump fish onto the shore',
itak-mak-kus-te 'use hidden words'), which he places at the
lexicalized fringe of the system rather than as evidence for productive double-NI. The
quasi-incorporation strategy discussed in § noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate markedness-qi is a separate, structurally different response to the ceiling on NI — it handles
cases where NI is grammatically blocked, not cases where noun stacking would otherwise
be possible.

The syntactic-vs-lexicalist debate: positions catalogued

Ainu noun incorporation has attracted theoretical attention because it sits uncomfortably
within any single prior account. Shibatani (1988: 210–211) reviewed three frameworks at
the 1988 LSJ annual meeting — Relational Grammar, Mithun's semantic account, and Baker's
syntactic derivation — finding each insufficient on its own; the 1988 source is a
one-page abstract, and the extended discussion appears in Shibatani (1990: 60–73).
Subsequent contributions from Satō and Bugaeva have refined the picture further. The
positions are catalogued below with evidence grades.

position
holder(s)
core claim
grade

Relational Grammar
Perlmutter & Postal (1984), reviewed in shibatani1988 210
only initial grammatical subjects and objects can incorporate
‹contested› — the grammatical-relation requirement is partly supported, but
'initial relations' is too restrictive given that applicative-derived objects
also incorporate

semantic / Patient-targeting
mithun1984
NI targets Patients on semantic grounds, following a four-type developmental
hierarchy (I → IV); NI is a lexical operation akin to compounding
‹contested› — Satō's Type IV (natural-force transitive subject NI) falsifies
the Patient-only characterization; the lexical-compounding parallel is
undermined by the near-absence of pure noun-noun compounds in the Ainu lexicon
‹contested›

syntactic head-movement
baker1988
NI = N-to-V head-movement regulated by ECP and Case theory; applicative
formation = P-incorporation; predicts that unaccusative clauses cannot
host applicative or P-incorporation
‹contested› — falsified for Ainu by the unaccusative-applicative data (see
below); the Mirror Principle argument for a syntactic derivation partially
survives even if Case-theoretic details do not

hybrid (grammatical relation + semantic role)
shibatani1988 210–211 ; shibatani1990 60–73
neither semantic role alone nor grammatical relation alone suffices; Ainu NI
targets the Patient-subject of intransitives and the object of transitives
(including applicative-derived objects); obliques incorporate only via
applicative, confirming that grammatical object status is required
‹contested› — the strongest reading of the accessible data; Satō's
subsequent revision narrows the semantic component from 'Patient' to 'low
agentivity'

two-type NI (lexical + syntactic)
Shibatani (1992), via satoni2022 §1
Ainu may carry both a lexical-component and a syntactic-component NI; the
two types are not mutually exclusive
‹speculative› — the 1992 position is reported second-hand via Satō (2022);
not operationalized against corpus data in the accessible sources

low-agentivity (not unaccusativity)
sato2008 221–227 ; sato2016 83–88 ;
satoni2022 §1, §5
because Ainu has natural-force transitive subject NI (Type IV), the
unaccusative account fails; the defining property is the incorporated noun's
low degree of agentivity; four types ranked by a markedness calculus
consensus among the sources consulted for this domain

NI⊃QI accessibility hierarchy
sato2016 88–92 ; satoni2022 §6
NI and quasi-incorporation form one accessibility scale; where NI is blocked
by markedness, quasi-incorporation fills the gap
‹corpus-suggested› — Satō's Chitose counts support the hierarchy, but the
frequencies come from one narrator

Baker-parameter polysynthesis
bugaeva2014
Ainu is polysynthetic in Baker's sense (holophrastic argument encoding)
but deviates from the Mohawk prototype in word order,
wh -in-situ, and unrestricted morphological causatives
‹contested› — the 2014 source is a one-page symposium abstract; full
argument not published in the accessible corpus

grammatical constraints > semantic specificity
yoshida2013 173
in Chitose data (compared with a Sakhalin sample), grammatical constraints
outrank the semantic specificity constraint on incorporated nouns
‹contested› — small sample; corroborates the markedness calculus but does not
decide between a syntactic and lexicalist mechanism

Baker's Case-theoretic prediction affords the clearest empirical test the debate contains.
Baker (1988) derives from Case theory that unaccusative clauses cannot undergo applicative
or P-incorporation, because the internal argument already fills the available Case position
baker1988 . Ainu falsifies this directly: applicatives attach freely to
unaccusative bases, yielding objects that can then incorporate. Shibatani (1990: 66) cites
mokor a=e-wen 'I missed sleeping' and
sake a=e-niste 'I am strong at wine', both with
e- on an unaccusative base, as the falsifying data
shibatani1990 66 ‹contested›. This result disfavours the specific
Case-theoretic derivation without settling whether an alternative syntactic mechanism
or a lexicalist account better describes the system.

Shibatani's hybrid position requires reference to both the semantic role (low agentivity)
and the grammatical position (derived or original object) of the incorporated element.
Satō (2008: 226; 2022, §1) revises the semantic-role component: natural-force transitive
subjects also incorporate (Type IV, 1.7% of Chitose tokens), so 'Patient' and 'unaccusative
subject' are both too narrow. The correct generalisation is the incorporated noun's low degree
of agentivity — a property shared by patients, natural forces, and possessed body-parts —
and no other currently defined property predicts all four types jointly
satoni2022 §1 . Shibatani's grammatical-relation requirement (object or
patient-subject, including applicative-derived objects) survives the revision intact.

Community discussion in the aynu-corpora Discord has noted that Ainu has very few
pure-noun endocentric or exocentric compounds — constructions built solely from two nouns
without a verbal head — with nominal formation concentrated on verb stems
(antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, June 2026; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›.
Taken at face value, this would contrast Ainu with the kind of free lexical noun-noun
compounding that Mithun (1984) treats as the prototype of Type I NI
mithun1984 , lending support to treating Ainu NI as morphologically
integrated into the verbal system. The observation is a community-proposed lead and has not
been submitted to systematic quantification across the corpus.

A related terminological divide runs through the valency literature and is relevant to the
syntactic-vs-lexicalist question. The prefix i- antipassive / generic object
is analysed as an antipassive on syntactic, semantic, and discourse grounds by Bugaeva
(2021), and labelled accordingly by Nakagawa (2024: 190–192) and Ijäs (Lesson 9)
bugaeva2021antip ; ijas2023 . Satō (2008: 232–233) treats the same
prefix as a derivational marker 'people, someone' without invoking the antipassive category
‹contested›. The antipassive analysis presupposes a syntactic operation demoting a
grammatical object; the derivational analysis is compatible with a fully lexicalist account
of the same data. The origin dispute and fuller analysis are in
antipassive-detransitive-i .

Satō's markedness calculus and the NI⊃QI hierarchy

Satō (2016: 88; 2022, §5) assigns a markedness penalty to each NI type by summing
restriction factors and subtracting rescue factors. The penalty scores differ between
the 2016 article and the 2022 Handbook chapter in their absolute values, but the rank
order and the Chitose frequencies — drawn from the late Shirasawa Nabe's corpus, a
single narrator — are identical across both sources. The table records both sets of
scores; Satō (2022, §5.7) himself describes them as "tentatively defined"
satoni2022 §5 ; sato2016 84–88 .

NI type
Chitose frequency
net penalty (2022 / 2016)
dominant rescue factor

Object NI
85.9% (444 tokens)
0 / 0
—

Natural-force intransitive subject NI
( sir-pirka , me-an )
6.8% (35)
5 / 2
Backgrounding: natural-force noun (−5 / −8)

Possessor-requiring intransitive subject NI (body-part; possessor stranded)
5.6% (29)
6 / 3
Backgrounding (−5 / −8) + Reflexive interpretation (−14 / −9)

Natural-force transitive subject NI
( rir-tursere , apto-kar )
1.7% (9)
9 / (varies)
Backgrounding: natural-force noun

Possessor-requiring Object NI
0% (0 tokens)
≥ 10 (ungrammatical)
no reflexive rescue available for an object position

Restriction factors: Subject NI [+10], Possessor Stranding [+15 in Satō 2022 / +10 in
Satō 2016], Ambiguity [+4 in 2022 / +7 in 2016]. Rescue factors: Backgrounding [−5 in
2022 / −8 in 2016], Reflexive interpretation [−14 in 2022 / −9 in 2016]. The absolute
values are tentative; the stable result is the rank order and the zero-frequency gap at
the bottom ‹corpus-suggested›.

The systematic zero at the foot of the table arises because the inflectional possessor
that body-part nouns require makes the incorporated noun definite and thereby removes the
reflexive-rescue option. Satō (2022, §4) illustrates the ungrammatical gap with
*a=i-par-oyki 'I am fed by others' (lit. 'fed at the mouth'):
i= would supply the possessor for par mouth ,
but i= on an object creates a definite, non-reflexive reading
that blocks the rescue satoni2022 §4 .

This gap is filled by what Satō terms quasi-incorporation (QI) —
[O V] units that are tight, idiomatic, and non-compositional
in meaning but remain transitive: the person index for the main object sits outside
the QI complex as a whole, whereas in true NI the person index precedes the
incorporated noun and the result is intransitive nakagawa2024 494–498 ;
satoni2022 §6 . Nakagawa (2024: 494–498) calls the same items
連動詞 (serial verbs / third-class verbs). The
noun-incorporation-object chapter lists the QI inventory in full; the example
below shows the transitive structure that distinguishes it from NI:

ku=kor yupi ney pak en=askeuk wa | 1SG.A=have elder.brother ever only 1SG.O=invite CONJ | My elder brother always invited me, and … | askeuk (lit. 'hand-take') is a QI compound meaning 'invite'; the first-singular-object prefix en= sits outside the entire askeuk complex, showing that the compound stays transitive. Under true NI, the incorporated noun would intransitivize the verb and the outer index would be a subject suffix. | nakagawa2024:497 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 1139, after Sunazawa 1983: 182

The NI⊃QI accessibility hierarchy that Satō (2016: 88–92; 2022, §6) derives runs:
Object NI > Natural-force intransitive subject NI > Possessor-requiring intransitive
subject NI > Natural-force transitive subject NI > Possessor-requiring Object NI —
with NI covering the left-hand, accessible portion and QI taking over once NI is
structurally or semantically blocked. The hierarchy extends Mithun's four-type scale into
the QI domain and provides a unified account of why certain noun-verb collocations
lexicalize as NI while others lexicalize as QI
sato2016 88–92 ; satoni2022 §6–7 .

A register asymmetry runs across the whole system. Nakagawa (2024: 480) notes that
incorporation is commoner in verse than in prose or conversation; Ainu rarely
incorporates two nouns into one verb. The verse preference is consistent with
observations in Mithun (1984) that NI often develops in rhetorically dense contexts
mithun1984 , though the Ainu data do not support a diachronic
claim about direction of change ‹speculative›.

Third-person undecidability

The person-affix test that defines incorporation fails for third-person contexts.
Hokkaido Ainu marks third-person A and O with a zero morph: a sequence like
cep koyki has no surface index to place inside or outside, so the
test cannot determine whether the sequence is a phrasal cep koyki
or an incorporated cep-koyki
nakagawa2024 482 ; shibatani1990 62 . For any incorporable
pair with two third-person arguments, lexicographic representation and interlinear glossing
must choose between the two analyses without a decisive test.

Two secondary diagnostics are sometimes invoked but neither is conclusive in the general
case. Accent and intonation might separate a compound from a phrase, but Nakagawa (2024:
482) states that no firm basis for an accentual test has been established. A derivational
prefix on the nominal-verbal sequence (e.g. an applicative over the whole pair) proves
one-word status when attested, since derivational morphology cannot straddle two separate
words; but the prefixed form and the bare form are distinct constructions and the
diagnostic applies only where the prefixed form independently exists. The open-class-verb
cases — apto as 'rain fell', upas as ,
rera as — are formally undecidable in the third person, and
Nakagawa (2024: 493–494) leaves their word-boundary status open.

Where the fourth person replaces the third, the test is again decisive. The fourth-person
subject suffix =an is licensed only on intransitives; its presence
on a noun-verb sequence is unambiguous evidence of incorporation and intransitivization.
This diagnostic has been observed in community practice with the pair
kinakar=an (incorporated, intransitive) beside
kina a=kar (phrasal, transitive with the fourth-person A prefix
a= ) for the colocation of
kina plant material, vegetables and
kar make, gather (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord, December 2024;
aynucorporadiscord ). The observation directly corroborates Nakagawa (2024:
482–483) and Satō (2022, §2) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The undecidability of third-person cases does not affect the theoretical positions
catalogued in § noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate debate — the incorporated forms with first- and fourth-person agreement supply sufficient
data for the relevant contrasts — but it does place a ceiling on frequency counts
derived from surface strings. The Chitose percentages in § noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate markedness-qi are from one narrator; comparable counts for Saru or Horobetsu are not available
because the undecidability of third-person tokens in other narrators' texts makes
systematic NI frequency comparison across dialects intractable at present ‹speculative›.
The typological comparison — Mithun's four-type hierarchy applied to Ainu — is
introduced in valency-voice-overview ;
subject incorporation and possessor-stranding data are developed in
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding ;
the transitivity diagnostics that ground the person-affix test in the wider grammar
belong to transitivity-and-valence-classes .

title: Subject Incorporation and Possessor-Stranding
summary: Subject incorporation with unaccusative verbs (sik-pirka 'eyes-be.good') and the possessor-stranding pattern — the classic Type IV test case.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Subject Incorporation and Possessor-Stranding
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: Subject incorporation with unaccusative verbs (sik-pirka 'eyes-be.good') and the possessor-stranding pattern — the classic Type IV test case.

Two kinds of subject incorporation

noun-incorporation-object Object incorporation accounts for the overwhelming
majority of Ainu noun incorporation, but the language also incorporates subjects. Satō's
Chitose count places subject incorporation at about 14% of all incorporation tokens, divided
into three functional subtypes that differ in which noun is incorporated, what happens to the
verb's valency, and whether an external possessor must be retained
satoni2022 §3 ; sato2016 83–84 .

subtype
Mithun type
% (Chitose)
n
valency effect

natural-force intransitive subject NI
I
6.8
35
zero-place result; no phrasal counterpart

possessor-requiring intransitive subject NI
II
5.6
29
none — possessor promoted to S

natural-force transitive subject NI
I
1.7
9
−1; former O surfaces as S

These figures come from the corpus of the late Shirasawa Nabe (Chitose) and reflect one
idiolect; no pan-Hokkaido NI-type frequency study has been published ‹corpus-suggested›.
The wordhood diagnostic is the same as for object incorporation: because the person index
is the outermost element of the verb word, an incorporated noun must sit inside it, and a
fourth-person S suffix on a normally transitive base is decisive proof of intransitivisation
nakagawa2024 482–483 ; satoni2022 §2 . The broader valency
architecture is in valency-voice-overview , and object incorporation in
noun-incorporation-object .

Natural-force intransitive subject NI

The incorporated noun in this subtype is not a full lexical noun but a bound stem denoting
a natural force or ambient condition. Two such stems are attested in Hokkaido:
sir weather, appearance, land and me cold
satoni2022 §3.2 ; nakagawa2024 489 . Neither appears as an
independent subject noun alongside the same predicate — the phrasal counterpart does not
exist — which distinguishes this subtype sharply from object incorporation, where phrasal
and incorporated forms often alternate
satoni2022 §3.2 . Nakagawa groups these predicates among the zero-place
verbs of the verb-class system; their person-prefix behaviour confirms that status, since
the zero-arity result takes no person affixes in its basic meteorological use
nakagawa2024 489 .

The weather stem sir weather combines with stative predicates to form a set
of meteorological expressions: sirpirka the weather is fine ,
sirkunne it is dark, night falls ,
sirramante the weather is calm , and similar. The corpus count for
sirpirka stands at 102 tokens (rank 1,104), confirming its standing
as the most frequent member of the set ‹corpus-confirmed›. The cold stem
me cold functions identically: me-an 'it is cold'
incorporates me as the subject of the existential
an , again without an independent-noun counterpart
satoni2022 §3.2 .

nisatta anak sir-pirka nankor | tomorrow TOP weather-be.good INFR | The weather will be fine tomorrow. | The absorbed sir 'weather' is the incorporated zero-argument subject; pirka 'be good' is the predicate. No person index appears because the result is a zero-place predicate; nankor expresses the inferential. | satoni2022:§3 | CHI | Satō 2022 ch. 16 Appendix (ii)

Nakagawa's subclassification requires that the incorporated noun appear in its concept form
(裸形), as opposed to the possessed form that characterises the possessor-requiring subtype
below
nakagawa2024 489 . The two forms — concept and possessed — are treated in
concept-form-affiliative-form .

Possessor-requiring intransitive subject NI and possessor stranding

The second intransitive subtype incorporates a body-part or relational noun that
grammatically requires a possessor. Because the incorporated noun carries its possessive
morphology into the verb word, that possessor must surface as an external argument — the
phenomenon Satō terms possessor stranding
satoni2022 §3.3 ; sato2016 87–89 . This corresponds to
Mithun's Type II: the construction rearranges grammatical relations rather than merely
lexically compounding
mithun1984 ; satoni2022 §3.3 .

The mechanism is this: a stative intransitive verb whose S argument is a body-part noun
takes that noun in its concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form
(所属形) and incorporates it. The possessed form grammatically encodes that the noun belongs
to someone; that possessor must therefore be supplied from outside the incorporated complex,
and it is promoted to the S of the whole predicate. Overall arity does not decrease: the
promoted possessor fills exactly the slot that the incorporated noun's argument position
vacated
satoni2022 §3.3 ; nakagawa2024 489–490 .

ku=tek-e-pase | 1SG.S=hand-POSS-heavy | My hands feel heavy (I feel I've aged). | tek-e is the possessed form of tek 'hand', with -e as the possession suffix (distinct from the applicative e-). The stative base is pase 'be heavy'. The possessor of the hands — the speaker — is promoted to S of the whole predicate and indexed by ku=. | satoni2022:§3 | CHI | Satō 2022 ch. 16 §3.3

The possessed-form noun appearing as an incorporated subject is itself the structural
signature of this type and distinguishes it from the natural-force type, where the
incorporand is in concept form. A further example shows the possessor-stranding structure
in context: Satō's Chitose Appendix (iii) gives
ku=ram-u-tuy wa ku=si-y-etu-esikari 'I was startled and grabbed at
my own nose'
satoni2022 §3 . The first clause incorporates
ram heart, mind in its possessed form as the subject of
u-tuy (RECP-cut, intransitive); the possessor of the heart, the
speaker, surfaces as S indexed by ku= . The second clause uses the
indirect reflexive si- as the quasi-possessive of the incorporated
etu nose — a pattern treated in reflexive-possessive-si .

What licenses the stranded possessor is a reflexive interpretation: the possessor must be
understood as coreferential with the subject of the predication — that is, the body-part
is read as belonging reflexively to whoever occupies S. Satō formalizes this as a
reflexive-interpretation rescue that offsets most of the markedness penalty that
stranding would otherwise attract
satoni2022 §5 ; sato2016 87–90 .

Satō's markedness calculus and the systematic gap

Satō ranks all incorporation subtypes by a constraint-and-rescue scoring system, where
each restriction adds penalty points and each licensing factor subtracts them
satoni2022 §5 ; sato2016 88 . Penalty values differ between
the 2022 Handbook chapter and the 2016 paper (alternative scores in parentheses), but the
rank order and frequency proportions are identical.

subtype
restrictions (+)
rescues (−)
net (2022 / 2016)
% Chitose

object NI
—
—
0
85.9

nat.-force intr. subject NI
Subject +10
Backgrounding −5 / −8
5 / 2
6.8

possessor-req. intr. subject NI
Subject +10, Stranding +15 / +10
Backgrounding −5 / −8, Reflexive −14 / −9
6 / 3
5.6

nat.-force tr. subject NI
Subject +10, Ambiguity +4 / +7
Backgrounding −5 / −8
9
1.7

possessor-req. object NI
Stranding +15 / +10, Ambiguity +4 / +7
— (no reflexive rescue)
10
0 (ungrammatical)

The Backgrounding rescue applies to natural-force and body-part nouns because their low
referential salience makes incorporation semantically unobtrusive. The Reflexive rescue
applies only where the stranded possessor is coreferential with the predicate's subject —
possible for subject-incorporated body-parts (possessor = experiencer = S) but not for
possessed object nouns, because the inflectional possessor i= there
makes the noun definite and blocks any reflexive reading
satoni2022 §4–5 .

The final row exposes the systematic gap: possessor-requiring object NI is
ungrammatical because no available rescue reduces the net penalty sufficiently. The slot is
instead filled by quasi-incorporation — Satō's third verb class of idiomatic tight phrasal
constructions such as par oyki 'feed' and
aske uk 'invite' — treated in
noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate
satoni2022 §6–7 .

The agentivity restriction

Shibatani observed that Ainu subjects do not incorporate freely: only the patient-subject
of an intransitive — not the agent — incorporates, and he proposed that this tracks
unaccusativity
shibatani1988 210–211 ; shibatani1990 62 . On that account,
the incorporable subjects are those that originate as the underlying object of an unaccusative
base, while agents of unergative predicates cannot incorporate.

Satō accepts the empirical restriction but rejects unaccusativity as the defining criterion,
on the grounds that the natural-force transitive subtype — in which the original A of a
transitive predicate incorporates — cannot be captured by the unaccusative account
sato2008 226 ; satoni2022 §1 . The property that covers all
subtypes is low degree of agentivity : a noun is incorporable as subject when it
denotes a force or body-part acting without volition, intention, or independent agency. Waves,
rain, wind, cold, and body-parts all meet this criterion; human or animal agents do not.
Unaccusativity then becomes a sufficient condition for incorporability — all unaccusative
subjects qualify — but not a necessary one ‹contested›.

The connection to the alignment-split-intransitive split-intransitive alignment
is direct: the distinction between patient-like and agent-like S arguments is already
grammaticalized in Hokkaido Ainu person marking, and the class of incorporable subjects maps
onto the patient-like side of that split. The theoretical debate between the unaccusativity
account and the low-agentivity account is not settled in the sources surveyed here ‹contested›.

Natural-force transitive subject NI

The rarest subtype incorporates the subject of a transitive verb. The incorporated noun is
always a natural-force concept — wave, rain, snow, wind, light, deity — and must appear in
concept form, not possessed form, which is the same morphological criterion that applies in
the natural-force intransitive subtype
nakagawa2024 489–491 ; satoni2022 §3.4 .

The structural result is passive-like: the incorporated noun was the A of the transitive
base; after incorporation the arity drops by one and the former O surfaces as the new S,
taking S person suffixes. The intransitive suffix =an (fourth-person
S) on the derived verb is the diagnostic
nakagawa2024 489–491 .

rir-tursere=an kor mom=an | wave-tumble=4.S CONJ drift=4.S | I was rolled by waves and drifted. | rir 'wave' is the incorporated former A subject of tursere 'roll/tumble'; the original O (the speaker) now surfaces as S, indexed by =an. The parallel mom=an 'drift' is a separate intransitive clause. | nakagawa2024:490 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 1119 (excerpt)

apto as kor apto-kar=an upas as kor upas-kar=an rera-kar=an | rain fall CONJ rain-strike=4.S snow fall CONJ snow-strike=4.S wind-strike=4.S | I was rained on, snowed on, and struck by wind. | apto 'rain', upas 'snow', and rera 'wind' are the incorporated former A subjects of kar 'strike/do'; =an on each compound proves the derived intransitive. The parallel phrases apto as and upas as present the same nouns as free subjects in coreferential adverbial clauses. | nakagawa2024:490 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 1121, after Chiba University Ainu oral-literature materials 2015: 190

The attested incorporands for this subtype include
kamuy deity , koy wave , rir wave, billow ,
me cold , nis cloud , rera wind ,
sukus light , apto rain , and upas snow
nakagawa2024 489–491 . All denote ambient natural forces with minimal
agentivity. Satō notes that this subtype is "probably impossible in many languages", which
makes it one of the more typologically distinctive features of Ainu noun incorporation
satoni2022 §3.4 .

The ko- applicative inside sir- verbs

The zero-argument meteorological predicates built on sir- can host
the applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding applicative
ko- , which adds a beneficiary or experiencer and promotes it to the
verb's sole indexed argument. Forms like sir-ko-kunne 'it darkens
for X / night falls for X' and ku=sir-ko-peker 'the weather
brightens for me / I experience daybreak' are current in Chitose and Saru
nakagawa2024 489 .

A community-proposed analysis (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-05) reads
ku=sir-ko-peker as follows: the base meteorological verb
sirpeker 'the weather brightens' is a closed stem in which
sir- is already incorporated; the applicative
ko- inserts into this closed stem between the incorporated noun
and the root rather than attaching to the outside of the compound. The
ko- object — the experiencer — is then promoted to the overall
subject, indexed by ku= . Parallel structures proposed in the same
discussion include maw-ko-wen 'the mist becomes bad for X' and
kur-ko-tunas 'it becomes cold for someone'
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›.

A related morphological observation from the same source concerns ordering. The attested
form is sir-ko-kunne , not the hypothetical
*ko-sir-kunne . This ordering — where the incorporated subject
sir- precedes the applicative ko- — is
consistent with subject incorporation occupying the pre-applicative, pre-root slot of the
verb template, not a position inside the applicative layer, and is proposed as evidence
that subject incorporation is structurally distinct from other prefix types
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. The verb-word template is treated in
valency-voice-overview , and the feeding relation between applicatives and
incorporation in applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding .

title: Noun–Noun Compounding: Headedness, Linking, and Semantics
summary: Productive N-N compound formation, its headedness and morphophonological linking, and the semantic typology of compounds, drawn from the dedicated Ainu compound-noun literature.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Noun–Noun Compounding: Headedness, Linking, and Semantics
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: Productive N-N compound formation, its headedness and morphophonological linking, and the semantic typology of compounds, drawn from the dedicated Ainu compound-noun literature.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu forms compound nouns by combining two or more roots into a single phonological
and morphological word. The process is productive across the lexicon and supplies much of the
vocabulary for tools, animals, plants, social roles, and place names
nakagawa2024 261–272 . Its dedicated structural study is
kirikae1984 , whose framework Nakagawa (2024: 261–272) reworks in terms of the
valency of the constituent roots.

The base structural type is the concatenation of two nominal concept forms: a modifier root
followed by a head root, the head carrying the compound's semantic class. Because intransitive
verbs and nouns share the same form in Hokkaido Ainu — the same root functions as either class
without morphological change (see word-class-inventory-and-diagnostics ) — roots of
any valency can serve as compound constituents. Nakagawa identifies six surface input patterns;
all reduce to the same underlying N+N type once intransitive roots are treated as nominal
nakagawa2024 267 .

The resulting compound is right-headed: the head is the rightmost constituent, which determines
the grammatical class of the whole and hosts the
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed) suffix and personal
possessive affixes. The diminutive -po is explicitly excluded from
compound formation — it is a derivational suffix on a finished noun, not a compound constituent
(see nominal-derivation-diminutive-augmentative ) nakagawa2024 265 .

Diagnostics: compound vs syntactic noun phrase

A two-root sequence might be either a compound noun or a syntactic noun phrase (attributive
verb or modifier noun + head noun). Nakagawa (2024: 261–264) gives four diagnostics, all
exploiting positional differences between one-word and two-word structures
nakagawa2024 261–264 .

Person-affix position

A possessive personal affix attaches at the left edge of the word it marks. In a syntactic
phrase it appears on the head noun alone; in a compound it appears outside the entire
compound. The pair below has the same referent but different morphological structure.

poro a=yupi | big 4.A=elder.brother | his distinguished elder brother (modifier and noun are separate words) | Syntactic phrase: the possessive affix a= precedes yupi alone; poro is an independent attributive verb. | nakagawa2024:261 | HK

a=poro-yupi | 4.A=big-elder.brother | his/one's great elder brother (compound, lexicalized as a title) | Compound: the possessive affix a= precedes the entire compound poro-yupi, which behaves as a single morphological word. | nakagawa2024:261 | HK

In the compound both poro and yupi appear in
their concept (bare) forms; the affix is placed outside both.

Affiliative-suffix position

The concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed) suffix marks the
noun it heads. In a syntactic phrase it attaches to the head noun, leaving the modifier
unsuffixed; in a compound it attaches to the outermost right edge of the whole compound.

a=mac-i utar | 4.A=woman-POSS people | his wife's companions / his wives (two words: affiliative on the head mac) | Phrase: affiliative suffix -i attaches to mac (the concept-form stem of mat 'woman, wife'), and utar 'people/companions' is a separate syntactic head. | nakagawa2024:262 | HK

a=mat-utar-i | 4.A=woman-people-POSS | his wives (compound: affiliative on the outermost edge) | Compound: mat appears in its concept form inside the compound; the affiliative -i attaches to the right edge of the whole matutar unit. | nakagawa2024:262 | HK

Lexicalization and non-compositional meaning

When the meaning of the combination is not predictable from its parts, the sequence is
necessarily a compound. retar be white {' '} cikap bird is a
transparent syntactic phrase meaning 'a white bird'; retatcir swan (where
retat is a sandhi reduction of retar and
cir does not occur as a free form) is a lexicalized compound meaning
specifically 'swan' nakagawa2024 263 . The bound status of the second
element and the non-compositional meaning together confirm the compound analysis.

The concept-form rule

The most practical criterion is the observation that two nominal concept forms placed in
immediate sequence without intervening morphology always form a compound: the absence of
any overt linker or predicative marking removes any phrase-level analysis
nakagawa2024 263–264 . The series built on the head
kap skin, bark illustrates the pattern.

sik-kap | eye-skin | eyelid | sik 'eye' and kap 'skin, bark' are both in concept form; their immediate concatenation yields the compound 'eyelid'. | nakagawa2024:263 | HK

The same head kap combines with ni tree, wood to
give ni-kap 'bark', and with cep fish to give
cep-kap 'fish scales / fish skin' — a productive derivational
series nakagawa2024 263–264 . Because property verbs such as
retar be white occur in concept form without any morphological marking, the
only reliable way to distinguish a compound from an attributive phrase in ambiguous cases is
the positional diagnostics above.

Headedness, morphological linking, and accent

Hokkaido Ainu compound nouns are head-final (right-headed): the right constituent is both the
semantic and morphological head. This follows directly from the diagnostics: person affixes and
affiliative suffixes mark the word-level node — the compound as a whole — not the internal
head constituent alone nakagawa2024 261–264 .

At the constituent juncture the modifier typically appears in its concept (bare) form, with no
linking morpheme; this is the default for the N+N, 1-arg-V+N, and N+1-arg-V types. In type ⑤
below the modifier appears in the affiliative (possessed) form instead. The
morphophonological behavior of the affiliative suffix at such junctures is treated in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Prosodically the compound constitutes a single phonological word with a single accent peak.
Community observation (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-09) records that
cisekorkur 'head of household' and
kotankorkur 'village chief' each carry their accent on the second
syllable of the compound as a single word, with no secondary peak on either internal
constituent aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. The general account of compound
prosody is in accent-in-compounds-and-affixation .

Formation types

Nakagawa (2024: 267–272), building on kirikae1984 , distinguishes six input
patterns by the valency of the constituent roots. The classification is arity-based: because
intransitive verbs freely convert to nouns in Hokkaido Ainu, types ②–④ surface as the same
N+N morphological structure as type ① nakagawa2024 267 . Types ⑤ and ⑥
are morphologically distinct: ⑤ uses the affiliative form of the first element, and ⑥ places
a transitive verb in the head position.

Type
Input pattern
Example
Gloss
Meaning

①
N + N
sik-kap
eye–skin
eyelid

②
1-arg-V + N
rep-ni
be.offshore–tree
driftwood

③
N + 1-arg-V
menoko-monrayke
woman–work
women's work

④
1-arg-V + 1-arg-V
—
—
reduces to types ①–③

⑤
N. poss + 1-arg-V
ami-tanne
claw.POSS–be.long
crab

⑥
N + 2-arg-V
cikap-koyki
bird–catch
hawk

For types ②③ the intransitive root — including property verbs such as
poro be big and retar be white — appears in what is
synchronically a nominal concept form, since no verbal morphology is available inside the
compound nakagawa2024 267–268 .

Type ⑤: affiliative modifier

In type ⑤ the modifier appears in the affiliative (possessed) form, giving the compound
a relative-clause reading: 'the one whose N V-s'. The two examples below share the same
head verb tanne be long, be tall .

ami-tanne | claw.POSS-be.long | crab (lit. 'the one whose claw is long') | ami is the affiliative form of am 'claw'; the compound names the crab by a characteristic body feature. Type ⑤: N.POSS + 1-arg-V. | nakagawa2024:269 | HK

teke-tanne | hand.POSS-be.long | thief (lit. 'the one whose hand reaches far') | teke is the affiliative form of tek 'hand'. The compound lexicalizes the thief as someone whose reach is long. Type ⑤: N.POSS + 1-arg-V. | nakagawa2024:269 | HK

The affiliative morphology of the first constituent makes the compound inherently
relational: the head noun functions as an implicit argument of the modifier verb. This
pattern overlaps with the external-possession construction treated in
external-possession-possessor-raising .

Type ⑥: incorporated transitive object

In type ⑥ the first element is a concept-form noun serving as the implicit object of a
transitive verbal head. The compound names the entity characterized by performing that
action on that object type.

cikap-koyki | bird-catch | hawk (lit. 'one that catches birds') | cikap 'bird' in concept form is the implicit object of the transitive verb koyki 'catch, seize'; the compound names the hawk by its characteristic predatory action. Type ⑥: N + 2-arg-V. | nakagawa2024:271 | HK

Type ⑥ is structurally close to noun incorporation (see
noun-incorporation-object ), the distinction being that the result here is a
lexicalized nominal, not a predicate frame with an internal incorporated argument
nakagawa2024 271 .

Semantic patterns

The modifier-head relationship in Hokkaido Ainu compounds is consistently endocentric: the
head names the class of the referent and the modifier restricts or characterizes it. Three
sub-patterns recur.

Attributive-restrictive compounds

The largest group has a modifier specifying material, origin, location, or a characteristic
property of the head referent. Place names illustrate the pattern transparently:
Poropet Horobetsu (place name) derives from
poro be big + pet river ('large river'), and
Siretoko Shiretoko (place name) from
sir appearance, land + etoko tip, front end
('land's tip, cape') nakagawa2024 233 . The same structure governs
common-noun compounds: sisam-itak 'the Japanese language (as a
named category)' is restrictive with sisam Japanese person, non-Ainu as
modifier ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Qualitative-possessive compounds: the N-kor-kur type

A productive sub-pattern combines a noun, the possession verb
kor have, own, hold , and the formal-class noun
kur person to name a person who possesses or is in charge of the first
noun. This N-kor-kur template is one of the few fully productive three-constituent patterns
in the language.

cise-kor-kur | house-have-person | head of household, houseowner | N-kor-kur compound type; confirmed as a single phonological word by accent data (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-09): accent falls on the second syllable of the whole compound.

The compound accent data (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024,
aynucorporadiscord ) confirms that cise-kor-kur and
kotan-kor-kur are single words with second-syllable accent ‹contested›.
The same template extends to mosir-kor-kur 'ruler of the land' and
similar formations. This construction must be distinguished from the syntactic alienable
possession phrase a=kor cise 'my house', where
kor is a fully inflected transitive verb taking its own person
prefix, not a bound medial element nakagawa2024 217–218 . The possession
constructions are treated in adnominal-possession-double-marking .

Attributive NP vs compound: the scope distinction

Two surface-similar structures differ in their grammatical and semantic status. The
syntactic NP kane cise 'a metal house' uses
kane metal, iron as a free-standing attributive noun; the first element
cannot receive a personal affix for this relationship ( *ku=kane cise
as 'my metal's house' is not a grammatical possessive frame here), and the combination is
compositional and productive with any house material nakagawa2024 218 . A
genuine compound, in contrast, passes the person-affix and affiliative-suffix tests and
typically names a culturally fixed category. The same applies to sequences with
kamuy spirit, deity; bear : a contextual phrase
kamuy cise 'a spirit's house (in this context)' is attributive; a
lexicalized compound naming a specific object type would be non-compositional and
semantically restricted.

Coordinate and exocentric compounds

Pure coordinate compounds (copulative; both elements of equal rank) and exocentric
compounds (whose semantic head is not expressed) are marginal in Hokkaido Ainu. Community
discussion (antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-06-02) proposes that this sparsity
reflects the verb-centred character of Ainu nominal derivation: because most roots can
serve as intransitive verbs, noun formation proceeds through verb+noun or noun+verb frames
rather than noun+noun coordination aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. The
attested examples cited in the same discussion —
haca-hapo parents (lit. father and mother) as coordinate and
po-at womb (lit. child-place) as exocentric — function as lexical items
rather than templates for new formation aynucorporadiscord .

The compound–phrase boundary

Deciding whether a given modifier+noun sequence is a compound or a syntactic phrase has
direct orthographic and grammatical consequences: compounds are typically written solid (no
space), phrases with a space between elements. The diagnostics in the preceding section
provide the formal criteria; their application requires attention to both morphological form
and meaning.

A minimal orthographic contrast illustrates the semantic stake:
sisam itak (two words) means 'speech/language of a Japanese person
(in context)', while sisamitak (one compound word) means 'the Japanese
language (as a named category)' (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-05)
aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-suggested›. The compound reading adds a categorical,
language-name interpretation absent from the phrase. Accent reinforces the distinction: the
compound bears a single peak, the phrase retains peaks on both members (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-12-20) aynucorporadiscord . Full treatment of the prosodic test
is in accent-in-compounds-and-affixation .

ijas2023 presents the compound–phrase distinction as a key point for learners of
Ainu, treating the presence or absence of internal syntactic modification (TAM morphology,
person marking) on the first element as the diagnostic: a root bearing its own grammatical
morphology is in a phrase; a root appearing directly before the head with no such morphology
is a compound constituent. This matches the morphological diagnostics above and makes the
distinction accessible without reference to accent.

The boundary also marks the limit of noun incorporation. The
noun-incorporation-object object incorporation construction shares the surface
appearance of an N+V compound, but the incorporated noun remains an internal verbal argument
rather than a constituent of a new nominal word. Community discussion (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2023-12-25) notes that verbs taking an intransitive-type modifier present an
ambiguity between incorporation and lexicalized compounding that cannot always be resolved by
surface form alone aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. Deverbal and denominal noun
derivation via -p/-pe and related suffixes is treated in
deverbal-denominal-noun-derivation .

title: The Structure of the Noun Phrase
summary: NP-internal constituent order and modification: the order of demonstratives, numerals/quantifiers, relative clauses, and adnominal modifiers relative to the head noun, apposition, determination without articles, NP-internal coordination, and recursion.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Structure of the Noun Phrase
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: NP-internal constituent order and modification: the order of demonstratives, numerals/quantifiers, relative clauses, and adnominal modifiers relative to the head noun, apposition, determination without articles, NP-internal coordination, and recursion.

The modifier-position template

All prenominal modifiers in Hokkaido Ainu precede the head noun, and postpositional material
follows it. No agreement morphology links a modifier to its head; modification is
juxtapositional throughout nakagawa2024 261, 283 ; shibatani1990 17–18 ; ijas2023 . The position hierarchy for attested modifier types is invariant:

Position
Category
Representative forms
Key restrictions

1
Demonstrative prenominal
tan , toan , ne ,
nea
no person affix; does not change form with the head

2
Numeral / quantifier prenominal
sine , tu , re , ine
no classifiers; nominalized numeral forms cannot modify a noun directly

3
Adnoun / property verb
pirka 'be good', pon 'be small', oar , kes
property verbs appear in their zero-marked (3rd-person) form; adnouns take no person
affix

4
Relative-clause VP
apkas 'walks' [in apkas utar ]
only common nouns and formal nouns serve as relativized heads; locative nouns and
pronouns cannot

5
HEAD NOUN
menoko , cise , kamuy , utar
common, formal, locative, or pronominal noun; affiliative form when possessed

6
Postposition (locative noun + case particle)
or ta , kasi ta , sam un
locative case particles require a ⟨place⟩ antecedent; the locative noun supplies it

Positions need not all be filled; most NPs contain one or two pre-head elements. When multiple
positions are present, their order is fixed — a demonstrative cannot follow a numeral, nor a
numeral follow a property verb. The following constructed example fills positions 1, 3, 5,
and 6:

toan pirka cise or ta | that be.good house place LOC | inside that good house | Constructed to show the full template: demonstrative (position 1), property verb (position 3), head noun (position 5), locative noun or 'place/inside' + case particle ta (position 6). Positions 2 and 4 are empty.

The line between an NP-internal modifier and a compound noun is drawn by four diagnostics
(person-affix position, affiliative-suffix position, lexicalization, and the concept+concept
rule) treated in noun-noun-compounding after kirikae1984 (via
nakagawa2024 261–264 ). The subclass properties of the head noun — which nouns
can serve as argument heads, which can be relativized, and which require a modifier — are in
nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns .

Demonstrative prenominals

The demonstrative system distinguishes two axes that Japanese conflates in a single この／その／あの series: on-site deixis (locating the referent in the shared perceptual field) and
discourse-anaphoric deixis (tracking a previously mentioned referent) nakagawa2024 281–283 ; nakagawa2022pos 480 .

Form (Saru)
Extended variant
Deixis type
Distance / discourse value

tan
—
on-site
proximal (at or near speaker)

taan
taoka(y)
on-site
mid-proximal (near speaker, further than tan )

toan
tooka(y)
on-site

toon
—
on-site

ne
nea / nerok

newaan
