tan cise e=uni ne ru-we | this house 2SG.POSS=home COP track-POSS | Is this house yours? | The proximal demonstrative tan precedes the head noun cise 'house'. Used here to illustrate the NP tan cise; the evidential frame ruwe and the polar-question reduction are treated in the ruwe-ne-inferential chapter. | sato2008:131 | CHI

The vowel quality of the initial syllable of the on-site series correlates with distance: the
narrow front vowel of tan encodes proximity and the wider back vowel of
toan encodes distance. This has been proposed as sound symbolism in the
deictic system, with longer forms ( taan ) encoding more information
content than shorter ones ( tan )
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2025-12-27) ‹contested›. The proposal has not been
evaluated against the full corpus.

Non-Saru/Chitose dialects use nean where Saru has
ne ; eastern Shizunai has ikia / ikirok
nakagawa2024 282 . A demonstrative prenominal combined with the formal noun
pe thing or hi thing, fact, time yields a demonstrative pronoun:
tanpe this thing , toanpe that thing , neap that aforementioned thing
nakagawa2024 287 . A demonstrative may be followed directly by a locative case
particle — taan ta 'over there' — because the formal noun
hi is historically elided in such constructions nakagawa2024 288 . The full paradigm — including the interrogative ney and anaphora
functions — is in demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness .

Numeral and quantifier prenominals

Numerals occupy position 2, between a demonstrative and any adnoun or property verb. A numeral
precedes its head noun directly with no intervening classifier — a typological contrast with
Japanese, which requires a numeral counter between numeral and noun nakagawa2024 461 ‹consensus›.

tu cip | two boat | two boats | nakagawa2024:461 | HK

asikne okkayo | five man | five men | nakagawa2024:461 | HK

Nominalized numeral forms — rep three things (re + pe) ,
sinep one thing , tup two things — are pronoun-like count
expressions, not prenominal quantifiers. They cannot modify a noun directly:
re mosir 'three worlds' is well-formed, but
*rep mosir is not, because rep is already the
nominalized form re + nominalizing enclitic -pe
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The vigesimal
numeral system, ordinals, and quantifiers are in vigesimal-numeral-system and
numeral-classifiers-ordinals-and-quantifier-syntax .

Property verbs and adnouns as modifiers

Property-meaning words —
pirka be good , pon be small , wen be bad ,
tanne be long , poro be big — are intransitive verbs in Ainu. In
attributive position they immediately precede the head noun with no morphological change
relative to their predicative form: the same pirka that takes a subject
person index as a predicate appears zero-marked (third person) before the noun as its modifier
nakagawa2022pos 474–476 ; shibatani1990 19 . The word-class
analysis and the adjective controversy are in word-class-inventory-and-diagnostics .

pon hekaci | be.small child | a small child | The property verb pon 'be small' modifies hekaci 'child' with no morphological change. In predicative function the same pon takes a subject person index. | nakagawa2022pos:475 | HK

Beyond property verbs, a closed set of adnouns (連体詞, prenominals) occupies the
same position. Adnouns differ from property verbs in having no predicative use without the
copula and in taking no person affix at all. The Saru inventory includes gender specifiers
( pinne 'male', matne 'female'), the
pair-selector oar 'one of a pair', the universal quantifier
kes 'every', and epistemic adnouns ( sonno / sino 'true/genuine', yayan 'ordinary', oya 'other')
nakagawa2024 283 .

A property verb or adnoun at position 3 scopes over the entire nominal complex to its right,
including a following formal noun such as utar :

wen cikap-po utar | be.bad bird-DIM people | bad little birds | The property verb wen modifies a head that is itself a diminutive compound (cikap 'bird' + -po DIM) followed by the plural formal noun utar. The full sentence hinak wa arki wen cikappo utar, 'the bad little birds that came from somewhere', also shows a relative-clause modifier (hinak wa arki) at position 4 preceding wen. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matuko, uwepeker minwa 15, [aa-irc/022#23]

Relative-clause modifiers

A full VP, including its person marking and aspectual material, can immediately precede a head
noun and function as its modifier. No relative pronoun or dedicated relative-clause morphology
marks the construction; the head noun simply appears to the right of the modifying VP
nakagawa2022pos 475 ; shibatani1990 17 :

apkas utar arki ru-we ne | walk people come.PL track-POSS COP | The people who were walking came. | The VP apkas 'walk' (zero-marked third person) modifies the head utar 'people'. The main-clause verb arki is the suppletive plural of ek 'come'. No relative marker appears between the modifying VP and its head. | nakagawa2022pos:475 | HK

Relativizability is restricted by noun subclass (表47,
nakagawa2024 215 ): common nouns can be relativized; formal nouns head
argument-saturated nominalizations; locative nouns cannot be relativized; pronouns cannot be
relativized
nakagawa2022pos 482 ‹consensus›.

The arity account in nakagawa2024 503–504 distinguishes the relative-clause
construction — which leaves an argument gap in the modifying VP that the head noun fills —
from nominalization with -p~-pe , which nominalizes a VP without
reducing its argument count. A community analysis converges on the same point: relativization
reduces the modifying clause's arity by one, whereas -p(e) nominalizes
the entire clause without creating a gap
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09) ‹contested›. The construction and its
gap types are in prenominal-gap-relative-clauses ; headless and
internally-headed varieties are in headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses ; argument-saturated nominalization is in nominalization-p-pe .

The postpositional phrase

Postpositional material follows the head noun and forms the outer boundary of the NP. The
postpositional layer consists of a locative noun in its basic or long form, optionally
followed by a locative case particle ( ta locative,
wa ablative, un allative,
peka perlative) nakagawa2024 294–296 . The four locative
case particles require a ⟨place⟩ antecedent; for a common noun, a locative noun must mediate:

sike-he kasi ta | luggage-POSS top LOC | on top of the luggage | The head noun sike 'luggage' is in affiliative form sikehe. The locative noun kasi (long form of ka 'on top of') follows it, and the case particle ta closes the postpositional phrase. The common noun sike does not satisfy ⟨place⟩ by itself; kasi provides the locative head. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matuko, uwepeker minwa 8 (sono 1), [aa-irc/006#143]

Type-2 locative nouns ( kim 'mountain-ward place',
soy 'outside', ra 'low place') are inherently
⟨place⟩ and take a case particle directly without a preceding common-noun antecedent:
kim ta arpa=an 'I went to the mountains' nakagawa2024 245–246 . The full inventory of locative and relational nouns, their basic and long forms, and the
case-particle restrictions are in relational-and-spatial-nouns .

Apposition and attributive juxtaposition

Two nouns in their concept forms placed in sequence yield either apposition or an
attributive-scope construction — both realizations of the same N(concept)+N(concept) surface
pattern nakagawa2022pos 486–487 ; nakagawa2024 218 . In the
attributive use, the first noun specifies the type, quality, or scope of the second:
kamuy rus means 'a pelt of the bear type' —
kamuy functions as an attribute, not a possessor. The diagnostic is
substitution: an N₁ that is a true possessor can be replaced by a transitive-subject person
prefix ( a=kor cip 'my boat'), but an N₁ that is a mere attribute
cannot — kamuy rus admits no person-prefix substitute for the bear
nakagawa2022pos 486–487 . When a specific animal's pelt is meant, the
affiliative form marks the inalienable relation:
kamuy rusihi (where rus-i-hi is the affiliative
form of rus 'pelt').

Apposition in the narrower sense — two NPs coreferential with the same individual — uses the
same juxtaposed structure without a copular linker. The boundary with compound nouns runs
through the four formal diagnostics in noun-noun-compounding : a phrase allows
person-affix placement and affiliative-suffix placement internal to the second noun, whereas a
compound moves both to the outermost boundary. The contrast between attributive juxtaposition,
the inalienable affiliative construction, and the kor -construction is
central to concept-form-affiliative-form .

The possessor slot

Three constructions express possession within the NP, selected according to the alienability of
the relationship nakagawa2024 217–219 ; nakagawa2022pos 484–485 :

Construction
Surface form
Semantic relation

Alienable
N₁ kor N₂(concept)
owner controls or owns N₂; N₂ in concept form

Inalienable
N₁ N₂(affiliative)
inherent relation: body parts, kinship, part-whole, material→product; N₂ in affiliative form

Attributive juxtaposition
N₁(concept) N₂(concept)
attribute or scope, not possession; N₁ cannot be replaced by a person prefix

a=kor cip | 4.A=have boat | my/one's boat | Alienable construction: the transitive-subject person prefix (here fourth-person a=, which in narrative contexts typically encodes the speaker) attaches to kor 'have'; the possessed noun cip is in concept form. Any transitive-subject prefix may substitute for a= to encode a different possessor. | nakagawa2024:218 | HK

ku=teke-he | 1SG.A=hand-POSS | my hand | Inalienable construction: the 1SG transitive-subject prefix ku= and the affiliative suffix -he combine on the possessed noun teke 'hand'. Body parts require the inalienable construction. | nakagawa2024:219 | HK

Pronominal possessors are expressed through the transitive-subject (A) person prefix — on
kor in the alienable construction, or on the head noun in its
affiliative form in the inalienable construction. The possessive morphology — the
affiliative-form paradigm, semantic classes of inalienable nouns, kinship-term dialect
variation, and the double-marking construction — belongs to Part VII: adnominal-possession-double-marking , kinship-honorific-possession , and concept-form-affiliative-form .

NP-internal agreement

Hokkaido Ainu has no agreement morphology within the NP. Demonstratives, property verbs,
adnouns, and numerals do not change form as a function of any grammatical property of the head
noun: there is no gender, case, or number agreement nakagawa2024 259, 283 ;
corbett2000 ‹consensus›. A modifier at positions 1–4 appears in its
uninflected citation form regardless of whether the head denotes one or many entities, whether
it is animate or inanimate, or whether the NP occupies subject, object, or oblique position.

The plural marker utar people; plural associates is a postposed formal noun,
not an agreement enclitic on the head or its modifiers. It follows the entire nominal complex
and is itself uninflected: wen cikappo utar and
wen cikappo differ only in whether utar is
present; the property verb wen is unchanged either way. Community
analysis confirms that Ainu plural is semantic addition rather than obligatory morphological
agreement, and that verse can add or omit utar freely for syllable
count aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-23) ‹corpus-confirmed›. Number, where
grammatically encoded, surfaces on the finite verb through suppletive stem alternation
( ek / arki 'come.SG/come.PL',
arpa / paye 'go.SG/go.PL') — see
suppletive-verbal-number . The semantics and animacy conditions of
utar , and the transnumeral character of Hokkaido nouns, are in
nominal-number-utar-transnumerality . Pronominal NPs are in
independent-personal-pronouns , and derivation within the NP is in
deverbal-denominal-noun-derivation and denominal-deverbal-verb-derivation .

title: Numeral Classifiers, Ordinals, and Quantifier Syntax
summary: Numeral classifiers, derived numeral categories, and the syntax of quantification and quantifier float.
part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Numeral Classifiers, Ordinals, and Quantifier Syntax
Part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
Summary: Numeral classifiers, derived numeral categories, and the syntax of quantification and quantifier float.

The three numeral word-classes and the classifier alternation

Hokkaido Ainu numerals appear in three formally distinct word-classes. The
numeral prenominal (数連体詞) is the bare cardinal stem that directly
precedes a head noun: tu cip 'two boats',
iwan kosonte 'six robes'. The numeral noun (数名詞)
is the cardinal stem carrying one of two suffixes — -p/-pe for
non-human referents or -n/-iw for human referents — and it
occupies a post-nominal appositional position: tuki sinep
'one cup (post-nominal)', tun katkemat 'two women'. A third
class, the counting-off form (数え上げ), is used in enumeration sequences
as an interjection-like particle and is not treated here
nakagawa2024 461, 465–466 .

The present chapter covers the numeral noun suffixes and their syntax, the periphrastic
ordinal strategies, quantifier float, and the universal and approximative quantifiers.
The cardinal system, the vigesimal arithmetic, and the 1–20 paradigm are treated in
vigesimal-numeral-system ; nominal -utar plurality
and transnumerality in nominal-number-utar-transnumerality .

value
prenominal (数連体詞)
thing-counting ( -p/-pe )
person-counting ( -n/-iw )
counting-off (Saru)

sine

sinen
sinep

tup

tup

ine

asik

iwaniw

arwanpe
arwaniw

tupesaniw

—

Sources: nakagawa2024 461, 464 ; sato2008 256–257 ;
refsing1986 110–111 .

The thing-counting suffix -p/-pe

The thing-counting suffix derives from the formal noun
p thing (formal noun) (alternant pe ) that also
functions as the general thing-nominalizer treated in
nominalization-p-pe . On the numeral stem it creates a counting noun whose
referential domain is any non-human entity, though it can extend to humans in brusque
register (see below). The phonological conditioning is regular: the allomorph
-p follows vowel-final stems (values 1–5: sine,
tu, re, ine, asikne ), and -pe follows consonant-final stems
(values 6–10: iwan, arwan, tupesan, sinepesan, wan )
nakagawa2024 464 ; refsing1986 110 .

The numeral noun carrying -p/-pe occupies the post-nominal
appositional position — it follows the noun it quantifies. This is in complementary
distribution with the prenominal position of the bare stem. The prenominal precedes the
head directly:

sine okkaypo ek a wa | one youth come PFV CONJ | A youth came, … | Prenominal sine immediately before the head noun okkaypo. Satō 2008: 257, Chitose; the sentence continues. | sato2008:257 | CHI

The numeral noun follows:

tuki sine-p | cup one-CL | one cup | Nakagawa 2024: 464. The numeral noun sinep is post-nominal. The inverted order *sinep tuki is ungrammatical: the numeral noun cannot precede its head (Nakagawa 2024: 465). | nakagawa2024:464 | SAR

toonta pon kamuy re-p an ma | there small bear three-CL exist FIN | There are three bear cubs over there. | Satō 2008: 256, Chitose. The numeral noun rep follows the modified NP pon kamuy 'small/young bear'. | sato2008:256 | CHI

When the referent is contextually recoverable, the numeral noun can stand alone as the
head of an NP without an overt co-argument:

aoka sine-p ka a=tuye somo ki | we one-CL even 4.A=cut NEG do | We did not cut even one (of them). | sinep heads the NP with the focus particle ka 'even'; no overt noun antecedent. | nakagawa2024:464 | SAR

Nakagawa characterises the -p/-n split as the native thing/person
dichotomy — comparable to Japanese ひとつ vs ひとり and
contrasting with Sinitic classifier systems that require an intervening counter word
between any numeral and any noun. The parallel with Nivkh, another non-Sinitic language
of the region, is also noted nakagawa2024 464 .

The person-counting suffix -n/-iw

The person-counting suffix parallels the thing-counting suffix in its allomorphy:
-n after vowel-final stems (values 1–5) and
-iw after consonant-final stems (values 6–10)
nakagawa2024 464 . The suffix -n/-iw is
otherwise rare in the Hokkaido Ainu morphological inventory; its other occurrence is
in the interrogative hempakiw 'how many people' (from
hempak-iw ) — see interrogative-pro-forms .

The primary domain of -n/-iw is human beings:
tun katkemat 'two women',
asiknen okkayo 'five men'. Nakagawa notes that
-p/-pe can also count human referents, but carries a brusquer
register; in practice the distinction is weak and speakers vary
nakagawa2024 464, exx. 1070–1071 . The person-counting suffix extends to
composite vigesimal numerals. For 100 (= 5×20), the suffix attaches to the whole
compound asiknehotne :

asiknehotne-n asir cise or ta uekarpa | five.twenty-HUM new house inside LOC gather | A hundred people (5×20) gathered in the new house. | Kayano Shirō (萱野志朗), Ainu Times (アイヌタイムズ) vol. 3 no. 2; Saru/Chitose dialect. asiknehotne = 5×20 = 100 (see vigesimal chapter); the person-counting suffix -n attaches to the compound. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR

The numeral noun with -n/-iw also combines with the translative
copula ne to form a predicative or adverbial phrase expressing
numerical composition. In this use the numeral noun functions without any overt head noun:

re-n a=ne wa oka=an | three-HUM 4.A=COP CONJ exist.PL=4.S | We were three and lived (together). | Nakagawa 2024: 300–301, exx. 431–433. ren ne is a predicative phrase 'being three (persons)'; wa links it to the main predicate oka=an 'we existed'. The 4th-person forms index the narrator + others. | nakagawa2024:300 | SAR

See also the parallel sinenne 'alone' derived by the same
copular contraction, discussed in the ordinals section below.

Word-classifiers: to 'day' and suy 'time/turn'

Outside the -p/-pe and -n/-iw paradigm,
two free nouns function as mensural elements when preceded by a numeral:
to day and suy time, turn .

to 'day' follows a numeral prenominal to form a temporal span
noun: sine to 'one day'. For values 2 and 3, the idiomatic forms
tutko '2 days' and rerko '3 days' are
common; from value 4 onwards the additive pattern ine rerko
'4 days', asikne rerko '5 days' shows that
rerko has bleached from '3 days' to the general day-span slot
nakagawa2024 465 .

suy 'time, turn' forms the iterative series. The element for 'once'
draws on the archaic root ar 'one' (also seen in
ar-itak 'one word' and the counting-off base) rather than the
regular prenominal sine :
arsuy 'once', tusuy 'twice',
resuy 'three times'. Intensive reduplication with
o- yields otusuy and
oresuy 'over and over'
refsing1986 108, 111 ; nakagawa2024 461 .
The numeral 6 ( iwan ) has a secondary 'many' meaning in oral
literary contexts; iwan suy therefore typically means 'many times'
rather than a precise six:

iwan suy a=i=hotuyekar hine | many time 4.A=4.O=call.out CONJ | (They) called out many times, … | Nakagawa 2024: 469, ex. 1080. iwan functions as 'many' in fixed expressions; suy follows as the mensural element. See also the quantifier section below. | nakagawa2024:469 | SAR

Classifier or counter? The terminological debate

Whether -p/-pe , -n/-iw ,
to , and suy constitute "numeral
classifiers" is unresolved in the literature, partly because the disagreement is
terminological ‹contested›.

Nakagawa argues that Hokkaido Ainu has no numeral classifiers (助数詞) in any
meaningful sense. Prenominal numerals attach directly to the noun without an intervening
counting word — tu cip 'two boats',
re nupuri 'three mountains' — just as English
two ships does. The -p vs -n
contrast is the native thing/person dichotomy, found in many non-Sinitic numeral systems
and present cross-linguistically without Sinitic influence
nakagawa2024 461–462, 464 .

The typology chapter of bugaeva2022 frames the picture differently: it
refers to -p/-pe and -n/-iw as
"counters" and to to and suy as
"word-classifiers", speculating that these may be remnants of a formerly more elaborate
noun-classifier system (Handbook 2022, epub — chapter and page are uncalibrated in the
current reading). Refsing takes a middle position, calling the suffixes "a rudimentary
system of 'classifiers' or 'counters'" while reserving "numeral" for the bare prenominal
stem and "number" for the stem plus counter refsing1986 110–111 .

The pivot of the disagreement is the definition of "classifier". In the Sinitic sense
a classifier is an obligatory mediating element: Mandarin requires 本
in 三本書 'three-CL-book'; no numeral can attach directly to the noun.
Hokkaido Ainu has no such requirement: the prenominal numeral combines with any noun
without an intervening element. On this definition — Nakagawa's — the suffixes are not
classifiers because they are post-nominal, suffixal, and optional relative to the
prenominal. On Refsing's and the Handbook's definition they are, because they encode a
semantic categorization of the counted entity. Authors citing either view must specify
which sense of "classifier" they intend.

Ordinal expressions

None of the primary Hokkaido Ainu grammars — Nakagawa 2024 §17, Satō 2008 §33,
Refsing 1986 §12.4, or Kindaichi and Chiri 1936 — describes a dedicated ordinal
word-class or ordinal paradigm
nakagawa2024 461–469 ; sato2008 256–258 ;
refsing1986 110–117 .
Ordinal meaning is expressed through several periphrastic strategies, two of which are
independently attested in the grammars, while others are documented in community
discussion ‹corpus-suggested›.

Two attested expressions involve a numeral noun combined with the translative copula
ne . The contraction of sinen ne 'being
one person' to sinenne yields the lexicalized meaning 'alone,
by oneself':

sine-n ne | one-HUM COP | sinenne | alone, by oneself | Refsing 1986: 117, ex. 105 (Shizunai). sinen ne contracts to sinenne; the translative copula ne gives the meaning 'being one person'. | refsing1986:117 | SHI

The same copular pattern with the thing-counting noun forms a partitive or distributive
expression:

tu-p ne ukosaraye | two-CL COP split | Divide it in two. | Refsing 1986: 117, ex. 104. tup ne 'being two things' functions as an adverbial predicate specifying how the action is carried out. | refsing1986:117 | SHI

For 'first', the adverb hoskino first, at the beginning fills the
ordinal-adverbial slot. The superlative phrase iyotta hoski
(where iyotta most, superlative intensifies hoski beginning )
expresses 'the very first, foremost'. A community discussion notes that ordinals are
generally avoided in Hokkaido Ainu — the expression sine to
serves both 'one day' and 'the first day' — and that hoskino
and iyotta hoski are the standard strategies for 'first';
for higher ordinals, one attested usage is the sequence
sine kamuy, tu kamuy, re kamuy 'first, second, third (one)'
with kamuy as a generic count noun, and the form
iyeere-p has been reported for 'the third (thing)'
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09, asserted) ‹speculative›.
These community-attested strategies await systematic description in the primary grammars.

The iterative series arsuy 'once', tusuy
'twice', resuy 'three times' (§ word-classifiers
above) overlaps functionally with the ordinal domain when it tracks the sequence of
occurrences.

Quantifier syntax: three positions

The three numeral categories — prenominal, numeral noun, and pure noun predicate — occupy
distinct syntactic positions with distinct meaning contributions
nakagawa2024 461, 464–465 ; refsing1986 115–117 .

Prenominal position

The bare numeral stem precedes the head noun. Other modifiers can intervene between
the numeral and the noun without ungrammaticality: re ku=mataki
'my three younger sisters' (possessive prefix between numeral and noun),
tu pon cep 'two small fish' (adjective between numeral and
noun) refsing1986 115 . The prenominal is the position available to
all cardinal values, including composite vigesimal numerals.

Post-nominal (appositional) position

The numeral noun ( -p/-pe or -n/-iw )
follows the noun it quantifies as an appositive count specification. The position
constraint is categorical: the numeral noun cannot precede the noun
( *sinep tuki for 'one cup' is ungrammatical)
nakagawa2024 465 . The NP-internal appositive pattern is discussed
alongside the possessor-appositive construction in noun-phrase-structure .

a=acautari re-n | 4.POSS=uncle-ASS three-HUM | my three uncles | Nakagawa 2024: 464. The 4th-person possessive prefix a= marks the narrator's kin; ren 'three (persons)' follows in the appositional slot. | nakagawa2024:464 | SAR

Whether a modifier may intervene between the head noun and an appositional numeral noun —
e.g. noun + adjective + numeral.noun — is documented as an
open research question: Satō reports no such example in his Chitose data and flags it
explicitly sato2008 256 ‹contested›.

Predicative (pure noun) position

Both numeral noun series can serve as predicates or standalone NP heads without a
co-occurring noun. The simplest predicative use places the numeral noun as the sole
argument of an intransitive verb:

sinen askay | one-HUM be.good.at | One person is good at it. | Refsing 1986: 116. sinen is the sole argument of the intransitive predicate askay; no head noun is present. | refsing1986:116 | SHI

The translative copula ne turns the numeral noun into an
adverbial predicate of composition or manner. The person-counting series:
ren a=ne wa oka=an 'we were three and lived' (§ §person-counting
above). The thing-counting series: tup ne ukosaraye 'divide
it in two' (§ §ordinals above). Both patterns use
ne as a translative that equates the group or object with a
numerical value — the same construction treated in the postpositions chapter
nakagawa2024 300–301 .

Quantifier float and the noun–numeral–verb pattern

The appositional numeral noun does not need to remain clause-adjacent to its head noun.
It can appear after the verb or in a position outside the canonical NP boundary — a
distribution consistent with the broader pronominal-argument and non-configurational
character of Hokkaido Ainu discussed in
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument . The pattern illustrated below
has the object noun followed by the numeral noun and then the verb:

pasuy sine-p uk | chopstick one-CL take | Take one chopstick. | Community-discussed form (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-12, proposed): the noun–numeral.noun–verb sequence is described as at least as common in Hokkaido Ainu as in Japanese. Consistent with the appositional numeral syntax documented in Refsing (1986: 116) and the appositional-possession pattern in Bugaeva (2021).

In this construction sinep is the appositional numeral noun
counting the object pasuy , but the two are separated by the
verb position. Refsing's Shizunai data include the parallel
a=kor mici akihi tun, ren an 'my father's brothers were two or
three', where the numeral noun follows a relational noun phrase rather than a simple
head refsing1986 116 . The noun–numeral.noun–verb
pattern is documented as community-observed and structurally consistent with the primary
sources aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-suggested›.

Numeral–nominal number interactions

A numeral specifying a small count (2 or 3) typically co-occurs with the singular form
of a noun that otherwise has a human-plural counterpart, while a large numeral (from 6
upwards), an indefinite quantity, or the quantifier inne 'many'
co-occurs with the plural form. Satō states the threshold explicitly (via community
discussion attributed to him); the pattern also coheres with Refsing's data on the
morphological forms involved:

context
example
noun form
verb form

small specified (2–3)
tu hekaci ek 'two children came'
singular hekaci
singular ek 'come'

large or indefinite
inne hekattar arki 'many children came'
plural hekattar
plural arki 'come (pl)'

aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-17, asserted, attributed to Satō);
corroborated by refsing1986 115–117 ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Mass-like or uncountable referents stay in the singular regardless of quantity. Under a
specified numeral greater than 1, a human-denoting noun may take the associative-plural
suffix -utar with phonologically conditioned allomorphy:
sine hekaci 'one child' but tu hekattar
'two children' (< hekaci-utar , with assimilation)
refsing1986 117 . The full -utar paradigm and the
transnumerality of bare nouns are treated in
nominal-number-utar-transnumerality ; the verbal number interaction belongs
to person-marking-architecture .

Universal and approximative quantifiers

Several items outside the numeral paradigm proper function as universal or approximative
quantifiers.

opitta all (of), every (variant obitta ) is the
universal quantifier 'all of, every'. Its formal status is that of a postpositional
adverb — it belongs to the class of originally adverbial postpositions
nakagawa2024 280 — and it typically follows the noun phrase or clause
it scopes over, distinguishing it positionally from the prenominal numeral. The closely
related epitta 'throughout, across the whole' has partly
overlapping distribution.

inne many (also inne ne 'being many') is the
standard approximative 'many'. It licenses the plural verb form and the plural
-utar form of the noun in the same way as a large cardinal
numeral (see § numeral–nominal number interactions above).

The numeral 6, iwan , has developed a secondary universal-like
meaning in oral-literary register. Fixed expressions including
iwan suy 'many times', iwan kosonte
'many/six robes' (a gift-register formula), and the intensive
noiwan (< no-iwan 'very many') all draw
on this conventionalized 'many' reading
nakagawa2024 469, exx. 1080–1083 . These expressions are genre-marked
and do not represent a productive grammaticalization of '6' as a plural marker in
everyday speech ‹corpus-confirmed›.

usa various, several is an approximative quantifier without a precise
numerical value. Its syntactic distribution parallels the prenominal numeral in modifier
position, but it is not a numeral in the classifier-paradigm sense treated above.

Distributive plurality and the full three-way interaction among nominal
-utar , verbal number, and quantifiers is systematically treated
in nominal-number-utar-transnumerality .

title: Object Indexing: Monotransitive and Ditransitive Clauses
summary: How transitive and three-place verbs index their object(s) via the objective affix set, the subject/object indexing asymmetries, and the secundative-vs-indirective alignment of ditransitive indexing.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Object Indexing: Monotransitive and Ditransitive Clauses
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: How transitive and three-place verbs index their object(s) via the objective affix set, the subject/object indexing asymmetries, and the secundative-vs-indirective alignment of ditransitive indexing.

The O set in the person paradigm

A monotransitive verb indexes its object with the O column of the personal-index paradigm. In
the standard Saru–Chitose baseline, that column contains en= for 1SG.O,
un= for 1PL.EXCL.O, e= for 2SG.O,
eci= for 2PL.O, a zero exponent for third person, and
i= for the fourth/indefinite person nakagawa2024 169 ;
bugaeva2012 471 ; sato2008 31–32 . The three-set S/A/O paradigm
and its cell-by-cell alignment are set out in person-marking-architecture ; this
chapter examines each O form in its monotransitive function, its morphological and positional
properties, and the allomorphic contrasts that arise in specific combinations.

formal person
free pronoun (Saru)
O: object index
A and S forms (contrast)
role pattern

1SG
kani
en=
A = S = ku=
nominative–accusative (S=A≠O)

1PL.EXCL
coka
un=
A = ci= ; S = =as
tripartite (S≠A≠O)

2SG
eani
e=
A = S = e=
neutral (S=A=O)

2PL
ecioka
eci=
A = S = eci=
neutral (S=A=O); also 1A→2O portmanteau

3
sinuma / oka
Ø
A = S = Ø
zero; analysis contested

4 / INDEF
asinuma / aoka
i=
A = a= (dialectally an= ); S =
=an
tripartite (S≠A≠O)

A finite verb carries at most one subject index and one object index; a third argument — such
as the theme in a ditransitive clause — is never additionally person-indexed and surfaces as a
bare noun phrase sato2023b 39, 47 ; sato2025a 369–371 . The
object index occupies the prefixal argument-marking layer immediately before the lexical stem,
alongside the transitive subject index; derivational material — applicatives, the causative, the
antipassive, the reflexive, and the root-attaching plural -pa — sits
closer to the stem. The full template is treated in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Ijäs's Lesson 5 introduces the O set through an "object markers" frame: the English object
pronoun corresponds to a verbal prefix on the Ainu finite verb, so that "me" and "you" map onto
en= and e= respectively ijas2023 . Her
pedagogical notation uses hyphens throughout; the convention in this grammar is that
= marks the personal-index boundary and - is
reserved for derivational and stem-number morphology.

First-person singular object: en=

The prefix en= indexes a first-person singular object. Its S/A
counterpart ku= covers both intransitive subject and transitive subject
functions; the opposition ku= (S=A) versus en=
(O) is the one nominative–accusative cell in the Hokkaido Ainu paradigm bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 ; nakagawa2024 166–169 ; sato2008 31–32 . The full 1SG row and the morphological properties of ku= are
developed in first-second-person-singular-affixes .

en=epunkine wa en=kore yan | 1SG.O=protect CONJ 1SG.O=give IMP | Please protect me. | An imperative clause with an implicit second-person subject; both instances of en= index the narrator as the object of 'protect' and 'give' respectively. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, menokoyukar 'Kamuy yukar 4' [aa-irc/011#5]

nep kameaske ene ne yakkay en=kasuy kun pe an cik pirka hike | what monster thus COP even 1SG.O=help should NMLZ exist if good although | It would be good if any monster were to help me. | en= indexes the speaker as the object of kasuy 'help'; the transitive subject is not specified by an overt prefix. | nakagawa2024:170 | TOK | Nakagawa ex. 122, after Takahashi 2007: 63

The combination 2A→1SG.O takes a dialect-specific shape. Most Hokkaido dialects form it with a
second-person A prefix followed by en= : the expected sequences are
e=en= (2SG.A→1SG.O) and eci=en=
(2PL.A→1SG.O). Saru and Chitose drop the second-person A prefix and use bare
en= for 2SG→1SG; Chitose extends the bare form to 2PL→1SG as well,
so that the same surface prefix covers both 3→1SG and, in those dialects, 2→1SG — subject
identification falls to discourse context or overt noun phrases nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 ‹consensus›.

An oral-literary parallel uses i= where the colloquial register uses
en= : in the elegant register the 2SG subject is again unexpressed, and
i= appears as the first-person object prefix in the 2SG→1SG
configuration sato2008 261 . The literary referential range of
i= is part of the fourth-person narrative system, developed in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

First-person plural exclusive object: un=

The prefix un= indexes a first-person plural exclusive object. It
belongs to the tripartite 1PL.EXCL row alongside ci= (A) and
=as (S), all three formally distinct nakagawa2024 166–169 ; bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 . The row is exclusive
throughout: un= marks a speaker-group object that excludes the
addressee, while addressee-inclusive 'us' is indexed in the object position by the fourth-person
prefix i= . Tokachi confirms the same exclusive–inclusive split, with
un= systematically opposed to the fourth-person object takahashi2015 206–209 ‹consensus›.

sanbyakugojuen a=un=kore hike ka | 350.yen 4.A=1PL.EXCL.O=give although even | Although they gave us 350 yen, … | The prefix a= marks an indefinite fourth-person transitive subject; un= indexes the exclusive first-plural group as the recipient-object of kore 'give'. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak 'Monologue 2' [aa-irc/004#109]

The full exclusive paradigm and the clusivity opposition are developed in
first-person-plural-and-clusivity .

Second-person objects: e= and eci=

Second-person indexes are role-neutral: e= marks 2SG as A, S, or O,
and eci= does the same for 2PL, with no dedicated object form for
either tamura1972 27 ; sato2008 32 ; bugaeva2012 472 . The role of e= in a transitive clause follows from discourse: a
form such as e=kore may be read as 2SG.A acting on a zero-indexed
third-person O, or as a zero-indexed third-person A acting on a 2SG.O, with overt noun phrases
or context resolving the ambiguity. Ijäs notes this as one of the practically challenging
features of the O set for learners of Saru ijas2023 .

kanpisos e=kore | book 2SG.O=give | She gave you the book. | Constructed following Ijäs Lesson 5. With e= read as 2SG.O, the transitive subject is a zero-indexed third person; the same e=kore is equally grammatical with e= as 2SG.A ('you gave the book (to her)'). | ijas2023 | SAR

The form eci= additionally serves as the portmanteau exponent for a
first-person A acting on a second-person O: the four combinations 1SG.A→2SG.O, 1SG.A→2PL.O,
1PL.EXCL.A→2SG.O, and 1PL.EXCL.A→2PL.O all surface as bare eci= ,
without a separate first-person subject prefix tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2008 38 ; bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 . The portmanteau
and its consequences for the combination paradigm are developed in
eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

Fourth-person object: i=

The prefix i= is the O member of the fourth-person tripartite row,
alongside a= (or dialectally an= ) as A and
=an as S. Nakagawa lists five referential uses for the form-set —
inclusive first-plural, second-person honorific, impersonal or agent-defocusing, oral-narrative
protagonist, and logophoric reference in reported speech; Bugaeva covers the same range under
"indefinite", reanalysing the quotation use as logophoric; Tamura distributes the functions
across analytically separate persons nakagawa2024 169, 174–179 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34, 39 ‹contested›. The full referential paradigm of
the form-set is treated in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm ; the concern here is
i= as the object prefix common to all of those contexts.

sunku tono mat i=kaopiwki wa i=kore | Sunku lord wife 4.O=help CONJ 4.O=give | Help me and take care of me, wife of the lord of Sunku. | Both i= prefixes index the oral-narrative protagonist as the object; kaopiwki 'help' and kore 'give' are linked by the conjunctive wa. This clause is an address within the narrative. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 'Folktale 8 (part 1)' [aa-irc/006#243]

a=rehe Ikuresuye sekor a=i=réko | 4.POSS=name Ikuresuye QUOT 4.A=4.O=call | I was named Ikuresuye. | The possessive a= on the noun rehe 'name' and the transitive-subject a= on the verb réko 'call/name' are independent instances of the fourth-person prefix; i= indexes the oral-narrative protagonist as the object of the naming action. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 'Folktale 7' [aa-irc/005#1]

The prefix i= is homophonous with the detransitivising prefix
i- , which derives intransitive predicates from transitives — as in
i-ruska be angry at something unspecified beside transitive
ruska be angry at someone . The two items belong to different grammatical layers:
the object marker i= is a personal index in the paradigm, while the
detransitivising i- belongs to valency-changing morphology and patterns
with the reflexive yay- and the reciprocal u- tamura1972 35 ; nam2021 62–74 . Their historical relationship is debated: the older
analysis derives the antipassive from the fourth-person object marker; Bugaeva reverses the
direction; Nam proposes a common source in the generic noun
*hi/*ihi 'thing, place, time' ‹contested›. The detransitivising prefix
is treated in antipassive-detransitive-i .

Zero third-person object

Third-person objects carry no overt index. With a first- or second-person subject, the visible
personal prefix is the A form alone — ku= , ci= ,
e= , eci= , or a= ; with
third-person subject and object alike, the finite verb carries no overt person prefix nakagawa2024 169 ; bugaeva2012 471 .

moyuk ku=nukar | raccoon.dog 1SG.A=see | I saw a raccoon dog. | Constructed following Ijäs Lesson 5. The third-person object has no overt index; only the 1SG.A prefix ku= appears on the verb. | ijas2023 | SAR

Whether the absence of a third-person index reflects a zero affix or the simple absence of any
third-person exponent is contested. Nakagawa treats third person as lacking an affix; Satō
argues that a zero third-person affix must be posited to sustain the two-index cap — the
ungrammaticality of sequences such as *Ø=en=e=kore 'one makes me give
you' follows if both subject and object slots are already occupied by zero, blocking a third
index nakagawa2024 169 ; sato2023b 39 ; sato2025a 369 ‹contested›. The alignment implications of the zero versus absent-exponent analysis are
pursued in alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

title: Oblique and Adjunct Syntax; Verbless and Presentational Clauses
summary: The syntax of postpositional/relational-noun obliques and adjuncts, plus minor verbless and presentational clause types.
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Oblique and Adjunct Syntax; Verbless and Presentational Clauses
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: The syntax of postpositional/relational-noun obliques and adjuncts, plus minor verbless and presentational clause types.

The predicate slot is invariably verbal

Hokkaido Ainu has essentially no verbless predication at the structural level. As Nakagawa states
in the Handbook chapter on parts of speech, "the element that occupies the predicate position in
Ainu is invariably a verb phrase and the head of the verb phrase is a verb or an auxiliary verb"
nakagawa2022pos 474 . A bare nominal or oblique phrase cannot stand as the main
predicate of a sentence. What looks verbless in discourse is always one of four things: an
ambient verb with an incorporated nominal root and no overt subject; an insubordinated
formal-noun clause reduced to its nominalizer; a vocative or exclamative fragment that is not a
predication at all; or genuine ellipsis of a recoverable predicate under discourse conditions
that license it. These are the phenomena organized in this chapter. The three productive
structural predicate types — copular, existential/locational, and property-concept — are treated
in copula-ne-predicate-nominals , existential-locational-an-oka , and
property-concept-predication respectively; the present chapter maps their borders.

Minor predicate and non-predicate patterns covered in this chapter

construction type
surface form
underlying structure
notes

ambient (0-place) predicate
mean , sirpirka , hawas
incorporated-noun + existential/posture verb
fully verbal; no subject slot

locational predicate
[NP] [loc or ta] an/oka
existential verb + oblique adjunct
no PP-predicate type; oblique is always an adjunct

labelling/specificational copular
[clause] hawe ne
formal-noun nominalization + copula
the naming/quoting use of the formal-noun frame

formal-noun polar question / exclamative
bare ruwe? / hawe? / siri?
insubordinated nominalized clause
without the following ne or an

elliptical VP answer
verb repeated from question
predicate-only clause under ellipsis
dialect variation on yes/no affirmation particles

vocative fragment
bare nominal
address utterance; no predication
illocutionary, not propositional

nominal-frame conditional
[Subj] [Compl] ne yakun
copular predicate + conditional particle
antecedent of a condition on class membership or identity

Ambient predicates: the 0-place (完全動詞) verb class

The nearest Ainu equivalent to an 'impersonal' or 'ambient' clause is the
zero-place verb ( 完全動詞 ), in which a nominal root is
incorporated into an existential or posture verb to form a word that predicates an ambient
state with no subject slot and no person indexing
nakagawa2024 126–128 ; sato2008 21 ; shibatani1990 19 .
The most transparent example is mean be cold (of weather) , etymologically
me coldness
+ the existential an exist — 'coldness exists' — which Shibatani explicitly
analyses as noun incorporation shibatani1990 19 . The same sir scene, weather, land
root combines with property verbs to make weather predicates:
sirpirka be fine weather (= sir ⁠- pirka be good ),
sirsesek be hot (weather) (= sir ⁠- sesek be hot ),
sirkunne become dark, night falls (= sir ⁠- kunne be dark ).
The posture verb as stand forms the parallel 'voice' ambient:
hawas there is a sound, it is said (= haw voice ⁠- as ).

me-an | coldness-exist | It is cold. | Zero-place ambient predicate: the incorporated nominal me 'coldness' + existential an. No subject NP; no person index. Nakagawa's §4.2.2 lists this as the type-① 完全動詞. | nakagawa2024:126; shibatani1990:19 | SAR

sir-pirka | scene-be.good | The weather is fine. | The sir- prefix incorporates the ambient-domain noun into the property verb. | nakagawa2024:126 | HK

haw-as | voice-stand | There is a sound; it is said (an utterance is occurring). | Nakagawa exx. 16–17 distinguish 0-place hawas (no subject) from 1-place hawean 'X says' (= haw- + -ean with 1sg/4-person suffix), showing the 0-place / 1-place minimal pair in the voice domain. | nakagawa2024:127–128 | HK

The grammar-diagnostic pair is the contrast between a 0-place ambient and a 1-place
experiential verb with the same root. mean be cold (of weather) 'the weather is
cold' takes no person affix and no subject, while merayke feel cold 'feel cold'
takes the standard intransitive subject index:

merayke=an ka somo ki no | feel.cold=4.S even NEG do ADV | Without even feeling cold… | The 1-place merayke indexes the experiencer via the fourth-person S suffix =an, as opposed to 0-place mean, which takes no index. | nakagawa2024:130 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 15

The parallel 0-place / 1-place split holds for hawas 'there is a sound'
vs hawean say 'X says'; a single nominal root surfaces in both, but the 0-place
member predicates an ambient event in the scene, while the 1-place member requires an indexed
agent-experiencer nakagawa2024 127–128 . The zero-place class is small and
largely lexicalized (weather, atmospheric light, acoustic ambiance); productivity is limited
nakagawa2024 126 . The noun-incorporation mechanism that creates them is treated
in noun-incorporation-object ; the existential an in the
aspect-auxiliary role is treated in existential-aspect-an-oka .

Oblique adjuncts and the locational predicate gap

There is no clause type in Hokkaido Ainu in which a bare oblique phrase — a postpositional
phrase, a locational NP, a temporal adverb — functions as the predicate of a main clause.
Nakagawa's clause-constituent analysis (§10.1) treats adverbs, case-marked NPs, and
postpositional adverbs uniformly as adjuncts on the verb phrase, never predicate heads
nakagawa2024 359–361 . Refsing states the complementary positive requirement:
locational and existential meanings are expressed with the existential verb, not with a bare
PP — she lists no 'locational copula' type separate from an / oka(y)
in the Shizunai grammar refsing1986 272–273 .

The practical consequence is that a Japanese-style bare 'topic–locative' sentence
( トキヨには大きな家が多い ) is dispreferred in Ainu: the intransitive
existential an cannot take a bare second NP because it is strictly
one-argument. The natural pattern marks the location as an oblique with
or ta 'in the place of' (or bare ta ), leaving
the existential as the predicate head sato2008 3–4 . The community discussion
of this constraint, confirmed against Satō's account, gives the rule concisely: an
is intransitive, so the frame is A B or ta an 'A is at B', while the
transitive un be at (transitive locative) licenses a bare nominal complement
A B un without
or ta
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09, confirmed against
sato2008 3–4 ) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

iwor or ta an wa | mountains inside.of LOC exist.S CONJ | being in the mountains … | The locational or ta is an oblique adjunct on the existential an; the NP iwor 'mountains' is the subject of the existential, not a second argument. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子, uwepeker 民話8, AA研アイヌ語資料 [aa-irc/006#128]

e=kor iwatarap apunno hon or ta anakne apunno an wa | 2SG.A=have infant gently womb inside.of LOC TOP gently exist.S CONJ | your infant, peacefully in your womb … | Locational predication: hon or ta is the locational oblique 'in the womb', anakne is the topic marker, apunno 'gently/peacefully' an adverb, and an is the predicate head. The subject of an is the infant (e=kor iwatarap 'your infant'), which is the topic of the wider discourse. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子, uwepeker 民話13, AA研アイヌ語資料 [aa-irc/016#166]

Because obliques never head a predicate, a phrase like kotan ta 'in the
village' requires an or oka to form a complete
predication: kotan ta an 'is in the village', not bare
*kotan ta . This parallels the constraint that the copula ne
is needed for predicate-nominal clauses (a Japanese-style bare-nominal predicate is also
excluded). In both cases the verbal head is obligatory, and what reads as a 'nominal predicate'
or 'locational predicate' in English always has a verb beneath it.

Labelling, naming, and specificational uses of the copular frame

The copular frame A B ne serves not only to classify (see
copula-ne-predicate-nominals for its full paradigm) but also to label and name
referents in discourse. The specificational pattern — where a nominalized clause is the subject
and a name or title is the complement — is particularly frequent in naming contexts and in
traditional oral narrative when a deity or character is formally introduced. Nakagawa's
example of this frame comes from the formal-noun-headed subject:

tap a=ye katu Piratur sekor a=ye hawe ne | now 4.A=say way Piratur QUOT 4.A=say NMLZ COP | The way it is now called is 'Piratur'. | Specificational copular: the nominalized clause tap a=ye katu 'the way of now saying' is the subject; the quoted name Piratur sekor a=ye hawe 'the formal-noun report of calling it Piratur' is the complement before ne. The formal noun hawe (voice-NMLZ) nominates the whole quotation as the complement. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 779

In this pattern the formal noun hawe 'voice, utterance' functions as
the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed-form nominalizer that packages the
quotative clause as a referential NP, and the copula ne predicates
identity between the subject and the named referent. This is not a separate construction from
the ordinary copular clause; it is the same frame with a nominalized complement. The formal
nouns that head these nominalized clauses are defined in formal-defective-nouns ;
the quotative particle sekor belongs to the
quotative-complementation-sekor quotative complementation chapter .

The sekor a=ye hawe ne frame is also the canonical 'it is called X'
formula for introducing traditional names of places, deities, and artefacts in oral narrative,
functioning as a specificational-naming copular in the context of cultural transmission. The
constituent structure — [[sekor a=ye] hawe] ne — keeps the copula
ne as the single predicate head; there is no head-stacking and no
verbless portion. The copula's person marking follows the standard transitive-subject set on
the nominative argument (see
person-marking-architecture the person-marking architecture chapter ); the
absence of an object person index on ne , despite its two-place
semantics, is the diagnostic idiosyncrasy noted by Nakagawa: the complement NP takes no
object affix on the copula nakagawa2024 132 .

Formal-noun fragments: polar questions, exclamatives, and answers

Nakagawa 2024 lays out a four-member paradigm for the sentence-final formal-noun frame, in
which the choice of predicate after the nominalizer encodes illocutionary force
nakagawa2024 392–396 . The declarative and content-question members take the
copula ne and the existential an respectively
(treated in the evidential chapters for ruwe-ne-inferential ,
siri-ne-situational , hawe-ne-reportative , and
humi-ne-sensory ); what concerns this chapter is the reduction of that frame to a
bare formal noun — without either ne or an —
which functions as a polar question or exclamative:

The formal-noun illocutionary paradigm (after Nakagawa 2024: 392–396)

frame
illocutionary value
example

NMLZ + ne
declarative (recognized fact)
… ruwe ne

NMLZ + an?
content question (with a wh-expression)
… ruwe an?

bare NMLZ?
polar question / exclamative
e=eraman hawe?

NMLZ + un
affirmative reply
ku=nu ruwe un

The bare-nominalizer polar question is an instance of insubordination : a nominalized
clause that would ordinarily be embedded under the copula or existential stands instead as a
main clause, retaining the illocutionary force of a question or exclamation from prosodic and
contextual cues rather than from any grammatical marker. The sentence
e=eraman hawe? 'you understood?' is a polar question in Saru without
the following ne or an
nakagawa2024 395, 409 . The same shape can function as an exclamative —
expressing surprise or emphatic assertion — again distinguished from the declarative only by
intonation. Nakagawa notes the Saru reinforced form
ruwe he an? (with the particle he and the
existential) as a more explicit polar-question variant nakagawa2024 395 .

e=eraman hawe | 2SG.S=understand NMLZ | You understood? / You understood! | The bare formal noun hawe (voice-nominalizer) without a following copula or existential; polar question or exclamative depending on intonation and context. This is the reduced/insubordinated frame of the hawe ne declarative. | nakagawa2024:395, 409 | SAR

The affirmative-answer member NMLZ + un likewise reduces the
declarative frame by replacing the copula with the final particle un ,
forming a minimally marked confirmation. The formal-noun head is retained, making the
affirmative answer morphologically kin to the declarative without being one:

ku=nu ru-we un | 1SG.A=hear track-POSS FIN | Yes, I heard it. | The final particle un forms the affirmative-reply frame while the formal noun ruwe is retained. Compare the declarative ku=nu ruwe ne 'I (certainly) heard it' and the content-question frame ku=nu ruwe an? 'did I hear [what]?'. | nakagawa2024:396 | HK | Nakagawa ex. 794

An additional bare-predicate use of isam functions as the full
predicate of a content question in the corpus. Nakagawa's example shows
isam, isam? 'it's gone, gone?' functioning as the rhetorical pivot of
a question where the missing entity is already established in discourse:

mak ne hine nea CAWAN isam isam | how COP CONJ that bowl NEG.exist NEG.exist | How can that bowl be gone, gone? | The negative existential isam stands as the predicate of a rhetorical question; its repetition is a discourse-emphatic pattern in oral narrative rather than a distinct construction. | nakagawa2024:410 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 849

These fragment-like patterns are not structurally verbless: the formal noun is nominalized
from a full clause and the bare-NMLZ frame still has a verbal predicate implicit in the
nominalization. Predicationally, the clause is complete; it is only the
final copula or existential that is elided under the reduced illocutionary frames. This is
therefore ellipsis at the right edge of the clause, conditioned by illocutionary force, rather
than an absence of predication. The full treatment of the evidential paradigm and its
illocutionary differentiation is in evidential-schema-overview ; sentence-final
particles that interact with these frames are treated in
sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force .

Elliptical answers and minimal clauses

In response to a content question the standard strategy is to repeat the relevant verb phrase,
matching the morphology of the questioned predicate. There are no dedicated 'yes' and 'no'
words in the affirmation/negation sense that English uses for all questions
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-04, asserted; reported
across multiple dialects; confirmed against the affirmative-frame ruwe un
of Nakagawa 2024: 396) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

For polar questions (yes/no questions), there is notable dialect variation. In the Saru
dialect the answer pattern mirrors Japanese: the respondent targets the propositional content
of the question and affirms or denies it ('no, I am not going' in reply to 'are you not
going?'). In other dialects — attested for Yakumo, Horobetsu, Tokachi, Bibai, Asahikawa, and
Sōya among others — the respondent avoids a simple yes/no particle altogether and instead
repeats the relevant verb phrase. To a negative polar question like
somo e=oman ya? 'are you not going?', a speaker from these dialects
would reply with the verb alone, such as somo ku=oman 'I am not going'
or ku=oman kus ne 'I am going (because)', rather than with an
affirmation or denial particle
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-04, asserted ✔;
reported across eight dialect localities: Yakumo, Horobetsu, Saru, Tokachi, Bibai, Asahikawa,
Nayoro, and Sōya) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

somo ku=oman | NEG 1SG.S=go | I am not going. | Constructed elliptical reply to a polar yes/no question. In non-Saru dialects, the VP is repeated without a yes/no affirmation particle; the negative particle somo is part of the predicate being affirmed, not a sentential denial-word.

The forms e , ho , and
m that superficially resemble affirmation/agreement particles are
response-to-call utterances, used when a speaker is addressed by name or summoned — not
as affirmations of propositional content
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-04, asserted ✔)
‹corpus-confirmed›. They are treated under sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force .

The minimal answer clause is therefore a predicate-only utterance: the subject is entirely
omitted and the verb phrase — including any person indexes that anchor the participants — is
the complete response. This is predicate ellipsis (the subject NP and topic marker are
recoverable from the question), not absence of predication. Interrogative strategies and the
full polar-question morphosyntax are treated in interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

Vocative fragments and address utterances

Direct address in Hokkaido Ainu uses bare nominal forms — personal names, kin terms, or title
nouns — with no predicate. These are not propositions and not even elliptical predications;
they are illocutionary acts of calling or summoning a participant. Their grammatical content
is entirely addressee-targeting: the utterance indexes who is being addressed, not what is
being said about them. In that sense vocative fragments are structurally parallel to the
response-to-call particles above — both are non-propositional speech acts that lack a
predicate by virtue of their illocutionary type, not by virtue of any structural anomaly.
Kin terms used in vocative address may appear in the plain uninflected (non-possessed) form
rather than the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed/affiliative form that the same term
would take when used referentially inside a clause, since the affiliative suffix presupposes a
possessor argument that is not relevant in a direct address. The illocutionary inventory of
Hokkaido Ainu, including the interaction of address, register, and honorific speech, is the
subject of sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force and
honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers .

Nominal-frame conditionals: B ne yakun

One productive minor construction builds on the copula by adding the conditional particle
yakun if, when (conditional) : the frame [Subject] [Complement]
ne yakun expresses a condition on class membership or identity — 'if (S) is (C), then
…' nakagawa2024 ;
sato2009 . Ijäs treats the construction as a standard conditional antecedent in her
pedagogical grammar, noting that the copula in the conditional takes the same transitive-subject
person indexes as in the declarative
ijas2023 . In the factual sense the construction is just a copular clause with
yakun in the conditional-particle slot that the clause-linkage system
places at the right edge of a dependent clause (see conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses
for the full conditional system).

aynu ku=ne yakun | Ainu 1SG.S=COP COND | if I am an Ainu … | The copular conditional frame: the copula ne takes the 1SG transitive-subject prefix ku= and is followed by the conditional particle yakun. The whole antecedent is a dependent clause; the consequent follows in the main clause.

nispa ne yakun | chief COP COND | if (he) is a chief … | Third-person subject: the copula ne takes no person affix (zero = 3rd person). The nominal complement nispa precedes ne in the standard V-final order.

The frame is especially frequent in discourse framing, where a speaker conditionally asserts
the identity of a referent as a premise for what follows. The negative counterpart places the
standard copula negator before ne :
X ka somo ne yakun 'if (S) is not (C)' (see
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation for the
ka somo ne copula negation pattern, and
negation-existential-possessive-isam for the existential counterpart). The
person-marking behaviour is the same as in the declarative copular clause, including the
idiosyncrasy whereby the copula takes no object-person affix even though it is semantically
two-place nakagawa2024 132 .

One micro-fact about ne in the ka somo ne
negation frame and sporadically in other contexts is that the copula appears without a subject
affix — the person marking is absent in a way not predicted by the zero-marking of third
person aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-25, speculative;
"reason unclear") ‹speculative›. This is an open gap in the description: no read primary source
addresses the pattern systematically, and no structural account of the affix loss has been
proposed. It may reflect the frozen or idiom-like status of the
ka somo ne string, but this remains without structural support.

The copula also participates in the inchoative 'become' reading when followed by the
existential an (the ne an sequence discussed by
community members as the Ainu equivalent of Japanese 〜になる )
aynucorporadiscord (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-13, proposed;
consistent with Refsing 1986: 145 and Nakagawa 2024: 118–119) ‹contested›. That inchoative
construction is the interface between the copula and the TAM system and is treated in
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation and tam-overview .

The predicate-omission environment: a unified account

Across the constructions in this chapter, a common thread is the conditions under which the
predicate — or its final element — is absent from the surface string. Three environments
license predicate-omission or predicate-reduction in Hokkaido Ainu:

Illocutionary reduction (formal-noun fragments, § above ): the
copula or existential that ordinarily closes the formal-noun frame is elided when the
illocutionary force is polar-interrogative or exclamative. The nominalizer is retained; only
the predicate over it is absent. The licensing condition is a specific illocutionary type, not
a general ellipsis rule.

Discourse-anaphoric ellipsis (elliptical answers, § above ): in
responses to polar and content questions, the subject NP and topic material are omitted under
identity with the antecedent question; what remains is the predicate alone (or predicate +
negator). This is VP-only predication, not verblessness.

Non-propositional use (vocatives, address particles): the utterance is not a
predication at all, so no predicate is expected. These are illocutionary primitives, outside
the scope of clausal syntax.

In none of these environments does an oblique phrase, a bare noun, or a bare adjective
function as a predicate head — the environment in which Japanese and many SOV languages
routinely omit the copula in informal speech has no structural counterpart in Hokkaido Ainu.
The copula ne and the existential an / oka
are obligatory in their respective predication environments and cannot be structurally omitted
to yield a bare-nominal or bare-locative predicate nakagawa2022pos 474 ;
nakagawa2024 359–361 ; sato2008 20–21 . The discourse conditions
that govern when topic NPs and subject indices may be absent — a separate phenomenon — are
treated in topic-marking-anakne , constituent-order-head-final , and
argument-realization-indexing .

title: The Oral-Literature Corpus and Spoken-Language Data
summary: The annotated oral-narrative text editions, recorded conversation, and audio archives that supply the grammar's connected-discourse examples, treated as a genre-tagged quantifiable corpus.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Oral-Literature Corpus and Spoken-Language Data
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: The annotated oral-narrative text editions, recorded conversation, and audio archives that supply the grammar's connected-discourse examples, treated as a genre-tagged quantifiable corpus.

Oral literature as the primary documentary resource

The grammar of Hokkaido Ainu is documented overwhelmingly through its oral-literary and
recorded spoken tradition. Written records from before the twentieth century are sparse and
linguistically unreliable; the descriptive grammars and dictionaries treated in
written-sources-grammars-dictionaries rest in turn on recordings made between the
1920s and the 1990s from a contracting community of native speakers. The corpus underlying this
grammar — approximately 209,878 aligned sentences across 45,225 distinct token types — consists
almost entirely of transcribed and annotated performances of traditional oral genres: heroic epics
( yukar heroic epic / sakorpe heroic epic (E Hokkaido) ), divine epics
( kamuyyukar divine epic / oyna ), prose folktales
( uwepeker prose folktale / tuytak prose folktale (E Hokkaido) ),
ritual prayer ( inonno itak prayer words / kamuynomi deity worship ),
and a smaller body of composed prose (the Ainu Times newsletter and
comparable texts). For the genre system and its grammatical correlates see
oral-literature-genre-taxonomy ; for poetic register,
poetic-archaic-elevated-register ; for corpus method and dialect sampling,
dialect-sample-and-corpus-method .

The recording and publication tradition

Chiri Yukie and the pre-war foundations

The earliest published Ainu-language oral-literature collection is
chiri1923 — seventeen Iburi (Horobetsu) divine epics with Ainu text and Japanese
translation, compiled by Chiri Yukie 知里幸惠 (1903–1922) in the final years of her life.
Linguistically, the collection is anchored in the Horobetsu idiolect of its compiler, who
drew on repertoires transmitted within her family. Its hero-narrators are indexed throughout
by the exclusive first-person plural prefix ci= and suffix
=as deployed as a singular narrator-voice — a convention
characteristic of Iburi divine epics and distinct from the Saru and Chitose practice of
using the fourth-person a= / =an in the same
role sato2008 261 ; endo2022 . This person-marking contrast and
its grammatical motivation are taken up in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and
fourth-person-honorific .

Kindaichi Kyōsuke's parallel scholarly activity in the 1930s–1940s — editing transcriptions
made by Kubodera Itsuhiko from speakers across Hokkaido — produced the main pre-war
inventory of heroic-epic texts kindaichi1931 ; kindaichi1933 . Kindaichi's yukar grammar established the five-syllable line as the canonical
metrical unit kindaichi1931 165 , a description subsequently refined by
Okuda's two-metre analysis distinguishing syllable-count from accent-oriented verse
okuda2022 ; the metrical system is treated in
sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure . The pre-war editions are read here
through Endō and Okuda and are cited as reported.

Post-war field documentation

The bulk of the aligned corpus derives from recordings made in the three decades after World
War II. The most extensively analysed set is the
Biratori Town oral-literature archive
biratori1969 , held by the National Ainu Museum (国立アイヌ民族博物館) and
available online. Principal narrators include 鍋澤ねぷき (Nabesawa Nepuki), 貝澤とぅるしの
(Kaizawa Torusino), and 川上まつ子 (Kawakami Matsuko), all from Biratori in the Saru basin.
The archive covers kamuy yukar, menoko yukar (female heroic epic), and uwepeker; it supplies
many of the attested examples cited throughout this grammar.

The ILCAA Ainu materials
ilcaa1976 — recordings made at the Tokyo University of Foreign Studies Research
Institute for Languages and Cultures of Asia and Africa, 1976–1984 — cover Saru and
Shizunai narrators performing uwepeker, kamuy yukar, and prayer texts. The primary narrators
are 川上まつ子 (Saru) and 織田ステノ (Shizunai); their performances are available with
transcription, Japanese translation, and audio at ainugo.aa-ken.jp. Tamura Suzuko's
contemporaneous field recordings in Biratori (principally 平賀サダモ and others) underlie
her Saru dictionary tamura1996 and the folktale and lyric texts from which Dal
Corso and Hirosawa draw evidential examples dalcorso2018 ;
hirosawa2026 .

An independent line of post-war documentation covers Iburi and neighbouring dialects. Satō's
study of an Abuta (虻田) yukar narrated by 遠島タンケ extends coverage east of Horobetsu and
establishes that yukar grammar — aspect marking, the prospective
anki , comparative
kane as if, to the extent that , and fourth-person indexing — is continuous
with the prose grammar of the same dialects sato2009yukar . Nakagawa's Chitose
dictionary nakagawa1995 provides a parallel primary reference for the
genre terms, the sakehe system, and ritual vocabulary in the Chitose dialect.

Continuing documentation: text series, corpora, and modern prose

Nakagawa Hiroshi's 24-volume annotated text series nakagawatexts2000 (2000–2025)
presents Chitose-dialect kamuy yukar and uwepeker narrated principally by 白沢ナベ
(Shirasawa Nabe), with interlinear glossing and Japanese commentary. This is the most
extensively annotated single-narrator Hokkaido corpus available in print. Its Chitose data
complement the Saru-centred Biratori and ILCAA collections and support dialect comparisons
for person marking, aspect, and evidential distribution across the corpus.

The online NINJAL Ainu oral-literature corpus
ninjal2003 aggregates recordings with audio across dialects (primarily Saru and
Chitose, with additional localities). The Chiba University materials
chiba2015 add Horobetsu and related dialect texts. The
Glossed Audio Corpus
glossedcorpus2016 provides interlinearly glossed Chitose and Saru folktale texts
online.

The Ainu Times ( アイヌタイムズ ) newsletter
ainukoraci1991 , published from 1991, supplies the main body of composed Ainu
prose from after the period of intensive oral recording. Contributors include speakers from
Saru, Chitose, and Samani; genres include first-person prose narration, prayer texts, and
songs. These texts differ from the transcribed oral record in being written compositions;
their register draws on the traditional prayer-language inventory
(see honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers ) and on the poetic-elegant
conventions of poetic-archaic-elevated-register , but their grammatical profile
is shaped partly by literacy practices rather than performance conventions.

Major collections represented in this grammar

The table below summarises the primary collections cited in this grammar. Narrators are listed
in Japanese to reflect the names as they appear in original sources. Genre abbreviations follow
oral-literature-genre-taxonomy . Attestation-level details for individual examples
are supplied in each chapter under the relevant phenomenon.

Collection
Reference

Recording/publication period

chiri1923

nakagawatexts2000

ninjal2003

glossedcorpus2016
