recent
various Saru/Chitose narrators

Ainu Times ( アイヌタイムズ )
ainukoraci1991
SAR, CHI, SHI
prose, inonno itak, song
1991–
神崎雅好, 浜田隆史, 丸野和子, others

Genre-conditioned grammar and the corpus as evidence

The collections differ not only in dialect but in the grammatical constructions they make
available. Verse genres (yukar, kamuy yukar) deploy the elegant poetic register
( atomte-itak ), featuring applicative prefixes in place of case
particles, expanded polysynthetic verb forms, and a distinct lexicon; they also show the
fourth-person prefix a= / =an as the
hero-narrator's voice in Saru and Chitose texts (and ci= / =as in Iburi, per Chiri 1923), a number-agreement override under which
ek-an 'I come.SG' is grammatical where everyday speech requires
k-ek , and formulaic couplet phrasing throughout
sato2008 259–264 . Prose folktales use a more conversational register with
dense quotative marking and a characteristically one-directional, low-variation intonation
that Tamura distinguishes from the rising pattern of conversational interaction
tamura1996 (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024;
aynucorporadiscord ). These differences are the empirical basis for
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre ,
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse , and
reference-tracking .

The contrast between verse and prose registers can be heard in native metalinguistic
commentary within the corpus. 川上まつ子 describes the menoko yukar genre explicitly in terms
of the sakehe (refrain): performing with the refrain marks the piece as menoko yukar, while
omitting it and delivering the same content in prose style makes it an uwepeker
ilcaa1976 — a speaker's formulation that identifies the sakehe as the
primary formal diagnostic. The sakehe system and its verse structure are analysed in
sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure ; rhetorical couplets and formulaic
phrases in parallelism-couplets-and-word-pairs .

sine kamuyyukar ku=ye so | one kamuy-yukar 1SG.A=tell FIN | Let me tell one kamuy yukar. | A native performance frame uttered before beginning a kamuy yukar. The first-person ku= (not the narrative fourth-person a=) appears here because this is conversational framing around the narration rather than inside it — illustrating the boundary between the everyday first person and the hero-narrator's fourth-person voice. | sato2008 | CHI | Chitose; Satō 2008 ch. 21 reading exercise

The evidential framing that pervades the corpus differs by genre:
ruwe ne marks the narrator's asserted knowledge of story-world events
in prose tales, while the formulaic ceremonial close
ruwe tapan na appears in prayer and ritual contexts
dalcorso2018 331 . For the evidential system see
evidential-schema-overview and ruwe-ne-inferential .

Dialect and genre coverage

The aligned corpus is heavily weighted toward the Saru and Shizunai dialects, which together
account for roughly 121,000 of the 209,878 sentences; Chitose adds approximately 4,400
(plus 5,753 classified as Saru–Chitose combined), Mukawa 13,424, and Horobetsu 3,098.
Tokachi contributes 1,550. Northern and eastern Hokkaido — the localities of the
sakorpe heroic epic and the distinct eastern song traditions — are
represented thinly endo2022 . This geographic bias reflects the concentration of
twentieth-century fieldwork in the Saru basin; comparable systematic recording of eastern and
northern varieties did not occur at comparable depth.

Genre coverage is similarly uneven. The kamuy yukar is well documented across multiple
dialects (Saru, Chitose, Iburi); the uwepeker is well represented in Saru and Chitose;
prayer texts are attested in Saru (川上まつ子, ILCAA), Shizunai (織田ステノ), and revival
prose (Ainu Times). The caranke (rhetorical negotiation-speech), which Kindaichi ranks
immediately below yukar for elegant-register density
kindaichi1935 382 , is identified in the scholarly literature but does not
appear as a genre tag in the aligned corpus. Songs ( upopo ,
yaysama ) are attested but their short lyric texts contribute
comparatively little to the grammatical evidence base for the phenomena analysed in this
grammar.

Philological constraints

Several features of the record limit what the corpus can establish. The transcription history
is uneven: Chiri Yukie's 1923 edition and the pre-war Kindaichi-era texts were produced
before modern phonemic conventions; their representations of coda consonants, the glottal
stop, and pitch accent vary from the orthographic norms used in this grammar (see the
orthography chapters). The ILCAA and Biratori recordings, made with audio and transcribed
against the audible record, are phonologically more reliable but reflect the idiolects of a
small number of elderly Saru-basin speakers.

The aligned corpus is not genre-tagged as a queryable facet. Genre must be inferred from
document titles (e.g. "uwepeker 民話8", "kamuy yukar フム パㇰパㇰ") rather than a
controlled metadata field. Per-genre frequency counts — for instance, the density of
quotative sekor in yukar versus uwepeker — are not directly
retrievable from the database; only whole-corpus token counts are available, and these
include metalinguistic uses of genre terms alongside performance tokens.

Certain corpus documents present genre-boundary ambiguities that resist the standard
taxonomy. One ILCAA text narrated by 織田ステノ — a kamuy yukar titled "怠け者の子供" —
combines a long sakehe with a short narrative body and closes as a first-person human
self-narration ( yayeisoytak ), making it not straightforwardly
assignable to the kamuy yukar or yukar categories ‹contested› (gengojiro, aynu-corpora
Discord 2025-12-12; aynucorporadiscord ). Genre fuzziness of this kind is
consistent with Endō's observation that regional repertoires vary and that speaker practice
does not always track scholarly category boundaries endo2022 .

A further philological note concerns Chiri Yukie's Ainu Shin'yōshū . The kamuy
yukar section headings appear in full capitals in the original edition (e.g.
AEKIRUSHI KAMUY ). The title element AEKIRUSHI has been proposed, after
Katayama Ryūmine 2003, to parse as
a=e-kir-usi 'the place one has memory of' rather than as a form of
ekiru 'turn back' ‹contested› (0penke, aynu-corpora Discord
2024-03-19; aynucorporadiscord ). The analysis is morphologically plausible but
remains community-proposed and has not been confirmed in independent philological
commentary. It is noted here without grammatical commitment.

The grammatical features conditioned by genre and register — fourth-person narration and its
logophoric function, TAM and evidential framing in verse versus prose, elegant-register
morphology — are analysed in logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse ,
reference-tracking , person-marking-architecture , and
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre . Verse structure and the sakehe
refrain belong to sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure and
parallelism-couplets-and-word-pairs ; the honorific and ritual registers of
prayer texts to honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers .

title: The Oral-Literature Genre System and Its Grammatical Signatures
summary: The taxonomy of Ainu oral-narrative genres and the bundle of grammatical features that indexes each one.
part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Oral-Literature Genre System and Its Grammatical Signatures
Part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
Summary: The taxonomy of Ainu oral-narrative genres and the bundle of grammatical features that indexes each one.

The genre system: parameters and macro-categories

No single Ainu term covers oral-literary activity as a whole; the domain is delimited
descriptively. The most widely adopted modern taxonomy, developed by Nakagawa and
followed by endo2022 , organizes oral activity into four macro-categories by
communicative purpose: songs (seated upopo , dance
songs rimse / horippa , lyric
yaysama , lullabies), word play
( kikinasi bird-cry mimicry, tongue-twisters, riddles),
chants directed to gods or humans (prayer
kamuy nomi itak / inonno itak ,
incantation, dispute-speech caranke , funeral words, greeting
words), and narrative (prose tales, divine epics, heroic epics, legend).
Within the narrative category the formal parameters that differentiate genres are: presence
or absence of melody; presence or absence of a sakehe refrain (of a kamuy yukar)
refrain; rhythmic tapping ( rep beat time ) with a stick
( repni rhythm-stick ); typical length; and narrator gender
endo2022 ; sato2008 259 .

The table below gives the Chitose-based three-way split of the narrative genres and its
primary formal diagnostics, which constitute the empirical baseline from which dialect
variants and contested cases depart sato2008 259 .

The table identifies the sakehe as the primary formal divide between yukar and kamuy
yukar: both are sung genres with melody, but yukar carries no sakehe while kamuy yukar
does. A fourth narrative form, irupaye , presents yukar content in
a prose frame with no melody or refrain; it was available to women narrators for whom
verse yukar was conventionally restricted sato2008 259 . Two further
genres stand outside the main three: upaskuma /
ucaskuma (oral tradition, legend, historical lore), which makes
no fixed verse or performance demands, and the coastal-Hidaka/Iburi
oyna (culture-hero epic with a distinct protagonist class), treated
separately below. The grammatical consequences of the sung/prose distinction —
person-marking conventions, evidential defaults, and metrical packaging — are surveyed in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Dialect-area variation in genre vocabulary

The Ainu genre vocabulary is not uniform across Hokkaido. Terms shift across dialect areas
in ways that reflect both sound change and the independent naming of genre categories by
distinct communities. The principal dialect correspondences, as documented by
endo2022 and chiri1955 , are:

Two naming collisions make this geography analytically difficult ‹contested›. First, the
term oyna covers different genres in different areas: in Tokachi and
Sakhalin it functions as a synonym of kamuy yukar (a divine epic
with sakehe), while in coastal Hidaka and Iburi it designates a distinct genre whose
protagonist is a culture-hero (Aynurakkur / Okikurmi / Oynakamuy) and which uses no sakehe
endo2022 ; nakagawa1995 s.v. oyna . Second, the term
tuytak means 'prose tale' across most of eastern Hokkaido but
'divine epic' in Samani — the same form names metrically and functionally opposite genres
depending on locality endo2022 . These are not irregular synonymies; they
reflect genre systems that were named independently in different dialect communities, a
distribution that Nakagawa treated as a problem in dialect linguistic geography
endo2022 .

The classification also shifts within a single dialect tradition. In Tamura's Saru materials,
informant 平賀サダモ (Sadamo) does not use oyna as a genre label at
all: the divine and culture-hero epics that Kindaichi and Kubodera classed as
oyna are subsumed under yúkar in her usage
tamura1996 ; endo2022 . This shows that even the yukar/kamuy-yukar
boundary, presented as basic in the Chitose table above, is not imposed uniformly on all
bearers of the Saru tradition.

kayano1996 provides a metalinguistic definition of yukar
as a practice of imitation and transmission: "誰かが聞かせてくれた長い長い物語，それを真似て次に誰か
に聞かせる，それをユカㇻと言う" ('the long story that someone told you, which you imitate and
tell the next person — that is called yukar'). The same entry records the dream-transmission
convention: a yukar heard in a dream ( wentarap or ta a=nu yukar )
must be retold at once or it is lost.

oyna ne ciki yukar-i ne ciki | oyna COP COND yukar-POSS COP COND | whether it be an oyna or a yukar | A prose narration passage in which the narrator uses oyna and yukar in a generic conditional frame. The possessed form yukar-i marks the noun in predicative position before ne; both genre terms appear as interchangeable labels for sung epic here. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子, AA研「uwepeker 民話8(その1)」[aa-irc/006#301]

The heroic epic: yukar and sakorpe

The yukar is the longest and formally most demanding of the sung
genres: an epic sung to a melody that is specific to the narrator (not fixed to the story
and subject to change mid-performance), stretching from a single evening to several nights
endo2022 . Its defining formal features in the Saru/Chitose baseline are the
absence of a sakehe refrain and the presence of rhythmic tapping with a
repni rhythm-stick on the hearth-rim throughout the performance
sato2008 259 . Listeners may add a chorus call
( hetce ). The narrator is traditionally a man; women conventionally told the
prose form irupaye when they wished to narrate yukar content
sato2008 259 , though modern documentation records women reciting verse
yukar knowingly against that convention endo2022 .

The protagonist is a parentless superhuman youth — in Saru texts canonically
Poy-ya-un-pe or Pon Sinutapka-un-kur , in
eastern Hokkaido Pon Otasam-un-kur — who flies, dives, travels
underground, and battles without moral framing (heroes may annihilate whole villages; the
worldview is entertainment, not instruction) endo2022 . The sub-genre
menoko yukar ('women's heroic epic') keeps the format but replaces
battle with marriage as its thematic core; female heroes wield shamanistic power
( tusu ) and foresight endo2022 . A speaker of the Saru
dialect, 川上まつ子, recorded an explicit account of the menoko yukar genre: the genre is
told with a sakehe refrain (sakekoye), but the narrator herself preferred to deliver it
without the sakehe, as an uwepeker
ilcaa1976 .

In eastern and central Hokkaido — Tokachi and adjacent areas — the heroic epic is named
sakorpe (the eastern term for this genre; its internal morphology
is debated) endo2022 . For the lexical domain of 'hero', the noun
rametok bravery; brave person (rametok kamuy rametok and sonno rametok ; a further
term cipapa appears in chiri1923 but is absent from
other dictionaries and texts (gengojiro, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-02-13;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹speculative›.

The Abuta dialect (Iburi, 虻田) yukar narrated by 遠島タンケ attests the same aspectual and
comparative apparatus as Saru/Chitose verse narrative, including durative
kor okay , resultative wa okay /
wa an , and the comparative particle
kane as if; to the extent that . The prospective
anki about to is also documented. A striking lexical item in this text is
the genre-specific verb sakehawki 'to sing / recite a yukar' —
a denominal verb built on the eastern heroic-epic root
sato2009yukar §2.2 .

A residual opening formula, the eyororpe , may repeat at the start
of a yukar performance. sato2008 259 reads this as a possible vestige of
a refrain that yukar once possessed — an inference rather than an established fact
‹speculative›.

The divine epic: kamuy yukar, the sakehe refrain, and oyna

The kamuy yukar is shorter than the yukar (from a few minutes to
around an hour), told mainly by women, and sung to a melody fixed per story — in contrast
to the narrator-specific melody of the heroic epic endo2022 . Its protagonist
is a kamuy god; divine being (animal, plant, fire, water, weather,
household object) narrating its own experiences in the first person. The genre's primary
formal diagnostic is the sakehe (折り返し, burden, refrain): a fixed string,
typically inserted after every line or every few lines, fixed per story endo2022 ;
sato2008 259 .

sakehe refrain (of a kamuy yukar); the sake of — is the possessed form of
sake ; tamura1996 s.v. sákehe marks the refrain
sense with a distinct pitch accent ( sákehe ) to distinguish it from
the homophonous 'for the sake of —'. Sakehe range from transparent onomatopoeia (the
cuckoo-god's sakehe han kakkok kakkok imitates the bird's cry;
the thunder-god's hum pakpak is the sound of rain or thunder,
glossed by its narrator as humihiruy 'it's a loud sound'
biratori1969 ) to completely opaque compounds such as the long sakehe recorded
by kayano1996 : tunoyake renoyake kutunke kamuyke kamuy
cikappo hum hum hum .

The Biratori examples are from biratori1969 ; the three marlin-tuna variants
and the cuckoo sakehe are cited via endo2022 .

The function of the sakehe is disputed ‹contested›. nakagawa2001b proposed
that sakehe originally served as an index of the protagonist. Against this, the marlin-tuna
data above show that the same story-type can carry different sakehe in different
performance areas, and that the protagonist may shift between variants (Chitose changes the
fish species and replaces the sakehe). The correspondence between story-type and sakehe is
therefore not fixed, and many sakehe cannot be related to the protagonist through any
recoverable semantic link endo2022 .

sine kamuy-yukar ku=ye so | one divine-epic 1SG.A=tell FIN | Let me tell one kamuy yukar. | The sentence-final particle so marks deliberative or hortative intent. ku= is the 1SG A-series prefix on the verb ye 'tell, say'. | sato2008 | CHI | Satō 2008, chapter 21 reading frame (Chitose)

The Ainu Shin'yōshū ( chiri1923 ) — Iburi (Horobetsu)
kamuy yukar compiled and translated into Japanese by 知里幸惠 (Chiri Yukie, 1903–1922) — is
the canonical first published Ainu-language oral-literature collection and remains a primary
grammatical resource for Iburi kamuy yukar. Its narrator consistently uses the exclusive
first-person plural prefix ci= / suffix =as
as a singular hero-narrator marker, throughout every text
sato2008 261 ; sato2004 . Table-of-contents titles in the
collection such as AEKIRUSHI have been morphologically reanalysed
as a=e-kir-usi 'the place one has memory of' (0penke, aynu-corpora
Discord 2024-03-19, after 片山龍峯 2003; aynucorporadiscord ; ‹contested›) rather
than the earlier parsing from ekiru 'turn back'.

The coastal-Hidaka/Iburi oyna is a distinct genre with a
culture-hero protagonist (Aynurakkur, Okikurmi, Oynakamuy) and no sakehe, making it
formally closer to the yukar than to the kamuy yukar endo2022 ;
nakagawa1995 s.v. oyna . It should not be conflated with the eastern use
of oyna as a synonym of kamuy yukar. The grammatical analysis of
the logophoric narrator's voice (the protagonist's first-person perspective) and the
evidence for classifying the narrator function as logophoric rather than indirect is in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Prose narration: uwepeker, tuytak, and ucaskuma

The prose tale — uwepeker in Saru/Chitose, tuytak in most of eastern
Hokkaido — uses no verse, no melody, and no sakehe. Length is intermediate. The narrative
range runs from near-conversational to lightly elevated prose; corpus token counts give
uwepeker 104 tokens and tuytak 24 tokens
in the aligned Hokkaido corpus (the counts conflate metalinguistic and performance
contexts).

endo2022 identifies four sub-genres within the prose tradition:

Human prose tales (the default): an unnamed human protagonist narrates
in the first person within a 'real event' frame, typically with a reward-virtue /
punish-vice arc.

Kamuy prose tales ( kamuy uwepeker ): a deity
narrates; the register leans toward elegant language
( atomte itak ), paralleling kamuy yukar in prose form.

Japanese-borrowed prose tales : Ainu-ized versions of Japanese story
types, told in the third person.

Pananpe–Penanpe tales : the dyadic pair 'downstream-dweller' /
'upstream-dweller', opening with the formula
pananpe an penanpe an hine … 'there were Panampe and Penampe,
and …', told in the third person and recognized as a children's genre.

The standard human prose tale is bracketed by formulaic structure: an opening that
establishes the protagonist's family and lifestyle ("I had father, mother, elder brother;
father hunted bear and deer; we lacked nothing…") and a closing identification formula in
which the first-person narrator discloses its own identity via the reportative quotative
frame sekor … isoytak / ye ('so X said /
told'), revealing retrospectively who the "I" was throughout endo2022 . This
closure connects to the broader system of narrative reference tracking treated in
reference-tracking .

Ucaskuma / upaskuma designates a looser
genre of oral tradition, legend, and historical account; it imposes no fixed verse or
performance constraints and covers knowledge about places, events, and customs passed down
across generations endo2022 .

The intonational profile of prose narration differs observably from conversation.
tamura1996 reports that uwepeker narration uses a one-directional, low-variation
melodic line, contrasting with the rising contour of conversation that seeks a response;
within the Saru basin, upper-Saru (mid-river) narration tends toward near-monotone, while
lower-Saru shows more pitch undulation (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-16;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›. The full TAM and evidential patterning
by genre is in narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Genre-conditioned grammar: person marking and number in sung epic

All three principal narrative genres are told in the first person from the protagonist's
viewpoint, but the person prefix used for that first person is not the everyday
ku= . In Saru and Chitose both verse and prose narrative, the
protagonist is indexed by the fourth-person prefix/suffix
a= / =an — the form that in conversational
Ainu marks an indefinite or inclusive-plural subject or, with a second-person referent, a
deferential addressee sato2008 260–261 ; endo2022 ;
bugaeva2008 . The full paradigm and typological analysis of this device are
in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm , fourth-person-honorific , and
person-marking-architecture ; the present section notes only the
genre-conditioned contrasts.

sato2008 259–261 names the use of non-everyday person marking in oral
literature the "indirection principle" ( 間接性の原理 ): on his hypothesis, the
narrative was originally framed as a god or spirit speaking to a human inside a dream, and
the non-everyday first-person signals the taboo-laden indirectness of that divine address.
The competing "shamaness-oracle-origin" account holds that the form arose from a ritual
medium's stance sato2008 259–260 ‹contested›. The relationship between
this narrator-perspective device and typological logophoricity is analysed by
nikitina2021 , who argue that logophoric speech constructions in Ainu are
direct — not indirect — reported speech; the consequences for the genre-narration system
are pursued in logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Region and genre interact on the person marking. In Iburi (Horobetsu) kamuy yukar — the
tradition documented in chiri1923 — the hero-narrator consistently uses the
exclusive first-person plural forms ci= /
=as as a singular narrator throughout each text
sato2008 261 . In Chitose, ci= /
=as may appear only at the narrative opening, with the body
of the text reverting to a= / =an
sato2008 261 . The hero-person choice is thus both genre-conditioned (verse
vs prose may condition the choice in Chitose) and region-conditioned (Iburi vs Saru/Chitose
use different forms throughout).

A second genre-conditioned morphological feature is the number-agreement override. In
everyday Saru/Chitose, the suffix =an never co-occurs with a
singular verb root: one says k=ek 'I come.SG' in conversation, and
=an appears only with the suppletive plural
( arki=an 'we come.PL'). In the elegant register of sung epic, the
combination is grammatical:

ek=an | come=4.S | I came. (hero's voice, singular) | In elegant narrative (atomte itak), the fourth-person suffix =an combines with the singular verb root ek 'come.SG'. In conversational Saru/Chitose this combination is ungrammatical; the everyday first-person singular would be k=ek 'I come'. The singular + 4.S combination is a diagnostic of the elegant register (Satō 2008: 263). | SAR

The broader elegant-register morphology (applicative case-marking by prefixes
e- / o- / ko-
rather than particles, more polysynthetic words, genre-specific lexicon) is covered in
poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

Verse structure: the two metres

Verse in Hokkaido Ainu is not organized by rhyme. kindaichi1931 noted the
absence of systematic rhyme; okuda2022 , following Philippi (1979), confirms
that repetition in Ainu poetry largely reflects the small vowel inventory and widespread
reduplication rather than a prosodic constraint ‹contested›. Against this consensus,
Tangiku (2020) argues that an 18th-century yukar text
( ルウェサニウンクㇽ ) uses deliberate head-rhyme, end-rhyme, and
imperfect rhyme — a finding that, if sustained, would require revising the standard
account (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-13; aynucorporadiscord )
‹contested›.

okuda2022 argues that Hokkaido Ainu verse deploys two metrically distinct
systems, genre-differentiated. The first, syllable-count orientation ,
targets five syllables per line as basic (four-to-seven is the attested range), as
described by kindaichi1931 165 and tamura1973 56 ;
it is characteristic of lyric songs ( yaysama ). The second,
accent orientation , places the first pitch accent in a fixed rhythmic
slot (the first disyllabic foot) and admits four-syllable lines when the accent is
line-initial; it characterizes divine epics ( kamuy yukar ). Heroic
epics ( yukar ) use both systems depending on the musical rhythm of
the performance okuda2022 ‹contested›. Okuda reports no other language in
which one reciter alternates between two distinct metrical systems — a typological claim
that rests on an argument from the present state of the literature rather than exhaustive
survey ‹speculative›.

Techniques for achieving the five-syllable target in syllable-count verse include: the
insertion of the light verb ki do between a verb and a following element,
uncontracted person prefixes, a prefixed initial u- , and retention
of vowels that elide in conversational speech sato2008 264 ;
okuda2022 . These techniques are also poetic-register diagnostics; they are
analysed as part of the couplet and formulaic system in
parallelism-couplets-and-word-pairs .

Competing taxonomies and contested genre boundaries

Each major scholar of Ainu oral literature has organized the genre system around different
primary criteria. kindaichi1933 and Kubodera classified the corpus primarily
by protagonist type (human vs divine vs culture-hero). Chiri Mashiho
( chiri1955 ) supplied the dialect-distribution data that revealed the naming
mismatches across regions. tamura1996 showed that the Saru category of
oyna is absent from at least one primary narrator's repertoire.
nakagawa2001b established the four-function classification adopted by
endo2022 and followed here. These schemes are not mutually falsifying — they
weight different parameters — but they produce different genre maps and different
descriptions of what counts as a variant of what.

At the level of individual texts, genre boundaries can be indeterminate. A kamuy yukar
from the Shizunai collection narrated by 織田ステノ, titled "怠け者の子供" ('The lazy
child'), carries a long sakehe ( manayta sanke tar panke tar
kocupu ) but is shorter than typical kamuy yukar and concludes as a
yayeisoytak (first-person self-narration) by a single human boy
rather than a divine protagonist. Having melody rules out classification as prose, yet the
text fits neither standard kamuy yukar (wrong protagonist type) nor yukar (carries a
sakehe) (gengojiro, aynu-corpora Discord 2025-12-12; aynucorporadiscord )
‹contested›. Such cases show that the sakehe-vs-no-sakehe and divine-vs-human-protagonist
parameters do not always co-vary as the taxonomy predicts.

The elegant-vs-everyday register distinction (see poetic-archaic-elevated-register )
raises a related question. kindaichi1935 381–382 ranked the density of
elegant-language features across genres from least to most: greeting < prayer <
incantation < caranke < yukar, treating the gradation as
qualitative differences. Nakagawa (2001a[1987]: 222, cited via endo2022 )
argues instead that elegant and everyday language are points on a continuum keyed to
verse/prose and fixedness/improvisation, and proposes further that a genre-phrase
classification (yukar-phrase vs prayer-phrase) may be more analytically effective than the
elegant/everyday binary ‹contested›. That proposal predicts that the register differences
between yukar and prayer, though real, are not of the same kind as the difference between
elegant and everyday language generally; the evidential and TAM consequences of this
distinction are tracked in narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

The Ijäs pedagogical grammar ( ijas2023 ) does not present a systematic genre
taxonomy and is focused on spoken, conversational Ainu rather than the verse tradition.
Where vocabulary or constructions associated with the oral-literature register appear in
that work, the present chapter routes the reader to the philological and descriptive
literature treated here.

title: Orthographic Standardization and the Word-Division Problem
summary: The competing romanization norms, the unresolved question of where word boundaries fall, and the spacing and hyphenation conventions this grammar adopts.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Orthographic Standardization and the Word-Division Problem
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: The competing romanization norms, the unresolved question of where word boundaries fall, and the spacing and hyphenation conventions this grammar adopts.

The two writing systems

Two writing systems serve Hokkaido Ainu in current practice. The Latin phonemic romanization is
the primary medium of academic grammars, dictionaries, and text editions; the katakana syllabary,
extended with small-kana coda letters to accommodate Ainu's closed syllables, is the standard of
classroom instruction, signage, and revitalization materials. Neither rests on an officially
imposed norm. What exists is a convergence of practice: academic romanizations have stabilized
around a shared phonemic base since the 1970s, and the Hokkaidō Utari Kyōkai's pedagogical
series Akor Itak utari1994 established the katakana norm for the modern
teaching tradition. The phoneme-to-grapheme details of the romanization are documented in
latin-phonemic-transcription , and the katakana mapping — including Unicode coverage
of the small-kana coda letters — in katakana-and-small-kana-codas . For the Sakhalin
tradition and its Cyrillic transcription, which lies outside the Hokkaido scope of this grammar,
see cyrillic-and-multiscript-rendering .

The present chapter addresses three questions those chapters leave aside: how the current
academic romanization came to prevail over its predecessors; what principles govern the boundary
between an inflectional person marker, a derivational affix, and an independent word in the written
form; and which conventions this grammar adopts where the literature is still divided. The
institutional context of standardization and revitalization is taken up in
sociolinguistic-situation-revitalization .

From early romanizations to the modern standard

The oldest systematic romanizations of Hokkaido Ainu were phonetically oriented rather than
phonemically principled. Batchelor's dictionaries batchelor1905 ;
batchelor1938 and the grammar of Kindaichi and Chiri kindaichi1936
used digraphs — ch , sh — to capture what were perceived as palatalized
realizations before front vowels, and variant spellings of the same phoneme appear across
sources because stop allophones and their voiced intervocalic counterparts were sometimes
represented as separate letters. Batchelor's system, in particular, drew heavily on English
letter values, leading to notations difficult to reconcile with later phonemic analysis
shibatani1990 §2 .

The shift to a phonemic approach was gradual. Chiri Yukie's 1923 collection of divine epics
chiri1923 is an early instance of romanization by an Ainu speaker, made possible
by her training at Batchelor's mission school in Hakodate, where she received instruction in
English and the Latin alphabet aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024–11–17). Through
the postwar period, fieldwork-based descriptions produced romanizations that moved progressively
toward phonemic consistency. Tamura's Saru dictionary tamura1996 and the grammars
that followed — Refsing on Shizunai refsing1986 , Satō's foundational description
sato2008 , Bugaeva on southern Hokkaido bugaeva2012 , and Nakagawa's
extensive grammar nakagawa2024 — share the same twelve-consonant, five-vowel
phonemic framework: /p t k c s m n r w y h/ and /a i u e o/, each with a single Roman-letter
correspondent.

The pivotal choices within this shared framework concern the affricate and the sibilant. The
modern standard writes c for the affricate /c/ and s for the sibilant /s/,
abandoning the digraphs ch / sh , the diacriticized forms č / š , and
the phonetically motivated ts . Usage of ch / sh now survives mainly in
reprints of pre-standardization materials and in writing by authors outside the academic Ainu
linguistics community aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023–12–08) ‹consensus›.
Community and native-speaker orthographic practices across the twentieth century, including the
tension between academic romanization and community-developed writing styles, are surveyed in
kirikae1997 and nakagawa2006 . Ijäs's online pedagogical grammar
ijas2023 represents the most accessible English-medium resource for learners, and
it applies the modern romanization throughout. The broader revitalization context — the role of
the FRPAC, Upopoy, and language-teaching infrastructure in norm diffusion — is documented by
ono2022 .

The = sign for person markers: an orthographic device, not a clitic analysis

Among the innovations of the modern romanization, the most consequential is the use of two
distinct boundary markers: the hyphen - for derivational prefixes and suffixes,
and the equals sign = for the inflectional person markers. The practice is not
universal: it dominates the Nakagawa-lineage descriptive and pedagogical literature, while
Satō consistently writes the person markers with hyphens ( ku-kor wa
k-ek 'I bring it') sato2008 , as does Ijäs ijas2023 ; Hattori's
dialect dictionary and much of Tamura's work print the inflected forms solid
( kumokor , kuytak ); and Kirikae separates the
person marker as an independent word ( ku mokor ). Nakagawa surveys
these four systems and their trade-offs himself nakagawa2024 58–60 .

Nakagawa traces the = notation's origin to his own data-management practice in the early
1980s: working on Ainu texts, he used hyphens for derivational morphemes such as
yay- 'self/reflexive' and -re 'causative' and
equals signs for person markers such as ku=
'1 sg transitive subject' and =an
'fourth-person intransitive subject'. The notation entered print in the orthographic
principles section of the 1986 publication 語りの中の生活誌 , from
where it spread to other researchers and teaching materials
nakagawa2024 58–60 ; aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024–11–09)
‹consensus›. Crucially, Nakagawa states in so many words that this use of = is
unrelated to the general-linguistic convention whereby the equals sign marks a
clitic boundary ( 接辞・接語の表示慣習とは無関係である ), and he
concedes that Satō's hyphen notation conforms better to standard practice; he retains
= for learnability — it keeps inflectional person marking visually distinct
from derivation and lets a learner recover the dictionary headword
( ku=itak → itak )
nakagawa2024 58–60 ‹consensus›.

The distinction the notation encodes is morphological: person affixes are inflectional —
every finite predication indexes its arguments through them — while derivational affixes change
the verb's argument structure or create lexical categories. A verb form carrying both types
displays both boundary markers simultaneously:

ku=yay-e | 1SG.A=REFL-eat | I eat (a meal).

The person marker ku= marks the first-person transitive agent; the
derivational prefix yay- is a reflexive, here producing the idiomatic
reading 'eat a meal'. The = carries no claim that the person markers are
clitics in the Leipzig-glossing sense: their morphological status is mixed and contested —
=an shows genuinely clitic-like separability, while the prefixal
markers pattern with ordinary affixes in accent and syllabification. That question, with
Nakagawa's and Sakaguchi's diagnostics, is treated in
personal-affix-template-and-ordering boundary-status ; the reflexive
yay- in reflexive-yay .

Satō's grammar and Ijäs's course — like older texts — use hyphens for all bound morphemes,
leaving the inflectional–derivational distinction unmarked in the written form. The
Akor Itak textbook and related classroom materials apply spacing conventions
designed for readability that do not always track the = / - distinction. The present grammar
uses = throughout for person markers and - for derivational morphemes, for orthographic
continuity with the now-dominant convention in Hokkaidō materials — not as an analysis of
the markers as clitics; examples quoted from hyphen-notation sources (Satō, Ijäs, Refsing)
are renotated accordingly. The gloss line follows the same boundary symbols, so the morphemic
form and its gloss align symbol-for-symbol. The full gloss abbreviation list is given in
glossing-abbreviations-and-citation .

Word spacing and compound boundaries

The most debated segmentation level is the space: where does one orthographic word end and the
next begin? Hokkaido Ainu morphology offers no fully decisive test, since the language's
polysynthetic character allows a verb complex with incorporated noun, multiple derivational
layers, and person markers to be parsed, in principle, as one word or as several.

A case where the written boundary carries semantic weight is the pair
sisam itak and sisamitak . Written as two words,
sisam itak is a phrase meaning 'the language of the Wajin (Japanese
person)'; written solid, sisamitak is a lexicalized compound that
functions as the proper name for the Japanese language. The accent pattern reinforces the
distinction: the phrase carries independent accentual domains on each member, while the
compound exhibits a unified domain. Writing the compound solid also avoids the misreading
si.sa.mi.tak that the concatenated form can produce and aids morphological processing
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024–11–05; 2024–12–20) ‹contested›. The compound
phonology is analyzed in accent-in-compounds-and-affixation and the morphology of
noun-noun compounding in noun-noun-compounding .

A related area of variation concerns particles and light verbs in aspectual and possessive
constructions. In the construction kor an 'be in a state of having',
kor and the existential an are standardly
written as two separate words. In the possessive complex ku=kor cise
'my house', the person marker is written attached to the verb kor by
the = sign (Satō's notation renders the same complex as ku-kor cise )
sato2008 85–90 ; nakagawa2024 . Both authors apply their respective
notations consistently without making the underlying analysis fully explicit; the relevant morphosyntactic
analysis is taken up in concept-form-affiliative-form . A further dimension concerns
the middle-dot sometimes found in the romanization of the language name
( Aynu·itak ): this derives from a 2007 Japanese Wikipedia entry that used the dot as
a reading aid for Japanese readers, not as an Ainu orthographic feature, and its subsequent
spread into digital platforms gave it the appearance of a convention it is not
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024–11–05).

Contested notational choices

Nasal neutralization before labials

Before the labial obstruents /p/ and /m/, the contrast between underlying /n/ and /m/ is
neutralized: both surface as [m] in connected speech. Published descriptions handle this
differently. Tamura's practice is etymological: where the underlying morpheme is recoverable
from alternations, the historical form is written; where it cannot be determined, she used
a macron-n ( n̄ ) as a neutral symbol
tamura1996 . Nakagawa and Satō write the morphologically determined form —
m or n as the morphemic composition
warrants — rather than introducing a separate neutral symbol
nakagawa2024 ; sato2008 . The community archive records further
variation: some writers use consistent surface m , others
Japanese-influenced surface n , and others the etymological split;
the assimilation of the nasal in loanwords like
kampi 'paper' (← Japanese 紙 kaNbi )
presents a particularly intricate case where different analytical stances yield different
spellings aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024–03–23; 2024–11–16) ‹contested›.
The phonological rule is analyzed in coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi .
This grammar follows the etymological-morphological convention: kamuy spirit, deity
is written with m because the morpheme is underlyingly labial-nasal,
while a form whose underlying nasal is n and which happens to precede
a labial in a complex is written with n where alternation evidence
supports the underlying form.

Glide notation in hiatus

A second unsettled area is the treatment of glides between adjacent vowels. Tamura's
romanization — and the practice of many scholars working on Saru — inserts an explicit
w or y between a vowel-final and a vowel-initial element: hence
uwepeker (not uepeker ) and
iyomante (not iomante )
tamura1996 . Nakagawa's more recent practice suppresses the inserted segment on
the grounds that the [w] and [j] realizations in these positions are phonologically
predictable and need not be marked orthographically; analogous hiatus-bridging glides are
left unwritten in the romanization of many other languages
nakagawa2024 ; aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024–11–26) ‹contested›.
Whether the glide is phonemically present or allophonic is a separate question, addressed
in glides-w-y . This grammar adopts the simplified notation:
uepeker , iomante , and related forms appear
without the inserted glide.

The glottal stop

Earlier articles by Tamura, written in the 1950s and 1960s, marked the glottal onset on
vowel-initial words with a preceding apostrophe: 'a , 'i , and so on. By the
1980s this notation had been almost universally abandoned for Hokkaido Ainu. Modern grammars
— including Nakagawa nakagawa2024 and Satō sato2008 — do not mark
the glottal onset in the standard romanization; the typographic overhead was regarded as
disproportionate to its analytic contribution, and the notation was inconsistently applied
in the very sources that introduced it aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli,
2023–12–08) ‹consensus›. The phonological status of word-initial glottalization —
whether a phonemic glottal stop is present, absent, or conditioned by prosodic environment —
is discussed in glottal-stop .

Conventions adopted in this grammar

The following table summarizes the decisions made above, drawn together for ready reference.
The entries cover features where published sources diverge and where a reader moving between
this grammar and other descriptions may encounter different practice.

Feature
Convention in this grammar
Alternatives in the literature

Katakana (pedagogical and revitalization materials)

Affricate /c/
c

ch (Batchelor, pre-standardization); č ; ts

Sibilant /s/
s
sh (pre-standardization)

Person-affix boundary

i- )

Etymological n or m per morpheme

(Japanese-influenced); macron-n n̄ (Tamura's indeterminate cases)

language'); spaced for syntactic phrases (e.g. sisam itak
'the Wajin's language')
