For the katakana correspondence and the Unicode encoding of the small-kana coda letters
(including the semi-voiced pu coda), see katakana-and-small-kana-codas .
For the complete gloss abbreviation list and the interlinear-example format, see
glossing-abbreviations-and-citation . Person-affix allomorphy and connected-speech
reduction affecting the written forms are treated in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction .

title: Parallelism, Couplets, and Word-Pair Doublets
summary: Syntactic-semantic parallelism, the verse couplet, and lexical doublets analyzed as structural rhetoric rather than mere style.
part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Parallelism, Couplets, and Word-Pair Doublets
Part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
Summary: Syntactic-semantic parallelism, the verse couplet, and lexical doublets analyzed as structural rhetoric rather than mere style.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu verse genres — the heroic epic ( yukar /
sakorpe ), the divine epic ( kamuy yukar /
oyna ), and ritual speech (prayer, caranke )
— are performed in an elevated register, atomte itak 'decorated
language', distinct from everyday prose speech ( yayan itak )
endo2022 §3 ; kindaichi1935 . The primary rhetorical
device of this register is parallelism: two or more consecutive verse lines share the
same syntactic frame while one or more lexical positions are filled by semantically
related but distinct words endo2022 §4 ; sato2008 264 ;
tamura1996 xv–xvii . Phonological matching plays no constitutive role:
"for lines to match or rhyme is not required" endo2022 §4 . The device
is syntactic and lexical, not phonological.

Parallelism in Ainu verse is described in the Japanese scholarship as 対句
( taigu ) and treated alongside formulaic phrasing and periphrasis as the core
of the poetic apparatus sato2008 264 ; endo2022 §4 . A
minimal pair of parallel lines — a couplet — is the basic unit. The device is "pervasive
in divine and heroic epics" endo2022 §4 and extends into prayer and
incantation; it is absent from everyday prose ( uwepeker folktale,
conversation). For the verse genres and their grammatical signatures, see
oral-literature-genre-taxonomy ; for verse metre, the sakehe refrain, and
sung-verse structure, see sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure ; for
the full elevated-register morphological and lexical cluster, see
poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

The couplet: structure and attested examples

The canonical couplet pairs two lines that are syntactically identical or near-identical,
with one lexical position — typically a modifier or head noun — replaced by a
semantically contrasted or associated word. The simplest attested type fills the
modifier slot with a spatial antonym pair:

hanke nispa | near chief | hanke nispa | the near chief | First line of an attested couplet (Endō 2022: ch.10 §4, ex.18). The parallel second line substitutes tuyma 'far' for hanke 'near' while the head noun nispa 'chief, lord' is constant. | endo2022 | HK

tuyma nispa | far chief | tuyma nispa | the far chief | Second line. The pair hanke 'near' // tuyma 'far' is itself a fixed word-pair; together the two lines designate the full social field — all chiefs, proximal and distal. | endo2022 | HK

The same antonymic pair extends to a larger syntactic frame with incorporated nouns and
causative morphology. Endō 2022: ch.10 §4, ex.19 gives:
hanke kotan sonko-kama-re / tuyma kotan e-sonko-ciw-re
— glossable as 'send word to the near village / drive word into the far village'. Here
both the spatial modifier ( hanke vs tuyma )
and the verb differ, while the nominal skeleton ( ___ kotan
'___ village') and the incorporated noun sonko word, message are
constant endo2022 §4 .

A couplet may extend to a three-line set (tricola) in which the first two parallel lines
lead into a shared predicate. A Chitose text gives:

tu imeru kur | two shining person | tu imeru kur | two shining ones | First line of a tricola (Nakagawa 1995: 45; cited in Endō 2022: ch.10 §4, ex.16). Describes embroidery beauty. The second parallel line replaces tu 'two' with re 'three'; the third line supplies the predicate kotuytuyke 'crossed over'. | nakagawa1995:45 | CHI

re imeru kur | three shining person | re imeru kur | three shining ones | Second line. With tu // re functioning as a 'many' doublet rather than an exact count, the pair together means 'many shining ones'. A third line, kotuytuyke 'crossed over', closes the verse unit. | nakagawa1995:45 | CHI

A carving couplet attested in Endō 2022: ch.10 §4, ex.17 shows synonymic elaboration
rather than antonymy: tomika nuye / ikorka nuye 'carve the
sword-sheath / carve the treasure-sword-sheath'. The verb nuye carve is
constant; tomika sword-sheath and ikorka treasure-sword-sheath
name the same type of decorated sheath at a plain and an elaborated grade
endo2022 §4 .

Types of parallelism

The sources endo2022 §4 and sato2008 264 document
the following structural types, which may co-occur within a single couplet:

Antonymic parallelism pairs words that occupy opposite poles of a
semantic domain — spatial ( hanke near // tuyma far ),
quantitative, or social. The two lines collectively cover the full extent of the
relevant domain.

Synonymic / elaborating parallelism pairs a plain term with a more
specific or more elevated one referring to the same referent
( tomika // ikorka ). The second member
typically adds a qualifier marking prestige or preciousness — a common escalation
pattern in heroic and divine verse.

Numeral parallelism deploys the tu two //
re three pair as an intensifying doublet meaning 'many' rather than
encoding an exact count (see § below ). This is the most
formulaic type and the most pervasive in the corpus.

Endō 2022 does not sort these into discrete sub-types, treating all three under the
single label 対句. The distinction between antonymic and synonymic types is analytical,
not a native speaker's metalinguistic category ‹contested›.

The tu // re formula: numeral parallelism as intensification

The numeral doublet tu // re ('two' //
'three') is a stock parallel pair that functions as a hyperbolic expression of
multiplicity rather than a precise count. Used across a couplet — as in Satō 2008:
264: otu maw-kur-puni kane / ore maw-kur-puni kane 'raising
two winds / raising three winds' — the pair means 'many winds', the adjacent small
numerals intensifying the predicate by iteration sato2008 264 ;
endo2022 §4 .

In that same example, Satō 2008 notes the archaic counting forms
otu and ore rather than the everyday
tu and re ; the archaic variants are
themselves markers of the elevated register sato2008 264 . The Chitose
tricola above ( tu imeru kur / re imeru kur ) uses the everyday
numeral forms, showing that the intensifying function of the doublet does not depend on
the archaic variants — it resides in the adjacent-numeral pattern itself.

The tu // re device belongs to the broader strategy of
hyperbole and escalation that Satō 2008: 264 identifies alongside periphrasis and
metaphor as the defining rhetorical inventory of atomte itak .

The conventional word-pair inventory

Both the individual lexical pairs and the templates in which they appear show
varying degrees of fixedness: some pairs are fully conventional and recur across
reciters and texts; others are the productive application of the parallelism template
to any semantically contrasted pair of words. The table below collects the pairs
directly attested in the primary sources read for this chapter
endo2022 §4 ; sato2008 264 ; nakagawa1995 45 ; tamura1996 xv–xvii .

Pair (A // B)
Gloss
Type
Function in verse
Source

hanke //
tuyma

near // far
spatial antonym
covers the full social/spatial field
endo2022 §4

tu //
re
(archaic otu // ore )

two // three
numeral doublet
hyperbolic 'many'; intensification by iteration

endo2022 §4 ; sato2008 264

tomika //
ikorka

sword-sheath // treasure-sword-sheath
synonymic elaboration
plain → elaborated escalation
endo2022 §4

tu imeru kur //
re imeru kur

two shining ones // three shining ones
numeral-head phrase (triplet)
hyperbolic beauty/abundance
nakagawa1995 45

The elegant-register kin terms ona father (elegant register) //
unu mother (elegant register) — contrasting with the colloquial
haca father (colloquial) / hapo mother (colloquial) —
constitute a lexical register pair rather than a verse-line couplet proper, but they
belong to the same broader phenomenon of paired synonymic substitution by which
atomte itak systematically replaces everyday vocabulary with
a distinct poetic word-stock endo2022 §3 ; aynucorporadiscord
(nukopoli, 2026, asserted ; corroborates Endō 2022: §3, characteristic 4).
The morphosyntax of kin terms in honorific contexts is treated in
kinship-honorific-possession .

Formulaic character and mnemonic function

Couplets in Ainu verse are typically also formulae : fixed word sequences learned
as units and redeployed across performances and across reciters, matching the available
metrical slot endo2022 §4 . The Hokkaido Ainu Center 2000 documentation
(via Endō 2022: §4) describes these phrases as "learned in bulk" — a reciter acquires a
stock of ready-made pairs and deploys whichever fits the narrative moment. An illustrative
stock phrase is tuk-no pinni si-kopa-yar / aynu pito 'a fine
person like a grown Manchurian-ash tree' (featuring the poetic similative
si-kopa-yar , absent from everyday speech), which serves as a
portable hero-praise formula endo2022 §4 .

The formulaic character of couplets serves at least three functions in performance.
First, mnemonic: in yukar performances lasting several hours or multiple nights (see
heroic-and-divine-verse ), prefabricated phrase-pairs reduce the cognitive
load of real-time composition, allowing the reciter to reproduce long texts with
exactitude. Second, intensifying: repetition of a semantic content across two adjacent
syntactic slots (with controlled lexical variation) constitutes verse-internal emphasis;
the tu // re doublet works by doubling the iteration rather than
by selecting a single emphatic word. Third, cohesive: paired lines frame episodes and
signal transitions within the narrative, binding adjacent performance segments through
structural symmetry endo2022 §4 ; sato2008 264 .

Nakagawa (via endo2022 §3 ) identifies couplets as one of the sub-devices
enabling "one phrase → multi-phrase expansion": a single semantic content is projected
across two lines, satisfying the metre. The syllable-count and accent-orientation
metrical constraints described in sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure
motivate this expansion directly — the choice between the tu
and re variants, or between a short and an elaborated form, can
resolve a metrical insufficiency ‹corpus-suggested›.

Register conditioning and co-occurring grammatical features

Parallelism and couplets are confined to atomte itak and are
absent from prose folktale ( uwepeker ) and everyday conversation.
The register is not, however, a discrete switch: Nakagawa 2001a[1987]: 222 (via
endo2022 §3 ) treats elegant and everyday language as points on a
continuum defined by the degree of verse vs prose and of fixed vs improvised delivery;
couplet density tracks this continuum, reaching its maximum in heroic epic ‹contested›.
Kindaichi's hierarchy — greeting < prayer < incantation <
caranke < yukar, each richer in elegant language than the
last ( kindaichi1935 , via endo2022 §3 ) — implies a
comparable gradient of couplet density, but this has not been quantified on a
genre-tagged corpus ‹speculative›.

Parallelism co-occurs with the other grammatical hallmarks of atomte
itak : applicative case-marking by prefixes e-/o-/ko- in place
of case particles, longer polysynthetic words, number-agreement override (the singular
verb with -an for the 1SG hero-narrator), and a distinct elevated
lexicon sato2008 263–264 ; endo2022 §3.4 . The couplet is
thus one component of a co-occurring cluster, not an isolated device. The full cluster is
treated in poetic-archaic-elevated-register ; the number-agreement override
belongs to person-marking-architecture .

Within verse narration, couplets typically accompany the fourth-person
( a=/-an ) hero-narrator marking that indexes the logophoric
first person. The interaction of this narrative convention with reported-speech structure
is described in logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse and
fourth-person-honorific . The evidential frame ruwe ne ,
which predicates narrative events as established facts (see ruwe-ne-inferential
and evidential-schema-overview ), is the unmarked clause-final frame in
verse narration and thus the primary syntactic environment in which couplets appear;
the sentence-final particle inventory of verse is treated in
sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force .

Typological note

Paired-line organization of the type found in Ainu verse — a fixed syntactic frame
with one or more varying lexical slots, without phonological matching as a constitutive
requirement — is documented in many oral-poetic traditions worldwide. Whether the
Hokkaido Ainu pattern shares an origin with neighboring North Pacific verse traditions
or developed independently is an open question: no source read for this grammar
addresses the diachrony or areality of the couplet device directly, and the primary
comparative literature on verse parallelism lies outside the sources available here
‹speculative›. The absence of a phonological requirement, confirmed for Hokkaido Ainu
by endo2022 §4 and consistent with the contested evidence on rhyme and
alliteration reviewed in okuda2022 §2.2 , is in itself typologically
diagnostic and merits future systematic comparison.

title: The Perfective/Anterior Particle a (a, a…a, aan)
summary: Postverbal a marks completion and anteriority ('already, have V-ed'), with reduplicated a…a and the anterior-stative aan.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Perfective/Anterior Particle a (a, a…a, aan)
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: Postverbal a marks completion and anteriority ('already, have V-ed'), with reduplicated a…a and the anterior-stative aan.

Form, etymology, and the number alternation

Two auxiliaries of anterior or perfective value form a closely related pair in the
Hokkaido Ainu auxiliary system: a sit (SG) (singular) and
rok sit (PL) (plural). Both are grammaticalized forms of the suppletive
intransitive verb pair a / rok 'sit (SG/PL)',
and they retain the number-sensitivity of that source pair: a appears
when the clause subject is singular, rok when it is plural
nakagawa2024 307 ; yoshikawa2022 ; bugaeva2012 493 .
Ijäs's Lesson 9 catalogues the a / rok
suppletive pair as a morphological special form alongside ek /
arki 'come' and an /
oka 'exist', without connecting the pair directly to the
grammaticalized auxiliary ijas2023 . The suppletive pair and its role in verbal
number are treated in suppletive-verbal-number .

Structurally, a and rok belong to the
auxiliary-verb frame: the shape is V AUX , with no conjunctive
particle between the lexical verb and the auxiliary. Person marking falls on the preceding
lexical verb; the auxiliary carries none. This single-indexed structure sets the
construction apart from the pair-verb frames ( kor an ,
wa an ), where each predicate bears its own person index because
each constitutes a finite clause in its own right
nakagawa2024 306, 439–443 ; bugaeva2012 492–495 . The
overall periphrastic architecture is set out in tam-overview ; the
existential-aspect pair verbs in existential-aspect-an-oka .

tane pakno a=e=tumam wa hotke=an a korka | now until 4.A=2SG.O=embrace CONJ lie.down=4.S PFV although | Until now I had held you in my arms and lain down with you, but — | Person marking (4.A=, =4.S) falls on the lexical verbs tumam 'embrace' and hotke 'lie down'; the auxiliary a carries no person index. The contrast particle korka signals that this clause supplies the background to a subsequent reversal — a discourse environment typical of a. | nakagawa2024:305 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 448

When the subject is plural, rok replaces
a . The plural verb stem and the plural auxiliary track the same
subject-number value independently:

arki=an rok | come.PL=4.S PFV.PL | They came. | rok replaces a when the subject is plural; the suppletive plural verb stem arki simultaneously replaces singular ek. The fourth-person suffix =an indexes the subject on the lexical verb; rok carries no person marking. Compare the singular ek=an a 'she came'.

Five competing analyses of a

What a marks is genuinely disputed, and the five positions in
circulation represent substantive rather than merely terminological disagreements
nakagawa2024 306–307 ; bugaeva2012 493 ;
yoshikawa2022 ‹contested›.

position
holder(s)
core claim
grade

Perfective / past

Kindaichi 1931 via bugaeva2012 493 ;
tamura2000 (gloss past )

a marks a completed or past event; the language defaults to
a past-tense frame

‹contested›

Perfect (kanryō)

chiri1942 157 via bugaeva2012 493

a marks a past event whose result is relevant at the current
moment, or possibly simply past

‹contested›

Actional perfect

bugaeva2012 493 ; sato2006 63

a is an action-predicate perfect; it cannot co-occur with
negation, and the stative / resultative perfect belongs to
wa an

‹contested›

Background-event contrast
Yoshikawa 2015 via nakagawa2024 306

a marks the event that forms the contrasted background to a
subsequent event; its function is discourse-structural rather than strictly
temporal or aspectual

‹contested›

Perfective + direct evidential
yoshikawa2022

a combines perfective aspect with direct evidentiality
(witnessed reminiscence), paralleling Classical Japanese
き ; the "exceptional a " on
time-invariant constants is reanalysed as a certainty marker anchored to the
speaker's direct knowledge

‹contested›

The perfective / past and perfect readings (positions 1 and 2) capture the most frequent
translation equivalence. Their limitation surfaces in what Yoshikawa calls "exceptional
a ": time-invariant constants and self-evident truths on which
a appears but a past reading is unmotivated, pointing toward a
non-temporal account yoshikawa2022 .

The actional-perfect restriction (position 3) carries the sharpest distributional claim.
Satō argues that a marks a completed action as still affecting the
current situation, and negating the action removes that link; hence
a cannot scope over a negated predicate. The stative or resultative
perfect — 'V-ed (and is still in that state)' — is expressed by
wa an instead
sato2006 63 ; bugaeva2012 493 . The stative-resultative
frame is treated in existential-aspect-an-oka .

The background-event analysis (position 4), following Yoshikawa 2015, draws on discourse
distribution. Clause-final a recurrently precedes a reversal
connector — korka 'but/although', sequential
wa , or the nominalizer p in an adversative
frame — marking the preceding event as contrasted background. Nakagawa takes this as
evidence that a is primarily discourse-structural, and any past-tense
reading a contextual by-product nakagawa2024 306–307 ‹contested›.

Yoshikawa's 2022 Handbook analysis (position 5) integrates the distributional and discourse
observations: a combines perfective aspect with direct evidentiality
and witnessed-reminiscence modal value. The "exceptional a " on
time-invariant truths is reanalysed as direct evidentiality marking the speaker's certain
knowledge. Yoshikawa explicitly acknowledges that the analysis rests heavily on
fourth-person narrative data, with few third-person-subject examples, and he marks the
evidential dimension as warranting further investigation yoshikawa2022 ‹contested›.

Positions 4 and 5 are partly complementary: the background-event account is a
synchronic-discourse claim about the typical environments of a ,
while the direct-evidential account is a semantic one about its grammatical value. An event
marked as the speaker's directly witnessed background is precisely the configuration that
both accounts predict nakagawa2024 306–307 ; yoshikawa2022 ‹speculative›.

Scene-shift in narrative

Cutting across the semantic debate, there is broad agreement on
a 's function as a narrative scene-shift signal. The pattern
[event] a p (perfective + nominalizer) or
[event] a korka recurrently marks the point at which the scene,
topic, or acting participant changes
yoshikawa2022 ; nakagawa2024 307 . The genre-level
distribution of a across narrative, ceremonial, and conversational
texts is treated in narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre ; the
temporal reference context in tenselessness-and-time-reference .

Iterative reduplication: the ~a ~a / ~rok ~rok pattern

The auxiliary may reduplicate to express an iterative or prolonged-continuity reading —
'went on V-ing, V-ed over and over'. The pattern repeats the verb-plus-auxiliary unit:
V a V a (singular) or V rok V rok (plural).
With a fourth-person subject, the person clitic may stand alone in the second occurrence
( =an a =an a ), abbreviating the repeated verb base
nakagawa2024 307–308 ; bugaeva2012 493 .

apkas a apkas a | walk PFV walk PFV | [she] walked on and on. | The verb-plus-auxiliary unit apkas a repeats to form the iterative; rok rok patterns identically with a plural subject. Nakagawa's attested form is onkami=an a =an a 'worshipped over and over' (Nakagawa 2024: 308, ex. 453), where =an abbreviates the repeated verb base in the second occurrence.

Bugaeva labels the pattern "emphatic continuative" bugaeva2012 493 . Ijäs
records an a … a pattern glossed 'verb and verb (prolonged
continuity)' but does not connect it to the auxiliary ijas2023 ; the connection
is established by Nakagawa's glossed corpus examples ‹corpus-confirmed›. The iterative
and habitual periphery shared with other aspect forms is treated in
phasal-iterative-habitual-aspect .

nisa: the recent-perfect companion

The auxiliary nisa just-finished; recent perfect marks an event as just
completed — closer to a terminative or hot-news perfect than the general anterior value
of a , and rendered in Japanese as '〜てしまった' with a freshness
nuance nakagawa2024 312, 436 ; bugaeva2012 493 . Like
a , nisa belongs to the auxiliary-verb
frame and takes no person marking; unlike a it shows no
number alternation. The two forms overlap in signalling a past event but differ in
aspectual profile: nisa foregrounds the just-completed endpoint,
while a in its primary use foregrounds the contrast with what
follows.

The admirative aan and rok(o)ka

Form, etymology, and dialect variants

Alongside a / rok stands the auxiliary
aan PRF.MIR (SG) (singular) and
rokoka PRF.MIR (PL) (plural), combining perfect aspect with indirect
evidentiality and a mirative (after-the-fact surprise) value. All major analyses
decompose the form as a (perfective auxiliary) +
an (existential 'exist')
nakagawa2024 308 ; yoshikawa2018 ; yoshikawa2022 .
The strongest morphological argument is the plural: rok-oka(y)
pairs the plural of a with the plural of
an , exactly as [a+an] predicts
yoshikawa2022 . Yoshikawa grants that several singular dialect forms admit
competing segmentations — anan (Chitose) invites
[an+an] , while haw'an /
awan (Shizunai, Tokachi, Horobetsu) invite
[haw+an] — so the unified etymology rests on the plural form
rather than on every singular surface shape
yoshikawa2022 ‹contested›.

dialect
SG form
PL form

Saru (SAR)
aan
rok(o)ka(y)

Chitose (CHI)
anan
rok(o)ka(y) anan (doubled; no meaning difference)

Shizunai (SHI)
haw'an
—

Horobetsu (HOR), Tokachi (TOK)
awan / hawan
awokay (HOR)

Sources: nakagawa2024 308 ; yoshikawa2022 ;
yoshikawa2018 .

Meaning: after-the-fact realization and indirect evidentiality

Nakagawa characterizes the core meaning as: at some reference point, the speaker
realizes what an earlier situation actually was — rendered in Japanese as
'〜だったのだなあ' ('so that's what it was!')
nakagawa2024 307–308 . Chiri's earlier formulation emphasizes the
affective dimension: surprise at an unexpected fact or result
chiri1942 158 . Yoshikawa integrates both under perfect aspect plus
indirect evidentiality, with mirativity as a contextual extension: the speaker
asserts a past event known only via inference or retrospective access, and the
unexpected discovery of that fact yields the surprise reading
yoshikawa2018 ; yoshikawa2022 .

oripak kamuy poho a=ne aan ru-we ne hine | noble deity child 4.A=COP PRF.MIR track-POSS COP CONJ | it turned out that I was the child of an honored deity, and … | The speaker discovers after the fact that she is the child of a deity; aan encodes indirect, retrospective access to this information. ruwe ne then packages the realized content as established fact. | biratori1969 | SAR | Hiraga Sadamo, ウエペケㇾ ウラユシウンクㇽ [biratori/010/003#421]

mokor=an aan ru-we ne akusu | sleep=4.S PRF.MIR track-POSS COP when | when it turned out that I had fallen asleep, | aan presents the sleep as discovered retrospectively; the following ruwe ne repackages the discovery as established knowledge. | biratori1969 | SAR | Kimura Kimi, ウエペケㇾ [biratori/021/004#82]

The Horobetsu dialect form awan carries the same value:

Penanpe kamuy poho ne awan | Penanpe deity child COP PRF.MIR | Penanpe was, surprisingly, the child of a deity. | The Horobetsu form awan carries the after-the-fact surprise (admirative) reading systematically noted by Chiri; the example is preserved in Yoshikawa 2018: 4. The competing segmentation [haw+an] is one of the etymological readings Yoshikawa discusses. | chiri1942:158; yoshikawa2018:4 | HOR

The a / aan opposition: aspect, evidentiality, and the Classical Japanese parallel

Yoshikawa's most productive contribution is treating a and
aan as a paradigmatic pair along two simultaneous parameters
yoshikawa2018 ; yoshikawa2022 :

auxiliary
aspect
evidentiality
extended value
Classical Japanese parallel

a / rok
perfective
direct (witnessed, personally recalled)
certainty, reminiscence

き
(direct-evidential past / witnessed reminiscence)

aan / rokoka
perfect
indirect (inferred, discovered after the fact)
mirativity (after-the-fact surprise)

けり
(indirect-evidential past; structurally
き + あり
= a + an )

The structural parallel between a+an and Classical Japanese
き+あり > けり provides an independent
argument for the [a+an] etymology: both pairs decompose the
indirect-past into a direct-past base plus an existential, and both the direct and
indirect members share a perfective component
yoshikawa2018 ; yoshikawa2022 . The same direct / indirect past
opposition is cross-linguistically attested and is placed in typological perspective by
bugaeva2012 493 ‹contested›.

The grammaticalization source a / rok 'sit'
fits a cross-linguistic cline in which posture verbs grammaticalize into aspect markers via
a resultant-state intermediate bybee1994 . The path 'sit (in the position
resulting from settling)' > 'be in a completed-state position' > anterior / perfective
auxiliary is typologically plausible ‹speculative›. The broader diachronic framing of the
Hokkaido TAM system is in tenselessness-and-time-reference .

aan stacked with ruwe ne

A frequent narrative pattern places aan immediately before the
inferential evidential ruwe ne : the speaker first signals an
after-the-fact discovery ( aan ), then packages that discovery as
established knowledge ( ruwe ne ). Hirosawa analyses the sequence as
a territory-of-information shift: aan places the content outside
the speaker's territory (the surprising, indirectly-accessed event), while
ruwe ne reclaims it inside as fact the speaker now owns
hirosawa2026 90 . Both corpus examples in the preceding section —
oripak kamuy poho a=ne aan ruwe ne and
mokor=an aan ruwe ne — show this stacking. The inferential
evidential is treated in ruwe-ne-inferential ; the system-level account of
evidential stacking and mirativity in mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

title: The Permissive/Sociative Causative -yar
summary: The indirect/permissive causative -yar 'let/have someone do' and the directive-vs-sociative split it forms with -re/-e/-te.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Permissive/Sociative Causative -yar
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The indirect/permissive causative -yar 'let/have someone do' and the directive-vs-sociative split it forms with -re/-e/-te.

Place in the causative system

Hokkaido Ainu derives causative verbs by two principal suffix types that differ in their
effect on the argument frame. The productive causative
-re/-te/-e , treated in causative-morphological-re-e-te ,
raises verb arity by one: a new causer takes the subject position and the original subject
is demoted to object. The suffix -yar , and its allomorph
-ar , introduces a causer as the new subject but suppresses the
causee entirely — the party that carries out the caused action is neither expressed as a
separate argument nor cross-referenced on the verb, so the net argument count of the derived
form equals that of its base nakagawa2024 201–202 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 ; kobayashi2015 12 .
Nakagawa's label for this type is 不定使役 'indefinite causative'
nakagawa2024 201 ; the Handbook chapter on verbal valency places it among
the valency-neutral alternations alongside the
reciprocal-u sociative uko-/uwe-
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 . The broader
valency-voice-overview arity calculus that frames all valency operations
is set out in the overview chapter; causee marking and double causatives are in
causee-marking-ditransitive-double-causative .

type
suffix
arity
causee
label

productive causative
-re / -te / -e
+1
expressed as direct object; cross-referenced by a person index
使役

indefinite causative
-yar / -ar
0
unexpressed; cannot be cross-referenced
不定使役

A causative suffix that leaves argument count unchanged is typologically unusual
nakagawa2024 201–202 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 .
Within the arity system the outcome is coherent: -yar encodes
a new causal event while the causee is structurally absorbed, in the same way that
noun incorporation satisfies an argument slot from inside the verb word without
expanding external valency nakagawa2024 201 .

Form and allomorphy

The suffix surfaces as -yar after a vowel or a glide and as
-ar after other consonants
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 ; nakagawa2024 202 .
The conditioning environment mirrors that of the productive causative, where
vowel- and glide-final stems take -re , most consonant-final
stems take -te , and r -final stems take
-e nakagawa2024 200–201 . The canonical
-yar form is nu hear (something) →
nu-yar 'make someone unspecified hear something, tell'. The
allomorph -ar would surface on consonant-final bases such as
a hypothetical sinot-ar 'have someone play'; the allomorphy is
stated in the Handbook but no consonant-final form is attested in the read corpus
sources bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Nakagawa's slot table places -yar/-ar in the causative suffix
position, after the stem and any forming material and before the suffixal person index
nakagawa2024 200, 210–211 . Any suffixal person index on the derived
form marks the new causer-subject, not the suppressed causee.

The unexpressed causee

The causee — the party who carries out the caused action — is structurally absent from
the derived verb: no person index cross-references it and no bare NP expresses it as a
core argument. Satō states that the suffix cannot license a separate causee NP;
Kobayashi's valency study describes the type as 不定使役 with the
causee role suppressed and valency unchanged
sato2008 234 ; kobayashi2015 12 . This separates
-yar from the productive causative, which expresses the causee
as a direct object, cross-references it with a person index, and produces a double-object
frame when the base is already transitive nakagawa2024 201–202 .

When a speaker wishes to indicate the implied original agent despite its exclusion from
the verb's argument frame, the postpositions or peka ,
or un , or eun supply a peripheral adjunct
slot. Nakagawa's Saru examples (exx. 961–963) include
nispa or peka a=korar 'marry off to a rich man', where
or peka specifies the intended recipient without making that
recipient a core verbal argument nakagawa2024 429 . The same option
appears in oral narrative, where a deity named in an eun phrase
receives the message conveyed by nu-yar while the verb itself
indexes no causee biratori1969 .

Readings: indefinite, permissive, and sociative

Three semantic characterisations appear in or near the literature, and the sources consulted
converge only on the structural valency facts while leaving the label question open
nakagawa2024 201–202 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1
‹contested›.

The indefinite-causation reading — the causer causes an event but the party
who performs it is unspecified ( 誰にでも 'to whoever') — is the
characterisation that Nakagawa and the Handbook share
nakagawa2024 201–202 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 .
It fits the most frequent attested form: nu-yar functions as the
regular verb for public announcement or reporting, a speech act whose point is precisely
that the recipient class is left open.

a=en=kopisi p anakne opitta ku=ye wa ku=nu-yar | 4.A=1SG.O=ask NMLZ TOP all 1SG.A=say CONJ 1SG.A=hear-CAUS | Everything I was asked I told in full — I made it all known. | ku=nu-yar retains the two-place transitivity of nu 'hear something'; the content made known is the sole object and no causee argument is present. | nakagawa2024:432 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 959, after Tamura 1984: 12

The permissive reading surfaces in contexts of polite or deferential causation,
where the causer enables an action that the unnamed causee has reason or inclination to
perform. Bugaeva and Kobayashi note that a host may say
ipe-yar=an ro 'let's have the guest eat' rather than naming the
guest as a direct causee, producing a softer formulation
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 ; tamura2000 214 .
Bugaeva's Saru study shows that the productive -re/-te/-e also
covers permissive causation alongside coercive causation, and that ingestive verbs like
'eat' and 'drink' take both readings with that suffix
bugaeva2014 139, 141–142 . The structural property of
-yar — suppression of the causee — is what distinguishes it from
-re/-te/-e in permissive contexts; the permissive vs coercive
axis alone does not predict the choice between the two suffix types.

ipe-yar=an ro | eat-CAUS=4.S HORT | Let's have (them) eat. | After Tamura 2000[1988]: 214. The intransitive S suffix =an on ipe-yar confirms that the derived verb remains intransitive; the eating party is implied by context but not indexed on the verb. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.3.1 | SAR

The label sociative causative — marking the causer's participation together
with or on behalf of others — has been mentioned in connection with
-yar alongside the indefinite and permissive labels. The
sources consulted do not adjudicate between the three characterisations. Bugaeva's 2014 causative
study, the most comprehensive treatment of Ainu causative morphology available, explicitly
excludes -yar for reasons of space
bugaeva2014 127 .

Contrast with the productive causative

parameter
-re / -te / -e
-yar / -ar

arity
+1 (causer added; old subject → object)
0 (causer added; old subject suppressed)

causee expression
direct object; person-indexed
unexpressed; no person indexing

specifying the implied agent
—
or peka / or un / eun adjunct

causation type
direct and indirect
indirect only

productivity
high (190 forms in Bugaeva's Saru sample)
low; few attested forms

The data for this table come from nakagawa2024 200–202 ,
bugaeva2014 127, 130, 143 , and
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 .

The pair nu-re / nu-yar makes the
contrast explicit. The productive causative nu-re 'make someone
hear something' is a three-place verb: the causer is subject, the causee occupies one
object slot and receives a person index, and the content heard fills the second object
slot. The indefinite causative nu-yar keeps the two-place frame
of the base nu hear (something) : the causer is subject, the content is
the sole object, and the recipient is absent from the verb's morphosyntax
nakagawa2024 201–202 .

Corpus profile

Frequency data from the Hokkaido corpus show the suffix active in only a small set of
forms. The token ipeyar returns approximately 2 hits (rank
14,619), making it marginal; nuyar is more frequent but the
absolute total remains small ‹corpus-confirmed›. The distribution reflects lexicalized
or semi-lexicalized combinations rather than productive attachment across the verb lexicon
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 .

nu-yar 's higher frequency follows from its speech-act function.
The indefinite-causee structure is suited to public announcement: a causer who makes
something known without restricting who will receive the information has systematic
recourse to this form wherever the open recipient set is the point. The permissive-polite
use — ipe-yar=an ro 'let's have the guest eat' — is attested
but at low frequency ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Ijäs's learner grammar does not treat -yar ; Lesson 9 on special
verb forms discusses the causative, reciprocal, and antipassive but covers only
-re among the causative types ijas2023 .

Open questions

Several questions are unanswered in the sources consulted. Bugaeva's 387-verb Saru causative
study — the most comprehensive available — excludes -yar for
reasons of space bugaeva2014 127 . No source in the present corpus
offers a distributional account of which verb classes the suffix selects, what semantic
or pragmatic factors predict the choice between -yar and
-re/-te/-e on the same base, or how the suffix patterns
dialectally outside Saru. The structural facts — arity 0, causee unexpressed,
or peka/or un/eun adjunct option for the implied agent — are
consistent across the four sources that address the suffix
nakagawa2024 201–202, 429 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.1 ;
kobayashi2015 12 ; sato2008 234 .
The semantic label — whether indefinite, permissive, or sociative causation best
characterises the suffix — remains open ‹contested›.

One diachronic proposal treats -yar as descending from a
pronominal-causative construction *i=a-de , with regular
intervocalic *d > r ; the zero-valency outcome would follow
from the prior grammaticalization of the indefinite causee pronoun into the verb complex,
parallel to how the meaning of i=…-re semantically overlaps with
that of -yar
( aynucorporadiscord ; antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, 26 May 2026)
‹speculative›. No peer-reviewed source develops this account.

title: Architecture of the Personal-Affix System: The Four Persons and the S/A/O Paradigms
summary: The master chapter laying out the whole personal-affix system — the four 'persons', the subjective (A/S) vs objective (O) sub-paradigms, prefix vs suffix exponents, and the terminological wars.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Architecture of the Personal-Affix System: The Four Persons and the S/A/O Paradigms
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The master chapter laying out the whole personal-affix system — the four 'persons', the subjective (A/S) vs objective (O) sub-paradigms, prefix vs suffix exponents, and the terminological wars.

The four formal persons

Hokkaido Ainu marks person on the finite verb. An intransitive predicate indexes its single
argument; a transitive predicate indexes the combination of its subject and its object.
Independent pronouns — kani ‘I’, eani ‘you’,
coka ‘we (excluding you)’, asinuma ‘the
fourth-person self’ — are available for emphasis or contrast and are used more sparingly than
their Japanese counterparts, while the verbal index carries the ordinary work of tracking
arguments nakagawa2024 163–165, 169 ; tamura1972 17 . Satō
describes the pattern as head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 : the verb,
the head of its clause, bears the central information about which participants fill its valence
frame, and a transitive verb may host both a subject index and an object index sato2025a 361 .

orwano ku=cise or ta ku=an kor | then 1SG.POSS=house place LOC 1SG.S=exist while | Then, while I was in my house, … | No pronoun appears; the intransitive existential ku=an indexes its 1SG.S subject. The possessor ku=cise carries the homophonous first-person prefix in the nominal domain. | nakagawa2024:166 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 104, after Sunazawa Kura 1983: 75

Nakagawa recognises four formal persons, numbered 1 to 4, each with a singular and a plural, and
treats the numerals as form-classes detached from any fixed speaker-or-addressee value; the
mapping between form and reference is many-to-one nakagawa2024 163 . The fourth
person is the limiting case: it is a single form-set into which everything that persons 1 to 3
cannot express is gathered, so that the referential range of those three is narrowed in turn
nakagawa2024 164, 174 .

Terminology for this set is unsettled. Nakagawa calls it the fourth person; Tamura divides the
same Saru forms into separate persons — the inclusive, the quotative, the second-person
honorific, and the indefinite — counting eleven persons in all; Bugaeva uses “indefinite” and
reanalyses the quotation use as logophoric; Satō treats the forms as polysemous; and Takahashi,
working on Tokachi, adopts “inclusive person” as the label that unifies the otherwise disparate
uses nakagawa2024 174–179 ; tamura1972 18–20, 39 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 ; sato2008 203–208 ; takahashi2015 205–210 ‹contested›. The form-label “fourth” or “indefinite” is used here; its referential functions
are set out in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm the indefinite/fourth-person chapter
and fourth-person-honorific the honorific and logophoric chapter .

The S/A/O paradigm

The person indexes divide into three role sets. S is the sole argument of an
intransitive verb, A the subject of a transitive verb, and O its object.
Nakagawa presents these as separate intransitive-subject, transitive-subject, and object tables,
and Bugaeva’s southern Hokkaido table gives the same Saru–Chitose paradigm in S/A/O terms nakagawa2024 165–169 ; bugaeva2012 471 . The three-way distinction is load-bearing: two persons keep
distinct forms for all three roles, one opposes an S=A pair to O, and two collapse the roles
entirely. The rows below give the architectural cell values for the standard Saru–Chitose
baseline; allomorphy, dialect microvariation, and the person-combination rules belong to the
chapters that follow.

formal person
free pronoun (Saru)
A: transitive subject
S: intransitive subject
O: object
alignment of the cell

1SG
kani
ku=
ku=
en=
S=A≠O; nominative–accusative

1PL.EXCL
coka
ci=
=as
un=
S≠A≠O; tripartite

2SG
eani
e=
e=
e=
S=A=O; neutral

2PL
ecioka
eci=
eci=
eci=
S=A=O; neutral

3
sinuma / oka
Ø
Ø
Ø
S=A=O; zero

4 / INDEF
asinuma / aoka
a= (dialectally an= )
=an
i=
S≠A≠O; tripartite

The first singular is the one nominative–accusative cell: ku= serves both
S and A, opposed to the object en= bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 . Its forms, together with the second singular, are treated in first-second-person-singular-affixes . The second-person cells are neutral, e= and
eci= spanning A, S, and O alike. The third person is unmarked. Whether the
blank reflects a zero affix or the absence of any third-person exponent is disputed: Satō argues
that a zero third-person affix is required by the cap of two indexes per verb and by the
ungrammaticality of three-index combinations, against an analysis that recognises no third-person
affix at all nakagawa2024 169 ; sato2023b 39, 47 ; sato2025a 369 ‹contested›.

aynu itak k=eramuan wa kusu icen k=eunkeray wa pirka pirka | Ainu language 1SG.A=understand CONJ because money 1SG.A=receive CONJ good good | Because I had learnt Ainu, I was able to receive money, which was very good. | The reduced 1SG.A prefix k= is the form ku= takes before a vowel. | nakagawa2024:168 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 111

Side-encoding: prefixes for A, suffixes for S

The architectural asymmetry of the paradigm is the side of the verb on which the subject index
appears. First singular and second person are prefixal, or zero, in both subject roles. First
plural exclusive and the fourth person switch sides by transitivity: ci=
and a= stand before a transitive stem as A, while
=as and =an follow an intransitive stem as S. This
side-encoding is the morphological basis on which Bugaeva calls these two rows tripartite nakagawa2024 166–167 ; bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 .

subject set
S on an intransitive verb
A on a transitive verb
object counterpart

1PL.EXCL
stem =as
ci= stem
un= stem

4 / INDEF
stem =an
a= stem
i= stem

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | One clause chain indexes the same exclusive group as A by the prefix ci= on the transitive o and as S by the suffix =as on the intransitive plural arki. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

wen kamuy a=maketa wa a=rayke ru-we ne | bad god 4.A=defeat CONJ 4.A=kill track-POSS COP | The evil kamuy was defeated and killed. | The prefix a= indexes an indefinite transitive subject and defocuses the agent; the patient stays the object of the transitive verbs. | nakagawa2024:177 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 145

The object counterparts of these two sets, un= and
i= , are prefixal, so each set keeps its three roles formally distinct in
both shape and position. The fourth-person object i= indexes, among other
referents, the narrator’s own in oral narrative nakagawa2024 169 :

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=lodge CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The fourth-person object i= recurs three times; with a= for A and =an for S, one person distinguishes all three roles, the tripartite pattern of the table above. | nakagawa2024:170 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

The choice of a= over an= for the fourth-person A
is itself dialectal: Saru and the western dialects use a= , while Tokachi,
Ishikari, Shiranuka, and Samani favour an= under conditions that vary by
locality, and Nakagawa takes an= to be the older form nakagawa2024 167–168 .

The boundary symbols used throughout the grammar follow a working convention: = writes the personal-index boundary and - the boundary of derivational and
number morphology. The convention is deliberately neutral, because the sources disagree about the
morphological status of the individual indexes. Bugaeva, on prosodic and separability grounds,
distinguishes word-like =an and =as , clitic-like
a= and eci= , and fuller prefixes such as
ku= , en= , and i= that shift
the accent kernel; Nakagawa writes the whole set with = for convenience
yet flags the unusual autonomy of =an , which can be separated from its verb;
Sakaguchi’s explicit clitic/affix criteria, developed for Sakhalin material, likewise rank
fourth-person =an as the most autonomous subject marker bugaeva2012 472–473 ; nakagawa2024 49, 183 ; sakaguchi2024 ix, 77–85, 163
‹contested›. The ordering and boundary diagnostics are pursued in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Syncretism and portmanteau cells

Second person collapses the role distinction: e= marks 2SG as A, S, and
O, and eci= does the same for 2PL. The same eci=
additionally serves as the portmanteau exponent for a first-person A acting on a second-person O,
covering 1SG→2SG, 1SG→2PL, 1PL.EXCL→2SG, and 1PL.EXCL→2PL with a single prefix in place of a
sequence of first- and second-person indexes tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2008 38 ; bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 . The surrounding
clause resolves the resulting ambiguity, and the cell is developed in eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

nep ka eci=kasuy pe an ya
2SG.O=help NMLZ exist Q"
tr="Is there anything I can help you with?"
cite="nakagawa2024:173"
dial="SAR"
place="Nakagawa ex. 128, after Honda 2001: 44"
note="The single exponent eci= encodes the 1SG.A→2SG.O combination; no separate ku= or e= appears."
/>

The mirror configuration is less regular. Most dialectal paradigms express 2→1 with a
second-person A index followed by the first-person object form, but Saru and Chitose drop the
expected second-person subject and use bare en= for 2SG→1SG, Chitose
extending the bare form to 2PL→1SG as well nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 .

A verb admits at most one subject index and one object index, and no third argument is ever
indexed. The cap shapes ditransitive marking. With verbs such as kore
‘give’ the object index falls on the human recipient while the theme stays a bare noun phrase,
a secundative pattern that Satō takes as further evidence for a zero third-person affix; Nakagawa
describes the same three-place verbs through valence, with two bare object phrases and neither
obligatorily indexed when both are third person sato2008 147 ; sato2023b 39, 47 ; sato2025a 368–371 ; nakagawa2024 363–365 ‹contested›. Object
indexing and the three-place verbs are treated in object-indexing-monotransitive .

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | Both non-subject participants are third person and surface as bare noun phrases; only the 1SG.A subject is indexed. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

Number and the fourth person

The person paradigm intersects with number at two layers. The first plural row is exclusive:
ci= , =as , and un= exclude
the addressee, while addressee-inclusive ‘we’ belongs to the fourth person. Tokachi confirms the
same division, an exclusive first-plural series beside an inclusive fourth-person series whose
forms take a plural verb even when the referent is single nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 ;
tamura1972 18–19 ; bugaeva2012 472, 475 ; takahashi2015 206–209 . The exclusive paradigm is developed in first-person-plural-and-clusivity .

Verb number is its own system. Hokkaido uses suppletive singular/plural stems —
ek / arki ‘come’,
arpa / paye ‘go’ — alongside a root-attaching
plural suffix -pa and, in Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari, an outer plural
pa that sits outside the person index. The minimal pair
iki=an pa and iki-pa=an places the outer
pa after the fourth-person suffix and the stem-forming suffix
-pa before it nakagawa2024 141–159 ; bugaeva2012 471, 481 ; sakaguchi2024 12 . The Sakhalin verbal plural =hci and
its relatives attach outside the person-inflected verb; their classification as number rather than
third-person marking is a point on which Nakagawa and Sakaguchi converge against earlier
descriptions nakagawa2024 151–155, 178 ; sakaguchi2024 77–83, 163 ;
baek2021 73 . Person–number agreement is taken up in person-number-interactions .

Several fourth-person uses select plural morphology for a single referent. Honorific address to
one esteemed person and the inclusive ‘you and I’ take the fourth-person form with a plural stem,
as does much narrative first-person use outside Saru and Chitose nakagawa2024 160–161, 174–179 ; tamura1972 28, 34 .

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | The honorific addressee is a single person, yet the fourth-person S suffix =an appears on the plural stem arki. | nakagawa2024:176 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140, woman to an adult man

The whole semantic range of the fourth person — inclusive first plural, honorific second person,
impersonal agent-defocusing, narrative first person, and logophoric reference inside a quotation
— rests on this one form-set. The old account of an “elevated” literary person distinct from an
everyday one is contested: Nakagawa assigns the narrative use to viewpoint and text type, Satō
derives it from a first-person-plural strategy, and Bugaeva analyses the quotation use as
logophoric and finds it outside folklore as well nakagawa2024 174–179, 600–604 ;
sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ; bugaeva2008 40–44 ‹contested›. The fourth-person object i= must be kept apart from the
homophonous detransitivising prefix i- ‘something’, whose origin is itself
debated nam2021 62–74 ; the detransitiviser belongs to the valency system and is
treated under antipassive-detransitive-i the antipassive .

Mixed alignment and the verb template

Read person by person, the system is mixed in morphological alignment: 1SG is
nominative–accusative with S=A≠O, the second and third persons are neutral with S=A=O, and the
first plural exclusive and the fourth person are tripartite with S≠A≠O. Bugaeva’s analysis of
Hokkaido Ainu rests directly on the S/A/O paradigm and on the side-encoding of
ci= / =as and
a= / =an , and she characterises the language as mixed
but basically tripartite bugaeva2012 472, 498 ; bugaeva2008 32 .

The person indexes do not split the intransitive subject by agentivity: agentive and patientive
intransitives take the same S set once valence is fixed, so the agreement paradigm is not
split-intransitive in the morphological sense nakagawa2024 126–127 . The split
that the literature pursues under that name surfaces in verb classes, noun incorporation, and
valency-changing morphology, and is treated in alignment-split-intransitive . The
competing system-wide analyses — tripartite, hierarchical, inverse — are sharpened by Sakhalin
work: Dal Corso rejects a tripartite component for Sakhalin in favour of a mixed
nominative–neutral–inverse account, while warning that the diachrony may not carry over to
Hokkaido dalcorso2025align 187–188, 200, 213 ‹contested›. The Hokkaido paradigm
above supplies the empirical base for alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

The personal indexes occupy the outer argument-marking layer of the verb. Subject and object
indexes precede the stem in transitives, while the intransitive S forms
=as and =an follow it; derivational material —
applicatives, causatives, the reflexive, the reciprocal, the antipassive
i- , and the stem-forming plural -pa — sits closer
to the root, and a reflexive or reciprocal fills the object slot rather than co-occurring with an
object index for the same participant tamura1972 35 ; nakagawa2024 183, 196–199 ; sato2023b 40 . The full ordering of these positions is the subject of personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

title: Person–Number Interactions and Plural Agreement
summary: How the personal affixes interact with number marking — the -pa plural, the =as suffix, and agreement resolution across A, S, and O.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Person–Number Interactions and Plural Agreement
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: How the personal affixes interact with number marking — the -pa plural, the =as suffix, and agreement resolution across A, S, and O.

Two layers of number

Person and number meet in Hokkaido Ainu at two formally distinct layers. Within the
personal-affix paradigm, number is built into specific person rows: the first plural exclusive
series ( ci= / =as / un= ),
second plural eci= , and the fourth-person series, whose number behavior
is reference-dependent rather than form-fixed. Verbal number forms a second layer, realized
through suppletive singular/plural stem alternations and through two homophonous formatives: a
root-attaching plural suffix -pa and an outer plural auxiliary or clitic
pa that follows the person-inflected verb nakagawa2024 140–162 ; bugaeva2012 471 ; nakagawa2022 . The person inventory is set out
in person-marking-architecture ; the stem alternations in
suppletive-verbal-number ; the exclusive row and clusivity in
first-person-plural-and-clusivity ; and the full range of fourth-person functions in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

exponent
layer
position
controller
Hokkaido distribution

=as
personal affix
intransitive stem-final (suffix)
1PL.EXCL subject
pan-Hokkaido

eci=
personal affix / portmanteau
pre-stem (prefix)
2PL A, S, O; also 1A→2O
pan-Hokkaido

suppletive plural stem
verbal number
root alternation
S (intransitives); O or event (transitives)
lexically governed

-pa
verbal number
inside person marking (root-attaching suffix)
S (intransitives) or O/event (transitives)
pan-Hokkaido V/ -pa stem class

outer pa
outer number
outside the person-inflected form
participant or event plurality
Saru, Chitose, Ishikari; clitic/auxiliary status ‹contested›

Shizunai ci / Horobetsu -ci
outer or derived number
varies by dialect (see below)
participant or event plurality
Shizunai (outer); Horobetsu (fossilized inner suffix)

Corbett's distinction between the indexing of participant number and the marking of event or
action number corbett2000 maps onto these two layers. Intransitive plural
marking in Hokkaido is agreement-like: the number of S determines both the stem choice and the
S-affix form. Transitive plural marking is oriented primarily to the object or to the action,
bringing it closer to event-number marking in Corbett's terms nakagawa2024 142–145 .

What controls the plural verb

With intransitive verbs the plural stem — whether suppletive ( ek / arki 'come', arpa / paye
'go', an / oka(y) 'exist') or suffixal (V/ -pa pairs such as rewe / rew-pa ) — agrees with S. The 1PL.EXCL suffix =as and a plural
fourth-person suffix =an both obligatorily co-occur with a plural
intransitive stem; a singular S affix selects the singular stem nakagawa2024 142–146 ; bugaeva2012 471 .

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | The intransitive arki is the plural suppletive of ek 'come'; it co-occurs with the 1PL.EXCL S suffix =as. The same group is indexed as A on the transitive o 'board' by the prefix ci=. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

With transitive verbs the picture diverges. Plural marking is tied primarily to the plurality of
O or of the action; a first- or fourth-person plural A prefix ( ci= , a= ) does not by itself reliably trigger the plural stem or outer pa . Nakagawa characterises intransitive plural as agreement-like and
transitive plural as action- or object-oriented and often optional nakagawa2024 142–146 ; bugaeva2012 471 . Ijäs's learner grammar states the same split: intransitive
plural follows S, transitive plural ordinarily follows O or the action ijas2023 . The
corpus shows verb-specific and dialect-variable departures from any simple threshold, and
Nakagawa's discussion of numerals and quantifiers treats the co-occurrence with plural morphology
as complex and speaker-variable nakagawa2024 160–162 .

apkas=as pa | walk=1PL.EXCL.S PL | We walk. | Constructed teaching form from Ijäs L9; illustrates outer pa following the 1PL.EXCL S affix on an intransitive verb in Saru-type dialects. | ijas2023

Stem-forming -pa and outer pa

Nakagawa distinguishes the two pa elements by their position relative to
the person-inflected form. Stem-forming -pa builds the plural member of a
V/ -pa pair and attaches to the root, inside any person marker or
causative suffix. Outer pa follows the fully person-inflected verb,
outside any person affix, and marks participant or event plurality nakagawa2024 151–159 . Sakaguchi applies a formal positional criterion — a clitic (接語) attaches to the
person-inflected form rather than to the root — and concludes that the Saru, Chitose, and
Ishikari outer element is a clitic, distinct from the root-attaching suffix sakaguchi2024 12, 77–83 . The hypothesis that outer pa is a later western Hokkaido development
that displaced an earlier pan-Ainu outer (h)ci is argued in nakagawa2022 ‹contested›.

The minimal pair demonstrating the position contrast uses the fourth-person intransitive suffix
=an . When outer pa is used, it follows =an ; when stem-forming -pa is used, it precedes =an .

nenpoka iki=an pa wa siknu=an wa | somehow do=4.S PL CONJ live=4.S CONJ | Somehow we managed and survived, and… | Outer pa follows the fourth-person S suffix =an. | nakagawa2024:159 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 87

irwak sinne sorekusu iki-pa=an | sibling like exactly do-PL=4.S | We were like siblings. | Stem-forming -pa precedes the fourth-person S suffix =an, confirming root-level attachment. | nakagawa2024:159 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 88

rus patek a=mi wa hotke=an pa | fur only 4.A=wear CONJ sleep=4.S PL | We wore only furs and slept. | Fourth-person transitive a=mi (wear) followed by intransitive hotke=an (sleep); outer pa follows the person-inflected form of the second predicate. | ilcaa1976:aa-irc/005#157 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, ILCAA Ainu materials, uwepeker 7

Outer pa can repeat across conjoined predicates in a clause chain because
its scope is over the person-inflected predicate, not over the root. The inner suffix -pa belongs to the stem and contributes its number reading at the lexical
level. The two may co-occur on the same verb — a V/ -pa stem combined with
outer pa — and both combine with causative suffixes while maintaining
their respective attachment sites nakagawa2024 156–159 .

Fourth-person plurals and number mismatches

The fourth-person series triggers plural verbal morphology in four of its five uses, regardless of
the actual referent count. Nakagawa enumerates five functions at 2024: 174–179; the number
behavior follows from the function nakagawa2024 160–161, 174–179 ; tamura1972 28, 34 ; bugaeva2012 475–476 .

Inclusive first plural (use ①, 'you and I') always takes the plural verb stem, since the
referent is inherently multiple nakagawa2024 174 ; takahashi2015 209 . In Tokachi, the inclusive series consistently co-occurs with plural verb forms even when
referring to a pair, and this co-selection is obligatory takahashi2015 209 .

eani hem paye=an ro | 2SG also go.PL=4.S HORT | Let's go together, you and I. | The plural suppletive paye co-occurs with =an; the reference is inherently multiple (speaker + addressee). | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 138

Honorific second-person address (use ②) requires the plural verb stem for a single addressee, as
a form of social amplification nakagawa2024 175–176 ; tamura1972 28 . The mismatch between grammatically plural morphology and a singular referent is systematic
and socially conditioned.

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | One person is addressed; the fourth-person S suffix =an appears on the plural suppletive stem arki. | nakagawa2024:176 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140, woman addressing an adult man

Indefinite/impersonal use (use ③) — the equivalent of French on or German man — also takes the plural verb stem, with an unspecified agent defocused from
the event nakagawa2024 176–177 ; tamura1972 34 ; sato2008 203–205 .

Narrative first-person use (use ④) is where dialectal variation is sharpest. In Saru and
Chitose, a single protagonist may appear with the singular verb stem. Horobetsu also permits
singular stems for a singular narrator, though the only available free pronoun is the formally
plural aokay . Shizunai shows the opposite configuration: the verb is
consistently plural regardless of narrator number, while the pronouns distinguish anani (singular) from anoka (plural) nakagawa2024 174–179, 254–256 . The crossed mismatches — singular verb with plural pronoun in Horobetsu, plural verb with
singular pronoun in Shizunai — confirm that the fourth-person form-set is not simply a plural
of the first person, and they supply evidence for the historically composite character of the
series ‹consensus›.

Shizunai ci and Sakhalin =hci: position as the diagnostic

Shizunai uses an outer ci where Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari use outer pa . The position is the same: Shizunai ci follows
the person-inflected form, outside =an , in exactly the outer-plural slot
nakagawa2024 151–155 .

hoski no hotke=an ci akus | first ADV sleep=4.S PL then | When we slept first, … | Shizunai ci follows the fourth-person S suffix =an in the outer-plural slot, parallel to Saru outer pa. | nakagawa2024:155 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 74, after Okuda 1999

Horobetsu presents a formally different element. Its -ci suffix appears
fossilized in items such as iki-ci and kar-ici ,
and the diagnostic ordering places it inside the person marker ( iki-ci=as ),
with the derivational layer rather than the outer plural slot. The Horobetsu form therefore
behaves like a root-level suffix, not like the outer elements of Shizunai or Saru nakagawa2024 154–155 .

The Sakhalin form =(a)hci (Aniwa Bay =(a)hsi ) was
described by Murasaki as an optional third-person plural marker murasaki1979 49–52 . Nakagawa disputes the classification: the element is non-obligatory and appears with subjects
of all persons, placing it with the outer-auxiliary set rather than with person-agreement affixes
nakagawa2024 151–155, 178 ‹contested›. Sakaguchi applies formal positional
diagnostics and arrives at the same result: =hci attaches to the
person-inflected verb, can follow an auxiliary, and precedes a nominalizer — the signature of a
clitic over the person-inflected form, not a root-level agreement suffix sakaguchi2024 77–83, 163 . Baek adds that Sakhalin -(a)hci extends to the nominal domain, a
trait shared with Uilta and Nivkh in the Sakhalin area and absent from Hokkaido baek2021 73 .

eciokaya-hcin naa eci=paye-hci-hi nee nanko | 2PL-PL still 2PL.S=go.PL-PL-NMLZ COP probably | You people must have gone too. | The Sakhalin plural element -hci/-hcin appears with a second-person plural subject, not a third-person one, undermining its analysis as third-person plural agreement. Hokkaido dialects lack productive =hci; the corresponding function is served by outer pa (Saru/Chitose/Ishikari) or outer ci (Shizunai). | murasaki1979:52; nakagawa2024:155 | SA | Murasaki ex. 70 (Sakhalin contrast)

The result for Hokkaido is that no dialect has a productive third-person plural agreement suffix.
Plural participant reference is encoded at the verbal-number layer — suppletive stems, inner -pa , outer pa or ci — and
through the number mismatches of the fourth-person row, not through an additional person-affix
position nakagawa2024 151–155 .

Multiple plural marking

The plural exponents occupy different layers of the verb and can therefore accumulate within a
single predicate. A V/ -pa stem encodes plural at the root level; outer pa can follow the person-inflected form of that same stem to mark
additional participant or event plurality at the outer layer. Similarly, a V/ -pa stem combines with causative morphology before the person affix while outer pa appears outside. In clause chains, outer pa may repeat on successive
predicates because its scope is over the person-inflected predicate rather than the clause
sequence as a unit nakagawa2024 156–159 .

Plural accumulation does not affect the cap on person affixes. A finite verb carries at most one
subject index and one object index, and no third argument is indexed by person. In ditransitives
such as kore 'give', plural morphology can co-occur with the ordinary
object-indexing pattern in which the object affix tracks the human recipient while the theme
remains an unindexed bare noun phrase sato2008 147 ; sato2023b 39, 47 . The two-affix cap and the ditransitive pattern are treated in
object-indexing-monotransitive and personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

title: Personal-Affix Junctural Sandhi, =an/a= Allomorphy, and Connected-Speech Reduction
summary: The junctural sandhi triggered by personal affixes (ku= before vowels, en=, un=, ci=, eci=), the =an/-an and a=/an= allomorphy as a morphophonological problem, and casual-register contraction, elision, and cliticization.
part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Personal-Affix Junctural Sandhi, =an/a= Allomorphy, and Connected-Speech Reduction
Part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
Summary: The junctural sandhi triggered by personal affixes (ku= before vowels, en=, un=, ci=, eci=), the =an/-an and a=/an= allomorphy as a morphophonological problem, and casual-register contraction, elision, and cliticization.

Overview

The person markers of Hokkaido Ainu undergo systematic phonological changes at their
juncture with a following verb or noun stem. The most salient is the deletion of the vowel
of ku= and ci= before stem-initial vowels
other than /i/, a feature of the southern varieties (Saru, Chitose, Mukawa). This deletion
interacts with the prosodic-word formation and accent rules treated in
accent-in-compounds-and-affixation and with the vowel-weakening mechanism
described in glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution . The general sandhi processes
that operate across all morpheme junctures — coda-/r/ assimilation, nasal-place assimilation
— are the subject of coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi ; the person-marker
inventory and its morphosyntactic analysis are laid out in
person-marking-architecture .

The ku= and ci= vowel-deletion rule

In Saru, Chitose, and Mukawa, the /u/ of ku= (first-person singular
agent/subject) deletes before a stem-initial /a/, /e/, /u/, or /o/; correspondingly, the
/i/ of ci= (first-person plural exclusive agent/subject) deletes in
the same environments nakagawa2024 33, 52 ; sato2008 xii .
The vowel /i/ is excluded from the deletion domain: before a stem beginning with /i/, a
distinct vowel-weakening process applies (§3 below). Outside the southern zone, most
Hokkaido dialects preserve the full marker forms without deletion
nakagawa2024 52 ; nakagawafukazawa2022 §3 .

The two deletion rules can be stated as:

/u/ → ∅ / ku= __ {a, e, u, o}…

/i/ → ∅ / ci= __ {a, e, u, o}…

Table 1. Deletion and weakening at the ku= / ci= juncture, Saru/Chitose/Mukawa

stem-initial vowel
underlying
surface
process

/a/
ku=a…
k=a…
/u/ deletion

/o/
ku=omare
k=omare
/u/ deletion

/e/ (with ci= )
ci=etu
c=etu
/i/ deletion

/i/ — open σ1
ku=ipe
ku=ype [kuˈype]
stem /i/ → [j]; NO marker deletion (§3)

/i/ — closed σ1
ku=ikra
ku=ikra
no change; both processes blocked (§3)

Learners of Saru and Chitose frequently overgeneralize the deletion to /i/-initial stems,
producing ungrammatical * k=ipe ; the restriction to {a, e, u, o} is
the corrective aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

ku=omare | 1SG.A=exit | k=omare | I go out. | Underlying ku=omare; in the Saru and Chitose dialects the /u/ of ku= deletes before the /o/-initial stem, yielding the spoken form k=omare [koˈmaɾe]. Standard romanized orthography writes the underlying form.

The /i/-initial block and vowel weakening

When the stem begins with /i/, deletion of ku= 's /u/ is blocked; the
stem-initial /i/ instead undergoes 母音弱化 (vowel weakening), becoming the glide [j]:
ku=ipe → ku=ype /kuˈype/, while
* k=ipe is ungrammatical nakagawa2024 52 ;
sato2008 xii . The same process operates with e= :
e=itak → eytak [ejˈtak] 'your words / you
speak' nakagawa2024 40 . The weakening process (/i/ → [j] after a
vowel-final prefix) is described in broader scope in
glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution .

A second environment blocks weakening: when the stem's first syllable is closed (CVC), the
result of weakening would produce an illicit consonant cluster. The stem
ikra has σ1 = ik (coda /k/); weakening
would yield the onset sequence /k-jk-/, which violates the (C)V(C) syllable canon
( syllable-template ). Accordingly ku=ikra is the only
licit form: neither * ku=ykra nor * k=ikra
arises nakagawa2024 53 ;
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28) ‹corpus-confirmed›. By contrast,
stems with an open /i/-initial syllable do undergo weakening:
eytasa (< e= + itasa )
and eywanke (< e= +
iwanke ) both show the [j] glide
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-28) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

ku=ipe | 1SG.A=eat | ku=ype | I eat. | Underlying ku=ipe; the /u/ of ku= is retained (deletion is blocked before /i/), and the stem-initial /i/ weakens to the glide [j], giving ku=ype [kuype]. The form *k=ipe is ungrammatical.

Prosodic integration and rule ordering

The markers ku= , e= , and
ci= form a single prosodic word with the following stem, so that the
pitch-accent placement rules (原則1 / 原則2; pitch-accent-placement-rule ) are
reapplied to the whole unit. The crucial ordering point is that the accent shift occurs
before vowel deletion: the surface accent is computed from the prefixed word, not
re-derived from the contracted form nakagawa2024 52 ;
shiraishi2022 §5.1 .

The derivation of k=ómare illustrates the two-step ordering:

Table 2. Rule ordering: accent shift precedes deletion

stage
form
syllable structure and accent
operation

underlying
omáre
o|má|re — σ1 = V (open) → 原則2: accent on σ2
—

after marker attachment
ku=ómare
ku|ó|ma|re — new σ1 = CV (open) → 原則2: accent on new σ2
prosodic-word formation; accent reapplied

surface (Saru/Chitose/Mukawa)
k=ómare [kómaɾe]
kó|ma|re — σ1 now CVC; accent fixed
/u/ deletion; σ1–σ2 merge

The same ordering holds with ci= : etú →
ci=étu (accent on σ2 of the new unit) →
c=étu [ˈcetu] nakagawa2024 52 . For consonant-initial
stems, deletion does not apply; only the accent shift operates:
hopúni → ku=hópuni 'I rise'
nakagawa2024 52 .

The markers a= (fourth-person agent/subject) and
eci= (second-person plural agent/subject) behave differently: they do
not form a single prosodic word with the stem, so the stem retains its underlying accent
without shift. Thus omáre → a=omáre (accent
unchanged) nakagawa2024 53 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 .

Table 3. Accent-class split among person markers

marker

Juncture tightness and the =an boundary

Within the person-marker class as a whole, the degree of phonological integration with the
host stem varies beyond the binary inner/outer accent-class split. Community phonetic
observation — uncorroborated by instrumental study in the sources read for this grammar —
reports that the marker group binds more tightly to its host than a compound constituent
does: a=kor itak 'speech one has' surfaces as [akorəʔitak], with a
glottal catch at the word boundary after kor , rather than
resyllabifying the coda /r/ across the compound juncture to *[ako.ri.tak]
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-03-18) ‹speculative›. Within the marker
paradigm, the inner-class markers ( ku= , e= )
are reported to be phonetically tighter than the outer-class markers
( a= , =an ), the latter tending to be realized
more independently in measured speech
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-03-18) ‹speculative›.

The suffix =an (fourth-person S) shows a juncture pattern distinct
from that of derivational suffixes: after a consonant-final stem, the coda does not
resyllabify onto the following /a/ by the regular resyllabification rule of
syllable-template . Instead the boundary is marked by a glottal stop or tense
onset: sap=an → [sapʔan], not *[sa.pan]
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-27) ‹speculative›. This contrasts with the
morpheme-internal resyllabification seen in true derivational junctures such as
sik-o → [si.ko] nakagawa2024 38–39 ;
shiraishi2022 §4.4 . The
=an suffix also carries a secondary accent when the verb stem closes
with a CV(+) syllable: iˈruska-ˌan 'one is angry' retains both
accents. This secondary accent disappears when the stem ends in a CVC syllable:
ˈcis-an 'one cries', ˈsap-an
shiraishi2022 §5.1 .

Connected-speech reductions

In fast or casual speech, the vowel-deletion process extends to sequences that have become
partially lexicalized. The independent pronoun kuani 'I' (analysed in
independent-personal-pronouns ) reduces to kani by the
same /u/-deletion that yields ciep → cep
'food (eaten thing)'
aynucorporadiscord (gengojiro, 2024-02-05) ‹corpus-suggested›. Both
kani and cep are the standard Saru/Chitose
dictionary entries, showing that these contractions have become conventionalized rather than
remaining casual variants.

The romanized orthography writes underlying (etymological) forms throughout —
ku=omare rather than k=omare ;
ran wa rather than ram ma — while katakana
practice tends to follow the phonetic output more closely
nakagawa2024 37–38 ;
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-07-09) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The sandhi forms
are produced in speech but not written in standard transcription, so corpus searches for
assimilated person-marker forms typically return nothing; the spoken distribution of the
rule is consequently under-documented in the written record
nakagawa2024 37–38 . The wider romanization policy and its divergence from
katakana practice are discussed in
orthographic-standardization-and-word-division .

The coda /r/ of a verb stem can interact with person-marker sandhi when a /r/-final stem
precedes a vowel-initial marker or suffix. The regressive assimilation rules of
coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi operate on the coda /r/ first and produce
the form that then serves as input to person-marker juncture rules. The fast-speech
deletion of word-final /r/ itself ( kur →
ku , kor → ko ) is
treated in rhotic-r .

The clitic–affix cline

Nakagawa's standard romanization applies the equals sign to all person markers at the
left or right edge of the verb: ku= ,
e= , a= , ci= ,
eci= , en= , un= ,
=an , and =as
nakagawa2024 33 . The = is an orthographic device — Nakagawa
states expressly that it is unrelated to the linguistic convention of marking clitics with
an equals sign nakagawa2024 58–60 — so the notation itself carries no claim
about clitic as against affix status. The orthographic policy is discussed in
orthographic-standardization-and-word-division equals-sign .

The phonological evidence surveyed in this chapter shows that the personal markers — written
uniformly with = — occupy different positions along the clitic-to-affix
cline. Three independent diagnostics converge on the same two-way split:

Accent shift : the inner class ( ku= ,
e= , ci= ) triggers prosodic-word formation
and reapplication of accent rules; the outer class ( a= ,
eci= ) does not (§4 above;
nakagawa2024 52–53 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 ).

Vowel deletion : only ku= and
ci= lose their vowels before non-/i/ stems in the southern
dialects, marking them as the most tightly bonded members of the marker paradigm (§2
above; nakagawa2024 52 ).

Juncture opacity : the =an boundary blocks the
resyllabification that is otherwise permitted at morpheme-internal junctures, placing it
above the level of a derivational suffix in phonological independence (§5 above;
aynucorporadiscord nukopoli, 2023-12-27, ‹speculative›).

The three criteria jointly define a gradient: inner clitics > outer clitics >
independent words, with ku= and ci= the most
tightly integrated members and =an the loosest. A parallel gradation
in morphophonological tightness across person-marking sub-classes has been documented for
Sakhalin Ainu sakaguchi2024 , providing a comparative point for the
Hokkaido two-way split. The morphosyntactic diagnostics for the Hokkaido system are taken up
in person-marking-architecture and the template position of each marker in
personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

title: The Personal-Affix Template: Position Classes and Affix Ordering
summary: The ordering of subject and object personal affixes relative to each other, to valency prefixes, and to the verb stem — the personal-affix portion of the verb template.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Personal-Affix Template: Position Classes and Affix Ordering
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The ordering of subject and object personal affixes relative to each other, to valency prefixes, and to the verb stem — the personal-affix portion of the verb template.

The position-class template

The cell values of the personal-index paradigm — which forms exist and what morphological
alignment each person row exhibits — are established in person-marking-architecture .
