This chapter examines how those indexes are ordered on the verb word: which positions they
occupy relative to the stem, how that ordering relates to valency-changing morphology, and what
the positional difference between subject prefixes and subject suffixes entails for the verb's
internal structure. The chapter covers the personal-affix layer of the verb; the full verb word
template, including TAM, negation, and nominalization layers, is treated in
verb-word-template .

position class
material
linear position

left personal layer

A prefixes: ku= , ci= ,
a= / an= ,
e= , eci= ; O prefixes:
en= , un= ,
i=

before the derived stem

valency-prefix layer

applicatives e- , ko- ,
o- ; antipassive/detransitive
i- ; reflexive yay- ,
si- ; reciprocal u-

inside personal prefixes, before the root

stem
lexical root; also suppletive and incorporated stems
template centre

stem-internal suffix

stem plural -pa ; causatives
-re , -e ,
-ka

after the root, before the right-edge person suffix

right personal layer

=as (1PL.EXCL.S); =an (4.S)

after the intransitive stem or derived intransitive

outer number layer

auxiliary pa (Saru, Chitose, Ishikari); auxiliary
ci (Shizunai)

outside the person-inflected verb

The table is a positional map, not a template in which every slot must be filled. Most verb
forms contain only the stem and one index; a verb with a third-person subject and third-person
object shows no overt personal material. The outer number layer is relevant here because it
supplies an ordering diagnostic for the right edge: material that follows a fully
person-inflected form belongs outside the personal-affix template, even where earlier
descriptions treated comparable segments as person markers nakagawa2024 151–159 ;
bugaeva2012 471, 481 ; sato2023b 39–40 .

The left personal layer: A and O prefixes

When a transitive verb is inflected for a non-third subject, the A index occupies the leftmost
position in the word: 1SG ku= , 1PL.EXCL
ci= , 4th/indefinite a= or
an= , 2SG e= , and 2PL
eci= all stand before the stem. Object indexes are prefixal across all
persons: 1SG en= , 1PL.EXCL un= , 4th
i= , and 2SG/2PL e= / eci=
stand before the stem when overt nakagawa2024 166–170 ; bugaeva2012 471 .

aynu itak k=eramuan wa kusu icen k=eunkeray wa pirka pirka | Ainu language 1SG.A=understand CONJ because money 1SG.A=receive CONJ good good | Because I had learnt Ainu, I was able to receive money, which was very good. | ku= reduces to k= before a vowel-initial stem. Both transitive predicates place the A index in the left personal layer. | nakagawa2024:168 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 111

The left personal layer is outside the valency-prefix layer. Applicatives
ko- , e- , and o- , the
reflexive and reciprocal prefixes yay- , si- ,
and u- , and the detransitivising prefix i- all
attach closer to the root than any personal index. The detransitivising
i- must be kept apart from the homophonous 4th-person object prefix
i= : the two occupy different positions, carry different boundary
symbols, and have distinct etymological histories; the detransitiviser is treated in
antipassive-detransitive-i . Valency morphology derives a transitive or intransitive
base from the root, and the personal indexes appear outside that derived base, indexing its
arguments tamura1972 35 ; nakagawa2024 196–199 ; sato2023b 40 .

The 4th-person A prefix shows a dialectal split within the left personal layer. Saru and the
western dialects use a= exclusively; Tokachi, Ishikari, Shiranuka, and
Samani favour an= under locally varying conditions, and Nakagawa takes
an= to be the older form nakagawa2024 167–168 . Both
shapes occupy the same left-edge position ‹consensus›.

wen kamuy a=maketa wa a=rayke ru-we ne | bad god 4.A=defeat CONJ 4.A=kill track-POSS COP | The evil kamuy was defeated and killed. | The prefix a= indexes an indefinite transitive subject; the patient is not subject-promoted. Both predicates place the 4th-person A prefix in the left personal layer. | nakagawa2024:177 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 145

The right personal layer: =as and =an

Two subject indexes appear at the right edge of the inflected verb: 1PL.EXCL
=as and 4th/indefinite =an . Both mark S on
intransitive verbs and follow any stem-internal suffix. Their transitive-subject counterparts,
ci= and a= / an= , stand in
the left personal layer. This side-switch — suffix on an intransitive S, prefix on a transitive
A — is the positional basis on which Bugaeva calls these two rows tripartite: each keeps A, S,
and O formally and positionally distinct nakagawa2024 166–167 ; bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 .

person row
S: intransitive (right-edge suffix)
A: transitive (left-edge prefix)
O (left-edge prefix)

1PL.EXCL
stem =as
ci= stem
un= stem

4 / INDEF
stem =an

a= stem (or an= stem, dialectal)

i= stem

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | The same exclusive group is A on the transitive o, marked by the prefix ci=, and S on the intransitive plural arki, marked by the suffix =as — both positions filled within a single clause chain. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

eani hem paye=an ro | 2SG even go.PL=4.S HORT | You too, let us go! | The 4th-person S suffix =an attaches to the intransitive plural stem paye. In this inclusive/hortative use the addressee is part of the referent; the hortative particle ro follows the fully inflected verb. | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 138

All other persons keep their subject index on the left. First singular
ku= marks both S and A as a prefix, opposed to the object
en= . Second-person e= and
eci= are neutral — the same form serves A, S, and O — and always
prefixal. Third person is zero in all roles. The portmanteau function of
eci= for a first-person A acting on a second-person O is a separate
rule and is developed in eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural . The right personal
layer is the exclusive domain of the two side-switching rows ‹consensus›.

Ordering with valency morphology and number suffixes

Valency-changing material and the stem-forming plural occupy positions between the root and any
right-edge person suffix. The causatives -re ,
-e , and -ka , and the stem-forming plural
-pa , all follow the root and precede =as or
=an . A causativised intransitive, for example, yields a derived stem to
which the right-edge person suffix then attaches: the personal layer is outside the valency
layer in both directions nakagawa2024 196–199 ; sato2023b 40 .

The clearest ordering diagnostic for the right edge comes from Nakagawa's minimal pair with
iki 'do'. Hokkaido has a stem-forming plural suffix
-pa that attaches to the root, and in Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari an
outer plural auxiliary pa that follows the person-inflected form. The
pair contrasts their positions directly:

nenpoka iki=an pa wa siknu=an wa | somehow do=4.S PL CONJ live=4.S CONJ | Somehow we managed to do it, and went on living. | The outer auxiliary pa follows the fully person-inflected form iki=an. It is outside the right personal layer. | nakagawa2024:159 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 87

irwak sinne sorekusu iki-pa=an | sibling one.like in.that.way do-PL=4.S | We lived like siblings. | The stem-forming plural suffix -pa precedes the person suffix =an, inside the right personal layer. The contrast with the preceding example places stem -pa inside and auxiliary pa outside. | nakagawa2024:159 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 88

Sakaguchi formalises the inside/outside distinction as a clitic diagnostic: a marker is a
clitic when it attaches to the fully person-inflected form, an affix when it attaches to the
root. He applies this to Sakhalin Ainu, where the verbal plural
=hci appears outside the inflected verb and the root-attaching plural
suffix -pa inside it sakaguchi2024 12, 77–85, 163 . His
criterion bears on the Hokkaido outer pa by extension from a
Sakhalin-focused analysis ‹contested›. The verbal number system, the
pa / ci auxiliary alternation across dialects, and
the interaction with suppletive stems are treated in person-number-interactions and
suppletive-verbal-number .

The two-index ceiling

A finite verb hosts at most one subject index and one object index. No additional personal
prefix or suffix is possible regardless of how many semantic participants the predicate
involves. The restriction is most visible in three-place predicates: with
kore 'give', recipient and theme cannot each receive an overt index.
When both are third person, neither is indexed and both surface as bare noun phrases nakagawa2024 363–365 ; sato2023b 39, 47 .

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | Both non-subject participants are third-person bare noun phrases; only the 1SG.A subject is indexed. The ceiling prevents a separate object index for either recipient or theme. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

When a non-third participant is present with a three-place predicate, the object index is
assigned to the human recipient. Satō analyses this as a secundative pattern, treating the
recipient as the selected undergoer of the indexing slot; Nakagawa frames the same verbs as
three-place predicates with two bare object phrases that may occur in varying word order, and
neither obligatorily indexed when both are third person sato2008 147 ; sato2023b 40, 47 ; sato2025a 368–371 ; nakagawa2024 363–365 ‹contested›.
Object-indexing rules and the recipient-vs-theme selection are developed in
object-indexing-monotransitive .

The ceiling also determines how reflexive and reciprocal derivations interact with personal
marking. Once yay- , si- , or
u- occupies the internal argument position as part of a derived stem,
the verb is indexed according to its derived valence; a separate object index for the same
participant would exceed the cap and is ungrammatical tamura1972 35 ; nakagawa2024 183, 196–199 . Satō uses this two-index restriction to argue that a zero third-person index must be posited
in the paradigm, so that the cap is stateable uniformly, in disagreement with Nakagawa's earlier
position that third person has no affix sato2023b 39 ; sato2025a 369 ; nakagawa2024 169 ‹contested›.

Clitic and affix status of the personal indexes

The boundary symbol = used throughout this grammar at personal-index
junctions is a notational convenience, not a settled morphological claim. Nakagawa employs
= for all person markers as a convenience mark explicitly decoupled
from phonology and syllable structure nakagawa2024 49 . Tamura and Satō write
the same markers with hyphens. Bugaeva grades the indexes into three classes on prosodic and
separability grounds bugaeva2012 472–473 :

grade
forms
diagnostic

word-like
=an , =as

may be separated from the stem by intervening particles; form autonomous
prosodic units

clitic-like
a= , eci=
inseparable; do not shift the verb's accent kernel

full prefix

ku= , ci= ,
e= , en= ,
un= , i=

inseparable and shift the accent kernel

Nakagawa arrives at a comparable assessment of =an through a different
route. His 分離人称 (separated-person) data show =an detached from its
verb by the additive particle ka or the topic marker
anak — a separability property he characterises as clitic or 倚辞 status,
possibly tracing the form to the existential verb an 'exist' nakagawa2024 183 :

tane iperusuy ka =an sinki ka =an kor | now hungry even 4.S tired even 4.S while | Now, being hungry and also tired, … | The particle ka intervenes between each intransitive stem and its =an, demonstrating the separability that distinguishes =an from the full prefixes and that Nakagawa cites for its clitic characterisation. | nakagawa2024:183 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 161

Sakaguchi's 2024 thesis on Sakhalin Ainu draws a formal distinction between affix boundaries
( - , root-attaching) and clitic boundaries ( = ,
attaching to the fully person-inflected form). Under his analysis the 4th-person
=an / an= is classified as highly autonomous and
clitic-like, while 1PL.EXCL -as is treated as a root-attaching affix —
a verdict that parts from Bugaeva, who places =as in the same word-like
class as =an sakaguchi2024 ix, 62–64, 77–85, 101, 163 ;
bugaeva2012 472–473 ‹contested›. Sakaguchi's systematic diagnostics were
developed for Sakhalin material; the only explicitly Hokkaido application in that work is the
clitic status of the outer verbal plural pa in Saru, Chitose, and
Ishikari, derived from Nakagawa's inside/outside ordering criterion. A dedicated clitic/affix
study of Hokkaido person-marking at comparable rigour has not been produced; the Hokkaido
boundary gradation rests on Bugaeva's prosodic classification and Nakagawa's separability data
‹contested›. The boundary analysis is taken further in
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

The Ijäs learner template

Ijäs's online pedagogical grammar presents the same left-right ordering for L2 learners,
grouping person indexes as the verb's obligatory argument positions and treating noun phrases
as optional outer constituents ijas2023 . The framing correctly reflects the
head-marking character of the system: the verb carries the obligatory participant indexes while
the independent pronouns are optional sato2025a 361 ; nichols1986 . Her examples are constructed teaching forms, not corpus attestations, and she writes
personal-index boundaries with hyphens as a pedagogical convention.

ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=return | I return. | Constructed following Ijäs lesson 2. She writes ku-hosipi; this grammar uses = for the personal-index boundary. The form shows 1SG.S on an intransitive stem in the left personal layer. | ijas2023 | SAR

kunneywa wakka e=ku | morning water 2SG.A=drink | You drink water in the morning. | Constructed following Ijäs lesson 3. The object is third person with no overt index; e= occupies the left personal layer as 2SG.A. She writes e-ku. | ijas2023 | SAR

Two features of the Ijäs presentation are pedagogical conventions rather than grammatical
generalisations. Her word-order guidance for ditransitives treats the indirect object as
occupying a fixed position between subject and direct object — a useful classroom rule that
does not correspond to a syntactic constraint; Nakagawa's data on three-place predicates allow
the two bare object phrases to appear in varying orders nakagawa2024 363–365 .
The zero-marking of third-person transitive arguments in her examples is factually accurate but
theoretically neutral: it is compatible with Nakagawa's position that there is no third-person
affix and with Satō's posit of a zero index required by the two-index ceiling nakagawa2024 169 ; sato2023b 39 ; sato2025a 369 ‹contested›.

title: Phasal, Iterative, and Habitual Aspect
summary: Inceptive and terminative phasal aspect via auxiliary verbs, plus iterative and habitual marking.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Phasal, Iterative, and Habitual Aspect
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: Inceptive and terminative phasal aspect via auxiliary verbs, plus iterative and habitual marking.

Phasal and iterative strategies in the auxiliary system

Three aspectual domains occupy the periphery of the core existential-aspect system. Completive
aspect — the marking that an event has been carried through to its endpoint — is expressed by
the pair verbs wa isam and wa okere , which
draw on the lexical content of isam not exist, be absent and
okere finish respectively. Iterative aspect, marking that an event or state
recurs, is expressed by the reduplicated perfective auxiliary
a … a and by the dedicated auxiliary
ranke do repeatedly, again and again . Habitual and generic readings arise from
kor an in appropriate temporal or frequentative contexts. The
recent-perfect auxiliary nisa just completed marks an event as already
finished and still current at the moment of speech.

All these strategies recruit the auxiliary and pair-verb frames whose architecture is laid out
in tam-overview . The progressive kor an and the resultative
wa an are treated as the core existential-aspect system in
existential-aspect-an-oka ; the perfective auxiliary a
and its admirative counterpart aan are treated in
perfective-anterior-a .

aspect domain
form
value
frame type

inceptive
kor an (extension)
change underway, endpoint in view
pair verb

completive (disappearance)
wa isam
action completes with subject or outcome gone
pair verb

completive (throughput)
wa okere
action carried through to its end
pair verb

iterative (reduplication)
a … a
completed event occurs repeatedly
auxiliary reduplication

iterative (auxiliary)
ranke
action done again and again
auxiliary

habitual
kor an + frequentative frame
customary or repeated action
pair verb

recent perfect
nisa
event just finished, result current
auxiliary

Completive aspect: wa isam and wa okere

Both wa isam and wa okere belong to the
pair-verb frame introduced in tam-overview : the second member remains a fully
finite verb, and both predicates in V wa V₂ carry their own subject
affixes. The bracketing is therefore
[V] VP wa [V₂] VP , formally two clauses joined by
the sequential conjunctive wa , in contrast to the bare auxiliaries
that attach directly without a conjunction
nakagawa2024 450–451 ; bugaeva2012 495 . The table below
situates the two completives alongside wa anu , which carries a
related but distinct value.

form
second verb
value
Japanese gloss
source

wa isam
isam 'not exist, be absent'
completion with disappearance or adverse outcome
〜てしまう
nakagawa2024 452 ; bugaeva2012 495

wa okere
okere 'finish'
action carried through to completion
〜てしまう / 〜おえる
nakagawa2024 452–453

wa anu
anu 'put, place'
leave the result in place (concrete objects)
〜ておく
nakagawa2024 453

wa isam marks completion of the event with the added sense that the
subject or its outcome disappears or turns out badly; the second verb
isam 'not exist, be absent' retains its concrete lexical force while
grammaticalising into the completion marker. Nakagawa's Japanese gloss is
〜てしまう , and Bugaeva gives it the label "completive"
nakagawa2024 452 ; bugaeva2012 495 .

tane rikin wa isam pe okake ta | now ascend CONJ be.gone NMLZ after LOC | Now, after (the god) had ascended and vanished, | rikin wa isam: the subject rises and then is absent — isam retains its lexical sense 'be absent'. pe okake ta nominalizes the wa isam clause and frames it temporally. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#251]

iyos onne pa wa isam | then grow.old PL CONJ be.gone | Then they grew old and passed away. | The postverbal plural clitic pa marks a plural subject; wa isam closes the sequence with the lexical force of disappearance. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8 (part 2) [aa-irc/007#438]

caricari wa isam ru-we ne kuni a=ramu kor | be.scattered CONJ be.gone track-POSS COP COMP 4.A=think while | (while she was) thinking that (things) had been scattered and vanished, | wa isam is embedded inside a nominalized complement (ru-we ne) governed by ramu 'think'; kuni is the complementizer. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 9 [aa-irc/009#127]

wa okere uses okere 'finish' as its second
predicate and foregrounds thoroughness of completion rather than disappearance of the outcome.
With stative predicates in Saru and Horobetsu, the construction shifts into an
intensive-stative reading 'be extremely Adj', a transparent extension of 'finish being in a
state' nakagawa2024 452–453 .

e=kar wa okere | 2SG.A=make CONJ finish | You finished making it. | wa okere foregrounds completion of the action; there is no sense of loss or disappearance as in wa isam.

The pair-verb form wa okere contrasts with the bare auxiliary
okere used without the conjunctive wa :
the conjunction-bearing version carries a nuance of regret or dismay at the completion,
while the bare auxiliary is unmarked for affect takahashi2022 . Nakagawa's
Table 74 lists bare okere as approximating
wa okere in meaning, confirming that the conjunctive version is the
emotionally marked option nakagawa2024 453 .

Bugaeva notes a structural parallel between the pair-verb set and Japanese aspectual
periphrases: wa isam ≈ 〜てしまう ,
wa okere ≈ 〜おえる ,
wa anu ≈ 〜ておく . Nakagawa resists a
simple calque account, pointing to wa inu 'try by hearing/tasting'
as a type that lacks a Japanese structural model
( *〜て聞く ), suggesting the pair-verb frame is native and certain
Japanese-facing semantic expansions developed under contact
bugaeva2012 495 ; nakagawa2024 455 ‹contested›.

Inceptive extensions of kor an

The progressive kor an , whose core function is described in
existential-aspect-an-oka , extends into an inceptive domain when the predicate
denotes a change of state and the temporal perspective falls near the onset of the change.
Satō's examples make the extension concrete: sirpeker kor an 'it is
getting light' describes the sky as in the process of lightening before full daylight is
established; the change is under way and its endpoint is in view
sato2008 197 . Bugaeva and Nakagawa both list the inceptive as one of the
readings available to kor an alongside the progressive core
bugaeva2012 494 ; nakagawa2024 441 .

sirpeker kor an | dawn.break while exist.SG | Dawn is breaking; it is getting light. | kor an with a change verb in its inceptive extension: the transition to daylight is ongoing and approaching its endpoint. | sato2008:197 | HK

epuykehe tane sipuske kor oka | bud-POSS now open while exist.PL | The buds are now about to open. | kor oka (plural existential) with a plural subject; the inceptive reading: the opening is under way with completion imminent. | sato2008:85 | HK

No dedicated inceptive auxiliary exists in Saru or Chitose. The inceptive reading of
kor an is driven by verb type and discourse context, not by a
distinct grammatical form. Prospective readings tied to intention or imminence —
etokus be about to , etokoyki be getting ready to ,
oasi surely will — are treated in epistemic-irrealis-modality .

Iterative aspect

The reduplicated a … a construction

The perfective auxiliary a perfective/anterior (SG) (plural
rok perfective/anterior (PL) ) reduplicates to mark iterative or
emphatic-continuative aspect. The construction V a V a signals
that the event occurred repeatedly; when the subject is fourth person, the person marker
=an migrates to the second auxiliary rather than remaining solely
on the lexical verb nakagawa2024 307–308 .

onkami=an a =an a | worship=4.S PFV =4.S PFV | (I) worshipped again and again. | Reduplicated a … a with a fourth-person subject: =an attaches to the second a. Only the person marker reduplicates with a fourth-person subject, not the full stem. | nakagawa2024:307 | SAR | ex. 453

Bugaeva labels this pattern "emphatic continuative" bugaeva2012 493 .
Ijäs, in her Lesson 9, treats prolonged continuity without connecting the pattern to the
auxiliary a ; the correspondence is established by Nakagawa
ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 307 ‹corpus-confirmed›. The full
treatment of a — its morphological source, its contrast with
aan , and its range of values — belongs to
perfective-anterior-a .

Lexical reduplication, a distinct phenomenon, operates inside roots and derivational
suffixes: -rototke encodes a violent repeating sound or act, and
-atki encodes repetition or persistence, both lexically restricted
to certain phonological shapes nakagawa2024 204–208 . That root-bound
reduplication is not a productive auxiliary strategy; it is treated alongside the other
Aktionsart suffixes in the lexical-aspect section of tam-overview .

The iterative auxiliary ranke

The auxiliary ranke do repeatedly, again and again attaches directly to
the lexical verb and marks iterative or habitual repetition:
何度も〜する nakagawa2024 312 . Bugaeva lists it
as item 5 in her Saru auxiliary inventory with the label "iterative/habitual"
bugaeva2012 493 . Like the other auxiliaries in Nakagawa's Table 74,
ranke takes no person marking; the lexical verb carries the
subject index. The following example shows ranke inserted between
the lexical verb and a progressive frame, stacking iterativity with a continuing
situation:

arpa ranke kor oka=an | go ITR while exist.PL=4.S | (They) keep going again and again. | ranke follows arpa 'go' and precedes kor oka=an; ranke takes no person marking. The combination marks repeated habitual going. | nakagawa2024:312 | SAR | exx. 471–472

The auxiliary tek (Saru/Chitose) covers adjacent ground —
brief or momentary action ('do briefly/suddenly') — and is listed alongside
ranke in Nakagawa's Table 74
nakagawa2024 309–313 . The iterative-durative overlap between
ranke and the continuative forms
kane an and kan an is addressed in
continuative-siri-siran-kane-an .

Habitual and generic aspect

Hokkaido Ainu has no dedicated habitual morpheme. Habitual and generic readings arise from a
combination of kor an , frequentative adverbs or temporal frames, and
the event-plural marker pa . The progressive and habitual are formally
identical; it is the temporal expression in the clause that licenses the habitual reading
nakagawa2024 441 ; sato2008 197 ; bugaeva2012 494 .

sittokap kor pon seta turano sinot=an kor an=an ayne | noon while little dog together play=4.S while exist=4.S as | Every noon she would play with the puppy, | sittokap kor 'whenever noon (comes)' provides the frequentative temporal frame; sinot=an kor an=an is the habitual kor an construction. ayne marks the clause as background to what follows. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 9 [aa-irc/009#14]

The postverbal plural clitic pa (the pa₂ of
suppletive-verbal-number ) contributes event-plural or pluractional force in
habitual and iterative contexts. Following a predicate whose participant plurality is already
marked by a suppletive stem, pa shifts the reading toward repetition
across occasions rather than a single plural event. The interaction of
pa with suppletive stems and the personal-agreement system is
treated in suppletive-verbal-number and person-number-interactions .

The auxiliary ranke (§ above) marks the habitual more explicitly
when the repeating character of the situation is foregrounded. Together these resources —
kor an with frequentative context, ranke , and
event-plural pa — occupy the space that a dedicated habitual
morpheme fills in languages where habituality is morphologically distinct from aspect
bybee1994 ; comrie1976 .

The recent perfect nisa

The auxiliary nisa just completed; recent perfect marks an event as already
finished and still current at the moment of speech:
すでに起こってしまったことを表す
nakagawa2024 312 . Bugaeva calls it "recent perfect aspect
nisa (just finished)" and lists it as item 7 of the Saru auxiliary
inventory bugaeva2012 493 . Nakagawa also groups nisa
with the completion and past strategies — alongside a and
wa isam — as optional devices for anchoring a situation before the
moment of utterance nakagawa2024 436 .

Like the other auxiliaries in Table 74, nisa takes no person marking;
the lexical verb before it carries the subject index. It may be followed by the evidential
frame ruwe ne , where the recent-perfect value and the
fact-asserting evidential combine naturally.

arwenkamuy ne an=an nisa ru-we ne na | truly.evil.kamuy COP exist=4.S PRF.REC track-POSS COP FIN | (I) have now truly become an evil kamuy. | nisa follows the existential an=an and precedes the evidential ruwe ne; the recent-perfect value and the fact-evidential reinforce each other. | nakagawa2024:311 | SAR | ex. 470

Within the completion field, three forms target different facets of the endpoint.
nisa foregrounds recency: the event is recent and its result is
present now. The auxiliary a foregrounds the event as a background to
what follows and carries direct-evidential force (treated in
perfective-anterior-a ). wa isam foregrounds
disappearance and belongs to the pair-verb frame; it adds the affective colouring of loss or
adverse outcome. Unlike wa isam , nisa is a
bare auxiliary — no conjunctive particle, no second person-indexed predicate — so the
construction belongs to the auxiliary frame, not to the pair-verb frame.

The surface token nisa returns 3,355 hits in the present corpus of
196,184 sentences (rank 71 by frequency), but the token is homophone-prone — bare nouns and
other elements sharing this phonological shape are not systematically distinguished at the
surface-string level — so the count is indicative of high frequency rather than an accurate
auxiliary tally.

title: Phrasal and Utterance Intonation
summary: Intonational contours above the word: declarative vs interrogative tunes, phrasing, and an f0 corpus study.
part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Phrasal and Utterance Intonation
Part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
Summary: Intonational contours above the word: declarative vs interrogative tunes, phrasing, and an f0 corpus study.

Rising-nucleus accent in utterance context

Hokkaido Ainu has a pitch-accent system in which the phonologically distinctive unit is the
syllable at which F0 rises — a rising-nucleus (上り核アクセント) system — in
contrast with Standard Japanese, whose falling-nucleus system marks the syllable at which F0
subsequently drops nakagawa2024 48–49 ; shiraishi2022 §5 .
The word-level placement rules for the accent nucleus are stated in
pitch-accent-placement-rule ; the theoretical question of whether the system is
best analysed as culminative accent or as an autosegmental tonal melody is addressed in
mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .

The rising-nucleus property means that F0 may remain high or drop after the accented syllable
without changing the lexical identity of a word — only the position of the rise is
phonologically distinctive. For the Saru minimal pair nísap 'suddenly'
vs nisáp 'shin', nísap may be realized with
sustained high pitch on both syllables ( nísáp ) without shifting to
nisáp ; only a realization in which the lowest F0 point falls at σ1
would misidentify the word nakagawa2024 48–49 . Community discussion
illustrates the same point with the pronoun ku=ani 'I', whose accent
falls on á (σ2, by regular rule): ku↗ani and ku↗a↘ni are both
acceptable; ku↘ani, with the F0 minimum at the accented syllable, is not
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-09).

Accent minimal pairs (Saru): position of the F0 rise (↗ marks the nucleus)

Form A
accent
meaning
Form B
accent
meaning

nísap
↗ σ1 (exception)
'suddenly'
nisáp
↗ σ2 (regular CV#)
'shin'

nína
↗ σ1 (exception)
'gather firewood'
niná
↗ σ2 (regular)
'crush'

útur
↗ σ1 (exception)
'western side of house'
utúr
↗ σ2 (regular)
'between'

( nakagawa2024 51 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 .) Words in the
exception class — which includes nísap , nína ,
húre 'red', sísam 'Japanese person', and a
score of others — have σ1 open (CV) yet accent σ1 instead of the regular σ2. Their Sakhalin
cognates show a corresponding long vowel at σ1: ‹SA› niisah vs
nisah , huure , siisam
(Nakagawa 2024: 27, 51). The diachronic relationship between Hokkaido accent and Sakhalin
vowel length is discussed in hokkaido-phonological-microvariation ;
additional minimal pairs are catalogued in lexical-contrastive-accent-minimal-pairs .

The accented syllable is slightly lengthened in polysyllabic words, more markedly in
north-eastern dialects (Asai 1972/1976, reported via shiraishi2022 §5 ). This
allophonic lengthening explains older Japanese-script representations such as ユーカラ for
yukar , where the long vowel corresponds to the accent on σ1
nakagawa2024 26 .

The accent-dialect split

Lexically contrastive pitch is found only in the accent dialects. Nakagawa and Fukazawa
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 distinguish three prosodic classes: the
standard-accent dialects (Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, Asahikawa, Nayoro, Obihiro,
Sōya), the irregular-accent dialects (Yakumo, Oshamambe, where the accent shifts one
syllable rightward when both σ1 and σ2 are open), and the unaccented
(無アクセント) varieties (Shizunai, Urakawa, Samani, Bihoro, Kushiro, Shiranuka), in which
pitch is not lexically distinctive nakagawa2024 49–50 . The dialect
classification is treated in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry .
The utterance-intonation descriptions below rest on data from Saru and, to a lesser extent,
Chitose; their applicability to the unaccented varieties is undocumented.

Declarative and continuation contours

In Saru declarative utterances, F0 follows a natural declining course from the beginning to the
end of the utterance, without a sentence-final intonation gesture — neither a terminal rise
nor a marked fall (Tamura 1996: 4, reported via shiraishi2022 §7 ). Acoustic
measurements on Saru speech confirm this pattern (Tamura and Motohashi 2002b; Makarova 2002,
both reported via shiraishi2022 §7 ). Within this declination, the successive
rising-nucleus accents of individual words produce a series of local F0 elevations against a
gradually lowering baseline.

Simeon (1968, reported via shiraishi2022 §7 ) characterizes the prosodic
status of non-final clause boundaries in Ainu: a level or falling contour signals that the
utterance is not yet complete and more follows, while a rising terminal marks the utterance as
prosodically finished. Under this description the default Saru declarative — ending with
gradual declination but no added terminal gesture — has the profile of a continuation contour,
and a clause that ends with a terminal rise (as in a polar interrogative or an exclamatory
utterance) is the marked, utterance-final form.

Interrogative contours and sentence-final particles

Polar interrogatives in Saru carry a rising final intonation, with the F0 and intensity
peak localized on the sentence-final particle. An acoustic study of 35 Saru interrogative
utterances found that the peak falls on the last word, normally one of the interrogative
particles hawe , ya ,
a , or he (Makarova 2002, reported via
shiraishi2022 §7 ). Tamura's Saru description extends the rising pattern to
any utterance in which the speaker expects a reaction — a question, a report from the
listener, a request, or an invitation (Tamura 1996: 4, reported via
shiraishi2022 §7 ). The sentence-final particles themselves are described in
sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force ; interrogative strategies and the
question particles are treated in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

One exception: the particle ya appears to select a falling rather than
a rising contour in some Saru data. A form transcribed as
amkir_a (from amkir ya 'have you been?') was
realized with falling pitch at the utterance end (Tamura and Motohashi 2002b, reported via
shiraishi2022 §7 ). A contact explanation is plausible: Hokkaido Japanese
uses falling intonation even in yes/no questions, and this influence has been suggested for
some falling interrogative realizations in Ainu
shiraishi2022 §7 ‹contested›. Whether the falling realization of
ya -questions is a property of the particle, a speaker variable, or a
contact phenomenon is not settled on current evidence.

A further contour type is documented when the sentence ends in a nominalized or conjunctive
form without a final predicate verb. In such cases the final F0 is level or falling, and the
utterance communicates incompleteness or a request for confirmation rather than a settled
assertion ( shiraishi2022 §7 , drawing on Tokachi data). This aligns with the
Simeon generalization noted above: absence of a terminal rise leaves the utterance
prosodically open.

Utterance type and final F0 contour (Saru; based on shiraishi2022 §7
and Tamura 1996: 4)

utterance type
terminal F0
notes

Declarative
↘ gradual declination; no added terminal gesture
default; speaker asserts without expecting a reaction

Interrogative (particles he ,
a , hawe )

↗ rise on the final particle
F0/intensity peak on the last word

Particle ya
↘ falling at utterance end
exception; possibly Japanese-contact ‹contested›

Exclamatory / wish
↑ whole-utterance F0 elevation; ↗↘ terminal
elevated register throughout; lengthened final rise-fall

Nominalized / conjunctive sentence-final
level or ↘
communicates incompleteness or request for confirmation

Exclamatory and wish prosody

Exclamatory and optative utterances in Saru are produced at a pitch level elevated above the
normal declarative baseline throughout, with the final syllable or syllables carrying a
lengthened rise-fall (Tamura 1996: 4, reported via shiraishi2022 §7 ). The
elevation is applied to the whole utterance, not only to the final position, so it interacts
with the lexical accents of each word without displacing them. The following Chitose example,
drawn from Nakagawa's grammar, illustrates the construction:

kera=an hum-i | delicious=4.S sound-POSS | How delicious! | Exclamatory utterance. The whole pitch level is elevated above the declarative baseline, and the final syllable carries a lengthened rise-fall. The formal noun hum-i, possessed form of hum 'sound, feeling', functions as an exclamative complement to the finite clause kera=an 'it is delicious'. Tamura 1996: 4 (reported via Shiraishi 2022: §7) characterizes this pattern; the form keraan shows vowel sequence from kera + =an without glide insertion (Nakagawa 2024: 43). | nakagawa2024:395 | CHI

The same elevated-register pattern extends to optative and invitation utterances, which share
the whole-utterance F0 raise without necessarily carrying the lengthened final gesture.

Phrasal grouping and prosodic domains

No published instrumental study addresses intermediate prosodic phrasing in Hokkaido Ainu —
the question of whether there are prosodic phrase boundaries between the word and the full
utterance at which F0 resets, phrase-final lengthening occurs, or pauses are inserted
systematically. The available descriptions treat the utterance as the domain of F0 declination
and the prosodic word as the domain of accent.

Two word-level prosodic facts bear on this question. First, the person-affix paradigm
distinguishes clitics by their degree of prosodic integration with the following stem.
Affixes ku= , e= , and
ci= form a single prosodic word with their host and shift the stem's
accent; a= and eci= do not shift accent and
are prosodically less integrated (Kindaichi 1931; Tamura 1970, both reported via
shiraishi2022 §5.1 ; nakagawa2024 52–53 ). This clitic-class
distinction is treated in person-marking-architecture and
accent-in-compounds-and-affixation ; it constitutes a gradient of prosodic
bonding that may correlate with phrase-boundary placement in connected speech.

Second, personal suffixes such as =as (exclusive 1pl) and
=an (4th person S) carry a secondary accent alongside the primary
stem accent in open-syllable stems: iˈruska=ˌas 'we (excl.) are
angry', ipˈe=ˌan (Kindaichi 1931; Tamura 1970, reported via
shiraishi2022 §5.1 ). The secondary accent is lost when the stem ends in a
closed syllable: ˈcis=an 'we cry', ˈsap=an .
The prosodic word template and the suffix interactions are treated in
verb-word-template .

Typological comparison: Hachijō intonation

Community discussion of Ainu prosody has noted that the intonation patterns of Hachijō-shima
Japanese feel "very close to Ainu" ( aynucorporadiscord nukopoli, 2024-12-08).
Hachijō Japanese is known from the Japanese dialectological literature to have a rising-nucleus
pitch-accent system, making it structurally parallel to the Hokkaido Ainu standard-accent
dialects and distinct from the falling-nucleus system of Standard Japanese. The perceived
intonational resemblance is therefore typologically plausible — both languages share the
consequence that the declarative default involves gradual F0 decline without a marked terminal
gesture, and that interrogative intonation involves a terminal rise — but no published
comparative acoustic study of Hachijō and Ainu intonation has been consulted for this grammar.
The observation is reported here at community grade: asserted and typologically grounded, but
awaiting instrumental verification ‹corpus-suggested›.

title: The Pitch-Accent Placement Rule and the Accented/Accentless Dialect Split
summary: States the synchronic high-pitch placement generalization and divides Hokkaido into accented and accentless dialect classes.
part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Pitch-Accent Placement Rule and the Accented/Accentless Dialect Split
Part: Part IV — Prosody and the Pitch-Accent System
Summary: States the synchronic high-pitch placement generalization and divides Hokkaido into accented and accentless dialect classes.

The rising-nucleus system

Hokkaido Ainu has a pitch-accent system in which the prominent syllable is marked by a pitch
rise , not a pitch fall. The theoretical distinction matters because Standard Japanese
pitch accent is analysed as a falling nucleus (下り核) — the distinctive cue is where
pitch drops — whereas Ainu has a rising nucleus (上り核アクセント) — the distinctive
cue is where pitch rises from low to high
nakagawa2024 48–49 ; shiraishi2022 §5 . A word's accent identity
is therefore fixed by the syllable at which the L→H transition occurs; post-nuclear syllables
may continue high, or fall at the utterance edge, without changing the word's accent class.

The contrast is illustrated by the near-minimal pair
nísap 'suddenly' (accent on σ1) vs
nisáp 'shin' (accent on σ2).
nísap can be produced with both syllables at high pitch ( nísáp )
without changing its identity, because the rising-nucleus cue is already satisfied at σ1; the
same flat-high production of nisáp would be heard as 'suddenly', since the
σ2-rise is missing nakagawa2024 48–49 . The Ainu accent system thus records where
pitch begins to be high; the Japanese system records where it ceases to be high.

Accent is marked in this grammar with an acute diacritic on the vowel of the prominent syllable:
kamúy (accent on σ2), áynu (accent on σ1). The
same event is representable with a rise arrow: ka↗muy ,
↗áynu . In IPA, a high-tone mark (◌́) on the nucleus of the accented
syllable expresses the same. Community participants summarize the typological point concisely:
ku↗a–ni and ku↗a↘ni are equivalent
pronunciations of the first-person pronoun; ku↘ani is deviant
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-09; corroborated by
nakagawa2024 48–49 ). Accent marks appear in citation forms and interlinear
examples throughout this grammar and are suppressed in running text.

The theoretical question of whether the system is better modelled as a culminative accent (one H
per prosodic word) or as an autosegmental tonal melody (L…LH…) is examined in
mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis . Intonation and sentence-level prosody are
treated in phrasal-and-utterance-intonation .

Dialect classes

The pitch-accent placement rules described in this chapter apply to the
standard-accent dialects. Hokkaido Ainu divides into three prosodic classes
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 ; nakagawa2024 49–50 :

class
dialects
accent behavior

Standard accent
Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, Asahikawa, Nayoro, Obihiro, Sōya
Rules 1 and 2 (below) apply as stated

Irregular accent
Yakumo, Oshamambe
Rule 2 shifts one syllable rightward when both σ1 and σ2 are open (σ3 high in that case)

Unaccented (無アクセント)
Shizunai, Urakawa, Samani, Bihoro, Kushiro, Shiranuka
Pitch is not lexically distinctive

Nakagawa (2024: 49–50) includes Bihoro, Shizunai, and the eastern Hidaka localities (Samani,
Urakawa) among the accentless varieties. Refsing (1986: 73) had earlier classified Shizunai as
an accent dialect with rules analogous to Saru; archival evidence — scattered accent marks from a
single Shizunai speaker showing both nisáppone and
nísappone for 'suddenly' — supports Nakagawa's accentless
classification ‹contested›. Unaccented varieties and the phonological consequences of
accent loss are treated in hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

The placement rule

In standard-accent dialects, the position of the accent depends entirely on the structure of
σ1 — the first syllable of the prosodic word — regardless of total word length
nakagawa2024 50 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 ; sato2008 §2.6 .
Two rules cover the system:

Rule 1: If σ1 is closed — i.e. has the shape (C)VC — σ1 bears the accent.

Rule 2: If σ1 is open — i.e. has the shape (C)V — σ2 bears the accent.

Shiraishi (2022: §5.1) recasts these as an iambic (LH) pattern : a light (open) first
syllable predicts accent on σ2; a heavy (closed) first syllable predicts accent on σ1.
Kindaichi and Chiri reached the same characterization: "the iambic rule is dominant" (Kindaichi
& Chiri 1936: 11, reported via shiraishi2022 §5.1 ).

σ1 shape
accent on
examples
gloss

Closed (C)VC
σ1

áynu , pónno ,
kútci , sínrit ,
téyne

'person', 'much', 'shoes', 'always', 'true/real'

Open (C)V
σ2

kamúy , reyé ,
itánki , menóko ,
irámmakaka

'deity', 'younger sibling', 'bowl', 'woman', 'be startled'

The rule is strictly local to σ1: no further syllable weight is consulted. A word of any length
whose first syllable is closed carries σ1-accent
( yáykosiramsuypa , nine syllables but σ1 = yay
[CVC] → σ1-accent); an open σ1 gives σ2-accent regardless of what follows
nakagawa2024 50 .

In Yakumo , Rule 2 applies one syllable further rightward when both σ1 and σ2
are open: standard notákam corresponds to Yakumo
notakám ; standard sinúye to Yakumo
sinuyé ; standard hoyúpu to Yakumo
hoyupú . When σ2 is closed, Yakumo and standard dialects agree
( parúnpe in both). Some Yakumo forms are additionally exceptional
( hopúni , osórusi )
nakagawa2024 50–51 ; nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 .

Accent placement in compounds and under person-marking prefixes, where the rules reapply over
the enlarged prosodic word, is covered in accent-in-compounds-and-affixation .
The syllable shapes that feed these rules — closed vs open, and the consonant-status of coda
-y and -w — are established in
syllable-template .

The pitch contour

The phonetic realization of the accent in standard-accent dialects follows a consistent contour:
syllables preceding the accent syllable are at low pitch; from the accent syllable onward pitch
is high. In connected speech a fall at the utterance boundary is typical, but this is a phrasal
boundary effect and is not the cue that identifies the accent class. What is contrastive is
solely the location of the L→H transition
nakagawa2024 48–49 ; shiraishi2022 §5 .

For a two-syllable word with σ2-accent, such as kamúy [ka(L) ↗muy(H)],
the first syllable is produced at a low pitch and the rise occurs at the onset of σ2. For a
word with σ1-accent, such as áynu [↗áy(H) nu(H or ↘)], the pitch is
high from the start of the word. The community characterization of the overall pattern —
syllables before the nucleus are low; from the nucleus onward pitch stays high; a fall occurs
toward the end of the prosodic phrase — contrasts with the Tokyo Japanese pattern in which the
diagnostic event is a drop after the nucleus
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-09;
corroborated by nakagawa2024 48–49 ) ‹corpus-suggested›.

In some northeastern dialects, the accented vowel is slightly lengthened, an observation that
underlies earlier romanization conventions such as ユーカラ for
yukar (accent on yu , hence formerly written with
a long vowel: Nakagawa 2024: 26, reported via nakagawa2024 26 ). Phonemic vowel
length is not otherwise contrastive in Hokkaido Ainu; the vowel inventory is discussed in
vowel-inventory .

Lexical exceptions

A set of words with open σ1 carry accent on σ1 despite the prediction of Rule 2 (open σ1 →
σ2-accent). These must be stored in the lexicon; they cannot be derived from the placement
rule. Nakagawa (2024: 51) lists the following as the principal exceptions in Saru/Chitose:

cápe 'cat', húci 'grandmother',
húra 'smell', húre 'red',
káni 'I (free pronoun)', kéra 'taste',
míci 'father', mína 'laugh',
nína 'gather firewood', nísap 'suddenly',
núman 'yesterday', páse 'heavy',
sápo 'elder sister', sések 'hot',
síno 'really', sísam 'Japanese person',
túki 'cup', túnas 'fast',
úsey 'hot water', yúpo 'elder brother'.

Several exceptions pair minimally or near-minimally with regular-rule forms:

form
accent
gloss
type

nísap
σ1
'suddenly'
exception

nisáp
σ2
'shin'
regular

nína
σ1
'gather firewood'
exception

niná
σ2
'crush'
regular

útur
σ1
'west side of the house'
exception

utúr
σ2
'between'
regular

( nakagawa2024 51 ; shiraishi2022 §5.1 .)

The exceptions are not random: each corresponds to a Sakhalin Ainu form with a long first vowel
(see the following section), and as a group they reflect the older vowel-length contrast that
has been restructured as lexical accent in Hokkaido. A quantitative analysis of the
exception set and its contrastive residue against the rule-conforming majority is given in
lexical-contrastive-accent-minimal-pairs .

The Sakhalin vowel-length correspondence

Hokkaido Ainu pitch accent stands in a systematic correspondence with Sakhalin Ainu vowel
length: a Hokkaido word with accent on σ1 (including exceptions to Rule 2) has a long vowel in
σ1 in Sakhalin; a Hokkaido word with accent on σ2 (regular Rule 2 output) has a short vowel in
σ1 in Sakhalin
nakagawa2024 27, 51 ; nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 .

Hokkaido (HA)
accent
Sakhalin (SA)
gloss

húre
σ1 (exception)
huure
'red'

sísam
σ1 (exception)
siisam
'Japanese person'

túki
σ1 (exception)
tuuki
'cup'

nísap
σ1 (exception)
niisah
'suddenly'

nisáp
σ2 (regular)
nisah
'shin'

(SA forms from nakagawa2024 27, 51 .)

Hattori (1967, reported via nakagawa2024 27 and
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 ) interprets the Sakhalin vowel-length
opposition as diachronically prior, with Hokkaido pitch accent having developed from it; the
diachronic relationship is argued in detail by itabashi2001 (not consulted
directly). Satō dissents on the direction of the change
( nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 ) ‹contested›. The Hokkaido accentless
varieties listed in the preceding section represent a third path: they have lost the length
opposition without acquiring pitch contrast, and are not converging on the standard-accent
system (Nakagawa & Fukazawa 2022 §3.1, reported via
nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 ). Sakhalin vowel length and its relationship
to the Hokkaido vowel inventory are discussed in vowel-inventory .

title: The Elevated/Poetic Register: Archaic Morphology and Formulaic Diction
summary: The grammar and lexicon of the elevated (雅語) register — archaic forms, verse-restricted morphology, and fixed formulae.
part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Elevated/Poetic Register: Archaic Morphology and Formulaic Diction
Part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
Summary: The grammar and lexicon of the elevated (雅語) register — archaic forms, verse-restricted morphology, and fixed formulae.

Overview: atomte-itak and yayan-itak

Hokkaido Ainu distinguishes two named registers:
atomte-itak (アトゥㇺテイタㇰ, lit. 'decorated/ornamented
speech'; Japanese 雅語, the elegant or poetic register) and
yayan-itak (ヤヤニタㇰ, lit. 'ordinary speech'; 口語/日常語,
the everyday register) endo2022 §3 ; kindaichi1935 .
The term atomte-itak is attested in metalinguistic use by
native speakers and in modern community discussion, where it is explicitly applied
to the speech of verse and prayer genres
aynucorporadiscord (成田英敏 / asirso, 2024). The distinction is
grammar-diagnostic across four dimensions: (1) applicative prefixes replace
postpositional case particles; (2) the fourth-person suffix
=an co-occurs with singular verb stems that exclude it in
conversation; (3) words are longer and more morphologically elaborated; (4) a
verse-specific vocabulary replaces everyday lexemes
sato2008 263–264 ; endo2022 §3 .

The boundary between the two registers is not categorical. Nakagawa argues that
elegant and everyday Ainu are stages on a continuum keyed to two axes —
verse vs prose, and fixed vs improvised — rather than two discrete levels
‹contested› endo2022 §3.4 . He further proposes that a
classification by genre (yukar-vocabulary vs prayer-vocabulary) may prove more
analytically useful than the single-level
atomte-itak label endo2022 §3.4 . Genre
taxonomy and the performance context of each verse genre are described in
oral-literature-genre-taxonomy ; the metrical and refrain structure
of verse in sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure ; the parallel
and formulaic rhetoric of verse in parallelism-couplets-and-word-pairs .

Genre density and the elegant-language gradient

Kindaichi established that elegant-language density varies across chant and verse
genres in a consistent gradient, rising from greeting speech through prayer
( kamuy nomi itak ) and incantation to dispute
negotiation ( caranke ), with the heroic epic
( yukar ) carrying the highest concentration of
atomte-itak features
kindaichi1935 ; endo2022 §3 . Prose folktale
( uwepeker ) falls largely in the everyday register;
the sub-genre of divine-protagonist prose tales
( kamuy uwepeker ) is an exception, narrated in elegant
language throughout endo2022 §3 .

Two parameters drive the gradient. The first is fixity: incantations are
word-for-word memorised and deploy dense elegant morphology; prayers are
improvised — "not memorised like scripture; spun out by improvisation,
different each time", in Nakagawa's characterisation
endo2022 §2.3 — yet draw on the same feature inventory with
more personal melody. The second is the verse/prose divide: all melodic,
sung genres (yukar, kamuy yukar, prayer) signal their generic status partly
through grammar, not only through melody and metre
sato2008 259–264 . The heroic and divine epics are described
in heroic-and-divine-verse . The formal/ceremonial evidential frame
ruwe tapan , which Tamura's dictionary registers as
register-marked, is one illustration of how elegance inflects evidential choice
across genres; it is analysed in ruwe-ne-inferential and situated
among genre-conditioned TAM choices in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Grammatical features of atomte-itak

Satō lists four grammatical hallmarks of the poetic register
sato2008 263–264 , which align with Nakagawa's four defining
traits of atomte-itak as reported in Endō
endo2022 §3 : (1) applicative case-marking in place of
particles; (2) number-agreement override; (3) greater polysynthetic complexity
and periphrasis; (4) a distinct verse/prayer lexicon. Features (1) and (2) are
directly grammar-diagnostic.

Applicative case-marking

In everyday speech, oblique relations — location, direction, goal — are
expressed by postpositional particles: ta (locative),
un (allative), and others. In
atomte-itak , the same relations are instead encoded
by applicative prefixes on the verb:
e- goal/locative applicative ,
ko- sociative/goal applicative ,
o- body-contact applicative . The register diagnostic is the
substitution of prefix for particle, which simultaneously increases syllabic
weight — a functional fit with both the metrical preference for longer units
and the 5-syllable line
sato2008 263 ; okuda2022 §3 .

tanto Ukurmem ta ku=sirepa | today Ukurmem LOC 1SG.S=arrive | I arrived at Ukurmem today. | Everyday (yayan-itak) pattern: locative marked by the particle ta. After Satō 2008: 263. | CHI

kamuy menoko uni-hi tane anakne a=ko-sirepa na | goddess woman house-POSS today TOP 4.A=APPL-arrive FIN | Now I arrived at the goddess's house. | Elegant (atomte-itak) pattern: the locative goal is marked by the applicative prefix ko- on the verb in place of the particle ta. The fourth-person prefix a= marks the hero-narrator. After Satō 2008: 263, ex. 20. | CHI

Prayer text is particularly applicative-dense. Endō's example from a
Monbetsu prayer (鍋澤モトゾウ / Motoanrek) shows stacked applicative layers
within a single verb: ku=e-ko-pase- and
k=e-ko-onkami '(I) reverently worship'
endo2022 §2.3 . The applicative morphemes and their
stacking interactions are described in
applicative-system-overview ,
applicative-e , and applicative-ko .

Number-agreement override

In Saru and Chitose everyday speech, the fourth-person intransitive suffix
=an attaches only to plural verb stems: suppletive
plural arki 'come.PL' gives
arki=an 'we/they come', while singular
ek 'come.SG' does not combine with
=an in conversation
sato2008 263 . In the poetic register this restriction is
suspended: the hero-narrator uses the singular stem together with
=an , yielding ek=an 'I came',
a formulaic feature of first-person verse narration throughout Saru and
Chitose ‹consensus›.

ek=an | come.SG=4.S | I came. | Elegant number-agreement override: the singular stem ek combines with the fourth-person suffix =an. In everyday Saru/Chitose this combination is blocked; arki=an (plural stem) is the everyday fourth-person plural form. After Satō 2008: 263. | SAR

The override is confined to varieties that use
a= / =an for the hero-narrator
(Saru, Chitose). Outside those varieties,
=an attaches only to plural stems without exception,
so this pattern is simultaneously a register and a dialect marker
sato2008 263 . Suppletive plural verb pairs are described in
suppletive-verbal-number ; the fourth-person paradigm in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Polysynthetic expansion and periphrasis

The third hallmark is the formation of morphologically heavier words than
everyday speech permits, achieved partly through periphrasis — spreading an
everyday lexeme across a complex verbal construction. Two attested
examples from Satō illustrate the device
sato2008 264 :

everyday ( yayan-itak )
elegant ( atomte-itak )
gloss

soyne
soyna-sanma o=si-ray-e
'go outside'

kamuy
kamuy ne manu p
'god; a divine being'

The periphrasis kamuy ne manu p (lit. 'what is said
to be a god') inserts an evidential distancing element
( manu 'it is said/alleged') between predicate and
nominalized head, simultaneously filling the 5-syllable metrical target and
conferring a deferential, distanced quality on divine reference. Satō also
documents the poetic use of kane 'as if, to the extent
that' as a simile device in the Abuta (虻田/Iburi) yukar of narrator
遠島タンケ — urar ruyka a=turi kane 'stretching the mist
as if it were a bridge' (l. 136) — where kane shades
from its circumstantial into a comparative-poetic value
sato2009yukar §2.2 . The interaction of periphrasis with
couplet structure is treated in
parallelism-couplets-and-word-pairs .

The hero-narrator person system

The person set used for the narrative hero is the most salient grammatical
marker of verse register. All three primary narrative genres are performed from
the protagonist's viewpoint in the first person, but the affix set used for the
hero differs from everyday ku= / =en .
The full paradigm and its logophoric analysis are treated in
person-marking-architecture and
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse ; what concerns
this chapter is the register conditionality of the choice.

In Saru and Chitose, the hero-narrator uses the fourth-person prefix
a= and suffix =an rather than
the everyday first person sato2008 260–261 ;
endo2022 §2.4 . A framing utterance outside the narration uses
ordinary ku= :

sine kamuy-yukar ku=ye so | one divine.epic 1SG.A=tell SOFT | Let me tell one divine epic. | Speaker's framing utterance uses everyday first person ku=. Inside the narration, the hero-narrator switches to the fourth-person a=/=an. From the reading-text frame in Satō 2008, chapter 21 (千歳). | sato2008 | CHI

Satō explains the hero's non-everyday person marking through what he terms the
"indirection principle" ( 間接性の原理 ): oral literature was
framed — in his hypothesis — as a god's telling inside a dream, and the
avoidance of ordinary first-person marking increases indirectness, softening the
taboo of a divine being addressing a living human directly
‹speculative› sato2008 259–261 . The competing "shamaness-oracle
origin" account ( 巫女の託宣 ), which Satō dissents from, locates
the origin in shamanic ritual; neither is established ‹contested›
sato2008 259 ; endo2022 §2.4 .

The Iburi kamuy yukar collected by Chiri Yukie — chiri1923 , the
first published Ainu-language oral-literature collection — uses a distinct
convention: the hero-narrator employs the exclusive first-person plural
ci= / =as consistently as a
singular, throughout each text sato2008 261 ;
sato2004 . In Chitose, ci= may appear at the
opening and then give way to a= / =an
sato2008 261 . The hero-person marker is therefore
simultaneously genre-conditioned (narrative verse, not prayer or conversation),
dialect-conditioned (Saru/Chitose vs Iburi), and register-conditioned (verse
or verse-grade prose, not everyday speech) ‹consensus›.

The pronoun set of elegant language likewise draws on the fourth-person and
honorific domain: Satō lists asinuma ,
aoka(y) , eani , and
ecioka(y) as the elegant-register free-pronoun set, the
same forms that function as second-person honorifics in conversation
sato2008 261 . The convergence of elevated and honorific function
in the fourth-person paradigm is described in
fourth-person-honorific .

The register lexicon

The distinctive verse and prayer vocabulary is documented across the sources consulted
in scattered but consistent testimony. The table below collects attested
everyday–elegant word pairs with genre specification and source attribution.

everyday ( yayan-itak )
elegant / genre-specific ( atomte-itak )
reference gloss
genre restriction
source

hapo / haca

(colloquial; heavy dialect variation)

ona 'father',
unu 'mother'

parental terms
verse / prayer (文語的雅語)

endo2022 §3 ;
nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord (2026-06-15, asserted)

soyne 'go outside'
soyna-sanma o=si-ray-e
'go outside' (periphrastic)
heroic / divine epic
sato2008 264

kamuy 'god'

kamuy ne manu p

(lit. 'what is said to be a god')

'god' (periphrastic, deferential)
heroic / divine epic
sato2008 264

ape huci

'fire grandmother'

i-resu huci /
i-resu kamuy

'(the goddess) who raised me'

fire deity
prayer (not heroic/divine epic)
endo2022 §1, §3.4

ape huci

mosir kor huci

'grandmother who holds the land'

fire deity (address epithet)
prayer only (not attested elsewhere)
endo2022 §2.3

kamuy 'god' (noun)

kamuy as modifier
= 'splendid, magnificent'

intensifying adnominal
heroic epic (absent from prayer)
endo2022 §3.4

The elegant parental terms ona 'father' and
unu 'mother' are confirmed as literary register forms
by community discussion: nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-06-15) asserts
that both are 文語的雅語 contrasting with colloquial
haca / hapo , and that the plural
forms onautari / unuutari can extend
to mean 'parents together' aynucorporadiscord . The observation
aligns with Endō's characterisation of a distinct verse lexicon (§3 characteristic 4)
without being independently stated there ‹corpus-suggested›. The possessed-form
morphology of forms like i-resu huci
('the-one-who-raised-me grandmother') is described in
kinship-honorific-possession .

The fire-deity substitutions reveal a genre division within
atomte-itak itself. The everyday address
ape huci is the default in heroic and divine epic;
prayer substitutes i-resu huci , foregrounding the
personal bond between worshipper and deity, and adds the prayer-exclusive epithet
mosir kor huci 'grandmother who holds the land',
documented for the Monbetsu (Biratori) prayer tradition and described as absent
from every other genre endo2022 §2.3 . Conversely, the
adnominal kamuy = 'splendid' appears in heroic epic but
is absent from prayer, where a deferential petitionary address would not employ
such a modifier endo2022 §3.4 . The taboo and honorific
extensions of the prayer address system are treated in
honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers .

Within the verse lexicon, hero-praise formulae are also genre-specific.
The formula tuk-no pinni si-kopa-yar aynu pito
'a fine person like a grown Manchurian-ash tree' — containing
si-kopa-yar 'like', a form not attested in everyday
speech — is used for hero description in heroic and divine epics and is not
productive outside that genre endo2022 §4 .

Prayer as a sub-register of atomte-itak

Prayer ( kamuy nomi itak ,
inonno itak ) is improvised rather than memorised, which
distinguishes it formally from incantation (fixed word-for-word) and from the
more formulaic yukar. Despite this improvisation, prayer deploys
atomte-itak features throughout: heavy applicative-prefix
stacking in place of postpositions, the elegant vocabulary set, and verse-grade
prosody endo2022 §2.3 ; sato2008 263 .

The address structure of prayer is genre-governed and register-distinctive in
its own right: the fire deity ( ape huci /
i-resu huci ) is addressed first in every prayer, acting
as intermediary to the other gods endo2022 §2.3 . Genre-conditioned
connective inventory is an additional sub-register signal: Endō notes that
conjunctive-phrase distributions differ across heroic epic, kamuy yukar, and prayer
in ways that go beyond vocabulary differences (Endō 2015, as cited in
endo2022 §3.4 ). Prayer is the domain in which the restriction
of kamuy as 'splendid' is blocked (§ register lexicon above),
and in which the applicative stacking is densest. The sentence-final particles
associated with formal and ceremonial illocution in prayer are treated in
sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force .

Debates

Register as dichotomy or continuum. Kindaichi's framing treats
elegant and everyday as two discrete levels
kindaichi1935 ; endo2022 §3 . Nakagawa dissents: the
gradient runs continuously through multiple genres, and a
yukar-vocabulary/prayer-vocabulary classification may be more tractable than the
single atomte-itak rubric
endo2022 §3.4 ‹contested›. The practical implication is that
claims about "elegant language" should specify which genre is the source.

Origin of non-everyday person marking in verse. Two accounts
compete for the historical motivation behind the hero's use of fourth-person
affixes: the "shamaness-oracle origin" theory (the consensus framing) and
Satō's "indirection/dream-framing" hypothesis. Both treat the fourth-person
marking as motivated by an avoidance principle, but their reference to
different social institutions makes them empirically distinct
sato2008 259–261 ; endo2022 §2.4 ‹contested›.
The honorific dimension of the fourth-person paradigm that underlies both
accounts is described in fourth-person-honorific .

Residual refrain in yukar. The opening formula
eyororpe in yukar is read by Satō as a vestige of a
former refrain, suggesting that heroic and divine epics diverged from a
common refrain-bearing ancestor sato2008 259 ‹speculative›.
The claim is an inference, not independently supported by text-comparative
evidence. The sakehe system is described in
sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure .

title: Postpositions, Local Case, Motion Events, Comitative, and Instrumental
summary: Postpositions proper and the local-case relations they encode, motion-event/path encoding, and comitative tura and instrumental ani/ari on the postposition-to-applicative continuum.
part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Postpositions, Local Case, Motion Events, Comitative, and Instrumental
Part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
Summary: Postpositions proper and the local-case relations they encode, motion-event/path encoding, and comitative tura and instrumental ani/ari on the postposition-to-applicative continuum.

Two classes of adpositional elements

Hokkaido Ainu marks local relations — location, source, goal, and path — through two
formally distinct classes. Satō groups both under the label postpositions
(後置詞) and draws a sharp internal split: case particles (格助詞) require a
preceding locative nominal (a ⟨place⟩-type noun) and are bound to it; postpositional
adverbs (後置副詞) are distributionally autonomous, take any preceding nominal, and
several carry person prefixes of their own
sato2008 34–36 ; nakagawa2024 280, 294–296 .
Ijäs 2023 presents the same two-class organization ijas2023 .

property
case particle (格助詞)
postpositional adverb (後置副詞)

requires ⟨place⟩ head?
yes (the four local ones)
no — takes any NP ( unarpe eun 'to auntie')

can open a phrase alone?
no (bound; needs an antecedent)
yes ( eun k=arpa 'I go there')

takes object person prefix?
no
some do: en=akkari 'more than me',
i=os 'after me'

core members
ta, wa, un, peka, ne
ani, tura, kari, pakno, akkari, eun, os …

The structural pivot of the case-particle system is the locative noun
or place, interior of (long form oro place, interior of ),
which converts a common noun into a ⟨place⟩-argument able to take a case particle:
atuy or un 'to the sea' ( atuy 'sea' +
or 'place-of' + un allative). Its syntax
and possessed morphology are examined in relational-and-spatial-nouns ;
the inalienability of locative nouns is set out in alienable-inalienable-split .

Case particles

Nakagawa identifies six case particles for the Saru baseline
nakagawa2024 296–303 :

particle
case name
gloss
⟨place⟩?
notes

ta
locative 位格
at, in, on; arrival goal; temporal
yes
with basic or fuses to otta

wa
ablative 起点格
from (source); wano (temporal source)
yes
marks source, not path; path through a route uses kari

un
allative 向格
to, toward
yes
dialect-split: un (日高西部–西), en
(日高東部–十勝), (e)ne (道東・石狩)

peka
perlative 経由格
through, over, around (a spread area)
yes
variant pekano ; requires ⟨place⟩ unlike postpositional adverb kari

ne
translative 転成格
as, become
no
also links numerals to copular predication; see copula-ne-predicate-nominals

un (adnominal)
adnominal/genitive 連体格
of, belonging to
(yes)
pan-HA invariant; etymologically distinct from allative un

The locative ta at, in, on covers both existence-location
( ta with an existential verb) and arrival goal
( ta as the endpoint of a motion event), and also marks temporal
reference-points nakagawa2024 296–297 :

cip pet-ka ta an | boat river-top LOC exist | The boat is on the river. | pet-ka = river-surface (the locative noun ka 'top/surface' heads the ⟨place⟩ phrase). Locative ta marks existence at a location. | sato2008:37 | CHI

tanto Ukurmem ta ku=sirepa | today Ukurmem LOC 1SG.A=arrive | Today I arrived at Ukurmem. | Locative ta marks the endpoint of arrival, identical in form to existence-location ta. | sato2008:37 | CHI

The ablative wa from (source) marks the source of a motion event, not
the path traversed. Nakagawa draws this directly: going out through a door or window
requires the postpositional adverb kari through, via , not
wa nakagawa2024 297 . The temporal source form
wano extends the same locative source semantics into the time
domain:

nupuri or wa a=ek | mountain place ABL 4.A=come | They came from the mountains.

The allative un to, toward marks directed movement toward a goal, and
the contrast with locative ta is a direction/arrival distinction:
un indicates a direction-headed-toward while ta
marks the completed arrival point. Nakagawa illustrates with
ni ka ta hemesu=an 'I climbed onto the tree' (arrival:
ta ) versus
cikuni kasi un hemesu=an 'I climb toward the tree-top' (direction:
un ) nakagawa2024 298–299 :

atuy or un cip ani paye=an p ne | sea place ALLAT boat INS go=4.S NMLZ COP | (She) always went to the sea by boat. | atuy or un = 'to the sea' (allative); cip ani = 'by boat' (instrumental postpositional adverb ani). | biratori1969 | SAR | Hiraga Sadamo, 'Urayushiunhur', Biratori Ainu oral literature

The allative also appears as a case-frame on interrogative place-words:
hunak un 'to where', hunak wa 'from where',
hunak ta 'where (at)' — the three-case paradigm on the one place-root
nakagawa2024 413 .
Interrogative pro-forms are examined in interrogative-pro-forms .

The perlative peka through, over an area marks traversal of a spatially
spread area. It differs from the postpositional adverb kari in
requiring a ⟨place⟩ head and in denoting a diffuse area rather than a single passage point
nakagawa2024 299 :

ne kenas or peka ekimne=an kor | DEM forest place PERL hunt=4.S while | While hunting through that forest, … | ne kenas or peka = 'through that forest area'; peka here covers the whole region, not a linear route. Simultaneous kor 'while' links the clause. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 7, ILCAA Ainu materials (aa-irc/005)

The translative ne become, as predicts conversion to a state or category
and does not require a ⟨place⟩ head. It also appears with numerals in copular
predications (e.g., ren a=ne wa oka=an 'we lived, being three')
nakagawa2024 300–301 :

aynu ne yaykar | human TRANSL transform | (It) transformed into a human. | nakagawa2024:300 | SAR

The adnominal un forms a genitive construction linking a locative
or place-name to a following noun, expressing origin or affiliation:
kim un kamuy 'mountain deity (bear)', Sinutapka
un kur 'person of Shinutapka' nakagawa2024 301–302 . This
un is pan-Hokkaido invariant across all dialects, which Nakagawa
takes as evidence that it is etymologically distinct from the allative un ,
which varies dialectally nakagawa2024 301–302 . The genitive construction
is treated in detail in adnominal-possession-double-marking .

Postpositional adverbs

Nakagawa organizes the postpositional adverbs into two etymological strata
nakagawa2024 280–282 . The first, originally adverbial forms
(本来型), includes pakno until, as far as , os after ,
koraci like, as if , and epitta throughout .
The second, derived from transitive verbs (他動詞転換型), includes
ani with, by means of (instrumental), tura together with
(comitative), kari through, via (perlative), akkari more than ,
eun to(ward) (animate allative), and hekote facing .
The verb-origin class retains the structural memory of its source: these forms take the
transitive-object person prefix ( en=akkari 'more than me',
i=os 'after me') sato2008 34–36 .

form
gloss
origin class
person prefix?
⟨place⟩ required?

ani
with, by means of (INS)
transitive verb
no
no

tura(no)
together with (COMIT)
transitive verb
yes (obj. affix)
no

kari
through, via (PERL)
transitive verb
yes
no

pakno
until, as far as
original adverb
no
no

akkari
more than, beyond
transitive verb
yes (obj. affix)
no

eun
to(ward) (animate goal)
transitive verb
yes
no

os(i)
after, following
original adverb
yes (obj. affix)
no

The contrast between case-particle peka and postpositional-adverb
kari is a key diagnostic for the two-class split.
peka requires a ⟨place⟩-type noun
( kim or peka 'through the mountains'); kari
takes any nominal and does not require the locative noun or
( ruyka kari 'across the bridge', puyar kari
'through the window') nakagawa2024 282 . The test sentence from Satō also
illustrates that kari heads a full motion event without any locative
noun:

ru kari arpa | road PERL go | (He/she) went along the road. | ru 'road' is a common noun, not a locative noun; kari therefore does not require the intervening or that peka would demand. | sato2008:38 | CHI

ruyka kari saramanto kari soyne=an akusu | bridge PERL skylight PERL go.outside=4.S when | Going out through the bridge and the skylight, … | Both ruyka 'bridge' and saramanto 'skylight' are common nouns; kari licenses them directly without or. | nakagawa2024:282 | SAR

The limitative pakno until, as far as sets a terminal boundary — spatial
or degree — on the action of its clause:

Tokiyo pakno k=arpa rusuy | Tokyo until 1SG.S=go want | I want to go as far as Tokyo. | sato2008:36 | CHI

The instrumental ani with, by means of is the standard tool-marking device,
corresponding to Japanese で in this function. The comitative
tura together with is among the most frequent items in the corpus
nakagawa2024 280–282 and may head an independent motion-clause constituent
( macihi tura ku=ek 'I came with (my) wife').
The distinction between animate-goal eun and place-goal
un mirrors Satō's minimal pair: Satporo un
requires the place-name ( *unarpe un is ill-formed), whereas
unarpe eun 'to auntie' is well-formed because eun
is a postpositional adverb sato2008 34–35 .

The locative noun or and the ⟨place⟩ requirement

The ⟨place⟩ requirement of the four local case particles is satisfied by a small closed
class of locative nouns (位置名詞) that derive the internal spatial structure of
a referent. or interior, place-of (long form oro interior, place-of )
is the most general: it maps any common noun to its associated location or interior.
Satō 2021 documents its distributional range in Chitose in detail
sato2021 .
The case particle ta fuses with basic or
to give otta ; the longer oro stays separate
( oro ta ) nakagawa2024 297 .

Other locative nouns include ka top, surface of (upper surface:
ni ka ta 'onto the tree'), un interior, inside of
(interior — this un is a locative noun distinct from both the
allative particle and the adnominal particle),
soy outside of (exterior: cise soy ta 'outside the
house'), kes end, tip of , and poka bottom, lower side of .
These interact with the concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative/concept-form
morphology: a locative noun heading a possessed ⟨place⟩ phrase takes the affiliative suffix
that marks inalienable possession, because locative nouns are inalienably related to their
host noun nakagawa2024 241 .

A community discussion (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-09) highlights a
distributional consequence of the ⟨place⟩ requirement: the existential verb
an exist (intransitive) requires a locative noun before the case particle
( A B or ta an 'A exists at/in B'), while the two-argument verb
un belong to (homophonous with the allative particle) takes a bare nominal
( A B un 'A belongs to B'). This is corroborated by Nakagawa's
analysis of adnominal un as etymologically a two-argument predicate
rather than a case particle
nakagawa2024 301–302 ; aynucorporadiscord
‹corpus-confirmed›.

Case or adposition? The analytical question

Whether ta / wa / un / peka constitute a local-case system
or are better analyzed as postpositions turns on how one weighs three
distributional facts against one morphophonological one.

The case-system argument rests on three properties
nakagawa2024 296–303 ; nakagawa2022pos 473–475 .
First, the four particles form a closed paradigm organized around locative semantic primitives
(at / from / toward / through) that parallels local-case inventories in languages with overt
case morphology. Second, the particles alternate on the single place-word hunak
('where') — hunak ta 'where-at', hunak wa
'from where', hunak un 'to where' — exactly as case suffixes do in
case-marking languages. Third, the allative particle shows regular dialectal allomorphy
( un / en / (e)ne ) that patterns as suppletive morphology rather than
distinct lexical items.

The adposition argument notes that the particles remain phonologically independent words,
never cliticize to their hosts, and that the ⟨place⟩ requirement is satisfied by a separate,
grammatically active head noun ( or ) rather than by the noun itself
taking an affix sato2008 34–36 ; refsing1986 109 .
The case label adopted by Nakagawa and Satō is therefore a functional/semantic label, not
a morphological one: these are postpositions that constitute a local-case subsystem
by virtue of their paradigmatic organization and the ⟨place⟩ selection constraint
‹contested›.

Motion-event encoding

Hokkaido Ainu has a substantial set of motion verbs that lexicalize direction, deictic
orientation, and vertical or coastal dimension. The most basic Saru–Chitose pair is
arpa go (singular) (go, singular subject) and
oman go (Chitose/north) (go, in northern and eastern dialects); the
suppletive plurals are paye go (plural) and arpa go 's
own plural form. For 'come', the singular is ek come (singular) with
suppletive plural arki come (plural) .

Beyond these general verbs, a lexical set encodes deictic path-direction:
san go seaward, go down (out) (move seaward / downhill / out to the open),
