yan go inland, go up (move inland / uphill),
ahun enter (enter an enclosed space),
rikin go up, ascend (ascend vertically),
ran come down, descend (descend),
soyne go outside (go out/outside),
and repun go out to sea (go out to sea).
Each of these lexicalizes a specific path-direction in the verb stem itself.
Nakagawa 2024 treats these as a distinct lexical class on account of their suppletive
number alternants and their complementary distribution with postpositional arguments
nakagawa2024 298–299 .

A motion clause typically combines one of these path-encoding verbs with a postpositional
phrase that specifies the Source (ablative wa ) or Goal (allative
un / locative ta ):

nupuri or wa k=arpa | mountain place ABL 1SG.S=go | I went away from the mountain.

atuy or un k=arpa | sea place ALLAT 1SG.S=go | I went to the sea.

Path through a specific route is marked by the postpositional adverb
kari (for a single passage point or linear route) or the case
particle peka (for a spread area). When both a direction verb and
a perlative phrase appear together, the verb encodes the terminal direction while the
perlative phrase encodes the route:

kim peka a=tura | mountain PERL 4.A=accompany | (He/she) accompanied (them) over/through the mountains. | kim peka = 'over the mountain area'; a=tura = 4A=accompany. The motion direction (away, toward) is understood from context; the postposition specifies the terrain traversed. | nakagawa2024:299 | SAR

The morphological alternation between the stem forms of direction verbs is itself a
coding device for motion: nukopoli notes that motion verbs characteristically show a
-n / -p stem alternation
( san/sap , yan/yap ,
ahun/ahup , ran/rap ,
rikin/rikip ), where -p is cognate with
the formal noun pa , though the etymology of -n
remains unsettled
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2026-05-18) ‹speculative›.
The causative of rikin go up is uniquely rikinka ;
*rikipka is unattested, suggesting the -n
stem is the causative base
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2026-04-27) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

The Talmy typology — verb-framing and path lexicalization

Talmy's distinction between verb-framed languages (where Path is encoded in the
main verb) and satellite-framed languages (where Path is in a satellite and
Manner in the verb) provides a useful frame for locating the HA system
talmy2000 . The HA data are consistent with verb-framed organization
at the lexical core — the path-direction verbs san ,
yan , ahun , rikin ,
and ran all lexicalize specific Path values in the verb stem —
while the postpositional system adds goal/source precision through the
un / wa / ta case particles and contributes a separate perlative
specification through peka / kari .

What makes the Ainu system typologically mixed is that the perlative particles function
as path-specifying satellites — they add information about the route that is not in the
verb stem — while the goal and source particles function as standard local-case markers
that any language, verb-framed or satellite-framed, may employ.
A manner verb like ekimne hunt (going hunting) can occur with a perlative
phrase ( ne kenas or peka ekimne=an kor ) in a way that resembles
satellite framing, but the path-specialized verbs such as san and
ahun carry Path in the verb itself. The precise typological
placement of Ainu within the Talmy framework depends on which lexical stratum is taken
as primary, and the accessible sources do not settle this as an explicit research claim
‹speculative›.

The interaction of postpositions with noun incorporation is treated in
noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate ; argument realization and
the role of postpositional phrases in oblique positions are examined in
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument .

Dialect variation in the allative

The allative particle is the most dialect-variable of the five case particles. Nakagawa
maps three main reflexes across Hokkaido
nakagawa2024 302, §9.2.2 :

dialect area
allative particle
example

日高西部, 西北海道 (Saru, Chitose, Yakumo)
un
pet or un 'to the river'

日高東部, 十勝 (Shizunai, Tokachi)
en
pet or en

道東, 石狩, 北部 (Bihoro, Ishikari, Asahikawa)
(e)ne
pet or ne

Nakagawa traces the historical sequence as
ne > ene >
en > un , with the translative/copula
ne the ultimate source
nakagawa2024 302 .
The reanalysis of ne from a translative into an allative marker is
plausible as a goal-marking grammaticalization, but the intermediate stages are not
uniformly attested and the direction of the change has not been independently demonstrated
‹speculative›.

The adnominal un ('of, belonging to') does not participate
in this variation: it is the same un in all dialects, including
those that use en or ne for the allative.
Nakagawa takes this pan-HA uniformity as the strongest evidence that the two
un 's are etymologically distinct, with adnominal
un deriving from the two-argument possession verb rather than from
any locative source nakagawa2024 301–302 ‹consensus›.
Interrogative-strategy uses of the allative ( ney un 'to where' in
eastern dialects, as opposed to hunak un in western ones) are
examined in interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

title: Pragmatic Word-Order Permutation, Dislocation, and Argument Ellipsis
summary: Departures from basic verb-final order for information-structural ends — scrambling, left/right dislocation, afterthought, and given-argument ellipsis.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Pragmatic Word-Order Permutation, Dislocation, and Argument Ellipsis
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: Departures from basic verb-final order for information-structural ends — scrambling, left/right dislocation, afterthought, and given-argument ellipsis.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu has one rigid clause-level constraint: the verb phrase — together with any
evidential — comes last, and all other elements precede it nakagawa2024 363 .
Within the preverbal domain, however, the relative sequence of nominal and adverbial phrases
is not grammatically fixed. Because grammatical roles are indexed on the verb by obligatory
person affixes (see person-marking-architecture ), and overt NPs stand in an
appositional relation to those affixes rather than filling assigned slots (see
argument-realization-indexing ), the preverbal domain is available for information
structure to impose its own ordering — independently of the S/A/O distinctions encoded
morphologically.

Several distinct operations occupy this space. Topical elements tend to appear at the left
edge of the preverbal sequence, most visibly when marked by the topic particle
anakne as for (topic marker) . A contrastively stressed indirect object may be
fronted and receive an allative postposition it would otherwise lack. An adverbial complex can
appear in any order relative to the NPs around it. And a third-person NP can be placed
after the verb and re-predicated by the light verb ki do — a
right-dislocation strategy that Bugaeva connects to processing and to the disambiguating
function that verb-initial order performs in other head-marking languages
bugaeva2022 ch.1 §4.3 .

This chapter describes those positional operations and the discourse conditions that license
each. The topic-particle system — anak(ne) , the additive-focus
particle ka also; even , and the exclusive-focus adverb
patek only, just — is treated in full in
topic-marking-anakne ; cleft and nominalization-based focus constructions belong
to cleft-nominalization-focus . Argument ellipsis proper — the conditions under
which a recoverable NP is simply omitted — is covered in
argument-realization-indexing and reference-tracking ; what this
chapter addresses is how ellipsis conditions the use (and non-use) of fronting and
dislocation.

Topic-initial order and left-fronting

The most consistently stated pragmatic generalization in the literature on Ainu word order
is that topical NPs tend to come first bugaeva2012 472 . Nakagawa frames the
same observation structurally: rather than a subject-before-object preference, the preverbal
sequence is organized by information status, so that the element made topic by
anak(ne) or ka tends to occupy the initial
position nakagawa2024 364 . His Chitose paradigm — the same ditransitive
event narrated with the three arguments of epakasnu teach in three different
orders — makes the point directly: the order shifts with the topic-marking, and order alone
cannot determine which argument is agent or recipient (Nakagawa 2024: 363, exx.700–702; see
constituent-order-head-final ).

Three corpus examples from different narrators and registers show the range of what can be
fronted:

a=kor seta anakne i=oyakke oma ka somo ki p ne kusu i=sam ta ukasuy ipe=an | 4.A=have dog TOP 4.O=leave approach even NEG do NMLZ COP PURP 4.O=side LOC together eat=4.S | My dog, as for it, never left my side, so it ate with me. | The possessed subject a=kor seta 'my dog' is fronted and marked with anakne; the complex predicate 'never even approached leaving me' follows with the result clause. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子, AA研アイヌ語資料, 沙流

ku=nukar seta anakne ney ta ne yakka a=nukar seta ne noyne siran | 1SG.A=see dog TOP where LOC COP even 4.A=see dog COP seem exist | The dog I saw looked like a dog you would see anywhere. | A relative-clause head (ku=nukar seta 'the dog I saw') is fronted and marked with anakne before the comment clause. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 丸野和子, アイヌタイムズ, 沙流・千歳

Seta anakne a=kor ikor ne kusu onne wa isam pakno pirkano kasi a=oyki kuni p ne na | dog TOP 4.A=have treasure COP PURP die.old CONJ absent until well properly 4.A=care COMP NMLZ COP FIN | Dogs are our treasure, so one must care for them well until they die of old age. | A bare lexical noun seta 'dog' heads a generic statement under anakne topic marking. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 神崎雅好, アイヌタイムズ, 沙流・千歳

All three share the positional structure — topic + anak(ne) at the
left edge — but the fronted constituent varies: a possessed noun phrase, a relative-clause
head, and a bare lexical noun. The register likewise varies: oral narrative, a modern written
newsletter, and a generic statement in the same newsletter. The anak(ne)
particle itself appears in different clause types and carries a pragmatic weight closer to
Japanese '～に関しては (as for, regarding —)' than to the bare thematic は; once a topic is
established in discourse, re-marking with anak on a continuing topic
is unnecessary aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-09;
asserted; consistent with shibatani1990 38 ). The full distributional
and functional account of anak(ne) is in
topic-marking-anakne .

The anak form is etymologically transparent: Tamura's Saru dictionary
derives it from an-yak 'if it is so / as for it being' (reported in
aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-08, asserted; after
tamura1996 ). This diachronic source is consistent with the particle's evidential
weight and its less-grammaticalized status relative to Japanese は ‹speculative›.

Topic-initial order occurs without the overt particle when context already establishes the
topic. The particle-free pattern is indistinguishable in position from neutral order or from
unmarked focus preposing, and in practice it is the particle that signals topicalization as
distinct from simple preverbal adjacency to the verb.

Focus preposing and allative-marked IO fronting

Left-fronting is available to focused constituents as well as to topics. Refsing observes
that an indirect object, normally embedded among the preverbal NPs without any postpositional
marking, "may, when stressed, be moved to the beginning of the sentence and marked with an
allative case postposition" refsing1986 (§13.4.3, Shizunai data). The allative
marking is the diagnostic: core arguments are never flagged by a case postposition in neutral
order, so when one appears on a fronted IO it signals a positional operation driven by focus
or contrast, not by grammatical role ‹contested›.

ku=macihi orun topenpe ku=kore | 1SG.A=wife ALLAT sweets 1SG.A=give | To my wife I gave the sweets (not to anyone else). | Constructed to illustrate IO fronting with the allative postposition orun under contrastive stress, as described in Refsing (1986, §13.4.3) for Shizunai. In neutral order the IO ku=macihi would appear without orun. | SHI

This pattern is phonologically and semantically driven — it signals narrow focus on the
indirect-object referent. Content-question words, by contrast, remain in situ: they occupy
the same preverbal position as the corresponding non-question constituent, and "questions do
not involve any distinctive word order" bugaeva2012 498 . No wh-movement
applies. The interrogative system — including the question particles
ya and ya an and the content-question
pro-forms ( hunna 'who', hemanta 'what',
hunak 'where') — is treated in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

In the majority of cases, focus is conveyed in situ through the focus particle
ka 'also/even/indeed' or the exclusive adverb
patek 'only', rather than by a positional operation. When a cleft or
pseudocleft construction is used to identify a focus referent, the nominalization-based
apparatus described in cleft-nominalization-focus is employed; this chapter
does not treat those constructions.

Right-dislocation and the afterthought construction

The mirror image of topic-initial order is a pattern documented by Bugaeva in which the
predicate comes first and the disambiguating subject or object NP appears after the verb,
re-predicated by the light verb ki do bugaeva2022 ch.1 §4.3 .
Bugaeva analyses this as right-dislocation: a discourse or processing device that "mimics
the verb-initial strategy of establishing relations at the outset" even in a verb-final
language, allowing the hearer to know the event-type before the participant-identities are
supplied. The following passage from a Chitose narrative has the predicate phrase
he=tuk-pa pa 'they all grew (plural, perfective)' before the two
subject NPs, which are then introduced as afterthoughts via the ki
construction:

he=tuk-pa pa a=mat-ne-po-utar-i a=po-utar-i ki pa pe ne kusu | body=grow-PL PFV 4.A=wife-COP-child-ASS-POSS 4.A=child-ASS-POSS do PFV NMLZ COP PURP | They all grew up — the daughters and the children — because the children did so. | The verb phrase he=tuk-pa pa 'grew (plural, perfective)' precedes the two dislocated subject NPs a=matnepoutari (daughters) and a=poutari (sons/children); the light verb ki re-predicates them in an afterthought clause. Translation follows Bugaeva 2022. | bugaeva2022 | CHI | Bugaeva 2022 ch.1 ex.19, NINJAL corpus K8109171UP.296

Bugaeva connects this pattern typologically to Nichols's observation that head-marking
languages, in which relations are established on the verb rather than on NPs, have the
greatest freedom to put the verb first — "establishing relations at the outset" — and that
this is the functional pay-off of verb-initial order in many head-marking families
nichols1986 104 . Verb-final Ainu achieves a structurally parallel
effect through right-dislocation: the event is predicated and the argument-tracking indices
are on the verb; the NP appears afterward as a lexical specification that allows the hearer
to recover participant identity bugaeva2022 ch.1 §7 .

The pattern is attested but not quantified: only two examples are documented in the
sources consulted (Bugaeva 2022 ch.1, exx.18–19), both from Chitose narrative texts, so its frequency
and range are open ‹corpus-suggested›. No right-dislocation marker has grammaticalized; the
ki that closes the afterthought is the ordinary light verb 'do', and
the construction has no dedicated morphological signature distinct from any other
ki -predicated clause. Its identification as right-dislocation rests
on the discourse position of the NP relative to the preceding predicate.

The fourth-person affixes are central to what makes right-dislocation functional in Ainu
narration. Third-person arguments carry a zero index — no morphological trace of their
identity — and it is precisely this zero-marking that motivates the afterthought: the hearer
cannot identify the participant from the affix and so the lexical NP is supplied after the
fact. See indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm for the agent-defocusing and
narrator-tracking functions of the fourth person, and
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse for the broader discourse role of
the a= / =an forms in oral narrative.

Adjunct placement and preverbal order

Time adverbs, manner adverbs, and postpositional adjunct phrases are all preverbal in Hokkaido
Ainu, but their order within the preverbal domain is unconstrained. Nakagawa's sentence
template allows zero to any number of adverbials (副詞句 0…x) in any order relative to one
another and relative to the argument NPs (名詞句 0…n), with the only requirement being that
all precede the verb phrase nakagawa2024 361 . Refsing states the same
freedom empirically: her grammar does not account for adjunct order because she found it
"very free" refsing1986 (§2.4). Negators somo not (negating adverb)
and iteki don't (prohibitive adverb) likewise precede the verb as adverbs;
they occupy no fixed structural slot beyond the general Adv–V requirement
nakagawa2024 365 .

One specific pair of adverbial forms illustrates how adjunct order can be sensitive to
contact-induced change. In traditional Ainu texts the complex
ani patek '(INS) only' — the instrumental postposition
ani following its nominal complement and followed by the exclusive
adverb patek — is the normal sequence. Modern speakers show roughly
equal frequency of the reverse order patek ani . The reversal is
attributed to Japanese influence: patek is an adverb and cannot be
nominalised as Japanese 丈/だけ can, so the Japanese-modelled
patek ani sequence imports a word-order pattern that is
category-inappropriate for the Ainu form aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-16; proposed) ‹contested›.

positional operation
position
morphological diagnostic
source

Topic-marking with anak(ne)
left edge of preverbal domain
overt particle; also unmarked topic-initial order in context
nakagawa2024 364 ; bugaeva2012 472

Contrastive focus on IO with allative orun
leftmost preverbal NP
allative postposition added; absent in neutral order
refsing1986 §13.4.3

Adjuncts (adverbials, postpositional phrases)
preverbal, freely ordered among NPs
no fixed position; template allows 0…x
nakagawa2024 361 ; refsing1986

Right-dislocation (afterthought with ki )
postverbal, clause-final NP + ki
third-person NP re-predicated by light verb ki
bugaeva2022 ch.1 §4.3

Content-question word (wh-in-situ)
argument or adjunct position (in situ)
no movement; position identical to non-question counterpart
bugaeva2012 498

Discourse conditions and the interaction with ellipsis

The positional operations described above are not alternatives to one another but
alternatives to argument ellipsis. An argument NP that is fully recoverable from context —
whether from a shared index on the verb, from the established discourse referent, or from
plausibility — is simply absent: "it is not necessary in Ainu for a noun phrase that is
clear from the context to appear in a sentence, and such a noun phrase is not even
represented by a pronoun" nakagawa2024 361 . Fronting or topic-marking
therefore signals something beyond what the obligatory person indices already convey.
Typically it marks re-establishment of a topic after a scene-switch or topic-lapse, the
introduction of a new referent that the hearer cannot retrieve from the index alone, or
contrastive focus on a participant against an alternative set. See
reference-tracking for the full system of how participant continuity is
maintained across clauses.

The discourse function of each operation is approximate rather than categorical:

Topic-initial with anak(ne) — marks a
discourse-topic at the start of a new unit, after a gap, or when shifting from a
previous topic; the particle has the weight of 'as for (X)' and is not re-applied
on every subsequent mention of the same topic.

In-situ order — the default when participants are known and the
predicate is the communicative center; any preverbal arrangement is grammatical
as long as roles are recoverable from the indices and context.

Focus preposing with allative — reserved for the indirect object
under narrow contrastive focus; the allative postposition is the marker that
distinguishes this from ordinary topic-initial position.

Right-dislocation with ki — supplies a
participant lexically after the predicate has committed to an event-type; attested
in narrative contexts where the third-person zero index is insufficient for
disambiguation.

The interaction between information structure and the nonconfigurationality question is
addressed in nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument . The head-marking
analysis of Ainu (see sato2025a 359–365 ; nichols1986 104 )
predicts precisely the observed pattern: because roles are encoded on the predicate, overt NPs
are free to appear, be omitted, or be placed wherever information structure directs them.
The clause-chaining apparatus — wa , hine ,
kor — maintains participant continuity across linked clauses and
thereby reduces the need for topic re-introduction within a chain (see
clause-linkage-overview ). Fronting and dislocation are most active at
chain boundaries and at discourse transitions, not inside the chain itself.

title: Predicative Possession: kor, Existential Possession, and isam
summary: Clause-level 'have/belong' predication via transitive kor, existential-based possession, and negative isam, plus the noun-level affiliative-vs-kor choice for alienable nouns; placed on Stassen's predicative-possession typology and interfaced with the nominal affiliative system (Part VII).
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Predicative Possession: kor, Existential Possession, and isam
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: Clause-level 'have/belong' predication via transitive kor, existential-based possession, and negative isam, plus the noun-level affiliative-vs-kor choice for alienable nouns; placed on Stassen's predicative-possession typology and interfaced with the nominal affiliative system (Part VII).

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu encodes predicative possession — 'X has Y' at the clause level — through two
verb-based strategies. The dominant one deploys the transitive verb kor have, hold ,
which takes the possessor as its subject (A argument) and the possessed entity as its object:
kasa ku=kor 'I have an umbrella'. The second strategy, confined to a
closed class of affiliative body-part and kinship nouns, places the possessed noun in its
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed) form and predicates it with
the intransitive existential verb an exist (singular) or
okay exist (plural) (plural): a=yup-i an 'one has an elder
brother'. A third pattern, kor wa an , combines possession with the
resultative auxiliary to encode stative or resultant possession; it belongs to the intersection
of predicative possession and the aspect system treated in existential-aspect-an-oka .

Adnominal possession — the ku=kor cise genitive-like construction, the
affiliative double-marking pattern, and the appositive construction — is covered in
adnominal-possession-double-marking and alienable-inalienable-split ; the
present chapter treats only predicative (clause-final, copula-free) possession constructions.
Typologically, the two-strategy system places Hokkaido Ainu in the mixed HAVE/BE category of
stassen1997 : the HAVE strategy with a dedicated transitive verb covers the
great majority of cases, while the BE strategy — here the existential an
— is confined to the inalienable domain ‹corpus-suggested›. Huber's corpus study finds that over
80% of predicative possession clauses employ the HAVE-type construction huber2025 254 .
The pedagogical grammar of ijas2023 likewise introduces
kor as the canonical possession verb.

The HAVE-construction: transitive kor

The verb kor have, hold is a two-place transitive verb (2項動詞) with the
possessor as its A argument and the possessed entity as its O argument
refsing1986 160–161 . It takes the standard S/A/O personal-affix paradigm
described in person-marking-architecture : the possessor is indexed by the
A-series (transitive subject) prefixes, while a third-person possessed noun carries no person
index. Refsing analyses kor as an "attributive postposition" when it
appears inside a noun phrase ( ku=kor cise 'my house') and as a
predicative verb when it closes a clause refsing1986 160–161 ; Nakagawa treats
both uses as the same transitive verb nakagawa2024 §4.2.2 .

kasa ku=kor | umbrella 1SG.A=have | I have an umbrella. | Canonical HAVE-type predicative possession. Community discussion confirms the same form for Saru: kasa kor 'hold an umbrella', first-person kasa ku=kor (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2025-12-31; Ref aynucorporadiscord). | refsing1986:160–161 | SHI

One caution on homonymy: kor also functions as the conjunctive particle
'while', marking simultaneous or background events in a clause chain. The two uses are
distinguished by their syntactic position — predicative kor is
clause-final, conjunctive kor occurs between two verbal clauses — and
are treated separately in simultaneous-kor-clauses . The corpus rank-5 frequency of
30,389 tokens for kor folds both functions together ‹corpus-suggested›.

Person marking on predicative kor

As a transitive verb, kor takes the A-series prefix for the possessor
and no prefix for a third-person possessed NP. The table shows the possessor-A cells for the
standard Saru–Chitose baseline nakagawa2024 165–169 ; the 1SG cell is attested,
the remaining cells are illustrative (constructed from the paradigm):

possessor (A)
prefix
example
translation

1SG
ku=
kasa ku=kor
'I have an umbrella' (attested)

2SG
e=
kasa e=kor
'you (sg) have an umbrella'

3
Ø
kasa kor
'he / she / it has an umbrella'

4 / INDEF
a=
kasa a=kor
'one has an umbrella'

1PL.EXCL
ci=
kasa ci=kor
'we (excl.) have an umbrella'

The 1SG prefix ku= and the 2SG prefix e= are
the same A-series forms they carry on any transitive verb. The 1PL.EXCL prefix
ci= is the standard tripartite-cell A form. In adnominal use the same
prefixes appear within the noun phrase: a=kor kamuy 'the god who holds
the sea' (cf. adnominal-possession-double-marking ).

The animacy restriction on kor

No published primary source states a categorical animacy ban on kor ,
but community intuition reported in discussion (nukopoli,
aynucorporadiscord , 2024-11-10) holds that an inanimate subject cannot serve as
the possessor of kor : 'a religion has three books' is judged
ungrammatical with kor and is instead paraphrased with the existential
— … sekor a=ye re kampisos an 'there exist three books called …'. This
pattern is consistent with the cross-linguistic HAVE(animate)/BE(existential) split documented
by stassen1997 and with Huber's finding that inanimate-possession contexts
shift toward the existential strategy huber2025 ‹contested›.

BE-type: existential possession with affiliative nouns

A second strategy is restricted to nouns that carry an
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (possessed) form — primarily body-part
and kinship nouns. The possessed noun appears in its affiliative form (root plus suffix
-i , -he , -ke , etc.),
and the possessor is encoded by the appropriate person prefix on that noun itself. The predicate
is then the intransitive existential verb an exist (singular) or
okay exist (plural) (plural), making the literal structure 'one's X exists'
huber2025 251–253 :

a=yup-i an | 4.POSS=elder.brother-POSS exist | One has an elder brother. | Story-opening family introduction. The noun yupi 'elder brother' takes the affiliative suffix -i; the 4th-person possessive prefix a= on the noun encodes the story protagonist as possessor. | huber2025:251–253 | HK

a=sa-ha an | 4.POSS=younger.sister-POSS exist | One has a younger sister. | Parallel BE-type form; sa 'younger sister' takes the affiliative suffix -ha. | huber2025:251–253 | HK

a=po-ho ka an | 4.POSS=child-POSS even exist | One even has a son / child. | ka 'even' is the additive focus particle. The noun po 'child, son' takes the affiliative suffix -ho. | huber2025:251–253 | HK

This is the same existential an that appears in bare existential and
locational clauses (see existential-locational-an-oka ), here pressed into possessive
service by the inherent person prefix on the possessed noun itself. Because the possessor is
already encoded on the noun, no additional verbal person marking is needed: the construction is
entirely intransitive from the verb's perspective. The number suppletion
an (sg) / okay ~ oka (pl)
tracks the number of the possessed entity, following the same agreement pattern as in all other
existential clauses nakagawa2024 146 . The kinship and honorific dimensions of
this construction — including which kin terms carry affiliative forms — are developed in
kinship-honorific-possession ; the morphophonology of the affiliative suffix itself
is in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Stative possession: kor wa an

The periphrasis kor wa an — possessive verb kor
+ conjunctive wa + existential an — encodes
stative or resultant possession: the possessor is in a persisting state of holding something as
the result of a prior obtaining event. This pattern is an instance of the wider resultative
V wa an construction analysed in existential-aspect-an-oka ;
in the possession domain it specifies that a possession relation has been established and
continues to hold.

The pattern extends to incorporated nominals, where a body-part or object noun is fused into
kor as a compound before the resultative tail:

ramat-kor wa an | soul-have CONJ exist | having gained a soul; being in possession of a soul | Noun incorporation: ramat 'soul' incorporated into kor yields the compound ramatkor 'soul-have'. The wa an resultative marks the ongoing state of being ensouled. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子, AA研アイヌ語資料 民話21 [aa-irc/021#530]

The same frame with the 4th-person intransitive suffix
=an — a=kohon-kor wa an=an 'was pregnant with
(lit. hold-belly-have, one-exists)' ilcaa1976 (川上まつ子, 民話23
[aa-irc/023#74]) — shows noun incorporation, possessive predication, resultative aspect, and
4th-person marking interacting in a single verb complex. The incorporation and valency dimensions
are treated in noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding .

Negation of possession

The two possession strategies negate by different means, each recruiting a suppletive lexical
negative rather than ordinary clausal negation bugaeva2012 496 .

possession type
positive predicate
primary negation
secondary negation
source

HAVE-type
kor (vt)

sak (vt) 'lack' — suppletive

somo kor — clausal
Nakagawa 2024: 323; Bugaeva 2012: 496

BE-type
an / okay (vi)

isam (vi) 'not exist' — suppletive

—
Bugaeva 2012: 496

For the HAVE-type, the transitive verb sak lack, be without takes the same
A/O argument frame as kor : the possessor is the subject and the lacked
entity is the object bugaeva2012 496 . Ordinary clausal negation
somo kor is also possible but less frequent than the suppletive
sak nakagawa2024 323 (see
negation-somo-standard-clausal ):

tamasay ka sak | necklace even lack | (one) has no necklace even | The transitive sak 'lack' is the suppletive negative of kor; tamasay 'necklace' is the object (lacked possession) and the possessor is third-person (zero-marked A). | nakagawa2024:323 | HK

ona sak pe | father lack NMLZ | one who has no father | Nominalized negative-possession clause. sak is transitive: ona 'father' is the O argument, and the possessor is third-person (zero). pe nominalizes the whole clause. | bugaeva2012:496 | HK

For the BE-type, the negative existential isam not exist, be absent — the
suppletive lexical negative of an / okay — follows
directly from the structure: if the possessed noun in affiliative form fails to exist, the
possessor lacks it. The possessive reading ('has no X') is thus the automatic negative of
'X exists (as one's possession)'. The full description of isam is in
negation-existential-possessive-isam .

The asymmetry — both possession strategies using suppletive, lexical negatives rather than the
plain somo operator — parallels the negation of the copula
( ka somo ne ) and of the existential ( isam )
documented in nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation . The interaction of these negators
with TAM, evidential tails, and scope is treated there.

Division of labour and diachrony

The choice between HAVE-type and BE-type tracks the semantic relationship between possessor and
possessed. Alienable possession — objects, tools, animals, goods — takes kor
exclusively. Inalienable possession, where the possessed entity (body part or kinship relation)
is inherently indexed to a possessor through the affiliative morphology, admits the BE-type
construction in addition to kor
huber2025 251–254 ; refsing1986 160–161 . The BE-type is further
restricted to those inalienable nouns that actually carry an affiliative suffix form (see
nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns and alienable-inalienable-split for
the full taxonomy).

Typologically, this profile maps onto the mixed HAVE/BE type in stassen1997 :
a language with a dedicated transitive HAVE verb as the default and an existential BE construction
for inalienable possession. The inalienable-domain restriction of the BE type is expected under
the cross-linguistic generalisation that languages permitting both strategies tend to use the
existential for possession relations that are encoded also in the morphology of the possessed
noun itself ‹consensus›.

A diachronic note: an early modern source (もしほ草, ca. 1806) records possessive expressions
where kor is absent and the possessor is instead encoded by a person
prefix on an existential verb — ci=oro okay kotan 'our village' (lit.
'our-in existing village'), ci=okay cas 'our castle' — suggesting that
kor had not yet grammaticalized as the dominant possessive predicate in
that textual register. This may reflect an earlier stage in which various existential strategies
were available before kor extended its possessive scope
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-23) ‹speculative›. The
current dominance of kor (>80% of predicative possession in Huber's
data huber2025 254 ) would then represent the outcome of that grammaticalization
trajectory, though the textual evidence is thin and the comparison of registers is uncontrolled.

title: Relative Clauses: The Gap Strategy and the Accessibility Hierarchy
summary: The head-final, relativizer-less gap relative clause and the Keenan–Comrie accessibility hierarchy — subject/object relativize directly, obliques only after e-/ko-/o- applicative promotion — plus possessor, locative/temporal, and standard-of-comparison relativization.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Relative Clauses: The Gap Strategy and the Accessibility Hierarchy
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The head-final, relativizer-less gap relative clause and the Keenan–Comrie accessibility hierarchy — subject/object relativize directly, obliques only after e-/ko-/o- applicative promotion — plus possessor, locative/temporal, and standard-of-comparison relativization.

Overview

The Hokkaido Ainu relative clause is prenominal and head-final: the modifying clause precedes
its head noun, there is no relative pronoun, and the relativized participant is simply absent
from the clause — a zero gap nakagawa2024 503 ; sato2008 168 ;
shibatani1990 39 ; bugaeva2015 47 . Stative property verbs
modify a following noun directly and without any special marking:
hure suwop 'red box'. Any verb, stative or dynamic, can head the
modifying clause; the verb inside the RC carries the same person prefixes, number-sensitive
suppletion (singular ek come vs plural arki come (PL) ),
polarity, and clause-internal morphology as it would in a main clause
shibatani1990 39–40 ; sato2008 168 . Subordination is
signalled by the structural position of the clause before its head, not by any morphological
change to the verb bugaeva2016 100 ; nakagawa2024 503 .

This architecture — a full finite clause immediately before its head — is shared by the
headless relative (where a formal noun such as
p thing, person ~ pe thing, person supplies the head) and the
complement clause (where a formal noun nominalizes a saturated clause). The boundary between
these constructions is governed by Nakagawa's valency (arity) principle, discussed in the
following section and with worked examples in
nominalization-p-pe the chapter on the nominalizer
p ~ pe . The headless variant is treated
in headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses ; lexicalized RC-headed
nominalizations such as aep food in
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

The arity ≥ +1 diagnostic

Nakagawa's arity accounting provides the formal test that separates the relative clause from
the complement clause. Each verb contributes a positive arity value equal to the number of NP
arguments it requires; each NP present inside the clause subtracts one; a formal noun has
arity −1. When the summed arity of the embedded clause plus its formal-noun head is still
positive (≥ +1), one argument slot remains unfilled — that is the gap — and the whole unit
functions as a relative clause. When the arity reaches zero, the clause is saturated and the
formal noun nominalizes the whole clause as a complement
nakagawa2024 261–262, 504, 507 .

clause arity before the formal noun
formal noun arity
total
result

+2 (transitive verb, both arguments missing)
−1
+1
relative clause (one participant gapped)

+1 (intransitive verb, subject present)
−1
0
complement clause ( hi or p as complementizer)

0 (clause fully saturated)
−1
−1
ungrammatical as an RC-plus-head combination

The surface string [clause] p/pe is identical in the relative and the
complementizer readings; only the arity count distinguishes them. Satō's rule of thumb
for the same distinction is whether the original sentence from which the head noun was
extracted can be reconstructed: for a relative clause it can, for a nominalizer it cannot
sato2008 174–175 .

The gap strategy and postposition retention

The gap is a zero element: no pronoun, resumptive or otherwise, marks the relativized
position inside the RC bugaeva2015 47 ; dalcorso2020 51 .
When the head noun fills a core argument position (S or O) in the embedded clause, this gap
is sufficient, because the verb's argument structure and the remaining NPs identify the role
of the missing participant. When the head fills an oblique position — instrumental, comitative,
locative, dative — the postposition that governs that oblique remains inside the RC, moved to
a position at or near the left edge of the embedded clause. This retained postposition is what
marks the semantic role of the gapped head when that role is not recoverable from the verb's
argument structure alone
bugaeva2015 47–49 ; nakagawa2024 504 .

The practical consequence is that a postposition's presence inside an RC signals an oblique
head. Conversely, removing the postposition collapses the arity to zero and makes the
relative-clause reading unavailable. Nakagawa's minimal pair makes this visible:
nea menoko ani turepta ni is grammatical as an instrumental RC
('the stick with which that woman dug uba-lilies'), but the postposition-free string
*nea menoko turepta ni has no gap and cannot relativize the instrument
nakagawa2024 505–506 .

nea menoko ani turepta ni | that woman INS dig.uba-lily stick | the stick with which that woman dug uba-lilies | The instrumental ani must stay in the clause; its removal collapses the arity. The lexicalized compound tureptani 'uba-lily-digging stick' contains the same morphological material but grammatically specifies no instrumental relation. | nakagawa2024:506 | HK

Only postpositions that are capable of appearing independently with a zero-anaphoric NP in a
main clause — ani with (instrumental) ,
turano together with (comitative) ,
eun to it (dative) , pakno up to, as far as — undergo this
retention directly. For locative and allative postpositions that require a case-marking
particle ( ta , un ), the relational noun
oro place, there must be inserted before the particle; it serves as the NP
host for the postposition and as the covert correlate of the gapped head
bugaeva2015 49 ; sato2008 172 ;
nakagawa2024 510–511 .

oro ta ku=yamuk cikuni | there LOC 1SG.A=gather.chestnuts tree | the tree at which I gathered chestnuts | The relational noun oro supplies the NP argument of the locative ta; cikuni 'tree' is the external head. Without oro the particle ta has no host. | sato2008:172 | CHI

The Keenan–Comrie accessibility hierarchy

Bugaeva maps Hokkaido Ainu relativization against the noun phrase accessibility hierarchy of
keenancomrie1977 and finds that HA relativizes all core and most oblique
positions; the only positions that resist are the alienable possessor and, directly, the
standard of comparison bugaeva2015 45, 47–49 . These findings are confirmed
for southern Hokkaido data by dalcorso2020 51 .

position in K&C hierarchy
attested in HA
strategy
grade

S (intransitive subject)
yes
gap
‹consensus›

A (transitive agent, 2-place)
yes
gap
‹consensus›

O (transitive patient, 2-place)
yes
gap
‹consensus›

O of 3-place verb (Recipient or Theme)
yes
gap
‹consensus›

A of 3-place verb (ditransitive subject)
no examples attested
gap (expected)
‹corpus-confirmed› (as gap)

oblique — instrumental
yes
ani retained
‹consensus›

oblique — comitative
yes
turano retained
‹consensus›

oblique — dative
yes
eun retained
‹consensus›

oblique — locative/allative
yes
oro + particle retained
‹consensus›

inalienable possessor
yes
POSS suffix on possessee retained
‹consensus›

alienable possessor
no
island constraint (see below)
‹consensus›

standard of comparison (direct)
no (workaround exists)
degree-adjunct construction
‹corpus-suggested›

Core argument positions

Subject (S) relativization leaves a gap in the single argument position of an intransitive
verb:

cise soy pakno arki utar | house outside up.to come.PL people | the men who came up to the doorway | arki is the suppletive plural of ek 'come'. The S (the men) is gapped; the directional adjunct cise soy pakno 'up to outside the house' stays in the clause. utar 'people' is the external head. | bugaeva2015:47 | HK

Agent (A) relativization gaps the subject of a transitive verb while leaving the object
inside the RC. A minimal pair from Bugaeva illustrates two parallel A-relativizations
under the same verb:

kem sawot pe tumi sawot pe | famine flee NMLZ war flee NMLZ | people fleeing famine, people fleeing war | sawot 'flee, escape' is transitive; kem 'famine' and tumi 'war' are the retained O arguments. The A (the fleeing people) is gapped and the formal noun pe supplies the generic head 'person'. In the corpus, O-relativization is more frequent than A-relativization (Bugaeva 2015: 48). | bugaeva2015:48 | HK

Object (O) relativization is the most frequent type in the corpus
bugaeva2015 48 . The head noun fills the O slot:

ku=nukar kanpisos | 1SG.A=see book | the book I read | Ijäs Lesson 11. The O (the book) is gapped; ku= is the 1SG agent prefix on the transitive verb nukar 'see, read'. The gap strategy is identical when the head is a bound noun rather than a common noun. | ijas2023 | HK

Three-place verbs

For three-place (ditransitive) predicates, either the Recipient or the Theme may be in O
position and relativized. Which argument occupies the O position is determined by the
object-indexing conventions treated in object-indexing-monotransitive ;
relativization follows that indexing. Bugaeva attests the Recipient as head with the
applicative e- applicative prefix (on the applicative system,
applicative-e ):

kampi a=e-pakasnu hekattar | paper 4.A=APPL-teach children | children who have been taught to read | hekattar 'children' is the Recipient-O head; kampi 'paper/literacy' is the retained Theme. The applicative e- licenses the Recipient in the O position. The A of the three-place verb (the teacher) is not the gapped position; it is simply unrealized (generic). | bugaeva2015:48 | HK

No attested example of the A of a three-place verb serving as RC head has been reported.
Bugaeva states the absence explicitly — "no examples attested so far" — without deciding
whether the position is grammatically excluded or merely rare
bugaeva2015 48 ‹corpus-confirmed› (as gap).

Oblique positions

The instrument, comitative, and dative are all relativizable with their postpositions
retained.

ani usey a=ku p | INS hot.water 4.A=drink NMLZ | a thing to drink hot water with | The instrumental ani is retained at the clause edge. The formal noun p is the generic instrument head. The indefinite fourth-person a= gives a generic reading. | nakagawa2024:504 | HK

turano ku=yupo kamuy cotca acapo | COMIT 1SG.POSS=older.brother bear shoot uncle | the uncle my brother shot the bear with | turano 'together with' is the retained comitative postposition. ku=yupo 'my older brother' is the A of cotca 'shoot'; kamuy 'bear' is the O. acapo 'uncle' is the external head — the gapped comitative co-participant. | bugaeva2015:48 | HK

eun a=nu usi ka isam | to.it 4.A=ask place even not.exist | there was no place to ask about it | eun is the retained dative postposition. usi 'place' is the formal-noun head; it fills the dative position. nu has the meaning 'ask, inquire about' here (Bugaeva 2015: 49 fn.9). | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

Locative and allative RC heads require the relational noun oro place, there
before the case particle; the oro hosts the particle and serves as
the implicit correlate of the gap:

oro un ku=arpa ipeusi | there ALLAT 1SG.S=go restaurant | the restaurant I went to | Ijäs Lesson 11. oro un 'to there' is the retained allative phrase. Without oro the allative un has no NP host and the sentence is ill-formed. ipeusi 'restaurant' is the external head. | ijas2023 | HK

Inalienable possessor

An inalienable possessor can be relativized. The possessed noun inside the RC retains its
possessive suffix, which serves as the morphological trace of the possessor-possessee
relation; the head noun fills the gapped possessor position
bugaeva2015 49 ; shibatani1990 41 .

cikir-ihi tanne kikir | leg-POSS long insect | an insect whose legs are long | cikir-ihi is the possessed form of cikir 'leg'; the POSS suffix -ihi remains inside the RC and identifies the possessor as the gapped position. kikir 'insect' is the external head — the gapped possessor. tanne 'long' is the stative predicate. | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

Unattested and blocked positions

Alienable possessor

Alienable possession in Hokkaido Ainu is itself expressed by a gapped prenominal relative
clause: ku=kor cep 'my fish' is literally '[the fish that I have]',
with kor have, possess as the transitive verb and ku=
as the 1SG agent bugaeva2022 ch. 1 ; bugaeva2015 49 .
Extracting a possessor from this structure would require relativization from inside a
relative clause — an island constraint — and the construction is accordingly unavailable
bugaeva2015 49 ‹consensus›.

Standard of comparison

Direct relativization of the standard of comparison is not attested. What Bugaeva
documents is a degree-adjunct workaround: a clause of the type 'a rich man [to whose
degree of wealth no rich man exists]' places the degree comparison inside the RC as a
modifier on the head, rather than gapping the standard participant from a transitive clause
bugaeva2015 45, 49 :

a=ona pakno nispa isam nispa ne | 4.A=father equal rich.man not.exist rich.man COP | my father was a rich man with no equal | Bugaeva's discovery example. The prenominal RC [pakno nispa isam] 'to whose degree of wealth no rich man exists' is a degree-adjunct modifier on the second nispa (the head). The standard of comparison is expressed as the negated content of the RC, not as a gapped participant. | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

Whether this is a grammaticalized equivalent to comparison-standard relativization or a
pragmatic repair is not settled ‹corpus-suggested›.

Ablative and mutative heads

Heads corresponding to ablative wa(no) 'from which' and mutative
ne 'as which' are absent from Bugaeva's inventory of retainable
postpositions and are not attested in any source read here
bugaeva2015 48–49 . Ijäs reports the same absence and explicitly withholds
constructed examples for these positions ijas2023 . They are not presented as
established ‹corpus-suggested›.

RC heads: common nouns and bound nouns

The external head of an HA relative clause may be a common noun or a
formal-defective-nouns formal (bound) noun . Among the formal nouns,
kur person (honorific) , utar people ,
p thing, person ~ pe thing, person ,
uske place, time , and hi thing, time, place (abstract) are,
in Bugaeva's account, "even more common" as RC heads than common nouns
bugaeva2015 47 . The bound noun supplies a generic semantic category —
person, people, thing, place — while the RC clause provides all the identifying content.

When the formal noun is p ~ pe , the
construction shades into the headless relative. That continuum is the subject of
nominalization-p-pe and headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses .
The lexicalized end — former RC-plus-formal-noun combinations frozen into compound nouns
such as aep food ( a=e-p , 'thing one eats') — is
treated in lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization . The interaction of
relativization with noun incorporation, where a patient argument merges into the verb stem
and changes the arity count, is treated in noun-incorporation-object .

Ijäs Lesson 11 — graded claims

Ijäs's English-language grammar guide covers relative clauses in Lesson 11
ijas2023 . Her examples are constructed teaching material; none are corpus-attested.
Her core grammatical claims — prenominal head-final order, absence of a relative pronoun,
gap strategy for core arguments, postposition retention for obliques (including the
oro -insertion rule for locatives/allatives) — align precisely with the
primary sources ‹consensus›. Her demonstration that
ku=kor cep 'my fish' is itself a relative clause ('the fish that I
have') follows the Handbook's alienable-possession analysis
bugaeva2022 ch. 1 ‹consensus›. And her identification of the ditransitive
subject as a problematic head position matches Bugaeva's "no examples attested so far"
bugaeva2015 48 ; she is consistent in flagging it as an open question rather
than asserting ungrammaticality ‹corpus-confirmed› (as gap).

Two of her claims need a hedge. Her report that the ordering of multiple stative modifiers
is free ( poro retar seta ~ retar poro seta
'big red dog, red big dog') is her own inference from the absence of counter-examples; she
acknowledges this explicitly. Modifier stacking is attested — Shibatani gives
ampene pirka sukup aynu from yukar data
shibatani1990 39 — but a free-order generalization across dialects exceeds
what the sources establish ‹corpus-suggested›. Her inability to locate attested examples of
ablative wa(no) or mutative ne as RC heads is
shared by all sources read here; the forms she cautiously suggests for these positions should
not be treated as established ijas2023 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Analyses and open questions

All major descriptive accounts converge on the externally-headed, prenominal gap strategy:
shibatani1990 39–42 , refsing1986 259–264 ,
sato2008 168–173 , nakagawa2024 503–511 , and
bugaeva2015 47–49 all describe the construction in these terms. The
differences among them are terminological — Refsing calls the process "adnominalization",
Nakagawa uses "関係文", Bugaeva "gapped prenominal relative clause" — rather than empirical.

Dal Corso 2020 proposes that certain southern Hokkaido indirect-evidential constructions
( siri ki , hawe as ,
humi as ) show a layout reminiscent of internally-headed relative
clauses, instantiated through what he calls classificatory noun incorporation
dalcorso2020 51–52 . This proposal covers the indirect-evidential
constructions specifically; it has not been adopted by Bugaeva, Satō, or Nakagawa; and its
claim of classificatory NI remains unconfirmed in the wider literature ‹contested›. The
relativization system described in this chapter is the externally-headed construction; the
IHRC/CNI question bears on the evidential construction and is addressed in
headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses .

Three gaps in the accessible evidence remain open. First, whether the ditransitive subject
position is grammatically excluded or merely unattested is not decided: the absence is
systematic — reported independently by Bugaeva 2015: 48 and by Ijäs — but no source
provides a grammaticality judgment supported by speaker elicitation ‹contested›. Second,
ablative and mutative RC heads are unconfirmed in any source read here ‹corpus-suggested›.
Third, the accessibility-hierarchy findings rest primarily on Saru and Chitose data
bugaeva2015 ; whether all Hokkaido dialects relativize the same positions has not
been systematically tested ‹speculative›.

title: Property-Concept (Adjectival) Predication without a Copula
summary: Predicate 'adjectives' as intransitive stative verbs taking person indexing — the clause-level consequence of the no-adjective-class thesis.
part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Property-Concept (Adjectival) Predication without a Copula
Part: Part XII — The Simple Clause and Nonverbal Predication
Summary: Predicate 'adjectives' as intransitive stative verbs taking person indexing — the clause-level consequence of the no-adjective-class thesis.

Property words as intransitive verbs

Property-concept words — pirka be good , poro be big ,
wen be bad , tanne be long, tall ,
pon be small , hure be red , pewre be young ,
rui be heavy, strong , ri be high and their class-mates —
occupy the predicate slot of a simple clause without a copula. The clause
cape pon 'the cat is small' is structurally parallel to
cape hoyupu 'the cat ran': in both, a bare intransitive verb is
the entire predicate nakagawa2024 118 ; nakagawa2022pos 474–475 .
This behavior reflects a categorical fact: Hokkaido Ainu has no dedicated adjective
word class ‹consensus›. What English and Japanese encode in a distinct adjective category
is covered in Ainu by a set of intransitive stative verbs. The three-criterion
argument — morphological identity with intransitive verbs, structural parallelism in the
prenominal slot, and evidential compatibility — is set out in
no-adjective-class-property-verbs ; the present chapter treats the predication
patterns that the analysis implies.

Because property words are verbs, they take subject-agreement affixes, combine with the
standard clausal negation morphology, and take the evidential tail
ruwe ne exactly as any other predicate does
nakagawa2024 118–119 ; sato2008 20–21 .
A learner who writes *pirka ne for '(it) is good' has imported a
copula requirement that the grammar does not impose
nakagawa2024 119 . The copula
ne is restricted to predicate-nominal clauses (see
copula-ne-predicate-nominals ) and cannot combine with a property verb.

opitta poro ru-we ne | everything be.big track-POSS COP | Everything was big. | The property verb poro 'be big' is the sole predicate; the evidential frame ruwe ne closes the clause exactly as it does on action verbs. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | Maruya Kazuko 丸野和子, '「Kuma」turano k=an', アイヌタイムズ 15/1 [ainu-times/015/1#5]

Person marking

Property verbs take the intransitive subject ( S ) person affixes — the same set that
marks the sole argument of action verbs — following the paradigm described in
person-marking-architecture . The prefix ku= marks
first-singular S on any intransitive stem, whether it denotes motion, existence, or a
property:

sonno ku=pewre or ta ku=nu yukar ne ru-we ne | really 1SG.S=be.young place LOC 1SG.A=hear epic COP track-POSS COP | It was when I was really young that I heard this epic. | Nakagawa ex. 1 (p. 118). The 1SG.S prefix ku= on pewre 'be young' is identical to its use on ku=an 'I exist' or ku=arpa 'I go'. | nakagawa2024:118 | SAR

The full intransitive paradigm applies without adjustment. The table below illustrates with
pirka be good (Saru–Chitose); the forms are constructed on the basis of
nakagawa2024 118, 165–167 :

person
S -affix
form on pirka
gloss

1SG
ku=
ku=pirka
'I am good'

2SG
e=
e=pirka
'you are good'

3
Ø
pirka
'(he / she / it) is good'

4 / INDEF
=an
pirka=an
'one is good; (they) are good'

The third-person cell is zero-marked, as for all intransitive verbs; the fourth-person
(INDEF) uses the enclitic =an . No object affix can appear: property
verbs are strictly intransitive and take no object slot. A member of the aynu-corpora
community observed that the zero-marked (3rd-person) form of pirka
shifts contextually toward 'that's fine / it's OK' when no overt subject is present, while
person-marked forms like e=pirka read as a direct evaluation of the
indexed person ('you are beautiful, in good shape')
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, November 2024) ‹contested›.

Attributive and predicative positions

Property verbs appear in two syntactic positions: as bare predicates heading the main clause,
and as prenominal modifiers preceding a noun head. The morphological form is identical in
both — a fact recognized since kindaichi1936 123 ‹consensus›. In predicative
position the property verb alone constitutes the predicate:

toan nupuri ri | that mountain be.high | That mountain is high. | Kindaichi & Chiri §189B. Predicative property verb with zero third-person subject marking. | kindaichi1936:123 | HK

In attributive position the same verb precedes the noun head with no morphological change —
no suffix, no linker, no case marker. This is structurally parallel to a prenominal relative
clause in Ainu, where the predicate verb likewise immediately precedes the head noun
nakagawa2022pos 475 ; see prenominal-gap-relative-clauses :

attributive (prenominal)
gloss
predicative
gloss
source

pon hekaci
small child
hekaci pon
the child is small
kindaichi1936 123

poro cise
big house
cise poro
the house is big
nakagawa2024 118

rui apto
heavy rain
apto rui
the rain is heavy
kindaichi1936 123 ; shibatani1990 19

The prenominal slot admits person-marked property verbs: ku=pewre or ta
in the example above reads as 'in the time of my being young', a headless relative clause
with the property verb predicated of the implicit head
nakagawa2024 118 . This confirms that the prenominal position is a syntactic
slot that verbs occupy, not a morphologically distinct adjectival form.

The stative–inchoative duality

Like other Ainu verbs, property-concept words encode both the state ('be X') and its onset
('become X') without distinct forms; context determines the reading
nakagawa2024 118–119 ; nakagawa2022pos 477 .
poro means 'be big' in poro cise an 'there is
a big house' (see existential-locational-an-oka ) and 'grow big' in the
following corpus example:

tane pakno e=poro ru-we ne korka | now till 2SG.S=be.big track-POSS COP but | You have grown big, but … | The 2SG.S prefix e= marks the single argument of poro in the inchoative reading; the evidential ruwe ne predicates the growth as a recognized fact. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko 川上まつ子, uwepeker 民話18, AA研アイヌ語資料 [aa-irc/025#266]

The inchoative reading is contextual, not morphological. It follows that the copula
ne cannot be inserted to force an inchoative:
*poro ne for 'become big' is ungrammatical in Hokkaido Ainu
nakagawa2024 119 . Where the copula does appear after an inchoative-type
expression (as in nispa ne 'become a chief'), the complement is a
nominal, not a property verb. The copula's own stative–inchoative duality belongs to the
predicate-nominal system treated in copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

A community discussion noted that both ne an and
pirka an carry 'become' readings, raising the question whether the
existential an contributes an inchoative-aspect value in such
environments
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, November 2024) ‹speculative›.
The aspectual uses of an as a periphrastic auxiliary are set out in
existential-aspect-an-oka .

Negation

Property verbs follow the standard clausal negation strategies of intransitive verbs: the
negator somo precedes the verb
( somo V ), or the frame
V ka somo ki is used for contrastive or emphatic negation
nakagawa2024 127 ; sato2008 21–22 :

merayke=an ka somo ki no | be.cold=4.S even NEG do CONJ | not feeling cold, and … | Nakagawa ex. 15 (p. 127, 表12). The 4th-person S suffix =an indexes the subject of the experiencer predicate merayke 'feel cold'; ka somo ki frames the negation contrastively. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR

These patterns contrast with the dedicated frames required by the copula and the existential
sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 407, 450 . The copula requires
X ka somo ne (the negator placed before
ne itself), and the existential an recruits
its suppletive negative isam 'not exist'. The property verb is
the only non-dynamic predicate type that takes ordinary clausal negation, a direct reflex
of its status as an ordinary intransitive verb. The full scope of negation across predicate
types is treated in negation-somo-standard-clausal and
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation .

Negation patterns for non-dynamic predicate types

predicate type
negation pattern
example
source

property verb

somo V /
V ka somo ki

merayke=an ka somo ki no
nakagawa2024 127

copula ne
X ka somo ne
tan pe anak seta ka somo ne wa
sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 407

existential an / oka
suppletive isam
nep ka isam 'there is nothing'
nakagawa2024 450

Degree and intensification

Property verbs carry no inherent comparative or degree morphology. Degree is expressed by
adverbs that precede the property verb without triggering any change to its form
nakagawa2024 120 . The most frequently recruited degree adverbs are
sonno really, truly , arikinne very ,
poronno greatly, a lot , and earkinne exceedingly .
The following example shows arikinne scoping over an attributive
property verb:

toan kur arikinne wen pe ne | that.one person very be.bad thing COP | That person is a very bad one. | Satō ex. 4 (p. 20). The degree adverb arikinne modifies the attributive property verb wen; the whole complex wen pe serves as the nominal complement of the copula. | sato2008:20 | SAR

The prefix si- combines directly with the verb root for intensification:
si-poro 'really big'
nakagawa2024 120 .

Property verbs also generate manner adverbs for other predicates through the suffix
-no : tunas be fast yields
tunasno 'fast(ly)', moyre be slow yields
moyreno 'slowly'
nakagawa2024 120 . The derived adverb quantifies the manner of the event
described by the main predicate and is not itself a predicate. The comparative construction
( akkari 'surpassing, more than') and the full adverb system are
treated in adverbs-degree-comparison ; the sources consulted do not provide a
systematic degree–comparison paradigm for property verbs, and the gap is noted there.

Property verbs as manner auxiliaries

A small set of property verbs — including moyre be slow ,
tunas be fast , and hayta be insufficient — can follow
a main predicate as a post-verbal auxiliary expressing the pace or adequacy of the
denoted event nakagawa2024 121 :

ek moyre ru-we an na | come be.slow track-POSS exist FIN | It is slow in coming. | Nakagawa §3.4.3 ex. 5. The property verb moyre follows the main predicate ek 'come' and qualifies its pace; the existential an (not the copula) closes the evidential frame, as expected for a content-question-capable sentence-final form. | nakagawa2024:121 | SAR

The adjective debate

The classical treatment in kindaichi1936 123–124 (§§129, 189–193) recognized
that property words inflect for person just as verbs do, yet nonetheless posited a separate
形容詞 'adjective' class defined by its dual attributive–predicative distribution. Kindaichi
conceded the instability of the boundary directly: "the difference between a verb and an
adjective is razor-thin" (動詞と形容詞の差は紙一重; §129). His own person-inflection tables
for 形容詞 reproduce the intransitive S paradigm without modification — the concession is
already implicit in the paradigm.

Chiri's reanalysis ( chiri1942 , summarized in nakagawa2022pos 478 )
proposed the 用詞 category — a class of stative verbs covering property meanings — and
collapsed the distinction. All subsequent major grammars of Hokkaido Ainu adopt that
position: refsing1986 classifies property words as a semantically defined subclass
of verbs without a distinct morphosyntactic status; shibatani1990 19 notes
that property words take "the same personal affixes" as intransitive verbs;
sato2008 21 places them within his four-verb-type classification as a
stative subtype; and nakagawa2022pos 474–478 provides the most detailed
argument, covering all three diagnostic dimensions ‹consensus›. In terms of the cross-linguistic
typology of intransitive predication stassen1997 , Hokkaido Ainu is an
unambiguous case of the verbal-property type: the coding of property meanings through
intransitive verbs extends without exception to every diagnostic — morphological marking,
syntactic distribution, TAM compatibility, and negation strategy.

One genuinely open question concerns the interaction of property predication with the
evidential system. Takahashi's study of Tokachi Ainu shows that individual-level (permanent)
property predicates select the trace nominalizer ru rather than
the visual nominalizer sir , because the visual form is restricted to
stage-level (transient) predicates takahashi2013 131–133 . This predication-type
constraint operates on individual-level vs. stage-level readings across the verb inventory
and is not specific to property words, but it shows that the ontological character of property
predicates is grammatically active in Ainu. The interaction of property predicates with the
evidential paradigm is treated in ruwe-ne-inferential and
evidential-schema-overview .

title: Proto-Ainu Reconstruction: Segments and Accent Classes
summary: Reconstruction of the Proto-Ainu consonant/vowel inventory and accent classes, the correspondence sets to modern Hokkaido reflexes, Shiratori's *ia revision of the palatalization account, the accented/accentless split, and the Sakhalin length to Hokkaido pitch correspondence.
part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Proto-Ainu Reconstruction: Segments and Accent Classes
Part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
Summary: Reconstruction of the Proto-Ainu consonant/vowel inventory and accent classes, the correspondence sets to modern Hokkaido reflexes, Shiratori's *ia revision of the palatalization account, the accented/accentless split, and the Sakhalin length to Hokkaido pitch correspondence.

Method and limits of Proto-Ainu reconstruction

Ainu is a language isolate: all attempts to establish a genetic relationship with any other
language or family have failed janhunen2022 §2 . For an isolate, the comparative
method — which enables deep-time reconstruction by aligning cognate sets across related branches
— cannot apply when only a single lineage survives. Three instruments remain available
alonso2022 §1 : internal reconstruction , which Alonso de la Fuente
calls "by far the most powerful" tool; contact-induced-change analysis , chiefly against
Japanese and, on Sakhalin, against Nivkh; and typological reasoning , which carries no
independent probatory weight and must be handled with caution. The reconstruction surveyed here
is accordingly shallow in time depth: internal evidence licenses proposals reaching roughly a
thousand years into the past, and the one firm external anchor is the thirteenth-century Great
Migration that separated the Hokkaido, Sakhalin, and Kuril varieties
alonso2022 §1, §4 . Deeper pre-history — the Pre-Proto-Ainu stage — is
reachable only "ad nauseam by internal reconstruction" and cannot be independently validated
alonso2022 §4 .

The two primary sources for this chapter are vovin1993 — the monograph-length
reconstruction of Proto-Ainu, abbreviated RPA — and the critical revision by
alonso2022 (abbreviated "New"). The RPA monograph is not held in the project
repository; all RPA reconstructions and associated table references are taken from Alonso de la
Fuente's reproductions, and citations of Vovin 1993 therefore carry the reported-evidence
status already assigned to that entry. The genetic-isolate question and the failed
macro-comparisons are treated in genetic-position-macro-comparison ;
grammaticalization pathways for individual morphemes (the affiliative, the applicatives, the
evidential nominals) are in internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways .

Periodization: Pre-Proto-Ainu, Proto-Ainu, Common Ainu

Alonso de la Fuente distinguishes three stages alonso2022 §4, Tables 18–19 ,
drawing an analogy with the Slavic case (Pre-PA ≈ PIE, PA ≈ Proto-Slavic, Common Ainu ≈
Common Slavic), while noting that only Common Ainu and later can claim anything resembling a
running comparative method:

stage
diagnostic features
representative form

Pre-Proto-Ainu
affiliative marker still *CVCVx ; classifiers (if real)
productive; grammaticalization pathways incomplete
*ti=(ʔ)e pe 'what all eat'

Proto-Ainu (PA)
a reconstructable node, not a dateable period; the stage targeted by internal
reconstruction
PA */ti-ʔe-p/ 'fish'

Common Ainu (CA)
immediately pre-Migration; palatalized series collapsed; *l/*ʈ
merging with *ɾ ; trochaic→iambic shift
CA */ciʔep/ → Saru
cep , ‹SA› Raiciska ceh

The CA innovations listed by Alonso de la Fuente (Table 19) are: merger of *l
and *ɾ to *ɾ ; merger of palatalized and plain
sibilants ( *sʸ, *s > *s ); shift of the sibilant before
*i ; aspiration of *h ; and the collapse of
*tʸ, *pʸ to *c . These CA-level changes account
for the shared features of all attested Hokkaido lects relative to the older reconstructed stage.
The Hokkaido-internal dialect archaeology built on this foundation is treated in
hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry .

The Proto-Ainu vowel system

Every documented Ainu lect — Hokkaido, Sakhalin, Kuril — has exactly five vowel qualities,
a e i o u . Vovin posited a seventeen-vowel PA system (twelve short
plus five long: i ü e ö E a ï ë A O o u plus long counterparts) to
regularize what he called the "chaotic" distribution of the
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative suffix
-V : in Vovin's account, the suffix vowel is selected by the last vowel
of the base according to a vowel-harmony-like table (RPA; reproduced in Alonso 2022, Table 2).
Alonso rejects the seventeen-vowel system on phonetic and historical grounds: the proposed
extra vowel qualities leave no trace in the oldest Nara-period loanword layer, and the
distributional alternations attributed to them are reassignable to the five basic vowels without
residue alonso2022 §2.1 ‹contested›.

An alternative account of the affiliative -(V)hV shape comes from
Janhunen's vowel-dropping hypothesis (Janhunen 2020, reported via Alonso 2022 §2.1): the
marker was originally an undefined final consonant *-x ; the synchronic
shape reflects the lost second vowel of a once-disyllabic base — *CVC.Vx
> CVC , but *CVC.Vx > CVC-Vx when the marker follows — and the
sequence later fused with the noun hi 'time, place, thing' to yield
the current possessive shape. Alonso calls this "a triumph of internal reconstruction"
alonso2022 §2.1 . Community discussion on the aynu-corpora Discord has
independently noted this as the more parsimonious account, opposing the introduction of a
phonemically unmotivated *H
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024-12-23 ‹contested›). The morphophonology
of the affiliative suffix — its echo vowels, allomorphic conditioning, and distributional
constraints — is treated in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

The reconstructed PA vowel system is the same five-quality inventory that all modern lects
share alonso2022 §2.1 ‹consensus›. Whether PA also had distinctive vowel
length is the central suprasegmental dispute, addressed in §6 below.

The Proto-Ainu consonant system: Vovin's reconstruction

For the modern Hokkaido consonant system as a baseline, see consonant-inventory .
Vovin proposed the following PA inventory (RPA; reproduced in alonso2022 Table 3 ):

labial
coronal
dorsal
glottal

stop (half-voiced)
*p
*t
*k
*ʔ

voiced stop

*d
*g

nasal
*m
*n

fricative / glide

*s *y

*h (*H)

flap

*ɾ

The half-voicing of *p *t *k is motivated by alternations in
pre-scientific wordlists — e.g. Dixon (1883) ⟨jabush⟩ for
capus 'lip' — which suggest that the stops were phonetically
intermediate between voiced and voiceless at an earlier period (via
alonso2022 §2.2 ). Contemporary reviews of RPA raised two structural
objections: the absence of *w , a phoneme present in every attested
Ainu lect (Sidwell 1996, via Alonso 2022 §2.2.3); and the background assumption of an
Austroasiatic affiliation that distorted the consonant correspondences (Sidwell 1996,
Abondolo 1997, Witczak 1999; all via alonso2022 §1 ).

Revisions to the consonant correspondences: Alonso 2022

Alonso de la Fuente's revision addresses eleven correspondence classes
alonso2022 §2.2, Table 5 . The table below lists each class in RPA notation,
its attested reflexes across the lect sample, and the revised reconstruction
(all second-hand via Alonso 2022, citing Vovin 1993):

#
RPA
key reflexes
Alonso "New"

1
*d-
HO r- , Nayoro t- ,
‹SA› RA r-
*l-

2
*ɾ
r- everywhere

3
*tr-
HO r- , Nayoro t- ,
‹SA› RA r-
*ʈ- (retroflex)

4
*g

i
*w (Sc. 1) / *s (Sc. 2)

*hd-

*pr-

*tʸ

9
*h- (initial)
HO h- , Nayoro ∅,
‹SA› h-/∅

10
*-ʔ- (medial)
∅ or -ʔ-
*-ʔ-

11
*H-
∅ (Bihoro h- )
— (rejected)

The most consequential revisions are summarised in the subsections below.

*d- reanalysed as *l-

Vovin's word-initial *d- is reinterpreted as a lateral
*l- alonso2022 §2.2.1 . Ainu synchronically
lacks /l/ — a typological rarity shared in the immediate region
only with Japanese. The key evidence is the Nairo subdialect of ‹SA›, where this class
surfaces as t- where all other lects have r- :
‹SA› Nairo taarak 'smooth' : raarak
elsewhere; teera 'wind' : rera . A lateral
that becomes a tap [ɾ] in most environments but a dental stop
word-initially in Nairo parallels the Korean lateral distribution. Early Ainu-to-Ghilyak
loans showing Proto-Ghilyak *l- corresponding to RPA
*d- provide an independent, contact-based check (RPA 160,
via alonso2022 §2.2.1 ; for the Nivkh contact background, see
northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu ). Community observation on the
aynu-corpora Discord that retar ~ tretar ~ tetar 'white' shows
the same initial-consonant split — reconstructing the initial as PA
*l (Alonso "New") — is consistent with this analysis
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024-11-24 ‹contested›).

*tr- reanalysed as *ʈ-

Vovin's cluster notation *tr- is reanalysed as a single retroflex
stop *ʈ- (or retroflex flap ~*ʈɽ )
alonso2022 §2.2.2 . The cluster form never appears in ‹SA› sources, where
Dobrotvorskij and Piłsudski recorded [tr]~[r] . Decisive phonetic
attestation comes from the Bihoro-dialect idiolect of Ginosuke Kikuchi, where the voiced
retroflex [ɖ] was still audible when Hattori's team collected data
(Hattori 1964: 34, cited via alonso2022 §2.2.2 ). Cognate sets include PA
*ʈam.u 'soul', *ʈek.e 'drumstick',
*ʈus 'skin' alonso2022 Table 6 .

The *g / *hd / *s tangle: two scenarios

Three RPA classes — *g , *hd , and one reflex
of *s — converge on h- and
s- before i in the attested lects,
reflecting the change *w > *h before front vowels (parallel to
Greek spiritus asper or Slavic prothetic *h- ). Rather than
adjudicating between the two economical analyses, Alonso presents them side by side
alonso2022 §2.2.3, Table 7 ‹contested›:

Scenario 1: *hd → *kʷ ;
*g → *w ; one *s class.

Scenario 2: *hd → *w ;
*g → *s ; a palatalized sibilant *sʸ
alongside *s .

Both scenarios restore a *w absent from Vovin's inventory —
a phoneme present in every attested Ainu lect — which is the principal structural
advance over RPA.

Initial *h- and medial *-ʔ-: complementary distribution

Vovin treated word-initial *h- and medial
*-ʔ- as separate phonemes. Following Shiraishi (1999, via
Alonso 2022 §2.2.4), Alonso argues they are in complementary distribution: the glottal
stop has no lexical function, plays no role in morphonology, and is epenthetic in modern
Hokkaido Ainu alonso2022 §2.2.4 . In most HA dialects the initial
*h- and *ʔ- merged as laryngeal
h- , while medial *ʔ was lost or preserved
as an epenthetic glottal. The phonemic status of the glottal stop in the modern Hokkaido
system is discussed in glottal-stop .

Bihoro h-prothesis: an independent innovation

Vovin posited a PA consonant *H to account for Bihoro prothetic
h- before original vowel-initial stems: Bihoro
haspa 'deaf' : aspa ,
ham 'nail' : am . Alonso rejects this:
prothetic h- in Bihoro is an independent innovation triggered by
initial-syllable stress (the typologically common #V → hV tendency,
citing Blevins 2008 via Alonso 2022 §2.2.5), and it functions to disambiguate homophones
(Bihoro ham 'claw' vs am 'nail',
Alonso 2022 Table 8) alonso2022 §2.2.5 . Kuril lects show the converse
pattern independently — prothetic h- alongside loss of original
h- (Alonso 2022 Table 9) — which further undermines the
*H analysis. Hokkaido microvariation in word-initial
h- , including h-elision in eastern standard-accent dialects, is
treated in hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

The palatalized series: a Pre-Proto-Ainu hypothesis

Alonso proposes a full Pre-PA palatalized consonant set —
*pʸ *tʸ *kʸ *hʸ *sʸ (and possibly
*mʸ *nʸ ) — which collapsed into modern
/c/ etc., analogously to the Latin palatal series in Spanish
alonso2022 §2.2.6, Tables 10–11 . The evidence for
*pʸ comes from correspondence class #6 in the table above:
southwestern Hokkaido par 'mouth' : northeastern and Kuril
car , with Nayoro and Asahikawa having both forms. This
distribution, called the pa-:ca- "pseudo-correspondence" in the
dialectological literature because it does not extend to words with other vowels, has
at least four competing analyses nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 :
Kindaichi and Chiri's diachronic change ( *pa → ca ); Kirikae's
(1994) two-morpheme-ab-initio account; Nakagawa's (1996) priority for
ca- ; and Fukazawa's (2014/2017) separate-morpheme analysis.
Alonso reconstructs *pʸaɾ as a fifth account, attributing the
southwest/northeast split to the collapse of the palatalized labial
alonso2022 §2.2.6 . The palatalized-series hypothesis is
explicitly typology-driven — posited because the *s/sʸ
and *t/tʸ doublets make it typologically expected, not because
reflex distributions force it ‹speculative›. Shiratori's (2026) proposal of PA
*ia as a new diagnostic for the palatalized series adds
independent support for at least *pʸ and
*tʸ shiratori2026 ‹speculative›.

The two revised PA consonant inventories proposed by Alonso de la Fuente are (Table 14;
alonso2022 Table 14 ):

scenario
PA consonant inventory

1
