2
p pʸ t tʸ k {kʸ} ʔ / m n / s {sʸ} y w [h] [hʸ] / ɾ~l ʈ

Items in {curly braces} are motivated by typological reasoning only; items in [square brackets]
are plausibly allophonic. The choice between the two scenarios depends on resolving the
*g/*hd/*s tangle in §4.3 and remains open ‹contested›.

Dialect correspondence sets

The cognate sets below illustrate the principal PA reconstructions against the lect sample
(Alonso 2022, Tables 1–6, 14, 18; via alonso2022 ). Lect abbreviations follow
Alonso 2022 §0: Y = Yakumo, HO = Horobetsu, SA = Saru (Alonso's convention), B = Bihoro;
‹SA› RA = Sakhalin Raiciska; ‹KU› KD/KK/KV = Kuril sources (Dybowski/Klaproth/Voznesenskij),
NA = ‹SA› Nairo:

PA (Alonso "New")

‹KU›

The ‹SA› final-consonant reflexes — sik → sis ,
cup → cuh — are instances of debuccalization: stop codas weaken to
h , then shift further to s after front vowels
tangiku2022 §2.3 . The underlying stop resurfaces in the ‹SA›
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative/possessive form
( sik-ihi , not *sis-ihi ), confirming the
reconstructed coda tangiku2022 §2.3, §5 . The full diachrony of this process
is treated in final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex and
sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast .

The internal reconstruction of cep 'fish' is Alonso de la Fuente's
showcase for what internal reconstruction can recover for an isolate
alonso2022 Table 18 :

cep | fish | fish | Reconstructed pre-form: PA */ti-ʔe-p/ (Alonso de la Fuente 2022, Table 18), decomposed as the medial/4th-person prefix *ti-, the verb *ʔe 'eat', and the nominalizer *-p — 'what all eat'. Common Ainu */ciʔep/ gives Horobetsu ciʔep (glottal retained), Saru cep (glottal lost), ‹SA› Raiciska ceh (p debuccalized). Triple-corroborated: Alonso 2022 Table 18, morpheme-db curated decomposition (confidence 1.0), and community discussion (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-06). | tamura1996 | SAR

ci=e-p | 4.A=eat-NMLZ | (Pre-PA reconstructed form) 'the thing that all eat' | The etymological decomposition underlying Saru cep. The prefix ci= descends from the pre-grammaticalized medial/resultative morpheme *ti=; -p is the nominalizer productive elsewhere in the grammar. See Xr internal-reconstruction-and-grammaticalization-pathways for the full grammaticalization pathway.

Proto-Ainu suprasegmentals: the length–pitch dispute

The most contested area of PA reconstruction concerns the suprasegmental system. Sakhalin and
Kuril lects have distinctive vowel length ( a aa e ee i ii o oo u uu ),
confined to open stressed syllables; Hokkaido Ainu has
pitch-accent-placement-rule pitch accent and no distinctive length.
Hattori (1967, via Alonso 2022 §2.3) first stated the systematic correspondence: ‹SA›/‹KU›
long vowel ↔ HA high pitch — e.g. HA húre 'red' ↔ ‹SA›
huure ; HA túki 'cup' ↔ ‹SA›
tuuki ; HA sísam 'Japanese person' ↔ ‹SA›
siisam
alonso2022 §2.3 ; tangiku2022 §2.1 .
Three reconstructions of this correspondence have been proposed:

view
proponents
claim

(a) length only; no PA pitch
Hattori (1967); De Boer (2010)
PA had vowel length; HA pitch accent developed through contact with Japanese
(via alonso2022 §2.3 )

(b) both length and pitch
Vovin (1993); Itabashi (2004); Shimabukuro (2004)
PA had both; Proto-‹SA› subsequently lost pitch under Amur-Sakhalin contact
(via alonso2022 §2.3 )

(c) pitch only; no PA length
Satō (2015, 2016)
PA had pitch accent; ‹SA› length is secondary, arising through open-syllable
phonetic lengthening (via alonso2022 §2.3 )

Alonso de la Fuente's chapter is internally inconsistent on this point: §2.3 sides with
Satō's pitch-first view (c), while §2.4 endorses Hattori's length-first view (a), and the
chapter identifies Vovin's mora-counting assumption in RPA as "the source of all problems"
alonso2022 §2.3, §2.4 . This inconsistency
cannot be resolved from the sources consulted here; the reconstruction of PA prosody is genuinely
unsettled ‹contested›.

A further complication is the trochaic→iambic shift hypothesis. Satō (2015/2016) and Shiraishi
(2017) — both via alonso2022 §2.3 — propose that PA/Common Ainu was
trochaic and that accent spread rightward to produce the historical iambic pattern visible
in most Hokkaido dialects. ‹SA› archaisms supply the key diagnostic forms: ‹SA› Nairo
noociw : HA nocíw 'star', and
niisew : HA niséw — where ‹SA› length
corresponds to HA low pitch rather than high, reversing the expected correspondence
and motivating the rightward-shift analysis. The aynu-corpora Discord has noted this debate
as an unresolved live question, recognising the Hattori/Satō opposition accurately
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11 ‹contested›). Itabashi's dedicated study
of the ‹SA› vowel-length ↔ HA pitch-accent historical relation itabashi2001 has
not yet been incorporated in detail here; the diachrony is captured via Hattori (1967) and Itabashi (2004) as reported
in Alonso 2022 and Tangiku 2022. The relationship between the accent classes and the
synchronic accent-placement rules is treated in pitch-accent-placement-rule
and the prosodic-unit debate in mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .

Open questions and honest gaps

Several questions bearing on PA segments cannot be settled from the sources consulted. The choice
between Alonso's two consonant scenarios (§4.3) turns on evidence that neither phonological
typology nor the attested reflex distribution yet settles decisively. The palatalized-series
proposal (§4.5) rests entirely on typological reasoning and is not required by the philological
data; Shiratori 2026 adds some support, but the hypothesis remains speculative. The RPA
monograph (Vovin 1993) is not in the project repository, so every page reference to RPA
is second-hand via Alonso 2022, and load-bearing RPA citations should be verified against
the original before finalization vovin1993 . The Hattori (1967) length-pitch
correspondence paper, Hattori and Chiri (1960), Asai (1974), and the Satō (2015/2016)
accent papers are all cited second-hand via the Handbook chapters read.

The three-stage model (§2) is heuristic. Pre-Proto-Ainu is a theoretical construct reached
only by sustained internal inference, without external validation; the thirteenth-century
Great Migration provides the one firm absolute anchor. The comparison with PIE/Slavic stages,
while structurally illuminating, cannot import the evidential standards that the comparative
method sustains in well-attested families — a limitation intrinsic to isolate reconstruction
alonso2022 §1, §4 ; janhunen2022 §2 .

title: Quotative Complementation and Reported Speech (sekor)
summary: The dominant complementation strategy: framing a (direct-form) clause with the quotative sekor under verbs of speech, cognition, and intention.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Quotative Complementation and Reported Speech (sekor)
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The dominant complementation strategy: framing a (direct-form) clause with the quotative sekor under verbs of speech, cognition, and intention.

sekor in the complementizer system

Hokkaido Ainu reports speech and thought by placing the invariable particle
sekor at the right edge of the quoted string, before the matrix
verb of saying or thinking. This mechanism occupies a distinct slot in Refsing's
four-way clause-embedding typology: conjunctionalization, nominalization,
quotationalization , and adnominalization — with quotationalization, realized by
sekor (and its variant ari ), forming a
separate category from the nominalized-complementizer system
refsing1986 §14.14 . The nominalized complementizers —
kuni , kunak , yak ,
kusu — that Nakagawa treats in his indirect-speech section
operate through the formal-noun nominalizing machinery described in
nominalization-p-pe and
nominalized-complements-control-raising ;
sekor does not nominalize — it quotes directly
nakagawa2024 475–480 .

The matrix verbs that take sekor overlap substantially with
those that select the indirect-speech markers: ye say ,
itak speak, say , haweok speak up , and
ramu think . The choice between the quotative and the nominalizing
complementizers encodes the direct-vs-indirect distinction and controls the
person-marking pattern in the embedded clause
nakagawa2024 475 ; bugaeva2008 31–35 . How the
formal-noun class that enables nominalized complementation is constituted is set out
in formal-defective-nouns .

Form and position

sekor is morphologically invariant: it carries no person
affixes, no TAM morphology, and no allomorphic alternation conditioned by the shape
of the preceding clause. Its canonical position is immediately after the last element
of the quoted string and before the matrix verb:
[quoted clause] sekor [matrix verb]. Refsing identifies a
variant ari with the same quotative function in the Shizunai
dialect; both are treated as "quotationalizers" forming a class distinct from the
nominalized-complementizer inventory refsing1986 §14.14 .

The quoted clause preserves all features of a main clause: person affixes, polarity,
TAM markers, and clause-internal particles are intact inside the quotation.
Sentence-final particles ( na , wa , etc.)
may appear within the quoted string but not between sekor and
the matrix verb — a distributional restriction shared with the indirect-speech markers,
whose embedded clauses also exclude final particles
nakagawa2024 475–476 .

pirka sekor a=ye | be.good QUOT 4.A=say | He/she said, 'It is good.' | Minimal sekor frame: an unmodified stative predicate quoted before the fourth-person matrix verb a=ye 'say'. | HK

ek=an sekor ku=ramu | come=4.S QUOT 1SG.A=think | I thought, 'He/she will come.' | sekor with the thought verb ramu: the fourth-person intransitive suffix =an on ek 'come' marks the referent of the thought inside the quote. The matrix ku=ramu carries the first-person speaker-agent. | HK

Direct and indirect quotation

The clearest evidence for the sekor vs indirect-complementizer
distinction is person marking. Under sekor , the original
speaker's person forms are preserved unchanged inside the quote. Under
kuni , kunak , and
yak , the embedded clause's person is adjusted to the reporting
narrator's viewpoint: an original first person shifts to the fourth, the
indefinite/logophoric person
nakagawa2024 475 . The indirect-speech markers also differ among
themselves in the temporal or semantic value they impose on the embedded proposition
nakagawa2024 475–480 ; sato2008 176–177 .

type
particle
temporal / semantic value
person shift
typical matrix verb

direct quotation

sekor (or ari )

any
none — original person preserved

ye , itak ,
ramu

indirect, future/directive
kuni
future, inferred, wished, directive
1 → 4
ye , ramu

indirect, inferred
kunak
future, inferred
1 → 4
ye , ramu

indirect, past/general
yak
already happened; ongoing; general truth
1 → 4

ye , ramu ,
takar

directive complement
kusu
causative-directive 'that X do Y'
—
ye

embedded question
ya
polar or content question ('whether, what')
—

eramiskari , eraman

The person contrast between sekor and
kuni is illustrated by the following pair. The proposition
'I will return' appears first as a direct quotation (constructed), then under the
indirect-speech complementizer kuni (attested in Nakagawa's
grammar):

ku=hosipi sekor a=ye | 1SG.S=return QUOT 4.A=say | He/she said, 'I will return.' | Direct quotation under sekor: the first-person intransitive prefix ku= on hosipi 'return' is the original speaker's own form, intact inside the quote. The matrix a=ye carries the fourth-person narrator-agent. | HK

hosipi=an kuni a=ye | return=4.S COMP 4.A=say | I said I would return. | Indirect quotation under kuni: the original first-person subject is shifted to the fourth-person intransitive suffix =an, adopting the narrator's viewpoint. kuni nominalizes the embedded clause as the nominal object of a=ye 'say'. | nakagawa2024:476 | HK

The temporal contrast between kunak (future proposition,
reported as ongoing) and yak (past or general proposition) runs
in parallel within the indirect system. Satō's kunak example
combines the indirect-speech marker with the ongoing-aspect phrase
kor an :

ku=matnepo tanto ek kunak ye kor an | 1SG.POSS=daughter today come COMP say CONJ exist | My daughter says she will come today. | kunak frames the future proposition ek 'come.SG'; kor an 'while/is' marks the report as ongoing. The embedded subject of ek is the shifted fourth person (the daughter). The possessive ku=matnepo 'my daughter' is the topic of the whole utterance. | sato2008:177 | CHI

The directive use of kuni — 'tell X to do Y' — appears
frequently in folktale dialogue and marks the complement as a directed action rather
than a proposition:

a ahun kuni a=ye akusu toykooripak | PFV enter COMP 4.A=say CONJ be.abashed | When I told them to enter, they were very abashed. | kuni follows the directed action ahun 'enter'; a=ye 'say/tell' is the fourth-person matrix verb. akusu marks the sequential relation to the consequent clause. The perfective a closes the speech event before the consequence. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話8 (その2) [aa-irc/007#213]

The purposive and deontic extensions of kuni beyond the
reported-speech domain are treated in
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements .

hawe and itak as framing elements

The formal noun hawe — possessed form of
haw voice , 'the voice/report of' — extends the quotative architecture
in two directions. As a bare complementizer it nominalizes a speech event and supplies
it as the perceptual object of verbs like nu hear, learn of : the
complement clause becomes "the voice/talk of" the event
sato2008 175–178 . Combined with the copula, hawe ne
forms the reportative evidential frame; when sekor precedes it,
the compound sekor hawe ne attributes a quoted proposition to
an unspecified external source ‹corpus-suggested›:

ek sekor haw-e ne | come QUOT voice-POSS COP | It is said that he/she came. | sekor hawe ne: the quotative particle sekor intervenes between the embedded clause ek 'come' and the reportative frame haw-e ne. The internal morphology haw-e shows the source noun haw 'voice' plus the possessed ending -e. | HK

The clause-final reportative evidential hawe ne standing alone —
where it marks information acquired through speech without framing an embedded quotation —
is treated in hawe-ne-reportative , and the four-way evidential paradigm
to which it belongs in evidential-schema-overview .

itak speak, say, talk is the most frequent speech verb in narrative
dialogue — narrators routinely use it to introduce character utterances — while
ye say appears predominantly in indirect-speech and complementizer
frames. Both appear after sekor and both select
kuni / kunak for indirect complements
nakagawa2024 476 ; sato2008 176 . A stable semantic or
register distinction between the two verbs in quotative syntax is not established in
the sources consulted ‹corpus-suggested›.

The logophoric fourth person in speech reports

Person adjustment in the indirect-speech complementizer frames reflects what Bugaeva
identifies as logophoricity in Southern Hokkaido Ainu. The fourth-person markers
a= / ci= on transitives and
=an on intransitives replace an original first person in the
embedded clause, reanchoring it to the reporting narrator's viewpoint. Nakagawa
formulates this as a categorical rule: under the indirect-speech markers, "final
particles cannot be embedded; the speaker's-viewpoint person shifts 1→3/4"
nakagawa2024 475 .

Bugaeva's study of reported discourse and logophoricity in Southern Hokkaido Ainu
provides the analytical framework: the fourth person here is not a simple third-person
referential shift but a logophoric device, indexing the embedded protagonist — the
original speaker or thinker — whose perspective the narrator temporarily adopts
bugaeva2008 . The surface form is identical to the generic/indefinite fourth
person (also a= / =an ); the logophoric
reading is determined by the presence of a complementizer frame and by contextual
co-reference with the matrix subject ‹contested›. The full fourth-person paradigm and
its logophoric distribution across narrative genres are treated in
fourth-person-honorific and indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Nikitina and Bugaeva develop a stronger claim: the construction is better characterized
as logophoric speech rather than indirect speech in the conventional theoretical
sense, because the person system encodes the narrator's perspective adoption, not a
syntactic binding of the embedded subject to a matrix controller
nikitina2021 . The discourse contexts — first-person narration in uwepeker
and yukar — where this perspective adoption is most systematic are examined in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse ‹contested›.

Under sekor direct quotation, no person shift applies: the
original speaker's person forms are preserved inside the quote, and the logophoric
reanchoring does not arise in the embedded clause. This is the morphological correlate
of the direct/indirect boundary:
sekor preserves the perspective of the original utterance;
the indirect-speech markers reanchor it to the narrator's viewpoint
bugaeva2008 31–35 ; nikitina2021 .

Analyses and open questions

The descriptions converge on the categorical point. Refsing separates quotationalization
from nominalization as coordinate clause-embedding types, placing
sekor / ari outside the nominalizer class
refsing1986 §14.14 . Nakagawa's §18.2, which treats the indirect-speech
markers extensively as nominalization-based complementizers that take a nominal-object
slot, is consistent with keeping sekor as a non-nominalizing
quotative; his account of the person shift and the final-particle ban applies to
kuni / kunak / yak
and not to sekor
nakagawa2024 475–480 . Shibatani, whose treatment foregrounds the
formal-noun complementizer system, does not single out a quotative particle in the
dialect data he covers shibatani1990 83–84 .

Three questions remain open in the sources consulted. First, the geographical and register
distribution of sekor vs ari across
Hokkaido dialects is documented only by Refsing's Shizunai attestation of both
refsing1986 §14.14 ; whether ari carries any
semantic or pragmatic distinction from sekor or is a
register/lexical variant is not established ‹speculative›. Second, the
grammaticalization status of the compound sekor hawe ne frame
is not settled: whether the sequence is transparently compositional (quotative + reportative
evidential) or a lexicalized reportative formula cannot be decided from the sources
read here ‹speculative›. Third, the logophoricity analysis of Nikitina and Bugaeva
awaits corpus-level testing at construction level; whether the person-shift pattern
under kunak / yak distributes differently
from the generic fourth-person use is not settled by evidence in the sources consulted
‹contested›.

Ijäs's pedagogical grammar covers relative clauses and the formal-noun nominalizer
system in Lesson 11 — the material banked for this grammar's nominalization domain —
but her treatment of direct and indirect speech falls in a separate lesson not yet
incorporated here ijas2023 . No Ijäs claims on
sekor or quotative framing are available for grading here.

title: The Reciprocal u-
summary: The reciprocal prefix u- 'each other/mutually', its collective/associative readings, and its interaction with plurality.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Reciprocal u-
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The reciprocal prefix u- 'each other/mutually', its collective/associative readings, and its interaction with plurality.

The prefix u- and its arity effect

The prefix u- reciprocal is a valency-decreasing derivational morpheme with
arity −1: it fills the object slot of a transitive verb with a mutual or collective
participant, detransitivizing the verb nakagawa2024 187–188 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 . The derived verb always takes a plural or
collective subject — only two or more participants can stand in a mutual relation. The
person-marking diagnostic (the operative test of derived transitivity set out in
valency-voice-overview ) confirms the intransitivization: a verb that takes the
prefixal A-indexes a= or ci= shifts to the
suffixal S-indexes =an or =as after
u- bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 ;
sato2008 232–233 .

The slot u- occupies is the same nominal/applicative position as the
reflexive-yay reflexive prefixes yay- reflexive (direct) and
si- reflexive (indirect) and the
antipassive-detransitive-i antipassive i- antipassive ; all
three subtract one arity unit by filling the object slot, but their semantic domains differ —
self-directed action, object defocusing, and mutual participation, respectively. The prefix
is highly productive: it attaches to base transitive verbs, to applicative-prefixed stems,
and to causativized forms, with the arity calculus predicting the transitivity of each output
(see valency-voice-overview inventory ). A further complication is that
u- covers not only canonical bilateral reciprocal meaning ('each
other') but also joint or associative participation ('together'), and the two readings are not
always distinguishable without context.

The canonical reciprocal

In its canonical use, u- expresses mutual action: each participant in
the plural subject simultaneously acts on and is acted on by the others. The paradigm pair
koyki fight, attack sb (transitive) →
u-koyki fight each other (intransitive), and
siru rub sth → u-siru rub each other , illustrates the
productive base pattern nakagawa2024 187–188 .

nea nispa utar u-koyki kor oka sir-i a=nukar | DEM chief ASS RECP-fight CONJ exist.PL appearance-POSS 4.A=see | I saw the chiefs fighting each other. | Bugaeva & Kobayashi 2022 ex. 11a. u-koyki is intransitive and takes no A-prefix; the clause sir-i a=nukar ('the appearance of which was seen') frames the event as a witnessed scene. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.1 | SAR | Nakagawa corpus K8010281UP.168

u- also operates on ditransitive (three-argument) bases. When the
Addressee or Recipient of a ditransitive is the argument filled by the mutual relation, the
construction is termed indirect reciprocal bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 . The
epenthetic glide w surfaces between u- and a
following vowel-initial prefix:

u-w-e-pakasnu | RECP-EP-APPL-teach | teach each other | Indirect reciprocal: the Addressee of e-pakasnu 'teach sb sth' is reciprocalized; the Theme content remains as the surviving object. The epenthetic glide w surfaces between u- and the vowel-initial applicative e-. | tamura1996:808 | SAR

The learner grammar of ijas2023 (Lesson 9-10) also lists
unukar meet each other (from nukar see sb ) as a standard
reciprocal, and gives the pair
u-hekote be a married couple (singular) /
u-hekotpa be a married couple (plural suppletive) ; both are confirmed by the
primary sources ‹consensus› nakagawa2024 187–188 .

Joint-participation and collective readings

Several forms with u- do not yield a strictly bilateral 'each other'
reading. Here u- signals joint involvement or a two-party social
relation without requiring symmetric action between every pair of participants
nakagawa2024 187–188 .

The clearest instance is u-tures-kor be siblings , which incorporates the noun
tures sibling of the same sex and the verb kor have . The
surface subject may be syntactically singular — a young man described in terms of having a
sister — yet the verb carries u- because the sibling relation
inherently involves two parties:

Iskar emko ta u-tures-kor okkaypo okay pe | Ishikari upper LOC RECP-sibling-have young.man exist.PL NMLZ | A young man with a sister, up the Ishikari River. | Nakagawa ex. 164. The singular okkaypo 'young man' is the surface subject, yet u-tures-kor carries u- because having a sibling is an inherently two-party relation. The plural existential okay reflects the broader story context. | nakagawa2024:187 | SAR

Similarly, u-mina-re laugh together does not mean 'make each other laugh' but
'all laugh together' — a sociative reading arising from the arity calculus:
u- (−1) prefixed to the already-causativized intransitive
mina-re (arity 2) yields net arity 1 (intransitive), encoding
collective action rather than imposing a new causer slot nakagawa2024 188 .
This u- + causative-on-intransitive path to a sociative reading is
treated further in reciprocal-u sociative below.

Semantic types

Bugaeva and Kobayashi identify five constructional types for u-
reciprocals, organized by the nature of the mutual relation and the morphosyntactic base
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 :

type
base
example
translation

canonical (subject-oriented)
2-arg transitive
u-tumam-an
'we held each other'

indirect (Recipient reciprocalized)
3-arg ditransitive
u-w-e-pakasnu
'teach each other'

object-oriented (causativized)
intransitive reciprocal + -re
u-koyki-re
'make them fight each other'

joining-type
2-arg transitive with applicative
u-ko-sina
'tie (bow and arrow) together'

quasi-possessive
u- + [possessed noun + verb]
u-oruspe-nu
"hear each other's stories"

The quasi-possessive type distributes each participant's possessed noun across the group:
u-tek-kisma 'shake hands' (lit. 'shake each other's hand') and
u-oruspe-nu are further instances
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 . For the parallel reflexive construction in
which a possessed noun is incorporated with yay- or
si- , see reflexive-possessive-si .

u-oruspe-nu=an kor oka=an ayne | RECP-story-hear=4.S CONJ exist.PL=4.S CONJ | listening to each other's stories, living on | Excerpt from Bugaeva & Kobayashi 2022 ex. 13. u-oruspe-nu: oruspe 'story' is the possessed noun distributed across the participants; nu 'hear' is detransitivized by u-, taking the 4th-person S suffix =an. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.1 | SAR | Nakagawa corpus K8010291UP.517

The causativized reciprocal

When the productive causative suffix -re is added to an already-reciprocalized
intransitive, arity rises by one, and the construction means 'cause sb to do X to each other'.
The chain is: base transitive (arity 2) → add u- (−1) → intransitive
(1) → add -re (+1) → transitive (2), with a new causer as subject:

u-koyki-re | RECP-fight-CAUS | make them fight each other | Object-oriented causativized reciprocal. Arity: koyki (2) + u- (−1) = 1 (vi); then u-koyki (1) + -re (+1) = 2 (vt). After Bugaeva & Kobayashi 2022 §4.2.1.

The contrasting sociative reading — 'all do X together' — arises when u-
is prefixed to a form already causativized from an intransitive base, so that the net arity is
1 − 1 = 0:
u-horip-pa-re all dance/exorcise together ,
u-kira-re all escape together
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.2 . The arity of the base verb and the order of
operations determine which reading obtains; surface form alone is insufficient
nakagawa2024 188 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Lexicalized forms and extension to non-verbal bases

A range of forms with u- have undergone semantic specialization to
the point where the mutual motivation is conventional. Kin- and social-relation verbs such as
u-tures-kor be siblings and u-hekote be a married couple
encode relational membership. unukar meet each other is the standard word for
'meet' in Saru nakagawa2024 187–188 .

The prefix extends beyond the verb paradigm to locative nouns, postpositions, and adverbs:
u-sam near each other , u-tura together ,
u-neno similar to each other
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 .

Two further forms show opacity. uwanpare gaze intently decomposes as
u-anpa-re (RECP-carry-CAUS), but the semantic path from 'cause each
other to carry' to 'observe intently' is unresolved in the sources ‹contested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024).
uwatte multiply, breed (of animals) has been analysed as
u-w-at-te (RECP-EP-attach-CAUS); the semantic path 'cause each other
to cling/pair' → 'breed' is plausible for a verb restricted to animal populations, and its
complement uwaste increase (of humans, plants) is semantically differentiated
aynucorporadiscord (gengojiro, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) ‹corpus-suggested›.

The sociative uko- and uwe-

The prefixes uko- together, mutually and uwe- together, mutually
are synchronically unitary markers but decompose as
u- + applicative-ko ko- and
u- + applicative-e e-
respectively nakagawa2024 208, 211–212 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.2 . Because the applicative contributes arity +1 and
u- contributes −1, the net arity is zero: the sociative is
valency-neutral. The minimal pair u-kor marry (lit. have each other) (arity −1,
intransitive) versus u-ko-kor have together (arity 0, transitive) makes this
decisive nakagawa2024 208 :

matkaci a=maci turano a=u-ko-kor hine oka=an wa | girl 4.POSS=wife together 4.A=RECP-APPL-have CONJ exist.PL=4.S CONJ | Together with my wife, I had a daughter, and we went on living. | Nakagawa ex. 218. a=u-ko-kor is transitive (arity 0 preserves the base kor's transitivity): the 4.A prefix a= (narrative first person) on the verb confirms this. The plain reciprocal u-kor 'marry each other' is intransitive and takes =an. | nakagawa2024:208 | SAR

kamuy-utar opitta uko-yay-kopuntek | spirit-ASS all RECP.APPL-REFL-rejoice | All the spirits rejoiced together. | Bugaeva & Kobayashi 2022 ex. 34. uko- (= u- + ko-) combines with the reflexive yay-; the whole form is intransitive (arity: uko- = 0, then yay- = −1). | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.3.2 | HK | Nakagawa corpus OI990423KY.066

uko- is commoner than uwe- in Saru texts,
reflecting the higher productivity of ko- as an applicative
bugaeva2006 188 . The two are partially interchangeable in derivationally
transparent forms ( uko-yaykopuntek ~
uwe-yaykopuntek 'rejoice together'), but
ko- 's comitative and goal-directed semantics give it the advantage
where joint orientation toward shared participants is foregrounded
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.2 . For the full applicative semantics of
ko- and e- , see
applicative-system-overview ; for their stacking with other prefixes,
applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding .

title: Reduplication Phonology and Rule Interaction
summary: Base-copy templates in ideophones, intensives, and pluractionals, and how reduplication interacts with the assimilation and epenthesis processes to diagnose rule ordering and the morphology–phonology interface.
part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Reduplication Phonology and Rule Interaction
Part: Part V — Morphophonology and Sandhi
Summary: Base-copy templates in ideophones, intensives, and pluractionals, and how reduplication interacts with the assimilation and epenthesis processes to diagnose rule ordering and the morphology–phonology interface.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu reduplication places a phonological copy of a verbal base alongside that
base. The process is most visible in iterative and distributive-plural forms, but this
chapter is restricted to its phonological dimension: the shape the reduplicant
takes, what happens at the boundary between reduplicant and base, how the general
resyllabification and hiatus-resolution rules interact with that boundary, and how the
sandhi assimilations documented in coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi bear
on the juncture when a suffix follows the reduplicated complex. Aspect and iterative
semantics belong to phasal-iterative-habitual-aspect ; verbal-number
pluractionality to suppletive-verbal-number ; nominalizer allomorphy
consulted in §5 to nominalization-p-pe . Live morphological decompositions of
reduplicated forms — full-copy, VC-echo ( cirir ), and
final-syllable ( hepokiki ) types with their argument-structure
annotations — are browsable in the Ainu morpheme database (mdb.aynu.org/processes).

The phonological shape of the reduplicant

Hokkaido Ainu reduplication is characteristically full : the reduplicant copies
the complete syllable or CVC template of the base
nakagawa2024 204–208 . The syllable canon is (C)V(C), so each copy is
an independently well-formed syllable
( shiraishi2022 §4.1 ; nakagawa2024 41–42 ); consonant
clusters at the juncture arise only when an assimilation rule applies (§§3–4 below),
not from the copying itself.

The copying preserves coda consonants: a CVC base yields a CVC reduplicant; a CV base
yields a CV reduplicant. The coda shape of the copy is identical to the coda shape of
the source form (see §5 on how coda alternation in the source propagates into the
reduplicant). The table below shows canonical mappings; phonetic representations are
supplied in §3 where the boundary is discussed.

base
underlying RED ~ BASE
surface
meaning
source

ay
/ay ~ ay/
[ˈay.ʔay]
'baby'

Tamura (1998: 41–42), reported in
shiraishi2022 §4.4

para
/para ~ para/
[paˈraparak-]
'cry' (CVCV base)

Tamura (1998: 41–42), reported in
shiraishi2022 §4.4

ket
/ket ~ ket/
[ˈketket-]
(base in ketketcep ; §4.1)

aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2025-11-03)

Accent follows the general iambic rule ( pitch-accent-placement-rule ): if
the first syllable of the reduplicated complex is closed, it carries the primary accent;
if open, the accent falls on the second syllable
nakagawa2024 50 . In ketketcep the first
syllable is the closed ket , giving [ˈketketcep]. Lexicalized
compounds with reduplicated elements follow the first-element priority rule governing
all compounds ( accent-in-compounds-and-affixation ;
nakagawa2024 54 ).

The reduplicant-base boundary: resyllabification blocking and glottal insertion

A reduplicant and its base do not form a seamless concatenation. Across ordinary
morpheme boundaries a coda consonant resyllabifies onto a following vowel-initial
syllable: sik-o → [ˈsi.ko],
mat-ak → [ˈma.tak]
( kindaichi1936 9 ; shiraishi2022 §4.4 ). Two blocking
conditions suspend this rule and trigger glottal insertion instead:

Secondary accent on the following syllable. When the vowel of
σ n+1 carries secondary accent — as in
paˈraparak-ʔan 'cry' — the accent domain prevents
resyllabification; [ʔ] is inserted at the juncture.

The reduplicant-base boundary itself.
ˈay-ʔay 'baby' shows glottal insertion at the
REDUP-BASE juncture independently of whether the base vowel happens to be
accented.

Both conditions are documented by Tamura (1998: 41–42), reported in
shiraishi2022 §4.4 .

Rule statement: ∅ → ʔ / V RED-final __ V BASE-initial
at the REDUP-BASE juncture (or when the following V carries secondary accent at any
juncture); resyllabification is otherwise suspended.

Derivation for ayˈay 'baby':

level
representation
operation

underlying
/ay/
base

reduplication
/ˈay ~ ˈay/
full copy; secondary accent assigned to RED

resyllabification
—
blocked at REDUP-BASE juncture → [ʔ] inserted

surface
[ˈay.ʔay]

Productive glide insertion at person-prefix boundaries — e.g.,
i= + omante →
iyomante — is a distinct operation in a different prosodic
domain; it is morpho-syntactically conditioned and does not extend to the
reduplicant-base boundary
( shiraishi2022 §4.3 ; glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution ).
The glottal option takes over at REDUP-BASE.

Sandhi at the reduplicant-affix juncture

The blocking in §3 applies specifically at the internal REDUP-BASE boundary.
The junction between the reduplicated complex and a following affix is an
ordinary morpheme juncture: sandhi rules operate there as they do everywhere else.

t + s → c in reduplicated compounds

Progressive assimilation at an underlying /t/ + /s/ juncture yields [c] ([ʨ~ʦ]).
Nakagawa documents the pattern at compound and historical suffix junctures:
pet-sam → petcam 'riverbank',
sinot-sa → sinotca 'improvised
song' ( nakagawa2024 37 ).

The compound ketketcep (a fish name) has been proposed as
deriving from an underlying structure
*ket~ket-se-pe
( aynucorporadiscord antitwilight, 2025-11-03). Derivation:

stage
form
rule applied

underlying
*ket ~ ket - se - pe
base ket , reduplicated; suffix -se-pe

t + s → c
*ket ~ ket - ce - pe
/t/ of base-final copy + /s/ of suffix → [c]

contraction (lexicalization)
ketketcep
suffix-boundary reduction; surface string

A parallel non-reduplicated case is nisatcawot ~
nisat-sawot 'Venus / morning star', the same /t/ + /s/ →
[c] at a compound boundary. The community analysis proposes that the rule applies
only at historically underlying /t#/, not to /t/ that arose secondarily; this
restriction has not been confirmed in a primary phonological source ‹speculative›
( aynucorporadiscord antitwilight, 2025-11-03).

Palatalization (t + i → ci) at the boundary

The palatalization rule t → c / __ i applies without exception
in Hokkaido Ainu: mat + ikor →
macikor , rekut-i →
rekuci
( nakagawa2024 36 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1 ). The rule
operates through resyllabification — coda /t/ moves to the onset of the /i/
syllable — so it shares the feeding mechanism that is blocked at REDUP-BASE (§3).

boundary type
resyllabification
t + i → ci applies?

stem-internal / regular morpheme boundary
applies
yes ( mat+ikor → macikor )

REDUP-BASE
blocked (§3)
no — feeds on resyllabification

reduplicated complex + suffix
applies
yes (ordinary morpheme junction)

No attested /t/-final + /i/-initial reduplication pair has been identified in the
sources read for this grammar; the blocking prediction follows from the interaction
of the independently documented resyllabification block (§3) and the palatalization
rule ( shiraishi2022 §4.4, §6.1 ;
nakagawa2024 36 ) ‹speculative›.

r-sandhi at reduplicant codas

Coda /r/ is the dominant regressive-assimilation trigger across all morpheme
boundaries ( coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi ;
rhotic-r ). In a reduplicated form with a /r/-final base, the coda /r/
of the reduplicant remains in coda position (resyllabification is blocked at
REDUP-BASE), and could in principle trigger r-sandhi against the onset of the
following base copy. Nakagawa documents r-sandhi word-internally and across words
( nakagawa2024 35–37 ), and Shiraishi establishes it as a purely
phonological rule with no prosodic-domain restriction
( shiraishi2022 §6.2 ). Whether r-sandhi applies at the REDUP-BASE
juncture is not documented in the sources read here; corpus investigation is needed
‹speculative›.

The lexicalized compound attus (elm-bark robe), deriving
from an underlying at-rus via r-sandhi (Saru intermediate
ahrus ), is attested 24× in the corpus
( aynucorporadiscord nukopoli, 2024-12-26) ‹corpus-confirmed›. This
involves compound-internal r-sandhi, not the REDUP-BASE boundary; it is treated
in citation-vs-combining-stem-shapes .

Summary: sandhi rule interactions at reduplication boundaries

shiraishi2022 §6.1

shiraishi2022 §4.3

Coda alternation in paired reduplicants: rikin and rikip

Because the reduplicant copies the phonological shape of the source form, any
alternation visible in the base propagates into the reduplicant. A community
observation on the verb rikin go up, ascend illustrates this point.

The verb 'go up / ascend' appears in two forms: rikin , with
final nasal coda /n/ (attested in corpus; see example below), and
rikip , with final stop coda /p/. In reduplication, either form
may serve as the base that is copied, yielding alternant shapes; the alternation is
summarized as rikin~rikip
( aynucorporadiscord nukopoli, 2026-04-27) ‹speculative›.

The causative suffix -ka causative attaches to the nasal-coda form
only: rikinka is attested; * rikipka
is not found ( aynucorporadiscord nukopoli, 2026-04-27) ‹speculative›.
This asymmetry indicates that the nasal-coda form is the morphological base for
further derivation, while the stop-coda form is a secondary shape.

The stop-coda form rikip is consistent with the nominalizer
allomorphy: the nominalizer -p/-pe nominalizer surfaces as
-p after a vowel-final stem
( shiraishi2022 §3 ; nakagawa2024 29 ). If the root
is vowel-final riki- , then riki-p
= root + nominalizer. The reduplicant then mirrors whichever form of the verb is
being used as the base — nominalized or plain — without neutralizing or
canonicalizing it. The coda alternation in the reduplicant is thus an instance of
morphological conditioning of the base shape feeding into the copying operation.
For the nominalizer system itself, see nominalization-p-pe and
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

tane rikin wa isam pe okake ta | now ascend CONJ be.gone NMLZ after LOC | Now, after (the god) had ascended and vanished, | rikin: the nasal-coda form of the ascent verb in non-reduplicated context. The corpus attestation confirms the form cited in the rikin~rikip alternation (§5); wa isam adds completive force. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#251]

Person-prefix attachment to reduplicated stems

Personal prefixes ku= , e= , and
ci= attach to the full reduplicated complex as if it were a
single prosodic word. The iambic accent-placement rule — first closed syllable gets
the nucleus; if the first is open, the second does — applies to the
[prefix + REDUP + BASE] string as a unit
( nakagawa2024 50–54 ;
pitch-accent-placement-rule ; accent-in-compounds-and-affixation ).

The vowel-deletion rule of ku= and ci=
— prefix vowel deletes before stem-initial /a e u o/, but not before /i/
( nakagawa2024 52 ;
aynucorporadiscord nukopoli, 2024-11-28) ‹corpus-confirmed› — applies at
the prefix-reduplicant junction exactly as it does before any vowel-initial stem. If
the first syllable of the reduplicant is vowel-initial, the prefix vowel deletes; if
consonant-initial, it does not. The full morphophonology is treated in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction and
first-second-person-singular-affixes .

Corpus illustration

caricari wa isam ru-we ne kuni a=ramu kor | be.scattered CONJ be.gone track-POSS COP COMP 4.A=think while | (while she was) thinking that (things) had been scattered and vanished, | caricari: full CVC reduplication of cari 'be scattered'; the reduplicated form expresses distributive or pluractional scattering; wa isam adds completive force with the sense of disappearance. The syllable structure [ˈca.ri.ca.ri] follows the iambic rule applied to the reduplicated string. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 9 [aa-irc/009#127]

No sandhi applies at the internal REDUP-BASE boundary in this example: the last syllable
of the reduplicant ( ri ) is open, so there is no coda consonant to
trigger the rules in §4. The following particle wa attaches at the
right edge of the whole reduplicated form; the only potential sandhi there is the
progressive nasal-place assimilation documented for this particle
( nakagawa2024 37 ).

Dialectal variation

The regressive cluster assimilations of eastern Hokkaido dialects —
tk → kk , pt → tt , rs → ss
in Bihoro and related varieties ( nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.3 ) — are
treated by Nakagawa as word-specific historical consolidations rather than active
synchronic rules ( nakagawa2024 36 ;
aynucorporadiscord antitwilight, 2024-11-17). No specific reduplicated
compound showing these eastern assimilations is attested in the sources consulted here
‹corpus-suggested›. The broader inventory of dialect-specific morphophonological
isoglosses is treated in hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

title: Reference Tracking and Argument Continuity
summary: How participants are tracked across clauses: zero anaphora and pro-drop, person marking, the indefinite/fourth person as a tracking device, demonstrative/anaphoric reference, and switch-reference-like clause linkage (hine/akusu), drawn together as one system.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Reference Tracking and Argument Continuity
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: How participants are tracked across clauses: zero anaphora and pro-drop, person marking, the indefinite/fourth person as a tracking device, demonstrative/anaphoric reference, and switch-reference-like clause linkage (hine/akusu), drawn together as one system.

Overview

Reference tracking in Hokkaido Ainu discourse rests on a cluster of mechanisms that work in
concert: obligatory verbal person indices, pervasive zero anaphora, the referential properties of
the fourth-person form-set, the lexical class of location nouns, and constructional
signals of topic continuity and scene transition. There is no morphological switch-reference
system. The bound person indices do not encode same-subject versus different-subject across clause
junctions, and the clause-linking particles carry no built-in reference-change signal; participant
continuity is inferred from index identity and from discourse structure, not marked by a dedicated
morpheme bugaeva2022 ; sato2025a 359–371 .

mechanism
level
function in tracking
primary source

bound person indices
morphological
identify participants per clause; obligatory on every finite verb
sato2025a 359–361 ; bugaeva2012 472

zero anaphora
pragmatic/syntactic
default anaphor for contextually recoverable referents; no pronoun used
nakagawa2024 361 ; bugaeva2022

fourth-person indexing
morphological
tracks story-internal first person distinct from narrating speaker; logophoric in quotations
nakagawa2024 174–179 ; bugaeva2008 40–43

<location> noun class
lexical/semantic
disambiguates role of two zero-marked third-person arguments
nakagawa2022pos ; bugaeva2022

anak(ne) topic-fronting
pragmatic
marks a referent as discourse topic at (re)introduction or contrast
nakagawa2024 364 ; shibatani1990 38–39

a p / a korka
constructional
signals scene or actor change at narrative episode boundaries
bugaeva2022

The formal person paradigm, the S/A/O sets, and the fourth-person cell are treated in
person-marking-architecture ; the semantics of the fourth-person form-set in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and fourth-person-honorific ;
clause-chaining and the wa / hine relationship in
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining ; topicalization and anakne
in topic-marking-anakne ; logophoric narration and reported speech in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Zero anaphora

Because every finite verb obligatorily indexes its arguments through bound person markers, an
overt NP is an optional supplement to the cross-reference already present on the verb. An NP
recoverable from context is simply absent, with no pronoun in its place: "it is not necessary in
Ainu for a noun phrase that is clear from the context to appear in a sentence, and such a noun
phrase is not even represented by a pronoun" nakagawa2024 361 ;
bugaeva2022 . The result is that participant continuity across a narrative is
maintained principally through the identity of the person indices across successive verbs, not
through a chain of co-referring pronouns.

Even when the person indices cannot identify an argument — specifically, when a three-place
(ditransitive) verb's two-affix cap leaves the theme unindexed — the verb's valency guarantees
the anaphoric reading without any overt NP or pronoun. The theme of
kore 'give' is zero in the following pair of sentences; its reference to
the sweet just mentioned is resolved by the verb's own argument-structure requirement
sato2025a 368–369 :

tan-pe topen-pe ne na ekasi ku=kore na | DEM-NMLZ sweet-NMLZ COP FIN old.man 1SG.A=give FIN | This is a sweet. I'll give it to the old man. | Satō ex. 21 (two sentences). The theme 'it' of the second clause is zero — no pronoun, no person index — yet its reference to the sweet of the first clause is unambiguous because kore's valency requires a theme argument. | sato2025a:368–369 | CHI

Overt NP mention is reserved for participant (re)introduction, contrast, or a shift in discourse
topic. When an NP does appear alongside a person index, it stands in an appositional relation to
that index: the index constitutes the argument; the NP specifies or highlights it
sato2025a 363–365 ; bugaeva2012 472 . The NP's position within
the preverbal field is set by pragmatic prominence; argument realization and appositional NPs are
treated in argument-realization-indexing and
pragmatic-word-order-dislocation .

The fourth person in reference tracking

The fourth-person form-set — intransitive subject suffix
=an , transitive subject prefix a= (dialectally
an= in Tokachi and Ishikari), and object prefix i=
— does substantial reference-tracking work in oral narrative genres. Its functions in this domain
fall into two related types: narrating the story-world protagonist's actions (narrative first
person), and marking the reported speaker's first person inside a quotation (logophoric use).
Both are documented principally for Saru and Chitose
nakagawa2024 174–179 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 .

Narrative first person

In kamuy yukar (divine verse) and uwepeker
(prose tales) narrated in the first person, the protagonist-narrator who is the hero of the
story uses the fourth-person set rather than the everyday first-person
ku= / =an / en= .
Nakagawa analyses this as a viewpoint distinction tied to text type: the protagonist inhabits
the story world and addresses it from within, and the fourth-person form signals that
inner-story perspective to the audience nakagawa2024 174–179 . Satō derives the
same usage from a first-person-plural origin: the fourth-person form encompasses the narrator
and any hearer who identifies with the protagonist, making it an inclusive strategy
sato2008 259–262 ; sato2004 179–184 ‹contested›. The
tracking function is real regardless of which analysis is adopted: wherever a narrator reserves
a= / =an / i= for the
hero's actions and ku= / en= for her own
narrating voice, the two form-sets constitute a formal contrast between two discourse roles.

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=lodge CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The fourth-person object prefix i= indexes the narrator-protagonist as recipient across three chained predicates. In kamuy yukar, i= here tracks the protagonist's first-person reference within the story world. | nakagawa2024:170 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

A community-observed instance corroborates the formal contrast in an attested narration. In an
uwepeker told by Sadamo, the narrator alternates
a= for the story-internal protagonist's speech and
ku= for her own voice as the framing narrator — the two person
form-sets tracking two distinct discourse roles simultaneously (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-07-04, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Logophoric use in quotations

Within a direct quotation, the reported speaker's first person is indexed by the fourth-person
set rather than by ku= . Bugaeva analyses this as a logophoric
function: the form marks the participant whose speech or thought is being reported, who is not
identical to the narrating speaker at the time of utterance
bugaeva2008 40–44 ; nikitina2021 . The switch from
ku= to a= / =an at a
quotation boundary is therefore a reference-change signal — "speaker in quote ≠ narrating
speaker" — even though it is realized through the person paradigm rather than through a
quotative morpheme. This use is attested for Saru and Chitose and is treated fully in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse
nakagawa2024 470–471 .

Characterization as obviation or switch-reference

The fourth person has been described as "obviative-like," and the functional analogy has
substance: like an obviative, it marks a participant (the story-internal first person) as
formally distinct from both the ordinary third person and the plain first person, functioning
as "the other first person" in the narrative nakagawa2024 177–179 .

The analogy does not constitute a category equivalence ‹contested›. Algonquian obviation is a
grammaticalized noun-phrase category that applies systematically to any third-person
participant ranked below the proximate in a referential hierarchy, independent of text type;
the Ainu fourth person applies to a specific set of referential roles — the narrative
protagonist, the honorific addressee, the inclusive referent, the reported speaker — without
encoding a proximate/obviative hierarchy on nouns or noun phrases. The form-set belongs to the
person paradigm; its reference-tracking properties are a consequence of those referential
roles, not of an independent grammaticalized tracking category.

Characterizing the fourth person as a switch-reference device is less apt still ‹contested›.
Switch-reference systems mark whether the subject of a dependent clause is co-referential with
the subject of the adjacent main clause, typically by morphology on the clause-linking element.
The Ainu fourth person is marked on the finite verb itself, not on a connective; it does not
encode a same-subject versus different-subject opposition across clause junctions. The apparent
reference-change at a quotation boundary follows from the logophoric function analyzed by
Bugaeva bugaeva2008 40–44 , not from switch-reference morphology. The
clause-linkage chapters provide the detailed comparison: see
hine-akusu-switch-reference and clause-linkage-overview .

The location noun class as role disambiguator

When two third-person participants share the same zero marking, the lexical class of
location nouns provides a means of role assignment. Nouns designating places or spatial
relations — or 'inside/place', ka 'above',
sam 'near', corpok 'below', and others — are
semantically constrained to the Location argument of a predicate. When one participant is or
includes a location noun, it is unambiguously the Location role, and the other must be theme or
patient. Role is resolved without case marking or fixed word order
nakagawa2022pos ; bugaeva2022 .

cep saranip or a=oma-re | fish basket LOC 4.A=put.in-CAUS | She put the fish into the basket. | Constructed to illustrate location-noun role assignment. The NP saranip or (basket + locative noun or 'inside') is unambiguously the Goal; cep (fish) must be the theme. Without or, both NPs would be semantically eligible for either argument role when both are zero-indexed.

The attested test case from Nakagawa's Handbook chapter is the contrast between
saranip or oma-re 'put into the basket' (where or
flags the goal) and the ambiguous saranip oma-re without it, where the
basket could be either theme or goal nakagawa2022pos . Bugaeva takes the location
class as evidence that argumenthood is shared between the bound person index and the
coreferential NP, since only a semantically contrastive NP can bear this tracking weight
bugaeva2022 .

The mechanism is a lexical resource, not a grammatical one: it operates only when one of the
participants is a location noun, which leaves the common case of two ordinary-noun third-person
arguments to plausibility and discourse context nakagawa2024 363 . Its full
treatment is in constituent-order-head-final and
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument .

Topic marking and participant tracking

A referent that is being introduced as the topic of a sustained passage, or that re-enters
discourse salience after an interval, is often fronted and marked by the adverbial particle
anak(ne) . The particle functions comparably to Japanese
は in establishing a topic for the clauses that follow, but it is used
more sparingly — it carries the weight of "as for (X), specifically" rather than neutral
givenness marking, and it is not re-applied clause by clause to a topic already active in the
discourse nakagawa2024 364 ; shibatani1990 38–39 . Community
practice corroborates this: an established topic does not need to be re-marked; omitting
anakne when the referent is clear from context is normal (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09, asserted; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

a=kor seta anakne i=sam ta arpa=an | 4.A=have dog TOP 4.O=near LOC go=4.S | As for my dog, it went right beside me. | Constructed to illustrate anakne topic-fronting. The possessed NP a=kor seta 'my dog' is marked as topic; subsequent clauses maintain reference by zero anaphora, with the dog as subject (=4.S on intransitives) and the narrator tracked by 4.O.

The attested corpus contains topicalized possessed-subject NPs of precisely this type. In an
uwepeker narrated by Kawakami Matsuko, the sentence
a=kor seta anakne i=oyakke oma ka somo ki p ne kusu i=sam ta ukasuy
ipe=an — 'my dog, as for it, never left my side, so it ate (together) with me' — introduces
the dog as topic with anakne and then tracks it by zero anaphora and
person indices through the remainder of the episode ilcaa1976
(Kawakami Matsuko, AA研アイヌ語資料, Saru [aa-irc/009#393]).

The full syntax of anak(ne) , including its scope, its interaction with
focus particles, and its distributional limits, is treated in topic-marking-anakne
and cleft-nominalization-focus .

Scene transition in narrative

Participant continuity within a narrative episode is sustained by clause-chaining with
wa and hine : where person indices remain constant
across chained clauses, a single participant is tracked through a sustained sequence. The
statistical tendency of wa toward same-subject reference — approximately
90% of wa -chained clauses in Refsing's Shizunai data share their
subject refsing1986 — reflects a discourse-structural preference rather than a
grammatical constraint; different-subject sequences with wa are attested
‹corpus-suggested›. See sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining .

Episode and actor changes are marked by the construction
a p / a korka , comprising the perfective auxiliary
a followed by the nominalizer p or the adversative
particle korka . Bugaeva describes this construction as marking "the point
at which the scene, topic, or actor in the story changes," typically followed by the orienting
adverb tane 'now' bugaeva2022 . The mechanism operates at the
narrative-structural level, above the clause-linkage system, and is developed in
tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion .

oka=an a p tane poro=an wa inkar=an | exist=4.S PFV NMLZ now grow.up=4.S CONJ look.around=4.S | [We] had been living — and now I had grown up and looked around. | Constructed to illustrate the a p scene-boundary construction. The fourth-person suffix =an indexes the protagonist-narrator throughout both episodes; a p closes the preceding episode; tane 'now' opens the following one. Based on the pattern described by Bugaeva (2022).

The absence of morphological switch-reference

Ainu has no morphological switch-reference system bugaeva2022 ; sato2025a 371 .
In switch-reference languages, a clause-linking morpheme encodes whether the subject of the
dependent clause is the same as or different from the subject of an adjacent clause. No such
morpheme exists in Ainu. The clause-linking particles
wa , hine , kor , and
kane are linkers between finite clauses, not non-finite subordinators
with an obligatory co-reference specification; and the language has no converbs — non-finite
verbal forms that typically carry switch-reference morphology — in the relevant typological sense
bugaeva2022 ; refsing1986 .

The functions that switch-reference systems serve elsewhere are distributed across the cluster of
mechanisms described in this chapter: the obligatory person indices identify participants at each
verb; zero anaphora propagates known referents; the fourth-person form signals the story-internal
perspective and the logophoric reported-speaker; the location noun class resolves two zero-marked
third-person roles; topic-fronting with anak(ne) re-establishes
prominence; and the a p construction flags episode boundaries. None of
these constitutes switch-reference in the typological sense, and the differences matter for
comparing Ainu with its geographically adjacent languages ‹consensus›.

Ijäs correctly describes Ainu reference tracking as a discourse-pragmatic matter without
positing a switch-reference system, noting the topic particle and the person-form contrast
without conflating them with morphological switch-reference ijas2023 . The formal
typological comparison is available in hine-akusu-switch-reference .

title: The Reflexive-Possessive si- and the yay-/si- Division
summary: The prefix si- 'oneself / one's own / by itself' and its division of labor with yay- in argument identification.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Reflexive-Possessive si- and the yay-/si- Division
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The prefix si- 'oneself / one's own / by itself' and its division of labor with yay- in argument identification.

Overview

The prefix si- reflexive; one's own reduces the argument frame of a
transitive verb by one, identifying the object position with the subject and yielding an
intransitive verb. Like the sister prefix yay- reflexive (direct)
(treated in reflexive-yay ), it is glossed 自分 'self'
and carries arity −1 in Nakagawa's calculus, so the derived verb shifts from the prefixal
A-set ( a= , ci= ) to the suffixal S-set
( =an , =as ), as diagnosed in
person-marking-architecture nakagawa2024 188–189 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 .

si- is more restricted in distribution than yay- : the latter attaches
to transitives, intransitives, and nouns alike (with arity 0 on nouns, as in
yay-kotan one's own village ), whereas si- is productive only on
transitive bases nakagawa2024 188 . Beyond the basic reflexive reading it
also functions as a reflexive-possessive , marking the subject-coreferent possessor
of an incorporated nominal, and it enters a 'pretend to' construction when combined with
the causative suffixes -re and -ka
nakagawa2024 189 ; sato2007 . The meaning that underlies all
three uses is the same: the subject's involvement passes through another participant or
external circumstance rather than arising from direct self-initiated action.

The yay-/si- division of labour

Four analyses of the yay-/si- contrast are on record, all working from the same minimal
pairs. The classic pair is pusu float something up →
yay-pusu pop up volitionally 'pop up by swimming (volitionally)' versus
si-pusu float up spontaneously 'float up of itself', attributed to Kindaichi
(1931: 149, reported by sato2007 ) and tamura1996 622 .
Nakagawa adds rayke kill → yay-rayke commit suicide
'commit suicide' versus si-rayke be ruined by ill fortune
'be ruined by ill fortune' nakagawa2024 188–189 .

Competing analyses of the yay- / si- contrast

analytic parameter
yay-
si-
source

intentionality
intentional ( 意志的 )
unintentional, spontaneous
Kindaichi 1931: 149 (via sato2007 ); tamura1996 622

self-role
agentive self
patientive self
Kirikae 1994: 316 (reported by sato2007 )

argument position
any thematic role
Theme or Patient only — never causee
Kobayashi (2008: 140, cited in bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 );
kobayashi2010

mode of involvement
direct self-action
indirect — via another participant or circumstance
sato2007 31–38 ; adopted by nakagawa2024 189
and bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2

The direct/indirect analysis (line four) is the framework Nakagawa (2024: 189) and the
Handbook adopt as the current working account ‹contested›. Satō (2007: ≈31–38) builds it
from three paradigm cases: si-nuye have oneself tattooed 'have oneself
tattooed' (traditional Ainu tattooing required another person to do the work);
si-kasuy-re have others help oneself 'have others help oneself' (the help
comes from outside); and si-ranpewtek-ka pretend not to know 'pretend not
to know' (the pretence succeeds only if another observer believes it). In each case the
subject's reflexive involvement is mediated by another party's action or perception. Satō
himself qualifies the account as not yet established
( 定説ではない ) sato2007 .

None of the earlier analyses is fully discredited. The intentionality framing (Kindaichi,
Tamura) captures the spontaneous reading of si-pusu but not the pretend-to
construction, where the subject acts with full intent. The agentive/patientive framing
(Kirikae 1994: 316, reported by sato2007 ) separates the forms at the
thematic role level, but feigning sleep — si-mokor-e pretend to sleep —
is agentive and falls outside the patientive characterisation. The argument-structural
restriction that Kobayashi (2008: 140) derives — si- occupies Theme or Patient
but never causee — correctly excludes causee arguments but does not explain the opposition
between si-pusu and yay-pusu , where both occupy a Theme role
kobayashi2010 .

The Handbook extends the empirical base with two additional minimal pairs:
yay-tuy-tuy-e brush dust off oneself versus
si-tuy-tuy-e shake off (while becoming a bird) — where the latter signals a
shedding of human clothes driven by the subject's transformation into bird form, an outward
change not self-initiated — and yay-etaye draw oneself outward 'draw
oneself out' versus si-etaye withdraw, pull back 'withdraw, pull back in
response to another's approach' bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 .

A discussion on the aynu-corpora Discord (nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord , 2024)
labels the contrast as anticausative ( si- ) versus reflexive proper
( yay- ), on the grounds that si- marks 'something that happens to the
subject through another force' and yay- marks 'the subject acting on itself
directly'. The labelling is consistent with the Satō analysis and invokes an explicit
anticausative category ‹contested›. A separate thread (aomidori_cs_,
aynucorporadiscord , 2023) proposed that one prefix may originally have encoded
a voice distinct from reflexive proper, without adducing corroborating evidence
‹speculative›.

sunku anakne peka ta si-pusu wa mom pe ne | Ezo.pine TOP surface LOC REFL-float.up CONJ flow NMLZ COP | The Ezo pine floats up to the water surface and flows along by itself. | si-pusu 'float up spontaneously' has no agentive subject; yay-pusu would require one. The absence of an A-set prefix confirms the intransitive output. | sato2007 | CHI | Shirasawa Nabe; Satō ex. 4 (approx. p. 36)

i=nukar rok pe si-etaye wa isam | 4.O=see PRF NMLZ REFL-withdraw CONJ not.exist | The thing that had caught sight of me withdrew and was gone. | si-etaye 'withdraw, pull back' contrasts with yay-etaye 'draw oneself outward'; the withdrawal here is triggered by the narrator's presence, the canonical indirect-reflexive context. The fourth-person object prefix i= on nukar references the narrator logophorically. | ilcaa1976 | SHI | Oda Steno, 'reyep kamuy'; ILCAA aa-irc/027#108

The reflexive-possessive function

When a transitive verb incorporates a body-part or closely possessed nominal,
si- can mark that nominal as belonging to the subject — the reflexive-possessive
function. The example cited by the Handbook is
si-etu-uyna hold one's own nose 'hold one's nose', where si-
supplies the possessor 'one's own' for the incorporated noun etu nose and
uyna hold something is thereby intransitivized
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . The parallel
yay- -possessive yay-par-oyki feed oneself; do to one's own mouth
— 'feed oneself', literally 'do to one's own mouth' — shows that both prefixes can supply
the possessor of an incorporated nominal, with the yay-/si- distinction projecting onto
the possessive domain in the same way it does onto the reflexive bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 .

The reflexive-possessive reading also surfaces obligatorily in locative contexts: when a
locative nominal's possessor is coreferential with the clause subject,
si- (or the applicative construction built around it) is required, and
substituting a non-coreferential possessor yields a distinct, non-reflexive reading
sato2008 251–253 .

In arity terms the function remains −1: the incorporated nominal fills one argument slot,
si- fills that nominal's possessor internally, and one external argument is
removed. The parallel in noun incorporation is the possessor-requiring subject type, where
the stranded possessor receives a reflexive interpretation by recovery from the clause
subject — the 'reflexive interpretation rescue' in Satō's markedness calculus, treated in
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding satoni2022 §3.3, §5 .

The 'pretend to' construction

si- combined with a causative suffix yields a 'pretend to V' reading.
Nakagawa (2024: 189) gives three examples with the productive suffix
-re : si-mokor-e feign sleep 'feign sleep',
si-kamuyne-re pretend to be a kamuy 'pretend to be a kamuy', and
si-ponpene-re act childishly 'act childishly'. With the direct-causative
-ka , Satō (2007: ≈36) gives
si-ranpewtek-ka pretend not to know 'pretend not to know'. In each
instance the state or appearance is externally attributed: sleep must be witnessed to be
believed, kamuy status requires another to be deceived, ignorance requires an observer to
accept it. The 'pretend to' reading is therefore a direct realisation of the
indirect-reflexive semantics of si- in a context of social inference.

ku=ko-si-ranpewtek-ka wa k=an | 1SG.A=APPL-REFL-know.nothing-CAUS CONJ IPFV | I was pretending not to know about it. | ko- is the applicative referencing what the subject ostensibly does not know; si- contributes the indirect-reflexive reading; -ka is the direct-causative suffix. Together si-ranpewtek-ka yields the pretend-to reading. The final k=an is a contracted progressive existential (kor an). | sato2007 | CHI | Satō ex. 6 (approx. p. 36)

The construction is limited to verbs of state or appearance assignable by an external
observer; verbs of direct physical action or creation do not enter it
nakagawa2024 189 .

Position in the verb template

In the slot ordering summarised in valency-voice-overview ,
si- precedes the root and any incorporated nominal but follows the applicative
prefixes. The ordering constraint with yay- is decisive:
yay-si- is attested — Nakagawa (2024: 189) cites
yay-si-turi-re lay oneself down while stretching 'lay oneself down while
stretching' — while the reverse *si-yay- is not
nakagawa2024 189 . This places si- closer to the root, in a
position Nakagawa compares to forming affixes.

yay-si-turi-re=an | REFL-REFL-stretch-CAUS=4.S | I lay down, stretching myself out. | The verb form yay-si-turi-re is attested in Nakagawa (2024: 189) to demonstrate the yay-outer / si-inner ordering; the surrounding clause is constructed. The fourth-person S suffix =an confirms that si-turi-re is intransitive.

The derivation chain illustrates the ordering across the arity calculus: the ditransitive
ko-i-ruska yields the intransitive
si-ko-i-ruska when si- is prefixed — it absorbs the
applicative complex's object slot while remaining to its left
nakagawa2024 210–211 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . In
the polysynthetic example discussed in valency-voice-overview —
a=e-yay-ko-tuyma-si-ram-suy-pa — a second si- sits
immediately before the incorporated noun ram heart in the
reflexive-possessive reading ('one's own heart'), while yay- occupies the outer
layer at the applicative boundary. The yay-outer / si-inner hierarchy is visible in a
single stacked verb form shibatani1990 73 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 .

title: The Reflexive yay-
summary: The reflexive prefix yay- 'self', its A/O coreference and detransitivization, and its autobenefactive/emotive extensions.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Reflexive yay-
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The reflexive prefix yay- 'self', its A/O coreference and detransitivization, and its autobenefactive/emotive extensions.

The reflexive yay- in the valency system

The prefix yay- reflexive fills the object position of a transitive verb with
a participant coreferential with the subject, reducing arity by one and converting the base
transitive to an intransitive nakagawa2024 188 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . Placed in the first-person plural, a verb bearing yay- takes the
intransitive S-suffixes =as (exclusive) or =an
(fourth-person) rather than the transitive A-prefixes — the person-affix test of the
derivation described in valency-voice-overview
sato2008 96 ; nakagawa2024 137 . A sister prefix
si- indirect reflexive occupies the same template slot and carries the same
arity value; its own distribution and the two prefixes' contrast are treated below
and in reflexive-possessive-si .

Nakagawa lists yay- among the 名詞的接頭辞 (nominal prefixes) of the
verb-derivation chapter alongside si- , the reciprocal
u- reciprocal , the antipassive i- antipassive , and the
lexical body-region prefixes he- head of / ho- bottom of , all
of them arity −1 and all filling the object slot from the inside of the verb word
nakagawa2024 185–192 . antipassive-detransitive-i and
reciprocal-u develop the parallel operators. Ijäs's learner lessons (L3 and L9)
do not cover the reflexive prefixes, as she addresses them in later material
ijas2023 .

Morphological range and the possessive yay-

yay- is nearly unrestricted in base selection: it attaches to
transitives and ditransitives of any semantic class, and to derived transitives
(causativized or applicativized stems) nakagawa2024 188 . The prefix also
attaches to nominals, where arity is unchanged because a noun carries no object slot to
absorb — the reading is then possessive, 'one's own':
yay-kotan one's own village ( yay- +
kotan village ) is a representative form
nakagawa2024 188 .

A productive subtype is the quasi-possessive reflexive
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 : where an incorporated body-part noun is interpreted as belonging to the subject,
yay- supplies the possessor rather than filling the object slot
directly. In yay-par-oyki feed oneself (literally 'do about one's own
mouth'), par mouth is incorporated as the object of
oyki do about, handle , while yay- identifies the
subject as the possessor of that mouth. The arity is still reduced by one because the
incorporated noun absorbs the object slot; the reflexive element determines whose body
part is implicated. This intersects with the object-incorporation system treated in
noun-incorporation-object .

The accent of yay- is stable: the prefix carries primary stress
as yáy- regardless of the following segment, unless lexicalization
has displaced it nakagawa2024 188 . Forms such as
yáyesiru confirm that the accent falls on
yáy even before a vowel-initial stem; this accent pattern is
corroborated by community corpus observation (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024,
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

si- is more restricted than yay- in base
selection, and when both co-occur the ordering is fixed:
yay-si-turi-re 'lay oneself down stretching' places
yay- outside si- , while the reverse
*si-yay- is unattested nakagawa2024 189 .
Nakagawa infers that si- sits closer to the root, behaving more
like a forming element than an independent prefix.

Self-affecting verb classes: grooming, emotion, and self-transformation

Three semantic fields attract yay- most productively. Body-treatment
and grooming verbs form the first: yay-kara treat oneself, attend to oneself
builds on kara treat, handle , the same verb that underlies
cise-kar build a house and related compounds
(see denominal-deverbal-verb-derivation ). Community corpus work independently
documents -kar as productive for 'handle/treat' operations —
nimawe-kar (treat with a bark compress),
osoro-kar — making yay-kara 'treat
oneself' the expected reflexive of that class (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024,
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›. A rolling-about verb in the Saru
narrative corpus shows causative stacking on a grooming-motion base:

asinuma ka nusa sam ta yay-karkarse-re=an kor paraparak | 1SG.EMPH even altar beside LOC REFL-roll-CAUS=4.S CONJ wail | asinuma ka nusa sam ta yaykarkarsere=an kor paraparak | I too rolled about by the altar, wailing. | yay-karkarse-re 'roll oneself / roll about': yay- fills the object slot of karkarse-re 'cause to roll (something)'; the fourth-person S suffix =an on the derived intransitive confirms the detransitivization. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話11; ILCAA materials aa-irc/013#377

A community decomposition of yayhonokka 'educate, discipline' as
yay-ho-nok-ka (REFL-bottom.body.region-CAUS) would show
yay- stacking over the body-region prefix
ho- and a causative suffix, with cross-dialectal support from Saru
and Shizunai forms
( ku=yayhonokka ; ci=honokka ;
taan hanokka )
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2026, aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›.
The decomposition has not been verified against the analytic literature.

Emotion and mental-state verbs make up a second field. Ainu transitive verbs of feeling
take a stimulus as their object; yay- reflexivizes that position,
yielding an intransitive of self-directed affect. The minimal pair
yay-rayke 'commit suicide' vs
si-rayke 'be destroyed by misfortune', both from
rayke kill , shows how the same base divides between the two prefixes; the
opposition is examined in the yay-/si- section below
nakagawa2024 188–189 .

Self-transformation verbs form the third class. With kar make, do , the
prefix yields yay-kar 'transform oneself, take on a different form':

aynu ne yay-kar wa kamuy ko-sunke wa an ru-we ne | human COP REFL-make CONJ deity APPL-lie CONJ exist track-POSS COP | He had taken on human form and was deceiving the deity. | yay-kar 'transform oneself': yay- fills the object slot of kar 'make', turning the transitive 'make X into Y' into an intransitive of self-directed change. an in an ruwe ne is the existential/copular verb 'be (in that state)', not a person suffix. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.1.1 | SAR | Nakagawa corpus K8007292UP; Bugaeva & Kobayashi ex. 4b

The yay-/si- distinction: competing analyses

The functional boundary between yay- and
si- has produced several accounts that remain in tension
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 ; nakagawa2024 187–189 ;
sato2007 . The four most developed positions are set out in the table below;
the sources for the first two and third are cited second-hand through the works that
survey them.

analysis
yay-
si-
source(s)

intentional vs unintentional
intentional, volitional action on self
unintentional; self undergoes without volition
Kindaichi (1931), Tamura (1988a, 1996: 622) — via
nakagawa2024 188 ; sato2007

agentive vs patientive self
agentive self: subject acts on itself
patientive self: subject undergoes the action
Kirikae (1994: 316) — via bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2

Theme/Patient restriction
no restriction on the role of the reflexive participant
reflexive participant must be Theme or Patient; causee impossible
Kobayashi (2008: 140) — via bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2

direct vs indirect reflexive
subject acts directly on itself, without mediation
subject is involved only indirectly: through others, through circumstance, or through social perception
sato2007 ; adopted by nakagawa2024 189 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2

Satō's direct/indirect account, developed for Chitose, is adopted by Nakagawa and the
Handbook as the current best analysis ‹contested›
sato2007 ; nakagawa2024 189 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . It holds that yay- marks an action the subject performs
directly on itself, without requiring any mediating agent or social condition.
si- marks a situation where the subject's involvement is indirect:
the outcome is achieved through another participant's action, through circumstances
beyond the subject's control, or through a state that holds only because others
perceive it as holding.

The canonical Chitose minimal pair is yay-pusu 'pop up by
swimming (volitionally)' vs si-pusu 'float of itself
(spontaneously)', both from pusu float something up
sato2007 . The si- form applies to a physical property
of spruce:

sunku anakne peka ta si-pusu wa mom pe ne | spruce TOP surface LOC REFL-float CONJ drift thing COP | Spruce floats on the water and drifts — that is its nature. | si-pusu 'float of itself': buoyancy is a property of the wood, not a directed self-act.
