The spruce has no volition, showing that the si-/yay- distinction is not intentional vs unintentional but direct vs indirect involvement. The contrast with yay-pusu 'pop up by swimming' is Satō's primary minimal pair. | sato2007 | CHI | Satō ex. 4; narrator 白沢ナベ, Chitose

The si- class also covers a productive 'pretend' construction:
si-mokor-e 'feign sleep',
si-kamuy-ne-re 'pretend to be a deity',
si-ranpewtek-ka 'pretend to know nothing'
nakagawa2024 189 ; sato2007 . In each case the subject's
performed state depends on how others perceive her — a condition that cannot hold
without an audience, hence the indirect marking:

ku=ko-si-ranpewtek-ka wa ki=an | 1SG.A=APPL-REFL-know-CAUS CONJ do=4.S | I was pretending to know nothing about it. | ko- promotes the object-of-pretence to applied object; si- marks that the unknowing-state depends on others' belief; -ka causativizes the resulting stative. ki=an (do=4.S) marks the ongoing activity as intransitive, confirming the final vi status of the complex. | sato2007 | CHI | Satō ex. 6; Chitose

From Nakagawa, a parallel emotional pair: yay-rayke 'commit
suicide' (direct, deliberate self-directed action) vs si-rayke
'be destroyed, come to ruin (by misfortune)' (the outcome is brought about by external
forces) nakagawa2024 188–189 . Bugaeva and Kobayashi add
yay-etaye 'draw oneself out (actively)' vs
si-etaye 'withdraw, pull oneself back in (in response to
something)' bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . The Shizunai corpus
supplies an attested instance of si-etaye where the withdrawal
is triggered by being seen:

i=nukar rok pe si-etaye wa isam | 4.O=see PRF thing REFL-withdraw CONJ be.absent | On seeing me, it withdrew and was gone. | si-etaye: withdrawal triggered by the act of being seen — an externally caused, indirect involvement of the subject in its own motion. Its yay- counterpart yay-etaye 'draw oneself out' involves no external trigger. | ilcaa1976 | SHI | Oda Suteno, reyep kamuy; ILCAA materials aa-irc/027#108

Satō himself notes that the direct/indirect analysis has not reached consensus
( 定説ではない ) sato2007 ; nakagawa2024 189 . The earlier intentional/unintentional framing (Kindaichi, Tamura) remains in
pedagogical use ‹contested›. A community reformulation characterizes
yay- as 'by one's own power' (reflexive) and
si- as 'happens to / gets through others' (anticausative), with
yaykaopiwki 'save oneself by one's own action' cited as a
diagnostic (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024, aynucorporadiscord )
‹contested›. This formulation is consistent with Satō's account while aligning the
si- end with anticausative typology. The hypothesis that the
two prefixes represent historically distinct voice categories rather than two subtypes
of reflexive (aomidori_cs_, aynu-corpora Discord 2023, aynucorporadiscord )
‹speculative› — no diachronic evidence bearing on a pre-reflexive function of either
prefix has been identified in the sources consulted for this grammar. Bugaeva and
Kobayashi note that no other reported language encodes the distinction between
dependence-on-others vs absence-of-such-dependence through a pair of reflexive
prefixes, making the Ainu opposition typologically unusual
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 .

The full treatment of si- — including its unaccusative uses,
its possessive-reflexive function with incorporated body-part nouns
( si-etu-uyna 'hold one's own nose'), and the pretend-to
constructions — is in reflexive-possessive-si .

Stacking with applicatives and the verb-word template

yay- combines freely with the applicative prefixes
e- , ko- , and
o- (see applicative-system-overview ). Since an
applicative raises arity by one (+1) and yay- lowers it by
one (−1), their combination on a transitive base preserves the original transitivity
while shifting which object position holds the reflexive participant. On a base that
already has an outer applicative-object, yay- fills the inner
original-object slot with 'self'; the outer applicative slot remains available for an
external NP.

The combination ko-yay-irayke 'be grateful to someone for
something one has experienced oneself' is attested in the Saru narrative corpus — here
ko- promotes the deity as the addressee-goal, and
yay- fills the inner object of
irayke be grateful with the subject:

petkorkamuy kamuy katkemat a=ko-yay-irayke hi a=ye a a=ye a kor | river.deity deity woman 4.A=APPL-REFL-be.grateful NMLZ 4.A=say PFV 4.A=say PFV CONJ | petkorkamuy kamuy katkemat a=koyayrayke hi a=ye a a=ye a kor | Again and again giving thanks to the river-deity woman, as she was telling it … | Surface koyayrayke: the phonological sequence yay-irayke surfaces as yayrayke at the morpheme boundary (dialectal and orthographic variants yayirayke ~ yairayke reflect the same junction). ko- = addressee applicative; yay- = reflexive on the inner object slot. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話9; ILCAA materials aa-irc/009#289

The derivational chain through ruska be angry at demonstrates the
calculus across further steps: ruska (vt) →
ko-ruska (vd, 'be angry toward') →
yay-ko-ruska (vt, 'be angry at oneself') →
i-ruska (vi, 'be angry', object defocused) — each step
changes arity by exactly one unit nakagawa2024 212 ; sato2023b 40 ‹consensus›. The antipassive i- in the same chain
is treated in antipassive-detransitive-i .

The verb template places reflexive and applicative prefixes inside the person-index
layer and outside the root: person-index — reflexive/applicative — root —
forming-suffix — causative — person-index
nakagawa2024 210–212 . Two reflexive prefixes and two applicatives can
co-occur on a single root, as in the showcase word
a=e-yay-ko-tuyma-si-ram-suy-pa discussed in
valency-voice-overview template
shibatani1990 73 ; bugaeva2014 .
A community-proposed decomposition of
yaykipniwkes as yay-ki-p-niwkes —
with ki 'do' as a light verb and
niwkes as the state predicate (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord
2024, aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested› — illustrates the opaque prefix
stacking that occurs in this zone; the analysis awaits verification against primary
morphological descriptions. The reciprocal u- , which parallels
yay- in its arity value while filling both S and O slots
simultaneously, is in reciprocal-u .

title: Relational and Spatial (Locative) Nouns and Their Possessed Forms
summary: The obligatorily-possessed relational/spatial noun system (or, ka, corpok, sam, tum...), the first-/second-class locative split, the affiliative forms of these nouns, and or's drift toward a general locative; a bridge to the postpositional case system.
part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Relational and Spatial (Locative) Nouns and Their Possessed Forms
Part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
Summary: The obligatorily-possessed relational/spatial noun system (or, ka, corpok, sam, tum...), the first-/second-class locative split, the affiliative forms of these nouns, and or's drift toward a general locative; a bridge to the postpositional case system.

The class of relational nouns

A small closed set of nouns in Hokkaido Ainu encodes the spatial or relational position of
one entity with respect to another. These nouns — or place/locus ,
ka top/surface , sam side , tum interior ,
corpok underside , and others catalogued below — are called
locative nouns (位置名詞) in the Japanese-medium descriptive tradition
nakagawa2024 241, 280, 296–299 ; sato2021 ;
nakagawa2022pos 478–480 .

Three distributional properties define the class. First, the members are
obligatorily possessed : a relational noun cannot head a free-standing noun
phrase in isolation; it must either be preceded by a noun phrase whose referent supplies
the reference object, or it must carry a personal prefix directly to express whose
spatial domain is at stake (§7 below; see alienable-inalienable-split ).
Second, Hokkaido Ainu case particles require a place-designating noun (〈場所〉)
immediately before them sato2008 34–35 ; nakagawa2024 296 ;
relational nouns are the main device for building that slot from an ordinary common
noun. The canonical pattern is cise or ta 'at the house'
(lit. house + place-of + at), and its extension to the other case particles
( or un , or wa ,
or peka ) constitutes the primary locative-case system, treated in
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events . Third, a relational noun
appears in its concept-form-affiliative-form possessed (affiliative) form
when combined with a host noun: or → oro ,
ka → kasi (§3). Whether the class
is best analysed as a set of inalienably-possessed nouns or as a set of postpositions
inflected for possession is the central analytical question of the chapter (§8).

Inventory and semantics

The table below lists the core members with their concept form, possessed (affiliative)
form, spatial meaning, and a representative construction. The affiliative forms of
or and ka are directly attested in the
sources cited; those of tum , etok ,
os , and so follow the regular
consonant-final suffix pattern but are inferred rather than directly verified in
the sources consulted, and should be treated as ‹speculative› pending a dedicated pass
through Tamura's dictionary and the Nakagawa corpus texts.
nakagawa2024 241, 296–299 ; tamura1996 s.v. or, ka, sam, tum, corpok, soy, kotor ;
sato2021

concept form
affiliative form
spatial relation
canonical construction

or
oro
'place/locus of X'

cise or ta → cise otta
(fused); cise oro ta (long, unfused)

ka
kasi
'top/surface of X, above X'

ni ka ta 'on/onto the tree';
cikuni kasi un 'toward the treetop'

enka
enkasi
'above X, over X' (vertical distance)
nupuri enka ta 'above the mountain'

sam
sami
'side of X, beside X'
cise sam ta 'beside the house'

tum
tumi ‹speculative›
'interior of X, amid X'
utar tum ta 'among the people'

corpok
corpoki
'underside of X, below/beneath X'
cise corpok ta 'below the house'

pok
poki
'bottom/underside of X'
sehpok ta 'beneath the bed'

etok
etoki ‹speculative›
'front/ahead of X'
cise etok ta 'in front of the house'

os
osi ‹speculative›
'rear/behind X, after X'
cise os ta 'behind the house'

kotor
kotoro ‹speculative›
'face/surface of X (vertical)'

tek kotor 'palm of the hand'
( tek + kotor )

soy
soyo ‹speculative›
'exterior/outside of X'
cise soy ta 'outside the house'

so
— ‹speculative›
'upper back/rear of X'
iwor so ka 'over the top of the hunting ground'

A few entries call for comment. The pair ka / enka
are often treated as co-members of the 'above' domain: ka encodes
contact or near-contact with a supporting surface ('on top of, resting on'), while
enka encodes vertical distance above without contact ('over, above').
The pair corpok / pok occupies a similar
near-duplicate slot in the 'below' domain; the distributional boundary between them is
not settled by the sources consulted ‹contested›. The noun kotor
appears reliably as a component of the compound
tekkotor 'palm of the hand' (lit. hand + surface)
nakagawa2024 250 , and the pattern suggests a class of vertical-surface
spatial nouns; but its use as a free relational noun head (i.e.
X kotor ta 'at the face of X') is less thoroughly documented
in the sources consulted. The noun so appears in corpus narrative in the
construction iwor so ka 'over the top of the hunting ground',
where so (upper back area) combines with ka
(top) — a stacking of two relational nouns into a single locative expression (see §4
below). Its standalone affiliative form has not been identified in the sources consulted.

Two relational nouns — or and os — also
appear in Nakagawa's catalogue of postpositional adverbs (後置副詞), the autonomous adverb
class that does not require a 〈場所〉 host
nakagawa2024 280–282 ; sato2008 34–36 . This dual
membership is a structural fact at the heart of the noun-vs-adposition debate in §8.

Obligatory possession and affiliative forms

Relational nouns belong to the inalienable (obligatorily-possessed) noun class,
alongside body parts, kinship terms, and part–whole relational stems. Like those classes,
they cannot appear as a syntactic head without an expressed or inferable possessor
nakagawa2024 241 . The morphology of this possession is the
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative (所属形) : in the construction
[NOUN + relational-noun + case-particle], the relational noun takes its possessed form.
The phonological mechanism that produces or →
oro , ka → kasi ,
sam → sami , and so on, is the
echo-vowel or suffix-vowel process described in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

A diagnostically important minimal pair is provided by
ni ka ta 'onto/on top of the tree' and
cikuni kasi un 'toward the treetop'
nakagawa2024 298–299 . The first uses the concept (bare) form
ka before the locative particle ta ;
the second uses the affiliative kasi before the allative
un . The distribution of concept vs affiliative form before
different case particles for this noun parallels what Satō
sato2021 documents for or in Chitose: both forms
are grammatical with a following locative noun, but the short-form fusion
( or + ta →
otta ) is the unmarked spoken realization, while
oro ta appears in more deliberate or expanded styles
nakagawa2024 297 ‹corpus-suggested›.

A community discussion on samampa (nukopoli,
aynucorporadiscord 2024-11-26) analyses it as
sam-am-pa 'lying on the side' — identifying
sam 'side' as a productive base entering compound verb
formation — and proposes that samama
'to place sideways' = sam-ama (Kayano's Saru dictionary) ‹speculative›.
This use of sam as an incorporated nominal base in complex
verbs is a point of intersection with the noun-incorporation system (see
noun-incorporation-object ).

The layered locative construction

The canonical locative construction in Hokkaido Ainu is a three-member sequence:
a reference-object noun phrase, followed by a relational noun in its affiliative or
concept form, followed by a case particle. Nakagawa labels the middle position the
〈場所〉 'place' slot — a morphosyntactic position that the locative, allative, ablative,
and perlative particles all require nakagawa2024 296 ;
sato2008 34 . Relational nouns are the primary filler of that slot for
ordinary common nouns.

orwano ku=cise or ta ku=an kor | then 1SG.POSS=house place LOC 1SG.S=exist while | Then, while I was in my house, … | The relational noun or follows the host noun cise 'house'; the possessor ku= marks the host noun, not or itself. The case particle ta attaches to or in the 〈場所〉 slot. | nakagawa2024:166 | ISH | after Sunazawa Kura 1983: 75; Nakagawa ex. 104

ne kenas or peka ekimne=an kor | DEM forest place PERL hunt=4.S while | while going through that forest to hunt | or peka 'through the area of' uses the perlative particle; the fused otta form only arises with ta, not with peka or un. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawagami Matsuko, AA-ken Ainu materials, uwepeker 民話7, doc. aa-irc/005, line 140

atuy or un cip ani paye=an | sea place ALLAT boat INS go=4.S | going to the sea by boat | or un 'to the place of (i.e. toward) the sea'; the instrumental postpositional adverb ani follows. The full clause is orowano atuy or un cip ani paye=an ranke p ne. | biratori1969 | SAR | Hiraga Sadamo, 'Urayushiunkur', Biratori Ainu oral literature, doc. biratori/010/003, line 124

The particle-specific fusion of or merits particular attention.
When or immediately precedes the locative particle
ta , the sequence contracts to otta :
cise otta 'at the house', nupuri otta
'at/on the mountain'. The long form oro ta remains uncontracted
nakagawa2024 297 . This fusion does not apply to
or un , or wa , or
or peka ; those remain uncontracted in all varieties.

The construction extends straightforwardly to other relational nouns. The same
case-particle set attaches to ka , sam ,
soy , and the other members of the inventory:

ni ka ta hemesu=an | tree top LOC climb=4.S | climbed onto the tree | Nakagawa ex. 419. ka ta 'on top of / onto' marks the locative goal on the tree. Contrast cikuni kasi un 'toward the treetop' (allative) in the immediately following example (Nakagawa 2024: 299), where the affiliative kasi appears before the allative particle un. | nakagawa2024:298 | SAR

cip pet ka ta an | boat river top LOC exist | The boat is on the river. | Satō 第5課 (Chitose). pet ka ta 'on (the surface of) the river'; ka here profiles contact with the water surface. | sato2008:37 | CHI

nean cise soy ta paye=as ki na | DEM house outside LOC go=1PL.EXCL.S do FIN | went outside that house | cise soy ta 'at the outside of the house'. The form =as is the first-person-plural-exclusive intransitive suffix; the Japanese source gloss uses singular first person, reflecting the narrative first-person reading that Ishikari shares with the fourth-person narrative use of Saru (see person-marking-architecture). | nakagawa2024:285 | ISH | via Ōtsuka 1990: 27; Nakagawa ex. 383, Ishikari

Two relational nouns may stack in sequence when the spatial description requires
a two-step reference: an outer reference object, then a spatial noun within it,
then a second spatial noun closer to the target. The corpus yields
iwor so ka a=ehóyupu wa 'flew over the top of the hunting
ground's upper part' (lit. hunting-ground + upper-back + top + 4.A=fly.around + CONJ)
ilcaa1976 , where so 'upper-back area'
specifies the subregion of iwor 'hunting ground',
and ka then positions the protagonist above that subregion
( ilcaa1976 , Kawagami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話8, aa-irc/006 line 52).
Such stacking is consistent with the head-initial, modifier-following structure of
Ainu nominal possession ‹corpus-suggested›.

The pivotal role of or

Among the relational nouns, or occupies a singular position:
its meaning has bleached to the point where it functions as a general locative
placeholder for any noun requiring the 〈場所〉 slot, not just for entities that
constitute physical places. Satō's dedicated Chitose study
sato2021 traces the drift of or from a concrete
relational noun 'the place of X' toward a grammaticalizing locative morpheme that
renders any nominal as a place-designating argument.

The bleaching is visible in combinations where the host noun is not inherently a
physical location. In tan KIKAY or ta a=ewkoytak hi an yakun
'if we had a conversation with this machine' (lit. this machine's-place at),
nakagawa2024 284 (ex. 373, Saru; 田村 1984: 50), the host noun
KIKAY (a recording machine) is an instrument, not a spatial
locus; or ta constructs the 〈場所〉 argument that the particle
ta requires, mapping the instrumental participant onto a
locative frame. The Chitose corpus adds many parallel instances where abstract events
or instruments appear in the host-noun position sato2021 .

Whether this drift constitutes the grammaticalization of or
into a general locative case-marker, or whether it reflects a productive but still
compositional use of the relational noun, is a point on which Satō
sato2021 and Nakagawa nakagawa2024 241, 296–299 take
partially different positions ‹contested›: Nakagawa treats the construction as
productive composition (noun + spatial-noun + particle), while Satō's Chitose
evidence shows or extending into contexts where the
'place of X' semantics is increasingly opaque, suggesting at least the beginning
of a grammaticalization cline. The issue connects directly to the noun-vs-adposition
debate in §8, because an advanced-stage grammaticalized
or would be functionally indistinguishable from a
locative postposition.

First-class and second-class locative nouns

Nakagawa nakagawa2024 241 distinguishes two sub-classes of locative
noun in Hokkaido Ainu, a distinction also partly reflected in Satō
sato2008 34–36 . The first-class locative nouns are those whose
primary function is to supply the 〈場所〉 slot: they follow a host noun and directly
host a case particle. or , ka ,
sam , soy , and
corpok are the clearest members. The second-class members
are those whose primary occurrence is in compound nouns and verb bases rather than
in the free-standing [NOUN + relational-noun + case-particle] frame.
kotor 'surface' is the paradigm case: it appears reliably
in the lexicalized compound tekkotor 'palm of the hand'
( tek hand + kotor )
nakagawa2024 250 and has left traces in the archaic directional forms,
but it is not a productive first-class head in the contemporary spoken register.
Similarly, tum 'interior/midst' appears in place-name
formation (proposed: o-tum-nay
'head-interior-river' for the toponym Otomonai; nukopoli,
aynucorporadiscord 2026-01-22) ‹speculative› and in compound verbs
( tumpu 'storehouse' = tum
'interior' + pu ; antitwilight,
aynucorporadiscord 2024-11-16) ‹speculative›, but its distributional
range in the first-class frame remains less thoroughly documented than that of
or or ka .

The class boundary is not sharp, and intermediate members exist. In the postpositional
adverb system nakagawa2024 280–281 , os(i)
'after' appears as an autonomous postpositional adverb that does not require a
〈場所〉 host. This dual membership — relational noun in one construction,
postpositional adverb in another — is also observed for
or itself: Nakagawa lists it in both noun paradigms
and in the broader locative system as a particle-combining locative element. The
overlap between the two classes is structural evidence for the noun-vs-adposition
debate (§8).

An archaic directional suffix -na is attested in frozen
forms: setunna 'from the front' and
kotunna 'from behind', analysed as
setur-na and kotor-na respectively
(antitwilight, aynucorporadiscord 2026-06-09); compare also
pokna 'below', which may preserve the same suffix on
the relational noun pok . This suffix is not a productive
case particle in contemporary Hokkaido Ainu, and no primary-source grammar in
the read set treats it as such ‹speculative›. Its existence as a fossil form is
consistent with the hypothesis that the relational-noun class is older than the
current case-particle system and that some members grammaticalized into the
particle system at an earlier stage.

Non-third-person possession

The possessor of a relational noun is third person (zero-marked) when the reference
object is supplied by the preceding host noun phrase. But relational nouns can also
be directly possessed by speech-act participants via the standard personal prefixes
treated in person-marking-architecture and
adnominal-possession-double-marking . In that case, the relational noun
itself — not the host noun — bears the person prefix:

ku=or ta ku=an | 1SG.POSS=place LOC 1SG.S=exist | I am at my (own) place. | Constructed to illustrate direct first-person possession of the relational noun or. The prefix ku= marks the possessor on or; ku=an is the first-person-singular intransitive subject form of the existential an.

The full person paradigm for or follows the ordinary nominal
possession pattern:
ku=or 'my place',
e=or 'your place',
or (zero) 'his/her/its/their place',
ci=or (1PL.EXCL) 'our place',
a=or (4th person) 'the protagonist's/their general place'
nakagawa2024 241, 296 .
The same paradigm applies to all other first-class relational nouns:
ku=ka ta 'on top of me', e=sam ta
'beside you' ‹consensus›. These forms appear in contexts where the locative
relation targets a person rather than a physical landmark, and they are attested
in body-part locatives and relational idioms (see
external-possession-possessor-raising for how this
intersects with external possession).

A productive derived construction involves the verb
eykaun 'to surpass/be superior to', analysed by
nukopoli ( aynucorporadiscord 2024-11-24) as
ka un 'be at the top of (someone)'
with an applicative or transitivizing suffix — effectively the relational noun
ka with allative un
grammaticalized as a comparative marker ‹speculative›. Whether
this represents a synchronically transparent construction or a frozen derivation
is not settled by the sources consulted.

Noun or adposition?

The central analytical question is whether relational nouns constitute a subclass of
nouns or a subclass of postpositions. The dispute turns on which grammatical
properties are weighted most heavily, and different descriptive traditions have
come down on different sides ‹contested›.

Evidence for the noun analysis.
Relational nouns take person affixes in exactly the same slots and with exactly the
same forms as other inalienably-possessed nouns ( ku=or ,
ku=sam , ku=ka parallel
ku=sapa 'my head', ku=tek 'my hand');
they appear in the possessed (affiliative) form under possession, following the same
morphophonological rules as the inalienable class
( nakagawa2024 241 ; nakagawa2022pos 478–480 ). They
enter compound-noun and incorporated-noun formation as ordinary nominal bases
( tekkotor , tumpu ), which is diagnostic
of noun status in Ainu (see noun-incorporation-object ). Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 241, 280 treats them as a noun subclass throughout.
The same analysis underlies Ijäs's pedagogical treatment
ijas2023 , which presents the locative construction as a
possessed-noun-plus-particle sequence.

Evidence for the adposition analysis.
Functionally, relational nouns occupy the same distributional slot as adpositions:
they appear between a nominal complement and a case particle, and they encode the
same spatial relations that languages with postpositions typically encode with a
postpositional head. Several members of the class — notably
os(i) 'after' — appear in Nakagawa's catalogue of
postpositional adverbs (後置副詞)
nakagawa2024 280–282 ; sato2008 34–36 .
Satō's Chitose evidence for or
sato2021 shows the form extending beyond referential 'place of X'
into a general locative-marking function, which is the expected trajectory of
a grammaticalizing postposition, not of a lexical noun. The pedagogical grammar
by Ijäs ijas2023 places some members of the class in its
postposition section alongside case particles — a pragmatic descriptive choice
that reflects the adpositional function these forms serve for learners.

The most defensible synchronic position is that the class is in transition: the
morphological behaviour (person affixes, affiliative alternation, incorporation)
identifies the members as nouns, while their functional distribution (obligatory
position between complement and particle, encoding spatial relations, extension
to non-spatial contexts) pushes in the adposition direction. A parallel situation
is found in several languages of Mesoamerica, where the same class of
inalienably-possessed nominal forms — often glossed as 'relational nouns'
in the descriptive literature — occupies this intermediate position between noun
and adposition bugaeva2022 . In those typological terms,
Hokkaido Ainu relational nouns are best labelled relational nouns rather
than postpositions or adpositions: they are nominal in morphology and adpositional
in function ‹contested›.

Whether first-class members like or and
ka are more advanced on a grammaticalization cline toward
postpositions than second-class members like kotor is a
plausible hypothesis supported by Satō's work on or
sato2021 but not yet generalized across the full inventory ‹speculative›.
The syntactic behavior of relational-noun phrases in nonconfigurational and
pronominal-argument contexts is taken up in
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument and
oblique-adjunct-verbless-clauses .

title: The Rhotic /r/ and Coda-r
summary: The single rhotic phoneme (flap [ɾ]) with positional allophones, and coda /r/ as the most morphophonologically active segment of the language.
part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Rhotic /r/ and Coda-r
Part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
Summary: The single rhotic phoneme (flap [ɾ]) with positional allophones, and coda /r/ as the most morphophonologically active segment of the language.

The phoneme /r/ in the consonant inventory

Hokkaido Ainu has one rhotic phoneme, /r/, the sole coronal sonorant that is neither a nasal
(/m/, /n/) nor a glide (/y/, /w/) in the eleven-consonant system described in
consonant-inventory
nakagawa2024 27 ; shiraishi2022 §3 ;
sato2008 §2.2 . The phoneme occurs in both onset and coda positions within the
canonical syllable-template (C)V(C) syllable , and its coda realization —
carrying a distinctive vocalic release and triggering a dense assimilation system — makes it
the most morphophonologically active segment in the language. The full assimilation rules are
given in coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi ; the present chapter covers the
phoneme's phonetic realization, the echo-vowel phenomenon, and the diagnostics that separate
/r/-final syllables from genuine /rV/ syllables.

Onset realization and positional variants

In syllable-onset position /r/ is realized as an alveolar flap [ɾ]
nakagawa2024 28 ; shiraishi2022 §3.1 . Speaker variation and
historical change produce a broader allophonic range; the table below collects documented
realizations.

position
realization
conditions / notes
source

onset — general
[ɾ] alveolar flap
predominant realization across Hokkaido dialects

nakagawa2024 28 ; shiraishi2022 §3.1

onset — speaker variation
[r] trill or [d]-like

trill and dental-approximant realizations attested in some speakers; Tamura (1988b:
13) reports [l] and [d] allophones

shiraishi2022 §3.1

onset — older recordings
[ɹ] approximant

approximant realization noted in early recordings; more recent speakers favour [ɾ]
‹corpus-suggested›

aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-05)

coda — general
[ɾ] + vocalic echo
flap released into an echo-vowel copying the syllable nucleus (§3 below)

nakagawa2024 30 ; shiraishi2022 §3.2

coda — after onset /k p t/
[ɾ̥] devoiced flap

voicelessness of the onset stop spreads to the coda rhotic; e.g.
nukar [nukaɾ̥] ‹corpus-suggested›

aynucorporadiscord (aomidori, 2023-12-11)

The allophonic range — flap, trill, dental-approximant — places Ainu /r/ in a different
perceptual space from Japanese ラ行, whose typical word-initial and post-nasal realization
leans lateral. Tamura's Saru dictionary grammar is reported by community members to emphasize
this perceptual distance explicitly
aynucorporadiscord (aomidori, 2023-12-11, citing Tamura 1996) ‹speculative›;
the source most directly available, Shiraishi (2022: §3.1), records [l] and [d] as
occasional allophones attributed to Tamura (1988b: 13) rather than as the default, which
remains the flap [ɾ]. In Sakhalin Ainu the cognate of Hokkaido /r/ is described as retroflex
[ʈ], acoustically close to [t] and distinct from the Hokkaido flap
shiraishi2022 §3.1 .

Coda /r/ and the echo-vowel

The coda realization of /r/ carries a vocalic release — known variously in the literature as
the re-sounding , the echo-vowel , or the echoing vowel — that has
attracted sustained analysis since the earliest systematic descriptions
shiraishi2022 §3.2 . In careful speech the release is audible as a short
vowel copying the nucleus of the syllable: kar [kaɾ(a)],
kor [koɾ(o)], sir [siɾ(i)]. In recitation
or emphatic delivery the release extends to a full vowel: [piriˑka], [mokoroˑ]
shiraishi2022 §3.2 .

Batchelor (1905) and the early Kindaichi transcriptions wrote post-/r/ vowels as full
phonemic segments — hence spellings such as koro ,
pirika , Ainurakkuru — without distinguishing
a genuine /rV/ syllable from the echo-vowel release of a /r/-final syllable
batchelor1905 ; shiraishi2022 §3.2 . Yukie Chiri (知里幸恵), in
correspondence with Kindaichi around 1923, established that /r/-final words form a coherent
phonological category and that the post-/r/ releases in Batchelor's transcriptions are
phonologically spurious
shiraishi2022 §3.2 . She identified three diagnostics that separate
/r/-final from /rV/-final words
nakagawa2024 30 ; shiraishi2022 §3.2 :

The echo-vowel weakens before a vowel-initial suffix; a genuine final vowel does
not. In mokor=an 'sleep.4S' the echo is reduced before
the suffix, whereas the final /o/ of poro=an 'be.big.4S' remains
unreduced.

Coda /r/ undergoes morphophonological sandhi; /rV/ does not. The
/r/-final verb a=kor 'have' assimilates its coda before the onset
of a following word ( a=kor nispa → [akonˈnispa] 'a wealthy man'),
whereas a word ending in /rV/ such as poro 'big' shows no
corresponding change. The sandhi system is treated in
coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi .

The nominalizer selects its consonant-allomorph after coda /r/. The
nominalizer -p/-pe nominalizer surfaces as -pe after
a consonant and -p after a vowel. A /r/-final stem takes
-pe :

mokor-pe | sleep-NMLZ | a sleeper; something asleep | The C-allomorph -pe selects after coda /r/, diagnosing mokor as phonemically /r/-final. Contrast poro-p below.

poro-p | big-NMLZ | something big; a large thing | The V-allomorph -p selects after the vowel-final stem poro, confirming that the stem ends in /o/, not /r/.

The minimal pair etor [etoro] 'snot' versus
etoro [etoːro] 'to snore' is now well established in the
literature: in the /rV/-final word the vowel preceding /r/ is measurably longer, whereas the
echo-vowel in the /r/-final word is audibly shorter
nakagawa2024 30 ; shiraishi2022 §3.2 . Asai (1976)
confirmed the length difference acoustically shiraishi2022 §3.2 . An
additional independent diagnostic, noted by Satō (2000), is that in pre-pausal position the
final vowel is repeated only for /r/-final words shiraishi2022 §3.2 .

The direction of vowel-copying is predominantly anticipatory: pirka
surfaces as [pirĭka] and arki as [ariki], with the echo
anticipating the following vowel rather than perseverating the preceding one
nakagawa2024 30 . A perseveratory case, citarpe
[citarube] (Asai 1976), shows that the copying direction is not categorically fixed
shiraishi2022 §3.2 . Northern dialect speakers vacillate between
-r and -rV in certain lexical items
( kikir ~ kikiri ), suggesting ongoing
redistribution between the two categories in those varieties
shiraishi2022 §3.2 .

One articulatory account of the echo-vowel, proposed within the community, treats it as a
timing offset: the tongue-tip flap gesture begins before the mouth has finished the preceding
vowel, so the flap releases into the vowel-gesture and produces an audible copy of the
nucleus aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹speculative›. Published
treatments describe the release as a phonological property of coda /r/ without committing to
a specific articulatory model shiraishi2022 §3.2 .

In casual connected speech, word-final /r/ is susceptible to deletion, producing reduced
forms such as ku for kur 'person' and
ko for kor 'have', while the verb
koro tends to stabilize its full form in many contexts
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-11) ‹corpus-suggested›. This casual
reduction is distinct from the diachronic Sakhalin coda-/r/ → /h/ change treated in
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex .

Devoicing of coda /r/ after voiceless-stop onsets

When the onset of a CVC syllable is a voiceless stop /k/, /p/, or /t/, the coda /r/ is
devoiced to [ɾ̥]. The verb nukar 'look at' illustrates the pattern:
the final syllable kar has onset /k/ and coda /r/, and the rhotic
surfaces as [ɾ̥], giving [nukaɾ̥]
aynucorporadiscord (aomidori, 2023-12-11) ‹corpus-suggested›. The pattern is
phonetically parallel to the partial devoicing of sonorant codas reported for other languages
after voiceless-onset syllables; Shiraishi's description of /r/ as a flap with
voicing-conditioned variants shiraishi2022 §3.1 is consistent with the
conditioning, though no instrumental study targeting this specific alternation has been found
in the sources read for this grammar.

The devoiced coda in turn affects the echo-vowel: when [ɾ̥] releases, the following vocalic
copy is likewise voiceless, yielding a realization such as [nukaɾ̥ɨ̥h] in citation-form
speech aynucorporadiscord (aomidori, 2023-12-11). Vowel devoicing in comparable
environments — high vowels between voiceless consonants — is independently attested in
Hokkaido Ainu and is described in vowel-inventory ; the rhotic devoicing here
extends the same voicelessness-spreading dynamic to the coda rhotic of CVC syllables with
voiceless onsets.

Morphophonological activity of coda /r/

Coda /r/ is the dominant trigger of the regressive assimilation rules described in
coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi . When a /r/-final morpheme or word precedes
a consonant-initial morpheme or word, the /r/ assimilates to, or is absorbed into a
geminate with, the following consonant. The principal alternations, drawn from Nakagawa
(2024: 35–37) and Shiraishi (2022: §6.1), are: /r/ + /t/ → [tt]
( a=kor tar → [aˈkottar]); /r/ + /n/ → [nn]
( a=kor nispa → [akonˈnispa]); /r/ + /c/ → [tc]
( retar cikap → [retatˈcikap] 'swan'); /r/ + /s/ → [ss]
( ar suy → [asˈsuy] 'once'); and, uniquely, a dissimilatory
/r/ + /r/ → [nn] ( ku=kor rusuy → [kukonˈrusuy] 'I want it')
nakagawa2024 35–37 ; shiraishi2022 §6.1 .

Unlike nasal-place assimilation (which is bounded by the phrase in Shiraishi's 2001
analysis), /r/-sandhi operates across phrasal and sentence boundaries as a postlexical rule
shiraishi2022 §6.2 ;
aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, 2024-11-23) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The rules
are not written in standard orthography, which notates underlying forms; they are fully
productive in speech nakagawa2024 37–38 . Personal-affix junctures that feed
the same /r/-sandhi rules are treated in
personal-affix-sandhi-connected-speech-reduction , and the echo-vowel's
interaction with possessive-form derivation is treated in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Dialectal variation and the Sakhalin coda-/r/ reflex

Across Hokkaido varieties, coda /r/ shows at least one documented lexically specific
neutralization: in some dialects mokor 'sleep' surfaces as
mokon , with /r/ replaced by [n]
aynucorporadiscord (keycja, 2024-07-20) ‹speculative›. Nakagawa and Fukazawa's
dialect classification documents extensive morphophonological isoglosses across the Hokkaido
area nakagawafukazawa2022 §3 , though the specific /r/ → /n/ change in this
lexical item has not been mapped to a named dialect zone in the sources read for this
grammar. The broader picture of dialectal consonant correspondences is taken up in
hokkaido-phonological-microvariation .

In Sakhalin Ainu, coda /r/ has merged with /h/ in syllable-final and word-final position,
so that Hokkaido kor 'have' corresponds to Sakhalin
koh . The change is attested in the Sakhalin corpus, and the
distribution of Sakhalin -h vis-à-vis the retained /rV/ type has
been the subject of dedicated distributional analysis (cited in
aynucorporadiscord nukopoli, 2024-12-19). Because the Sakhalin /r/ → /h/ shift
belongs primarily to the diachronic and comparative treatment, details are routed to
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex . The Sakhalin onset cognate of
Hokkaido /r/ is the retroflex [ʈ] mentioned in §2 above, distinct both from the Hokkaido
flap and from the Sakhalin /h/ that the coda reflex produces.

title: ruwe ne — the Inferential / Visual-Trace Evidential
summary: The default assertive-evidential ruwe ne ('it is the trace/fact that'), marking inference from evidence or established fact, the most frequent and most grammaticalized term of the paradigm.
part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: ruwe ne — the Inferential / Visual-Trace Evidential
Part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
Summary: The default assertive-evidential ruwe ne ('it is the trace/fact that'), marking inference from evidence or established fact, the most frequent and most grammaticalized term of the paradigm.

Place in the evidential paradigm

Hokkaido Ainu marks the speaker's source of information periphrastically: a finite clause is
nominalized by a bound formal noun and the result is predicated with the copula
ne . The language has no dedicated evidential verbal affix. Four formal
nouns build the core paradigm, each
the possessed form of a sensory noun: ruwe (< ru track, path ), siri (< sir visible appearance, land, weather ),
hawe (< haw voice ), and
humi (< hum sound, feeling )
nakagawa2024 258, 392 ; hirosawa2026 64 . Mapped onto the
typology of aikhenvald2004 63–65 , the system is of the
C1 type — visual, non-visual sensory, inferred, and reported — realized analytically,
through a native nominalization-plus-copula construction
hirosawa2026 64 ; takahashi2013 129 . The architecture common to
all four terms is set out in evidential-schema-overview ; the present chapter
treats the central member ruwe ne , and its siblings in
siri-ne-situational the situational evidential ,
hawe-ne-reportative the reportative , and
humi-ne-sensory the sensory evidential . The closed class of formal nouns that
makes the construction possible is defined in formal-defective-nouns .

formal noun
possessed morphology
source noun
information source

ruwe
ru-we ‘the trace of —’
ru track, path
fact recognized through knowledge, reasoning, or an assimilated trace

siri
sir-i
sir appearance, land
directly visible situation

hawe
haw-e
haw voice
verbal report or hearsay

humi
hum-i
hum sound, feeling
non-visual sensation

Dal Corso resolves these four direct terms into an eight-form system whose indirect
counterparts replace the copula with the existential an , the verb
ki ‘do’, or as ‘stand’, organized by two
parameters, source reliability and event accessibility ; within it
ruwe ne is the highest-reliability direct form, and the trace-noun
ru forms direct evidentiality alone — the southern dialects have no
indirect *ru -counterpart to siri an
dalcorso2018 304, 309, 337 .

ruwe ne also dominates the paradigm quantitatively. In Dal Corso's
Hokkaido reference texts it supplies 1508 of 2232 evidential tokens — roughly two thirds of the
eight-form system, and far ahead of hawe ne ,
siri ne , and humi ne dalcorso2018 72 . The present Hokkaido corpus of 167,890 sentences makes the skew sharper. A string search
returns 16,350 tokens of ruwe ne beside 796 hawe ne ,
607 siri ne , and 200 humi ne among the direct
forms, and 304 siri an , 395 hawe as ,
210 humi as , and 218 siri ki among the indirect:
some 86% of the eight forms together, and about 91% of the four direct ones (the two corpora are
compared below). The string count folds in the homophonous lexical noun
ruwe and the reduced final particle, so 86% is an upper bound on the
evidential proper. The asymmetry is far too large to follow from that conflation, and it confirms
the descriptive consensus that ruwe ne is the unmarked, most
grammaticalized term of the four ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Morphology: ruwe is ru-we, ‘the trace of —’

The head ruwe is the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form of the noun
ru track, path . In Tamura's Saru dictionary the ordinary possessed form is
ruwe(he) ‘the track that — passed along, the path made by —’s passing’,
and the evidential formal noun is analysed as ru-e , the noun plus a
possessive ending tamura1996 s.v. ru, ruwe . For glossing this is decisive: the
morpheme is the possessed ru-we , ‘the trace of —’, whose implicit
possessor is the event described by the nominalized clause. The same
possessed-noun pattern underlies sir-i ,
haw-e , and hum-i nakagawa2024 258, 390 . A subtle point distinguishes the evidential from the full noun: the lexical possessed forms
may carry an extra ending ( ruwehe , humihi ),
whereas the evidential formal nouns block it ( *ruwehe is ungrammatical
as the evidential) tamura1996 s.v. ruwe .

The construction is therefore a copular predication over a nominalized clause —
[clause] ruwe ‘the trace/fact that [clause]’, closed by the copula
ne treated in copula-ne-predicate-nominals . It is not,
however, an ordinary relative-clause head plus a copula. The head has lost the syntax of a free
noun: it admits no demonstrative or attributive modifier of its own (contrast a true lexical
head such as pirka menoko ‘a beautiful woman’), it cannot head a common
noun ( *pirka ruwe ne kewtum ), and it is fixed in linear position —
kusu ne ruwe ne is well formed but *ruwe ne kusu ne
is not nakagawa2024 393 ; dalcorso2018 24–25 . The accurate
synchronic label is thus a partially grammaticalized formal-noun evidential,
intermediate between a fused verbal suffix and a fully lexical noun, and it is best analysed
alongside the
formal-noun nominalization treated in lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization and
the general clausal nominalizers of nominalization-p-pe ‹consensus›.

eci=ekanok kusu ek=an ru-we ne | 2PL.O=meet PURP come=4.S track-POSS COP | I came to meet you. | Dal Corso ex. 21. The indefinite/fourth person =an serves as the narrative first person; ek=an ruwe ne presents the speaker's own arrival as an established fact, the reasoning-based direct value (DIR.RSN) in Dal Corso's terms. | dalcorso2018:24 | CHI | Ito Oda, Chitose; Dal Corso siglum BUG:126

Meaning: fact from knowledge, not raw perception

Four framings of ruwe ne compete, and they are best read as describing
one form from different angles. The oldest, the mood tradition, classed
ruwe as the 確説法 / 事実法
‘assertive / fact mood’, with Chiri deriving it diachronically from ‘there is a trace of X’ to
‘there is the fact of X’ kindaichi1936 ; hirosawa2026 65–68 . The
evidential framing, now standard, retains a source semantics: Nakagawa defines
ruwe ne as marking that the speaker recognizes the clause content
as fact nakagawa2024 392 , and Dal Corso treats it as a direct
evidential of reasoning or knowledge (glossed dir.rsn /
dir.knw ), the highest-reliability direct source. This reliability is a
matter of source, not of epistemic modality: it names the psychophysical or cognitive channel
of acquisition and says nothing about the speaker's degree of certainty, and the original
stimulus is typically backgrounded once the information has been assimilated into personal
knowledge dalcorso2018 305, 327, 335, 339 .

Satō's question data refine what that channel is. Among the four nominalizers,
ruwe applies where the relevant content is an abstract state of affairs,
recognized only indirectly through the exercise of understanding — the domain left once
hawe takes an utterance, siri a visible ongoing
scene, and humi a sensation sato2008 134–135 . He further
notes that the very fact that these nominalizers stand in the possessed form
presupposes an antecedent — a possessed noun needs something to belong to — so the form
grammatically encodes that the speaker already has the relevant situation in hand
sato2008 135 . The diagnostic case is a predicate whose truth no sense can
deliver:

tan cise e=uni ne ru-we | this house 2SG.POSS=home COP track-POSS | Is this house yours? | Whether a house ‘is one's own’ is an abstract relation, not a perceptible state; hence ruwe, not the visual siri. The bare clause-final ruwe is the polar-interrogative reduction discussed in the section on frames. | sato2008:131, 135 | CHI

The fourth framing is Hirosawa's, who relocates ruwe ne into
information structure. He argues it marks discourse- new information much as Japanese
のだ does, and more generally declares the content to lie inside the
speaker's territory of information (情報のなわ張り); since ruwe
references processed knowledge (知識), this reconciles its two
apparently opposite uses — introducing brand-new information and ‘summing up’ (前方統括)
information already developed in the discourse hirosawa2026 75–81, 89 . The
self-identification below involves no inference and no perceptual source at all; the sea-god
simply supplies his identity to the protagonists as new information:

atuy kor kamuy a=ne hine an=an ru-we ne | sea have god 4.A=COP CONJ exist=4.S track-POSS COP | I am the god who holds the sea. | Hirosawa ex. 16. The fourth-person transitive prefix a= marks the copula and the suffix =an the existential; a self-identification packaging discourse-new information for the addressee. | hirosawa2026:77 | SAR | Wateke, Tamura audio materials 05-02 ‘Folktale 1’: 6

The same mechanism lets a narrator assert events of the story world that she could not have
witnessed, presenting them under ruwe ne as traditional, shared
knowledge — the use that makes it the default predicate of the narrative frame-text
dalcorso2018 331 ; hirosawa2026 75 :

pananpe an penanpe an hine sir-an pe ne ru-we ne hike | Pananpe exist Penanpe exist CONJ appearance-exist thing COP track-POSS COP although | There were Panampe and Penampe, and … | Hirosawa ex. 13; the storyteller's frame-text presents a story-world state of affairs under ruwe ne as established narrative fact. | hirosawa2026:75 | SAR | Wateke, Tamura audio materials 05-02 ‘Folktale 1’: 4

These accounts are not interchangeable, and at one point they genuinely conflict: Hirosawa
builds his analysis on territory-of-information, whereas Dal Corso explicitly argues that the
territory-of-information theory is inapplicable to Hokkaido direct evidentiality
dalcorso2018 335–337 ; hirosawa2026 89 ‹contested›. The
synthesis adopted here is that ruwe ne grammatically encodes
epistemic incorporation : the clause is predicated as a possessed trace
( ru-we , ‘the trace of —’) whose possessor is the described event, so
that the proposition is offered as content already taken into the speaker's knowledge and
thereby made assertable. This single device derives both Dal Corso's
observation that the stimulus is backgrounded once information enters personal knowledge and
Hirosawa's that the form marks new information and claims it for the speaker's territory:
incorporation is exactly what lets a freshly perceived, inferred, remembered, or just-narrated
state of affairs be presented as a fact the speaker now owns. The possessed morphology — which,
as Satō notes, presupposes an antecedent — is the formal anchor of this account, and it
explains why the older ‘fact mood’ label, the modern source label, and the discourse-new label
each capture a true facet of the same form ‹speculative›.

The ruwe ne / siri ne puzzle

The hardest boundary in the system is between ruwe ne and
siri ne , because both can rest on what the speaker sees. Dal Corso states
plainly that a sense-channel hierarchy does not predict the split: if both are ‘visual’, it
fails to say why Ainu so often chooses the trace-form where sight is available, and he leaves
the question open, suspecting a driver beyond elicitation dalcorso2018 311–312, 335–336 . Hirosawa's response is to separate raw sight from processed knowledge —
siri profiles the visible situation, ruwe the
knowledge the situation yields — but he offers this as an explicit hypothesis (仮説), not a
settled result hirosawa2026 81, 89 ‹contested›.

The double-evidential distribution gives a positive argument that resolves part of the puzzle.
When two evidentials stack, the indirect visual siri an is reinforced
specifically by ruwe ne , and not by siri ne
dalcorso2018 326–327, 331–333 . Were ruwe and
siri two co-equal visual channels this would be unmotivated; it follows
naturally if ruwe is not a fourth sense parallel to sight, sound, and
speech, but a higher knowledge-from-trace operator that ratifies the output of another
evidential as established fact. On this reading Dal Corso's unresolved ‘why
ruwe and not siri when sight is available’
dalcorso2018 311–312 and Takahashi's Tokachi finding that individual-level —
cognitively stable — properties take ru while transient scenes take
sir takahashi2013 131–133 become two faces of one
contrast: siri profiles the accessible appearance, and
ruwe the cognized fact extracted from it ‹speculative›. The
boundary with hawe is similarly non-rigid — Hirosawa cites near-identical
sentences alternating hawe ne and ruwe ne , which
fits a form that can absorb information of any original channel once it has become knowledge
hirosawa2026 81 .

Clause-final frames and illocution

ruwe heads a small family of sentence-final frames that vary the
following predicate and particle. Nakagawa arranges them into a paradigm shared by all four
formal nouns: NMLZ + ne declarative,
NMLZ + an content question (with a question word),
bare NMLZ polar question and exclamation, and
NMLZ + un affirmative answer nakagawa2024 392–396 . The
bare polar reduction is an instance of insubordination — a once-dependent nominalized clause
used as a main clause — analysed for the Tokachi cognate by takahashi2013 132 . These interrogative uses belong with the wider interrogative system in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles , and the existential frame with
existential-locational-an-oka the existential an .

frame
value
note

ruwe ne
declarative, recognized fact
formal noun + copula; may take a , nankor , and final particles

ruwe an?
content question
existential frame with a question word; also expanded ruwe an hi an?

ruwe?
polar question / exclamation
reduced, insubordinated frame; Saru reinforced ruwe he an?

ruwe un
affirmative answer
un is a final particle, not the evidential itself

ruwe tapan
formal / ceremonial assertion
register-marked frame in Tamura's dictionary, not a separate category

ruwe somo ne
rhetorical / convictional
outer frame, distinct from inner-scope somo negation

The content question replaces the copula with the existential and is opened by a question word,
the formal noun staying in place:

mak an pe kusu e=moyre a ru-we an | how exist NMLZ because 2SG.S=be.late PFV track-POSS exist | Why were you late? | Dal Corso ex. 124. The wh-expression mak an pe kusu ‘for what reason’ opens the clause and the existential an replaces the copula; the perfective a closes the embedded predicate before ruwe. | dalcorso2018:143 | SAR | Wateke, Saru; Dal Corso siglum TMA: 12

ku=nu ru-we un | 1SG.A=hear track-POSS FIN | Yes, I heard it. | The final particle un forms the affirmative-answer frame while the formal noun is retained. | nakagawa2024:396 | HK | Nakagawa ex. 794

Negation belongs inside the evidential: the proposition is denied by negating the
verb before the formal noun (the pattern … somo ki ruwe ne ‘it is a fact
that [one does not …]’), which leaves ruwe ne itself outside the scope of
negation. The rhetorical ruwe somo ne is a separate outer frame, a
high-scope convictional construction distinct from this inner propositional negation
nakagawa2024 393 . The interaction of the evidential with the
sentence-final negation and TAM apparatus on a copular predicate is treated in
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation .

nep eci=e-sirkirap ka somo ki ru-we ne na | something 2PL.A=APPL-worry even NEG do track-POSS COP FIN | You will not be troubled by anything. | Dal Corso ex. 128. Negation sits inside the evidential: somo ki denies the verb sirkirap, while ruwe ne stays outside the scope of negation and predicates the whole as recognized fact. | dalcorso2018:145 | SAR | Kaizawa Tsurushino, Biratori; Dal Corso siglum KAY: 2-2,18

Scope over TAM and the finiteness of the embedded clause

The constituent before ruwe ne is a finite, clause-sized predication. It
carries its own person marking, number-sensitive verb choice
(singular ek vs. suppletive plural arki ‘come’,
so íne nispa ek ruwe ne ‘four men came’), aspect and perfective
auxiliaries, and negation; the evidential then scopes over that whole TAM-marked clause
nakagawa2024 393 . This is also why ruwe ne can close a
non-verbal predicate, as in the copular kamuy a=ne … ruwe ne example
above. Ordering confirms the layering: the evidential may be followed by aspectual and clausal
material ( a , nankor , na ,
wa , hine , kusu ), and it
sits above clause-internal aspect but below the clause-linkers, so
kusu ne ruwe ne is grammatical and *ruwe ne kusu ne
is not nakagawa2024 393 .

mat-kor hine oka=an ru-we ne a p | wife-have CONJ exist.PL=4.S track-POSS COP PFV NMLZ | he had taken a wife, and we went on living — and yet, | Dal Corso ex. 170 (excerpt). The perfective a follows ruwe ne in the post-evidential slot, and the nominalizer p carries the adversative ‘but’ that turns the narrative — confirming that aspect and clause-linking material attach above the evidential. | dalcorso2018:196 | SAR | Wateke, Saru; Dal Corso siglum TMA: 2

Dal Corso ties the choice of post-nominal predicate to perspective: the copula
ne expresses an internal (speaker-anchored) viewpoint and so hosts the
direct forms, while the existential and posture/action verbs an ,
as , and ki express an external viewpoint and host
the indirect forms dalcorso2018 313–315 . For ruwe the
paradigm is asymmetrical — in the southern dialects there is no indirect
*ru form — so ruwe ne functions as the direct,
personal-knowledge anchor of the whole system dalcorso2018 309, 326 . What the
accessible sources do not settle is the formal size of the nominalized clause: that it
is finite is clear from its TAM and person marking, but no read source provides a constituency
account that would decide between a finite nominalization, a nominalized predicate phrase, and a
construction-specific evidential projection ‹speculative›. The complementation side of the same
formal-noun strategy — ruwe and hi clauses under
perception and cognition verbs — is taken up in nominalized-complements-control-raising .

Predication type: the Tokachi constraint

Takahashi's Tokachi study adds a factor outside the reach of both the mood tradition and a pure
source typology. In Tokachi the visual nominalizer sir is restricted to
imperfective, stage-level (transient) predication: it presents a passing scene. For an
individual-level (permanent) property — even one known by sight — Tokachi instead uses
ru , which is unmarked for information type. Of the four, only
sir carries this restriction; hum ,
haw , and ru work at both levels
takahashi2013 131–133 . The constraint surfaces on the nominalizer itself, which
Takahashi parallels with the ban on individual-level complements of English perception verbs.

taan imo siwnin ru an | this potato be.green track exist | This potato is green. | In Tokachi the bare concept form ru (not the possessed ruwe) closes the clause, and the existential an replaces the copula. An individual-level property takes ru even though it is visually accessible, because sir is barred from permanent properties. | takahashi2013:131–133 | TOK | Honbetsu; consultant Sawai Tomeno

For the present form this matters in two ways. First, the ru domain is
broader than ‘inference’ narrowly understood: it is the unmarked choice where
sir is blocked by predication type, which is consistent with the
knowledge-from-trace reading developed above. Second, it shows the Hokkaido system to be
organized partly by the ontology of the predicate — eventive, stage-level, individual-level —
alongside source of information; this does not overturn Dal Corso's reliability/accessibility
account but bounds its domain, marking where the grammar of predication independently
constrains the available evidential ‹consensus›.

Mirativity and double evidentiality

ruwe ne is often rendered ‘it turned out that …’ in surprising contexts,
but the surprise is not contributed by ruwe . Mirativity in Hokkaido Ainu
is carried mainly by the perfective auxiliary aan and by
anan ; ruwe ne then packages the resulting
proposition as fact hirosawa2026 90 . Dal Corso is explicit that Hokkaido shows
no evidence of the evidential paradigm itself systematically generating mirative meanings — so
mirativity may co-occur with ruwe ne but is not its value
dalcorso2018 336 ‹contested›. The system-level treatment of the
evidential-to-mirative interface, evidential stacking, and the cline to sentence-final particles
is given in mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

supuya at kor sir-an ru-we ne anan | smoke rise while appearance-exist track-POSS COP MIR | Smoke was rising, it turned out! | Dal Corso ex. 268: the indirect visual siri an is layered under ruwe ne, with the admirative tail anan glossed mirative here. The supporting form is systematically ruwe ne, never siri ne. | dalcorso2018:327 | HK | Nakagawa text, Dal Corso siglum NGF:176

Double evidentiality, more frequent in Hokkaido than Sakhalin, confirms the special standing of
ruwe ne argued for above. Two patterns recur: a character's indirect
source layered under the narrator's ruwe ne as shared knowledge
( … hawe as ruwe ne ), and ruwe ne reinforcing a
low-reliability direct form ( … hawe ne nankor ruwe ne )
dalcorso2018 331–333 . In both, ruwe ne is the member
that can ratify an already evidentially marked proposition as established fact, behaving as a
knowledge operator that scopes over the other evidentials.

Diachrony, dialect microvariation, and a Sakhalin contrast

The diachronic path is transparent and is stated already in the earliest grammars: the noun
ru ‘track, path’ > the possessed formal noun
ru-we ‘the trace of —’ > the clause-final evidential
[clause] ruwe ne , part of the larger cline by which
sir , hum , haw , and
ru all move from sensory nouns to nominalizers to evidentials
tamura1996 s.v. ruwe ; hirosawa2026 65 . Dal Corso stresses that
the result is only partially grammaticalized — the lexical source stays visible and the
choice of post-nominal predicate still matters — so grammaticalization here is a matter of
degree dalcorso2018 24–25 ; takahashi2018 111 .

The endpoint of that cline is reached unevenly across Hokkaido, and the differences are
morphosyntactic: a single semantic field realized by varied forms, which cautions against
writing one morphology for all of Hokkaido ‹contested›:

Saru, Chitose, Biratori — the southern core of Dal Corso's data — keep the
possessed forms ruwe / siri / hawe / humi and the eight-form
direct/indirect system dalcorso2018 304–337 .

Tokachi (and frequently Ishikari) close the clause with the bare concept
forms ru / sir / haw / hum instead of the possessed forms
nakagawa2024 110, 390 ; takahashi2013 129–131 .

Horobetsu grammaticalizes the possessed-form nominalizers a step further,
into adverbial-clause and conjunction-like markers — a ‘versatile nominalization’ use not
reported for well-described Saru takahashi2018 107–112 .

Asahikawa and Shiranuka have an inferential ru an ,
apparently in complementary distribution with the siri an of the
south — a genuine exception to the generalization that ru forms direct
evidentiality alone dalcorso2018 326 .

The Sakhalin reflex. The Sakhalin varieties split the trace-form where the
southern Hokkaido system has a single member. Beside the copular
ruwehe ne(e) they carry an existential ruwehe ʔan ,
and the two divide one inferential field by reliability: the existential encodes inference from a
less-processed visual stimulus, the copular form inference from a stimulus already worked through,
the more reliable of the pair dalcorso2018 281–284 . Southern Hokkaido has only
ruwe ne and admits no existential *ruwe an ; the
ru an of Asahikawa and Shiranuka is the one Hokkaido locus where the
trace-noun enters the existential frame, and Dal Corso reads it as the Hokkaido counterpart of
Sakhalin ruwehe ʔan dalcorso2018 326, 336 . The split sits
within a wider reorganization of nominalization in the Sakhalin variety
bugaeva2016 .

pon nay ohta i-huraye ruwe-he ne | be.small river LOC 4.O-wash track-POSS COP | She washed me in a small river. | Dal Corso ex. 224. The copular ruwehe ne(e) presents a fully processed, personally witnessed event — the more reliable counterpart of the existential ruwehe ʔan; the indefinite-person object i- here references the narrator. East Sakhalin keeps the possessed -he that the southern Hokkaido evidential drops (ruwe-he, not ru-we). | pilsudski1912; dalcorso2018:281 | SA | Piłsudski corpus; Dal Corso siglum PLA: 227

tan husko karauto an ruwe-he an manuy | this be.old box exist track-POSS exist REP | There was this old box. | there is a trace that this old box is there | Dal Corso ex. 226. The inferential is built on the existential an over a clause that itself ends in existential an; the literal ‘there is a trace of —’ stays transparent. Southern Hokkaido lacks this existential ruwehe ʔan and uses only the copular ruwe ne. | pilsudski1912; dalcorso2018:284 | SA | Piłsudski corpus; Dal Corso siglum PLA: 200

title: /s/ and the s ~ š Palatalization Alternation
summary: The allophonic [s]~[ʃ] alternation before and after /i/ and word-finally (Saru sisam vs šišam), its conditioning, and its dialect distribution.
part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: /s/ and the s ~ š Palatalization Alternation
Part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
Summary: The allophonic [s]~[ʃ] alternation before and after /i/ and word-finally (Saru sisam vs šišam), its conditioning, and its dialect distribution.

The single fricative and its positional variants

Hokkaido Ainu has a single fricative phoneme, /s/ (see consonant-inventory ).
Its phonetic realization depends on syllabic position. In onset function /s/ appears as a
laminal sibilant with a degree of palatalization that varies by speaker and vowel context.
In coda function the realization splits by the preceding vowel: after /i/ the coda /s/
surfaces as [ɕ], merging perceptually with the initial consonant of Japanese シ; after /a/,
/e/, /o/, and /u/ it surfaces as a partially palatalized laminal [sʲ]~[ʃ], intermediate
between Japanese ス and シ. A third context overrides the coda rule: immediately before the
glide /w/ at a following syllable or word boundary, the coda /s/ surfaces as a plain [s]
nakagawa2024 27–28 ; shiraishi2022 §3 .

Because /s/ is the only fricative in the Hokkaido Ainu phoneme inventory, the alternants
[sʲ], [ʃ], [ɕ], and [s] are all allophones of one underlying segment, never
contrastive with each other. The distribution does, however, produce perceptual near-mergers
that led early transcribers working within a Japanese kana frame to hear palatal colouring
in unexpected environments, and it creates systematic difficulty for Japanese-background
listeners distinguishing morphological structures that differ in syllabic affiliation rather
than in the consonant itself.

Onset realization

In onset position /s/ is realized as a laminal alveolar fricative. A community phonetics
survey characterizes it as [s̻] — with the blade of the tongue making the primary
constriction rather than the tip — and notes a perceptible difference from the
palato-alveolar [ɕ] of Japanese シ even when both are heard as "sh-like"
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹speculative›.

The palatalization tendency of onset /s/ in older recordings extends beyond the environment
of a following /i/. Community phonetic analysis of early twentieth-century audio reports
that the onset /s/ was palatalized in a wider range of vowel environments than is typical
of the better-documented mid-century speakers
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-05) ‹corpus-suggested›. Shiraishi
describes Ainu /s/ as "often palatalized"
shiraishi2022 §3 , citing Kindaichi (1931: 9) and Tamura (1988b: 13) for
the forms sini [ʃini] and as [aʃ] as
early printed attestations.

Early Japanese transcriptions of Ainu rendered the onset sa, su, se,
so syllables with the digraphs シャ, シュ, シェ, ショ rather than
サ, ス, セ, ソ , reflecting a palatal impression before non-high
vowels. Tamura (1996) states that in Saru "the fricative is /s/ alone, close to the
consonant of シ, but with individual variation; sa, se, so, su
vary between a サ-type and a シャ-type pronunciation, while si
is invariably シ" (reported via aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli, 2023-12-11;
shiraishi2022 §3 corroborates the general pattern) ‹speculative›.

Coda allophony: the /i/-conditioning

The realization of coda /s/ is conditioned by the vowel of the syllable nucleus it closes.
When that vowel is /i/, the coda /s/ surfaces as [ɕ]; when it is /a/, /e/, /o/, or /u/,
the coda surfaces as a partially palatalized [sʲ]~[ʃ]. The rule as stated in
nakagawa2024 27–28 and corroborated by
shiraishi2022 §3 can be formulated:

/s/ (coda) → [ɕ] / i_ ; → [sʲ]~[ʃ] / (a, e, o, u)_

The following table gives attested examples in word-citation (pause) form. All coda forms
are taken from nakagawa2024 27–28 ; the general rule applies equally to
coda /s/ after /u/, though no dedicated example is cited there.

Vowel before coda /s/
Phonetic realization
Example
Approximate gloss

/i_/
[ɕ]
cis [tʃiɕ]
'to cry'

/a_/
[sʲ]~[ʃ]
as [asʲ]~[aʃ]
'to stand'

/e_/
[sʲ]~[ʃ]
tures [tuɾesʲ]
'younger sister'

/o_/ or /u_/
[sʲ]~[ʃ]
—
(same rule applies)

The near-merger between coda /s/ after /i/ and onset /s/ before /i/ generates a
systematic perceptual difficulty. The pair tures 'younger sister'
— with coda /s/ in the syllable /res/ — and tures-i 'his/her
younger sister' — in which the same /s/ resyllabifies as the onset of the following
possessive suffix -i , giving the syllable /si/ [ɕi] — both
approach [tureɕ] at their right edge as heard by Japanese listeners, even though they
are structurally distinct nakagawa2024 27–28 . For Ainu speakers the
difference is in the syllabic affiliation of /s/ (coda vs. onset), recoverable from
morphological structure. Resyllabification at morpheme boundaries is treated in
syllable-template ; the morphophonology of the possessive suffix is taken
up in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

The onset /s/ before /i/ participates in the same [ɕ] realization. The reflexive prefix
si- reflexive surfaces as [ɕi], the same phonetic output as coda /s/
after /i/. The two environments converge on [ɕ] from different structural positions
(coda conditioned by preceding /i/, onset conditioned by following /i/) without forming a
phonemic /ɕ/ distinct from /s/.

Coda /s/ before /w/: the override constraint

At a juncture before the labio-velar glide /w/, the coda /s/ surfaces as plain [s] rather
than the [sʲ]~[ʃ] expected from the non-/i/ conditioning rule. Tamura (1996)
describes as 'stand' in isolation as
アㇱ ([aʃ]), but the same verb followed by the conjunctive
particle wa and then; conjunctive becomes アㇲワ
([aswa]), with the fricative shifting to a plain sibilant before the /w/ onset
aynucorporadiscord (aomidori, 2023-12-11, reporting Tamura 1996);
see also nakagawa2024 27 for the coda-/s/ allophony frame within which
this constraint operates ‹corpus-suggested›.

as wa | stand CONJ | standing (and then) | Kana transcription アㇲワ shows plain [s] before /w/, contrasting with アㇱ [aʃ] for the citation form as alone. Reported from Tamura (1996) dictionary grammar sketch via aomidori (2023-12-11). | aynucorporadiscord | SAR

The phonetic interpretation is straightforward: the labial co-articulation of /w/ suppresses
the palatal colouring that the coda /s/ otherwise carries. Whether this is best analysed as
anticipatory labial assimilation blocking palatalization, or as a context-sensitive
allophone rule that takes precedence over the general coda rule, is not resolved in the
sources consulted. The broader sandhi environment in which /s/ participates at word junctures —
including the /r/+/s/ cluster that produces progressive effects (see
coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi ) — is treated there.

Ainu [ɕ] and Japanese /ɕ/: a phonetic note

The [ɕ] that appears as the coda /s/ allophone after /i/ and as the onset /s/ allophone
before /i/ is phonetically distinct from the Japanese palato-alveolar [ɕ] of シ. Community
phonetic analysis characterizes the Ainu surface [ɕ] as having less high-frequency friction
than its Japanese counterpart, placed somewhat further back in the oral cavity — on the
boundary between Japanese [ɕ] and an English postalveolar [ʃ] — while never reaching a
full retroflexed or postalveolar [ʃ]
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-11) ‹speculative›. The analysis
characterizes it as a laminal sound, with the tongue blade rather than the tip engaged,
which distinguishes it from the Japanese [ɕ] even within the palato-alveolar region.

This distinction has no phonemic status: there is no /ɕ/ phoneme in Hokkaido Ainu separate
from /s/; the [ɕ] surface form is entirely predictable from the vowel context. The use in
earlier descriptions of /ʃ/ as a coda symbol (as in Shiraishi's notation
as [aʃ], and Tamura's representation of coda
-s after /i/ as シ) reflects the impressionistic transcription of
the fully palatalized allophone, not a phonemic /ʃ/ distinct from /s/
shiraishi2022 §3 .

Speaker and dialect variation

The degree of coda palatalization shows inter-speaker variation within and across dialects.
Analysis of NINJAL recordings identifies Hiraga Sadamo (平賀さだも, Saru dialect) as
producing "pure [ɕ]" at the coda and onset-before-/i/ position, while Mukawa dialect
recordings from the same archive frequently show a reduced variant [ɕ̱̥] — a
partially devoiced, laminal palato-alveolar — in the same environment
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-03-24) ‹speculative›. The Mukawa variant
suggests an interaction between coda /s/ palatalization and the vowel devoicing that
affects /i/ between voiceless consonants (treated in
vowel-inventory ): when /i/ devoices in the environment of the coda /s/,
the palatalization feature is retained on the fricative but the overall phonetic output
moves toward a devoiced palatal fricative [ç] or a devoiced [ɕ̥].

These inter-speaker differences have not been quantified from published acoustic
measurements in any directly-read source. The phonetic detail is graded accordingly.
The vowel devoicing pattern that creates the relevant environment in Mukawa is
independently attested in shiraishi2022 §2.1 and
nakagawa2024 28 .

Progressive palatalization in sirsak

A sporadic case of progressive palatalization has been observed in NINJAL corpus recordings
of the compound word sirsak
ninjal2003 . The underlying form /sir.sak/ has initial /si/ → [ɕi] by the
regular onset rule, followed by the coda /r/ of sir and then the
onset /s/ of sak . In the speech of 木村きみ (Kimura Kimi, Saru), the
coda /r/ after /i/ is palatalized ([ɾʲ]), and this palatalization carries forward
progressively onto the following /s/, shifting it from the expected laminal [s̻]
toward a laminal postalveolar [ᶘ], as in [ɕiɾʲᶘak˚]; an
alternative surface realization is [ɕiɾʲɕak˚]
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2026-05-11) ‹speculative›.

The speaker 小田イト (Oda Ito, also documented in Saru at the same NINJAL site) does not
show the same progressive shift: her realization is [ɕiɾʲis̻ak˚],
with an echo vowel after the /r/ coda (a property of coda /r/ discussed in
rhotic-r ) and a plain laminal [s̻] — not a palatalized
[ᶘ] or [ɕ] — as the onset before /a/
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2026-05-11) ‹speculative›.

The contrast between the two speakers illustrates two independent strategies for the
/ɾʲ.s/ juncture: progressive coarticulation of palatality from [ɾʲ] onto
the following /s/ (Kimura), versus insertion of an echo vowel that interposes a vocalic
buffer between the palatalized rhotic and the onset /s/ (Oda). The observation is
idiolect-specific in the available NINJAL data, has not been confirmed in other
compounds or other speakers in any read source, and the IPA transcription reflects
community analysis rather than published spectral measurement ‹speculative›. The
broader /r/+/s/ sandhi environment — where a coda /r/ interacts with a following /s/
onset — is treated in coda-r-assimilation-sonorant-sandhi .

title: The Sakehe Refrain, Verse Meter, and the Structure of Sung Verse
summary: The sakehe burden and the metrical/structural organization of sung genres as performance grammar: refrain types and placement, verse-line segmentation and syllable/mora-count meter, and melodic vs linguistic pitch (proto-accent reconstruction in Part XX).
part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Sakehe Refrain, Verse Meter, and the Structure of Sung Verse
Part: Part XIX — Discourse and the Grammar of Oral Literature
Summary: The sakehe burden and the metrical/structural organization of sung genres as performance grammar: refrain types and placement, verse-line segmentation and syllable/mora-count meter, and melodic vs linguistic pitch (proto-accent reconstruction in Part XX).

The sung/recited/spoken distinction

Melody presence is the most grammar-relevant parameter dividing Hokkaido Ainu oral genres.
Three verse-and-narrative traditions each carry a distinct constellation of features — melody,
the sakehe refrain (in sung verse) refrain, and rhythm-tapping — that
co-condition the grammar of the text sato2008 259 ;
endo2022 §2.4 .

genre (Ainu)
melody
sakehe refrain
rhythm-tapping ( rep / repni )
length
canonical narrator

heroic epic yúkar
yes (narrator's own tune, variable)
no (but see opening eyororpe discussed below)
yes
long (hours to multi-night)
men (traditionally)

divine epic kamuy yukar
yes ( fixed per story )
yes
no
short (minutes to ~1 hr)
women (and men)

prose-style epic irupaye
no
no
no
long
women

prose tale uwepeker
no
no
no
medium
women (and men)

(After sato2008 259 , table of Chitose oral-literature genres and features.
Genre taxonomy, regional dialect-term variation, and the oyna
nomenclature debate are covered in oral-literature-genre-taxonomy .)

The sung/prose split is not merely a performance fact. Sung verse genres deploy the
poetic register ( atomte itak , 'ornamented language') with its
distinct morphological and lexical features, while prose ( yayan itak ,
'ordinary language') does not — even when the prose narrator's voice rises and falls with
a narrative lilt endo2022 §3 ; sato2008 263–264 .
The elevated register is described in poetic-archaic-elevated-register ; its
TAM and evidential patterning by genre in narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .
Folktale intonation — one-directional, low-variation melody, with upper-Saru narration more
monotone than lower-Saru — sits apart from the strict melodic structure of sung verse
( aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli 2024, asserted , after
tamura1996 ).

The verb sakehawki sing/recite a yukar (E-Iburi) captures the sung
dimension lexically; it appears in a Abuta yukar passage meaning 'about to sing a yukar'
( sakehawki anki ), confirming that for at least the eastern Iburi
dialects, sung performance was lexically distinguished from prose narration
sato2009yukar §2.2 .

The sakehe: morphology and the fixed-melody criterion

The term sakehe refrain; the sake of — is morphologically the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form of sa
(Japanese Wiktionary: "sa の所属形"). Tamura's Saru dictionary records a prosodic
distinction: the genre-refrain sense carries initial accent ( sákehe )
while the common-noun sense 'sake (the beverage)' is unaccented, creating near-homophones
that occasionally coincide within a single verse line (see the hancikiki
example below) tamura1996 s.v. sákehe, sakehe .
The corpus registers fifteen tokens of the form (rank 4,602 among 45,225 distinct tokens),
spanning both senses; the refrain sense is attested in narrator metalinguistic commentary
(see example below) ‹corpus-suggested›.

In kamuy yukar, the sakehe is a fixed refrain per story , inserted into
the narration — every line or every few lines depending on the text — and held constant
throughout one performance of that story endo2022 §2.4.2 ;
sato2008 259 . The melody to which the text is sung is equally fixed per
story (contrast yukar, where each narrator deploys a personal tune that may change
mid-performance; see the melody section below). Together, the fixed melody and the fixed
sakehe define the formal identity of a kamuy yukar text: a reciter who knows a story
knows both its sakehe and its melody as an inseparable package endo2022 §2.4.2 .

A canonical instance of sakehe-every-line placement comes from a marlin-tuna kamuy yukar
narrated by Nabezawa Nepuki (鍋沢ネブキ) of Biratori. The sakehe tusunapanu
precedes every narrative word-group, producing a strict alternation of refrain and narrative
that generates the rhythmic frame of the performance
( endo2022 §2.4.2 , reporting kayano1996 s.v. sakehe ; narrator 鍋沢ネブキ, Biratori):

refrain
narrative segment
refrain
narrative segment
