TUSUNAPANU
Okikurmi
TUSUNAPANU
Samayunkur

TUSUNAPANU
u-tura hine
TUSUNAPANU
repa kusu arki

TUSUNAPANU
ki hi kusu
TUSUNAPANU
etok-oho ta

('Okikurmi and Samayunkur went offshore to fish, so… before that… ahead of [them]…'; the
sakehe in capitals precedes every narrative phrase.)

Attested sakehe from the aligned Hokkaido corpus and oral-literature archives show the
range from onomatopoeic to opaque. The following is the thunder-god's sakehe from a Biratori
kamuy yukar, with the narrator's own gloss preserved:

hum pakpak | sound pattering | hum-pakpak (the sakehe of the thunder-god) | The narrator's own metalinguistic comment (biratori/001/004#170): humihiruy だわ。sakehe. 'it's a loud sound — that's the sakehe.' Onomatopoeia of thunder's cracking/pattering. | biratori1969 | SAR | Kaizawa Turushino 貝澤とぅるしの, Biratori; kamuy yukar 'フㇺ パㇰパㇰ（雷の神が自叙する神謡）'

The next example, from a sparrow-feast kamuy yukar, illustrates a structural property of the
Saru verse line: the refrain hancikiki immediately precedes a
narrative line whose first word is the common noun sakehe 'sake
(millet wine)', creating a momentary homophony between the genre-term and the beverage:

hancikiki sakehe ci=kar | (refrain) sake 1PL.EXCL.A=make | [HANCIKIKI] — we made sake | The sakehe hancikiki precedes a narrative line that opens with the homophonous common noun sakehe 'sake (beverage)'. The refrain and the narrative vocabulary coincide accidentally. | biratori1969 | SAR | Nabezawa Nepuki 鍋澤ねぷき, Biratori; kamuy yukar 'ハンチキキ（スズメの酒盛り）'

The story-type and sakehe are demonstrably not in fixed correspondence: the same narrative
situation (marlin-tuna confrontation) carries sakehe tusunapanu
in Saru (Biratori), tussay turi tussay matu in Horobetsu, and
nissō in Chitose (where the protagonist is swapped for the fish
okina ) endo2022 §2.4.2 . Kayano's Saru dictionary
preserves a long multi-word sakehe ( tunoyake renoyake kutunke kamuyke
kamuy cikappo hum hum hum ) as one illustration of the form's capacity for extended,
rhythmically organized strings kayano1996 s.v. sakehe .

Melody: fixed per story in kamuy yukar, personal in yukar

The melodic contrast between kamuy yukar and yukar is the clearest formal divide in the genre
system. A kamuy yukar text comes with a melody that is bound to that story and is transmitted
alongside the words and the sakehe; a performer who learns the story learns all three as an
integrated unit endo2022 §2.4.2 . Prayer ( kamuy nomi )
also has a melodic delivery, but its melody is improvised and personal — different each time
and not transmitted with the words — a property Nakagawa (via endo2022 §2.3 )
uses to distinguish prayer from the fixed-word, fixed-melody incantation.

Yukar stands apart: each narrator constructs or inherits his own tune for a borrowed story,
and the melody is liable to change mid-performance endo2022 §2.4.3 . The
absence of a sakehe in yukar corresponds to this melodic instability: where the kamuy yukar
uses the repeating sakehe to bind melody, narrative line, and rhythmic unit together, the
yukar uses rhythm-tapping ( rep keep time / repni rhythm-stick
— 'the act of keeping time', confirmed in the Mukawa-dialect dictionary as 拍子棒
sato2008 259 ) to provide rhythmic scaffolding without a textual refrain.
Listeners add a sung chorus ( hetce chorus response ; corpus count 13) during
yukar performance, a further rhythmic layer absent from kamuy yukar
sato2008 259 .

Satō notes that an opening formula — the eyororpe opening formula (yukar) —
may recur at the start of a yukar and could represent a vestige of a refrain that yukar
once possessed sato2008 259 ‹speculative›. The inference is Satō's own and
rests on structural analogy with kamuy yukar rather than on textual attestation.

Function of the sakehe: the protagonist-index debate

The functional motivation of the sakehe has generated competing accounts. Nakagawa proposes
that it originally indicated the protagonist : the refrain was an
identifying tag that signalled whose voice or experience was being narrated
endo2022 §2.4.2 , reporting Nakagawa 2001b: 7 ‹contested›. The
counter-evidence is substantial: the same story-type does not consistently carry the same
sakehe across dialects (the marlin-tuna case above), and many sakehe have no recoverable
semantic or deictic link to their protagonist. The thunder-god's hum
pakpak can be read as onomatopoeia of thunder's sound (the narrator's own gloss
supports this), but that is iconic, not indexical, and it is not generalizable to the
full sakehe inventory endo2022 §2.4.2 .

The community discussion archives one live genre-boundary case that bears on the sakehe's
defining role. A kamuy yukar narrated by Oda Suteno (織田ステノ, 'Lazybones Child') carries
a long sakehe ( manayta sanke tar panke tar kocupu ) but an
unusually short narrative body, and its closing move — the protagonist self-identifying as
a human boy ( sine wen toranne hekaci , 'a single weak miserable
child') — formally resembles a yayeisoytak (self-narration) rather
than a divine-epic conclusion ( aynucorporadiscord , gengojiro 2025-12-12,
proposed ). This text, with melody and a long sakehe but a
human-protagonist resolution, resists placement in either kamuy yukar or yukar, illustrating
that the sakehe alone does not uniquely define genre membership ‹contested›.

One additional philological datum: the title-line AEKIRUSHI in Chiri
Yukie's chiri1923 has been proposed to parse as a=e-kir-usi
'the place one has memory of' ( aynucorporadiscord , 0penke 2024-03-19,
proposed , after 片山龍峯 2003) rather than deriving from
ekiru 'turn back'. If correct, the title-line encodes the hero's
logophoric self-location, consistent with the divine-epic pattern of first-person
self-presentation ‹contested›.

Verse metre: two analytical systems

The metrical organization of HA verse has been described under two competing frameworks.
The long-standing analysis treats verse as syllable-count oriented , with
five syllables per line as the basic unit. Okuda's more recent work identifies a second
system, accent oriented , in which the first pitch-accent falls in a fixed
rhythmic position regardless of line length. The two frameworks are not simply rival
accounts of the same data: Okuda argues they are genre-differentiated and co-active
within a single performer's repertoire ‹contested›
okuda2022 §2, §3 .

Syllable-count orientation

The syllable-count description goes back to Kindaichi (1931: 165, via
okuda2022 §2.1 ) and was adopted by Tamura (1973: 56, via
okuda2022 §2.1 ): the basic line unit is five
syllables , with four to seven as the attested range. The five-syllable
target is met through a set of compositional techniques catalogued by Tamura and
Satō sato2008 264 ; okuda2022 §2.1 :

use of formulaic verse phrases already calibrated to syllable count;

choice between synonyms for their syllable count;

insertion or omission of final particles;

insertion of the dummy verb ki do (light verb) between a verb and its
following element;

use of the uncontracted ku- person prefix rather than its
reduced form;

prefixing an initial u to a line when reciting;

splitting a long phrase across two five-syllable lines;

not eliding vowels that would contract in everyday speech;

not pronouncing the line-final syllable (a performance strategy, not a morphological
rule).

These techniques confirm that the syllable target is a compositional constraint on the
verse line rather than a property of the grammar at large. They are the mechanisms through
which the sung verse actively shapes morphosyntax: word choice, prefix realization, and
particle use are all subordinated to the five-syllable frame. This is the most direct
route by which genre conditions grammatical form in Hokkaido Ainu.

Accent-orientation metre

Okuda's comparative analysis of divine epics and lyric songs identifies a second system
that he terms accent-orientation (アクセント指向): the first
pitch-accent of the line falls in the first disyllabic foot, regardless of how many
syllables the line contains okuda2022 §3 . Under this system, a
four-syllable line is metrically well-formed as long as the accent is initial; the
syllable-count system, by contrast, disfavours four-syllable lines.

The scansion evidence comes from divine epics recorded by narrators in Monbetsu and
Chitose. In the divine epic Amamecikappo narrated by Hatozawa
Wateke (幌別/Monbetsu; text in Tamura 2001, via okuda2022 §3.3 ), of
90 non-refrain lines, 14 are four-syllable lines; of these 14, twelve carry initial
accent on the first syllable, matching the accent-orientation prediction.
Representative lines (● = first accented syllable; ○ = unaccented):

line
syllables
scansion
gloss

si-né-a-mam-pus
5
○●○○○
'one ear of grain'

ci-tá-ta-ta-ta
5
○●○○○
'I crushed and crushed' (iterative)

kí-wa-tap-ne
4
●○○○
'and then'

c-é-a-hun-ke
4
●○○○
'I invited (it) into the house'

(After okuda2022 §3.3 , Hatozawa Wateke, Biratori/Monbetsu; primary text
Tamura 2001.)

In Chitose divine epics, the pattern holds with similar consistency. The text
Wawo kamuy (Shirasawa Nabe; 226 lines) has 62 four-syllable
lines, of which 56 carry initial accent; Apehuci kamuy
(same narrator; 220 lines) has 79 four-syllable lines, of which 71 do
okuda2022 §4 . The same reciters' lyric songs ( yaysama )
are overwhelmingly five-syllable (e.g., 33 of 34 lines in Hatozawa Wateke's lyric sample),
confirming that the same performer switches between systems by genre
okuda2022 §3.2 .

Genre distribution and the debate

Okuda maps the two systems onto genres and musical rhythms
okuda2022 §3, §5.1 :

metre
primary genres
four-syllable lines
theoretical type

syllable-count orientation
lyric song ( yaysama ), prayer ( inonno itak )
disfavoured (<5% attested)
pure-syllabic (Lotz type A)

accent orientation
divine epic ( kamuy yukar ), heroic epic ( yukar ; both systems)
admitted (15–30%); accent obligatorily initial
accentual-patterning (Lotz type B2)

Okuda's claim that a single performance tradition deploys two such metrically distinct
systems is, on his own account, without parallel in the reported typological literature
okuda2022 §5.1 ‹contested›. The traditional syllable-count description
(Kindaichi, Tamura) remains well evidenced for lyric and prayer; the accent-orientation
analysis adds explanatory coverage for the divine and heroic epics, where four-syllable
lines with initial accent resist treatment under a pure syllable-count rule. The two
analyses are therefore complementary across genres rather than mutually exclusive
‹contested›.

A further hypothesis, explicit in Okuda and flagged as conjectural, relates the
syllable-count system to Japanese 7-5 mora metre: the contact with Japanese literary
tradition could have reinforced or introduced the strict syllable target
okuda2022 §5.2 ‹speculative›. No positive evidence is offered and the
hypothesis rests on structural analogy alone.

Alliteration and rhyme

Philippi's influential judgment (1979, via okuda2022 §2.2 ) was that rhyme
in Ainu poetry is 'quite accidental' and alliteration 'sporadic' — not prosodically
functional as in Mongolic or Tungusic traditions. Okuda formalizes the reasoning: given
five vowels and roughly twelve onset consonants, chance repetition in a corpus of five-syllable
lines is high, and a sequence counts as technique only when it demonstrably exceeds chance
and is not forced by morphological reduplication, which is pervasive in Ainu
okuda2022 §2.2 .

A counter-position has been raised in the community literature. Tangiku (2020, 北大言語
アーカイヴ報告書) argues that an 18th-century Ainu yukar — ルウェサニウンクㇽ
— uses deliberate head-rhyme, end-rhyme, and imperfect rhyme as compositional devices
( aynucorporadiscord , nukopoli 2023-12-13, asserted ,
after Tangiku 2020). If the Tangiku analysis survives scrutiny, it would demonstrate at
least one historical period or textual tradition in which rhyme was a conscious structural
choice rather than an artefact of phonological probability ‹contested›. The Tangiku source
has not been consulted directly for this grammar; the claim is flagged as needing verification
against the primary text.

Fourth-person narration in sung verse

Both yukar and kamuy yukar are narrated in the first person from the protagonist's
viewpoint. The grammatical marker of this 'first person' is, in Saru and Chitose, the
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm fourth-person prefix a=
and suffix =an , not the everyday first person ku=
sato2008 259–261 ; endo2022 §2.4 . The choice of the
fourth-person forms is grammatically productive: in elegant language, =an
combines with singular verbs (e.g., ek-an 'I come.SG'), a
combination that everyday Saru disallows ( *ek-an is ill-formed
there but arki-an 'we come.PL' is fine)
sato2008 263 . This number-agreement override is a diagnostic of the
elegant register in sung verse — see poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

The Iburi dialect tradition, canonically represented by Chiri Yukie's
chiri1923 , uses instead the exclusive first-person plural ci=
/ =as as a singular hero-narrator marker, consistently throughout
the collection sato2008 261 ; sato2004 . The regional split
is systematic: a= / =an in Saru and Chitose;
ci= / =as in Iburi (Horobetsu and related
traditions) — with Chitose showing an intermediate pattern where ci=
/ =as may appear in the opening lines before reverting to
a= / =an sato2008 261 .

The motivation for the non-everyday person-marking in oral narrative is discussed under two
competing accounts: the shamaness-oracle origin theory (standard), in which the verse is
framed as spirit-speech, and Satō's 'indirection principle' ( 間接性の原理 ),
in which the god's first-person speech inside a dream required indirectness to soften the
taboo on direct divine address sato2008 259–261 ‹contested›. The
logophoric and direct-speech dimensions of the fourth person as used in narration are
treated in logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse and
fourth-person-honorific ; reference tracking across verse episodes in
reference-tracking ; the full paradigm in person-marking-architecture .

One grammatically significant consequence of sung-verse performance is quotative density.
Because narration is cast as direct speech and closes with a self-identification formula
( …sekor … isoytak 'so X told the story'), the quotative particle
sekor quotative with speech verbs ( ye say ,
hawki say/recite , isoytak tell a story ) clusters heavily
in verse text-closings. The evidential tail hawe ne / hawe as
(< haw voice + concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form )
carries much of the reportative marking in these contexts
endo2022 §2.4 ; the broader evidential system is treated in
evidential-schema-overview and ruwe-ne-inferential .

title: Sakhalin and Kuril Ainu: The External Comparison
summary: Systematic contrast of Hokkaido with Sakhalin (Enciw) Ainu and the fragmentary Kuril record, framing the dialect-vs-language question.
part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Sakhalin and Kuril Ainu: The External Comparison
Part: Part XX — Diachrony, Reconstruction, and Dialectology
Summary: Systematic contrast of Hokkaido with Sakhalin (Enciw) Ainu and the fragmentary Kuril record, framing the dialect-vs-language question.

Ainuic: three varieties, two branches

Ainu has no demonstrated genetic relatives and is treated as a language isolate
janhunen2022 §2 ; the failed macro-comparisons are surveyed in
genetic-position-macro-comparison . Internally the language forms a small
family — sometimes labelled Ainuic — of three entities: Hokkaido (HA), Sakhalin, and
the Northern Kuril varieties janhunen2022 §2 . The tripartition was
formalised on geographic grounds in the early twentieth century and appeared to receive
objective support from the lexicostatistic studies of hattorichiri1960 and
asai1974 . ono2020 demonstrates with spectral-clustering
methods, however, that the three-way "Major Division" carries no statistically robust
support: the cleaner two-way structure is Hokkaido (including the Kuril varieties) versus
Sakhalin. The internal Hokkaido classification and the dialectometry underlying that
finding are treated in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry ;
the boundary-dialect methodology is refined further by fukazawaono2024 .

The branching order follows the archaeological record of the Satsumon culture's northward
expansion from Honshu: the language reached Hokkaido from around the seventh century CE,
southern Sakhalin around the thirteenth century, and the Northern Kuriles around the
fifteenth to sixteenth century janhunen2022 §3a–3b . The Sakhalin branch
diverged from Common Ainu approximately 800 years ago; the Kuril varieties diverged later
and cluster more closely with northeast Hokkaido in the lexicostatistic data
alonso2022 §4 ; janhunen2022 §3b . Both the Sakhalin and
Kuril varieties ceased to be community languages during the twentieth century. The primary
Sakhalin documentation consists of the grammar and texts of murasaki1979 and
the folkloric corpus of pilsudski1912 ; the Kuril varieties are known only from
questionnaire records assembled by eighteenth- and nineteenth-century travellers. Within
Hokkaido, the dialect geographically and lexically closest to Sakhalin is Sōya, at
the northern tip of the island hattorichiri1960 .

The prosodic correspondence: length and pitch

The Sakhalin varieties have a vowel-length contrast on the five vowels
a e i o u , with long vowels occurring only in open stem syllables;
Hokkaido has a pitch-accent system and no distinctive vowel length. Hattori (1967) first
established the correspondence: a Sakhalin long vowel corresponds to a Hokkaido high
pitch, and a Sakhalin short vowel in a closed-syllable stem likewise corresponds to
Hokkaido high pitch in the same environments
alonso2022 §2.3 ; tangiku2022 §2.1 . The pitch-accent
rules are described in pitch-accent-placement-rule ; the diachronic account of
the length–pitch relationship is the subject of
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex .

Prosodic correspondences: Hokkaido high pitch versus Sakhalin long vowel (selected cognates)

Hokkaido (Saru)
Sakhalin (Raiciska)
gloss
source

húre
huure
red
alonso2022 §2.3

sísam
siisam
Japanese (person)
alonso2022 §2.3

hése
heese
breathe
alonso2022 §2.3

nína (Nairo niina )
niina
tree, wood
tangiku2022 §2.1

Three competing reconstructions of Proto-Ainu prosody account for this correspondence.
(a) Hattori (1967) and De Boer (2010) held that Proto-Ainu had vowel length alone and
Hokkaido developed pitch accent through contact with Japanese. (b) vovin1993
and itabashi2001 argued that Proto-Ainu had both length and pitch, with the
Sakhalin branch losing accent under Amur-Sakhalin contact. (c) Satō (2015, 2016) proposed
Proto-Ainu had pitch and no length, the Sakhalin vowel length being a phonetically
conditioned open-syllable phenomenon. Alonso de la Fuente endorses view (c) in the body
of his chapter but (a) in the conclusion, leaving the question unresolved
alonso2022 §2.3–2.4 ‹contested›. Community discussion (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2023; aynucorporadiscord ) arrives at the same impasse,
recognising both the Hattori and the Satō positions as defensible.

Final-consonant debuccalization in Sakhalin

The phonological signature of the Sakhalin varieties relative to Hokkaido is systematic
debuccalization of coda obstruents. Where Hokkaido has a word-final or syllable-final
p , t , or k , the
core Sakhalin varieties produce h ; final
m becomes n ; and final
r receives a following vowel, keeping the syllable open
tangiku2022 §2.3 ; alonso2022 §1 . A secondary rule
applies within Sakhalin: the h produced by debuccalization becomes
s following i
( h > s / i__ ), yielding alternants such as
sis for HA sik 'eye'. Alonso de la Fuente
uses this set of alternations as his showcase for what internal reconstruction can
accomplish for a language isolate without recourse to the comparative method
alonso2022 §1 . The correspondences are set out in Table 2; the coda
consonants of the Hokkaido system are described in consonant-inventory .

Final-consonant correspondences: Hokkaido versus Sakhalin (Raiciska), with possessed form

Hokkaido (Saru) independent
Sakhalin (Raiciska) independent
Sakhalin possessed (affiliative)
gloss

cep
ceh
cep-ihi
fish

cup
cuh
cup-ihi
sun, moon

tek
teh
tek-ehe
hand

sik
sis
sik-ihi
eye

mat
mah
mat-ihi
woman, wife

kor
koh
—
have

The Hokkaido forms in the first column are corpus-attested throughout; the constructed
example below places cep in a transitive clause to show the
coda -p in context. The corresponding Sakhalin Raiciska
form ceh is the regular debuccalization reflex
alonso2022 Table 18 .

cep ku=e | fish 1SG.A=eat | I ate fish. | The Saru coda -p in cep 'fish' (PA *ciʔep, Alonso de la Fuente 2022 Table 18) corresponds to Sakhalin Raiciska ceh. The Horobetsu dialect retains the Common Ainu form ciʔep with the glottal break. | SAR

Community corroboration: the kor > koh change and the general
pattern of coda-consonant debuccalization in Sakhalin are noted, with examples, in
aynu-corpora Discord discussion (nukopoli 2024-12-19, aldehyde8908 2025-04-22;
aynucorporadiscord ), consistent with tangiku2022 §2.3 .

The debuccalization applies across the Sakhalin varieties with notable subdialect
variation. The eastern coast varieties Taraika and Tarantomari preserve the coda stops
p/t/k like Hokkaido; the Esutoru subdialect distinguishes
-is from -ih
tangiku2022 §2.3 . The phonological status of SA coda
h itself is disputed: Alonso de la Fuente treats it as a velar
fricative /x/ (with realisations [ax], [iɕ], [uf]), while Tangiku and community members
analyse it as a laryngeal breath h
alonso2022 §1 ; tangiku2022 §2.3 ‹contested›.

The affiliative and the underlying base consonant

The concept-form-affiliative-form possessed (affiliative) form of
Sakhalin nouns reveals the base consonant that debuccalization masks in the independent
form. The affiliative suffix -ihi / -ehe attaches directly to
the underlying stem: the noun whose independent form is
sis 'eye' (< * sih , with
h > s before i ) surfaces in the
possessed form as sik-ihi — the base /k/ restored — not as the
unattested * sis-ihi . The underlying consonant is thus directly
recoverable from morphological alternation even where phonology has eliminated it from
the independent form tangiku2022 §2.3, §5 ; alonso2022 §1 .
The Hokkaido Saru counterpart is identical: sik-ihi .

sik-ihi | eye-POSS | the eye (of —) | Sakhalin Raiciska affiliative (possessed) form. The independent SA form is sis (debuccalised from *sik, then h becomes s before i), but the affiliative suffix -ihi attaches to the underlying stem /sik-/, restoring the base /k/. Demonstrates that the debuccalisation is a surface phonological process leaving the lexical representation intact. | tangiku2022 | SA

Sakhalin uses only the long affiliative form — there is no short/long alternation of
the kind found in Hokkaido tangiku2022 §5 . The affiliative insert is
transparently the suffix -hi 'thing, trace' attached to the base:
san+hi = sanihi , makan+hi = makanihi
tangiku2022 §5 — a pathway corroborated in community analysis
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-16; aynucorporadiscord ), and
consistent with the general account of the affiliative's origin from the noun
hi 'thing, trace' argued by Janhunen (2020) via
alonso2022 §2.1 .

Word-initial consonants in the Nairo subdialect

Within Sakhalin, the Nairo subdialect (eastern Sakhalin) shows word-initial
t- where Hokkaido and the remaining Sakhalin varieties have
r- : Nairo teera 'wind' corresponds to
HA rera and non-Nairo SA reera ; Nairo
taarak 'smooth' corresponds to HA raarak
tangiku2022 §2.5 . Alonso de la Fuente interprets this as retention of a
Proto-Ainu lateral * l that merged with
r in most dialects but with t in Nairo
— on this account Nairo preserves the most archaic reflex for the relevant set
alonso2022 §2.2.1–2.2.2 ‹contested›. The PA consonant reconstruction,
including the evidence from Ainu→Ghilyak loans showing Proto-Ghilyak * l-
opposite these forms, is treated in proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction .

Morphological and lexical divergences

Numerals: vigesimal versus decimal

Hokkaido Ainu counts in a vigesimal (base-20) system; Sakhalin diverges above ten.
The Sakhalin varieties use a decimal structure for eleven and above, with
kunkutu '10' and tanku '100' as the
key items tangiku2022 §6.1 . The word tanku
'100' is a Tungusic borrowing; kunkutu '10' may derive from
Itelmen (the language of the Kamchatka Peninsula), on the basis of a resemblance to
forms recorded by Krasheninnikov in 1755 — this is a comparison, not a demonstrated
etymology tangiku2022 §6.1 ‹speculative›. Community discussion
(antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-22; aynucorporadiscord ) also
notes the Itelmen resemblance for kunkutu , attributing the
decimal restructuring to Sakhalin trade contact ‹speculative›. The Hokkaido vigesimal
system is described in vigesimal-numeral-system .

Person affixes and plural marking

The Sakhalin varieties lack the first-person-plural-exclusive forms that Hokkaido
marks with ci= / =as
tangiku2022 §7.4 . In an "elders' style" of Sakhalin speech, the
fourth-person forms an= / =an cover
first-singular reference alongside the dedicated
ku= tangiku2022 §7.4 . The Hokkaido
first-person plural and the exclusive/inclusive distinction are described in
first-person-plural-and-clusivity .

Plural marking across the three Ainuic entities shows a layered historical picture.
Sakhalin uses the suffix -hci / -hcin ;
most Hokkaido dialects outside Saru–Chitose use ci ; Saru,
Chitose, and Ishikari use pa
nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.2 ; tangiku2022 §7.5 .
Nakagawa establishes that Hokkaido ci is the older Hokkaido
form, and pa is an innovation that spread through the Saru and
Ishikari regions nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.2 .

The allative particle and a puzzle of shared isoglosses

Sakhalin uses the allative particle (e)ne , which coincides
with the form found in northern and eastern Hokkaido (Sōya, Asahikawa, Bihoro,
Kushiro, Shiranuka) rather than the southwestern HA forms
un (Horobetsu, Chitose, Yakumo) or
en (Tokachi) nakagawafukazawa2022 §4.3 .
Other shared features between Sakhalin and the geographically distant southern
Hokkaido dialects (Saru–Chitose) are harder to explain: the interrogative form
hemata 'what' and the second-person-plural object prefix
eci= occur in both Saru–Chitose and Sakhalin, a distribution
that Nakagawa and Fukazawa describe as "hard to believe independent"
nakagawafukazawa2022 §5 ‹contested›. Whether these features reflect
retention in peripheral varieties, a contact relic, or convergence is treated in
hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry .

The Kuril varieties

The Northern Kuril varieties are known only from questionnaire records compiled by
eighteenth- and nineteenth-century travellers — abbreviated in Alonso de la Fuente's
discussion as KD (Dybowski), KK (Krašeninnikov), KL (Klaproth), KT (Torii), and KV
(Voznesenskij) alonso2022 §2.2 . The lexicostatistic reanalysis of
ono2020 finds the Kuril varieties clustering with the northeast Hokkaido
group — Samani, Obihiro, Kushiro, Bihoro, Sōya — rather than with Sakhalin, supporting
a two-way Hokkaido-plus-Kuril versus Sakhalin division over the classic three-way one.
A separate analysis using nineteenth-century Kuril material by Satō and Bugaeva (2019)
places the Kuril items closer to southern Hokkaido (Saru/Chitose); the two results rest
on different data sets and are not directly comparable ono2020 ‹contested›.

Phonologically the Kuril varieties largely preserve the coda stops
p/t/k that Sakhalin debuccalises, but they also show prothetic
h- in some forms alongside loss of original
h- in others — an independent innovation parallel to the
prothetic h- found in the Bihoro dialect of Hokkaido
alonso2022 §2.2.5 . The word for 'mouth' appears as
car in Kuril records (aligning with northeastern HA and Sakhalin
against the southwestern HA form par ), an isogloss documented in
place-name data by kirikae1994 (via nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.4 )
and diachronically accounted for as a palatalized-series relic * pʸaɾ
by alonso2022 §2.2.6 . The diachrony of that split is treated in
proto-ainu-segmental-reconstruction . Hokkaido phonological microvariation,
including the Bihoro and northeastern features that align partially with Kuril, is
described in hokkaido-phonological-microvariation . A Kuril contrast text
appears in sakhalin-contrast-text .

Northern contact and the Hokkaido residue

Sakhalin was the primary zone of Ainu contact with Nivkh (Ghilyak), Uilta (Tungusic),
and Manchu-mediated trade vocabulary from Qing China. The multilingualism was
"extremely limited and localised", concentrated among individual interpreters rather
than being community-wide shiraishitangiku2022 §2.5 . Most of the
resulting loanword layer remained specific to the Sakhalin varieties; the full contact
account belongs in northern-contact-nivkh-tungusic-manchu . The items with
a direct reflex in Hokkaido are:

kaya fish-skin garment; sail — borrowed on Sakhalin from Nivkh,
reaching Hokkaido with the specialised sense 'sail' only
shiraishitangiku2022 §3.2 ;

sintoko lacquerware cask — morphologically unanalysable in Ainu,
possibly connected to the Eurasian sanduk Wanderwort, also appearing as
Nivkh sinduχ
shiraishitangiku2022 §3.4 ;

san offering shelf — Nivkh cʰaŋ , Uilta
saan
shiraishitangiku2022 §3.6 ;

tunakay reindeer — from Pre-Proto-Ghilyak
* tola-ŋa.y , also the source of Japanese
トナカイ
janhunen2022 §3c .

In the reverse direction, several Ainu lexical items passed into Nivkh and Uilta on
Sakhalin shiraishitangiku2022 §3.2–3.6 ; the directionality in each case
is argued from morphological analysability, plausible sound correspondences, and the
dialect distribution of the forms across Sakhalin versus the continent.

title: A Glossed Sakhalin (Enciw) Text with Hokkaido Contrast
summary: A Sakhalin Ainu text glossed and annotated against Hokkaido norms, foregrounding phonemic vowel length, the final-h reflex, and divergent person, number, and negation morphology.
part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: A Glossed Sakhalin (Enciw) Text with Hokkaido Contrast
Part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
Summary: A Sakhalin Ainu text glossed and annotated against Hokkaido norms, foregrounding phonemic vowel length, the final-h reflex, and divergent person, number, and negation morphology.

Source, speakers, and orthographic conventions

The sentences glossed in this chapter come from murasaki2025 , Murasaki
Kyōko's revised introductory textbook of West-Sakhalin (Enciw) Ainu. The primary data
were elicited at Tokoro on the Sea of Okhotsk coast, chiefly from Fujiyama Haru
(c. 1895–1974) and Asai Take (c. 1916–1994), the two principal consultants in Murasaki's
fieldwork programme spanning 1960–1994. A small number of illustrations also draw on
Nagara Iso (Shiretoko/Chirijiri). The dialect represented is the western coastal
(Raichiishka/Laichiśka) variety; minor contrasts with the slightly more southerly Maoka
variety and the northeastern Taraika variety are noted where they bear on the comparisons
below tangiku2022 ch. 9 .

The orthography follows Hattori's phonemic transcription
hattori1964 , as adopted by Murasaki. Long vowels are written double
( aa ee oo etc.); every vowel-initial syllable carries the glottal
onset written ʼ ; the six permissible coda consonants are
-h -m -n -s -w -y . A person prefix is separated from its verb
stem by = . Piłsudski's 1912 Sakhalin corpus
pilsudski1912 , the earliest substantial Enciw text collection, uses a
different Romanization; forms cited from that source elsewhere in this grammar have been
converted to the Hattori convention following dalcorso2024 . No Cyrillic
script is reproduced. The broader typological and diachronic profile of Sakhalin Ainu
relative to Hokkaido is developed in sakhalin-and-kuril-ainu-contrast .

Interlinear text and free translation

Five sentences drawn from Murasaki's lesson dialogues are glossed below. Together they
sample existential predication, attributive and locative structure, equational copula,
analytic negation, suppletive verbal number, and the first-person object prefix. Each
sentence was produced by Fujiyama Haru or Asai Take in elicitation sessions conducted
by Murasaki murasaki2025 .

teeta 'itanki 'an | here bowl exist | There is a bowl here. | SA teeta 'here' carries the phonemic long vowel ee; the Saru cognate locative is ta (short). 'an is the singular existential; the suppletive plural form 'okay appears in (2). | murasaki2025:16 | SA | Raichiishka; speaker Fujiyama Haru

tara too kaa-ta cikah renkayne 'okay | DEM lake above-LOC bird many exist.PL | There are many birds on that lake. | 'okay is the suppletive plural existential (singular 'an). kaa is the position noun 'above (in contact)' in the elderly register, taking the locative suffix -ta. SA enforces strict grammatical number agreement; the singular 'an would be ungrammatical with a plural subject here. | murasaki2025:16 | SA | Raichiishka; speaker Fujiyama Haru

tah seta ka hannehka nee | DEM dog FOC NEG COP | This is not a dog. | SA analytic negation: the focus particle ka is followed by the negative predicate hannehka (

Phonological contrasts: vowel length and the final-h reflex

The most immediately audible distinction between SA and Hokkaido (HK) Ainu is prosodic.
Where Saru and other HK varieties carry a lexically contrastive pitch accent on stems,
West-Sakhalin has phonemic vowel quantity — long vowels (written double) versus short
vowels — with no accentual contrast. The two systems correspond diachronically: what HK
encodes as a high pitch on a short vowel, SA encodes as length on that vowel
itabashi2001 ; tangiku2022 ch. 9 . A selection of cognate
pairs from Murasaki's textbook illustrates the pattern murasaki2025 5 :

gloss
SA (Raichiishka)
HK (Saru)

year
paa
pa

child
poo
po

fat, grease
kee
ke

laugh
miina
mina

drink/smoke (tobacco)
'ikuu
'iku

A second phonological divergence involves the coda consonants. HK retains the Proto-Ainu
coda obstruents /-p -t -k -r/ directly in the citation form of nouns and verb stems.
SA replaces all four with a single surface segment /-h/, which is the reflex of the
historical closure when no vowel follows; the original coda emerges when a vowel-initial
suffix is added murasaki2025 2 ; tangiku2022 ch. 9 . The
affiliative (possessed) suffix therefore restores the underlying consonant, as the table
of nominal pairs shows:

gloss
SA citation
SA affiliative
HK citation (Saru)
HK affiliative

fish
ceh
cep-ihi
cep
cep-ihi

hand
teh
tek-ihi
tek
tek-e

eye
sih
sik-ihi
sik
sik-i

skin, bark
kah
kap-uhu
kap
kap-uhu

The SA citation forms ceh teh sih kah all surface with /-h/;
the historical /-p -k -k -p/ re-emerge in the affiliative. HK retains the coda
obstruents in the citation form itself. The HK affiliative suffix morphophonology is
described in affiliative-suffix-morphophonology ; the diachronic
correspondence between the SA surface /-h/ and the HK retained obstruents, with its
implications for the internal reconstruction of Proto-Ainu codas, is the subject of
final-h-history-and-sakhalin-length-reflex ‹consensus›.

Morphosyntactic contrasts

Analytic negation: hannehka versus somo

Example (3) illustrates the SA negative copula construction. The sequence
ka hannehka (nee) negates an equational proposition:
ka is the focus particle, and hannehka
is the negative predicate, historically the copula root with the negative prefix
han- prefixed murasaki2025 11, 15 . The same
prefix attaches directly to verb stems for verbal negation:
hannehka ku='oman 'I did not go'. The copula
nee following the negative is frequently omitted in
conversational speech.

HK Saru negates an equational predicate with the independent negative particle
somo before the copula, yielding somo ne
(NEG COP). The HK somo particle and its distribution across
existential, copular, and verbal contexts are described in
negation-somo-standard-clausal . Dal Corso's study of SA negation
dalcorso2025neg treats the structural history of the SA
han- series and its divergence from the Hokkaido system
in detail ‹contested›.

Person and number marking

The SA person-prefix inventory parallels HK in its core forms — first-person singular
ku= (A/S), first-person singular object
'en= — but diverges in what it assigns to the paradigm cell
that HK labels 'fourth person' or 'indefinite'. In SA the morpheme
'an-/='an functions as first-person plural in the speech of
younger speakers, and as first-person singular in the elderly register, the inverse of
its value in HK murasaki2025 84–85 ; sakaguchi2024 . SA
accordingly has no productive indefinite/fourth-person paradigm of the HK type; the HK
system is described in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and
person-marking-architecture .

Example (4) shows the SA third-person plural subject suffix
=hci cliticizing to the verb:
'ariki=hci 'they came'. HK grammars report no cognate suffix
in this structural position; Dal Corso's alignment study dalcorso2025align
traces the SA morpheme to a grammaticalized pronoun and argues that the SA system
encodes a richer nominal-tracking morphology than any Hokkaido variety. Sakaguchi
sakaguchi2024 provides the most detailed morphological analysis of the
SA person–number paradigm to date ‹speculative›.

Suppletive verbal number

SA and HK share suppletive verbal number — distinct verb stems indexed to the number of
the core argument — but enforce it differently. In SA, grammatical number agreement is
strict: a plural subject requires the plural verb form, with no tolerance for the
singular at low counts that is documented in Hokkaido murasaki2025 84–85 .
The existential opposition in examples (1) and (2) — singular
'an versus plural 'okay — is cognate
with HK an versus oka=an
tangiku2022 ch. 9 . Similarly,
'eh (singular 'come') and
'ariki (plural 'come') in example (4) are cognate with
HK ek / arki . The final long vowel in SA
'ariki versus the short vowel in HK
arki is consistent with the length-accent correspondence
itabashi2001 noted in the preceding section. The HK suppletive verbal
number system is described in suppletive-verbal-number ‹consensus›.

title: The Sentence-Final Particle System and Illocutionary Force
summary: The inventory of clause-final particles (na, wa, ya, nankor, …) encoding assertion, emphasis, confirmation, and softening, and their grammaticalization from formal nouns.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Sentence-Final Particle System and Illocutionary Force
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: The inventory of clause-final particles (na, wa, ya, nankor, …) encoding assertion, emphasis, confirmation, and softening, and their grammaticalization from formal nouns.

The 終助詞 class: definition and position

Hokkaido Ainu has a closed class of sentence-final particles (終助詞, hereafter SFPs) that
occupy the outermost position in the finite clause. The distributional test for the class,
first systematised by fukuda1961 §20 , is that an SFP follows a predicate
and closes the utterance: nothing may follow within the same clause
nakagawa2024 331 ; sato2008 71 . This positional
exclusivity distinguishes SFPs from the adverbial particles (副助詞) treated in
topic-marking-anakne and cleft-nominalization-focus , which are
category-preserving and do not close a clause; a 副助詞 attached to a verb phrase must
be re-verbalised by the light verb ki before a clause-linker may
attach, while a SFP makes that attachment impossible altogether
nakagawa2024 317, 320 ; fukuda1961 §2 .

Two frequency generalizations apply across the class. SFPs are less frequent in Hokkaido
Ainu than in Japanese: a bare finite predicate routinely ends a sentence, and SFP absence
signals nothing marked sato2008 72 ; nakagawa2024 333 ;
fukuda1961 §20 . Stacking is also restricted: only
hani admonitive final particle freely follows another SFP
( na hani , yan hani );
no other combination is freely productive
sato2008 72 ; fukuda1961 §28 ; nakagawa2024 333 .

The class encodes the speaker's illocutionary intent — command, invitation, question,
assertion, admonition — and a stratum of discourse tuning that softens, reinforces, or
appeals for a response from the addressee
nakagawa2024 331 ; sato2008 71 .

The illocutionary inventory

The Saru–Chitose baseline inventory is given below; dialect variants are noted where
documented nakagawa2024 331–337 ; sato2008 71–75 ;
fukuda1961 §20–33 .

SFP
illocutionary value
host / notes
dialect

yan
plural/polite imperative
plural verb stem required; C-final → an
pan-HA

ya (imper.)
sg-addressee polite command
imperative; homophonous with Q ya
‹ISH› only

ya / a
polar question
a post-consonant; ya also COMP 'whether'
pan-HA

ro / rok / no
hortative (seeks addressee's assent)
any person, freq. 4th-pl; see debate below
rok ‹TOK› (Shiranuka 白糠); no (Urakawa 浦河, Samani 様似)

so
deliberative / self-directed volition
declarative VP; not addressed to interlocutor
pan-HA

wa
soft assertive (談話調整)
declarative only; never Q or imperative
pan-HA; ≠ sequential connective wa

na
assertive-appeal (demands attention/response)
declarative or command; also connective before commands
pan-HA

un
soft address / affirmation
VP, noun, adverb; an un = polite dubitative
pan-HA

hani
admonitive ('won't you; please do')
stacks after na , yan
pan-HA; yan hani → yanani

oka
counterfactual wish
concord with emphatic ta ; optional
pan-HA

nek
emphatic assertion
concord with tas(i) / esta(p) ; mainly 雅語
pan-HA; ≠ copula ne

yo
emotive reminiscence (strong affect)
after nominalizer p/pe or hi
pan-HA

tapan
formal register (ございます調)
replaces ne/an/un after ruwe/hawe/siri
pan-HA

Imperative and hortative: yan, ya, ro, so

The polite imperative yan come up from water; polite imperative particle
requires the plural verb stem even when the addressee is one person:
arki yan 'please come' uses the suppletive plural stem
arki , not singular ek
nakagawa2024 332 ; fukuda1961 §26 .
Imperative predicates take no subject person-index;
*a=arki yan is ungrammatical
fukuda1961 §26 — the full morphological constraint is set out in
imperative-and-prohibitive . When the stem ends in a consonant,
yan reduces to an . Ijäs's pedagogical
grammar title a-eyaypakasnu ro 'let us learn [it]'
ijas2023 illustrates the hortative in natural use (see below).

Ishikari has a distinct singular-addressee command ya —
formally identical to the Q-particle but functionally opposed to it
nakagawa2024 332 ; fukuda1961 §26 .
The two ya s are disambiguated by the predicate type that precedes them
(imperative vs. declarative) and by dialect; the Q-particle treatment is in
the section on boundary cases below.

kanna suy arki yan hani | again again come.PL IMP ADM | Do come again, won't you. | The plural suppletive stem arki is required by yan even for a single addressee. hani stacks after yan to add the gentle admonitive 'won't you / I hope you will'. The cluster yan hani contracts to yanani in the Chitose speech-stream (Nakagawa 2024: 333). | sato2008:74 | CHI

The hortative ro hortative particle (‹TOK› Shiranuka rok ;
Urakawa/Samani no ) seeks the addressee's assent for a proposed joint
action nakagawa2024 333 ; sato2008 73 ;
fukuda1961 §23 . An earlier analysis held that
ro is a "first-plural-inclusive command particle",
a claim advanced in kindaichi1936 §215 and repeated in
chiri1942 §118 . fukuda1961 §23 rejects this: the particle
is attested after first-singular, second-person, and third-person predicates, not only
after the fourth-person (inclusive) plural; the frequent co-occurrence with the fourth
person reflects the hortative meaning 'let us …', not a morphosyntactic person
restriction fukuda1961 §23 ; nakagawa2024 333 ‹contested›.
Further discussion is in hortative-and-optative .

paye=an ro | go.PL=4.S HORT | Let us go. | The fourth-person intransitive suffix =an on the plural suppletive stem paye; ro adds hortative force. Constructed for illustration; functionally equivalent expressions with ro are documented in Nakagawa 2024: 333.

The deliberative so deliberative sentence-final particle converts a
predicate into a self-addressed musing — consideration of an action for one's own benefit,
not aimed at an interlocutor nakagawa2024 334 ; sato2008 74 ;
fukuda1961 §22 .

ek=an so | come=4.S DEL | Should I come too, I wonder. | The fourth-person S suffix =an on the singular stem ek; so converts the predicate into a soliloquy deliberation. Nakagawa 2024: 334 gives the meaning as 独語の意志 (self-directed volition).

Assertive and softening: na, wa, un

The assertive-appeal particle na assertive sentence-final particle rouses
the hearer's attention and implies a response — verbal or behavioural — is expected
nakagawa2024 332 ; sato2008 73 ; fukuda1961 §27 .
It attaches to declarative and command predicates alike and is among the most frequent
SFPs in the Saru and Chitose corpus.

pirka huton poronno an na | good cushion many exist FIN | There are plenty of fine cushions here, you know. | Nakagawa ex. 583. na follows the existential predicate and signals that the speaker expects the hearer to act on the information (sit down, use the cushions). The existential an is a 3rd-person (zero-marked) predication. | nakagawa2024:332 | CHI

The softening particle wa soft assertive sentence-final particle adds a
mild conversational tone to a declarative, serving the function Nakagawa calls 談話調整
(discourse tuning) nakagawa2024 334 ; sato2008 74–75 ;
fukuda1961 §21 . It is restricted to declarative predicates: neither
questions nor imperatives take it. In answers and in-dialogue assertions,
wa is particularly frequent, pulling blunt propositions toward
a gentler register:

wen huci ka somo ne wa | bad old.woman even NEG COP SOFT | She is no pauper of an old woman. | Nakagawa ex. 582, first clause of a pair (the second is pirka huci ne wa 'she is a fine old woman'). wa softens the emphatic negation ka somo ne; the exchange is in dialogue. Fukuda 1961: §21 notes that the particle carries a feel comparable to the Tokyo-female / Hokkaido-unisex Japanese「わ」, without claiming it is female-restricted in Ainu — see §register-gender. | nakagawa2024:334 | CHI

The soft-address particle un soft address / affirmation particle conveys
gentle appeal or mild affirmation and is unusual in the inventory for attaching to nouns
and adverbs as well as to VPs
nakagawa2024 332–333 ; sato2008 74 ;
fukuda1961 §30 . The sequence an un
(existential + un ) serves as a polite dubitative. In the
affirmative-answer frame of the evidential tail (§evidential-frame below),
un follows the formal noun directly:
ku=nu ruwe un 'yes, I heard it'
nakagawa2024 396 .

Admonitive and stacking: hani

The admonitive hani admonitive final particle adds gentle urging to
commands and admonitions — a 'won't you; I hope you will' coloring
nakagawa2024 333 ; sato2008 72 ;
fukuda1961 §28 . Its structural property is free stacking after
na or yan , yielding bipartite clusters
na hani and yan hani ; the latter
contracts to yanani in Chitose. Crucially,
hani is not restricted to imperatives, as the following shows:

k=onaha or-o ta ku=teskar kusu ne na hani | 1SG.POSS=father place-POSS LOC 1SG.A=report PURP COP FIN ADM | I am going to report this to my father, you know. | Satō ex. = Nakagawa 2024: 333. na hani on a purposive declarative; neither clause is an imperative, demonstrating that hani attaches regardless of illocutionary mode. or-o is the possessed form (or-POSS) of the relational noun or 'place, location of'. | sato2008:72 | CHI

Members of the Discord-based aynu-corpora community have proposed that
hani derives from the motion verb
hanke 'approach, come near' by grammaticalization into a
command particle, parallel to the proposed path of
yan from a lexical verb meaning 'come up from the water'
(antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-18;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹speculative›. Both etymologies are plausible given
the cross-linguistically common motion-verb-to-imperative cline, but neither has been
established in the primary literature; the proposals are leads for diachronic work.

Kakari-musubi concord: oka, nek

Three pairs in the Ainu particle inventory exhibit a concord relationship structurally
parallel to Japanese 係り結び: an emphatic adverbial particle (副助詞) inside the clause
licenses or requires a specific SFP at sentence end. The 係助詞 label for the emphatic
members of these pairs was introduced by chiri1942 §118 (via
fukuda1961 §11 ) and is endorsed by
nakagawa2024 330 ; sato2008 73 .

emphatic 副助詞
concordant SFP
illocutionary reading
sources

ta 'こそ (wish)'
oka (optional in Saru)
counterfactual wish '〜だったらなあ'
Nk 330; Fk §12/§25; St 73

tas(i) 'まさに〜こそ'
nek (optional in Saru)
assured assertion '〜のだよ (indeed it is)'
Nk 330; Fk §14/§24; St 73

esta ‹SHI› / estap ‹TOK›
ne
assertion 'こそ〜だ'
Nk 330

In Saru the concordant SFP is optional — the closing particle "is not necessarily
present" fukuda1961 §11 — which raises the question of whether the
concord is a productive grammatical constraint or a set of conventionalized collocations.
The sources consulted do not settle this ‹contested›.

cikap ta ku=ne oka | bird EMPH 1SG.S=COP FIN | If only I were a bird! | Nakagawa ex. 560 (= Tamura 1988b: 33; the Fukuda 1961: §12.2 citation gives the form with oka explicit). The emphatic ta triggers the counterfactual wish reading; the concordant oka is here present but may be omitted in Saru. The couplet continues rera ta ku=ne ('if only I were the wind!'). | nakagawa2024:330 | SAR

newaanpe patek tasi a=eranak nek | that only EMPH 4.A=be.troubled FIN | About that alone am I truly troubled. | Nakagawa ex. 594 (source: Chiba University Ainu materials 2015: 160). The emphatic tasi triggers the concordant nek; patek 'only' restricts scope. The 4th-person A prefix a= tracks the narrator/speaker in the oral-literature register. | nakagawa2024:334 | SAR

sato2008 65–66 identifies a structurally more opaque intensification
construction in oral-literary register: a bare concept-form noun
( hum 'sound', ru 'trace') followed by
the particle konna / kan and a verb
ending in the concord suffix -natara ,
-atki , or -rototke :

ek hum konna kewrototke | come sound EMPH resonate.mightily | The sound of his coming thundered on. | Satō ex. (oral-literature register). The bare concept form hum (not possessed humi) appears before konna. Satō classifies konna/kan provisionally as a 強調の助詞 (emphatic 副助詞) but flags its grammatical status as 'unclear'; the appearance of the concept form rather than the possessed form before konna is unexplained — the same nouns take possessed forms when functioning as formal-noun evidential heads. See formal-defective-nouns for the possessed/concept-form alternation. | sato2008:66 | HK

The formal noun / possessed-form puzzle that the konna
construction poses is discussed in formal-defective-nouns ; the
construction is also adjacent to the kakari-musubi debate in
sato2008 65–66 ‹contested›. Whether any of these three concord pairs
constitute a productive focus-concord (like Japanese 係り結び) or merely a set of
frozen collocations remains an open descriptive question
nakagawa2024 330 ; fukuda1961 §11 ‹contested›.

Register-marked forms: yo and tapan

The emotive particle yo emotive sentence-final particle attaches after
a clausal nominalizer ( p/pe or hi ) and
signals strong affective involvement — reminiscence, wonder, or pathos
sato2008 75 . Its position after the nominalizer, rather than directly
after the verb, makes it one of the few SFPs whose attachment site is lexically
conditioned. See nominalization-p-pe for the nominalizer inventory.

The formal-register marker tapan replaces
ne , an , or un
after the evidential formal nouns ruwe ,
hawe , and siri , yielding the
ございます-register frame ruwe tapan
fukuda1961 §33 . It is the one SFP that enters the evidential tail
proper (the predicator slot of the formal noun) rather than stacking outside it.

Ordering and stacking at the clause edge

The general clause-final ordering runs: verb stem → TAM auxiliaries →
evidential tail ( ruwe/hawe/siri/humi +
ne / an ) → SFP. The ordering is confirmed
by the grammaticality of … ruwe ne na and the ungrammaticality
of *… na ruwe ne
nakagawa2024 393 ; fukuda1961 §20 .
The two exceptions within this slot are un and
tapan , which intrude into the evidential predicator position itself
( ruwe un , ruwe tapan ) rather than
stacking outside it — see evidential-schema-overview and
ruwe-ne-inferential .

Within the outer SFP slot, only hani freely follows another SFP
fukuda1961 §28 . No other combination of two SFPs is freely attested in
the sources consulted; longer stacks have not been systematically studied in the Hokkaido
corpus ‹corpus-suggested›. tapan does not add to the outer SFP
slot: it occupies the predicator position of the formal noun and is not a stacking
particle in the same sense.

Boundaries: SFP, connective, and the evidential tail

na as clause-boundary connective

When na immediately precedes a command clause, it functions as a
clause-boundary signal rather than a sentence-closing SFP:
… na [command] reads 'since … / because … [do X]', where
na provides the motivating ground for the instruction
nakagawa2024 332 ; fukuda1961 §27 . The following
corpus instance shows na closing a declarative clause
inside a direct quote, where the SFP value is foregrounded by the
sekor hawean framing:

an epitta paye=an kusu ne na | one throughout go.PL=4.S PURP COP FIN | I plan to go [there] travelling the whole night. | na closes the quoted declarative clause inside sekor hawean '(she) said'. The sequence kusu ne na (purpose + copula + na) is common in discourse: the assertive-appeal force of na urges the hearer to acknowledge the speaker's stated intention. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, 'isoytak dokowa 2', ILCAA Ainu materials (Saru)

The ambiguity between the SFP and connective readings is resolved by whether a further
clause follows: sentence-final na ends the utterance, while the
connective na always precedes something else. The connective
function is treated fully in clause-linkage-overview and
conjunctions-discourse-connectives .

ya/a as Q-particle vs. complementizer

The Q-particle ya question particle; complementizer 'whether' /
a post-consonant question particle closes a polar question as a
SFP nakagawa2024 332–333 ; fukuda1961 §29 . The same
ya (but not a ) also nominalizes a
clause as an embedded polar question ('whether …'), functioning as a complementizer;
in that use it is not a SFP because the clause it heads is embedded
nakagawa2024 333 ; sato2008 74 . The category of
ya / a is therefore function-dependent:
SFP in main clauses, COMP in embedded indirect questions ‹contested›
fukuda1961 §29/§31 ; nakagawa2024 333 .

The Q-副助詞 he polar question focus particle and the Q-SFPs
ya / a occupy distinct positions:
he attaches to the constituent in doubt and can appear
mid-clause, while ya / a are
clause-final nakagawa2024 323–324 ; fukuda1961 §8/§31 .
The full interrogative strategy — the polar vs. content split, wh-in-situ, and
the ne / an / ∅
alternation on the evidential tail — is in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles .

wa as SFP vs. sequential connective

The SFP wa (soft assertive, §assertive-softening) is formally
identical to the sequential clause-linker wa (CONJ 'and then,
having done X') treated in sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining .
They are distinguished by position: the linker always precedes another predicate, the
SFP ends the utterance. In many Hokkaido dialects the wa morpheme
surfaces phonologically as ma
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-07-09, asserted;
aynucorporadiscord ). Whether this phonological shift applies equally to
both the connective and the SFP, or whether only one of the two undergoes it in a given
dialect, is not documented in the sources consulted ‹speculative›.

The community discussion has also proposed a morphological analysis of
newa 'and' as ne + wa
(copula + sequential linker) and neya 'or' as
ne + ya (copula + Q-SFP)
(aomidori_cs_ and nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-12;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›. If correct, the SFP
ya would participate directly in the formation of a common
NP-coordinator — but the analysis remains community-proposed and the morphological
reanalysis of these particles has not been verified in the primary literature.

The evidential tail as sentence-type switch

The evidential formal nouns ( ruwe , hawe ,
siri , humi ) that anchor the
nominalization-plus-copula evidential construction (see
evidential-schema-overview and ruwe-ne-inferential ) also host
a sentence-type switch: the predicator following the formal noun — copula
ne , existential an , or zero —
signals declarative, content-question, or polar-question force respectively
sato2008 136–137 ; nakagawa2024 394–395 .

sentence type
evidential-tail predicator
example

declarative
ruwe ne (COP)
sirpirka ruwe ne 'the weather is fine (recognized fact)'

content (wh) question
ruwe an (existential)
hemanta ne kusu ene siran hi an 'why is it like this?'

polar (yes/no) question
bare ruwe (verb omitted)
e=eraman ruwe 'did you understand?'

affirmative answer
ruwe un (SFP un )
ku=nu ruwe un 'yes, I heard it' (Nakagawa 2024: 396)

formal assertion
ruwe tapan (SFP tapan replaces COP)
ceremonial/ございます-register; Fukuda 1961: §33

sato2008 137 proposes that in a declarative the copula predicates the
subject's nature; in a content question the existential asserts that the subject exists
while its nature is questioned; in a polar question even existence is at issue, so the
verb is omitted. The un and tapan
entries in the table are the two SFPs that enter this predicator slot.
The outer SFPs ( na , wa ,
hani , …) stack outside the evidential frame:
ruwe ne na is grammatical;
*na ruwe ne is not
nakagawa2024 393 .

tane pirka ru-we he an | now good track-POSS Q exist | Is it all right now? | Nakagawa 2024: 304, ex. (corpus Shizunai). The bare formal noun ru-we (no copula) combines with the Q-副助詞 he and the existential an, realising the reduced polar-Q frame ruwe he an (Nakagawa 2024: 409e). This illustrates the sentence-type switch at the evidential tail: copula ne (declarative) is replaced by existential an and the Q-particle he. | ilcaa1976 | SHI | Oda Suteno, 'reyep kamuy', ILCAA Ainu materials (Shizunai)

sekor is not a sentence-final particle

The quotative sekor quotative marker is sometimes encountered in
clause-final position of a quoted clause, preceding a speech verb
( sekor hawean 'said, …'). It is not a SFP: it functions
as a quotative adverb/particle (QUOT) that heads the quoted clause as an adverbial,
not as a particle that closes a main clause nakagawa2024 470 ;
bugaeva2008 35–36 . The etymological analysis
sekor < se 'make a sound' +
kor 'while' (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-09;
aynucorporadiscord , asserted, corroborated by Nakagawa 2024: 470)
underscores its adverbial origin: it is 'saying while', not a clause-closing
illocutionary marker. The full syntactic and typological treatment of
sekor is in quotative-complementation-sekor and
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Register, genre, and the gender question

SFPs in Hokkaido Ainu are not conditioned by the speaker's biological sex.
sato2008 72 states this explicitly: "Ainu final particles are in
principle used by both sexes; there is no particle specific to men or women" —
a direct contrast with Japanese, where particles are gender-indexed.
fukuda1961 §21 noted that the SFP wa carries
a feel similar to the Tokyo-female / Hokkaido-unisex Japanese「わ」, but did
not claim the Ainu particle is female-restricted. The two descriptions converge: HA
SFPs are gender-neutral nakagawa2024 331 ‹consensus›.

A register observation does correlate with speaker sex, but at the level of the
evidential tail, not the SFP class. Community discussion in the aynu-corpora
Discord identifies ruwe ne as "used for objective fact,
frequent in folktales/legends, favoured by men, like
のだ , and doing scene-shift and summary work"
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-09; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›.
This skew belongs to the evidential-nominal layer, not to the SFP layer; it does not
contradict Satō's gender-neutrality claim for SFPs, which operates at a different
level of the clause. The sociolinguistic conditions behind the
ruwe ne frequency pattern, and register differences in
evidential and particle use across genres, are treated in
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre and
gendered-register-speech .

Mirative meaning in Hokkaido Ainu is carried by the perfective auxiliary
aan / anan , not by a dedicated SFP
nakagawa2024 393 ; dalcorso2018 336 . When
ruwe ne appears in surprising contexts the surprise comes from
the discourse frame, not from the formal noun itself. The mirativity–evidentiality
interface is in mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

The Shizunai dialect materials in refsing1986 contain an SFP inventory
that likely includes forms not in the Saru–Chitose baseline described above; the
Refsing treatment (pp.253, 265–267) was not re-read for this chapter and the
Shizunai inventory has not been compiled at Fukuda's level of rigour. Whether a
form glossable as awa belongs to the Shizunai SFP inventory
is one unsettled gap; the present description is limited to the Saru–Chitose core
supplemented by documented dialect variants ‹speculative›.

title: Sequential wa and Clause Chaining
summary: The default connective wa 'and (then)', multi-clause event chains, and the culminative linker aine, disambiguated from the homophonous resultative/perfect wa.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Sequential wa and Clause Chaining
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The default connective wa 'and (then)', multi-clause event chains, and the culminative linker aine, disambiguated from the homophonous resultative/perfect wa.

Overview

The particle wa and (then), so (connective) is the most versatile and most
frequent of Hokkaido Ainu's conjunctive particles. Its core function, in Tamura's formulation
as transmitted by Nakagawa, is to gather two events E1 and E2 into one coherent action and
present them as a single sentence-unit
nakagawa2024 342 . Where hine merely lists events in
temporal order, wa asserts a closer bond: the first event supplies the
result-state, the manner, or the cause that licenses the second. The three semantic readings
share the surface form and overlap in natural speech; the context, the verb's aktionsart, and
the presence of reinforcing adverbs (such as the sequence-marker
orowa(no) ) determine which reading is primary in any given clause pair
nakagawa2024 339–342 ; sato2008 45 . The system as a whole is
laid out in clause-linkage-overview ; the present chapter treats
wa and its culminative companion ayne
specifically.

Three semantic readings

Satō identifies three uses of wa : sequential continuation (often
reinforced by orowa 'and then'), cause-to-effect (reinforced by
kusu 'therefore'), and attendant circumstance or manner
sato2008 45 . Nakagawa adds a unifying temporal criterion: the first VP must be
at its result-state stage — the action has concluded and that result-state frames E2 — rather
than ongoing (simultaneity, kor ) or stative-negated (absence of change,
no ) nakagawa2024 341 .

particle
phase of first VP
core reading
minimal example

wa
result-state after an action
sequence; manner; cause–effect

ipe wa soyne

'ate, then went out'

kor
during the action
simultaneity

ipe kor soyne

'went out while eating'

no
stative or negated action
'in the state of'; 'without doing'

somo ipe no soyne

'went out without eating'

The minimal triad comes from Nakagawa's analysis of the Saru particle system
nakagawa2024 341 ; for kor and no
see simultaneous-kor-clauses . Nakagawa also notes that the result-state account
alone does not predict every wa collocation: the fixed phrase
apkas wa arpa 'walk-and-go' shows wa marking
walking as the route or method of going, even though the walking partly overlaps the going.
Tamura's integration criterion (via Nakagawa) accommodates this under a "state/method/reason"
cluster that subsumes all three readings nakagawa2024 342 . The manner reading
appears clearly in the following Saru corpus example:

corpokke a=eapkas wa arpa=an ayne | beneath.it 4.A=walk.along CONJ go=4.S CONJ | Walking beneath it, I went along, and then… | wa marks the walking as manner and route of the going; ayne is the culminative particle treated in §5 below. | nakagawa2024:339 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 605, after Kayano 1974a: 68

The cause–effect reading is clearest when E1 directly occasions E2:

hokure pirkano e=ipere wa mimus yakne | INTERJ properly 2SG.A=feed CONJ get.fat COND | Now, feed her well so that, once she has fattened up… | wa links the feeding (E1, cause) to the fattening (E2, effect); the subsequent conditional yakne opens a premise on the result-state. | nakagawa2024:339 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 604

Subject continuity: statistical preference without a constraint

In Refsing's Shizunai material, approximately ninety percent of the clause pairs joined by
wa share a subject refsing1986 240 . The preference
reflects the particle's integration semantics: if E1 is the manner or prior stage of E2, the
same actor typically performs both. Yet the preference is not a syntactic rule. Satō shows
that the two clauses joined by wa may have different subjects — unlike
Japanese 〜て , which requires coreference — and that fact bears on its
analysis as a conjunctive particle rather than a same-subject converb
sato2008 45–46 ; bugaeva2022 .

hekaci suma osura wa ontaro hokus | child stone throw CONJ barrel fall.over | The child threw a stone, and the barrel fell over. | The thrower (child) is the A of the first clause; the barrel is the S of the second. Satō uses this example to show that wa carries no same-subject requirement. | sato2008:45 | SAR

Reference tracking across a wa chain rests on the obligatory person
indices that each finite verb carries independently. The statistical same-subject preference,
combined with the absence of a formal constraint, places wa in the
configuration that the cosubordination typology of
foleyvanvalin1984 describes — finite clause-chaining without converb
morphology and without a grammaticalized same/different-subject opposition. The comparison with
hine and akusu , and the broader question of
whether any Hokkaido particle encodes subject continuity in a switch-reference-like way, belong
to hine-akusu-switch-reference .

Multi-clause chains in narrative

Hokkaido Ainu prose narrative strings several wa -linked clauses into
a single chain. Each medial predicate closes on its own person indexing followed by
wa , before the chain continues to the next step. The result presents a
process as an integrated sequence of stages:

sikerpeni kapu a=sospa wa siwnin pe a=uyna wa kusuri ne a=kar | Phellodendron bark 4.A=peel CONJ yellow.bark NMLZ 4.A=take CONJ medicine COP 4.A=make | She peeled the bark of the Phellodendron tree, took the yellow inner bark, and made it into medicine. | A three-step wa-chain; each step is a completed result-state enabling the next. pe after siwnin nominalizes the NP; the fourth-person a= carries the narrating subject. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 横山裕之, 'sikerpeni', Ainu Times no. 18/3, text 12; corpus ainu-times/018/3#12

The free connective adverb orowa(no) 'and then, from there after' can
follow a wa -marked clause to make the sequential boundary more prominent
nakagawa2024 516 ; ijas2023 . This stacking — connective particle
before, connective adverb after — is one of the clearest diagnostics separating the two word
classes. The treatment of orowa(no) as a discourse boundary marker
belongs to tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion .

The culminative ayne

The particle ayne and finally, at last (after a protracted action) closes a
stretch of sustained effort and points to the eventual outcome. Where
wa integrates events step by step, ayne marks
the endpoint of a duration — a process that went on and finally yielded a result
nakagawa2024 346 ; sato2008 44 . Refsing notes a nuance of
impatient delay ('and then at last') absent from the neutral chainer
hine refsing1986 247 .

The most characteristic environment is the intensifier pattern
V a V a ayne , in which the perfective auxiliary
a reduplicates the verb to signal repeated iteration before the
particle closes the stretch:

ku=cis a ku=cis a ayne | 1SG.S=cry PFV 1SG.S=cry PFV CONJ | Crying and crying, and finally… | The V a V a ayne intensifier pattern: the perfective a marks each iteration; ayne closes the protracted stretch and points to the outcome. The full sentence continues with the result clause. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 丸野和子, '"Kuma" turano k=an', Ainu Times no. 15/1, text 24 (excerpt)

The particle easir 'only then, at that point' frequently follows
ayne to underscore the delay before the outcome
sato2008 44 ; ijas2023 . The pattern
wa…wa…ayne is common in narrative: a run of
wa -chained steps leads up to a final ayne
that marks the point of resolution, as in the manner example above
( corpokke a=eapkas wa arpa=an ayne ).

The etymology is transparent: durative auxiliary a plus the neutral
chainer hine , grammaticalized into a separate particle (Chiri 1936;
Tamura, via refsing1986 247–248 ). Tokachi uses
ahinne (occasionally ainne ), and some Tokachi
varieties such as Honbetsu lack the form altogether, distributing its function across
hine and wa
nakagawa2024 346–347 . With a negated preceding clause,
ayne shifts to a 'before having V-ed' reading (negated-protraction),
attested in Chitose nakagawa2024 347 .

wa as a gateway to the auxiliary system

Wa is the conjunctive particle most recruited as the pivot of Ainu's
periphrastic aspect constructions refsing1986 240 . The result-state reading
extends naturally into a family of analytic forms in which the result-state of a closed event
is overlaid by an existential or auxiliary verb, yielding a resultative, a terminative, a
prospective, an attemptive, or a benefactive nuance. Nakagawa's §15 (pp. 452–455) lists the
principal forms; the full aspectual analysis belongs to
existential-aspect-an-oka .

construction
glossed structure
core value

V wa an / V wa oka
V CONJ exist.SG / exist.PL
resultative state: 'be in the state resulting from V-ing'

V wa isam / V wa okere
V CONJ be.no.more / finish
terminative/completive: 'have V-ed (to completion)'

V wa anu
V CONJ put.aside
prospective: 'do V in preparation, keep V-ed for future use'

V wa inkar / V wa inu
V CONJ try.seeing / try.hearing
attemptive: 'try V-ing'

V wa kore
V CONJ give
benefactive: 'do V for someone'

The resultative wa an — where the preceding verb closes on its
result-state and the existential an predicates that state as ongoing —
produces a modifier-head construction interpretable as a resultative relative clause:

racitke wa an tuki | hang CONJ exist cup | a hanging cup | racitke wa an is the attributive resultative: 'the cup that hangs / is in the state of being hung'. The construction modifies tuki as a head noun. | nakagawa2024:339 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 607

The terminative wa isam foregrounds completion rather than persistence:

arpa wa isam | go CONJ be.no.more | S/he has gone.

In all these constructions wa is the conjunctive particle, the same
item that links finite clauses in the sequential reading; the boundary with the connective use
is syntactic (an auxiliary follows immediately and predicates only aspect) rather than formal.
The perfective auxiliary a that often precedes
wa in scene-switch positions adds a further layer, completing the
situation before the chain continues; that interaction is treated in
perfective-anterior-a .

The dialectal allomorph ma

When wa immediately follows a stem ending in
-n or -m , the initial
w- assimilates to the preceding nasal and the particle surfaces as
[ma] . Refsing documents the alternation for Shizunai
refsing1986 240 , and Nakagawa writes the allomorph phonetically in his
transcriptions: the resultative an wa an appears as
an ma an nakagawa2024 339 . The pattern is phonological
and pan-dialectal at the segment level ‹corpus-confirmed›: the standard romanized orthography
retains wa throughout to represent the underlying form, while katakana
transcription writes マ in the relevant environment, reflecting the surface pronunciation
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024; aynucorporadiscord ).

The same assimilation applies before any stem ending in a nasal, including
an 'be/exist', oman 'go (honorific)', and
verbs whose final syllable carries -n . The allomorph is predictably
conditioned; no separate particle ma exists alongside
wa .

Disambiguation

The form wa covers at least three distinct grammatical entries, kept
apart by position and syntactic host rather than by form
nakagawa2024 340 ; ijas2023 .

entry
grammatical class
key diagnostic
example

wa sequential
conjunctive particle
follows a finite VP; always opens a further clause; swappable for
hine

ipe wa soyne

'ate and went out'

wa(no) ablative
case particle / postposition
follows a NP or locative nominal; glossed ABL; extends to
wano

mosir wa

'from the land'

wa sentence-final
final particle
clause-final; emphatic or exclamatory; no following clause

pirka wa

'it's fine! (emph.)'

The connective wa always follows a finite verbal predicate and always
opens a following clause; it never occurs clause-initially. The ablative
wa(no) attaches to a nominal and typically co-occurs with a spatial or
temporal noun (cf. orowa = or +
wa 'from that place'). The sentence-final emphatic is isolable at the
end of a complete utterance. In natural text the three are rarely ambiguous; glossing discipline
requires keeping them distinct, since the particle counts for the sequential
wa are inflated by ablative and final-particle tokens in any raw
word-form search ‹corpus-suggested›.

title: Simultaneous kor 'while' and Overlapping-Event Clauses
summary: The conjunctive particle kor marking temporal overlap, distinguished from the homophonous possessive verb kor and the progressive auxiliary kor an.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Simultaneous kor 'while' and Overlapping-Event Clauses
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The conjunctive particle kor marking temporal overlap, distinguished from the homophonous possessive verb kor and the progressive auxiliary kor an.

The form and its environments

The conjunctive particle kor while, as (simultaneous) joins two finite
clauses in Hokkaido Ainu and carries two distinct linking readings: a
simultaneous reading ('while, as, at the same time as') and a
habitual/factual conditional reading ('whenever, when habitually'). The same
segment is also the transitive verb kor have, possess 'have, possess',
creating a three-way collision of forms that both Nakagawa and Ijäs flag explicitly
nakagawa2024 340, 347 ; ijas2023 . Word-class and linear
position resolve the collision: the conjunctive particle follows a finite predicate and
precedes the next clause; the possession verb carries its own person-index prefixes
directly ( ku=kor 'I have it',
a=kor huci 'one's grandmother'). As a conjunctive particle,
kor belongs to the bound post-predicate class
( 接続助詞 ) inventoried in clause-linkage-overview .

Whole-corpus token counts place kor at approximately 29,300
occurrences (rank 5 in the aligned Hokkaido corpus), but that figure conflates the
possession verb, the simultaneous particle, and the conditional reading, so the raw total
is an upper bound on any single function ‹corpus-suggested›
nakagawa2024 340 .

Overlapping events: the simultaneous reading

The simultaneous reading of kor signals that two events or states
co-occur: the first predicate is ongoing while the second takes place, or both proceed
together. Nakagawa's primary gloss is Japanese 〜しながら
nakagawa2024 340 ; Satō gives the same value for Chitose
sato2008 49 .

Among the temporal-succession connectives of Saru, kor occupies a
phase-specific slot. Nakagawa draws a three-way contrast according to the temporal phase of
the first predicate nakagawa2024 341 :

particle
temporal phase of the first predicate
primary reading
illustrative pair

wa
result-state following completion of the action
〜して、〜したあとで 'and then; after doing'

ipe wa soyne
'ate, then went out'

kor
action or state ongoing at the time of the second event
〜しながら 'while doing; as'

ipe kor soyne
'went out while eating'

no
stative or negated predicate (no temporal change)

〜の状態で、〜しないで
'being X; without doing'

somo ipe no soyne
'went out without eating'

Nakagawa notes that the phase account alone does not explain every distribution: the
fixed collocation apkas wa arpa 'walk and go' resists
*apkas kor arpa , suggesting that additional manner or cause semantics
govern some wa collocations (Tamura 1972b: 152, via
nakagawa2024 342 ). The no particle and its
negation distribution are treated in manner-and-degree-adverbial-clauses ;
sequential wa belongs to
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining .

The following example from Nakagawa's Saru materials shows the simultaneity construction
in a narrative sequence. The silver knife descends
( san ) while gleaming
( mikemike ):

sirokane otcike or sirokane makiri saya eusi wa kasi mikemike kor san hine | silver tray place silver small.knife sheath be.with CONJ surface sparkle CONJ descend CONJ | The silver knife on the silver tray, with its sheath, came descending while gleaming on the surface. | The mikemike kor san sequence is the simultaneity construction: gleaming (ongoing) kor descending. The preceding saya eusi wa closes an attendant-state clause ('sheathed, and'); hine continues the chain. | nakagawa2024:340 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 610

The simultaneous kor carries no same-subject requirement. Unlike
Japanese 〜ながら , which requires coreferential subjects in both
clauses, Ainu kor permits the two linked predicates to have
different subjects. Satō demonstrates this with an example where the speaking subjects
(mother and father) are distinct from the entity that acts in the matrix clause
sato2008 49–50 ; Ijäs makes the same observation, citing a Tamura example
where the dancer and the looker are different persons ijas2023 :

ku=horipi kor en=nukar | 1SG.S=dance CONJ 1SG.O=look.at | While I was dancing, s/he looked at me. | Different-subject kor clause: the dancer (first person, ku=) and the observer (third person, unexpressed) are distinct. After Tamura 2000: 155, adapted by Ijäs as a teaching example. | ijas2023

Reference tracking across the chain relies on the obligatory person-index prefixes on
each finite predicate. Ainu has no morphological switch-reference — no dedicated
same-subject or different-subject converb morphology — as established in
clause-linkage-overview and examined in hine-akusu-switch-reference .
For the Shizunai cognate kane , Refsing observes a statistical
tendency toward same-subject co-occurrence in her materials, with documented exceptions
refsing1986 250 ; no equivalent quantitative statement has been made for
Saru kor ‹corpus-suggested›.

The progressive: kor followed by an existential

When the simultaneous kor is followed immediately by the
existential verb an (singular) or oka
(plural), the combination functions as the Ainu progressive: the event of the first
