narrative frame-text in Saru Hokkaido narrations — the form through which the narrator
presents story-world propositions as established, known facts — and traces its dominance in
the paradigm partly to this function dalcorso2018 331 . The evidential
architecture underlying that use is treated in ruwe-ne-inferential .

Community observation from the aynu-corpora Discord corroborates and extends this picture:
nukopoli notes that ruwe ne is especially frequent in folktales and
legends, that it functions for scene transitions and summaries analogously to Japanese
のだ , and that the sequence
ruwe ne akusu is a common narrative collocation (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2024; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›. This is
consistent with Dal Corso's finding and with the observation by Satō that
akusu can attach after a ruwe ne tail —
a combination that would be anomalous in Japanese, where the factual
と/たら conditionals reject a preceding
のだ sato2009 52–53 .

The scope ordering is fixed: the evidential sits above clause-internal aspect but below the
connective, giving the narrative formula [VP] – ruwe ne –
connective, where the connective can be akusu (discovery pivot),
p or hike (loose or adversative link), or
korka (counter-expectation) nakagawa2024 393 . The
following Chitose example illustrates the ruwe ne akusu
episode-pivot:

kanpi ku=sanke ru-we ne akusu itasa kanpi ek ru-we ne | letter 1SG.A=send.out track-POSS COP CONJ in.return letter come track-POSS COP | I sent a letter, and (then, it turned out) a reply arrived. | The ruwe ne tail closes the first event as an established fact before akusu pivots to the discovered consequence. In oral narrative the same sequence marks episode-level transitions, signalling the boundary between what has been established and what unfolds next. | sato2008:47; sato2009:52 | CHI

The perfective auxiliary a perfective (completive aspect) may appear
between the evidential and the connective, adding a signal that the first situation is
completed and detached from the present. The Handbook's descriptive grammar reports that
the sequence perfective a plus a connective such as
korka , wa , or nominal
p tends to fall at points where the scene, topic, or actor of a
narrative changes bugaeva2022 ‹speculative› (the chapter-and-page attribution is
unconfirmed). The structure appears in the following Saru example
from Dal Corso's evidence corpus:

mat-kor hine oka=an ru-we ne a p | wife-have CONJ exist.PL=4.S track-POSS COP PFV NMLZ | He had taken a wife, and they went on living — and yet, (now something changed) … | The hine-chain (took a wife; lived on) is nominalized into a ruwe ne predicate, then the perfective a and the connective nominal p mark a narrative turn. The p is not an argument of the following clause — it is at the connective end of the formal-noun grammaticalization cline (see the nominalization chapter). The turn coincides with a change in the narrative state of affairs. | dalcorso2018:196 | SAR | Wateke, Saru; Dal Corso siglum TMA: 2

The ruwe ne a p formula functions as the episode-boundary
counterpart of the pe ne hike opening frame. Where
pe ne hike closes a background-setting chain and pivots forward,
the ruwe ne a p / ruwe ne akusu cluster
closes a completed narrative segment and marks entry into a new situation. Both depend on
the grammaticalization of formal nouns ( pe , p ) from relative-head through complement-marker to connective use, as described in
nakagawa2024 507–516 .

The free discourse connective orowa(no)

Inter-sentential cohesion — the link between sentences that stand as separate prosodic and
information units — is carried by free connective adverbs rather than by the bound
post-predicate particles. The most frequent in oral narrative is
orowa and then, after that, from there ( no ), derived
from the locative noun or 'place, location' plus the ablative
wa 'from', with the compositional meaning 'from there onward'.
Related forms include ora and oraun
nakagawa2024 516–517 . Ijäs notes orowa as a
sequence-emphasiser that can follow wa in a compound linking phrase
ijas2023 .

What distinguishes these free connectives from the bound particles is their syntactic
freedom: they can begin a new sentence, they need no host predicate, and they can stack
after a bound particle rather than replacing it. Nakagawa draws the organizing cut between
bound 接続助詞 (conjunctive particles) and free
副詞句 (connective adverb phrases), placing
orowa(no) , ora , and
rapok(ke)(ta) 'meanwhile' in the second class
nakagawa2024 516 ; Refsing makes the same cut for Shizunai between bound
conjunctionalizers and free conjunctions refsing1986 238 . For narrative
cohesion, this syntactic freedom makes orowa(no) the appropriate
device when the narrator wants to jump across a topic or scene boundary — where a bound
particle like hine would imply continuity within the same
participant chain — and to re-orient the listener from one starting point to a new one.

Reference tracking across narrative chains

Hokkaido Ainu has no morphological switch-reference system: there are no dedicated
same-subject or different-subject verb forms, and no converb series whose morphology
encodes participant continuity bugaeva2022 . Reference tracking across a clause
chain therefore rests on the obligatory person indexes on each finite verb and on the
statistical preferences associated with each connective. The general treatment of this
mechanism belongs in reference-tracking ; the points specific to narrative
chains are as follows.

Within a hine -chain, the participant set established in the opening
frame tends to persist across junctures. For wa , Refsing finds that
the predicates it joins share a subject in approximately ninety percent of her Shizunai
material, making it a same-subject-preferring chainer without a same-subject requirement
refsing1986 240 . Hine is structurally looser — it
separates events rather than integrating them as one coherent act — and within an extended
narrative segment the participant set introduced at the opening tends to carry through its
junctures ‹corpus-suggested›. The person indexes on each finite verb make any departure from
that continuity visible: a shift from fourth-person
a= / =an to first-person
ku= inside a chain signals a shift of narrated perspective, most
commonly into a direct-speech quotation or a moment of vivid self-identification within the
story.

Discovery connectives — awa and akusu —
regularly accommodate reference changes across the junctures they bridge, since their
semantics (the first event is completed and a new, often unexpected situation is
encountered) naturally accompanies a shift in the narrated actor or scene. At the macro
level of episodes, the ruwe ne a p /
ruwe ne akusu boundary markers identified in the preceding section
tend in corpus narrative to coincide with just those points where the actor or scene
changes bugaeva2022 ‹speculative› (the chapter-and-page attribution is
unconfirmed). The interaction of the evidential system with narrative genre is
pursued further in narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

title: Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality: Overview
summary: The architecture of the TAM/modality system and how its pieces fit: tenselessness, the existential-aspect core, the phasal/iterative/habitual periphery, the directive/optative moods, and the modal field, with the boundaries to evidentiality and negation.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality: Overview
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: The architecture of the TAM/modality system and how its pieces fit: tenselessness, the existential-aspect core, the phasal/iterative/habitual periphery, the directive/optative moods, and the modal field, with the boundaries to evidentiality and negation.

Tenselessness and the periphrastic architecture

Hokkaido Ainu has no grammatical tense. Past, present, and future reference are never read off
the shape of the finite verb; the categories the verb does engage are aspect, mood, modality,
and evidentiality, and even these are optional rather than obligatory inflections
nakagawa2024 434 ; bugaeva2012 491, 493 ; takahashi2022 .
Takahashi states the point for the modern reference literature: aspect and evidentiality are not
obligatory grammatical categories in Ainu, and the perfective/imperfective opposition is not
fixed either lexically or grammatically takahashi2022 ; yoshikawa2022 . The
grammatical work that other languages assign to tense and bound aspect is carried here by
periphrastic complex predicates : a second finite verb, an existential verb, or the
copula follows the lexical verb and supplies the temporal, aspectual, or modal value. In the
terms of aspectual typology and the grammaticalization of tense–aspect–mood, the system uses
optional analytic operators where an inflecting language would use bound morphology
comrie1976 ; bybee1994 . The time-reference system itself is
treated in tenselessness-and-time-reference ; this chapter maps the forms that supply
aspectual, modal, and illocutionary values and routes each to its detail chapter.

Whether a tenseless predicate has a preferred temporal translation was debated through the
twentieth century. Kindaichi held that Ainu makes no temporal distinction yet renders everything
as past, the moment of speech being too fleeting to fix; Chiri narrowed the past reading to
non-stative predicates, leaving statives to translate as a Japanese
-te iru present; and Refsing argued that a language without the category of
tense does not order events on a linear timeline at all, so that no past translation is more
correct than a present one chiri1942 96–97 ; refsing1986 §14.9.1
‹contested›. The descriptive consensus that displaced these positions is the one stated above:
there are no pure tense markers, and a bare finite verb takes whatever English tense its event
type and discourse setting require bugaeva2012 491, 493 .

Three constructional frames carry almost all of this load, distinguished by their shape and by
which verb hosts the person index. The distinction is diagnostic: in the auxiliary frame only the
lexical verb is indexed, whereas in the pair-verb frame both predicates can be, because each
remains a finite verb in its own right bugaeva2012 492–495 ;
nakagawa2024 306, 439–443 .

frame
shape
person marking
representative forms
detail chapter

auxiliary verb
V AUX
on V ; the auxiliary is unmarked
a , aan , nisa , nankor , rusuy , easirki
perfective-anterior-a , epistemic-irrealis-modality

pair verb
V wa/kor/hine V2
both predicates may be indexed; subjects need not corefer
kor an , wa an , wa isam , wa okere , wa kore
existential-aspect-an-oka , phasal-iterative-habitual-aspect

transitive-verb auxiliary
V (ka) Vt
on V ; the transitive verb is unmarked
easkay , eaykap , koyaykus , amkir
abilitative-easkay-eaykap

directive / final-particle layer
clause FIN
bare-stem imperative; clause unchanged otherwise
yan , iteki , ro , hani
imperative-and-prohibitive , hortative-and-optative

One principle organizes the pair-verb frame: a verb phrase has exactly one person-bearing main
verb. In ku=tere wa k=an 'I am waiting', the
existential-locational-an-oka existential verb an 'exist'
carries the same ku= as the lexical tere 'wait',
so the string is two clauses linked by wa rather than a single inflected
predicate. Nakagawa accordingly treats kor an and
wa an not as aspect proper but as forms that express aspect-like meaning,
and Yoshikawa develops the same point under the cover term "existential aspectual forms": the
existential an here is less grammaticalized than Japanese
iru / aru and keeps its concrete existential sense
nakagawa2024 442 ; yoshikawa2022 ‹contested›.

The lexical material of these frames comes from the suppletive singular/plural verb pairs that
Ijäs's Lesson 9 catalogues as special verb forms — a / rok
'sit', an / oka 'exist',
ek / arki 'come',
arpa / paye 'go'
ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 142–151 . Two of these pairs feed the TAM
system directly: a / rok grammaticalizes into the
perfective/anterior auxiliary, and an / oka into the
existential-aspect frames, the number of the auxiliary tracking the number of the subject
nakagawa2024 306–308, 439–443 ; bugaeva2012 471, 493–495 ‹consensus›.

Time reference without tense

Temporal location is recovered from four overt resources. Time adverbs such as
numan 'yesterday' and nisatta 'tomorrow' place the
event directly; prospective and necessity forms such as kusu ne ,
oasi 'surely will', and etokus 'be about to' yield
future readings; the situational evidential siri anchors a visible
ongoing scene in the present; and completion forms such as a ,
nisa , and wa isam support past translation
nakagawa2024 434–436 . Each yields a temporal reading only relative to the
moment of utterance, and context can override the default.

sine ancikar ne wa ek=an kusu ne na | one night COP CONJ come=4.S PURP COP FIN | After one night, I will come. | kusu ne supplies the scheduled-future reading; ek is the singular 'come' stem and =an the fourth person used as the narrative first person. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 10 [aa-irc/010#200]

The same prospective frame surfaces in the grammars as intention, scheduled future, and, with a
second-person subject, near-command. Ijäs glosses it 'be going to, intend to, plan to', matching
Nakagawa and Satō, but her pedagogical orthography writes kusune as one
word; the analysis followed here is the conjunction kusu plus the copula
ne , with the one-word spelling kept as a teaching convention and carrying
no separate morphological claim nakagawa2024 435 ; sato2008 85–86 ;
ijas2023 .

Lexical aspect and the existential-aspect core

Before any periphrasis applies, each verb carries an inherent aspectual character that conditions
which frame it enters. Nakagawa divides the lexicon into action, change, and stative verbs, a
division that aligns with the actional classes Takahashi and Yoshikawa use; derivational
Aktionsart suffixes on (often onomatopoeic) roots, such as -kosanu 'happen
once, briefly' or -natara 'persist', add a lexically restricted second
layer nakagawa2024 204–208, 438 ; takahashi2022 ; yoshikawa2022 .
A stative verb can also be read as the change into its state, so the same lexeme may shift class:
poro is 'be big' or 'become big', and the choice of aspect frame turns on
that reading nakagawa2024 438 .

verb type
semantic profile
usual frame
reading

action
activity with no inherent change of state
V kor an
action in progress; also habitual or repeated

change
subject or object enters a new state or location
V wa an ; V kor an for the process
resulting state; process of changing

stative
property or stable condition, with a possible inchoative reading
V wa an on the change-into reading
state reached and continuing

The progressive kor an joins a lexical verb, the simultaneous conjunctive
kor 'while', and the existential an (plural
oka ). With action verbs it marks an action in progress; with change verbs
it marks the process leading into the change; and it covers habitual and repeated situations as
well nakagawa2024 440–441 ; sato2008 196–197 ;
bugaeva2012 494 . Because the construction is a clause chain, the lexical verb and
an share the subject index:

kesto kesto iki=an kor an=an | every.day every.day do=4.S while exist=4.S | I went on living, doing this day after day. | Both iki and the existential an carry the fourth-person =an, the diagnostic that kor an is a chain of two finite clauses; the value here is habitual. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 9 [aa-irc/009#23]

The resultative wa an joins a lexical verb, the sequential conjunctive
wa , and the existential verb, and denotes the state that exists after a
change nakagawa2024 439–444 ; sato2008 197–199 ;
bugaeva2012 494–495 . Satō draws the selectional line sharply: an action verb
leaves no direct result for wa an to predicate, so action perfects fall to
the auxiliary a instead, and only change and stative verbs enter
wa an sato2006 63 ; sato2008 198 .

yuk as wa an na | deer stand CONJ exist FIN | A deer is standing. | wa an presents the standing as the state resulting from the posture-change verb as 'stand'. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 1 [aa-irc/003#27]

Perception and cognition verbs such as nukar 'see' and
eramuan 'understand' take either frame, and how the contrast should be
stated is unsettled. Nakagawa's 1981 account separates nukar kor an from
nukar wa an by active observation against passive coming-into-view; Satō
rejects activeness as primary and derives the contrast from continuation of action against
continuation of result, treating any activeness as a contextual by-product. Nakagawa concedes the
matter needs further discussion nakagawa2024 445–446 ; sato2006 65
‹contested›. A dialect-specific restriction compounds the picture: in Tokachi,
kor an does not co-occur with a first-person subject, the progressive of a
first-person subject using kan an instead; no equivalent is reported for
the southern dialects takahashi2022 ‹contested›.

Several dialectal continuatives occupy the same field. Saru and Chitose
kane an adds a noteworthy-situation nuance, while Shizunai and Samani use
it as a plain merger of kor an and wa an ; Tokachi
and Ishikari have kan an , and Tokachi tek an
nakagawa2024 446–449 ; sato2006 84–85 . Yoshikawa groups these
with wa an and kor an as existential aspectual
forms — forms that may express aspectual meanings without being grammatical aspect
yoshikawa2022 . The full treatment of the progressive and resultative belongs to
existential-aspect-an-oka , the appearance-based and merged continuatives to
continuative-siri-siran-kane-an , and the iterative and habitual periphery to
phasal-iterative-habitual-aspect .

Completion, anteriority, and the admirative

The auxiliary a (plural rok ), grammaticalized from
the verb a / rok 'sit', is the most disputed member
of the system, and the disagreement is substantive rather than terminological. The Kindaichi–Tamura
tradition reads it as perfective or past; Bugaeva and Satō read an actional perfect that, on
Satō's account, cannot combine with a negative and so leaves the stative perfect to
wa an ; Nakagawa, following Yoshikawa, denies it is tense or aspect at all,
taking it to mark an event set in contrast to a following one; and Yoshikawa's handbook chapter
analyses it as perfective with a direct-evidential, witnessed-reminiscence component
bugaeva2012 493 ; sato2006 63 ; nakagawa2024 306–307 ;
yoshikawa2022 ‹contested›.

tane pakno a=e=tumam wa hotke=an a korka | now until 4.A=2SG.O=embrace CONJ lie.down=4.S PFV although | Until now I had held you in my arms and lain down, but — | Nakagawa reads a here as marking the event that forms the background to the following korka clause, not simply as a past. | nakagawa2024:305 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 448

The admirative aan (plural rokoka ) forms a tidy
paradigm against a . It signals that, at some reference point, the speaker
recognizes after the fact what an earlier situation was, often with surprise; Yoshikawa analyses
it as perfect plus indirect evidentiality, with mirativity as an extension, where
a is perfective plus direct evidentiality, the two echoing the
direct/indirect past of Classical Japanese ki and
keri yoshikawa2022 ; bugaeva2012 493 .

form
aspect
evidentiality
extended value

a / pl. rok
perfective
direct
certainty, witnessed reminiscence

aan / pl. rokoka
perfect
indirect
mirativity (after-the-fact surprise)

The plural rok-oka is the strongest internal argument that
aan is the auxiliary a plus existential
an , since it pairs the plural of a with the plural
of an . The singular surface forms vary by locality —
anan in Chitose, awan in Horobetsu and Tokachi,
haw'an in Shizunai — and Yoshikawa grants that some invite alternative
segmentations, so the unified a + an etymology rests on the plural rather
than on every singular form nakagawa2024 307–308 ; yoshikawa2022 ‹contested›.

mokor=an aan ru-we ne akusu | sleep=4.S PRF.MIR track-POSS COP when | when it turned out that I had fallen asleep, | aan joins perfect aspect to indirect evidentiality and after-the-fact realization; the following ru-we ne repackages the proposition as established fact. | biratori1969 | SAR | Kimura Kimi, uwepeker [biratori/021/004#82]

Completion has a wider periphery than a . The recent perfect
nisa marks an event just finished, and the completive pair verbs
wa isam and wa okere carry the lexical traces of
isam 'be absent' and okere 'finish', patterning
with clause linkage because both members can stay person-bearing predicates
nakagawa2024 312, 452–453 ; bugaeva2012 493, 495 . Ijäs's
Lesson 9 records a repeated a ... a pattern as prolonged continuity; this
is the reduplicated perfective with an iterative value, and the full account of
a , its reduplication, and aan is given in
perfective-anterior-a nakagawa2024 307–308 ; ijas2023
‹corpus-confirmed›.

The modal field

Modality is distributed across the auxiliary, transitive-verb-auxiliary, and final-particle
layers, with epistemic, ability, deontic, and desiderative values occupying different positions
in the clause nakagawa2024 305–319, 421–426 ; bugaeva2012 493–495 .

domain
principal forms
value
detail chapter

prospective / intentive
kusu ne , oasi , etokus
intention, scheduled future, impending event
epistemic-irrealis-modality

epistemic
nankor
probability, conjecture, expectation
epistemic-irrealis-modality

ability
easkay , eaykap , koyaykus
ability, broad inability, circumstantial inability
abilitative-easkay-eaykap

deontic
easirki , kuni , yak pirka
obligation, expectation, advice, permission
deontic-necessity

desiderative
rusuy
want to do
epistemic-irrealis-modality

Epistemic nankor marks probability or conjecture, and with a second-person
subject and final na it softens into expectation or command
nakagawa2024 311 . Ijäs gives the same range — 'must have' on a past event,
'probably' on a future one — and adds an etymology from a verb 'be trustworthy'; no source read
for this account confirms that derivation, and it is best left as her own suggestion
ijas2023 ‹speculative›.

nani pirka nankor | soon be.good probably | It will probably get better soon. | nankor follows the predicate and supplies epistemic probability. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2 [aa-irc/004#84]

Ability is expressed through the transitive-verb-auxiliary group, whose negative is lexical
rather than built with somo . easkay gives general
ability, eaykap the broad inability (lack of skill or of circumstance),
and koyaykus the specifically circumstantial inability; the scalar
ka 'even' may intervene, especially under negation. Ijäs treats
easkay and eaykap correctly as a lexical
positive–negative pair nakagawa2024 314–316 ; ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

aoka ka sikunu=an easkay pe ne na | we even survive=4.S be.able NMLZ COP FIN | We too can survive. | The transitive-verb auxiliary easkay follows the person-marked intransitive verb to express ability. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8(2) [aa-irc/007#127]

soyne=an ka eaykap kusu | go.out=4.S even be.unable because | because I cannot go outside, | Negative ability is the dedicated word eaykap, not somo easkay; the scalar ka intervenes before it. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#48]

Deontic necessity and permission draw on several sources: the auxiliary
easirki expresses obligation, kuni an expectation
or 'should', and conditional predicates such as yak pirka 'should',
yak wen 'should not', and yakka pirka 'may' express
advice and permission nakagawa2024 309–310, 423–424 . The full inventory belongs
to deontic-necessity , the ability auxiliaries to abilitative-easkay-eaykap ,
and the epistemic and desiderative forms to epistemic-irrealis-modality .

e=e yakka pirka | 2SG.A=eat even.if be.good | You may eat it. | Ijäs's Lesson 10 permission frame, cited as a constructed illustration rather than a corpus token. | ijas2023

Directive and hortative mood

The direct imperative to a singular addressee is the bare verb with no second-person subject
prefix; object prefixes may remain, and adding a second-person subject affix is ungrammatical.
A plural or polite addressee takes final yan , number-sensitive verbs
using the plural stem, and the softener hani or a benefactive
wa kore 'do for (me)' turns the command into a request
nakagawa2024 421–423 . Ijäs's command lesson matches these points and warns
explicitly against the second-person subject affix ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

hetak pusu wa inkar | come.on dig CONJ look | Come on, dig it up and look! | The singular imperative is a chain of bare verbs; no second-person subject prefix appears. | nakagawa2024:422 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 911

Prohibition is formed by a preverbal prohibitive adverb, most often Saru and Chitose
iteki , with dialect variants itekke ,
etekke , and ecikki . The adverb precedes both
imperatives and indirect command forms such as kusu ne na and
nankor na nakagawa2024 424–426 .

iteki ape sam ta ek | PROH fire side LOC come | Do not come near the fire! | The prohibitive adverb iteki precedes the verb, which keeps the bare imperative shape. | nakagawa2024:424 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 924

The hortative uses the final particles ro , rok , or
no , distributed by dialect, after a fourth-person verb. This is the main
directive environment in which the fourth person expresses an addressee-inclusive 'we'
nakagawa2024 334–335 . Imperative and prohibitive are developed in
imperative-and-prohibitive , the hortative and optative in
hortative-and-optative , and the person base in person-marking-architecture .

ney ta ka kuruma ani mosir epitta payoka=an wa inkar=an ro | when LOC even car INS land throughout go.around=4.S CONJ look=4.S HORT | Someday let us travel around the whole country by car and see it. | The hortative particle ro follows a clause chain with the fourth-person inclusive =an. | nakagawa2024:333 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 575, after Honda 2001: 19

Boundaries with evidentiality, negation, and clause linkage

TAM and evidentiality are adjacent systems because several aspect forms carry evidential or
mirative values. The perfective a has been analysed as direct evidential,
aan as indirect-evidential and mirative, and the situational
siri frame anchors a visible ongoing event in present time
yoshikawa2022 ; nakagawa2024 435 . The formal-noun evidential system —
ruwe ne , siri ne , hawe ne ,
humi ne — is treated in evidential-schema-overview , with the
central member in ruwe-ne-inferential the inferential ruwe ne .

Scope ordering keeps the two systems distinct. The evidential predicate sits above clause-internal
aspect and modality but below the clause linkers, so kusu ne ruwe ne is
well formed while *ruwe ne kusu ne is not, and negation goes inside the
frame, as in … somo ki ruwe ne 'it is a fact that one does not …'
nakagawa2024 393 . In the ability domain negation is lexicalized as
eaykap or koyaykus , and prohibition uses the
iteki adverbs, so both belong to the negation system as well as to mood.

Clause linkage supplies the connective material of the periphrastic frames. The sequential
wa that chains events appears in wa an ,
wa isam , and wa okere , and the simultaneous
kor of adverbial clauses appears in kor an . When
the second predicate keeps its person marking and verbal properties, the construction retains the
shape of a linked predication, which is why the existential-aspect frames stand between aspect and
clause chaining. The linking grammar these forms recruit is set out in
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining sequential wa and
simultaneous-kor-clauses simultaneous kor .

title: Tenselessness and the Time-Reference System
summary: Hokkaido Ainu lacks a grammatical tense category; temporal location is inferred from aspect, discourse anchoring, and temporal adverbs.
part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Tenselessness and the Time-Reference System
Part: Part XIV — Tense, Aspect, Mood, and Modality
Summary: Hokkaido Ainu lacks a grammatical tense category; temporal location is inferred from aspect, discourse anchoring, and temporal adverbs.

The absence of a tense category

Hokkaido Ainu has no grammatical tense. No alternation in verb form encodes whether an event
precedes, coincides with, or follows the moment of speech. Nakagawa states the finding plainly:
past, present, and future reference "cannot be expressed by any change of verb form, and normally
need not be" nakagawa2024 434 . Bugaeva draws the same conclusion twice: "there
are no pure tense markers" and the verb "may be marked for aspect, modality, and evidentiality"
bugaeva2012 491, 493 . Takahashi adds that even aspect and evidentiality are not
obligatory grammatical categories in Ainu takahashi2022 ; Yoshikawa reports that a
clear perfective–imperfective opposition is absent both lexically and grammatically
yoshikawa2022 . A finite verb, unmarked for any of these, yields whatever temporal
interpretation its event type and discourse context allow.

Nakagawa draws a typological parallel with Mandarin Chinese, which likewise recovers temporal
reference from adverbs, aspect particles, and contextual position rather than from verb
inflection nakagawa2024 434 . The grammatical categories the Hokkaido Ainu verb
does engage — aspect, mood, modality, and evidentiality — form a periphrastic system of complex
predicates whose architecture is mapped in tam-overview . The present chapter
focuses on the theoretical status of the tense absence and the mechanisms through which temporal
reference is achieved in its place.

The tenselessness debate and the status of a

Whether Ainu is best described as radically tenseless, as implicitly past, or as encoding a
covert two-way tense opposition through the auxiliary a perfective auxiliary was
debated across most of the twentieth century and remains contested at one point. Four positions
have been held.

position
holder
core claim
grade

"everything is past"
Kindaichi (1931, via refsing1986 )

The language makes no temporal distinction but expresses everything in the past; the
present moment is too transient to locate linguistically

‹contested›

split by Aktionsart
chiri1942 96–97 (via refsing1986 )

Only change and action predicates require a past translation; stative predicates
translate as Japanese -te iru (present progressive)

‹contested›

truly tenseless
refsing1986 §14.9.1

A language without the category of tense does not order events on a linear timeline; no
translation tense is more correct than another, and calling the past "more proper" is
incorrect

‹contested›

no pure tense markers

bugaeva2012 491, 493 ; nakagawa2024 434 ;
takahashi2022 ; yoshikawa2022

There are no tense markers; the verb engages aspect, modality, and evidentiality
optionally; temporal interpretation follows from these categories and from context

consensus

Kindaichi's claim that Ainu "expresses everything in the past" rested on the observation that
finite forms in oral narrative translate with past-tense equivalents. This describes a discourse
default of the narrative register: events are ordered against a temporal frame set at the
opening, so a bare verb reads as past by pragmatic inference, not by inflection. Chiri's
refinement — that the past reading is obligatory only for predicates denoting change or action,
while stative predicates prefer a present-progressive translation — anticipates the modern
aspectual classification (treated in existential-aspect-an-oka ) but treats a
discourse-aspectual tendency as a grammatical rule. Refsing's position is the sharpest
departure: a language without the category of tense has no grammatical linear time ordering
and so admits no preferred translation tense; calling the past "more proper" misapplies a
European temporal grid to a system that lacks the category altogether
refsing1986 §14.9.1 ‹contested›.

The auxiliary a perfective auxiliary (plural rok perfective auxiliary, plural )
is the form closest to a tense marker, and its analysis is genuinely unsettled
nakagawa2024 306–307 . Kindaichi and Tamura read it as
past , which would produce a covert two-way opposition: marked
a = past, unmarked = non-past. Bugaeva and Satō treat it as an actional
perfect — a past event with present relevance — which yields a past reading but is not a tense
category bugaeva2012 493 ; sato2006 63 . Nakagawa, following
Yoshikawa's reanalysis, denies that a encodes tense or aspect at all,
taking it to mark an event set in contrast to what follows rather than to locate that event in
past time nakagawa2024 306–307 ; yoshikawa2022 ‹contested›. The
full debate is developed in perfective-anterior-a ; what this chapter records is
that even the form most likely to encode tense is not analysed as a tense marker by the modern
descriptive consensus.

Temporal adverbs and discourse anchoring

When explicit temporal location is required, the primary device is a free temporal adverb.
numan yesterday and nisatta tomorrow are the basic past- and
future-anchoring items; comparable expressions specify this morning, last year, and other
reference points nakagawa2024 434–435 . The adverb occurs freely in
clause-initial or preverbal position, and the bare verb it modifies requires no additional
tense marking. Temporal information is distributed across the clause: the adverb locates the
event, and the verb contributes its aspectual character.

nisatta kunneywa tunas soyne=an nankor kusu | tomorrow morning early go.out=4.S probably because | Tomorrow morning I will probably go out early — | The temporal adverb nisatta 'tomorrow' supplies the future anchor; the epistemic auxiliary nankor adds probability. The verb soyne=an 'go out' carries no future inflection. | nakagawa2024:310 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 468

In narrative, discourse position does the anchoring work that temporal adverbs do in speech. A
story-world frame established at the opening locates subsequent bare verbs as past without any
overt marker; transitions in scene or topic are often marked by the completive
a , which places the preceding event in the background before the shift
nakagawa2024 306–307 ; yoshikawa2022 . This relative,
discourse-anchored time reference — where temporal interpretation tracks a reference point
established in prior context rather than the absolute speech moment — is the dominant mode of
temporal location in Hokkaido Ainu nakagawa2024 436 .

Aspect and completion forms as temporal anchors

Three of the four overt strategies for temporal location recruit grammatical forms whose
primary value is aspectual, modal, or evidential; the temporal reading is an inference
nakagawa2024 434–436 . The situational evidential siri
anchors a visible, ongoing event in the present: a clause closed by
siri ne profiles an event the speaker observes unfolding at the moment
of speech nakagawa2024 435 ; its own description belongs to
siri-ne-situational .

The progressive kor an progressive and resultative wa an resultative
do not specify tense, but in narrative context they deliver recognizable temporal orientations.
kor an presents an action as ongoing within the story frame and so
reads as past from the perspective of the outside hearer; wa an
presents the state left after a change and reads similarly. The example below illustrates both
the progressive and the bare existential in a narrative setting where the past reading is
entirely a function of discourse context.

apeoy anak an hine ape ruyruy kor an | hearth TOP exist CONJ fire burn.vigorously while exist | The hearth was there, and the fire was burning vigorously. | Neither the bare an 'exist' nor the kor an progressive carries a tense morpheme; the past reading comes from the narrative reference frame. Both an tokens are zero-marked third person. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#52]

The completion forms — a / rok , the recent perfect
nisa recent perfect , and the pair verbs wa isam and
wa okere — carry the strongest past-temporal implicature, because a
completed event is canonically prior to the speech moment. Nakagawa notes the limit explicitly:
these forms yield a temporal reading "only relative to the utterance moment" and "are not, in
themselves, tense forms"; context can override the default past reading
nakagawa2024 436 . A past English gloss for a bare verb or a
kor an clause renders the discourse context, not a grammatical tense
feature.

Prospective and future reference

Future reference draws on modal forms rather than a future tense.
epistemic-irrealis-modality The prospective–intentive
kusu ne intend to; be scheduled to marks the speaker's intention or a scheduled
event; oasi surely will marks an event as certainly forthcoming;
etokus be about to indicates imminence; and
etokoyki be getting ready to marks incipient preparation
nakagawa2024 435 ; sato2008 85–86 . Each carries its modal
value — intention, scheduled certainty, imminence — and the future temporal reading follows
as pragmatic inference.

an epitta paye=an kusu ne na | one throughout go=4.S PURP COP FIN | I intend to travel through the night. | kusu ne supplies the intentive reading; the future temporal implication follows from the modal value. The verb paye=an 'go' carries no future inflection. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2 [aa-irc/004#51]

Ijäs's pedagogical grammar covers the same set under 'be going to, intend to, plan to',
correctly grouping them as modal rather than tense, and observes that
nankor with a future-oriented proposition yields a probability reading
ijas2023 . The nisatta … nankor example above illustrates
the modal–adverb combination that together delivers an unambiguous future reading without any
future tense marker. The segmentation of kusu ne as the conjunction
kusu plus the copula ne — rather than a single
morpheme — is followed here in preference to the one-word pedagogical spelling
kusune nakagawa2024 435 ; sato2008 85–86 ;
ijas2023 .

Typological placement

Tenseless languages — in which tense is not a grammaticalized, obligatory category of the verb
— are well attested cross-linguistically, and the universality of tense is not confirmed by the
typological record. Bybee, Perkins, and Pagliuca, surveying tense, aspect, and modality across
76 languages, find that many languages mark temporal reference optionally or through aspect
alone, encoding it in the same formal position as mood and evidentiality rather than through a
dedicated tense paradigm bybee1994 . Comrie's foundational account of aspect
explicitly separates the aspectual category from tense precisely because many languages
grammaticalize the former without the latter comrie1976 . Hokkaido Ainu falls
in this class: its verb's grammatical categories are aspect, modality, and evidentiality, none
of which is tense.

Within the typological space of tenseless systems, Ainu's aspect-prominent profile is
characteristic: the verb's grammatical distinctions map onto aspectual, modal, and evidential
values, and temporal reference is delegated to peripheral adverbs and contextual inference.
The grammaticalization sources of the aspect forms — the existential
an exist feeding the progressive and resultative frames, the posture verb
a sit feeding the perfective auxiliary — are intransitive verbs that carry no
tense meaning and acquire none through grammaticalization. The system as a whole confirms that
the absence of tense is a primary fact about the language rather than a surface disguise for a
covert category nakagawa2024 306–308, 439–443 ; yoshikawa2022 ;
bybee1994 .

title: Topic and Focus Marking: anak(ne), patek, and ka
summary: The topic/contrastive-topic particle anak~anakne and the focus-sensitive particles patek 'only' and ka 'also/even': thematic vs contrastive topic, restrictive/additive/scalar focus, and the NPI use of ka.
part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Topic and Focus Marking: anak(ne), patek, and ka
Part: Part XVIII — Information Structure, Sentence-Final Particles, and Minor Classes
Summary: The topic/contrastive-topic particle anak~anakne and the focus-sensitive particles patek 'only' and ka 'also/even': thematic vs contrastive topic, restrictive/additive/scalar focus, and the NPI use of ka.

The delimiting-particle (副助詞) layer

Hokkaido Ainu encodes information structure — topic, focus, and contrast — through a class of
delimiting particles (副助詞) that adjoin to a noun phrase, postpositional phrase, or
verb phrase and add meaning without changing the host's grammatical category
nakagawa2024 316, §9.4 ; fukuda1961 §2 ;
sato2008 61, §7.1 . The language carries no morphological case on core
arguments and no topic or focus inflection on the verb; the agreement a verb shows with its
arguments is governed by valency, not by whether one of those arguments has been focus- or
topic-marked nakagawa2024 316–319 . The word-order consequences of topic
marking — the strong tendency for a topicalized phrase to occur clause-initially, and the
patterns of left- and right-dislocation — are treated in
pragmatic-word-order-dislocation ; the present chapter is restricted to the
particles themselves and their semantic scope.

One structural property shared by all delimiting particles is that they cannot host a
clause-linking suffix directly when they follow a verb phrase. The verb phrase must first be
re-verbalised by the dummy light transitive ki do before the connective
attaches ( ki -support). Fukuda's foundational treatment of the Saru particle system
already identifies this as the single clearest diagnostic of the 副助詞 class
fukuda1961 §2 ; nakagawa2024 317, 320 .

particle
function
attachment
dialect note

anak / anakne
topic / contrastive topic 'as for …'
NP, PP, VP
pan-Hokkaido

patek
restrictive focus 'only, nothing but'
NP, VP (ki-support), AdvP
pan-Hokkaido

takup / takupi
'only' (more noun-like; see §)
NP, VP (ne-support)
W. Hokkaido (Horobetsu, Saru, Chitose)

poka / pokay
minimal threshold 'at least, if not more'
NP

poka W. Hokkaido; pokay Samani,
Tokachi, Shiranuka, Asahikawa

ka / kay
additive / scalar focus 'also, even'
NP, VP
kay Samani / Tokachi / Shiranuka / Kushiro

hem / hemem
plain additive 'also, and'
NP
Saru / Chitose; hemem mostly Horobetsu

hene / ene
'for example; or … or …'
NP
ene Tokachi

Sources for all rows: nakagawa2024 320–330, §9.4.2 ; fukuda1961 §4–14 ;
sato2008 62–68 . The kakari-musubi emphatic concord particles
( ta , tasi , tap ) and the
focus-concord sentence-final particles that pair with them are treated in
sentence-final-particles-illocutionary-force ; the nominalization-based focus
construction ( …p ne , …hi ne ) is in
cleft-nominalization-focus .

anak and anakne: the topic particle

Form and attachment

anak and anakne mark the 提題 ('topic-raising') function: a phrase is
singled out as the current topic of talk and implicitly set in contrast to other possible
topics nakagawa2024 320, §9.4.2① . Nakagawa and Satō agree that the two
forms carry no difference in meaning: "whether anak and
anakne differ at all is an open question" sato2008 64 ;
nakagawa2024 320 . The particle follows its host immediately. Three attachment
sites are documented: after a bare noun phrase, after a postpositional phrase (noun + relational
noun), and after a verb phrase that functions as the topic of the sentence
nakagawa2024 320–321 . Ijäs's online pedagogical grammar gives a
learner-oriented account of the same functions ijas2023 .

imi anak neun oka yakka pirka korka e=kewtumu a=ramosma wa ne | clothes TOP how exist even.if good but 2SG.POSS=heart 4.A=please CONJ COP | The clothes, whatever they are like, are fine; your heart is what pleased me. | anak after a bare NP (imi 'clothes'); the topic phrase is separated from its comment by the adversative korka 'but'. | nakagawa2024:321 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 515

The particle does not mark the grammatical role of its host. A topicalized phrase may be the
agent, the patient, or an oblique of the comment predicate, and the verb's person agreement
is determined entirely by its valency, not by topic status. This is consistent with the
absence of morphological case on core arguments and with the head-marking profile of the
language described in person-marking-architecture : it is the verb that tracks
participants through its prefixal paradigm, and a separate topic particle does not alter
that tracking.

Topic versus contrastive topic

Satō distinguishes two uses of a single form: plain topic (主題) and contrastive topic
(対照) sato2008 62, §7.2 . In contrastive use, two phrases each receive
anak or anakne and the comment clauses are set in explicit opposition,
typically with korka but :

ku=nu hi anak k=eramuan pe ne korka tasa itak anak k=eranpewtek | 1SG.A=hear NMLZ TOP 1SG.S=know NMLZ COP but reply speech TOP 1SG.S=not.know | The question I understand, but the answer I don't know. | Two anak-marked phrases in explicit contrast; each heads a separate predicate clause. | sato2008:62 | CHI

A contrastive pair does not require both members to be overtly topic-marked. In the
following example the first member ( wakka water ) bears
anak , but the second ( sake sake ) carries no particle; the
contrast is established by the korka but connective and the parallel
structure sato2008 62–63 :

wakka anak ku=ku rusuy ka somo ki korka sake ku=ku rusuy wa | water TOP 1SG.A=drink DES even NEG do but sake 1SG.A=drink DES FIN | I don't want to drink water, but I do want to drink sake. | First contrastive member (wakka) is topic-marked; the second (sake) is not. The particle ka reinforces the negation on the first member with a scalar 'not even' reading. | sato2008:62–63 | CHI

The given-information restriction

anak requires a referent that the hearer can already be assumed to know
sato2008 63 . Satō illustrates with a pointing context: a demonstrative
like toan pe 'that thing' is situationally identifiable but is being
raised as a topic for the first time, so anak is blocked even though the
referent is visible. The constraint is sharper than Japanese 「は」, which permits
'あれは何だ?' in the same situation; Ainu does not allow the equivalent construction
sato2008 63 ; nakagawa2024 320 . A participant already
mentioned or recoverable from context can freely be topic-marked; given that Hokkaido Ainu
regularly omits core arguments when they are contextually recoverable (see
reference-tracking ), anak signals that the referent is being made
the active topic in contrast to something else, not merely that it is recoverable.

Scope and weight compared with Japanese wa

Nakagawa notes that anak is used considerably less than Japanese 「は」: a
word-for-word substitution of 「は」 with anak produces unnatural Ainu sentences
nakagawa2024 320 . The particle carries something of the weight of
'〜というものに関しては' ('as for the thing that is X'), marking a topic that is actively
contrasted with alternatives. Once a topic has been established in discourse, it need not be
re-marked on subsequent clauses within the same topic chain. This assessment is corroborated
by community linguistic discussion: the particle is reserved for contexts where singling-out
or contrast is genuinely intended, and omitting it when topic continuity is clear from context
is the natural choice (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-11-09;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›.

Person affixes and independent pronouns

A delimiting particle cannot attach to a person-agreement prefix. Those prefixes are clitics
that form a morphological unit with the stem; they are not free words and cannot host a
postposition or particle. Topicalizing a first- or second-person participant therefore
requires the free independent pronoun ( kani I ,
eani you (sg.) , etc.) as the syntactic host
nakagawa2024 253, ex.323 . The construct
*ku= anakne or *en= anakne , where the
particle would attach to a bare prefix, is ungrammatical
nakagawa2024 253 :

kani anakne Sikot ta k=an menoko ka somo ne kusu | 1SG TOP Chitose LOC 1SG.S=be woman even NEG COP because | As for me, I am not even a woman from Chitose — (so …). | The free 1SG pronoun kani hosts anakne; the clitic ku= could not. The clause is a reason clause; its continuation is implicit in context. | nakagawa2024:256 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 323

The same constraint applies to all persons. For the contrastive topicalization of a
fourth-person or logophoric referent through the free pronoun
asinuma self (logophoric) , see
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm , and for the full independent-pronoun
paradigm, independent-personal-pronouns .

The corpus supplies further examples with possessed NPs as topic. In the following passage
the narrator's dog is established as the topic of a multi-clause stretch by
anakne on the possessed NP:

a=kor seta anakne i=oyakke oma ka somo ki p ne kusu i=sam ta ukasuy ipe=an | 4.A=have dog TOP 4.O=go.far go even NEG do NMLZ COP because 4.O=vicinity LOC together eat=4.S | My dog never went far from me, so we ate together beside each other. | anakne attaches to the possessed NP a=kor seta 'my dog'; the topic discourse-tracks the dog across the following clauses. ka reinforces the negation on the embedded predicate. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakamu Matsuko (川上まつ子), uwepeker (AA研アイヌ語資料)

anak versus anakne and diachrony

Both forms function identically in the texts, but the frequency picture is contradictory
across sources. Satō, working from Chitose texts, reports that
anakne occurs roughly twice as often as anak
sato2008 64 , while the whole aligned Hokkaido corpus (over 1.5 million
tokens) gives the reverse — approximately 9,355 tokens of anak against 4,789 of
anakne . Satō's counts cover a single dialect and a small text set; the corpus
aggregates all Hokkaido varieties without disambiguating functions. Whether there is any
conditioned distribution between the two forms remains open ‹contested›.

Etymologically, anak derives from an-yak — the
existential verb an plus the conditional/contrastive particle
yak — as recorded in the Saru dictionary tamura1996 s.v. anak
(corroborated by nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-08;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹speculative›. anakne adds
ne (the copula or a reinforcing particle) to the same base. The corresponding
Sakhalin forms have been proposed as neyke or
neanpe (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-27;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹speculative›; the correspondence remains to be established
through systematic comparative work.

Restrictive focus: patek and its near-synonyms

patek marks the exclusion of all alternatives: the domain of the focus contains
one item and nothing else nakagawa2024 327–328, §9.4.2④ ;
fukuda1961 §9 ; sato2008 68 . The particle has a high degree
of autonomy: it may attach to a noun phrase, an adverb phrase, or a verb phrase (with
ki -support before any following connective), and it can carry its own accent and
appear without a following clause. Tamura (via nakagawa2024 327 )
places patek at the boundary between adverb and delimiting particle for this
reason.

cis patek a=ki kor an=an pe ne ru-we ne a p | cry only 4.A=do while exist=4.S NMLZ COP track-POSS COP PFV NMLZ | (She) was doing nothing but crying, and yet … | patek follows a VP (cis 'cry'); ki-support is required before the sequential connective kor. | nakagawa2024:328 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 548

Two near-synonyms with different morphosyntactic behaviour are documented for parts of
Hokkaido. takup (possessed form takupi ), restricted to western
Hokkaido (Horobetsu, Saru, Chitose), is more noun-like than patek : after a
verb phrase it requires ne -support (the copula, not
ki ), it has a possessed-form variant, and it cannot follow an
adverb phrase — properties that place it between noun, 名助詞, and 副助詞
nakagawa2024 328–329 ; fukuda1961 §10 .

poka (eastern variant pokay ) denotes the minimal threshold: 'at
least, even just this much'. Its use is restricted to contexts where the speaker demands
realisation of a minimum requirement or embeds it in a negation; it does not appear with
already-accomplished events nakagawa2024 329 ; fukuda1961 §7 .
poka is the western form; pokay is found at
Samani, Tokachi, Shiranuka, and Asahikawa nakagawa2024 329 .

A community observation notes that traditional Hokkaido Ainu texts favour
ani patek (instrumental ani + patek ) while
modern community usage shows increased patek ani . The order reversal
may reflect the influence of Japanese 「だけ」, which derives from a grammaticalized noun and
can appear as a free NP before に ; patek cannot function
as a noun headword, so the rising frequency of patek ani is
interpreted as contact-induced reanalysis (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-16;
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›. The core point — that patek does
not nominalize — is consistent with Nakagawa's and Fukuda's analyses
nakagawa2024 327 ; fukuda1961 §9 .

Additive and scalar focus: ka, hem, and hene

ka: additive, scalar, and NPI-forming

ka (southeastern Hokkaido variant kay ) adds the host to a
contextually established set and carries a scalar or contrary-to-expectation force: 'also,
even, and — perhaps surprisingly'
nakagawa2024 321–322, §9.4.2② ; fukuda1961 §5 ;
sato2008 64–65 . Fukuda formulates its Saru semantics as 'beyond or
contrary to expectation' fukuda1961 §5 , and Satō illustrates with
examples carrying emotional weight: 'even a dog won't eat it'; 'even two or three young men
came' sato2008 64 . This distinguishes ka from the plain additive
hem (§ below).

In negative clauses, ka reinforces the negation with a scalar 'not even'
reading. When two or more noun phrases each carry ka , the construction
lists members of a set all of which fall within the scope of the negation:

seta ka nep ka isam | dog FOC thing FOC not.exist | There was neither a dog nor anything at all. | Both seta 'dog' and nep (indefinite 'thing/what') are marked with ka; doubled ka under negation yields total exclusion. | nakagawa2024:322–323 | SAR

The combination of an indefinite pro-form with ka produces an indefinite or
negative-polarity series: nep ka 'something / anything',
nen ka 'someone / anyone',
ney ta ka 'somewhere / anywhere'; under negation these shift to
'nothing / no-one / nowhere'
nakagawa2024 322–323 ; sato2008 137 .
The interface between ka and the interrogative pro-form system —
including the question-word vs indefinite-word distinction — is treated in
interrogative-pro-forms . For the evidential use of the same pro-forms in
content questions, see evidential-schema-overview .

hem, hemem, and hene: plain additive and alternative focus

hem (Saru / Chitose; Horobetsu variant hemem ) is the plain additive
particle: it adds one more item to a list with no scalar or contrary-to-expectation
implication. In a paired construction X hem Y hem 'both X and Y',
each member carries hem and the reading is symmetrical addition
nakagawa2024 321–322 ; fukuda1961 §4 . The particle is
attested only in Saru and Chitose; elsewhere plain juxtaposition or, in Tokachi and
Kushiro, the coordinator usa serves the same function.

hene (Tokachi variant ene ) introduces alternatives or illustrative
examples: 'for example; or … or …'. A repeated hene … hene
presents two or more members as mutually interchangeable options, as in
nisatta hene oyasim hene 'tomorrow or the day after'
nakagawa2024 323–324 ; fukuda1961 §6 ;
sato2008 65 .

title: Transitivity, Valence Classes, and Ambitransitivity
summary: Lexical classification of verbs by valence (intransitive agentive/patientive, transitive, ditransitive, complete/incomplete) and the system of labile (ambitransitive) verbs and morphological transitivity pairs (有対).
part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Transitivity, Valence Classes, and Ambitransitivity
Part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
Summary: Lexical classification of verbs by valence (intransitive agentive/patientive, transitive, ditransitive, complete/incomplete) and the system of labile (ambitransitive) verbs and morphological transitivity pairs (有対).

Transitivity as a formal property

Hokkaido Ainu classifies its verbs by the number and kind of core arguments each licenses,
and the classification is decided by structure rather than by the meaning of a translation
nakagawa2024 125–127 ; sato2008 96 . Two diagnostics fix a
verb's class. The first is the count of core arguments, where a core argument is a bare noun
phrase carrying no postposition or case particle; a phrase marked by a local postposition is
adverbial and can be added without altering the count, so that in
cise or ta menoko hoku tura ipe 'in the house a woman ate with her
husband' only menoko is an argument of ipe
nakagawa2024 127 . The second diagnostic is person marking: an intransitive
verb indexes a single subject, while a transitive verb may index a subject and an object
nakagawa2024 126 . The indexing system that supports both diagnostics is the
subject of person-marking-architecture , and the order of indexes inside the verb
word of verb-word-template .

Three descriptive vocabularies coexist in current work and must be kept apart. Nakagawa, with
Bugaeva and Kobayashi, prefers numeric labels — 0-, 1-, 2-, and 3-argument verb — because the
bare-noun-phrase count and the intransitive/transitive person-affix fact do not always line up
and are best named separately; Satō, Tamura, and Ijäs retain the traditional terms
intransitive, transitive, and ditransitive nakagawa2024 128 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §3 ; sato2008 94–98 . The abbreviations
used here are structural labels and carry no claim about semantic agency: an intransitive verb
may take an agent-like or an affected subject, and both receive the same subject index, a point
developed in alignment-split-intransitive .

class
core frame
numeric label (Nakagawa; Bugaeva & Kobayashi)
traditional label (Satō, Tamura, Ijäs)
examples

v0
no bare-NP argument
0-place / 0-argument
complete / avalent verb
sirpirka , mean

vi
S
1-place / 1-argument
intransitive
inkar , apkas , ipe

vt
A + O
2-place / 2-argument
transitive (including the copula)
nukar , koyki , ne

vc
A/S + bare locative object
2- or 3-place subtype
locative-object verb
oma , osma , kus

vd
A + O + O
3-place / 3-argument
ditransitive
kore , epakasnu , o

Ijäs's Lessons 2 and 3 introduce the same inventory for learners as avalent, intransitive,
transitive, and ditransitive verbs, and present Hokkaido Ainu as a pro-drop, verb-final,
head-marking language whose verb has argument "pockets" that are filled before, and more
obligatorily than, any external noun-phrase "slot" ijas2023 . That image renders
head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 and matches Satō's argument-slot
account of the same fact sato2023b 42–46 . Two background conditions stated in
the same lessons frame the classification: verb forms carry no tense inflection, and nouns are
number-neutral unless plurality is supplied lexically or by context, so that
cep 'fish' may be read as singular or plural ijas2023 ;
nakagawa2024 124–125 ; shibatani1990 32–33 . Number enters the
verbal system separately, through stem alternation and the plural clitics treated in
suppletive-verbal-number .

Person marking as the transitivity test

The person rows that make the intransitive/transitive contrast visible in surface form are the
first-plural exclusive and the fourth (indefinite) person. On an intransitive verb these
appear as subject suffixes, =as and =an ; on a
transitive verb the corresponding subject markers are prefixes, ci= and
a= . Satō gives the operational test in these terms: put the verb in the
first-person plural, and inkar look yields inkar=as
while nukar see yields ci=nukar sato2008 96 .
He notes that this side-switch is highly irregular and still without a convincing explanation
sato2008 96 . The remaining rows are uninformative: the first singular uses the
same prefix ku= for subject of either class, the second person is
neutral across subject and object, and the third person has no overt segment in any role.

person
S (intransitive subject)
A (transitive subject)
O (object)
diagnostic value

1SG
ku=
ku=
en=

1PL.EXCL

2SG

e=

2PL

eci=

4 / INDEF

side-switch: diagnoses transitivity

The pair inkar and nukar is the standard
demonstration that a translation is an unreliable guide to class. Both render English visual
predicates, but inkar names an intransitive act of directing the gaze
with no fixed object, whereas nukar takes a specified object. In one
clause chain the fourth person therefore surfaces as a suffix on the intransitive stem and as a
prefix on the transitive stem nakagawa2024 131 .

inkar=an akus e=an ru-we a=nukar hi kusu | look=4.S then 2SG.S=exist track-POSS 4.A=see NMLZ because | When I looked, since I saw that you were there, ... | The intransitive inkar takes the fourth-person S suffix =an; the transitive nukar takes the fourth-person A prefix a=. The possessed formal noun ru-we nominalizes e=an as the object of a=nukar. | nakagawa2024:131 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 18

Translation cannot decide class because the verb itself carries the argument structure. Ijäs
illustrates the ordinary transitive frame with a third-person subject and a third-person
object: neither is segmentally marked, yet the two bare noun phrases fill the A and O positions
of a two-place verb ijas2023 . The verb word indexes at most one subject and one
object — the two-index cap — even where a clause has more arguments than that, a limit that
becomes load-bearing for three-place verbs and for the debates of the final section
sato2023b 39 .

numan hekaci cep koyki | yesterday boy fish catch | The boy caught fish yesterday. | Ijäs, Lesson 3. A two-place transitive frame with two bare noun phrases and a segmentally unmarked third-person verb. | ijas2023

Zero-place and one-place verbs

The zero-place class contains predicates with no grammatical subject and no object. Most are
ambient or weather expressions built on a bound element such as sir-
'surroundings' or a non-free root plus a verb: sirpirka 'be fine
weather', sirkunne 'get dark', and mean 'be cold
(weather)', the last analysable as me- 'cold' plus
an 'exist' nakagawa2024 128–130 ; Shibatani analyses the
type as subject incorporation shibatani1990 33 . Ijäs groups the same
predicates as avalent and notes that descriptive meanings belong to the verbal system, since
Hokkaido Ainu has no adjective class distinct from verbs and a stative verb such as
hure 'be red' carries the inchoative reading 'become red' as well
ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 124 ; sato2008 2 . Where the
contributing element is itself a free noun the analysis is open: Ijäs treats
apto as 'it rains' as a single avalent unit, whereas Nakagawa leaves it
undecided, since apto 'rain' is an autonomous noun and the string may be
a two-word subject-plus-verb clause nakagawa2024 129–130 ; ijas2023
‹contested›.

The boundary between zero-place and one-place verbs is sharpest in the temperature vocabulary.
Climate predicates take no person index; the felt-temperature counterparts take a subject.

semantic field
v0: ambient predicate
vi: subject-felt predicate

cold
mean
merayke

cool
sirmeman
meman

warm
sirpopke
popke

hot
sirsesek
sesek

mean cuk ne pe merayke=an ka somo ki no | be.cold.weather autumn COP NMLZ feel.cold=4.S even NEG do ADV | It was a cold autumn, but I did not feel cold. | The ambient v0 predicate mean carries no person index; the felt-temperature vi merayke takes the fourth-person S suffix =an, both in one sentence. | nakagawa2024:130 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 15, after Kayano 1974a

One-place verbs cover agentive activities such as apkas 'walk' and
ipe 'eat a meal', changes of state such as rewke
'bend', and properties such as pirka 'be good' and
pewre 'be young'. The single argument is grammatical and need not be a
semantic agent: a patientive subject still takes the subject index, as in
rittursere=an 'I am rolled by the waves' and
ku=merayke 'I feel cold', where the affected participant is marked with
the ordinary S set nakagawa2024 126–127 . The active/unaccusative cut does
resurface, but in derivation and incorporation rather than in the person paradigm, and it does
not split the intransitive subject marking bugaevakobayashi2022 §5 .

Two- and three-place verbs

A two-place verb licenses a subject and a single object, both bare noun phrases, whatever the
translation suggests about patient, goal, or other role. Hokkaido Ainu draws no formal
distinction between a direct and an indirect object: what a Japanese rendering marks with
を or に is the same bare object in Ainu
nakagawa2024 127 . This is why cep ipe cannot mean 'eat
fish': ipe is the intransitive 'eat a meal', and a specified food object
requires a transitive such as e 'eat (something)'
nakagawa2024 127 . Several near-basic pairs show that close semantic
correspondence does not entail formal identity; the most basic of them have to be learned as
distinct stems sato2008 97 ; nakagawa2024 131 .

meaning
vi (one-place)
vt (two-place)

see / look
inkar
nukar

hear
inu
nu

steal
ikka
eikka

laugh (at)
mina
emina

eat
ipe
e

say / speak
itak
ye

think
yaynu
ramu

The copula ne belongs with the two-place verbs in argument structure,
relating a subject to a predicate nominal, and it takes transitive-type subject marking. Its
person behaviour is exceptional, however: it indexes only the subject and admits no object
index, so that okkayo ku=ne 'I am a man' is well formed while an
object-indexed *en=ne is not nakagawa2024 132 ;
shibatani1990 50 . Predicate-nominal syntax is treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals , and the copula reappears in the final section as
Satō's central case for distinguishing several measures of valence.

okkayo ku=ne | man 1SG.A=COP | I am a man. | The copula is two-place but indexes only its subject, and with transitive-type marking; an object index such as *en=ne is excluded. | nakagawa2024:132 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 23

Three-place verbs license a subject and two bare objects, both omissible and without a fixed
grammatical order between them. With kore 'give', the theme and the
recipient are both bare third-person phrases and only the subject is indexed below. Almost all
three-place verbs are derived, by an applicative such as ko- or the
causative -re , the chief base-form member being the locative
o 'put X into a place' bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2 ;
nakagawa2024 134 . Satō analyses the object index on
kore as tracking the recipient, a secundative pattern, a point on which
Bugaeva's account differs; the indexing options are set out in
object-indexing-monotransitive and argument-realization-indexing
‹contested›.

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | Both non-subject arguments are bare third-person noun phrases; only the 1SG.A subject is indexed. topenpe ku=kore and hekattar ku=kore are identical in form, the を/に contrast belonging to the Japanese translation. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

Ijäs presents the ditransitive frame as subject, indirect object, and direct object, with the
recipient placed before the theme ijas2023 . Nakagawa's examples of
epakasnu 'teach' show the two objects and the subject in several
preverbal orders with one set of argument roles, so the recipient-before-theme sequence is a
teaching convention rather than a grammatical requirement nakagawa2024 363–365 .
The productive operations that build and rearrange these frames are surveyed in
valency-voice-overview .

Locative-object verbs

A small closed class takes a place as a bare object, with no ta or
or ta . These locative-object verbs cut across the two- and three-place
distinction: oma 'be in, enter a place', osma
'plunge into a place', and kus 'pass through a place' are two-place,
while o 'put X into a place' is three-place and is the chief base-form
ditransitive of the language nakagawa2024 212–213 . The defining property is the
absence of a local postposition, so that *cise or ta osma is
ungrammatical against intransitive cise or ta ahun 'enter into the
house', where ta is required nakagawa2024 128 .

verb
meaning
arity

oma
be in / enter a place
+2

osma
plunge into a place
+2

kus
pass through a place
+2

o
put something into a place
+3

upas tum a=oma wa karkarse=an | snow inside 4.A=enter CONJ roll=4.S | I sank into the snow and rolled. | The place expression upas tum is the bare object of oma; the following intransitive karkarse takes the fourth-person S suffix =an. | nakagawa2024:212 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 221

The place noun within such a frame works like a covert case marker. With
omare 'put X into a place', ikayop omare is read
as 'put the quiver somewhere', since a quiver is a theme, while
ikayop or omare is read as 'put something into the quiver', with
or supplying the locative object nakagawa2024 213 . The
applicative o- derives further locative-object verbs from non-locative
bases, and the local-postposition system that these verbs bypass is treated in
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events and applicative-o .

Arity and the derivational calculus

Nakagawa generalizes valence into an arithmetic over the verb word. Every element — the base
verb, each derivational affix, an incorporated noun, and each person index — carries an arity
value, and the sum predicts how many bare external arguments the surface predicate still
licenses nakagawa2024 136–140 . A base intransitive contributes +1, a base
transitive +2, and a base ditransitive +3; applicatives and the productive causative add +1; a
noun root or a concept-form prefix that fills an argument slot subtracts 1; a single person
index subtracts 1 and a subject-plus-object pair subtracts 2.

element
examples
arity

base vi
apkas , mokor
+1

base vt
kik , yasa
+2

base vd
o 'put X into a place'
+3

applicative prefix
e- , ko- , o-
+1

productive causative
-re , -e , -te
+1

noun root or concept-form prefix
me- , i- , yay- , u-
−1

affiliative part prefix
e- 'the head of', o- 'the bottom of'
0

adverbial prefix or plural suffix
ar- , ru- , -pa
0

one person index
ku= , en= , =an
−1

subject plus object indexes
a=en= , a=i=
−2

The calculus earns its keep where one segment has several functions. Built on intransitive
itak 'speak' (+1), the applicative ko- gives
ko-itak 'speak to' (+2), the subject index in
ku=ko-itak brings the count back to +1, and the reflexive
yay- in ku=yay-ko-itak fills the object position
to leave 0, so that a further bare object is excluded
nakagawa2024 138 . The same arithmetic separates the three uses of prefixal
e- : it must be the applicative (+1) in
a=ureepunpa 'I kicked it up with my foot' for the count to admit a bare
object, and the affiliative prefix (0) in a=koinawepunpa 'we offered
inau' nakagawa2024 138–139 . Satō credits this arity concept as Nakagawa's
central theoretical contribution sato2025b 20 . The valency-changing operations
that feed the calculus — the applicatives, the causatives, the antipassive
i- , and the reflexive and reciprocal prefixes — are catalogued in
valency-voice-overview , causative-morphological-re-e-te ,
applicative-system-overview , antipassive-detransitive-i ,
reflexive-yay , and reciprocal-u
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4 ; bugaeva2021antip ; nam2021 .

One commitment of the system bears directly on the debates below. Third person contributes no
affix and therefore no negative value; assigning −1 to an unpronounced third-person affix would
throw off the arithmetic, so Nakagawa treats third person as having arity 0
nakagawa2024 137 . Number morphology is likewise arity-neutral, while
causatives and applicatives change the verb's class, so the verbal-number system can be kept
apart from valence and is developed in suppletive-verbal-number
nakagawa2024 137, 140 .

Ambitransitivity and lability

Hokkaido Ainu has little productive ambitransitivity of the English type. A single bare form
serving as both intransitive and transitive is vanishingly rare; Satō's inventory of
correspondence types yields effectively one identical-form pair, the plural
roski 'stand' and 'stand something up'. The other pairs show either
partial derivational relatedness, as with transitive nunpa 'wring'
beside intransitive inunpa , or no formal resemblance at all, as in the
basic vocabulary of the preceding table sato2008 97 . Valence alternation is
thus coded by derivation, incorporation, or a separate lexical stem rather than by lability.

What looks like lability is in most cases an uncoded alternation of semantic role within a
fixed argument count. Bugaeva and Kobayashi, after Kobayashi's work on valency, reduce the
roles to agent, theme, and space-location, and observe that some intransitives admit either an
agent or a theme as their single argument: as 'stand' takes a standing
person or a standing tree, whereas apkas 'walk' is agentive only and
tuy 'be cut' theme-like only bugaevakobayashi2022 §5.1.1 .
The alternation becomes visible in causativization, where a role-flexible intransitive forms
two causatives: the direct asi 'stand something up' for the theme
reading and the indirect aste 'make someone stand' for the agent reading
bugaevakobayashi2022 §5 . The same active/theme split governs which noun a verb
can incorporate, a domain pursued in noun-incorporation-object .

Verbal number gives a further reflex of the formal nature of transitivity. The intransitive
plural stem tracks the number of the subject and behaves much like agreement in Nakagawa's
description, while the transitive plural stem tracks the plurality of the object or of the
event, the singular remaining the default even when a plural object is overt; Shibatani,
following Durie, treats the alternation as a selectional restriction on the principally
affected argument — subject of an intransitive, object of a transitive
nakagawa2024 142–145 ; sato2008 101 ;
shibatani1990 51–53 . A small set of cognition transitives is exceptional in
that the plural stem tracks the subject instead, as in the plural understanding verb below.

uomano eci=eramuoka ya | all 2PL.A=understand.PL Q | Do you all understand? | The transitive plural stem eramuoka (singular eramuan) here agrees with the plural subject, an exceptional subject-sensitive transitive plural noted by Nakagawa. | nakagawa2024:142 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 45

Arity against the three-valency analysis

What kind of valence the grammar should count is the central open question of this domain, and
the two leading positions are to be held side by side rather than adjudicated. Nakagawa's arity
is a single surface-computable property: the value of the final form predicts how many bare
noun phrases remain and how person indexes reduce that capacity, and it disambiguates
homophonous prefixes, incorporated nouns, and person indexes in the same calculation
nakagawa2024 136–140 .

Satō argues that one number collapses distinctions a head-marking language needs to keep, and
proposes three valencies operating at once. The first is the argument-slot count, an invariant
abstract property of the lexical or derived verb, filled equally by a person affix, a
nominal-derivational prefix, or an incorporated noun — so that incorporation is slot-filling
and needs no head-movement analysis of the kind associated with baker1988 .
The second is M-transitivity, the number of macroroles available for person indexing in the
sense of foleyvanvalin1984 , capped at two. The third is morphological
valency, the intransitive or transitive behaviour of the final form
sato2023b 42–52 . The copula displays all three: its argument-slot count is two,
but the predicate-nominal term is not a referential undergoer, so its M-transitivity is one and
it takes no object index, while its morphological valency is transitive, as the incorporating
nispa a=ne 'I am a master' shows. This derives the excluded
*en=ne from a principle sato2023b 52 . Satō notes that
his own scheme is complex and remains to be justified sato2023b 61 ‹contested›.

Two narrower disputes follow from the same divide. Nakagawa records a marginal four-place
category from causativized ditransitives such as epakasnure 'make
someone teach someone something', korere , and a textual
omarere 'made someone put something into something'
nakagawa2024 135 . Satō declines to raise so rare a configuration to a regular
class, given that the ordinary three-place verb already exceeds the two-index cap and is
syntactically special on its own sato2025b 22–23 ‹contested›. The second
dispute is the third-person zero. Nakagawa's arity excludes any third-person affix, while Satō
holds that a zero third-person affix is required to explain why a verb cannot host three person
indexes — why, for instance, *en-e-raykere 'he makes me kill you' is
impossible, the two overt affixes having already filled the cap with no room for the
third-person causer nakagawa2024 137 ; sato2023b 39 ;
sato2025b 23–24 ‹contested›. Ijäs's zero sign for the unmarked third person is
compatible with either side if it is read as the absence of an overt segment
ijas2023 .

The observable diagnostics remain bare-noun-phrase valence, person-index behaviour, and
derivational morphology, and these are stable across the analyses. Arity serves as the working
calculation for stem formation and argument realization; Satō's three-valency account is the
reference wherever the copula, the ditransitives, incorporation, or the zero-affix question
make a single count inadequate. The realization of overt and omitted arguments continues in
argument-realization-indexing , and the voice operations that alter these frames in
valency-voice-overview .

title: The Transitivizer -ka and Lexical Valency Pairs
summary: The transitivizer/causative -ka on stative bases, and the system of labile and suppletive causative–inchoative verb pairs.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Transitivizer -ka and Lexical Valency Pairs
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The transitivizer/causative -ka on stative bases, and the system of labile and suppletive causative–inchoative verb pairs.

Overview

Three morphological mechanisms build transitive verb forms from intransitive bases in
Hokkaido Ainu, all operating within the arity calculus set out in
valency-voice-overview . The oldest layer consists of stem-specific
forming suffixes — a vowel suffix -V or the suffix
-ke — that produce lexically listed transitivity pairs; these are
non-productive and the intransitive and transitive members are recorded as separate
dictionary entries nakagawa2024 198–199 . A second suffix,
-ka , overlaps in function with the forming suffixes but extends to
stative and property bases and to derived stems, placing it on the boundary between the
forming-suffix category and the productive direct causative; the literature has not settled
its classification bugaeva2014 129–130, 143 ;
kobayashi2015 33 ‹contested›. The third mechanism, the transitivizer
-kar , productively adds an object to any intransitive base
(arity +1) and must be distinguished from two surface-identical strings: a verse-filler
-kar appended to transitives with no arity effect, and the verb
kar make, do that appears as the head of incorporated-object constructions
such as cise-kar 'build a house'
nakagawa2024 209–210 .

The productive indirect causative -re/-te/-e , which supplies the
default transitivizing strategy and gradually displaces
-ka in the more southerly dialects, is treated separately in
causative-morphological-re-e-te . The valency classes and the transitivity
