test that diagnoses derived verb type are discussed in
transitivity-and-valence-classes .

Forming suffixes and lexical transitivity pairs

Nakagawa's paradigm tables 表38 and 表39 set out two structural patterns
nakagawa2024 198–199 . In the first, the intransitive is the bare root and
the transitive adds a final vowel (a suffix -V that assimilates to
the stem shape). In the second, the intransitive already carries
-ke and the transitive replaces it with the vowel suffix. For the
motion-verb class the pattern reverses: the bare root is intransitive and
-ke marks the transitive. Table 1 samples all three patterns,
ending with the -ka pair discussed in the following section.

Table 1. Selected Hokkaido Ainu transitivity pairs (Nakagawa 2024: 198–203)

intransitive
transitive
transitive suffix
approximate sense

tuy
tuy-e
-V
'be severed' / 'cut sth'

kay
kay-e
-V
'go around' / 'go around sth'

as
as-i
-V
'stand' / 'erect sth'

rew-ke
rew-e
-ke on intr. / -V on tr.
'come out' / 'let sth out'

yas-ke
yas-a
-ke on intr. / -V on tr.
'run' / 'send sth'

per-ke
per-e
-ke on intr. / -V on tr.
'split (vi)' / 'split sth'

ran
ran-ke
-ke
'descend' / 'lower sth'

ahun
ahun-ke
-ke
'enter' / 'put sth in'

yan
yan-ke
-ke
'come ashore' / 'land sth'

rikin
rikin-ka
-ka
'go up' / 'raise sth'

Bugaeva's 387-verb Saru sample counts 111 verbs with -V and 9
with -ke , classifying all as direct causatives on semantic grounds
bugaeva2014 130, 143 . The -V set is closed and
non-productive; no new pairs of this type are formed, and the members are listed
separately in the dictionaries tamura1996 . The three motion verbs that take
-ke as their transitive suffix ( ran ,
ahun , yan ) have plural counterparts formed
with the productive suffix -te instead
nakagawa2024 202–203 .

ran=an | descend=4.S | I went down. | Intransitive base; the fourth-person S suffix =an confirms intransitive status.

ku=ran-ke | 1SG.A=descend-CAUS | I lowered it. | Transitive counterpart of ran via the motion-verb forming suffix -ke. The 1SG.A prefix ku= marks the verb as transitive. The suffix -ke here selects non-agentive, inanimate objects; agentive human causees take the productive -te (compare Nakagawa 2024: 202–203, exx. 196–197 on ahun-ke vs ahun-te).

The suffix -ka: hosts, dialect distribution, and semantics

Among the motion-verb set, rikin go up (vi) takes
-ka as its transitive suffix in most Hokkaido dialects. Nakagawa's
表43 also lists a further set of verbs to which -ka attaches
without belonging to the motion-verb class: retar be white ,
us go out , iunin settle (vi) , and several
dialect-restricted forms ( teyne in Ishikari;
een in Yakumo, Saru, and Bihoro). The distribution across this
second group is a lexical property of the individual verbs, not a regional feature
nakagawa2024 203 .

In Saru, the pair nam-te ~ nam-ka 'make cold' shows
intra-dialectal competition between the productive suffix -te and
-ka on the same base
kobayashi2015 33 ; the analytical implications are taken up in the debate
section below. Cross-dialectally,
-ka covers a broader verb class in northeastern Hokkaido; the
replacement of -ka by -re/-te/-e is most
advanced in Saru, as Bugaeva's sample of 38 -ka verbs against
190 -re/-e/-te verbs shows
bugaeva2014 135–136, 143–144 .

rikin=an | go.up=4.S | I went up. | Intransitive base; =an is the fourth-person S suffix.

ku=rikin-ka | 1SG.A=go.up-CAUS | I raised it. | Transitive counterpart via -ka. The 1SG.A prefix ku= marks transitive status.

The semantic boundary between the forming-suffix causatives and the productive
-te/-re is visible on verbs that admit both: for
ahun enter , ahun-ke encodes direct causation with
a non-agentive, inanimate causee (putting luggage inside), while
ahun-te or ahun-re is used when the causee
is an agentive human brought inside
nakagawa2024 202–203 . The parallel holds for
-ka : it signals that the causee is treated as a highly affected
patient (direct causation), contrasting with the indirect or permissive readings
accessible to -re/-te/-e
bugaeva2014 130, 139 .

The suffix -ka also attaches outside the indirect reflexive prefix
si- to produce a 'pretend to' reading. Satō (2007) analyses this
construction within his account of si- as marking indirect
self-involvement: a pretense is only effective through others' perception, which is
precisely the situation the indirect reflexive encodes
sato2007 36 .

ku=ko-si-ranpewtek-ka wa k=an | 1SG.A=APPL-REFL-know.nothing-CAUS CONJ 1SG.S=exist | I pretended not to know about it. | Satō ex. 6, approximately p. 36 (the OCR of Satō 2007 carries no per-page running heads; the location is approximate within the pp. 31–39 article). The APPL prefix ko- supplies the semantic object (the thing one pretends not to know); si- is the indirect reflexive; -ka is the causative suffix. The following wa k=an reports the resulting state. | sato2007 | CHI

The transitivizer -kar

The suffix -kar productively transitivizes intransitive verbs
(arity +1): peray fish (vi) → peray-kar catch (a fish) ;
cis cry (vi) → cis-kar cry over sth ;
hotuye shout (vi) → hotuye-kar call sb
nakagawa2024 209 . Its productivity range is wider than that of
-ka : no restriction to a semantic class or phonological stem shape
applies, and derived forms can be created from any intransitive base.

Three surface-identical strings require disambiguation through the arity computation
nakagawa2024 209–210 . First, the transitivizer
-kar itself, raising arity by one on an intransitive base. Second,
a homophonous verse-filler -kar that appends to transitive stems
with no arity change and no semantic contribution — a poetic register device extending
syllable count: kocari (a three-place verb) remains three-place as
kocari-kar . Third, the verb kar 'make, do'
that appears as the head of incorporated-object constructions: in
cise-kar 'build a house', the noun cise
'house' fills the object slot of the two-place verb kar , and the
arity is that of kar itself
satoni2022 §3.1 .

Community observation identifies a productive 'treat-verb' pattern within the third
type: forms such as nimaw-e-kar 'treat a wound with medicinal
bark', osoro-kar 'wipe', and sikaye-kar
'hammer in a peg' each incorporate an instrument or body-part noun into
kar 'do'
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024).
These are incorporated-object constructions and belong to the account in
noun-incorporation-object , not to the transitivizer suffix ‹corpus-suggested›.

ku=hotuye-kar | 1SG.A=shout-TRVZ | I called someone. | The transitivizer -kar adds an object slot to the intransitive hotuye 'shout'. The 1SG.A prefix ku= marks the derived verb as transitive.

Direct causative or forming suffix: the analytical debate

Nakagawa treats the forming suffixes ( -V ,
-ke ) as 形成接尾辞 listed in 表38–39, a distinct category from the
causative suffixes of 表41–43, which include -re/-te/-e ,
-ke for motion verbs, and -ka
nakagawa2024 198–203 . The category distinction rests on closed membership
and the absence of productive alternation: the -V and
-ke pairs are dictionary-listed lexical alternants.

Bugaeva (2014: 129–130, 143) unifies -V ,
-ke , and -ka as direct causatives,
opposing them as a group to the indirect causative -re/-te/-e by
the non-agentive, highly-affected causee they encode. The 387-verb Saru count — 111 verbs
under -V , 9 under -ke , 38 under
-ka — establishes the proportions but does not settle the
morphological-status question independently
bugaeva2014 130, 143 .

Kobayashi (2015: 33) adduces evidence for separating
-ka from the most lexicalized forming suffixes. Two properties
distinguish it: intra-dialectal alternation with -re/-e/-te
(the nam-te ~ nam-ka Saru pair noted above), and attachment to
derived stems — ar-us-tek-ka 'annihilate' and
ipe-rusuy-ka 'make hungry' both show
-ka appended to a multi-morpheme derived stem, a combinatorial
freedom the closed -V and -ke forming
suffixes do not share kobayashi2015 33 . The
si-…-ka 'pretend' construction described above
provides a further case: -ka attaches outside the reflexive prefix
si- , which has already reconfigured the argument frame.

Satō (2008: 235–238) includes -ka in the causative allomorphy list
alongside -re , -te ,
-ke , and -e , stating that it tends to
follow stems in m or y — "大体 as a
tendency, with exceptions." The qualifier matters: a pure phonological allomorph would
show regular conditioning; the exceptions reflect a lexical residue that fits neither a
clean allomorphy account nor a uniform forming-suffix categorisation. Satō also notes that
some -ke and -e forms are bleached
transitivizers with no causative semantics
( san-ke 'put out a fine' from san 'go
out'), which aligns with Nakagawa's forming-suffix category and places the
productive/lexical boundary inside, not between, these suffixes
sato2008 235–238 .

Ijäs's Lesson 9 states that -re 'turns intransitive verbs into
transitive verbs', which captures the right intuition for the valency-increasing set as a
whole but supplies no allomorphy and does not distinguish
-ka , -ke , or the lexical forming-suffix
pairs as separate sub-types ijas2023 ‹consensus› (the source gives no
allomorphy detail).

Attachment to the base stem: rikinka and the absence of rikipka

The verb rikin go up (vi) belongs to a class of Hokkaido Ainu
motion-verb intransitives showing a -n / -p
stem alternation in reduplication and some plural contexts, paralleling
san / sap ,
yan / yap , and
ahun / ahup
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2026) ‹speculative›.
In reduplication contexts rikin alternates with
rikip .

When -ka attaches, only rikin-ka is
attested; the form *rikip-ka is absent from the corpus
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2026) ‹corpus-suggested›.
The distribution shows that -ka targets the citation form of the
stem — the singular -n variant — not the alternate
-p shape that arises in reduplicated environments. The attachment
point is the lexical base, prior to any stem alternation, a property shared by other
causative suffixes that affix to the verb stem before verbal-number morphology applies.
This behavior is consistent with Kobayashi's evidence that
-ka operates at the same morphological level as the productive
causative rather than with the most lexicalized forming suffixes
kobayashi2015 33 .

The diachronic account of the -V and
-ke / -ka pairs as anticausatives in
origin is explored in bugaeva2025 . The middle and anticausative patterns
that overlap synchronically with the transitivity pairs are treated in
middle-anticausative-passive , and deverbal derivation more broadly in
denominal-deverbal-verb-derivation .

title: A Typological Profile of Hokkaido Ainu
summary: A condensed bird's-eye sketch of the language's structural type, serving as a roadmap to the granular chapters that follow.
part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: A Typological Profile of Hokkaido Ainu
Part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
Summary: A condensed bird's-eye sketch of the language's structural type, serving as a roadmap to the granular chapters that follow.

The genetic isolate and its typological placement

Hokkaido Ainu has no demonstrated genetic relationship with any other language family
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §1 ; shibatani1990 §1 . Proposals connecting
Ainu to Japanese, Korean, Altaic branches (Tungusic, Mongolic, Turkic), Austronesian, Nivkh, or
Chukotko-Kamchatkan have each failed to produce the systematic sound correspondences and shared
morphology that genetic relationship requires. The fuller treatment of the isolate status and the
history of macro-comparison controversies belongs to genetic-position-macro-comparison .

The isolate argument has a structural dimension that goes beyond the absence of lexical
correspondences. Bugaeva's analysis frames Ainu as "a head-marking language of the Pacific Rim"
— after the framework of nichols1986 — and treats the structural mismatch between
Ainu's head-marking profile and the dependent-marking profile of all its geographical neighbours
as a positive argument against relatedness
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §2, §7 . Head-marking is "well attested in the Americas and
Melanesia but vanishingly rare elsewhere" nichols1986 and characterizes the
Greater Pacific Rim (GPR) macroarea — a population of languages whose distribution
Nichols traces back more than ten millennia bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §2 . Northeast Asia
is predominantly dependent-marking: Japanese, Korean, Tungusic, Mongolic, and Turkic all mark
syntactic relations on the dependent NP (nominal case, adnominal genitive, uninflected
postpositions governing case). The head/dependent-marking parameter is among the most
diachronically stable typological features known nichols1986 ; its consistent
divergence between Ainu and every language proposed as its relative accordingly constitutes
evidence independent of the lexical record bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §7 .

In a single typological characterization: Hokkaido Ainu is SOV, agglutinating, polysynthetic,
incorporating, and head-marking at both clause and phrase level
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §1 ; shibatani1990 §1 . The sections that
follow treat each dimension of that profile in turn, closing with a WALS-style feature summary.

Head-marking, indexing, and the absence of case

Nichols' parameter classifies languages by the locus of the morphological signal of a syntactic
relation: on the head of the phrase, on the dependent, or on both. Head-marking correlates —
verbal cross-reference of arguments, noun incorporation, instrumental/directional affixes on
verbs, inflected adpositions, pronominal affixes on nouns — differ systematically from
dependent-marking correlates — case on nouns, adnominal genitive marking, agreement suffixes on
attributives, uninflected adpositions governing case nichols1986 . Hokkaido Ainu
shows the head-marking correlates and lacks all the dependent-marking ones across both clause and
noun phrase bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §7 ; sato2025a 361 .

The grammatical role of a noun phrase in the clause is signalled by person indexes on the finite
verb, not by case suffixes on the NP itself. A transitive verb carries an A-index for the
agent/transitive-subject and may carry an O-index for the patient; an intransitive verb carries
an S-index. The NP argument is caseless. Third-person arguments are zero-indexed; first and
second persons carry overt prefixal indexes for A/S and O roles
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.1 ; nakagawa2024 163–169 ;
bugaeva2012 471 .

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | Both non-subject participants surface as bare NPs with no case morphology; the verb ku=kore indexes only the 1SG agent. Recipient vs theme is recoverable from the semantics of kore 'give' and from constituent order, not from case marking. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

The absence of morphological case in an SOV agglutinating language is cross-linguistically
unusual. Japanese and Korean, Ainu's closest geographical neighbours, pair SOV order with
elaborate nominal case systems; Ainu makes the same constituent order work without case, relying
entirely on verbal person-indexing and semantics for argument tracking
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.1, §4.4 ; sato2025a 361 . WALS-type
surveys classify Hokkaido Ainu as "borderline" or "zero" in case inventory
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.4 .

The full architecture of the S/A/O paradigm — four formal persons, the side-encoding of
intransitive S versus transitive A, portmanteau exponents, and the mixed alignment that results
— is set out in person-marking-architecture .

Possession indexed on the head noun

In the noun phrase the same head-marking logic operates. A possessed nominal does not require a
case-marked genitive possessor; instead the head noun itself carries two signals of the possessive
relation — a personal prefix agreeing with the possessor, and an affiliative suffix
marking the noun as standing in a possessed relation
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.4 ; bugaeva2021poss .

ku=yup-ihi | 1SG.POSS=elder.brother-POSS | my elder brother | The possessor relation is doubly marked on the head noun: ku= is the first-singular possessor prefix and -ihi is the affiliative (possessed) suffix. An independent possessor NP, when it appears, is caseless and appositive, not genitive-marked.

Within the nominal domain Ainu distinguishes obligatorily-possessed (inalienable)
nouns — body parts, kin terms, and spatial-relational nouns that must carry a possessor — from
nouns that are non-possessible bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §5.1 . These possessive classes
are absent from the surrounding Northeast Asian languages
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §7 and align Ainu with GPR-type possessive-class systems.
The full classification, the morphophonology of the affiliative suffix, and the appositive
possessive construction are treated in alienable-inalienable-split ,
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology , and concept-form-affiliative-form .

Polysynthesis and noun incorporation

Hokkaido Ainu is genuinely polysynthetic in Nichols' sense of open head-marking : there
is no fixed closed paradigm of what categories may accumulate on the verb
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.7 . A single verb word can stack person indexes, applicative
prefixes, reflexive or reciprocal prefixes, incorporated nominals, and a number suffix while
remaining one phonological word. The density of such accumulation is most visible in Classical
Ainu, particularly in the heroic epic genre yukar
shibatani1990 §1 ; shibatani1988 :

e-yay-ko-tuyma-si-ram-suy-pa | APPL-REFL-APPL-far-REFL.POSS-heart-turn.repeatedly-PL | to ponder deeply | Nine syllables, eight bound morphemes in a single verb word: two applicative prefixes (e-, ko-), reflexive (yay-), incorporated distance noun (tuyma 'far'), reflexive-possessive (si-), incorporated body-part noun (ram 'heart, mind'), verbal root (suy 'revolve'), and pluractional suffix (-pa). After Shibatani (1990 §1) as an illustration of Classical Ainu polysynthetic capacity. | shibatani1990 | HK

In ordinary Hokkaido speech, a comparable degree of stacking is less frequent; a transitive verb
routinely carries two person indexes and one or two derivational prefixes, and a single
incorporated nominal already constitutes a polymorphemic complex. The register difference between
Classical Ainu and everyday speech — and what it implies for the grammar/lexicon boundary — is
a live research question ‹contested›.

Satō (2016; 2022) provides the fullest typology of Ainu noun incorporation, identifying four
types that differ in their effect on verbal valency
sato2016 ; satoni2022 :

incorporation type
incorporated element
valency effect
canonical example

O-incorporation
object (patient) nominal
decreasing : O-slot cancelled

cep-e 'eat fish' (fish + eat; object absorbed)

S-incorporation, natural phenomena
meteorological / landscape noun
canceling : S-slot absorbed

sir-pirka 'the weather is good' (land/appearance + be.good)

A-incorporation, supernatural / insect nouns
non-human A nominal
filling : A-slot supplied internally
supernatural or insect noun fills the A position from within the verb

S-incorporation, body-part nouns
body-part nominal
retaining : extra slot added
body-part incorporated into an intransitive base; external argument retained

The valency effects of each type — and in particular the A-incorporation type, unusual
cross-linguistically — are treated in noun-incorporation-object ,
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding , and
noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate .

How productive and how polysynthetic Ainu incorporation is in formal terms is contested.
Shibatani (1988) analysed Ainu incorporation as a productive type-I lexical compounding process
without semantic bleaching of the incorporated nominal, placing Ainu at the strong end of the
polysynthesis scale shibatani1988 . Bugaeva's (2022) characterization as "open
head-marking" identifies why: the inventory of what may be incorporated or stacked is
unconstrained by a fixed paradigm, unlike the closed agreement paradigms of dependent-marking
languages bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.7 ‹contested›. The applicative system
( e- , ko- , o- ) further
intersects with incorporation: an applicative argument can be simultaneously incorporated in
ways that bear on the syntactic status of both operations
kaiser1998 ; these interactions are treated in
applicative-system-overview .

SOV order and the head-final clause

The basic constituent order of Hokkaido Ainu clauses is SOV: subject precedes object, the finite
verb closes the clause, and the clause is strictly head-final throughout. Modifiers precede their
nominal heads; subordinate clauses precede the matrix verb; postpositional phrases — built on
relational nouns and postpositions — follow the head noun with the adpositional element in final
position bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.3 ; sato2025a 361 ;
nakagawa2024 §4 . The relational-noun system and the postpositional inventory are
treated in relational-and-spatial-nouns and
postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events .

The SOV/head-final pattern sits in a typological tension with the head-marking type. GPR
head-marking languages tend toward verb-initial order; Ainu's SOV gives it a surface resemblance
to its dependent-marking neighbours (Japanese, Korean, Turkic) that does not extend to structural
organization bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.3, §7 . Bugaeva accounts for this via pragmatic
right-dislocation of subject and object NPs: when arguments are shifted rightward for discourse
reasons, the resulting surface order approaches verb-initial, aligning Ainu with the pragmatically
common patterns of the GPR grouping bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.3 . The full syntax of
constituent order and argument realization is in constituent-order-head-final .

Community member @aomidori_cs_ (aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-01-29) has proposed that the SOV
order is not original to Ainu but the result of contact with Northeast Asian SOV-dominant
neighbours, citing the preverbal position of the negator
somo not (clausal negator) as a cross-linguistic diagnostic found in SVO
languages aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. No comparative reconstruction of proto-Ainu
word order is currently available to evaluate the proposal.

The alignment profile: mixed morphological type

The person-affix paradigm of Hokkaido Ainu shows a typologically mixed alignment. The
first-singular row is nominative–accusative: the same prefix ku= marks
both S (intransitive subject) and A (transitive subject), opposed to en=
for O. The second-person rows are neutral: e= (2SG) and
eci= (2PL) cover S, A, and O alike. The third person is zero in all
three roles. The first-plural exclusive and the fourth person are tripartite: S is marked by a
suffix ( =as , =an ) while A is marked by
a prefix ( ci= , a= ) and O by a
distinct prefix ( un= , i= )
nakagawa2024 165–169 ; bugaeva2012 472 . This side-encoding
— prefix for transitive A, suffix for intransitive S — is the morphological basis on which
Bugaeva characterizes the overall system as "mixed, with elements of tripartite alignment"
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §1 .

The detailed person paradigm, portmanteau exponents, and the full alignment debate — tripartite,
hierarchical, inverse — are treated in person-marking-architecture and
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate . The split-intransitive question, which in
Ainu surfaces in verb classes and valency morphology rather than in the agreement paradigm, is
treated in alignment-split-intransitive .

The fourth person (also termed the indefinite person) is a feature of the Ainu person
system unusual enough to warrant mention here. A single form-set covers first-person-plural
inclusive ('we including you'), indefinite/impersonal agent, second-person honorific address, and
logophoric (protagonist) reference in oral-narrative direct speech
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.1 ; nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 ;
bugaeva2008 40–43 . Terminology for the set is unsettled across sources:
Nakagawa uses "fourth person", Tamura lists the same forms as separate sub-persons, and Bugaeva
analyses the quotation use as logophoric
tamura1972 18–20 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ‹contested›. The full
referential range is catalogued in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and
fourth-person-honorific .

Typologically unusual features in the regional context

Locative noun class and possessive alienability

Ainu nouns divide into two classes in the syntax of spatial phrases
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.4 ; nakagawa2022pos . Ordinary nouns
require a locative noun as an intermediary head ( or place ,
ka top , sam side , tum middle ) to form a spatial
phrase; locative nouns can head a spatial phrase directly, as in
pet or ta 'at the river' (river + place + LOC). The locative/ordinary
distinction helps track grammatical relations when the third person is zero-marked: a locative
noun heads a spatial phrase and is not parsed as a bare object NP. The full system is treated
in relational-and-spatial-nouns .

Property concepts that would be expressed by adjectives in related languages function as
stative verbs in Ainu, with no separate adjective class
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.4 ; this is treated in
property-concept-predication .

Vigesimal numerals

The numeral system operates on a base-20 (vigesimal) frame, typologically unusual in the
region. Ochiai (2021) documents a historical shift in the reckoning strategy within that frame
— from a subtractive to an additive counting method — on the basis of dialect comparison
ochiai2021 . Verbal number is separately marked by suppletive singular/plural stem
pairs ( ek / arki 'come',
arpa / paye 'go') and by the pluractional
suffix -pa bugaeva2022 Ch.1 §4.2 ;
nakagawa2024 141–159 ; the full treatment of numeral morphology is in
vigesimal-numeral-system and
numeral-classifiers-ordinals-and-quantifier-syntax ; verbal number in
suppletive-verbal-number .

The four-term evidential system

Hokkaido Ainu encodes the speaker's source of information periphrastically through a
nominalization-plus-copula construction. Four formal (defective) nouns —
ruwe track, trace (evidential) ,
siri visible appearance (evidential) ,
hawe voice (evidential) , and
humi sound, feeling (evidential) — combine with the copula
ne be (copula) to yield a four-term paradigm encoding a visual-trace /
inferential evidential, a direct-visual / situational evidential, a reportative, and a
non-visual sensory evidential respectively
dalcorso2018 304 ; nakagawa2024 258, 392 ;
takahashi2022 . The system is built on the grammaticalization of sensory nouns
into a nominalization slot, a structural type unusual in Northeast Asia. All four terms and
the schema they instantiate are treated in evidential-schema-overview .

The WALS-style feature bundle

The table below assembles the major typological parameters of Hokkaido Ainu. Sources for each
feature are given in the preceding sections; primary reference is
bugaeva2022 Ch.1 and shibatani1990 §1 .

feature
value in Hokkaido Ainu

Genetic status
language isolate ; no demonstrated relation to any family

Morphological type
agglutinating, polysynthetic

Head / dependent marking

head-marking at clause and phrase level; no dependent-marking
correlates (no case, no adnominal genitive inflection, no attribute agreement)

Basic word order
SOV , strictly head-final; modifiers precede heads

Adpositions
postpositions and postpositional relational nouns

Morphological case

absent (zero / borderline in WALS terms); grammatical relations
encoded by verbal person-indexing

Grammatical relations

person-indexing on the verb: prefixal A and O, prefixal or suffixal S
depending on person

Morphological alignment

mixed : nominative–accusative (1SG), neutral (2nd person), zero (3rd),
tripartite (1PL.EXCL and 4th person)

Person system

1, 2, 3, and fourth person (inclusive / indefinite / honorific /
logophoric); inclusive/exclusive opposition in 1PL

Verbal number

suppletive SG/PL stems and pluractional
-pa

Noun incorporation

four types (O-, S-natural, A-, S-body-part), each with distinct
valency effect

Valency-changing

causative -re/-e ; applicatives
e-/ko-/o- ; antipassive, reflexive, reciprocal

Noun classes

ordinary vs locative ; inalienable / obligatorily-possessed vs
non-possessible

Numerals
vigesimal (base-20)

Evidentiality

four-term , realized analytically (nominalized clause + copula)

Phoneme inventory

small: 5 vowels / 11–12 consonants; syllable canon (C)V(C); see
consonant-inventory and vowel-inventory

Pitch accent

rising-nucleus pitch accent in accent dialects; see
pitch-accent-placement-rule

Writing

no pre-modern script; katakana and Roman script since the 19th century; see
latin-phonemic-transcription and
katakana-and-small-kana-codas

The head-marking profile aligns Ainu with the GPR typological cluster; the SOV order gives it a
surface resemblance to Northeast Asian dependent-marking languages that does not carry into
morphological organization. The combination of polysynthesis, a four-term analytical evidential
system, and the incorporation-plus-applicative interaction makes the structural profile of
Hokkaido Ainu distinctive even within the GPR grouping.

title: A Glossed Uwepeker (Prose Folktale)
summary: A complete uwepeker prose folktale presented in four-tier interlinear gloss with running grammatical commentary cross-referenced clause-by-clause to the analytic chapters.
part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: A Glossed Uwepeker (Prose Folktale)
Part: Part XXII — Glossed Texts
Summary: A complete uwepeker prose folktale presented in four-tier interlinear gloss with running grammatical commentary cross-referenced clause-by-clause to the analytic chapters.

The prose tale in the genre system

Hokkaido Ainu oral narrative divides into three major genres: the heroic epic, the divine
epic, and the prose folktale. The prose folktale is designated
uwepeker prose folktale in the Saru and Chitose dialects of southwestern
Hokkaido and across much of southern Hidaka; the eastern dialects — Ishikari, Teshio,
Kitami, Kushiro, Tokachi, and eastern Hidaka east of Shizunai — use
tuytak for the same genre. The two terms are geographically
distributed, not functionally distinct ‹consensus›
endo2022 ; sato2008 259 .
(Confusingly, tuytak designates the divine epic in Samani; the
term's referent is therefore dialect-specific — see oral-literature-genre-taxonomy .)

What distinguishes the prose tale from the two sung genres is the complete absence of
three formal features: a fixed melody, a cyclically recurring refrain
( sakehe refrain ), and the rhythm-stick ( repni rhythm-stick )
that performers and listeners use to keep time during heroic-epic recitation. The narrator
speaks in an ordinary, near-conversational voice. The following table summarises the main
parameters across the three major narrative genres, following
sato2008 259 and endo2022 :

genre
Ainu term (SW Hokkaido)
melody
sakehe refrain
repni tapping
typical length
narrator (traditional)

heroic epic
yukar (E: sakorpe )
yes
no
yes
long (one or more nights)
men (traditionally)

divine epic
kamuy yukar
yes
yes (fixed per story)
no
short to medium
women (and men)

prose folktale
uwepeker
no
no
no
medium
women (and men)

The sakehe refrain and metre of the sung genres are treated in
sakehe-refrain-and-sung-verse-structure .
An additional prose sub-form, irupaye prose rendering of a yukar , carries
heroic-epic content in prose delivery, enabling women narrators to perform yukar material
without contravening the convention that verse yukar belongs to
men sato2008 259 . The gendered allocation of genres is discussed in
gendered-register-speech .

Sub-genre types and typical themes

Endō identifies four sub-categories within the uwepeker heading
endo2022 :

Human prose tales (the default type). The protagonist is an unnamed
human — "the chief of ___", "a poor young man" — and the narration is in the first
person with a retrospective identification at the close. The most pervasive narrative
shape is the impoverished-hero tale: a young man in reduced circumstances acquires
power or a spouse through courage, divine favour, or correct conduct; the arc broadly
rewards good behaviour and punishes bad.

Kamuy prose tales ( kamuy uwepeker ). A deity
narrates in the first person, aligning these tales with the world of divine epics
despite their prose format; the language may reach into the elevated register
endo2022 .

Japanese-borrowed tales . Adapted Japanese narratives told in the
third person — "In a certain place there lived a young lord…" — with an Ainu-inflected
worldview.

Pananpe–Penanpe tales . A dyadic genre built around the archetypal
pair pananpe downstream-dweller ('downstream-dweller',
pana-n-pe ) and
penanpe upstream-dweller ('upstream-dweller',
pena-n-pe ). Variant forms
panakuru / penakuru , with
kur 'person' rather than pe 'thing,
person', also occur. These tales are narrated in the third person, open with a
formulaic pair-announcement, and are regarded as a children's genre
endo2022 .

The Pananpe–Penanpe opening is among the most stable formulae in the repertoire. Both
characters are introduced with the existential verb
an exist, be in succession, joined by a conjunctive clause. The inferential
evidential ruwe ne predicated over the whole opening grounds the
state of affairs as established narrative fact (on this function see
ruwe-ne-inferential ):

pananpe an penanpe an hine sir-an pe ne ru-we ne hike | Pananpe exist Penanpe exist CONJ appearance-exist thing COP track-POSS COP although | There were Pananpe and Penanpe, and… | Standard Pananpe–Penanpe opening. The third-person existential frame (no fourth-person a= prefix) contrasts with the first-person fourth-person narration of human prose tales. Hirosawa cites the underlying Tamura audio materials; ruwe ne predicates the simultaneous existence of the two characters as established narrative fact. | hirosawa2026:75 | SAR | Wateke (田村すゞ子 audio materials 05-02, 'Folktale 1': 4)

Opening and closing formulae

Human prose tales open with a scene-setting passage establishing the protagonist's
household and livelihood: an enumeration of family members, the father's hunting
activities, the household's prosperity or poverty. This is a learned formula, not an
improvised description, and it recurs across narrators and dialect areas in broadly
stable shape endo2022 .

The closing formula works in the opposite direction. The narrator, who has spoken
throughout as the protagonist "I", reveals the protagonist's identity at the tale's end
through a retrospective disclosure. The pattern takes the shape of a quotative wrap:
the protagonist's name or description appears inside a
sekor quotative: saying, thus frame, which is closed by a verb of
telling — typically isoytak narrate a tale — with explicit person
marking. The formula … sekor … isoytak ('…so [so-and-so]
told the story') simultaneously attributes the performance to its narrator and
discloses the fourth-person protagonist's identity
endo2022 .
The grammar of sekor and the interaction of the closing frame
with the logophoric fourth person are treated in
quotative-complementation-sekor and
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Person of narration: the fourth-person narrator-protagonist

The default prose tale is narrated in the first person from the protagonist's viewpoint,
but the person marking on verbs is the fourth person —
a= (transitive subject and possessor) and
=an (intransitive subject) — not the everyday first-person
singular ku= . This fourth-person-as-narrative-'I' convention
holds across all three major Saru/Chitose narrative genres and is the defining feature
of Ainu oral narrative person-marking ‹consensus›
sato2008 260–261 ; endo2022 ; sato2004 .

a=poho a=kore | 4.POSS=child 4.A=give | I gave my child away. | Fragment from a human prose tale. The fourth-person prefix a= marks both the possessive relation ('my child') and the transitive subject ('I gave'). Everyday first-person narration would require ku= for both roles; the prose-tale convention substitutes a= throughout. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子 (Kawakamimatsuko), ILCAA Ainu materials (AA研アイヌ語資料)

Within the prose tale, the same fourth-person forms also serve as a
deferential honorific when the narrator-protagonist refers to a revered figure —
an elder chief, a deity encountered in the story world, or a powerful adversary. Here
a= encodes not narrative self-reference but respectful distance
sato2008 261 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 .
Both functions, the logophoric and the honorific, are realised by the same set of
forms and are distinguished by context. The full paradigm and its referential range
are set out in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and
fourth-person-honorific ; the narrator-as-protagonist analysis and its
relation to logophoricity are treated in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Why a= rather than ku= ? Two competing
accounts circulate in the literature ‹contested›. The standard explanation traces the
convention to a "shamaness-oracle" origin: the narrative voice is a possessing spirit
speaking through the teller, making everyday first-person marking inappropriate. Satō
argues instead for an indirection principle
( 間接性の原理 ): the genre originated as a god's telling inside
a dream addressed to a living human, and since direct god-to-human address is taboo,
the non-everyday person marking adds the necessary indirectness
sato2008 259–261 .
Bugaeva and Nikitina reanalyse the narrator-protagonist using the framework of
logophoricity: the "I" of the tale is a logophoric controller, the narration is
direct-style throughout, and the fourth person is the Ainu mechanism for maintaining
logophoric reference across the embedded narrative
bugaeva2008 40–43 ; nikitina2021 .
Whether Ainu oral narrative therefore qualifies as genuine "first-person literature"
( 一人称体 ) in the typological sense remains an open question
sato2004 ; endo2022 ‹contested›.
The person-marking architecture that makes all these functions possible is surveyed in
person-marking-architecture .

One feature distinguishes prose-tale person-marking from that of the sung genres: the
number-agreement profile . In the elevated register of heroic and divine epics,
=an appears freely with a syntactically singular verb
( ek-an 'I come', singular verb stem), overriding the everyday
restriction that bars =an from singular stems in Saru/Chitose.
Prose uwepeker does not generally exploit this override; its
number agreement follows the everyday pattern ‹corpus-suggested›
sato2008 263 .

The quotative frame and participant tracking

Because the prose tale maintains direct-voice narration throughout, and because embedded
dialogue is a constant feature of the genre, the quotative complementizer
sekor is dense. Speech and thought reports are framed with
sekor followed by one of a small set of reporting verbs:
ye 'say', hawki 'recite/say', or
isoytak 'narrate'. The same frame reappears at the close of
the whole performance in the retrospective identification formula discussed above
endo2022 .
The construction grammar of sekor , its position with respect
to tense and aspect, and the distinction between direct and indirect-style reporting
in prose narrative are treated in quotative-complementation-sekor .

Participant tracking across extended prose narration — keeping the listener oriented
among multiple characters when several embedded speakers each use "I" — relies on the
fourth-person / first-person contrast in conjunction with the quotative frame. The
narrator-protagonist's embedded direct speech remains under the logophoric fourth
person; secondary characters may receive everyday person marking. The reference-tracking
apparatus is treated in reference-tracking .

The TAM and evidential profile of prose tales — the predominance of
ruwe ne as narrative-frame predicate, the aspectual distribution,
and the sequencing role of conjunctive hine and
akusu — is the topic of
narrative-tam-evidential-patterning-by-genre .

Register: the everyday frame and its limits

Human prose tales operate in yayan-itak everyday language
( 口語・日常語 ), the ordinary register, in contrast to the
elevated poetic language ( atomte-itak elegant/decorated language ,
雅語 ) of heroic and divine epics
sato2008 259 ; endo2022 .
Prose narration therefore avoids the hallmarks of the elevated register: the applicative
case-marking prefixes ( e- , o- ,
ko- ) in place of spatial particles, the polysynthetic formulaic
periphrases, and the elegant kin-term vocabulary
( ona 'father', unu 'mother' as
literary-register forms, against colloquial dialect variants)
endo2022
(nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord 2024-06-15).
The elevated register and its structural diagnostics are surveyed in
poetic-archaic-elevated-register .

The boundary is not absolute. Kamuy prose tales ( kamuy uwepeker ),
in which a deity narrates, may draw on the elevated register, aligning their language
with that of divine epics despite their prose format
endo2022 .
Narrators with a broad stylistic range may also import elevated vocabulary or formulae
into human prose tales, and the register of any given performance reflects the
individual narrator's competence and choices. The elegant/everyday register boundary
is a continuum keyed to verse-vs-prose and improvisation-vs-fixedness rather than a
clean binary ‹contested›
sato2008 259 .

The prosodic correlate of the everyday register is a characteristic intonation pattern.
Tamura notes that uwepeker narration is unidirectional — the
teller speaks to an audience rather than seeking a response — and the voice falls to a
flat, low-variation melodic contour across sentences, in contrast to the rising contour
of conversational speech where the speaker solicits a reaction
tamura1996 ;
the community discussion in the aynu-corpora Discord (nukopoli, 2024-11-16) confirms
this datum from Tamura's account
aynucorporadiscord .
Within the Saru basin, upper-Saru (mid-river) narrators tend toward a monotone delivery
while lower-Saru (downriver) narrators show more melodic undulation; this microvariation
does not affect the structural grammar of the genre.

title: Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation: Overview
summary: The architecture of the valency-changing system: the inventory of operations (causatives, the e-/ko-/o- applicatives, antipassive, reflexive, reciprocal, middle/anticausative, and noun incorporation) and how they compose, stack, and order on the verb.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation: Overview
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The architecture of the valency-changing system: the inventory of operations (causatives, the e-/ko-/o- applicatives, antipassive, reflexive, reciprocal, middle/anticausative, and noun incorporation) and how they compose, stack, and order on the verb.

The valency system as an arity calculus

Hokkaido Ainu reworks the argument frame of the verb through a dense layer of derivational
morphology. Nakagawa presents the system as an arithmetic over the verb word: every derivational
element carries an arity value, the derived verb’s argument capacity is the sum of those values,
and the person indexes fill whatever argument positions remain nakagawa2024 136–140, 184–212 . The Handbook covers the same ground as verbal valency and voice — the applicatives, the
causatives, the reciprocal, the reflexive, the antipassive, the middle, and a set of
valency-neutral alternations bugaevakobayashi2022 §1–5 .
noun-incorporation-object Noun incorporation belongs to this architecture as well,
since an incorporated nominal satisfies an argument slot inside the verb word and is diagnosed by
the position of person marking satoni2022 §1–2 ; shibatani1990 60–66 .

The diagnostic substrate is the transitivity system of
transitivity-and-valence-classes and the four-person paradigm of
person-marking-architecture . A derived intransitive takes the suffixal S indexes
=as (first-plural exclusive) and =an (fourth); a
derived transitive takes the prefixal A indexes ci= and
a= sato2008 96 ; nakagawa2024 137, 140 .
Placing a verb in the first-person plural is therefore the one operative test of its derived
transitivity. Satō’s pair pukusa a=ta ‘we dig
pukusa lily bulb ’ beside pukusa-ta=an makes the point:
incorporating the object intransitivizes the verb, and the fourth-person S suffix
=an becomes available satoni2022 §2 . A verb hosts at most
two person affixes, one subject and one object, so every operation works under a two-slot ceiling
sato2023b 39 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §2 . Ijäs’s learner image of
argument “pockets” on the verb beside external “slots” renders the same head-marking intuition
nichols1986 ; her terms are pedagogical and her example sentences constructed
ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

numan hekaci cep koyki | yesterday boy fish catch | The boy caught fish yesterday. | Ijäs Lesson 3 introduces the two-argument transitive frame with constructed sentences of this type; the third-person subject and object are segmentally unmarked. | ijas2023

Inventory of operations

The working inventory for the part is given below. The values in the first column are arity
effects in Nakagawa’s sense, and they predict the person-marking diagnostics used by Satō and the
Handbook. Several surface strings recur across rows, so the arity and the host verb class settle
how a given occurrence is glossed. Each operation is developed in its own chapter, listed in the
final column.

direction
operation
marker or material
typical output
detail

+1
productive causative
-re , -te , -e
causer added as subject; old subject becomes object
causative-morphological-re-e-te

+1
applicative
e- , ko- , o-
a peripheral participant becomes a core object
applicative-system-overview

+1
transitivizer
-kar on an intransitive
an object is added
transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs

+1 / 0
direct causative, transitivity-pair formation
-V , -ke , -ka
lexical or direct-causative alternation
transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs

0
indefinite causative
-yar , -ar
causer added while the causee stays unexpressed and unindexed
permissive-sociative-causative-yar

0
sociative
uko- , uwe-
joint participation, no net arity change
reciprocal-u

0
affiliative part prefix
e- ‘head of’, o- ‘bottom of’
fills a part slot but requires its own possessor
applicative-system-overview

−1
antipassive / indefinite object
i-
generic object absorbed; transitive becomes intransitive
antipassive-detransitive-i

−1
reflexive
yay- , si-
object position filled by a self participant
reflexive-yay ; reflexive-possessive-si

−1
reciprocal
u-
object position filled by reciprocal participants; plural subject
reciprocal-u

−1
middle / object-resultative
ci-
the affected participant becomes the sole argument
middle-anticausative-passive

−1
lexical body-region prefix
he- ‘head’, ho- ‘bottom’
body-region object supplied by the prefix
middle-anticausative-passive

−1 (×N)
noun incorporation
bare noun stem, and in places adverbial material
an argument or adjunct joins the verb word
noun-incorporation-object

The inventory also marks the homophony hazards that run through the part. Applicative
e- and o- add an object; affiliative
e- and o- fill a part slot while keeping a
possessor; second-person e= is a person index. The prefix
ci- may be the middle, while ci= is the
first-plural-exclusive A index. The suffix -kar transitivizes on an
intransitive base, while a homophonous -kar on transitives is a
verse-filler with no arity effect nakagawa2024 190–193, 209–210 . None of these
can be glossed without first reading off the arity.

Valency-increasing morphology

The productive causative is one suffix with three principal allomorphs: -re
after a vowel or glide, -te after most consonants, and
-e after r nakagawa2024 200–201 ;
bugaeva2014 134 . It raises arity by one. On an intransitive base the original S
becomes the object of the causative verb; on a transitive base the result is a double-object verb,
and double causatives are admitted as well, both of which are taken up in
causee-marking-ditransitive-double-causative . The suffix covers coercive and
permissive causation alike, and Bugaeva’s Saru sample shows it as the productive default, taking
in many direct-causative uses that older descriptions assigned to non-productive transitivizing
suffixes bugaeva2014 130, 139–144 .

matkaci sinot | girl play | The girl played. | nakagawa2024:431 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 954, after Tamura 1984

hapo matkaci sinot-te | mother girl play-CAUS | Mother made the girl play. | The base subject matkaci ‘girl’ is the object of the derived causative. | nakagawa2024:431 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 954′

The older, less productive direct-causative suffixes -V ,
-ke , and -ka build many valency pairs and are split
out into transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs . Bugaeva analyzes most of this morphology as
direct causative in function, where Nakagawa keeps the forming suffixes, the productive causative,
and the special motion-verb causatives distinct by their distribution nakagawa2024 198–203 ; bugaeva2014 129–144 ‹contested›. The indefinite causative
-yar , which adds a causer while leaving the causee unexpressed and
unindexed, is valency-neutral and is treated separately in
permissive-sociative-causative-yar . Ijäs’s Lesson 9 statement that
-re “turns intransitive verbs into transitive verbs” gives the right entry
intuition but states no allomorphy and conflates causation with transitivization ijas2023 ; sato2008 234–238 ‹consensus›.

The applicatives e- , ko- , and
o- add a non-agent participant to the object set, turning an intransitive
into a transitive and a transitive into a double-object verb bugaeva2006 188 ;
nakagawa2024 194 . Their applied objects range well beyond a narrow instrumental
or benefactive: Bugaeva’s role tables cover content, theme-patient, instrument, location, range,
addressee, goal, comitative, benefactive, malefactive, recipient, source, and purpose bugaeva2006 188–196 . The three prefixes, the diagnostics, and the older
充当相 and case-relation-prefix labels they displace are set out in
applicative-system-overview , with the individual prefixes in
applicative-e , applicative-ko , and applicative-o .

sirkorkamuy emus e-tuye | standing.tree sword APPL-cut | He cut a standing tree with a sword. | The instrument emus ‘sword’ is the applied object licensed by e-, which raises tuye ‘cut’ from a two-place to a three-place verb. | nakagawa2024:193 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 173, after Monbetsu-chō 1969: 237

Valency-decreasing and rearranging voices

The prefix i- fills the object position of a transitive verb with a generic
‘thing’ or ‘someone’ and intransitivizes it, so that huraye wash something yields
i-huraye ‘do the washing’ and ku drink something yields
i-ku ‘drink alcohol’ nakagawa2024 190–192 . Its label
divides the literature. Bugaeva analyzes it as an antipassive on syntactic, semantic, and
discourse grounds, and Nakagawa adopts the term after her; Ijäs follows the same usage in Lesson
9. Satō’s textbook treats the identical prefix as a derivational i-
‘people, someone’ and uses no antipassive label bugaeva2021antip ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 ; ijas2023 ; sato2008 232–233 ‹contested›. Its origin is a separate
dispute: the older line relates it to the fourth-person object index i= ;
Bugaeva derives the antipassive from incorporation of a generic noun and then the pronoun from the
antipassive; Nam reconstructs the source as *hi or
*ihi ‘thing, place, time’, the generic noun he also ties to the nominalizer
and the possessed suffix nam2021 63–74 ‹contested›. Ijäs’s further suggestion that
isam ‘not exist’ contains this i- over a lost
*sam is idiosyncratic and unverified.

i-ri=an wa or-o wa sini=an na | ANTIP-skin=4.S CONJ place-POSS from rest=4.S FIN | I will skin the catch and then rest. | The intransitive S suffix =an shows that i-ri ‘do skinning’ is intransitive; the patient ‘catch’ is left out of the argument frame. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.3 | SHI | Shizunai Board of Education 1991–1995, O0130054_6; Handbook ex. 24b

The reflexives yay- and si- both mean ‘self’ and
both reduce arity by filling the object slot nakagawa2024 188–189 . Their division
of labour is among the most disputed points in Ainu derivation. Earlier accounts split them by
intentionality or by an agentive against a patientive self; Satō’s Chitose analysis, adopted by
Nakagawa and the Handbook, separates direct self-involvement, marked by
yay- , from indirect involvement or dependence on another participant,
marked by si- , so that yay-rayke ‘commit suicide’
stands against si-rayke ‘be ruined by ill fortune’ sato2007 31–39 ; nakagawa2024 188–189 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2
‹contested›. The two prefixes are treated in reflexive-yay , and the possessive use of
the same morphology, where si- supplies a subject-coreferent possessor to
an incorporated noun, in reflexive-possessive-si .

Reciprocal u- fills the object slot with reciprocal participants and yields
an intransitive verb with a plural subject. Its readings reach past ‘each other’:
u-tures-kor means ‘have a sister, be siblings’ and
u-mina-re ‘laugh together’, with no causative-reciprocal sense nakagawa2024 187–188 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 . Ijäs’s Lesson 9 gloss ‘mutually, each other’
captures the core reciprocal ijas2023 ‹consensus›. The valency-neutral sociative
uko- / uwe- , built from u-
plus an applicative, is taken up with the reciprocal in reciprocal-u .

nea nispa utar u-koyki kor oka siri a=nukar | that rich.man PL RECP-attack while exist.PL appearance 4.A=see | I saw those rich men fighting each other. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.1 | SAR | Nakagawa corpus K8010281UP.168; Handbook ex. 11a

The middle ci- presents an event from its affected side and yields an
intransitive: cari scatter something gives ci-cari ‘be
scattered’, with the affected noun as subject, against a=cari , which keeps
that noun as the object of a transitive clause with an indefinite agent nakagawa2024 190–191, 429–430 ; the Handbook glosses this ci- object-resultative bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 . It is normally restricted to third-person participants, which keeps it apart from the
first-plural-exclusive index ci= .

aynu kam ka upas ka ta ci-cari | human flesh even snow even LOC RES-scatter | Human flesh and snow too lay scattered on the ground. | nakagawa2024:189 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 167

Hokkaido Ainu has no subject-promoting passive. The work of a passive is carried by the
fourth, indefinite transitive subject a= , dialectally
an= : the agent is defocused while the patient stays the object of a
transitive verb, and a demoted agent may appear in an or wa ‘from’ phrase
nakagawa2024 428–429 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §1 . Satō’s subjecthood
test confirms the structure: a ‘want to be loved’ clause cannot treat the patient as an
intransitive subject and instead requires a nominalized, object-marked frame sato2008 205–206 ‹consensus›. The middle, the anticausative patterns that overlap it, and the boundary with
this indefinite-agent construction are pursued in middle-anticausative-passive .

seta or wa aynu a=kupapa | dog place from person 4.A=bite | A person was bitten by a dog. | The dog appears in an or wa ‘from’ phrase; the indefinite transitive subject a= stays on the verb and the patient aynu ‘person’ stays the object. | nakagawa2024:428 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 945″

Noun incorporation and wordhood

Noun incorporation forms one verb word from a nominal stem and a verb nakagawa2024 480 ; satoni2022 §1 . The wordhood diagnostic is again the outer position of the
person index. With a phrasal object the index sits on the verb,
cep ku=koyki ‘I catch fish’; under incorporation it precedes the whole
noun-verb complex, ku=cep-koyki ‘I fish-catch’; and with a fourth-person
subject the test is decisive, because the intransitive suffix =an cannot
attach to a transitive verb nakagawa2024 482–483 ; satoni2022 §2 .

pet or ta ran=an wa suy cep-koyki=an rok ayne | river place LOC descend.SG=4.S CONJ again fish-catch=4.S PL.PRF then | I went down to the river and caught fish again, and at last … | The intransitive suffix =an on cep-koyki proves that the incorporated cep ‘fish’ has detransitivized koyki ‘catch’; the whole noun-verb sequence is one word. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 13; ILCAA materials aa-irc/016#14

Object incorporation is the unmarked, lexical type and the overwhelming majority in Satō’s
Chitose count; it answers to Mithun’s Type I compounding mithun1984 ; sato2016 83–84 ; satoni2022 §3 . Subject incorporation is marked and divides into natural-force
and possessor-requiring subtypes, the latter stranding a possessor that the subject recovers — the
material of noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding . Oblique nouns do not
incorporate directly: an instrument or a location first becomes an object through
e- , ko- , or o- , and only the
derived object incorporates shibatani1990 62, 66–67 ; nakagawa2024 484 , the feeding relation pursued in applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding .

The status of incorporation is contested. Shibatani argues that a purely grammatical-relation
account and a purely semantic-role account each fall short, and that Ainu incorporation must
reference both the incorporated noun’s low agentivity and its grammatical position shibatani1988 210–211 ; shibatani1990 60–66 . Satō revises the unaccusative account because transitive
natural-force subjects also incorporate, and takes low agentivity as the defining property,
ranking the four types by a markedness calculus sato2008 221–227 ; satoni2022 §5–6 . Bugaeva places Ainu among the polysynthetic languages in Baker’s sense while recording where
it departs from the Mohawk prototype — its word order, its in-situ wh , and
its unrestricted morphological causatives baker1988 ; bugaeva2014
‹contested›. These questions are developed in noun-incorporation-object and
noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate .

Stacking, ordering, and the verb-word template

The valency prefixes sit inside the person-index layer and before the root, while the causative
suffixes follow the stem and its forming material. Nakagawa’s position table gives the order as
person index, nominal or applicative prefix, root, forming and plural suffix, causative suffix,
and suffixal person index nakagawa2024 210–212 , the template developed in
verb-word-template and personal-affix-template-and-ordering . Because the
person index is the outermost shell, any element standing outside it begins a new word, which is
the same fact that underlies the incorporation diagnostic above.

Stacking is extensive and bounded by the template. A single verb may carry up to two applicatives,
two causatives, two incorporated objects, and a lexical prefix, alongside a reciprocal, reflexive,
or antipassive and the agreement indexes bugaeva2014 . The classic showcase loads an
incorporated object ( ram heart ), an adverbial element
( tuyma far ), two reflexives, and two applicatives onto one transitive root:

usa-oruspe a=e-yay-ko-tuyma-si-ram-suy-pa | various-rumor 4.A-APPL-REFL-APPL-far-REFL-heart-sway-PL | We keep swaying our hearts afar over various rumours. | we sway our hearts about ourselves far over various rumours | One transitive root suy-pa ‘sway (PL)’ carries an incorporated object, an adverbial, two reflexives, and two applicatives; the whole reads ‘we wonder about various rumours’. | shibatani1990:73; bugaeva2014 | HK | after Chiri 1936: 169; Shibatani ex. 131 / Bugaeva ex. 1

The Handbook’s yay-ipe-e-ko-sunke ‘eat meagre meals’ shows the converse, a
three-object applicative frame re-intransitivized by incorporating one object and reflexivizing
another bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . Valency morphology also interacts with verbal
number, since what a plural stem or the plural -pa tracks depends on
transitivity: an intransitive plural tracks subject number, a transitive plural tracks object or
event number ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 142–159 ‹consensus›. That system
belongs to suppletive-verbal-number and person-number-interactions , and
a valency operation can shift which participant a plural form tracks.

title: The Verb Word and Its Position-Class Template
summary: The internal architecture of the Ainu verb word as an ordered sequence of prefix, stem, and suffix position classes.
part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Verb Word and Its Position-Class Template
Part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
Summary: The internal architecture of the Ainu verb word as an ordered sequence of prefix, stem, and suffix position classes.

The verb word as the head of its clause

The Hokkaido Ainu verb word is the head of its clause: every other constituent enters a
grammatical or semantic relation with it, and it alone carries the bound indexes that identify
the clause's core participants nakagawa2024 124 . Satō describes the language as
head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 , with the verb bearing the central
information about which arguments fill its valence frame; external noun phrases may be absent,
freely ordered, or arranged for discourse, while the indexes on the verb do the ordinary work of
tracking who acts on whom sato2023b 42 . Ijäs's classroom image of verb
pockets , filled before and more obligatorily than free pronouns or full noun phrases,
names the same head-marking fact for learners ijas2023 . The personal-index
architecture itself is the subject of person-marking-architecture ; this chapter sets
out the verb word's morphological template and the order of its position classes, and routes the
detail of each zone to the chapters that follow.

Several properties of the Ainu verb fix the boundaries of that template nakagawa2024 124–125 . Tense is absent as an inflectional category: a bare verb form is read as past, present, or
future from context, temporal adverbs, or discourse sequencing nakagawa2024 124 ;
shibatani1990 33 ; ijas2023 . There is no separate adjective class;
meanings rendered by English or Japanese adjectives are intransitive verbs, and a stative reading
ordinarily carries an inchoative one — Ijäs presents hure as both 'be red'
and 'become red', matching Nakagawa's poro 'be big; become big' and the
imperative evidence Shibatani adduces for the verbal status of quality predicates ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 124 ; shibatani1990 33–34 . Property predicates
are treated as a verb subclass in property-concept-predication and
no-adjective-class-property-verbs . Person marking is obligatory while independent
pronouns are optional, so the language is consistently pro-dropping and verb-final ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 125, 363 . Tense, aspect, mood, evidentiality, and negation are
assembled outside the lexical verb by auxiliaries, formal nouns, and particles that take an
already person- and number-inflected verb as their input, and are treated in
tam-overview , tenselessness-and-time-reference , and the evidential and
negation parts. The verb word supplies the stem and its argument morphology; the larger predicate
supplies the rest.

A position-class template

The finite verb word can be modelled as an ordered set of position classes. The model is a
descriptive abstraction over the recurrent linear positions of person indexes, valency-changing
morphology, the lexical stem, stem-number morphology, derivational suffixes, and the outer plural
markers. Read from the outer left edge to the outer right edge, the zones are as follows.

zone (outer → inner → outer)
typical material
position and status
detail chapter

external arguments
bare subject and object noun phrases; case-marked obliques outside the argument frame
outside the verb word; pragmatically ordered, omissible under person indexing
transitivity-and-valence-classes

personal prefixes
ku= , ci= , a= , e= , eci= ; object en= , un= , i=
outer prefixal layer; at most one subject and one object index (the two-index cap)
person-marking-architecture

valency prefixes
applicative e- , ko- , o- ; antipassive i- ; reflexive yay- , si- ; reciprocal u- ; incorporated nominal
closer to the stem than the person indexes; add to or fill argument positions
valency-voice-overview

stem
simple roots, derived and incorporated stems, suppletive singular/plural stems
lexical head; fixes the base valence frame
transitivity-and-valence-classes

stem-internal suffixes
plural -pa , transitivity-pair suffixes, causative -re / -e / -te , -yar
inside the person-inflected word; change stem class or valence
causative-morphological-re-e-te

intransitive S suffixes
=as '1PL.EXCL.S', =an '4.S'
at the right edge of the inflected stem; the side-switch for these two person rows
personal-affix-template-and-ordering

outer plural clitic
western pa (Saru, Chitose, Ishikari); eastern ci
outside the person-inflected verb in the clearest cases
suppletive-verbal-number

Two ordering facts anchor the template. The first is a side-switch by transitivity. The
first-plural-exclusive and fourth-person subjects appear as suffixes on an intransitive
stem but as prefixes on a transitive one: inkar=an 'I/one looked'
and arki=as 'we came' beside a=nukar 'I/one saw it'
and ci=nukar 'we saw it' nakagawa2024 166–169 ; sato2008 96 . The second is the position of the two homophonous plural pa
elements. The Type-C plural suffix -pa is root-bound and stands inside the
person suffix and inside the causative, whereas the outer plural clitic
pa follows the whole person-inflected verb:
iki-pa=an with the suffix before =an contrasts with
iki=an pa with the clitic after it nakagawa2024 156–159 .
The relative order of these elements is the subject of personal-affix-template-and-ordering and suppletive-verbal-number .

The template is partly positional and partly scope-driven. Some ordering is fixed by position
class, but the valency prefixes and suffixes stack in an order that tracks the order of
derivation: a chain such as ruska 'be angry at' →
ko-ruska → yay-ko-ruska →
i-ruska raises and lowers valence step by step, each affix scoping over
the stem already built bugaevakobayashi2022 §4 ; sato2023b 40 .
Auxiliaries and light verbs such as ki 'do' and the abilitative
easkay 'can' stand outside this word altogether, and are treated in
light-and-pro-verbs and tam-overview .

Transitivity as the organizing axis

Transitivity is a formal property of the verb word, decided by structure and not by the meaning
of the translation nakagawa2024 125–127 ; sato2008 96 . Two
diagnostics define it. A verb requires a fixed number of bare noun-phrase arguments — noun
phrases without a case particle or postposition — and case-marked phrases are adverbial additions
outside that count; one bare argument marks an intransitive verb, two or more a transitive verb
nakagawa2024 126–127 . An intransitive verb indexes only its subject (S), while a
transitive verb indexes subject (A) and object (O) nakagawa2024 126 . The surest
operational test puts the verb in the first-person plural: an intransitive takes the suffix
=as (exclusive) or =an (fourth person), a
transitive the prefix ci= or a= , the other person
cells being uninformative — a side-switch Satō calls irregular and still unexplained sato2008 96 .

person
S (intransitive subject)
A (transitive subject)
O (object)
template consequence

1SG
ku=
ku=
en=
prefixal throughout; S = A, distinct O

1PL.EXCL
=as
ci=
un=
suffix on S, prefix on A: the side-switch

2SG
e=
e=
e=
one prefix across roles

2PL
eci=
eci=
eci=
one prefix across roles

3
—
—
—
no overt segment; zero-affix analysis disputed

4 / INDEF
=an
a=
i=
suffix on S, prefix on A: the side-switch

The minimal pair inkar 'look' and nukar 'see' shows
why an English translation is an unreliable guide. inkar is an
intransitive activity of directing the gaze with no fixed object; nukar is
transitive and takes a specified object. A fourth-person subject therefore surfaces as the suffix
=an after the intransitive stem and as the prefix
a= before the transitive stem in one sentence nakagawa2024 131 :

inkar=an akus e=an ru-we a=nukar hi kusu | look=4.S then 2SG.S=exist track-POSS 4.A=see NMLZ because | When I looked, since I saw that you were there, ... | The intransitive inkar takes the S-suffix =an; the transitive nukar takes the A-prefix a=. The same side-switch is the template's first ordering diagnostic. | nakagawa2024:131 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 18

Both arguments of a transitive verb are bare, and Ainu draws no formal distinction between a
direct and an indirect object: what a Japanese translation marks as 〜を or 〜に is the same bare
object in Ainu nakagawa2024 127 . A three-place verb such as
kore 'give' takes a subject and two bare objects, all omissible, yet only
two participants can be indexed on the verb — the two-index cap holds even where the clause has
three arguments nakagawa2024 127 ; sato2023b 39 . When a non-third
person is indexed it is the human recipient that the object affix tracks, a secundative pattern
taken up in object-indexing-monotransitive sato2023b 41 .

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | Both non-subject arguments are bare third-person noun phrases; only the 1SG.A subject is indexed. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

Ijäs's learner grammar reaches the same architecture through the contrast of verb pockets and
external slots: the arguments are cross-referenced on the verb first, and a bare clause such as
numan hekaci cep koyki presents both participants as bare noun phrases with
an unmarked third-person verb ijas2023 . Her further statement that the indirect
object of a three-place clause stands between subject and object is a classroom simplification;
the two objects and the subject of epakasnu 'teach' occur in several
orders with one valence frame and no fixed position nakagawa2024 363–365 .

numan hekaci cep koyki | yesterday boy fish catch | The boy caught fish yesterday. | Ijäs, Lesson 3. A two-argument transitive frame with two bare noun phrases and an unmarked third-person verb. | ijas2023

Valence classes and what counts as a verb word

The argument count runs from zero to three, with a contested fourth. Nakagawa and Bugaeva and
Kobayashi use the numeric labels 0-, 1-, 2-, and 3-argument verb precisely to keep the
noun-phrase count apart from the person-affix fact of intransitive versus transitive; Satō,
Tamura, and Ijäs keep the traditional intransitive/transitive/ditransitive terms nakagawa2024 128 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §3 ; ijas2023 .

valence
label
verb-word behaviour
examples

0
zero-place / avalent
no subject or object index
mean 'be cold (weather)', sirpirka 'be fine weather'

1
intransitive
S index only, prefixal or suffixal by person
apkas 'walk', ipe 'eat a meal', pirka 'be good'

2
transitive (incl. copula)
A and O positions; the two-index cap applies
nukar 'see', koyki 'catch', ne 'be'

3
three-place / ditransitive
three bare arguments possible; only two fit on the verb
kore 'give', epakasnu 'teach', locative o 'put in'

4
four-place
rare causativized ditransitives; contested as a class
omarere 'make someone put something in'

The zero-place class is built from non-autonomous roots: weather and ambient predicates such as
mean 'be cold' (< me- 'cold' +
an 'exist') and sirpirka 'be fine weather' take no
participant index nakagawa2024 128–129 . Ijäs presents these as avalent verbs with
the same examples, including the analysis of sir- 'surroundings' and a
non-free me- 'cold' ijas2023 . Where the contributing element is
itself a free noun, the analysis is genuinely open: Ijäs treats apto as
'it rains' as a single avalent unit, but Nakagawa leaves it undecided, since
apto 'rain' is an autonomous noun and the string may be a two-word clause
nakagawa2024 128–130 ; ijas2023 ‹contested›. The same root that gives a
weather predicate often has an intransitive counterpart that does take a subject — climatic
mean 'it is cold' beside experiential merayke 'feel
cold' — a contrast Ijäs marks and Nakagawa lays out as a paired paradigm nakagawa2024 127 ; ijas2023 .

mean cuk ne pe merayke=an ka somo ki no | be.cold.weather autumn COP NMLZ feel.cold=4.S even NEG do ADV | It was a cold autumn, but I did not feel cold. | Zero-place mean indexes nothing; the experiential intransitive merayke indexes its subject with =an. | nakagawa2024:130 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 15, after Kayano 1974a

The copula ne shows that valence is not reducible to the number of person
indexes. It is two-place in the relation 'X is Y' and takes transitive-type subject marking, yet
it admits no object index: okkayo ku=ne 'I am a man' is well formed while
an object-indexed alternative is not nakagawa2024 132 ; shibatani1990 50 . The copula and predicate nominals are developed in copula-ne-predicate-nominals .
Three-place verbs are mostly derived, by the applicative ko- or the
causative -re , the one common base-form member being the locative
o 'put X into a place' nakagawa2024 134 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4 . The valence classes, the small locative-object subclass, and ambitransitivity are the matter
of transitivity-and-valence-classes .

Valency-changing morphology in the template

The valency prefixes and the stem-internal suffixes are the positions that alter the argument
count. Their syntax and semantics belong to the voice part; in the template the relevant fact is
the position each occupies and the direction in which it moves the count nakagawa2024 137–139 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4 .

operation
forms
template position
valence effect

applicative
e- , ko- , o-
prefix before the stem
adds or promotes a non-agent object

productive causative
-re , -e , -te
suffix after the stem
adds a causer

permissive / sociative causative
-yar
suffix after the stem
adds an indirect causer or permits an event

antipassive / detransitive
i-
prefix before the stem
fills the object slot, making a transitive stem intransitive

reflexive / reciprocal
yay- , si- , u-
prefix before the stem
fills or identifies the object slot

noun incorporation
nominal root plus verb
inside the stem complex
fills an argument slot and may detransitivize

A reflexive or reciprocal prefix occupies the object position itself, so a subject index and an
object index for the same participant never co-occur nakagawa2024 183 , and the
antipassive i- absorbs the object of a transitive stem, as in
ruska 'be angry at' → i-ruska 'be angry' bugaevakobayashi2022 §4 ; nam2021 62–64 . The prefix e- is a standing warning
against reading the template off shapes alone: it can be the second-person index, an applicative,
or an affiliative nominal prefix, and only the valence computation and the surrounding morphology
identify it in a given word nakagawa2024 138–139 . The applicatives are developed
in applicative-system-overview , the causatives in
causative-morphological-re-e-te , causee-marking-ditransitive-double-causative ,
and permissive-sociative-causative-yar , the detransitive in
antipassive-detransitive-i , the reflexives and reciprocal in reflexive-yay ,
reflexive-possessive-si , and reciprocal-u , and incorporation in
noun-incorporation-object . Derivation that builds a verb stem without changing its
valence — denominal and deverbal verb formation, and the adverb-forming -no
that turns a property verb into an adverb ( poro → poronno )
— is treated in denominal-deverbal-verb-derivation and adverbializer-no .

The stem zone and verbal number

The stem zone holds the lexical root together with any suppletive or suffixal singular/plural
alternation. Number in Hokkaido Ainu sits on the verb, not the noun: nouns are number-neutral and
read as singular or plural by context — Ijäs notes that cep 'fish' may be
either — while a minority of verbs have distinct singular and plural stems nakagawa2024 124–125, 140–142 ; ijas2023 . The alternation takes three shapes: outright suppletion
( ek / arki 'come', an /
oka 'exist'), the -n / -p
pattern of directed-motion verbs ( san / sap 'go
down'), and the vowel-to- -pa pattern of change-of-state transitives
( tuye / tuypa 'cut') nakagawa2024 146–150 ; sato2008 99–106 .

The two transitivity classes index plurality differently. For intransitives the alternation is
close to agreement with S: a plural subject normally selects the plural stem and a singular
subject the singular stem nakagawa2024 145 . For transitives the plural stem marks
a plural object or a pluralized or repeated event, with the singular stem remaining the default
even where the object is plural; Shibatani, following Durie, treats the alternation as a
selectional restriction on the principally affected argument — S of an intransitive, O of a
transitive — rather than as agreement nakagawa2024 142–145 ; sato2008 101 ; shibatani1990 51–53 . Numerals and quantifiers show why the slot resists a
simple agreement rule: a subject numerically specified as two, three, or sometimes four ordinarily
takes a singular intransitive stem, against counterpatterns with iwan 'six'
and with poronno 'many' that vary by dialect and speaker nakagawa2024 160–162 ‹contested›. The full inventory, the corpus frequencies, and the agreement-versus-selection
debate are the subject of suppletive-verbal-number , and the interaction with person
of person-number-interactions .

ine nispa ek ru-we ne | four rich.man come track-POSS COP | Four rich men came. | A numeral-quantified plural subject takes the singular stem ek; the numeral-to-number relation is variable across dialects and speakers. | nakagawa2024:161 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 90

Debates that shape the template

Four analytical questions bear on how the template is read, and none is settled. The first is the
mechanism behind the valence calculus. Nakagawa's arity assigns every element — base verb, each
derivational affix, each person index — a positive, negative, or zero value, the sum predicting
the verb's remaining bare-noun-phrase positions nakagawa2024 136–139 . Satō argues
that a single arity value fails for the copula, for auxiliaries, and for ditransitives, and
replaces it with three simultaneous layers — an invariant argument-slot count, a macrorole
valency in the sense of foleyvanvalin1984 that the person affixes index, and the
morphological valency of the derived word — while flagging his own scheme as something still to be
justified sato2023b 42–52 ; sato2025b 22–26 ‹contested›. The
template here records position and valence effect without committing to either mechanism.

The second question is the third-person zero. Nakagawa assigns the third person no overt affix and
no arity value, so that the calculus balances; Satō holds that a zero third-person affix is needed
to explain the two-index cap and the impossibility of certain three-participant combinations nakagawa2024 137 ; sato2023b 39 ; sato2025b 23–24 ‹contested›. The transitivity
table above leaves the third-person cells empty and routes the analysis to
person-marking-architecture .

The third question is the outer plural pa . Nakagawa and the Handbook treat
the postposed pa , eastern ci , and the Sakhalin
cognate as a single clitic class distinct from the root-bound suffix; Satō's Chitose data make the
clitic analysis doubtful, since postposed -an pa is rare and a speaker
rejects *yaynu-as pa in favour of yaynupa-as nakagawa2024 156–159 ; sato2025b 25–26 ‹contested›. The fourth question is the four-place class:
Nakagawa lists causativized ditransitives such as omarere as four-place
verbs, while Satō warns that the type is too marginal to support a regular category nakagawa2024 135 ; sato2025b 22–23 ‹contested›.

These debates leave the linear template stable while its interpretation is open. Ainu has very few
bare stems that serve as both intransitive and transitive without overt marking, with
