roski 'stand' effectively the sole instance, so valence alternation is almost always coded by the
derivational positions catalogued above rather than by lability sato2008 97 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §5 . The valence classes and ambitransitivity are pursued in
transitivity-and-valence-classes , and the productive derivation in
valency-voice-overview .

title: Verbal Subclasses: A Valence-Based Word-Class Taxonomy
summary: A word-class-level survey of verb subclasses by valence — intransitive (agentive vs patientive), transitive, ditransitive, and 'complete/incomplete' verbs — with detailed transitivity deferred to Part X.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Verbal Subclasses: A Valence-Based Word-Class Taxonomy
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: A word-class-level survey of verb subclasses by valence — intransitive (agentive vs patientive), transitive, ditransitive, and 'complete/incomplete' verbs — with detailed transitivity deferred to Part X.

A valence-based taxonomy

Verbs are the class of words that head clausal predicates and carry person-marking for their
arguments nakagawa2024 116 ; sato2008 2 . The class inventory and
its formal diagnostics are established in word-class-inventory-and-diagnostics .
Within the verbal class, Nakagawa organizes the subclasses along two partially independent axes:
argument count (how many core nominal arguments the verb governs) and affix set
(which portion of the person paradigm the verb admits)
nakagawa2024 116–117 ; nakagawa2022pos 473 .
sato2008 2–3 works within the same bipartite framework with slightly different
labelling; both follow the scheme of tamura2000 . refsing1986 74–76 places Ainu verbs in a broader macro-class (Verbals) that covers what the present taxonomy
separates into intransitive, transitive, and copular verbs, without further subdivision by
valence internally. The pedagogical account in ijas2023 uses the person-affix set
as its primary classificatory criterion, organizing the verb inventory in the same way.

Hokkaido Ainu verbal subclasses (Nakagawa 2024: 116–117)

subclass
argument count
affix set
representative verbs

intransitive (vi)
1-place (S)
S affixes: ku= , =an , …

arpa go , ek come (SG) , pirka be good ,
mina laugh

transitive (vt)
2-place (A + O)
A + O affixes: ku= (A), en= (O), …

e eat , nukar see , respa rear, raise ,
ramu think about (something)

three-place (vd)
3-place (A + O₁ + O₂)
A + one O affix; second object unindexed when 3rd person

kore give , kar make for , ye say to

copula ( ne )
2-place (A + predicate nominal)
A affixes only; predicate nominal not indexed
ne be, become (copula)

existential ( an / oka )
1-place (S)
S affixes
an exist, be , oka exist, be (alternate / often plural)

negative existential ( isam )
1-place (S) or predicating
S affixes in predicative use
isam not exist, be absent

avalent (0-place)
0-place
no argument-affix slot
impersonal predicates (see transitivity-and-valence-classes )

Two further structural types cross-cut the table: locative-object verbs (場所目的語動詞), which
take a locative phrase in the O position, and serial predicates (連動詞), in which two stems
share argument slots. Both are introduced below and described in depth in
transitivity-and-valence-classes . The valency-changing morphology that moves verbs
between classes — applicatives, causative affixes, the antipassive prefix
i- , and the transitivizer -ka — is treated in
valency-voice-overview .

The person-marking diagnostic

Assigning a verb to a subclass amounts to determining which argument roles its affixes encode.
The Hokkaido Ainu personal-affix paradigm distinguishes three roles: S (sole argument of an
intransitive), A (subject of a transitive), and O (object of a transitive). The full paradigm
and its mixed alignment are set out in person-marking-architecture ; what matters
here is its use as a classificatory probe. A verb whose subject takes S affixes and permits no O
affix is intransitive. A verb whose subject takes A affixes and whose object takes O affixes is
transitive. A verb that takes A affixes on its subject but licenses no O-affix slot is copular.

The first singular provides the sharpest probe because 1SG distinguishes S=A ( ku= ) from O ( en= ). At fourth person the contrast is still more transparent:
the intransitive subject bears the suffix =an , the transitive subject
the prefix a= , and the transitive object the prefix
i= — three distinct exponences for three distinct roles
nakagawa2024 163–167 ; bugaeva2012 471–472 .

Subclass membership is grammatical, not semantic. The verb ramu think about (something)
is transitive — it licenses A and O affixes — even though its meaning is stative. The verb
yaynu think, feel (intransitively) is intransitive for the same semantic domain.
The diagnostic difference is purely morphological. This contrast was highlighted in community
discussion: *sekor a=ramu an is ungrammatical because
ramu requires the transitive A prefix a= , not
the intransitive S suffix =an
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Intransitive verbs

One-place predicates assign a single S role. The class is large and semantically heterogeneous:
it encompasses motion predicates ( arpa go , ek come (SG) ,
san go down, go out , ahun go in, enter ), postural predicates
( as stand ), cognitive and emotional predicates
( mina laugh , yaynu think, feel ), and the large class of property
predicates ( pirka be good , poro be big ,
hure be red , tanne be long ) that in other languages would be
adjectives. All share the S affix set; no O slot is available.

kim ta arpa=an akusu | mountain-ward LOC go=4.S when | When (I) went to the mountains… | arpa 'go' is intransitive; the fourth-person subject is marked by the S suffix =an following the stem. The type-2 locative noun kim 'mountain-ward' directly heads the locative phrase with the case particle ta. | nakagawa2024:246 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 300

Property predicates — stative predicates describing qualities or states — take the identical
S affixes as action intransitives, and head bare predicates without a copula. The third person is
zero, so a property verb in a simple declarative clause carries no overt person affix:

toan nupuri ri | DEM mountain be.tall | That mountain is high. | Shibatani ex. 13b. The property predicate ri 'be tall, be high' heads the predicate with zero marking for 3rd person. No copula intervenes between the subject NP and the property predicate. | shibatani1990:19 | HK

The argument for treating property predicates as intransitive verbs rather than as a separate
adjective class — and the morphological evidence for that analysis — is developed in
no-adjective-class-property-verbs . The predication of property concepts without a
copula is treated in property-concept-predication .

Within the intransitive class the descriptive literature distinguishes two semantic groupings:
agentive intransitives, whose S argument is volitional and agent-like (motion, action,
and activity predicates), and patientive intransitives, whose S argument is an
experiencer or the subject of a state (property predicates, involuntary experience verbs).
Crucially, the basic person-affix paradigm does not encode this contrast morphologically: both
groupings select the same S affix set across all persons
nakagawa2024 126–127 . The agreement system is therefore not split-intransitive
in the morphological sense — S-set assignment depends only on valence, not on the
agent-vs-patient character of the S argument. Whether semantic or lexical criteria support a
deeper Split-S or Fluid-S analysis ‹contested› is examined in
alignment-split-intransitive and alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

Transitive verbs

Two-place predicates assign an A role to their subject and an O role to their object. When both
arguments are non-third-person, each may be person-indexed on the verb. The ceiling is two
indexes per verb — one A index and one O index — and a third argument is never indexed
nakagawa2024 125–127 ; sato2025a 361 . Third-person arguments
surface as bare noun phrases.

kani ku=respa p anakne | 1SG 1SG.A=rear.raise NMLZ TOP | As for those (children) I raised… | respa 'rear, raise' is transitive; ku= marks the 1SG.A subject. The object is a relativized clause headed by the formal noun p, whose internal gap is the relativized third-person object — which is why no O affix appears on the verb. | nakagawa2024:214 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 230

One morphological consequence of transitivity is that verbal number marks plurality of the
object , not the subject. The suppletive pair rayke kill (O.SG) and
ronnu kill (O.PL) signals how many entities are killed, regardless of how many
kill: the intransitive S and the transitive O are treated alike for number marking, in an
ergative-like pattern aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-12) ‹consensus›. The
full system of number-sensitive stems and the pluractional suffix -pa
are treated in suppletive-verbal-number .

Three-place verbs

A lexically small set of predicates takes three argument slots: subject (A), a primary object
(typically a recipient or addressee), and a secondary object (typically a theme or propositional
content). The principal members are kore give , ye say to , and
kar make for someone , along with morphologically complex forms derived from them
sato2023b ; bugaevakobayashi2022 ; kobayashi2015 .

Because the verb carries at most two person indexes, the second object is unindexed when it is
third person. The indexed O slot falls on the recipient or the more animate participant; the
theme or propositional content remains a bare noun phrase. When both non-subject arguments are
third person, only A is indexed:

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | kore 'give' is a three-place verb. Both the recipient hekattar 'children' and the theme topenpe 'sweets' are third person and surface as bare noun phrases; only the 1SG.A subject is indexed by ku=. Were the recipient first or second person, an O affix for that participant would appear on the verb. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

A related pattern arises with the serial construction
aske uk invite (lit. take someone's hand) : its A role indexes on the action
verb uk take, receive while the recipient role indexes on the relational noun
aske hand (in grip) , giving en=aske e=uk 'you invite
me'. Satō calls this type 連他動詞 (serial transitive); it is the clearest case of a wider
class of serial predicates in which argument slots are shared between adjacent stems
sato2008 147 ; aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-17)
‹corpus-confirmed›.

Whether the language is best analysed as secondary-object or secundative for this argument
pattern, and how three-place structure interacts with applicative morphology, is taken up in
transitivity-and-valence-classes and object-indexing-monotransitive .

The copula and the existential verbs

Three predicates hold a special position in the taxonomy: the copula
ne be, become (copula) , the existentials an exist, be and
oka exist, be , and the negative existential
isam not exist, be absent . They function as predication operators and each
admits a distinctive affix set.

The copula ne be, become heads equative and classificatory clauses of the
structure [NP subject — NP predicate — ne ].
Its affix behaviour places it with the transitive class: the subject takes A-type affixes, not S.
At first singular this means ku=ne 'I am X', with the same prefix
ku= that marks the A of a transitive verb. The predicate nominal,
however, carries no O person index regardless of its person or number. Nakagawa groups this under
a separate copular affix set — alongside the intransitive and transitive sets — precisely because
it is A-marked on the subject yet has no object-affix slot
nakagawa2024 116–117 ; sato2008 2–3 .

wen menoko ku=ne | poor woman 1SG.A=COP | I am a poor woman. | Constructed illustration (cf. Nakagawa 2024: 118; Bugaeva ed. 2022, ch. 14: 474). The copula ne takes the A-type ku= prefix for 1SG; the predicate nominal wen menoko 'poor woman' receives no person index.

The copula also covers the translative reading 'become X': the same form serves both equative
and inceptive predication in context nakagawa2024 118–119 . The full treatment
of copular predication and its TAM and evidential extensions is in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

The existential an exist, be is strictly intransitive. It takes S affixes, and
the located entity is its S argument; the locative phrase specifying the location is an oblique
adjunct, not an O. The diagnostic contrast with the transitive existential-locative
un be located in/at (vt) is crisp: an requires a
locative-case frame ( cise or ta an 'is in the house'), while
un takes the location as a direct O and may carry an O person affix
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-09) ‹corpus-confirmed›. The existential
paradigm — an , oka , and their progressive
and resultative extensions — is treated in existential-locational-an-oka .

The negative existential isam not exist, be absent predicates non-existence of
its S argument. In possession frames it serves as the negative counterpart of
kor have, possess . Its inflection and possessed-construction interaction are
treated in negation-existential-possessive-isam and
predicative-possession-clauses .

One community observation bears noting: in certain negative frames
( ka somo ne 'it is not the case that…') and occasionally elsewhere,
ne appears without a person affix. The reason is not settled in the
accessible sources aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2023-12-25) ‹speculative›. The
negation–copula interaction is discussed in copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

Avalent predicates

Nakagawa's arity framework reserves a zero-place (完全 / 0項) category for predicates that take
no referential core argument nakagawa2024 116–117 . The category is defined
formally: a zero-place predicate has no argument-affix slot, not merely an impersonal or
unspecified subject. On the arity account, a clause whose total argument count resolves to zero
takes a complementizer rather than heading a relative-clause nominalization
nakagawa2024 504 , which connects this class to the complementizer-vs-nominalizer
distinction treated in nominalization-p-pe .

The sources read for this chapter did not bank specific lexical members of the zero-place class
nakagawa2024 116–117 . A fuller inventory and the syntactic treatment of avalent
predicates — including weather expressions and impersonal event frames — are provided in
transitivity-and-valence-classes .

Locative-object verbs and ambitransitives

Two structural types cut across the valence-based taxonomy and have word-class consequences.

Locative-object verbs (場所目的語動詞) take a locative noun phrase in the structural
position of the O argument, and the locative receives an O-type person affix in the paradigm.
The subject of these verbs takes A affixes, making them transitive in their affix behaviour even
though their object is a location rather than an entity. The valency of verbs taking place or
space as an argument is examined in kobayashi2020 ;
nakagawa2024 116–117 .

Ambitransitive patterns arise where the same stem alternates between an S-marked
(intransitive) frame and an A-marked (transitive) frame depending on whether an O argument is
present. The alternation is closely related to the productivity of denominal and deverbal
derivation: Ainu intransitive verbs freely convert to noun-modifying and compound-forming roots,
and transitivisers can reinstate a transitive frame
nakagawa2024 267–272 ; sato2016 .

Both types, and the full inventory of valency-alternating morphology, are described in
transitivity-and-valence-classes . The applicative system that promotes locatives
into O position is treated in applicative-system-overview , and the antipassive that
converts transitives to intransitives in antipassive-detransitive-i .

title: The Vigesimal Numeral System
summary: The base-20 cardinal numeral system, its subtractive/additive arithmetic morphosyntax, and its diachrony.
part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Vigesimal Numeral System
Part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
Summary: The base-20 cardinal numeral system, its subtractive/additive arithmetic morphosyntax, and its diachrony.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu numerals fall into three grammatical categories — numeral prenominals
(数連体詞), numeral nouns (数名詞), and a counting-off form (数え上げ) — and the three are
not interchangeable in syntax nakagawa2024 461 . Underlying all three is a
shared set of numeral roots from 1 to 10, with the integer 20 ( hotne )
serving as the base of a vigesimal counting system: even decades are formed
multiplicatively from hotne , while the odd tens (30, 50, 70, 90) are
built subtractively, expressing how many units fall short of the next even decade
nakagawa2024 462–463 ; refsing1986 110–111 .

The vigesimal base sets Saru–Chitose Hokkaido Ainu apart from the surrounding Japanese
decimal system and from several of its own dialects — Asahikawa and Sōya use a decimal
base with the same root hot at the value of ten rather than twenty
nakagawa2024 467 . Sakhalin Ainu is also decimal. A shift in modern usage
away from the subtractive architecture and toward simpler additive or decimal expressions
is documented by ochiai2021 .

This chapter covers the numeral roots and their three form classes, the multiplicative and
subtractive decade structure, the syntax of numeral phrases, the debate over classifier
status, and the dialect geography of the base. Ordinals, the interaction of numerals with
quantifier syntax, and the day- and time-counting constructions are treated in the sibling
chapter numeral-classifiers-ordinals-and-quantifier-syntax ; numeral phrases and
the noun suffix -utar are set out in
nominal-number-utar-transnumerality .

The numeral roots 1–10 and the three form classes

Each numeral value from 1 to 10, and 20, is represented in three paradigmatic shapes. The
prenominal precedes the noun directly; the numeral noun stands after the noun or alone; and
the counting-off form is used for reckoning aloud in sequence
nakagawa2024 461, 464–466 ; refsing1986 110 .

value
prenominal (数連体詞)
numeral-noun, things ( -p/-pe )
numeral-noun, humans ( -n/-iw )
counting-off (Saru/Chitose)

sine
sinep
sinen
sinep

2

tup

tup

asiknep
asiknen
asik

arwanpe
arwaniw

sinepesaniw

—

The suffix -p (after vowel-final stems 1–5) and
-pe (after consonant-final stems 6–10) are instances of the formal
noun p thing treated in concept-form-affiliative-form .
The human counterparts -n and its suppletive partner
-iw (6–10) are the same -n that appears in
the interrogative nen 'who' and in
hempak-iw 'how many people' — see
interrogative-pro-forms
nakagawa2024 464 . The choice between -p and
-n for human referents is weak: -p is
described as "more brusque" but neither is ungrammatical with persons
nakagawa2024 464 . The community observation that the numeral-noun form
rep cannot be placed before its noun — that 'three worlds' must be
re mosir , not
*rep mosir — is consistent with Nakagawa's syntactic description
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-09);
nakagawa2024 461 .

A secondary '1' root ar- appears before certain nouns:
ar-suy (also assuy ) 'once',
ar-itak 'one word'
nakagawa2024 461 . The same element appears in
arwan (7) and in the iteratives arsuy ,
tusuy , resuy 'once, twice, three times'
refsing1986 108 .

The etymology of roots 6–9 is historical, not synchronic. Working from Chiri's
finger-counting data and Patrie's comparative materials, Refsing analyses these as old
"ten-minus" compounds: iwan (6) <
i(ne) + wan "4+ten";
arwan (7) < ar + wan
(where ar is glossed as an old '3' or old '1' — contested);
tupesan (8) and sinepesan (9) as
tu/sine + pesan , or in Patrie's reconstruction
tu-upis / sine-upis , with
upis an old '10' attested in Kuril Ainu
refsing1986 111 . These reconstructions are ‹speculative›; synchronically
the forms are opaque roots. The root hot (20) is analysed as 'a set'
— a full complement of fingers and toes, the somatic basis commonly attributed to vigesimal
systems refsing1986 111 .

The vigesimal counting architecture

The counting system above 10 operates in three zones: an additive zone for 11–29, a
multiplicative zone for even decades, and a subtractive zone for odd decades
nakagawa2024 462–463 ; refsing1986 111 .

11–19 are formed by adding a unit prenominal to the verb
ikasma exceed, surpass followed by wan '10': 11 =
sine ikasma wan , 13 =
re ikasma wan , 19 =
sinepesan ikasma wan . The construction frames each value as the
amount by which the count surpasses the last decade — 'three exceeds ten' nakagawa2024 462 .

re-p ikasma wan to ta | three-NMLZ exceed ten day LOC | rep ikasma wan to ta | on the thirteenth (day) | re-p ikasma wan = 13 (3 + exceed + 10). The noun to 'day' and locative ta follow the numeral phrase. Here the numeral-noun form rep occurs as a free numeral expression rather than in apposition to a counted noun. | ainukoraci1991 | CHI | 萱野志朗, アイヌタイムズ (沙流・千歳)

20 = hotne , the vigesimal base. 21–29
replace wan with hotne :
sine ikasma hotne (21) through
sinepesan ikasma hotne (29).

sinepesan pa ikasma hotne pa | nine year exceed twenty year | sinepesan pa ikasma hotne pa | twenty-nine years old | sinepesan (9) + ikasma (exceed) + hotne (20) = 29. The noun pa 'year/spring' is repeated after each numeral component in this age expression. | ainukoraci1991 | CHI | 平石清隆, アイヌタイムズ (沙流・千歳)

Even decades from 40 to 100 are multiplicative: the unit prenominal
compounds directly with hotne , yielding
tuhotne (2×20=40), rehotne (3×20=60),
inehotne (4×20=80), and asiknehotne
(5×20=100) nakagawa2024 463 . The 100-value
asiknehotnep is independently confirmed ‹corpus-confirmed›
aynucorporadiscord (palestine_2026, aynu-corpora Discord, 2026-06-08).

asikne-hotne-n asir cise or ta uekarpa | five-twenty-HUM.NMLZ new house inside LOC gather | asikne-hotnen asir cise or ta uekarpa | A hundred people gathered in the new house. | asikne-hotne-n = asikne (5) × hotne (20) = 100; the human numeral-noun suffix -n marks the referents as persons. The locative noun or 'place-of' precedes the case particle ta. | ainukoraci1991 | CHI | 萱野志朗, アイヌタイムズ (沙流・千歳)

Odd decades are subtractive. The pattern is
wan + the prefix e- + the next even decade:
wan etuhotne = "ten toward forty" = 30;
wan erehotne = 50; wan einehotne = 70;
wan easiknehotne = 90. The prefix
e- marks the following even decade as the reference point from which
ten units are counted off; this is the defining property that the literature labels
vigesimal subtractive counting (二十進法; 下方算法)
nakagawa2024 463 ; ochiai2021 .

wan e-tu-hotne | ten toward-two-twenty | wan etuhotne | thirty (lit. ten short of forty) | Subtractive odd decade: wan (10) + e- (toward/short of) + tuhotne (2×20=40). The prefix e- is formally identical to the applied-object prefix treated elsewhere in the grammar; its numeral function is directional — marking the following even decade as the target quantity.

The community discussion of the subtractive pattern (aomidori_cs_, aynu-corpora Discord,
2024-05-28) independently corroborates Nakagawa's 30 and 50 formulas; the same contributor
extends the pattern analogically to propose expressions for negative quantities
( *sine e-isam for −1, combining e- with
isam 'be absent'), but that extension is a modern coinage
‹speculative› and lies outside the attested system
aynucorporadiscord .

In the counting-off form, the bare root hot replaces
hotne : 20 = hot , 30 =
wanpe etuhot , 40 = tuhot
nakagawa2024 466 .

For composite numeral-noun phrases above 29, each component stands as an independent noun
form: "31 things" is sinep ikasma wanpe etuhotnep ; "11 loads" is
sinep ikasma wan sike
nakagawa2024 466 . Nakagawa observes that composites above 10 "almost never
appear in recorded classical texts"; the attested vigesimal compounds in the broader corpus
are concentrated in modern アイヌタイムズ prose from the 1990s–2000s
nakagawa2024 468 .

Numeral syntax

Prenominals precede the head noun directly, without an intervening classifier or particle;
numeral nouns follow the head noun in apposition or stand alone; and the counting-off forms
are used only for enumerating in sequence, without an NP head
nakagawa2024 461–465 ; refsing1986 115–117 .

sine okkaypo ek a wa | one youth come PFV CONJ | sine okkaypo ek a wa | A young man came. | The prenominal sine precedes the noun okkaypo directly. No postposition or classifier intervenes. | sato2008:257 | CHI

toonta pon kamuy re-p an ma | there small bear three-NMLZ exist FIN | toonta pon kamuy rep an ma | There are three young bear cubs over there. | The numeral noun rep (re-p, 'three-thing') follows the head noun kamuy in apposition. The prenominal construction *re kamuy rep would be redundant; the head noun takes the prenominal form alone (re kamuy 'three bears') or the postposed numeral-noun alone (kamuy rep 'bears, three'). | sato2008:256 | CHI

The error of placing the numeral-noun form before the noun — *sinep tuki
for 'one cup' instead of sine tuki or
tuki sinep — is explicitly flagged as a common learner mistake
nakagawa2024 465 . The [noun + postposed numeral-noun + verb] construction,
as in pasuy sinep uk 'take one chopstick', is common and has been
noted as productive — arguably more so than its Japanese near-counterpart
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-12-12);
refsing1986 116 ‹corpus-suggested›. Whether a modifier can intervene between
a head noun and its following numeral-noun is undocumented: Satō notes that no such example
appears in his data and flags the question as open
sato2008 256 ‹contested›.

Numerals with the copula ne

A numeral noun combined with the copula ne — treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals — predicates a count or produces a count-adverbial.
The case particle ne here is the translative copula; the numeral noun
takes its ordinary form:

re-n a=ne wa oka=an | three-HUM.NMLZ 4.A=COP CONJ exist.PL=4.S | ren a=ne wa oka=an | Three of us lived. | The numeral noun re-n (three-human) predicates the count via copula ne; the conjunctive wa links to the following existential plural oka=an. The translative copula ne appears here in a count predication, distinct from its use as an anaphoric demonstrative. | nakagawa2024:301 | SAR

A frozen copular numeral produces the adverb
sinenne 'alone' (< sinen +
ne ), and the same pattern yields
tup ne ukosaraye 'divide in two'
refsing1986 117 .

Numerals and plural marking

When a count greater than one is specified with a prenominal, the head noun may take
the associative plural suffix -utar :
sine hekaci 'one child' but
tu hekattar (< hekaci-utar ) 'two children'
refsing1986 117 . The conditions for its omission versus realization, and
its interaction with the transnumerality of Ainu nouns, are set out in
nominal-number-utar-transnumerality .

Small, specified numbers (2, 3) take singular verb agreement; six or more, or unspecified
plurals, take plural agreement aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024-11-17, attributed to Satō) ‹corpus-suggested›; this is consistent with
Refsing's numeral syntax data
refsing1986 117 . The person-number agreement system is described in
nonconfigurationality-pronominal-argument .

Special number uses and lexicalized expressions

Several numeral roots carry extended or bleached meanings in fixed constructions beyond their
cardinal count function.

iwan (6) functions as an intensifier glossable as 'many' in oral
literary registers and fixed expressions. The expression
iwan suy means 'many times', and the form
noiwan is an intensified variant
nakagawa2024 469 .

iwan suy a=i=hotuyekar hine | six time 4.A=4.O=call.out CONJ | iwan suy a=i=hotuyekar hine | (I) was called out to many times | iwan suy: the cardinal 6 is used with the force of 'many/several'. This use is restricted to fixed collocations; a compositional 'six times' reading is also possible in context. | nakagawa2024:469 | SAR

The pair tu / re in oral-literary formulaic
contexts carries the sense 'many' rather than exactly 'two' or 'three':
tu cup ka re cup 'many months',
tu ray onkami / re ray onkami 'bow many times'
nakagawa2024 468 . By contrast, tutko rerko '2–3 days'
genuinely denotes a short span nakagawa2024 468 .

Day-counting uses the combining form -ko :
tutko '2 days', rerko '3 days', then
ine rerko '4 days', asikne rerko '5 days'.
In the last two expressions rerko has bleached from 'three-day-span'
to a general 'days' noun nakagawa2024 465 .

The animal count noun atuyta is lexically restricted to Saru/Chitose
and denotes 10 head of animals in Saru but 20 head in Chitose — a lexical split internal to
the same dialect cluster
nakagawa2024 467 .

The counter and classifier question

Whether -p/-pe and -n/-iw constitute numeral
classifiers depends on the criterion applied. Nakagawa's answer is unambiguous: Ainu has no
numeral classifiers (助数詞) of the Sinitic type; quantification uses the prenominal plus
noun directly, analogous to English counting, and the thing/person suffix pair is instead the
native derivational contrast parallel to Japanese ひとつ/ひとり , a
trait shared with the neighbouring Nivkh
nakagawa2024 461–462, 464 . Refsing explicitly calls
-p(e) and -n/-iw "a rudimentary system of
'classifiers' or 'counters'" and reserves 'numeral' for the bare stem
refsing1986 110–111 . The Handbook goes further, applying the label
"word-classifiers" to to 'X days' and
suy 'X times', and speculates about remnants of an earlier, more
elaborate classifier system — though the passage is labelled as speculation in the source
bugaeva2022 .

The disagreement reduces to a terminological question: Nakagawa's criterion requires
Sinitic-style morphological obligatoriness and categorial specificity (absent in Ainu);
Refsing's and the Handbook's criterion requires only semantic categorization in regular
numeral co-occurrence (present in Ainu)
refsing1986 110 ; nakagawa2024 461 ‹contested›. The fuller
treatment of the classifier debate, the status of to and
suy , and the day-counting constructions are reserved for
numeral-classifiers-ordinals-and-quantifier-syntax .

Dialect variation and the vigesimal-to-decimal shift

The vigesimal base is the defining property of the Saru–Chitose standard but is not
pan-Hokkaido. Two dialect areas use a decimal base, and in those the root
hot takes the value ten rather than twenty.

Asahikawa is decimal above ten: sinehot = 10,
tuhot = 20, rehot = 30, and so on. The Sōya
(宗谷) dialect similarly has wanhot = 100 (decimal
'ten-ten') nakagawa2024 467–468 . Community records confirm that the
collision between vigesimal and decimal speakers produced misunderstandings in commercial
exchange ‹corpus-confirmed› aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord,
2024-12-25; Deleted User, 2024-05-13).

The form for 100 varies markedly across Hokkaido. Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, and Nayoro use
asiknehotne(p) (5×20); Yakumo and Obihiro use
sineik ; Bihoro uses ik ; Sōya uses
wanhot
nakagawa2024 468 .

Sakhalin Ainu is decimal throughout sakaguchi2022 . The root for 10 is
kunkutu , plausibly an Itelmen loan
‹speculative› aynucorporadiscord (antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord,
2024-11-22), and 100 is sinetanku , from Tungusic
tanku
nakagawa2024 468 . The Tungusic and Itelmen contact reflected in these forms
has no counterpart in the Hokkaido system.

A directional shift in modern usage, from the subtractive architecture toward additive or
decimal expressions, is documented by Ochiai
ochiai2021 . The subtractive structure is complex to produce and rare in classical
oral texts nakagawa2024 468 , and the pedagogical grammar of
ijas2023 teaches both the vigesimal and a simplified decimal alternative,
reflecting classroom practice. The direction of change belongs to the sociolinguistics of the
revitalization context; the attested classical system is as described above.

title: The Vowel Inventory and Vowel Realization
summary: The five-vowel triangular system /a e i o u/, the absence of phonemic length in Hokkaido, and allophonic centralization/devoicing.
part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Vowel Inventory and Vowel Realization
Part: Part III — Segmental Phonetics, Phonology, and Phonotactics
Summary: The five-vowel triangular system /a e i o u/, the absence of phonemic length in Hokkaido, and allophonic centralization/devoicing.

The Five-Vowel System

Hokkaido Ainu has five vowel phonemes: /a/, /e/, /i/, /o/, /u/
nakagawa2024 26 ; sato2008 §2.1 ; shiraishi2022 §2 . The inventory is triangular, symmetric, and stable: no Hokkaido dialect reduces it
to four vowels or expands it to six
nakagawa2024 25 . Phonemic vowel length is absent from all Hokkaido dialects,
distinguishing them from Sakhalin Ainu, which maintains contrastive length nakagawa2024 27 . The length/accent diachrony is discussed in pitch-accent-placement-rule and mora-syllable-accent-vs-tone-analysis .

Vowel phonemes of Hokkaido Ainu

phoneme
phonetic target
approximate articulatory description

/a/
[a]
open, unrounded; cross-dialect stable

/e/
[e̞]–[ɛ̝]
mid front, slightly lowered; phonetically equivalent to Japanese え

/i/
[i]
close front, unrounded

/o/
[o̞]–[ɔ̝]
mid back, slightly lowered; phonetically equivalent to Japanese お

/u/
[ɯ̹]
close back, with stronger lip rounding than Japanese /ɯ/; auditorily near [o]

Phonetic Realization

Formant measurements of the five vowels are available from Obata & Teshima (1933; three
male speakers, Asahikawa and Sakhalin, the first acoustic study of Ainu) and Ōhashi (1985; two
female Saru speakers, spectrographic analysis), consolidated and discussed in
shiraishi2022 §2 . A nine-speaker study (Saru, Mukawa, Kushiro) by Tamura &
Motohashi (2002a), also reported in shiraishi2022 §2 , confirms the main
findings: /i/ and /e/ have closely spaced F1–F2 values, and /u/ has a markedly lower F2 than
Japanese [ɯ].

The mid vowels e and o

Neither /e/ nor /o/ occupies the higher position often assumed in traditional transcriptions.
At least in the well-recorded Saru (Hioka Sadamo) speech, /e/ is realized as [e̞] to [ɛ̝]
and /o/ as [o̞] to [ɔ̝] — positions equivalent to the standard Japanese mid vowels え and お
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-11-08; corroborated by the Obata &
Teshima and Ōhashi formant data, per shiraishi2022 §2 ). The close F-space
proximity of /i/ and /e/ accounts for the observation, recurring in older descriptions, that
the two vowels are auditorily difficult to distinguish
shiraishi2022 §2 . Similarly, /u/ and /o/ are hard to separate in unaccented,
lax positions sato2008 §2.1 .

The back vowel u

/u/ is a close back vowel with lip rounding distinctly stronger than Japanese /ɯ/, and a
lower F2, placing it further back in the vowel space than Japanese /u/
nakagawa2024 26 ; sato2008 §2.1 ; shiraishi2022 §2 . Nakagawa characterizes it as 円唇後舌母音 ('rounded back vowel'), and
sato2008 §2.1 notes that /o/ is also protruded, making the distinction
between /o/ and /u/ difficult in casual speech. To Japanese-speaking ears, /u/ sounds close to
[o], which is why early Japanese records transcribed aynu 'person' as
アイノ and inaw 'ritual stick' as イナオ
nakagawa2024 26 .

aynu | person | [ái̯nʊ] | person; human being | The historical Japanese transcription as アイノ reflects the back-rounded quality of /u/, which Japanese listeners perceived as near [o]. | nakagawa2024:26 | HK

Vowel Devoicing

/i/ and /u/ devoice when flanked by voiceless consonants; /e/ is occasionally affected in the
same environment. The phenomenon is well documented across Hokkaido dialects, with the earliest
systematic account in Kindaichi (1931) and detailed treatment in Tamura (1998), both discussed in
shiraishi2022 §2.1 . Representative forms: ci̥se 'house',
su̥tu 'root', se̥ta 'dog'.

The degree of devoicing shows heavy inter-speaker and inter-dialectal variation. Northern
dialects devoice more frequently. A longitudinal comparison reported by Tamura (1998) —
two Saru speakers from different generations — showed the younger speaker (b. 1912)
devoicing approximately 25 times more often than the older (b. 1890) and in a wider
range of phonological environments, including closed syllables under accent and
consecutive vowels ( eci̥ci̥snupehe 'your tears'); this is discussed
in shiraishi2022 §2.1 . The syllable types most prone to devoicing are
si and ci ; the types
tu , pi , and pu are
reported never to devoice — a contrast with Japanese, which devoices ピ and
プ in the same environment shiraishi2022 §2.1 . A Chitose-dialect
recording shows eci=kay realized as [et͡sɨ̥kaj], with devoiced [ɨ̥] in
the ci sequence
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-12-20) ‹corpus-confirmed›. L2 learners
influenced by kana spelling tend to over-devoice, applying devoicing beyond its natural
distribution (Tamura 1998: 55, cited in shiraishi2022 §2.1 ).

The interaction of devoicing with consonant voicing and the /s/ palatalization pattern
is discussed in consonant-inventory and s-palatalization .

Phonemic Length and Allophonic Lengthening

Hokkaido Ainu has no phonemic vowel length. All length observed in performance is allophonic,
arising from two distinct sources nakagawa2024 26–27 ; shiraishi2022 §2.2 .

First, monosyllabic CV words lengthen the vowel in isolation or in pause-final position:
kaː 'thread', toː 'lake',
niː 'tree'. Second, the vowel of the accented syllable lengthens in
polysyllabic words: ˈnuːman 'yesterday',
poˈroː 'big'. Both processes are automatic and "not conducted
consciously" by speakers (Kindaichi 1931: 4, cited in shiraishi2022 §2.2 );
this accent-conditioned lengthening is why the yukar epic was formerly written ユーカラ (accent
on yu ) nakagawa2024 26 .

A grammaticalized emphatic lengthening, distinct from the allophonic process, breaks the
extended vowel with a glottal onset: wen 'bad' →
weʔen 'very bad'; to 'lake' →
toʔo 'faraway' (Kindaichi 1931; shiraishi2022 §2.2 ).
The glottal element here marks the emphatic lexical expansion, not a phonemic long vowel.

Sakhalin Ainu, by contrast, maintains genuine vowel-length oppositions (e.g. ‹SA›
niisah vs. nisah ) corresponding to the
Hokkaido pitch-accent contrast (HA nísap 'suddenly' vs.
nisáp 'shin') nakagawa2024 27 ; nakagawafukazawa2022 §3.1 . The reconstructed diachronic relationship — vowel length as the older opposition, later
replaced by pitch-accent in Hokkaido — is argued at length in
itabashi2001 and discussed in the prosody chapters
pitch-accent-placement-rule .

Vowel Sequences and Coda Glides

Sequences such as ay , aw ,
uy , ew , and
oy are phonetically falling diphthongs — [ai̯], [au̯], [ui̯], [eu̯],
[oi̯] — but their phonological analysis is not as diphthongs in the classical sense. The
elements -y and -w behave as syllable-coda
consonants in morphological tests (they trigger the consonant allomorph of the nominalizer
-pe rather than the vowel allomorph -p :
okay-pe , siw-pe ) and cannot carry accent
independently nakagawa2024 29 ; shiraishi2022 §3 . The full
analysis — including the phonemic status of /y/ and /w/, glide insertion at hiatus, and the
diphthong vs. coda-glide question — is given in glides-w-y . Morphophonological
consequences of vowel hiatus at morpheme boundaries are treated in
glide-epenthesis-hiatus-resolution .

Vowel Frequency

/a/ is the most frequent vowel in the Hokkaido corpus, a point in common with Japanese. /e/ is
markedly more frequent than in Japanese, a characteristic that persists when person-affix tokens
( a= , =an , and related forms) are excluded from
the count
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-09-25) ‹corpus-suggested›. The corpus tools
available for this grammar count word tokens rather than phoneme occurrences, so these frequency
ranks cannot be reproduced from per-segment counts at this writing; the underlying pattern is,
however, consistent with the high frequency of the participial suffix
-e , the applicative prefixes, and the evidential formal nouns all
containing /e/.

The Vowel Co-occurrence Restriction

In two morphological contexts — the suffix that derives transitive verbs from intransitives, and
the vowel suffix that forms the possessed (affiliative) form of nouns — the choice of suffix
vowel is conditioned by the vowel of the root: kay →
kay-e 'break (tr.)', mos →
mos-o 'wake (tr.)', yak →
yak-u 'crush'; possessed tek →
tek-e 'hand', nan →
nan-u 'face' chiri1952 ; shiraishi2022 §4.5 . Chiri (1952) named this pattern 母音調和 ('vowel harmony'), hypothesizing that the suffix
was originally -i (the 3sg possessive), which underwent full
assimilation to the root vowel chiri1952 .

The characterization as vowel harmony is contested. The pattern is confined to two derivational
contexts; it is absent from the plural suffix -pa
( kom-pa , not * kom-po ), from personal prefixes
( ku-kor , not * ko-kor ), and from underived
forms; and no backness or tongue-root feature uniformly characterizes the alternating set
shibatani1990 15 ; vovin1993 43 ; shiraishi2022 §4.5 . The phenomenon is therefore better described as a morphologically restricted
vowel co-occurrence pattern than as productive harmony
‹contested›. The morphophonological analysis of the possessed-form suffix, including the
competing accounts of the assimilation mechanism, is pursued in
affiliative-suffix-morphophonology .

Dialect Variation in Vowel Quality

While the five-vowel inventory is constant across Hokkaido dialects, vowel quality shifts are
attested in individual dialect records. In recordings of the Mukawa dialect (Katayama corpus),
/i/ is realized as a lowered, fronted vowel approximating [e], and /e/ as a more open [ɛ];
the shift is general in the recording, though /i/ retains a canonical [i] realization outside
the affiliative form in some tokens
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, 2024-03-18) ‹corpus-suggested›. The pattern is
consistent with a lowering chain in which the entire front series shifts one step downward;
whether it is a stable feature of Mukawa or reflects idiolectal or recording conditions is not
settled by the available evidence.

The Shizunai (静内) and Samani dialect areas are accentless ( pitch-accent-placement-rule ),
which affects the realization of vowels under accent but does not alter the underlying inventory.
Systematic acoustic comparisons of vowel space across Hokkaido dialect regions remain a gap in
the literature nakagawafukazawa2022 §3 .

title: The Word-Class Inventory and the Noun/Verb Bipartition
summary: The full parts-of-speech inventory of Hokkaido Ainu, the dominant noun/verb split, the morphological and distributional diagnostics that define each class, and noun↔verb category conversion.
part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Word-Class Inventory and the Noun/Verb Bipartition
Part: Part VI — Word Classes and Nominal Morphology
Summary: The full parts-of-speech inventory of Hokkaido Ainu, the dominant noun/verb split, the morphological and distributional diagnostics that define each class, and noun↔verb category conversion.

Principles and the class inventory

Word class in Hokkaido Ainu is determined by two converging criteria: grammatical role in the
clause and formal (morphological) behaviour. Meaning is explicitly excluded from the
classification — words designating properties are verbs, words denoting events can be nouns, and
the same root may function in more than one class without derivation
nakagawa2024 115–116 ; nakagawa2022pos 473 . Classes are
prototype-based and their boundaries are gradient; between canonical members and the periphery
lies a continuum of items sharing some but not all defining properties
nakagawa2024 115–116 .

Three schemes dominate the modern literature. Nakagawa's nakagawa2024 116–117
recognises six classes, distributing auxiliary verbs under particles and treating nominalizers as
a grammatical role rather than a separate class. His English Handbook chapter
nakagawa2022pos 473 adds a seventh by splitting auxiliary verbs out of
particles. Satō sato2008 2–3 , following Tamura's scheme, also arrives at six
but subdivides noun to accommodate nominalizers and numeral nouns as distinct subclasses. Ijäs
ijas2023 follows the six-class scheme in her pedagogical grammar. Refsing's
Shizunai grammar refsing1986 74–76 collapses the inventory into three
macro-classes (Nominals, Verbals, Clitics), treating adnouns as clitics and grouping
conjunctions, interjections, and adverbs together ‹contested›.

class
Nakagawa 2024 (6)
Handbook ch. 14 (7)
Satō 2008 (6)
primary diagnostic

verb 動詞
✓
Verb ✓
動詞 ✓
heads predicate; takes 1–2 person affixes

noun 名詞
✓
Noun ✓
名詞 ✓
heads NP; serves as verb argument; ≤1 person affix (possessive)

adverb 副詞
✓
Adverb ✓
副詞 ✓
modifies VP; not predicate, not argument; objective affix only

adnoun / prenominal 連体詞
✓
Prenominal ✓
連体詞 ✓
modifies NP; not predicate, not argument; no person affix

interjection 間投詞
✓
Interjection ✓
間投詞 ✓
no syntactic relation; stands alone as a complete sentence

auxiliary verb 助動詞
(under particle)
Auxiliary verb ✓
(under particle)
heads predicate but cannot be an independent word

particle 助詞
✓
Particle ✓
助詞 ✓
attaches to autonomous element; expresses grammatical relation

The consensus at the macro level is solid: six or seven classes, with the treatment of auxiliary
verbs as the only top-level disagreement. Two deeper disputes — whether property-meaning words
form an adjective class, and where nominalizers belong — are taken up in the no-adjective
section and the nominalizer section below.

The defining diagnostics

Verbs

A verb can function as the head of a predicate without a copula and indexes its arguments with
person affixes. An intransitive verb takes one affix (the sole argument S), a transitive verb
takes up to two (the transitive subject A and the object O), placed on different sides of the
stem nakagawa2024 116 . Verbs subclassify by valency: 0-argument
existential ( an exist ), 1-argument intransitive ( arpa go ),
2-argument transitive ( e eat ), and 3-argument ditransitive
( kore give ). The full taxonomy is in verbal-subclasses-taxonomy ,
the valency system in transitivity-and-valence-classes , and the person-affix
paradigm in person-marking-architecture .

Property-meaning words form no separate adjective class: they take the same S affix as action
verbs, occur as bare predicates with no copula, and accept verbal derivational morphology.
The two examples below illustrate bare property-verb predicates:

Toan nupuri ri. | DEM mountain be.high | That mountain is high. | shibatani1990:19 | HK

Rera ruy. | wind be.strong | The wind is strong. | shibatani1990:19 | HK

The same 1SG intransitive-subject prefix ku= that marks the action
verb ku=mina 'I laugh' marks the property verb
ku=pewre 'I am young' nakagawa2024 118 .
The full argument for dissolving the adjective class is in
no-adjective-class-property-verbs .

Nouns

A noun heads a noun phrase serving as a verb argument, does not occupy the predicate slot on
its own, and carries at most one person affix — the possessive (transitive-subject) affix,
not the nominative affix of verbs nakagawa2024 116, 214–216 ;
nakagawa2022pos 473, 480 . Four subclasses differ in independence, in
relativisation potential, and in the type of person affix they admit:

subclass
stands alone as S/O
relativised by a clause
person affix type
is a place argument

common noun — concept form
○
○

○

✗ (requires antecedent)

○

only hi , uske

The table synthesises Nakagawa's 表47–48 nakagawa2024 215–216 . The
person-affix asymmetry is the sharpest diagnostic: the
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative form of a common noun takes the
possessive (A-type) affix — ku=tekehe 'my hand' — whereas a locative
noun takes the objective (O-type) affix — en=sam 'my side'
nakagawa2024 215–216, 241 . An affix cannot appear on a bare concept-form
noun: *ku=tek is ungrammatical as a possessive.

Common nouns split by possession type: alienable possession uses a
kor have -construction ( a=kor cip 'my boat');
inalienable possession uses the affiliative form ( ku=tekehe 'my
hand'); an appositive N₁ N₂(concept) construction marks attribute or scope rather than
ownership ( menoko tek 'a woman's hand', not replaceable by a
possessive prefix) nakagawa2024 217–219 . These constructions are developed
in alienable-inalienable-split and concept-form-affiliative-form .

Locative nouns (位置名詞) are a small, closed set expressing relative position ('on, beside,
inside, behind'). Their basic forms require a preceding antecedent NP; their long forms can
head a clause independently. Both take the objective affix and always function as place
arguments, distinguishing them formally from common nouns nakagawa2024 238–248 .
The following example shows the Type-1 locative noun sam beside with the
first-singular objective affix:

en=sam un tup kor oka. | 1SG.O=beside ALLAT move.across CONJ exist | (she) has come over to my side | Nakagawa ex. 281, Chitose. The locative noun sam takes the objective prefix en= (first-singular O), contrasting with the possessive prefix ku= that would appear on an affiliative common noun. Type-2 locative nouns (kim 'mountain-ward', soy 'outside') stand at clause-head without an antecedent. | nakagawa2024:241 | CHI

The full inventory of locative nouns, their long-form morphology, and the Type-1/Type-2 divide
are in relational-and-spatial-nouns .

Hokkaido Ainu nouns carry no grammatical number: the same form serves for one or many, and
number marking is never obligatory nakagawa2024 259 ;
kindaichi1936 30–31 . The formal noun utar companions, people
can optionally mark a plural or associative group; in the Saru dialects its range extends to
inanimates on animist grounds, since all things carry a spirit in the Ainu worldview
nakagawa2024 259 :

a=kor rus utar | 4.A=have fur ASS | my furs | utar marks an inanimate noun (furs); the 4th-person transitive prefix a= encodes the narrative first person. Kindaichi & Chiri 1936: 31 restrict utar to animates unless personified — a genuine descriptive disagreement with Nakagawa's Saru data ‹contested›. | biratori1969 | SAR | 川上まつ子 '和人になった兄' (1986), NAM database nam-archive/kawakami-minwa/13, line 226

Community members have elaborated that utari (formally the affiliative form of
utar ) carries a distinct semantic range — 'family, kinship companions' — with the
possessed form implying a specific relational anchor in a way the bare plural marker does not
(thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord 2025; aynucorporadiscord ; corroborated by
nakagawa2024 251–252 ) ‹contested›. Transnumerality, the animacy-scope
dispute, and the interaction with verb number are in
nominal-number-utar-transnumerality ; the four noun subclasses are in
nominal-subclasses-and-bound-nouns .

Formal (defective) nouns

Formal nouns (形式名詞, also called 名助詞 noun-particles) cannot head an NP without a
preceding modifier and supply the nominal head of a nominalised clause
nakagawa2024 257–258 ; nakagawa2022pos 480–482 . Canonical
members include p thing, one (after V) / pe thing, one (after C) ,
hi fact, time, place , katu manner, state ,
kur person , uske place , and
utar people, companions . The allomorphy
p after a vowel / pe after a consonant is
phonologically conditioned sato2008 177 .

tan-pe anak su sekor a=ye p ne. | DEM-NMLZ TOP pot QUOT 4.A=say NMLZ COP | This is what is called a pot. | Nakagawa ex. 389, Saru. The demonstrative prenominal tan fuses with pe to yield the demonstrative pronoun tanpe; the second formal noun p nominalises the quotative VP su sekor a=ye 'is called a pot', supplying the predicate complement of the copula ne. | nakagawa2024:287 | SAR

Four formal nouns grammaticalise further into the evidential system:
ruwe , siri , hawe , and humi are
concept-form-affiliative-form affiliative forms of the sensory nouns
ru track, path , sir appearance, land ,
haw voice , and hum sound, feeling that become the
inferential, situational, reportative, and sensory evidentials; the full architecture is in
evidential-schema-overview . A productive instrument-nominalisation pattern makes
the clause-head role of formal nouns visible: ani 'with' plus a
transitivised verb plus p yields 'the thing for X-ing with' —
ani e=kanpinuye p 'pencil' (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2026;
aynucorporadiscord ). The full inventory is in formal-defective-nouns
and the nominalizing function in nominalization-p-pe .

Adverbs and postpositional adverbs

Adverbs modify verb phrases without entering the predicate slot or argument structure. They
take neither the nominative person affix nor the possessive affix. Formation types include
bare underived forms ( ramma always , earkinne very );
intransitive verbs plus the adverbialiser -no -ly
( pirkano well , tunasno quickly ); and directionals built from
locative-noun stems ( ekimne mountain-ward ,
esoyne to the outside ) nakagawa2024 273–282 . The
adverbialiser and the degree/comparison system are in adverbializer-no and
adverbs-degree-comparison .

A subclass of postpositional adverbs (後置副詞) takes an antecedent NP and
functions like a postposition while remaining adverb in syntax: it can stand clause-initially
and does not require its argument to be a place NP, unlike a locative case particle
nakagawa2024 280–282 . When the antecedent is pronominal, a postpositional
adverb takes the objective person affix. Canonical members include
sekor thus, quotative , pak until ,
osi after , ani with, by means of , and
tura together with nakagawa2024 280–281 . Their relationship to
the case system is in postpositions-local-case-and-motion-events .

Adnouns and prenominals

Adnouns (連体詞, also called prenominals) modify noun phrases directly and share none of the
verbal properties: they do not head predicates, do not appear as arguments, and take no person
affix. The sole distributional privilege of the adnoun is that a case particle can attach to
it directly, without an intervening formal noun — toan ta 'over there'
joins a demonstrative prenominal with the locative particle directly, while a common noun
requires a formal noun first nakagawa2024 283, 288 . Subclasses include
demonstratives ( tan this , toan that ), numeric
( sine one , tu two ), interrogative
( hemanta what kind of ), indefinite, and general prenominals
( pinne male , matne female , sonno true, real )
nakagawa2024 283 . A prenominal followed by the formal noun
pe thing yields a demonstrative pronoun
( tanpe this thing , toanpe that thing );
the demonstrative system is in demonstratives-anaphora-definiteness .

The property verb pon be small occupies the same pre-noun position as an
adnoun but retains full verbal morphology in the predicate, illustrating the verb/noun
continuum developed further below:

pon hekaci | be.small child | a small child | pon 'be small' precedes the noun without morphological change, in the same attributive position as a true prenominal; in predicate position it takes person affixes and verbal derivational morphology like any intransitive verb. | nakagawa2022pos:475

Particles and auxiliary verbs

Particles attach to autonomous clause elements and express grammatical relations without
entering the predicate slot or argument structure. Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 295 recognises five subtypes: case particles (格助詞 —
ta at/in , wa from , un to ,
peka via , ne become (translative) ), auxiliary particles
(助動詞), adverbial/focus particles (副助詞), final particles (終助詞), and conjunctive
particles (接続助詞). Ainu has no nominative or accusative case particles: subject and object
are identified through word order, person affixes, and context
sato2008 3 ; nakagawa2024 296 . The four locative case
particles all require a place argument and are distinct from postpositional adverbs that can
stand clause-initially nakagawa2024 296, 281–282 .

The Handbook chapter nakagawa2022pos 473 splits auxiliary verbs into a
seventh class on the grounds that they head predicates but cannot stand as independent words.
Items such as rusuy want to and easkay be able to typically
fuse with the preceding verb ( arpa-rusuy=an 'I want to go'), while
the perfective particle a PFV never does; the boundary is gradient rather
than sharp nakagawa2024 291–292 ‹contested›.

Interjections

Interjections stand alone as complete sentences with no syntactic relation to a surrounding
clause nakagawa2024 290 . They form a large and dialect-specific inventory of
greetings ( irankarapte greetings ), calls, acknowledgements, and emotional
expressions. The class is treated in interjections-ideophones-sound-symbolism .

The no-adjective analysis

The most consequential classification decision in modern Hokkaido Ainu description is the
absence of an adjective class. Words translating English adjectives —
hure be red , tanne be long , poro be big ,
pirka be good/healthy , wen be bad ,
mea be cold — are all intransitive verbs
nakagawa2024 118 ; nakagawa2022pos 474 ;
sato2008 2 ; shibatani1990 19 ; refsing1986 76 .
Historically, Kindaichi & Chiri kindaichi1936 123–124 still posited a
形容詞 class with its own person-inflected paradigm; Chiri's later work
chiri1942 rejected the verb/adjective distinction and coined the unified term 用詞
for the merged class; all subsequent description has inherited that move — historically contested, now the
consensus of the modern literature ‹consensus›.

Three arguments converge nakagawa2022pos 474–477 .
Syntactically , property words occupy the predicate slot with no copula, in
exactly the same frame as action verbs. Morphologically , causative
( -re/-e/-ka ) and applicative ( e-/o-/ko- )
affixes attach to property words under the same semantic conditions as to action verbs —
i-ruska 'be angry at something' uses the applicative on
ruska be angry nakagawa2022pos 476 .
Semantically , the same form serves both the stative reading ('be X') and the
inchoative reading ('become X'): poro = 'be big' and 'become big'. This
stative/inchoative identity is general to Ainu intransitive verbs —
ne be; become , siknu be alive; revive — and does not
distinguish property words from action verbs
nakagawa2024 118–119 ; shibatani1990 19 . The inchoative
reading surfaces in imperatives:

tunasno pirka | quickly be.good | Get well quickly! | pirka means both 'be good/healthy' and 'become good/healthy'; the bare imperative form receives the inchoative reading here. *pirka ne for 'become good' is ungrammatical because the copula ne cannot add an inchoative value — pirka already encodes change-of-state as an intransitive verb. | nakagawa2024:119 | SAR

In attributive position, property words precede the noun with no morphological change, in the
same slot occupied by relative-clause verbs: pon hekaci 'small child'
has the same surface structure as arki utar 'people who came', where
arki come.PL is unambiguously a verb nakagawa2022pos 475 . The
residual adjective-like behaviour — the split between the linkers
wa and no in conjoined VPs — reduces to a
semantic (static vs dynamic) distinction in the whole VP, not a difference in the part of speech
of its head nakagawa2022pos 479 . The full argument with all diagnostics is in
no-adjective-class-property-verbs .

Three disputed boundaries

The verb–noun continuum

Intransitive verbs convert to nouns without morphological change: the same form is a predicate
in one clause and a noun-modifier or compound-noun constituent in another, so the verb/noun
boundary is positional and relational rather than marked on the word itself
nakagawa2024 267–272 . Compound-noun formation makes this visible: Nakagawa's
arity-based analysis after kirikae1984 shows that many items classed as N+N
compounds have a verbal non-head — onture-p 'strawberry' (literally
'that which swims upstream') has a 1-argument-verb head
nakagawa2024 267–268 . Community observation has noted that Ainu has very few
coordinate or exocentric pure-noun compounds ( haca-hapo 'parents',
po-at 'womb'); most nominal compound formation is verb-centred, in
keeping with the near-universal convertibility of intransitives to nouns (antitwilight,
aynu-corpora Discord 2026; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›. Compound structure
is in noun-noun-compounding ; nominal and verbal derivation in
denominal-deverbal-verb-derivation .

Conjunctions: a distributed category

No independent conjunction class (接続詞) is set up. Items glossed as conjunctions in earlier
work — akusu then, when , yakun if ,
oraun after that , kusu because ,
yakne as for, if — are distributed between conjunctive particles and adverbs
on the basis of their primary function nakagawa2024 116–117 ;
sato2008 3 . Even Tamura, who had set up a conjunction category, noted that
it was handled as conjunctive particles for convenience
nakagawa2024 117 . Refsing refsing1986 76 keeps
conjunctions, interjections, and adverbs together in one macro-class — the counterpart of the
modern distributed analysis ‹contested›. The same item ( oraun ,
kusu ) receives the label conjunction, conjunctive particle, or
postpositional adverb depending on the analytical framework, a terminological split with no
consequence for the forms themselves. The discourse-connective system is in
conjunctions-discourse-connectives .

Nominalizers: subclass or grammatical role?

The status of nominalizers is the deepest terminological split in the literature. Satō
sato2008 174–182 distinguishes 名詞化辞 (nominalizer) — producing an
internally complete content clause — from 形式名詞 (formal noun) — the head of a relative
clause with a gap; both are noun subclasses in his system. Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 503–504 retains 形式名詞 as the word-class label and uses 補文標識
(complementizer) for the grammatical role a formal noun plays in certain clausal contexts, so
that nominalising a VP and heading a relative clause are two uses of one class, not two
classes. Refsing refsing1986 259–264 uses "nominalizer" more broadly to
cover what Chiri treated as mood morphemes ( ruwe , siri ,
hawe ) as one of four sentence-conversion types. The three analyses are incompatible
at the word-class level, though they agree on the inventory of forms involved ‹contested›.

A diachronic proposal that the affiliative suffix -i derives from
the formal noun hi is Nakagawa's own and flagged as a hypothesis
nakagawa2022pos 493 ‹speculative›. Community discussion has noted that the
absence of a true infinitive in Hokkaido Ainu — where head-marking and
nominalisation-dependent grammar leave no role for a bare verbal noun — means that
pe , hi , and kuni nominalisations with auxiliary-like
predicates handle what many languages express with infinitives or purpose clauses (nukopoli,
aynu-corpora Discord 2024; aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›. The formal-noun
inventory is in formal-defective-nouns and the nominalizing function in
nominalization-p-pe .

title: Written Sources: Grammars, Dictionaries, and the Philological Record
summary: The book-length descriptive tradition, lexicography, and Edo-period manuscript sources underpinning the grammar, with an audit of works that must be cited but are physically absent.
part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Written Sources: Grammars, Dictionaries, and the Philological Record
Part: Part II — Sources, Conventions, Glossing, and Orthography
Summary: The book-length descriptive tradition, lexicography, and Edo-period manuscript sources underpinning the grammar, with an audit of works that must be cited but are physically absent.

The classical grammars

The chronological account of how the descriptive tradition developed — from the Edo-period
glossaries through Batchelor and Kindaichi to the modern typological turn — is given in
history-of-description-research . The present chapter identifies the specific
works this grammar draws on: reference grammars, dictionaries and lexical resources, and the
philological record. Spoken corpus sources are treated in
oral-literature-and-spoken-corpora .
